By letter of 13 January 1997, CCE's General Counsel confirmed in writing that it gave that undertaking whilst approving the Commission's decision to authorise the notified operation without attaching that condition (CCE and the other parties welcome the decision to approve the proposed transaction without condition and I am pleased to confirm that so long as CCE controls CCSB, CCSB shall adopt the undertakings given to the Commission by The Coca-Cola Export Corporation in 1989. We anticipate that this assurance will result in resolving all outstanding issues with the Commission related to this transaction). 103. The substance of that correspondence between the Commission and CCE was thus reproduced in paragraph 212 of the contested decision. It is clear from that paragraph that the Commission noted the undertaking given by CCE without making it a formal obligation within the meaning of Article 8(2) of Regulation No. 4064/89. (In any event, however, the Commission takes note of the fact that CCE undertakes that, so long as CCE controls CCSB, CCSB will adopt the undertakings given to the Commission by The Coca-Cola Export Corporation in 1989. That undertaking would alleviate some of the concerns raised by third parties in the course of the procedure.) 104. It is thus clear from the foregoing that, in adopting the contested decision, the Commission, as it stated in its correspondence with CCE, did not intend to make the authorisation granted conditional upon the undertaking at issue. 105. In any event, TCCC's argument that the undertaking was required by the Commission is contradicted by the fact that a month after the adoption of the contested decision, CCE again proposed to adopt the same undertaking in order, on that occasion, to obtain authorisation for exclusive licensing arrangements concluded between itself and CS which, although an integral part of the notified operation, had to be examined in the light of Article 85 of the Treaty (see letter from CCE to the Commission of 17 February 1997, [e]nclosed in final form as agreed is the undertaking that CCE gives voluntarily in this case, and Commission Press Release IP/97/148). 106. It follows that the contested undertaking has no binding legal effects in the sense that a breach of its terms would not affect the contested decision in any way and would not entail its revocation. Accordingly, it is not an act open to challenge within the meaning of Article 173 of the Treaty, so that TCCC's application must be declared inadmissible in so far as it concerns the legality of that undertaking. The finding that TCCC controls CCE 107. As to the question whether the Commission's finding that TCCC controls CCE constitutes an act open to challenge within the meaning of the case-law cited above (see paragraph 96 above), it must be borne in mind that, in finding that the notified operation had a Community dimension within the meaning of Article 1(2) of Regulation No. 4064/89, the Commission based itself exclusively on the worldwide and Community-wide turnover of CCE and ABGB. Since the turnover of TCCC, as the undertaking concerned within the meaning of Article 5(1) and (4) of Regulation No. 4064/89, was not taken into account by the Commission as the basis for its exclusive authority to monitor the notified operation, the contested finding has no legal effects with regard to the applicants (Case T-3/93 Air France v. Commission [1994] ECR II-121, paragraphs 45 to 47). 108. That conclusion is not undermined by CCE's argument that the contested finding has legal effects in that it obliges it to notify the Commission of any future merger plans because of the combined turnover of itself and TCCC, on penalty of fines under Articles 4 and 14 of Regulation No. 4064/89, and in that it exposes it to the risk of fines under Regulation No. 17 for TCCC's anti-competitive conduct. Like the finding of a dominant position, the finding that TCCC exercises a decisive influence over CCE, within the meaning of Article 3(3) of Regulation No. 4064/89, is determined by a series of factors which are constantly changing, such as the participation of shareholders in annual general meetings of CCE. Consequently, the contested decision does not result in paralysing for the future the nature of the commercial relationship or the structural or other links between TCCC and CCE. Thus, it cannot serve as a basis for involving the applicants in any future proceedings in application of the competition rules because of the control which the Commission asserts TCCC exercised over CCE at the time when the contested decision was adopted. 109. It follows that the applications are inadmissible in so far as they seek the annulment of the Commission's finding that TCCC controls CCE, The alternative claims for annulment by TCCC 110. Since the contested findings of the Commission concerning the definition of the relevant market, the holding of a dominant position by CCSB and the control of CCE by TCCC have no binding legal effects affecting the applicant's interests and thus do not constitute acts open to challenge within the meaning of Article 173 of the Treaty, the claims in the alternative by TCCC seeking the annulment of the contested decision as a whole, in so far as such annulment is necessary to annul those findings, must also be declared inadmissible. 111. It follows from all the foregoing that the applications must be dismissed as inadmissible in their entirety. Costs 112. Under Article 87(2) of the Rules of Procedure, the unsuccessful party is to be ordered to pay the costs if they have been applied for in the successful party's pleadings. Under the third subparagraph of Article 87(4) thereof, the Court may order an intervener other than a Member State to bear its own costs. 113. In accordance with the forms of order sought by the parties, TCCC and CCE must, therefore, be ordered to pay the costs in Cases T-125/97 and T-127/97 respectively. The intervener, Virgin, must bear its own costs. 114. In accordance with Article 87(4) of the Rules of Procedure, the Federal Republic of Germany must bear its own costs.

On those grounds, The Court of First Instance (First Chamber, Extended Composition) hereby: 1. Dismisses the applications as inadmissible. 2. Orders the Coca-Cola Company and Coca-Cola Enterprises Inc. to pay the costs in Cases T-125/97 and T-127/97 respectively. 3. Orders The Virgin Trading Company Ltd and the Federal Republic of Germany to bear their own costs.

Vesterdorf Tiili Pirrung Meij Vilaras Delivered in open court in Luxembourg on 22 March 2000. H. Jung Registrar Pirrung B. Vesterdorf President
juris_court: European Union Court of First Instance of the European Communities (CFI)

title: Back to the Future: Merger Control Outside the Merger Regulation
container_title: Common Market Law Review

sections.title: Introduction
sections.text: 1. Introduction The adoption of the original European Community Merger Regulation in 1989 (1989 ECMR)1 Council Regulation (EEC) 4064/89 of 21 Dec. 1989 on the control of concentrations between undertakings, O.J. 1989, L 395/1. was a hard-fought political compromise capping almost 25 years of discussion and a 16-year legislative process. Member States’ reservations about the Commission’s ambitious proposals included suspicions (or hopes) that the Commission’s decisions would be motivated by industrial policy considerations. But Member States also had strong, and strongly different, views about the procedural features of a European merger review system, including the proper division of powers between the Commission and national competition authorities (NCAs) and how the new system could guarantee legal certainty and avoid unnecessary layers of bureaucracy for cross-border consolidation in the run-up to the creation of the internal market in 1993. Compromise was possible only because the 1989 ECMR included a number of limiting principles with respect to the Commission’s jurisdiction and process. First and foremost, the Commission’s competence was to be limited to a relatively small number of large concentrations whose European dimension could not be in doubt. The turnover thresholds of the 1989 ECMR ended up being orders of magnitude higher than foreseen by the Commission’s first proposal in 1973 (1973 Proposal)2 COM(73)1210 final of 18 July 1973, Proposal for a Regulation (EEC) of the Council on the control of concentrations between undertakings, available at <aei-dev.library.pitt.edu/12286/1/12286.pdf> (all online sources last visited 2 Aug. 2024). – and required a clear European nexus.3 See Art. 4(1) and (2) of the 1973 Proposal, which required prior notification of concentrations involving undertakings whose aggregate (global) turnover for the last financial year exceeded “one thousand million units of account”, provided that the target undertaking had a (global) turnover of more than “thirty million units of account”. By contrast, Art. 1(2)(a) and (b) of the 1989 ECMR required that the undertakings collectively exceed EUR 5 billion in turnover and each achieved Community-wide turnover of at least EUR 250 million. Second, the turnover thresholds provided a clear dividing line between concentrations with a European dimension, for which the Commission would have exclusive competence (“one-stop shop”), and transactions below these thresholds, falling into the competence of Member States, subject to narrowly defined and strictly time-limited corrective mechanisms at the insistence of certain Member States. Third, in contrast to the 1973 Proposal, which would have given the Commission the ability to “call in” concentrations falling below the turnover thresholds, the 1989 ECMR provided exclusively for an ex-ante review mechanism with mandatory notification and an obligation to suspend the concentration’s implementation. Parallel, or ex-post review under what is now Articles 101 and 102 TFEU4 References to Arts. 101 and 102 TFEU in this article should be read as including their predecessor versions (Arts. 81 and 82 and before that Arts. 85 and 86). was also to be excluded to the greatest possible extent by virtue of the disapplication of the Commission’s procedural regulations (now Regulation 1/2003) and a Commission declaration that it would “normally” not seek to apply the substantive provisions on the basis of what is now Article 105 TFEU.5 See the Commission’s statement accompanying the ECMR’s adoption, Nineteenth Report on Competition Policy (1990), available at <op.europa.eu/en/publication-detail/-/publication/c75ce23d-7128-4db4-8e25-b0bcb4809322/language-en>, at 268. Fourth, the Commission’s review process was to be governed by tight deadlines – in contrast with the far more relaxed timelines contained in the 1973 Proposal. The amendments of 1997 (1997 ECMR) and recasting of the Merger Regulation in 2004 (EUMR) did not fundamentally change these procedural principles, except that the proliferation of national merger control regimes shifted the focus from filling enforcement gaps at Member State level to limiting parallel merger reviews and avoiding potentially conflicting outcomes. However, in key jurisdictional and procedural respects, contemporary merger enforcement practice is a far cry from what the EU legislature envisioned in 1989, and in many ways resembles the 1973 Proposal that the Council decisively rejected. First, the requirement that the Commission’s jurisdiction be clear and foreseeable, and limited to large concentrations with sufficient European nexus, has been gutted by the Commission’s decision – more than 30 years after the 1989 ECMR’s entry into force – to repurpose the referral system under Article 22 EUMR in order to be able to effectively review any concentration (even those too small to be reviewed by any national authority), including ex-post after closing.6 See C(2021)1959 final, “Commission Guidance on the application of the referral mechanism set out in Article 22 of the Merger Regulation to certain categories of cases”. The General Court blessed this reversal in its Illumina judgment,7 Case T-227/21, Illumina v. Commission, EU:T:2022:447. citing, among other things, the EUMR’s alleged objective of permitting “effective control of all concentrations with significant effects on the structure of competition in the European Union”.8 Ibid., para 140. Second, whereas during the legislative process the Commission went out of its way to assure the Council that it would not jeopardize the legal certainty that the ECMR was to bring by the residual application of Article 102 TFEU below the thresholds, it argued in Towercast 9 Case C-449/21, Towercast, EU:C:2022:207. that the EUMR “does not preclude the application of Article 102”, and that its enforcement by NCAs formed part of a “safety net” to ensure that problematic concentrations do not fall between the cracks of “imperfect” turnover thresholds.10 McNelis, “Below-threshold mergers can be pursued as dominance abuse, Tower Cast tells EU’s top court – Insight”, available at Mlex, <content.mlex.com/#/content/1390632/below-threshold-mergers-can-be-pursued-as-dominance-abuse-towercast-tells-eu-s-top-court>. With success, as the Advocate General and ultimately the Court of Justice agreed and breathed new life into the 50-year old Continental Can precedent.11 Case C-6/72, Europemballage Corporation and Continental Can Company v. Commission, EU:C:1973:22. Third, and perhaps most significantly as an every-day practical matter, the Commission has effectively rendered the EUMR’s statutory timetable irrelevant. While paying lip-service to the “need for speed” where it favours its own decision-making,12 See e.g. Case C-170/02 P, Schlüsselverlag Moser, EU: C:2003:501, paras. 33–34; Case C-42/01, Portugal v. Commission, EU:C:2004:379, paras. 51–53 ; and Case C-202/06 P, Cementbouw, EU:C:2007:814, paras. 36–37 and 39. the Commission’s current practice of insisting on months of pre-notification,13 See section 5.1 infra. liberally using information requests to “stop the clock” after notification,14 See section 5.2 infra. and increasingly demanding “up-front buyer” remedies artificially extending the EUMR’s suspension period15 See section 5.3 infra. means that overall merger review timelines are now typically two to three times longer than envisioned by the legislature. They are indeed far longer than the generous time limits in the Commission’s 1973 Proposal (three months for a Phase I investigation and an additional nine months for a Phase II investigation) that were firmly rejected by the Council at the time. The world has changed significantly since the ECMR’s adoption in 1989, and, as a matter of policy, it is appropriate for the Commission to be equipped with the right tools to meet new challenges resulting from technological and economic developments. But, for the reasons explained in this article, this is no justification for allowing the Commission to expand the EUMR’s scope and practical implementation beyond anything the EU legislature could have imagined when adopting and amending it. The structure of this article is as follows. Section 2 examines the key procedural guiding principles emerging from the legislative process leading up the adoption of the 1989 ECMR, and later the 1997 ECMR and 2004 EUMR. In particular, it focuses on the Council’s strong resistance to concepts the Commission repeatedly proposed – concepts that the Commission has nevertheless been reviving in its merger review process in recent years. Section 3 discusses the Commission’s recent initiative to repurpose Article 22 EUMR as an instrument to capture any concentration that might merit scrutiny – irrespective of EUMR or national merger thresholds. It takes issue with key aspects of the Commission’s positions and the General Court’s ruling in Illumina, in particular the notion that Article 22 EUMR was designed to correct inherent shortcomings of the ECMR’s turnover-based thresholds. Section 4 examines the Towercast judgment. Among other things, it notes the contrast between the Commission advocating for enforcement of Article 102 TFEU below the EUMR’s thresholds by NCAs and having promised self-restraint in order not to jeopardize legal certainty during the legislative process leading up to the ECMR. Section 5 shows how the Commission’s practice has managed to extend the review periods under the EUMR in a way that arguably undermines the legislature’s intentions to have concentrations examined in the shortest possible time frame, and raises serious questions with respect to the principle of good administration. Section 6 concludes with the hope that the Court of Justice will correct at least some of the aberrations in the Commission’s merger review practice to re-align it with the Commission’s mandate.

sections.title: Key procedural aspects discussed during the legislative process leading to the adoption of the ECMR and subsequent amendments
sections.text: 2. Key procedural aspects discussed during the legislative process leading to the adoption of the ECMR and subsequent amendments 2.1. Genesis of the ECMR When the Treaty of Rome – establishing the European Economic Community – was signed in 1957, merger control did not feature among the core competition law provisions. This is noteworthy given that the founding Member States were well aware of the instrument, having included comprehensive sectoral merger control provisions in Article 66 of the Treaty of Paris establishing the European Coal and Steel Community. Moreover, the need to prevent the creation of monopolies through mergers had been recognized in the 1956 Spaak Report.16 Spaak, “Intergovernmental Committee on European Integration. The Brussels Report on the General Common Market” (Spaak Report) (1956), at 55–56, avaialble at <aei.pitt.edu/996/1/Spaak_report_french.pdf>. The following decade was characterized by significant economic growth and consolidation,17 The number of concentrations rose from 173 in 1962 to 612 in 1970, meaning that the number of concentrations increased by three and a half times in that period. See Explanatory Memorandum, at 4, in the 1973 Proposal cited supra note 2. and the Commission realized that merger review was an important element of effective competition policy. It thus explored applying the equivalent of today’s Articles 101 and 102 TFEU18 For ease of reference, this article cites the provisions of the TFEU also for time periods during which the corresponding provisions of the Treaty of Rome and the Treaty of Amsterdam were in force. to concentrations.19 See Communauté Economique Européenne, “Le problème de la concentration dans le marché commun”, (1966) Série concurrence 3, available at <aei.pitt.edu/40303/1/A4698.pdf>. In 1973, the Court of Justice confirmed in Continental Can that the prohibition of abuses of a dominant position could, at least as a matter of principle, capture concentrations if they lead to an already dominant undertaking strengthening its dominant position: “[a]buse may therefore occur if an undertaking in a dominant position strengthens such position in such a way that the degree of dominance reached substantially fetters competition, i.e. that only undertakings remain in the market whose behaviour depends on the dominant one”.20 Case C-6/72, Continental Can, para 26. See Case C-449/21, Towercast, para 52, also discussed infra in section 4. Shortly after the Continental Can judgment, the Commission adopted its 1973 Proposal.21 The 1973 Proposal, cited supra note 2. The proposal’s jurisdictional reach was broad. Parties were to notify all concentrations with an aggregate worldwide turnover of the equivalent of EUR 1 billion, provided that the target had a (worldwide) turnover of more than EUR 30 million, as long as one of the two parties was “established in the common market”.22 Ibid., Arts. 1(1), 4(1) and (2). The Commission’s explanatory memorandum explicitly discarded market-share thresholds as a trigger for notification due to the lack of legal certainty.23 Ibid., at p. 12. Even below these thresholds, the Commission was to have the power to examine transactions, subject to a very limited safe harbour,24 Ibid., Art. 1(2): aggregate (worldwide) turnover of less than EUR 200 million and combined shares in any relevant market in a Member State of less than 25%. without any explicit distinction between proposed and already completed concentrations. But undertakings also had the option of voluntarily notifying transactions falling below the thresholds if they wanted to obtain legal certainty.25 Ibid., Art. 4(4). The 1973 Proposal’s timelines were generous: the Commission was to have up to three months (extendable by agreement of the parties) to conclude its initial investigation and a second-phase investigation of an additional nine months.26 Ibid., Art. 17. The time limit was subject to an agreement by the parties to extend the period, as well as situations in which the Commission had to request information by formal decision. The 1973 Proposal contained no possibility of referrals of transactions from and to Member States. It explicitly excluded the application of Regulation 17 (the predecessor of Regulation 1/2003) to all concentrations, and thus the Commission’s ability to apply the equivalent of Article 102 TFEU.27 Ibid., Art. 20. The 1973 Proposal sparked a protracted legislative process characterized by the Council’s consistent push-back to the Commission’s proposed powers.28 As the 1989 ECMR was to be based on the predecessor provision of Art. 352 TFEU, its adoption required unanimity in the Council (and only consent from the European Parliament). The Council did not act on the proposal despite the Commission amending it three times.29 COM(88)87 final of 25 April 1988, Amended Proposal for a Council Regulation (EEC) on the control of concentrations between undertakings, at pp. 4–5, available at <aei.pitt.edu/12232/1/12232.pdf>. Member States disagreed on fundamental aspects of the substantive test, as well as the division of competence between the Commission and the Council, but also on procedural aspects.30 Schwartz, “Politics as usual: The history of European Community merger control”, (1993) Yale Journal Of International Law, available at <core.ac.uk/download/pdf/72837596.pdf>. Against the background of the fast approaching 1992 deadline for the creation of the single internal market, and the increasing proliferation of national merger control regimes,31 Member States with a merger control regime before 1989 were France (adopted in 1977), Germany (adopted in 1973), Greece (adopted in 1977), Ireland (adopted in 1978) and the UK (adopted in 1965). Member States without a merger control regime in 1989 were Belgium, Denmark, Italy, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Portugal and Spain. the Commission re-engaged with the Member States and presented a new proposal in 1988 (the 1988 Proposal).32 COM(88)734 final, “Amended Proposal for a Council Regulation (EEC) on the control of concentrations between undertakings” (1988), available at <eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:51988PC0734> (1988 Proposal). Key changes vis-à-vis the 1973 Proposal included the requirement that the concentration have a “Community dimension” (requiring that at least two of the undertakings concerned have their principal (or at least “substantial”) activities in different Member States), and the removal of the Commission’s power to “call in” transactions below the thresholds.33 Ibid., Art. 1(2). Still, the discussions with the Council remained difficult. The final text adopted in 1989 contained numerous important changes vis-a-vis the 1988 Proposal. With respect to procedure, the Council found agreement on the following key principles. First, in line with the principles of subsidiarity and legal certainty, the Commission was to have exclusive competence only for concentrations of a truly European dimension, based on clear-cut turnover thresholds, whereas Member States remained exclusively competent to review concentrations falling below those thresholds.34 As the Commission subsequently put it in its 1996 Green Paper COM(96)19 final, “Community Merger Control. Green Paper on the Review of the Merger Regulation” (1996 Green Paper), available at <eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:51996DC0019>: “[t]he allocation of cases between the Community and the Member States in the area of merger control was thus inspired by the same principles that underpin the notion of subsidiarity” and “[t]he application of the ‘one-stop shop’ principle to concentrations with a Community dimension is related to the notion of subsidiarity: exclusive control at Community level is justified in view of the scale and effects of such transactions”, paras. 24 and 30. The EU legislature opted for purely turnover-based thresholds as they guaranteed the highest level of legal certainty due to their objective, clear and predictable nature.35 While the market share thresholds were included in Merger Regulation proposals from 1973, 1981 and 1984, the 1988 Proposal eventually dropped the concept as a result of consistent criticism. It did so in full awareness of the downsides – due to its rigid nature not all potentially problematic concentrations would be reviewable – but it accepted these downsides for the benefit of legal certainty and predictability. As summarized by Lord Brittan, at the time European Commissioner for Competition:“The turnover threshold is a necessarily arbitrary way of defining which concentrations have sufficient impact on the Community as a whole to merit decision by the Commission rather than by Member States. Alternative tests have been considered over the years, but the turnover test is the only one which is both reasonably certain in its application and not excessively complex …”.36 Brittan, “Competition policy and merger control in the single European market”, (1992) University of Cambridge research centre for international law, at 33. As mentioned above, the ECMR thresholds were substantially higher than in the Commission’s 1973 Proposal, and also provided for a clear jurisdictional nexus, requiring at least two undertakings to have turnover in the EU of at least EUR 250 million. Second, the strict division between Commission and Member State competence37 The ECMR’s aim to clearly divide competence was also recognized by the ECJ in, amongst others, Case C-170/02 P, Schlüsselverlag Moser, para 32; Case C-42/01, Portugal v. Commission, para 50, and Case C-202/06 P, Cementbouw, para 35. was subject to two narrowly defined exceptions: the so-called “German clause” (Art. 9 ECMR), which enables a Member State to request the Commission to refer a concentration with a European dimension to its NCA to the extent that the concentrations affects national or local markets;38 On the “German clause”, see Berg, “New EC Merger Regulation: A first assessment of its practical impact, the Symposium on European Competition Law”, 24 Northwestern Journal of International Law and Business (2004), at 689–691. and the so-called “Dutch clause” (Art. 22(3) ECMR),39 Brittan, op. cit. supra note 36, at 52 and 53 on Art. 22(3) ECMR: “This may look like a general exception to the one stop shop principle. But it is not, and the Regulation would not have been adopted if any such provision had been included.” Brittan adds that “the clear division of tasks brought about by the Regulation will mean that there will be no scope for argument about jurisdiction between the Commission and Member States. The turnover threshold was chosen as a criterion for that very purpose.” inserted at a late stage of the negotiations at the request of the Netherlands, Belgium and Italy, none of which had an operational competition law system at the time. Somewhat ironically, the push for Article 22(3) ECMR was a consequence of the Commission’s efforts to tie its own hands regarding the residual application of the equivalent of Articles 101 and 102 TFEU to concentrations falling below the ECMR thresholds. Indeed, the Commission first proposed to do this by way of a specific block exemption, and later by stating that it would not intervene against concentrations not exceeding certain thresholds, or against concentrations having their main impact in a single Member State, as these types of concentrations could be deemed not to appreciably affect trade between Member States.40 See the Commission’s statement accompanying the ECMR’s adoption, Nineteenth Report on Competition Policy cited supra note 5, at 268. This, in turn, was perceived to create an enforcement gap in Member States without their own competition laws. In summarizing the Council’s intentions in adopting Article 22 ECMR, Lord Brittan emphasized that “the Commission may intervene only at the request of a Member State, and not of its own motion”, and that the narrowly-worded provision “would not permit the Commission to deal with mergers below the threshold on a general basis, even if it were inclined to evade the spirit of the threshold provision in this way”.41 Brittan, op. cit. supra note 36, at 42. Third, consistent with the jurisdictional division between the Commission and NCAs, the ECMR did not give the Commission the power to intervene in concentrations falling below the ECMR’s thresholds. The notification and suspension obligations and the Commission’s power to intervene were thus fully congruent and the ECMR based on a system of exclusive ex-ante review. As Lord Brittan put it, the legislature understood that it is easier to “deal with eggs rather than omelettes” if the Commission’s intervention was required.42 Ibid., at 27. Fourth, already during the first discussion of the 1973 Proposal in the Council, Member States stressed the need for the shortest possible deadlines given undertakings’ legitimate interest to gain legal certainty about their proposed concentration, and the obligation to suspend implementation pending approval. The result was a much more compressed timeline than the Commission had initially proposed. The Council gave the Commission “at most” one month to conclude its first-phase investigation43 Art. 10(1) of the 1989 ECMR. and an additional four months if it chose to initiate proceedings. The implementation of concentrations was automatically suspended only for a three-week period following notification, and the Commission had to take an affirmative decision to extend that period in appropriate cases.44 Ibid., Art. 7(1) and (2). The ECMR did not provide for any “voluntary” extension of deadlines in agreement with the parties, something that the Commission had insisted on in every one of its proposals.45 Art. 17(1)(a) of the 1973 Proposal cited supra note 2. See also Art. 9(2) in 1988 Proposal cited supra note 32. The ECMR also imposed a corresponding burden on undertakings, requiring them to notify proposed concentrations within a week of the conclusion of a binding agreement or announcement of a public bid.46 Art. 4(1) of the 1989 ECMR. 2.2. The 1997 ECMR amendments After the ECMR’s adoption, the Commission continued to push for lower turnover thresholds. In a 1993 report to the Council, the Commission concluded that there were strong arguments in favour of a substantial reduction of the thresholds. However, in light of Member States’ resistance, it refrained from making a formal proposal.47 COM(93)385 final, “Report from the Commission to the Council on the implementation of the Merger Regulation” (1993), e.g. at 21–22, available at <eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:51993DC0385>. In its 1996 Green Paper, the Commission again proposed a substantial lowering of the worldwide and EU thresholds, stressing the importance of the “one-stop shop” and enlisting the plight of companies facing multiple filings given the proliferation of national merger regimes.48 1996 Green Paper cited supra note 34, at 2 which states that: “The information available to the Commission suggests that thresholds of ECU 2 billion [world-wide] and ECU 100 million [Community-wide]...would be more appropriate.” As a “second-best” solution to the general lowering of the thresholds, “it could be provided that cases of multiple notification below the current thresholds would only be controlled by the Commission”. While the Green Paper also proposed an amendment of Article 22(3) ECMR to allow multiple Member States to refer a concentration to the Commission,49 Ibid., para 97. it did so in a single paragraph. Indeed, Article 22 ECMR was not particularly controversial at the time, and there were very few such referrals in the early 1990s (all by Member States without an operational national competition law system).50 See e.g. Case COMP/M.278, British Airways/Dan Air (referred by Belgium in 1992) or Case COMP/M.553, RTL/Veronica/Endemol (referred by the Netherlands in 1995). Given continued Member State opposition to a general lowering of the thresholds, the Commission’s subsequent proposal (1996 Proposal) introduced the possibility that the Council could change any aspect of the thresholds with qualified majority.51 Art. 1(1) of COM(96)313 final, “Proposal for a Council Regulation (EC) amending Regulation (EEC) No 4064/89 of 21 December 1989 on the control of concentrations between undertakings” (1996), available at <eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:51996PC0313(02)>. The Council eventually agreed to introduce an alternative set of turnover thresholds to capture in the abstract situations in which multiple filings would be typically required (Art. 1(3) ECMR)52 In addition to a combined global turnover of more than EUR 2.5 billion, each of at least two of the undertakings had to have turnover of more than EUR 100 million in the “European Community”, as well as the undertakings would need to meet the combined and individual thresholds of more than 100 and EUR 25 million, respectively, with regard to at least three Member States. – thresholds that were still substantially higher than those contemplated in the 1996 Green Paper. The 1997 ECMR also extended the purpose of Article 22. The Council recognized that following the introduction of national merger regimes in most Member States, the problem was no longer an enforcement gap at Member State level but rather the opposite, namely ensuring that multiple national reviews did not lead to conflicting outcomes.53 COM(2001)745 final, “Green Paper on the Review of Council Regulation (EEC) 4064/89” (2001 Green Paper), available at <eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:52001DC0745&from=EN>. In particular, see para 86: “The amendments that took effect in 1998 were intended to give Art. 22(3) a new function as a means of allowing two or more Member States to make joint referrals to the Commission where they felt that the Commission was better placed to act. Thus, the intention was to strengthen the application of Community competition law in cases with cross-border effects, to strengthen the one-stop shop principle and to alleviate the problem of multiple filings.” Article 22 ECMR was thus amended to allow for referrals to the Commission by more than one Member State, and to impose a suspension obligation for concentrations not yet put into effect once a referral request was made.54 Art. 22(3) and (4) es revised by the 1997 ECMR. 2.3. The 2004 EUMR In 2004, the 1997 ECMR was substantially revised, in particular with the aim of achieving a more economic approach to merger policy. Contrary to the Commission’s earlier intentions,55 2001 Green Paper, cited supra note 53, paras. 34–42. the Commission’s 2002 Proposal did not seek a further reduction of the turnover thresholds. The Commission acknowledged that the latter “continue to function effectively as proxies for those cases that are most appropriately dealt with at the Community level”.56 Explanatory Memorandum, para 11, in COM(2002)711 final, “Proposal for a Council Regulation on the control of concentrations between undertakings” (2002 Proposal), available at <eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/PDF/?uri=CELEX:C2003/020/06&from=EN>. Recognizing the importance of “short and definitive deadlines for the assessment of notified concentrations”,57 2001 Green Paper, cited supra note 53, para 190. Explanatory Memorandum, para 74, in the 2002 Proposal, ibid., which states: “… the benefits of the time limits provided for in the Merger Regulation should certainly be preserved ….” the Commission proposed – and the Council accepted – several amendments to introduce somewhat more flexibility into the EUMR’s timelines, namely the elimination of the (in any event unenforced) obligation to notify concentrations within one week of conclusion of a binding agreement, the ability to notify transactions prior to the conclusion of a binding agreement, calculating time lines in working rather than calendar days, lengthening Phase I from one month to 25 working days, the introduction of consensual extensions in Phase II, and the automatic extension of the suspension obligation until the Commission’s final decision on the merits. As with previous reforms, the 2004 EUMR reform saw the Commission’s efforts to expand its jurisdiction run into opposition from the Council. The 2001 Green Paper advocated the establishment of automatic Commission competence, even for transactions that fell below the existing turnover thresholds, when three or more Member States had to be notified (“three plus system”). This system, however, was not even introduced in the 2002 Proposal. Similarly, the Commission’s proposal that the referral by one or more Member States is sufficient to establish automatic competence of the Commission, under certain conditions, never made it into the final text of the 2004 EUMR. Indeed, along with the ability of undertakings to “opt into” Commission jurisdiction if their concentration required notifications in at least three Member States (Art. 4(5)), the 2004 EUMR merely introduced a new Article 22(5), which provides that the Commission “may invite” one or more Member States to make a referral request. The Explanatory Memorandum to the Commission’s 2002 Proposal clarifies that Article 22(5) EUMR refers to invitations “after the case has been notified” at national level, whereas the “pre-notification referrals mechanism should only be triggered by the merging parties themselves”.58 Explanatory Memorandum, para 28, in the 2002 Proposal cited supra note 56. It does not appear that at the time, Article 22(5) EUMR was perceived as changing any fundamental aspect of the delineation of jurisdiction between the Commission and Member States.

sections.title: Soliciting referrals from NCAs without jurisdiction – The Commission’s repurposing of Article 22 EUMR
sections.text: 3. Soliciting referrals from NCAs without jurisdiction – The Commission’s repurposing of Article 22 EUMR 3.1. The Commission’s pivot Member States have historically used the possibility of making Article 22 EUMR referrals sparingly, both before and after the reforms of 1997 and 2004. A prominent example is the Dutch Government’s referral of the acquisition of Dutch Toys “R” Us stores by Blokker. The Commission accepted the referral, identified competition concerns in the Netherlands, and ordered Blokker to divest the Toys “R” Us stores.59 Case COMP/M.890, Blokker/Toys “R” Us (II). This was the intended application of Article 22, as the Netherlands did not have a national merger control regime at all at that point. Until very recently, the issue of whether Member States with national merger regimes could, as a legal matter, refer concentrations falling below the applicable thresholds was not at the forefront of competition policy discussions. The Commission claims that it had developed a consistent, albeit informal, practice of “discouraging national authorities from referring [such] cases”,60 Speech of Margrethe Vestager at International Bar Association 24th Annual Competition Conference, “The future of EU merger control” (2020), available at <ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/speech_20_2884>. but there does not appear to be any evidence that Member States took the view that they were legally entitled to make such referrals and tried to persuade the Commission to accept them. At any rate, it does not appear that any such “practice” changed even as the public discussion began focusing on whether the EUMR’s turnover thresholds were still adequate to capture all problematic concentrations, in particular, the acquisition of nascent competitors without material revenues by incumbents in technology and pharmaceutical industries, sometimes referred to as “killer acquisitions”.61 On the question of killer acquisitions, see Cunningham, Ederer and Ma, “Killer acquisitions”, 129 Journal of Political Economy (2020), at 649–702, available at <papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3241707>, and Pike, “Start-ups, killer acquisitions and merger control”, (2020) OECD Competition Papers, available at <papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3597964>. The Commission launched a public consultation in 2016 on a possible reform of the EUMR thresholds, but only three of 15 respondent NCAs considered that there was any enforcement gap.62 SWD(2021)66 final, “Commission Staff Working Document. Evaluation of procedural and jurisdictional aspects of EU merger control” (2021), in fn. 110, available at <competition-policy.ec.europa.eu/document/download/fec9441a-3fca-4d51-851f-6a0e22a52b35_en?filename=SWD_findings_of_evalution.pdf>. Subsequently, Germany and Austria amended their merger control laws to introduce transaction-value thresholds capturing high-value acquisitions of targets largely irrespective of their existing turnover.63 See Section 35(1a) of the German Act against Restraints of Competition and Section 9(4) of the Austrian Cartel Act. Statements by a senior Commission official at the end of 2018 appeared to confirm that the Commission did not view itself competent to accept referrals from Member States without jurisdiction, but drew comfort from the legislative changes in Germany and Austria.64 Comments by K. Dekeyser, Acting Director “Policy and Strategy” at the European Commission’s Directorate General for Competition, in “EU’s merger-regime review honing in on thresholds, official says” (2018), MLex, available at <content.mlex.com/#/content/1048956/eu-s-merger-regime-review-honing-in-on-thresholds-official-says>. It thus came as a surprise to the competition law community when, in September 2020, Commissioner Vestager, citing a perceived enforcement gap due to turnover-based thresholds not allowing the Commission to capture concentrations where the target’s revenues do not reflect its competitive significance,65 Speech of Margrethe Vestager, cited supra note 60. rejoiced that the solution had been “hiding in plain sight”, namely to change the Commission’s alleged past practice of discouraging referrals from Member States without jurisdiction. This would “not happen overnight” as “we need time for everyone to adjust to the change”, but “if all goes well”, she hoped to be able to put the new policy into effect in the middle of 2021.66 Ibid. As it happened, the Commission applied the new policy much sooner, namely to the reacquisition by Illumina of cancer screening company GRAIL – a transaction announced 10 days after Vestager’s speech. The transaction did not require notification in any Member State as GRAIL had not yet achieved any turnover in the EU. Following third-party complaints, the Commission issued a letter to Member States under Article 22(5) EUMR, inviting them to refer the transaction in late February 2021.67 Case T-227/21, Illumina, para 12. The French competition authority, subsequently joined by various other NCAs, took up the Commission’s invitation, and the Commission accepted the referral on 19 April 2021.68 Ibid., para 19. Clearly with the Illumina/GRAIL transaction in mind, the Commission issued its Guidance on Article 22 EUMR on 26 March 2021 (Guidance).69 C(2021)1959 final, “Communication from the Commission. Commission Guidance on the application of the referral mechanism set out in Article 22 of the Merger Regulation to certain categories of cases” (2021), available at <eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A52021XCO331%2801%29>. Rather than discouraging referrals from NCAs without jurisdiction, the Commission would now, in fact, actively encourage Member State referrals in certain circumstances using its powers under Article 22(5) EUMR.70 Ibid., para 11. This is particularly the case where “the turnover of at least one of the undertakings concerned does not reflect its actual or future competitive potential”.71 Ibid., paras. 19–20 (listing a number of examples that suggest broad application of this concept, before pointing out that this list “is provided for purely illustrative purposes. It is not limited to any specific economic sector or sectors and cannot be deemed in any way comprehensive”). The fact that a transaction had already closed was irrelevant and there was no specified time limit if the concentration’s implementation was not in the public domain, or the merger raised particular competition concerns.72 Ibid., para 21. The Guidance invites merging parties to voluntarily come forward with information about their intended transaction, stating that the Commission “may” in such cases give them an early indication that it does not consider it a candidate for referral.73 Ibid., para 24. According to the Commission, “[t]his change in the current practice does not require a modification of the relevant provisions of the Merger Regulation”.74 Ibid., para 11. Rather, “[i]t is clear from the wording, the legislative history and the purpose of Article 22 of the Merger Regulation, as well as from the Commission's enforcement practice, that Article 22 is applicable to all concentrations, not only those that meet the respective jurisdictional criteria of the referring Member States”.75 Ibid., para 6. 3.2. The General Court’s endorsement of the Commission’s approach On appeal, the General Court endorsed the Commission’s new policy in its Illumina judgment76 Case T-227/21, Illumina. At the time of writing, the General Court judgment was still under appeal before the Court of Justice (Case C-611/22 P, Illumina v. Commission). However, in the meantime, the Art. 22 referral policy is actively used by the Commission to review transactions that fall outside the thresholds of the EUMR or national merger control thresholds. See the Commission Daily News of 18 Aug. 2023, “Mergers: Commission to assess the proposed acquisition of Autotalks by Qualcomm”, available at <ec.europa.eu/commission/presscorner/detail/en/mex_23_4201>. based on a literal, historical, contextual and teleological interpretation of Article 22 EUMR.77 Case T-227/21, Illumina, para 183. The General Court’s key arguments relate to the historical and teleological interpretation. According to the Court, “[t]he objective of [the EUMR] is to permit effective control of all concentrations with significant effects on the structure of competition in the European Union”.78 Ibid., para 140 (emphasis added). To achieve this objective, the EUMR must be interpreted as including “effective corrective mechanisms” to address the inherent inadequacies of turnover thresholds in terms of capturing potentially problematic transactions. In the words of the Court, Article 22’s referral mechanisms “are an instrument intended to remedy control deficiencies inherent in a system based principally on turnover thresholds which, because of its rigid nature, is not capable of covering all concentrations which merit examination”.79 Ibid., para 142. 3.3. A critique of the Commission’s and General Court’s approach A point-by-point critique of the Commission’s and the General Court’s arguments is beyond the scope of this article, but the following general observations can be made. As a starting point, it is striking that the powers that the Commission is now found to have possessed all along are very similar to those the Commission sought in its 1973 Proposal – powers that the Council clearly refused to give to the Commission. This is particularly the case for the ability to “call in” transactions below the thresholds. According to its Guidance, the Commission can and will now use Article 22(5) EUMR to call in for review any concentration that it deems worthy of scrutiny, as long as it finds a willing NCA to play the game. This includes transactions already closed by the parties in full compliance with applicable competition rules. The Commission is now doing exactly what Lord Brittan acknowledged the Commission had no legislative mandate to do, namely dealing “with mergers below the threshold on a general basis” and “evad[ing] the spirit of the threshold provision in this way”.80 Brittan, op. cit. supra note 36, at 42. Indeed, the Commission has developed a practice of systematically monitoring transactional activity, and, in some cases, contacting undertakings with extensive requests for information that in many respects resemble a Form CO. Moreover, just like under the 1973 Proposal, the Commission’s purported power to “call in” concentrations is not subject to turnover thresholds that would ensure a material European nexus. As illustrated by the Illumina/GRAIL transaction, in which the target had zero revenues in the EU, this approach is liable to invite the kind of controversy over the extraterritorial application of the EUMR that the Council sought to avoid when agreeing the thresholds in 1989.81 Goldman, Montag and Pecman, “Statement of Concerns regarding the jurisdictional issues arising in the European Commission’s position in relation to the Illumina/GRAIL transaction”, (2023) Concurrences, available at <www.concurrences.com/en/review/issues/no-3-2023/foreword/statement-of-concerns-regarding-the-jurisdictional-issues-arising-in-the#:~: text=On%20December%205%2C%202022%2C%20the, transaction%20on%20September%206%2C%202022>. Indeed, from the perspective of the merging parties, the Commission’s instrumentalization of Article 22 EUMR is even more problematic than its 1973 Proposal. The latter at least allowed them to gain legal certainty for concentrations below the thresholds by making a voluntary notification to the Commission. As explained further below, there is no comparable “one-stop shop” mechanism for parties to obtain legal certainty under the Commission’s new policy. Surprisingly, the General Court’s examination of Article 22 EUMR’s legislative history relies entirely on Commission (rather than Council) documents post-dating the ECMR’s adoption, and omits any mention of the legislative process in the run-up to the ECMR.82 See e.g. Case T-227/21, Illumina, para 98 (relying on a passage in the Commission’s subsequent 1996 Green Paper and its own reading of Recital 29 of the ECMR), which is no substitute for analysis of the legislature’s intentions in 1989. In para 72 of his recent Opinion in Joined Cases C-611 & 625/22 P, Illumina and Grail v. Commission, EU:C:2024:264, A.G. Emiliou calls this approach “particularly perplexing in this present case”. The Court’s attempt to base its interpretation on the 1997 ECMR reform is also unpersuasive. As explained above, the amendments to Article 22(3) ECMR, and the insertion of the corresponding Recital 11, were clearly designed to strengthen the “one-stop shop” principle by addressing the “multiple filing problem” resulting from the proliferation of national merger control regimes. The “multiple filing problem” does not exist where no Member State has jurisdiction. The consistent theme of the EUMR’s legislative history, including the 1997 ECMR reform, has been the Commission’s desire to expand the regulation’s scope by lowering the thresholds, and the Council’s resistance to those attempts. Against this background, the notion that the Council implicitly agreed in the context of the 1997 ECMR reform (or, for that matter, the 2004 EUMR reform) to a system in which the Commission could effectively reduce the turnover thresholds to zero by engineering a referral from a sympathetic Member State is rather fanciful. Indeed, the Commission’s position on appeal before the ECJ was that Article 22’s broad scope was already implicit in the 1989 ECMR.83 See Opinion in Joined Cases C-611 & 625/22 P, Illumina and Grail, para 73. For the same reason, it appears unpersuasive to draw conclusions about the Council’s intentions from the Commission’s considerations on how to best expand its jurisdiction in the case of multiple national filings in the run-up to its 2002 Proposal.84 Cf. Case T-227/21, Illumina, paras. 104–114. In particular, the Commission’s decision not to propose a mandatory “three plus system” (which would have automatically conferred a European dimension on concentrations notifiable in at least three Member States) because of potentially divergent interpretations of national merger notification thresholds,85 Ibid., paras. 108–109. Even if the Council had explicitly shared those concerns, this, in itself, would not support the Court’s view, as the Commission retains discretion as to whether to accept referrals under Art. 22(3) EUMR, and it is thus not the case that the Commission is “forced” to take a position on the interpretation of national merger control laws. is not an indication that the Council tacitly agreed to change the very purpose of Article 22 EUMR and to make the question of national jurisdiction wholly irrelevant.86 More generally, it is striking that throughout the judgment the General Court relies exclusively on documents authored by the Commission to deduce the will of the legislature, but at the same time dismisses inconvenient Commission statements because they were made after the 2004 EUMR reform (See Case T-227/21, Illumina, para 115). The reliance on Kesko v. Commission (T-22/97, EU: T:1999:327) is also misplaced as the case did not concern the Member States’ competence (at the time Finland had no merger control regime), but rather whether the authority making the request was entitled to do so under Finnish law (which clearly the Merger Regulation has nothing to say about). The Commission’s new policy is not a crisis response to a sudden flurry of referrals from Member States. Rather, in the author’s experience it involves the deliberate and systematic use of the Commission’s ability to issue “invitations” to Member States under Article 22(5) EUMR in order to identify transactions that are not notifiable in any Member State and to engineer a referral of concentrations that may be of interest to the Commission. However, as mentioned above, the 2002 Proposal introduced Article 22(5) EUMR to address situations where a transaction had already been notified to at least one Member State in order to encourage joint referrals to the extent possible. As regards the General Court’s teleological interpretation, it is at odds with the legislative history to claim that the EUMR’s objective is to permit effective control of all concentrations with significant effects on the structure of competition within the European Union87 Case T-227/21, Illumina, para 140. and that Article 22 is intended as an “effective corrective mechanism” for the “deficiencies inherent in a system based principally on turnover thresholds”. As discussed above, the EUMR’s high turnover thresholds have always been a feature, not a bug of the Regulation. Recital 11 of the EUMR refers to the term “corrective mechanism” not in the context of turnover thresholds and the need to plug enforcement gaps, but in the context of determining the best placed authority, while stressing the principles of subsidiarity, legal certainty, and the “one-stop shop” principle. If the Council had ever agreed with the need to “correct” any enforcement gaps resulting from the EUMR thresholds, it would have simply given the Commission the power to “call in” concentrations below the thresholds that the Commission sought in its 1973 Proposal. But it did not. Moreover, any “deficiencies” in merger enforcement across the EU are not the result of the EUMR thresholds, but of the consequence of Member States’ legislative choices about their national merger control regimes, and any such deficiencies are most appropriately addressed by Member States changing their thresholds – as, for example, Germany and Austria have done. Just as Article 9 EUMR, Article 22 constitutes an exception to the system of clear ex ante allocation of competencies between the Commission and NCAs, and, as the General Court determined in Royal Philips with respect to Article 9(2)(a) EUMR, such exceptions should be interpreted restrictively.88 Case T-119/02, Royal Philips Electronics NV v. Commission, EU: T:2003:101, para 354. The General Court also fails to fully appreciate the impact of the subsidiarity principle, which is explicitly mentioned in four different recitals of the EUMR.89 Recitals 6,8, 11 and 14. While recognizing that the principle remains applicable to the EUMR,90 Case T-227/21, Illumina, para 160 (noting that even though the competition rules are now (post-Lisbon Treaty) an exclusive competence of the Union pursuant to Art. 3(1)(b) TFEU, the EUMR is based (also) on what is now Art. 352 TFEU so that the exception Art. 5(3) TEU does not apply). the General Court dismisses its relevance by insisting that the review of concentrations that are not notifiable in any Member State concerns an action that “cannot” be achieved by the Member States,91 Case T-227/21, Illumina, para 163. and points to the territorial scope of the Commission’s review under Article 22 EUMR being limited to the territory of the requesting Member State.92 Ibid., paras. 164–165. Both points are not persuasive. It is not true that Member States cannot take action against concentrations with limited turnover in that Member State – they have simply made a legislative choice not to require such transactions to be notified. There are perfectly respectable policy reasons for such a choice, ranging from giving the owners of small- and medium-sized enterprises the possibility to monetize their investment, ensuring that NCAs use their scarce administrative resources in an efficient way, and avoiding the complications of extraterritorial enforcement. Indeed, even very recently, Member States have chosen to increase their domestic turnover thresholds for precisely those reasons.93 See e.g. Germany, 10th Amendment to the Act against Restraints of Competition, entered into force on 19 Jan. 2021, increasing the second domestic turnover threshold from EUR 5 million to EUR 17.5 million, available at <www.bundeskartellamt.de/SharedDocs/Meldung/EN/Pressemitteilungen/2021/19_01_2021_GWB%20Novelle.html>; Amendment to the Austrian Cartel Act, entered into force 10 Sept. 2021, available at <www.bwb.gv.at/fileadmin/user_upload/PDFs/Cartel_Act_2005_Sep_2021_english.pdf>. The notion of a “jurisdictional sandwich” – in which the Commission is responsible for scrutinizing the very largest and the very smallest mergers, and the NCAs mergers of intermediate size – seems inherently odd. Even if one were to assume – as the General Court seems to do – that an NCA can simply override the national legislature’s policy choice to endow the Commission with jurisdiction, where before the Commission had none, this also undermines the policy choices of other Member States. Many, if not most, product markets extend beyond the territory of a single Member State, and in a fully integrated internal market it will often be impossible to design remedies limited to the territory of a single Member State. By way of example, an Article 22 EUMR referral of a merger of two German companies (not notifiable in Germany as a result of a policy choice by the German legislature, as well as those of other Member States) by the French competition authority to the Commission may well lead to a prohibition decision if the markets in question are EU-wide, notwithstanding the ostensible limitation of the Commission’s jurisdiction to the concentration’s impact in France. The General Court also rejects the argument that the Commission’s new approach undermines the principle of legal certainty and goes even further to claim that “only” the Commission’s interpretation of Article 22 EUMR “ensures the necessary legal certainty and uniform application” of the provision.94 Case T-227/21, Illumina, para 178. The Court again relies on the potential vagaries of interpreting national merger control rules. But this is a form of legal uncertainty inherent in any national merger regime and one that undertakings have always had to contend with,95 Luxembourg was the last Member State in the European Union to adopt a merger control regime in Aug. 2023. In over three decades since the ECMR’s adoption, Luxembourg has not made a single referral to the Commission. and not the result of any affirmative Commission action. By contrast, it is inevitable that the untethered ability of any NCA to refer any concentration to the Commission, and the Commission taking an active role in soliciting such referrals based on open-ended criteria, significantly increases legal uncertainty. Indeed, past judgments have emphasized the principle of legal certainty in the context of allocation of jurisdiction for merger review. In Cementbouw, the Court of Justice stressed the need for “precise and objective” criteria to determine jurisdiction because “concern for legal certainty implies that the authority having competence to examine a concentration must be able to be identified in a way which is foreseeable”.96 Case C-202/06 P, Cementbouw, para 38. In Illumina, the General Court interprets this as applying only to the notification obligation and possible fines for failing to make a formal notification.97 Case T-227/21, Illumina, para 180. But this ignores the risk for undertakings to have to divest the company they have already acquired, likely at a significant discount that may well be more punitive than a possible fine for having failed to notify. The General Court points to an undertaking’s ability to obtain legal certainty since Member States must make referrals within 15 working days of the concentration having been “made known” to them.98 Ibid., para 181. But, at the same time, the General Court’s highly restrictive interpretation of “made known” immediately takes that modicum of legal certainty away again. It interprets “made known” as requiring the “active transmission of relevant information to the [Member State] concerned...contain[ing] sufficient information to enable that [Member State] to carry out a preliminary assessment of the conditions laid down in the first subparagraph of Article 22(1)”.99 Ibid., para 204. The General Court appears to confuse legal certainty for undertakings – which the EUMR’s jurisdictional rules and short time limits are designed to protect – with the purported difficulties facing NCAs. Indeed, the General Court cites potential concerns of NCAs feeling unduly pressured to make precautionary referral requests100 Ibid., paras. 206 and 210. or even “to carry out continuous and diligent monitoring of the press and public announcements throughout the world” to track M&A activity in order to identify potentially problematic deals,101 Ibid., para 210. when, in fact, national legislatures gave their NCAs no such mandate with respect to transactions below national merger thresholds. In practical terms, in order to exclude any risk of a referral, merging parties must proactively submit sufficient information to each and every NCA in the EU – a de facto requirement to notify 27 NCAs. This makes a mockery of the European legislature’s very aim of eliminating multiple national filing requirements, in particular in the 1997 ECMR and 2004 EUMR reforms, and to “simplify[y] administrative procedures, thereby enabling both competition authorities and companies to minimise the costs of merger control”.102 2001 Green Paper, cited supra note 53, para 4. It is also extraordinary when considering that the Council rejected the Commission’s 1973 Proposal allowing it to “call in” transactions, even though the latter provided for a voluntary, centralized “one stop” notification to the Commission (with the same time limits as for obligatory notifications) for those undertakings that valued the resulting legal certainty.

sections.title: Revival of the residual application of 102 TFEU – Towercast
sections.text: 4. Revival of the residual application of 102 TFEU – Towercast As explained in the introduction, the uncertainty created by the Continental Can judgment was a facilitating element for the ECMR’s adoption, and the latter designed to remove this uncertainty by declaring it the only legal instrument to be applied to concentrations.103 The 1989 ECMR, cited supra note 1, Recital 8. For more than 40 years, the question of whether NCAs (as opposed to the Commission) could apply Articles 101 and 102 TEFU to mergers remained dormant as a practical matter. Advocate General Kokott summed up the general consensus in a footnote of her Opinion in Austria Asphalt, stating that the previous practice “whereby concentrations between undertakings were sporadically reviewed against [Article 101/102 TFEU]...became obsolete with the entry into force of the separate EU merger control provisions which are now contained in the EC Merger Regulation”. 104 Opinion in Case C-248/16, Austria Asphalt, EU: C:2017:322, para 37 and fn. 18. See also ibid., para 35 (“[b]oth the EC Merger Regulation and the related Regulation No 1/2003 serve ultimately to implement the competition rules for the internal market which are contained in Article 101 and 102 TFEU, only one of those regulations being capable of application at any one time”). The Court agreed with the Advocate General in that case that extending the EUMR’s application to non-full function joint ventures previously under sole control of one of its parent companies would needlessly extend the EUMR’s reach – while at the same time limiting the scope of Regulation 1/2003 – even though such JVs might actually lead to spill-over effects in breach of Article 101 TFEU. The Court gave no consideration to the residual application of the Article 101 TFEU by NCAs in this context. Similarly, in Ernst & Young, the Court seemed to accept that application of the EUMR implied the automatic disapplication of Regulation 1/2003, and, on that basis, rejected a more expansive interpretation of the stand-still provision under Article 7(1) EUMR.105 Case C-633/16, Ernst & Young, EU: C:2018:371, paras. 55–58. Again, it did not discuss whether NCAs’ intervention could help close any potential “enforcement gap” resulting from such disapplication. Nonetheless, Advocate General Kokott, and ultimately the Court, revived the Continental Can case law in Towercast, holding that Article 21(1) EUMR did not preclude NCAs from applying Article 102 TFEU to concentrations falling below national merger control thresholds.106 Opinion in Case C-449/21, Towercast, EU: C:2022:777, para 39; Case C-449/21, Towercast, para 33. In reaching its conclusion, the Court relied on the wording, legislative history and general context and purpose of Article 21(1),107 Case C-449/21, Towercast, paras. 30–39. as well as Recital 7 of the EUMR.108 Ibid., para 38: “Articles [101 and 102 TFEU], while applicable,...are not sufficient to control all operations which may prove to be incompatible with the system of undistorted competition envisaged in the Treaty”. The Court pointed to the EUMR being based (also) on Article 103 TFEU, and thus designed to “give effect to the principles set out in Articles 101 and 102 TFEU”.109 Ibid., para 40. It then emphasized more general points concerning the direct effect of Article 102 TFEU,110 Ibid., paras. 44 and 51. the absence of any “exemption” comparable to Article 101(3) TFEU111 Ibid., para 45. and the non-exhaustive nature of the list of practices mentioned in Article 102 TFEU.112 Ibid., para 46. The Court also rejected the notion that to preserve legal certainty, its judgment should apply only prospectively.113 Ibid., para 62. The Court’s only concession to legal certainty is a slightly narrower definition of “structural abuses” than is apparent from Continental Can: it clarifies that the “mere finding that an undertaking’s position has been strengthened is not sufficient for a finding of abuse”, since “it must be established that the degree of dominance thus reached would substantially impede competition” (emphasis added).114 Ibid., para 52. The Court also seems to limit the doctrine’s scope to situations in which the target company is active on the same product and geographic market as the dominant acquirer, i.e. to horizontal mergers.115 Ibid., para 52 (NCA is to verify that “a purchaser who is in a dominant position on a given market and who has acquired control of another undertaking on that market”)(emphasis added). In contrast, A.G. Kokott also identified acquisitions for companies in upstream, downstream or neighbouring markets as potential candidates for the application of both Art. 22 of the Merger Regulation as well as Art. 102 TFEU by NCAs; see Opinion in Case C-449/21, Towercast, para 48. Towercast can be seen as consistent with the line of case law stressing the need to preserve Article 102 TFEU’s effet utile, not just by national courts but also by NCAs.116 See Case C-681/11, Schenker & Co. and Others, EU:C:2013:404. For a critical view, see Völcker, “Ignorantia legis non excusat and the demise of national procedural autonomy in the application of the EU competition rules: Schenker”, 51 CML Rev. (2014), 1497. The Court did not explicitly embrace policy arguments related to the alleged need to close perceived enforcement gaps, such as the Advocate General’s reference to “killer acquisitions”117 Opinion in Case C-449/21, Towercast, para 48. or the Commission’s argument that the residual application of Article 102 TFEU is a necessary part of the “safety net” to correct deficiencies of turnover-based thresholds. Towercast is thus not comparable to the General Court’s judgment in Illumina and its attempt to rewrite the legislature’s intentions when adopting the ECMR. Indeed, unlike the General Court’s ruling in Illumina, Towercast is arguably compatible with the principle of subsidiarity on which the ECMR was premised, and relatively less detrimental to legal certainty for transactions as it addresses a narrower category of particularly severe and permanent impediments to effective competition. As regards subsidiarity, the Council’s persistent resistance to the Commission’s attempts to lower the EUMR’s thresholds is premised on the assumption that Member States are better placed to enforce the competition rules below those thresholds. The EUMR is agnostic as to whether Member States do so by adopting national merger control rules or through the residual application of Article 102 TFEU. In fact, Towercast’s recognition of NCAs’ ability to apply Article 102 TFEU only further undermines the argument that Article 22 EUMR should be interpreted in a way as to close any remaining enforcement gaps. As described in section 2 above, at the very origin of Article 22 ECMR was a concern by Member States without domestic competition law systems that a lack of Commission enforcement of Article 102 TFEU could lead to an enforcement vacuum. Now that such a vacuum has been filled not only by national merger control rules, but also by the Court’s recognition that NCAs can apply Article 102 TFEU to concentrations below the thresholds, there is even less justification for the expansive application of Article 22 EUMR advocated by the Commission and General Court in Illumina. It therefore seems somewhat curious that the Commission advocated for the recognition of NCAs’ powers under Article 102 TFEU in Towercast.118 Another odd aspect of the Commission’s position is that uniquely in the field of merger control, it creates a situation in which NCAs can now apply Art. 102 TFEU, while the Commission is deprived, by virtue of Art. 21(1) EUMR, of the mechanisms provided in Regulation 1/2003 to ensure coherent application of the EU competition rules. Indeed, the Commission seems to have argued before the Court that Art. 21(1) EUMR should not be read as including Art. 5 of Regulation 1/2003, which empowers NCAs to apply Arts. 101 and 102 TFEU in the first place and thus ensures symmetry between the NCAs’ and the Commission’s powers; see Case C-449/21, Towercast, para 47. As regards legal certainty, the EUMR was arguably not designed to create a system for providing such certainty to concentrations falling below the thresholds. Obviously ex-post interventions by NCAs under Article 102 TFEU will create greater uncertainty for undertakings contemplating transactions than the absence of such a possibility. However, given the restrictive conditions set out in Continental Can, and particularly in Towercast, as well as NCAs’ generally limited enforcement resources and the difficulties of investigating mergers under Article 102 TFEU, one could expect that such interventions will remain rare and limited to cases with a particularly strong nexus to the NCA’s jurisdiction. The Belgian Competition Authority’s successful interim measures case against Proximus’ acquisition of edpnet119 See Belgian Competition Authority Press Release 10/23, “The Belgian Competition Authority opens an ex officio investigation into a possible abuse of dominance by Proximus in the context of the takeover of edpnet, in application of the Towercast case law”, available at <www.belgiancompetition.be/en/about-us/actualities/press-release-nr-10-2023>. is a good example of such an unusual situation and contrasts with the Commission’s vague and open-ended criteria for candidate cases for Article 22 EUMR referrals, and its systematic efforts to identify such cases, even those with very limited nexus to any particular Member State. Clearly, legal certainty would be undermined if an NCA first cleared a concentration under its national merger control rules and subsequently sought to undo it (or even fine the companies involved) by applying Article 102 TFEU. But such a “second bite at the apple” should be rarer still as a practical matter, and it is difficult to see how the higher-ranking legal principles of good administration and legitimate expectations would not trump the need to safeguard Article 102 TFEU’s effet utile in such extraordinary circumstances.120 See e.g. Case C-116/20, Avio Lucos, EU:C:2022:273, para 92 (emphasizing the principle of res judicata, even where it may be impossible to remedy a domestic situation incompatible with EU law). Given the Court’s general reluctance to rule on hypothetical issues, one should perhaps not read too much into the Court not having addressed the pertinent dicta in the Advocate General’s Opinion.121 Opinion in Case C-449/21, Towercast, paras. 59–60. The way the A.G. seeks to address the issue is in any event not particularly persuasive. The notion that the very character of Art. 21(1) EUMR as lex specialis could depend on the prior application of national merger rules in an individual case appears odd, and it is not clear why a dissolution order (as opposed to behavioural remedies or a fine) would not be an appropriate remedy in the rare case that NCAs do feel compelled to apply Art. 102 TFEU to concentrations.

sections.title: The demise of the EUMR’s statutory timetable
sections.text: 5. The demise of the EUMR’s statutory timetable As discussed in the introduction, strict statutory timetables were a key feature of the ECMR. The Council was unwilling to grant the Commission anything like the generous timelines envisioned in its 1973 Proposal (three months for a Phase I investigation and nine months for Phase II). The EUMR reform in 2004 introduced slightly more flexibility into the timetable, in particular to allow for remedy negotiations, but was not meant to change the basic principle that concentrations should generally be cleared within 25 working days. In a series of judgments, the Court of Justice confirmed the “need for speed” and the importance of legal certainty as inherent features of the Merger Regulation. In Schlüsselverlag Moser, the Court found an action for failure to act inadmissible where a complainant had waited more than four months after a national merger clearance decision to argue that the transaction should have been notified to the Commission.122 Case C-170/02 P, Schlüsselverlag Moser, paras. 33–34. In Portugal v. Commission, the Court relied on the need for speed to argue that Member States must proactively notify the Commission or lose the right to invoke protected national interests under Article 21(3) EUMR.123 Case C-42/01, Portugal v. Commission, paras. 51–53. And, in Cementbouw, the Court accepted that the Commission could ignore changes to a concentration after its notification for purposes of jurisdiction, as the latter cannot be “in a state of constant flux”.124 Case C-202/06 P, Cementbouw, paras. 36–37 and 39. In each case, the Commission itself had stressed the need for speed, and the Court relied on the EUMR’s statutory deadlines as the appropriate yardstick for the principle of good administration and the parties’ legitimate business interest to have their concentration reviewed in a meaningful timeframe. Thus, in Schlüsselverlag Moser, the Court pointed out that the four-month time period that it took the complainant to allege that the concentration fell within the Commission’s jurisdiction was as long as the time period provided for a full phase-II investigation by the Commission.125 Case C-170/02 P, Schlüsselverlag Moser, para 37. Similarly, the Court recognized that the need for speed justifies certain shortcuts in the Commission’s administrative procedure, for instance short timelines for the market testing of remedies,126 Case T-251/19, Wieland-Werke v. Commission, EU:T:2022:296, para 634. limits on the extent to which the Commission must undertake additional investigative measures towards the end of its statutory timetable,127 Case T-584/19, ThyssenKrupp, EU:T:2022:386, para 954; Case C-202/06 P, Cementbouw, para 39. or the extent of reasoning required in the Commission’s decisions.128 Case T-251/19, Wieland-Werke, para 516. Against this background, it may be almost shocking to the outside observer how far the overall timelines for EUMR merger review have moved away from the statutory time limits and the promotion of self-discipline that Advocate General Kokott ascribed to them.129 Opinion in Case C-265/17 P, United Parcel Service, EU:C:2019:23, para 53; Case C-413/06 P, Bertelsmann and Sony v. Impala, EU: C:2008:392, paras. 90–91. Indeed, the average duration of pre-notification and formal review in Phase II cases is now between 16 and 18 months.130 Considering the past three years (i.e. 2020 and 2023). Linklaters, “Rhino: EU merger control analysis”, available at <www.linklaters.com/en/insights/publications/rhino/rhino-eu-merger-control-analysis>. This is an increase of almost 50% even compared to 2013, when Phase II cases lasted on average 12 months (including pre-notification).131 Ibid. 5.1. Pre-notification as an investigative and timing tool Neither the ECMR, nor the EUMR contain any mention of pre-notification contacts, even though the Commission’s practice in this respect was already well established when the EUMR entered into force. Article 10(1) EUMR simply provides that the remedy for incomplete notifications is that the statutory time limits begin to run only once the notifying parties submit the missing information.
