Are there rights of appeal or the possibility of judicial review? Yes, depending on the relevant sector involved, administrative decisions might ultimately be reviewed by the Ministry that oversees the authority that issued the decision on the merger. However, this depends on specific conditions that vary on a case-by-case basis. 48. What are the range or remedies the authority may impose? Remedies for mergers that are completed without complying with mandatory approval from the relevant authority are limited to orders of injunction or fines. The ranges vary on a case-by-case basis, largely depending on the specific sector involved. 49. When will related arrangements, such as ancillary restrictions, be covered by a final decision? There is no clear-cut guidance in that regard. 50. Does the authority cooperate with antitrust authorities in other jurisdictions in assessing the merger? Traditionally, authorities tend to cooperate with foreign ones. However, this largely depends on grounds related to reciprocity (e.g., where there is precedent showing that the foreign authority in question has previously cooperated with local authorities).

sections.title: Changes
sections.text: Changes 51. What are the recent enforcement record and current enforcement priorities of the authority? No. 52. Are any changes to the merger regime being considered? No.

title: Canada
container_title: Merger Thresholds Monitor
copyright: 2024 Kluwer Law International BV, and/or its subsidiaries, licensors, and contributors. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

sections.title: Regime information
sections.text: Regime information 1. What legislation governs merger control? Competition Act (Act) Competition Tribunal Act 2. Which authorities are responsible for merger control? The Commissioner of Competition (Commissioner), the head of the Competition Bureau (Bureau), is responsible for administering and enforcing the Act,including its merger control provisions. Transactions can be challenged before the Competition Tribunal (Tribunal). 3. Is the jurisdiction a member of a supranational jurisdiction? No. 4. Is there an FDI screening regime? The foreign direct investment screening regime in Canada is governed by the Investment Canada Act. For more details on the FDI screening regime in Canada, please see FDI Procedure Pro.

sections.title: Notification requirements
sections.text: Notification requirements 5. Is filing mandatory or voluntary? Filing a notification with the Commissioner is mandatory when parties to a proposed transaction exceed certain financial and ownership statutory thresholds under the Act,subject to some exemptions. Parties may also voluntarily notify the Commissioner of a proposed transaction by applying to receive an advanced ruling certificate (ARC) for a proposed transaction that falls below the statutory thresholds. 6. What types of transactions must be notified? The notification filing requirements under the Actapply to the following types of transactions if they meet the applicable statutory threshold requirements: acquisition of assets, acquisition of shares, amalgamation of two or more entities, combination of two or more entities to carry on business through an entity other than a corporation, and acquisition of an interest in a combination. The Actalso includes anti-avoidance provisions requiring the notification filing of transactions that were structured to intentionally bypass the notification filing requirements. 7. Is “change of control” of a business required for notification, and if so, how is it defined? Change of control is not necessary for a transaction to be notifiable. If the size of transaction and size of parties thresholds are met (as described in response to Question 12), the following types of transactions are subject to mandatory notification under the Act: - the acquisition of 20% or more of the voting shares of a publicly traded company; or - the acquisition of 35% or more of the voting shares of a privately held company or an interest in a combination. Incremental acquisitions of voting interests that result in a party having 50% or greater of the voting shares of either a publicly traded or privately held company will trigger an additional notification requirement. 8. How is the nexus to the jurisdiction established? The notification filing requirementsapply to an acquisition of an “operating business in Canada,” which is defined in the Actas a business undertaking to which its employees report to work in Canada. This requires an office or a physical business location in Canada. If a business undertaking is partially or mainly located outside of Canada, it may still be captured by the definition if it has some physical presence in Canada. 9. Is the acquisition of a minority interest notifiable? Acquisition of a minority interest is notifiable if the proposed transaction meets the applicable statutory threshold requirements under the Act, if the acquiring party is acquiring more than 20% of the voting shares of a public company or more than 35% of the voting shares of a private company. 10. Are joint ventures notifiable? Joint ventures must comply with notification filing requirements if they meet the applicable statutory threshold requirements under the Act.However, if a joint venture constitutes a combination (an unincorporated entity),it will be exempt from notification requirements if it meets the following criteria: (i) there is a written agreement (or an agreement that is intended to be put into writing) that obliges on one or more of the parties to contribute assets and governs their relationship; (ii) the combination would not result in a change of control over any of the parties; and (iii) the agreement restricts the combination’s range of activities and includes provisions for the combination’s orderly termination. 11. Must a series of transactions or interdependent transactions be notified as a single transaction? A series of proposed transactions may be considered as a single transaction if all the steps in these transactions constitute a sufficiently connected sequence of events. This can be achieved with legal documents that clearly demonstrate that each event in the series may proceed only if each prior event has been completed, and all the events together will be completed within one year from the date of the notification filing.

sections.title: Thresholds
sections.text: Thresholds 12. What are the notification thresholds? A notification filing is required under the Act if the size of party and the size of transaction thresholds are both met: Size of transaction threshold. The target has assets in Canada or gross revenues in, from or into Canada exceeding CAD 93 million. Size of parties threshold. The parties, together with their affiliates, have assets in Canada or gross revenues from sales in, from or into Canada exceeding CAD 400 million. Furthermore, for an acquisition of shares or an acquisition of an interest in a combination (for example, a non-corporate partnership), the acquiring party must also be acquiring more than 20% of the voting shares of a public company, more than 35% of the voting shares of a private company, or more than 50% of the voting shares for any acquisition. The above notification thresholds apply to the following types of transactions: acquisitions of assets, acquisitions of shares, amalgamations of two or more entities, combinations of two or more entities to carry on business through an entity other than a corporation, and acquisitions of an interest in a combination. The size of transaction threshold is reviewed annually; however, this threshold has not been adjusted since 2021. 13. What are the rules on calculating and allocating turnover or thresholds? The party’s assets and gross revenues should be obtained from their most recent audited financial statements. In the absence of audited financial statements, the party can obtain the relevant information from unaudited statements or internal accounting records. Affiliated entities must also be included in these calculations. 14. Can the authority require a notification for a below threshold transaction? The Bureau cannot request a notification filing from the parties where the proposed transaction falls below the applicable statutory thresholds set out in the Act. However, regardless of whether a transaction is notifiable, the Bureau may investigate for up to three years after closing.

sections.title: Exemptions and special rules
sections.text: Exemptions and special rules 15. Are there special rules for specific sectors? The Actdoes not provide sector-specific rules, especially as it relates to notification requirements. However, certain sectors such as transportation, financial services and telecommunications and broadcasting are subject not only to the provisions under the Act but also to the requirements set out in the sector-specific legislation, including the Canada Transportation Act,the Bank Actand the Broadcasting and Telecommunications Act. 16. Are there exemptions? General exemptions include transactions among affiliates, the issuance of an ARC and a no-action letter, asset securitization transactions and certain transactions under the Bank Act,the Cooperative Credit Associations Act, the Insurance Companies Act and the Trust and Loan Companies Act, in respect of which the Minister of Finance has certified that the transaction would be in the public interest. Exempt acquisitions include acquisitions in the ordinary course of business; acquisitions for the purposes of underwriting; creditor acquisitions; acquisitions that result from a gift, intestate succession and testamentary disposition; and acquisitions pertaining to Canadian resource properties. 17. Are foreign-to-foreign transactions caught? Notification filing requirements under the Actare mandatory for foreign-to-foreign transactions that exceed the statutory thresholds and pertain to an operating business in Canada. The term “in Canada” requires that the business have an office or a physical business location in Canada. If a business is partially or mainly located outside of Canada, it may still be captured by the definition if it has some physical presence in Canada. 18. Are there rules on referral to or from other authorities? No. 19. How does the authority deal with mergers in digital markets? The Actdoes not have provisions dealing with mergers in digital markets. 20. Are there any special rules applicable for public takeover bids? In the case of a hostile takeover bid, if the Commissioner receives information about the proposed transaction from the bidder, the Commissioner must notify the target immediately that a notification filing was received. The target is then required to provide the required information to the Commissioner within ten days. The thirty-day statutory waiting period is triggered when the bidder submits the initial notification to the Commissioner and is not impacted by the target’s timeline to submit the required information. 21. Are there any special considerations or potential red flags in this jurisdiction? The Act includes a rebuttable presumption that a merger is anti-competitive if it significantly increases market share or concentration. The presumption exists if the merger causes the concentration index (HHI) to increase by more than 100 and either: (i) the concentration index (HHI) will be greater than 1,800; or (ii) the total market share of the parties will be greater than 30% after the merger.

sections.title: Standstill obligation and gun-jumping
sections.text: Standstill obligation and gun-jumping 22. Must the transaction be cleared before completion? A notifiable transaction cannot be closed until the parties receive an Advanced Ruling Certificate or a no-action letter from the Commissioner, or the statutory waiting period has expired. 23. Are there provisions for a carve out or derogation from any prohibition against completing the transaction? Parties to a transaction may be permitted to close while preserving or holding separate some assets to complete the review process. In the case of carving out a Canadian portion of a global transaction, the parties must receive clearance from the Commissioner in the form of an Advanced Ruling Certificate or a no-action letter, or wait for the expiry of the statutory waiting periods to close. 24. What actions may constitute implementing the transaction before closing? Parties should continue to operate as separate companies until closing. During an interim period, the proposed purchaser should not seek to influence or exercise control or decision-making power over the acquired business. Parties can engage in prospective planning but should not alter their operations. 25. Are there penalties for gun-jumping? It is a criminal offense to complete a notifiable transaction without filing a notification with the Commissioner, with a fine of CAD 50,000. The Commissioner can also apply to the Tribunal, the Federal Court or the superior court of a province to: (a) issue an interim order prohibiting the completion of the transaction, or in the case of a completed transaction; (b) dissolve the merger or dispose of the assets or shares; or (c) order an administrative monetary penalty of CAD 10,000 for each day during which the party failed to comply with the waiting period requirements.

sections.title: Procedure: filing requirements and timelines
sections.text: Procedure: filing requirements and timelines 26. Which parties are responsible for filing? All parties to the proposed transaction are responsible for filing their respective portion of the notification with the Commissioner. In the case of a share acquisition, the party to the transaction is the company whose shares are being acquired. 27. Is there a filing notice or template? The Act does not specify the form in which a notification must be made; however, there are regulations that set out in detail the information to be provided, and the Bureau strongly encourages parties to the proposed transaction to use the template that is available on their website, which captures this required information. 28. What supporting documentation must be submitted as part of the filing? For notification filings, the parties to a proposed transaction must submit information pertaining to the transaction and its intended objectives; legal documents that will be used to implement the transaction; a list of the foreign competition or antitrust agencies that were informed of the transaction (and the notification date); information about the business, including customers and suppliers of all parties; and all studies, reports, surveys, analyses and reports prepared or received by an officer or director of the corporation to evaluate the competitive effects of the transaction in addition to other applicable information. For an ARC request, the Actdoes not outline the specific information that parties to a proposed transaction must submit to the Commissioner. Parties may consider submitting the type of information that is required under the notification filing requirements, as well as market share information that speaks to the impact on competition. 29. What languages are accepted? The Bureau accepts notification filings of proposed transactions and ARC requests in either English or French. Documents submitted to the Bureau as part of a Supplementary Information Request (SIR) need to be translated into English or French. 30. What are the phases and timelines for review? The receipt of a notification by the Bureau triggers a thirty-day statutory waiting period during which the parties to a proposed transaction cannot complete the transaction until they receive clearance from the Commissioner. The Commissioner may waive the remainder of the waiting period by issuing a no-action letter or an ARC. The parties would be entitled to complete the transaction upon the expiration of the thirty-day waiting period if an SIR was not issued, subject to any interim orders or alternative arrangements, such as “timing agreements.” If the Bureau issues an SIR, then the parties cannot close the transaction until the conclusion of the thirty-day waiting period following compliance with the SIR. Upon expiry of the waiting period, the parties are eligible to complete the transaction. 31. Is there a simplified procedure? Parties to a proposed transaction may submit an ARC request letter to the Commissioner for an exemption from the notification filing requirements. Issuing an ARC precludes the Commissioner from challenging the transaction as long as the parties will be able to substantially complete the transaction within one year from the ARC issuance date and there are no substantial changes to the information submitted as part of the ARC request letter. Alternatively, the Commissioner may waive the notification filing requirements by issuing a no-action letter stating that the Commissioner does not intend to challenge the transaction. 32. Are there filing fees? As of April 1, 2025, the total filing fee is CAD 88,690.45 (updated annually). 33. What F/X rates should be used? The conversion from foreign currency to Canadian dollars must use the Bank of Canada’s daily published exchange rate as quoted on the last day of the applicable period for the financial statement. 34. Are there penalties for failing to file or supplying wrong information? There are criminal and civil penalties for failing to file; however, the criminal penalties have never been used. It is a criminal offense under the Actto complete a notifiable transaction without submitting the notification filing to the Commissioner, which can be fined CAD 50,000. The Commissioner can also apply to the Tribunal, the Federal Court or the superior court of a province to: (a) issue an interim order prohibiting the completion of the transaction, or in the case of a completed transaction; (b) dissolve the merger or dispose of the assets or shares; or (c) order an administrative monetary penalty of CAD 10,000 for each day during which the party failed to comply with the waiting period requirements.

sections.title: Assessment and decisions
sections.text: Assessment and decisions 35. What criteria are applied in assessing the merger? When assessing a merger, the Bureau evaluates whether a merger will result in anti-competitive effects. The legal test that is asked is whether the merger “prevents or lessens, or is likely to prevent or lessen, competition substantially.” The Act sets out several factors that the Bureau may use in its argument, including: (i) availability of foreign competition, (ii) viability of the business of any party to the merger, (iii) availability of substitutes, (iv) barriers to entry, (v) potential competition after completion of the merger, (vi) incidence of change and innovation in the relevant market. In 2024, new provisions of the Act came into force that added additional criteria to consider, including the change in concentration that the merger has brought about or is likely to bring about, and the likelihood that the merger will result in express or tacit coordination between competitors. 36. Are there any presumptions for specific mergers? In Canada, market power is a screening device to evaluate the impact of specific mergers. The Act includes a rebuttable presumption that a merger is anti-competitive if it significantly increases market share or concentration. The presumption exists if the merger causes the concentration index (HHI) to increase by more than 100 and either: (i) the concentration index (HHI) will be greater than 1,800; or (ii) the total market share of the parties will be greater than 30% after the merger unless the contrary is proved on a balance of probabilities. 37. Are there special criteria for joint ventures? The Act carves out a specific exception for joint ventures when conducted in the form of unincorporated combinations. Ventures conducted through a joint venture are permitted under Canadian competition law when they: (i) would not have occurred in the absence of the joint venture, or are sufficiently risky that without a joint venture, they would not have occurred; (ii) do not result in a change in control over any party to the combination; (iii) have a written agreement setting out each party’s contribution to the joint venture, the relationship between the parties, and restricting the range of activities that may be carried on pursuant to the combination; and (iv) that the combination does not prevent or lessen, or is not likely to prevent or lessen, competition. 38. What is the authority’s approach to sustainability factors? The Act is silent as to whether sustainability factors should be considered in the evaluation of a merger. 39. What is the authority’s approach to the effect of a merger on labour? The Act contains an explicit reference to the effect of a transaction on labor. 40. What is the authority’s approach to economic efficiencies in assessing the merger? Until 2023, the Act allowed for a so-called efficiencies defense to mergers that stipulated that if the economic efficiencies resulting from a merger outweigh the anticompetitive effects, i.e., that total surplus captured in the market after the merger is higher than prior to the merger, notwithstanding the possible impact on prices in the market, the merger will be permitted to proceed. In 2023, the Act was amended, repealing this provision. 41. What is the role of customers, competitors and complainants in assessing the merger? Customers, competitors and complainants are often consulted by the Bureau for evidence and information to substantiate any claims of lessening or prevention of competition. Interested parties may file complaints on the Bureau website to provide information to the Bureau. Further, the Act sets out that any six residents of Canada who believe that a merger may result in a substantial lessening or prevention of competition may apply to the Commissioner to instigate an inquiry. Once a challenge of a merger has commenced, interested parties may seek leave to intervene in the proceedings. 42. Is access to the file available and how is it managed? Upon notification of a merger to the Bureau, the Bureau publishes the proposed merger on its “Report of merger reviews” database found here. This website is updated by the Bureau upon a final decision. 43. At what stage(s) will the authority consider proposals from the parties to remedy any concerns? What is the time frame for providing them? The Bureau should accept proposals from the parties to remedy competition concerns at any time. However, this was not the case in the 2022 Rogers/Shawtransaction,where the Bureau argued that a proposed remedy offered after initiation of the merger challenge should not be considered by the Tribunal. This argument was ultimately rejected by the Tribunal, suggesting that future proposals should be considered at any stage of the process. 44. What criteria does the authority apply when assessing proposed remedies? The Bureau evaluates proposed remedies by considering whether the remedies sufficiently address the risk of a substantial lessening or prevention of competition associated with the merger, such that it restores competition to the prevailing level before the merger. The Bureau has indicated that it prefers remedies that can be actioned prior to or concurrently with the completion of the merger, also known as fix-it-first remedies. 45. Which decisions is the authority empowered to make? Are any decisions made by another entity and if so, which entity, and which decisions? When notified of a merger, the Bureau can: (i) allow the merger through the issuance of an ARC or no-action letter, or (ii) seek additional information and issue an SIR to the parties. Once the parties comply with an SIR, the Bureau can allow the merger through the issuance of an ARC, a no-action letter, or the expiry of the thirty-day statutory waiting period. If the Bureau still has concerns with the transaction, it may seek an injunction to delay the closing of the merger, or it may formally challenge the merger to the Tribunal and request a full block of the transaction. If the merger is challenged, the Tribunal is empowered to make such decisions as prohibiting the transaction, forcing a dissolution of the merger, compelling certain divestitures as a condition of allowing the transaction, or permitting the transaction. These decisions may be upheld or reversed by the Federal Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court of Canada on Appeal. 46. Will the final decision be published and if so, where? Final decisions of the Bureau can be found on its website here. The Bureau also provides position statements on select mergers on its website here. 47. Are there rights of appeal or the possibility of judicial review? The Act provides a statutory right of appeal to the Federal Court of Appeal for decisions made by the Tribunal. Appeals on a question of law or of mixed fact and law are available as of right, and appeals on questions of fact are available with leave of the court. On questions of law, the standard of review is correctness; on questions of mixed fact and law, the standard of review is palpable and overriding error, except when there is an extricable legal question, in which case the standard is correctness and on questions of fact, the standard of review is palpable and overriding error. Appeals from a decision of the Federal Court of Appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada are available only if leave is obtained from the Supreme Court. Further, all decisions made by tribunals in Canada may be subject to judicial review. Canada’s administrative law regime permits judicial review of tribunal decisions. There is a presumption that the standard to be used during judicial review proceedings is reasonableness; however, certain categories of questions, such as constitutional questions, general legal questions of central importance to the legal system as a whole, and issues of overlapping jurisdiction, require that a standard of correctness be used. Any judicial review in the competition context would likely be evaluated on a reasonableness standard. 48. What are the range or remedies the authority may impose? The Bureau cannot impose remedies; however, remedies may be negotiated between the parties and the Bureau. These remedies fall into two broad categories: structural and behavioral. A structural remedy includes the divestiture of assets or businesses. A behavioral remedy focuses on the behavior of the merging firms, such as requiring a firm to continue supplying an input to competitors. 49. When will related arrangements, such as ancillary restrictions, be covered by a final decision? During a merger review, ancillary restrictions will form part of the Bureau’s consideration of the transaction. The Act contemplates that certain restrictions that result in anti-competitive effects are permissible so long as they are ancillary to a broader, separate legal agreement. The Bureau will investigate whether the restrictions are truly ancillary to the merger or if they are part of the purpose of the merger. 50. Does the authority cooperate with antitrust authorities in other jurisdictions in assessing the merger? The Bureau has negotiated cooperation instruments with several international counterparts, including the Mexican Comisión Federal de Competencia Económica, the Japanese Fair Trade Commission, and the Competition Commission of Singapore. Further, the Bureau is a signatory to a multilateral memorandum of understanding between Australia, New Zealand, the United Kingdom, and the United States committing to mutual assistance and cooperation for competition authorities. In 2025, the Bureau signed a memorandum of understanding with the United Kingdom’s Competition and Markets Authority. More information on Canada’s international cooperation agreements can be found here.

sections.title: Changes
sections.text: Changes 51. What are the recent enforcement record and current enforcement priorities of the authority? The Bureau works closely with corporations proposing mergers that fall under the notification guidelines and typically is successful in addressing competition concerns early in the process. A list of recent merger review results can be found on the Bureau website here. Following recent amendments to the Act, the Bureau has launched market studies into Canada’s domestic airline and small and medium-sized enterprise lending sectors, signaling future areas of focus for its enforcement activities. In 2025, the Bureau reached two consent agreements for transactions within Canada’s oil and natural gas industry. 52. Are any changes to the merger regime being considered? The merger regime in Canada has undergone many recent changes, most notably the removal of the efficiencies defense and the introduction of a rebuttable presumption that a merger is anti-competitive if it results in significant increases in market share or concentration. More information about the Bureau’s position on changes to the Act can be found here.

plain_text:
Regime information 1. What legislation governs merger control? Competition Act (Act) Competition Tribunal Act 2. Which authorities are responsible for merger control? The Commissioner of Competition (Commissioner), the head of the Competition Bureau (Bureau), is responsible for administering and enforcing the Act,including its merger control provisions. Transactions can be challenged before the Competition Tribunal (Tribunal). 3. Is the jurisdiction a member of a supranational jurisdiction? No. 4. Is there an FDI screening regime? The foreign direct investment screening regime in Canada is governed by the Investment Canada Act. For more details on the FDI screening regime in Canada, please see FDI Procedure Pro.

Notification requirements 5. Is filing mandatory or voluntary? Filing a notification with the Commissioner is mandatory when parties to a proposed transaction exceed certain financial and ownership statutory thresholds under the Act,subject to some exemptions. Parties may also voluntarily notify the Commissioner of a proposed transaction by applying to receive an advanced ruling certificate (ARC) for a proposed transaction that falls below the statutory thresholds. 6. What types of transactions must be notified? The notification filing requirements under the Actapply to the following types of transactions if they meet the applicable statutory threshold requirements: acquisition of assets, acquisition of shares, amalgamation of two or more entities, combination of two or more entities to carry on business through an entity other than a corporation, and acquisition of an interest in a combination. The Actalso includes anti-avoidance provisions requiring the notification filing of transactions that were structured to intentionally bypass the notification filing requirements. 7. Is “change of control” of a business required for notification, and if so, how is it defined? Change of control is not necessary for a transaction to be notifiable. If the size of transaction and size of parties thresholds are met (as described in response to Question 12), the following types of transactions are subject to mandatory notification under the Act: - the acquisition of 20% or more of the voting shares of a publicly traded company; or - the acquisition of 35% or more of the voting shares of a privately held company or an interest in a combination. Incremental acquisitions of voting interests that result in a party having 50% or greater of the voting shares of either a publicly traded or privately held company will trigger an additional notification requirement. 8. How is the nexus to the jurisdiction established? The notification filing requirementsapply to an acquisition of an “operating business in Canada,” which is defined in the Actas a business undertaking to which its employees report to work in Canada. This requires an office or a physical business location in Canada. If a business undertaking is partially or mainly located outside of Canada, it may still be captured by the definition if it has some physical presence in Canada. 9. Is the acquisition of a minority interest notifiable? Acquisition of a minority interest is notifiable if the proposed transaction meets the applicable statutory threshold requirements under the Act, if the acquiring party is acquiring more than 20% of the voting shares of a public company or more than 35% of the voting shares of a private company. 10. Assessment and decisions 35. What criteria are applied in assessing the merger? When assessing a merger, the Bureau evaluates whether a merger will result in anti-competitive effects. The legal test that is asked is whether the merger “prevents or lessens, or is likely to prevent or lessen, competition substantially.” The Act sets out several factors that the Bureau may use in its argument, including: (i) availability of foreign competition, (ii) viability of the business of any party to the merger, (iii) availability of substitutes, (iv) barriers to entry, (v) potential competition after completion of the merger, (vi) incidence of change and innovation in the relevant market. In 2024, new provisions of the Act came into force that added additional criteria to consider, including the change in concentration that the merger has brought about or is likely to bring about, and the likelihood that the merger will result in express or tacit coordination between competitors. 36. Are there any presumptions for specific mergers? In Canada, market power is a screening device to evaluate the impact of specific mergers. The Act includes a rebuttable presumption that a merger is anti-competitive if it significantly increases market share or concentration. The presumption exists if the merger causes the concentration index (HHI) to increase by more than 100 and either: (i) the concentration index (HHI) will be greater than 1,800; or (ii) the total market share of the parties will be greater than 30% after the merger unless the contrary is proved on a balance of probabilities. 37. Are there special criteria for joint ventures? The Act carves out a specific exception for joint ventures when conducted in the form of unincorporated combinations. Ventures conducted through a joint venture are permitted under Canadian competition law when they: (i) would not have occurred in the absence of the joint venture, or are sufficiently risky that without a joint venture, they would not have occurred; (ii) do not result in a change in control over any party to the combination; (iii) have a written agreement setting out each party’s contribution to the joint venture, the relationship between the parties, and restricting the range of activities that may be carried on pursuant to the combination; and (iv) that the combination does not prevent or lessen, or is not likely to prevent or lessen, competition. 38. What is the authority’s approach to sustainability factors? The Act is silent as to whether sustainability factors should be considered in the evaluation of a merger. 39. What is the authority’s approach to the effect of a merger on labour? The Act contains an explicit reference to the effect of a transaction on labor. 40. What is the authority’s approach to economic efficiencies in assessing the merger? Until 2023, the Act allowed for a so-called efficiencies defense to mergers that stipulated that if the economic efficiencies resulting from a merger outweigh the anticompetitive effects, i.e., that total surplus captured in the market after the merger is higher than prior to the merger, notwithstanding the possible impact on prices in the market, the merger will be permitted to proceed. In 2023, the Act was amended, repealing this provision. 41. What is the role of customers, competitors and complainants in assessing the merger? Customers, competitors and complainants are often consulted by the Bureau for evidence and information to substantiate any claims of lessening or prevention of competition. Interested parties may file complaints on the Bureau website to provide information to the Bureau. Further, the Act sets out that any six residents of Canada who believe that a merger may result in a substantial lessening or prevention of competition may apply to the Commissioner to instigate an inquiry. Once a challenge of a merger has commenced, interested parties may seek leave to intervene in the proceedings. 42. Is access to the file available and how is it managed? Upon notification of a merger to the Bureau, the Bureau publishes the proposed merger on its “Report of merger reviews” database found here. This website is updated by the Bureau upon a final decision. 43. At what stage(s) will the authority consider proposals from the parties to remedy any concerns? What is the time frame for providing them? The Bureau should accept proposals from the parties to remedy competition concerns at any time. However, this was not the case in the 2022 Rogers/Shawtransaction,where the Bureau argued that a proposed remedy offered after initiation of the merger challenge should not be considered by the Tribunal. This argument was ultimately rejected by the Tribunal, suggesting that future proposals should be considered at any stage of the process. 44. What criteria does the authority apply when assessing proposed remedies? The Bureau evaluates proposed remedies by considering whether the remedies sufficiently address the risk of a substantial lessening or prevention of competition associated with the merger, such that it restores competition to the prevailing level before the merger. The Bureau has indicated that it prefers remedies that can be actioned prior to or concurrently with the completion of the merger, also known as fix-it-first remedies. 45. Which decisions is the authority empowered to make? Are any decisions made by another entity and if so, which entity, and which decisions? When notified of a merger, the Bureau can: (i) allow the merger through the issuance of an ARC or no-action letter, or (ii) seek additional information and issue an SIR to the parties. Once the parties comply with an SIR, the Bureau can allow the merger through the issuance of an ARC, a no-action letter, or the expiry of the thirty-day statutory waiting period. If the Bureau still has concerns with the transaction, it may seek an injunction to delay the closing of the merger, or it may formally challenge the merger to the Tribunal and request a full block of the transaction. If the merger is challenged, the Tribunal is empowered to make such decisions as prohibiting the transaction, forcing a dissolution of the merger, compelling certain divestitures as a condition of allowing the transaction, or permitting the transaction. These decisions may be upheld or reversed by the Federal Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court of Canada on Appeal. 46. Will the final decision be published and if so, where? Final decisions of the Bureau can be found on its website here. The Bureau also provides position statements on select mergers on its website here. 47. Are there rights of appeal or the possibility of judicial review? The Act provides a statutory right of appeal to the Federal Court of Appeal for decisions made by the Tribunal. Appeals on a question of law or of mixed fact and law are available as of right, and appeals on questions of fact are available with leave of the court. On questions of law, the standard of review is correctness; on questions of mixed fact and law, the standard of review is palpable and overriding error, except when there is an extricable legal question, in which case the standard is correctness and on questions of fact, the standard of review is palpable and overriding error. Appeals from a decision of the Federal Court of Appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada are available only if leave is obtained from the Supreme Court. Further, all decisions made by tribunals in Canada may be subject to judicial review. Canada’s administrative law regime permits judicial review of tribunal decisions. There is a presumption that the standard to be used during judicial review proceedings is reasonableness; however, certain categories of questions, such as constitutional questions, general legal questions of central importance to the legal system as a whole, and issues of overlapping jurisdiction, require that a standard of correctness be used. Any judicial review in the competition context would likely be evaluated on a reasonableness standard. 48. What are the range or remedies the authority may impose? The Bureau cannot impose remedies; however, remedies may be negotiated between the parties and the Bureau. These remedies fall into two broad categories: structural and behavioral. A structural remedy includes the divestiture of assets or businesses. A behavioral remedy focuses on the behavior of the merging firms, such as requiring a firm to continue supplying an input to competitors. 49. When will related arrangements, such as ancillary restrictions, be covered by a final decision? During a merger review, ancillary restrictions will form part of the Bureau’s consideration of the transaction. The Act contemplates that certain restrictions that result in anti-competitive effects are permissible so long as they are ancillary to a broader, separate legal agreement. The Bureau will investigate whether the restrictions are truly ancillary to the merger or if they are part of the purpose of the merger. 50. Does the authority cooperate with antitrust authorities in other jurisdictions in assessing the merger? The Bureau has negotiated cooperation instruments with several international counterparts, including the Mexican Comisión Federal de Competencia Económica, the Japanese Fair Trade Commission, and the Competition Commission of Singapore. Further, the Bureau is a signatory to a multilateral memorandum of understanding between Australia, New Zealand, the United Kingdom, and the United States committing to mutual assistance and cooperation for competition authorities. In 2025, the Bureau signed a memorandum of understanding with the United Kingdom’s Competition and Markets Authority. More information on Canada’s international cooperation agreements can be found here.

Changes 51. What are the recent enforcement record and current enforcement priorities of the authority? The Bureau works closely with corporations proposing mergers that fall under the notification guidelines and typically is successful in addressing competition concerns early in the process. A list of recent merger review results can be found on the Bureau website here. Following recent amendments to the Act, the Bureau has launched market studies into Canada’s domestic airline and small and medium-sized enterprise lending sectors, signaling future areas of focus for its enforcement activities. In 2025, the Bureau reached two consent agreements for transactions within Canada’s oil and natural gas industry. 52. Are any changes to the merger regime being considered? The merger regime in Canada has undergone many recent changes, most notably the removal of the efficiencies defense and the introduction of a rebuttable presumption that a merger is anti-competitive if it results in significant increases in market share or concentration. More information about the Bureau’s position on changes to the Act can be found here.

title: About the Author
container_title: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition)

sections.text: Abir Roy is the Co-Founder of Sarvada Legal, a law firm based in New Delhi, India. He heads the antitrust practice at Sarvada Legal. He is a counsel specializing in competition law and he regularly represents clients across judicial and quasi-judicial forums in India. Abir has represented clients on competition law issues across all industry segments, including technology, e-commerce, digital markets. Abir has also been recognized as a leading practitioner in competition law by Whos Who Legal, Chambers & Partners, Legal 500 and Asia Law. Abir is also a visiting lecturer on Competition Law at Symbiosis Law University, Noida & LawSikho, a leading online legal education portal.

title: Foreword
container_title: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition)

sections.text: Law, they say, is jargonized common sense. The difficulty only is in overcoming the jargon associated with the law before embracing the underlying common sense. In the case of a highly complex and technical area such as competition law, the use of jargon is inevitable due to the nature of the subject. Given the fact that competition law is essential and integral to the economy, the Need of the Hour is a book that can deftly address the issues and present them in simple and easily readable prose. This is where the author – Shri Abir Roy – has done yeoman service to the citizenry by releasing the second edition of his book – Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide. The first edition of this book was published in 2016. Thereafter, the author has taken pains to incorporate subsequent developments in the law and the statutory regime. The clarity with which the concepts have been handled in this book bears testimony to the surefootedness that the author brings to bear while navigating the thickets of competition law. His passion for the subject and the corresponding mastery is evident from the in-depth analysis that this intricate subject has received at his hands. The author has not hesitated to take on abstract concepts and explain them to the reader by breaking them down into easily comprehensible points. The book maintains a seamless flow from one chapter to another, employing a smooth narrative style without compromising on the quality of the content. This book stands out for its ability to succinctly explore the fundamental concepts integral to the Competition Act. It provides meticulous references and analyses of the decisional patterns of both the Competition Commission of India and the adjudicatory bodies including the Apex Court. In the chapter dedicated to Anti-Competitive Agreements, the book illustrates how the regulator utilizes both direct and circumstantial evidence to uncover arrangements between parties that have potential anti-competitive effects. The author astutely highlights the regulator’s predominant reliance on circumstantial evidence to infer the existence of an agreement where the market participants are ‘instructed based on nods and winks’. Given the nature of such circumstantial evidence, one should however expect the regulators and adjudicatory bodies to bear in mind the caution administered in words memorable by Baron Alderson J. in R. v. Hodge [(1838) 2 Lew 227]: The mind was apt to take a pleasure in adapting circumstances to one another, and even in straining them a little, if need be, to force them to form parts of one connected whole; and the more ingenious the mind of the individual, the more likely was it, considering such matters, to overreach and mislead itself, to supply some little link that is wanting, to take for granted some fact consistent with its previous theories and necessary to render them complete. Furthermore, in the same chapter, the book extensively references various forms of cartels, encompassing vertical and horizontal agreements, that are covered by the Act. It elaborates on the key identifiers of each agreement and elucidates the regulator’s perspective on such agreements. As the author rightly observes, cartelization, like conspiracies, is shrouded in secrecy. Akin to criminal law, uncovering such conspiracies is a challenge for the regulator. Given the similarities between conspiracies in criminal law, and cartels governed by competition law, this would be a great opportunity for creative lawyers to cross-pollinate from the former to the latter, to devise yardsticks that the regulator can utilize to govern cartels. As the author predicts, one would expect that there would be stronger enforcement and stricter stance taken by the regulator towards cartels. A noteworthy aspect of this book is the author’s meticulous consideration of the Amendments introduced to the Competition Act in 2023. Each chapter of the book examines how these amendments address key concerns and delves into the conscious decisions made by legislators while introducing these amendments. For instance, in the chapter on Anti-Competitive Agreements, there is a reference to how the Act has been amended to regulate all parties which could be part of a hub-and-spoke cartel. The chapter on ‘Mergers’ discusses how the Amendments made to the CCI Regulations in 2023 have resulted in the inclusion of every valuable consideration in determining the deal value threshold for the purpose of notification to the Regulator. Further, the chapter on Abuse of Dominant Position also explores the absence of statutory recognition for collective dominance by multiple enterprises, which is different from the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union – an aspect which is sub-judice in India. This meticulous effort renders the book a valuable resource for readers who are keen on tracking the evolution of competition law in India. The enormous hard work of the author in simplifying complex jargon becomes evident through the tremendous effort undertaken in punctuating each chapter with tools such as graphs, charts, diagrams, and comparative tables to enhance the reader-friendly nature of the text. In the chapter on ‘Mergers’, for instance, the author adeptly traverses through challenging topics such as the calculation of asset value and turnover using illustrative tables. The discussion on how the regulator perceives the transfer of control of an entity is also duly supported by diagrammatic representations, elucidating intricate company structures and control dynamics. Further, in the same chapter, the author pithily discusses the concept of Gun Jumping – another jargon associated with the Act which refers to parties consummating a transaction even before notifying the Competition Commission of India. The author takes care to carve out and emphasize that only Substantive Gun Jumping would fall foul of the law. To substantiate, the author, as he has done with other chapters, populates the text with multiple circumstances which would be considered Substantive Gun Jumping. These are, as the author points out, cases where there is sharing of commercially sensitive information, allocation of customers, or undertaking joint marketing efforts while waiting for approval from the Regulator. Each such instance is dealt with in substantial detail, making the concepts accessible to any reader. The chapter on intellectual property and competition law offers a virtual treat to the discerning reader. It delves into protection entitled to intellectual property rights, offering a nuanced discussion on the delicate balance between safeguarding these rights and ensuring fair access in the realm of competition. The author captures how the intellectual property regime and competition law function like two friends in disagreement, since both of them continue to work in tandem, one complementing the other, while their objectives may appear diametrically opposed and daggers drawn. The analysis distinguishes between the safeguarding of intellectual property rights and the abuse of the same. Further, the chapter discusses the expanded protection of Intellectual Property Rights introduced under the 2023 amendment. It engages in discussion on the legislative choices regarding the extension (or lack thereof) of intellectual property rights protection in cases involving a dominant player in the market. This recognizes the blurring lines between the impact of protecting such rights in anti-competitive agreements and situations involving a dominant player in the market. The analysis by the author arrests the attention of the reader and makes for compelling reading. A whole chapter has been devoted to digital markets and competition law, offering an in-depth perspective for the readers. The author grapples with the subtle issue of how online and offline channels of distribution are interdependent, and sometimes substitutable, which impacts the meaning of relevant market under the Competition Act. The author rightly concludes that in defining a market in the digital space, the definition cannot be fossilized and must keep pace with the times. The chapter delves into pricing strategies adopted in multisided platforms and addresses the rapid accumulation of market power in such platforms. The approach of the Competition Commission of India emphasizing the assessment of the interdependence of different sides to a transaction and determining whether appreciable adverse effects of competition are felt by both sides have been highlighted. In an interesting passage, the author sows the seed for a future Digital Competition Act, to identify and regulate the small number of digital players, termed as Systematically Important Digital Intermediaries (SIDI) in order to ensure free and fair markets. This is a book that must adorn the library of anyone and everyone who is concerned with competition law. I am sure the legal community, especially, young legal professionals will make the best use of the book to acquire mastery over this technical and complex but nevertheless fascinating subject. I wish the book all success and Abir many more books to his pen. Justice K.V. Viswanathan Judge, Supreme Court of India

It elaborates on the key identifiers of each agreement and elucidates the regulator’s perspective on such agreements. As the author rightly observes, cartelization, like conspiracies, is shrouded in secrecy. Akin to criminal law, uncovering such conspiracies is a challenge for the regulator. Given the similarities between conspiracies in criminal law, and cartels governed by competition law, this would be a great opportunity for creative lawyers to cross-pollinate from the former to the latter, to devise yardsticks that the regulator can utilize to govern cartels. As the author predicts, one would expect that there would be stronger enforcement and stricter stance taken by the regulator towards cartels. A noteworthy aspect of this book is the author’s meticulous consideration of the Amendments introduced to the Competition Act in 2023. Each chapter of the book examines how these amendments address key concerns and delves into the conscious decisions made by legislators while introducing these amendments. For instance, in the chapter on Anti-Competitive Agreements, there is a reference to how the Act has been amended to regulate all parties which could be part of a hub-and-spoke cartel. The chapter on ‘Mergers’ discusses how the Amendments made to the CCI Regulations in 2023 have resulted in the inclusion of every valuable consideration in determining the deal value threshold for the purpose of notification to the Regulator. Further, the chapter on Abuse of Dominant Position also explores the absence of statutory recognition for collective dominance by multiple enterprises, which is different from the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union – an aspect which is sub-judice in India. This meticulous effort renders the book a valuable resource for readers who are keen on tracking the evolution of competition law in India. The enormous hard work of the author in simplifying complex jargon becomes evident through the tremendous effort undertaken in punctuating each chapter with tools such as graphs, charts, diagrams, and comparative tables to enhance the reader-friendly nature of the text. In the chapter on ‘Mergers’, for instance, the author adeptly traverses through challenging topics such as the calculation of asset value and turnover using illustrative tables. The discussion on how the regulator perceives the transfer of control of an entity is also duly supported by diagrammatic representations, elucidating intricate company structures and control dynamics. Further, in the same chapter, the author pithily discusses the concept of Gun Jumping – another jargon associated with the Act which refers to parties consummating a transaction even before notifying the Competition Commission of India. The author takes care to carve out and emphasize that only Substantive Gun Jumping would fall foul of the law. To substantiate, the author, as he has done with other chapters, populates the text with multiple circumstances which would be considered Substantive Gun Jumping. These are, as the author points out, cases where there is sharing of commercially sensitive information, allocation of customers, or undertaking joint marketing efforts while waiting for approval from the Regulator. Each such instance is dealt with in substantial detail, making the concepts accessible to any reader. The chapter on intellectual property and competition law offers a virtual treat to the discerning reader. It delves into protection entitled to intellectual property rights, offering a nuanced discussion on the delicate balance between safeguarding these rights and ensuring fair access in the realm of competition. The author captures how the intellectual property regime and competition law function like two friends in disagreement, since both of them continue to work in tandem, one complementing the other, while their objectives may appear diametrically opposed and daggers drawn. The analysis distinguishes between the safeguarding of intellectual property rights and the abuse of the same. Further, the chapter discusses the expanded protection of Intellectual Property Rights introduced under the 2023 amendment. It engages in discussion on the legislative choices regarding the extension (or lack thereof) of intellectual property rights protection in cases involving a dominant player in the market. This recognizes the blurring lines between the impact of protecting such rights in anti-competitive agreements and situations involving a dominant player in the market. The analysis by the author arrests the attention of the reader and makes for compelling reading. A whole chapter has been devoted to digital markets and competition law, offering an in-depth perspective for the readers. The author grapples with the subtle issue of how online and offline channels of distribution are interdependent, and sometimes substitutable, which impacts the meaning of relevant market under the Competition Act. The author rightly concludes that in defining a market in the digital space, the definition cannot be fossilized and must keep pace with the times. The chapter delves into pricing strategies adopted in multisided platforms and addresses the rapid accumulation of market power in such platforms. The approach of the Competition Commission of India emphasizing the assessment of the interdependence of different sides to a transaction and determining whether appreciable adverse effects of competition are felt by both sides have been highlighted. In an interesting passage, the author sows the seed for a future Digital Competition Act, to identify and regulate the small number of digital players, termed as Systematically Important Digital Intermediaries (SIDI) in order to ensure free and fair markets. This is a book that must adorn the library of anyone and everyone who is concerned with competition law. I am sure the legal community, especially, young legal professionals will make the best use of the book to acquire mastery over this technical and complex but nevertheless fascinating subject. I wish the book all success and Abir many more books to his pen. Justice K.V. Viswanathan Judge, Supreme Court of India

title: Acknowledgements
container_title: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition)

sections.text: While working on the second edition of the book, I realized that with so much which has happened in the Indian competition law circles in the last eight years, it would be a mammoth task to revise this book, and may I add, it was essentially and for all practical purposes, writing a new book. With the existing work pressures and jostling between work deliverables and other personal commitments, writing the second edition of my book seemed a herculean task. This book would not have been possible without the support of my family (my parents; my wife, Neha; my five-year-old lifeline, Aryana; and my pet, Biscuit). Their support and encouragement have been invaluable throughout since I had to keep on working at odd hours and miss much of our family time. Having said that, I would be remiss in my duty if I did not acknowledge the contribution of two people, without whom this book would not have been possible. The first person I would like to thank is Shreya Kapoor. Shreya is a final-year law student at National Law University Odisha. My association with Shreya started when she interned with Sarvada in her third year, and since then, it has been a matter of great fortune for Sarvada and me personally that she has been working with me and her analysis is exceptional. She has a keen interest in competition law and has actively contributed to legal scholarship on the subject through several publications. Beyond academic excellence, Shreya has actively engaged in internships at prestigious law firms, leveraging practical experience to complement theoretical knowledge. She has also excelled in moot court competitions and debates focusing on the domain of competition law. The second person I would like to thank is Sasthibrata Panda. My association with Sasthi also dates back more than eighteen months, when I first saw him assisting a lawyer on an antitrust matter while he was still a law student. Thereafter, Sasthi interned in my office last year when he was in his final year, and since then, he has been a part of Sarvada Legal. He completed his BA LLB degree from DSNLU, Vishakhapatnam. Sasthi has assisted in matters before the Hon’ble Supreme Court of India, High Courts, Tribunals & quasi-judicial bodies such as the National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT), Competition Commission of India, etc. I am extremely proud of the fact that both Shreya and Sasthi are part of the competition law practice at Sarvada Legal, and their assistance has been invaluable. Like I said, this book would not have been possible without them.

sections.title: MARCH TOWARDS COMPETITION LAW
sections.text: §1.01 MARCH TOWARDS COMPETITION LAW Competition law is intended to protect competition in the market in order to maximize consumer welfare. A robust competition law and policy is quintessential for enhancing efficiency and consumer welfare. The Competition Act, 2002 (‘Competition Act’) was enacted with the intention of preventing anti-competitive practices which would have an adverse effect on the market and its market participants. It seeks to protect the market participants and promote competition since effective competition helps the consumer to get the best quality product at the most competitive price. An effective competition law regime helps to attain efficiencies which ensure the application of laws, rules and regulations to ensure businesses and companies compete fairly with each other. This encourages enterprises to attain efficiency, creates a wider choice for consumers and helps reduce prices and improve quality of products/services. While the idea of perfect competition may be a farfetched concept in most industries, effective competition is the goal of any competition law dispensation. Effective competition is to be assessed in terms of rivalry among firms, low barriers and where no single firm can influence the market conduct. The aim and objective of the Competition Act were explained by the Supreme Court in Competition Commission of India v. Steel Authority of India Limited (‘SAIL case’),1 Competition Commission of India v. Steel Authority of India Limited [2010], (SC) (2010) 10 SCC 744, para. 5. where it was said that the purpose of the Competition Act is to control the concentration of an industry from the hold of a particular market participant and prevent monopolies or similar kinds of concentration in an industry. Competition law is not against attaining monopolistic power, but it abhors the misuse of the monopolistic power to harm the market system and stakeholders. The primary purpose of the competition law is to increase competition in the market, protect the cost of the product, prevent losses to society and product quality, and safeguard other competitors. The Supreme Court held that the perfect competition is the economic model where the market is competitive. Similarly, in Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel Ltd. (‘Bharti Airtel case’),2 Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel Ltd. [2019], (SC) (2019) 2 SCC 521, para. 81. the Supreme Court observed that the Competition Act was enacted to bring out the paradigm shift in terms of globalization and economic development in India. This Competition Act would be anathema to unfair trade practices which cause an adverse effect on the market and ensure fair competition. [A] The Aim and Objective of the Competition Act The aim and objective of the Competition Act have been discussed in Excel Crop Care Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India (‘Excel Crop case’),3 Excel Crop Care Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2017], (SC) (2017) 8 SCC 47, para. 29; Competition Commission of India v. State of Mizoram [2022], (SC) (2022) 7 SCC 73, paras 7, 21-25. and it was said that the purpose behind enacting the Competition Act was to prohibit anti-competitive practices and promote market’s competition, consumer welfare and fair trade. The Competition Act sets the ‘rules of the game’ to ensure effective competition in order to bring economic efficiency, economic growth and development and consumer welfare in the market. The benefits of the Competition Act have been elaborated below:(i) Economic Efficiency: In a competitive market, market participants must make effective use and allocation of the resources by forcing market participants to produce at the lowest cost and sell the product at a competitive price. The main advantage of a competitive market is the continuous pressure on the market participants to use resources in a manner that keeps costs down without compromising the quality of products. This always favors the consumer as the market participants must constantly strive to invest in research and development to provide a better deal to the consumer. (ii) Economic Growth and Development: The competitive force in the market brings economic growth to the country by rapidly reallocating resources and increasing the value of the products and services in the market. The growth of the economy is one of the major indicators of economic development. Economic development shows the improvement in literacy rates, reduction in mortality rates, easy market entry and exit, development in innovation, improvement in physical infrastructure and overall economic welfare of the country. Thus, with economic growth and development, the economic efficiency of the country will improve, and there will be optimal usage of products and services. (iii) Consumer Welfare: The ultimate beneficiary of competition is the consumer. Consumers will get better choices, better quality at a cheaper rate, improve their bargaining position in the market, etc. Along with the remedies provided in the consumer laws, the end-consumers can use the tenets of the Competition Act to resolve the issue of market failures. The Supreme Court in Excel Crop case also noted a few more benefits of the Competition Act, such as an improvement in employment rate, better industrial and trade policies, an improvement in the workplace environment, an increase in foreign investment, benchmarking by exposing inferior performance, etc. Again, the ratio on the purpose of the Competition Act in Excel Crop case was re-emphasized by the Supreme Court in Rajasthan Cylinders & Containers Ltd. v. Union of India (‘LPG-I case’).4 Rajasthan Cylinders & Containers Ltd. v. Union of India [2020], (SC) (2020) 16 SCC 615, para. 80. In LPG-I case, it was held that the Competition Act not only intends to eliminate anti-competitive practices but also to promote and sustain competition in the market. The CCI should enforce the Competition Act to curb the practices which will harm the competition. Hence, effective enforcement is important to prohibit practices having adverse effects on the competition as well as to deter future anti-competitive practices. As discussed above, the primary objective of the Competition Act is to promote economic efficiency through effective enforcement of competition to create a market responsive to consumer preferences. Competition law is to promote economic efficiency by using competition as one of the means of assisting in the creation of a market responsive to consumer preferences.5 SAIL case, paras 6, 7, 125; in Competition Commission of India v. Fast Way Transmission (P) Ltd. [2018], (SC) (2018) 4 SCC 316, para. 12. The advantages of having an effective competition regime are threefold:(i) Allocative efficiency, which ensures the effective allocation of resources. Allocative efficiency can be achieved only if all firms are of sufficient size to realize significant economies of scale, and markets are either competitively structured (i.e., they comprise a significant number of producers) or entry barriers are low. In such cases, all companies are price takers, and the demand and supply forces in the market determine the price. Allocative efficiency is attained when prices are equated to marginal costs, which is a condition for perfect competition. (ii) Productive efficiency, which ensures that costs of production are kept at a minimum. (iii) Dynamic efficiency, which promotes innovative practices. Dynamic efficiency refers to the development of new products. This can be done by ensuring proper reward to the inventor in lieu of developing and disclosing the invention to the public. A high level committee on Competition Policy and Law (‘High Level Committee’) was formed to examine the various aspects and accordingly suggest keeping in view the competition policy of India. On 22 May 2022, the High Level Committee submitted its report, and thereafter, the Competition Act, 2002 came into operation on 12 December 2003. The Competition Act was enacted as a response to market liberalization, ease of doing business by opening up India’s economy and removing the strict government restrictions. In India, a High Level Committee was constituted to examine its various aspects and make suggestions keeping in view the competition policy of India. This Committee made recommendations and submitted its report on 22 May 2002. After the completion of the consultation process, the Competition Act was enacted. As per the statement of objects and reasons of the Competition Act, this enactment is India’s response to the opening up of its economy, removing controls, and resorting to liberalization. The natural corollary of this is that the Indian market should be geared to face competition from within the country and outside. [B] The Purpose Behind Enforcing the Competition Act The natural corollary of the Competition Act is to bring evolution to the Indian market by creating a competitive force within the country and outside.6 Coal India Limited and Another v. CCI and Another [2023], (SC) 2023 SCC OnLine SC 740, para. 67. So, with the avowed objective of a free-market economy, it was felt essential to have a quasi-judicial body to ensure that the practices which have an adverse effect on competition in India are prevented. And thereby, to ensure effective enforcement and fulfil the purpose of the Competition Act, a quasi-judicial body, namely the CCI, was established under section 7 of the Competition Act. Apart from the CCI, the Competition Act provides for the appointment of the Director General (DG) under section 16(1) of the Competition Act. The DG is a specialized investigating wing or the investigatory arm of the CCI. The various provisions of the Competition Act deal with the establishment, powers and functions as well as the discharge of adjudicatory functions by the CCI. Under the scheme of the Competition Act, the CCI is vested with inquisitorial, investigative, regulatory, adjudicatory and advocacy functions. Further, the proviso of section 18 of the Competition Act states that for better enforcement, the CCI can have tie-ups with other Indian government agencies and foreign competition agencies. For international cooperation and promotion of competition law, CCI has signed a memorandum of understanding with the DG for Competition of the European Commission, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission, the Japan Fair Trade Commission, etc. Such cooperation will help the competition agencies exchange information, share best practices and conduct various capacity-building programmes. Also, on 22 November 2022,7 Notification No. G.S.R. 832(E), dated 22 Nov. 2022. the Union Finance Ministry through a notification mandated the CCI to share information with the Enforcement Director, directly or indirectly. So, keeping in view the economic development of the country as a whole, a positive role has been given to the CCI to enforce the competition law in India. Chapter II of the Competition Act can be referred to as the heart and soul of the competition law. Chapter II spells out three substantial provisions of the Competition Act because it prohibits the practices which are anathema to the concept of fair and competitive market. The substantive provisions of the Competition Act were notified in a phased manner, provisions relating to anti-competitive agreements (section 3) and abuse of dominance (section 4) were notified in May 2009, and provisions with respect to merger control (sections 5 and 6) were notified in June 2011. To fulfil the purpose of the Competition Act, the CCI must prohibit such practices to create a healthy competition in the market. Because the aim and objective of the Competition Act clearly show that the enterprises that independently determine their commercial policies are prohibited from indulging in agreements which affect the competition adversely or concerted practices by taking advantage of their dominant position in their markets.8 CCI v. Coordination Committee, (2017) 5 SCC 17, para. 45. Also see, Excel Crop case, (2017) 8 SCC 47, paras 21, 26. As the aim and objective also prohibit future anti-competitive effects, the parties before entering into a combination must notify the CCI by showing that the combination will not adversely affect the competition.9 SCM Solifert Ltd. v. CCI, (2018) 6 SCC 631, para. 18. The Competition Act in totality has a significant departure from the letter and spirit of the erstwhile Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969. The Competition Act is premised on the effect theory, which means the primary purpose of the Competition Act is to prevent practices which cause or are likely to cause an anti-competitive effect on the market. It does not condemn monopoly per se but rather focuses on the enterprise’s use of the monopoly status. Further, the earlier Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Commission had very limited enforcement powers since they could not penalize any enterprise. Their power was limited to passing cease and desist orders. Conversely, the Competition Act has empowered the CCI to penalize a delinquent enterprise, apart from passing ceasing-and-desist orders and also ordering for modification of the business practices. The penalties which are imposed by the CCI are one of the highest economic penalties in India. Also, the CCI can pass directions to modify the business practices by imposing behavioural remedies10 See, OECD, Remedies and Commitments in Abuse Cases, p. 5. Behavioural Remedies are the remedies which mandates an enterprise to conduct in a particular manner, for example, with regard to pricing clauses, contractual obligations, etc. under section 27 of the Competition Act and modify the structure of the enterprises by passing structural remedies11 See, OECD, Remedies and Commitments in Abuse Cases, p. 5. Structural Remedies are the remedies which involve changes to the structure of an enterprise, thereby effectively changing the structure of the market. under section 28 of the Competition Act. In the context of anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominance, CCI generally passes behavioural changes in terms of the enterprise’s conduct with other market participants and end customers. In relation to merger control, under section 31 of the Act, structural and/or behavioural remedies are imposed through commitments on the parties to the combination. Further, the CCI, unlike its predecessor regime, has extraterritorial jurisdiction; i.e., the CCI can investigate the practices of companies based outside India if their conduct causes any anti-competitive effects in India. Further, the Competition Act seeks to maintain a level playing field between the private and the public sector and seek competitive neutrality; i.e., government or state-owned enterprises should not use their legislative or fiscal powers to advantage their own businesses over the private sector. The provisions of the Competition Act are applicable to any enterprise which carries on economic activity, irrespective of whether it is publicly or privately owned. We will see from the definition of enterprise, discussed in detail below, that all the commercial activities of the government, excluding the sovereign functions (energy, currency, defence and space), come under the provisions of the Competition Act and that no antitrust exemptions are applicable to such state-owned enterprise. In fact, the CCI has penalized state-owned enterprises, like Coal India, since they have indulged in activities which constitute abuse of dominance. The erstwhile Competition Appellate Tribunal (COMPAT) also upheld the order passed by the CCI against Coal India. Thereafter, in Coal India Ltd. v. CCI and Another (‘Coal India case’),12 Coal India Limited and Another v. CCI and Another [2023], (SC) 2023 SCC OnLine SC 740, paras 7-13, 88, 98, 99, 100, 101, 105, 119, 122. Coal India appealed before the Supreme Court by arguing that Coal India is a monopoly created by the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973 with an objective of protecting it from challenges, and is guarded under Article 31B of the Constitution of India, 1950.13 Article 31B of the Constitution of India, 1950, says that the enactments placed in the ninth schedule of the Constitution of India shall not become void on the ground that they violate fundamental rights and no judicial review is possible against such enactments. Article 31B validates certain enactments (such as, the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973) which have been inserted under the Ninth Schedule of the Constitution of India, 1950. Coal India also relied upon section 19(4)(g) of the Competition Act to say that Coal India is not a dominant player under the Competition Act because Coal India is dominant due to a statute; hence, it should be treated differently that an ordinary dominant player.
