It was argued that Coal India was created under the said statute for the equitable distribution of coal in order to secure the ‘common good’ under Article 39(b) of the Constitution of India, 1950.14 Article 39(b) of the Constitution of India, 1950, states that the state can enact laws to secure control of the resources of the community, and the distribution of the resources must subserve the common good. Accordingly, Coal India took support of Article 39(b) of the Constitution of India, 1950, to say that it has not violated the Competition Act because it is following the principle of ‘common good’ while distributing minerals and resources of the highest importance for India’s economy. Hence, the provisions of the Competition Act will not be applicable to the conduct of Coal India because Coal India is an instrument of the state, and its conduct is in accordance with the directive principles under Article 39(b). Furthermore, it was also argued that there is no abuse of dominance under section 4(2)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act because the conduct of Coal India relating to the pricing and production of coals is based on the constitutional mandate under Article 39(b) and the objective of Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973. Additionally, it was also argued that section 28 of the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973 is a non-obstante clause; hence, it prevails over the Competition Act. However, the Supreme Court, after taking consideration of all the arguments, dismissed the appeal. The Supreme Court held that the purpose of the Competition Act is to attain faster economic growth in India and foster competitive economy. To achieve the purpose, public sector companies or bodies created under statutes cannot abuse their dominance. The parliament was aware of the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973, while including bodies created under statute within the purview of the term ‘enterprise’ under section 2(h) of the Competition Act. This shows that Coal India is an enterprise, and it can’t evade its obligation under the Competition Act. Only those enterprises which are engaged in sovereign activities are exempt, but Coal India was not involved in any such sovereign activities. The Supreme Court then noted that the expression in section 19(4)(g) of the Competition Act regarding creating monopolies under statutes for determining the existence of dominant player shows the intention of the legislature to bring Coal India within the ambit of the Competition Act. Regarding the application of Article 31B of the Constitution of India, 1950, the Supreme Court held that the said article provides constitutional immunity from being challenged for violating fundamental rights and being invalidated by the courts. The said article protects the entities which are created under statutes, but these entities are not immunized from their anti-competitive conduct by acting with caprice, unfairly or discriminating against similarly situated persons or things with different conditions. The term ‘common good’ in Article 39(b) means achieving the highest good of the maximum number of people. But to serve the purpose of ‘common good’ and attain the goals in the directive principles, the principles of competition law must also be harmoniously construed along with the goals of directive principles. Finally, on the point of non-obstante provision under the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973, the Supreme Court looked at the purpose of the Competition Act and held that the Competition Act will prevail over the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973.

sections.title: PROCEDURE FOR INQUIRY
sections.text: §1.02 PROCEDURE FOR INQUIRY A detailed procedure has been established under the Competition Act and the regulations of the CCI with respect to the investigation process for a violation under sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act. Merger filings and procedures have been dealt with in Chapter 4. A diagrammatic illustration of key stages of inquiry under the Competition Act is as follows: [A] Initiation of the Case The informant, i.e., the person who wishes to complain about a conduct to the CCI, would make such information available in writing to the CCI by paying the requisite filing fee outlining in detail the facts, evidence in support and the alleged contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act. The information could also be made by way of reference by the Central Government, State Government, statutory authority or on its own knowledge as provided under section 19(1)(a) of the Competition Act. Hence, the CCI can initiate an inquiry under section 26 of the Competition Act under the following three conditions:15 Coal India case, para. 90. (i) information given under section 19; (ii) reference made by the Government or a statutory authority; or (iii) on its own motion (‘suo motu’). It must be noted that under the Competition Act, there is no locus standi requirement; i.e., it is not necessary that the affected party only can file information. As a part of the information, it is not required for the informant to show how it has been harmed by the conduct of the alleged infringer. This is a precise reason why the term ‘information’ is used in the Competition Act and not ‘complaint’. Before the Competition Act was notified in 2009, the term used in the Competition Act was filing of a ‘complaint’ which was changed to filing of ‘information’ in 2007. One of the major reasons for this change was that the legislature did not want to have a locus standi requirement. Further, the use of the word ‘complaint’ would mean an adversarial proceeding which is not the case with ‘information’ being used. There have been instances where the informant has filed the information, and the CCI has proceeded with the investigation and final orders, even when the informant is not present during the course of proceedings. On the issue of locus standi under the Competition Act, the NCLAT in Samir Agrawal v. Competition Commission of India and Others 16 Samir Agrawal v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2020], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (At) No. 11 of 2019, paras 10, 15, 16, 17. erred in holding that the informant had no right to file an information because he was neither aggrieved nor prejudiced by the conduct of the cab aggregators. The substantial question of law was whether a ‘person’ would mean any natural person irrespective of whether he was a consumer who suffered harm or a person whose legal rights have been harmed by the anti-competitive activity. The NCLAT held that CCI cannot take cognizance against the allegation of violation under sections 3 and/or 4 because the informant never suffered harm at the hands of the cab aggregators while using the apps as a consumer, so he has no locus to file an information. On an appeal against the judgment of the NCLAT, the Supreme Court in Samir Agrawal v. CCI and Others (‘Cab aggregator case’)17 Samir Agrawal v. CCI and Others [2021], (SC) (2021) 3 SCC 136, paras 14-17, 21; Flipkart Interned Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. CCI and Others, Writ Appeal No. 562 of 2021, paras 29, 35, 36, the Karnataka High Court rejected the argument that the person who filed the information was not an aggrieved person but was sponsored by someone else. The High Court reiterated the ratio of Supreme Court in Samir Agrawal v. CCI and Others and held that the Competition Act allows any person and not just an aggrieved person to file an information under section 19 of the Competition Act. set aside the reasoning of the NCLAT on the issue of the locus standi. The Supreme Court said that the term ‘any person’ as defined in section 2(l) of the Competition Act is a wide and inclusive definition. Further, by looking at section 19(1)(a) of the Competition Act, it was held that an information can be filed by any person irrespective of the person being affected or not. As the proceeding under the Competition Act is a proceeding in rem which affects the public interest at large, anyone can file an information under section 19(1)(a) of the Competition Act. Thus, the informant need not be personally aggrieved by the anti-competitive agreement and/or the abuse of dominance. Information is essentially made by a person or by way of reference from another authority. The purpose of information is to inform the CCI of an anti-competitive practice, and it is up to the CCI to determine whether the information has any merit and whether the factual situation mentioned in the information warrants any further investigation by the DG. The CCI is not bound by the issues and pleadings taken in the information and can go beyond the scope of the information and ask the DG to investigate additional grounds. Further, unlike in a complaint scenario, there is no mechanism under the Competition Act to withdraw an information. Once information is made, it is up to the CCI to decide whether a fit case exists for further investigation or to close the matter.18 Bharti Airtel case, para. 1. The informant need not be present throughout the proceedings, and the CCI can pass appropriate orders based on the facts and circumstances of the case. Furthermore, it must be noted that the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023 incorporated a statutory limitation of three years, which means CCI shall not initiate an inquiry against a matter unless the information or reference under section 19(1) of the Act is filed within three years. Also, the said amendment introduced the principle of res judicata in the Competition Act. Now, the CCI may refuse inquiry if a similar matter has already been decided under section 26(2A) of the Competition Act. However, it is important to note that section 26(2A) uses the term ‘Commission may not inquire’. Therefore, it gives discretion to the CCI to interfere and there is no strict prohibition. There may be a situation where conduct which was not considered to be anti-competitive earlier by the CCI becomes anti-competitive with a change in market dynamics over a period of time, development in competition law jurisprudence or for any other reason. Considering the fact that the market is very dynamic and is constantly changing, the Karnataka High Court in Flipkart Interned Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission India and Others 19 Flipkart Interned Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission India and Others [2021], (Karnataka High Court) Writ Appeal No. 562 of 2021, para. 42; WhatsApp LLC and Another v. Competition Commission of India and Another [2022], (Delhi High Court) 2022 SCC OnLine Del 2582, para. 36. held that just because CCI had looked into some matter previously, it does not stop the CCI under the principle of res judicata from looking into any information subsequent to the development of new practices. [B] Prima Facie Case When such information mentioned above is received, the CCI is expected to satisfy itself and express its opinion on whether a prima facie case exists from the record produced before it and then to pass a direction to the DG to cause an investigation to be made into the matter under section 26(1) of the Competition Act. However, if the CCI is of the opinion that there exists no prima facie case based on the information placed before it, it may close the matter under section 26(2) of the Competition Act. The Supreme Court of India has succinctly described the ambit of the direction passed under section 26(1) of the Competition Act in the case of SAIL.20 SAIL case, para. 38. The Supreme Court noted that the direction of the CCI under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, for the formation of a prima facie opinion requesting the offices of the DG to investigate the matter, is a direction simpliciter to cause an investigation into the matter. The Supreme Court noted that issuance of such a direction is an administrative direction to one of its own wings departmentally and is made without entering upon any adjudicatory process. It does not effectively determine any right or obligation of the parties to the lis. The Supreme Court further noted that a direction under section 26(1) of the Competition Act does not entail civil consequences for any person. In fact, in all the prima facie orders made by the CCI, it is clearly stated that the CCI makes clear that nothing stated in the prima facie order shall be tantamount to a final expression of opinion on the merit of the case, and the DG shall conduct the investigation without being swayed in any manner whatsoever by the observations made herein. It must be noted herein that although the prima facie order is a direction simpliciter akin to an administrative function, the Gujarat High Court in Vardayani Offset v. Competition Commission of India and Other 21 Vardayani Offset v. Competition Commission of India and Other [2020], (Gujarat High Court) MANU/GJ/1046/2020, paras 7, 10, 12, 17. quashed a prima facie order against the allegation of bid rigging because the CCI failed the test of legality by not recording reasons while passing the prima facie order. Also, it was impossible to read the prima facie order because it was heavily redacted. So, based on the reasoning of the SAIL case, the Gujarat High Court reiterated that the CCI may not record detailed reasons, but it must record some or minimum reasons to show that a prima facie case exists. Hence, the CCI failed to satisfy the basic requirement of a prima facie order, which expresses the mind of the CCI. Another issue that was deliberated in detail by the Supreme Court in the SAIL case22 SAIL case, paras 21, 31, 91, 71. was whether a notice of hearing had to be given to the informant or the alleged infringer prior to passing an order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act. It was noted by the Supreme Court, based on the statutory scheme of the Competition Act that the direction under section 26(1) of the Competition Act could be issued by the CCI with or without hearing the party against whom the information has been filed. Section 19(1) of the Competition Act does not mandate a requirement of notice to be given to the informant, affected party or any other person at the stage of deciding whether a prima facie case exists or not. Such parties cannot claim the right to notice or hearing as a matter of right, but it is always open to the CCI to call any ‘such person’ to render assistance or produce such records as the CCI may consider appropriate. The relevant observations made by the Supreme Court in the SAIL case, in this regard, are that the proceeding under section 26(1) is the very beginning of the procedure against the matter raised under section 19(1) of the Competition Act. Unlike an adjudicatory proceeding which requires the CCI to hear the parties before passing a prima facie order, proceeding under section 26(1) is merely an administrative function and the power of CCI under section 26(1) is characteristic of an inquisitorial and regulatory power. The statutory language of section 26(1) of the Competition Act does not mandate the CCI to issue notice or allow the parties to claim audi alteram partem as a matter of right. CCI at this stage does not deal with the rights of the parties and only forms a prima facie opinion directing the DG to submit an investigation report or close the inquiry under section 26(2) of the Competition Act. However, this exclusion of audi alteram partem at the initial stage does not vitiate the due process of law because an opportunity for a full hearing will be given before final disposal of the matter. After the CCI forms prima facie opinion and subsequently receives the DG investigation report, thereafter, the CCI has to invite written submissions and oral hearings from the parties concerned. This means that before reaching the final decision, the concerned parties will get an opportunity to raise objection to or support the DG report and argue before CCI. [C] Investigation and Inquiry Process Once the CCI has found that there exists a prima facie case for violation of the provisions of section 3 and/or section 4 of the Competition Act, it will direct the offices of the DG to conduct a detailed investigation under section 26(1) of the Competition Act. The inquiry process of the CCI and the investigation process of the DG have been distinctly separated in the Competition Act. The CCI acts as the watchdog on anti-competitive practice, whereas the DG is the investigative wing which assists the inquiry process by finding the facts and evidence. In the SAIL case,23 SAIL case, paras 115, 116. the Supreme Court dealt with an important distinction between the terms ‘inquiry’ and ‘investigation’ in the context of section 26 of the Competition Act. An ‘inquiry’, as defined under Regulation 18(2) of the Competition Commission of India (General) Regulations, 2009 (‘General Regulations’), is deemed to have commenced once the CCI provides the DG with a direction to investigate under section 26(1) of the Competition Act. The terms ‘inquiry’ and ‘investigation’ are often treated as synonyms; however, the Supreme Court clarified that both are different under the Competition Act. The inquiry continues until the final order is passed by the CCI, whereas an investigation is merely a part of the process of inquiry. Thus, inquiry also includes the phase after the investigation, where the CCI considers the DG’s investigation report and hears the objections of the parties. In terms of Regulation 18(2) of the General Regulations, once the CCI has directed the DG to investigate the matter, it is deemed that an inquiry has commenced. Then, the CCI considers the DG’s investigation report and the objections and submissions made by the concerned parties. This inquiry will continue till the time a final order gets passed under section 27 or 26(6) of the Competition Act24 SAIL case, para. 116. or when the CCI, by not agreeing to the DG investigation report’s finding on contravention, passes a final order holding the parties not liable. Further, under Regulation 20(4) of the General Regulations, the DG report should contain his findings of each of the allegations made in the information or reference, together with all evidence collected during the investigation. On a concomitant reading of section 36(2) with section 41(2) of the Competition Act, both the CCI and DG have been vested with the wide and sweeping power of a civil court while discharging their functions. Sections 36(2) and 41(2) empower them to summon and examine any person, issue commissions for the examination of witnesses and documents, and render assistance or production of evidence.25 Google Inc. and Others v. CCI and Another, [2015], (Delhi High Court) 2015 SCC OnLine Del 8992, para. 18(c). Further, evidence is collected in the context of section 36(4) of the Competition Act, read with Regulation 41 of the General Regulations. While the DG investigates, subject to confidentiality, the concerned parties can apply inspection and certified copies of documents as per Regulation 37 of the General Regulations.26 Vardayani Offset v. Competition Commission of India and Other [2020], (Gujarat High Court) MANU/GJ/1046/2020, para. 15.2. The DG is further empowered for the search and seizure of documents by section 41(3) of the Competition Act. The detailed procedure for conducting an investigation is contained in section 41, read with section 36 and Regulations 20, 21, 35, 41, 42 and 45 of the General Regulations. Also, even in the absence of any particular provision, the CCI has some inherent power, such as to review or recall an illegal order. In Google Inc. and Others v. CCI and Another,27 Google Inc. and Others v. CCI and Another, [2015], (Delhi High Court) 2015 SCC OnLine Del 8992, paras 8, 18, 18(ZA), 18(ZQ), 18(ZT), 20-24. Google filed a writ petition before the Delhi High Court, praying to direct CCI for the recall of its prima facie order which has been passed without following the principles of natural justice. It was further noted that the omission of section 37 of the Competition Act, which empowered the CCI to review its order prior to the omission, cannot be a conclusive indication of the legislature’s intention to take away the CCI’s power to review. The High Court concluded that the prima facie order directing the DG to investigate can be reviewed or recalled by the CCI. To avoid any delay in the investigation process, the High Court directed the CCI to dispose of the application for review or recall on the very first date without entertaining any lengthy arguments or going into the merits of the case. Chapter 2 shows the various techniques used by the DG to investigate a case of cartels. As a matter of practice, the DG has been collecting evidence in the form of personal/official e-mails, e-mail dumps, telephonic records, minutes of the meeting, travel details, physical meetings and conferences, etc. Further, the DG has not only relied on the traditional modes of collecting evidence, i.e., issuance of information requests and depositions followed by written submissions but also conducted raids on the premises of the companies. Section 41(3) of the Competition Act empowers the DG with investigative tools, and one of the effective investigative tools is to conduct raids. Such raids are known as ‘dawn raid’ which means an unannounced inspection by the DG on the parties that have violated or are likely to violate the Competition Act. The DG has broad power while conducting a dawn raid and may search and seize documents (including electronic data residing on electronic devices), office premises and other properties of the parties. A dawn raid is also known as an on-site inspection or surprise inspection. In September 2014, the DG conducted the first dawn raid by its unannounced search and seizure operation on JCB Limited, a leading player in the earth-moving and construction equipment industry. The legality of the dawn raid was thereafter challenged by the JCB Limited before the Delhi High Court, and the said High Court passed an ad interim order restraining the DG and the CCI from utilizing the seized material to pass a final report and final order, respectively. Then CCI filed a special leave petition before the Supreme Court challenging the Delhi High Court’s interim order. The Supreme Court in CCI v. JCB (India) Ltd.28 CCI v. JCB (India) Ltd. [2020], (SC) (2020) 17 SCC 446, paras 13-18. vacated the interim order and held that the High Court erred in restraining the statutory authority of DG to conduct a fair investigation. Reading section 41(3) of the Competition Act with section 240-A of the Companies Act, 1956, the DG has been expressly conferred with the power to investigate anti-competitive behaviours via search and seizure. The Supreme Court was of the view that the High Court’s blanket restrains on analysing the seized material for the purpose of investigation. Hence, vacating the interim order of the Single Bench of the High Court, the Supreme Court remitted back to the Division Bench of the High Court. After the Supreme Court passed this judgment in 2019, many dawn raids were carried out by the DG. For instance, only in the year 2022 the DG conducted dawn raids on e-commerce platforms like Flipkart and Amazon,29 Business Standard, Top traders body CAIT lauds CCI raids on Amazon sellers Cloudtail, Appario, 28 Apr. 2022, available at: https://www.business-standard.com/article/companies/top-traders-body-cait-lauds-cci-raids-on-amazon-sellers-cloudtail-appario-122042800642_1.html. mining services companies,30 Deccan Herald, CCI raids engineering firms after Coal India antitrust complaint, 6 May 2022, available at: https://www.deccanherald.com/national/east-and-northeast/cci-raids-engineering-firms-after-coal-india-antitrust-complaint-1106982.html. tyre companies,31 Business Today, CCI conducts raids on tyre companies CEAT, Apollo, Continental: Report, 30 Mar. 2022, available at: https://www.businesstoday.in/latest/corporate/story/cci-conducts-raids-on-tyre-companies-ceat-apollo-continental-report-327911-2022-03-30. and cement companies.32 Business Standard, Competition Commission conducts searches at India Cements’ Chennai office, 23 Dec. 2022, available at: https://www.business-standard.com/article/companies/competition-commission-conducts-searches-at-india-cements-chennai-office-122122201123_1.html. If the DG recommends that there is contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act, the said recommendation also is not binding on the CCI, and it may enquire into the matter if in its opinion a further inquiry is called for or it may if it does not agree with the DG, close the matter forthwith. If the CCI agrees with the DG, it can proceed to pass appropriate orders in terms of sections 27, 28 and 31 of the Competition Act. Based on the above, it can be seen from the scheme of the Competition Act that the recommendations of the DG are not binding on the CCI. Also, NCLAT in Hyundai Motor India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Others 33 Hyundai Motor India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2018], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal No. 06 of 2017, para. 37. noted that the DG’s report is merely an opinion primarily to assist the CCI in appreciation of evidence in arriving at the final conclusion; hence, it is not binding at all. Further, the scheme of the Competition Act does not restrict the scope of DG’s investigation into any matter which was not considered by the CCI, while forming a prima facie opinion under section 26(1) of the Competition Act. [D] The Scope of DG’s Investigation It must be noted herein that a plain reading of the Competition Act would show that under the provisions of the Competition Act, the DG has no suo moto powers of investigation, and they can act and investigate a matter only if it is directed by the CCI. This is a marked difference from the earlier Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices regime where the DG had suo moto powers of investigation under the Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969. However, the scope of the DG’s investigation should not be limited to the direction of the CCI’s prima facie order, and it must involve analysis of the facts from all angles and finding out every possible violation of the Competition Act. The DG’s scope of the investigation was sufficiently discussed in the Excel Corp case.34 Excel Corp case, paras 44, 45. In this case, the inquiry was initiated by the CCI based on information from the Chairman and Managing Director of Food Corporation of India (FCI). It was alleged that four manufacturers of Aluminium Phosphide Tablets (APT) have formed a bid rigging cartel by quoting identical rates in the tenders floated by FCI. Accordingly, a prima facie order was passed to initiate investigation, and ultimately, the APT manufacturers were held liable for rigging several tenders. One of the contentions of the APT manufacturers was that there was no specific prima facie direction by the CCI directing the DG to investigate the 2011 tender, so the DG couldn’t investigate the tender. The Supreme Court held that the DG should not be restricted from investigating outside the scope of the prima facie order. The purpose of the investigation by the DG is to cover all the necessary facts to see whether there is any contravention of the Competition Act by the enterprise(s) whose conduct needs to be investigated. Though the information is the starting point of the investigation, the DG can carry out a further investigation if another enterprise is also involved in the violation of the Competition Act or if other facts reveal another violation of the Competition Act. Since the prima facie order gets passed in an initial stage, it is not possible to foresee and predict all the violations of the Competition Act. Hence, the DG would be well within its power to include the violation and the enterprise(s) revealed during the investigation. Further, the Delhi High Court in Cadila Healthcare Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission of India 35 Cadila Healthcare Ltd. & Ors. v. Competition Commission of India [2018], (Delhi High Court) 2018 SCC OnLine Del 11229, para. 43. An appeal is pending before the Supreme Court. See, Cadila Healthcare Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India, (SC) SLP(C) No. 030641/2018. noted that the CCI investigated Cadila Healthcare Ltd. which is not impleaded as the Opposite Party in the prima facie order. Cadila Healthcare Ltd. objected to the investigation being conducted by the DG, but the High Court held that the DG’s scope of investigation shall not be restricted only to the enterprises named in the prima facie order. Similarly, in Competition Commission of India v. Grasim Industries Ltd.,36 Competition Commission of India v. Grasim Industries Ltd. [2019], (Delhi High Court) 2019 SCC OnLine Del 10017, paras 35, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45, 48, 49-52. An appeal is pending before the Supreme Court. See, M/s Grasim Industries Ltd. v. CCI, SLP(C) No. SLP(C) No. 008546/2020. the Delhi High Court held that the DG could suo motu expand its scope of the investigation beyond the prima facie order to investigate Grasim Industries for the violation of section 3 of the Competition Act, and allowed the DG to investigate Grasim Industries for abusing its dominant position under section 4 of the Competition Act. The Delhi High Court relied upon the observation made by the same court in Cadila Healthcare Limited v. CCI, where it was held that DG can travel outside the prima facie opinion to investigate an anti-competitive behaviour which was not even mentioned in the order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act. Further, the Delhi High Court relied on the SAIL case and Excel Crop case to reiterate that the prima facie opinion at the stage of issuing directions to the DG does not restrict the scope of the investigation. The stage when the directions are issued under section 26(1) of the Competition Act is a very early stage of inquiry; hence, CCI could not foresee or predict all the anti-competitive behaviour and the parties who are engaged in it. Similarly, the High Court also noted that the CCI directed the DG to investigate ‘the matter’, and this was broad enough to enable the DG to investigate all the anti-competitive behaviour that will come to the notice of the DG while undertaking the investigation. As the language of prima facie opinion also plays an important role for DG while conducting the investigation, using broad language helps the DG to investigate in an unrestricted manner. To protect the purpose of the Competition Act, ensure fair competition and prohibit anti-competitive trade practices in the market, the DG can expand its scope of investigation in order to include facts, additional enterprise and additional information that were not present in the prima facie order. [E] Standard of Proof The standard of proof required for proving a case of contravention under the Competition Act is that of ‘preponderance of probability’ akin to civil law; the DG brings forth evidence which includes a mix of direct and circumstantial evidence. The reason behind applying ‘preponderance of probability’ instead of ‘beyond reasonable doubt’ as standard of proof is because of the difficulty in finding direct evidence to prove the allegations against the contravention under the Competition Act. Generally, in cartel cases,37 GAIL (India) Limited v. PMP Infratech Private Ltd. and Another, Case No. 41 of 2019, para. 35; In Re: Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry, Suo Motu case No. 05 of 2016, para. 153; Mr. Rizwanul Haq Khan v. Mersen (India) Pvt. Ltd. and Another, Reference Case No. 02 of 2016, para. 44, in re, alleged cartelization by steel producers, RTPE 09 of 2008, para. 111. there is rarely any direct evidence to establish the anti-competitive agreement as the concerted practice takes place in a clandestine manner. So, in most cartel cases, the cartelization was inferred from a number of co-incidences and indicia which were taken together to constitute evidence of the contravention of section 3(3) of the Competition Act.38 Builders Association of India v. Cement Manufacturers’ Association & Ors., Case No. 29 of 2010, para. 183. Hence, for efficient enforcement and to fulfil the purpose of the competition law, CCI has set the standard of proof on the basis of the benchmark of preponderance of probability. [F] Further Investigation/Enquiry Once the DG submits his report to the CCI, a copy of the said report is required to be forwarded to the parties concerned, including the Central Government, State Government, or Statutory Authority in case the investigation was directed on a reference received from such Government/Authority. This exercise is required to be undertaken irrespective of whether the DG reports a contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act or otherwise. In case the DG recommends that no contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act is made, the CCI is required to invite objections or suggestions on the said report from the Central Government, or the State Government, or the Statutory Authority of the parties concerned, as the case may be. Such a report is then required to be considered by the CCI in light of the objections or suggestions received by it. If the CCI, on consideration of the matter in light of the objections or suggestions, if any, received by it, agrees with the DG, the matter is closed forthwith. However, the recommendation made by the DG in this regard is not binding on the CCI,39 Google Inc. and Others v. CCI and Another, 2015 SCC OnLine Del 8992, para. 18(ZJ). and after considering the said report in light of the objections or suggestions received by it, if the CCI is of the opinion that further investigation is required, it may direct such further investigation into the matter by the DG. The CCI, instead of directing further investigation by the DG, may also cause a further inquiry to be made into the matter, or it may itself hold a further inquiry into the matter. There is a detailed procedure for further investigation and inquiry under the Competition Act when the DG report records that there is no violation under sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act. A diagrammatic illustration of the key stages of proceeding for further investigation/inquiry under the Competition Act is as follows: On the CCI’s power to direct DG for further investigation, the NCLAT in Amit Mittal v. DLF and Others 40 Amit Mittal v. DLF and Others [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 82 of 2018, paras 18-23. made some important observations. In this case, an appeal was filed against the CCI’s order passed under section 26(6) of the Competition Act. In the CCI’s order, the DG’s main investigation report disclosed that DLF is abusing its dominant position under section 4(2)(a)(ii) of the Competition Act, but the CCI directed the DG to further investigate and submit a supplementary investigation report under section 26(7) of the Competition Act read with Regulation 20(6) of the General Regulations. In the supplementary investigation report, the DG found that DLF is not a dominant player in the relevant market; therefore, the question of abuse of dominance will not arise. Accordingly, the CCI passed an order to close the proceeding against DLF for the violation of section 4(2)(a)(ii) of the Competition Act. At the appellate stage, the NCLAT held that the CCI can direct the DG for further investigation only if the DG finds there is no contravention of the Competition Act in its main investigation report as per section 26(5) of the Competition Act. Hence, section 26(5) is a prerequisite to pass an order for further investigation under section 26(7) of the Competition Act. Thereby, NCLAT remitted the matter back to CCI and directed the CCI to pass an order based on the main investigation report of the DG. The NCLAT’s order was subsequently stayed by the Supreme Court, and the fate of the CCI’s order is still undecided.41 Stay order dated 17 Mar. 2023, Competition Commission of India v. Amit Mittal and Others, (SC) Civil Appeal 1486 of 2023. There is enough indication in the Competition Act to suggest that the restrictive interpretation adopted by NCLAT may not stand judicial scrutiny from the Supreme Court. After conducting further inquiry, if the DG’s supplement investigation report recommends that there is a contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act, the said recommendation also is not binding on the CCI, and it may enquire into the matter if in its opinion a further inquiry is called for or it may if it does not agree with the DG, close the matter forthwith. If the CCI agrees with the DG, it can proceed to pass appropriate orders in terms of sections 27, 28 and 31 of the Competition Act.

sections.title: ENFORCEMENT POWERS OF THE CCI
sections.text: §1.03 ENFORCEMENT POWERS OF THE CCI [A] Penalties and Directions on Companies/Group The Competition Act regulates the manner in which businesses should operate in the market. It has emerged as the most important piece of economic legislation governing both behavioural and structural aspects of a business. Every company should take into consideration the legal issues of competition relating to both inorganic growth and behavioural models adopted by companies. The CCI is emerging as a market regulator in India which is as assertive as the market regulators in developed jurisdictions. Unlike the predecessor regime which lacked teeth, the Competition Act provides for extensive enforcement powers. The CCI can impose a monetary penalty of up to 10% of the average turnover of the last three preceding financial years on every enterprise for abuse of dominance or for entering into anti-competitive agreements. A still higher penalty is reserved for cartel activities where the penalty can extend up to three times the amount of profits or 10% of the turnover during the period of the existence of the cartel, whichever is higher. Further, the CCI has the powers to modify terms of agreements, modify business practices, declare agreements to be void, direct division of an enterprise, etc. In essence, apart from monetary penalties, the CCI can also direct enterprises to change the manner of conducting their business operations. Therefore, we see that the law enables the CCI to have vast powers to prevent practices which have an anti-competitive effect. The CCI has also used its powers under the Competition Act to penalize a large number of companies. The CCI is using the vast powers provided to it under the Competition Act to levy penalties. However, the manner in which the penalties are being imposed is not very structured and clear. It is apposite to note that India has moved from a regime of no penalties for anti-competitive activities (under the Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969) to a regime of heavy penalties and liabilities. As mentioned above, the CCI can penalize up to 10% of the average turnover of the parties, and further, a higher penalty is reserved for cartel activities. In 2023, the Competition Act was amended to clarify that turnover means the global turnover derived from all the products and services of the parties. While a headroom of penalties has been provided under the Competition Act (from 0.1% to 10%), the CCI, in their orders, do not spell out any reason for granting, say, penalties of 5% of average turnover or 9% of the average turnover. It is a well-established principle laid down by the Supreme Court that the CCI and NCLAT are quasi-judicial authorities, and hence, their orders must be speaking orders; i.e., the orders must clearly spell out reasons which show application of mind. Having said that, the orders of the CCI do not spell out the reason for quantum of the penalty. This is the same reason why when the matter goes up in appeal before the NCLAT, one of the hotly contested topics in the appellate forum is the quantum of penalty. Since the CCI effectively determines the rights and liabilities of the parties, and the penalties that are imposed by them are the highest economic penalties in India, it is essential that due reasons are provided in the order of the quantum of penalty. Such reasons for penalties are required to determine proportionality by a higher forum, and indeed, it is a part of due process. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations in the case of Excel Crop case, 42 Excel Crop case, paras 80, 84, 91, 94, 95, 96, 106-108, 111-114. where the Supreme Court relied on the decision of the Competition Appeal Court of South Africa in Southern Pipeline Contractors v. Competition Commission to hold that imposition of the penalty on the total turnover would be inequitable for a multi-product company. The Supreme Court looked beyond the literal interpretation and applied rule of purposive interpretation while interpreting section 27(b) of the Competition Act and held that there is no justification for including other products of such companies if the contravention of the Competition Act involves one specific unit or business. So, any penalty imposed under the Competition Act should be imposed only on the ‘relevant turnover’, i.e., the turnover of the specific unit or business of a multi-product company, with respect to which the violation has taken place. The Supreme Court applied the principle of proportionality to arrive at this conclusion and believed that imposing a penalty on the total turnover of multi-product companies would be grossly disproportionate to the degree of contravention. Hence, by looking at the principle of proportionality, the CCI can’t impose more than a 10% penalty on the company’s relevant turnover. Having said that, the Supreme Court discussed a two-step calculation approach to calculate the penalty. The first step would be determination of the relevant turnover based on audited financial statements or any other reliable records. In the second step, the aggravating and mitigating circumstances while imposing an appropriate percentage of penalty. Furthermore, the Supreme Court also looked at the guidelines of foreign competition agencies, such as the European Commission and the United Kingdom’s Office of Fair Trade, to suggest that guidelines for the calculation of penalty need to be framed to ensure proportionality while imposing penalty. It was further noted that imposing penalty on the relevant turnover aligns with the purpose of the Competition Act because if penalty is calculated based on total turnover, then it will result in over-deterrence and stifle the economy of the country. So, the penalties under section 27(b) of the Competition Act must be calculated as per the relevant turnover, or else an excessively high penalty may be imposed which will ultimately harm the economic growth, investment, entrepreneurship, employment and the interest of national economy, which is not the intention of the Competition Act. However, in 2023, the Competition Act was amended to impose penalties on the global turnover of the companies, which need not be limited specifically to the infringing products. This might result in discouraging investors and has the potential to have far-reaching implications on multinational companies to do business in India. This amendment may, thus, be the subject matter of judicial scrutiny in future appropriate cases for including all the units and businesses (all over the world) of the company while calculating the penalty under section 27(b) of the Competition Act since it may be argued that it is against the principle of proportionality as was laid down by the Supreme Court in the Excel Crop case (discussed supra). Apart from the monetary penalties, the decisional practice of the CCI would show that under section 27 of the Competition Act, it has also directed the enterprise to change its business practice, for example, by directing cease-and-desist order and behavioural remedies. In the case of Shri Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Ltd. and Others (‘Automobile spare parts case’),43 Shri Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Ltd. and Others, Case No. 03 of 2011, para. 22.3. the CCI imposed the following behavioural remedies on the automobile manufacturers:(i) allow suppliers to sell spare parts in the open market without any restriction on independent repairers; (ii) the automobile manufacturers must develop an effective system regarding the availability of spare parts and diagnostic tools in the market, sell spare parts under fair and reasonable terms, and train independent and provide technical support to repairers; (iii) to decrease the prices and provide more choices to the consumers; (iv) automobile manufacturers should standardize the increasing number of different spare parts; (v) the automobile manufacturers shall not impose any restrictive condition that warranties would be cancelled if the consumer avails of services of any independent repairer; (vi) the automobile manufacturers have to be transparent regarding the information of the spare parts, their maximum retail prices (MRPs), and any such information by publishing such information on their websites. Similarly, the CCI in Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Others v. MakeMyTrip and Others (‘MMT-Go case’)44 Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Others v. MakeMyTrip and Others, Case No. 14 of 2019 and 01 of 2020, para. 312. passed a set of directions to MakeMyTrip for abuse of dominance and vertical anti-competitive agreement with OYO to delist the inventories of competitors of OYO. The CCI imposed the following direction on the MakeMyTrip:(i) remove the price and room availability parity obligation and remove the exclusive conditions from the agreement with hotels; (ii) MakeMyTrip must impose fair conditions on the hotels; there must be transparent disclosures regarding the non-availability of inventories on MakeMyTrip’s platform, etc.; (iii) listing of hotels on fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms. In Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC,45 Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC, Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 617. the CCI found Google to be abusing its dominant position in the Android ecosystem. The CCI ordered the following behavioural remedies on Google:(i) Google shall prohibit mandating the smartphone manufacturers to pre-install Google’s app and allow the smartphone manufacturers to decide on the pre-installation of apps. (ii) Google shall not bundle Play Store services with its other services. (iii) Google shall not offer any incentives for ensuring exclusivity for its search services and for not selling Android fork devices. (iv) Google shall not impose any anti-fragment obligations on smartphone manufacturers by restricting them from manufacturing Android fork devices. (v) Google shall allow the end-users to set the default search engine for all search entry points in the minimum steps possible. In the app store market, CCI passed some behavioural remedies on Google for violating section 4 of the Competition Act in XYZ and Others v. Alphabet Inc. and Others.46 XYZ and Others v. Alphabet Inc and Others, Case No. 07 of 2020, 14 and 35 of 2021. para. 395. CCI passed the following remedies:(i) Google shall allow app developers to use any third-party billing system without imposing any adverse measurers. (ii) Google shall not restrict any app developers from communicating with end-users about their apps and offers. (iii) Google shall not restrict end-users from accessing all the features of the app developers and shall not impose any unfair pricing conditions on app developers. (iv) the criteria for the application of fees and payment policy of Google shall be transparent, and Google shall not impose any discriminatory conditions for using third-party billing systems. (v) the policy relating to the collection and processing of data from the app developers must be transparent, and Google shall allow the app developers to access the data collected through the concerned app. Further, as per the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, the CCI can accept the commitment of the enterprise in terms of change in their anti-competitive agreements and abusive practices. In case of combinations, the CCI has the power to give behavioural and/or structural remedies under section 31 of the Competition Act by issuing or accepting commitments of the parties. For instance, in relation to the merger agreement between Zee Entertainment and Sony,47 Culver Max Entertainment Private Limited, Zee Entertainment Enterprises Limited, Bangla Entertainment Private Limited, and Essel Group Participants, Combination Registration No. C-2022/04/923, 4 Oct. 2022. a structural remedy was proposed by Zee, to divest its three channels. The proposal to divestiture of channels was accepted by CCI to avoid potential harm to Zee’s business users as well as its end-users. Behavioural remedies were undertaken by Google for acquiring a non-controlling minority stake of 1.28% in Airtel.48 Google International LLC and Bharti Airtel Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/03/913, para. 24. It was noted that Google already had a stake in Jio and through this proposed combination, it was also acquiring a stake in Airtel (which is a competitor of Jio). Therefore, the CCI pointed out the possibility of the flow of competitively sensitive information between Airtel and its competing firm, i.e., Jio. Accordingly, Google proposed a modification in their Co-Marketing Agreement by saying that it will maintain an appropriate firewall to prevent the flow of competitively sensitive information between Airtel and Jio. The parties further undertook, that they will comply with all applicable privacy and data protection laws governing customer and/or consumer data and will not share customer data with each other. These cases and concepts have been discussed in the appropriate portions of the book. Thus, the Competition Act provides for vast sweeping powers provided to the CCI. Further, the CCI may pass behavioural remedies to modify the anti-competitive agreements which have been found to be in contravention of section 3 of the Competition Act. However, as per the strict interpretation of the statute, the power to modify the agreement is limited to the agreements which are in contravention of section 3 can only be modified. In DLF Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission of India and Others,49 DLF Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2014], (COMPAT) MANU/TA/0012/2014, para. 74. An appeal is pending before the Supreme Court against this erstwhile COMPAT’s order. See, DLF Home Developers Ltd v. CCI and Another, Civil Appeal No. 019945 of 2017. the erstwhile COMPAT said that under section 27 of the Competition Act, the CCI is empowered to modify the agreements for violating section 3. However, the same is not possible for the violation of section 4. The erstwhile COMPAT interpreted section 27 to say that the wording of the said provision clearly states that the CCI can order to modify the which are in contravention of section 3 of the Competition Act, and the said provision is silent about the CCI’s power to modify agreement for the contravention of section 4 of the Competition Act. Nevertheless, as noted above, the decisional practice of CCI shows that it may completely or partially modify the agreements which are in contravention of section 4 of the Competition Act. Furthermore, for conducts that amount to anti-competitive agreement or abuse of dominance, CCI has the power to pass orders against the entire group that holds control over the entity and not limit the penalty only on the concerned entity, which committed the contravention. The group may be penalized if it holds responsibility (direct attribution) or even contributes (acts in common) towards such anti-competitive/abusive conduct. The penalty clause (proviso of section 27(g) of the Competition Act) which is rather loosely worded may be interpreted to impose liability on the group companies akin to the concept of joint and several liability, often found in tortious claims. Similarly, the penalty may be imposed on the global turnover of the group. Further, in case of abuse of dominance, the CCI has been given the power to order the division of the enterprise. Considering the vast enforcement powers under the Competition Act, every enterprise must ensure that their practices are in compliance with the provisions of the Competition Act, as non-compliance will result in huge penalties, apart from consequential cascading effects such as reputational loss and change in business practices. Further, the Competition Act is applicable to all companies, irrespective of the size of the company and the sector. Even a loss-making company has no safety net prescribed under the Competition Act, and penalties can be imposed on such loss-making companies as a percentage of their turnover. In this climate of inflow of investments into India, it is critical that the enterprises undertake a thorough due diligence of the target enterprise for competition law violations prior to acquisition since the liabilities of the target enterprise may flow to the acquirer. [B] Penalties on Directors/Officers-in-Charge Companies are a juristic person as they have no mind of their own; thus, CCI can penalize the officer-in-charge who is responsible for the conduct of the company during the time when the contravention took place. Unless the officer-in-charge shows that he diligently tried to prevent the contravention of the Competition Act or the contravention was committed without his knowledge, he will be deemed to be responsible for the anti-competitive conduct of the company.50 A.N. Mohana Kurup v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 423, para. 29. Hence, apart from fining the companies or trade associations, as the case may be, the CCI can also penalize directors, managers, secretaries and any other officers-in-charge of companies under section 48 of the Competition Act. Directors owe a fiduciary duty towards the stakeholders of the company and further in a general manner to the government and the public at large. The directors must, therefore, have to identify the risks of competition law violations since failure to take note of competition law provisions can result in heavy penalties, which affect the bottom line of the company. The directors face a real risk of non-compliance, considering the rigorous regulatory regime brought in by the CCI. Under section 48 of the Competition Act, the CCI can proceed against directors and officers-in-charge of the company (e.g., Managing Director, Director, Chief Executive Officer, Chief Operating Officer, etc.) for conducting the business of a company at that relevant point of time when the violation of the Competition Act was committed by the concerned company. The threshold is quite high for a managing director to avail the due diligence exemption. A chain of evidence must be clearly shown and demonstrated by the director to show that he undertook all actions to prevent the commission of the contravention. Such evidence may be in terms of proper training being imparted to employees or business partners of the company, undertaking periodic competition compliance training and manual, etc. The CCI generally do not accept the defence raised by such officer of a company, in terms of section 48(2) of the Competition Act, that the contravention committed by the company was committed without his knowledge.51 In Re: Cartelisation by broadcasting service providers by rigging the bids submitted in response to the tenders floated by Sports Broadcasters, Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2013, para. 112. The erstwhile COMPAT, too, has stressed that the directors and the officers-in-charge responsible to the company be aware of the provisions of the Competition Act and ensure that their activities (like pricing policy, etc.) are in compliance with the provisions of the Competition Act. In fact, the erstwhile COMPAT in the National Stock Exchange (NSE) case (discussed in Chapter 3) has referred to the fiduciary duty of the director to undertake the activities of the company in compliance with the provisions of the Competition Act. The erstwhile COMPAT opined that directors must be aware of the provisions of the Competition Act, as the duty of the director is very onerous in ensuring that the business of the company is run in a competition law-compliant manner. If one sees the pattern of the CCI, the CCI directs the offices of the DG in their prima facie order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act to investigate the role of persons who were in charge of the company when the violation of the Competition Act was committed. The reason behind such a pattern is that while conducting an inquiry against the company, the CCI should simultaneously proceed against the officer-in-charge as well. Further, the officer-in-charge must get an opportunity to be heard and raise objection against the proceeding against it. If the CCI holds a person liable without providing an opportunity or does not hear the person, then the order will be vitiated due to violation of the principles of natural justice.52 A.N. Mohana Kurup v. CCI, Appeal No. 05 of 2016, para. 34. Also, the insights provided by the officer-in-charge would help the CCI analyse the matter holistically and pass a well-reasoned order. Most importantly, this would protect the principles of natural justice of the officer-in-charge by allowing them to raise objections during the inquiry process.53 Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare v. M/s Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited, Ref. 02/2015, 107/2015, 03/2016, Ref. 01/2016 and 10/2016, paras 32, 33. The Delhi High Court in Cadila Healthcare Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2018], (Delhi High Court) 252 (2018) DLT 647, paras 68, 69, validated the reasoning of Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare v. M/s Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited and held that power to investigate can be suo motu expanded to proceed against the officer in charge. Nevertheless, even if the prima facie order has not explicitly directed DG to investigate the officer-in-charge, the DG is still duty-bound to investigate all the officer-in-charge.54 Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare v. M/s Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited, Ref. 02/2015, 107/2015, 03/2016, Ref. 01/2016 and 10/2016, para. 35. This is done to ensure that directors and officers-in-charge are made vicariously liable for the violations of the company, lift the corporate veil and penalize the persons who were responsible for the conduct of the company. It is important to note that a finding against the company is a pre-requisite to impose penalty on the officer-in-charge. As per the erstwhile COMPAT in A.N. Mohan Kurup v. Competition Commission of India,55 A.N. Mohana Kurup v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 423, paras 29, 30. proceeding under section 48 of the Competition Act can’t be invoked against the officer-in-charge of the company unless there is an affirmative finding against the company. It is important to note that if the CCI could not find any contravention of the Competition Act by the company, then the CCI could not penalize the officer-in-charge of the company under section 48 of the Competition Act. Nevertheless, the CCI can simultaneously conduct an inquiry under section 26 of the Competition Act against the company and its officer-in-charge. The Delhi High Court, in Pran Mehra v. Competition Commission of India,56 Pran Mehra v. Competition Commission of India [2015], (Delhi High Court) 2015 SCC OnLine Del 7929, paras 1, 5, 6. said that for efficacious and expeditious inquiry, there can’t be two separate proceedings, one in respect of the company and another proceeding for the officer-in-charge. Thus, while the officer in charge will be held liable only if the company is found to be in violation of the Competition Act, the proceedings against the company and the directors can carry on simultaneously. This Delhi High Court judgement was re-emphasized by CCI in Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare v. M/s Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited,57 Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare v. M/s Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited, Ref. 02/2015, 107/2015, 03/2016, Ref. 01/2016 and 10/2016, paras 30, 32, 33, 35. to hold that when an inquiry under section 26 of the Competition Act is carried out, the finding of contravention has to be first recorded against the company. Pursuant to a finding of an anti-competitive conduct against the company, the question of the personal liability of officers arise. However, the findings against both the company and its officer-in-charge would be in the same order. As a practical step, once the CCI has identified that the company is in contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act, it asks for the last three years’ income tax statements of the directors so that they can levy a penalty as a percentage of the income of the directors, as shown in the income tax statement. Further, the CCI has not only levied penalties on officers-in-charge but also barred the President and General Secretary of Kerala Film Exhibitors Federation (KFEF), an association representing 315 film theatres in Kerala, from participating in the association’s affairs, including its administration, management and governance, for a period of two years.58 In Re: M/s. Crown Theatre v. Kerala Film Exhibitors Federation (KFEF), Case No. 16 of 2014, para. 9(d), (e). Therefore, apart from penalties, they can ask a person to leave the management of the company. [C] Advocacy Remedy Apart from penalties and directions to companies and directors, CCI also awarded an advocacy-based remedy. Under the power under section 27(g) of the Competition Act, the CCI in Madhya Pradesh Chemists and Distributors Federation (MPCDF) v. Madhya Pradesh Chemists and Druggist Association (MPCDA) and Others 59 Case No. 64 of 2014, para. 161. had awarded advocacy based remedy to a trade association by mandating the trade associations to organize competition awareness and compliance programmes for all its members. Similarly, in Re, M/s. Crown Theatre v. Kerala Film Exhibitors Federation (KFEF),60 Case No. 16 of 2014, para. 9(f). Also see, XYZ v. Tamil Film Producers Council and Others, Case No. 07 of 2018, para. 58. it mandated the trade association to organize, in letter and in spirit, at least five competition awareness and compliance programmes over the next six months in the State of Kerala for its members and the same was directed to get commenced before the expiry of sixty days from the receipt of the order by trade association. This is path-breaking, considering that the CCI has now mandated a trade association to follow competition compliance sessions, which earlier was generally recommended. Thus, the other trade associations and companies must take note of the latest order and amendments to put in place an efficient competition compliance programme to educate their members about competition law. Having in place an effective competition compliance programme may act as a mitigating factor in competition law proceedings. [D] Fines for Non-cooperation/Furnishing False Information The Competition Act also provides the power to the CCI to levy fines on any person or company who fails to comply with the direction of the CCI or the DG in furnishing information or deposing on oath or other directions issued by the CCI or DG to collect information. Failure to comply with such directions is punishable with a fine which may extend up to INR 1 lakh for each day during which such failure continues subject to a maximum of INR 1 crore, as may be determined by the CCI. Further, in the event that any person makes a false statement, omits to state any material facts, or wilfully alters/hides any document knowing it to be material before the CCI or the DG, such person shall be liable to a penalty which shall not be less than INR 50 lakhs, but which may extend to INR 1 crore, as may be determined by the CCI. Similarly, for non-furnishing information on combinations, the CCI can impose a penalty which may extend to 1% of the total turnover, assets, or the value of the transaction. In Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC. v. CCI and Others,61 Competition Appeal (at) No. 1 of 2022, paras 263. 284, 314, 316, 318, 326, 330-333, 339, 340, 345, 346, 375, 376. wherein the NCLAT upheld the order of the CCI which revoked the approval granted to Amazon’s acquisition of Future Coupon Private Ltd.2 It was held that Amazon was misleading and inadequately disclosures its strategic intent of the transaction. It was found that the said transaction was not a mere investment in Future Coupon Private Limited but a strategy of Amazon to become the single largest shareholder of Future Retail Limited (FRL). Thereby, the acquisition process was suspended, and sections 43A, 44 & 45 of the Competition Act were applied to impose a monetary penalty on Amazon because it failed to disclose certain material facts as per section 6(2) of the Competition Act. Under section 45(2) of the Competition Act, Amazon was directed to file a notice in Form II, which will disclose the material facts of the Amazon and Future deal in a detailed manner. Furthermore, the NCLAT upheld the CCI’s direction to revoke the approval by pointing out that section 45 (2) of the Competition Act clearly points out that the CCI may also pass such other order as it deems fit. In terms of monetary penalties, the NCLAT also upheld the penalty of INR 200 crores under section 43A but reduced the penalty under sections 44 and 45 from INR 2 crores to INR 1 crores. Thus, the total penalty imposed under sections 43A, 44 and 45 of the Competition Act is INR 201 crores. Soon after the NCLAT passed its order, Amazon filed an appeal against the order of NCLAT before the Supreme Court, and the recovery proceeding against the penalty was stopped by the Supreme Court.62 Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC. v. CCI and Others, Civil Appeal No. 004974 of 2022. Moreover, making false statements or omissions to provide any material information for the combination shall not be less than INR 50 lakhs but may extend to INR 5 crores, as may be determined by the CCI. [E] Interim Relief The Competition Act also provides the power to the CCI to provide interim relief under section 33. A plain reading of section 33 of the Competition Act says that the CCI has the discretionary power to issue an interim relief to temporarily restrict contravention of section 3(1), or 4(1) or 6(1) of the Competition Act. The procedure for the interim order has to be mandated according to Regulation 31 of the General Regulation, 2009. Interim relief is a competition enforcement tool that can be used to prohibit anti-competitive activities till the completion of the inquiry. Further, the CCI is empowered to reverse or modify its interim order, and the CCI also has the power to pass an ex-parte interim order. However, after passing an ex parte interim order, the CCI shall give a short notice to hear the parties.63 SAIL case, para. 118. Regulation 31(2) of the General Regulations mandates that the CCI shall hear the parties against whom such an order has been passed as soon as possible. [1] Stage of Granting Interim Relief Once the inquiry has begun, the CCI can exercise the powers vested under section 33 of the Competition Act. The Supreme Court, in the SAIL case,64 SAIL case, paras 117, 118, 120, 122, 123. has observed that at the stage of section 33 proceedings, the CCI is required to record a satisfaction that there has been contravention of the provisions of sections 3, 4 or 5 of the Competition Act and such contravention has been committed, continues to be committed or is about to be committed. This satisfaction must be understood differently from what is required while expressing a prima facie view, asking the DG to conduct an investigation in terms of section 26(1) of the Competition Act. The former is a definite expression of the satisfaction recorded by the CCI upon due application of mind, while the latter is a direction simpliciter to the DG to investigate, akin to a department proceeding. While the SAIL case did opine on the power of the CCI to issue interim relief only post the order passed by CCI under section 26(1) of the Competition Act in terms of the definition of inquiry provided under the General Regulations, in practicality, the purpose of interim relief has been withered away due to the time lag between the informant filing the information and its prayer for interim relief being heard since the CCI first has to make a determination under section 26(1) or section 26(2) of the Competition Act as to whether a prima facie case is being made out. In practice, the time lag between these two events would be around three to five months. This time lag creates challenges for the parties in order to get urgent interim relief to avoid irreparable injury. The decisional practice of the CCI shows that CCI rarely exercises its power under section 33 of the Competition Act. This is because the conditions to grant interim relief are difficult to satisfy since the anti-competitive harm to the market, if any, is pending investigation. [2] Conditions to Grant Interim Relief In a case of interim relief, the Supreme Court in the SAIL case65 SAIL case, para. 119. noted that CCI should pass a reasoned order, which, inter alia, should satisfy the following three conditions: (i) record its satisfaction (which has to be of much higher degree than the formation of a prima facie view under section 26(1) of the Act) in clear terms that an act in contravention of the stated provisions has been committed and continues to be committed or is about to be committed; (ii) it is necessary to issue order of restraint, and (iii) from the record before the CCI, there is every likelihood that the party to the lis would suffer irreparable and irretrievable damage, or there is definite apprehension that it would have adverse effect on competition in the market. [a] Stronger Prima Facie Case To fulfil the first condition, there must be a probability of the existence of a stronger prima facie violation of sections 3(1), 4(1) or 6(1) of the Competition Act. At the interim relief stage, the satisfaction of the CCI should be of a higher degree, depicting a definite expression recorded by the CCI that a strong case is made out and necessitating interim reliefs must be passed to protect the competition and competitors. Accordingly, at the interim relief stage, the evidence required to pass the threshold of the first condition is also higher than the prima facie view under section 26(1) of the Competition Act. In the MMT-Go case,66 Interim order dated 9 Mar. 2021, MMT-Go case, paras 91, 92. the CCI in its interim order held that the threshold for satisfaction for forming a prima facie view under section 26(1) is a tentative view at that stage, whereas to qualify the first condition for interim relief, a definite expression of the satisfaction of existence of a prima facie case needs to be recorded by the CCI. However, the threshold for satisfaction differs from case to case according to the availability of evidence. Suppose at the prima facie stage, evidence is high enough for the CCI to record its satisfaction with the interim order. Then, there may not be a requirement to raise the threshold of its ‘satisfaction’ for the interim relief. Further, in Fast Track Call Cab v. ANI Technologies,67 Section 33 order in Fast Track Call Cab v. ANI Technologies, 2015 SCC OnLine CCI 141, para. 12. the CCI observed that an interim relief can’t be granted just because of the existence of a strong prima facie case. The parties seeking interim relief have to show that the balance of convenience lies in their favour, and they will face irreparable loss or injury if interim relief is not granted. [b] Necessary to Issue Interim Order or Balance of Convenience The CCI has to compare the inconvenience between the competitor and all the stakeholders of the market on the one side and the alleged parties on the other side. In Confederation of Real Estate Developer v. Department of Town and Country Planning,68 Section 33 order in Confederation of Real Estate Developer v. Department of Town and Country Planning, Case No. 40 of 2017, para. 19. it was said that while considering the balance of convenience, the CCI has to contemplate whether justice would be best served by passing an interim order. In the M/s Nuziveedu Seeds Limited & Ors. v. Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited & Others,69 Section 33 order in M/s Nuziveedu Seeds Limited & Ors. v. Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited & Others, Case No. 107 of 2015, para. 25. it was noted that, while granting interim relief, CCI needs to answer what will be the impact of the relief on the parties. If the interim relief is granted, then Monsanto will not face any major financial challenges, whereas if the relief isn’t granted, then the risk to Nuziveedu Seeds Limited and the whole market ecosystem is imminent. So, the CCI analyses what would be the impact on both sides if the interim relief is granted. In this case, the CCI granted interim relief because if the CCI had not intervened at the interim stage, then neither can the extant position be restored ex ante at a later stage, nor the likely damage to the Informants be compensated. Similarly, in the MMT-Go case,70 Section 33 order in MMT-Go case, para. 106. the CCI, while deciding whether an interim order needs to be passed to grant access to FabHotels and Treebo on MakeMyTrip’s online platform, the CCI found that the balance of convenience lies in favour of FabHotels and Treebo, and MMT-Go will not be put to much inconvenience by providing access to its online platform. Thus, for the second condition, the CCI does a balancing exercise, where the CCI weighs the inconveniences that would be felt by the competitors and all stakeholders of the market if the interim order isn’t granted versus the inconvenience of the alleged parties if the interim relief is granted. [c] Irreparable Harm The third condition is divided into two sub-conditions, separated by an ‘or’, inferring any one of the sub-conditions is enough to fulfil the third condition. The first sub-condition considers the irreparable harm that is caused to the informant; however, the second sub-condition considers the irreparable harm to the competition in the market. Thus, the third condition will be fulfilled when there is a likelihood of irreparable harm to the informant or the competition in the market. The determination of irreparable harm is based on factual circumstances. In the M/s Nuziveedu Seeds Limited & Ors. v. Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited & Others,71 Section 33 order in the M/s Nuziveedu Seeds Limited & Ors. v. Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited & Others, Case No. 107 of 2015, paras 24, 25. the CCI observed that the destruction of the patented products would cause irreparable harm to the informant, the other stakeholder of the competition (i.e., farmers), and the ecosystem of cotton cultivation. Similarly, in the Confederation of Real Estate Developer v. Department of Town and Country Planning,72 Section 33 order in Confederation of Real Estate Developer v. Department of Town and Country Planning, Case No. 40 of 2017, para. 23. an interim order was granted to prevent irreparable harm to the informant and the buyers of the buildings (final consumers) by way of cancellation of licences and levying of penal interest. Also, in the MMT-Go case,73 Section 33 order in MMT-Go case, paras 108, 114, 117. irreparable harm would have been caused to FabHotels and Treebo, as well as the demand side of the market (i.e., hotel partners), if the CCI had not restrained MakeMyTrip from carrying on its anti-competitive practice. The anti-competitive practice has denied market access to FabHotels and Treebo, and therefore, they are not able to compete in the market. So, the CCI noted that the anti-competitive practice would irreversibly alter the competitive landscape by driving existing competitors from the market; hence, interim relief was passed directing MakeMyTrip to allow FabHotels and Treebo on its online platform.

sections.title: COMPETITION LAW COMPLIANCE
sections.text: §1.04 COMPETITION LAW COMPLIANCE The Competition Act is in its fourteenth year of existence. CCI has been extremely assertive in its enforcement outlook. According to the CCI Annual Report, 2022-23, the CCI has received more than 1,224 information alleging violations under sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act and 1,015 merger filings under sections 5 and 6 of the Competition Act as of March 2023. The CCI has closed 699 matters against violation under sections 3 and 4, out of which 185 cases of contravention were found. Regarding the merger filings, CCI disposed of 1,007 merger filings.74 CCI Annual Report, 2022-23, Pg. xiv. During 2022-23, a total of forty-eight final orders against contravention of sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act were passed, and ninety-nine merger filings were disposed. As mentioned above, based on figures available in the public domain, the CCI has imposed an aggregate penalty of nearly INR 2672.48 crore.75 CCI Annual Report, 2022-23, p. Pg. xiv. Further, the CCI has imposed penalties on directors and officers-in-charge. Further, along with the monetary penalties, CCI imposes directions to modify the business practices or the structures of the companies for violating the Competition Act. Therefore, we see that the ramifications of not following the Competition Act are very severe. In addition, the CCI also conducts numerous awareness programs. Due to the all-round efforts by CCI, a fair degree of awareness has been created among the companies with respect to the Competition Act.76 Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development, Annual Report on Competition Policy Developments in India, 2018, para. 2. The CCI also directs the companies to conduct events educating their respective members about the awareness of competition law and the consequent need for competition compliance.77 XYZ v. Tamil Film Producers Council, Case No. 07 of 2018, para. 58. Considering the ramifications of noncompliance with competition law, there is an urgent need for advocacy of competition law principles to Indian companies. Every business model, operation or process shall be evaluated from the perspective of whether there is a risk of anti-competitive conduct therein. Steps must be taken to eliminate or minimize such risks. Companies should actively engage in such risk management activities, also known as Active Risk Management (ARM) initiatives. ARM is a function of the boards of companies. As any anti-competitive conduct would have disastrous consequences for the operations of the companies, the boards must be sensitive to ‘red flags’ in the operations of the company and monitor them. Non-compliance would bring in, besides fines, damages to reputation or brand value. Further, a positive determination of anti-competitive behaviour by a company would, in turn, lead to ripple regulatory effects; i.e., more governmental agencies may start investigations and start more intense scrutiny on the various business aspects of the company. The board should send an unequivocal message from top to bottom that business must be done in a compliant manner. Further, not only the companies but also each of the channel partners must act in a manner compliant with the competition law, and non-compliance by the channel partner may lead to termination of the agreement between the company and the channel partners.
