The CCI noted that the film industry is growing at a Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of 13.7% by 2023. The major factors behind the growth of the film industry are significant supply and demand, accessibility of smartphones, and inexpensive data. The CCI analysed previous competition law cases involving film associations and surveyed various stakeholders from different film industries. The CCI noted that the multiplex owners have a higher bargaining power than the film producers. Hence, considering the prevailing practices in deal-making between multiplexes and film producers, the CCI made the following recommendations:(i) the standard form of contract must be avoided between multiplexes and film producers, and the contract must not be a unilateral, one-sided contract favouring the multiplexes; (ii) multiplexes should provide fair terms to the film producers for their promotion of films; (iii) multiplexes should not restrain the producers from releasing their films; (iv) transparency in box office revenue collection by adopting a monitoring system; (v) film associations must not facilitate anti-competitive practices in the industry.

sections.title: CONSUMER
sections.text: §1.13 CONSUMER One of the main objectives of the Competition Act is to promote economic efficiency by assisting in the creation of markets which are responsive to consumer preferences.135 SAIL case, paras 6, 8, 125. The prohibition of anti-competitive practices has direct or indirect benefits to the consumers who are at the end of the supply chain of the market. Therefore, the CCI enforces the principles of competition law to ensure that the consumer policies of the market players provide adequate and affordable choices for such consumers.136 Excel Crop case, para. 21. Along with enhancing the welfare of the supply side’s consumers, the Competition Act also protects the demand-side or supply-side business users from being exploited or excluded from the market.137 Ibid. Though the consumers can protect their rights by applying the consumer protection policies, i.e., the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, however, the Competition Act will be in addition to the remedies available under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019. As we discussed earlier, section 62 of the Competition Act says that the competition law is in addition to other laws. Similarly, section 100 of the Consumer Protection Act also says that the consumer protection law is in addition to other laws. Further, in accordance with this, the Supreme Court in the Excel Crop case,138 Ibid. said that the Competition Act will complement the Consumer Protection Act while holding that:Consumer welfare protection may be required in order to redress a perceived imbalance between the market power of consumers and producers. The imbalance between consumers and producers may stem from market failures such as information asymmetries, the lack of bargaining position towards producers and high transaction costs. Competition policy may serve as a complement to consumer protection policies to address such market failures. The CCI will provide a remedy to the consumer by rectifying the market failure. Whereas the Consumer Forums will protect consumers by directing the enterprise to compensate for the personal harm of the consumer. So, both laws prohibit unfair trade practices and protect the interests of consumers, but on different tangents. In Shri Sunil Bansal v. M/s Jaiprakash Associates Ltd. and Ors., 139 Shri Sunil Bansal v. M/s Jaiprakash Associates Ltd. and Ors., 2015 SCC OnLine CCI 180, paras 59, 63, 68, 69. a group of house owners filed information against the builder for abusing its dominance by imposing unfair terms on them. Challenging the jurisdiction of the CCI, the builder argued that the relevant product market is buildings which are immovable goods and CCI can’t deal with immovable goods. Relying on a Supreme Court judgement on the Consumer Protection Act, the builder submitted that the activity of selling buildings amounts to the sale of immovable property and not ‘goods’ as ‘goods’ under the Competition Act, making reference to the Sale of Goods Act, 1930, which expressly excludes immovable property from its ambit. Thus, the buildings are also outside the scope of the CCI’s jurisdiction. Finally, the builder submitted that this is a consumer law matter and that the Consumer Forum is the appropriate body to adjudicate the matter. However, the CCI rejected the contention of the builder because a Supreme Court’s judgement on the Consumer Protection Act will not be applicable to a competition-related matter. The CCI held that the definitions of ‘consumer’ and ‘service’ under the Competition Act are wider than the definition of these terms provided in the Consumer Protection Act. Further, the CCI held that the purpose of the Competition Act allows the CCI to protect consumers from unfair practices. Finally, the CCI held that the availability of remedies before the Consumer Forum does not oust the jurisdiction of the CCI, as section 62 of the Competition Act says that the Consumer Protection Act is in addition to the remedies available under the Competition Act. Hence, CCI has jurisdiction to deal with the anti-competitive practices of the builder. Furthermore, the definition of the word ‘consumer’ in the Competition Act is wider than the definition of ‘consumer’ in the Consumer Protection Act, 2019. The Competition Act has defined the meaning of the word ‘consumer’ under section 2(f) as any person who purchases goods or avails services for resale, commercial or personal purposes. The meaning of the word ‘consumer’ under the Competition Act is wider than the meaning that is given under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019. Section 2(7) of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019 defines the word ‘consumer’ as any person who buys or purchases goods or avails services for personal purposes, not commercial purposes, or to resale them further for profit. Hence, from the definition given under the Competition Act, a consumer can be:(i) the end-consumer, which purchases goods or avails services for personal use or consumption; or (ii) the business user who also purchases goods or avails services for commercial purposes or resale them. The Competition Act protects a person who is involved in a business-to-business transaction because such a person will also fall within the definition of ‘consumer’. While the Consumer Protection Act, 2019 is only concerned with the person who does not buy goods for commercial or business purposes, the Competition Act takes into account even intermediate consumers. The Consumer Protection Act, 2019 would not protect people who purchased goods or availed services for a ‘commercial purpose’.

sections.title: ENTERPRISE
sections.text: §1.14 ENTERPRISE The word enterprise has been defined in a broad manner in the Competition Act. It is imperative to discuss the contours of the term enterprise because the scheme of the Competition Act allows the application of section 4 of the Competition Act to an enterprise or group and not every enterprise. Section 2(h) of the Competition Act defines the term ‘enterprise’ as a person or a Department of the Government which is engaged in any economic activity relating to the production, storage, supply, investment, etc., either directly or through one or more of its units or divisions or subsidiaries. However, the government bodies discharging sovereign functions, such as activity relating to atomic energy, currency, defence and space, will be excluded from the definition of ‘enterprise’. Person has been defined in a broad manner to include any company, firm or juridical person. From the definition of the ‘enterprise’, the following key points emerge:(i) The statutory scheme of the Competition Act, the definition of enterprise and the decisional practice of the CCI with respect to enterprise have ensured that competitive neutrality is achieved in the present competition law dispensation. This means that state-owned and private businesses compete on a level playing field. Competitive neutrality requires that governments or state-owned enterprises should not misuse their powers to get an advantage over the private sector. Upon a bare perusal of the definition, it becomes clear that all the commercial activities of the government, excluding the sovereign one, come under the provisions of the Competition Act and that no anti-trust exemptions are applicable to state-owned enterprises. State-owned enterprises or public sector undertakings/enterprises (PSUs) are government majority-held companies, statutory corporations set up by an act of Parliament and fully owned by the government. Reading this with section 19(4)(g) of the Competition Act, the CCI can look into the activities of dominant enterprises, even if the dominant enterprise has acquired its dominance by way of statutory monopoly. In this regard, it is apposite to note that the CCI has looked into the activities of state-owned enterprises, details of which are provided in Chapter 3. (ii) The definition explicitly excludes any function of the government that is relatable to sovereign functions that includes activity relating to energy, currency, defence and space. It has been observed by the Supreme Court that in a welfare state, functions of the state are not only the defence of the country or administration of justice or maintaining law and order but also extends to regulating and controlling the activities of people in almost every sphere, inter alia, educational, commercial, social, economic, political and even martial. In 2011, the Delhi High Court in Union of India v. Competition Commission of India,140 Union of India v. Competition Commission of India [2012], (Delhi High Court) 2012 SCC OnLine Del 1114, para. 23. explained the distinction between sovereign and non-sovereign functions with respect to the definition of enterprise under the Competition Act. The Delhi High Court noted that:Any welfare, commercial and economic functions are not sovereign functions and state while discharging such functions is as much amenable to the jurisdiction of CCI as any other private entity discharging such functions. (iii) It must be kept in mind that the objective of the Competition Act is wide, and it seeks to prevent practices, having adverse effects on competition, promote and sustain competition in the market, protect the interests of consumers and ensure freedom of trade carried out by market participants. The Competition Act applies to all enterprises and persons based on the activity performed. Although the definition of the term ‘enterprise’ does exclude sovereign functions, it is to be noted that the Competition Act does not provide for exemption for enterprises from the purview of the Competition Act. Section 54 of the Competition Act provides that the Central Government may, by notification, exempt from the application of the Competition Act any enterprise which performs a sovereign function on behalf of the Central Government or the State Government. Thus, the scheme of the Competition Act provides that there must be an exemption which has to be granted by the Central Government under section 54 to state that the Competition Act is not applicable to such enterprises/class of enterprises. (iv) It must be noted that the Supreme Court in Coal India Ltd. v. CCI and Another,141 Coal India case, paras 81, 82. stated that Coal India Ltd., which is engaged in coal mining services, will fall under the definition of the word ‘enterprise’, as the definition includes any person or a Department of the Government which is engaged in an activity related to production, storage, supply, distribution, acquisition or control of articles or goods. Further, the legislature expressly includes even Departments of the Government separately within the ambit of the word ‘enterprise’. Hence, government companies are not excluded from the definition of ‘enterprise’. It’s only the sovereign activity of the government bodies which is exempted. However, Coal India Ltd. is not discharging any sovereign function, so it will not be exempted. Similarly, the Delhi High Court in Uttrakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board (‘UAPMB’) and Others v. Competition Commission of India 142 Uttrakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board (‘UAPMB’) and Others v. Competition Commission of India, 2017 SCC OnLine Del 10906, para. 11. said that the exemption under section 2(h) will not be applicable to the writ petitioners, which was a government body, charged with the function of supply and distribution of liquor. (v) While the definition of enterprise is wide, it must be noted that for a person to be called an enterprise, it must perform a commercial activity. In Mr. Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC and Another,143 Mr. Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC and Another, Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 210. the CCI held that even if Google is not charging anything for its general web service, it can still be considered commercial or economic activity. Through the general web service, Google was collecting user data and monetizing it through advertisements. Further, it must be noted that the CCI in some of the cases has held that trade association is not an enterprise since they do not perform any commercial function and earn revenue, although some anti-competitive decisions may be taken under the aegis of the trade association. Having said that, trade associations, which are not engaged in any commercial activity, will not fall under the exemption of enterprise because in some cases, the CCI has held in some other cases that trade associations are enterprises since they earn revenues from their decisions.144 M/s Shivam Enterprises v. Kiratpur Sahib Truck Operators Co-operative Transport Society Limited & Ors, Case No. 43 of 2013, paras 13, 14. In the Competition Commission of India v. Coordination Committee of Artistes & Technicians of W.B. Film & Television (‘Coordination Committee case’),145 Competition Commission of India v. Coordination Committee of Artistes & Technicians of W.B. Film & Television [2017], (SC) (2017) 5 SCC 17, paras 46, 47, 48. the Supreme Court held that a non-profit making entity may be considered an enterprise when it is engaged in some commercial or economic activities, even if it is operating in its charitable capacity at other times. If the activity of the trade association yields profits by selling goods or providing services, then the activity will be considered economic activity, and the entity will be constituted as an enterprise. The Supreme Court further noted that the members of the trade association were engaged in the production, distribution and exhibition of films/serials in West Bengal. The collective call to boycott the production of their competing members shows that the trade association takes decisions on the exhibition of films and serials, which is an economic activity. Hence, protection in the name of being a trade association goes against the object of the Competition Act. Therefore, the test is whether the person in question performs any commercial activity and earns a revenue stream. It is because of this reason that the CCI has not exercised jurisdiction over policy formulation functions of the government because they cannot be considered to be ‘commercial activities’ and hence not an enterprise under section 2(h) of the Competition Act.146 Vineet Kumar v. Ministry of Civil Aviation, Case No. 18/2013, paras 9, 10. Therefore, a government body while formulating policy guidelines cannot be said to be indulging in policy decisions and hence cannot be termed as an enterprise and therefore section 4 will not be applicable to such entities.

sections.title: RELEVANT MARKET
sections.text: §1.15 RELEVANT MARKET Under the statutory scheme of the Competition Act, the definition of relevant market is of paramount importance. In an abuse of dominance analysis, an enterprise will be considered dominant only when the said enterprise holds a position of strength in the relevant market. Similarly, in a combination analysis, the CCI has to analyse whether the said combination will cause or is likely to cause an appreciable adverse effect in the relevant market in India. Also, in a vertical anti-competitive agreement analysis under section 3(4) of the Competition Act, although the Competition Act does not lay down expressly that the CCI has to define a relevant market, the decisional practice of the CCI (as elucidated in Chapter 2) would show that they have adopted the de minimis doctrine; i.e., parties with minimal market share entering into an agreement will not cause any appreciable adverse effect on competition (AAEC) in India.147 Shri Ghanshyam Das Vij v. M/s Bajaj Corp Limited, Case No. 68 of 2013, paras 77, 80. The Supreme Court in the Coordination Committee case, 148 Coordination Committee case, paras 14, 33-36. held that the first and foremost aspect that needs to be determined by the CCI is the relevant market in which the competition is affected due to the anti-competitive agreement. The Supreme Court interpreted the word ‘market’ in section 19(3) as ‘relevant market’ and held that it is important to determine the relevant market to identify the competitors of the concerned party, the boundaries of the competition, and the competitive constraints faced by market participants in order to examine the concentration of the market. Similarly, the Supreme Court in the Bharti Airtel case149 Bharti Airtel case, para. 86. also noted the term ‘market’ in section 19(3) as ‘relevant market’. However, later the Supreme Court, in the Coordination Committee case,150 Clarification order of Coordination Committee case, [2018], (SC) Miscellaneous Application No. 490 of 2017 in Civil Appeal No. 6691 of 2014. clarified that determination of ‘relevant market’ is not mandatorily determined for the violation of section 3(3) (cartel provisions) of the Competition Act. The Supreme Court clearly said that determining the relevant market is not a mandatory pre-condition in section 3(3) violations because of the statutory presumption contained in section 3(3) of the Competition Act. However, this clarification may not be applicable to vertical anti-competitive agreements because the Coordination Committee case specifically dealt with violation of section 3(3) of the Competition Act. Such statutory presumption is not present for violation of section 3(4) of the Competition Act, hence, it can be assumed that the Supreme Court never implied that delineation of relevant market is not mandatory for section 3(4). Although the legislative framework does not mandate the CCI to define a relevant market for a section 3(4) analysis, the final outcome of the analysis of the appreciable adverse effects on the competition may still require determining relevant market. Therefore, a broad understanding of the market contours has to be undertaken, even in section 3(4) analysis. Relevant market is essentially a tool to identify and define the boundaries of competition between firms. The primary purpose of market definition is to identify the competitive constraints faced by enterprises.151 Bharti Airtel case, para. 87. In an abuse of dominance investigation, the main purpose behind identification of relevant market is to understand whether an enterprise has the position of strength to behave independently of the competitive forces. In a combination analysis, the CCI identifies the relevant market to see whether the combination would cause an AAEC in the relevant market. The mode of analysis in a combination analysis is whether the combination would create or strengthen market power in the relevant market. Generally, the CCI has approved combinations in the first phase itself because post-combination, the combined market share of the combining parties in the relevant market is minimal. Therefore, the objective of determining a relevant market is to identify those competitors of the enterprises involved that are capable of constraining the behaviour of the concerned enterprise and of preventing them from behaving independently of any effective competitive pressure. Further, regarding the scope of defining relevant market for combinations cases and abuse of dominance cases, the erstwhile COMPAT, in National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India,152 National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2014], (COMPAT) MANU/TA/0016/2014, para. 54. observed that combination analysis is an ex-ante process of the proposed combination where CCI examines whether the proposed combination will adversely affect the competition of the market in the future. Thus, in combination cases, the CCI looks at the probabilities and likelihood of anti-competitive effects due to the combination. Therefore, it is natural that the determination of relevant market for combination cases becomes ‘as narrow as possible’ to evaluate the impact of combination in the future. However, such an analysis of a combination case is in sharp contra distinction with an abuse of dominance case, which is an ex post proceeding. In an abuse of dominance case, the CCI decides the past abusive conduct of an enterprise. Therefore, the analysis of abuse of dominance is an analysis of a past conduct. Thus, the mode of determination of relevant market in combination cases may differ from determination of relevant market in abuse of dominance cases. Relevant market has been defined in section 2(r) of the Competition Act as the market which may be delineated according to the relevant product market and/or the relevant geographic market. Relevant market has been defined both in product terms as well as geographic terms. Relevant product market is defined in section 2(t) of the Competition Act to mean a market comprising the products or services which are regarded as interchangeable by the customer due to the characteristics of the products or services, their prices and use, or interchangeable by the supplier due to the ease of switching production without incurring any significant additional cost or risk. Therefore, the primary thrust of defining relevant product market is the interchangeability or substitutability of a given product in terms of price, characteristics, use and switching cost. [A] Relevant Product Market Section 19(7) of the Competition Act provides for the indices which have to be taken into account by the CCI, while determining the relevant product market. While determining the relevant product market, the CCI shall consider all or any of the following factors, namely:(a) physical characteristics, end-use of goods or the nature of services; (b) price of goods or service; (c) consumer preferences; (d) exclusion of in-house production; (e) existence of specialized producers; (f) classification of industrial products; (g) costs associated with switching demand or supply to other goods or services; (h) categories of customers. Thus, we see that demand side and supply side substitutability is the primary factor to be taken into account while determining relevant product market. Demand side substitutability assesses the extent to which customers could and would switch among substitute products in response to a change in relative prices or quality or availability or other features. It is to be noted that the relevant product market has to be defined by taking into account all those products or services which are regarded as interchangeable or substitutable by the consumer. So, two products which are not perfectly substitutable but functionally interchangeable would form part of the same relevant product market. Apart from demand side substitutability, competition law authorities also look at supply side substitutability. Previously, the Competition Act only provided for consideration of demand side substitutability, and the CCI has also held in a few cases that it only considers demand side substitutability, and not supply side substitutability, which is to be considered for the purpose of determining the relevant market.153 Informant (Confidential) v. Grasim Industries Limited, Case No. 51 of 2017, para. 20. However, at the same time, in JK Tyre & Industries Limited and J.K. Asia Pacific (S) Pte Ltd.,154 JK Tyre, J.K. Asia Pacific (S) Pte Ltd., Cavendish Industries Ltd. and Kesoram Industries Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2015/10/322, 18 Feb. 2016, para. 10. for the purpose of competition assessment in the tyre market, the CCI has also given due consideration to supply side substitutability. Having said that, the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, amended the definition of relevant product market and also added a specific factor in section 19(7) of the Competition Act to introduce the concept of supply side substitutability. The wording of section 2(t) of the Competition Act states that relevant product market is that basket of products which are considered substitutable by the consumer or supplier. Therefore, the criteria for defining relevant product market under the Competition Act includes both demand side substitutability and supply side substitutability. [B] Relevant Geographic Market Section 2(s) of the Competition Act defines relevant geographic market as:market comprising the area in which the conditions of competition for supply of goods or provision of services or demand of goods or services are distinctly homogenous and can be distinguished from the conditions prevailing in the neighbouring areas. Section 19(6) of the Competition Act further provides some factors which need to be taken into account by the CCI while determining relevant geographic market. While determining the ‘relevant geographic market’, CCI shall have due regard to all or any of the following factors, namely: –(a) regulatory trade barriers; (b) local specification requirements; (c) national procurement policies; (d) adequate distribution facilities; (e) transport costs; (f) language; (g) consumer preferences; (h) need for secure or regular supplies or rapid after-sales services; (i) characteristics of goods or nature of services; (j) costs associated with switching supply or demand to other areas. [C] Trends of the CCI While the definitions and the approach of defining relevant market under the Competition Act are similar to other anti-trust jurisdictions, the important factor to note is the manner in which the CCI adopts the same in its analysis against vertical anti-competitive agreements, dominance investigation and merger control proceedings. Some of the key trends which can be seen are as follows: [1] Starting Point of Analysis As discussed earlier in the Coordination Committee case,155 Coordination Committee case, paras 33-38. the first step of the analysis is the determination of the relevant market. It is only by defining the relevant market that a firm’s market power can be assessed. The purpose of defining the relevant market is to identify which products and services operate as a competitive constraint on the behaviour of the suppliers of those respective products and services. The process of determining the relevant market starts by looking into a relatively narrow potential product market. Thereafter, the potential product market is expanded to include those substituted products which buyers or suppliers would still prefer, even after there is a price increase above the competitive price. Likewise, the relevant geographic market needs to be defined. In fact, the Supreme Court in the Bharti Airtel case,156 Bharti Airtel case, para. 87. held that ‘market’ definition is a tool to identify the concentration of the market and the competition boundaries of the market participants. The relevant market is the key to determining which market participant holds the power over the market and the impact of the alleged conduct on the market. Thus, it is necessary to delineate the relevant product market and relevant geographical market to determine the market participant who has power over the market. The CCI, in the HT Media Ltd v. Super Cassettes Ltd., 157 HT Media Ltd v. Super Cassettes Ltd., Case No. 40 of 2011, para. 134. has noted that ‘since the allegation of the informant pertains to certain conduct of the opposite party in licensing its repertoire of songs to the informant, the market for licensing of music content (protect as intellectual property) is a good starting point for determination of the relevant market in this case’. Therefore, the approach of the CCI in identifying relevant market starts from the relevant product or service of the alleged dominant enterprise, and then the substitutes of the said product are identified to determine the contours of the relevant market. Similarly, in a combination case, the CCI first identifies the overlaps between the parties in India as the starting point for relevant market determination. [2] Analysis in Multi-sided Markets Is from the Perspective of Affected Parties The tools of relevant market analysis remain the same, irrespective of how many ‘sides’ or user groups the enterprise in question may cater to. But in such situations, a question emerges as to from whose perspective will the relevant market be defined. This question was answered in the MMT-Go case.158 MMT-Go case, paras 190, 295. In this case, the anti-competitive conduct of an online booking platform which connected users with various travel/tour-related service providers, such as hotels, airlines, trains, buses, was under consideration. The CCI held that since the alleged conduct under scrutiny was vis-à-vis hotels and franchise hotel service providers, assessment of dominance and relevant market delineation would have to be carried out from their perspective. To contextualize the discussion, the first thing to be considered here is who is this ‘user’ that should be the focus of assessment. When two distinct products/services are supplied, ascertaining substitutability only from the perspective of one user group may erroneously conflate the competitive constraints that exist separately and distinctly in relation to each user side. At the same time, the impact of such interactions on the substitutability has to be analysed primarily from the aggrieved user side.159 Ibid., paras 190-194. Hence, in the MMT-Go case, since the alleged conduct was related to the contractual arrangements between hotels and the online booking platform, the prime focus was the hotels’ side of the market. Yet, the consumer side perspective (i.e., the other user group served by the platform) was not completely ignored and actually supplemented this understanding to account for the interdependencies between both sides of the market. The functioning of two-sided markets has been discussed in more detail in Chapter 6 on digital markets. Thus, the principle emerges that the starting point for defining a relevant market is to first understand the service provided by the enterprise under scrutiny and then to identify alternate services that are substitutable in terms of price, characteristics and intended use from the perspective of the affected user group. Since market definition provides a tool to draw the boundaries within which to assess the anti-competitive harm alleged, the precise boundaries of markets will change depending on the context in which they are drawn. Therefore, even in two-sided markets, one needs to look at the alleged conduct and the affected party in question to define the relevant market. [3] Relevant Geographic Market Can Only Be India As explained in detail in Chapter 3, the dominant position has been defined under the Competition Act as position of strength enjoyed by an enterprise in the relevant market in India. Based on this technical reading of the provisions, it can be said that the Competition Act only allows that relevant geographic market can only be India or territories of India, and it can be interpreted that the scheme of the Competition Act does not allow for a worldwide definition of the relevant geographic market. In Mr. Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC and Another,160 Mr. Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC and Another, Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 219. the CCI noted that even if general search services can be accessed by users anywhere in the world, the main general search services offer localized sites in different countries and in a variety of languages. The majority of users use the general web search in their local language; thus, India was considered the relevant geographic market for general web search because the conditions of competition are distinct and homogenous in India. Similarly, in combination proceedings of M/s Atos Worldline India Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s Verifone India Sales Pvt. Ltd. and Others,161 M/s Atos Worldline India Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s Verifone India Sales Pvt. Ltd. and Others, Case No. 56 of 2012, paras 6.12, 6.13. the CCI only looked at overlaps between the parties in India and the CCI determined the relevant geographical market to be the whole of India because the condition of competition in the market of Point of Sale Terminals throughout India is similar. In the Automobile spare parts case,162 Automobile spare parts case, para. 20.5.26. the CCI has taken the view that the relevant geographical market was pan India because the conditions of competition for the sale of spare parts and after-sale maintenance services are homogeneous for the authorized dealer and the independent repairers across the territory of India. [4] Online Versus Offline Distribution Model In the last decade, there has been massive growth in the online sector, and business enterprises as well as customers are preferring digital platforms over traditional offline markets. The market dynamics and character of the online market are different from those of the traditional offline market. However, earlier, the CCI did not delineate online and offline markets as separate product markets. In Ashish Ahuja v. Snapdeal.com and Another,163 Ashish Ahuja v. Snapdeal.com and Another, Case No. 17 of 2014, para. 16. a seller of electronic products filed an information alleging that Snapdeal.com has abused its dominance by denying the sale of the informant’s electronic product on its online e-commerce platform. The CCI passed an order under section 26(2) of the Competition Act, where it held that both offline and online markets were different in terms of shopping experience and discounts, and if the price in the online market increased, then consumers would shift to the offline markets and vice versa. Therefore, online and offline markets are substitutable; the CCI was of the view that offline and online markets were two different channels of distribution of the same product and were not two different relevant markets. However, market definition, particularly in digital markets, is not a static concept, and the relevant market assessment must be done at each point in time based on the prevailing circumstances. Hence, CCI shifted from the narrow approach of painting online and offline channels with the same brush to thoroughly account for the challenges in digital markets and effectively enforce the Competition Act on online market players by delineating online markets as distinct relevant market. For instance, the CCI shifted its approach while delineating relevant market in All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Pvt. Ltd.164 All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 20 of 2018, paras 21-25. In this case, it was alleged that Flipkart was engaged in deep discounting, denied market access and leveraged its position to enter another market by extending discounts and manufacturing products under private labels. The CCI in its prima facie order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, observed that Flipkart operates as a marketplace-based e-commerce platform, which facilitates trade between consumers and third-party sellers. Online platforms offer convenience for third-party sellers as well as consumers. The online marketplace provides a platform for third-party sellers to interact with a larger number of consumers, and it also benefits consumers by saving them time and commuting charges. Hence, CCI determined the relevant product market as services provided by online marketplace platforms for selling goods. Similarly, the CCI in Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd., 165 Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 40 of 2019, para. 18. where it received information from a union of Micro, Small and Mini Enterprises engaged in the trade of mobile phones and other related accessories called Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh, against two big e-commerce platforms, Flipkart and Amazon, which are alleged to be engaged in vertical agreements under section 3(4) of the Competition Act, under which Flipkart and Amazon are preferring certain sellers. Further, it was also alleged that the e-commerce platforms contravene section 4 of the Competition Act by abusing their dominant position by engaging in predatory pricing and imposing unfair conditions. So, CCI passed a prima facie order, directing the DG to investigate the allegations. In this order, the CCI made a similar observation to the Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd, and by saying that the online platform offers access to new markets to third-party sellers and increases consumers’ choice of goods and services. An online platform ecosystem provides more benefits than a traditional brick-and-mortar set-up in terms of fixed costs and reach. Again, in Lifestyle Equities C.V. v. Amazon Seller Services Pvt.,166 Lifestyle Equities C.V. v. Amazon Seller Services Pvt., Case No. 09 of 2020, para. 23. the CCI passed a dismissal order under section 26(2) of the Competition Act against the allegation against Amazon and Cloudtail. CCI reiterated the distinguishing characteristics of online platforms from offline platforms. CCI further noted that the digital sector is rapidly changing, and the delineation of the online platform cannot have a static approach. Hence, the relevant market should be delineated by considering the market reality and factual circumstances of each case. Similarly, in Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC and Others,167 Case Nos 07 and 30 of 2012, para. 97. the CCI distinguished between online and offline advertising services and found that neither of these markets is comparable. The CCI noted that online advertising is undertaken using the internet as a medium and, hence, it has a large coverage and wider reach. Similarly, online advertising is not substitutable for traditional offline advertisements, such as newspapers, radio or television, as mediums to advertise because traditional offline advertisements target fewer customers. Furthermore, online advertising also allows advertisers to accurately monitor the effectiveness of their advertisement, which is not possible for the advertiser who is using a newspaper or radio. Hence, the online advertisement market was held to be non-substitutable with the offline advertisement market. Similarly, in the MMT-Go case,168 MMT-Go case, para. 198. the CCI held that the online mode of booking service is not substitutable with offline mode of booking service. The CCI observed that end-consumer who is using online modes of payment would always prefer online channels to book hotels. And therefore, such end-consumers are considered distinct from those who prefer using traditional mode of booking by directing walk-ins to the hotels. Thus, CCI considered the online modes of booking services as a different relevant market. From the previous orders of the CCI, it can be concluded that both the seller side and buyer side perceive online platform differently than offline platform. Hence, the CCI has now taken a firm stance that online platform is neither substitutable nor interchangeable with traditional offline platforms. CCI’s decisional practice and the trends in the delineation of online and offline forms of relevant markets have been analysed in more detail in Chapter 6 on digital markets. [5] Aftermarket Versus Unified Systems Market In the Automobile spare parts case169 Automobile spare parts case, para. 20.5.54. (discussed in Chapter 3), the DG concluded that the relevant market would be the aftermarket for spares of automobiles. The reason adopted by the DG was that once a customer purchases a car, he is locked in, and he is forced to buy the spare parts of the same brand since spare parts of one brand are not substitutable with spare parts of other brands. In such an aftermarket, each automobile manufacturer was dominant in respect of its respective spares since a customer who purchases a car cannot substitute spares of a different manufacturer and is locked into the aftermarket suppliers of the car manufacturer. This case best illustrates the concept of the relevant market being an aftermarket of a primary product. Consider the said example: a consumer wishes to buy a car. When he is exploring the options to buy a car, he has several options at each price segment, but once he has purchased the car, the consumer is ‘locked in’ and has to use the spare parts of that particular car manufacturer (for instance, a consumer who has bought a Maruti car cannot use spare parts of Toyota and or avail of the services of an authorized dealer of Hyundai). In such cases, there is a presence of two different markets: one a primary market and another, the aftermarket. While determining whether the relevant market can be an aftermarket of a primary product, it has to be seen whether a consumer can shift the primary product when the prices of the spare parts of the said primary product go up without incurring substantial shifting costs. The CCI observed that consumers cannot change the car (i.e., Maruti to Toyota) if the prices of spare parts of Maruti go up exponentially. Further, due to the technical specifications and the complex engineering used to manufacture each brand of cars by the original equipment manufacturers (‘car companies’ / car manufacturers or ‘OEMs’), the spare parts of one brand are not compatible with that of another brand, and therefore, the products in the secondary market for spare parts are not interchangeable with those of the primary market, and therefore they constitute distinct relevant product markets. Based on the above and actual economic evidence, the CCI came to the conclusion that the relevant market is the aftermarket for the supply of spare parts and technical designs for each car manufacturer separately. It was argued by the car manufacturers that both the ‘sale of primary product’ as well as its aftermarket may be considered one unified systems market. In furtherance to this submission, it was argued that once the customer purchases a car, he is locked in to purchase the spare parts from the same car manufacturer. But this practice would protect the reputation of the car manufacturers as well as benefit the customers as they would be well equipped with adequate information to make the right decisions while purchasing another car or spare parts. Further, the customers could ascertain the total cost that they would likely incur during the life cycle of the car. However, the unified systems market proposed by the car manufacturers was rejected, and CCI held that the car manufacturers were unable to provide any evidence to show the positive impact on consumers due to their lock-in practices. Additionally, the lock-in practices facilitated their ability to increase the cost of their products and exploit the consumers. Based on these parameters, the CCI concluded that manufacturers were unable to demonstrate that they could, or consumers could, bring about a pro-competitive effect and hence, rejected the systems market contention. Finally, the CCI held that the sale of primary products and aftermarket products can’t be considered one unified market. The erstwhile COMPAT170 Automobile spare parts case [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 452, paras 69, 81. also agreed with the CCI’s reasoning in relation to the issue of whether the automobile and auto spare parts market constitutes a unified systems market. The erstwhile COMPAT looked at the practice of car manufacturers and held that car manufacturers failed to show any evidence to substantiate the claim of the positive impact on consumers. Finally, it was held that the choice of an Indian customer is driven by the mileage of the car, budget of the customer, and a broad understanding of the availability of ‘good models’ in the market. And therefore, the erstwhile COMPAT upheld the decision of CCI to separate the relevant markets for the sale of cars and the aftermarket (i.e., sale of spare parts and sale of repair services). While in the Automobile spare parts case, the CCI did not agree with the unified systems market, in later cases, they agreed that a unified systems market may exist. In the case of Trend Electronics v. Hewlett Packard Sales Pvt. Ltd. 171 Trend Electronics v. Hewlett Packard Sales Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 92 of 2015, paras 14, 15. (‘HP case’), it was argued by the informant that there were deficiencies in the after-sales services provided by Hewlett Packard (HP). The Informant has submitted that the laptop is the primary product in the instant case, and the market for repair, maintenance and after-sales services is the secondary market. While the order passed in the HP case is silent, it seems like the informant must have relied on the reasoning laid down by the CCI in the Automobile spare parts case. Having said that, the CCI, in the HP case, did not agree that there is a separate market for aftermarket in case of laptops. It noted that although the informant had submitted relevant market as the secondary market for repair, maintenance and after-sales services, no justification had been provided for considering the secondary market as a separate and distinct relevant market. Further, the CCI noted, based on information available/accessible in the public domain, that a consumer purchasing a laptop could reasonably ascertain the total cost that he is likely to incur during the life cycle of the laptop. Further, factors such as the high rate of obsoletion and low life span of laptops suggested that any unfair business strategy by the laptop manufacturers, in the after-sales services, is likely to affect the sales of the primary product. Further, the CCI noted that there are many options available to resell used laptops, and the customers can switch to substitutable products without incurring substantial switching costs. Based on the same, one can see that CCI will look at the particular sector and analyse the market dynamics to determine if an aftermarket exists in the first place. [6] Lack of Scientific Sampling The entire exercise of determination of relevant market is to identify the products/services which are considered substitutable and also identify the competitive constraints that the parties face. While the exercise is extremely pertinent in an abuse of dominance or a merger control investigation, the orders of the CCI are silent as to whether they conduct or appoint a third party to conduct a proper market survey of the sector in question. Further, the application of certain concepts like the Small but Significant and Non-Transitory Increase in Price (SSNIP) test is not uniform by the CCI. While in some cases, the CCI relies on the SSNIP test to determine the market,172 Belaire Owners’ Association v. DLF Limited, HUDA & Ors., Case No. 19 of 2010. However, at the appellate stage, the erstwhile COMPAT held that there is no need to conduct any SSNIP test. See, DLF Limited and Ors. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors., MANU/TA/0012/2014, para. 84. in others, they do not adopt the said test.173 MCX Stock Exchange Ltd. v. National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. and Another, Case No. 13 of 2009, para. 10.16. The CCI held that applicant of SSNIP test is unnecessary. The erstwhile COMPAT upheld the CCI’s order in the National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Another [2014], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 15 of 2011. The CCI must place more reliance on scientific sampling in its analysis. The CCI and DG should, in some cases, take assistance from independent third parties to conduct analysis in their investigations. [7] Relevant Markets for Section 3(4) of the Competition Act Need Not Be Precisely Delineated There is no need to go into precise relevant market determination for the cases against vertical anti-competitive agreements.174 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd., Case No. 18 of 2021, para. 43. In National Restaurant Association of India (‘NRAI’) v. Zomato Limited (‘Zomato’) & Others,175 National Restaurant Association of India (‘NRAI’) v. Zomato Limited (‘Zomato’) & Others, Case No. 16 of 2021, para. 34. the CCI held that for the contraventions under section 3(4) of the Competition Act, there is no need to precisely determine the relevant market. In this case, while passing an order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, the CCI widely delineated the relevant market of Zomato and Swiggy as ‘online food delivery platforms’ and ‘operate as online intermediaries for food ordering and delivery’. Also, in the case of Alleged anti-competitive conduct by Maruti Suzuki India Limited in implementing discount control policy vis-à-vis dealers, 176 Alleged anti-competitive conduct by Maruti Suzuki India Limited in implementing discount control policy vis-à-vis dealers, Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2019, para. 16. while holding Maruti Suzuki liable for imposing resale price maintenance on its dealers under section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act, the CCI felt that there is no requirement of defining a precise relevant market for the assessment of a vertical anti-competitive agreement. The CCI held that Maruti is a manufacturer dealing in the upstream market with significant market power without precisely delineating the relevant market. [8] No Static Approach to Relevant Market Analysis In the case of Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Association of India v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd,177 Case No. 14 of 2019. the CCI has noted that in rapidly changing markets, market assessment cannot have a static approach and that market definition adopted in a previous combination approval involving the same parties, which happened two years before the assessment in that case, should not hold true as market dynamics change have changed due to the rapid evolution of the digital market. The CCI in that case noted that:Delineation of relevant market is based on market realities as they exist at the time of assessment. In rapidly changing markets in particular, market assessment cannot have a static approach. The Combination case cited by MMT-Go took place over two and a half years back. While this may not be a period sufficiently long for traditional markets to undergo perceptible change, the pace of evolution of digital markets is significantly faster. The intervening period has seen the online travel portals and the customised service that they provide to consumers on account of big data analytics to have established a distinct and significantly more prominent position in the hotel reservation space in India. In view of the increased popularity and use of OTAs by a large segment of consumers in India, hotel operators now perceive them as a distinct mode of distribution which cannot be simply replaced or substituted by other offline modes or direct sale without losing out significantly on consumer reach. Thus, on an overall consideration of the market realities and competition dynamics, the Commission finds it difficult to accept the assertion made by MMT-Go. Similar attempts to rely on the previous case decided by CCI were made to object to the jurisdiction of the CCI to examine their present conduct by Amazon and Flipkart. The Karnataka High Court rejected the contention of Flipkart and noted that in a competitive market, various agreements are executed, new practices are adopted every day, and merely because some other issue has been looked into by the CCI earlier, it does not mean that on the ground of res judicata, the CCI cannot look into any information subsequently against the appellants. The Court further noted that the marketplace is by very nature a constantly evolving and dynamic space, that market forces can evolve in the course of few months, and that by no stretch of imagination, it can be held that enterprises shall be out of bound for all times and no action can be taken against them only because at some point of time the matter has been looked into by the CCI.

sections.title: CONCLUSION
sections.text: §1.16 CONCLUSION In the last fourteen years, the CCI has examined cases relating to anti-competitive agreements (discussed in Chapter 2 of this book), abuse of dominance (discussed in Chapter 3 of this book), and merger control (discussed in Chapter 4 of this book) from diverse sectors of the economy. According to the findings based on evidence, appropriate penalties and directions were imposed on the enterprises that were found to be engaged in anti-competitive practices. Also, India has emerged as the pioneer of the digital revolution all over the world. There is a steady rise in cases emanating from the digital markets (discussed in Chapter 6 of this book). To keep the regulatory vires abreast of the changing market dynamics, the CCI may need to develop its enforcement mechanisms so that market participants in digital markets do not evade the scrutiny of the CCI. Further, for the detection and enforcement of the Competition Act, a multidisciplinary approach needs to be taken by looking at both facts and law. Accordingly, amendments to the Competition Act and its Regulations may be made in order to enhance the enforcement of the principles of competition law.

plain_text:
§1.01 MARCH TOWARDS COMPETITION LAW Competition law is intended to protect competition in the market in order to maximize consumer welfare. A robust competition law and policy is quintessential for enhancing efficiency and consumer welfare. The Competition Act, 2002 (‘Competition Act’) was enacted with the intention of preventing anti-competitive practices which would have an adverse effect on the market and its market participants. It seeks to protect the market participants and promote competition since effective competition helps the consumer to get the best quality product at the most competitive price. An effective competition law regime helps to attain efficiencies which ensure the application of laws, rules and regulations to ensure businesses and companies compete fairly with each other. This encourages enterprises to attain efficiency, creates a wider choice for consumers and helps reduce prices and improve quality of products/services. While the idea of perfect competition may be a farfetched concept in most industries, effective competition is the goal of any competition law dispensation. Effective competition is to be assessed in terms of rivalry among firms, low barriers and where no single firm can influence the market conduct. The aim and objective of the Competition Act were explained by the Supreme Court in Competition Commission of India v. Steel Authority of India Limited (‘SAIL case’),1 Competition Commission of India v. Steel Authority of India Limited [2010], (SC) (2010) 10 SCC 744, para. 5. where it was said that the purpose of the Competition Act is to control the concentration of an industry from the hold of a particular market participant and prevent monopolies or similar kinds of concentration in an industry. Competition law is not against attaining monopolistic power, but it abhors the misuse of the monopolistic power to harm the market system and stakeholders. The primary purpose of the competition law is to increase competition in the market, protect the cost of the product, prevent losses to society and product quality, and safeguard other competitors. The Supreme Court held that the perfect competition is the economic model where the market is competitive. Similarly, in Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel Ltd. (‘Bharti Airtel case’),2 Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel Ltd. [2019], (SC) (2019) 2 SCC 521, para. 81. the Supreme Court observed that the Competition Act was enacted to bring out the paradigm shift in terms of globalization and economic development in India. This Competition Act would be anathema to unfair trade practices which cause an adverse effect on the market and ensure fair competition. [A] The Aim and Objective of the Competition Act The aim and objective of the Competition Act have been discussed in Excel Crop Care Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India (‘Excel Crop case’),3 Excel Crop Care Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2017], (SC) (2017) 8 SCC 47, para. 29; Competition Commission of India v. State of Mizoram [2022], (SC) (2022) 7 SCC 73, paras 7, 21-25. and it was said that the purpose behind enacting the Competition Act was to prohibit anti-competitive practices and promote market’s competition, consumer welfare and fair trade. The Competition Act sets the ‘rules of the game’ to ensure effective competition in order to bring economic efficiency, economic growth and development and consumer welfare in the market. The benefits of the Competition Act have been elaborated below:(i) Economic Efficiency: In a competitive market, market participants must make effective use and allocation of the resources by forcing market participants to produce at the lowest cost and sell the product at a competitive price. The main advantage of a competitive market is the continuous pressure on the market participants to use resources in a manner that keeps costs down without compromising the quality of products. This always favors the consumer as the market participants must constantly strive to invest in research and development to provide a better deal to the consumer. (ii) Economic Growth and Development: The competitive force in the market brings economic growth to the country by rapidly reallocating resources and increasing the value of the products and services in the market. The growth of the economy is one of the major indicators of economic development. Economic development shows the improvement in literacy rates, reduction in mortality rates, easy market entry and exit, development in innovation, improvement in physical infrastructure and overall economic welfare of the country. Thus, with economic growth and development, the economic efficiency of the country will improve, and there will be optimal usage of products and services. (iii) Consumer Welfare: The ultimate beneficiary of competition is the consumer. Consumers will get better choices, better quality at a cheaper rate, improve their bargaining position in the market, etc. Along with the remedies provided in the consumer laws, the end-consumers can use the tenets of the Competition Act to resolve the issue of market failures. The Supreme Court in Excel Crop case also noted a few more benefits of the Competition Act, such as an improvement in employment rate, better industrial and trade policies, an improvement in the workplace environment, an increase in foreign investment, benchmarking by exposing inferior performance, etc. Again, the ratio on the purpose of the Competition Act in Excel Crop case was re-emphasized by the Supreme Court in Rajasthan Cylinders & Containers Ltd. v. Union of India (‘LPG-I case’).4 Rajasthan Cylinders & Containers Ltd. v. Union of India [2020], (SC) (2020) 16 SCC 615, para. 80. In LPG-I case, it was held that the Competition Act not only intends to eliminate anti-competitive practices but also to promote and sustain competition in the market. The CCI should enforce the Competition Act to curb the practices which will harm the competition. Hence, effective enforcement is important to prohibit practices having adverse effects on the competition as well as to deter future anti-competitive practices. As discussed above, the primary objective of the Competition Act is to promote economic efficiency through effective enforcement of competition to create a market responsive to consumer preferences. Competition law is to promote economic efficiency by using competition as one of the means of assisting in the creation of a market responsive to consumer preferences.5 SAIL case, paras 6, 7, 125; in Competition Commission of India v. Fast Way Transmission (P) Ltd. [2018], (SC) (2018) 4 SCC 316, para. 12. The advantages of having an effective competition regime are threefold:(i) Allocative efficiency, which ensures the effective allocation of resources. Allocative efficiency can be achieved only if all firms are of sufficient size to realize significant economies of scale, and markets are either competitively structured (i.e., they comprise a significant number of producers) or entry barriers are low. In such cases, all companies are price takers, and the demand and supply forces in the market determine the price. Allocative efficiency is attained when prices are equated to marginal costs, which is a condition for perfect competition. (ii) Productive efficiency, which ensures that costs of production are kept at a minimum. (iii) Dynamic efficiency, which promotes innovative practices. Dynamic efficiency refers to the development of new products. This can be done by ensuring proper reward to the inventor in lieu of developing and disclosing the invention to the public. A high level committee on Competition Policy and Law (‘High Level Committee’) was formed to examine the various aspects and accordingly suggest keeping in view the competition policy of India. On 22 May 2022, the High Level Committee submitted its report, and thereafter, the Competition Act, 2002 came into operation on 12 December 2003. The Competition Act was enacted as a response to market liberalization, ease of doing business by opening up India’s economy and removing the strict government restrictions. In India, a High Level Committee was constituted to examine its various aspects and make suggestions keeping in view the competition policy of India. This Committee made recommendations and submitted its report on 22 May 2002. After the completion of the consultation process, the Competition Act was enacted. As per the statement of objects and reasons of the Competition Act, this enactment is India’s response to the opening up of its economy, removing controls, and resorting to liberalization. The natural corollary of this is that the Indian market should be geared to face competition from within the country and outside. [B] The Purpose Behind Enforcing the Competition Act The natural corollary of the Competition Act is to bring evolution to the Indian market by creating a competitive force within the country and outside.6 Coal India Limited and Another v. CCI and Another [2023], (SC) 2023 SCC OnLine SC 740, para. 67. So, with the avowed objective of a free-market economy, it was felt essential to have a quasi-judicial body to ensure that the practices which have an adverse effect on competition in India are prevented. And thereby, to ensure effective enforcement and fulfil the purpose of the Competition Act, a quasi-judicial body, namely the CCI, was established under section 7 of the Competition Act. Apart from the CCI, the Competition Act provides for the appointment of the Director General (DG) under section 16(1) of the Competition Act. The DG is a specialized investigating wing or the investigatory arm of the CCI. The various provisions of the Competition Act deal with the establishment, powers and functions as well as the discharge of adjudicatory functions by the CCI. Under the scheme of the Competition Act, the CCI is vested with inquisitorial, investigative, regulatory, adjudicatory and advocacy functions. Further, the proviso of section 18 of the Competition Act states that for better enforcement, the CCI can have tie-ups with other Indian government agencies and foreign competition agencies. For international cooperation and promotion of competition law, CCI has signed a memorandum of understanding with the DG for Competition of the European Commission, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission, the Japan Fair Trade Commission, etc. Such cooperation will help the competition agencies exchange information, share best practices and conduct various capacity-building programmes. Also, on 22 November 2022,7 Notification No. G.S.R. 832(E), dated 22 Nov. 2022. the Union Finance Ministry through a notification mandated the CCI to share information with the Enforcement Director, directly or indirectly. So, keeping in view the economic development of the country as a whole, a positive role has been given to the CCI to enforce the competition law in India. Chapter II of the Competition Act can be referred to as the heart and soul of the competition law. Chapter II spells out three substantial provisions of the Competition Act because it prohibits the practices which are anathema to the concept of fair and competitive market. The substantive provisions of the Competition Act were notified in a phased manner, provisions relating to anti-competitive agreements (section 3) and abuse of dominance (section 4) were notified in May 2009, and provisions with respect to merger control (sections 5 and 6) were notified in June 2011. To fulfil the purpose of the Competition Act, the CCI must prohibit such practices to create a healthy competition in the market. Because the aim and objective of the Competition Act clearly show that the enterprises that independently determine their commercial policies are prohibited from indulging in agreements which affect the competition adversely or concerted practices by taking advantage of their dominant position in their markets.8 CCI v. Coordination Committee, (2017) 5 SCC 17, para. 45. Also see, Excel Crop case, (2017) 8 SCC 47, paras 21, 26. As the aim and objective also prohibit future anti-competitive effects, the parties before entering into a combination must notify the CCI by showing that the combination will not adversely affect the competition.9 SCM Solifert Ltd. v. CCI, (2018) 6 SCC 631, para. 18. The Competition Act in totality has a significant departure from the letter and spirit of the erstwhile Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969. The Competition Act is premised on the effect theory, which means the primary purpose of the Competition Act is to prevent practices which cause or are likely to cause an anti-competitive effect on the market. It does not condemn monopoly per se but rather focuses on the enterprise’s use of the monopoly status. Further, the earlier Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Commission had very limited enforcement powers since they could not penalize any enterprise. Their power was limited to passing cease and desist orders. Conversely, the Competition Act has empowered the CCI to penalize a delinquent enterprise, apart from passing ceasing-and-desist orders and also ordering for modification of the business practices. The penalties which are imposed by the CCI are one of the highest economic penalties in India. Also, the CCI can pass directions to modify the business practices by imposing behavioural remedies10 See, OECD, Remedies and Commitments in Abuse Cases, p. 5. Behavioural Remedies are the remedies which mandates an enterprise to conduct in a particular manner, for example, with regard to pricing clauses, contractual obligations, etc. under section 27 of the Competition Act and modify the structure of the enterprises by passing structural remedies11 See, OECD, Remedies and Commitments in Abuse Cases, p. 5. Structural Remedies are the remedies which involve changes to the structure of an enterprise, thereby effectively changing the structure of the market. under section 28 of the Competition Act. In the context of anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominance, CCI generally passes behavioural changes in terms of the enterprise’s conduct with other market participants and end customers. In relation to merger control, under section 31 of the Act, structural and/or behavioural remedies are imposed through commitments on the parties to the combination. Further, the CCI, unlike its predecessor regime, has extraterritorial jurisdiction; i.e., the CCI can investigate the practices of companies based outside India if their conduct causes any anti-competitive effects in India. Further, the Competition Act seeks to maintain a level playing field between the private and the public sector and seek competitive neutrality; i.e., government or state-owned enterprises should not use their legislative or fiscal powers to advantage their own businesses over the private sector. The provisions of the Competition Act are applicable to any enterprise which carries on economic activity, irrespective of whether it is publicly or privately owned. We will see from the definition of enterprise, discussed in detail below, that all the commercial activities of the government, excluding the sovereign functions (energy, currency, defence and space), come under the provisions of the Competition Act and that no antitrust exemptions are applicable to such state-owned enterprise. In fact, the CCI has penalized state-owned enterprises, like Coal India, since they have indulged in activities which constitute abuse of dominance. The erstwhile Competition Appellate Tribunal (COMPAT) also upheld the order passed by the CCI against Coal India. Thereafter, in Coal India Ltd. v. CCI and Another (‘Coal India case’),12 Coal India Limited and Another v. CCI and Another [2023], (SC) 2023 SCC OnLine SC 740, paras 7-13, 88, 98, 99, 100, 101, 105, 119, 122. Coal India appealed before the Supreme Court by arguing that Coal India is a monopoly created by the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973 with an objective of protecting it from challenges, and is guarded under Article 31B of the Constitution of India, 1950.13 Article 31B of the Constitution of India, 1950, says that the enactments placed in the ninth schedule of the Constitution of India shall not become void on the ground that they violate fundamental rights and no judicial review is possible against such enactments. Article 31B validates certain enactments (such as, the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973) which have been inserted under the Ninth Schedule of the Constitution of India, 1950. Coal India also relied upon section 19(4)(g) of the Competition Act to say that Coal India is not a dominant player under the Competition Act because Coal India is dominant due to a statute; hence, it should be treated differently that an ordinary dominant player. It was argued that Coal India was created under the said statute for the equitable distribution of coal in order to secure the ‘common good’ under Article 39(b) of the Constitution of India, 1950.14 Article 39(b) of the Constitution of India, 1950, states that the state can enact laws to secure control of the resources of the community, and the distribution of the resources must subserve the common good. Accordingly, Coal India took support of Article 39(b) of the Constitution of India, 1950, to say that it has not violated the Competition Act because it is following the principle of ‘common good’ while distributing minerals and resources of the highest importance for India’s economy. Hence, the provisions of the Competition Act will not be applicable to the conduct of Coal India because Coal India is an instrument of the state, and its conduct is in accordance with the directive principles under Article 39(b). Furthermore, it was also argued that there is no abuse of dominance under section 4(2)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act because the conduct of Coal India relating to the pricing and production of coals is based on the constitutional mandate under Article 39(b) and the objective of Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973. Additionally, it was also argued that section 28 of the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973 is a non-obstante clause; hence, it prevails over the Competition Act. However, the Supreme Court, after taking consideration of all the arguments, dismissed the appeal. The Supreme Court held that the purpose of the Competition Act is to attain faster economic growth in India and foster competitive economy. To achieve the purpose, public sector companies or bodies created under statutes cannot abuse their dominance. The parliament was aware of the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973, while including bodies created under statute within the purview of the term ‘enterprise’ under section 2(h) of the Competition Act. This shows that Coal India is an enterprise, and it can’t evade its obligation under the Competition Act. Only those enterprises which are engaged in sovereign activities are exempt, but Coal India was not involved in any such sovereign activities. The Supreme Court then noted that the expression in section 19(4)(g) of the Competition Act regarding creating monopolies under statutes for determining the existence of dominant player shows the intention of the legislature to bring Coal India within the ambit of the Competition Act. Regarding the application of Article 31B of the Constitution of India, 1950, the Supreme Court held that the said article provides constitutional immunity from being challenged for violating fundamental rights and being invalidated by the courts. The said article protects the entities which are created under statutes, but these entities are not immunized from their anti-competitive conduct by acting with caprice, unfairly or discriminating against similarly situated persons or things with different conditions. The term ‘common good’ in Article 39(b) means achieving the highest good of the maximum number of people. But to serve the purpose of ‘common good’ and attain the goals in the directive principles, the principles of competition law must also be harmoniously construed along with the goals of directive principles. Finally, on the point of non-obstante provision under the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973, the Supreme Court looked at the purpose of the Competition Act and held that the Competition Act will prevail over the Coal Mines (Nationalization) Act, 1973.

§1.02 PROCEDURE FOR INQUIRY A detailed procedure has been established under the Competition Act and the regulations of the CCI with respect to the investigation process for a violation under sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act. Merger filings and procedures have been dealt with in Chapter 4. A diagrammatic illustration of key stages of inquiry under the Competition Act is as follows: [A] Initiation of the Case The informant, i.e., the person who wishes to complain about a conduct to the CCI, would make such information available in writing to the CCI by paying the requisite filing fee outlining in detail the facts, evidence in support and the alleged contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act. The information could also be made by way of reference by the Central Government, State Government, statutory authority or on its own knowledge as provided under section 19(1)(a) of the Competition Act. Hence, the CCI can initiate an inquiry under section 26 of the Competition Act under the following three conditions:15 Coal India case, para. 90. (i) information given under section 19; (ii) reference made by the Government or a statutory authority; or (iii) on its own motion (‘suo motu’). It must be noted that under the Competition Act, there is no locus standi requirement; i.e., it is not necessary that the affected party only can file information. As a part of the information, it is not required for the informant to show how it has been harmed by the conduct of the alleged infringer. This is a precise reason why the term ‘information’ is used in the Competition Act and not ‘complaint’. Before the Competition Act was notified in 2009, the term used in the Competition Act was filing of a ‘complaint’ which was changed to filing of ‘information’ in 2007. One of the major reasons for this change was that the legislature did not want to have a locus standi requirement. Further, the use of the word ‘complaint’ would mean an adversarial proceeding which is not the case with ‘information’ being used. There have been instances where the informant has filed the information, and the CCI has proceeded with the investigation and final orders, even when the informant is not present during the course of proceedings. On the issue of locus standi under the Competition Act, the NCLAT in Samir Agrawal v. Competition Commission of India and Others 16 Samir Agrawal v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2020], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (At) No. 11 of 2019, paras 10, 15, 16, 17. erred in holding that the informant had no right to file an information because he was neither aggrieved nor prejudiced by the conduct of the cab aggregators. The substantial question of law was whether a ‘person’ would mean any natural person irrespective of whether he was a consumer who suffered harm or a person whose legal rights have been harmed by the anti-competitive activity. The NCLAT held that CCI cannot take cognizance against the allegation of violation under sections 3 and/or 4 because the informant never suffered harm at the hands of the cab aggregators while using the apps as a consumer, so he has no locus to file an information. On an appeal against the judgment of the NCLAT, the Supreme Court in Samir Agrawal v. CCI and Others (‘Cab aggregator case’)17 Samir Agrawal v. CCI and Others [2021], (SC) (2021) 3 SCC 136, paras 14-17, 21; Flipkart Interned Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. CCI and Others, Writ Appeal No. 562 of 2021, paras 29, 35, 36, the Karnataka High Court rejected the argument that the person who filed the information was not an aggrieved person but was sponsored by someone else. The High Court reiterated the ratio of Supreme Court in Samir Agrawal v. CCI and Others and held that the Competition Act allows any person and not just an aggrieved person to file an information under section 19 of the Competition Act. set aside the reasoning of the NCLAT on the issue of the locus standi. The Supreme Court said that the term ‘any person’ as defined in section 2(l) of the Competition Act is a wide and inclusive definition. Further, by looking at section 19(1)(a) of the Competition Act, it was held that an information can be filed by any person irrespective of the person being affected or not. As the proceeding under the Competition Act is a proceeding in rem which affects the public interest at large, anyone can file an information under section 19(1)(a) of the Competition Act. Thus, the informant need not be personally aggrieved by the anti-competitive agreement and/or the abuse of dominance. Information is essentially made by a person or by way of reference from another authority. The purpose of information is to inform the CCI of an anti-competitive practice, and it is up to the CCI to determine whether the information has any merit and whether the factual situation mentioned in the information warrants any further investigation by the DG. The CCI is not bound by the issues and pleadings taken in the information and can go beyond the scope of the information and ask the DG to investigate additional grounds. Further, unlike in a complaint scenario, there is no mechanism under the Competition Act to withdraw an information. Once information is made, it is up to the CCI to decide whether a fit case exists for further investigation or to close the matter.18 Bharti Airtel case, para. 1.
