The Supreme Court held that section 53B of the Act does not impose any condition of pre-deposition for admitting the appeal. Therefore, the right to file the appeal and have the said appeal decided on merits is conferred by the statute. Hence, the Supreme Court held that the NCLAT can’t just dismiss an appeal just because the appellate failed to pay the pre-deposit amount. However, for non-deposit of the amount, any interim order of stay may stand vacated, but appeal will be continued to be heard on merits. The issue of the pre-deposit amount was incorporated in the statute after the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, amended section 53B of the Competition Act to mandate the appellate to deposit 25% of the penalty amount while filing an appeal.

§1.06 PRIVATE ACTION The Competition Act also allows for private action/damages claims. Section 53N of the Competition Act provides that any person can file an application before the NCLAT for compensation that arises from the findings of the CCI, NCLAT, Supreme Court or an order of settlement. The NCLAT can pass an order for the recovery of compensation from any enterprise for any loss or damage shown to have been suffered by the Central Government or a State Government or a local authority or any enterprise or any person as a result of any contravention of the substantive provisions of the Competition Act (sections 3, 4, 5 and 6), having been committed by an enterprise or as a result of settlement order passed by the CCI. To date, there has not been one decided private action claim by the NCLAT; however, based on cause lists of NCLAT, there have been some cases which have been filed where compensation has been sought. Since the approach is unclear, it remains to be seen whether the Competition Appellate Tribunal will give compensation taking into account only actual loss or whether the compensation will also cover loss in profits. It must be apposite to note in this regard that while section 46 of the Competition Act provides for lesser penalty for leniency applicant in case of cartels, the risk of follow-on actions and compensation has to be borne by every cartel participant, even the leniency applicant.

§1.07 DUE PROCESS Due Process, and in particular, conformity with the principles of natural justice, has been a key issue in matters that have been contested before the CCI. The right to cross-examine witnesses and the right to have access to information and documents relied upon by the CCI has been highly contested when the matters are taken up in higher forums like the High Court and NCLAT. While the scheme of the Competition Act does not provide for appeals to the High Court, however, the parties have approached the High Court on issues of due process and not substantive findings. One of the earliest challenges on the nature of the CCI (whether it is a regulator or a tribunal) was made in the case of Braham Dutt v. Union of India.84 Braham Dutt v. Union of India [2005], (SC) (2005) 2 SCC 431, paras 3, 4, 5. In that case, the constitutional validity of Rule 3 of the Competition Commission of India (Selection of Chairperson and other Members of the Commission) Rules, 2003, was challenged. The principal point of contention was that the appointment of non-judicial person as Chairperson of the CCI violated the doctrine of separation of power, as enshrined in the Constitution of India. It was argued that the CCI, which was essentially a judicial body having adjudicatory powers on the questions of importance, should be headed by a retired Judge of the Supreme Court or a High Court. The Union of India contested the writ petition and pleaded that the CCI is a Regulatory Body which requires expertise in the field, and such expertise cannot be supplied by the members of the judiciary alone, and in any case, the power of judicial review is available with the High Courts and the Supreme Court. During the pendency of the writ petition, two additional counter-affidavits were filed on behalf of the Union of India, in which it was submitted that the Government was proposing to make certain amendments so as to enable the Chairman and the Members to be selected by a Committee presided over by the Chief Justice of India or his nominee. The Supreme Court noticed the background in which the Competition Act was enacted, referred to the statements contained in the additional affidavits filed on behalf of the Union of India and observed:We find that the amendments which the Union of India proposes to introduce in Parliament would have a clear bearing on the question raised for decision in the writ petition essentially based on the separation of powers recognised by the Constitution. The challenge that there is usurpation of judicial power and conferment of the same on a non-judicial body is sought to be met by taking the stand that an Appellate Authority would be constituted and that body would essentially be a judicial body conforming to the concept of separation of judicial powers as recognised by this Court… We may observe that if an expert body is to be created as submitted on behalf of the Union of India consistent with what is said to be the international practice, it might be appropriate for the respondents to consider the creation of two separate bodies, one with expertise that is advisory and regulatory and the other adjudicatory. This followed up by an appellate body as contemplated by the proposed amendment, can go a long way in meeting the challenge sought to be raised in this writ petition based on the doctrine of separation of powers recognised by the Constitution. While an affidavit was given in the Supreme Court by the Union of India to constitute the CCI with two bodies, one dealing with regulatory and advisory functions and the other dealing with adjudicatory functions, a half-hearted attempt was made in 2006 to denude the CCI of its adjudicatory functions by proposing addition of paragraphs 3(b) in the Competition (Amendment) Bill, 2006 to omit the provisions relating to adjudication of disputes between two or more parties. This is evident from the following extracts of the Bill presented before the Parliament:3. The Competition (Amendment) Bill, 2006, inter alia, seeks to make the following amendments to the Competition Act so as to address various legal issues and to make the CCI fully operational on a sustainable basis, namely: (a) to provide the CCI would be an expert body which will function as a market regulator for preventing anticompetitive practices in the country and it would also have advisory and advocacy functions in its role as a regulator; (b) to omit the provisions relating to adjudication of disputes between two or more parties by the CCI and to provide for investigation through the Director General in case there exist a prima facie case relating to anti-competitive agreements or abuse of dominant position under the Competition Act, 2002 and conferring power upon the CCI to pass orders on completion of an inquiry and impose monetary penalties and in doing so the CCI would work as a collegium and its decisions would be based on simple majority. However, when the amendments were finally made in the Competition Act, the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2007, most of the provisions relating to adjudicatory functions of the CCI were retained with minor modifications. [A] Quorum of CCI In furtherance to the earlier discussion by the Supreme Court in the case of Braham Dutt v. Union of India, a key issue, which was decided whether it is mandatory to have a person from the field of law in CCI’s quorum, has been discussed in the following cases. It has been clarified by the Delhi High Court that the absence of a judicial member would not stop the CCI from performing its functions. For instance, the Division Bench of the Delhi High Court in Mahindra Electric Mobility Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India 85 Mahindra Electric Mobility Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2019], (Delhi High Court) 2019 SCC OnLine Del 8032, paras 137, 138, 139, 142, 148, 150. had to deal with the writ challenging the validity of two administrative orders of CCI which were passed without the presence of a judicial member in the quorum of the CCI. One of the principal points of contention in the writ petition before the Delhi High Court is that the CCI is not a mere regulator but a judicial tribunal. It has been argued that CCI is principally an authority that exercises judicial functions which have been conferred by the statute, has the trappings of a court and is a tribunal for the purposes of sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act. It was further argued that CCI perceive itself as a judicial body and it predominantly engaged in adjudicatory functions. Thus, the writ petitioner submitted before the High Court that the presence of a judicial member should be made mandatory while passing an administrative order. However, the Delhi High Court held that CCI need not require a judiciary member while passing an administrative order. Then, it was held that CCI not only performs adjudicatory functions but also discharges administrative functions like directing DG to conduct investigations or further investigation. So, the Competition Act requires CCI to discharge multiple functions. Though the CCI’s task as the primary regulator of the marketplace and watchdog of anti-competitive practices is undoubtedly required to adjudicate at a stage, it cannot be held that the CCI must necessarily comprise judicial members while carrying on all its functions. Just because the CCI discharges adjudicatory functions does not imply that the CCI is purely a judicial tribunal. Hence, the Court concluded that the CCI is a Tribunal, but it’s not a judicial tribunal or mandatorily needs judiciary members. However, when the CCI passes an adjudicatory order which determines the rights and liabilities of parties, then the presence of at least one judicial member is necessary. Further, the High Court read section 8 of the Competition Act, which deals with the composition of CCI, to say that CCI should have a judicial member. Finally, the Delhi High Court directed to ensure the presence of a judicial member in the CCI, and the Government was directed to expeditiously appoint a judicial member in the CCI. The Single Bench of the High Court of Delhi in CADD Systems and Services Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India 86 CADD Systems and Services Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2019], (Delhi High Court) 2019 SCC OnLine Del 9252, paras 18, 19, 21. validated the adjudicatory function or final order of the CCI that was passed in the absence of a judicial member. The Delhi High Court relied on the Supreme Court’s judgment in B.K. Srinivasan v. State of Karnataka, wherein the so-called Ganga Clause was upheld, i.e., with the objective to put beyond challenge defects in the constitution of statutory bodies and defects of procedure which do not lead to any substantial prejudice. Section 15 of the Competition Act is used as a saving clause because it states that the proceedings of the CCI would not be invalidated due to vacancy or any defect in its constitution. Further, the Supreme Court’s judgment in K.R. Tamizhmani v. The State of Tamil Nadu was also discussed by the Delhi High Court to say that till the time a judiciary member was selected, the existing tribunal would decide all the cases. Therefore, it was concluded that the final orders passed without a judicial member would not be invalidated by the CCI. Similarly, the NCLAT in Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC v. Competition Commission of India 87 Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC v. Competition Commission of India [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 1 of 2022, paras 336-338. held that no proceedings of the CCI will be invalid by reason of any vacancy in the quorum, and as per the legislative intent behind section 22(3) of the Competition Act, a judicial member does not interdict the functioning of CCI. After the Competition (Amendment) Act 2007, section 22(3) of the Competition Act requires at least three members for a valid quorum for discharging the functions of CCI, and the said provision does not mandate the presence of a judiciary member in the quorum. Thereafter, in Pawan Jagetia v. Competition Commission of India,88 Pawan Jagetia v. Competition Commission of India [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 16 of 2021, para. 31. the NCLAT again held that the absence of a judicial member does not render the CCI order void. Furthermore, the NCLAT interpreted sections 8 and 9 of the Competition Act to show that nowhere has it been indicated that CCI must consist of a judicial member to decide the proceeding. As per the proviso of section 22(3) of the Competition Act, the CCI’s quorum requires three members to preside and cast votes. Between October 2022 and October 2023, the CCI had only two members on its board after the retirement of the Chairperson. This resulted in a lack of quorum at CCI. Due to a lack of quorum, CCI could not approve proposed combinations and could not perform its other adjudicatory duties. However, in order to discharge its adjudicatory role in terms of merger filings, the CCI adopted the doctrine of necessity and started passing orders in terms of combinations. Further, in relation to the adjudication of antitrust matters (sections 3 and 4 of the Act), the Delhi High Court in Alliance of Digital India Foundation v. Competition Commission of India and Others 89 Alliance of Digital India Foundation v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2018], (Delhi High Court) (2023) SCC OnLine Del 2330, paras 67-75, 93. held that any adjudicatory process wherein there is a vacancy on the CCI would not invalidate the proceedings of CCI. In this case, the CCI had passed a certain direction against Google for abusing its dominance in the app store market. However, due to the lack of a quorum, the CCI could not take action against Google on an application filed by the app developers, stating that Google is not complying with the CCI directions. Thereby, the petitioner filed a writ and submitted that even if CCI is not in quorum, it can still discharge its functions and entertain and adjudicate on the said application. Allowing the writ petition, the Delhi High Court held that there is no provision in the Competition Act which prescribes a minimum number of members required to constitute a valid quorum of CCI for its adjudicatory functions. Thereby, the High Court directed the CCI to adjudicate the application filed under section 42 of the Competition Act. [B] Principles of Natural Justice and Due Process of Law The rule of law remains an important cornerstone of India’s democratic setup, and principles of natural justice were required to be followed by CCI while passing an order which affected the rights of the parties. Reliance can be placed on a plethora of authorities across various courts in India and abroad who believe that denial of a right to a hearing constitutes a serious danger to the due process under the law as well as violates natural justice. [1] Parties Must Be Heard Before the Final Order Regarding the orders which affect the rights of the parties, the CCI must follow the due process of law and the principles of natural justice. The Gujarat High Court in Casa2 Stays Private Limited v. Oravel Stays Private Limited (‘OYO’) and Another 90 Casa2 Stays Private Limited v. Oravel Stays Private Limited (‘OYO’) and Another [2021], (Gujarat High Court) R/Special Civil Application No. 5085 of 2021, paras 2,3. set aside the CCI’s interim order by holding that OYO was not provided an equal opportunity of being heard before the interim order was passed. So, while granting interim relief under section 33 of the Competition Act, the CCI must follow principles of natural justice and fair play by providing all the parties with an opportunity to be heard. Further, in relation to final orders, the erstwhile COMPAT in Board of Control for Cricket in India v. Competition Commission of India and Another 91 Board of Control for Cricket in India v. Competition Commission of India and Another [2015], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 17 of 2013, paras 15, 24. emphasized that due process and the principles of natural justice are sacrosanct in final proceedings before the CCI. The erstwhile COMPAT has held that the CCI is obliged to comply with the various facets of the principles of natural justice and is required to comply with the rule of audi alteram partem and grant an effective opportunity of hearing to a person against whom a finding of contravention is likely to be recorded. In this background, the erstwhile COMPAT held that while the CCI did not finally agree with the findings recorded by its investigating arm, i.e., DG on the issue of the relevant market, it did not indicate anywhere that it did not agree with such findings and did not afford an opportunity to the party concerned an opportunity to contest the determination of the relevant market. Therefore, it was held that the finding of the CCI was in violation of the rule of audi alteram partem. Further, even the information relied upon by the CCI in its conclusion that the enterprise concerned (viz. Board of Control for Cricket in India) had abused its dominant position was not made available to the Board of Control of Cricket in India to refute and, therefore, the finding of the CCI was set aside. However, the party which is being investigated has no right to be heard at the stage of section 26(1) of the Competition Act because an order directing the DG to conduct investigation is required to be passed expeditiously and without spending undue time. Such an order is administrative in nature and does not entail any civil consequences. Thus, there is no need to follow the principle of audi alteram partem. 92 SAIL case, para. 91; J.K. Paper Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (Gujarat High Court) 2023 SCC OnLine Guj 1790, paras 15, 17. [2] CCI Must Record Reasons in its Administrative Order as Well as Adjudicatory Order The Supreme Court has also emphasized the due process and the need of the CCI and Appellate Tribunal to pass speaking orders. In the case of Rangi International Limited v. Nova Scotia Bank and Others,93 Rangi International Limited v. Nova Scotia Bank and others [2013], (SC) (2013) 7 SCC 160, para. 3. a two-judge Bench of the Supreme Court considered the question of whether the CCI and the erstwhile COMPAT should record reasons in support of their orders. The Supreme Court observed that:The Competition Commission as well as the Competition Appellate Tribunal are exercising very important quasi judicial functions. The orders passed by the Commission and the Appellate Tribunal can have far-reaching consequences. Therefore, the minimum that is required of the Commission as well as the Appellate Tribunal is that the orders are supported by reasons, even briefly. Though the CCI need not follow the principle of audi alteram partem in its administrative orders, it must record some reasoning while passing such orders. Because in the case of Vardayani Offset v. Competition Commission of India and Other, 94 Vardayani Offset v. Competition Commission of India and Other [2020], (Gujarat High Court) MANU/GJ/1046/2020, paras 7, 10, 12, 17. the Gujarat High Court quashed the prima facie order for not recording reasons while passing the said order. Further, it was impossible for the parties to read the prima facie order because it was heavily redacted. So, the Gujarat High Court relied on the reasoning of the SAIL case to hold that CCI must record some or minimum reasons to show the existence of a prima facie case in the order passed under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, as the CCI failed to satisfy the basic requirement of fairness and natural justice by passing a prima facie order without reasoning. Also, in WhatsApp LLC and Another v. Competition Commission of India and Another,95 WhatsApp LLC and Another v. Competition Commission of India and Another [2022], (Delhi High Court) 2022 SCC OnLine Del 2582, para. 43. the Delhi High Court observed that a prima facie order need not contain detailed reasons, but CCI must record all the material present at the stage before passing a prima facie order. It can be concluded that even though the CCI need not give an opportunity to hear the parties while passing a mere administrative order, it is expected of the CCI to record some reasoning while passing such orders. [3] CCI Must Display Application of Its Mind and Follow Due Process of Law In another case,96 Chemists and Druggist Association v. CCI and Another [2015], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 21 of 2014, para. 51. the erstwhile COMPAT has also deprecated the findings of the CCI where it mechanically approved the findings of the DG without examining the material on record. In doing so, the erstwhile COMPAT emphasized that being an adjudicatory body, the CCI is bound to act in accordance with the statute, the principles of natural justice and record reasons for its orders. Further, it held that such reasons should reflect and demonstrate the application of the mind. Similarly, the erstwhile COMPAT, in the case of Dr. L.H. Hiranandani Hospital v. Competition Commission of India 97 Dr. L.H. Hiranandani Hospital v. Competition Commission of India [2015], (COMPAT) [2015] Comp AT 1144, paras 30-35. noted that CCI committed a failure because it ‘did not independently examine the various factors enumerated in section 19(3), (5), (6) and (7) for deciding what constituted relevant market for the purpose of the present case.’ The erstwhile COMPAT suggested that the CCI cannot merely rubber-stamp the analysis of DG and must, in appropriate circumstances, conduct its own examination/independent testing of allegations. Further, the erstwhile COMPAT noted that the DG and CCI cannot act on pure conjectures and must act on proper evidence. Also, the NCLAT in Hyundai Motor India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Others 98 Hyundai Motor India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2018], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal No. 06 of 2017, para. 37. noted that the DG’s investigation report is merely an opinion primarily to assist the CCI in appreciating the evidence in arriving at a final decision during the inquiry. And therefore, the DG’s investigation report is not binding on the CCI. It is expected of the CCI to analyse the evidence in conjunction with other evidence on record and then form its final opinion. Apart from the DG investigation report, the CCI gets evidence from the parties through submissions, applications, etc. Hence, to reach a conclusion, the CCI needs to provide opportunities to all the parties to adduce evidence supporting their side. In another instance,99 Himachal Pradesh Society of Chemist & Druggist Alliance & ors. v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 58 of 2015, paras 15, 17, 19. the erstwhile COMPAT reversed the CCI findings on failure to observe due process. In one of the cases, the appellants had argued before the CCI on due process violations by DG wherein DG had relied upon unverified documents and also did not provide an opportunity to cross-examine the witness and the documents. Further, the DG relied on his personal knowledge to find out a case of violation against the appellants. When the appellants had argued that the DG had not followed fair procedures and due process, the CCI only briefly mentioned the objections raised by the appellants and did not opine in detail on the fairness of the procedure and continued to opine on the merits of the case. When the matter went into appeal, the erstwhile COMPAT deprecated the approach taken by the CCI and ordered a fresh investigation to be done by the DG. [C] Problem of ‘Revolving Door’ Practice in CCI In light of principles of natural justice, the CCI should not indulge in the practice of ‘revolving door’ and must follow the concept of ‘who hears must decide’. Specifically for final orders, the CCI must ensure that, in all cases, the members who participate in the final hearings also decide the conclusion of the cases. To prevent the practice of the ‘revolving door’, the Delhi High Court in Mahindra Electric Mobility Ltd v. Competition Commission of India, 100 Mahindra Electric Mobility Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2019], (Delhi High Court) 2019 SCC OnLine Del 8032, paras 163, 164, 169, 170, 177, 212. directed the CCI to frame guidelines with mandates that once final hearings in any matter or batch of matters begin, the constitution of the CCI should not vary. This direction was passed to prevent prejudice to the parties, but unfortunately, CCI is not following the direction of the Delhi High Court. In Balrampur Chini Mills Ltd. and Other v. Competition Commission of India and Others,101 Balrampur Chini Mills Ltd. and Other v. Competition Commission of India and Others (2023), (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 86 of 2018, paras 49, 59, 60, 67, 77, 78, 79. the NCLAT set aside the order of the CCI by holding that the CCI failed to follow the principles of natural justice because five members of the CCI heard the final arguments, but only three members were present while passing the final orders. The NCLAT held that the ‘coram’ of CCI that heard the final arguments should remain the same till the time the necessary order is passed. To reach this conclusion, the NCLAT relied on the Delhi High Court’s direction on the prohibition of ‘revolving door’ practice which was not followed by CCI in letter and spirit. NCLAT held that the CCI must follow the principle of one ‘who hears must decide’ to protect the spirit of natural justice and the principle of judicial procedure. Accordingly, the NCLAT remanded the matter to the CCI for the constitution of an appropriate ‘coram’ which would undertake a fresh hearing of this matter.

§1.08 JURISDICTIONAL BOUNDARY OF CCI The CCI, as a competition watchdog, is empowered to promote competition and resolve competition law-related disputes. Section 18 of the Competition Act casts a special responsibility on the CCI to ‘eliminate’ anti-competitive practices and promote competition in the market. Further, section 61 of the Competition Act grants exclusive power to the CCI to exercise its jurisdiction in respect of any matter in which the Competition Act empowers the CCI. However, the jurisdiction of CCI has been challenged in numerous cases. The foundation of most jurisdictional challenges is the overlapping of jurisdictions and conflict with other regulatory authorities. [A] CCI’s Conflict and Co-existence with Other Regulatory Authorities To know the limits of the judiciary boundary of the CCI, it is important to understand how the Supreme Court dealt with this issue of overlapping jurisdiction between the CCI and the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI) in Bharti Airtel case. In Bharati Airtel case,102 Bharti Airtel case, paras 88, 90, 95, 99, 100, 101-105, 109, 110, 111, 112, 113, 114, 122. Reliance Jio filed information against Airtel, Vodafone and Idea Cellular Ltd. (‘Incumbent Dominant Operators’ (IDOs)) for forming a cartel and denying Points of Interconnectivity to block the entry of Reliance Jio in the telecommunication market. CCI passed an order that there was a prima facie anti-competitive practice and accordingly directed DG to investigate the matter. Aggrieved by the order of CCI, IDOs filed a writ petition before the High Court challenging the jurisdiction of CCI. The High Court held that the CCI erred in passing such an order while the matter was pending before the TRAI. The CCI challenged the High Court’s decision before the Supreme Court by contending that it has jurisdiction to adjudicate the said matter. Further, the CCI argued that the issue of whether the IDOs formed a cartel to block the entry of Reliance Jio is a competition law issue under section 3(3) of the Competition Act; however, the issue before TRAI was a violation of section 11 of the TRAI Act, 1997, in terms of fair competition among service providers, maintaining standards of Quality of Service, etc. So, it was submitted by CCI that both issues are different, and CCI only wants to look into the competition law issue without encroaching on the jurisdiction of TRAI. However, the Supreme Court held that the CCI has jurisdiction to deal with the anti-competitive agreement but only after the TRAI finds fault with the IDOs on the jurisdictional aspects. The TRAI was established to regulate the healthy growth of the telecom industry, protect the interests of consumers, ensure technical compatibility and effective inter-relationships between service providers, ensure compliance with licence conditions by telecom service providers (TSPs), resolve disputes between TSPs, and ensure fair competition in the telecom market. The TRAI has been constituted as an expert regulator of the telecom sector, hence, it is the most appropriate regulatory authority to adjudicate the issues. The Supreme Court held that the CCI should not initiate an inquiry into the matter until the time the TRAI adjudicates the issues under the TRAI Act, 1997, i.e., whether IDOs are under any obligation to provide Point of Interconnection (POIs),103 POIs are points between two network operators which allow voice call originating from one operator (for instance, Airtel) to reach the network of other operator (for instance, Reliance Jio). whether the Reliance Jio is asking the POIs under reasonable terms, whether IDOs are denying the POIs to Reliance Jio as per licensing conditions, etc. Interestingly, the Supreme Court in the Bharati Airtel case104 Ibid. further held that TRAI does not have the exclusive jurisdiction to deal with matters related to anti-competitive practices in the telecom sector. The TRAI is an expert in dealing with matters pertaining to telecom services, such as unfair conditions in the agreements of telecom operators and denial of the POIs. Whereas the CCI is an expert regulator of the market and overall economy. The CCI is better equipped to deal with the practices having appreciable adverse effects on the competition, to promote competition in the market, and protect the business users as well as the end customers. So, both regulators operate in different fields, and they can co-exist. But as the TRAI is the most appropriate regulator, first it needs to opine on the jurisdictional facts as to whether IDO were under any obligation to provide the POIs and quality issues during the testing period, then only the CCI can start the inquiry under section 26 of the Competition Act, and thereby decide whether the agreement between IDOs was anti-competitive in nature or not. The Supreme Court concluded by making the following observations:In a nutshell, that leads to the conclusion that the view taken by the High Court is perfectly justified. Even the argument of the learned ASG is that the exercise of jurisdiction by CCI to investigate an alleged cartel does not impinge upon TRAI’s jurisdiction to regulate the industry in any way. It was submitted that the promotion of competition and prevention of competitive behaviour may not be high on the change of sectoral regulator which makes it prone to ‘regulatory capture’ and, therefore, CCI is competent to exercise its jurisdiction from the standpoint of the Competition Act. However, having taken note of the skilful exercise which TRAI is supposed to carry out, such a comment vis-à-vis TRAI may not be appropriate. No doubt, as commented by the Planning Commission in its report of February 2007, a sectoral regulator, may not have an overall view of the economy as a whole, which CCI is able to fathom. Therefore, our analysis does not bar the jurisdiction of CCI altogether but only pushes it to a later stage, after TRAI has undertaken necessary exercise in the first place, which it is more suitable to carry out. Further, the Bombay High Court in Star India Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India 105 Star India Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2019], (Bombay High Court) 2019 SCC Online Bom 3038, para. 91. set aside the CCI’s prima facie order directing DG to investigate the conduct of Star India Pvt. Ltd. and Sony Pictures Network India Pvt. Ltd. causing refusal to deal with Noida Software Technology Park Ltd. The High Court of Bombay relied on the Bharati Airtel case and held that CCI has no jurisdiction to pass an order to investigate when the matter is pending before the Telecom Disputes Settlement and Appellant Tribunal. Similarly, in the Trustees Association of India and Other v. Competition Commission of India and Other,106 Trustees Association of India and Other v. Competition Commission of India and Other [2022], (Bombay High Court) 2022 SCC OnLine Bom 10263, paras 4, 5, 7; Trustees Association of India and Other v. Competition Commission of India and Other [2022], (Bombay High Court) 2022 SCC OnLine Bom 10264, para. 4. a writ was filed before the Bombay High Court challenging the CCI’s prima facie order. The writ petitioner argued that CCI is holding a separate investigation on the issue which is pending before the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI). The Bombay High Court passed a stay order against the CCI’s inquiry until SEBI concluded its enquiry and formed its prima facie view. The Bombay High Court also directed SEBI to complete its enquiry within sixty days and to form its prima facie opinion on the matter. Further, it was also directed to SEBI that if it concludes that there is no violation of the SEBI Act, 1992, and the matter should properly proceed before CCI, then whatever material SEBI has obtained shall be transmitted to CCI to save time in DG’s investigation. The objective behind such directions is to prevent a scenario of conflicting orders due to parallel investigations by two different regulatory authorities into the same complaint. In Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) and Others v. Competition Commission of India and Another, 107 Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) and Others v. Competition Commission of India and Another [2023], (Delhi High Court) LPA 247 of 2016, paras 21, 27, 49-51. Ericsson filed a writ petition before the Delhi High Court challenging the prima facie orders of the CCI to investigate an abuse of dominance by Ericsson with regards to its Standard Essential Patents (SEPs). The facts of these cases have been discussed in detail in the chapter relating to Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) and competition law. The Single Bench dismissed the writ and allowed the CCI to continue with its inquiry. Against the Single Bench’s judgement, Monsanto filed an appeal before the Division Bench. The issue before the Division Bench of the Delhi High Court was whether the CCI has jurisdiction to deal with the allegations against Ericsson for imposing unfair terms for licensing its SEPs because the Patent Act, 1970 shall have the exclusive jurisdiction to deal with patent-related issues. But the Division Bench of the Delhi High Court set aside the Single Bench’s judgement and noted that if there is a conflict between two special laws, i.e., the Competition Act and the Patents Act, 1970, then the latest law would prevail over the prior law. After the Competition Act was enacted, the Patent Act had to go through an amendment in 2003. As per the rule of interpretation, the Patent Act, 1970 would prevail over the Competition Act for adjudicating disputes relating to patents. Hence, the CCI has no jurisdiction to investigate Ericsson for abusing its dominant position through its SEPs. However, it is important to note that an appeal is pending before the Supreme Court against the Division Bench’s judgement.108 Competition Commission of India v. Monsanto Holdings Pvt Ltd. and Others, (SC) SLP(C) No. 025026 of 2023. It will be interesting to see the view which may be adopted by the Supreme Court if the matter were to go up in appeal because, unlike the Bharti Airtel case, there is no overlap between these two laws. Additionally, another point which one needs to bear in mind is that the proceedings under the Competition Act are in rem proceedings, whereas the proceedings under the Patents Act, 1970 are in personam proceedings. Moving on to those case laws where no conflict exists between CCI and another regulator, hence the jurisdiction of CCI has not been restricted. In Competition Commission of India v. State of Mizoram,109 Competition Commission of India v. State of Mizoram [2022], (SC) (2022) 7 SCC 73, paras 45, 46, 50, 60-64. the Supreme Court, allowing the CCI’s jurisdiction into bid rigging matters in the lottery business, held that there is no conflict in the interplay between the Competition Act and the Lotteries (Regulation) Act 1998. The scope of CCI’s jurisdiction is limited to examining issues of bid rigging or manipulation in the tendering process for the appointment of selling agents and distributors for the lottery business under section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act. The Supreme Court held that lotteries may be a regulated commodity and may even be res extra commercium (i.e., a thing outside commerce), but it would not prevent CCI from exercising its jurisdiction as there is no conflict. [B] Jurisdiction of CCI in Data Privacy and Data Management Further, it is also important to note that the CCI exercises its jurisdiction to deal with anti-competitive practices in the digital communication market. In the context of competition in the digital communication market, the conflict is related to controlling, storing and sharing customers’ data. In the era of data aggregation, the CCI has initiated a suo motu investigation against WhatsApp’s exploitative conduct with respect to data collection and sharing it with its parent company (i.e., Meta).110 In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users, Suo Moto Case No. 01 of 2021. As per the prima facie order, WhatsApp is abusing its dominant position by exploiting end-customers through its Privacy Policy 2021, where WhatsApp does not provide an ‘opt-out’ option to its end-customers from providing personal data. Challenging the order of CCI directing the DG to conduct a suo motu investigation, WhatsApp filed a writ petition before the Delhi High Court in WhatsApp LLC and Another v. Competition Commission of India and Another.111 WhatsApp LLC and Another v. Competition Commission of India and Another [2022], (Delhi High Court) 2022 SCC OnLine Del 2582, paras 30, 31, 32, 33. The primary contention of WhatsApp before the High Court was that a matter is pending against WhatsApp’s Privacy Policy, 2021, before the Supreme Court. So, till the time the matter is sub judice, CCI should not pass any order. Further, WhatsApp argued that to avoid conflicting opinions, the CCI’s suo motu investigation must be stopped. However, the High Court rejected such an argument by holding that the matter pending before the Supreme Court is the violation of the right to privacy under the Constitution of India, 1950, whereas the matter before the CCI is the abuse of dominance under section 4 of the Competition Act. Hence, there will be no conflict because the Supreme Court will not be dealing with the competition angle of WhatsApp’s Privacy Policy. Therefore, in the absence of any irreconcilable repugnancy between the jurisdiction of the CCI and the Supreme Court, the CCI has the liberty to proceed with its investigation under section 26(1) of the Competition Act. Hence, the jurisdiction of CCI has not been ousted to deal with the policies on data collection under the ambit of the Competition Act. At present, CCI is conducting inquiries against the policies and conduct of big tech companies, such as Apple,112 Together We Fight Society v. Apple Inc. and Anr., Case No. 24 of 2021. Google,113 Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., Case No. 41 of 2021. Flipkart,114 Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and Amazon Seller Services Private Limited, Case No. 40 of 2019. Amazon,115 Ibid. Swiggy116 National Restaurant Association of India v. Zomato Limited and Bundl Technologies Private Limited, Case No. 16 of 2021. and Zomato.117 Ibid. In previous orders also,118 Umar Javeed v. Google LLC and Ors., Case No. 39 of 2018; XYZ and Ors. v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., Case Nos 07 of 2020, 14 of 2021 and 35 of 2021. the CCI has shown concern regarding the economic activity in relation to the collection, management, usage and sharing of data by big tech companies. In order to provide an efficient enforcement tool that will keep a check on these big tech companies, the legislation is also planning to introduce the Digital Competition Act, which will address the rising challenges in the field of competition law. These cases and concepts have been discussed in Chapter 6 of this Book. It is also important to note that, after the enactment of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, a Data Protection Board of India will be constituted under the said enactment. It would be interesting to see how the CCI and the Data Protection Board of India will coexist and coordinate with each other to parallelly enforce their respective laws. [C] Contractual Remedies Can Simultaneously Co-exist with the Competition Act The Supreme Court in the SAIL case119 SAIL case, (2010) 10 SCC 744, para. 41. held that the legislature, while enacting the Competition Act, was aware of all the laws in existence and the consequences of the laws, including the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. Further, the legislature enacted the Competition Act with the intention to provide an additional remedy against the anti-competitive practices. Therefore, section 62 of the Competition Act states that the provisions of the Competition Act shall be in addition to, and not in derogation of, the provisions of any other law for the time being in force. It means the Competition Act clearly is not in derogation of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, but in addition to them. The Delhi High Court, in Union of India v. Competition Commission of India,120 Union of India v. Competition Commission of India [2012], (Delhi High Court) 2012 SCC OnLine Del 1114, paras 6, 14-16. said that the scope of inquiry before the CCI is entirely different from the scope of proceeding before a civil court or arbitration tribunal for breach of contract. A civil court may focus on the contractual obligations of the parties and would not go into the aspects of abuse of dominance by one of the contracting parties. However, the CCI is not concerned with the breach of contract or inter-party commercial disputes; its purpose is to prevent anti-competitive effects in the market due to violations of the provisions (sections 3 and 4) of the Competition Act. As the provisions of the Competition Act are in addition to the Contract Act, the party can approach both forums seeking relief. Moreover, multiple remedies that arise from a set of facts would not prevent recourse to more than one proceeding. In Uttrakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board (‘UAPMB’) and Others v. Competition Commission of India,121 Uttrakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board (‘UAPMB’) and Others v. Competition Commission of India [2017], (Delhi High Court) 2017 SCC OnLine Del 10906, paras 17-21. a writ was filed by UAPMB challenging the prima facie order passed by the CCI. UAPMB argued that the Uttarakhand High Court had already considered this issue, so the CCI can’t proceed against the UAPMB. However, the High Court rejected the argument because the issues before the Uttarakhand High Court and the CCI are materially different. It was noted that the allegations before the Uttarakhand High Court related to the arbitrary manner in which the UAPMB was administering the procurement of liquor, whereas the proceedings before the CCI relate to whether there are any anti-competitive practices in the market. The Delhi High Court referred to section 62 of the Competition Act and held that even if other remedies are available under different laws, that would not prevent a party from availing of the remedies under the Competition Act. Thus, recourse to multiple proceedings on the same set of facts is not barred as per section 62 of the Competition Act. Also, the parties which have already availed remedies from another forum are not barred from approaching the CCI on the same set of facts. In Naveen Kataria v. Jaiprakash Associates Limited,122 Naveen Kataria v. Jaiprakash Associates Limited, Case No. 99 of 2014, para. 44. where the informant had approached the Real Estate Regulatory Authority to complaint against the builder for the breach of contract, he filed an information under section 19(1) of the Competition Act alleging abuse of dominance against the builder. The CCI held that the informant is at liberty to approach other sectorial authorities because the provisions of the Competition Act are in addition to other laws. Hence, the informant is allowed to seek remedies from the Real Estate Regulatory Authority or any other forum along with the remedies from the CCI. Though section 61 ousters the jurisdiction of the civil court in competition law matters, a party is still allowed to seek remedies from a civil court or arbitration tribunal regarding a monetary claim arising out of a contract. In Nuziveedu Seeds Ltd and Others v. Mahyco Monsanto Biotec,123 Nuziveedu Seeds Ltd and Others v. Mahyco Monsanto Biotec [2020], (Bombay High Court) 2020 SCC OnLine Bom 816, paras 11, 12, 13, 17 245, 270, 272, 280, 282, 283, 285, 287, 288, 289, 293. a prima facie order was passed to investigate Monsanto for the contravention of sections 3(4) and 4 of the Competition Act in relation to the unfair terms of the Sub-License Agreement. Meanwhile, an arbitration proceeding also took place between the same parties under the Sub-License Agreement for monetary claims, and the arbitration tribunal was awarded in favour of Nuziveedu Seeds Ltd. and directed Monsanto to pay the amount. So, the issue before the Bombay High Court was whether section 61 of the Competition Act would oust the jurisdiction of the arbitration tribunal from entertaining monetary claim arising out of the Sub-License Agreement. The High Court held that the powers of the arbitration tribunal and the powers of the CCI are different and are not overlapping. In this case, the arbitration tribunal could not decide the issue relating to the breach of any of the provisions of the Competition Act. The High Court noted that the arbitration tribunal specifically dealt with the contractual obligation of Monsanto under the Indian Contract Act, 1872, and it had not touched upon the allegations of Monsanto’s anti-competitive behaviour. On the expressive jurisdiction bar of civil courts or arbitration tribunals under section 61 of the Competition Act, the High Court held that if the subject matter of the pleadings falls outside the exclusive jurisdiction of the CCI or if the CCI is not well equipped to grant such relief, then the jurisdiction of a civil court or arbitration tribunal is not ousted. Thus, merely because Nuziveedu Seeds Ltd. had filed an information before the CCI, it would not oust the arbitration tribunal from deciding the monetary claims under any other commercial law. So, at the same time, the parties can approach CCI against the anti-competitive agreements as well as abuse of dominance, and the same parties can also approach a civil court or arbitration tribunal to seek enforcement of their contractual obligations for monetary claims. Both proceedings can be conducted parallelly and do not oust each other’s jurisdiction in respect of the issues that can be exclusively decided by each of these forums.

§1.09 SCOPE OF JUDICIAL REVIEW UNDER ARTICLE 226 OF THE CONSTITUTION OF INDIA, 1950 The Competition Act nowhere provides jurisdiction to High Courts for adjudication of issues relating to competition law. However, relying on various decisions of the High Courts, it can be observed that the orders of the CCI are amenable to a writ under Article 226 of the Constitution of India, 1950. Article 226 of the Constitution of India, 1950, states that every High Court has the power to issue directions to quash the orders of regulatory authorities, and thus, a writ can also be filed to challenge an order of the CCI before the High Court. Answering the question of maintainability of a writ petition or scope of writ jurisdiction against an order of CCI, the Delhi High Court in Mahindra Electric Mobility Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India,124 Mahindra Electric Mobility Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2019], (Delhi High Court) 2019 SCC OnLine Del 8032, para. 85. held that CCI is amenable to judicial review under Article 226 of the Constitution of India, 1950, only for the procedural issues of the CCI. For instance, power under Article 226 can be invoked if it is shown that the CCI’s prima facie order was passed without any reasoning, and then the prima facie orders can be corrected in writ proceedings. Similarly, regarding the conduct of proceedings at a later stage, CCI must ensure fair procedure and compliance with natural justice, or else the final order can be challenged before a writ court. Further, the Supreme Court in the Bharati Airtel case125 Bharti Airtel case, paras 116, 118, 119. upheld the High Court’s direction which quashed the order of the CCI because it was related to the very jurisdiction of CCI. For such procedural error, the High Court was competent to exercise its power under Article 226 of the Constitution of India, 1950. Similarly, in the Institute of Chartered Accounts of India (‘ICAI’) v. Competition Commission of India,126 Institute of Chartered Accounts of India (‘ICAI’) v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (Delhi High Court) 2023 SCC OnLine Del 3422, paras 52, 54, 64-67. the CCI arbitrarily used its power to investigate the Institute of Chartered Accounts of India (ICAI) (a regulatory authority) which was only discharging its regulatory functions. The Delhi High Court quashed the prima facie order of the CCI as it has no jurisdiction to investigate the decisions, policies, or the way ICAI discharges its functions. It is interesting to note that, rather than quashing the prima facie order of the CCI, the High Court in Google Inc. and Others v. Competition Commission of India and Another 127 Google Inc. and Others v. Competition Commission of India and Another [2015], (Delhi High Court) 2015, SCC OnLine 8992, paras 18(L), (M), 19-24. directed the CCI to recall/review its prima facie order for violating the principles of natural justice. Thus, parties aggrieved by CCI’s irregular inquiry can always seek a remedy by invoking the power of the High Court under Article 226. And if the High Court is convinced that some prejudice has been caused to the aggrieved party because due process of law has not been followed or principles of natural justice have been violated, then the High Court can provide an appropriate remedy. However, the remedies of Article 226 of the Constitution of India, 1950 are only limited to procedural error and violation of principles of natural justice. Power under Article 226 of the Constitution of India, 1950, can’t be invoked by the High Court to adjudge the validity of an order of CCI based on merits.128 Bharati Airtel case, para. 121. The Karnataka High Court in Flipkart Interned Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. CCI and Others,129 Flipkart Interned Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission India and Others [2021], (Karnataka High Court) Writ Appeal No. 562 of 2021, para. 26. while dealing with a writ against a prima facie order of CCI, held that a prima facie order is administrative in nature and the High Court would not be competent to adjudicate the validity of such an order on merits. In this case, the writ petitioner argued on the merits of the case, but the High Court dismissed the same by saying that the jurisdiction of a writ court is limited to the issue of prima facie order. Hence, all the grounds raised by Flipkart and Amazon on merits cannot be entertained in a writ petition.

§1.10 POWER OF RECALL As discussed above, under the Competition Act, an investigation into an anti-competitive agreement or an abuse of a dominant position can be undertaken by the DG only if the CCI first finds a prima facie case under section 26(1). Therefore, upon the receipt of information under section 19 of the Competition Act, the CCI in a preliminary hearing determines whether a prima facie case has been made out by the informant. However, the enterprise against whom the information has been filed does not have a right of opportunity/hearing before the CCI when the prima facie order is passed.130 SAIL case, para. 91. It is up to the volition of the CCI to call the other party or not. Therefore, the prima facie order can be passed ex parte without the presence of the opposite party. Also, a prima facie order passed by the CCI is not appealable to the NCLAT,131 Under s. 53A (1) (a) of the Competition Act only certain orders mentioned therein are appealable in the light of the dicta of the Supreme Court in SAIL case, para. 41. and the parties are, therefore, required to participate in the investigation before the DG. This investigation, depending on the case and the volume of information, may last anywhere between a year and a few years. Therefore, during this period, the parties can either participate in the investigation proceedings before the DG for a few years or challenge the prima facie order under limited grounds before the High Courts in a writ petition under Article 226 of the Constitution of India, 1950. Another option that is available to parties is to seek a recall of the prima facie order passed by the CCI. As noted above, the Delhi High Court in the case of Google Inc. and others v. Competition Commission of India and Another 132 Google Inc. and Others v. Competition Commission of India and Another [2015], (Delhi High Court) 2015, SCC OnLine 8992, paras 18(L), (M), 19-24. has now held that the CCI has the power to recall its prima facie order. Although vide the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2007, the power relating to the review of the orders was stripped of the CCI, the High Court has held that the CCI has the power to recall its prima facie order and further held that there exists no specific bar in the Competition Act or any of the attendant regulations passed by the CCI to preclude the invocation of the inherent power to recall/review. The High Court has, however, held that the right of recall would be sparingly exercised and should be apparent on the face of the material before the CCI and without lengthy arguments and only if, without entering into any factual controversy, the CCI finds no merit in the information on which investigation has been ordered. This way it is possible for the parties to avoid the rigmarole of the complete investigation process before the DG by seeking a recall of the order. Further, even if the CCI were to reject the application seeking recall of the order, the right of the parties to approach the High Courts would still be available.

§1.11 SETTLEMENT OR COMMITMENT UNDER THE COMPETITION ACT [A] Legal Framework for Settlement and Commitment The Competition Act has a leniency mechanism to allow members in a cartel arrangement to come clean by filing a leniency application under section 46 of the Competition Act (discussed in Chapter 2). However, before the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, there was no separate provision in the Competition Act allowing for settlements/commitments to be accepted by the enterprises. The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, metamorphoses the enforcement mechanism of CCI by introducing ‘settlement’ or ‘commitment’. These enforcement tools will expedite the inquiry, as they would enable the CCI to resolve disputes by closing an inquiry against the contravention of sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act if an enterprise offers a settlement or a commitment, under the following terms:– under section 48A of the Competition Act, an application for settlement (i.e., settlement amount) may be moved by the enterprise after the DG submits its report and before the final order under section 27 or 28 of the Competition Act is passed by the CCI; and – under section 48B of the Competition Act, an application for commitment (i.e., behavioural and structural remedies) may be moved by the enterprise after the prima facie order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act is received by the enterprise and before receipt of the DG’s investigation report under section 26(4) of the Competition Act. Section 48C of the Competition Act also provides for revocation of the settlement/commitment order and penalty under three circumstances – first, if an enterprise fails to comply with the settlement/commitment order; second, if the enterprise has not made full and true disclosure; and third, if there has been a material change in the facts. However, while it has been expressly stipulated that no appeal shall lie to the NCLAT against an order for settlement/commitment under the Act, it has not been clarified whether an order revoking settlement/commitment will be appealable to the NCLAT. The absence of such a provision may lead to issues for enterprises, which may challenge this provision before the court of law. Interestingly, settlement/commitment was impliedly read into the Competition Act even before their express introduction by way of the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023. The Madras High Court, in the case of the Tamil Nadu Film Exhibitors Association v. Competition Commission of India and Others, 133 Tamil Nadu Film Exhibitors Association v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2015], (Madras High Court) 2015 SCC OnLine Mad 7099, paras 33, 35, 41, 44, 45. had identified that even without the existence of a specific provision enabling settlement/commitment, the scheme of the Competition Act allows for settlements and commitments to be reached between parties. The Madras High Court held that the CCI has residuary powers to pass orders which are wide in amplitude, and which empower the CCI to accept a settlement/commitment between parties. Further, it was observed that once a party agrees not to abuse its dominant position or continue with an anti-competitive agreement, the only question left for the CCI would be to deal with the question of imposition of penalty. However, the Madras High Court also struck a note of caution and held that CCI would be obliged to look into the settlement/commitment and check whether it was a cover-up to prevent an investigation being made into anti-competitive practices or abuse of dominant position. In doing so, the CCI would have to ensure that such settlements/commitments: (a) would not lead to the continuance of anti-competitive practices; (b) would not allow the abuse of the dominant position to continue; and (c) would not be prejudicial to the interest of consumers or to the freedom of trade. Further, competition law deals with the prevention of practices which cause or are likely to cause market distortion. A private settlement may settle a dispute between parties which may not remedy the entire market situation, which the CCI seeks to address. Nevertheless, the express introduction of settlement and commitment into the Competition Act will bring immense clarity and certainty into the system, therefore encouraging parties to opt for settlement and commitment. It may also be noted that an informant or aggrieved party has been permitted to file for compensation under section 53N of the Competition Act, even after a settlement has been offered by the enterprise. The mechanism is in tandem with the objective of ‘ease of doing business in India’ as well as in the interest of the stakeholders and, if implemented properly, could reduce litigation and allow easier resolution of a dispute relating to the Competition Act, ultimately benefitting the market. [B] Procedure of Settlement and Commitment Further, the CCI introduced the Competition Commission of India (Settlement) Regulations, 2023, and the Competition Commission of India (Commitment) Regulations, 2023, to specify the procedures for settlement and commitment, respectively. According to the said regulations, an enterprise has to file a written settlement/commitment application to the CCI with fees and certain details. A settlement application must be filed within forty-five days of receiving the DG’s report, and a commitment application must be filed within forty-five days of receiving a prima facie order. A thirty-day extension may be given as per CCI’s discretion. After receiving the settlement/commitment application, the CCI must hold a meeting within seven days. If the CCI is not prima facie satisfied, then it will ask the enterprise to revise its application within fifteen days. Once CCI is satisfied, the inquiry against the enterprise will be in abeyance until the final order. However, other parties shall be given an opportunity to raise objections against the settlement/commitment application. Finally, considering the factual scenario, application, and objections, the CCI may allow, reject, or direct the enterprise to revise (within fifteen days) its settlement/commitment application. After receiving the revised settlement/commitment application, the CCI will again decide whether to allow it or not. If the CCI is allowing settlement applications from the enterprise, then the enterprise must accept the same and deposit the settlement amount within thirty days. Whereas, if the commitment application gets approval, then the enterprise has to comply with the commitments. The entire timeline of a settlement application should be concluded within 120 days, whereas the entire timeline of a commitment application should be concluded within 90 days. However, the CCI can extend the timeline. The timeline of the entire settlement application and commitment application process has been described below. A diagrammatic illustration of the timeline of an approved settlement application process is as follows: A diagrammatic illustration of timeline of a commitment application process is as under: [C] The Challenges Ahead It must be noted that neither the Competition Act nor the said regulations clarify whether a settlement/commitment may amount to an admission of guilt by an enterprise. The Standing Committee observed that134 Standing Committee on Finance, The Competition (Amendment) Bill, 2022, 52nd Report, p. 39. admission of guilt is not assumed in the settlement/commitment mechanism. Assuming that a settlement/commitment amounts to an admission of guilt, then the purpose of settlement/commitment will be lost since enterprises will be reluctant to file an application under section 48A or 48B of the Competition Act, even at the risk of prolonged litigation. Further, there can be a scenario wherein only one party (‘settlement/commitment applicant’) of a vertical anti-competitive agreement files a settlement/commitment application and agrees to change the concerning agreement, but the other party to the same vertical anti-competitive agreement has not participated in the said proceeding. In such a scenario, though the other party will get a chance to object to the settlement/commitment application, however, once the CCI approves the settlement/commitment application, then the commercial agreement between two private parties shall stand modified unilaterally based on the settlement/commitment offered only by the applicant without the consent of the other party. Thus, the CCI must take some steps to avoid unilateral novation/alteration of the contract in the garb of settlement/commitment proceedings by the applicant and prevent the applicant from taking any undue advantage of the same. Finally, implementation of the settlements/commitments would be a great challenge for the CCI, so how the CCI would effectively monitor the compliance of settlement and commitment would be an interesting issue to watch out for in the future.

§1.12 SECTOR INQUIRY The CCI regularly conducts market studies of different sectors or industries in order to understand the business structure and the problems of stakeholders. Such an exercise provides the CCI with better insights into the market, which would later help the CCI take action against the anti-competitive practices that are hindering the full pro-competitive potential of the market. The CCI interacts with market participants, end-customers, government agencies, academicians, etc., to collect data and have a deep understanding of the market and its practices. Also, some of the issues in specific circumstances may cause an anti-competitive effect on the competition in the market. Thus, after CCI recognizes existing anti-competitive practices, it can conduct a suo motu investigation and pass appropriate orders. Further, the CCI, through the findings of these market studies, also educates market participants to change their practices before they contravene the provisions of the Competition Act. Let’s look at the market studies carried out by the CCI and the recommendations offered to the market participants. [A] E-Commerce Market The CCI has conducted a market study into the online markets to understand the practices of the e-commerce platforms and to realize the competitive force of the e-commerce market. The CCI noted major issues in the e-commerce market, such as platform neutrality, unfair contract terms of platforms, the imposition of price parity clauses to prevent business users from selling their goods at lower prices, exclusive agreements by online travel agencies, deep discounting, etc. After conducting the market study, the CCI noted that the marketplace platforms should adopt the following self-regulatory measures:(i) the market participants must draft the search ranking parameters in simple language and display it in their Terms and Conditions; (ii) the terms of collection, usage and sharing of the data with other market participants must be transparent; (iii) user review and rating mechanism needs to be transparent, and fraudulent reviews or ratings must be prohibited; (iv) modify the contractual terms and make them fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory towards business users; (v) draft fair discount policies which include the basis of discount rates to show that there is no negative implication on the business users for not following the discount policy. [B] Telecom Sector The CCI research on the recent evolution of the telecom industry analysed challenges to the competition, identified the pro-competitive factors in the industry, and noted the competition between TSPs, etc. The CCI carried out the market study by collecting data from published reports, interviewing stakeholders, etc. The CCI noted that the telecom sector used to be a tightly controlled duopoly market due to high entry barriers. But now more participants are entering the market because the entry barrier has been relaxed, tariffs are subject to forbearance, and spectrum has been delinked from the license and is available in the secondary market. The erstwhile license regime of the telecom sector has been liberalized to keep pace with the market development. Further, the CCI noted that non-pricing-based competition is driving competition in the telecom industry. With respect to data privacy and competition issues, the CCI noted that the market participants must allow access and protect consumer privacy. Accordingly, the TSP must draft their privacy policies so that it may not exploit the consumer. [C] Pharmaceutical Sector In this market study, the CCI targeted the pharmaceutical sector. The CCI opined that it has come across various issues in branded generic drugs in India, such as competition in the generic drug market, drug prices, quality control of generic drugs, etc. CCI noted the price dispersion across different pharmaceutical companies, the market concentration, the leader of the market, trade association practices, etc. The CCI focused on demand-side and supply-side issues with respect to pharmaceutical distributors. The CCI also analysed the emerging competitive dynamics in online pharmacy. CCI said that there is huge growth in e-pharmacy after COVID-19 because e-pharmacy platforms provide an omnichannel experience, doorstep delivery, wide reach, etc. Finally, the CCI has made its key findings and observations at the end of the report:(i) E-Pharmacies must adopt self-regulatory measures in relation to collecting, using and sharing data; (ii) every stage of the pharmaceutical industry must be transparent; (iii) there shall be uniformity in the quality standards across the sector, and steps need to be taken for effective implementation of existing quality standards; (iv) organized drug testing to improve the quality of pharmaceutical products; (v) building a national digital drug databank to bring information asymmetry across the sector. [D] Cab Aggregators The CCI published this report, which contains an economic analysis of a survey of drivers and riders, a controlled experiment, and stakeholder consultation. CCI collected data from two tier-I cities and two tier-II cities to get a diverse response on surge pricing in the cab aggregator market. Thereafter, the CCI studied the pricing behaviour of cab aggregators and found that the prices were not uniform across riders; hence, personalized pricing exists in the sector. The CCI also noted that the parameter that governs the pricing of fares is not transparent. Hence, the CCI recommended the following:(i) There must be transparency regarding the break-down of the total fare. (ii) The surge in pricing has not been perceived as counterproductive by the riders, however, the cab aggregators must put a cap on the pricing to prevent exploitative price situations. (iii) Cab aggregators must maintain a basic level of transparency and information asymmetries on their platforms. [E] Film Distribution Chain The CCI conducted a market study on the film industry to understand the competition in the said industry. The CCI noted that the film industry is growing at a Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) of 13.7% by 2023. The major factors behind the growth of the film industry are significant supply and demand, accessibility of smartphones, and inexpensive data. The CCI analysed previous competition law cases involving film associations and surveyed various stakeholders from different film industries. The CCI noted that the multiplex owners have a higher bargaining power than the film producers. Hence, considering the prevailing practices in deal-making between multiplexes and film producers, the CCI made the following recommendations:(i) the standard form of contract must be avoided between multiplexes and film producers, and the contract must not be a unilateral, one-sided contract favouring the multiplexes; (ii) multiplexes should provide fair terms to the film producers for their promotion of films; (iii) multiplexes should not restrain the producers from releasing their films; (iv) transparency in box office revenue collection by adopting a monitoring system; (v) film associations must not facilitate anti-competitive practices in the industry.

§1.13 CONSUMER One of the main objectives of the Competition Act is to promote economic efficiency by assisting in the creation of markets which are responsive to consumer preferences.135 SAIL case, paras 6, 8, 125. The prohibition of anti-competitive practices has direct or indirect benefits to the consumers who are at the end of the supply chain of the market. Therefore, the CCI enforces the principles of competition law to ensure that the consumer policies of the market players provide adequate and affordable choices for such consumers.136 Excel Crop case, para. 21. Along with enhancing the welfare of the supply side’s consumers, the Competition Act also protects the demand-side or supply-side business users from being exploited or excluded from the market.137 Ibid. Though the consumers can protect their rights by applying the consumer protection policies, i.e., the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, however, the Competition Act will be in addition to the remedies available under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019. As we discussed earlier, section 62 of the Competition Act says that the competition law is in addition to other laws. Similarly, section 100 of the Consumer Protection Act also says that the consumer protection law is in addition to other laws. Further, in accordance with this, the Supreme Court in the Excel Crop case,138 Ibid. said that the Competition Act will complement the Consumer Protection Act while holding that:Consumer welfare protection may be required in order to redress a perceived imbalance between the market power of consumers and producers. The imbalance between consumers and producers may stem from market failures such as information asymmetries, the lack of bargaining position towards producers and high transaction costs. Competition policy may serve as a complement to consumer protection policies to address such market failures. The CCI will provide a remedy to the consumer by rectifying the market failure. Whereas the Consumer Forums will protect consumers by directing the enterprise to compensate for the personal harm of the consumer. So, both laws prohibit unfair trade practices and protect the interests of consumers, but on different tangents. In Shri Sunil Bansal v. M/s Jaiprakash Associates Ltd. and Ors., 139 Shri Sunil Bansal v. M/s Jaiprakash Associates Ltd. and Ors., 2015 SCC OnLine CCI 180, paras 59, 63, 68, 69. a group of house owners filed information against the builder for abusing its dominance by imposing unfair terms on them. Challenging the jurisdiction of the CCI, the builder argued that the relevant product market is buildings which are immovable goods and CCI can’t deal with immovable goods. Relying on a Supreme Court judgement on the Consumer Protection Act, the builder submitted that the activity of selling buildings amounts to the sale of immovable property and not ‘goods’ as ‘goods’ under the Competition Act, making reference to the Sale of Goods Act, 1930, which expressly excludes immovable property from its ambit. Thus, the buildings are also outside the scope of the CCI’s jurisdiction. Finally, the builder submitted that this is a consumer law matter and that the Consumer Forum is the appropriate body to adjudicate the matter. However, the CCI rejected the contention of the builder because a Supreme Court’s judgement on the Consumer Protection Act will not be applicable to a competition-related matter. The CCI held that the definitions of ‘consumer’ and ‘service’ under the Competition Act are wider than the definition of these terms provided in the Consumer Protection Act. Further, the CCI held that the purpose of the Competition Act allows the CCI to protect consumers from unfair practices. Finally, the CCI held that the availability of remedies before the Consumer Forum does not oust the jurisdiction of the CCI, as section 62 of the Competition Act says that the Consumer Protection Act is in addition to the remedies available under the Competition Act. Hence, CCI has jurisdiction to deal with the anti-competitive practices of the builder. Furthermore, the definition of the word ‘consumer’ in the Competition Act is wider than the definition of ‘consumer’ in the Consumer Protection Act, 2019. The Competition Act has defined the meaning of the word ‘consumer’ under section 2(f) as any person who purchases goods or avails services for resale, commercial or personal purposes. The meaning of the word ‘consumer’ under the Competition Act is wider than the meaning that is given under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019. Section 2(7) of the Consumer Protection Act, 2019 defines the word ‘consumer’ as any person who buys or purchases goods or avails services for personal purposes, not commercial purposes, or to resale them further for profit. Hence, from the definition given under the Competition Act, a consumer can be:(i) the end-consumer, which purchases goods or avails services for personal use or consumption; or (ii) the business user who also purchases goods or avails services for commercial purposes or resale them. The Competition Act protects a person who is involved in a business-to-business transaction because such a person will also fall within the definition of ‘consumer’. While the Consumer Protection Act, 2019 is only concerned with the person who does not buy goods for commercial or business purposes, the Competition Act takes into account even intermediate consumers. The Consumer Protection Act, 2019 would not protect people who purchased goods or availed services for a ‘commercial purpose’.

§1.14 ENTERPRISE The word enterprise has been defined in a broad manner in the Competition Act. It is imperative to discuss the contours of the term enterprise because the scheme of the Competition Act allows the application of section 4 of the Competition Act to an enterprise or group and not every enterprise. Section 2(h) of the Competition Act defines the term ‘enterprise’ as a person or a Department of the Government which is engaged in any economic activity relating to the production, storage, supply, investment, etc., either directly or through one or more of its units or divisions or subsidiaries. However, the government bodies discharging sovereign functions, such as activity relating to atomic energy, currency, defence and space, will be excluded from the definition of ‘enterprise’. Person has been defined in a broad manner to include any company, firm or juridical person. From the definition of the ‘enterprise’, the following key points emerge:(i) The statutory scheme of the Competition Act, the definition of enterprise and the decisional practice of the CCI with respect to enterprise have ensured that competitive neutrality is achieved in the present competition law dispensation. This means that state-owned and private businesses compete on a level playing field. Competitive neutrality requires that governments or state-owned enterprises should not misuse their powers to get an advantage over the private sector. Upon a bare perusal of the definition, it becomes clear that all the commercial activities of the government, excluding the sovereign one, come under the provisions of the Competition Act and that no anti-trust exemptions are applicable to state-owned enterprises. State-owned enterprises or public sector undertakings/enterprises (PSUs) are government majority-held companies, statutory corporations set up by an act of Parliament and fully owned by the government. Reading this with section 19(4)(g) of the Competition Act, the CCI can look into the activities of dominant enterprises, even if the dominant enterprise has acquired its dominance by way of statutory monopoly. In this regard, it is apposite to note that the CCI has looked into the activities of state-owned enterprises, details of which are provided in Chapter 3. (ii) The definition explicitly excludes any function of the government that is relatable to sovereign functions that includes activity relating to energy, currency, defence and space. It has been observed by the Supreme Court that in a welfare state, functions of the state are not only the defence of the country or administration of justice or maintaining law and order but also extends to regulating and controlling the activities of people in almost every sphere, inter alia, educational, commercial, social, economic, political and even martial.
