In 2011, the Delhi High Court in Union of India v. Competition Commission of India,140 Union of India v. Competition Commission of India [2012], (Delhi High Court) 2012 SCC OnLine Del 1114, para. 23. explained the distinction between sovereign and non-sovereign functions with respect to the definition of enterprise under the Competition Act. The Delhi High Court noted that:Any welfare, commercial and economic functions are not sovereign functions and state while discharging such functions is as much amenable to the jurisdiction of CCI as any other private entity discharging such functions. (iii) It must be kept in mind that the objective of the Competition Act is wide, and it seeks to prevent practices, having adverse effects on competition, promote and sustain competition in the market, protect the interests of consumers and ensure freedom of trade carried out by market participants. The Competition Act applies to all enterprises and persons based on the activity performed. Although the definition of the term ‘enterprise’ does exclude sovereign functions, it is to be noted that the Competition Act does not provide for exemption for enterprises from the purview of the Competition Act. Section 54 of the Competition Act provides that the Central Government may, by notification, exempt from the application of the Competition Act any enterprise which performs a sovereign function on behalf of the Central Government or the State Government. Thus, the scheme of the Competition Act provides that there must be an exemption which has to be granted by the Central Government under section 54 to state that the Competition Act is not applicable to such enterprises/class of enterprises. (iv) It must be noted that the Supreme Court in Coal India Ltd. v. CCI and Another,141 Coal India case, paras 81, 82. stated that Coal India Ltd., which is engaged in coal mining services, will fall under the definition of the word ‘enterprise’, as the definition includes any person or a Department of the Government which is engaged in an activity related to production, storage, supply, distribution, acquisition or control of articles or goods. Further, the legislature expressly includes even Departments of the Government separately within the ambit of the word ‘enterprise’. Hence, government companies are not excluded from the definition of ‘enterprise’. It’s only the sovereign activity of the government bodies which is exempted. However, Coal India Ltd. is not discharging any sovereign function, so it will not be exempted. Similarly, the Delhi High Court in Uttrakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board (‘UAPMB’) and Others v. Competition Commission of India 142 Uttrakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board (‘UAPMB’) and Others v. Competition Commission of India, 2017 SCC OnLine Del 10906, para. 11. said that the exemption under section 2(h) will not be applicable to the writ petitioners, which was a government body, charged with the function of supply and distribution of liquor. (v) While the definition of enterprise is wide, it must be noted that for a person to be called an enterprise, it must perform a commercial activity. In Mr. Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC and Another,143 Mr. Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC and Another, Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 210. the CCI held that even if Google is not charging anything for its general web service, it can still be considered commercial or economic activity. Through the general web service, Google was collecting user data and monetizing it through advertisements. Further, it must be noted that the CCI in some of the cases has held that trade association is not an enterprise since they do not perform any commercial function and earn revenue, although some anti-competitive decisions may be taken under the aegis of the trade association. Having said that, trade associations, which are not engaged in any commercial activity, will not fall under the exemption of enterprise because in some cases, the CCI has held in some other cases that trade associations are enterprises since they earn revenues from their decisions.144 M/s Shivam Enterprises v. Kiratpur Sahib Truck Operators Co-operative Transport Society Limited & Ors, Case No. 43 of 2013, paras 13, 14. In the Competition Commission of India v. Coordination Committee of Artistes & Technicians of W.B. Film & Television (‘Coordination Committee case’),145 Competition Commission of India v. Coordination Committee of Artistes & Technicians of W.B. Film & Television [2017], (SC) (2017) 5 SCC 17, paras 46, 47, 48. the Supreme Court held that a non-profit making entity may be considered an enterprise when it is engaged in some commercial or economic activities, even if it is operating in its charitable capacity at other times. If the activity of the trade association yields profits by selling goods or providing services, then the activity will be considered economic activity, and the entity will be constituted as an enterprise. The Supreme Court further noted that the members of the trade association were engaged in the production, distribution and exhibition of films/serials in West Bengal. The collective call to boycott the production of their competing members shows that the trade association takes decisions on the exhibition of films and serials, which is an economic activity. Hence, protection in the name of being a trade association goes against the object of the Competition Act. Therefore, the test is whether the person in question performs any commercial activity and earns a revenue stream. It is because of this reason that the CCI has not exercised jurisdiction over policy formulation functions of the government because they cannot be considered to be ‘commercial activities’ and hence not an enterprise under section 2(h) of the Competition Act.146 Vineet Kumar v. Ministry of Civil Aviation, Case No. 18/2013, paras 9, 10. Therefore, a government body while formulating policy guidelines cannot be said to be indulging in policy decisions and hence cannot be termed as an enterprise and therefore section 4 will not be applicable to such entities.

§1.15 RELEVANT MARKET Under the statutory scheme of the Competition Act, the definition of relevant market is of paramount importance. In an abuse of dominance analysis, an enterprise will be considered dominant only when the said enterprise holds a position of strength in the relevant market. Similarly, in a combination analysis, the CCI has to analyse whether the said combination will cause or is likely to cause an appreciable adverse effect in the relevant market in India. Also, in a vertical anti-competitive agreement analysis under section 3(4) of the Competition Act, although the Competition Act does not lay down expressly that the CCI has to define a relevant market, the decisional practice of the CCI (as elucidated in Chapter 2) would show that they have adopted the de minimis doctrine; i.e., parties with minimal market share entering into an agreement will not cause any appreciable adverse effect on competition (AAEC) in India.147 Shri Ghanshyam Das Vij v. M/s Bajaj Corp Limited, Case No. 68 of 2013, paras 77, 80. The Supreme Court in the Coordination Committee case, 148 Coordination Committee case, paras 14, 33-36. held that the first and foremost aspect that needs to be determined by the CCI is the relevant market in which the competition is affected due to the anti-competitive agreement. The Supreme Court interpreted the word ‘market’ in section 19(3) as ‘relevant market’ and held that it is important to determine the relevant market to identify the competitors of the concerned party, the boundaries of the competition, and the competitive constraints faced by market participants in order to examine the concentration of the market. Similarly, the Supreme Court in the Bharti Airtel case149 Bharti Airtel case, para. 86. also noted the term ‘market’ in section 19(3) as ‘relevant market’. However, later the Supreme Court, in the Coordination Committee case,150 Clarification order of Coordination Committee case, [2018], (SC) Miscellaneous Application No. 490 of 2017 in Civil Appeal No. 6691 of 2014. clarified that determination of ‘relevant market’ is not mandatorily determined for the violation of section 3(3) (cartel provisions) of the Competition Act. The Supreme Court clearly said that determining the relevant market is not a mandatory pre-condition in section 3(3) violations because of the statutory presumption contained in section 3(3) of the Competition Act. However, this clarification may not be applicable to vertical anti-competitive agreements because the Coordination Committee case specifically dealt with violation of section 3(3) of the Competition Act. Such statutory presumption is not present for violation of section 3(4) of the Competition Act, hence, it can be assumed that the Supreme Court never implied that delineation of relevant market is not mandatory for section 3(4). Although the legislative framework does not mandate the CCI to define a relevant market for a section 3(4) analysis, the final outcome of the analysis of the appreciable adverse effects on the competition may still require determining relevant market. Therefore, a broad understanding of the market contours has to be undertaken, even in section 3(4) analysis. Relevant market is essentially a tool to identify and define the boundaries of competition between firms. The primary purpose of market definition is to identify the competitive constraints faced by enterprises.151 Bharti Airtel case, para. 87. In an abuse of dominance investigation, the main purpose behind identification of relevant market is to understand whether an enterprise has the position of strength to behave independently of the competitive forces. In a combination analysis, the CCI identifies the relevant market to see whether the combination would cause an AAEC in the relevant market. The mode of analysis in a combination analysis is whether the combination would create or strengthen market power in the relevant market. Generally, the CCI has approved combinations in the first phase itself because post-combination, the combined market share of the combining parties in the relevant market is minimal. Therefore, the objective of determining a relevant market is to identify those competitors of the enterprises involved that are capable of constraining the behaviour of the concerned enterprise and of preventing them from behaving independently of any effective competitive pressure. Further, regarding the scope of defining relevant market for combinations cases and abuse of dominance cases, the erstwhile COMPAT, in National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India,152 National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2014], (COMPAT) MANU/TA/0016/2014, para. 54. observed that combination analysis is an ex-ante process of the proposed combination where CCI examines whether the proposed combination will adversely affect the competition of the market in the future. Thus, in combination cases, the CCI looks at the probabilities and likelihood of anti-competitive effects due to the combination. Therefore, it is natural that the determination of relevant market for combination cases becomes ‘as narrow as possible’ to evaluate the impact of combination in the future. However, such an analysis of a combination case is in sharp contra distinction with an abuse of dominance case, which is an ex post proceeding. In an abuse of dominance case, the CCI decides the past abusive conduct of an enterprise. Therefore, the analysis of abuse of dominance is an analysis of a past conduct. Thus, the mode of determination of relevant market in combination cases may differ from determination of relevant market in abuse of dominance cases. Relevant market has been defined in section 2(r) of the Competition Act as the market which may be delineated according to the relevant product market and/or the relevant geographic market. Relevant market has been defined both in product terms as well as geographic terms. Relevant product market is defined in section 2(t) of the Competition Act to mean a market comprising the products or services which are regarded as interchangeable by the customer due to the characteristics of the products or services, their prices and use, or interchangeable by the supplier due to the ease of switching production without incurring any significant additional cost or risk. Therefore, the primary thrust of defining relevant product market is the interchangeability or substitutability of a given product in terms of price, characteristics, use and switching cost. [A] Relevant Product Market Section 19(7) of the Competition Act provides for the indices which have to be taken into account by the CCI, while determining the relevant product market. While determining the relevant product market, the CCI shall consider all or any of the following factors, namely:(a) physical characteristics, end-use of goods or the nature of services; (b) price of goods or service; (c) consumer preferences; (d) exclusion of in-house production; (e) existence of specialized producers; (f) classification of industrial products; (g) costs associated with switching demand or supply to other goods or services; (h) categories of customers. Thus, we see that demand side and supply side substitutability is the primary factor to be taken into account while determining relevant product market. Demand side substitutability assesses the extent to which customers could and would switch among substitute products in response to a change in relative prices or quality or availability or other features. It is to be noted that the relevant product market has to be defined by taking into account all those products or services which are regarded as interchangeable or substitutable by the consumer. So, two products which are not perfectly substitutable but functionally interchangeable would form part of the same relevant product market. Apart from demand side substitutability, competition law authorities also look at supply side substitutability. Previously, the Competition Act only provided for consideration of demand side substitutability, and the CCI has also held in a few cases that it only considers demand side substitutability, and not supply side substitutability, which is to be considered for the purpose of determining the relevant market.153 Informant (Confidential) v. Grasim Industries Limited, Case No. 51 of 2017, para. 20. However, at the same time, in JK Tyre & Industries Limited and J.K. Asia Pacific (S) Pte Ltd.,154 JK Tyre, J.K. Asia Pacific (S) Pte Ltd., Cavendish Industries Ltd. and Kesoram Industries Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2015/10/322, 18 Feb. 2016, para. 10. for the purpose of competition assessment in the tyre market, the CCI has also given due consideration to supply side substitutability. Having said that, the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, amended the definition of relevant product market and also added a specific factor in section 19(7) of the Competition Act to introduce the concept of supply side substitutability. The wording of section 2(t) of the Competition Act states that relevant product market is that basket of products which are considered substitutable by the consumer or supplier. Therefore, the criteria for defining relevant product market under the Competition Act includes both demand side substitutability and supply side substitutability. [B] Relevant Geographic Market Section 2(s) of the Competition Act defines relevant geographic market as:market comprising the area in which the conditions of competition for supply of goods or provision of services or demand of goods or services are distinctly homogenous and can be distinguished from the conditions prevailing in the neighbouring areas. Section 19(6) of the Competition Act further provides some factors which need to be taken into account by the CCI while determining relevant geographic market. While determining the ‘relevant geographic market’, CCI shall have due regard to all or any of the following factors, namely: –(a) regulatory trade barriers; (b) local specification requirements; (c) national procurement policies; (d) adequate distribution facilities; (e) transport costs; (f) language; (g) consumer preferences; (h) need for secure or regular supplies or rapid after-sales services; (i) characteristics of goods or nature of services; (j) costs associated with switching supply or demand to other areas. [C] Trends of the CCI While the definitions and the approach of defining relevant market under the Competition Act are similar to other anti-trust jurisdictions, the important factor to note is the manner in which the CCI adopts the same in its analysis against vertical anti-competitive agreements, dominance investigation and merger control proceedings. Some of the key trends which can be seen are as follows: [1] Starting Point of Analysis As discussed earlier in the Coordination Committee case,155 Coordination Committee case, paras 33-38. the first step of the analysis is the determination of the relevant market. It is only by defining the relevant market that a firm’s market power can be assessed. The purpose of defining the relevant market is to identify which products and services operate as a competitive constraint on the behaviour of the suppliers of those respective products and services. The process of determining the relevant market starts by looking into a relatively narrow potential product market. Thereafter, the potential product market is expanded to include those substituted products which buyers or suppliers would still prefer, even after there is a price increase above the competitive price. Likewise, the relevant geographic market needs to be defined. In fact, the Supreme Court in the Bharti Airtel case,156 Bharti Airtel case, para. 87. held that ‘market’ definition is a tool to identify the concentration of the market and the competition boundaries of the market participants. The relevant market is the key to determining which market participant holds the power over the market and the impact of the alleged conduct on the market. Thus, it is necessary to delineate the relevant product market and relevant geographical market to determine the market participant who has power over the market. The CCI, in the HT Media Ltd v. Super Cassettes Ltd., 157 HT Media Ltd v. Super Cassettes Ltd., Case No. 40 of 2011, para. 134. has noted that ‘since the allegation of the informant pertains to certain conduct of the opposite party in licensing its repertoire of songs to the informant, the market for licensing of music content (protect as intellectual property) is a good starting point for determination of the relevant market in this case’. Therefore, the approach of the CCI in identifying relevant market starts from the relevant product or service of the alleged dominant enterprise, and then the substitutes of the said product are identified to determine the contours of the relevant market. Similarly, in a combination case, the CCI first identifies the overlaps between the parties in India as the starting point for relevant market determination. [2] Analysis in Multi-sided Markets Is from the Perspective of Affected Parties The tools of relevant market analysis remain the same, irrespective of how many ‘sides’ or user groups the enterprise in question may cater to. But in such situations, a question emerges as to from whose perspective will the relevant market be defined. This question was answered in the MMT-Go case.158 MMT-Go case, paras 190, 295. In this case, the anti-competitive conduct of an online booking platform which connected users with various travel/tour-related service providers, such as hotels, airlines, trains, buses, was under consideration. The CCI held that since the alleged conduct under scrutiny was vis-à-vis hotels and franchise hotel service providers, assessment of dominance and relevant market delineation would have to be carried out from their perspective. To contextualize the discussion, the first thing to be considered here is who is this ‘user’ that should be the focus of assessment. When two distinct products/services are supplied, ascertaining substitutability only from the perspective of one user group may erroneously conflate the competitive constraints that exist separately and distinctly in relation to each user side. At the same time, the impact of such interactions on the substitutability has to be analysed primarily from the aggrieved user side.159 Ibid., paras 190-194. Hence, in the MMT-Go case, since the alleged conduct was related to the contractual arrangements between hotels and the online booking platform, the prime focus was the hotels’ side of the market. Yet, the consumer side perspective (i.e., the other user group served by the platform) was not completely ignored and actually supplemented this understanding to account for the interdependencies between both sides of the market. The functioning of two-sided markets has been discussed in more detail in Chapter 6 on digital markets. Thus, the principle emerges that the starting point for defining a relevant market is to first understand the service provided by the enterprise under scrutiny and then to identify alternate services that are substitutable in terms of price, characteristics and intended use from the perspective of the affected user group. Since market definition provides a tool to draw the boundaries within which to assess the anti-competitive harm alleged, the precise boundaries of markets will change depending on the context in which they are drawn. Therefore, even in two-sided markets, one needs to look at the alleged conduct and the affected party in question to define the relevant market. [3] Relevant Geographic Market Can Only Be India As explained in detail in Chapter 3, the dominant position has been defined under the Competition Act as position of strength enjoyed by an enterprise in the relevant market in India. Based on this technical reading of the provisions, it can be said that the Competition Act only allows that relevant geographic market can only be India or territories of India, and it can be interpreted that the scheme of the Competition Act does not allow for a worldwide definition of the relevant geographic market. In Mr. Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC and Another,160 Mr. Umar Javeed and Others v. Google LLC and Another, Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 219. the CCI noted that even if general search services can be accessed by users anywhere in the world, the main general search services offer localized sites in different countries and in a variety of languages. The majority of users use the general web search in their local language; thus, India was considered the relevant geographic market for general web search because the conditions of competition are distinct and homogenous in India. Similarly, in combination proceedings of M/s Atos Worldline India Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s Verifone India Sales Pvt. Ltd. and Others,161 M/s Atos Worldline India Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s Verifone India Sales Pvt. Ltd. and Others, Case No. 56 of 2012, paras 6.12, 6.13. the CCI only looked at overlaps between the parties in India and the CCI determined the relevant geographical market to be the whole of India because the condition of competition in the market of Point of Sale Terminals throughout India is similar. In the Automobile spare parts case,162 Automobile spare parts case, para. 20.5.26. the CCI has taken the view that the relevant geographical market was pan India because the conditions of competition for the sale of spare parts and after-sale maintenance services are homogeneous for the authorized dealer and the independent repairers across the territory of India. [4] Online Versus Offline Distribution Model In the last decade, there has been massive growth in the online sector, and business enterprises as well as customers are preferring digital platforms over traditional offline markets. The market dynamics and character of the online market are different from those of the traditional offline market. However, earlier, the CCI did not delineate online and offline markets as separate product markets. In Ashish Ahuja v. Snapdeal.com and Another,163 Ashish Ahuja v. Snapdeal.com and Another, Case No. 17 of 2014, para. 16. a seller of electronic products filed an information alleging that Snapdeal.com has abused its dominance by denying the sale of the informant’s electronic product on its online e-commerce platform. The CCI passed an order under section 26(2) of the Competition Act, where it held that both offline and online markets were different in terms of shopping experience and discounts, and if the price in the online market increased, then consumers would shift to the offline markets and vice versa. Therefore, online and offline markets are substitutable; the CCI was of the view that offline and online markets were two different channels of distribution of the same product and were not two different relevant markets. However, market definition, particularly in digital markets, is not a static concept, and the relevant market assessment must be done at each point in time based on the prevailing circumstances. Hence, CCI shifted from the narrow approach of painting online and offline channels with the same brush to thoroughly account for the challenges in digital markets and effectively enforce the Competition Act on online market players by delineating online markets as distinct relevant market. For instance, the CCI shifted its approach while delineating relevant market in All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Pvt. Ltd.164 All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 20 of 2018, paras 21-25. In this case, it was alleged that Flipkart was engaged in deep discounting, denied market access and leveraged its position to enter another market by extending discounts and manufacturing products under private labels. The CCI in its prima facie order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, observed that Flipkart operates as a marketplace-based e-commerce platform, which facilitates trade between consumers and third-party sellers. Online platforms offer convenience for third-party sellers as well as consumers. The online marketplace provides a platform for third-party sellers to interact with a larger number of consumers, and it also benefits consumers by saving them time and commuting charges. Hence, CCI determined the relevant product market as services provided by online marketplace platforms for selling goods. Similarly, the CCI in Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd., 165 Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 40 of 2019, para. 18. where it received information from a union of Micro, Small and Mini Enterprises engaged in the trade of mobile phones and other related accessories called Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh, against two big e-commerce platforms, Flipkart and Amazon, which are alleged to be engaged in vertical agreements under section 3(4) of the Competition Act, under which Flipkart and Amazon are preferring certain sellers. Further, it was also alleged that the e-commerce platforms contravene section 4 of the Competition Act by abusing their dominant position by engaging in predatory pricing and imposing unfair conditions. So, CCI passed a prima facie order, directing the DG to investigate the allegations. In this order, the CCI made a similar observation to the Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd, and by saying that the online platform offers access to new markets to third-party sellers and increases consumers’ choice of goods and services. An online platform ecosystem provides more benefits than a traditional brick-and-mortar set-up in terms of fixed costs and reach. Again, in Lifestyle Equities C.V. v. Amazon Seller Services Pvt.,166 Lifestyle Equities C.V. v. Amazon Seller Services Pvt., Case No. 09 of 2020, para. 23. the CCI passed a dismissal order under section 26(2) of the Competition Act against the allegation against Amazon and Cloudtail. CCI reiterated the distinguishing characteristics of online platforms from offline platforms. CCI further noted that the digital sector is rapidly changing, and the delineation of the online platform cannot have a static approach. Hence, the relevant market should be delineated by considering the market reality and factual circumstances of each case. Similarly, in Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC and Others,167 Case Nos 07 and 30 of 2012, para. 97. the CCI distinguished between online and offline advertising services and found that neither of these markets is comparable. The CCI noted that online advertising is undertaken using the internet as a medium and, hence, it has a large coverage and wider reach. Similarly, online advertising is not substitutable for traditional offline advertisements, such as newspapers, radio or television, as mediums to advertise because traditional offline advertisements target fewer customers. Furthermore, online advertising also allows advertisers to accurately monitor the effectiveness of their advertisement, which is not possible for the advertiser who is using a newspaper or radio. Hence, the online advertisement market was held to be non-substitutable with the offline advertisement market. Similarly, in the MMT-Go case,168 MMT-Go case, para. 198. the CCI held that the online mode of booking service is not substitutable with offline mode of booking service. The CCI observed that end-consumer who is using online modes of payment would always prefer online channels to book hotels. And therefore, such end-consumers are considered distinct from those who prefer using traditional mode of booking by directing walk-ins to the hotels. Thus, CCI considered the online modes of booking services as a different relevant market. From the previous orders of the CCI, it can be concluded that both the seller side and buyer side perceive online platform differently than offline platform. Hence, the CCI has now taken a firm stance that online platform is neither substitutable nor interchangeable with traditional offline platforms. CCI’s decisional practice and the trends in the delineation of online and offline forms of relevant markets have been analysed in more detail in Chapter 6 on digital markets. [5] Aftermarket Versus Unified Systems Market In the Automobile spare parts case169 Automobile spare parts case, para. 20.5.54. (discussed in Chapter 3), the DG concluded that the relevant market would be the aftermarket for spares of automobiles. The reason adopted by the DG was that once a customer purchases a car, he is locked in, and he is forced to buy the spare parts of the same brand since spare parts of one brand are not substitutable with spare parts of other brands. In such an aftermarket, each automobile manufacturer was dominant in respect of its respective spares since a customer who purchases a car cannot substitute spares of a different manufacturer and is locked into the aftermarket suppliers of the car manufacturer. This case best illustrates the concept of the relevant market being an aftermarket of a primary product. Consider the said example: a consumer wishes to buy a car. When he is exploring the options to buy a car, he has several options at each price segment, but once he has purchased the car, the consumer is ‘locked in’ and has to use the spare parts of that particular car manufacturer (for instance, a consumer who has bought a Maruti car cannot use spare parts of Toyota and or avail of the services of an authorized dealer of Hyundai). In such cases, there is a presence of two different markets: one a primary market and another, the aftermarket. While determining whether the relevant market can be an aftermarket of a primary product, it has to be seen whether a consumer can shift the primary product when the prices of the spare parts of the said primary product go up without incurring substantial shifting costs. The CCI observed that consumers cannot change the car (i.e., Maruti to Toyota) if the prices of spare parts of Maruti go up exponentially. Further, due to the technical specifications and the complex engineering used to manufacture each brand of cars by the original equipment manufacturers (‘car companies’ / car manufacturers or ‘OEMs’), the spare parts of one brand are not compatible with that of another brand, and therefore, the products in the secondary market for spare parts are not interchangeable with those of the primary market, and therefore they constitute distinct relevant product markets. Based on the above and actual economic evidence, the CCI came to the conclusion that the relevant market is the aftermarket for the supply of spare parts and technical designs for each car manufacturer separately. It was argued by the car manufacturers that both the ‘sale of primary product’ as well as its aftermarket may be considered one unified systems market. In furtherance to this submission, it was argued that once the customer purchases a car, he is locked in to purchase the spare parts from the same car manufacturer. But this practice would protect the reputation of the car manufacturers as well as benefit the customers as they would be well equipped with adequate information to make the right decisions while purchasing another car or spare parts. Further, the customers could ascertain the total cost that they would likely incur during the life cycle of the car. However, the unified systems market proposed by the car manufacturers was rejected, and CCI held that the car manufacturers were unable to provide any evidence to show the positive impact on consumers due to their lock-in practices. Additionally, the lock-in practices facilitated their ability to increase the cost of their products and exploit the consumers. Based on these parameters, the CCI concluded that manufacturers were unable to demonstrate that they could, or consumers could, bring about a pro-competitive effect and hence, rejected the systems market contention. Finally, the CCI held that the sale of primary products and aftermarket products can’t be considered one unified market. The erstwhile COMPAT170 Automobile spare parts case [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 452, paras 69, 81. also agreed with the CCI’s reasoning in relation to the issue of whether the automobile and auto spare parts market constitutes a unified systems market. The erstwhile COMPAT looked at the practice of car manufacturers and held that car manufacturers failed to show any evidence to substantiate the claim of the positive impact on consumers. Finally, it was held that the choice of an Indian customer is driven by the mileage of the car, budget of the customer, and a broad understanding of the availability of ‘good models’ in the market. And therefore, the erstwhile COMPAT upheld the decision of CCI to separate the relevant markets for the sale of cars and the aftermarket (i.e., sale of spare parts and sale of repair services). While in the Automobile spare parts case, the CCI did not agree with the unified systems market, in later cases, they agreed that a unified systems market may exist. In the case of Trend Electronics v. Hewlett Packard Sales Pvt. Ltd. 171 Trend Electronics v. Hewlett Packard Sales Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 92 of 2015, paras 14, 15. (‘HP case’), it was argued by the informant that there were deficiencies in the after-sales services provided by Hewlett Packard (HP). The Informant has submitted that the laptop is the primary product in the instant case, and the market for repair, maintenance and after-sales services is the secondary market. While the order passed in the HP case is silent, it seems like the informant must have relied on the reasoning laid down by the CCI in the Automobile spare parts case. Having said that, the CCI, in the HP case, did not agree that there is a separate market for aftermarket in case of laptops. It noted that although the informant had submitted relevant market as the secondary market for repair, maintenance and after-sales services, no justification had been provided for considering the secondary market as a separate and distinct relevant market. Further, the CCI noted, based on information available/accessible in the public domain, that a consumer purchasing a laptop could reasonably ascertain the total cost that he is likely to incur during the life cycle of the laptop. Further, factors such as the high rate of obsoletion and low life span of laptops suggested that any unfair business strategy by the laptop manufacturers, in the after-sales services, is likely to affect the sales of the primary product. Further, the CCI noted that there are many options available to resell used laptops, and the customers can switch to substitutable products without incurring substantial switching costs. Based on the same, one can see that CCI will look at the particular sector and analyse the market dynamics to determine if an aftermarket exists in the first place. [6] Lack of Scientific Sampling The entire exercise of determination of relevant market is to identify the products/services which are considered substitutable and also identify the competitive constraints that the parties face. While the exercise is extremely pertinent in an abuse of dominance or a merger control investigation, the orders of the CCI are silent as to whether they conduct or appoint a third party to conduct a proper market survey of the sector in question. Further, the application of certain concepts like the Small but Significant and Non-Transitory Increase in Price (SSNIP) test is not uniform by the CCI. While in some cases, the CCI relies on the SSNIP test to determine the market,172 Belaire Owners’ Association v. DLF Limited, HUDA & Ors., Case No. 19 of 2010. However, at the appellate stage, the erstwhile COMPAT held that there is no need to conduct any SSNIP test. See, DLF Limited and Ors. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors., MANU/TA/0012/2014, para. 84. in others, they do not adopt the said test.173 MCX Stock Exchange Ltd. v. National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. and Another, Case No. 13 of 2009, para. 10.16. The CCI held that applicant of SSNIP test is unnecessary. The erstwhile COMPAT upheld the CCI’s order in the National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Another [2014], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 15 of 2011. The CCI must place more reliance on scientific sampling in its analysis. The CCI and DG should, in some cases, take assistance from independent third parties to conduct analysis in their investigations. [7] Relevant Markets for Section 3(4) of the Competition Act Need Not Be Precisely Delineated There is no need to go into precise relevant market determination for the cases against vertical anti-competitive agreements.174 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd., Case No. 18 of 2021, para. 43. In National Restaurant Association of India (‘NRAI’) v. Zomato Limited (‘Zomato’) & Others,175 National Restaurant Association of India (‘NRAI’) v. Zomato Limited (‘Zomato’) & Others, Case No. 16 of 2021, para. 34. the CCI held that for the contraventions under section 3(4) of the Competition Act, there is no need to precisely determine the relevant market. In this case, while passing an order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, the CCI widely delineated the relevant market of Zomato and Swiggy as ‘online food delivery platforms’ and ‘operate as online intermediaries for food ordering and delivery’. Also, in the case of Alleged anti-competitive conduct by Maruti Suzuki India Limited in implementing discount control policy vis-à-vis dealers, 176 Alleged anti-competitive conduct by Maruti Suzuki India Limited in implementing discount control policy vis-à-vis dealers, Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2019, para. 16. while holding Maruti Suzuki liable for imposing resale price maintenance on its dealers under section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act, the CCI felt that there is no requirement of defining a precise relevant market for the assessment of a vertical anti-competitive agreement. The CCI held that Maruti is a manufacturer dealing in the upstream market with significant market power without precisely delineating the relevant market. [8] No Static Approach to Relevant Market Analysis In the case of Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Association of India v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd,177 Case No. 14 of 2019. the CCI has noted that in rapidly changing markets, market assessment cannot have a static approach and that market definition adopted in a previous combination approval involving the same parties, which happened two years before the assessment in that case, should not hold true as market dynamics change have changed due to the rapid evolution of the digital market. The CCI in that case noted that:Delineation of relevant market is based on market realities as they exist at the time of assessment. In rapidly changing markets in particular, market assessment cannot have a static approach. The Combination case cited by MMT-Go took place over two and a half years back. While this may not be a period sufficiently long for traditional markets to undergo perceptible change, the pace of evolution of digital markets is significantly faster. The intervening period has seen the online travel portals and the customised service that they provide to consumers on account of big data analytics to have established a distinct and significantly more prominent position in the hotel reservation space in India. In view of the increased popularity and use of OTAs by a large segment of consumers in India, hotel operators now perceive them as a distinct mode of distribution which cannot be simply replaced or substituted by other offline modes or direct sale without losing out significantly on consumer reach. Thus, on an overall consideration of the market realities and competition dynamics, the Commission finds it difficult to accept the assertion made by MMT-Go. Similar attempts to rely on the previous case decided by CCI were made to object to the jurisdiction of the CCI to examine their present conduct by Amazon and Flipkart. The Karnataka High Court rejected the contention of Flipkart and noted that in a competitive market, various agreements are executed, new practices are adopted every day, and merely because some other issue has been looked into by the CCI earlier, it does not mean that on the ground of res judicata, the CCI cannot look into any information subsequently against the appellants. The Court further noted that the marketplace is by very nature a constantly evolving and dynamic space, that market forces can evolve in the course of few months, and that by no stretch of imagination, it can be held that enterprises shall be out of bound for all times and no action can be taken against them only because at some point of time the matter has been looked into by the CCI.

§1.16 CONCLUSION In the last fourteen years, the CCI has examined cases relating to anti-competitive agreements (discussed in Chapter 2 of this book), abuse of dominance (discussed in Chapter 3 of this book), and merger control (discussed in Chapter 4 of this book) from diverse sectors of the economy. According to the findings based on evidence, appropriate penalties and directions were imposed on the enterprises that were found to be engaged in anti-competitive practices. Also, India has emerged as the pioneer of the digital revolution all over the world. There is a steady rise in cases emanating from the digital markets (discussed in Chapter 6 of this book). To keep the regulatory vires abreast of the changing market dynamics, the CCI may need to develop its enforcement mechanisms so that market participants in digital markets do not evade the scrutiny of the CCI. Further, for the detection and enforcement of the Competition Act, a multidisciplinary approach needs to be taken by looking at both facts and law. Accordingly, amendments to the Competition Act and its Regulations may be made in order to enhance the enforcement of the principles of competition law.

title: Chapter 2: Anti-competitive Agreements and Competition Law
container_title: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition)

sections.title: LEGAL FRAMEWORK
sections.text: §2.01 LEGAL FRAMEWORK Any agreement between persons or enterprises or association of enterprises or persons or association of persons, in respect of production, supply, distribution, storage, acquisition or control of goods or provisions of services, which causes an AAEC in India, is void under section 3(2) of the Competition Act, 2002 (‘Competition Act’). The Competition Commission of India (CCI) has noted, in the case of Shri Neeraj Malhotra v. Deutsche Post Bank Home Finance,1 Shri Neeraj Malhotra v. Deutsche Post Bank Home Finance, Case No. 5 of 2009, para. 17.5. The erstwhile COMPAT in GlaxoSmithKline Pharmaceuticals Limited & Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 400, para. 51, reiterated the reasoning of the Shri Neeraj Malhotra v. Deutsche Bank to hold that no case for collusive bidding was made out. that under the provisions of section 3, in an analysis of ‘agreement’, an agreement with an end consumer is not included. It has been observed that an end consumer is not involved in any activities related to the supply side of the market and the word ‘agreement’ in section 3(1) refers to any agreement in respect of activities which are on the supply side. Thus, an ‘agreement’ for the purpose of section 3 analysis cannot be entered between a supplier and a consumer as consumer can never be involved in supply side activities. For the purposes of section 3, first, the existence of an ‘agreement’ must be determined. The term ‘agreement’ normally refers to joint action or understanding by two or more parties and not unilateral action of one party.2 Section 2(b) of the Competition Act, 2002. As such, the term ‘agreement’ is construed in a very wide manner and includes any agreement or understanding or action in concert. The erstwhile COMPAT in GlaxoSmithKline Pharmaceuticals Limited & Anr. v. Competition Commission of India 3 GlaxoSmithKline Pharmaceuticals Limited & Anr. v. Competition Commission of India[2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 400, para. 48. observed that the definition of ‘agreement’ in section 2(b) of the Act is inclusive in nature. An ‘agreement’ includes any arrangement or understanding or action in concert, whether such arrangement, understanding or action is formal or in writing or is intended to be enforceable by legal proceedings. After the determination of an agreement, the effect of the agreement will have to be determined to see whether such an agreement causes, or is likely to cause, any AAEC in India. The AAEC analysis must be determined based on the parameters under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. It is the onus of the CCI to prove that an agreement causes or is likely to cause an AAEC in India. However, there are certain kinds of agreements which are mentioned under section 3(3) of the Act (i.e., cartel arrangements) which are presumed to have an AAEC in India. In such cases, the onus shifts on the parties to such an agreement to prove that the agreement will not cause or likely cause an AAEC in India based on parameters laid down in section 19(3) of the Competition Act. Thus, there are certain categories of agreements which are mentioned under section 3(3) of the Act on presumptive rule approach, meaning that once the essential ingredients of an agreement are established, there is no further need to launch into an elaborate enquiry to find out the impact of such conduct on the market and that AAEC is presumed. In that situation, the burden shifts to the contravening parties to rebut the presumption by showing that their conduct does not result in AAEC in India.

sections.title: LEGAL FRAMEWORK OF CARTELS IN INDIA
sections.text: §2.02 LEGAL FRAMEWORK OF CARTELS IN INDIA Section 2(c) of the Competition Act defines a ‘cartel’ to include: an association of producers, sellers, distributors, traders or service providers who, by agreement amongst themselves, limit, control or attempt to control the production, distribution, sale or price of, or, trade in goods or provision of services. As per the definition, the scope and ambit of the term ‘cartel’ is inclusive.4 GlaxoSmithKline Pharmaceuticals Limited & Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 400, para. 48. In the erstwhile regime, the term ‘cartel’ was not defined in the Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969. As such, through judicial pronouncement, the cartel was recognized as an agreement relating to restrictive trade practice. In Union of India v. Hindustan Development Corporation, 5 Union of India v. Hindustan Development Corpn. [1993], (SC) (1993) 3 SCC 499, para. 14. the Supreme Court relied on dictionaries and American jurisprudence to interpret the word ‘cartel’ as:…The cartel therefore is an association of producers who by agreement among themselves attempt to control production, sale and prices of the product to obtain a monopoly in any particular industry or commodity. Analysing the object of formation of a cartel in other words, it amounts to an unfair trade practice which is not in the public interest. The intention to acquire monopoly power can be spelt out from formation of such a cartel by some of the producers. However, the determination whether such agreement unreasonably restrains the trade depends on the nature of the agreement and on the surrounding circumstances that give rise to an inference that the parties intended to restrain the trade and monopolise the same… It is apposite to note that the enforcement regime of the present competition law regime in India with respect to cartel enforcement is well established. Empirically, most of the decided cases on cartels deal with bid rigging in the government procurement sector. Generally, the cartels are formed in secrecy to hamper free and fair competition by controlling or rigging the prices, products and customer/territorial allocation. Cartel members continue this concerted practice in secrecy to avoid the attention of the CCI. Thus, cartels are hard to trace and bust. So, to discourage cartelization, the statutory scheme under the Competition Act provides for a presumption wherein any agreement entered into between enterprises or associations of enterprises or persons or associations of persons or between any person and enterprise or practice carried on, or decision taken by, any association of enterprises or association of persons, including cartels, engaged in identical or similar trade of goods or provision of services, which: (a) directly or indirectly determines purchase or sale prices; (b) limits or controls production, supply, markets, technical development, investment or provision of services; (c) shares the market or source of production or provision of services by way of allocation of the geographical area of the market, or type of goods or services, or number of customers in the market or any other similar way; (d) directly or indirectly results in bid rigging or collusive bidding, shall be presumed to have an AAEC in India. Unlike in the case of dominance investigation, the provisions with respect to anti-competitive agreement are applicable to even an association of persons (like a trade association) and are not only limited to the unilateral conduct of an enterprise. Further, the ambit of section 3(3) also applies to a decision taken by an association of persons. The CCI has used the expanded ambit of section 3(3) of the Competition Act to include even decisions taken in a trade association meeting since a trade association is nothing but an association of enterprises or an association of persons.

sections.title: EXISTENCE OF AN AGREEMENT
sections.text: §2.03 EXISTENCE OF AN AGREEMENT It must be noted that to invoke the provisions of section 3 of the Competition Act, the existence of an ‘agreement’ is sine qua non. Such ‘agreement’ covered under section 3 of the Competition Act must be related to an economic activity, regardless of whether the activity intends to earn profit. Competition law frowns upon the activities of those enterprises or persons who, while undertaking their economic activities, indulge in practices which effect the competition adversely.6 Competition Commission of India v. Coordination Committee Artistes & Technicians of W.B. Film & Television [2017], (SC) (2017) 5 SCC 17, paras 44, 45. The term ‘agreement’ has been defined in section 2(b) of the Competition Act. Section 2(b) of the Competition Act reads as: ‘agreement’ includes any arrangement or understanding or action in concert –(i) whether or not, such arrangement, understanding or action is formal or in writing; or (ii) whether or not such arrangement, understanding or action is intended to be enforceable by legal proceedings. Based on the definition, we see that agreement has been defined in an extremely broad manner under the Competition Act to include any agreement, whether oral or written. The Supreme Court in CCI v. Coordination Committee Artistes & Technicians of W.B. Film & Television (‘Coordination Committee case’) 7 Ibid. not only held that the definition of ‘agreement’ is inclusive and that the ambit of the agreement also includes any arrangement or understanding or even concerted practice. The CCI has, in fact, noted that the understanding between the competitors might be tacit, and the definition of the agreement includes situations where the parties act on the basis of a nod or a wink. It has been observed from the decisional practice of the CCI that there is rarely direct evidence of action in concert between the market participants. In the absence of such direct evidence, the CCI must determine, based on circumstantial evidence, whether the market participants are involved in any dealings and have some form of understanding which points to the fact as to whether they are acting in coordination with each other. It must be noted herein that section 36(2), read with section 41(2) of the Competition Act, confers on the CCI and DG all the powers of a Civil Court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 which include summoning of a person and examining him on oath, requiring the discovery and production of documents, receiving evidence on affidavit, issuing commissions for the examination of witnesses or documents. In spite of being provided with vast sweeping powers of investigation, at times, it is very difficult to obtain direct evidence with respect to the existence of a cartel since cartels traditionally are always hatched in secrecy. In such a situation, the DG and CCI rely on circumstantial evidence. Circumstantial evidence includes evidence of communications among suspected cartel operators and economic evidence concerning the market and the conduct of those participating in it which suggests the existence of consensus ad idem among market participants as opposed to independent decision-making. Examples of circumstantial evidence can be receipts, visitor entry registers, credit card bills, travel details, email dumps, telephone/mobile bills, etc., which show that the market participants had interacted with each other on a regular basis to show some concerted action. It would be seen that most of the analysis done by the DG and the CCI, while deciding cartel cases, is to analyse from direct communication and economic evidence whether there is an agreement between the market participants or whether the market participants were acting independently. The task of the DG and CCI becomes all the more onerous because most of the cartel investigations happen in an oligopolistic industry where parallel behaviour is very common. The CCI must, therefore, differentiate between a case of an anti-competitive agreement among market participants and conscious parallelism among market participants. In this regard, the CCI has noted that the evidence of agreement has to be tested on the benchmark of ‘preponderance of probabilities’.8 In Re: Alleged cartelization by steel producers, RTPE 09 of 2008, para. 111. In this regard, as observed by CCI in the case of Alleged cartelization by steel producers, 9 Ibid., para. 112. an order holding that there is no violation of section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act:Since the prohibition on participating in anti-competitive agreements and the penalties which offenders may incur are well known, it is normal for the activities which those practices and those agreements entail to take place in a clandestine fashion, for meetings to be held in secret and for the associated documentation to be reduced to a minimum. Considering the remote possibility of getting direct evidence in the case of a cartel in many cases, the existence of an anti-competitive practice or agreement can also be inferred from the conduct of the colluding parties which may include a number of coincidences and indicia which, taken together, may, in the absence of any other plausible explanation, constitute evidence of the existence of an agreement. Thus, in case of agreements as listed in Section 3(3) of the Act, once it is established that such an agreement exists, it will be presumed that the agreement has an appreciable adverse effect on competition; the onus to rebut this presumption would lie upon the opposite party. As mentioned above, agreement has been very widely defined in section 2(b) of the Competition Act. From the definition of agreement provided in the Competition Act, it can be seen that an agreement need not be in writing, nor necessarily has to be legally enforceable.10 In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India, Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2017, para. 86. The CCI held that implementation of anti-competitive agreement under section 3(3) of the Competition Act or actual causing of AAEC is not a sine qua non for establishing contravention. An arrangement or understanding is as good as a formal written agreement. The legislative intent is quite clear, and the definition encompasses both overt and tacit forms of agreements, and it is not required that it has to be an agreement as is understood in common parlance under the principles of contract law. Based on the wide definition provided under the Competition Act, the CCI has, in various decisions, noted that it is not the requirement under law to demand direct evidence of agreement in all cases and in the absence of direct evidence, existence of circumstantial evidence that tends to exclude the possibility of independent action would be sufficient to give rise to an inference of an agreement. In this regard, it is apposite to point out the observations of the CCI in the Builders Association of India v. Cement Manufactures Association and Ors. 11 Builders Association of India v. Cement Manufactures Association and Ors., Case No. 29 of 2010, para. 183. (‘Cement cartel case’) that market participants often hatch anti-competitive conspiracies in secrecy. As the CCI would hardly find any direct evidence of the anti-competitive conduct, it has to look at the circumstantial or indirect evidence to establish the anti-competitive conduct. The word ‘agreement’ under the Competition Act has been given an inclusive definition, which is not restricted to written agreements, because market participants do not reveal their anti-competitive conduct to avoid being punished under the Competition Act. Hence, the CCI will consider all the circumstantial evidence before it concludes whether there is an anti-competitive agreement among the market participants. The definition of agreement under the Competition Act also uses the term ‘action in concert’. The erstwhile COMPAT in Indian Jute Mills Association, Royal Exchange and Others v. CCI and Others (‘Jute Mill case’)12 Indian Jute Mills Association, Royal Exchange and Others v. CCI and Others [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 144, para. 96. relied on the interpretation of similar terms from other laws, such as ‘person acting in concert’ in securities law. The erstwhile COMPAT cited Daichi Sankyo Company Limited v. Jayaram Chigurupati, where the phrase ‘persons acting in concert’ used in Regulation 2(1)(e)(1) of SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 1997, has been interpreted by the Supreme Court as a shared common objective between two or persons. There may be a direct or indirect agreement between such persons to share the common objective or purpose of acquisition or substantial acquisition of shares, etc., of the target company. Thus, the element of the shared common objective or purpose is the sine qua non for the relationship of persons acting in concert:…There can be no ‘persons acting in concert’ unless there is a shared common objective or purpose between two or more persons of substantial acquisition of shares, etc. of the target company. For, dehors the element of the shared common objective or purpose the idea of ‘person acting in concert’ is as meaningless as a criminal conspiracy without any agreement to commit a criminal offence. The idea of ‘persons acting in concert’ is not about a fortuitous relationship coming into existence by accident or chance. The relationship can come into being only by design, by meeting of minds between two or more persons leading to the shared common objective or purpose of acquisition or substantial acquisition of shares, etc. of the target company. It is another matter that the common objective or purpose may be in pursuance of an agreement or an understanding, formal or informal; the acquisition of shares, etc. may be direct or indirect or the persons acting in concert may cooperate in actual acquisition of shares, etc. or they may agree to cooperate in such acquisition. Nonetheless, the element of the shared common objective or purpose is the sine qua non for the relationship of ‘persons acting in concert’ to come into being. It has been seen that there are situations where competitors come together, either under the aegis of a trade association or otherwise, to carry out legitimate business activities, like industry lobbying, etc. The degree of cooperation among competitors can also be for the purposes of carrying out research and development activities. Thus, such agreements can be a means to share risk, save costs, increase investments, pool know-how, enhance product quality and facilitate invention. These agreements, therefore, can have pro-competitive benefits which are highly beneficial to the competitive structure of the market. Having said that, there can be situations wherein competitors enter into an agreement to maximize their own profits to the detriment of the consumers. It must be observed that the operation of agreements can vary from cartels to conscious parallelism. It has been seen, from the decisional practice of the CCI, that the operation of cartels tends to be very complex, and it varies according to the number of participants involved and the nature of the market in question. The decisional practice (discussed supra) would show the way the CCI has relied on communication and economic evidence to identify whether the market participants have indulged in a cartel or not. Such analysis helps the CCI to conclude whether the parties have acted in furtherance of an anti-competitive agreement or unilaterally. It is the precise reason why concerted practice is caught under the provisions of the Competition Act. It is because, by its very nature, concerted practice does not have all the elements of a binding agreement but arises out of the behaviour of the parties concerned. The necessity to include any such arrangement or understanding within the ambit of an agreement caught under the prohibition of antitrust laws has been aptly described by Lord Denning in the case of PRTA v. W.H.Smith and Sons Ltd., as quoted by the CCI in the In Re: suo-motu case against LPG cylinder manufacturers (‘LPG-I case’),13 In Re: suo-motu case against LPG cylinder manufacturers, Suo-Moto Case No. 03 of 2011, para. 14.11. namely: People who combine together to keep up prices do not shout it from the housetops. They keep it quiet. They make their own arrangements in the cellar where no one can see. They will not put anything into writing nor even into words. A nod or wink will do. Parliament as well is aware of this. So it included not only an ‘agreement’ properly so called, but any ‘arrangement’, however informal. Further, as mentioned above, it is apposite to note that under the statutory scheme of the Competition Act, cartels are presumed to have an AAEC in India. With respect to the presumption stated, it has been mentioned by the Supreme Court of India that ‘shall be presumed’ is a presumption and not evidence in itself. In Excel Crop Care Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India (‘Excel Crop case’),14 Excel Crop Care Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2017], (SC) (2017) 8 SCC 47, paras 41.2, 49. the Supreme Court held that the kinds of agreements mentioned in section 3(3) of the Competition Act would be treated as ipso facto causing AAEC. If the CCI finds that the parties entered into an agreement covered under the categories of section 3(3) of the Competition Act, then the CCI need not undertake any further enquiry. Section 3(3) of the Competition Act shifts the onus on the parties entering into an agreement for one of the categories mentioned in section 3(3)(a) to section 3(3)(d) above to show, based on the indices mentioned under section 19(3) of the Competition Act, that such agreement does not cause an AAEC in India. The burden would shift upon such enterprises or persons to rebut the said presumption by providing adequate evidence. Similarly, in Uniglobe Mod Travels (P) Ltd v. Travel Agents Federation of India,15 Uniglobe Mod Travels (P) Ltd v. Travel Agents Federation of India, Case No. 03 of 2009, para. 68.2.1. while holding that the trade association had violated section 3(3) by collectively boycotting Singapore Airlines, the CCI explained the presumptive nature of section 3(3) of the Competition Act. The CCI said that once the existence of a horizontal anti-competitive agreement is established, the burden of proof shifts upon the parties who had entered into such agreement to prove that there is no anti-competitive effect from the said agreement. Further, the Competition Act provides for an exception to the per se rule provided under section 3(3) of the Competition Act. The stated exception provides that the provision of section 3(3) shall not be applicable to an agreement entered into by way of joint venture arrangements which increase efficiency in terms of production, supply, distribution, storage, acquisition or control of goods or services.

sections.title: STANDARD OF PROOF
sections.text: §2.04 STANDARD OF PROOF In order to establish the existence of a cartel, it is important to prove the existence of an agreement between market participants. As has been mentioned above, it has been observed from the decisional practice of the CCI that there is rarely direct evidence of action in concert between the market participants. In this regard, the CCI in the case of GAIL (India) Limited v. PMP Infratech Private Ltd. and Another 16 GAIL (India) Limited v. PMP Infratech Private Ltd. and Another, Case No. 41 of 2019, para. 3; In Re: Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry, Suo Motu Case No. 05 of 2016, para. 153; Mr. Rizwanul Haq Khan v. Mersen (India) Pvt. Ltd. and Another, Reference Case No. 02 of 2016, para. 44. observed that the standard of proof required to prove an anti-competitive agreement would be based on ‘preponderance of probabilities’ and not ‘beyond reasonable doubt’. Thus, in the absence of such direct evidence, the CCI has to determine, based on circumstantial evidence, whether the market participants are involved in any dealings and have some form of understanding which points to the fact as to whether they are acting in coordination with each other instead of acting unilaterally. It would be seen that most of the analysis done by the DG and the CCI, while deciding cartel cases, is to analyse from communication and economic evidence whether there is an agreement between the market participants or not. While the competition authority was going through the transaction of Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969, regime to the present competition law regime, the DG investigated major steel manufacturers for colluding to raise the prices of steel products In Re: Alleged cartelization by steel producers.17 In Re: Alleged cartelization by steel producers, RTPE 09 of 2008, paras 90, 101, 120, 121, 123. From the investigation report, it was concluded that the steel manufacturers coordinated to raise their prices, and they also limited and controlled the production of steel products. However, the CCI made a note of flawed investigation since the CCI noted that multiple factors were the reason behind the price pattern moving in tandem, such as increase in landed import price, cost of production and the demand and supply analysis. Without any direct evidence to indicate the collusion, an anti-competitive agreement cannot be assumed. So, the standard of proof to establish the violation of section 3(3) required additional evidence or what is commonly referred to as ‘plus factor’, was not met in this case. A wide range of circumstantial evidence can be used to establish the additional evidence or plus factors. It is imperative to note that conscious parallelism must be differentiated from cartel behaviour. Though parallel pricing or other parallel behaviour amounts to strong evidence, it does not justify the presence of cartel or anti-competitive agreement.18 Excel Corp case, para. 52. This means the CCI cannot shift the burden of proof by simply establishing parallel behaviour. In A.R. Polymers Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission of India,19 A.R. Polymers Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 240, paras 23, 35, 36, 39, 43. the erstwhile COMPAT set aside the order of CCI where the opposite parties were held guilty for violating section 3(3)(a) and (d) of the Act by forming a bid rigging cartel to quote identical bids for the supply of Jungle Boots. The erstwhile COMPAT heavily relied on Union of India v. Hindustan Development Corporation because of the similarity in factual matrix to hold that mere quoting of identical tender offers by opposite parties does not mean a cartel has been formed. In that case, three manufacturers of steel bogies quoted identical prices to a tender notice issued by the Railway Board for the supply of cast steel bogies. The Tender Committee considered that the three manufacturers colluded and quoted a higher price. Thereafter, a counteroffer was given to the three manufacturers where the quotation was reduced. When the matter finally reached the Supreme Court, it was held that there was no cartel because ‘a mere quotation of identical price and an offer of further reduction by themselves would not entitle them automatically to comer the entire market by way of monopoly since the final allotment of quantities vested in the authorities who in their discretion can distribute the same to all the manufacturers including these three big manufacturers on certain basis’. Further, in the absence of a plus factor, the quoting of identical bids is not enough to infer as bid rigging under section 3(3)(d) of the Act. This, thus, makes it clear that parallel behaviour neither shifts the burden of proof nor conclusively establishes a contravention under section 3(3) of the Competition Act. On similar lines, in the case of Narendra Explosive v. Competition Commission of India,20 Narendra Explosive v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 422, paras 45, 47, 48, 52. The erstwhile COMPAT in Deputy Chief Materials Manager, Rail Coach Factory, Kapurthala, Punjab v. Faiveley Transport India Ltd.[2016], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 10 of 2016 set aside the order of CCI by holding that mere identity of price is not enough for holding that the opposite parties had indulged in bid rigging. the erstwhile COMPAT ruled that CCI had erred in presuming the similar bid quotation of the opposite parties and they had been fixing the price of the CN Containers with disc. The Appellate Tribunal noted that DG did not collect any circumstantial evidence to conclude that the opposite parties communicated before submitting their bids or any consensus on the pricing of the product. The erstwhile COMPAT found the reasoning behind the similar quotation to be plausible because the concerned product was limited to three ordinance factories and was required to be manufactured as per specifications. The opposite parties’ quotations were based on the last purchase price which was available in the public domain and acted as a guiding factor for the opposite parties. The quotations of the last purchase were available on the website of the ordinance factories; thus, the justification advanced by the opposite parties that the information available in the public domain behind the similar quotation or parallel pricing was considered plausible by the Tribunal. Thus, the Courts have consistently held that mere parallel pricing will not suffice; one needs to demonstrate plus factors to conclusively demonstrate cartelization. Thus, if the parties under investigation can show business reasons for such parallel behaviour, they may not be held guilty of cartelization. In Re: All India Tyre Dealers Federation v. Tyre Manufactures Association (‘Tyre case’),21 In Re: All India Tyre Dealers Federation v. Tyre Manufactures Association, MRTP Case: RTPE No. 20 of 2008, paras 86, 102. the case was initiated based on a complaint made by All India Tyre Dealers Association. Based on the complaint, the CCI undertook a detailed price-cost comparison of the tyre manufacturers to analyse whether the tyre companies have engaged in a cartel. Before analysing the communication and the economic evidence, the CCI noted that the industry is an oligopolistic market; it is extremely likely that each market participant is aware of the other participant’s behaviour. In an oligopolistic market, interdependence between firms is an important characteristic of such a market which would mean that each firm in such a market considers the likely reactions of other firms while making independent decisions, particularly as regards prices and output. Though oligopolistic markets can lead to competitive outcomes, the outcomes may not always be market-driven but rather a result of concerted effort or collusion. Therefore, the parallel behaviour of competitors can be a result of intelligent market adaptation in an oligopolistic market or a case of anti-competitive agreement. So, the key factor of analysis to be done is whether there is an agreement between market participants to engage in cartelization or not. Even the decisional practice of the CCI has recognized the aspect of parallel behaviour among enterprises, especially in an oligopolistic market. What the DG and CCI look for, in a cartel investigation, is whether there are any ‘plus factors’ which prove that there is an agreement among the market participants. Unlike contract law, the definition of ‘agreement’ under the competition law is much wider and includes both overt and tacit forms. In the LPG-I case,22 Rajasthan Cylinders and Containers Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Anr.[2020], (SC) (2020) 16 SCC 615, paras 89-94, 105. an investigation was conducted by the CCI to analyse whether the manufactures of LPG cylinders had colluded and manipulated their bids for the supply of LPG cylinders to the Indian Oil Corporation Ltd. (IOCL). While conducting the said investigation, the CCI analysed the market in great detail and noted that the industry has structural characteristics which support cartelization. The DG noted that even though bid rigging can occur in any sector, there are some sectors in which it is more likely to occur due to particular features of the industry or of the product involved. The CCI held that there was a violation of section 3(3)(d) read with section 3(1) of the Competition Act. Post the decision of the CCI, the manufacturers preferred to go on appeal to the erstwhile COMPAT. On the basis of the facts mentioned above, the erstwhile COMPAT agreed with the observations made by the CCI and rejected the appeals made by the manufacturers. However, the said judgment of both the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT was set aside by the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court noted that CCI and the erstwhile COMPAT erred in not considering the ‘plus factors’ by analysing the ground realities of the market. The way the tenders are floated indicated that IOCL was deciding the rate of the award and was in complete control over the tender process. The IOCL, through negotiation, set the final price which was less than the price quoted by the L-1 bidders. Furthermore, L-2 and L-3 bidders were also awarded the tender at the rate of L-1. The only difference was that an L-1 bidder received the order for larger quantities than L-2 and L-3. Similarly, an L-2 bidder received larger orders than an L-3. IOCL ensured that all the manufacturers got some orders. Furthermore, in the LPG-I case, the CCI and the erstwhile COMPAT failed to consider the reasonable justification for the ‘plus factors’ by ignoring the fact that a few days before IOCL floated its tender, Bharat Petroleum Corporation Limited (BPCL) floated a tender for LPG cylinders. The BPCL tender acted as a guiding factor for the manufacturers while quoting their bids for the IOCL tender. The market conditions were not taken into account considering the fact that there were a small number of suppliers present in the market. The manufacturers quoted an identical price so that they could get some orders, or else they would be driven out of the market; hence, the argument that the manufacturers quoted an identical price despite varying costs loses its significance. Also, only nineteen manufacturers attended the meeting of the association. Also, there are twelve new entrants in the market, which is a significant number. Moreover, the market condition is described as an oligopsony where only a few buyers are present in the market, and they are controlling the market. In an oligopsony market, the Supreme Court noted that parallel pricing could not be concluded as a concerted practice, and further corroborative evidence is required to substantiate the presence of a cartel. Setting aside the judgments of the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT, the Supreme Court held that IOCL was controlling the tender process. The Supreme Court noted that the CCI failed to carry out further analysis of the factors under section 19(3) of the Competition Act to hold that there was an adverse effect on the market. Finally, the CCI should have summoned IOCL before concluding that there was a violation of section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act. Thus, by way of this case, the Supreme Court, while acknowledging the ill effects of cartelization, has created a defence for participants to the agreement to show that their arrangement does not cause AAEC in the market. As mentioned in Chapter 1 of this book, the penalties on the cartel participants are huge under the Competition Act.
