Owing to such onerous fines, it will be very rare that one will find direct evidence, like minutes of meetings, etc., to conclude the existence of a cartel. It has to be seen whether a cartel exists or not from the totality of the circumstances. In the Express Industry Council of India v. Jet Airways, Indigo, SpiceJet 23 Express Industry Council of India v. Jet Airways, Indigo, SpiceJet, Case No. 30 of 2013, paras 88, 93, 94, 106, 112, 113, 114. (‘Airline surcharge cartel case’), the allegation was that certain domestic airlines introduced a Fuel Surcharge (FSC) for transporting cargo. This surcharge was fixed at a uniform rate of INR 5/kg. It was the case of the informant that, although the levy of FSC was ostensibly introduced as being an extra charge linked to fuel prices, investigation showed that when such prices were reduced (as in the past), there had been no corresponding decrease in FSC. It was further stated that FSC has actually been increased by the airlines by acting in concert, and that too, by almost the same rate and from almost the same date. Likewise, FSC was uniformly increased in the past, even without a corresponding increase in fuel prices. Further, it was also averred that when fuel prices declined substantially, the airlines had, in concert, uniformly increased FSC. The CCI held that the market participants acted in a concerted manner in fixing the FSC rate. Regarding the definition of ‘agreement’, the CCI observed that the definition is inclusive and not exhaustive. As there is rarely any direct evidence, the CCI considers the situations as ‘agreement’ where the market participants are instructed based on nods or winks. Generally, the market participants engage in anti-competitive conduct in a clandestine manner, with secret meetings and hardly any explicit written documents exist. So, by taking together all the coincidences and indicia, the CCI had to reconstruct certain details by using the deduction method. In this case, the CCI unfolded the entire conspiracy by looking at the airlines’ conduct, such as the issuance of circulars to increase FSC at a fixed price by different airlines. Further, CCI also noted that airlines had common agents through whom they were exchanging information. After a holistic appreciation of the evidence, the CCI could see that the airlines violated section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act by entering into an anti-competitive agreement to fix the FSC. Further, the statutory scheme provided under the Competition Act provides that the CCI has been conferred power to impose only civil fines, and it does not have criminal jurisdiction. Further, as described in the preceding chapter, under section 36 of the Competition Act, the CCI is vested with some powers of a Civil Court in the conduct of its enquiries. Therefore, based on the statutory scheme, the offences provided to in the Competition Act, including cartelization, not being criminal offences, the test of proof will be only ‘balance’ of probability’ and ‘liaison of intention’ which can be established with the support of indirect or circumstantial evidence. This is in contradiction to a criminal offence where the offence has to be proved beyond reasonable doubt. In this regard, while upholding an order passed under section 27 of the Competition Act against a bid rigging cartel, the erstwhile COMPAT noted, in the case of MDD Medical Systems India Pvt. Ltd. v. Foundation for Common Cause and Others 24 MDD Medical Systems India Pvt. Ltd. v. Foundation for Common Cause and Others [2013], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 93 Of 2012, para. 18. and held that even though section 27 of the Competition Act speaks about penalty, the process against anti-competitive conduct under competition law is not similar to criminal offence where proof beyond reasonable doubt is required. Hence, in competition law jurisprudence, the test of probability would be a good test. Based on the above, it can be seen that the CCI and DG can rely on both direct and circumstantial evidence. The key test in a cartel investigation, as had been laid down by the CCI, is analysis of the evidence which establishes that a particular set of acts and conduct of the market participants cannot be explained ‘but for’ some sort of anti-competitive agreement and action in concert among them. Consequently, the existence of an anti-competitive agreement can be inferred from the intention or conduct of the parties and may be established by circumstantial evidence alone in the absence of any alternate explanation. In analysing whether there was a concerted action among the market participants, the CCI undertakes a detailed econometric analysis. In the backdrop of the legal position as delineated above, it becomes quite clear that in order to find contravention of section 3 of the Competition Act, it is not the requirement of law to demand direct evidence of agreement in all cases. In the absence of direct evidence, existence of circumstantial evidence that tends to exclude the possibility of independent action would be sufficient to give rise to an inference of existence of an agreement.

sections.title: DIRECT EVIDENCE
sections.text: §2.05 DIRECT EVIDENCE In a cartel case, it is very difficult to find direct evidence wherein there is a clearly formulated written agreement wherein the competitors have decided to engage in cartel behaviour. The Supreme Court in the LPG-I case25 LPG-I case, para. 82. reproduced excerpts of the ‘Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Policy Roundtables Prosecuting Cartels without Direct Evidence 2006’ to discuss the nature of evidence. The Supreme Court noted that direct evidence, such as documents or emails or any kind of communication describing the working of cartel, can rarely be found. However, in some of the cases, direct evidence is unearthed wherein members of a bid rigging cartel share their rate of the bid via email or a minute of the meeting which shows that the trade association in its meeting mandated the members to limit their production or dispatch to create an artificial demand. As mentioned above, most of the cartel activities are hatched in secrecy; hence, direct evidence is very difficult to find. Although direct evidence is very hard to find, there have been instances of direct evidence which have been unearthed by the DG and CCI. For example, in cases relating to the entertainment industry, there have been instances wherein there have been minutes of the meeting of the trade association to show that the members decided to collectively boycott a non-member. In M/s International Subscription Agency v. Federation of Publishers’ and Booksellers’ Associations in India (FPBAI),26 M/s International Subscription Agency v. Federation of Publishers’ and Booksellers’ Associations in India (FPBAI), Case No. 33 of 2019, paras 18, 22. the discount policy of the FPBAI explicitly stated that ‘any of our associated companies shall not offer any discount on the publishers’ price fixed for customers in India’. Further, FPBAI issued notice to its members for non-compliance with its discount policy. Based on this discount policy and the coercive action for non-compliance, the CCI held FPBAI for violating section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act. Even in In Re: Cartelization by public sector insurance companies in rigging the bids submitted in response to the tenders floated by the Government of Kerala for selecting insurance service provider for Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojna. v. National Insurance Co. Ltd. and Others,27 In Re: Cartelization by public sector insurance companies in rigging the bids submitted in response to the tenders floated by the Government of Kerala for selecting insurance service provider for Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojna. v. National Insurance Co. Ltd. and Others, Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2014, para. 32. (‘Insurance cartel case’), the case was initiated based on anonymous information which complained that four public sector insurance companies, namely National Insurance Co. Ltd., New India Assurance Co. Ltd., Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. and United India Insurance Co. Ltd., had rigged the tender floated by the Government of Kerala for selecting an insurance service provider for implementation of the Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojna (RSBY) for the year 2010-2011. The information alleged that the insurance companies had formed a cartel and quoted higher premium rates in response to the tender. Further, by way of the ‘Inter Company Coordination Committee’ (ICCC) meeting attended by the officials of insurance companies, they had decided on an anti-competitive business sharing model to share the business. In the Insurance cartel case,28 Ibid., paras 32, 33, 38, 39, 60. the CCI relied on the investigation by the DG to note that the insurance companies had held a meeting under the aegis of the trade association with the sole agenda to discuss sharing of business and submission of quotation of a bid. The minutes of the meetings were considered direct evidence because they revealed that the decision to collude in terms of sharing of business and submission of quotation was taken by the insurance companies. Having said that, since the fines on cartels are huge, it will be very difficult in future cases once the companies are aware of the principles of competition law to find direct evidence of cartels. The table below shows the decisional trends of the CCI in terms of analysis of direct evidence across multiple decisional cases. S. No. Case Name (Citation) Evidence (Paragraph No.) Reasoning (Paragraph No.) 1. In Re: Alleged Cartelisation in Supply of the LPG Cylinders Procured through Tenders by Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. (HPCL). Suo Motu Case No. 1 of 2014 Direct evidence, such as emails were exchanged between bidders regarding price, business strategies, etc. (Paragraph 77) The CCI held that the bidders discussed and shared sensitive and confidential information regarding the tender to manipulate the bidding process of tender no. 2. (Paragraphs 107, 111-113) In this case, the CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 2. Mr. Sajjan Khaitan v. Eastern India Motion Picture Association & Others. Case No. 16 of 2011. Coordination Committee passed a resolution and issued letters to the TV channels to stop the telecast of the dubbed version of the Mahabharata serial or face non-cooperation. (Paragraphs 5.4, 5.5, 7.1.4, 7.1.5) On 07 April 2011, Coordination Committee further threatened by demonstrating a strike against the TV Channels. (Paragraph 7.1.6) The CCI held that the Coordination Committee imposed restrictions on the two TV Channels to prevent the telecast of a dubbed version of the serial ‘Mahabharata’. (Paragraph 7.1.12) 3. Cement Cartel Case. Case No. 29 of 2010. Collection and exchange of retail prices of cement from different companies. (Paragraphs 212, 213) The Rules and Regulations of the Cement Manufacturers Association show that it was used as a platform to share information among the cement manufacturers. (Paragraph 214) In this case, the CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 4. In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India. Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2017. The beer manufacturers exchanged commercial information regarding MRP and Basic Prices through email and WhatsApp (Paragraphs 11(1)(iv) and (v), 18, 19, 20) The information regarding Beer production, revenue details, targets, stock sold and stock held was exchanged to monitor each other’s actual stock movement, and they can keep track of each other’s market share. (Paragraphs 74, 78) The beer manufacturers agreed to share their periodical sales and sales data. (Paragraphs 56, 72, 73) The beer manufacturers decided to stop producing and restricted the supply of Beer. (Paragraphs 11(1)(vi), 67) The beer manufacturers exchanged emails regarding the purchase of second-hand bottles. (Paragraphs 11(1)(viii), 151) The beer manufacturers were able to hold discussions under the umbrella of the trade association. (Paragraphs 11(1)(ix), 163, 164) Beer manufacturer decided to lower their production of beer to put pressure on the Excise Authorities to lower the Excise duty on Beer. Limiting supplies impacted the beer market because the price of beer increased. (Paragraphs 69, 70, 71) CCI found that the beer manufacturers shared information to decide the rate at which they would buy second-hand bottles. This resulted in limiting the supply of second-hand bottles. (Paragraphs 152, 153, 157) The CCI found that office bearers of beer manufacturers were holding discussions relating to anti-competitive practice under the aegis of the trade association. The trade association also arranged conference calls and facilitated discussions. (Paragraphs 164, 170). In this case, the CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 5. Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others. Reference Case No. 02 of 2020. Physical meeting between cartel members in Hyderabad and Bombay to fix the quotes of the tenders. (Paragraph 80(i)) The cartel members exchanged commercial data either through physical meetings or virtual communication. Before submitting quotes, the cartel members were regularly communicating with each other via calls, messages, or mails. (Paragraph 100) Exchange of commercial data, along with identical pricing or providing cover bids amounts to a violation of section 3(3)(d) of the Act. (Paragraph 95) In this case, the CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 6. National Association of Container Freight Stations, Chennai Chapter v. Trailer Owners Association and Others. Case No. 04 of 2018 DG noted that the trailer/truck trade associations restrict their members (i.e., operators of Inland Container Depots or Container Freight Stations) from plying more than twenty trailers. (Paragraph 4) The trade associations colluded to demand higher tariffs from the informant. (Paragraph 12) Hence, the price was fixed, and the output of the products was decided in various meetings of trade associations. (Paragraph 71) The cartel members used the trade association as an instrument to fix price and restrict output. (Paragraphs 77, 78) 7. In Re: Cartelisation in the supply of Protective Tubes to Indian Railways. Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2020 The cartel members were continuously exchanging emails regarding the bidding process for the procurement of polyacetal protective tubes. (Paragraph 24) The communication clearly shows the modus operandi of the cartel, which includes the bid allocation, revision of sharing pattern, induction of new members, calculation methods of the quotation, discussion on quotation, complaints regarding undercutting, and communication to withdraw offers. (Paragraph 25) 8. Chief Materials Manager, North Western Railway v. Moulded Fiberglass Products and Others. Reference Case No. 03 of 2018 The CCI found that various e-mails and WhatsApp texts were exchanged between the cartel members. (Paragraph 37) Cartel members were constantly exchanging information so that there would be a clear understanding of what their final bidding price and price breakdown would be (Basic Unit, Excise Rate, etc.). Similarly, geographical markets were also allocated for the cartel members. (Paragraphs 44-47) 9. In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct by various bidders in supply and installation of signages at specified locations of State Bank of India across India. Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2020. Emails containing a summary of agreements, bidding sequence (categorization of the geographical market), and bidding amount were exchanged between the cartel members. (Paragraphs 29-33) The bidding sequence and the bidding amount exactly matched. However, it is not necessary that the information exchanges need to be implemented to establish a violation The call records showed that the cartel members were engaged in telephonic conversations during the bidding process. (Paragraph 58) under section 3(3) of the Competition Act. Mere presence of an agreement between competitors is enough to presume AAEC. (Paragraphs 34, 35) 10. In Re: Cartelisation by Shipping Lines in the matter of provision of Maritime Motor Vehicle Transport Services to the Original Equipment Manufacturers. Suo Motu Case No. 10 of 2014 The cartel members regularly meet with each other to share commercially sensitive information with the objective of enforcement of the ‘Respect Rule’. ‘Respect Rule’ is an understanding between the cartel members to maintain their established position within their main customer accounts and avoid competition. (Paragraphs 125, 173.7, 178.3, 207) Instead of competing, the cartel members had a mutual understanding to avoid competition by fixing price, allocating marker and manipulating the bidding process. (Paragraph 207) 11. T.R. Chandran v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC). Case Nos 09 and 36 of 2017. Active communication (via WhatsApp texts, emails, meetings) between regional zones to fix and follow the price and production of eggs. (Paragraphs 154, 155, 192, 193) Determination and implementation of price and production of eggs through coordination is a violation of section 3(3)(a) of the Act. (Paragraph 153) 12. In Re: Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry. Suo Motu Case No. 05 of 2016. From email exchanges and physical meetings, the cartel members were deciding to increase prices and monitor the implementation of their decisions. (Paragraphs 164, 197) The CCI held that if rivals of a market are meeting to discuss the prices, then it is beyond any doubt that such conduct violates section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act, as it is likely to cause AAEC. (Paragraph 204) 13. Mr. Rizwanul Haq Khan v. Mersen (India) Pvt. Ltd. and Another. Reference Case No. 02 of 2016. WhatsApp messages and emails were exchanged between cartel members to exchange the details regarding the various tenders for the Carbon Brushes. (Paragraphs 49, 50, 52) Exchange of commercially sensitive information established that there was a ‘meeting of minds’ to manipulate the bidding process by pre-determining the quotation for tenders. (Paragraphs 51, 53) 14. Food Corporation of India v. Shivalik Agro Poly Products Ltd. and Others. Case No. 07 of 2018 WhatsApp messages showed that the cartel members were coordinating. (Paragraphs 47, 48) As per the evidence, the CCI concluded that there was a violation of section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act. (Paragraph 63) 15. Eastern Railway, Kolkata v. M/s Chandra Brothers and Others. Reference Case No. 02 of 2018. The cartel members were frequently discussing commercially sensitive information through emails, telephone calls, and SMS. (Paragraphs 118, 119) The cartel members were deciding the price bids through constant communication. (Paragraph 119) 16. XYZ v. Tamil Film Producers Council and Others. Case No. 07 of 2018. Circulars, letters, press releases and e-mails were issued by the trade The CCI noted that the film associations called for a boycott so that they could have a better associations calling for a boycott against certain producers and theatre owners (Paragraphs 41-43) bargaining position while negotiating the Virtual Print Fee charged by the Digital Cinema Service Providers/Digital Service Providers. (Paragraphs 43-45) M/s International Subscription Agency v. Federation of Publishers’ and Booksellers’ Associations in India. Case No. 33 of 2019. The trade association directed its members not to provide any discount and issued advisories to its members. The trade association prohibited the members from participating in certain procurement advertisements. (Paragraph 24) The conduct of the trade association limited the product and eliminated the quantum of discount. (Paragraphs 26, 39) 17. Chief Materials Manager, South Eastern Railway v. Hindustan Composites Limited. Reference Case No. 03 of 2016. Tender-related information was mailed to the cartel members. (Paragraph 34) The CCI held that such mails are direct evidence to establish an arrangement to rig the bidding process. (Paragraph 35) 18. In Re: Cartelisation in Industrial and Automotive Bearings. Case No. 05 of 2017. Emails were exchanged between cartel members, and those emails contained price-sensitive information relating to the tender. Further, the cartel members made several telephonic calls during the tender processing period. (Paragraph 15) A cartel was formed because price revision, along with minimum percentage of a price increase to be quoted, were discussed. (Paragraph 18) 19. M/s Suman Distributors, Murshidabad v. Bengal Chemists and Druggists Association and Others. Case No. 36 of 2015 From letters and telephonic conversations, it was clear that Alkem was forcing stockists to obtain Stock Availability Information (SAI) from the Bengal Chemists and Druggists Association (BCDA) before Alkem commenced making supplies of drugs to them. (Paragraphs 20.2, 20.3, 21.9) Alkem was in agreement with BCDA by issuing OLS to prospective stockists and was indulging in the practice of demanding SAI/Non-Objection Certificate/Approval Letter/Circulation Letter from BCDA before commencing supplies. This constitutes an anticompetitive agreement between Alkem and BCDA. (Paragraph 21.5)

sections.title: INDIRECT EVIDENCE: ROLE OF CIRCUMSTANTIAL EVIDENCE
sections.text: §2.06 INDIRECT EVIDENCE: ROLE OF CIRCUMSTANTIAL EVIDENCE The DG and CCI also look at the chain of circumstantial evidence to analyse whether the market participants entered into an anti-competitive agreement or not. As mentioned above, the key area of analysing circumstantial evidence is to find out whether the particular set of acts and conduct of the market participants cannot be explained ‘but for’ some sort of anti-competitive agreement and action in concert among them.29 Airline Surcharge Cartel case, para. 39. The decisional practice of the CCI shows that most of the cartel investigations that have happened till now are in oligopolistic industries. As has been explained above, in an oligopolistic market, parallel behaviour is extremely common due to the interdependence of firms’ conduct. The key chain of evidence must be seen whether such parallel behaviour is an action of conscious parallelism or an agreement to collude. The key thing that needs to be kept in mind is that the CCI looks at the totality of the evidence to come to a conclusion as to whether there is a case of cartelization. In this regard, it is to be noted that in most of the cartel cases, the market participants take a plea that their conduct is a case of parallel behaviour and not cartel behaviour. However, the CCI has noted that parallel behaviour, although common in an oligopolistic industry, may not carry on for a lengthy period of time unless there is a cartel arrangement between the market players. It is apposite to note the observations of the erstwhile COMPAT, where it was observed that: We do not think that this is a case of mere price parallelism. We have already made observation that price parallelism on one or two occasions though might raise strong suspicion, it may not be enough for drawing the inference of cartelization. We reiterate that observation. However, in this case it was not a sweet coincidence and in fact it is shown that this was not only a common pattern or practice, but this continued in case of tenders floated by other corporations. This consistent practice and common pattern when continues for a long time, there could be no other inference excepting that of cartelization. There are two recognized forms of circumstantial evidence which are taken into account by the DG and the CCI in a cartel investigation: (a) communication evidence and (b) economic evidence. Communication evidence indicates that cartel operators met or otherwise communicated but does not describe the substance of their communications. It includes, for example, meetings at a trade association, records of telephone conversations among suspected cartel participants, their travel to a common destination, common hotel receipts, common entry in registers, and notes or records of meetings in which they participated. Economic evidence, however, can be categorized as either conduct or structural evidence. The former includes, most importantly, evidence of parallel conduct by suspected cartel members, e.g., simultaneous and identical price increases or suspicious bidding patterns in public tenders, lower capacity utilization, and high correlation on prices. The CCI has observed that among the set of circumstantial evidence, evidence of communication among the participants to an anti-competitive agreement may give an important clue for establishing any contravention. Communication evidence might prove that contravening parties met and communicated with each other to determine their future or present behaviour. Therefore, in terms of the weight of evidence, communication evidence is given more importance than economic evidence. The CCI in the Cement cartel case30 Cement cartel case, para. 232. considered various circumstantial evidence as plus factors to establish violation under section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act. The CCI observed the plus factors, such as the exchange of price and production using the Cement Manufacturers Association (CMA) as a platform, lowering the capacity utilization, and lowering the supply of cement which resulted in price increases in the cement industry. Further, it is to be noted that it is not necessary that cartel participants must raise prices at the same time, to be caught under the ambit of section 3(3) of the Competition Act. In fact, the CCI, in the Airline surcharge cartel case,31 Airline surcharge cartel case, para. 79. (Order dated 7 Mar. 2018). has noted that it is necessary to analyse the behaviour of the cartel participants, in entirety, to see whether they have engaged in a cartel. There may be situations wherein the market participants intentionally create artificial time gaps among themselves to increase the price, to show that it is a case of intelligent market adoption to the other competitors’ action, but not a cartel. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations of the CCI in the Airline surcharge cartel case:32 Airline surcharge cartel case, para. 114. (Order dated 17 Nov. 2015). The so-called time gap is just a matter of few days. It would be travesty of competition norms if such lag theory is countenanced by the Competition Agency. It is neither the requirement of law nor any other jurisprudence that cartels must originate symmetrically, progress symmetrically and culminate in a similar fashion. More often than not, the colluding parties would like to break the patterns through artificial gaps and arrangements so as to create a façade of competitive behaviour. In such a projected ‘competitive landscape’, it is the bounden duty of the Authority to pierce this artificial veil and to examine the real behaviour of the colluding parties. The present case perfectly fits such stratagem where artificial lags and gaps were sought to be passed off and projected to envision a competitive scenario when none existed. In fact, such justifications and explanations only complete the chain of the arrangement and understanding reached amongst the parties. Now, we will discuss the manner in which the DG and CCI have looked at both communication and economic evidence in deciding cartel matters. [A] Communication Evidence Communication evidence is a type of indirect evidence consisting of proof that the cartel members attended a physical meeting or communicated virtually via telephone, WhatsApp, email, etc. As opposed to direct evidence, the substance of the communication may not be available in communication evidence.33 LPG-I case, para. 82. (SC). When an entity exchanges strategic information or price-related information with its competitors, who are accepting the information constitutes evidence, it will be presumed that the entity had accepted the information and implemented the information in line with the anti-competitive conduct. Information could be exchanged through mail, phone calls or meetings. Mere receiving calls or mails or attending meetings where some strategic information was passed would be enough to use it as evidence to establish an anti-competitive agreement. To avoid violation of section 3 of the Competition Act, the entity who received the information should object to the exchange of information.34 Ibid., para. 84. (SC). The DG and the CCI, in any cartel investigation, investigate whether the market participants regularly meet, either under the aegis of a trade association or otherwise. Frequent meetings between competitors followed by similar market conduct have always been seen as a plus factor in a cartel investigation. The following are some instances of communication evidence which has been relied upon by the CCI while deciding cartel cases. [1] Frequent Meetings among Competitors In the LPG-I case,35 LPG-I case, para. 14.17. (CCI). the CCI took note of the OECD’s Document on detecting bid rigging in public procurement. The CCI noted that: The fact of agreement can be either communicated by words or conduct. Bidders need to know and communicate with each other to reach an agreement. Once bidders know each other well enough to discuss bid rigging, they need a convenient location where they can talk. Of course, communications can occur by telephone, email, fax or letter, and they often do; however, many bid riggers believe that they are less likely to leave evidence of their communications if they have face-to-face meetings. These meetings occur most often at, or in association with trade association meetings, or other professional or social events. They are also likely to occur prior to the opening of the tender process. In the Cement cartel case, the CCI noted that the cement companies participated in the meetings of the CMA. Further, under the aegis of CMA, the companies interacted on a regular basis on sensitive information like pricing, production details, dispatch details, etc. It was observed by the CCI that CMA, based on a governmental directive, collected retail prices and wholesale prices of the cement from different centres and transmitted them onwards to the government. It was observed by the CCI that the prices of cement for all the cement companies went up after two meetings of the CMA. One of the defences that was raised by the cement companies was that the prices of cement went up after two meetings of the Cement Manufactures Association and not all the meetings of the said trade association. However, the CCI noted that it is not required that prices of goods need to go up after each meeting, especially when the price of cement is already artificially inflated. This approach of the CCI to penalize cement companies when the prices went up after two meetings, and not all meetings show the assertive approach of the CCI. In this background, it is very important for companies to have proper document management systems and pricing policies to show, by way of contemporaneous documents, changes in prices of companies and reasons thereof so that during an investigation, such documents can be shown to justify the rationale for price change. Even in the Insurance cartel case,36 Insurance cartel case, paras 37, 38. the CCI observed that the insurance companies held meetings under the aegis of the trade association to discuss the sharing of business and quotation for each bid. Such kind of communication evidence is taken by the CCI as evidence of cartel behaviour. Having said that, the CCI has also noted that just because the market participants have met in a trade association does not ipso facto mean that they indulged in an anti-competitive behaviour. For illustration, in the Tyre case, the CCI noted that the tyre industry had a trade association. However, on investigation, it was revealed that the trade association was used as a forum for lobbying for the welfare of the trade industry and making representations to governmental organizations, which cannot be viewed as anti-competitive. What the DG and CCI look at is whether the communication evidence so collected can be seen as a plus factor, i.e., whether such communication aims to formulate a cartel strategy. The CCI in People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others,37 People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others, Case No. 90 of 2016, paras 57-61, 63. Appeal against the CCO order was dismissed by NCLAT. See, People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha Internationals [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 46 of 2022. from all the call data records obtained by the DG, noted that the competitors were frequently interacting with each other was considered a plus factor while proving a bid rigging cartel. In fact, the erstwhile COMPAT has noted that the existence of a trade association gives an opportunity to the competitors to interact with each other and discuss trade problems. Further, it is not required to prove that any party actually discussed the prices by actively taking part in the meeting. If there is direct evidence to that effect, that is certainly a pointer towards the fact that such a party had a tacit agreement with its competitors. However, the existence of an association and further holding of the meetings just one or two days prior to the submitting bids, followed by quoting similar bid prices, gives a strong indication of collusive conduct. [2] Publication of Price Bulletin In two landmark cases decided by the CCI (such as the Cement cartel case), the CCI has noted that the practice of publishing price bulletins by a trade association is also a plus factor when deciding on a case for cartelization. In the Cement cartel case,38 Cement cartel case, paras 7(xlvii), 202, 247, 258. the CCI noted that the CMA collected wholesale and retail prices of cement. The retail prices collected from different centres are transmitted to the Department of Industrial Policy and Promotion (DIPP), while the wholesale prices are transmitted to the office of the Economic Advisor. The retail and wholesale prices are collected from all over the country. Although the mandate to collect the cement prices was given to the CMA, it was also observed that the CMA was nominating some representatives from each company to collect the prices for each region for all the companies operating in that region. Based on the same, it was noted by the CCI that the prices of all competing cement companies are being collected. In such a situation, when the cement companies nominated by CMA are collecting prices of other competing cement companies over telephones and e-mails, coordination on prices is easily facilitated. Based on the above, it was concluded by the CCI that when competitors are interacting using the platform of CMA, and they are in touch with each other over the phone and e-mails as regards prices – both retail and wholesale, it cannot be denied that there is always an opportunity to discuss the determination and fixation of prices for future, which is prohibited under the provisions of the Competition Act. The fact that it is being done under the instruction of DIPP (a wing of the Government of India) does not absolve CMA or the cement companies engaged in this exercise from running afoul of the provisions of the Competition Act. Further, the investigation showed that CMA had several publications, such as Executive Summary-Cement Industry and Cement Statistics-Interregional Movement of Cement which give details of the production and dispatch of each company and are circulated only among the members. Therefore, the cement companies not only get information on the prices of other cement companies but also receive details about the production and dispatch details of the other companies. The CCI noted that sharing such sensitive information makes coordination easier among cement companies and help them plan their future conduct. In light of the above communication evidence, the CCI identified that there was information sharing among the competitors on prices, quantity and dispatch details, which in light of the economic evidence (discussed below) shows an agreement among the cement companies to cartelize. Similarly, in T.R. Chandran v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC),39 T.R. Chandran v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC), Case Nos 09 and 36 of 2017, paras 114, 153, 208, 209. the trade association (i.e., NECC) displayed daily egg prices, zone-wise, on its website and monitored the monthly/annual prices of eggs at various egg production and consumption centres. The CCI held that by mandating the farmers to follow the price fixed and by ensuring adherence to such fixed prices, the NECC has violated section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act. [3] Commonality of Key Business Personnel In a bid rigging case, the investigation done by the DG and CCI had revealed that there was cross-ownership and common directors among the various bidders in the market. It was revealed that the companies involved in the bidding process are closely related, having common directors or different members of the same family at the helm of affairs. Having such a relationship indicated that out of the thirteen companies under investigation, the modus operandi of at least ten of these firms was governed by the principles of mutual understanding and benefit. This was seen as a plus factor since there is a chance of sharing commercially sensitive information among various companies, which will help in agreeing on future conduct. Similarly, in the in Re: Alleged cartelisation in supply of LPG Cylinders procured through tenders by Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. (HPCL), (‘LPG-II case’),40 Re: Alleged cartelisation in supply of LPG Cylinders procured through tenders by Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. (HPCL), Suo Motu Case No. 1 of 2014, paras 74, 109. The NCLAT upheld the order passes by the CCI by holding that the identical behaviours of the parties were a result of collusion. See, Sahuwala Cylinders Pvt. Ltd. & Others v. Competition Commission of India and Another[2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 38 of 2019. and the Airline surcharge cartel case,41 Airline surcharge cartel case, para. 106. it was noted by the CCI that there were common agents that were appointed by market participants, which were seen to share commercial sensitive and future information of one company with another. Based on the same, it was noted that there was exchange of information between companies, which eventually led to collusion. Therefore, it is key that while appointing agents or other trading partners, a very strong non-disclosure agreement be signed with such entities that is scrupulously followed to ensure that there is no transmission of commercially sensitive information of one company to its competitors. Having said that, in the Airline surcharge cartel case, the airline companies said that there was the presence of common agents among airlines who could share commercially sensitive information among themselves. Although the agents are not allowed by the airline to disclose commercial information to other airlines, it cannot be ruled out that agents do share commercially sensitive information among airlines. Thus, while appointing agents, distributors or other trading partners, who are also the trading partners of their competitors, the companies must be extra cautious. Similarly, In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing,42 In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing, Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2020, paras 30, 88. some of the cartel members were family friends, and they submitted cover bids under the influence of friendship. The CCI noted this evidence as a plus factor to conclude the presence of a bid rigging cartel. Further, In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing,43 In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing, Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2016, paras 5, 56-59. the CCI held that the bid rigging cartel was controlled by one person or family because the director of 2nd opposite party is a close friend of 1st opposite party. Further, the sole proprietor of 3rd opposite party and also the son of 4th opposite party’s director. [4] Other Evidence The investigation pattern of the DG shows that they rely on other kinds of evidence to show that the market participants met before submitting a bid, like common visitor entries, common bank accounts or places used to submit bids, common agents, or hotel bills to show that the officers of companies met for a meal one day before the submission of a bid. There are instances where bidders use common IP address to submit bids in order to manipulate the bidding process. CCI also considers other login details, including the user ID, username, login date and time, proximity of logout date and time and caller ID, to establish that the bids of all cartel members were filed by a common person.44 In Re: Chief Materials Manager, North Western Railway v. Molded Fiberglass Products and Others. Case No. 03/2018, paras 34, 35. The table below shows the decisional trends of the CCI in terms of analysis of other types of communication evidence across multiple decisional cases. S. No. Case Name (Citation) Evidence (Paragraph No.) Reasoning (Paragraph No.) 1. In Re: Cartelisation in the supply of Protective Tubes to Indian Railways. Suo Moto 06/2020 The cartel members used common IP address to submit their bids in twelve bidding processes. (Paragraph 20) The CCI acknowledged the presence of a cartel because the cartel members filed their bids from common IP address. (Paragraph 29) 2. In Re: Chief Materials Manager, North Western Railway v. Moulded Fibreglass Products and Others. Case No. 03/2018 The cartel members used common IP address to submit bids in thirty-four tenders. (Paragraph 33) From other login details to conclude that all bids were submitted from a common IP address. (Paragraph 34) The holistic evaluation of evidence showed that the cartel members colluded to fix prices, allocate markets, and rig the bidding process. (Paragraphs 58, 59) 3. GAIL (India) Limited v. PMP Infratech Private Ltd. and Another. Case No. 41 of 2019. The bids were submitted from a common IP address. Cartel members used the same office premises and systems to submit their bids for which they were competing. (Paragraph 43) The CCI held that the cartel members were constantly communicating with each other to coordinate their actions, which shows the existence of anti-competitive activity. (Paragraph 39) 4. People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others. Case No. 90 of 2016. The office bearer of one cartel member (designated bidder to win the tender) paid the Tender Charge on behalf of other cartel members who were providing only submitting cover bids. After the designated bidder won the tender, the other cartel members returned the refunded Tender Charge. (Paragraphs 47, 48) All the evidence, i.e., usage of common IP address, call records, and close relationships between cartel members, has to be seen holistically to determine the existence of an agreement. (Paragraph 56) All the cartel members used a single IP address to submit their bids. (Paragraph 53) [B] Economic Evidence Economic evidence is also analysed by the CCI on the touchstone of the test explained above. Economic evidence is the conduct of the market participants which is important to identify the presence of an anti-competitive. To analyse economic evidence in a cartel case, the DG and the CCI undertake elaborate analysis of data relating to demand trends of the product, production, production capacity, capacity utilization, cost analysis, price analysis, cost of sales/sales realization/margin; cost of production and final price movement for each market participant to see whether there is any agreement among the companies. Economic evidence includes the parallel behaviour of the market participants, the industrial performance of the market participants (huge profit margin, stable market shares, repetitive violators of competition law), and a market structure providing a suitable environment to form a cartel (characteristics of the market structure: high concentration, high entry barriers, high degree of vertical integration, homogenous product), etc.45 LPG-I case, (2018) 16 SCC 615, para. 82. (SC). The investigation pattern suggests that the DG asks for data on price, costs, demand, capacity, etc., for a minimum of preceding three years. Such an exercise is done for each of the market participants which are being investigated to see whether there is a pattern undertaken by such market participants to indicate the existence of an agreement. For example, in the Cement cartel case,46 Cement cartel case, paras 239, 269, 276. the DG analysed the capacity utilization figures of 2010-2011 in comparison to the previous year to conclude that the cement companies had intentionally engaged in limiting the production of cement in order to raise the price of cement. This conclusion was reached because the CCI noted that the demand for cement is inelastic, and the demand for cement would more or less remain the same for a particular period of time every year. In light of the demand scenario, it was seen that the cement companies reduced their capacity utilization from 82% in 2009-2010 to 73% in 2010-2011 for the month of November, when there was a high demand for cement. Similar exercise was also done by the DG in the Excel Crop case,47 Excel Crop case, paras 3.17, 3.19. (CCI). where it was observed that the price of APT had increased exponentially in comparison to previous years, and it was also noted that the profit margin of the parties was also increased. As mentioned above, parallel behaviour is extremely common in an oligopolistic market where each market participant considers the likely reactions of other firms while making independent decisions, particularly regarding prices and output. What must be analysed, from economic evidence, is whether the said action is a result of independent action or as a result of agreement. It is to be noted that economic evidence must be seen in the totality of the industry circumstances to see whether a case of cartel can be made out. There is no one-size-fits-all approach which can be taken in a cartel investigation. For example, in the Cement cartel case, the DG and the CCI found that cement companies indulged in limiting production by reducing their capacity utilization. Conversely, in the Tyre case,48 Tyre case, para. 114. it was found that while some of the tyre companies reduced their capacity utilization, it was not seen as evidence of cartelization since the overall production capacity had increased. While economic evidence is seen and analysed by the company, it must be ensured that proper data on pricing and other sensitive information are properly maintained by the company. It was seen in the Airline surcharge cartel case49 Airline surcharge cartel case, paras 92, 93, 100, 106. that all the airlines were giving the reason that the primary reason for charging a fuel surcharge was the rise in the aviation turbine fuel (ATF) price. However, upon investigation, it was found that none of the airlines could provide any contemporaneous documents to show how the rise in ATF prices had led to fixing and subsequent rises in fuel surcharge rates. In fact, on conducting a correlation analysis, it was found that at times, FSC rates were increased when ATF prices were falling. Due to lack of maintaining any pricing data by airlines, the CCI raised an adverse inference against them in a cartel investigation. This raised an important aspect of document retention policies wherein CCI hinted that companies should maintain proper contemporaneous documents to show that pricing decisions were taken unilaterally. The CCI took an adverse inference of not maintaining pricing data by airlines. It must be noted that economic evidence is seen as an addition to communication evidence in a cartel investigation. Absence of economic evidence cannot be used as a defence to show that there is no cartelization. There has hardly been any cartel decision where the CCI has found an infringement of section 3(3) of the Competition Act only on the basis of economic evidence. Some of the key economic tests identified by the decisional practice of the CCI are detailed below. [1] Analysis of Final Price in Comparison to the Input Price Business prudence requires that the final price must account for the input price of the raw material and other overheads plus a markup to ensure that the business is profitable. Having said that, in a market where there are choices of market participants, each company undertakes to give the best possible price to have better revenue, more customers and more purchase orders. However, in a cartel case, the companies collude so that the prices of all companies are kept artificially high so that each one can earn supra-normal profits. In light of the above, in most of the cartel cases, the DG analyses the final price increase of the product for each of the market participants during the period of investigation and compares such price increase with the price of input costs, during the same time, for producing the concerned product. During the same time, if it is seen that even the input price, i.e., the price of raw material, has gone up during the same point of time, an economic justification can be made for the price increase of the final product. In the absence of a price increase of the raw material, it can be concluded (based on various other additional factors) that the market participants colluded to keep the price levels high. In fact, in the Cement cartel case,50 Cement cartel case, para. 269. the CCI had noted that due to concreted practices, there was an increase in the cement price by all market participants, and the price was not increased due to a change in excise duty. Conversely, the CCI noted in the case of Shubham Sanitarywares v. Hindustan Sanitaryware and Industries 51 Shubham Sanitarywares v. Hindustan Sanitaryware and Industries, Case No. 99 of 2013, para. 14. The CCI order was upheld by the erstwhile COMPAT in Shubham Sanitarywares v. Competition Commission of India and Ors., Appeal No. 19 of 2016. that the price of the product under question has not increased for the past three years. Such stagnant prices were taken by the CCI to indicate there was no cartel. [2] Price Correlation Exercise for All Market Participants In order to find out whether there is an agreement and concerted action among the market participants to raise prices in a consistent manner, the DG generally undertakes a price correlation exercise for each of the players; i.e., a correlation exercise is seen whether the prices of products for all market participants are similar and moving in the same direction. In the Cement cartel case,52 Cement cartel case, para. 7(xxv), 7(xxvii), 92, 223. it was seen by the DG, based on the data on market prices, that the correlation of change in prices or the movement of prices of all the companies is positive and very close to each other (more than 0.5%). Such a strong correlation between cement prices for all cement companies across regions in India gave a strong indication of price parallelism. Since the cost of production, transportation charges, etc., varies from company to company, economic prudence suggests that the price of individual companies should also vary. Therefore, the DG noted that the movement of prices of all the companies in the same range and in the same direction is not possible unless there is prior consultation and discussion about the prices among them. Based on analysis of correlations of absolute price, price change and percentage price change, it was concluded by the DG that the prices of the cement manufacturers show a positive correlation in every state of operation and hence was taken as evidence of cartelization. The observations of the DG were also agreed by the CCI in their final decision. Conversely, in the Tyre case,53 Tyre case, para. 320. the range between prices of tyres of different manufacturers was more than INR 1,000 for the period 2005-2009, and the range had come down to INR 600 in 2010. The CCI noted that the product under investigation, i.e., tyre, is homogeneous. For a homogenous product, a 6%-12% range of difference in prices implies that the prices are dissimilar and there is no parallelism in absolute prices. Further, with respect to price movement in percentage terms, it was noted that there were wide variations among various tyre manufactures. Such wide variations showed that there was no agreement among tyre companies. Depending upon the facts and circumstances, the CCI considers whether the price parallelism by the competitors was due to the specific market condition or due to collusion before leading to an inference of cartelization. This economic evidence is commonly found in bid rigging cases. One of the forms of bid rigging is Identical or Level bidding, where bidders coordinate to quote identical price of their bids. By publishing price lists, raising prices by a specific increment, fixing or removing discounts, or maintaining a decided price differentials based on quantity, type, or size of product, the bidders can manipulate the bidding process in their favour. For instance, in Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others,54 Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others, Reference Case No. 02 of 2020, paras 85, 86, 95. the CCI held that there was a violation of section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act because the bidders indulged in identical pricing in the bids in Eastern Railway tenders for the eight years. Similarly, in People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others,55 People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others, Case No. 90 of 2016, paras 41, 43, 44. the CCI held that the prices quoted were similar; hence, the competitors were neither competitive nor independent. [3] Production Capacity and Capacity Utilization In all cartel investigations, the DG analyses the production capacity of the market participants during the period of investigation and also the capacity utilization during the same time frame. Further, the trend in the demand for said product under investigation is also seen during the same time frame. The reason for such an analysis is required to see whether the market participants have indulged in deliberately curtailing production in the face of rising demand, which will have the effect of an increase in the final price. For illustration, in the Cement cartel case,56 Cement cartel case, paras 7(xxix), 7(xxxvi), 7(xxxix), 245, 246. the DG noted that the production capacity of cement had increased from 157 MMT in 2005-06 to 287 MMT by the end of March 2011. However, capacity utilization was on a continuous downward trend from 2008 to 2009. During the financial year 2010-2011, capacity utilization had come down to 73% from 82% in the previous years, in spite of a very high demand for cement. A lower capacity utilization by all cement manufacturers, in the absence of any justifiable reason, was taken by the CCI as a plus factor to prove the existence of an anti-competitive agreement among cement manufacturers to limit the production of cement, which in turn, increased the price of cement. As such, the DG undertook a comparison of price index with capacity utilization index of each of the cement companies. It was noted that ever since 2006-2007, the capacity utilization and cement price index of cement companies have been moving in the opposite direction. While the capacity utilization has been declining, the price index has been increasing. It was also noted that the production capacity in total was increasing, and yet the capacity utilization of all cement companies was decreasing. The DG noted that the cement companies could not come up with any business rationale for the decrease in capacity utilization in spite of a surge in demand. Based on the same, the DG noted that this is a result of a deliberate attempt to reduce supply by not utilizing full capacity and thereby increasing the price of cement in the market. The DG also concluded that the cement companies are charging unreasonable and higher than competitive prices. The last quarter of the financial year 2010-2011 witnessed a price increase of 20%-50% throughout the country in comparison to prices in the third quarter of 2010-2011 which was a result of a reduction in capacity utilization and controlling the supply in the market. The observations of the DG were also endorsed by the CCI in the final decision. Similarly, In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India 57 In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India, Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2017, paras 71, 74. The NCLAT also upheld the CCI order in Pawan Jagetia and Others v. CCI and Others [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 16 of 2021. At present, at appeal is pending before the Supreme Court, Civil Appeal No. 000747 of 2023. The Supreme Court passed a stay on the CCI’s recovery proceeding of the penalty, and the matter is sub judice. (‘Beer cartel case’), the CCI initiated a suo motu investigation pursuant to the receipt of a leniency application by Crown Beers India Private Limited and SABMillers India Limited under section 46 of the Competition Act read with Regulation 5 of the Competition Commission of India (Lesser Penalty) Regulations, 2009. From the evidence collected from the leniency application, the CCI prima facie noted that in several states and union territories, price fixation and implementation of price adjustment existed in the beer industry. After a holistic analysis of the evidence under the leniency application and the DG report, the CCI concluded the existence of a beer cartel in the ten states and union territories and found that the beer cartel violated section 3(3)(a), (b) and (c) of the Competition Act. The beer manufacturers reduced their beer production to create an artificial scarcity of their product in the market. The beer manufacturers were also sharing their commercial information, such as beer production, revenue details, targets, stock sold and stock held by the companies, and details of their stocks lying with the distributors. This was done to limit the production of beer in the market and put pressure on the government to lower the excise duty on beer. The CCI found both direct evidence and indirect evidence to hold such anti-competitive to be violative of section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act. [4] Profit Margin of the Market Participants As a part of a cartel investigation, the DG analyses the profit margin of each of the market participants to see whether there is a trend which shows the existence of an agreement. In the Cement cartel case,58 Cement cartel case, paras 277, 283. the CCI noted the cement manufacturing-wise trend in profit margins and net profit. Because of cartelization, cement manufacturers were able to make huge profits over the years by limiting and controlling supplies and by imposing a higher cost per cement bag. Furthermore, the net profit of the cement manufacturers shows that they were able to make a high net profit by eliminating competition and charging unreasonable prices. [5] Economic Evidence to Establish Bid Rigging [a] Bid Price Versus Input Price In all the bid rigging cases, the DG makes an analysis of the final bid prices, the price of the inputs and other key indices of costs like freight, transportation, etc. This analysis is required to be done to see whether the price being quoted in a bid is an independent price of the market participants or if there is a case of bid rigging where the companies have colluded with each other during the process of bidding. In one of the bid rigging cases, it was noted that the bids quoted for the product by the manufacturers were the same and not based on cost and expected profit margins. It was noted, during the investigation, that though the source of procurement of raw material was the same for all the manufacturers, the cost incurred by each of the manufacturers for such procurement of raw material was not the same. The input cost was different for each company, yet all the companies submitted bids with the same price. Similarly, the CCI undertook a detailed analysis of the bidding pattern in the Aluminium Phosphide case, where it was seen that there was an identity in the rates quoted by the bidders even when the factories and the offices of these parties were not located in one and same state and they had to make supplies to locations far off from their factories located at different places. Therefore, in cases where in spite of a varying cost structure for companies, price bids submitted by companies have identical (to the last decimal) or similar bids have been held by the CCI, as a case of bid rigging. Therefore, to conduct a final price versus input analysis, the DG and CCI undertook a detailed costing analysis of each of the market participants and analysed the same in light of the final prices quoted by the concerned company. Further, it is apposite to note the observations of the Supreme Court in the Excel Crop case.59 Excel Crop case, paras 48, 49. (SC). One of the defences which are raised in every cartel matter is that it is a case of parallel pricing by the market participants, and such actions are not borne out due to any preconceived agreement. In this regard, the Supreme Court, in the context of bid rigging, had noted that identical pricing on one or two occasions, though it raises strong suspicion, may not be enough to draw the inference of the concerted agreement, but when is repeated constantly with odd figures (same to the last decimal) and without any reasonable explanation for the same, would only draw an inference of a pre-concerted agreement. In Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others,60 Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others, Reference Case No. 02 of 2020, paras 87, 88, 93. the CCI observed the quotations of bidders and noted that despite differences in manufacturing, transportation, packaging, and insurance, the cartel members fixed their quotation at INR 17,800. Hence, this, coupled with other evidence, was held to be in violation of section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act for manipulating the bidding process. [b] Cover Bids There have been instances wherein entities participate in the bidding process without any intention to compete but to create a façade of competition. Such entities have no technical qualification to participate in tenders because they might not be having proper equipment or experience certificate to fulfil the technical eligibility. Their sole purpose of participating in the bidding process is to support one of the entities to win the bid. Generally, the sister company or the entities have a good relationship with the designate winner who tends to submit bids so that the bid of the designate bidder will not be rejected due to insufficient number of bids,61 In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing, Suo Motu No. 01 of 2020, paras 60, 97. thereby creating a mirage of competition, where none exists. [c] Sub-contracting In sub-contracting, the cartel member will suppress its bid or submit a losing bid so that in exchange it will get the sub-contract from the winner of the tender. This results in higher profits for both cartel members. Further, the cartel members can rotate their roles in subsequent tenders. The CCI in Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra) 62 Surendra Prasad v. Maharashtra State Power Generation Co. Ltd. & Others, Case No. 61 of 2013, paras 41, 124, 125. considered that the rivals working as sub-contractors for each other were used to establish the anti-competitive arrangement. [d] Other Economic Evidence for Establishing Bid Rigging Cartel There are also other types of economic evidence, such as collective withdrawal and common reason for withdrawal,63 In Re: Alleged Cartelisation in Supply of the LPG Cylinders Procured through Tenders by Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. (HPCL), Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2014, paras 52-55. pattern of market allocation,64 In Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra), Case No. 61 of 2013, paras 99-111. bogus expenses by the cartel members for showing losses,65 Ibid., paras 126, 127. etc., that can be considered a plus factor to establish a presence of bid rigging cartel. The table below shows the decisional trends of the CCI in terms of analysis of communication and economic evidence across multiple decisional cases: S. No. Case Name (Citation) Evidence (Communication/Economic) Reasoning 1. Airline surcharge case. Case No. 30 of 2013 The airline increased the FSC rate on the same date. (Paragraph 90) The airlines discussed the FSC rates in meetings. (Paragraph 106) The alleged airlines had hired common agents. (Paragraph 104) The CCI held that the levy of FSC is at a fixed rate on per kilogram basis of the cargo weight. None of the airlines was able to furnish any data in support of the determination of FSC rates. Further, the airlines used common agents to exchange information among themselves. Hence, the airline acted in parallel to increase the FSC rates. (Paragraphs 84, 99, 106, 107) 2. People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others. Case No. 90 of 2016. The call data record proved that the cartel members were constantly communicating during the tender proceeding. (Paragraphs 57-61) The rates quoted by the cartel members are very close, less than INR 30, despite the input cost and other conditions of the market. (Paragraph 41) Frequent interaction among cartel members is considered a plus factor in proving a bid rigging cartel. (Paragraph 63) The CCI noted that the identical pricing shows that the cartel members are not competitive and independent. (Paragraphs 43, 44) 3. Cement cartel case (Case No. 29 of 2010) Market is Oligopolistic because Cement, as a product, has only two or three categories. The monthly prices of cement of the cement manufacturers were moving in tandem. (Paragraph 229) The cement manufacturers were unable to provide any reasonable explanation for the determination of prices. (Paragraph 233) The cement manufacturers’ overall capacity utilization reduced from 83% in 2009-10 to 73% in 2010-11. All the cement manufacturers reduced their production of cement in 2009 and 2010. Similarly, the cement manufacturers were colluding regarding the dispatch of cement. (Paragraphs 239, 245, 250-252, 257, 258, 260, 261) The price of cement per bag was increased by the cement manufacturers. Further, the sale, sale The cement manufacturers were aware of the actions of each other and colluded in a manner which led to non-competitive outcomes. (Paragraph 220) There was price parallelism between different cement manufacturers because of the strong positive correlation in the prices. (Paragraphs 224, 232) The cement manufacturers were unable to corroborate the change in cement prices with the market dynamics. (Paragraph 238) After looking at the annual reports of the cement manufacturers, it was clarified that they were not utilizing the overall capacity. During 2009-10 and 2010-11, the production of cement has fallen drastically. The cement manufacturers similarly entered into an identical realization and margin of cement manufacturers increased. Hence, the cement manufacturers earned huge profits. (Paragraphs 268, 280, 282) behaviour relating to the dispatch of cement to control the supply of cement. (Paragraphs 252, 259, 263, 266) The cement manufacturer created an artificial demand by shortening production and, thereafter, increased the price of cement. Hence, the cement manufacturers deliberately reduced capacity utilization to manipulate and control the supply in the market and earned huge profits by increasing the prices. (Paragraphs 273, 275, 277, 283) The CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 4. In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India. Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2017 The beer manufacturers decided to stop producing and restricted the supply of Beer. (Paragraphs 11(1)(vi), 67) The CCI held that exchange of commercially information competitors compromises the integrity of independent bidding process and was likely to stifle competition. (Paragraph 23) Beer manufacturer decided to lower their production of beer to put pressure on the Excise Authorities to lower the Excise duty on Beer. Limiting supplies impacted the beer market because the price of beer increased. The CCI noted that the conduct of the beer manufacturer violated section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act. (Paragraphs 69, 70, 71) In this case, CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 5. LPG-II case. Suo Motu Case No. 1 of 2014 Direct evidence, such as emails were exchanged between bidders regarding price, business strategies, etc. (Paragraph 77) The CCI also found certain indirect evidence, such as the bidders discussed and parallel acted in terms of: (a) quotation of bids (paragraph 48) (b) collective withdrawal of bids (paragraph 53) The CCI held that the bidders discussed and shared sensitive and confidential information regarding the tender to manipulate the bidding process of tender no. 2. (Paragraphs 107, 111-113) (c) common reason in withdrawal letters (paragraphs 54, 55) (d) common format of withdrawal letter (paragraph 60) (e) common IP address and agents (paragraphs 65, 74) 6. In Re: Aluminium Phosphide Tablets Manufacturers. Suo Motu No. 02 of 2011. The bidders concerted to manipulate the bidding process by quoting identical rates even if their production costs were different. Also, they collectively abstained from bidding in certain tenders. (Paragraphs 3.14, 6.2) The CCI said that the parallel behaviour of the bidders can be considered evidence of coordinated behaviour on the part of the three bidders regarding the tender. (Paragraph 6.29) 7. T.R. Chandran v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC). Case Nos 09 and 36 of 2017. The trade association (i.e., NECC) displayed daily egg prices, zone-wise, on its website and monitored the monthly/annual price of eggs at various egg production and consumption centres. (Paragraph 114) Mandating the farmers to follow the price fixed by the NECC is a violation of section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act. (Paragraphs 153, 208, 209) 8. In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing. Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2020 Some of the cartel members were family friends, and they submitted cover bids under the influence of friendship. (Paragraph 30) The CCI used this evidence as a plus factor to conclude the presence of bid rigging cartel. (Paragraph 88) 9. In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing. Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2016 A director of a 2nd cartel member is the close friend of 1st cartel member, the sole proprietor of 3rd cartel member and the son of 4th cartel member’s director. (Paragraphs 5, 56) Additionally, 2nd cartel member arranged the relevant documents, prepared demand drafts and authorization certificates, and uploaded the tender documents (common IP address) of all cartel members. (Paragraphs 57-58) 4th cartel member aided the 2nd cartel member in fulfilling the eligibility criteria for participation in the tenders. Even though the 4th cartel member was eligible to bid, it did not participate in the tender process and suppressed its bid. (Paragraph 60) Even though the cartel members were separate legal entities and had bid as competitors, they were controlled by one person and had a commonplace for business. (Paragraphs 56-59) There is a meeting of mind among cartel members to rig the bidding process. Once an agreement on bid rigging is established, then it will be presumed to have an adverse effect. The onus to rebut the presumption is on the cartel members. (Paragraph 63) 10. Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others. Reference Case No. 02 of 2020 The CCI took note of the identical pricing in the bids submitted by the cartel members in Eastern Railway tenders for the eight years 2012-19. (Paragraphs 85, 86) By mutually deciding the prices to be quoted, the cartel members were manipulating the tenders. (Paragraph 95) CCI took note of the quotations and found that despite differences in manufacturing, transportation, packaging, and insurance, the cartel members fixed their quotation at INR 17,800. (Paragraphs 87, 88) The cartel members did not indicate the price break-down of their quotations as stipulated in the ‘Instructions to Tenderers for Manual and E-Tender and General Tender Conditions for Open Tenders’ as issued by the procurement authority. This was done with the sole intention of keeping their bids identical. (Paragraph 93) 11. Food Corporation of India v. Shivalik Agro Poly Products Ltd. and Others. Reference Case No. 07 of 2018. From the depositions of the cartel members, it was clear that they had decided to quote an identical price. (Paragraphs 54, 59) The CCI held that the cartel members indulged in bid rigging. (Paragraph 63) 12. In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing. Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2020 Cartel members submitted bids without prior experience in soil testing work and did not have any soil testing machine. (Paragraphs 10 (h), (j), (l)-(n).) One of the cartel members participated in the bidding process to support the designated bidder from being rejected due to insufficient number of bids. (Paragraph 60) To help the designated bidder win, other cartel members indulged in submitting fake invoices and false certificates to act as cover bidders. (Paragraph 132) 13. In Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra). Case No. 61 of 2013. The previous record showed the cartel members worked as sub-contractors for each other in various contracts. (Paragraphs 41, 124) Competitors working as sub-contractors for each other shows the anti-competitive arrangement. (Paragraph 125)

sections.title: SINGLE ECONOMIC ENTITY
sections.text: §2.07 SINGLE ECONOMIC ENTITY It must be kept in mind that the provision, with respect to agreement, is only applicable in cases when there is an agreement between parties who are completely independent of each other and operate autonomously. The CCI has noted and accepted the internationally accepted doctrine of single economic entity in its decisional practice.66 Exclsuive Motors Pvt. Ltd. v. Automobili Lamborghini S.P.A, CCI order, Case No. 52 of 2012, para. 6. Exclsuive Motors Pvt. Ltd. v. Automobili Lamborghini S.P.A and Another [2016], (COMPAT) 2014 SCC OnLine Comp AT 1, paras 11, 14. It has been noted by the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT that agreements between enterprises which form part of the same group, owing to the fact that the sole control is exercised by one company, cannot be assessed under section 3 of the Competition Act. The rationale adopted by the CCI is that the agreement referred to under section 3 refers to agreement between two enterprises that are independent of each other and not between two enterprises which are part of the same group. The rationale is that enterprises within the same group are not autonomous in their operations since the decisive influence over such enterprises can be held by a single enterprise. In the Exclusive Motors Private Limited v. Automobili Lamborghini S.P.A. (‘Lamborghini case’),67 Luxury cars such as Audi, SEAT, Lamborghini, Volkswagen, Skoda, Bentley, Bugatti and Porsche – were all considered within the same group of Volkswagen. even when ‘Volkswagen India’ had a separate legal existence, the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT noted since it belonged to the same group as Automobili Lamborghini, they were considered a single economic entity and internal agreements between them were outside the scope of section 3. Further, in the said case, the erstwhile COMPAT looked in detail at the manner in which operations are carried out by Volkswagen India and Automobili Lamborghini. It was seen that the operations were totally controlled by a common parent entity, and hence, they formed a single economic entity. A note of caution must be struck herein that in the Lamborghini case, it was clear that the control was exercised by one entity. There can be situations, while interpreting the Competition Act, where one enterprise can form a part of two or more different groups. So, in such a case, agreements between enterprises may or may not form a part of the single economic entity. The concept of single economic entity has to be seen on a de facto basis to see whether the parties to the agreement are acting in a manner which shows that they are in fact, a single unit. The aspect of single economic entity was also discussed in the Insurance cartel case,68 Insurance cartel case, paras 22-26. where section 27 order was passed against the cartelization by public sector insurance companies in rigging the bids submitted in response to the tenders floated by the Government of Kerala for selecting an insurance service provider for RSBY. The CCI was analysing whether the public sector insurance companies had engaged in bid rigging under the provisions of section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act. A defence was raised by the insurance companies, stating that they constitute a single economic entity as all the insurance companies were owned by General Insurance Company. It was also submitted by the insurance companies that, pursuant to the enactment of the General Insurance Business (Nationalization) Amendment Act, 2002, the Government of India holds 100% shares of each of the insurance companies and controls the management and affairs of the insurance companies through Department of Financial Services (Insurance Division), Ministry of Finance. However, the CCI did not accept the defence of single economic entity raised by the insurance companies. The CCI noted that although the public sector insurance companies are presently under the overall supervision of the Central Government, each of the insurance companies had placed a separate bid in response to the tenders issued by the Government of Kerala for the implementation of RSBY/CHIS schemes. Further, all decisions relating to the submission of bids, determination of bid amounts, business sharing arrangements, etc., were taken internally at the company level without any ex ante approval/directions from the Ministry of Finance. Even the decisions taken by the companies were not notified ex post to the Ministry. Based on the same, it was noted by the CCI that the insurance companies participated in the tenders independently, and the Ministry of Finance had no role to play.
