On this basis, the CCI held that the Ministry of Finance did not exercise any de facto (i.e., where an enterprise holds less than the majority of voting rights but in practice controls half of the votes) or de jure control (i.e., where an enterprise holds more than 50% of voting rights) over business decisions of insurance companies in submitting bids for impugned tenders and therefore, it was decided that insurance companies cannot be said to constitute a single economic unit. The approach adopted by the CCI in the cases discussed above shows that the CCI will look into the manner in which the business decisions are carried out by enterprises and not limit itself to the shareholding pattern and board composition to check whether the actions of enterprises are independent of each other or not, to decide whether the enterprises form a single economic unit. To form a single economic entity, the CCI will look into both de jure control (control by way of shareholding, board of directors, etc.) and de facto control (the manner in which operations of a company are carried out).

sections.title: DETERMINATION OF RELEVANT MARKET
sections.text: §2.08 DETERMINATION OF RELEVANT MARKET The relevant market is the boundary within which all anti-competitive practices are analysed by the CCI. In the High Committee Report,69 High Level Committee on Competition Policy and Law, 2000, para. 4.3.4. it is said that for horizontal agreement it is important to define relevant market. This will help to determine whether the concerned agreement between the parties is on substitutable products or not. It must be kept in mind that as per the statutory scheme provided under the Competition Act, there is no explicit requirement to construct a relevant market in an anti-competitive agreement investigation, like in an abuse of dominance investigation or a merger analysis. The decisional practice of the CCI clearly shows that there is no mandatory requirement under section 3(1) and section 3(3) of the Act as also under section 19(3) to determine and construct a relevant market. The CCI, in the Cement cartel case,70 Cement cartel case, para. 6.5.44. However, the erstwhile COMPAT in Lafarge India Limited and Others v. CCI and Another [2015], (COMPAT) 2015 SCC OnLine Comp AT 1120, para. 137, remanded the matter back to the CCI because of the procedural errors and the principles of natural justice was not followed while passing the final order. Thereafter, on 31 Aug. 2016, the CCI passed the final order (para. 219) reiterating that there is no requirement of the Competition Act to determine a relevant market under the provisions of section 3. held that there is no need to determine relevant market for establishing an anti-competitive agreement because there is a distinction between ‘market’ as in section 3 and ‘relevant market’ defined in section 4 of the Competition Act. However, in the case of the Coordination Committee,71 Coordination Committee case, paras 14, 33. the Supreme Court deviated from the decisional practice of CCI and observed that the first and foremost aspect that needs determination is the relevant market. In this judgment, the Supreme Court dealt with the substantive issue of contravening section 3(3)(b) by the members of the film and television artists trade union. The cartel members caused appreciable adverse effects by entering into agreements to prevent the telecast of the dubbed ‘Mahabharat’ serial on TV channels. Further, it was also held that the AAEC must be seen in the context of ‘relevant market’ as defined under section 2(r) of the Competition Act. However, if such an approach becomes the norm, it has the potential to have far-reaching implications, as it is likely to lead to an unnecessary pre-condition for analysing cartels. The CCI filed a clarification application to which the Supreme Court clarified that the determination of ‘relevant market’ is not a mandatory pre-condition for making assessment of cartels, particularly having regard to the statutory presumption contained in section 3 of the Competition Act itself.

sections.title: TYPES OF CARTEL
sections.text: §2.09 TYPES OF CARTEL Section 3(3) of the Competition Act enlists specific types of agreements/arrangements among persons or enterprises or association of persons which are engaged in identical or similar trade of goods or provision of services which are considered per se anti-competitive. According to section 3(3) of the Competition Act, agreements, including cartels, which: (a) directly or indirectly determine purchase or sales prices; (b) limit or control production, supply, markets, technical development, investment or the provision of services; (c) share the market or source of production or provision of services by way of allocation of the geographical area of the market, type of goods or services, or number of customers in the market or any other similar way; (d) directly or indirectly result in bid rigging or collusive bidding, are presumed to cause an AAEC in India. The four kinds of horizontal agreements, recognized as cartels under section 3(3) of the Competition Act, are discussed herein below. [A] Price Fixing Section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act provides that an agreement among enterprises at the same level of trade, which directly or indirectly determines purchase or sale prices, will be presumed to have an AAEC. The wording of the section is broad enough to cover not only the final price but also include other instances of terms of trade, like discounts, etc., which have an impact on the final price. Fixing prices is a concerted activity between competitors to set their prices to buy or sell goods/services. For instance, In Re: Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry,72 In Re: Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry, Suo Motu Case No. 05 of 2016, paras 159, 160, 192. competitors of the paper manufacturing industry decide to coordinate to draw a road map for increasing the price of paper. The CCI found direct evidence in the form of emails where it was clearly written that the paper manufacturers were discussing to collude and increase the price of the product. The fear of competition was stopping them form increasing the price. However, the paper manufacturers found a way to dodge the prevailing competition in the market by colluding through participating in physical meetings or exchanging emails to share commercially sensitive information. Sharing commercial information between market participants takes away the freedom to decide the price of the goods as per the supply and demand of the market. In People for Animals (PFA) and Another v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC) and Other,73 People for Animals (PFA) and Another v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC) and Other, Case Nos 09 and 36 of 2017, paras 114, 116, 197, 198, 201. The NCLAT has upheld the order of CCI in People of Animals v. CCI and Others, Competition Appeal (AT) 25 of 2022. the CCI decided that the National Egg Coordination Committee (NECC) had indulged in fixing and declaration of prices of eggs from 2009 to 2020. The NECC’s zonal production centres coordinated with the zonal consumption centres as well as other production centres, and after conducting a thorough demand and supply analysis, declared daily prices for eggs to be followed by all farmers. It was also observed that NECC was strictly implementing its anti-competitive conduct on farmers in each zone by informing them not to sell at the fixed price and threatening to impose penalties in case of non-compliance. Apart from violating section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act, NECC was also limiting the production and supply of eggs. Controlling production helped NECC to ensure that the farmers were selling eggs at fixed prices. From a competition law standpoint, the price of the product should be based on the holistic assessment of demand and supply. Thus, it should not be subject to the vagaries of whimsical diktats of NECC or any of its members which would determine the pricing of a product. CCI found that NECC was trying to control prices by threatening the farmers by levying or threatening to levy penalties on them. In terms of imposing other pricing conditions, such as discounts, CCI in M/s International Subscription Agency v. Federation of Publishers’ and Booksellers’ Associations in India (FPBAI) 74 M/s International Subscription Agency v. Federation of Publishers’ and Booksellers’ Associations in India (FPBAI), Case No. 33 of 2019, paras 18, 39. held that FPBAI’s discount policy prohibited all its members who deal in print journal and e-resources from offering any discount on the publishers’ price to the Indian subscribers. FPBAI was a federation of publishers, subscription agents and booksellers, and it provided a platform for all its members to discuss their problems. FPBAI constituted a committee with the objective of maintaining uniformity in the supply of books and journals, ensuring fair margins to booksellers and providing efficient service to libraries. However, under the rules of the committee, the members of FPBAI were not allowed to offer discounts to Indian subscribers and were threatened with dire consequences for violating the rules of GOC. Therefore, FPBAI was held guilty of contravention of section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act. [B] Output Controls/Limiting Production Section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act provides that any agreement among enterprises at the same level of trade which limits or controls production, supply, markets, technical development, investment or provision of services will be presumed to have an AAEC. The decisional practice of the CCI would show that, among other factors, the CCI looks at demand analysis of the concerned product, production capacity and capacity utilization of the market participants to understand whether there is any trend/pattern wherein they limit the production. In addition, a demand analysis is also done for a period of time to see whether the market participants indulged in limiting production even when there was a high demand for the product under the investigation, which will, in turn, have a resultant impact on the rise in prices. Economic analysis would show that prices cannot be kept at elevated levels if market participants do not keep supply levels sufficiently low in relation to demand. Therefore, in order to ensure that production and supply are low, there is very little capacity addition over time by the market participants and in some cases, the companies may engage in a reduction of capacity. There may also be cases where the market participants are not operating at optimal capacity. Such an action will lead to a demand-supply mismatch and, thereby, an increase in price. The CCI has made detailed observations of this clause in cases relating to the cement industry, entertainment industry, and pharmaceutical industry, among others. The CCI has noted in the Cement cartel case75 Cement cartel case, paras 276, 277, 290. that the act of limiting and controlling supplies on the part of the cement companies over the years has been aimed at first creating shortages leading to built-up demand which, in turn, caused upward rise in prices in the wake of high demand of the product in the market. Since in some seasons, the demand is more, the cement companies restricted the supplies just before the peak demand which allowed the cement companies to sell cement at a higher price. Therefore, the act of limiting and controlling production and supplies in the market causes upward movement in the price of that product/service. Similarly, the deliberate act of shortage in production and supplies as a result of cartelization in a product/service which has inelastic demand, inevitably results in high prices. Similar observations were also made in the NECC case (discussed supra). In the pharmaceutical industry too, the CCI has passed an order under section 27 of the Competition Act, holding the Chemist and Druggist Association liable for limiting and controlling supply76 Varca Chemists and Druggists v. Chemists and Druggists Association, Goa MRTPC 127/2009/DGIR4/28, para. 33; Vedant Bio Sciences v. Chemists and Druggists Association of Baroda C-87/2009/DGIR, paras 20.26, 20.27; M/s Sandhya Drug Agency v. ADDA, BDDA, AIOCD, IDMA, OPPI, Case No. 41 of 2011, para. 24; Peeveear Medical Agencies v. AIOCD and Janssen Case No. 30 of 2011, para. 18. and restricting the ability of the enterprises to supply and sell drugs in the market. The CCI’s order was a cease-and-desist order, along with certain directions to prohibit such anti-competitive practices. (as explained under the ‘Trade Association’ heading). Similarly, in the entertainment industry, the CCI has taken note of the practices carried out by regional film association as being anti-competitive since such film associations frame rules and regulations to impose restrictions on the free and unrestricted distribution and exhibition of serials/films. Also, in the transportation industry, the CCI has observed that trade associations have violated section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act by imposing restrictive terms for availing transportation services of their members. Such restrictions put the goods at indefinite halt and harm the business users and end users of the market. For instance, in CJ Darcl Logistics Ltd. v. Dumper and Dumper Truck Union Lime Stone and Another,77 CJ Darcl Logistics Ltd. v. Dumper and Dumper Truck Union Lime Stone and Another, Case No. 31 of 2019, paras 34, 35; National Association of Container Freight Stations, Chennai Chapter v. Trailer Owners Association and Others, Case No. 04 of 2018, paras 79-81. the Dumper Truck Union prevented the informant, which itself was a logistics company, from using its own vehicles to transport limestone. Further, threats were issued to force the informant to avail the service of the Dumper Truck Union and its association members. The concerted practice of the Dumper Truck Union resulted in limitation of transport services and helped all its members to fix a higher transportation rate. [C] Market Sharing Section 3(3)(c) of the Competition Act provides that any agreement among enterprises at the same level of trade, which shares the market or source of production or provision of services by way of allocation of the geographical area of the market, or type of goods or services, or number of customers in the market or any other similar way will be presumed to have an AAEC. Market sharing refers to agreements between competitors that divide up the market so that the market participants do not compete among themselves. Such market sharing can happen by way of geographical allocation or customer allocation. For instance, in the Signages bidding case,78 Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct by various bidders in supply and installation of signages at specified locations of State Bank of India across India, Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2020, paras 11.2, 39, 31, 37, 90, 91. The NCLAT upheld the decision of CCI in Hith Impex Pvt. Ltd and Another v. CCI, Competition App. (AT) Nos 21 and 65 of 2022; Macromedia Digital Imaging Pvt Ltd. and Another v. CCI, Competition App. (AT) Nos 24 and 26 of 2022. the CCI found that the opposite parties formed a cartel and decided to split the geographically allocated market among themselves with regard to the tender floated by SBI Infra Management Solutions Private Limited for the supply and installation of signages of SBI. The cartel member categorized the geographical market into three to four categories:(i) Type 1 (Bangalore, Chennai, Mumbai, Delhi): geographical presence and manufacturing presence. (ii) Type 2 & 3 (Hyderabad, Lucknow, Bhopal, Patna, Jaipur): additional cost is required to transport signages. (iii) Type 4 (Chandigarh, Kashmir, Kolkata, North East States): logistic constraints (remote areas). Accordingly, the cartel members allocated the market and violated section 3(3)(c) and (d) by entering into an agreement to share the geographical circles they would want to be geographically allocated for the tender. [D] Bid Rigging Section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act provides that any agreement among enterprises at the same level of trade, which directly or indirectly results in bid rigging or collusive bidding, will be presumed to have an AAEC. Bid rigging, defined in the explanation to section 3(3) of the Competition Act, means any agreement, between enterprises or persons engaged in identical or similar production or trading of goods or provision of services, which has the effect of eliminating or reducing competition for bids or adversely affecting or manipulating the process for bidding. The Competition Act has explained ‘bid rigging’ as an agreement, between parties engaged in identical or similar production or trading of goods or provision of services, which has the effect of eliminating or reducing competition for bids or adversely affecting or manipulating the process for bidding. Since both bid rigging and collusive bidding are interchangeable, so the explanation is equally applicable to collusive bidding.79 Excel Crop case, paras 40-42, 46, 49, 56, 57. (SC). The CCI has noted that collusive bidding or bid rigging may be of different kinds, namely, agreements to submit identical bids, agreements as to who shall submit the lowest bid, agreements for the submission of cover bids (voluntarily submit inflated bids), agreements not to bid against each other, agreements on common norms to calculate prices or terms of bids, agreements to squeeze out outside bidders, agreements designating bid winners in advance on a rotational basis, or on a geographical or customer allocation basis. There are various forms of collusive bidding or bid rigging, such as: (i) Identical or Level bidding: Bidders coordinate to quote identical prices of their bids. Bidders can agree to adhere to publish price lists, raise prices by a specific increment, fix or eliminate discount, avoid advertisement of prices, or maintain specified price differentials based on quantity, type, or size of product. (ii) Proxy or Cover bidding: Bidders artificially increase the price so that the bid will never be accepted or submit the bid without fulfilling the technical requirement. (iii) Bid Suppression: Bidders refrain from bidding or withdrawing a previously submitted bid so that the designated bidder will win the tender. (iv) Bid rotation: Bidders collude to take turns to win bids on different occasions. (v) Bid allocation: Bidders share the geographical or product market and agree not to participate or compete for the tender. The CCI has observed that in the event the tenders submitted are not a result of one’s individual economic calculation but a result of consultation among several players submitting the tender, competition is said to be distorted in such a case. A case in this regard is the Excel Crop case80 Ibid. where all the firms set identical prices in their bids in spite of the firms having very distinct cost structures and sale prices. In all bid rigging cases, the DG and CCI make a detailed assessment of the cost structure of the company, a comparison of the input cost and final cost, production capacity and capacity utilization, etc., of each of the market participants to see whether there is any agreement among the market participants. The Supreme Court, in the case of the Excel Crop case,81 Ibid. has indicated that collusive tendering or bid rigging is the practice whereby firms, which hold 100% market share, agree among themselves to collaborate on the response of a tender either by fixing the price or by boycotting the tender. The firms quoted identical bids for various tenders, even when the cost of production, geographical location and profit margins were different. Further, the firms disagreed with certain conditions given in the 2011 tender, so they acted in concert by boycotting the said tender floated by the procurement authority. The firms had the option to approach the procurement authority to seek clarification or amendment of such conditions. Rather than following the genuine path, the firms colluded among themselves to suppress their bids.82 Ibid. Bid rigging occurs when two or more enterprises (competing bidders or potential bidders) agree among themselves not to compete genuinely with each other for particular tenders which affects the prices they bid for, or they attempt to secretly influence the outcome of a contract or series of contracts. This process allows one of the participants in the agreement to win the tender. Participants may fix prices, suppress, cover, rotate and allocate bids. It is to be noted that the definition of bid rigging also includes any agreement among enterprises to manipulate the process of bidding. The entire process of bidding would entail the submission of technical and financial bids, negotiations with the counterparty, winning the bid, and the performance of the contract. In this regard, the erstwhile COMPAT has held, in the Excel Crop case, that bid rigging cases would encompass rigging in any or all of the processes involved in bidding, and not only limiting to first submission of technical and pricing bids. The purpose of rigging the bid is to concert their bargaining power over the procurement authority and ultimately tilt the balance of negotiations towards their own favour to gain higher profit without worrying about the force of competition in the market and that they will be poached by competitors. Generally, in cartelization, cartels tend to artificially increase the bidding price or lower the quantity of goods or services.83 Excel Crop case, para. 41. (SC); LPG-I case, para. 78. (SC). Through collusion, the cartel members can use their bargaining power to get the upper hand during negotiations with the procurement authority. As noted above, the decisional practice of the CCI shows that instances of bid rigging are very common in cases of government procurement. One of the common defences which are raised by cartel participants is that they are dealing with government, which is a strong counterparty, and hence, their practice of bid rigging does not cause any appreciable adverse effect. However, this defence has not been accepted by the CCI. The CCI In Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra) (‘Surendra Prasad order’),84 In Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra), Case No. 90 of 2016, paras 114, 122, 131. Maharashtra State Power Generation Co. Ltd. (MAHAGENCO) which was incorporated by the Government of Maharashtra for the generation of power in the State of Maharashtra invited tenders for coal liaisoning, and to supervise the quality and quantity of coal supplied. The opposite parties 2, 3 & 4 provided the service for coal liaisons, and they participated in the bidding process. The opposite parties 2 to 4 colluded to distribute the tenders among themselves. Ultimately, the opposite parties 2 to 4 got the bid. The CCI held the opposite parties guilty of section 3(3)(c) and (d) by establishing parallel conduct, sub-contracting and market allocation which was further corroborated with various corroborative evidence like purchasing the tender document on the same dates, communication between opposite parties, including details of financial information, etc. Similarly, in In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing 85 In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing, Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2016, paras 44, 45, 46. (‘Pune Municipal Corporate order’), the CCI made an extensive analysis on whether the cartel members were engaged in identical business or not. In this case, the tender was floated for procurement of sewing machines for distribution among the backward class people. The informant alleged that opposite parties colluded among themselves to rig the tendering process. The opposite party 1 was the seller of the sewing machine, the opposite parties 2, 3 and 4 were authorized dealers of opposite party 1, and the opposite party 5 was the government authority that floated the tender. Some opposite parties opposed the proceeding because they were engaged in a different business; hence, section 3(3)(d) would not apply to them. However, the CCI widely interpreted the term ‘engaged in’ to include all the opposite parties/cartel members in identical trade because they were participating in the same bidding process. This interpretation allows CCI to proceed against the proxy bidders & suppressive bidders. Such interpretation has only been applied to parties entering into an anti-competitive agreement to rig the bidding procedure, not to the other categories of anti-competitive agreements under section 3(3) of the Competition Act. [1] Role of the Procurement Authority in Bid Rigging Inquiries The decisional practice of the CCI has shown that most of the bid rigging case (if not all) happens in case of government procurement. In matters where there is an involvement of the Public Procurement Authority in facilitating the bid rigging cartel, then CCI uses the argument that the Public Procurement Authority and the bidders are not engaged in identical business. Thereby, Public Procurement Authority and bidders can never form a cartel. Further, in cases where there was an involvement of the Public Procurement Authority in facilitating the bid rigging cartel, there has been uncertainty regarding the collection of evidence from the Public Procurement Authority. For example, in Surendra Prasad order,86 Surendra Prasad order, para. 89. the CCI did not investigate the MAHAGENCO (the Public Procurement Authority) or examine the officials of MAHAGENCO. The officers of the Public Procurement Authority provide important evidence in their deposition, so taking statements from them helps the CCI to reach a conclusion. In the People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd.,87 People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd., Case No. 90 of 2016, paras 19, 32, 33. the DG investigated Pune Zilla Parishad and found evidence that helped CCI establish the agreement between bidders. Similarly, in the LPG-II case,88 LPG-II case, paras 42, 43. The NCLAT upheld the order passes by the CCI by holding that the identical behaviours of the parties were a result of collusion. where CCI examined HPCL and considered the submissions made by HPCL to hold that certain bidders were not liable under section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act. The facts of this case are similar to the LPG-I case. Unlike the LPG-I case, the fact of the LPG-II case shows that the tendering authority did not set the final price for the bids. The CCI initiated an inquiry against the cartelization to rig the bidding process of Tender No. 1 and Tender No. 2 floated by HPCL for 14.2 kg LPG cylinders. The tenders are for the supply of 14.2 kg LPG cylinders. The parties parallelly increased their quotation for procurement of the tenders, and HPCL suffered losses by placing orders at a higher rate. The CCI could not establish the violation of section 3(3)(d) for Tender No. 1. However, for Tender No. 2, sixty-six bidders were investigated and held liable for the contravention of section 3(3)(d) read with section 3(1) by manipulating the bidding process. Apart from certain bidders, fifty-one bidders were found to have indulged in bid suppression by withdrawing their bids from Tender No. 2. However, the CCI in Surendra Prasad order failed to consider the procurement authority’s consistent erring practice prevailing in the past to understand the involvement of the procurement authority in the manipulation of the bidding process. On 3 March 2005, MAHAGENCO had invited tenders for coal liaisoning. The four participants of the bidding process were B.S.N. Joshi & Sons Ltd. and all three opposite parties. B.S.N. Joshi was the L1 bidder, but MAHAGENCO did not award the contract to B.S.N. Joshi due to an ongoing litigation before the Bombay High Court (Nagpur Bench). B.S.N. Joshi appealed before the Supreme Court and the MAHAGENCO was ordered to consider the offer of B.S.N. Joshi. The order of the Supreme Court was disobeyed by MAHAGENCO. So, again, B.S.N. Joshi went to the Supreme Court to file a contempt petition against MAHAGENCO. In the contempt petition. In the contempt petition, the Supreme Court acknowledged that MAHAGENCO is favouring the cartelization among opposite parties 2 to 489 B.S.N. Joshi & Sons Ltd. v. Ajoy Mehta & Anr. [2008], (SC) Contempt Petition 245 of 2007, para. 17. and said that:17. We are distressed to see that MAHAGENCO had been encouraging the formation of a cartel and, thus, allowing the rate of transportation of coal to go high up. Unless a power generating company takes all measures to cut down such malpractices, the generation cost of electricity is bound to go higher and ultimately the same would be passed on to the consumers of electricity. We hope a public sector undertaking would take adequate and appropriate measures to meet the said contingency in future. Then, MAHAGENCO rectified its conduct by awarding the contract to B.S.N. Joshi on 3 January 2009. However, on 12 September 2009, the contract was terminated by MAHAGENCO. After the termination, MAHAGENCO again awarded the contract to opposite parties 2 to 4. Moreover, since September 2009, MAHAGENCO has floated four tenders, but all four tenders were cancelled for various reasons. Due to cancellations, opposite parties 2-4 continued liaisoning coal to MAHAGENCO. The opposite parties 2-4 benefitted from this shot-gap arrangement. Mr Surendra Prasad filed information under section 19(1) of the Competition Act. The informant alleged that MAHAGENCO was facilitating the formation of cartelization between opposite parties 2 to 4. The CCI, with a 5:1 majority, passed a section 26(2) order.90 Surendra Prasad order, Case No 61 of 2013. Justice (retire) S.N. Dhingra gave a dissenting order. Justice (retire) S.N. Dhingra held that there is a prima facie violation of section 3(3)(d) by opposite parties 2, 3 & 4. The procurement authority is facilitating the cartelization for bid rigging. Further, the procurement authority along with opposite parties 2 to 4 are violating section 3(4)(d). Also, the procurement authority abused its dominant position under section 4(2)(c). Then, the informant appealed before the erstwhile COMPAT and got a favouring order.91 Shri Surendra Prasad v. CCI, Appeal No. 43 of 2014, paras 26, 29. The erstwhile COMPAT noted the observation of the Supreme Court and directed the DG to conduct an investigation, but in the said order, the DG was directed not to investigate the MAHAGENCO as it was a part of the cartel. So, in the Surendra Prasad order, MAHAGENCO was not investigated, and it was held that:No doubt, the purport of the order of the Hon’ble Appellate Tribunal was to caution the DG not to proceed against MAHAGENCO upon the premise that it was part of the cartel. Hence, the DG, upon investigation, did not deem it appropriate to proceed against MAHAGENCO presumably because a cartel can be formed only by players who are engaged in the similar activities. In these circumstances, nothing would have turned up by even investigating MAHAGENCO in the present matter when the allegations of cartelisation could have been analyzed only against the bidders under the scheme of the Act as provided under Section 3(3) read with Section 3(1) of the Act. Further, the Supreme Court, in the LPG-I case,92 LPG-I case, paras 102, 103. (SC). made an observation regarding the importance of examining Public Procurement Authority while investigating a bid rigging matter. The Supreme Court held that:we come to the conclusion that the inference drawn by the CCI on the basis of evidence collected by it are duly rebutted by the appellants and the appellants have been able to discharge the onus that shifted upon them on the basis of factors pointed out by the CCI. However, at that stage, CCI failed to carry the matter further by having required and necessary inquiry that as needed in the instant case… We are emphasising here that in such a watertight tender policy of IOCL which gave IOCL full control over the tendering process, it was necessary to summon IOCL. This would have cleared many aspects which are shrouded in mystery and the dust has not been cleared. It is important to note that previously, the Supreme Court of India had also observed that there is an involvement of the Public Procurement Authority in encouraging and facilitating the bid rigging cartel. As noted above, the CCI in People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. 93 People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd., Case No. 90 of 2016, paras 31, 32. could not adjudicate Public Procurement Authority for facilitating the collusion because such conduct does not fall within the domain of current competition law. With the amendment in competition law, through the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, the scope of anti-competitive agreements was widened which gives the power to the CCI to examine the conduct of enterprises which may not be at the same horizontal level but plays a key role is facilitating cartel (akin to hub-and-spoke issues). We can expect that the CCI would enforce competition law by including any facilitator of anti-competitive agreement under section 3(3) of the Competition Act. Hence, this amendment would also check the Public Procurement Authority from facilitating bid rigging under section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act. This amendment would make the Act more comprehensive and effective by preventing Public Procurement Authority from escaping liability. [E] Hub-and-Spoke Cartel The ambit of section 3(1) of the Competition Act is wider, and it covers other anti-competitive agreements apart from the horizontal and vertical anti-competitive agreements. Moreover, the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, broadens the scope of anti-competitive agreements and would help the CCI to enforce competition law by including a party facilitating an anti-competitive horizontal agreement under section 3(3) of the Competition Act. After the said amendment, section 3(3) violation is applicable to those parties who may not be engaged in identical or similar trade. Hence, common agents, distributors and even Public Procurement Authority will now be amenable under violation of section 3 as third parties facilitating the existence of cartel. Though this amendment has not explicitly mentioned the term ‘hub-and-spoke,’ but the ultimate objective behind this amendment is to deal with such cartels. Previously, the CCI was hindered from enforcing competition law due to the absence of a specific provision for ‘hub-and-spoke’ cartel. In the past, the allegations of a hub-and-spoke cartel have been expressly raised before the CCI in the Fx Enterprise Solutions India Pvt. Ltd. v. Hyundai Motors (‘Hyundai Motors case’)94 Fx Enterprise Solutions India Pvt. Ltd. v. Hyundai Motors, Case Nos 36 and 82 of 2014, para. 7. and later in the Samir Agrawal v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd. and Others (‘Cab Aggregators case’). 95 Samir Agrawal v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd. and Others, Case No. 37 of 2018, para. 15. (CCI). In both orders, the CCI did not find a reason to make a finding on the presence of a hub-and-spoke cartel. In the Hyundai Motors case, there is no finding on the existence of a hub-and-spoke cartel, however, in the Samir Agrawal order, the CCI elaborated on the following traditional circumstances for a hub-and-spoke cartel to exist: (a) the spokes must use a third-party platform (or, the ‘hub’) to exchange sensitive information, including information on prices which can facilitate price fixing; and (b) there needs to be a conspiracy to fix prices, which requires the existence of collusion. The Cab Aggregators case96 Cab Aggregators case [2021], (SC) (2021) 3 SCC 136, paras 4, 8. (SC). reached the Supreme Court, where the Supreme Court also explained the concept of hub-and-spoke arrangement by saying the spokes (i.e., competitors of a relevant market) require the hub (i.e., third-party platform) to facilitate cartelization by exchanging price-sensitive information which can facilitate price fixing. In the Cab Aggregators case, it was alleged that cab aggregators like Ola and Uber are facilitating cartelization among taxi drivers. However, the Supreme Court could not find that cab aggregators had a hub-and-spoke arrangement with the drivers.

sections.title: STRUCTURAL FACTORS AIDING CARTELIZATION
sections.text: §2.10 STRUCTURAL FACTORS AIDING CARTELIZATION While cartelization can happen in any sector, the CCI has noted that there are certain industries which are more prone to cartelization than other industries due to certain inherent structural factors prevalent in those industries. For instance, there are numerous investigations in the pharmaceutical industry, film and entertainment industry, tyre industry, cement industry, paper industry, etc. Although the CCI has observed that just because such industry characteristics are present, it does not ipso facto mean that market participants have indulged in a cartel. In fact, there have been instances where the CCI has noted that although the industry has the structural characteristics that aid cartelization, the market participants have not indulged in cartelization (e.g., the Tyre case). However, as a part of economic analysis, the CCI does look at the industry characteristics in all cartel cases. The CCI has, in its decisions, referred to the following structural factors in an industry which are prone to cartelization. [A] Highly Concentrated/Oligopolistic Market The CCI has noted that in an oligopolistic market, it is more likely that each market participant is aware of the other participants’ behaviour. In an oligopolistic market, interdependence between firms is an important characteristic which would imply that each firm in such a market considers the likely reactions of other firms while making independent decisions particularly regarding prices and output. Though oligopolistic markets can lead to competitive outcomes, the outcomes may not always be market-driven but rather a result of concerted effort or collusion. Oligopoly markets are infamous for being recognized as the perfect market for cartels to thrive by charging higher prices at a supra-competitive level. The Supreme Court in the LPG-I case97 LPG-I case, para. 97. (SC). and the NCLAT in the LPG-II case98 LPG-II case, paras 33, 52. (NCLAT). discuss the ‘Theory of Oligopolistic Interdependence’ by saying that the oligopoly market itself facilitates cartels because the market players are interdependent, they are aware of each other’s presence and follow each other’s business strategies. Interdependency allows them to collude without any direct or indirect exchange of information, hence, creating a realm of conscious parallelism which lies outside the scope of competition law. In this regard, it is apposite to point out the observations of the CCI in the Tyre case: In a market which is oligopolistic in nature, it is more likely that each market player is aware of the actions of the other and influences each other’s decisions. No doubt, interdependence between firms is an important characteristic of such a market which would mean that each firm in such a market takes into account the likely reactions of other firms while making independent decisions particularly as regards prices and output. Though oligopolistic markets can lead to competitive outcomes, the outcomes may not always be market driven but rather the result of concerted effort or collusion. The interdependence between firms can lead to collusion – both implicit as well as explicit. Thus, high concentration may provide a structural reasoning for collusive action resulting in parallelism (price or output), yet it is very important to differentiate between ‘rational’ conscious parallelism arising out of the interdependence of the firms’ strategic choices and parallelism stemming from purely concerted action. Thus, inferring of cartels would require further evidences. Economic theory has demonstrated convincingly that ‘conscious parallelism’, is not uncommon in homogeneous oligopolistic markets. Competing firms are bound to be conscious of one another’s activities in all phases, including marketing and pricing. Aware of such outcomes especially where there is little real difference in product the Commission is of the opinion that it is quite probable that in many such instances, conscious parallelism may be dictated solely by economic necessity. Avoidance of price wars is a common instance where this takes place. Often, DG and CCI have observed that empirical studies have shown that bid rigging is more likely to occur in a concentrated market where only a small number of companies supply the goods or service and the suppliers are repetitive bidders.99 In Re: Shri Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Ltd. & Others, Case No. 03 of 2011, paras 14.14.2; Director General (Supplies and Disposals) v. Puja Enterprises Basti and Others, Case No. 1 of 2012, para. 5. Being limited in number, it becomes easier for them to coordinate and reach an agreement. Therefore, in a highly concentrated market, the market participants may engage in parallel behaviour, but the key area of analysis is to differentiate between rational conscious parallelism arising out of the interdependence of the firms’ strategic choices and purely concerted action. However, under the garb of an oligopolistic market, an entity cannot justify its identical bidding as a general feature of a highly concentrated market. In case of the Aluminium Phosphide case, the Supreme Court rejected the defence of identical bidding in bid rigging cases because bids are quoted by considering the supply and demand of the product.100 Excel Crop case, para. 49. (SC). The said ratio of the Supreme Court has been applied by the CCI to reject the defence that identical price quotes for the tenders of sand and coal transportation is not anti-competitive because of the oligopolistic nature of the market. The CCI held that the defence of identical bidding being a general feature of an oligopolistic market cannot be used as a defence in bid rigging cases.101 Western Coalfields Limited v. SSV Coal Carriers Private Limited and Others, Case No. 34 of 2015, paras 52, 58. [B] Demand and Supply Conditions The CCI has noted that significant changes in demand or supply conditions tend to destabilize cartel arrangement (in that case, it was big rigging agreements), but a constant, predictable flow of demand, by way of frequent orders from the consumer, tends to increase the risk of collusion. Further, the CCI noted that if the demand for a product is seasonal and predictable, producers may take strategic decisions and tend to cartelize. However, if the demand for a product itself varies, because of strong substitutes in the market, or unpredictable sale conditions, or unpredictable bids being floated, strategic decisions cannot be taken, and cartelization may become difficult owing to the uncertain demand-supply conditions of the product itself.102 Tyres case, para. 280. The reason for that is that in an industry which has a stable and predictable flow of demand, the market participants can have an understanding among themselves as to who will ‘win’ the contract for a particular time and thereafter, if necessary, share the spoils with the other market participants. In a market which has a predictable demand (like in the case of frequent government procurement), the market participants can indulge in bid rigging as well as market sharing so that everyone can win a contract at an inflated rate and not a competitive rate. [C] Homogeneous Product The CCI, in various decisions, has noted that when the products or services offered by companies are identical or very similar, it is easier for them to reach an agreement on a common price structure. In the event the demand for the good is inelastic (like cement, LPG, etc.), firms intending to rig bids or engage in other cartel behaviour feel more secure, knowing that the purchaser has few, if any, good alternatives and thus their efforts to raise prices are more likely to be successful. In the Cement cartel case,103 Cement cartel case, para. 7(x). it was noted by the DG that cement has only two to three categories, i.e., Ordinary Portland Cement, Portland Pozzolana Cement and Portland Blast Furnace Slag Cement. This shows that the nature of cement is almost homogenous in nature. Market consisting of homogenous products allows the cement manufacturers to fix the prices at a huge profit margin. [D] Active Trade Association A famous quote by Adam Smith, which has even been mentioned in some of the decisions of the CCI, is that: ‘People of the same trade seldom meet together, even for merriment and diversion, but the conversion ends in a conspiracy against the public, or some contrivance to raise prices.’ Sometimes, trade associations or the members of trade associations collude together to form a cartel. In the Coordination Committee case,104 Coordination Committee case, para. 47. two associations joined together in giving a call of boycott of the competing member. In such cases, the trade association cannot take the defence of the right of trade union to hide their anti-competitive practices under the cloak of the fundamental right of trade union under Article 19 of the Constitution of India, 1950.105 Article 19(1)(c) of the Constitution of India, 1950 guarantees to all the citizen of India the right to form associations and union or cooperative societies. However, the mere formation of a trade association is not per se anti-competitive. The provisions of competition law would be applicable when that trade association transgress its legal contours and facilitates collusive or collective decision-making with the intention of fixing price, limiting or controlling the sale or production, or any other anti-competitive activities by its members, it violates the provisions of the Competition Act.106 LPG-II case, para. 46 (CCI); Cj Darcl Logistics Ltd. v. Dumper and Dumper Truck Union Lime Stone and Another, Case No. 31 of 2019, para. 35, where the trade association was limiting the transportation services by mandating stakeholders to only avail the services of members of the trade association. Further, rather than competing, all the members of trade association uniformly fixed a higher price for its service. Trade associations can be used as legitimate forums for members of a business to promote standards, innovation and competition. In fact, trade associations are present in most industries with the primary objective of taking up and discussing industry issues, industry lobbying, resolving disputes between members, etc. The trade associations are forums for building consensus among its members on policy-related issues of the industry and safeguarding the interests of all the stakeholders. The stakeholders of an industry use trade associations as a platform to meet and discuss common issues. The CCI in National Association of Container Freight Stations v. Trailer Owner Association and Others 107 National Association of Container Freight Stations v. Trailer Owner Association and Others, Case No. 04 of 2018, para. 76; Advertising Agencies Guild v. IBF & its Members, Case No. 35 of 2013, para. 7. says if the member of a trade association uses this platform for legitimate purposes, then it would provide benefits, such as setting common technical standards for products or interfaces; setting the standards for admission to membership of a profession; arranging education and training for those wishing to join the industry; paying for and encouraging research into new techniques or developing a common response to changing government policy. Thus, mere membership or participation in a trade association may not amount to a violation of competition law. But when the members misuse the facilities provided by a trade association to meet and enter into an anti-competitive agreement, then the members and trade association would fall under the violation of section 3(3) of the Competition Act. Internationally, as well as in India, trade associations remain vulnerable to stepping beyond the limits placed by competition law because, by definition, they involve meetings, discussions and cooperation among various, often virtually all competitors, in a particular line of business. As such, the decisional practice of the CCI would show that the CCI makes a detailed analysis of whether there is an active trade association in the industry concerned, and if yes, what are the roles and responsibilities of such a trade association and identify the nature of communication which happen in such trade association meetings. Trade association has been seen as a plus factor in most cartel cases decided by the CCI. It is apposite to note that even the CCI has realized the anti-competitive risks that may emerge from a trade association meeting and has passed a decision in an entertainment industry matter108 M/s Crown Theatre v. Kerala Film Exhibitors Federation, Case No. 16 of 2014, para. 9(f). mandating the concerned trade association to have, in place, a competition compliance manual and undertake effective competition law compliance training for all the office bearers of the trade association. This is an interesting advocacy-based initiative taken by the CCI, especially in the light of improving efficiency in the competition law enforcement.

sections.title: AAEC IN INDIA
sections.text: §2.11 AAEC IN INDIA As mentioned above, section 3(3) of the Competition Act envisages that once ingredients of section 3(3) are established, i.e., the market participants have entered into an agreement for price fixing, market allocation, limiting production/supply or bid rigging, there is no further need for the CCI to determine whether the factors mentioned in section 19(3) of the Act (described in details below) are met as there is a presumption in the Act that such agreements cause AAEC. The AAEC is presumed because the anti-competitive agreement covered under section 3(3) of the Competition Act is of the most pernicious nature, whereas the anti-competitive agreement mentioned under section 3(4) of the Competition Act must be seen with the factors under section 19(3) of the Competition Act because of they are less pernicious agreements.109 FICCI v. United Producers and Others, Case No. 01 of 2009, para. 23.6. Thereafter, the statutory scheme of the Act is that the onus shifts on entities facing charges of a cartel to prove that there are pro-competitive effects of such agreements which outweigh the anti-competitive effects. The entities must rebut the said presumption by showing the positive factors contained in section 19(3)(d) to (f) of the Competition Act with adequate evidence. Once the entities lead adequate evidence, the CCI examines it from the prism of the ‘rule of reason’ by balancing the factors mentioned in section 19(3) of the Competition Act.110 LPG-II case, paras 104, 105. (CCI). In the case of an investigation of an anti-competitive agreement, a balancing test has to be conducted of the pro-competitive effects of the agreement and the anti-competitive effects thereof. The balancing test needs to be done in light of any or all the factors enumerated in section 19(3) of the Competition Act. Clause (a) to (c) of section 19(3) deals with negative factors which restrict the competition in the market because of the anti-competitive effect of the agreement, whereas clause (d) to (f) of section 19(3) deals with positive factors which provides pro-competitive effects, such as, enhancing the efficiency of the distribution process and contributing to consumer welfare.111 Cement cartel case, Case No. 29 of 2010, para. 288. It is for the entities to prove the pro-competitive effects of their arrangement.112 Vedanta Bio Sciences, Vadodara v. Chemists and Druggists Association of Baroda, Case No. C-87/2009/DGIR, para. 96. It must be said, however, in a case of cartel investigation, once it has been proved that the market participants have engaged in a cartel, it is very difficult to show that such an arrangement has a pro-competitive element. The primary analysis done is whether there is any agreement among the market participants for fixing price, bid rigging or market allocation and if there is any agreement among them, the CCI has imposed orders under section 27 of the Competition Act. It is important to note that the Supreme Court in the Coordination Committee case113 Coordination Committee case, para. 9. held that cartels have no defence since they are considered to have ipso facto AAEC. However, there was a change in trend after the Supreme Court in the LPG-I case114 LPG-I case, paras 71, 72, 75, 87, 91-92. (SC). exonerated the bid rigging cartel because the entities were able to show pro-competitive effects in the market, such as twelve new entrants in the market. It was found that the market was an oligopsony market with only three oil companies, and the price to determine the supply of cylinders to these oil companies had become an open secret for all market participants. Further, prior to the bidding process, another oil company floated a tender, and the rates of quotation were available publicly. Hence, the previous tender obviously became a guiding factor for bidders in the concerning bidding process. Even if the entities are from different regions having different production costs, they had to quote the same amount and also accept a lesser margin so that they could survive in the market. The CCI interprets the ratio laid down in the LPG-I case to say entities need to rebut the presumption of appreciable adverse effect by showing positive effects under section 19(3)(d)-(f) emanating from the existence of such agreement.115 In Re: Cartelisation in the supply of Protective Tubes to Indian Railways, Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2020, para. 33. Also, see Chief Materials Manager, North Western Railway v. Moulded Fibreglass Products, Reference Case No. 03 of 2018, paras 55, 56. However, by looking at previous case laws, where the Appellate Authority or the Supreme Court set aside the CCI order in cartel matter, it is because CCI failed to appreciate the evidence holistically. Nonetheless, ignorance of the factors under section 19(3) of the Competition Act could be fatal, so it’s always better to analyse the factors mentioned under section 19(3). A thorough analysis would lead to a well-reasoned order and would reduce the chances of being challenged and set aside by a higher judiciary body. For example, in the Coordination Committee case,116 Coordination Committee case, para. 48. See, CCI order of Coordination Committee case, Case No. 16 of 2011, para. 7.2.11. even after holding that there was an anti-competitive agreement between the film associations to control and limit the distribution and exhibition of dubbed serials, the CCI also analysed the factors under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. Based on the CCI’s observation, the conduct of the film associations acted as an entry barrier to a new content into the market, and the bylaws of the film associations could not show any efficiency in terms of improvement of services or production. While one of the defences that have been raised before the CCI is that the cartel participants were small and had minimal market share, and hence an agreement among them should not cause any AAEC in India, the CCI has noted that companies involved in a cartel may be having minuscule market shares individually; the CCI, however, is concerned about their ability to collectively affect the competition in the market. The CCI is concerned about the effect their action will have when seen in aggregation to the actions of their co-players in the market. As such, the CCI, in the LPG-I case, Coordination Committee case and the Cement cartel case, undertook a brief analysis as to whether the agreement entered into between market participants has caused any AAEC in India. Based on the factors mentioned under section 19(3) of the Competition Act, the CCI noted the following: [A] Creation of Barriers to Dissuade New Entrants/Existing Player to Expand in the Market In the Coordination Committee case, 117 Coordination Committee case, para. 48. the film association acted in concert to create an entry barrier for the entry of dubbed content in the State of West Bengal. The concerted action of the film association limited the supply of dubbed serials in West Bengal, amounting to a violation of section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act because the producer, distributor and exhibitor of dubbed serials and films were stopped from telecasting dubbed TV series. In Maruti & Company v. Karnataka Chemists & Druggists Association & Others,118 Maruti & Company v. Karnataka Chemists & Druggists Association & Others, Case No. 71 of 2013, para. 5.36. the chemist and druggist association mandated non-objection certificates (NOC) from the association before the appointment of pharmaceutical stockiest. The CCI noted that seeking NOC amounts to the creation of entry barriers for the stockiest because it hinders the entry or growth of the stockiest in the market and restricts their freedom to do business with persons of their choice. [B] Foreclosure of Competition by Hindering Entry into the Market The Supreme Court in the Coordination Committee case upheld the observation of the CCI by holding that the film associations were harming the freedom of trade by hindering the entry of dubbed serials into the market. Such restrictive practice forecloses the business opportunities for the entities which were engaged in the production, distribution, and exhibition of dubbed serials.119 Coordination Committee case, paras 16, 18. Similarly, in Mr. G. Krishnamurthy v. Karnataka Film Chamber of Commerce (KFCC) & Others (‘KFCC case’),120 Mr. G. Krishnamurthy v. Karnataka Film Chamber of Commerce (KFCC) and Others, Case No. 42 of 2017, paras 107, 120. CCI order was upheld by NCLAT in Karnataka Film Chamber of Commerce KFCC & Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2018], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 96 of 2018. the KFCC and its members acted in concert to create entry barriers against the screening of dubbed cinemas. The film associations restored to threats and prevented the release of non-Kannada films dubbed in Kannada language, hence creating barriers to entry of new content in the market as well as causing foreclosure of competition. [C] Accrual of Benefits to Consumers In the Cement cartel case,121 Cement cartel case, para. 289. the CCI noted that the cement manufactures had intentionally acted to reduce production so that the prices could go up. Such concerted action resulted in an increase in cement prices which was detriment to the consumer. Similarly, in the Coordination Committee case,122 Coordination Committee case, para. 48. Also, see CCI order, Case No. 16 of 2011, para. 5.24. it was observed that by restricting the dubbed series in the market, the film associations were depriving the consumers of watching programmes of their choice. Also, in the KFCC case,123 KFCC case, paras 119, 120. the CCI could not perceive any benefits to consumers under section 19(3)(d) or has resulted in any improvement of production or distribution of goods or services under section 19(3)(e) of the Competition Act by preventing entry of dubbed cinemas. In bidding cases, fair competition in the bidding process would ensure benefit to the consumer, i.e., procurement authority and end consumer. In this regard, the CCI In Re: Cartelisation in the supply of Protective Tubes to Indian Railways 124 In Re: Cartelisation in the supply of Protective Tubes to Indian Railways, Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2020, para. 37. has noted that bidders cannot be allowed to fix any arbitrary price or limit the quantities. By manipulating the bidding process, the bidders are infringing the right of the procurement authority to make free choices as a consumer and ultimately harming the railway passengers. Furthermore, the CCI in the People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. 125 People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd, Case No. 90 of 2016, para. 97. held that collusion to fix the price for the quotation would have an adverse effect on the fair price discovery process and the price paid by the public procurement authority. [D] Improvements in Production or Distribution or Promotion of Technical, Scientific and Economic Development The past orders of CCI show that a cartel has never been able to demonstrate the consumer benefits, improvement in product or promotion of innovation or economic development.126 In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct by various bidders in supply and installation of signages at specified locations of State Bank of India across India, Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2020, para. 100; In Re: Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry, Suo Motu Case No. 05 of 2016, para. 202; In Re: Cartelisation by Shipping Lines in the matter of provision of Maritime Motor Vehicle Transport Services to the Original Equipment Manufacturers, Suo Motu Case No. 10 of 2014, para. 178; In Re: Cartelisation in Industrial and Automotive Bearings, Suo Motu Case No. 05 of 2017, para. 25. The previous decisions of CCI show that in cartel cases, there can’t be any improvement in the production or distribution of goods or services, as provided under section 19(3)(e) of the Competition Act.127 KFCC case, para. 120. The CCI in Uniglobe Mod Travels (P) Ltd v. Travel Agents Federation of India 128 Uniglobe Mod Travels (P) Ltd v. Travel Agents Federation of India, Case No. 03 of 2009, paras 46.7, 68.9.2. The travel associations appealed before the erstwhile COMPAT, but their appeals were rejected in Travel Agents Association of India v. Uniglobe Mod Travels Pvt Ltd, 2013 SCC OnLine Comp AT 101. found a violation of section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act by the travel associations for calling for a boycott of the sale of tickets of international airlines, i.e., Singapore Airlines. Both DG and CCI concluded that the boycott called by the associations does not promote the technical, scientific, and economic development of production or distribution of goods or services. In fact, it harmed the consumers because of the reduction in the availability of tickets. Although section 19(3) of the Competition Act provides for certain pro-competitive effects of any agreement which need to be seen, the CCI, in their decisional practice, has never appreciated any such defences in a cartel case. Having said that, the statutory scheme does provide that the market participants can show that the cartel arrangement will not cause an AAEC in India. One of the factors mentioned under section 19(3) of the Competition Act is to see whether the agreement results in accrual of benefits to consumers. Since the approach of the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT has mostly been consumer-driven, a cartel would, in most situations, result in price increases for consumers and hence would be seen as anti-competitive. It remains to be seen, however, in what cases the CCI will agree that a cartel arrangement has pro-competitive effects.

sections.title: TRADE ASSOCIATION
sections.text: §2.12 TRADE ASSOCIATION The establishment of a trade association can be for varied objectives such as industry lobbying and standard setting. However, in some cases, trade association can be seen as a conduit for conducting anti-competitive activities. If the trade association acted only as a trade unionist, then the provisions of competition law will not be applicable. However, if the trade association acts as a place to hatch a conspiracy against the competition of the market or uses it as an instrument by the members of the association to assert their collective bargaining over the non-members or the members who are challenging the trade association, then such trade associations would be held liable for violating the provisions of competition law. The ambit of section 3(3) of the Competition Act is very wide, and it covers any agreement entered into between enterprises or associations of enterprises or persons or associations of persons or between any person and enterprise or practice carried on or ‘decision’ taken by, any association of enterprises or association of persons, including cartels. Thus, section 3(3) covers anti-competitive decisions taken by companies under the aegis of a trade association. Therefore, the CCI, in their decisional practice, imposes a twofold liability when companies engage in cartel behaviour under the aegis of a trade association: one, a liability on the company/constituent member of the trade association and second, on the trade association itself. For illustration, in the Cement cartel case,129 Cement cartel case, paras 284, 292. the CCI imposed a liability on the cement companies as well as the association of cement manufacturers, i.e., CMA. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations of the CCI, in one of its earlier decisions, in the case of Uniglobe Mod Travels (P) Ltd v. Travel Agents Federation of India,130 Uniglobe Mod Travels (P) Ltd v. Travel Agents Federation of India, Case No. 03 of 2009, para. 28. wherein it was observed that: Trade association and their activities often tend to go beyond the terms of facilitation required by their members and thus attract scrutiny by competition authorities. Experiences in Canada, European Commission etc. has lead competition authorities to examine their activities from the lens of competition policy while dealing with trade association. A decision taken by a trade association which has the purpose of fixing prices, or limiting the output of members, or allocating the market among its members, will be prohibited under Section 3 of the Act as a form of anti-competitive co-ordination [emphasis supplied]. Similarly, the Act prohibits the individual members of a trade association from entering into an agreement or engaging in a concerted practice which limits output or allocates the markets. This will be the case regardless of whether the intention is to restrict competition or not. Similarly, in case of the entertainment and pharmaceutical industry, it has been noticed that there are anti-competitive decisions taken by the trade association, which must be mandatorily followed by the members. Since section 3(3) of the Competition Act also covers anti-competitive decisions taken by the association of persons, such decisions of the trade associations are also covered under this clause. There have been multiple decisions where the CCI has imposed a penalty on the trade association. For example, in the Cement cartel case,131 Cement cartel case, para. 286. the CCI noted that cement companies were interacting using the platform of the CMA, and such interaction gave them an opportunity to determine and fix prices and limit production. The fact that it was being under the instruction of a governmental direction did not absolve them of any liability. The fact that CMA published statistics on production and dispatch of each company (factory-wise) and circulated information among its members; it facilitated the sharing of price, production and dispatch data which in turn made coordination among companies easier. Therefore, it is imperative that the companies who are a part of the trade association are cautious in their dealings with other companies while dealing under the aegis of the trade association. It must be noted, however, that trade association membership alone is not sufficient evidence of collusion or a conspiracy of anti-competitive practices. For considering whether any anti-competitive decisions were taken at the meeting of the trade association, sufficient evidence needs to be adduced to suggest that the trade association’s members reached an actual explicit or tacit agreement which has an AAEC in India. Similarly, in the case of Shri Ghanshyam Das Vij v. M/s Bajaj Corp Limited 132 Shri Ghanshyam Das Vij v. M/s Bajaj Corp Limited, Case No. 68 of 2013, paras 52, 53, 64. (‘Bajaj case’) decided by the CCI, it was noted that section 3(3) of the Competition Act covers not only agreements entered into between enterprises or associations of enterprises but also the practices carried on or decision taken by any association of enterprises engaged in identical or similar trade of goods or provision of services. Thus, all actions and practices of a trade association, including laying down provisions like imposing geographical restrictions on the dealers by the trade association or laying down criteria for carrying out business activities by the association, squarely fall within the ambit of the expression decision taken by an association of enterprises under section 3(3) of the Competition Act. Few of the examples in the pharmaceutical Industry are worth noting herein wherein the CCI has held that the actions of the concerned trade associations were in violation of section 3(3) of the Competition Act. The CCI has come down heavily on the actions of the various Chemist and Druggist Association as being anti-competitive under section 3 of the Competition Act. The CCI in Varca Chemists and Druggists v. Chemists and Druggists Association, Goa, has broadly held the following kinds of activities by Chemist and Druggist Association133 Varca Chemists and Druggists v. Chemists and Druggists Association, Goa MRTPC 127/2009/DGIR4/28, para. 33; Vedant Bio Sciences v. Chemists and Druggists Association of Baroda C-87/2009/DGIR, paras 20.26, 20.27; M/s Sandhya Drug Agency v. ADDA, BDDA, AIOCD, IDMA, OPPI, Case No. 41 of 2011, para. 24; Peeveear Medical Agencies v. AIOCD and Janssen Case No. 30 of 2011, para. 18. to be anti-competitive:(i) non-appointment of a stockiest or a wholesaler from among the non-members of the respective trade association; (ii) requirement of an NOC from the association for appointment of a stockiest or wholesaler; (iii) associations fixing trade margins below which the stockiest were not allowed to sell; (iv) the distributors/retailers not allowed to give discounts to customers; (v) compulsory approval from the trade association for the introduction of drugs in the market; (vi) requirement for routing bids for the supply of drugs to the government and the hospitals through authorized stockiest only. The CCI has held that the above terms and conditions amounted to restricting the ability of enterprises to supply and sell drugs in the market. Just to delve deep into the various activities of the chemist and druggist association, the CCI observed that members of the various chemist and druggists Associations of the districts/states have been seeking and enforcing fixed trade margins of 20% to retailer and 10% to distributor/stockist, in terms of the various Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) signed between three notable trade associations in the pharmaceutical industry: All India Organization of Chemists and Druggists (AIOCD) and the Organization of Pharmaceutical Producers of India (OPPI) and the Indian Drugs Manufacturers’ Association (IDMA). The above fixed margins were possible only if drugs were sold at their respective MRP and the sellers did not indulge in undercutting by way of offering a discount to their customers. Even though the written MOU was rescinded, the practice of the sale of drugs only at MRP so as to ensure fixed trade margins to the retailers and the wholesalers was still being practised. Upon a detailed investigation by the DG, the following were noticed: [A] Boycott The minutes of the executive committee meetings of the trade association showed that organizational movements were proposed to be launched against chain stores that indulged in undercutting and home delivery service. It was also proposed that those who are not members of the trade association should not be serviced, and the wholesalers should stop the medicine supply to such a chain store. The minutes of the trade association also revealed that the areas where undercutting was rampant were identified, and action was taken against such stores. Also, in Reliance Agency v. Chemists and Druggists Association of Baroda and Others,134 Reliance Agency v. Chemists and Druggists Association of Baroda and Others, Case No. 97 of 2013, paras 9, 31, 90. the CCI found an e-mail where the trade association was directing the stockiest from Gujarat not to purchase goods of AIMIL Pharmaceuticals (India) Ltd and calls were being made to boycott AIMIL. The trade association was interfering with the freedom of choice of a distributor of a pharmaceutical company by restricting it from choosing its business partner. Such interference not only disrupts the distribution chain but also results in limiting and controlling the supply of drugs in the market. [B] No Objection Certificate It was noticed in Varca Chemists and Druggists v. Chemists and Druggists Association, Goa 135 Varca Chemists and Druggists v. Chemists and Druggists Association, Goa, MRTPC 127/2009/DGIR4/28, para. 27.27. that the trade association mandated that a drug manufacturing company can appoint a stockiest only in consultation with the concerned State/District Chemists & Druggists Association. A NOC must be obtained for the appointment of a stockiest. Furthermore, where there is only one stockiest of the company in the district, the second stockiest can be appointed only in consultation with the concerned State/District Association, and even the second stockiest should be a bona fide member of the associations affiliated with the AIOCD. Upon investigation by the DG, it was observed that production of NOC or ‘Letter of Consent/Cooperation’ (LOC) from the respective District/State Chemists and Druggists Associations, affiliated to AIOCD, to the pharmaceutical companies by the prospective stockists are sine qua non for being appointed as a stockiest/wholesaler/distributor of pharmaceutical companies. The CCI took note of the practice and noted that the conduct of trade association in the matter of grant of NOC attracts the provisions section 3(3)(b) read with section 3(1) of the Competition Act as such actions create restraint on freedom of trade on account of NOC which has the effect of limiting or controlling the market or supply. In M/s Suman Distributors, Murshidabad v. Bengal Chemists and Druggists Association and Others,136 M/s Suman Distributors, Murshidabad v. Bengal Chemists and Druggists Association and Others, Case No. 36 of 2015, paras 20.2, 20.3, 21.9, 21.5; Madhya Pradesh Chemists and Distributors Federation (MPCDF) v. Madhya Pradesh Chemists and Druggist Association (MPCDA) & Others, Case No. 64 of 2014. it was clear that Alkem Laboratories Limited (‘Alkem’) was forcing stockiest to obtain SAI from BCDA before Alkem commenced making supplies of drugs to them. Alkem was in agreement with BCDA by issuing an Offer Letter for Stockiest to prospective stockiest and was indulging in the practice of demanding SAI/Non-Objection Certificate/Approval Letter/Circulation Letter from BCDA before commencing supplies. This constituted an anticompetitive agreement between Alkem and BCDA. [C] PIS Approval Upon an investigation by the DG, it was noted in Varca Chemists and Druggists v. Chemists and Druggists Association, Goa 137 Varca Chemists and Druggists v. Chemists and Druggists Association, Goa, MRTPC 127/2009/DGIR4/28), paras 10.2, 27.27, 27.43, 27.44. that product information service (PIS) approval entails payment of prescribed charges for the purpose of publication of the product information in the PIS bulletin, which is published state wise. The PIS bulletin is a part of the magazine published at periodic intervals by the respective state chemists and druggist associations affiliated with AIOCD. The DG observed that pharmaceutical companies have to obtain PIS approval from the respective state chemists and druggists associations affiliated with AIOCD before they can introduce new products in the market. Further, the evidence collected by the DG revealed that the practice of PIS approval from the state chemists & druggist association on payment of the prescribed charges in the name of advertisement in the association bulletin is again a sine qua non in the absence of which new products are not allowed to be introduced in the distribution channel. The DG noted that the launch of a new product in the market being made contingent on PIS approval by the concerned association of Chemists & Druggists sometimes results in restraint of trade and leads to denial of market access/controlling of supply/market for any product of a company which can also deprive consumers of the benefits of such drugs. The evidence also revealed many instances where the association of chemists and druggists refused to grant PIS approval on a variety of factors, including asking for charges in excess of the prescribed charges. Based on further investigation, the CCI noted that the action of the trade association in delaying or withholding PIS approval on any ground is in violation of the provisions of section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act. [D] Fixing of Trade Margins The investigation by the DG in Varca Chemists and Druggists v. Chemists and Druggists Association, Goa 138 Ibid., para. 27.37. revealed the fact that the trade margins had been decided for the wholesalers and retailers operating in the pharmaceutical market by way of an agreement between the trade and the industry. By way of this agreement, the DG noted that prices of drugs are directly or indirectly getting fixed and are not getting determined by the interplay of market forces. Therefore, it was observed that it implies that the manufacturer while deciding the MRP of the drugs cannot fix the prices without providing for the agreed minimum trade margins for the wholesalers and retailers of the entire Industry. The investigation further revealed that as the minimum trade margins are part of an agreement among the members of the trade and industry, there is hardly any occasion for price competition between one retailer and the other, resulting in drugs being generally sold on the MRP. The DG noted that, since the consumers have no choice, the retailers exploit the situation by not selling the drugs below the MRP, wherein the trade margins of 20% on MRP are already built in as per the terms of their agreement. Based on the above, the CCI also observed that the practice of fixing trade margins is in violation of section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act.

sections.title: JOINT VENTURE DEFENCE
sections.text: §2.13 JOINT VENTURE DEFENCE The proviso to section 3(3) of the Competition Act provides for a defence to the joint ventures which increases efficiency in the market. The proviso states that the prohibition under section 3(3) of the Competition Act will not be applicable if the parties of the same level of production chain enter into an agreement by way of the joint venture to increase efficiency in production, supply, distribution, storage, acquisition or control of goods or provision of services.
