However, the onus is on the parties to such an agreement, claiming joint venture defence to show such agreement will result in increasing efficiency which will outweigh the anti-competitive effects of such horizontal agreement. If the parties fail to satisfy the CCI that the horizontal agreement increased efficiency, then they can’t avail of the protection under the proviso. For instance, in FICCI Multiplex Association of India, New Delhi v. United Producers/Distributors Forum, Mumbai and Others, 139 FICCI Multiplex Association of India, New Delhi v. United Producers/Distributors Forum, Mumbai and Others, Case No. 1 of 2009, paras 23.35-23.38, 23.52, 23.54. An appeal was filed before the erstwhile COMPAT, but it was dismissed. However, the defence of joint venture was not discussed before COMPAT. See, Nandu Ahuja and Others v. CCI and Others [2014], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 01 of 2012. the film producers and distributors entered into a horizontal agreement to fix a high revenue sharing ratio from the multiplexes. The film producers and distributors enforced their unfair demands by refusing to release Hindi Motion Pictures for exhibitions in multiplexes. This resulted in contravention of section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act. One of the defences taken by the film producers and distributors was that the horizontal agreement must be viewed in the nature of an efficiency-enhancing joint venture as per the proviso of section 3(3) of the Competition Act. However, rejecting the defence of joint venture, the CCI held that the proviso does not provide a blanket exemption. Further, the parties claiming the defence of joint venture first have to show how the horizontal agreement increased efficiency in the film industry. The CCI furthermore noted that rather than increasing efficiency, the horizontal agreement delayed the release of films and stopped the supply of films to the multiplexes. The CCI expressed an interesting observation against cartels by saying that the parties indulging in horizontal anti-competitive agreement can never increase efficiency in production, distribution, etc., benefit the consumer, or improve the technology. This is an interesting observation from the CCI since the statutory scheme does allow the parties to raise an efficiency defence. It would be really interesting to see in what situation the CCI does entertain the efficiency defence provided in the proviso of section 3(3) of the Act. However, the CCI changed its stance in Shri Yogesh Ganeshlaji Somani v. ZEE Turner Ltd. and Another,140 Shri Yogesh Ganeshlaji Somani v. ZEE Turner Ltd. and Another, Case No. 31 of 2011, paras 3.11, 3.12, 10, 12, 14. where the information was filed against two distributors of TV channels for entering into a joint venture and contravention of section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act was alleged. The informant argued that the joint venture would not bring efficiency but cause anti-competitive effect on the market and deprive the consumers of the prices for the channels at competitive rates. But, after investigation, it was found that the objective clause of the joint venture agreement clearly shows that it would increase efficiency in the market by optimum utilization of resources and cost reduction, promote digitization and addressability, and curb piracy of TV channel signals. The CCI found no evidence to establish the violation of section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act; hence, the joint venture had not resulted in fixing the price or limiting the production of TV channels. Similarly, in Association of Third Party Administrators v. General Insurers’ (Public Sector) Association of India and Others,141 Association of Third Party Administrators v. General Insurers’ (Public Sector) Association of India and Others, Case No. 107 of 2013, paras 64, 67, 75, 78-81. information was filed against four insurance companies for entering into an anti-competitive agreement and collectively abusing their dominant position. These four insurance companies were set up as a joint venture company. Regarding the issue of whether the formation of the joint venture caused anti-competitive effect on the competition of the health insurance market, it was held that the joint venture brought efficiency enhancement in the insurance sector. The evidence collected with regard to the conduct of the insurance companies showed that there was no attempt to fix the prices or to restrict the supply of services in the market of health insurance. Furthermore, the decision to form a joint venture was found to be a commercial decision to bring efficiency by improving the level of service. In terms of services, the joint venture would ensure speedy cashless hospitalization for the end customers, cost-efficient services to the insurance companies and timely reimbursements for the hospitals. Thereafter, factors under section 19(3) were analysed, and it was held that the joint venture would bring pro-competitive efficiency to the market, and consumers would benefit because the quality and efficiency of service would improve. It can be concluded that if parties to the horizontal agreement want to avail the defence of joint venture, then the objective of their joint venture agreement needs to align with the pro-competitive factors of section 19(3)(d)-(f) of the Competition Act. This would help them establish that the joint venture would generate efficiency in the market; hence, it falls outside the purview of section 3(3) of the Competition Act.

sections.title: LENIENCY REGIME
sections.text: §2.14 LENIENCY REGIME As discussed in the earlier parts of this chapter, it is very hard to detect cartels. For this reason, the leniency regime was introduced under the Competition Act, which acts as an incentive for the enterprise to come clean to the CCI by admitting their guilt for being involved in cartelization.142 Pawan Jagetia and Others v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2022], (NCLAT), Competition Appeal (AT) No. 16 of 2021, para. 33. Under section 46 of the Competition Act, the CCI is empowered to grant a reduction in the penalty of the enterprise in exchange for reporting a cartel and active cooperation during the investigation. Thus, leniency has been created as a tool to collect evidence which would help the CCI detect cartels. [A] Legal Framework for Leniency Application Section 46(1) of the Competition Act allows an enterprise to file a lesser penalty application by providing full and true disclosure of the cartel activity which would be considered vital disclosure with respect to its participation in a cartel. In the lesser penalty application, the applicant must provide all relevant information, documents and evidence which would help the CCI pass a prima facie order or final order. Apart from providing vital disclosure, the enterprise must offer complete and active cooperation during the investigation and inquiry process. The CCI will not grant lesser penalty after the DG completes its investigation and forwards a copy of its investigation report to the CCI. Later, in 2023, the Competition Act was amended to allow the applicant to withdraw its application. This amendment also added sub-section (4) of section 46 of the Competition Act, which speaks about ‘lesser penalty plus’ regime. The objective behind introducing lesser penalty plus regime it to attract more vital disclosures regarding the cartels in the market by incentivizing a lesser penalty applicant to give vital disclosures about another cartel which is unknown to the CCI. For providing vital disclosures on the existence of another cartel, the applicant will not only get immunity for its participation in the other cartel but also an additional reduction (up to 30%) in its penalty for its participation in the cartel for which it had filed lesser penalty application. The applicant must file the lesser penalty plus application before the DG completes its investigation on the cartel for which the applicant filed lesser penalty application and also before CCI forms a prima facie view regarding the other second (the + ) cartel. The CCI has prescribed the process under section 46 of the Competition Act in the CCI (Lesser Penalty) Regulations, 2009 (‘Lesser Penalty Regulations’). The Lesser Penalty Regulations govern the procedure and extent to which reduction of penalties could be granted by the CCI to lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus applicant. [B] Process of Filing Lesser Penalty/Lesser Penalty Plus Application The benefit of leniency will be granted to those applicants who have filed the lesser penalty application prior to the DG submitting its investigation report. The lesser penalty application must contain all material information as per Schedule I of the Lesser Penalty Regulations. After receiving the lesser penalty application, the CCI will record the date and time of receiving and mark the priority status of the applicant. Thereafter, the CCI will evaluate the lesser penalty application. Till the time the evaluation of an applicant is concluded, the lesser penalty application of the next applicant will not be considered. If CCI rejects the lesser penalty application of the first applicant, then the next applicant shall move up in order of priority for grant of priority status. Along with a lesser penalty application, the applicant may further reduce its penalty by making vital disclosure about another cartel. In order to avail the benefit of up to 30% reduction (in addition to the reduction granted for the lesser penalty application), the lesser penalty applicant has to file a lesser penalty plus application containing all the information as specified in Schedule II of the Lesser Penalty Regulation. The lesser penalty plus application must be filed prior to the submission of DG’s report regarding the first cartel (for which lesser penalty application was filed) and before CCI passes a prima facie order against the second (the +) cartel (for which lesser penalty plus application has been filed). The CCI can reject a lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application if the applicant fails to comply with the conditions to vital disclosure of the information as referred to and described in Schedule I or Schedule II of the Lesser Penalty Regulations, then the CCI may reject the application. Also, if the applicant, who had not filed the written application as per Schedule I or II while submitting material information to CCI, fails to file the application in the format of Schedule I or II within fifteen days, the applicant forfeits its claim for the benefit of grant of lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus. In addition to these two conditions for dismissing an application, Regulation 11 of the Lesser Penalty Regulations provides three more conditions for forfeiture of the benefit under the leniency regime. Regulation 11 says if the CCI finds that the applicant has failed to:(i) comply with the conditions on which the benefit of lesser penalty was granted; (ii) adduced false evidence or failed to submit any material information knowing it to be material; or (iii) the disclosure made is not vital. For any of these abovementioned conditions, a lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application can be dismissed, and the applicant will not be immune to the monetary penalty imposed under section 27 of the Competition Act. Hence, the lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application made to the CCI must be extremely granular so that they can protect their priority status and also make vital disclosure to ensure that the grant of the reduction in monetary penalty. [C] Priority Status of the Lesser Penalty Applicant and Lesser Penalty Plus Applicant The Lesser Penalty Regulations provide that only after receiving an application in the format of Schedule I or II can the CCI mark the applicant’s priority status. Hence, an applicant should file an application for lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus containing all material information as specified in Schedule I or Schedule II to the CCI for furnishing the vital disclosures relating to the existence of the cartel. On the receipt of the application, the CCI shall there upon mark the priority status of the applicant which will be intimated to the applicant. And thereafter, the CCI determines priority status by looking at when the application was filed, the evidence in possession of the CCI during the stage when the lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application was filed, whether the evidence provided through that application significantly added value to the investigation/inquiry, fulfilment of the essential conditions given in Regulation 3 of the Lesser Penalty Regulations, and the entire factual scenario of the case. Depending upon the priority status, the CCI grants the leniency and decides the quantum of reduction. Further, the same priority status will also be applicable to the office-in-charge of the applicant because the CCI allows the same quantum of reduction in penalty under section 48 of the Competition Act. A diagrammatic illustration of priority status for the reduction of penalty is as follows: For the first lesser penalty applicant, the CCI can grant up to a 100% reduction of penalty. To get a 100% reduction, the applicant must be the first to make a vital disclosure by submitting evidence which would help the CCI to pass an order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act or make a vital disclosure at an early stage of the investigation to help CCI in finding the contravention of the Competition Act. Further, the applicant must actively cooperate in the investigation and inquiry. The second lesser penalty applicant can also benefit from a reduction in penalty of up to or equal to 50% upon making a disclosure of evidence that provides significant added value to the evidence already in possession of the DG/CCI. After the second applicant, there can be more than one applicant which can be marked as third lesser penalty applicant since the word used in the Lesser Penalty Regulations is applicant(s) marked as third in the priority list. The third lesser penalty applicant(s) may be granted a reduction of penalty up to 30% of the full penalty leviable. Along with providing significant added value, the applicant must cooperate during the investigation and help CCI establish the complete trail of anti-competitive conduct. Further, if the lesser penalty applicant makes vital disclosure of a second cartel unknown to the CCI, then the CCI may grant an additional reduction in penalty up to 30% of the penalty imposed with regard to lesser penalty plus application. [D] Evaluation of Lesser Penalty/Lesser Penalty Plus Application [1] Vital Disclosure to Establish the Cartel Moreover, the applicant who files a lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application must make vital disclosure by submitting all the evidence that would materially contribute to passing a prima facie view or establishing the existence of the cartel.143 The CCI can also use its inherent power to grant lesser penalty even without a leniency application. M/s. Federation of Gujarat State Chemists & Druggists Associations and Others, Case Nos 65, 71, 72 of 2014 & 68 of 2015, paras 190, 191. The CCI remitted 40% penalty amount on Hetero Healthcare Ltd. even without a leniency application. The CCI considered the mitigating factors such as the admission of the Hetero Healthcare Ltd. regarding the violation of the Competition Act and active cooperation with CCI during inquiry. Vital disclosure has been defined under Regulation 2(j) of the Lesser Penalty Regulations as full and true disclosure of information by the applicant to the CCI, which would satisfy the CCI in forming a prima facie view about the existence of a cartel under section 3 of the Competition Act. The ambit of vital disclosure is extremely specific, and it should assist the CCI in forming a prima facie view that there exists a cartel; based on such disclosure provided by the applicant, the CCI will direct the offices of the DG to investigate the matter. Therefore, the lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application to the CCI has to be extremely specific and granular, seeking leniency under the Lesser Penalty Regulations, and such application must contain all material information and evidence relating to the establishment or existence of a cartel.144 However, admission of presence of cartel through leniency application does not always lead to finding on violation of section 3 of the Competition Act. In Re: Alleged Cartelisation in Flashlights Market in India, Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2017, paras 99, 100, 101, leniency application was filed by Eveready revealing instances of exchange of commercially sensitive information between competitors through the Association. Subsequently, Panasonic also filed an application disclosing information exchange between competitors in the flashlights market. Based on which investigation was also carried out by the DG, however it was found that emails were exchanged to monitor the market of flashlight but could not establish the presence of cartelization under section 3 of the Competition Act. Further, the applicant must be very careful before filing such an application because the CCI and DG can still use the evidence from the application even after the applicant withdraws it. [2] Other Conditions to Evaluate a Lesser Penalty/Lesser Penalty Plus Application Furthermore, the applicant must actively cooperate with the CCI till the completion of the inquiry. If the applicant fails to comply with the condition on which the lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus was granted, or provides false evidence in its application, or provides evidence which is not vital, then the CCI will not lessen the penalty amount. The Regulation 3 of the Lesser Penalty Regulations stipulate the following essential conditions that the CCI must take into account before reducing the penalties: (i) The applicant should not have any further participation in the cartel after filing lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application, unless CCI directs otherwise. (ii) The information provided should be a ‘vital disclosure’, as mentioned under Regulation 2(j) of the Lesser Penalty Regulations. (iii) The applicant should cooperate to the best of its ability with the CCI, inter alia, by providing all relevant information, documents and evidence as required. (iv) The applicant should cooperate genuinely, fully, continuously and expeditiously throughout the investigation and other proceedings before the CCI. (v) Relevant evidence should not be concealed, destroyed, manipulated or removed by the applicant. (vi) Names of all the office bearers of the enterprise which is involved in cartelization shall be provided by the applicant. [E] Trend of CCI in Adjudicating Lesser Penalty Applications While the lesser penalty plus can be granted to only one applicant, the CCI can grant lesser penalty to more than one applicant. Provided that such information in the lesser penalty application significantly adds value and helps the DG/CCI reach a conclusion about the existence of a cartel. The term ‘significant added value’ has been defined as the extent to which the evidence provided enhances the ability of the DG/CCI to establish the existence of a cartel under section 3 of the Competition Act. In this regard, the applicant(s) generally contends that the investigation report of the DG has remained incomplete, and the disclosure via application added value to the investigation/inquiry.145 Nagrik Chetna Manch v. Fortified Security Solutions and Others, Case No. 50 of 2015, para. 77. In Nagrik Chetna Manch v. Fortified Security Solutions and Others,146 Case No. 50 of 2015, paras 104 and 105. the CCI granted 50% leniency to the first applicant for making a good value addition to the ongoing investigation, and its evidence provided a clearer picture of the modus operandi of the cartel. But, the first applicant did not get 100% leniency because he did not file the application before CCI formed its prima facie view or at the very beginning of the investigation. It is to be noted that the imposition of penalty is discretionary under the provisions of the Competition Act and the Lesser Penalty Regulations. The discretion of the CCI in regard to reduction in monetary penalty will be exercised with due regard to the stage at which the lesser penalty applicant comes forward with the disclosure, the evidence already in possession of the DG/CCI, the quality of the information provided by the applicant and the entire facts and circumstances of the case. In Re: Cartelization in respect of tenders floated by Indian Railways for supply of Brushless DC Fans and other electrical items,147 In Re: Cartelization in respect of tenders floated by Indian Railways for supply of Brushless DC Fans and other electrical items, Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2014, paras 7.7, 7.8, 7.10, 7.11. was the order where CCI for the first time granted leniency, and in this order, the CCI granted leniency by reducing 75% in the monetary penalty. Thought the applicant disclosed vital information relating to the existence of the cartel; however, 100% leniency was not granted because the application was not made at an early stage of the investigation. When the application was submitted, the CCI already had e-mail exchanges which satisfied it to direct an investigation for cartelization in tenders for the Indian Railway’s Brushless DC Fans. While dealing with a leniency application, the CCI considers a few factors, as mentioned in Regulation 3 of the Lesser Penalty Regulations. Taking into account all these factors, the CCI decided to grant 100% leniency to Panasonic Energy, In Re: Cartelisation in respect of zinc carbon dry cell batteries market in India.148 In Re: Cartelisation in respect of zinc carbon dry cell batteries market in India, Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2016, paras 10.2, 10.3, 10.4. The CCI noted that the evidence and cooperation provided by Panasonic Energy were crucial to initiate the investigation and helped during the investigation to seize direct evidence, such as email exchanges, handwritten notes, and other documents. However, the CCI decided to grant a 30% lesser penalty to Eveready Industries and a 20% lesser penalty to Indo National. Though the leniency application provided by Eveready Industries and Indo National actively cooperated during the investigation, however, their applications could not provide any ‘significant value addition’ because almost all evidence was already available with the CCI. Let’s see how the CCI has exercised its powers under section 46 read with the Lesser Penalty Regulations. The orders where the CCI has granted leniency to the cartel members have been tabulated below: Sl. No. Case (Citation) Priority Status Vital Disclosure Lesser Penalty (%) 1. Cartelization in tenders for the supply of Brushless DC Fans. Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2014. (18.01.2017) 1st: Pyramid Electronics Application was filed after CCI directed the DG to start investigation. The details submitted by the applicant helped the CCI to connect the dots to reveal the matching of prices among the bidders. 75% 2. Cartelisation in zinc carbon dry cell batteries market. Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2016. (19.04.2018) 1st: Panasonic The CCI directed the DG to start an investigation based on the application of Panasonic. Further, the application also helped to establish the presence of cartel. Cartelization to control the price and limit the production of zinc-carbon dry cell batteries was proved due to the leniency application. 100% 2nd: Eveready Application was filed after three days of initiating investigation. Provided active cooperation during investigation. 30% 3rd: Indo National Application was filed after three weeks of initiating investigation. Provided active cooperation during investigation. 20% 3. Nagrik Chetna Manch v. Fortified Security Solutions and Others. Case No. 50 of 2015 (01.05.2018) 1st: Mahalaxmi Steels Application was filed after investigation was initiated. First to admit the existence of cartel and cooperated during investigation and inquiry. 50% 2nd: Sanjay Agency Application was filed after investigation was initiated. Disclosed roles of other persons and provided copies of email exchanges. 40% 3rd: Lahs Green Application was filed after investigation was initiated. Copies of email exchanges and active cooperation during investigation and inquiry. 50%149 Nagrik Chetna Manch v. Fortified Security Solutions and Others, Case No. 50 of 2015, para. 119. The quantum of reduction of penalty is more for Lahs Green, than Sanjay Agency. It is because, though Lahs Green was marked as 3rd in the priority status in the case, but Lahs Green got 1st priority status with respect to disclosure of a different tender for the first time. 4th: Ecoman Enviro Solutions Application was filed after investigation was initiated. Admitted the presence of cartel and co-operated throughout the investigation and inquiry. 25% 4. Cartelisation by broadcasting service providers by rigging the bids submitted in tenders floated by Sports Broadcasters. Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2013. (11.07.2018) 1st: Globecast Application enabled to form prima facie opinion to pass an order directing DG to investigate. Submitted vital evidence, such as the details of sporting events in which bid rigging took place, the role of ex-employees, email exchanges for sharing commercially sensitive information, etc. Also, cooperated during investigation. 100% 2nd: ESCL Filed application after receiving notice from the DG. Provided corroborative evidence and additional evidence to the ongoing investigation. 30% 5. Anticompetitive conduct in the Dry-Cell Batteries Market in India. Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2017. (30.08.2018) 1st: Panasonic Pursuant to the application of Panasonic, the CCI was able to form prima facie opinion and directed the DG to investigate. Provided vital evidence, such as Product Supply Agreement and email exchanges which helped to disclose the modus operandi of the Cartel. Also, the applicant actively cooperated during investigation. 100% 6. Anticompetitive conduct in the Dry-Cell Batteries Market in India. Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2017. (15.01.019) 1st: Panasonic The Application helped the CCI to pass an order to initiate investigation. Provided vital evidence to form prima facie opinion and contravention of section 3, and the applicant actively cooperated during investigation. 100% 7. Cartelisation in the supply of Electric Power Steering Systems. Suo Motu Case No. 07 (01) of 2014. (09.08.2019) 1st: NSK Limited Based on the application, the CCI initiated investigation. NSK provided vital disclosures by submitting evidence of the cartel. 100% 2nd: JTEKT Corporation The application was filed during pendency of investigation. JTEKT provided significant added value to the evidence already in possession of the CCI and actively cooperated with the investigation and inquiry. 50% 8. In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India. Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2017.150 Also, it is important to note that, in Pawan Jagetia and Others v. CCI and Others [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 16 of 2021. paras 33-37, NCLAT held that once the applicant files an application under section 46 of the Act, and lesser penalty or lesser penalty plus is granted, then the applicant cannot contest on the merit of the case. (24.09.2021) 1st: SABMiller India Limited Based on the application, the CCI initiated investigation. When SABMiller India Limited filed, the CCI and DG had no evidence. The application explained the nature and modus operandi of the beer cartel and explained the market structure of the Beer industry. Further, active cooperation helped during the investigation and inquiry. 100% 2nd: United Breweries Limited The application was filed during pendency of investigation. However, the application was filed before the dawn raid took place. The United Breweries Limited provided additional evidence on coordination in respect of premium institutions in Bengaluru, Karnataka and with respect to the purchase of old/used bottles. Further, OP1 helped DG and CCI in mapping price parallelism. 40% 3rd: Carlsberg Application filed during the investigation and after the DG conducted the search and seizure operations. Carlsberg helped in forming the complete trail of anti-competitive conduct of the beer cartel. 20% 9. Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry. Suo Motu Case No. 05 of 2016. (17.11.2021) 1st: Trident Limited Application filed during the investigation. Admitted that the applicant had formed a cartel and supported with vital disclosure. The applicant also actively cooperated during the investigation. 100% 10. Cartelisation by Shipping Lines in the matter of provision of Maritime Motor Vehicle Transport Services. Suo Motu Case No. 10 of 2014. (20.01.2022) 1st: Nippon Based on the application, the CCI initiated investigation. Full and true disclosures and continuous cooperation provided by Nippon enabled the CCI to order an investigation but helped establish contravention. 100% 2nd: Mitsui Joint application151 In Re: Cartelisation by Shipping Lines in the matter of provision of Maritime Motor Vehicle Transport Services, Suo Motu Case No 10 of 2014, para. 230, the parties have not been able to show any provision in the Competition Act or the Regulations which enable the parties to file joint lesser penalty application. was filed after the commencement of investigation. Application added value and the applicant cooperated during investigation. 50% 3rd: Nissan Motors Joint application was filed after the commencement of investigation. Application added value and the applicant cooperated during investigation. 30% 11. Chief Materials Manager, North Western Railway v. Moulded Fibreglass Products and Others. Case No. 03 of 2018. (04.04.2022) 1st: Black Burn Joint application was filed after the commencement of investigation. Made full and true disclosures of information and continuous co-operation during investigation. 80% 2nd: Moulded Fibreglass Joint application was filed after the commencement of investigation. Could not provide much additional value but made full disclosures and continuously co-operated during investigation. 40% 3rd: Jai Polypan Joint application was filed after the commencement of investigation. Provided value addition which was used by the CCI to form a complete trail evidencing anti-competitive conduct. 30% 4th: Quadrant EPP Joint application was filed after the commencement of investigation. Provided value addition which was used by the CCI to form a complete trail evidencing anti-competitive conduct. 20% 12. Cartelisation in the supply of Protective Tubes to Indian Railways. Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2020. (09.06.2022) 1st: Jai Polypan Based on the application, the CCI initiated investigation. Prima facie order for investigation was passed due to the application. Further, full and true disclosures of evidence and continuous cooperation helped the CCI establish the cartel. 100% The essential factors under Regulation 3 of the Lesser Penalty Regulations, based on which CCI exercise its discretionary power to allow a lesser penalty application, vary on a case-by-case basis. Consequently, the reduction of monetary penalty also varies; thus, the trend of CCI in relation to adjudication of the lesser penalty application is not consistent. Therefore, as noted above, the key thing in any application is that such application must be extremely granular with all relevant details so that the CCI can take help from such evidence to establish a contravention under section 3 of the Competition Act. In addition to providing evidence which was not in possession by the offices of DG/CCI or which the previous applicant did not give, the applicant must actively cooperate with the DG/CCI during the investigation or inquiry proceeding. Importantly, any information and document submitted under the Lesser Penalty Regulations are treated as confidential (including in respect of the identity of the applicant) unless such information is already in the public domain or is required to be disclosed by law. Further, the application made under the Lesser Penalty Regulations is kept confidential, so there is no chance for the future applicant to know that the contents of the information are already available to the authorities. It can be a tricky situation if a lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application is made without any vital or added disclosure since the applicant has already confessed to being involved in a cartel and may not still get any immunity. Similarly, an applicant can withdraw its lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application, but under Regulation 10, the DG/CCI will be at liberty to use the evidence submitted by the applicant except for its admission. Finally, it is to be noted that under the statutory scheme of the Competition Act, the CCI, in its discretion, can grant lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus. However, the risk of such an applicant being sued for compensation under section 53 of the Competition Act is not taken away.

sections.title: LEGAL FRAMEWORK FOR VERTICAL AGREEMENT
sections.text: §2.15 LEGAL FRAMEWORK FOR VERTICAL AGREEMENT Vertical agreement means the agreement/arrangement/understanding between enterprises that are at different stages or levels of the production chain and, therefore, in different markets. Section 3(4) of the Competition Act enlists a non-exhaustive list of vertical agreements which would be treated as void agreements if they cause or are likely to cause AAEC.152 Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India (FHRAI) and Others v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. (MMT) and Others, Case No. 14 of 2019 and Case No. 01 of 2020, para. 291. Also, see M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. Intel Corporation (Intel Inc.) & Ors., Case No. 48 of 2011, paras 8.3.3, 8.3.6. Unlike certain kinds of horizontal anti-competitive agreements (i.e., cartel arrangements discussed supra), vertical agreements are not pre-se anti-competitive, and the CCI assess these vertical agreements under the rule of reason analysis. The concept of rule of reason stated that the vertical agreement would fall foul of section 3(4) of the Competition Act only if such agreement has caused or has the potential to cause AAEC as per the factors laid down in section 19(3) of the Competition Act.153 Jasper lnfotech Private Limited (Snapdeal) v. KAFF Appliances (India) Pvt. Ltd. (Kaff), Case No. 61 of 2014, paras 48, 51. For assessing vertical anti-competitive agreements, the rule of reason is applicable because such agreements are concluded between entities operating at different stages of the production chain and are not between competitors of the same market. So, for the operation of business, these entities enter into several commercial agreements, many of which enhance efficiency with sound economic justification. As these entities are not competitors, their incentives are generally aligned with that for the benefit of the customers. Hence, such vertical agreements may not necessarily cause AAEC of the market, and treating such vertical agreements as per se anti-competitive might result in restraining pro-competitive commercial agreements.154 Ibid., para. 48. The High Level Committee on Competition Policy and Law, 2000 (‘High Level Committee’)155 High Level Committee on Competition Policy and Law, 2000, paras 4.4.0, 4.3.1. had noted that:generally, vertical agreements are treated more leniently than horizontal agreements as, prima facie, a horizontal agreement is more likely to reduce competition than an agreement between firms in a buyer-seller relationship… In the past, the U.S. anti-trust laws had treated vertical restraints, like tie-in arrangements quite harshly. This thinking has changed in recent times, and, under the rule of reason, vertical agreements are generally treated more leniently than horizontal agreements. This is because vertical agreements can often perform pro-competitive functions. Such agreements are generally considered anti-competitive if one or more of the firms that are party to the agreement have market power. In such a situation, the agreement is, in any case, likely to attract the provisions of the law relating to abuse of dominance. In light of the aforesaid, the CCI adjudicates the allegation of vertical agreement under the rule of reason to see whether the vertical agreement caused or is likely to cause an adverse effect. Looking at the steps taken by CCI while deciding the matters related to vertical anti-competitive agreements suggests that CCI follows an ‘effect-based analysis’ through the rule of reason framework. Thus, to determine the violation of section 3(4) of the Competition Act, the CCI follows the following steps, i.e., first, nature of the vertical agreement under inquiry; second, delineation of relevant markets; third, significant market power of the party; and fourth, effect-based analysis to determine AAEC.

sections.title: TYPES OF VERTICAL AGREEMENTS
sections.text: §2.16 TYPES OF VERTICAL AGREEMENTS Section 3(4) of the Competition Act provides an inclusive list of vertical agreements. There can be other forms of vertical agreements, however, all vertical agreements must be analysed under the rule of reason analysis. It is noteworthy that vertical agreements are often analysed by the CCI on a joint investigation of section 3(4) read with section 4 of the Competition Act, since a vertical agreement entered into by an undertaking with insignificant market power will generally not cause any AAEC in India. We will now discuss the various kinds of vertical agreements mentioned under section 3(4) of the Competition Act below. [A] Tie-In Arrangement Explanation (a) of section 3(4) defines tie-in arrangement or tying arrangement as an agreement imposing a condition on a customer to purchase the goods or services in order to purchase some other goods or services. In a tie-in arrangement, the seller ties two distinct or separate products or services with each other so that the customer is forced to purchase the tied product or service along with the tying product or service. The product or service that the customer wants to purchase is called tying product, and the product or service which is forced upon the customer is called tied product or service. For example, the product that the buyer wants to purchase is called the tying product (Product X), and the product which is required to be purchased to get Product X is called tied product (Product Y). The producer of Product X imposes a condition on the buyers that in order to purchase Product X, they have to purchase Product Y. Also, for a better understanding of tie-in arrangement, it is important to distinguish tie-in arrangement from bundling arrangement. The CCI made detailed observations on the difference between tie-in and bundling in the Sonam Sharma v. Apple (‘Apple case’). 156 Sonam Sharma v. Apple, Case No. 24 of 2011, paras 19, 65-71. In this case, the DG found that Apple locked its iPhone with the cellular services of Airtel or Vodafone. It was a case of ‘contractual tying’, which means a buyer can only purchase an iPhone by subscribing to the cellular services of Airtel or Vodafone. However, looking at the minuscule market share of the tying party, the CCI held that the said tie-in arrangement was not an anti-competitive agreement between Apple and cellular service providers. Enough evidence was not adduced to prove that the tie-in arrangement foreclosed the market or created entry barriers for new entrants. Though ‘tie-in’ and ‘bundling’ are used interchangeably in common market parlance, the CCI explained the difference between both the terms by saying that ‘tie-in arrangement’ and ‘bundling’ are strategies to sell products and services, and both the strategies are used for different purpose. A producer uses a ‘tie-in arrangement’ as a strategy when it does not want to specify to the customer that the two products are fixed at the time of purchase, whereas a ‘bundling’ strategy is used when the products are sold in fixed proportions. The CCI in the Apple case further noted that ‘a tying arrangement occurs when, through a contractual or technological requirement, a seller conditions the sale or lease of one product or service on the customer’s agreement to take a second product or service. In other words, a firm selling products X and Y makes the purchase of product X conditional to the purchase of product Y. Product Y can be purchased freely on the market, but product X can only be purchased together with product Y.’ Thereafter, the CCI in the Apple case,157 Apple case, Case No. 24 of 2011, para. 66. explained the term ‘tie-in arrangement’ by providing examples of the tied sales of machines with maintenance services or complementary products. Also, producers force the buyers to purchase two or more products because all these products are compatible with each other. More often, tying is a sales strategy usually adopted by producers to promote/introduce a slow-selling or unknown product when it has in its portfolio a fast-selling or well-known product over which it has certain market power (in this example, Product X). The aspect of tie-in is also seen, while deciding abuse of dominance investigation, under the abuse of leveraging (discussed in the chapter on abuse of dominance). However, ‘bundling’ or ‘package tie-in’ is a strategy used by the producer to make the buyer purchase more goods. Further, the CCI held that ‘price bundling is a strategy whereby a seller bundles together many different goods/items for sale and offers the entire bundle at a single price. There are two forms of price bundling – pure bundling, where the seller does not offer buyers the option of buying the items separately, and mixed bundling, where the seller offers the items separately at higher individual prices’.158 Apple case, para. 67. Mixed bundling is usually preferable to pure bundling because the reference price effect makes it appear even more attractive to buyers, and there are fewer laws forbidding mixed bundling. The CCI has noted that there can be pro-competitive rationale behind tie-in arrangements like assembly benefits (economies of scale and scope), quality improvement and addressing pricing inefficiencies. The following conditions are required to be analysed to see whether a tie-in arrangement is anti-competitive for a section 3(1) analysis:159 Ibid., para. 69. (i) Presence of two separate products or services capable of being tied. Tie-in arrangements must consist of two products where the seller forces the buyer to purchase one product (i.e., tied product – Product X) if the buyer purchases another product (i.e., tying product – Product Y). (ii) The seller must have significant market power in the market of the tying product to appreciably restrain free competition in the market of the tied product. To establish the presence of tying arrangements, the sufficient market power of the seller in the tying market to leverage into the market of tied product must be presence. Thereafter, it has to be shown that the seller has used its market power to force the buyers to purchase the tied product along with the tied product. (iii) The tying arrangement must have a substantial effect on the commerce. Considering the area of analysis is whether an agreement causes or is likely to cause an AAEC, tie-in arrangements are generally not perceived as being anti-competitive when a substantial portion of the market is not affected. Similarly, in Arshiya Rail Infrastructure Ltd. (ARIL) v. Ministry of Railway (MoR) & Others,160 Arshiya Rail Infrastructure Ltd. (ARIL) v. Ministry of Railway (MoR) & Others, Case No. 64 of 2010, para. 21. the CCI held that tie-in arrangement is used by an entity which has a dominant position in one product/service (i.e., transportation services – tying service) and ties it with a laggard product (i.e., maintenance of container – tied service) to promote the laggard product. However, there was no tie-in arrangement in the said case because none of the entities were in the business of maintenance services. As per the facts, MoR granted a licence and concessionaire agreement entered with fifteen private operators to move container trains on the railway lines on a licence basis. The informants, who were private operators, alleged that MoR, Indian Railway and Container Corporation of India, had indulged in abusive conduct as a group or a single economic entity in the market for the transport of freight via railways. One of the allegations was on violation of section 3(4)(a) of the Competition Act because MoR insisted that maintenance of container trains can only be sourced from it. However, the CCI dismissed this allegation by saying that the supplementary condition to obtain a fitness certificate of train from MoR does not amount to a tie-in arrangement in terms of section 3(4)(a) of the Competition Act. The CCI observed that MoR imposed such conditions to maintain railway operation and protect the overall safety of passengers. [B] Exclusive Dealing Agreement Explanation (b) to section 3(4) of the Competition Act defines an exclusive dealing agreement as an exclusive agreement imposed by the seller which restricts the buyer from purchasing any goods or services other than those of the seller. Exclusive dealing agreement also covers the vertical restraint imposed by the buyer on the seller from dealing with other buyers. The seller who is accepting this restrictive term has no option but to supply its product or service to the designated purchaser as per the condition. For example, Manufacturer A entered into an exclusive dealing agreement with all the retailers to buy only the product of Manufacturer A or the authorized dealers of Manufacturer A. Prior to the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, this explanation was called an ‘exclusive supply agreement’, and it used to cover only the vertical agreement where restraint was exclusively imposed on the buyer by the seller. So, the Competition Act was amended to recognize the imposition of the exclusive agreement from both the seller side and the buyer side.161 Report of Competition Law Review Committee, 2019, paras 5.5-5.7. The issue of exclusive dealing agreement (supply-side) was discussed in the Shri Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Ltd. and Others (‘Automobile spare parts case’),162 Shri Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Ltd. and Others, Case No. 03 of 2011, para. 20.6.4; Also, see Toyota Kirloskar Motor Private Limited and Others v. CCI [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 452, para. 125. where both the CCI and the erstwhile COMPAT held that the authorized dealers were prohibited from sourcing spare parts from any other enterprise, apart from the OEMs. These exclusive agreements imposed restrictions on the buyer side of the market to buy and sell the spare parts exclusively; hence, section 3(4)(b) of the Competition Act was violated. In another case, the CCI in M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. Intel Corporation and Others 163 M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. Intel Corporation and Others, Case No. 48 of 2011, para. 8.3.7. held that exclusive agreement means that the agreement by the seller restricts the distributors from dealing with any competitors of the seller. In the absence of any evidence, the CCI was unable to show that the concerned agreement with Intel exclusively imposed a restriction on the distributors to deal with Intel. Also, there was evidence which showed that Intel allowed the distributors to deal with other sellers. [C] Exclusive Distribution Agreement Explanation (c) to section 3(4) of the Competition Act defines exclusive distribution agreement as an exclusive agreement to limit, restrict or withhold the output or supply of any goods or services or allocate any area or market for the disposal or sale of the goods or services. Such an exclusive agreement restricts the distribution side of the market. For example, a supplier entered into an exclusive distribution agreement with the manufacturer to specifically distribute the product with authorized dealers, and not any independent dealers. The aspect of exclusive distribution agreement was discussed in the Bajaj case.164 Bajaj case, para. 75. The CCI discussed the anti-competitive effects which can flow from an exclusive distribution agreement. The CCI observed that exclusive distribution agreement is when a supplier enters into an agreement with the distributor, wherein the distributor sells the product within a particular group or area. Such an agreement limits the distribution, creates entry bars and harms the intra-brand competition. While assessing the violation under section 3(4)(c) of the Competition Act, the CCI considers whether the distributor is restricted to deal in the products of other brands in or outside the particular customer or region that caused any harm to the intra-brand or inter-brand competition. In M/s Karni Communication Private Ltd. & Other v. Haicheng Vivo Mobile (India) Private Ltd. and Others,165 M/s Karni Communication Private Ltd. & Other v. Haicheng Vivo Mobile (India) Private Ltd. & Others, Case No. 35 of 2018, para. 27. it was alleged that Vivo imposed a clause in the distribution agreement to restrict smartphone distributors from selling Vivo smartphones via online platforms to customers, hence, violated section 3(4)(c) of the Competition Act. This allegation was dismissed because Vivo had allowed the distributors to sell Vivo’s products online on various e-commerce portals like www.Flipkart.com, www.Amazon.in, www.Snapdeal.com, etc. The CCI held that there was no exclusive distribution agreement. Further, the CCI said that Vivo never directly restricted the distribution of its products, and consumers had options to buy Vivo products from various sources. Similarly, in M/s K.C. Marketing v. OPPO Mobiles MU Private Limited,166 M/s K.C. Marketing v. OPPO Mobiles MU Private Limited, Case No. 34 of 2018, paras 30, 31. the CCI held that to protect the interests of all distributors who have made an investment in the product, the producer can designate specific sale regions to distributors and restrict the distributors from selling outside their specified regions. Such exclusive distribution agreement causes no AAEC because there is no bar for the customers of one region to purchase the product from a distributor in another sales region. Further, there is no restriction on the distributor to deal in the products of other producers in or outside the sales region. Thus, in effect, the CCI has accepted that there can be certain restrictions which may be imposed in a vertical agreement to prevent free-riding (this was specifically highlighted in the Bajaj case), however, the parties who are in market power must keep in mind the factors mentioned under section 19(3) of the Act prior to imposing such vertical restraints. In the Automobile spare parts case,167 Automobile spare parts case, para. 20.6.29. The erstwhile COMPAT in Toyota Kirloskar Motor Private Limited and Others v. CCI [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 452, upheld the order of CCI. it was found that the OEMs and their authorized dealers entered into agreements/arrangements pursuant to which the authorized dealers of the OEMs sell cars and provide after-sales services to the consumers of the OEMs. Further, there are agreements entered into between OEMs and the original equipment suppliers (OESs). Further, apart from the agreement between OEM with their dealers, there were agreements between OEMs with the OESs prohibiting them from selling the spare parts manufactured by them for OEMs in the open market. The CCI concluded by saying that OEMs violated section 3(4)(b), (c) and (d) of the Competition Act. OEMs entered into exclusive agreement and refusal to deal to restrict the access of spare parts and diagnostic tools directly to the Indian aftermarket; the arrangement between OEMs and authorized dealers violated section 3(4) of the Competition Act. The OEMs increased the risk of consumer safety because their restrictive conduct led to a rise in the use of fake spare parts and prevented independent repairers from using effective diagnostic tools. After identifying the various kinds of agreements, the CCI analysed that the said network of agreements created foreclosure effects and barriers to entry into the market. Based on the same, the CCI identified AAEC in terms of the factors mentioned under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. [D] Refusal to Deal Explanation (d) of section 3(4) of the Competition Act defines refusal to deal as an agreement which restricts or is likely to restrict their business partners in terms of dealing with the competitors of the enterprise which is imposing such restriction. It must be noted that refusal to deal has mostly been dealt with under an abuse of dominance investigation. An example of refusal to deal is when an E-Commerce Platform – ‘B’ enters into an agreement with a smartphone retailer prohibiting it from listing its smartphones on the E-Commerce Platform – ‘A’ and E-Commerce Platform – ‘C’. The issue of refusal of deal will depend upon the facts and circumstances of each case. Interestingly, in Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd.,168 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd., Case No. 18 of 2021, paras 45, 46, 49, 51, 55. while passing an order under section 26(2) of the Competition Act, the CCI held that no distributor can ask for an absolute right to deal from a manufacturer to deal with them. In this case, Hiveloop Technology alleged that Britannia refused to deal directly with it, and there is a vertical agreement between Britannia and its distributors to restrict Hiveloop Technology from dealing with certain brands and biscuits of Britannia. Britannia argued that it has not imposed any such restrictions on its distributors, and it has commercial freedom to choose its business partners in the vertical chain. CCI accepted the argument of Britannia and held that an enterprise can refuse to choose their trading partners as long as there is fair functioning of the markets. The CCI recognized that Britannia has commercial right when it comes to choosing its business partners, and CCI can’t interfere with such right. In two cases with similar facts concerning non-dealing of a goods provider with a distributor, the CCI expounded the position that every enterprise, including a dominant enterprise, has the freedom to choose its business partners.169 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd., Case No. 18 of 2021; Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Parle Products Private Limited, Case No. 28 of 2021. The crux of the allegations in both cases was that the goods provider (i.e., Britannia (discussed supra) and Parle, respectively) was not providing its biscuits, which were regarded as a ‘must stock item’ to the distributor (i.e., Hiveloop Technologies/Udaan). The CCI noted that this matter required a resolution of two competing rights: the informant’s right to do business with Britannia/Parle with respect to certain products versus Britannia/Parle’s right not to engage with the informant since it had the freedom of choice as to whom to contract within the vertical chain as well as its level of engagement. In this case, CCI noted that both Britannia and Parle had their own commercial justifications for refusing engagement with the informant, viz., the capacity and quality of service provided and that no foreclosure had arisen in the market. As a result, the CCI held that manufacturers have some autonomy available to deal with their goods the way they want, in alignment with their business requirements. If this were not the case, then it has a consequence of replacing the business/commercial prudence of an entity by taking away the autonomy of such undertaking/entity. Hence, the type and nature of distributors a manufacturer desires to partner with is part and parcel of its business autonomy, and the CCI cannot ipso facto substitute its regulatory wisdom for that of the commercial wisdom of the businesses unless the commercial wisdom is palpably in the face of the provisions of the Competition Act and ‘the criteria laid down are ex facie unfair and/or discriminatory and designed with a view to exclude/eliminate competition on merits’.170 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Parle Products Private Limited, Case No. 28 of 2021, para. 33. Thus, nobody can ask for an absolute right to deal with a particular business. Similarly, there is no absolute right of refusal. This will depend upon the facts and circumstances of each case; even a dominant entity, at times, has the freedom to refuse to conclude contracts based on objective justifications. Further, refusal to deal need not be absolute and ought to be seen in the context of the factual setting coupled with the factors under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. A refusal, either total or partial, could also have underlying valid justifications with commercial consideration, especially in the backdrop that it makes little economic sense for businesses to take a restrictive approach when it comes to expanding their horizon and reach. Consequently, though refusal to deal may be, inter se, two or more parties, its anti-competitive effect would have to be seen in the context of the overall market conditions. Hence, a manufacturer cannot be forced to onboard any person/entity in the chain merely on asking because distribution networks are built and strengthened over a period of time with sound underlying commercial considerations. Thus, the question to be asked in business decisions to engage or not to engage with a downstream entity from a competition perspective is whether such a decision is likely to cause AAEC should the upstream entity choose not to deal with the downstream entity. Thus, the takeaway is that firms have an autonomy to choose their trading partners as long as the exercise of such autonomy does not affect the fair functioning of the markets because, depending upon the market power commanded by such firms, their conduct may cause foreclosure of the market for other players. The CCI, in the Bajaj case,171 Bajaj case, paras 69, 76, 81. where it was alleged that Bajaj exercised its market power to impose restrictions on its distributors from supplying Bajaj’s products to retailers which were from other territories, the distributor had no other option but to accept such restrictive conditions. The CCI noted that competition law issues with respect to refusal to deal/supply will arise when an enterprise, generally with stronger market power, refuses to deal with its customers or suppliers. The effect in such a scenario is that the downstream market gets affected due to such refusal. However, the CCI held that though there were vertical restraints on the distributors, there was no violation of section 3(4)(d) due to the lack of sufficient evidence to satisfy the presence of entry barriers, foreclosure of the market, or harm to the end consumers. Also, in a case relating to the automobile sector, the CCI found that there was no exclusive agreement or refusal to deal between the car manufacturers and their distributors in the Hyundai Motors case172 Hyundai Motors case, paras 71, 72, 74, 75. because the prohibition to deal with other car companies was never mandated on the distributors. Mere requirements of prior written permission of Hyundai do not impose any exclusivity or cannot be concluded as refusal to deal under section 3(4) of the Competition Act. Additionally, it was found that there were distributors who were dealing with other car companies without seeking permission from Hyundai, and despite the knowledge of such parallel dealerships, no action was taken against such distributors. Though generally vertical anti-competitive agreements are imposed as vertical restraints where one party has no other option than accepting the condition of the other party, however, it could also be a mutually agreed condition to eliminate their competitors. The CCI in its most prominent decision on refusal to deal in the Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Others v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. and Others (‘MMT case’),173 Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Others v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. and Others, Case No. 14 of 2019 and Case No. 01 of 2020, paras 289, 291, 292, 293, 295, 298, 300, 301. passed an order against MakeMyTrip (MMT – an online travel agency) and Oravel Stay Ltd. (OYO – franchisee service provider to independent hotels) for their anti-competitive practices, including MMT’s refusal to deal and delisting of the competitors of OYO. The CCI observed that section 3(4) is neither exhaustive in nature nor does it require that the restriction is in the form of an imposition. So, the CCI can also look into the restrictive agreement which was mutually agreed by both parties to harm the competitors or competition in the market. In this case, MMT agreed to delist OYO’s competitors, i.e., Fabhotels and Treebo, whereas OYO agreed not to list its inventories on the platforms of MMT’s competitors, i.e., Yatra, Cleartrip, PayTM, etc. Such a mutually agreed clause between MMT and OYO was held to be anti-competitive because it foreclosed both markets and took away the opportunity for the competitors to get a fair chance to compete. Further, refusal to list Fabhotels and Treebo on MMT’s platform would not only reduce consumer choices but also stifle the innovation in the market. MMT was a dominant player, and OYO enjoyed a significant market position in their respective markets, and entering into a vertical anti-competitive agreement caused AAEC to create entry barriers and harm the competitors. Similarly, while passing an order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, the CCI in Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and Another 174 Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and Another, Case No. 40 of 2019, paras 6, 7, 11, 18, 20, 23, 25. identified online platform services as fulcrum of the commercial success of the sellers to reach customers. If such platforms refuse to deal with sellers, then it would create a major hindrance for the sellers to sell their products to the customers. In this case, a group of sellers filed information alleging that Flipkart and Amazon entered into exclusive agreements and tie-ups with their preferred sellers. Through these vertical exclusive agreements, Flipkart and Amazon are exclusively launching mobile phones of the preferred sellers on their e-commerce platforms, selling the products of preferred sellers at a heavily discounted price and providing preferential listing/promotion of private labels. This leads to other sellers being excluded and foreclosed from the market because they are not allowed to compete equally on the said e-commerce platforms. By considering all these allegations against two e-commerce platforms, which hold significant market power in the relevant market, CCI directed the DG to investigate the said vertical agreements. [E] Resale Price Maintenance Explanation (e) to section 3(4) defines resale price maintenance. Resale price maintenance means any agreement to sell goods or services where the prices to be charged on the resale by the purchaser shall be stipulated by the seller unless it is clearly stated that prices lower than those prices may be charged. The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023 modified the said explanation to include indirect restrictions on the pricing condition, such as threats and imposition of sanctions if the seller fails to maintain the resale price or promotional offers to the seller for maintaining the resale price. Like in other jurisdictions across the globe, resale price maintenance remains one of the most vexed areas of antitrust enforcement. Contrary to some international authorities, in India, an arrangement for resale price maintenance is only void if it causes an AAEC in India. In fact, the High Level Committee175 High Level Committee on Competition Policy and Law, 2000, para. 4.4.1. noted that ‘in a number of countries, RPM is presumed to be per se anti-competitive. The majority of the Members of the Committee also felt that RPM should be treated as presumed to be illegal. However, after considerable discussions, in order to arrive at a consensus, it was decided not to treat it as presumed to be illegal. It will be judged under the “rule of reason”’. It has been alleged in many cases, that the object of vertical price restraints is primarily restriction on price competition between enterprises operating in the market. Resale price maintenance implies a direct or indirect object to fix or enforce a minimum resale price to be observed by the distributors or any downstream resellers of the manufacturer’s products. The CCI, in M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. and Others,176 M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. and Others, Case No. 48 of 2011, para. 8.3.8. explained section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act by saying resale price maintenance includes the agreement to sell goods on the condition that the resale price shall be fixed by sellers unless it is clearly stated that prices were lower than those prices may be charged. In this case, the CCI found that resale prices were not stipulated by Intel, and the distribution agreement allowed the distributors to fix their own resale price. On the allegation that Intel was monitoring the downstream price, the CCI held that such conduct can’t be termed anti-competitive. The CCI has witnessed various developments with regard to the treatment of vertical agreements having conditions of resale price maintenance, especially in the online distribution sector. Disputes on the competition law have been brewing in India between e-tailers and brick-and-mortar businesses for a long time now, with the emergence of strong e-tailers in the market. The strategy of the e-tailers in tying up exclusive launches and offering significant discounts for various products being sold on their respective platforms has left an impact on the businesses of conventional brick-and-mortar retailers. The brick-and-mortar outlets have stated that they are going out of business due to the actions of the strong e-tailers, and the companies are also facing a difficult time in managing their distribution chain in the process. An emerging issue in the area of vertical agreements is the maintenance of resale price maintenance both in the context of online and offline markets. Issues of resale price maintenance pertain to the effort on the part of companies to ensure a price of their product to ensure parity between online and offline sellers, thereby preventing free-riding by offline agents so as to protect the interest of dealers who maintain brick-and-mortar stores. This issue was first decided by the CCI in the case of Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Others, 177 Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Others, Case No. 80 of 2014, para. 17. in the context of the sale of a book of a famous Indian author, (Chetan Bhagat’s book, ‘Half Girlfriend’) which was exclusively available on Flipkart’s website. Allegations were raised that such arrangements slowly destroy players in the brick-and-mortar space and tend to create product-specific monopoly, leading to manipulation of price, control of production and supply, imposition of terms and conditions detrimental to the interests of consumers and distortion of fair competition in the marketplace. However, CCI did not agree with these allegations and held that such exclusive arrangements do not create any entry barriers for new entrants as the products (such as mobile phones, tablets, books, cameras, etc.) sold through the exclusive platforms face competitive constraints and are neither dominant nor hold a monopoly status. Further, the introduction of the e-platforms brought pro-competitive effectiveness to the market. Similarly, in Jasper Infotech (Snapdeal) v. KAFF Appliances, 178 Jasper Infotech (Snapdeal) v. KAFF Appliances, Case No. 61 of 2013, paras 55, 63, 67, 70. the online retailers have also instituted an information against companies who imposed restrictions on the manner in which online distribution is to be done by the e-tailers. Jasper (the company which owns and operates snapdeal.com) instituted an information against a manufacturer which had placed restrictions on its dealers in dealing with e-tailers. In the said case, the CCI has directed the DG to investigate the allegation pertaining to minimum resale price maintenance imposed by KAFF Appliances (India) Pvt. Ltd. (KAFF). Snapdeal is an online marketplace which provides a medium for buyers and sellers to meet and transact. It was alleged that KAFF, being aggrieved by the sale of its products at discounted prices on Snapdeal, started imposing resale price maintenance and adduced an email exchange between KAFF and Snapdeal to the effect that KAFF will not allow the sales of its products on Snapdeal if the Minimum Operating Price (MOP) is not maintained. However, the DG’s main investigation report and supplementary investigation report could not find any evidence showing imposition of resale price maintenance by KAFF. With regard to the email exchange between the parties, the CCI accepted the justification of the KAFF that the email was to safeguard the goodwill and protect the right of KAFF to choose the most efficient distribution channel. Finally, the conduct of KAFF was not sufficient to cause AAEC. In another case, though, the NCLAT set aside the CCI order in the Hyundai Motors case179 Hyundai Motors case, paras 79, 81, 85, 88-92, 116; See, NCLAT order Hyundai Motor India Ltd. v. CCI and Others [2018], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal No. 06 of 2017, paras 40, 41. because the CCI and DG failed to delineate the relevant markets and no proper inquiry was carried out by the CCI, and appeal against the NCLAT order is pending before the Supreme Court. Nevertheless, the reasoning of the CCI on resale price maintenance is important for academic purposes. Here, in a claim pertaining to resale price maintenance, CCI found that Hyundai placed restrictions on its dealers on the maximum permissible discount that may be given by a dealer to the end-consumer, coupled with the practice of price monitoring put in place by Hyundai wherein the dealers were penalized on account of any deviation. After considering the DG report, the CCI held that Hyundai contravened section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act by imposing resale price on its distributors and monitoring the maximum permissible discount level through the Discount Control Mechanism. Under the Discount Control Mechanism (DCP), Hyundai maintains the maximum discount which a distributor can provide to the end consumers, and Hyundai monitors its enforcement by regularly conducting meetings with its distributors to discuss the change in price and discount. As per the DCP, the distributors can’t offer a higher discount which is above the authorized discount. The CCI held that resale price maintenance can prevent competition at both the intra-brand and inter-brand levels. Not allowing to offer a higher discount harms the end customers because they have to pay a higher price. Resale price maintenance reduces the intra-brand competition which results in reduction of the pressure on the competing car manufacturers to sell their product at a competing price. In addition to resale price maintenance, the CCI also ruled that Hyundai entered into a tie-in arrangement by tying the sale of its car only with the purchase of recommended lubricants and oils. The conduct of Hyundai caused AAEC because the cost of cars increased which is a case of consumer harm. In the case of Alleged anti-competitive conduct by Maruti Suzuki India Limited in implementing discount control policy vis-à-vis dealers (‘Maruti Suzuki case’),180 Alleged anti-competitive conduct by Maruti Suzuki India Limited in implementing discount control policy vis-à-vis dealers Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2019, paras 18, 21, 34, 39, 42, 45, 46, 48-50. the CCI passed an order under section 27 of the Competition Act against Maruti Suzuki for enforcing maximum discount limits on its distributors through the DCP comes within the ambit of resale price maintenance. Further, it was found that failure to comply with the DCP by offering extra discount, the distributors were threatened with penalty and suspension of supplies. There were many instances where the distributors had to pay a penalty amount for violating DCP. Hence, it was established that the resale price maintenance was enforced on distributors. Thereafter, the CCI analysed the factors under section 19(3) and held that the resale price maintenance prevented intra-brand competition and inter-brand competition by not allowing the distributors to decrease the cost of the vehicles. This facilitated all the distributors from selling the vehicles at higher prices because there was no price competition. On the other hand, the customers had to pay higher prices to buy the vehicles. Hence, the CCI held that Maruti Suzuki violated section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act and harmed both the competition and the stakeholders of the market. Another important observation on resale price maintenance was made in the case of Shubham Sanitarywares v. Hindustan Sanitarywares and Industries Ltd and others,181 Shubham Sanitarywares v. Hindustan Sanitarywares and Industries Ltd and others, Case No. 99 of 2013, para. 11. where the opposite party was offering higher discounts to bulk builders and less discounts to retail customers. The CCI passed an order under section 26(2) by holding that offering different discounts to different customers does not violate section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act. However, it was also noted that forcing the dealers to implement specific discounts on different customers may be construed as a violation of section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act. Further, the CCI held that the discount policy in that case did not seem to have any AAEC in India and dismissed the case.

sections.title: ESSENTIAL CONDITIONS NECESSARY TO ESTABLISH VERTICAL ANTI-COMPETITIVE AGREEMENT
sections.text: §2.17 ESSENTIAL CONDITIONS NECESSARY TO ESTABLISH VERTICAL ANTI-COMPETITIVE AGREEMENT As a part of effect-based analysis, the CCI examines two key conditions, i.e., (a) the relevant markets of the parties to the agreement; and (b) their market power in the relevant market. So, before analysing the factors under section 19(3) of the Competition Act (discussed in the subsequent part), the CCI essentially requires delineating the relevant market. Then, in the context of the delineated relevant market, the CCI analyse the market presence of the party and its position of strength. It is important to know the market concentration and which enterprise holds the significant market position because the vertical anti-competitive agreements imposed by an enterprise having significant market strengths generally have a foreclosure effect, would create entry barriers, cause harm to the customers or drive the existing competitors out of the market. In some cases, the CCI delineates relevant markets and determines the market strength of both parties to the vertical anti-competitive agreement, especially in cases where it was alleged that both parties mutually agreed to the vertical anti-competitive agreement.182 MMT case, paras 22, 23, 291, 292, 297; Apple case, para. 33. For example, in the MMT case, the CCI identified two markets: both upstream and downstream markets where MMT and OYO (the parties to the said exclusive agreement) and then came to the conclusion that MMT and OYO were respectively holding market power in their respective markets. [A] Delineation of Relevant Markets of the Parties The decisional practice of the CCI shows that delineation of relevant market of the alleged parties must take place as a pre-requisite condition to analyse AAEC in the market. Thus, as a first and foremost step to analyse the AAEC, the CCI should correctly delineate the relevant market according to the factors enumerated in section 19(5), (6) and (7) of the Competition Act. The issue relating to tie-in arrangement between Hiranandani Hospital and Cryobanks was found to be in contravention of section 3(4) of the Competition Act in the market of stem cell banking in Mr. Ramakant Kini v. Dr. L.H. Hiranandani Hospital (‘Hiranandani case’).183 Mr. Ramakant Kini v. Dr. L.H. Hiranandani Hospital, Case No. 39 of 2012, para. 25. Mrs Jain entered into an agreement with M/s Life Cell India Pvt. Ltd. (Life Cell) to avail its services for banking of stem cells. Mrs Jain was registered with Hiranandani Hospital for maternity-related services and for the delivery of her child. As the time of delivery of the child drew near, Mrs Jain requested Hiranandani Hospital to allow Life Cell to collect the stem cell blood. Hiranandani Hospital refused to accede to the request of Mrs Jain on the grounds that she could avail the services of Cryobank and not any other stem cell banking service provider. The CCI held that the Hiranandani Hospital had an exclusive agreement with Cryobank under section 3(4) of the Competition Act. The CCI also said that for the purpose of section 3, there is no requirement to identify the relevant market but must see if the agreement has an anti-competitive effect in the market of the product/service of any party of the agreement. However, at the appellate stage, section 3 was read with section 19(3), (5), (6) and (7) of the Competition Act to set aside the order of CCI by the erstwhile COMPAT.184 Dr. L.H. Hiranandani Hospital v. CCI and Another [2015], (COMPAT) [2015] Comp AT 1144, paras 29, 30, 32, 33(70), 34. It was held that CCI failed to independently analyse factors of section 19(3), (5), (6) and (7) of the Competition Act to determine a violation of section 3(4) of the Competition Act. Further, the CCI erred in delineating the relevant market as the market of maternity services, ignoring that the whole dispute revolves around the agreement on stem cell bank services. It was also noted that CCI overlooked that the agreement entered between Hiranandani Hospital and Cryobanks did not restrict the choice of the service provider in the market for stem cell bank services. To understand the importance of the delineation of relevant market for analysing the factors of section 19(3), the erstwhile COMPAT largely relied on the Apple case, where tying-arraignment was not established because there was no foreclosure in either of the relevant markets of tying parties. Thus, previously decided cases show that relevant market was delineated to analyse the appreciable effect of alleged vertical anti-competitive agreements on the market, the other players of the market and the end consumer. Without delineation of the relevant product markets and relevant geographical markets, the inquiry against the vertical anti-competitive agreement would be considered incomplete. In the Hyundai Motors case,185 Hyundai Motor case, paras 40, 50. (NCLAT). the NCLAT set aside the order of CCI because CCI failed to delineate the relevant product market and the relevant geographical market while deciding the violation of section 3(4)(a) and (e) of the Competition Act. In the Hyundai Motors case, an information was filed by an authorized dealer of Hyundai, engaged in the business of reselling and servicing vehicles and spare parts.
