The business activities of an authorized dealer comprised of sales of vehicles, spare parts, accessories, and other related activities. The CCI held that the conduct of Hyundai contravened section 3(4)(a) and (e), however, the NCLAT set aside the CCI’s order by holding that the CCI’s inquiry under section 26 was incomplete because the relevant market has not been taken into consideration by the CCI. The finding of the NCLAT was based on the reasoning provided by the Supreme Court in the Coordination Committee case, where the Supreme Court held that CCI should delineate the relevant market in which the competition is affected due to the anti-competitive agreement. The Supreme Court interpreted the word ‘market’ in section 19(3) as ‘relevant market’ and held that it is important to determine the relevant market to identify the competitors of the concerned party, the boundaries of the competition, and the competitive constraints faced by market participants in order to examine the concentration of the market. The concentration of the market is key to determining which market participant holds the power over the market and whether its anti-competitive conduct would appreciably affect the market. Thus, it is necessary to delineate the relevant market to determine the market participant who has power over the market while analysing whether its conduct has an anti-competitive effect. However, unlike merger control and abuse of dominance cases, the statute is silent, and it may be argued that for the cases under section 3(4) of the Competition Act (at least at the prima facie stage), there is no need to go into precise relevant market delineation.186 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd., Case No. 18 of 2021, para. 43. For instance, in National Restaurant Association of India (‘NRAI’) v. Zomato Limited (‘Zomato’) & Others,187 National Restaurant Association of India (‘NRAI’) v. Zomato Limited (‘Zomato’) & Others, Case No. 16 of 2021, para. 34. the CCI held while passing a prima facie order directing the DG to conduct an investigation against Swiggy and Zomato for the alleged contravention of section 3(4) of the Competition Act, the relevant market need not be delineated precisely in vertical anti-competitive agreement cases. In furtherance to this observation, the CCI delineated the relevant market of Zomato and Swiggy as online food delivery platforms and operate as online intermediaries for food ordering and delivery. Also, while holding Maruti Suzuki liable for imposing resale price maintenance in the Maruti Suzuki case,188 Maruti Suzuki case, para. 16. the CCI did not precisely delineate the relevant market. CCI held that Maruti is a manufacturer dealing in the upstream market. [B] Significant Market Power in the Relevant Market The decisional practice of CCI shows that the market share or market power of the party to the vertical agreement is a pre-requisite requirement for establishing violation of section 3(4) read with section 3(1) of the Competition Act.189 Arshiya Rail Infrastructure Ltd. (ARIL) v. Ministry of Railway (‘MoR’) & Others, Case No. 64 of 2010, para. 21; Tamil Nadu Consumer Products Distributors Association v. Fangs Technology Private Limited & Other, Case No. 15 of 2018, para. 23, the CCI observed that the market share of the opposite party 1 has declined from 14.4% to 12.1% during 2017-18, and the turnover was also lower than its competitors. So, the CCI passed an order under section 26(2) of the Competition Act by holding that the opposite party-1 does not hold significant market power to violate section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act. So, for the proper analysis of the anti-competitive effect, the CCI must look at the market shares of the parties entering into such an agreement, and also the market shares of other market players.190 M/s Karni Communication Private Ltd. & Other v. Haicheng Vivo Mobile (India), Case No. 35 of 2018, para. 25. The market share of the party is compared with that of other market players to analyse whether vertical restraint by the concerned party would cause or is likely to cause adverse effects on the competition. Also, it is implicit that the vertical agreement between two insignificant players will not affect a substantial portion of the market.191 Re: Accessories World Car Audio Private Limited v. Sony India Private Limited & Other, Case No. 03 of 2020, paras 22, 23. For instance, In Re: Accessories World Car Audio Private Limited v. Sony India Private Limited & Other,192 In Re: Accessories World Car Audio Private Limited v. Sony India Private Limited & Other, Case No. 03 of 2020, paras 22, 23. while rejecting the allegation of vertical anti-competitive agreement, the CCI held that the market of car audio products consisted of a large number of players and there was no player who possesses significant market share. The CCI was of the opinion that if the market had been fragmented with the presence of a large number of players exerting competitive constraints on each other, then the vertical agreement couldn’t cause any AAEC. Also, in Automobiles Dealers Association, Hathras, UP v. Global Automobiles Ltd. & Ors.,193 Automobiles Dealers Association, Hathras, UP v. Global Automobiles Ltd. & Ors., Case No. 33 of 2011, paras 12.2, 12.5, 12.11, 12.12, 12.13. the informant alleged that clause 3 of the letter of intent and statement of Global Automobiles shows that there was an exclusive distribution agreement with its dealers for the sale of two-wheeler vehicles. Clause 3 of the letter of intent restricts the dealers from dealing with the competitors of Global Automobile without their permission. However, due to the non-significant market share of both parties, Global Automobile was a new entrant in the two-wheeler manufacturing industry. So, even if there was a restrictive vertical agreement, the presence of adverse effects on competition was not established. To consider a vertical agreement as an anti-competitive agreement under section 3(4), the enterprises who are a part of the vertical agreement must have a significant market share, the absence of which is unlikely to create any AAEC in the market. For instance, in the Apple case,194 Apple case, paras 73, 74, 78. the CCI examined the ‘share of market’ to assess whether there is anti-competitive effect due to the tie-in arrangement between Apple and mobile network service providers (i.e., Vodafone and Airtel) to limit the market for iPhone in India. As per the available data, the CCI observed that Apple at that relevant point in time had a market share of less than 3% in the smartphone market, and no market participant in the mobile network services market had a market share of more than 30%. Thus, due to insignificant market shares of the parties involved in their relevant market, the CCI observed that none of the parties to the vertical agreement has the strength to adversely affect the market outcome in terms of creating entry barriers, market foreclosure, etc. This principle was also followed in the case of Shri Dhanraj Pillay v. M/s Hockey India,195 Dhanraj Pillay and others v. Hockey India, Case No. 73/2011, paras 10.13.2, 10.13.3. where the CCI approving its earlier Apple case held that for an agreement to have an AAEC in India, the person imposing the vertical restriction should be in a dominant position, and the intent behind the restriction should be foreclosure without any obvious efficiency justifications. Further, in an order passed by the CCI in the Bajaj case,196 Bajaj case, paras 77, 80. the CCI invoked the doctrine of de minimis (i.e., where the agreements of the enterprises will have an insignificant effect on the market) to oppose the submission against a vertical agreement to have an AAEC in India. The CCI observed that such an agreement can’t harm the market where the position of the party to the agreement is weak. As Bajaj Corp. Ltd. does not hold a strong position in the relevant market of the hair oil segment, the restriction imposed by Bajaj Corp. Ltd. would not cause any negative effect on the inter-brand competition. Conversely, if an agreement has been entered into by an enterprise having a strong market power, it is more likely that such an agreement will cause or likely to cause AAEC in India. The CCI has noted that when an enterprise with strong market power enters into an agreement, the CCI will place greater reliance on the anti-competitive factors mentioned in section 19(3) of the Competition Act which are entry barriers and foreclosure effects. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations of the CCI in the Automobile spare parts case, 197 Automobile spare parts case, paras 20.6.31. 20.6.32, 20.6.35, 20.6.38. where it relied on EU jurisprudence and noted that the European Court of Justice (ECJ) in its judgment of Nederlandsche Banden-Industrie Michelin (Michelin) v. Commission explained that a dominant player holds a special responsibility to prevent distortion of competition. Hence, the law forbids a market player holding significant market strength from entering into a vertical agreement. Once the market player crosses the threshold of becoming a significant market player, then entering into a vertical anti-competitive agreement would cause an adverse effect on the market. Nevertheless, the stance of the CCI is that one of the parties in the agreement must have a sizeable market presence and may not enjoy a dominant position, as mentioned under section 4 of the Competition Act, to have any anti-competitive concerns.198 Hiranandani case, paras 29, 30. The key area of analysis that has to be seen under an agreement analysis under section 3 is that the agreement must cause or likely to cause an AAEC in India. Therefore, for an agreement to be void, it must distort the competitive structure of the market appreciably. It must be noted that the CCI has noted that an agreement having insignificant effects will not be a void agreement under section 3 of the Competition Act. To be void, such anti-competitive effects in the agreement must cause AAEC. While there are no straight jacket formulae given under the Competition Act to conclude when an enterprise will have a market power amenable to section 3(4) of the Act, it may be presumed at least on a prima facie basis that any enterprise having a market share of 30% or more will be deemed to be an enterprise having a strong market power, and hence any vertical restrictions so imposed by the enterprise must take into account the factors mentioned under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. The market participant must have significant market power in its relevant market to restrain the free competition in the downstream or upstream market, create an entry barrier, drive the existing market participants out of the market, or affect the market outcome by deterring entry. Hence, the market power of the parties to the vertical agreement needs to be looked at while assessing the factors to the AAEC under section 19(3) of the Competition Act.

sections.title: FACTORS TO DETERMINE AAEC IN INDIA
sections.text: §2.18 FACTORS TO DETERMINE AAEC IN INDIA As mentioned above, an agreement will be void under section 3(2) of the Competition Act if it causes or is likely to cause an AAEC in India. It would be pertinent to note that for all agreements (other than those agreements mentioned under section 3(3) of the Competition Act), whether at the horizontal level, vertical level or pure commercial agreements,199 The CCI in the Hiranandani case, Case No. 39 of 12, para. 11, found an independent violation under section 3(1) which was not a violation of section 3(3) or 3(4) of the Competition Act. Although the Hiranandani case was overturned in appeal on market issues, the point of law that there can be a separate violation of section 3(1) is stated herein. the burden of proof would be on the CCI to demonstrate that the agreement causes or is likely to cause an AAEC in the market in India. The CCI undertakes an effect-based analysis based on the factors to determine AAEC as provided under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. It is worthwhile to note that section 19(3) contains a checklist of both negative factors and positive factors. The negative factors bring anti-competitive effects, such as the creation of barriers to new entrants, likely to drive the existing competitors and the potential to foreclose the competition or harm consumers. The positive factors bring pro-competitive effects, such as improvement in production/distribution, scientific/economic development, or benefit to consumers. The CCI have to consider all or any of the following factors while determining whether the vertical agreement has caused or is likely to cause AAEC. The negative factors indicate that there is an AAEC, whereas the absence of these negative factors would normally indicate that there is no AAEC. The positive factors are shown by the enterprise to justify efficiency of their agreement; thus, the presence of these positive factors indicates there is no AAEC.200 Automobiles Dealers Association, Hathras, UP v. Global Automobiles and Others, Case No. 33 of 2011, para. 12.9. Though there is no straight jacket formula as everything depends on a case-by-case basis, so, for a complete analysis of the net impact of the vertical anti-competitive agreement, all the factors under section 19(3) need to be examined while assessing. [A] Negative Factors The negative factors due to vertical agreements disrupt healthy and fair competition in the market. The negative factors result in anti-competitive effects which might foreclose other suppliers/distributors by raising barriers to entry, drive out the existing competitors to reduce intra-brand or inter-brand competition, exploit the end-consumer of the market to earn more profit or create entry barriers to lock the consumers by depriving them of better choices. In the MMT case,201 MMT case, paras 297, 298, 299 304, 306, 308. where MMT was an online travel agency providing travel and tourism-related services, it entered into an agreement with OYO which was providing franchising services to budget hotels, to delist Fabhotels and Treebo (competitors of OYO). The CCI noted that the agreement between MMT and OYO had foreclosed an important online channel of distribution for the Fabhotels and Treebo in the form of denial of market access. Moreover, the agreement created entry and expansion barriers which made it difficult for a new player to enter the market and impossible for existing players to survive. Driving out Fabhotel and Treebo from the market adversely effected the budget hotels in the downstream market because this agreement limited the options of budget hotels while seeking franchise services. The CCI noted that for both franchisors and budget hotels, MMT was the key access route to reach the end consumers. So, this foreclosure had a significant impact on their business in terms of visibility, and thus, they were not getting a fair chance and had no opportunity to compete on merits. More than the harm to the competitors, the CCI is more concerned about the harm to the competitive process. Thereby, along with anti-competitive effects on the competitors, the CCI also looks at the harm caused to the end consumers due to the vertical restraints. In the Maruti Suzuki case, 202 Maruti Suzuki case, paras 48, 50, 55. it was observed that the vertical restraint did not result in accrual of any consumer benefits; rather, it resulted in the denial of benefits to consumers as they were made to pay high prices. The vertical restraint in the form of resale price maintenance eliminated the price competition between the dealers and thereby did not incentivize the dealers to pass on the benefit of extra margin to the end consumers by providing better services. Similarly, in the MMT case,203 MMT case, para. 298. the CCI observed that the vertical anti-competitive agreement harmed the consumers side by depriving the end-consumers of a wide range of choices, better schemes and policies, technological developments and other benefits of a competitive market. The CCI should examine all vertical agreements which were entered between vertical parties in order to look into the totality of all the factors mentioned in section 19(3). In this regard, it is noteworthy to look at the observations of the CCI in the Automobile spare parts case.204 Automobile spare parts case, paras 20.6.31. 20.6.32, 20.6.33, 20.6.38. The CCI analysed all the agreements entered between OEMs and their dealers and OEMs and their suppliers. The OEMs had entered into a selective distribution system, and they had prohibited their dealers from sourcing any spare parts from any third party apart from OEMs or their authorized vendors. Further, it was also observed that the conduct of the OEMs acts as an entry barrier for independent repairers because the authorized dealers were also prohibited from selling the spare parts to the independent repairers over the counter. The CCI looked into the totality of the agreements to see whether there was a case of anti-competitive behaviour that could be made out. The CCI emphasized the point that efficiencies claimed by the OEMs cannot allow the creation of entry barriers to eliminate the competitive force in the market. For the sake of efficiency gains, the CCI can’t ignore the negative effect of the said agreement on the consumers and the market stakeholders. Hence, it was held that short-term efficiency gains must not outweigh the long-term negative effects stemming from the elimination of competition in the market. Understanding the nature of competitive constrain is necessary while analysing AAEC under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. The CCI relied upon the General Court’s order in GlaxoSmithKline Services Unlimited v. Commission, wherein it was held that an analysis of the effect of the concerned agreement can be done by balancing the negative factors and positive factors of the agreement. However, the negative factors will be prioritized to prevent the market player from indulging in anti-competitive behaviour. The CCI held that even if one or two of the vertical agreements do not violate the competition law and bring efficiency, they may still be held to be anti-competitive if the holistic analysis of all the vertical agreements shows long-term anti-competitive effects to the market by creating entry barrier, driving out existing players, foreclosing the market, and charging exploitative prices from end-customers. [B] Positive Factors Factors mentioned in sub-clause (d)-(f) of section 19(3) deal with the pro-competitive effects to the competition in the market. These factors enhance the efficiency of the distribution process and contribute to consumer welfare; hence, they are called positive factors. To determine whether the vertical agreement caused AAEC or not, the CCI should always balance the effect of both positive and negative factors because such an agreement which creates barriers to entry may also induce improvements in the promotion or distribution of goods.205 Bajaj case, para. 81. For instance, in Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Others,206 Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Others, Case No. 80 of 2014, para. 17. where the CCI dismissed the allegation against e-tailers for causing appreciable adverse effects through their vertical restraint in terms of purchase and price of goods in their e-platforms. To reach its conclusion, the CCI analysed the pro-competitive effect of the agreement on the touchstone of factors elucidated under section 19(3) and held that there was no violation under section 3(4) of the Competition Act. It was found that e-tailers increased competition and provided a mechanism for price comparison and comparison of other pros and cons of the products. Hence, it was found that the agreement did not result in an AAEC in the Indian market. The CCI pointed out that the online distribution system had, in fact, shown considerable consumer efficiencies by providing the option of doorstep delivery to consumers which was more convenient than the delivery service provided by brick-and-mortar retail outlets. Thus, while balancing the anti-competitive effect and pro-competitive effect due to the agreement under section 3, the CCI generally take note of the importance of competition in the market. In a competitive market, the market players are under continuous pressure to improve their capacity and product efficiency to favour customers’ welfare. Also, the consumer will benefit because the price of the product will reduce. Further, to survive in the market, market players would be innovative and develop new technologies.207 Hiranandani case, para. 18. [1] Business Justifications or Rationales Apart from the positive factors mentioned in section 19(3) of the Competition Act, the CCI can dismiss the allegation of section 3(4) of the Competition Act by considering the business purpose or rationale behind the vertical restraints. In fact, the CCI in the Bajaj case208 Bajaj case, para. 75. has noted that business purpose or rationale of agreements must be looked into and even acknowledged that vertical arrangements can be justified on certain grounds like protection from free riding, efficient management of sales of products, economic efficiency, etc. The business rationales which were considered by the CCI in its previous orders include:(i) the objective of the requirement of written permission for the dealers from the entity before dealing with the competitors of the entity is to ensure no one is free-riding on facilities and services provided by the entity and monitor the financial activity of the dealers;209 Hyundai Motor case, Case Nos 36 and 82 of 2014, para. 70. (CCI). (ii) exclusive distribution agreement can be used as protection from free riding, efficient management of sales of products, and economic efficiency;210 Bajaj case, para. 75. (iii) exclusively selling the product through its authorized distributors to protect the brand image and goodwill;211 Ashish Ahuja v. Snapdeal.com & Ors., Case No. 17 of 2014, para. 19. (iv) to protect the interest of all distributors, an exclusive distribution agreement can designate specific sale regions to distributors and restrict the distributors from encroaching on each other’s sale regions;212 M/s K.C. Marketing v. OPPO Mobiles MU Private Limited, Case No. 34 of 2018, paras 30, 31. (v) monitoring the resale price of its own products cannot by itself be said to be anti-competitive;213 M/s. ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. Intel Corporation and Others, Case No. 48 of 2011, para. 8.3.8. and (vi) agreement to sell products exclusively on online platforms results in benefiting the consumers in terms of providing opportunity to the consumers to compare the prices and better delivery services.214 Mr. Mohit Mangslani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited & Ors., Case No. 80 of 2014, para. 17. Hence, the CCI also looks at the business rationale behind the agreement along with the pro-competitive effects like consumer benefits, improvement in production/distribution, or technological/economic development to hold section 3(4) of the Competition Act is not applicable to such agreements.

sections.title: EXEMPTION UNDER SECTION 3 OF THE COMPETITION ACT
sections.text: §2.19 EXEMPTION UNDER SECTION 3 OF THE COMPETITION ACT Section 3(5) of the Competition Act provides for two exemptions, which are intellectual property exemption and export exemption. Section 3(5)(i) of the Competition Act provides for the intellectual property exemption. The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, widens the scope of section 3(5)(i) of the Competition Act by making this provision more inclusive. The inclusive list of IPRs includes: the Copyright Act, 1957; the Patents Act, 1970; the Trade Marks Act, 1999; the Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999; the Designs Act, 2000; the Semi-conductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act, 2000; and any other intellectual property law. The Competition Act provides a limited defence to agreements under section 3 relating to IPR, wherein a person has the right to restrain any infringement of or impose reasonable conditions, as may be necessary, for protecting any rights conferred under IPR legislation. The CCI has observed, in certain decisions, the ambit of the exemption provided under this section. The ambit of the said clause has been discussed in detail in Chapter 5 on IPR and competition law. Section 3(5)(ii) provides for an exemption in cases of an agreement, for the purposes of section 3, which relates primarily to exports. Section 3 of the Competition Act provides that any agreement which causes or is likely to cause any AAEC in India is void. Therefore, section 3 only covers agreements which have an effect in India. In this backdrop, section 3(5)(ii) provides for an export exemption. Section 3(5)(ii) of the Competition Act provides a safe harbour for the enterprise to enter into an anti-competitive agreement relating to the export of goods from India. Therefore, the Competition Act exempts export cartels, i.e., a cartel between enterprises located in India which intend to cartelize in markets outside India, as opposed to import cartels, i.e., a cartel between enterprises located outside India with the aim of cartelizing in a relevant market within India. Similarly, any vertical agreement which has the sole purpose of exporting goods outside India will not be covered under section 3 of the Competition Act. In Shri Nirmal Kumar Manshani v. M/s Ruchi Soya and Others,215 Shri Nirmal Kumar Manshani v. M/s Ruchi Soya and Others, Case No. 76 of 2012, para. 99. the parties to the horizontal anti-competitive agreement pleaded that the agreement pertained to export sales, and therefore, export cartels would be exempted under section 3(5)(ii) because such an agreement had no effects on the markets in India. But, the CCI rejected the plea because neither the Parties were exporters, nor was the agreement exclusively related to exports. Merely because the end product was being exported by the millers and the processors would not immunize the contravention of section 3 of the Competition Act. The exemption under section 3(5)(ii) of the Competition Act can only be granted to enterprises which are actual exporters of goods from India. In Re: Cartelisation by Shipping Lines in the matter of provision of Maritime Motor Vehicle Transport Services to the Original Equipment Manufacturers v. Nippon Yusen Kabushiki Kaisha and Others,216 In Re: Cartelisation by Shipping Lines in the matter of provision of Maritime Motor Vehicle Transport Services to the Original Equipment Manufacturers v. Nippon Yusen Kabushiki Kaisha and Others, Suo Motu Case No. 10 of 2014, para. 37.2. the CCI rejected the plea for the exemption under section 3(5)(ii) because the opposite parties were not the exporters as per the provision but were merely providing maritime transport services to the OEMs who were actually exporters. It was further noted that the exemption under section 3(5)(ii) would not be allowed merely because the end product was being exported out of India. To claim such an exemption, the enterprises must have entered into an anti-competitive agreement exclusively for the supply distribution or control of any exported goods or services. There are not many orders on the exemption under section 3(5)(ii) of the Competition Act. Thus, the jurisprudence on exemption from the anti-competitive agreements pertaining to the export of products has not been developed. In the coming years, we can expect the CCI to apply and analyse this exemption in a detailed manner.

sections.title: CONCLUSION
sections.text: §2.20 CONCLUSION Over the past year, the CCI has dealt with various cases involving anti-competitive agreements. Through active enforcement of the Competition Act, the market is protected from the ill effects of anti-competitive agreements. This chapter discussed past case laws and how anti-competitive agreements are treated under competition law. Looking forward, we can expect stronger enforcement and a stricter stance towards cartels and vertical restraints. Along with these two traditional anti-competitive agreements, CCI will also deal with ‘hub-and-spoke cartels’. Also, on the side of enforcement, it would be interesting to see how CCI is going to use ‘leniency plus’ to detect more cartels. Thus, competition law is expecting a huge development in its jurisprudence.

plain_text:
§2.01 LEGAL FRAMEWORK Any agreement between persons or enterprises or association of enterprises or persons or association of persons, in respect of production, supply, distribution, storage, acquisition or control of goods or provisions of services, which causes an AAEC in India, is void under section 3(2) of the Competition Act, 2002 (‘Competition Act’). The Competition Commission of India (CCI) has noted, in the case of Shri Neeraj Malhotra v. Deutsche Post Bank Home Finance,1 Shri Neeraj Malhotra v. Deutsche Post Bank Home Finance, Case No. 5 of 2009, para. 17.5. The erstwhile COMPAT in GlaxoSmithKline Pharmaceuticals Limited & Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 400, para. 51, reiterated the reasoning of the Shri Neeraj Malhotra v. Deutsche Bank to hold that no case for collusive bidding was made out. that under the provisions of section 3, in an analysis of ‘agreement’, an agreement with an end consumer is not included. It has been observed that an end consumer is not involved in any activities related to the supply side of the market and the word ‘agreement’ in section 3(1) refers to any agreement in respect of activities which are on the supply side. Thus, an ‘agreement’ for the purpose of section 3 analysis cannot be entered between a supplier and a consumer as consumer can never be involved in supply side activities. For the purposes of section 3, first, the existence of an ‘agreement’ must be determined. The term ‘agreement’ normally refers to joint action or understanding by two or more parties and not unilateral action of one party.2 Section 2(b) of the Competition Act, 2002. As such, the term ‘agreement’ is construed in a very wide manner and includes any agreement or understanding or action in concert. The erstwhile COMPAT in GlaxoSmithKline Pharmaceuticals Limited & Anr. v. Competition Commission of India 3 GlaxoSmithKline Pharmaceuticals Limited & Anr. v. Competition Commission of India[2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 400, para. 48. observed that the definition of ‘agreement’ in section 2(b) of the Act is inclusive in nature. An ‘agreement’ includes any arrangement or understanding or action in concert, whether such arrangement, understanding or action is formal or in writing or is intended to be enforceable by legal proceedings. After the determination of an agreement, the effect of the agreement will have to be determined to see whether such an agreement causes, or is likely to cause, any AAEC in India. The AAEC analysis must be determined based on the parameters under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. It is the onus of the CCI to prove that an agreement causes or is likely to cause an AAEC in India. However, there are certain kinds of agreements which are mentioned under section 3(3) of the Act (i.e., cartel arrangements) which are presumed to have an AAEC in India. In such cases, the onus shifts on the parties to such an agreement to prove that the agreement will not cause or likely cause an AAEC in India based on parameters laid down in section 19(3) of the Competition Act. Thus, there are certain categories of agreements which are mentioned under section 3(3) of the Act on presumptive rule approach, meaning that once the essential ingredients of an agreement are established, there is no further need to launch into an elaborate enquiry to find out the impact of such conduct on the market and that AAEC is presumed. In that situation, the burden shifts to the contravening parties to rebut the presumption by showing that their conduct does not result in AAEC in India.

§2.02 LEGAL FRAMEWORK OF CARTELS IN INDIA Section 2(c) of the Competition Act defines a ‘cartel’ to include: an association of producers, sellers, distributors, traders or service providers who, by agreement amongst themselves, limit, control or attempt to control the production, distribution, sale or price of, or, trade in goods or provision of services. As per the definition, the scope and ambit of the term ‘cartel’ is inclusive.4 GlaxoSmithKline Pharmaceuticals Limited & Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 400, para. 48. In the erstwhile regime, the term ‘cartel’ was not defined in the Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969. As such, through judicial pronouncement, the cartel was recognized as an agreement relating to restrictive trade practice. In Union of India v. Hindustan Development Corporation, 5 Union of India v. Hindustan Development Corpn. [1993], (SC) (1993) 3 SCC 499, para. 14. the Supreme Court relied on dictionaries and American jurisprudence to interpret the word ‘cartel’ as:…The cartel therefore is an association of producers who by agreement among themselves attempt to control production, sale and prices of the product to obtain a monopoly in any particular industry or commodity. Analysing the object of formation of a cartel in other words, it amounts to an unfair trade practice which is not in the public interest. The intention to acquire monopoly power can be spelt out from formation of such a cartel by some of the producers. However, the determination whether such agreement unreasonably restrains the trade depends on the nature of the agreement and on the surrounding circumstances that give rise to an inference that the parties intended to restrain the trade and monopolise the same… It is apposite to note that the enforcement regime of the present competition law regime in India with respect to cartel enforcement is well established. Empirically, most of the decided cases on cartels deal with bid rigging in the government procurement sector. Generally, the cartels are formed in secrecy to hamper free and fair competition by controlling or rigging the prices, products and customer/territorial allocation. Cartel members continue this concerted practice in secrecy to avoid the attention of the CCI. Thus, cartels are hard to trace and bust. So, to discourage cartelization, the statutory scheme under the Competition Act provides for a presumption wherein any agreement entered into between enterprises or associations of enterprises or persons or associations of persons or between any person and enterprise or practice carried on, or decision taken by, any association of enterprises or association of persons, including cartels, engaged in identical or similar trade of goods or provision of services, which: (a) directly or indirectly determines purchase or sale prices; (b) limits or controls production, supply, markets, technical development, investment or provision of services; (c) shares the market or source of production or provision of services by way of allocation of the geographical area of the market, or type of goods or services, or number of customers in the market or any other similar way; (d) directly or indirectly results in bid rigging or collusive bidding, shall be presumed to have an AAEC in India. Unlike in the case of dominance investigation, the provisions with respect to anti-competitive agreement are applicable to even an association of persons (like a trade association) and are not only limited to the unilateral conduct of an enterprise. Further, the ambit of section 3(3) also applies to a decision taken by an association of persons. The CCI has used the expanded ambit of section 3(3) of the Competition Act to include even decisions taken in a trade association meeting since a trade association is nothing but an association of enterprises or an association of persons.

§2.03 EXISTENCE OF AN AGREEMENT It must be noted that to invoke the provisions of section 3 of the Competition Act, the existence of an ‘agreement’ is sine qua non. Such ‘agreement’ covered under section 3 of the Competition Act must be related to an economic activity, regardless of whether the activity intends to earn profit. Competition law frowns upon the activities of those enterprises or persons who, while undertaking their economic activities, indulge in practices which effect the competition adversely.6 Competition Commission of India v. Coordination Committee Artistes & Technicians of W.B. Film & Television [2017], (SC) (2017) 5 SCC 17, paras 44, 45. The term ‘agreement’ has been defined in section 2(b) of the Competition Act. Section 2(b) of the Competition Act reads as: ‘agreement’ includes any arrangement or understanding or action in concert –(i) whether or not, such arrangement, understanding or action is formal or in writing; or (ii) whether or not such arrangement, understanding or action is intended to be enforceable by legal proceedings. Based on the definition, we see that agreement has been defined in an extremely broad manner under the Competition Act to include any agreement, whether oral or written. The Supreme Court in CCI v. Coordination Committee Artistes & Technicians of W.B. Film & Television (‘Coordination Committee case’) 7 Ibid. not only held that the definition of ‘agreement’ is inclusive and that the ambit of the agreement also includes any arrangement or understanding or even concerted practice. The CCI has, in fact, noted that the understanding between the competitors might be tacit, and the definition of the agreement includes situations where the parties act on the basis of a nod or a wink. It has been observed from the decisional practice of the CCI that there is rarely direct evidence of action in concert between the market participants. In the absence of such direct evidence, the CCI must determine, based on circumstantial evidence, whether the market participants are involved in any dealings and have some form of understanding which points to the fact as to whether they are acting in coordination with each other. It must be noted herein that section 36(2), read with section 41(2) of the Competition Act, confers on the CCI and DG all the powers of a Civil Court under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 which include summoning of a person and examining him on oath, requiring the discovery and production of documents, receiving evidence on affidavit, issuing commissions for the examination of witnesses or documents. In spite of being provided with vast sweeping powers of investigation, at times, it is very difficult to obtain direct evidence with respect to the existence of a cartel since cartels traditionally are always hatched in secrecy. In such a situation, the DG and CCI rely on circumstantial evidence. Circumstantial evidence includes evidence of communications among suspected cartel operators and economic evidence concerning the market and the conduct of those participating in it which suggests the existence of consensus ad idem among market participants as opposed to independent decision-making. Examples of circumstantial evidence can be receipts, visitor entry registers, credit card bills, travel details, email dumps, telephone/mobile bills, etc., which show that the market participants had interacted with each other on a regular basis to show some concerted action. It would be seen that most of the analysis done by the DG and the CCI, while deciding cartel cases, is to analyse from direct communication and economic evidence whether there is an agreement between the market participants or whether the market participants were acting independently. The task of the DG and CCI becomes all the more onerous because most of the cartel investigations happen in an oligopolistic industry where parallel behaviour is very common. The CCI must, therefore, differentiate between a case of an anti-competitive agreement among market participants and conscious parallelism among market participants. In this regard, the CCI has noted that the evidence of agreement has to be tested on the benchmark of ‘preponderance of probabilities’.8 In Re: Alleged cartelization by steel producers, RTPE 09 of 2008, para. 111. In this regard, as observed by CCI in the case of Alleged cartelization by steel producers, 9 Ibid., para. 112. an order holding that there is no violation of section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act:Since the prohibition on participating in anti-competitive agreements and the penalties which offenders may incur are well known, it is normal for the activities which those practices and those agreements entail to take place in a clandestine fashion, for meetings to be held in secret and for the associated documentation to be reduced to a minimum. Considering the remote possibility of getting direct evidence in the case of a cartel in many cases, the existence of an anti-competitive practice or agreement can also be inferred from the conduct of the colluding parties which may include a number of coincidences and indicia which, taken together, may, in the absence of any other plausible explanation, constitute evidence of the existence of an agreement. Thus, in case of agreements as listed in Section 3(3) of the Act, once it is established that such an agreement exists, it will be presumed that the agreement has an appreciable adverse effect on competition; the onus to rebut this presumption would lie upon the opposite party. As mentioned above, agreement has been very widely defined in section 2(b) of the Competition Act. From the definition of agreement provided in the Competition Act, it can be seen that an agreement need not be in writing, nor necessarily has to be legally enforceable.10 In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India, Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2017, para. 86. The CCI held that implementation of anti-competitive agreement under section 3(3) of the Competition Act or actual causing of AAEC is not a sine qua non for establishing contravention. An arrangement or understanding is as good as a formal written agreement. The legislative intent is quite clear, and the definition encompasses both overt and tacit forms of agreements, and it is not required that it has to be an agreement as is understood in common parlance under the principles of contract law. Based on the wide definition provided under the Competition Act, the CCI has, in various decisions, noted that it is not the requirement under law to demand direct evidence of agreement in all cases and in the absence of direct evidence, existence of circumstantial evidence that tends to exclude the possibility of independent action would be sufficient to give rise to an inference of an agreement. In this regard, it is apposite to point out the observations of the CCI in the Builders Association of India v. Cement Manufactures Association and Ors. 11 Builders Association of India v. Cement Manufactures Association and Ors., Case No. 29 of 2010, para. 183. (‘Cement cartel case’) that market participants often hatch anti-competitive conspiracies in secrecy. As the CCI would hardly find any direct evidence of the anti-competitive conduct, it has to look at the circumstantial or indirect evidence to establish the anti-competitive conduct. The word ‘agreement’ under the Competition Act has been given an inclusive definition, which is not restricted to written agreements, because market participants do not reveal their anti-competitive conduct to avoid being punished under the Competition Act. Hence, the CCI will consider all the circumstantial evidence before it concludes whether there is an anti-competitive agreement among the market participants. The definition of agreement under the Competition Act also uses the term ‘action in concert’. The erstwhile COMPAT in Indian Jute Mills Association, Royal Exchange and Others v. CCI and Others (‘Jute Mill case’)12 Indian Jute Mills Association, Royal Exchange and Others v. CCI and Others [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 144, para. 96. relied on the interpretation of similar terms from other laws, such as ‘person acting in concert’ in securities law. The erstwhile COMPAT cited Daichi Sankyo Company Limited v. Jayaram Chigurupati, where the phrase ‘persons acting in concert’ used in Regulation 2(1)(e)(1) of SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 1997, has been interpreted by the Supreme Court as a shared common objective between two or persons. There may be a direct or indirect agreement between such persons to share the common objective or purpose of acquisition or substantial acquisition of shares, etc., of the target company. Thus, the element of the shared common objective or purpose is the sine qua non for the relationship of persons acting in concert:…There can be no ‘persons acting in concert’ unless there is a shared common objective or purpose between two or more persons of substantial acquisition of shares, etc. of the target company. For, dehors the element of the shared common objective or purpose the idea of ‘person acting in concert’ is as meaningless as a criminal conspiracy without any agreement to commit a criminal offence. The idea of ‘persons acting in concert’ is not about a fortuitous relationship coming into existence by accident or chance. The relationship can come into being only by design, by meeting of minds between two or more persons leading to the shared common objective or purpose of acquisition or substantial acquisition of shares, etc. of the target company. It is another matter that the common objective or purpose may be in pursuance of an agreement or an understanding, formal or informal; the acquisition of shares, etc. may be direct or indirect or the persons acting in concert may cooperate in actual acquisition of shares, etc. or they may agree to cooperate in such acquisition. Nonetheless, the element of the shared common objective or purpose is the sine qua non for the relationship of ‘persons acting in concert’ to come into being. It has been seen that there are situations where competitors come together, either under the aegis of a trade association or otherwise, to carry out legitimate business activities, like industry lobbying, etc. The degree of cooperation among competitors can also be for the purposes of carrying out research and development activities. Thus, such agreements can be a means to share risk, save costs, increase investments, pool know-how, enhance product quality and facilitate invention. These agreements, therefore, can have pro-competitive benefits which are highly beneficial to the competitive structure of the market. Having said that, there can be situations wherein competitors enter into an agreement to maximize their own profits to the detriment of the consumers. It must be observed that the operation of agreements can vary from cartels to conscious parallelism. It has been seen, from the decisional practice of the CCI, that the operation of cartels tends to be very complex, and it varies according to the number of participants involved and the nature of the market in question. The decisional practice (discussed supra) would show the way the CCI has relied on communication and economic evidence to identify whether the market participants have indulged in a cartel or not. Such analysis helps the CCI to conclude whether the parties have acted in furtherance of an anti-competitive agreement or unilaterally. It is the precise reason why concerted practice is caught under the provisions of the Competition Act. It is because, by its very nature, concerted practice does not have all the elements of a binding agreement but arises out of the behaviour of the parties concerned. The necessity to include any such arrangement or understanding within the ambit of an agreement caught under the prohibition of antitrust laws has been aptly described by Lord Denning in the case of PRTA v. W.H.Smith and Sons Ltd., as quoted by the CCI in the In Re: suo-motu case against LPG cylinder manufacturers (‘LPG-I case’),13 In Re: suo-motu case against LPG cylinder manufacturers, Suo-Moto Case No. 03 of 2011, para. 14.11. namely: People who combine together to keep up prices do not shout it from the housetops. They keep it quiet. They make their own arrangements in the cellar where no one can see. They will not put anything into writing nor even into words. A nod or wink will do. Parliament as well is aware of this. So it included not only an ‘agreement’ properly so called, but any ‘arrangement’, however informal. Further, as mentioned above, it is apposite to note that under the statutory scheme of the Competition Act, cartels are presumed to have an AAEC in India. With respect to the presumption stated, it has been mentioned by the Supreme Court of India that ‘shall be presumed’ is a presumption and not evidence in itself. In Excel Crop Care Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India (‘Excel Crop case’),14 Excel Crop Care Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2017], (SC) (2017) 8 SCC 47, paras 41.2, 49. the Supreme Court held that the kinds of agreements mentioned in section 3(3) of the Competition Act would be treated as ipso facto causing AAEC. If the CCI finds that the parties entered into an agreement covered under the categories of section 3(3) of the Competition Act, then the CCI need not undertake any further enquiry. Section 3(3) of the Competition Act shifts the onus on the parties entering into an agreement for one of the categories mentioned in section 3(3)(a) to section 3(3)(d) above to show, based on the indices mentioned under section 19(3) of the Competition Act, that such agreement does not cause an AAEC in India. The burden would shift upon such enterprises or persons to rebut the said presumption by providing adequate evidence. Similarly, in Uniglobe Mod Travels (P) Ltd v. Travel Agents Federation of India,15 Uniglobe Mod Travels (P) Ltd v. Travel Agents Federation of India, Case No. 03 of 2009, para. 68.2.1. while holding that the trade association had violated section 3(3) by collectively boycotting Singapore Airlines, the CCI explained the presumptive nature of section 3(3) of the Competition Act. The CCI said that once the existence of a horizontal anti-competitive agreement is established, the burden of proof shifts upon the parties who had entered into such agreement to prove that there is no anti-competitive effect from the said agreement. Further, the Competition Act provides for an exception to the per se rule provided under section 3(3) of the Competition Act. The stated exception provides that the provision of section 3(3) shall not be applicable to an agreement entered into by way of joint venture arrangements which increase efficiency in terms of production, supply, distribution, storage, acquisition or control of goods or services.

§2.04 STANDARD OF PROOF In order to establish the existence of a cartel, it is important to prove the existence of an agreement between market participants. As has been mentioned above, it has been observed from the decisional practice of the CCI that there is rarely direct evidence of action in concert between the market participants. In this regard, the CCI in the case of GAIL (India) Limited v. PMP Infratech Private Ltd. and Another 16 GAIL (India) Limited v. PMP Infratech Private Ltd. and Another, Case No. 41 of 2019, para. 3; In Re: Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry, Suo Motu Case No. 05 of 2016, para. 153; Mr. Rizwanul Haq Khan v. Mersen (India) Pvt. Ltd. and Another, Reference Case No. 02 of 2016, para. 44. observed that the standard of proof required to prove an anti-competitive agreement would be based on ‘preponderance of probabilities’ and not ‘beyond reasonable doubt’. Thus, in the absence of such direct evidence, the CCI has to determine, based on circumstantial evidence, whether the market participants are involved in any dealings and have some form of understanding which points to the fact as to whether they are acting in coordination with each other instead of acting unilaterally. It would be seen that most of the analysis done by the DG and the CCI, while deciding cartel cases, is to analyse from communication and economic evidence whether there is an agreement between the market participants or not. While the competition authority was going through the transaction of Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969, regime to the present competition law regime, the DG investigated major steel manufacturers for colluding to raise the prices of steel products In Re: Alleged cartelization by steel producers.17 In Re: Alleged cartelization by steel producers, RTPE 09 of 2008, paras 90, 101, 120, 121, 123. From the investigation report, it was concluded that the steel manufacturers coordinated to raise their prices, and they also limited and controlled the production of steel products. However, the CCI made a note of flawed investigation since the CCI noted that multiple factors were the reason behind the price pattern moving in tandem, such as increase in landed import price, cost of production and the demand and supply analysis. Without any direct evidence to indicate the collusion, an anti-competitive agreement cannot be assumed. So, the standard of proof to establish the violation of section 3(3) required additional evidence or what is commonly referred to as ‘plus factor’, was not met in this case. A wide range of circumstantial evidence can be used to establish the additional evidence or plus factors. It is imperative to note that conscious parallelism must be differentiated from cartel behaviour. Though parallel pricing or other parallel behaviour amounts to strong evidence, it does not justify the presence of cartel or anti-competitive agreement.18 Excel Corp case, para. 52. This means the CCI cannot shift the burden of proof by simply establishing parallel behaviour. In A.R. Polymers Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission of India,19 A.R. Polymers Pvt. Ltd. and Others v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 240, paras 23, 35, 36, 39, 43. the erstwhile COMPAT set aside the order of CCI where the opposite parties were held guilty for violating section 3(3)(a) and (d) of the Act by forming a bid rigging cartel to quote identical bids for the supply of Jungle Boots. The erstwhile COMPAT heavily relied on Union of India v. Hindustan Development Corporation because of the similarity in factual matrix to hold that mere quoting of identical tender offers by opposite parties does not mean a cartel has been formed. In that case, three manufacturers of steel bogies quoted identical prices to a tender notice issued by the Railway Board for the supply of cast steel bogies. The Tender Committee considered that the three manufacturers colluded and quoted a higher price. Thereafter, a counteroffer was given to the three manufacturers where the quotation was reduced. When the matter finally reached the Supreme Court, it was held that there was no cartel because ‘a mere quotation of identical price and an offer of further reduction by themselves would not entitle them automatically to comer the entire market by way of monopoly since the final allotment of quantities vested in the authorities who in their discretion can distribute the same to all the manufacturers including these three big manufacturers on certain basis’. Further, in the absence of a plus factor, the quoting of identical bids is not enough to infer as bid rigging under section 3(3)(d) of the Act. This, thus, makes it clear that parallel behaviour neither shifts the burden of proof nor conclusively establishes a contravention under section 3(3) of the Competition Act. On similar lines, in the case of Narendra Explosive v. Competition Commission of India,20 Narendra Explosive v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 422, paras 45, 47, 48, 52. The erstwhile COMPAT in Deputy Chief Materials Manager, Rail Coach Factory, Kapurthala, Punjab v. Faiveley Transport India Ltd.[2016], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 10 of 2016 set aside the order of CCI by holding that mere identity of price is not enough for holding that the opposite parties had indulged in bid rigging. the erstwhile COMPAT ruled that CCI had erred in presuming the similar bid quotation of the opposite parties and they had been fixing the price of the CN Containers with disc. The Appellate Tribunal noted that DG did not collect any circumstantial evidence to conclude that the opposite parties communicated before submitting their bids or any consensus on the pricing of the product. The erstwhile COMPAT found the reasoning behind the similar quotation to be plausible because the concerned product was limited to three ordinance factories and was required to be manufactured as per specifications. The opposite parties’ quotations were based on the last purchase price which was available in the public domain and acted as a guiding factor for the opposite parties. The quotations of the last purchase were available on the website of the ordinance factories; thus, the justification advanced by the opposite parties that the information available in the public domain behind the similar quotation or parallel pricing was considered plausible by the Tribunal. Thus, the Courts have consistently held that mere parallel pricing will not suffice; one needs to demonstrate plus factors to conclusively demonstrate cartelization. Thus, if the parties under investigation can show business reasons for such parallel behaviour, they may not be held guilty of cartelization. In Re: All India Tyre Dealers Federation v. Tyre Manufactures Association (‘Tyre case’),21 In Re: All India Tyre Dealers Federation v. Tyre Manufactures Association, MRTP Case: RTPE No. 20 of 2008, paras 86, 102. the case was initiated based on a complaint made by All India Tyre Dealers Association. Based on the complaint, the CCI undertook a detailed price-cost comparison of the tyre manufacturers to analyse whether the tyre companies have engaged in a cartel. Before analysing the communication and the economic evidence, the CCI noted that the industry is an oligopolistic market; it is extremely likely that each market participant is aware of the other participant’s behaviour. In an oligopolistic market, interdependence between firms is an important characteristic of such a market which would mean that each firm in such a market considers the likely reactions of other firms while making independent decisions, particularly as regards prices and output. Though oligopolistic markets can lead to competitive outcomes, the outcomes may not always be market-driven but rather a result of concerted effort or collusion. Therefore, the parallel behaviour of competitors can be a result of intelligent market adaptation in an oligopolistic market or a case of anti-competitive agreement. So, the key factor of analysis to be done is whether there is an agreement between market participants to engage in cartelization or not. Even the decisional practice of the CCI has recognized the aspect of parallel behaviour among enterprises, especially in an oligopolistic market. What the DG and CCI look for, in a cartel investigation, is whether there are any ‘plus factors’ which prove that there is an agreement among the market participants. Unlike contract law, the definition of ‘agreement’ under the competition law is much wider and includes both overt and tacit forms. In the LPG-I case,22 Rajasthan Cylinders and Containers Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Anr.[2020], (SC) (2020) 16 SCC 615, paras 89-94, 105. an investigation was conducted by the CCI to analyse whether the manufactures of LPG cylinders had colluded and manipulated their bids for the supply of LPG cylinders to the Indian Oil Corporation Ltd. (IOCL). While conducting the said investigation, the CCI analysed the market in great detail and noted that the industry has structural characteristics which support cartelization. The DG noted that even though bid rigging can occur in any sector, there are some sectors in which it is more likely to occur due to particular features of the industry or of the product involved. The CCI held that there was a violation of section 3(3)(d) read with section 3(1) of the Competition Act. Post the decision of the CCI, the manufacturers preferred to go on appeal to the erstwhile COMPAT. On the basis of the facts mentioned above, the erstwhile COMPAT agreed with the observations made by the CCI and rejected the appeals made by the manufacturers. However, the said judgment of both the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT was set aside by the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court noted that CCI and the erstwhile COMPAT erred in not considering the ‘plus factors’ by analysing the ground realities of the market. The way the tenders are floated indicated that IOCL was deciding the rate of the award and was in complete control over the tender process. The IOCL, through negotiation, set the final price which was less than the price quoted by the L-1 bidders. Furthermore, L-2 and L-3 bidders were also awarded the tender at the rate of L-1. The only difference was that an L-1 bidder received the order for larger quantities than L-2 and L-3. Similarly, an L-2 bidder received larger orders than an L-3. IOCL ensured that all the manufacturers got some orders. Furthermore, in the LPG-I case, the CCI and the erstwhile COMPAT failed to consider the reasonable justification for the ‘plus factors’ by ignoring the fact that a few days before IOCL floated its tender, Bharat Petroleum Corporation Limited (BPCL) floated a tender for LPG cylinders. The BPCL tender acted as a guiding factor for the manufacturers while quoting their bids for the IOCL tender. The market conditions were not taken into account considering the fact that there were a small number of suppliers present in the market. The manufacturers quoted an identical price so that they could get some orders, or else they would be driven out of the market; hence, the argument that the manufacturers quoted an identical price despite varying costs loses its significance. Also, only nineteen manufacturers attended the meeting of the association. Also, there are twelve new entrants in the market, which is a significant number. Moreover, the market condition is described as an oligopsony where only a few buyers are present in the market, and they are controlling the market. In an oligopsony market, the Supreme Court noted that parallel pricing could not be concluded as a concerted practice, and further corroborative evidence is required to substantiate the presence of a cartel. Setting aside the judgments of the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT, the Supreme Court held that IOCL was controlling the tender process. The Supreme Court noted that the CCI failed to carry out further analysis of the factors under section 19(3) of the Competition Act to hold that there was an adverse effect on the market. Finally, the CCI should have summoned IOCL before concluding that there was a violation of section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act. Thus, by way of this case, the Supreme Court, while acknowledging the ill effects of cartelization, has created a defence for participants to the agreement to show that their arrangement does not cause AAEC in the market. As mentioned in Chapter 1 of this book, the penalties on the cartel participants are huge under the Competition Act. Owing to such onerous fines, it will be very rare that one will find direct evidence, like minutes of meetings, etc., to conclude the existence of a cartel. It has to be seen whether a cartel exists or not from the totality of the circumstances. In the Express Industry Council of India v. Jet Airways, Indigo, SpiceJet 23 Express Industry Council of India v. Jet Airways, Indigo, SpiceJet, Case No. 30 of 2013, paras 88, 93, 94, 106, 112, 113, 114. (‘Airline surcharge cartel case’), the allegation was that certain domestic airlines introduced a Fuel Surcharge (FSC) for transporting cargo. This surcharge was fixed at a uniform rate of INR 5/kg. It was the case of the informant that, although the levy of FSC was ostensibly introduced as being an extra charge linked to fuel prices, investigation showed that when such prices were reduced (as in the past), there had been no corresponding decrease in FSC. It was further stated that FSC has actually been increased by the airlines by acting in concert, and that too, by almost the same rate and from almost the same date. Likewise, FSC was uniformly increased in the past, even without a corresponding increase in fuel prices. Further, it was also averred that when fuel prices declined substantially, the airlines had, in concert, uniformly increased FSC. The CCI held that the market participants acted in a concerted manner in fixing the FSC rate. Regarding the definition of ‘agreement’, the CCI observed that the definition is inclusive and not exhaustive. As there is rarely any direct evidence, the CCI considers the situations as ‘agreement’ where the market participants are instructed based on nods or winks. Generally, the market participants engage in anti-competitive conduct in a clandestine manner, with secret meetings and hardly any explicit written documents exist. So, by taking together all the coincidences and indicia, the CCI had to reconstruct certain details by using the deduction method. In this case, the CCI unfolded the entire conspiracy by looking at the airlines’ conduct, such as the issuance of circulars to increase FSC at a fixed price by different airlines. Further, CCI also noted that airlines had common agents through whom they were exchanging information. After a holistic appreciation of the evidence, the CCI could see that the airlines violated section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act by entering into an anti-competitive agreement to fix the FSC. Further, the statutory scheme provided under the Competition Act provides that the CCI has been conferred power to impose only civil fines, and it does not have criminal jurisdiction. Further, as described in the preceding chapter, under section 36 of the Competition Act, the CCI is vested with some powers of a Civil Court in the conduct of its enquiries. Therefore, based on the statutory scheme, the offences provided to in the Competition Act, including cartelization, not being criminal offences, the test of proof will be only ‘balance’ of probability’ and ‘liaison of intention’ which can be established with the support of indirect or circumstantial evidence. This is in contradiction to a criminal offence where the offence has to be proved beyond reasonable doubt. In this regard, while upholding an order passed under section 27 of the Competition Act against a bid rigging cartel, the erstwhile COMPAT noted, in the case of MDD Medical Systems India Pvt. Ltd. v. Foundation for Common Cause and Others 24 MDD Medical Systems India Pvt. Ltd. v. Foundation for Common Cause and Others [2013], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 93 Of 2012, para. 18. and held that even though section 27 of the Competition Act speaks about penalty, the process against anti-competitive conduct under competition law is not similar to criminal offence where proof beyond reasonable doubt is required. Hence, in competition law jurisprudence, the test of probability would be a good test. Based on the above, it can be seen that the CCI and DG can rely on both direct and circumstantial evidence. The key test in a cartel investigation, as had been laid down by the CCI, is analysis of the evidence which establishes that a particular set of acts and conduct of the market participants cannot be explained ‘but for’ some sort of anti-competitive agreement and action in concert among them. Consequently, the existence of an anti-competitive agreement can be inferred from the intention or conduct of the parties and may be established by circumstantial evidence alone in the absence of any alternate explanation. In analysing whether there was a concerted action among the market participants, the CCI undertakes a detailed econometric analysis. In the backdrop of the legal position as delineated above, it becomes quite clear that in order to find contravention of section 3 of the Competition Act, it is not the requirement of law to demand direct evidence of agreement in all cases. In the absence of direct evidence, existence of circumstantial evidence that tends to exclude the possibility of independent action would be sufficient to give rise to an inference of existence of an agreement.

§2.05 DIRECT EVIDENCE In a cartel case, it is very difficult to find direct evidence wherein there is a clearly formulated written agreement wherein the competitors have decided to engage in cartel behaviour. The Supreme Court in the LPG-I case25 LPG-I case, para. 82. reproduced excerpts of the ‘Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Policy Roundtables Prosecuting Cartels without Direct Evidence 2006’ to discuss the nature of evidence. The Supreme Court noted that direct evidence, such as documents or emails or any kind of communication describing the working of cartel, can rarely be found. However, in some of the cases, direct evidence is unearthed wherein members of a bid rigging cartel share their rate of the bid via email or a minute of the meeting which shows that the trade association in its meeting mandated the members to limit their production or dispatch to create an artificial demand. As mentioned above, most of the cartel activities are hatched in secrecy; hence, direct evidence is very difficult to find. Although direct evidence is very hard to find, there have been instances of direct evidence which have been unearthed by the DG and CCI. For example, in cases relating to the entertainment industry, there have been instances wherein there have been minutes of the meeting of the trade association to show that the members decided to collectively boycott a non-member. In M/s International Subscription Agency v. Federation of Publishers’ and Booksellers’ Associations in India (FPBAI),26 M/s International Subscription Agency v. Federation of Publishers’ and Booksellers’ Associations in India (FPBAI), Case No. 33 of 2019, paras 18, 22. the discount policy of the FPBAI explicitly stated that ‘any of our associated companies shall not offer any discount on the publishers’ price fixed for customers in India’. Further, FPBAI issued notice to its members for non-compliance with its discount policy. Based on this discount policy and the coercive action for non-compliance, the CCI held FPBAI for violating section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act. Even in In Re: Cartelization by public sector insurance companies in rigging the bids submitted in response to the tenders floated by the Government of Kerala for selecting insurance service provider for Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojna. v. National Insurance Co. Ltd. and Others,27 In Re: Cartelization by public sector insurance companies in rigging the bids submitted in response to the tenders floated by the Government of Kerala for selecting insurance service provider for Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojna. v. National Insurance Co. Ltd. and Others, Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2014, para. 32. (‘Insurance cartel case’), the case was initiated based on anonymous information which complained that four public sector insurance companies, namely National Insurance Co. Ltd., New India Assurance Co. Ltd., Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. and United India Insurance Co. Ltd., had rigged the tender floated by the Government of Kerala for selecting an insurance service provider for implementation of the Rashtriya Swasthya Bima Yojna (RSBY) for the year 2010-2011. The information alleged that the insurance companies had formed a cartel and quoted higher premium rates in response to the tender. Further, by way of the ‘Inter Company Coordination Committee’ (ICCC) meeting attended by the officials of insurance companies, they had decided on an anti-competitive business sharing model to share the business. In the Insurance cartel case,28 Ibid., paras 32, 33, 38, 39, 60. the CCI relied on the investigation by the DG to note that the insurance companies had held a meeting under the aegis of the trade association with the sole agenda to discuss sharing of business and submission of quotation of a bid. The minutes of the meetings were considered direct evidence because they revealed that the decision to collude in terms of sharing of business and submission of quotation was taken by the insurance companies. Having said that, since the fines on cartels are huge, it will be very difficult in future cases once the companies are aware of the principles of competition law to find direct evidence of cartels. The table below shows the decisional trends of the CCI in terms of analysis of direct evidence across multiple decisional cases. S. No. Case Name (Citation) Evidence (Paragraph No.) Reasoning (Paragraph No.) 1. In Re: Alleged Cartelisation in Supply of the LPG Cylinders Procured through Tenders by Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. (HPCL). Suo Motu Case No. 1 of 2014 Direct evidence, such as emails were exchanged between bidders regarding price, business strategies, etc. (Paragraph 77) The CCI held that the bidders discussed and shared sensitive and confidential information regarding the tender to manipulate the bidding process of tender no. 2. (Paragraphs 107, 111-113) In this case, the CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 2. Mr. Sajjan Khaitan v. Eastern India Motion Picture Association & Others. Case No. 16 of 2011. Coordination Committee passed a resolution and issued letters to the TV channels to stop the telecast of the dubbed version of the Mahabharata serial or face non-cooperation. (Paragraph 7.1.12) 3. Cement Cartel Case. Case No. 29 of 2010. Collection and exchange of retail prices of cement from different companies. (Paragraphs 212, 213) The Rules and Regulations of the Cement Manufacturers Association show that it was used as a platform to share information among the cement manufacturers. (Paragraph 214) In this case, the CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 4. In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India. Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2017. The beer manufacturers exchanged commercial information regarding MRP and Basic Prices through email and WhatsApp (Paragraphs 11(1)(iv) and (v), 18, 19, 20) The information regarding Beer production, revenue details, targets, stock sold and stock held was exchanged to monitor each other’s actual stock movement, and they can keep track of each other’s market share. (Paragraphs 74, 78) The beer manufacturers agreed to share their periodical sales and sales data. (Paragraphs 56, 72, 73) The beer manufacturers decided to stop producing and restricted the supply of Beer. (Paragraphs 11(1)(viii), 151) The beer manufacturers were able to hold discussions under the umbrella of the trade association. (Paragraphs 11(1)(ix), 163, 164) Beer manufacturer decided to lower their production of beer to put pressure on the Excise Authorities to lower the Excise duty on Beer. Limiting supplies impacted the beer market because the price of beer increased. (Paragraphs 69, 70, 71) CCI found that the beer manufacturers shared information to decide the rate at which they would buy second-hand bottles. This resulted in limiting the supply of second-hand bottles. (Paragraphs 152, 153, 157) The CCI found that office bearers of beer manufacturers were holding discussions relating to anti-competitive practice under the aegis of the trade association. The trade association also arranged conference calls and facilitated discussions. (Paragraphs 164, 170). In this case, the CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 5. Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others. Reference Case No. 02 of 2020. Physical meeting between cartel members in Hyderabad and Bombay to fix the quotes of the tenders. (Paragraph 80(i)) The cartel members exchanged commercial data either through physical meetings or virtual communication. Before submitting quotes, the cartel members were regularly communicating with each other via calls, messages, or mails. (Paragraph 4) The trade associations colluded to demand higher tariffs from the informant. (Paragraphs 20.2, 20.3, 21.9) Alkem was in agreement with BCDA by issuing OLS to prospective stockists and was indulging in the practice of demanding SAI/Non-Objection Certificate/Approval Letter/Circulation Letter from BCDA before commencing supplies. This constitutes an anticompetitive agreement between Alkem and BCDA. (Paragraph 21.5)

§2.06 INDIRECT EVIDENCE: ROLE OF CIRCUMSTANTIAL EVIDENCE The DG and CCI also look at the chain of circumstantial evidence to analyse whether the market participants entered into an anti-competitive agreement or not. As mentioned above, the key area of analysing circumstantial evidence is to find out whether the particular set of acts and conduct of the market participants cannot be explained ‘but for’ some sort of anti-competitive agreement and action in concert among them.29 Airline Surcharge Cartel case, para. 39. The decisional practice of the CCI shows that most of the cartel investigations that have happened till now are in oligopolistic industries. As has been explained above, in an oligopolistic market, parallel behaviour is extremely common due to the interdependence of firms’ conduct. The key chain of evidence must be seen whether such parallel behaviour is an action of conscious parallelism or an agreement to collude. The key thing that needs to be kept in mind is that the CCI looks at the totality of the evidence to come to a conclusion as to whether there is a case of cartelization. In this regard, it is to be noted that in most of the cartel cases, the market participants take a plea that their conduct is a case of parallel behaviour and not cartel behaviour. However, the CCI has noted that parallel behaviour, although common in an oligopolistic industry, may not carry on for a lengthy period of time unless there is a cartel arrangement between the market players. It is apposite to note the observations of the erstwhile COMPAT, where it was observed that: We do not think that this is a case of mere price parallelism. We have already made observation that price parallelism on one or two occasions though might raise strong suspicion, it may not be enough for drawing the inference of cartelization. We reiterate that observation. However, in this case it was not a sweet coincidence and in fact it is shown that this was not only a common pattern or practice, but this continued in case of tenders floated by other corporations. This consistent practice and common pattern when continues for a long time, there could be no other inference excepting that of cartelization. There are two recognized forms of circumstantial evidence which are taken into account by the DG and the CCI in a cartel investigation: (a) communication evidence and (b) economic evidence. Communication evidence indicates that cartel operators met or otherwise communicated but does not describe the substance of their communications. It includes, for example, meetings at a trade association, records of telephone conversations among suspected cartel participants, their travel to a common destination, common hotel receipts, common entry in registers, and notes or records of meetings in which they participated. Economic evidence, however, can be categorized as either conduct or structural evidence.
