The former includes, most importantly, evidence of parallel conduct by suspected cartel members, e.g., simultaneous and identical price increases or suspicious bidding patterns in public tenders, lower capacity utilization, and high correlation on prices. The CCI has observed that among the set of circumstantial evidence, evidence of communication among the participants to an anti-competitive agreement may give an important clue for establishing any contravention. Communication evidence might prove that contravening parties met and communicated with each other to determine their future or present behaviour. Therefore, in terms of the weight of evidence, communication evidence is given more importance than economic evidence. The CCI in the Cement cartel case30 Cement cartel case, para. 232. considered various circumstantial evidence as plus factors to establish violation under section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act. The CCI observed the plus factors, such as the exchange of price and production using the Cement Manufacturers Association (CMA) as a platform, lowering the capacity utilization, and lowering the supply of cement which resulted in price increases in the cement industry. Further, it is to be noted that it is not necessary that cartel participants must raise prices at the same time, to be caught under the ambit of section 3(3) of the Competition Act. In fact, the CCI, in the Airline surcharge cartel case,31 Airline surcharge cartel case, para. 79. (Order dated 7 Mar. 2018). has noted that it is necessary to analyse the behaviour of the cartel participants, in entirety, to see whether they have engaged in a cartel. There may be situations wherein the market participants intentionally create artificial time gaps among themselves to increase the price, to show that it is a case of intelligent market adoption to the other competitors’ action, but not a cartel. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations of the CCI in the Airline surcharge cartel case:32 Airline surcharge cartel case, para. 114. (Order dated 17 Nov. 2015). The so-called time gap is just a matter of few days. It would be travesty of competition norms if such lag theory is countenanced by the Competition Agency. It is neither the requirement of law nor any other jurisprudence that cartels must originate symmetrically, progress symmetrically and culminate in a similar fashion. More often than not, the colluding parties would like to break the patterns through artificial gaps and arrangements so as to create a façade of competitive behaviour. In such a projected ‘competitive landscape’, it is the bounden duty of the Authority to pierce this artificial veil and to examine the real behaviour of the colluding parties. The present case perfectly fits such stratagem where artificial lags and gaps were sought to be passed off and projected to envision a competitive scenario when none existed. In fact, such justifications and explanations only complete the chain of the arrangement and understanding reached amongst the parties. Now, we will discuss the manner in which the DG and CCI have looked at both communication and economic evidence in deciding cartel matters. [A] Communication Evidence Communication evidence is a type of indirect evidence consisting of proof that the cartel members attended a physical meeting or communicated virtually via telephone, WhatsApp, email, etc. As opposed to direct evidence, the substance of the communication may not be available in communication evidence.33 LPG-I case, para. 82. (SC). When an entity exchanges strategic information or price-related information with its competitors, who are accepting the information constitutes evidence, it will be presumed that the entity had accepted the information and implemented the information in line with the anti-competitive conduct. Information could be exchanged through mail, phone calls or meetings. Mere receiving calls or mails or attending meetings where some strategic information was passed would be enough to use it as evidence to establish an anti-competitive agreement. To avoid violation of section 3 of the Competition Act, the entity who received the information should object to the exchange of information.34 Ibid., para. 84. (SC). The DG and the CCI, in any cartel investigation, investigate whether the market participants regularly meet, either under the aegis of a trade association or otherwise. Frequent meetings between competitors followed by similar market conduct have always been seen as a plus factor in a cartel investigation. The following are some instances of communication evidence which has been relied upon by the CCI while deciding cartel cases. [1] Frequent Meetings among Competitors In the LPG-I case,35 LPG-I case, para. 14.17. (CCI). the CCI took note of the OECD’s Document on detecting bid rigging in public procurement. The CCI noted that: The fact of agreement can be either communicated by words or conduct. Bidders need to know and communicate with each other to reach an agreement. Once bidders know each other well enough to discuss bid rigging, they need a convenient location where they can talk. Of course, communications can occur by telephone, email, fax or letter, and they often do; however, many bid riggers believe that they are less likely to leave evidence of their communications if they have face-to-face meetings. These meetings occur most often at, or in association with trade association meetings, or other professional or social events. They are also likely to occur prior to the opening of the tender process. In the Cement cartel case, the CCI noted that the cement companies participated in the meetings of the CMA. Further, under the aegis of CMA, the companies interacted on a regular basis on sensitive information like pricing, production details, dispatch details, etc. It was observed by the CCI that CMA, based on a governmental directive, collected retail prices and wholesale prices of the cement from different centres and transmitted them onwards to the government. It was observed by the CCI that the prices of cement for all the cement companies went up after two meetings of the CMA. One of the defences that was raised by the cement companies was that the prices of cement went up after two meetings of the Cement Manufactures Association and not all the meetings of the said trade association. However, the CCI noted that it is not required that prices of goods need to go up after each meeting, especially when the price of cement is already artificially inflated. This approach of the CCI to penalize cement companies when the prices went up after two meetings, and not all meetings show the assertive approach of the CCI. In this background, it is very important for companies to have proper document management systems and pricing policies to show, by way of contemporaneous documents, changes in prices of companies and reasons thereof so that during an investigation, such documents can be shown to justify the rationale for price change. Even in the Insurance cartel case,36 Insurance cartel case, paras 37, 38. the CCI observed that the insurance companies held meetings under the aegis of the trade association to discuss the sharing of business and quotation for each bid. Such kind of communication evidence is taken by the CCI as evidence of cartel behaviour. Having said that, the CCI has also noted that just because the market participants have met in a trade association does not ipso facto mean that they indulged in an anti-competitive behaviour. For illustration, in the Tyre case, the CCI noted that the tyre industry had a trade association. However, on investigation, it was revealed that the trade association was used as a forum for lobbying for the welfare of the trade industry and making representations to governmental organizations, which cannot be viewed as anti-competitive. What the DG and CCI look at is whether the communication evidence so collected can be seen as a plus factor, i.e., whether such communication aims to formulate a cartel strategy. The CCI in People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others,37 People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others, Case No. 90 of 2016, paras 57-61, 63. Appeal against the CCO order was dismissed by NCLAT. See, People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha Internationals [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 46 of 2022. from all the call data records obtained by the DG, noted that the competitors were frequently interacting with each other was considered a plus factor while proving a bid rigging cartel. In fact, the erstwhile COMPAT has noted that the existence of a trade association gives an opportunity to the competitors to interact with each other and discuss trade problems. Further, it is not required to prove that any party actually discussed the prices by actively taking part in the meeting. If there is direct evidence to that effect, that is certainly a pointer towards the fact that such a party had a tacit agreement with its competitors. However, the existence of an association and further holding of the meetings just one or two days prior to the submitting bids, followed by quoting similar bid prices, gives a strong indication of collusive conduct. [2] Publication of Price Bulletin In two landmark cases decided by the CCI (such as the Cement cartel case), the CCI has noted that the practice of publishing price bulletins by a trade association is also a plus factor when deciding on a case for cartelization. In the Cement cartel case,38 Cement cartel case, paras 7(xlvii), 202, 247, 258. the CCI noted that the CMA collected wholesale and retail prices of cement. The retail prices collected from different centres are transmitted to the Department of Industrial Policy and Promotion (DIPP), while the wholesale prices are transmitted to the office of the Economic Advisor. The retail and wholesale prices are collected from all over the country. Although the mandate to collect the cement prices was given to the CMA, it was also observed that the CMA was nominating some representatives from each company to collect the prices for each region for all the companies operating in that region. Based on the same, it was noted by the CCI that the prices of all competing cement companies are being collected. In such a situation, when the cement companies nominated by CMA are collecting prices of other competing cement companies over telephones and e-mails, coordination on prices is easily facilitated. Based on the above, it was concluded by the CCI that when competitors are interacting using the platform of CMA, and they are in touch with each other over the phone and e-mails as regards prices – both retail and wholesale, it cannot be denied that there is always an opportunity to discuss the determination and fixation of prices for future, which is prohibited under the provisions of the Competition Act. The fact that it is being done under the instruction of DIPP (a wing of the Government of India) does not absolve CMA or the cement companies engaged in this exercise from running afoul of the provisions of the Competition Act. Further, the investigation showed that CMA had several publications, such as Executive Summary-Cement Industry and Cement Statistics-Interregional Movement of Cement which give details of the production and dispatch of each company and are circulated only among the members. Therefore, the cement companies not only get information on the prices of other cement companies but also receive details about the production and dispatch details of the other companies. The CCI noted that sharing such sensitive information makes coordination easier among cement companies and help them plan their future conduct. In light of the above communication evidence, the CCI identified that there was information sharing among the competitors on prices, quantity and dispatch details, which in light of the economic evidence (discussed below) shows an agreement among the cement companies to cartelize. Similarly, in T.R. Chandran v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC),39 T.R. Chandran v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC), Case Nos 09 and 36 of 2017, paras 114, 153, 208, 209. the trade association (i.e., NECC) displayed daily egg prices, zone-wise, on its website and monitored the monthly/annual prices of eggs at various egg production and consumption centres. The CCI held that by mandating the farmers to follow the price fixed and by ensuring adherence to such fixed prices, the NECC has violated section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act. [3] Commonality of Key Business Personnel In a bid rigging case, the investigation done by the DG and CCI had revealed that there was cross-ownership and common directors among the various bidders in the market. It was revealed that the companies involved in the bidding process are closely related, having common directors or different members of the same family at the helm of affairs. Having such a relationship indicated that out of the thirteen companies under investigation, the modus operandi of at least ten of these firms was governed by the principles of mutual understanding and benefit. This was seen as a plus factor since there is a chance of sharing commercially sensitive information among various companies, which will help in agreeing on future conduct. Similarly, in the in Re: Alleged cartelisation in supply of LPG Cylinders procured through tenders by Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. (HPCL), (‘LPG-II case’),40 Re: Alleged cartelisation in supply of LPG Cylinders procured through tenders by Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. (HPCL), Suo Motu Case No. 1 of 2014, paras 74, 109. The NCLAT upheld the order passes by the CCI by holding that the identical behaviours of the parties were a result of collusion. See, Sahuwala Cylinders Pvt. Ltd. & Others v. Competition Commission of India and Another[2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 38 of 2019. and the Airline surcharge cartel case,41 Airline surcharge cartel case, para. 106. it was noted by the CCI that there were common agents that were appointed by market participants, which were seen to share commercial sensitive and future information of one company with another. Based on the same, it was noted that there was exchange of information between companies, which eventually led to collusion. Therefore, it is key that while appointing agents or other trading partners, a very strong non-disclosure agreement be signed with such entities that is scrupulously followed to ensure that there is no transmission of commercially sensitive information of one company to its competitors. Having said that, in the Airline surcharge cartel case, the airline companies said that there was the presence of common agents among airlines who could share commercially sensitive information among themselves. Although the agents are not allowed by the airline to disclose commercial information to other airlines, it cannot be ruled out that agents do share commercially sensitive information among airlines. Thus, while appointing agents, distributors or other trading partners, who are also the trading partners of their competitors, the companies must be extra cautious. Similarly, In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing,42 In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing, Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2020, paras 30, 88. some of the cartel members were family friends, and they submitted cover bids under the influence of friendship. The CCI noted this evidence as a plus factor to conclude the presence of a bid rigging cartel. Further, In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing,43 In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing, Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2016, paras 5, 56-59. the CCI held that the bid rigging cartel was controlled by one person or family because the director of 2nd opposite party is a close friend of 1st opposite party. Further, the sole proprietor of 3rd opposite party and also the son of 4th opposite party’s director. [4] Other Evidence The investigation pattern of the DG shows that they rely on other kinds of evidence to show that the market participants met before submitting a bid, like common visitor entries, common bank accounts or places used to submit bids, common agents, or hotel bills to show that the officers of companies met for a meal one day before the submission of a bid. There are instances where bidders use common IP address to submit bids in order to manipulate the bidding process. CCI also considers other login details, including the user ID, username, login date and time, proximity of logout date and time and caller ID, to establish that the bids of all cartel members were filed by a common person.44 In Re: Chief Materials Manager, North Western Railway v. Molded Fiberglass Products and Others. Case No. 03/2018, paras 34, 35. The table below shows the decisional trends of the CCI in terms of analysis of other types of communication evidence across multiple decisional cases. S. No. Case Name (Citation) Evidence (Paragraph No.) Reasoning (Paragraph No.) 1. In Re: Cartelisation in the supply of Protective Tubes to Indian Railways. Suo Moto 06/2020 The cartel members used common IP address to submit their bids in twelve bidding processes. (Paragraph 20) The CCI acknowledged the presence of a cartel because the cartel members filed their bids from common IP address. (Paragraph 29) 2. In Re: Chief Materials Manager, North Western Railway v. Moulded Fibreglass Products and Others. Case No. 03/2018 The cartel members used common IP address to submit bids in thirty-four tenders. (Paragraph 33) From other login details to conclude that all bids were submitted from a common IP address. (Paragraph 34) The holistic evaluation of evidence showed that the cartel members colluded to fix prices, allocate markets, and rig the bidding process. (Paragraphs 58, 59) 3. GAIL (India) Limited v. PMP Infratech Private Ltd. and Another. Case No. 41 of 2019. The bids were submitted from a common IP address. Cartel members used the same office premises and systems to submit their bids for which they were competing. (Paragraph 43) The CCI held that the cartel members were constantly communicating with each other to coordinate their actions, which shows the existence of anti-competitive activity. (Paragraph 39) 4. People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others. Case No. 90 of 2016. The office bearer of one cartel member (designated bidder to win the tender) paid the Tender Charge on behalf of other cartel members who were providing only submitting cover bids. After the designated bidder won the tender, the other cartel members returned the refunded Tender Charge. (Paragraphs 47, 48) All the evidence, i.e., usage of common IP address, call records, and close relationships between cartel members, has to be seen holistically to determine the existence of an agreement. (Paragraph 56) All the cartel members used a single IP address to submit their bids. (Paragraph 53) [B] Economic Evidence Economic evidence is also analysed by the CCI on the touchstone of the test explained above. Economic evidence is the conduct of the market participants which is important to identify the presence of an anti-competitive. To analyse economic evidence in a cartel case, the DG and the CCI undertake elaborate analysis of data relating to demand trends of the product, production, production capacity, capacity utilization, cost analysis, price analysis, cost of sales/sales realization/margin; cost of production and final price movement for each market participant to see whether there is any agreement among the companies. Economic evidence includes the parallel behaviour of the market participants, the industrial performance of the market participants (huge profit margin, stable market shares, repetitive violators of competition law), and a market structure providing a suitable environment to form a cartel (characteristics of the market structure: high concentration, high entry barriers, high degree of vertical integration, homogenous product), etc.45 LPG-I case, (2018) 16 SCC 615, para. 82. (SC). The investigation pattern suggests that the DG asks for data on price, costs, demand, capacity, etc., for a minimum of preceding three years. Such an exercise is done for each of the market participants which are being investigated to see whether there is a pattern undertaken by such market participants to indicate the existence of an agreement. For example, in the Cement cartel case,46 Cement cartel case, paras 239, 269, 276. the DG analysed the capacity utilization figures of 2010-2011 in comparison to the previous year to conclude that the cement companies had intentionally engaged in limiting the production of cement in order to raise the price of cement. This conclusion was reached because the CCI noted that the demand for cement is inelastic, and the demand for cement would more or less remain the same for a particular period of time every year. In light of the demand scenario, it was seen that the cement companies reduced their capacity utilization from 82% in 2009-2010 to 73% in 2010-2011 for the month of November, when there was a high demand for cement. Similar exercise was also done by the DG in the Excel Crop case,47 Excel Crop case, paras 3.17, 3.19. (CCI). where it was observed that the price of APT had increased exponentially in comparison to previous years, and it was also noted that the profit margin of the parties was also increased. As mentioned above, parallel behaviour is extremely common in an oligopolistic market where each market participant considers the likely reactions of other firms while making independent decisions, particularly regarding prices and output. What must be analysed, from economic evidence, is whether the said action is a result of independent action or as a result of agreement. It is to be noted that economic evidence must be seen in the totality of the industry circumstances to see whether a case of cartel can be made out. There is no one-size-fits-all approach which can be taken in a cartel investigation. For example, in the Cement cartel case, the DG and the CCI found that cement companies indulged in limiting production by reducing their capacity utilization. Conversely, in the Tyre case,48 Tyre case, para. 114. it was found that while some of the tyre companies reduced their capacity utilization, it was not seen as evidence of cartelization since the overall production capacity had increased. While economic evidence is seen and analysed by the company, it must be ensured that proper data on pricing and other sensitive information are properly maintained by the company. It was seen in the Airline surcharge cartel case49 Airline surcharge cartel case, paras 92, 93, 100, 106. that all the airlines were giving the reason that the primary reason for charging a fuel surcharge was the rise in the aviation turbine fuel (ATF) price. However, upon investigation, it was found that none of the airlines could provide any contemporaneous documents to show how the rise in ATF prices had led to fixing and subsequent rises in fuel surcharge rates. In fact, on conducting a correlation analysis, it was found that at times, FSC rates were increased when ATF prices were falling. Due to lack of maintaining any pricing data by airlines, the CCI raised an adverse inference against them in a cartel investigation. This raised an important aspect of document retention policies wherein CCI hinted that companies should maintain proper contemporaneous documents to show that pricing decisions were taken unilaterally. The CCI took an adverse inference of not maintaining pricing data by airlines. It must be noted that economic evidence is seen as an addition to communication evidence in a cartel investigation. Absence of economic evidence cannot be used as a defence to show that there is no cartelization. There has hardly been any cartel decision where the CCI has found an infringement of section 3(3) of the Competition Act only on the basis of economic evidence. Some of the key economic tests identified by the decisional practice of the CCI are detailed below. [1] Analysis of Final Price in Comparison to the Input Price Business prudence requires that the final price must account for the input price of the raw material and other overheads plus a markup to ensure that the business is profitable. Having said that, in a market where there are choices of market participants, each company undertakes to give the best possible price to have better revenue, more customers and more purchase orders. However, in a cartel case, the companies collude so that the prices of all companies are kept artificially high so that each one can earn supra-normal profits. In light of the above, in most of the cartel cases, the DG analyses the final price increase of the product for each of the market participants during the period of investigation and compares such price increase with the price of input costs, during the same time, for producing the concerned product. During the same time, if it is seen that even the input price, i.e., the price of raw material, has gone up during the same point of time, an economic justification can be made for the price increase of the final product. In the absence of a price increase of the raw material, it can be concluded (based on various other additional factors) that the market participants colluded to keep the price levels high. In fact, in the Cement cartel case,50 Cement cartel case, para. 269. the CCI had noted that due to concreted practices, there was an increase in the cement price by all market participants, and the price was not increased due to a change in excise duty. Conversely, the CCI noted in the case of Shubham Sanitarywares v. Hindustan Sanitaryware and Industries 51 Shubham Sanitarywares v. Hindustan Sanitaryware and Industries, Case No. 99 of 2013, para. 14. The CCI order was upheld by the erstwhile COMPAT in Shubham Sanitarywares v. Competition Commission of India and Ors., Appeal No. 19 of 2016. that the price of the product under question has not increased for the past three years. Such stagnant prices were taken by the CCI to indicate there was no cartel. [2] Price Correlation Exercise for All Market Participants In order to find out whether there is an agreement and concerted action among the market participants to raise prices in a consistent manner, the DG generally undertakes a price correlation exercise for each of the players; i.e., a correlation exercise is seen whether the prices of products for all market participants are similar and moving in the same direction. In the Cement cartel case,52 Cement cartel case, para. 7(xxv), 7(xxvii), 92, 223. it was seen by the DG, based on the data on market prices, that the correlation of change in prices or the movement of prices of all the companies is positive and very close to each other (more than 0.5%). Such a strong correlation between cement prices for all cement companies across regions in India gave a strong indication of price parallelism. Since the cost of production, transportation charges, etc., varies from company to company, economic prudence suggests that the price of individual companies should also vary. Therefore, the DG noted that the movement of prices of all the companies in the same range and in the same direction is not possible unless there is prior consultation and discussion about the prices among them. Based on analysis of correlations of absolute price, price change and percentage price change, it was concluded by the DG that the prices of the cement manufacturers show a positive correlation in every state of operation and hence was taken as evidence of cartelization. The observations of the DG were also agreed by the CCI in their final decision. Conversely, in the Tyre case,53 Tyre case, para. 320. the range between prices of tyres of different manufacturers was more than INR 1,000 for the period 2005-2009, and the range had come down to INR 600 in 2010. The CCI noted that the product under investigation, i.e., tyre, is homogeneous. For a homogenous product, a 6%-12% range of difference in prices implies that the prices are dissimilar and there is no parallelism in absolute prices. Further, with respect to price movement in percentage terms, it was noted that there were wide variations among various tyre manufactures. Such wide variations showed that there was no agreement among tyre companies. Depending upon the facts and circumstances, the CCI considers whether the price parallelism by the competitors was due to the specific market condition or due to collusion before leading to an inference of cartelization. This economic evidence is commonly found in bid rigging cases. One of the forms of bid rigging is Identical or Level bidding, where bidders coordinate to quote identical price of their bids. By publishing price lists, raising prices by a specific increment, fixing or removing discounts, or maintaining a decided price differentials based on quantity, type, or size of product, the bidders can manipulate the bidding process in their favour. For instance, in Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others,54 Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others, Reference Case No. 02 of 2020, paras 85, 86, 95. the CCI held that there was a violation of section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act because the bidders indulged in identical pricing in the bids in Eastern Railway tenders for the eight years. Similarly, in People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others,55 People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others, Case No. 90 of 2016, paras 41, 43, 44. the CCI held that the prices quoted were similar; hence, the competitors were neither competitive nor independent. [3] Production Capacity and Capacity Utilization In all cartel investigations, the DG analyses the production capacity of the market participants during the period of investigation and also the capacity utilization during the same time frame. Further, the trend in the demand for said product under investigation is also seen during the same time frame. The reason for such an analysis is required to see whether the market participants have indulged in deliberately curtailing production in the face of rising demand, which will have the effect of an increase in the final price. For illustration, in the Cement cartel case,56 Cement cartel case, paras 7(xxix), 7(xxxvi), 7(xxxix), 245, 246. the DG noted that the production capacity of cement had increased from 157 MMT in 2005-06 to 287 MMT by the end of March 2011. However, capacity utilization was on a continuous downward trend from 2008 to 2009. During the financial year 2010-2011, capacity utilization had come down to 73% from 82% in the previous years, in spite of a very high demand for cement. A lower capacity utilization by all cement manufacturers, in the absence of any justifiable reason, was taken by the CCI as a plus factor to prove the existence of an anti-competitive agreement among cement manufacturers to limit the production of cement, which in turn, increased the price of cement. As such, the DG undertook a comparison of price index with capacity utilization index of each of the cement companies. It was noted that ever since 2006-2007, the capacity utilization and cement price index of cement companies have been moving in the opposite direction. While the capacity utilization has been declining, the price index has been increasing. It was also noted that the production capacity in total was increasing, and yet the capacity utilization of all cement companies was decreasing. The DG noted that the cement companies could not come up with any business rationale for the decrease in capacity utilization in spite of a surge in demand. Based on the same, the DG noted that this is a result of a deliberate attempt to reduce supply by not utilizing full capacity and thereby increasing the price of cement in the market. The DG also concluded that the cement companies are charging unreasonable and higher than competitive prices. The last quarter of the financial year 2010-2011 witnessed a price increase of 20%-50% throughout the country in comparison to prices in the third quarter of 2010-2011 which was a result of a reduction in capacity utilization and controlling the supply in the market. The observations of the DG were also endorsed by the CCI in the final decision. Similarly, In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India 57 In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India, Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2017, paras 71, 74. The NCLAT also upheld the CCI order in Pawan Jagetia and Others v. CCI and Others [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 16 of 2021. At present, at appeal is pending before the Supreme Court, Civil Appeal No. 000747 of 2023. The Supreme Court passed a stay on the CCI’s recovery proceeding of the penalty, and the matter is sub judice. (‘Beer cartel case’), the CCI initiated a suo motu investigation pursuant to the receipt of a leniency application by Crown Beers India Private Limited and SABMillers India Limited under section 46 of the Competition Act read with Regulation 5 of the Competition Commission of India (Lesser Penalty) Regulations, 2009. From the evidence collected from the leniency application, the CCI prima facie noted that in several states and union territories, price fixation and implementation of price adjustment existed in the beer industry. After a holistic analysis of the evidence under the leniency application and the DG report, the CCI concluded the existence of a beer cartel in the ten states and union territories and found that the beer cartel violated section 3(3)(a), (b) and (c) of the Competition Act. The beer manufacturers reduced their beer production to create an artificial scarcity of their product in the market. The beer manufacturers were also sharing their commercial information, such as beer production, revenue details, targets, stock sold and stock held by the companies, and details of their stocks lying with the distributors. This was done to limit the production of beer in the market and put pressure on the government to lower the excise duty on beer. The CCI found both direct evidence and indirect evidence to hold such anti-competitive to be violative of section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act. [4] Profit Margin of the Market Participants As a part of a cartel investigation, the DG analyses the profit margin of each of the market participants to see whether there is a trend which shows the existence of an agreement. In the Cement cartel case,58 Cement cartel case, paras 277, 283. the CCI noted the cement manufacturing-wise trend in profit margins and net profit. Because of cartelization, cement manufacturers were able to make huge profits over the years by limiting and controlling supplies and by imposing a higher cost per cement bag. Furthermore, the net profit of the cement manufacturers shows that they were able to make a high net profit by eliminating competition and charging unreasonable prices. [5] Economic Evidence to Establish Bid Rigging [a] Bid Price Versus Input Price In all the bid rigging cases, the DG makes an analysis of the final bid prices, the price of the inputs and other key indices of costs like freight, transportation, etc. This analysis is required to be done to see whether the price being quoted in a bid is an independent price of the market participants or if there is a case of bid rigging where the companies have colluded with each other during the process of bidding. In one of the bid rigging cases, it was noted that the bids quoted for the product by the manufacturers were the same and not based on cost and expected profit margins. It was noted, during the investigation, that though the source of procurement of raw material was the same for all the manufacturers, the cost incurred by each of the manufacturers for such procurement of raw material was not the same. The input cost was different for each company, yet all the companies submitted bids with the same price. Similarly, the CCI undertook a detailed analysis of the bidding pattern in the Aluminium Phosphide case, where it was seen that there was an identity in the rates quoted by the bidders even when the factories and the offices of these parties were not located in one and same state and they had to make supplies to locations far off from their factories located at different places. Therefore, in cases where in spite of a varying cost structure for companies, price bids submitted by companies have identical (to the last decimal) or similar bids have been held by the CCI, as a case of bid rigging. Therefore, to conduct a final price versus input analysis, the DG and CCI undertook a detailed costing analysis of each of the market participants and analysed the same in light of the final prices quoted by the concerned company. Further, it is apposite to note the observations of the Supreme Court in the Excel Crop case.59 Excel Crop case, paras 48, 49. (SC). One of the defences which are raised in every cartel matter is that it is a case of parallel pricing by the market participants, and such actions are not borne out due to any preconceived agreement. In this regard, the Supreme Court, in the context of bid rigging, had noted that identical pricing on one or two occasions, though it raises strong suspicion, may not be enough to draw the inference of the concerted agreement, but when is repeated constantly with odd figures (same to the last decimal) and without any reasonable explanation for the same, would only draw an inference of a pre-concerted agreement. In Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others,60 Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others, Reference Case No. 02 of 2020, paras 87, 88, 93. the CCI observed the quotations of bidders and noted that despite differences in manufacturing, transportation, packaging, and insurance, the cartel members fixed their quotation at INR 17,800. Hence, this, coupled with other evidence, was held to be in violation of section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act for manipulating the bidding process. [b] Cover Bids There have been instances wherein entities participate in the bidding process without any intention to compete but to create a façade of competition. Such entities have no technical qualification to participate in tenders because they might not be having proper equipment or experience certificate to fulfil the technical eligibility. Their sole purpose of participating in the bidding process is to support one of the entities to win the bid. Generally, the sister company or the entities have a good relationship with the designate winner who tends to submit bids so that the bid of the designate bidder will not be rejected due to insufficient number of bids,61 In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing, Suo Motu No. 01 of 2020, paras 60, 97. thereby creating a mirage of competition, where none exists. [c] Sub-contracting In sub-contracting, the cartel member will suppress its bid or submit a losing bid so that in exchange it will get the sub-contract from the winner of the tender. This results in higher profits for both cartel members. Further, the cartel members can rotate their roles in subsequent tenders. The CCI in Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra) 62 Surendra Prasad v. Maharashtra State Power Generation Co. Ltd. & Others, Case No. 61 of 2013, paras 41, 124, 125. considered that the rivals working as sub-contractors for each other were used to establish the anti-competitive arrangement. [d] Other Economic Evidence for Establishing Bid Rigging Cartel There are also other types of economic evidence, such as collective withdrawal and common reason for withdrawal,63 In Re: Alleged Cartelisation in Supply of the LPG Cylinders Procured through Tenders by Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. (HPCL), Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2014, paras 52-55. pattern of market allocation,64 In Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra), Case No. 61 of 2013, paras 99-111. bogus expenses by the cartel members for showing losses,65 Ibid., paras 126, 127. etc., that can be considered a plus factor to establish a presence of bid rigging cartel. The table below shows the decisional trends of the CCI in terms of analysis of communication and economic evidence across multiple decisional cases: S. No. Case Name (Citation) Evidence (Communication/Economic) Reasoning 1. Airline surcharge case. Case No. 30 of 2013 The airline increased the FSC rate on the same date. (Paragraph 90) The airlines discussed the FSC rates in meetings. (Paragraph 106) The alleged airlines had hired common agents. (Paragraph 104) The CCI held that the levy of FSC is at a fixed rate on per kilogram basis of the cargo weight. None of the airlines was able to furnish any data in support of the determination of FSC rates. Further, the airlines used common agents to exchange information among themselves. Hence, the airline acted in parallel to increase the FSC rates. (Paragraphs 84, 99, 106, 107) 2. People’s All India Anti-Corruption and Crime Prevention Society v. Usha International Ltd. and Others. Case No. 90 of 2016. The call data record proved that the cartel members were constantly communicating during the tender proceeding. (Paragraphs 57-61) The rates quoted by the cartel members are very close, less than INR 30, despite the input cost and other conditions of the market. (Paragraph 41) Frequent interaction among cartel members is considered a plus factor in proving a bid rigging cartel. (Paragraph 63) The CCI noted that the identical pricing shows that the cartel members are not competitive and independent. (Paragraphs 43, 44) 3. Cement cartel case (Case No. 29 of 2010) Market is Oligopolistic because Cement, as a product, has only two or three categories. The monthly prices of cement of the cement manufacturers were moving in tandem. (Paragraph 229) The cement manufacturers were unable to provide any reasonable explanation for the determination of prices. (Paragraph 233) The cement manufacturers’ overall capacity utilization reduced from 83% in 2009-10 to 73% in 2010-11. All the cement manufacturers reduced their production of cement in 2009 and 2010. Similarly, the cement manufacturers were colluding regarding the dispatch of cement. (Paragraphs 239, 245, 250-252, 257, 258, 260, 261) The price of cement per bag was increased by the cement manufacturers. Further, the sale, sale The cement manufacturers were aware of the actions of each other and colluded in a manner which led to non-competitive outcomes. (Paragraph 220) There was price parallelism between different cement manufacturers because of the strong positive correlation in the prices. (Paragraphs 224, 232) The cement manufacturers were unable to corroborate the change in cement prices with the market dynamics. (Paragraph 238) After looking at the annual reports of the cement manufacturers, it was clarified that they were not utilizing the overall capacity. During 2009-10 and 2010-11, the production of cement has fallen drastically. The cement manufacturers similarly entered into an identical realization and margin of cement manufacturers increased. Hence, the cement manufacturers earned huge profits. (Paragraphs 268, 280, 282) behaviour relating to the dispatch of cement to control the supply of cement. (Paragraphs 252, 259, 263, 266) The cement manufacturer created an artificial demand by shortening production and, thereafter, increased the price of cement. Hence, the cement manufacturers deliberately reduced capacity utilization to manipulate and control the supply in the market and earned huge profits by increasing the prices. (Paragraphs 273, 275, 277, 283) The CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 4. In Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct in the Beer Market in India. Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2017 The beer manufacturers decided to stop producing and restricted the supply of Beer. (Paragraphs 11(1)(vi), 67) The CCI held that exchange of commercially information competitors compromises the integrity of independent bidding process and was likely to stifle competition. (Paragraph 23) Beer manufacturer decided to lower their production of beer to put pressure on the Excise Authorities to lower the Excise duty on Beer. Limiting supplies impacted the beer market because the price of beer increased. The CCI noted that the conduct of the beer manufacturer violated section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act. (Paragraphs 69, 70, 71) In this case, CCI found both direct and indirect evidence. 5. LPG-II case. Suo Motu Case No. 1 of 2014 Direct evidence, such as emails were exchanged between bidders regarding price, business strategies, etc. (Paragraph 77) The CCI also found certain indirect evidence, such as the bidders discussed and parallel acted in terms of: (a) quotation of bids (paragraph 48) (b) collective withdrawal of bids (paragraph 53) The CCI held that the bidders discussed and shared sensitive and confidential information regarding the tender to manipulate the bidding process of tender no. 2. (Paragraphs 107, 111-113) (c) common reason in withdrawal letters (paragraphs 54, 55) (d) common format of withdrawal letter (paragraph 60) (e) common IP address and agents (paragraphs 65, 74) 6. In Re: Aluminium Phosphide Tablets Manufacturers. Suo Motu No. 02 of 2011. The bidders concerted to manipulate the bidding process by quoting identical rates even if their production costs were different. Also, they collectively abstained from bidding in certain tenders. (Paragraphs 3.14, 6.2) The CCI said that the parallel behaviour of the bidders can be considered evidence of coordinated behaviour on the part of the three bidders regarding the tender. (Paragraph 6.29) 7. T.R. Chandran v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC). Case Nos 09 and 36 of 2017. The trade association (i.e., NECC) displayed daily egg prices, zone-wise, on its website and monitored the monthly/annual price of eggs at various egg production and consumption centres. (Paragraph 114) Mandating the farmers to follow the price fixed by the NECC is a violation of section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act. (Paragraphs 153, 208, 209) 8. In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing. Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2020 Some of the cartel members were family friends, and they submitted cover bids under the influence of friendship. (Paragraph 30) The CCI used this evidence as a plus factor to conclude the presence of bid rigging cartel. (Paragraph 88) 9. In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing. Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2016 A director of a 2nd cartel member is the close friend of 1st cartel member, the sole proprietor of 3rd cartel member and the son of 4th cartel member’s director. (Paragraphs 5, 56) Additionally, 2nd cartel member arranged the relevant documents, prepared demand drafts and authorization certificates, and uploaded the tender documents (common IP address) of all cartel members. (Paragraphs 57-58) 4th cartel member aided the 2nd cartel member in fulfilling the eligibility criteria for participation in the tenders. Even though the 4th cartel member was eligible to bid, it did not participate in the tender process and suppressed its bid. (Paragraph 60) Even though the cartel members were separate legal entities and had bid as competitors, they were controlled by one person and had a commonplace for business. (Paragraphs 56-59) There is a meeting of mind among cartel members to rig the bidding process. Once an agreement on bid rigging is established, then it will be presumed to have an adverse effect. The onus to rebut the presumption is on the cartel members. (Paragraph 63) 10. Mr. Rakesh Khare v. Krishna Engineering Works and Others. Reference Case No. 02 of 2020 The CCI took note of the identical pricing in the bids submitted by the cartel members in Eastern Railway tenders for the eight years 2012-19. (Paragraphs 85, 86) By mutually deciding the prices to be quoted, the cartel members were manipulating the tenders. (Paragraph 95) CCI took note of the quotations and found that despite differences in manufacturing, transportation, packaging, and insurance, the cartel members fixed their quotation at INR 17,800. (Paragraphs 87, 88) The cartel members did not indicate the price break-down of their quotations as stipulated in the ‘Instructions to Tenderers for Manual and E-Tender and General Tender Conditions for Open Tenders’ as issued by the procurement authority. This was done with the sole intention of keeping their bids identical. (Paragraph 93) 11. Food Corporation of India v. Shivalik Agro Poly Products Ltd. and Others. Reference Case No. 07 of 2018. From the depositions of the cartel members, it was clear that they had decided to quote an identical price. (Paragraphs 54, 59) The CCI held that the cartel members indulged in bid rigging. (Paragraph 63) 12. In Re: Alleged bid-rigging in E-Tenders invited by the Department of Agriculture, Government of Uttar Pradesh for soil sample testing. Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2020 Cartel members submitted bids without prior experience in soil testing work and did not have any soil testing machine. (Paragraphs 10 (h), (j), (l)-(n).) One of the cartel members participated in the bidding process to support the designated bidder from being rejected due to insufficient number of bids. (Paragraph 60) To help the designated bidder win, other cartel members indulged in submitting fake invoices and false certificates to act as cover bidders. (Paragraph 132) 13. In Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra). Case No. 61 of 2013. The previous record showed the cartel members worked as sub-contractors for each other in various contracts. (Paragraphs 41, 124) Competitors working as sub-contractors for each other shows the anti-competitive arrangement. (Paragraph 125)

§2.07 SINGLE ECONOMIC ENTITY It must be kept in mind that the provision, with respect to agreement, is only applicable in cases when there is an agreement between parties who are completely independent of each other and operate autonomously. The CCI has noted and accepted the internationally accepted doctrine of single economic entity in its decisional practice.66 Exclsuive Motors Pvt. Ltd. v. Automobili Lamborghini S.P.A, CCI order, Case No. 52 of 2012, para. 6. Exclsuive Motors Pvt. Ltd. v. Automobili Lamborghini S.P.A and Another [2016], (COMPAT) 2014 SCC OnLine Comp AT 1, paras 11, 14. It has been noted by the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT that agreements between enterprises which form part of the same group, owing to the fact that the sole control is exercised by one company, cannot be assessed under section 3 of the Competition Act. The rationale adopted by the CCI is that the agreement referred to under section 3 refers to agreement between two enterprises that are independent of each other and not between two enterprises which are part of the same group. The rationale is that enterprises within the same group are not autonomous in their operations since the decisive influence over such enterprises can be held by a single enterprise. In the Exclusive Motors Private Limited v. Automobili Lamborghini S.P.A. (‘Lamborghini case’),67 Luxury cars such as Audi, SEAT, Lamborghini, Volkswagen, Skoda, Bentley, Bugatti and Porsche – were all considered within the same group of Volkswagen. even when ‘Volkswagen India’ had a separate legal existence, the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT noted since it belonged to the same group as Automobili Lamborghini, they were considered a single economic entity and internal agreements between them were outside the scope of section 3. Further, in the said case, the erstwhile COMPAT looked in detail at the manner in which operations are carried out by Volkswagen India and Automobili Lamborghini. It was seen that the operations were totally controlled by a common parent entity, and hence, they formed a single economic entity. A note of caution must be struck herein that in the Lamborghini case, it was clear that the control was exercised by one entity. There can be situations, while interpreting the Competition Act, where one enterprise can form a part of two or more different groups. So, in such a case, agreements between enterprises may or may not form a part of the single economic entity. The concept of single economic entity has to be seen on a de facto basis to see whether the parties to the agreement are acting in a manner which shows that they are in fact, a single unit. The aspect of single economic entity was also discussed in the Insurance cartel case,68 Insurance cartel case, paras 22-26. where section 27 order was passed against the cartelization by public sector insurance companies in rigging the bids submitted in response to the tenders floated by the Government of Kerala for selecting an insurance service provider for RSBY. The CCI was analysing whether the public sector insurance companies had engaged in bid rigging under the provisions of section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act. A defence was raised by the insurance companies, stating that they constitute a single economic entity as all the insurance companies were owned by General Insurance Company. It was also submitted by the insurance companies that, pursuant to the enactment of the General Insurance Business (Nationalization) Amendment Act, 2002, the Government of India holds 100% shares of each of the insurance companies and controls the management and affairs of the insurance companies through Department of Financial Services (Insurance Division), Ministry of Finance. However, the CCI did not accept the defence of single economic entity raised by the insurance companies. The CCI noted that although the public sector insurance companies are presently under the overall supervision of the Central Government, each of the insurance companies had placed a separate bid in response to the tenders issued by the Government of Kerala for the implementation of RSBY/CHIS schemes. Further, all decisions relating to the submission of bids, determination of bid amounts, business sharing arrangements, etc., were taken internally at the company level without any ex ante approval/directions from the Ministry of Finance. Even the decisions taken by the companies were not notified ex post to the Ministry. Based on the same, it was noted by the CCI that the insurance companies participated in the tenders independently, and the Ministry of Finance had no role to play. On this basis, the CCI held that the Ministry of Finance did not exercise any de facto (i.e., where an enterprise holds less than the majority of voting rights but in practice controls half of the votes) or de jure control (i.e., where an enterprise holds more than 50% of voting rights) over business decisions of insurance companies in submitting bids for impugned tenders and therefore, it was decided that insurance companies cannot be said to constitute a single economic unit. The approach adopted by the CCI in the cases discussed above shows that the CCI will look into the manner in which the business decisions are carried out by enterprises and not limit itself to the shareholding pattern and board composition to check whether the actions of enterprises are independent of each other or not, to decide whether the enterprises form a single economic unit. To form a single economic entity, the CCI will look into both de jure control (control by way of shareholding, board of directors, etc.) and de facto control (the manner in which operations of a company are carried out).

§2.08 DETERMINATION OF RELEVANT MARKET The relevant market is the boundary within which all anti-competitive practices are analysed by the CCI. In the High Committee Report,69 High Level Committee on Competition Policy and Law, 2000, para. 4.3.4. it is said that for horizontal agreement it is important to define relevant market. This will help to determine whether the concerned agreement between the parties is on substitutable products or not. It must be kept in mind that as per the statutory scheme provided under the Competition Act, there is no explicit requirement to construct a relevant market in an anti-competitive agreement investigation, like in an abuse of dominance investigation or a merger analysis. The decisional practice of the CCI clearly shows that there is no mandatory requirement under section 3(1) and section 3(3) of the Act as also under section 19(3) to determine and construct a relevant market. The CCI, in the Cement cartel case,70 Cement cartel case, para. 6.5.44. However, the erstwhile COMPAT in Lafarge India Limited and Others v. CCI and Another [2015], (COMPAT) 2015 SCC OnLine Comp AT 1120, para. 137, remanded the matter back to the CCI because of the procedural errors and the principles of natural justice was not followed while passing the final order. Thereafter, on 31 Aug. 2016, the CCI passed the final order (para. 219) reiterating that there is no requirement of the Competition Act to determine a relevant market under the provisions of section 3. held that there is no need to determine relevant market for establishing an anti-competitive agreement because there is a distinction between ‘market’ as in section 3 and ‘relevant market’ defined in section 4 of the Competition Act. However, in the case of the Coordination Committee,71 Coordination Committee case, paras 14, 33. the Supreme Court deviated from the decisional practice of CCI and observed that the first and foremost aspect that needs determination is the relevant market. In this judgment, the Supreme Court dealt with the substantive issue of contravening section 3(3)(b) by the members of the film and television artists trade union. The cartel members caused appreciable adverse effects by entering into agreements to prevent the telecast of the dubbed ‘Mahabharat’ serial on TV channels. Further, it was also held that the AAEC must be seen in the context of ‘relevant market’ as defined under section 2(r) of the Competition Act. However, if such an approach becomes the norm, it has the potential to have far-reaching implications, as it is likely to lead to an unnecessary pre-condition for analysing cartels. The CCI filed a clarification application to which the Supreme Court clarified that the determination of ‘relevant market’ is not a mandatory pre-condition for making assessment of cartels, particularly having regard to the statutory presumption contained in section 3 of the Competition Act itself.

§2.09 TYPES OF CARTEL Section 3(3) of the Competition Act enlists specific types of agreements/arrangements among persons or enterprises or association of persons which are engaged in identical or similar trade of goods or provision of services which are considered per se anti-competitive. According to section 3(3) of the Competition Act, agreements, including cartels, which: (a) directly or indirectly determine purchase or sales prices; (b) limit or control production, supply, markets, technical development, investment or the provision of services; (c) share the market or source of production or provision of services by way of allocation of the geographical area of the market, type of goods or services, or number of customers in the market or any other similar way; (d) directly or indirectly result in bid rigging or collusive bidding, are presumed to cause an AAEC in India. The four kinds of horizontal agreements, recognized as cartels under section 3(3) of the Competition Act, are discussed herein below. [A] Price Fixing Section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act provides that an agreement among enterprises at the same level of trade, which directly or indirectly determines purchase or sale prices, will be presumed to have an AAEC. The wording of the section is broad enough to cover not only the final price but also include other instances of terms of trade, like discounts, etc., which have an impact on the final price. Fixing prices is a concerted activity between competitors to set their prices to buy or sell goods/services. For instance, In Re: Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry,72 In Re: Anti-competitive conduct in the paper manufacturing industry, Suo Motu Case No. 05 of 2016, paras 159, 160, 192. competitors of the paper manufacturing industry decide to coordinate to draw a road map for increasing the price of paper. The CCI found direct evidence in the form of emails where it was clearly written that the paper manufacturers were discussing to collude and increase the price of the product. The fear of competition was stopping them form increasing the price. However, the paper manufacturers found a way to dodge the prevailing competition in the market by colluding through participating in physical meetings or exchanging emails to share commercially sensitive information. Sharing commercial information between market participants takes away the freedom to decide the price of the goods as per the supply and demand of the market. In People for Animals (PFA) and Another v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC) and Other,73 People for Animals (PFA) and Another v. National Egg Co-ordination Committee (NECC) and Other, Case Nos 09 and 36 of 2017, paras 114, 116, 197, 198, 201. The NCLAT has upheld the order of CCI in People of Animals v. CCI and Others, Competition Appeal (AT) 25 of 2022. the CCI decided that the National Egg Coordination Committee (NECC) had indulged in fixing and declaration of prices of eggs from 2009 to 2020. The NECC’s zonal production centres coordinated with the zonal consumption centres as well as other production centres, and after conducting a thorough demand and supply analysis, declared daily prices for eggs to be followed by all farmers. It was also observed that NECC was strictly implementing its anti-competitive conduct on farmers in each zone by informing them not to sell at the fixed price and threatening to impose penalties in case of non-compliance. Apart from violating section 3(3)(a) of the Competition Act, NECC was also limiting the production and supply of eggs. Controlling production helped NECC to ensure that the farmers were selling eggs at fixed prices. From a competition law standpoint, the price of the product should be based on the holistic assessment of demand and supply. Thus, it should not be subject to the vagaries of whimsical diktats of NECC or any of its members which would determine the pricing of a product. CCI found that NECC was trying to control prices by threatening the farmers by levying or threatening to levy penalties on them. In terms of imposing other pricing conditions, such as discounts, CCI in M/s International Subscription Agency v. Federation of Publishers’ and Booksellers’ Associations in India (FPBAI) 74 M/s International Subscription Agency v. Federation of Publishers’ and Booksellers’ Associations in India (FPBAI), Case No. 33 of 2019, paras 18, 39. held that FPBAI’s discount policy prohibited all its members who deal in print journal and e-resources from offering any discount on the publishers’ price to the Indian subscribers. FPBAI was a federation of publishers, subscription agents and booksellers, and it provided a platform for all its members to discuss their problems. FPBAI constituted a committee with the objective of maintaining uniformity in the supply of books and journals, ensuring fair margins to booksellers and providing efficient service to libraries. However, under the rules of the committee, the members of FPBAI were not allowed to offer discounts to Indian subscribers and were threatened with dire consequences for violating the rules of GOC. Therefore, FPBAI was held guilty of contravention of section 3(3)(a) and (b) of the Competition Act. [B] Output Controls/Limiting Production Section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act provides that any agreement among enterprises at the same level of trade which limits or controls production, supply, markets, technical development, investment or provision of services will be presumed to have an AAEC. The decisional practice of the CCI would show that, among other factors, the CCI looks at demand analysis of the concerned product, production capacity and capacity utilization of the market participants to understand whether there is any trend/pattern wherein they limit the production. In addition, a demand analysis is also done for a period of time to see whether the market participants indulged in limiting production even when there was a high demand for the product under the investigation, which will, in turn, have a resultant impact on the rise in prices. Economic analysis would show that prices cannot be kept at elevated levels if market participants do not keep supply levels sufficiently low in relation to demand. Therefore, in order to ensure that production and supply are low, there is very little capacity addition over time by the market participants and in some cases, the companies may engage in a reduction of capacity. There may also be cases where the market participants are not operating at optimal capacity. Such an action will lead to a demand-supply mismatch and, thereby, an increase in price. The CCI has made detailed observations of this clause in cases relating to the cement industry, entertainment industry, and pharmaceutical industry, among others. The CCI has noted in the Cement cartel case75 Cement cartel case, paras 276, 277, 290. that the act of limiting and controlling supplies on the part of the cement companies over the years has been aimed at first creating shortages leading to built-up demand which, in turn, caused upward rise in prices in the wake of high demand of the product in the market. Since in some seasons, the demand is more, the cement companies restricted the supplies just before the peak demand which allowed the cement companies to sell cement at a higher price. Therefore, the act of limiting and controlling production and supplies in the market causes upward movement in the price of that product/service. Similarly, the deliberate act of shortage in production and supplies as a result of cartelization in a product/service which has inelastic demand, inevitably results in high prices. Similar observations were also made in the NECC case (discussed supra). In the pharmaceutical industry too, the CCI has passed an order under section 27 of the Competition Act, holding the Chemist and Druggist Association liable for limiting and controlling supply76 Varca Chemists and Druggists v. Chemists and Druggists Association, Goa MRTPC 127/2009/DGIR4/28, para. 33; Vedant Bio Sciences v. Chemists and Druggists Association of Baroda C-87/2009/DGIR, paras 20.26, 20.27; M/s Sandhya Drug Agency v. ADDA, BDDA, AIOCD, IDMA, OPPI, Case No. 41 of 2011, para. 24; Peeveear Medical Agencies v. AIOCD and Janssen Case No. 30 of 2011, para. 18. and restricting the ability of the enterprises to supply and sell drugs in the market. The CCI’s order was a cease-and-desist order, along with certain directions to prohibit such anti-competitive practices. (as explained under the ‘Trade Association’ heading). Similarly, in the entertainment industry, the CCI has taken note of the practices carried out by regional film association as being anti-competitive since such film associations frame rules and regulations to impose restrictions on the free and unrestricted distribution and exhibition of serials/films. Also, in the transportation industry, the CCI has observed that trade associations have violated section 3(3)(b) of the Competition Act by imposing restrictive terms for availing transportation services of their members. Such restrictions put the goods at indefinite halt and harm the business users and end users of the market. For instance, in CJ Darcl Logistics Ltd. v. Dumper and Dumper Truck Union Lime Stone and Another,77 CJ Darcl Logistics Ltd. v. Dumper and Dumper Truck Union Lime Stone and Another, Case No. 31 of 2019, paras 34, 35; National Association of Container Freight Stations, Chennai Chapter v. Trailer Owners Association and Others, Case No. 04 of 2018, paras 79-81. the Dumper Truck Union prevented the informant, which itself was a logistics company, from using its own vehicles to transport limestone. Further, threats were issued to force the informant to avail the service of the Dumper Truck Union and its association members. The concerted practice of the Dumper Truck Union resulted in limitation of transport services and helped all its members to fix a higher transportation rate. [C] Market Sharing Section 3(3)(c) of the Competition Act provides that any agreement among enterprises at the same level of trade, which shares the market or source of production or provision of services by way of allocation of the geographical area of the market, or type of goods or services, or number of customers in the market or any other similar way will be presumed to have an AAEC. Market sharing refers to agreements between competitors that divide up the market so that the market participants do not compete among themselves. Such market sharing can happen by way of geographical allocation or customer allocation. For instance, in the Signages bidding case,78 Re: Alleged anti-competitive conduct by various bidders in supply and installation of signages at specified locations of State Bank of India across India, Suo Motu Case No. 02 of 2020, paras 11.2, 39, 31, 37, 90, 91. The NCLAT upheld the decision of CCI in Hith Impex Pvt. Ltd and Another v. CCI, Competition App. (AT) Nos 21 and 65 of 2022; Macromedia Digital Imaging Pvt Ltd. and Another v. CCI, Competition App. (AT) Nos 24 and 26 of 2022. the CCI found that the opposite parties formed a cartel and decided to split the geographically allocated market among themselves with regard to the tender floated by SBI Infra Management Solutions Private Limited for the supply and installation of signages of SBI. The cartel member categorized the geographical market into three to four categories:(i) Type 1 (Bangalore, Chennai, Mumbai, Delhi): geographical presence and manufacturing presence. (ii) Type 2 & 3 (Hyderabad, Lucknow, Bhopal, Patna, Jaipur): additional cost is required to transport signages. (iii) Type 4 (Chandigarh, Kashmir, Kolkata, North East States): logistic constraints (remote areas). Accordingly, the cartel members allocated the market and violated section 3(3)(c) and (d) by entering into an agreement to share the geographical circles they would want to be geographically allocated for the tender. [D] Bid Rigging Section 3(3)(d) of the Competition Act provides that any agreement among enterprises at the same level of trade, which directly or indirectly results in bid rigging or collusive bidding, will be presumed to have an AAEC. Bid rigging, defined in the explanation to section 3(3) of the Competition Act, means any agreement, between enterprises or persons engaged in identical or similar production or trading of goods or provision of services, which has the effect of eliminating or reducing competition for bids or adversely affecting or manipulating the process for bidding. The Competition Act has explained ‘bid rigging’ as an agreement, between parties engaged in identical or similar production or trading of goods or provision of services, which has the effect of eliminating or reducing competition for bids or adversely affecting or manipulating the process for bidding. Since both bid rigging and collusive bidding are interchangeable, so the explanation is equally applicable to collusive bidding.79 Excel Crop case, paras 40-42, 46, 49, 56, 57. (SC). The CCI has noted that collusive bidding or bid rigging may be of different kinds, namely, agreements to submit identical bids, agreements as to who shall submit the lowest bid, agreements for the submission of cover bids (voluntarily submit inflated bids), agreements not to bid against each other, agreements on common norms to calculate prices or terms of bids, agreements to squeeze out outside bidders, agreements designating bid winners in advance on a rotational basis, or on a geographical or customer allocation basis. There are various forms of collusive bidding or bid rigging, such as: (i) Identical or Level bidding: Bidders coordinate to quote identical prices of their bids. Bidders can agree to adhere to publish price lists, raise prices by a specific increment, fix or eliminate discount, avoid advertisement of prices, or maintain specified price differentials based on quantity, type, or size of product. (ii) Proxy or Cover bidding: Bidders artificially increase the price so that the bid will never be accepted or submit the bid without fulfilling the technical requirement. (iii) Bid Suppression: Bidders refrain from bidding or withdrawing a previously submitted bid so that the designated bidder will win the tender. (iv) Bid rotation: Bidders collude to take turns to win bids on different occasions. (v) Bid allocation: Bidders share the geographical or product market and agree not to participate or compete for the tender. The CCI has observed that in the event the tenders submitted are not a result of one’s individual economic calculation but a result of consultation among several players submitting the tender, competition is said to be distorted in such a case. A case in this regard is the Excel Crop case80 Ibid. where all the firms set identical prices in their bids in spite of the firms having very distinct cost structures and sale prices. In all bid rigging cases, the DG and CCI make a detailed assessment of the cost structure of the company, a comparison of the input cost and final cost, production capacity and capacity utilization, etc., of each of the market participants to see whether there is any agreement among the market participants. The Supreme Court, in the case of the Excel Crop case,81 Ibid. has indicated that collusive tendering or bid rigging is the practice whereby firms, which hold 100% market share, agree among themselves to collaborate on the response of a tender either by fixing the price or by boycotting the tender. The firms quoted identical bids for various tenders, even when the cost of production, geographical location and profit margins were different. Further, the firms disagreed with certain conditions given in the 2011 tender, so they acted in concert by boycotting the said tender floated by the procurement authority. The firms had the option to approach the procurement authority to seek clarification or amendment of such conditions. Rather than following the genuine path, the firms colluded among themselves to suppress their bids.82 Ibid. Bid rigging occurs when two or more enterprises (competing bidders or potential bidders) agree among themselves not to compete genuinely with each other for particular tenders which affects the prices they bid for, or they attempt to secretly influence the outcome of a contract or series of contracts. This process allows one of the participants in the agreement to win the tender. Participants may fix prices, suppress, cover, rotate and allocate bids. It is to be noted that the definition of bid rigging also includes any agreement among enterprises to manipulate the process of bidding. The entire process of bidding would entail the submission of technical and financial bids, negotiations with the counterparty, winning the bid, and the performance of the contract. In this regard, the erstwhile COMPAT has held, in the Excel Crop case, that bid rigging cases would encompass rigging in any or all of the processes involved in bidding, and not only limiting to first submission of technical and pricing bids. The purpose of rigging the bid is to concert their bargaining power over the procurement authority and ultimately tilt the balance of negotiations towards their own favour to gain higher profit without worrying about the force of competition in the market and that they will be poached by competitors. Generally, in cartelization, cartels tend to artificially increase the bidding price or lower the quantity of goods or services.83 Excel Crop case, para. 41. (SC); LPG-I case, para. 78. (SC). Through collusion, the cartel members can use their bargaining power to get the upper hand during negotiations with the procurement authority. As noted above, the decisional practice of the CCI shows that instances of bid rigging are very common in cases of government procurement. One of the common defences which are raised by cartel participants is that they are dealing with government, which is a strong counterparty, and hence, their practice of bid rigging does not cause any appreciable adverse effect. However, this defence has not been accepted by the CCI. The CCI In Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra) (‘Surendra Prasad order’),84 In Re: Surendra Prasad (MAHAGENCO’s Tender for Coal Liaison service in Maharashtra), Case No. 90 of 2016, paras 114, 122, 131. Maharashtra State Power Generation Co. Ltd. (MAHAGENCO) which was incorporated by the Government of Maharashtra for the generation of power in the State of Maharashtra invited tenders for coal liaisoning, and to supervise the quality and quantity of coal supplied. The opposite parties 2, 3 & 4 provided the service for coal liaisons, and they participated in the bidding process. The opposite parties 2 to 4 colluded to distribute the tenders among themselves. Ultimately, the opposite parties 2 to 4 got the bid. The CCI held the opposite parties guilty of section 3(3)(c) and (d) by establishing parallel conduct, sub-contracting and market allocation which was further corroborated with various corroborative evidence like purchasing the tender document on the same dates, communication between opposite parties, including details of financial information, etc. Similarly, in In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing 85 In Re: Cartelization in Tender Nos 21 and 28 of 2013 of Pune Municipal Corporation for Solid Waste Processing, Suo Motu Case No. 03 of 2016, paras 44, 45, 46. (‘Pune Municipal Corporate order’), the CCI made an extensive analysis on whether the cartel members were engaged in identical business or not. In this case, the tender was floated for procurement of sewing machines for distribution among the backward class people. The informant alleged that opposite parties colluded among themselves to rig the tendering process. The opposite party 1 was the seller of the sewing machine, the opposite parties 2, 3 and 4 were authorized dealers of opposite party 1, and the opposite party 5 was the government authority that floated the tender. Some opposite parties opposed the proceeding because they were engaged in a different business; hence, section 3(3)(d) would not apply to them.
