(04.04.2022) 1st: Black Burn Joint application was filed after the commencement of investigation. Made full and true disclosures of information and continuous co-operation during investigation. 80% 2nd: Moulded Fibreglass Joint application was filed after the commencement of investigation. Could not provide much additional value but made full disclosures and continuously co-operated during investigation. 40% 3rd: Jai Polypan Joint application was filed after the commencement of investigation. Provided value addition which was used by the CCI to form a complete trail evidencing anti-competitive conduct. 30% 4th: Quadrant EPP Joint application was filed after the commencement of investigation. Provided value addition which was used by the CCI to form a complete trail evidencing anti-competitive conduct. 20% 12. Cartelisation in the supply of Protective Tubes to Indian Railways. Suo Motu Case No. 06 of 2020. (09.06.2022) 1st: Jai Polypan Based on the application, the CCI initiated investigation. Prima facie order for investigation was passed due to the application. Further, full and true disclosures of evidence and continuous cooperation helped the CCI establish the cartel. 100% The essential factors under Regulation 3 of the Lesser Penalty Regulations, based on which CCI exercise its discretionary power to allow a lesser penalty application, vary on a case-by-case basis. Consequently, the reduction of monetary penalty also varies; thus, the trend of CCI in relation to adjudication of the lesser penalty application is not consistent. Therefore, as noted above, the key thing in any application is that such application must be extremely granular with all relevant details so that the CCI can take help from such evidence to establish a contravention under section 3 of the Competition Act. In addition to providing evidence which was not in possession by the offices of DG/CCI or which the previous applicant did not give, the applicant must actively cooperate with the DG/CCI during the investigation or inquiry proceeding. Importantly, any information and document submitted under the Lesser Penalty Regulations are treated as confidential (including in respect of the identity of the applicant) unless such information is already in the public domain or is required to be disclosed by law. Further, the application made under the Lesser Penalty Regulations is kept confidential, so there is no chance for the future applicant to know that the contents of the information are already available to the authorities. It can be a tricky situation if a lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application is made without any vital or added disclosure since the applicant has already confessed to being involved in a cartel and may not still get any immunity. Similarly, an applicant can withdraw its lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus application, but under Regulation 10, the DG/CCI will be at liberty to use the evidence submitted by the applicant except for its admission. Finally, it is to be noted that under the statutory scheme of the Competition Act, the CCI, in its discretion, can grant lesser penalty/lesser penalty plus. However, the risk of such an applicant being sued for compensation under section 53 of the Competition Act is not taken away.

§2.15 LEGAL FRAMEWORK FOR VERTICAL AGREEMENT Vertical agreement means the agreement/arrangement/understanding between enterprises that are at different stages or levels of the production chain and, therefore, in different markets. Section 3(4) of the Competition Act enlists a non-exhaustive list of vertical agreements which would be treated as void agreements if they cause or are likely to cause AAEC.152 Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India (FHRAI) and Others v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. (MMT) and Others, Case No. 14 of 2019 and Case No. 01 of 2020, para. 291. Also, see M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. Intel Corporation (Intel Inc.) & Ors., Case No. 48 of 2011, paras 8.3.3, 8.3.6. Unlike certain kinds of horizontal anti-competitive agreements (i.e., cartel arrangements discussed supra), vertical agreements are not pre-se anti-competitive, and the CCI assess these vertical agreements under the rule of reason analysis. The concept of rule of reason stated that the vertical agreement would fall foul of section 3(4) of the Competition Act only if such agreement has caused or has the potential to cause AAEC as per the factors laid down in section 19(3) of the Competition Act.153 Jasper lnfotech Private Limited (Snapdeal) v. KAFF Appliances (India) Pvt. Ltd. (Kaff), Case No. 61 of 2014, paras 48, 51. For assessing vertical anti-competitive agreements, the rule of reason is applicable because such agreements are concluded between entities operating at different stages of the production chain and are not between competitors of the same market. So, for the operation of business, these entities enter into several commercial agreements, many of which enhance efficiency with sound economic justification. As these entities are not competitors, their incentives are generally aligned with that for the benefit of the customers. Hence, such vertical agreements may not necessarily cause AAEC of the market, and treating such vertical agreements as per se anti-competitive might result in restraining pro-competitive commercial agreements.154 Ibid., para. 48. The High Level Committee on Competition Policy and Law, 2000 (‘High Level Committee’)155 High Level Committee on Competition Policy and Law, 2000, paras 4.4.0, 4.3.1. had noted that:generally, vertical agreements are treated more leniently than horizontal agreements as, prima facie, a horizontal agreement is more likely to reduce competition than an agreement between firms in a buyer-seller relationship… In the past, the U.S. anti-trust laws had treated vertical restraints, like tie-in arrangements quite harshly. This thinking has changed in recent times, and, under the rule of reason, vertical agreements are generally treated more leniently than horizontal agreements. This is because vertical agreements can often perform pro-competitive functions. Such agreements are generally considered anti-competitive if one or more of the firms that are party to the agreement have market power. In such a situation, the agreement is, in any case, likely to attract the provisions of the law relating to abuse of dominance. In light of the aforesaid, the CCI adjudicates the allegation of vertical agreement under the rule of reason to see whether the vertical agreement caused or is likely to cause an adverse effect. Looking at the steps taken by CCI while deciding the matters related to vertical anti-competitive agreements suggests that CCI follows an ‘effect-based analysis’ through the rule of reason framework. Thus, to determine the violation of section 3(4) of the Competition Act, the CCI follows the following steps, i.e., first, nature of the vertical agreement under inquiry; second, delineation of relevant markets; third, significant market power of the party; and fourth, effect-based analysis to determine AAEC.

§2.16 TYPES OF VERTICAL AGREEMENTS Section 3(4) of the Competition Act provides an inclusive list of vertical agreements. There can be other forms of vertical agreements, however, all vertical agreements must be analysed under the rule of reason analysis. It is noteworthy that vertical agreements are often analysed by the CCI on a joint investigation of section 3(4) read with section 4 of the Competition Act, since a vertical agreement entered into by an undertaking with insignificant market power will generally not cause any AAEC in India. We will now discuss the various kinds of vertical agreements mentioned under section 3(4) of the Competition Act below. [A] Tie-In Arrangement Explanation (a) of section 3(4) defines tie-in arrangement or tying arrangement as an agreement imposing a condition on a customer to purchase the goods or services in order to purchase some other goods or services. In a tie-in arrangement, the seller ties two distinct or separate products or services with each other so that the customer is forced to purchase the tied product or service along with the tying product or service. The product or service that the customer wants to purchase is called tying product, and the product or service which is forced upon the customer is called tied product or service. For example, the product that the buyer wants to purchase is called the tying product (Product X), and the product which is required to be purchased to get Product X is called tied product (Product Y). The producer of Product X imposes a condition on the buyers that in order to purchase Product X, they have to purchase Product Y. Also, for a better understanding of tie-in arrangement, it is important to distinguish tie-in arrangement from bundling arrangement. The CCI made detailed observations on the difference between tie-in and bundling in the Sonam Sharma v. Apple (‘Apple case’). 156 Sonam Sharma v. Apple, Case No. 24 of 2011, paras 19, 65-71. In this case, the DG found that Apple locked its iPhone with the cellular services of Airtel or Vodafone. It was a case of ‘contractual tying’, which means a buyer can only purchase an iPhone by subscribing to the cellular services of Airtel or Vodafone. However, looking at the minuscule market share of the tying party, the CCI held that the said tie-in arrangement was not an anti-competitive agreement between Apple and cellular service providers. Enough evidence was not adduced to prove that the tie-in arrangement foreclosed the market or created entry barriers for new entrants. Though ‘tie-in’ and ‘bundling’ are used interchangeably in common market parlance, the CCI explained the difference between both the terms by saying that ‘tie-in arrangement’ and ‘bundling’ are strategies to sell products and services, and both the strategies are used for different purpose. A producer uses a ‘tie-in arrangement’ as a strategy when it does not want to specify to the customer that the two products are fixed at the time of purchase, whereas a ‘bundling’ strategy is used when the products are sold in fixed proportions. The CCI in the Apple case further noted that ‘a tying arrangement occurs when, through a contractual or technological requirement, a seller conditions the sale or lease of one product or service on the customer’s agreement to take a second product or service. In other words, a firm selling products X and Y makes the purchase of product X conditional to the purchase of product Y. Product Y can be purchased freely on the market, but product X can only be purchased together with product Y.’ Thereafter, the CCI in the Apple case,157 Apple case, Case No. 24 of 2011, para. 66. explained the term ‘tie-in arrangement’ by providing examples of the tied sales of machines with maintenance services or complementary products. Also, producers force the buyers to purchase two or more products because all these products are compatible with each other. More often, tying is a sales strategy usually adopted by producers to promote/introduce a slow-selling or unknown product when it has in its portfolio a fast-selling or well-known product over which it has certain market power (in this example, Product X). The aspect of tie-in is also seen, while deciding abuse of dominance investigation, under the abuse of leveraging (discussed in the chapter on abuse of dominance). However, ‘bundling’ or ‘package tie-in’ is a strategy used by the producer to make the buyer purchase more goods. Further, the CCI held that ‘price bundling is a strategy whereby a seller bundles together many different goods/items for sale and offers the entire bundle at a single price. There are two forms of price bundling – pure bundling, where the seller does not offer buyers the option of buying the items separately, and mixed bundling, where the seller offers the items separately at higher individual prices’.158 Apple case, para. 67. Mixed bundling is usually preferable to pure bundling because the reference price effect makes it appear even more attractive to buyers, and there are fewer laws forbidding mixed bundling. The CCI has noted that there can be pro-competitive rationale behind tie-in arrangements like assembly benefits (economies of scale and scope), quality improvement and addressing pricing inefficiencies. The following conditions are required to be analysed to see whether a tie-in arrangement is anti-competitive for a section 3(1) analysis:159 Ibid., para. 69. (i) Presence of two separate products or services capable of being tied. Tie-in arrangements must consist of two products where the seller forces the buyer to purchase one product (i.e., tied product – Product X) if the buyer purchases another product (i.e., tying product – Product Y). (ii) The seller must have significant market power in the market of the tying product to appreciably restrain free competition in the market of the tied product. To establish the presence of tying arrangements, the sufficient market power of the seller in the tying market to leverage into the market of tied product must be presence. Thereafter, it has to be shown that the seller has used its market power to force the buyers to purchase the tied product along with the tied product. (iii) The tying arrangement must have a substantial effect on the commerce. Considering the area of analysis is whether an agreement causes or is likely to cause an AAEC, tie-in arrangements are generally not perceived as being anti-competitive when a substantial portion of the market is not affected. Similarly, in Arshiya Rail Infrastructure Ltd. (ARIL) v. Ministry of Railway (MoR) & Others,160 Arshiya Rail Infrastructure Ltd. (ARIL) v. Ministry of Railway (MoR) & Others, Case No. 64 of 2010, para. 21. the CCI held that tie-in arrangement is used by an entity which has a dominant position in one product/service (i.e., transportation services – tying service) and ties it with a laggard product (i.e., maintenance of container – tied service) to promote the laggard product. However, there was no tie-in arrangement in the said case because none of the entities were in the business of maintenance services. As per the facts, MoR granted a licence and concessionaire agreement entered with fifteen private operators to move container trains on the railway lines on a licence basis. The informants, who were private operators, alleged that MoR, Indian Railway and Container Corporation of India, had indulged in abusive conduct as a group or a single economic entity in the market for the transport of freight via railways. One of the allegations was on violation of section 3(4)(a) of the Competition Act because MoR insisted that maintenance of container trains can only be sourced from it. However, the CCI dismissed this allegation by saying that the supplementary condition to obtain a fitness certificate of train from MoR does not amount to a tie-in arrangement in terms of section 3(4)(a) of the Competition Act. The CCI observed that MoR imposed such conditions to maintain railway operation and protect the overall safety of passengers. [B] Exclusive Dealing Agreement Explanation (b) to section 3(4) of the Competition Act defines an exclusive dealing agreement as an exclusive agreement imposed by the seller which restricts the buyer from purchasing any goods or services other than those of the seller. Exclusive dealing agreement also covers the vertical restraint imposed by the buyer on the seller from dealing with other buyers. The seller who is accepting this restrictive term has no option but to supply its product or service to the designated purchaser as per the condition. For example, Manufacturer A entered into an exclusive dealing agreement with all the retailers to buy only the product of Manufacturer A or the authorized dealers of Manufacturer A. Prior to the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, this explanation was called an ‘exclusive supply agreement’, and it used to cover only the vertical agreement where restraint was exclusively imposed on the buyer by the seller. So, the Competition Act was amended to recognize the imposition of the exclusive agreement from both the seller side and the buyer side.161 Report of Competition Law Review Committee, 2019, paras 5.5-5.7. The issue of exclusive dealing agreement (supply-side) was discussed in the Shri Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Ltd. and Others (‘Automobile spare parts case’),162 Shri Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Ltd. and Others, Case No. 03 of 2011, para. 20.6.4; Also, see Toyota Kirloskar Motor Private Limited and Others v. CCI [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 452, para. 125. where both the CCI and the erstwhile COMPAT held that the authorized dealers were prohibited from sourcing spare parts from any other enterprise, apart from the OEMs. These exclusive agreements imposed restrictions on the buyer side of the market to buy and sell the spare parts exclusively; hence, section 3(4)(b) of the Competition Act was violated. In another case, the CCI in M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. Intel Corporation and Others 163 M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. Intel Corporation and Others, Case No. 48 of 2011, para. 8.3.7. held that exclusive agreement means that the agreement by the seller restricts the distributors from dealing with any competitors of the seller. In the absence of any evidence, the CCI was unable to show that the concerned agreement with Intel exclusively imposed a restriction on the distributors to deal with Intel. Also, there was evidence which showed that Intel allowed the distributors to deal with other sellers. [C] Exclusive Distribution Agreement Explanation (c) to section 3(4) of the Competition Act defines exclusive distribution agreement as an exclusive agreement to limit, restrict or withhold the output or supply of any goods or services or allocate any area or market for the disposal or sale of the goods or services. Such an exclusive agreement restricts the distribution side of the market. For example, a supplier entered into an exclusive distribution agreement with the manufacturer to specifically distribute the product with authorized dealers, and not any independent dealers. The aspect of exclusive distribution agreement was discussed in the Bajaj case.164 Bajaj case, para. 75. The CCI discussed the anti-competitive effects which can flow from an exclusive distribution agreement. The CCI observed that exclusive distribution agreement is when a supplier enters into an agreement with the distributor, wherein the distributor sells the product within a particular group or area. Such an agreement limits the distribution, creates entry bars and harms the intra-brand competition. While assessing the violation under section 3(4)(c) of the Competition Act, the CCI considers whether the distributor is restricted to deal in the products of other brands in or outside the particular customer or region that caused any harm to the intra-brand or inter-brand competition. In M/s Karni Communication Private Ltd. & Other v. Haicheng Vivo Mobile (India) Private Ltd. and Others,165 M/s Karni Communication Private Ltd. & Other v. Haicheng Vivo Mobile (India) Private Ltd. & Others, Case No. 35 of 2018, para. 27. it was alleged that Vivo imposed a clause in the distribution agreement to restrict smartphone distributors from selling Vivo smartphones via online platforms to customers, hence, violated section 3(4)(c) of the Competition Act. This allegation was dismissed because Vivo had allowed the distributors to sell Vivo’s products online on various e-commerce portals like www.Flipkart.com, www.Amazon.in, www.Snapdeal.com, etc. The CCI held that there was no exclusive distribution agreement. Further, the CCI said that Vivo never directly restricted the distribution of its products, and consumers had options to buy Vivo products from various sources. Similarly, in M/s K.C. Marketing v. OPPO Mobiles MU Private Limited,166 M/s K.C. Marketing v. OPPO Mobiles MU Private Limited, Case No. 34 of 2018, paras 30, 31. the CCI held that to protect the interests of all distributors who have made an investment in the product, the producer can designate specific sale regions to distributors and restrict the distributors from selling outside their specified regions. Such exclusive distribution agreement causes no AAEC because there is no bar for the customers of one region to purchase the product from a distributor in another sales region. Further, there is no restriction on the distributor to deal in the products of other producers in or outside the sales region. Thus, in effect, the CCI has accepted that there can be certain restrictions which may be imposed in a vertical agreement to prevent free-riding (this was specifically highlighted in the Bajaj case), however, the parties who are in market power must keep in mind the factors mentioned under section 19(3) of the Act prior to imposing such vertical restraints. In the Automobile spare parts case,167 Automobile spare parts case, para. 20.6.29. The erstwhile COMPAT in Toyota Kirloskar Motor Private Limited and Others v. CCI [2016], (COMPAT) 2016 SCC OnLine Comp AT 452, upheld the order of CCI. it was found that the OEMs and their authorized dealers entered into agreements/arrangements pursuant to which the authorized dealers of the OEMs sell cars and provide after-sales services to the consumers of the OEMs. Further, there are agreements entered into between OEMs and the original equipment suppliers (OESs). Further, apart from the agreement between OEM with their dealers, there were agreements between OEMs with the OESs prohibiting them from selling the spare parts manufactured by them for OEMs in the open market. The CCI concluded by saying that OEMs violated section 3(4)(b), (c) and (d) of the Competition Act. OEMs entered into exclusive agreement and refusal to deal to restrict the access of spare parts and diagnostic tools directly to the Indian aftermarket; the arrangement between OEMs and authorized dealers violated section 3(4) of the Competition Act. The OEMs increased the risk of consumer safety because their restrictive conduct led to a rise in the use of fake spare parts and prevented independent repairers from using effective diagnostic tools. After identifying the various kinds of agreements, the CCI analysed that the said network of agreements created foreclosure effects and barriers to entry into the market. Based on the same, the CCI identified AAEC in terms of the factors mentioned under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. [D] Refusal to Deal Explanation (d) of section 3(4) of the Competition Act defines refusal to deal as an agreement which restricts or is likely to restrict their business partners in terms of dealing with the competitors of the enterprise which is imposing such restriction. It must be noted that refusal to deal has mostly been dealt with under an abuse of dominance investigation. An example of refusal to deal is when an E-Commerce Platform – ‘B’ enters into an agreement with a smartphone retailer prohibiting it from listing its smartphones on the E-Commerce Platform – ‘A’ and E-Commerce Platform – ‘C’. The issue of refusal of deal will depend upon the facts and circumstances of each case. Interestingly, in Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd.,168 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd., Case No. 18 of 2021, paras 45, 46, 49, 51, 55. while passing an order under section 26(2) of the Competition Act, the CCI held that no distributor can ask for an absolute right to deal from a manufacturer to deal with them. In this case, Hiveloop Technology alleged that Britannia refused to deal directly with it, and there is a vertical agreement between Britannia and its distributors to restrict Hiveloop Technology from dealing with certain brands and biscuits of Britannia. Britannia argued that it has not imposed any such restrictions on its distributors, and it has commercial freedom to choose its business partners in the vertical chain. CCI accepted the argument of Britannia and held that an enterprise can refuse to choose their trading partners as long as there is fair functioning of the markets. The CCI recognized that Britannia has commercial right when it comes to choosing its business partners, and CCI can’t interfere with such right. In two cases with similar facts concerning non-dealing of a goods provider with a distributor, the CCI expounded the position that every enterprise, including a dominant enterprise, has the freedom to choose its business partners.169 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd., Case No. 18 of 2021; Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Parle Products Private Limited, Case No. 28 of 2021. The crux of the allegations in both cases was that the goods provider (i.e., Britannia (discussed supra) and Parle, respectively) was not providing its biscuits, which were regarded as a ‘must stock item’ to the distributor (i.e., Hiveloop Technologies/Udaan). The CCI noted that this matter required a resolution of two competing rights: the informant’s right to do business with Britannia/Parle with respect to certain products versus Britannia/Parle’s right not to engage with the informant since it had the freedom of choice as to whom to contract within the vertical chain as well as its level of engagement. In this case, CCI noted that both Britannia and Parle had their own commercial justifications for refusing engagement with the informant, viz., the capacity and quality of service provided and that no foreclosure had arisen in the market. As a result, the CCI held that manufacturers have some autonomy available to deal with their goods the way they want, in alignment with their business requirements. If this were not the case, then it has a consequence of replacing the business/commercial prudence of an entity by taking away the autonomy of such undertaking/entity. Hence, the type and nature of distributors a manufacturer desires to partner with is part and parcel of its business autonomy, and the CCI cannot ipso facto substitute its regulatory wisdom for that of the commercial wisdom of the businesses unless the commercial wisdom is palpably in the face of the provisions of the Competition Act and ‘the criteria laid down are ex facie unfair and/or discriminatory and designed with a view to exclude/eliminate competition on merits’.170 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Parle Products Private Limited, Case No. 28 of 2021, para. 33. Thus, nobody can ask for an absolute right to deal with a particular business. Similarly, there is no absolute right of refusal. This will depend upon the facts and circumstances of each case; even a dominant entity, at times, has the freedom to refuse to conclude contracts based on objective justifications. Further, refusal to deal need not be absolute and ought to be seen in the context of the factual setting coupled with the factors under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. A refusal, either total or partial, could also have underlying valid justifications with commercial consideration, especially in the backdrop that it makes little economic sense for businesses to take a restrictive approach when it comes to expanding their horizon and reach. Consequently, though refusal to deal may be, inter se, two or more parties, its anti-competitive effect would have to be seen in the context of the overall market conditions. Hence, a manufacturer cannot be forced to onboard any person/entity in the chain merely on asking because distribution networks are built and strengthened over a period of time with sound underlying commercial considerations. Thus, the question to be asked in business decisions to engage or not to engage with a downstream entity from a competition perspective is whether such a decision is likely to cause AAEC should the upstream entity choose not to deal with the downstream entity. Thus, the takeaway is that firms have an autonomy to choose their trading partners as long as the exercise of such autonomy does not affect the fair functioning of the markets because, depending upon the market power commanded by such firms, their conduct may cause foreclosure of the market for other players. The CCI, in the Bajaj case,171 Bajaj case, paras 69, 76, 81. where it was alleged that Bajaj exercised its market power to impose restrictions on its distributors from supplying Bajaj’s products to retailers which were from other territories, the distributor had no other option but to accept such restrictive conditions. The CCI noted that competition law issues with respect to refusal to deal/supply will arise when an enterprise, generally with stronger market power, refuses to deal with its customers or suppliers. The effect in such a scenario is that the downstream market gets affected due to such refusal. However, the CCI held that though there were vertical restraints on the distributors, there was no violation of section 3(4)(d) due to the lack of sufficient evidence to satisfy the presence of entry barriers, foreclosure of the market, or harm to the end consumers. Also, in a case relating to the automobile sector, the CCI found that there was no exclusive agreement or refusal to deal between the car manufacturers and their distributors in the Hyundai Motors case172 Hyundai Motors case, paras 71, 72, 74, 75. because the prohibition to deal with other car companies was never mandated on the distributors. Mere requirements of prior written permission of Hyundai do not impose any exclusivity or cannot be concluded as refusal to deal under section 3(4) of the Competition Act. Additionally, it was found that there were distributors who were dealing with other car companies without seeking permission from Hyundai, and despite the knowledge of such parallel dealerships, no action was taken against such distributors. Though generally vertical anti-competitive agreements are imposed as vertical restraints where one party has no other option than accepting the condition of the other party, however, it could also be a mutually agreed condition to eliminate their competitors. The CCI in its most prominent decision on refusal to deal in the Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Others v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. and Others (‘MMT case’),173 Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Others v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. and Others, Case No. 14 of 2019 and Case No. 01 of 2020, paras 289, 291, 292, 293, 295, 298, 300, 301. passed an order against MakeMyTrip (MMT – an online travel agency) and Oravel Stay Ltd. (OYO – franchisee service provider to independent hotels) for their anti-competitive practices, including MMT’s refusal to deal and delisting of the competitors of OYO. The CCI observed that section 3(4) is neither exhaustive in nature nor does it require that the restriction is in the form of an imposition. So, the CCI can also look into the restrictive agreement which was mutually agreed by both parties to harm the competitors or competition in the market. In this case, MMT agreed to delist OYO’s competitors, i.e., Fabhotels and Treebo, whereas OYO agreed not to list its inventories on the platforms of MMT’s competitors, i.e., Yatra, Cleartrip, PayTM, etc. Such a mutually agreed clause between MMT and OYO was held to be anti-competitive because it foreclosed both markets and took away the opportunity for the competitors to get a fair chance to compete. Further, refusal to list Fabhotels and Treebo on MMT’s platform would not only reduce consumer choices but also stifle the innovation in the market. MMT was a dominant player, and OYO enjoyed a significant market position in their respective markets, and entering into a vertical anti-competitive agreement caused AAEC to create entry barriers and harm the competitors. Similarly, while passing an order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act, the CCI in Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and Another 174 Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and Another, Case No. 40 of 2019, paras 6, 7, 11, 18, 20, 23, 25. identified online platform services as fulcrum of the commercial success of the sellers to reach customers. If such platforms refuse to deal with sellers, then it would create a major hindrance for the sellers to sell their products to the customers. In this case, a group of sellers filed information alleging that Flipkart and Amazon entered into exclusive agreements and tie-ups with their preferred sellers. Through these vertical exclusive agreements, Flipkart and Amazon are exclusively launching mobile phones of the preferred sellers on their e-commerce platforms, selling the products of preferred sellers at a heavily discounted price and providing preferential listing/promotion of private labels. This leads to other sellers being excluded and foreclosed from the market because they are not allowed to compete equally on the said e-commerce platforms. By considering all these allegations against two e-commerce platforms, which hold significant market power in the relevant market, CCI directed the DG to investigate the said vertical agreements. [E] Resale Price Maintenance Explanation (e) to section 3(4) defines resale price maintenance. Resale price maintenance means any agreement to sell goods or services where the prices to be charged on the resale by the purchaser shall be stipulated by the seller unless it is clearly stated that prices lower than those prices may be charged. The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023 modified the said explanation to include indirect restrictions on the pricing condition, such as threats and imposition of sanctions if the seller fails to maintain the resale price or promotional offers to the seller for maintaining the resale price. Like in other jurisdictions across the globe, resale price maintenance remains one of the most vexed areas of antitrust enforcement. Contrary to some international authorities, in India, an arrangement for resale price maintenance is only void if it causes an AAEC in India. In fact, the High Level Committee175 High Level Committee on Competition Policy and Law, 2000, para. 4.4.1. noted that ‘in a number of countries, RPM is presumed to be per se anti-competitive. The majority of the Members of the Committee also felt that RPM should be treated as presumed to be illegal. However, after considerable discussions, in order to arrive at a consensus, it was decided not to treat it as presumed to be illegal. It will be judged under the “rule of reason”’. It has been alleged in many cases, that the object of vertical price restraints is primarily restriction on price competition between enterprises operating in the market. Resale price maintenance implies a direct or indirect object to fix or enforce a minimum resale price to be observed by the distributors or any downstream resellers of the manufacturer’s products. The CCI, in M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. and Others,176 M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. and Others, Case No. 48 of 2011, para. 8.3.8. explained section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act by saying resale price maintenance includes the agreement to sell goods on the condition that the resale price shall be fixed by sellers unless it is clearly stated that prices were lower than those prices may be charged. In this case, the CCI found that resale prices were not stipulated by Intel, and the distribution agreement allowed the distributors to fix their own resale price. On the allegation that Intel was monitoring the downstream price, the CCI held that such conduct can’t be termed anti-competitive. The CCI has witnessed various developments with regard to the treatment of vertical agreements having conditions of resale price maintenance, especially in the online distribution sector. Disputes on the competition law have been brewing in India between e-tailers and brick-and-mortar businesses for a long time now, with the emergence of strong e-tailers in the market. The strategy of the e-tailers in tying up exclusive launches and offering significant discounts for various products being sold on their respective platforms has left an impact on the businesses of conventional brick-and-mortar retailers. The brick-and-mortar outlets have stated that they are going out of business due to the actions of the strong e-tailers, and the companies are also facing a difficult time in managing their distribution chain in the process. An emerging issue in the area of vertical agreements is the maintenance of resale price maintenance both in the context of online and offline markets. Issues of resale price maintenance pertain to the effort on the part of companies to ensure a price of their product to ensure parity between online and offline sellers, thereby preventing free-riding by offline agents so as to protect the interest of dealers who maintain brick-and-mortar stores. This issue was first decided by the CCI in the case of Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Others, 177 Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Others, Case No. 80 of 2014, para. 17. in the context of the sale of a book of a famous Indian author, (Chetan Bhagat’s book, ‘Half Girlfriend’) which was exclusively available on Flipkart’s website. Allegations were raised that such arrangements slowly destroy players in the brick-and-mortar space and tend to create product-specific monopoly, leading to manipulation of price, control of production and supply, imposition of terms and conditions detrimental to the interests of consumers and distortion of fair competition in the marketplace. However, CCI did not agree with these allegations and held that such exclusive arrangements do not create any entry barriers for new entrants as the products (such as mobile phones, tablets, books, cameras, etc.) sold through the exclusive platforms face competitive constraints and are neither dominant nor hold a monopoly status. Further, the introduction of the e-platforms brought pro-competitive effectiveness to the market. Similarly, in Jasper Infotech (Snapdeal) v. KAFF Appliances, 178 Jasper Infotech (Snapdeal) v. KAFF Appliances, Case No. 61 of 2013, paras 55, 63, 67, 70. the online retailers have also instituted an information against companies who imposed restrictions on the manner in which online distribution is to be done by the e-tailers. Jasper (the company which owns and operates snapdeal.com) instituted an information against a manufacturer which had placed restrictions on its dealers in dealing with e-tailers. In the said case, the CCI has directed the DG to investigate the allegation pertaining to minimum resale price maintenance imposed by KAFF Appliances (India) Pvt. Ltd. (KAFF). Snapdeal is an online marketplace which provides a medium for buyers and sellers to meet and transact. It was alleged that KAFF, being aggrieved by the sale of its products at discounted prices on Snapdeal, started imposing resale price maintenance and adduced an email exchange between KAFF and Snapdeal to the effect that KAFF will not allow the sales of its products on Snapdeal if the Minimum Operating Price (MOP) is not maintained. However, the DG’s main investigation report and supplementary investigation report could not find any evidence showing imposition of resale price maintenance by KAFF. With regard to the email exchange between the parties, the CCI accepted the justification of the KAFF that the email was to safeguard the goodwill and protect the right of KAFF to choose the most efficient distribution channel. Finally, the conduct of KAFF was not sufficient to cause AAEC. In another case, though, the NCLAT set aside the CCI order in the Hyundai Motors case179 Hyundai Motors case, paras 79, 81, 85, 88-92, 116; See, NCLAT order Hyundai Motor India Ltd. v. CCI and Others [2018], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal No. 06 of 2017, paras 40, 41. because the CCI and DG failed to delineate the relevant markets and no proper inquiry was carried out by the CCI, and appeal against the NCLAT order is pending before the Supreme Court. Nevertheless, the reasoning of the CCI on resale price maintenance is important for academic purposes. Here, in a claim pertaining to resale price maintenance, CCI found that Hyundai placed restrictions on its dealers on the maximum permissible discount that may be given by a dealer to the end-consumer, coupled with the practice of price monitoring put in place by Hyundai wherein the dealers were penalized on account of any deviation. After considering the DG report, the CCI held that Hyundai contravened section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act by imposing resale price on its distributors and monitoring the maximum permissible discount level through the Discount Control Mechanism. Under the Discount Control Mechanism (DCP), Hyundai maintains the maximum discount which a distributor can provide to the end consumers, and Hyundai monitors its enforcement by regularly conducting meetings with its distributors to discuss the change in price and discount. As per the DCP, the distributors can’t offer a higher discount which is above the authorized discount. The CCI held that resale price maintenance can prevent competition at both the intra-brand and inter-brand levels. Not allowing to offer a higher discount harms the end customers because they have to pay a higher price. Resale price maintenance reduces the intra-brand competition which results in reduction of the pressure on the competing car manufacturers to sell their product at a competing price. In addition to resale price maintenance, the CCI also ruled that Hyundai entered into a tie-in arrangement by tying the sale of its car only with the purchase of recommended lubricants and oils. The conduct of Hyundai caused AAEC because the cost of cars increased which is a case of consumer harm. In the case of Alleged anti-competitive conduct by Maruti Suzuki India Limited in implementing discount control policy vis-à-vis dealers (‘Maruti Suzuki case’),180 Alleged anti-competitive conduct by Maruti Suzuki India Limited in implementing discount control policy vis-à-vis dealers Suo Motu Case No. 01 of 2019, paras 18, 21, 34, 39, 42, 45, 46, 48-50. the CCI passed an order under section 27 of the Competition Act against Maruti Suzuki for enforcing maximum discount limits on its distributors through the DCP comes within the ambit of resale price maintenance. Further, it was found that failure to comply with the DCP by offering extra discount, the distributors were threatened with penalty and suspension of supplies. There were many instances where the distributors had to pay a penalty amount for violating DCP. Hence, it was established that the resale price maintenance was enforced on distributors. Thereafter, the CCI analysed the factors under section 19(3) and held that the resale price maintenance prevented intra-brand competition and inter-brand competition by not allowing the distributors to decrease the cost of the vehicles. This facilitated all the distributors from selling the vehicles at higher prices because there was no price competition. On the other hand, the customers had to pay higher prices to buy the vehicles. Hence, the CCI held that Maruti Suzuki violated section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act and harmed both the competition and the stakeholders of the market. Another important observation on resale price maintenance was made in the case of Shubham Sanitarywares v. Hindustan Sanitarywares and Industries Ltd and others,181 Shubham Sanitarywares v. Hindustan Sanitarywares and Industries Ltd and others, Case No. 99 of 2013, para. 11. where the opposite party was offering higher discounts to bulk builders and less discounts to retail customers. The CCI passed an order under section 26(2) by holding that offering different discounts to different customers does not violate section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act. However, it was also noted that forcing the dealers to implement specific discounts on different customers may be construed as a violation of section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act. Further, the CCI held that the discount policy in that case did not seem to have any AAEC in India and dismissed the case.

§2.17 ESSENTIAL CONDITIONS NECESSARY TO ESTABLISH VERTICAL ANTI-COMPETITIVE AGREEMENT As a part of effect-based analysis, the CCI examines two key conditions, i.e., (a) the relevant markets of the parties to the agreement; and (b) their market power in the relevant market. So, before analysing the factors under section 19(3) of the Competition Act (discussed in the subsequent part), the CCI essentially requires delineating the relevant market. Then, in the context of the delineated relevant market, the CCI analyse the market presence of the party and its position of strength. It is important to know the market concentration and which enterprise holds the significant market position because the vertical anti-competitive agreements imposed by an enterprise having significant market strengths generally have a foreclosure effect, would create entry barriers, cause harm to the customers or drive the existing competitors out of the market. In some cases, the CCI delineates relevant markets and determines the market strength of both parties to the vertical anti-competitive agreement, especially in cases where it was alleged that both parties mutually agreed to the vertical anti-competitive agreement.182 MMT case, paras 22, 23, 291, 292, 297; Apple case, para. 33. For example, in the MMT case, the CCI identified two markets: both upstream and downstream markets where MMT and OYO (the parties to the said exclusive agreement) and then came to the conclusion that MMT and OYO were respectively holding market power in their respective markets. [A] Delineation of Relevant Markets of the Parties The decisional practice of the CCI shows that delineation of relevant market of the alleged parties must take place as a pre-requisite condition to analyse AAEC in the market. Thus, as a first and foremost step to analyse the AAEC, the CCI should correctly delineate the relevant market according to the factors enumerated in section 19(5), (6) and (7) of the Competition Act. The issue relating to tie-in arrangement between Hiranandani Hospital and Cryobanks was found to be in contravention of section 3(4) of the Competition Act in the market of stem cell banking in Mr. Ramakant Kini v. Dr. L.H. Hiranandani Hospital (‘Hiranandani case’).183 Mr. Ramakant Kini v. Dr. L.H. Hiranandani Hospital, Case No. 39 of 2012, para. 25. Mrs Jain entered into an agreement with M/s Life Cell India Pvt. Ltd. (Life Cell) to avail its services for banking of stem cells. Mrs Jain was registered with Hiranandani Hospital for maternity-related services and for the delivery of her child. As the time of delivery of the child drew near, Mrs Jain requested Hiranandani Hospital to allow Life Cell to collect the stem cell blood. Hiranandani Hospital refused to accede to the request of Mrs Jain on the grounds that she could avail the services of Cryobank and not any other stem cell banking service provider. The CCI held that the Hiranandani Hospital had an exclusive agreement with Cryobank under section 3(4) of the Competition Act. The CCI also said that for the purpose of section 3, there is no requirement to identify the relevant market but must see if the agreement has an anti-competitive effect in the market of the product/service of any party of the agreement. However, at the appellate stage, section 3 was read with section 19(3), (5), (6) and (7) of the Competition Act to set aside the order of CCI by the erstwhile COMPAT.184 Dr. L.H. Hiranandani Hospital v. CCI and Another [2015], (COMPAT) [2015] Comp AT 1144, paras 29, 30, 32, 33(70), 34. It was held that CCI failed to independently analyse factors of section 19(3), (5), (6) and (7) of the Competition Act to determine a violation of section 3(4) of the Competition Act. Further, the CCI erred in delineating the relevant market as the market of maternity services, ignoring that the whole dispute revolves around the agreement on stem cell bank services. It was also noted that CCI overlooked that the agreement entered between Hiranandani Hospital and Cryobanks did not restrict the choice of the service provider in the market for stem cell bank services. To understand the importance of the delineation of relevant market for analysing the factors of section 19(3), the erstwhile COMPAT largely relied on the Apple case, where tying-arraignment was not established because there was no foreclosure in either of the relevant markets of tying parties. Thus, previously decided cases show that relevant market was delineated to analyse the appreciable effect of alleged vertical anti-competitive agreements on the market, the other players of the market and the end consumer. Without delineation of the relevant product markets and relevant geographical markets, the inquiry against the vertical anti-competitive agreement would be considered incomplete. In the Hyundai Motors case,185 Hyundai Motor case, paras 40, 50. (NCLAT). the NCLAT set aside the order of CCI because CCI failed to delineate the relevant product market and the relevant geographical market while deciding the violation of section 3(4)(a) and (e) of the Competition Act. In the Hyundai Motors case, an information was filed by an authorized dealer of Hyundai, engaged in the business of reselling and servicing vehicles and spare parts. The business activities of an authorized dealer comprised of sales of vehicles, spare parts, accessories, and other related activities. The CCI held that the conduct of Hyundai contravened section 3(4)(a) and (e), however, the NCLAT set aside the CCI’s order by holding that the CCI’s inquiry under section 26 was incomplete because the relevant market has not been taken into consideration by the CCI. The finding of the NCLAT was based on the reasoning provided by the Supreme Court in the Coordination Committee case, where the Supreme Court held that CCI should delineate the relevant market in which the competition is affected due to the anti-competitive agreement. The Supreme Court interpreted the word ‘market’ in section 19(3) as ‘relevant market’ and held that it is important to determine the relevant market to identify the competitors of the concerned party, the boundaries of the competition, and the competitive constraints faced by market participants in order to examine the concentration of the market. The concentration of the market is key to determining which market participant holds the power over the market and whether its anti-competitive conduct would appreciably affect the market. Thus, it is necessary to delineate the relevant market to determine the market participant who has power over the market while analysing whether its conduct has an anti-competitive effect. However, unlike merger control and abuse of dominance cases, the statute is silent, and it may be argued that for the cases under section 3(4) of the Competition Act (at least at the prima facie stage), there is no need to go into precise relevant market delineation.186 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd., Case No. 18 of 2021, para. 43. For instance, in National Restaurant Association of India (‘NRAI’) v. Zomato Limited (‘Zomato’) & Others,187 National Restaurant Association of India (‘NRAI’) v. Zomato Limited (‘Zomato’) & Others, Case No. 16 of 2021, para. 34. the CCI held while passing a prima facie order directing the DG to conduct an investigation against Swiggy and Zomato for the alleged contravention of section 3(4) of the Competition Act, the relevant market need not be delineated precisely in vertical anti-competitive agreement cases. In furtherance to this observation, the CCI delineated the relevant market of Zomato and Swiggy as online food delivery platforms and operate as online intermediaries for food ordering and delivery. Also, while holding Maruti Suzuki liable for imposing resale price maintenance in the Maruti Suzuki case,188 Maruti Suzuki case, para. 16. the CCI did not precisely delineate the relevant market. CCI held that Maruti is a manufacturer dealing in the upstream market. [B] Significant Market Power in the Relevant Market The decisional practice of CCI shows that the market share or market power of the party to the vertical agreement is a pre-requisite requirement for establishing violation of section 3(4) read with section 3(1) of the Competition Act.189 Arshiya Rail Infrastructure Ltd. (ARIL) v. Ministry of Railway (‘MoR’) & Others, Case No. 64 of 2010, para. 21; Tamil Nadu Consumer Products Distributors Association v. Fangs Technology Private Limited & Other, Case No. 15 of 2018, para. 23, the CCI observed that the market share of the opposite party 1 has declined from 14.4% to 12.1% during 2017-18, and the turnover was also lower than its competitors. So, the CCI passed an order under section 26(2) of the Competition Act by holding that the opposite party-1 does not hold significant market power to violate section 3(4)(e) of the Competition Act. So, for the proper analysis of the anti-competitive effect, the CCI must look at the market shares of the parties entering into such an agreement, and also the market shares of other market players.190 M/s Karni Communication Private Ltd. & Other v. Haicheng Vivo Mobile (India), Case No. 35 of 2018, para. 25. The market share of the party is compared with that of other market players to analyse whether vertical restraint by the concerned party would cause or is likely to cause adverse effects on the competition. Also, it is implicit that the vertical agreement between two insignificant players will not affect a substantial portion of the market.191 Re: Accessories World Car Audio Private Limited v. Sony India Private Limited & Other, Case No. 03 of 2020, paras 22, 23. For instance, In Re: Accessories World Car Audio Private Limited v. Sony India Private Limited & Other,192 In Re: Accessories World Car Audio Private Limited v. Sony India Private Limited & Other, Case No. 03 of 2020, paras 22, 23. while rejecting the allegation of vertical anti-competitive agreement, the CCI held that the market of car audio products consisted of a large number of players and there was no player who possesses significant market share. The CCI was of the opinion that if the market had been fragmented with the presence of a large number of players exerting competitive constraints on each other, then the vertical agreement couldn’t cause any AAEC. Also, in Automobiles Dealers Association, Hathras, UP v. Global Automobiles Ltd. & Ors.,193 Automobiles Dealers Association, Hathras, UP v. Global Automobiles Ltd. & Ors., Case No. 33 of 2011, paras 12.2, 12.5, 12.11, 12.12, 12.13. the informant alleged that clause 3 of the letter of intent and statement of Global Automobiles shows that there was an exclusive distribution agreement with its dealers for the sale of two-wheeler vehicles. Clause 3 of the letter of intent restricts the dealers from dealing with the competitors of Global Automobile without their permission. However, due to the non-significant market share of both parties, Global Automobile was a new entrant in the two-wheeler manufacturing industry. So, even if there was a restrictive vertical agreement, the presence of adverse effects on competition was not established. To consider a vertical agreement as an anti-competitive agreement under section 3(4), the enterprises who are a part of the vertical agreement must have a significant market share, the absence of which is unlikely to create any AAEC in the market. For instance, in the Apple case,194 Apple case, paras 73, 74, 78. the CCI examined the ‘share of market’ to assess whether there is anti-competitive effect due to the tie-in arrangement between Apple and mobile network service providers (i.e., Vodafone and Airtel) to limit the market for iPhone in India. As per the available data, the CCI observed that Apple at that relevant point in time had a market share of less than 3% in the smartphone market, and no market participant in the mobile network services market had a market share of more than 30%. Thus, due to insignificant market shares of the parties involved in their relevant market, the CCI observed that none of the parties to the vertical agreement has the strength to adversely affect the market outcome in terms of creating entry barriers, market foreclosure, etc. This principle was also followed in the case of Shri Dhanraj Pillay v. M/s Hockey India,195 Dhanraj Pillay and others v. Hockey India, Case No. 73/2011, paras 10.13.2, 10.13.3. where the CCI approving its earlier Apple case held that for an agreement to have an AAEC in India, the person imposing the vertical restriction should be in a dominant position, and the intent behind the restriction should be foreclosure without any obvious efficiency justifications. Further, in an order passed by the CCI in the Bajaj case,196 Bajaj case, paras 77, 80. the CCI invoked the doctrine of de minimis (i.e., where the agreements of the enterprises will have an insignificant effect on the market) to oppose the submission against a vertical agreement to have an AAEC in India. The CCI observed that such an agreement can’t harm the market where the position of the party to the agreement is weak. As Bajaj Corp. Ltd. does not hold a strong position in the relevant market of the hair oil segment, the restriction imposed by Bajaj Corp. Ltd. would not cause any negative effect on the inter-brand competition. Conversely, if an agreement has been entered into by an enterprise having a strong market power, it is more likely that such an agreement will cause or likely to cause AAEC in India. The CCI has noted that when an enterprise with strong market power enters into an agreement, the CCI will place greater reliance on the anti-competitive factors mentioned in section 19(3) of the Competition Act which are entry barriers and foreclosure effects. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations of the CCI in the Automobile spare parts case, 197 Automobile spare parts case, paras 20.6.31. 20.6.32, 20.6.35, 20.6.38. where it relied on EU jurisprudence and noted that the European Court of Justice (ECJ) in its judgment of Nederlandsche Banden-Industrie Michelin (Michelin) v. Commission explained that a dominant player holds a special responsibility to prevent distortion of competition. Hence, the law forbids a market player holding significant market strength from entering into a vertical agreement. Once the market player crosses the threshold of becoming a significant market player, then entering into a vertical anti-competitive agreement would cause an adverse effect on the market. Nevertheless, the stance of the CCI is that one of the parties in the agreement must have a sizeable market presence and may not enjoy a dominant position, as mentioned under section 4 of the Competition Act, to have any anti-competitive concerns.198 Hiranandani case, paras 29, 30. The key area of analysis that has to be seen under an agreement analysis under section 3 is that the agreement must cause or likely to cause an AAEC in India. Therefore, for an agreement to be void, it must distort the competitive structure of the market appreciably. It must be noted that the CCI has noted that an agreement having insignificant effects will not be a void agreement under section 3 of the Competition Act. To be void, such anti-competitive effects in the agreement must cause AAEC. While there are no straight jacket formulae given under the Competition Act to conclude when an enterprise will have a market power amenable to section 3(4) of the Act, it may be presumed at least on a prima facie basis that any enterprise having a market share of 30% or more will be deemed to be an enterprise having a strong market power, and hence any vertical restrictions so imposed by the enterprise must take into account the factors mentioned under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. The market participant must have significant market power in its relevant market to restrain the free competition in the downstream or upstream market, create an entry barrier, drive the existing market participants out of the market, or affect the market outcome by deterring entry. Hence, the market power of the parties to the vertical agreement needs to be looked at while assessing the factors to the AAEC under section 19(3) of the Competition Act.

§2.18 FACTORS TO DETERMINE AAEC IN INDIA As mentioned above, an agreement will be void under section 3(2) of the Competition Act if it causes or is likely to cause an AAEC in India. It would be pertinent to note that for all agreements (other than those agreements mentioned under section 3(3) of the Competition Act), whether at the horizontal level, vertical level or pure commercial agreements,199 The CCI in the Hiranandani case, Case No. 39 of 12, para. 11, found an independent violation under section 3(1) which was not a violation of section 3(3) or 3(4) of the Competition Act. Although the Hiranandani case was overturned in appeal on market issues, the point of law that there can be a separate violation of section 3(1) is stated herein. the burden of proof would be on the CCI to demonstrate that the agreement causes or is likely to cause an AAEC in the market in India. The CCI undertakes an effect-based analysis based on the factors to determine AAEC as provided under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. It is worthwhile to note that section 19(3) contains a checklist of both negative factors and positive factors. The negative factors bring anti-competitive effects, such as the creation of barriers to new entrants, likely to drive the existing competitors and the potential to foreclose the competition or harm consumers. The positive factors bring pro-competitive effects, such as improvement in production/distribution, scientific/economic development, or benefit to consumers. The CCI have to consider all or any of the following factors while determining whether the vertical agreement has caused or is likely to cause AAEC. The negative factors indicate that there is an AAEC, whereas the absence of these negative factors would normally indicate that there is no AAEC. The positive factors are shown by the enterprise to justify efficiency of their agreement; thus, the presence of these positive factors indicates there is no AAEC.200 Automobiles Dealers Association, Hathras, UP v. Global Automobiles and Others, Case No. 33 of 2011, para. 12.9. Though there is no straight jacket formula as everything depends on a case-by-case basis, so, for a complete analysis of the net impact of the vertical anti-competitive agreement, all the factors under section 19(3) need to be examined while assessing. [A] Negative Factors The negative factors due to vertical agreements disrupt healthy and fair competition in the market. The negative factors result in anti-competitive effects which might foreclose other suppliers/distributors by raising barriers to entry, drive out the existing competitors to reduce intra-brand or inter-brand competition, exploit the end-consumer of the market to earn more profit or create entry barriers to lock the consumers by depriving them of better choices. In the MMT case,201 MMT case, paras 297, 298, 299 304, 306, 308. where MMT was an online travel agency providing travel and tourism-related services, it entered into an agreement with OYO which was providing franchising services to budget hotels, to delist Fabhotels and Treebo (competitors of OYO). The CCI noted that the agreement between MMT and OYO had foreclosed an important online channel of distribution for the Fabhotels and Treebo in the form of denial of market access. Moreover, the agreement created entry and expansion barriers which made it difficult for a new player to enter the market and impossible for existing players to survive. Driving out Fabhotel and Treebo from the market adversely effected the budget hotels in the downstream market because this agreement limited the options of budget hotels while seeking franchise services. The CCI noted that for both franchisors and budget hotels, MMT was the key access route to reach the end consumers. So, this foreclosure had a significant impact on their business in terms of visibility, and thus, they were not getting a fair chance and had no opportunity to compete on merits. More than the harm to the competitors, the CCI is more concerned about the harm to the competitive process. Thereby, along with anti-competitive effects on the competitors, the CCI also looks at the harm caused to the end consumers due to the vertical restraints. In the Maruti Suzuki case, 202 Maruti Suzuki case, paras 48, 50, 55. it was observed that the vertical restraint did not result in accrual of any consumer benefits; rather, it resulted in the denial of benefits to consumers as they were made to pay high prices. The vertical restraint in the form of resale price maintenance eliminated the price competition between the dealers and thereby did not incentivize the dealers to pass on the benefit of extra margin to the end consumers by providing better services. Similarly, in the MMT case,203 MMT case, para. 298. the CCI observed that the vertical anti-competitive agreement harmed the consumers side by depriving the end-consumers of a wide range of choices, better schemes and policies, technological developments and other benefits of a competitive market. The CCI should examine all vertical agreements which were entered between vertical parties in order to look into the totality of all the factors mentioned in section 19(3). In this regard, it is noteworthy to look at the observations of the CCI in the Automobile spare parts case.204 Automobile spare parts case, paras 20.6.31. 20.6.32, 20.6.33, 20.6.38. The CCI analysed all the agreements entered between OEMs and their dealers and OEMs and their suppliers. The OEMs had entered into a selective distribution system, and they had prohibited their dealers from sourcing any spare parts from any third party apart from OEMs or their authorized vendors. Further, it was also observed that the conduct of the OEMs acts as an entry barrier for independent repairers because the authorized dealers were also prohibited from selling the spare parts to the independent repairers over the counter. The CCI looked into the totality of the agreements to see whether there was a case of anti-competitive behaviour that could be made out. The CCI emphasized the point that efficiencies claimed by the OEMs cannot allow the creation of entry barriers to eliminate the competitive force in the market. For the sake of efficiency gains, the CCI can’t ignore the negative effect of the said agreement on the consumers and the market stakeholders. Hence, it was held that short-term efficiency gains must not outweigh the long-term negative effects stemming from the elimination of competition in the market. Understanding the nature of competitive constrain is necessary while analysing AAEC under section 19(3) of the Competition Act. The CCI relied upon the General Court’s order in GlaxoSmithKline Services Unlimited v. Commission, wherein it was held that an analysis of the effect of the concerned agreement can be done by balancing the negative factors and positive factors of the agreement. However, the negative factors will be prioritized to prevent the market player from indulging in anti-competitive behaviour. The CCI held that even if one or two of the vertical agreements do not violate the competition law and bring efficiency, they may still be held to be anti-competitive if the holistic analysis of all the vertical agreements shows long-term anti-competitive effects to the market by creating entry barrier, driving out existing players, foreclosing the market, and charging exploitative prices from end-customers. [B] Positive Factors Factors mentioned in sub-clause (d)-(f) of section 19(3) deal with the pro-competitive effects to the competition in the market. These factors enhance the efficiency of the distribution process and contribute to consumer welfare; hence, they are called positive factors. To determine whether the vertical agreement caused AAEC or not, the CCI should always balance the effect of both positive and negative factors because such an agreement which creates barriers to entry may also induce improvements in the promotion or distribution of goods.205 Bajaj case, para. 81. For instance, in Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Others,206 Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Others, Case No. 80 of 2014, para. 17. where the CCI dismissed the allegation against e-tailers for causing appreciable adverse effects through their vertical restraint in terms of purchase and price of goods in their e-platforms. To reach its conclusion, the CCI analysed the pro-competitive effect of the agreement on the touchstone of factors elucidated under section 19(3) and held that there was no violation under section 3(4) of the Competition Act. It was found that e-tailers increased competition and provided a mechanism for price comparison and comparison of other pros and cons of the products. Hence, it was found that the agreement did not result in an AAEC in the Indian market. The CCI pointed out that the online distribution system had, in fact, shown considerable consumer efficiencies by providing the option of doorstep delivery to consumers which was more convenient than the delivery service provided by brick-and-mortar retail outlets. Thus, while balancing the anti-competitive effect and pro-competitive effect due to the agreement under section 3, the CCI generally take note of the importance of competition in the market. In a competitive market, the market players are under continuous pressure to improve their capacity and product efficiency to favour customers’ welfare. Also, the consumer will benefit because the price of the product will reduce. Further, to survive in the market, market players would be innovative and develop new technologies.207 Hiranandani case, para. 18. [1] Business Justifications or Rationales Apart from the positive factors mentioned in section 19(3) of the Competition Act, the CCI can dismiss the allegation of section 3(4) of the Competition Act by considering the business purpose or rationale behind the vertical restraints. In fact, the CCI in the Bajaj case208 Bajaj case, para. 75. has noted that business purpose or rationale of agreements must be looked into and even acknowledged that vertical arrangements can be justified on certain grounds like protection from free riding, efficient management of sales of products, economic efficiency, etc. The business rationales which were considered by the CCI in its previous orders include:(i) the objective of the requirement of written permission for the dealers from the entity before dealing with the competitors of the entity is to ensure no one is free-riding on facilities and services provided by the entity and monitor the financial activity of the dealers;209 Hyundai Motor case, Case Nos 36 and 82 of 2014, para. 70. (CCI). (ii) exclusive distribution agreement can be used as protection from free riding, efficient management of sales of products, and economic efficiency;210 Bajaj case, para. 75. (iii) exclusively selling the product through its authorized distributors to protect the brand image and goodwill;211 Ashish Ahuja v. Snapdeal.com & Ors., Case No. 17 of 2014, para. 19. (iv) to protect the interest of all distributors, an exclusive distribution agreement can designate specific sale regions to distributors and restrict the distributors from encroaching on each other’s sale regions;212 M/s K.C. Marketing v. OPPO Mobiles MU Private Limited, Case No. 34 of 2018, paras 30, 31. (v) monitoring the resale price of its own products cannot by itself be said to be anti-competitive;213 M/s. ESYS Information Technologies Pvt. Ltd. v. Intel Corporation and Others, Case No. 48 of 2011, para. 8.3.8. and (vi) agreement to sell products exclusively on online platforms results in benefiting the consumers in terms of providing opportunity to the consumers to compare the prices and better delivery services.214 Mr. Mohit Mangslani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited & Ors., Case No. 80 of 2014, para. 17. Hence, the CCI also looks at the business rationale behind the agreement along with the pro-competitive effects like consumer benefits, improvement in production/distribution, or technological/economic development to hold section 3(4) of the Competition Act is not applicable to such agreements.

§2.19 EXEMPTION UNDER SECTION 3 OF THE COMPETITION ACT Section 3(5) of the Competition Act provides for two exemptions, which are intellectual property exemption and export exemption. Section 3(5)(i) of the Competition Act provides for the intellectual property exemption. The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, widens the scope of section 3(5)(i) of the Competition Act by making this provision more inclusive. The inclusive list of IPRs includes: the Copyright Act, 1957; the Patents Act, 1970; the Trade Marks Act, 1999; the Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999; the Designs Act, 2000; the Semi-conductor Integrated Circuits Layout-Design Act, 2000; and any other intellectual property law. The Competition Act provides a limited defence to agreements under section 3 relating to IPR, wherein a person has the right to restrain any infringement of or impose reasonable conditions, as may be necessary, for protecting any rights conferred under IPR legislation. The CCI has observed, in certain decisions, the ambit of the exemption provided under this section. The ambit of the said clause has been discussed in detail in Chapter 5 on IPR and competition law. Section 3(5)(ii) provides for an exemption in cases of an agreement, for the purposes of section 3, which relates primarily to exports. Section 3 of the Competition Act provides that any agreement which causes or is likely to cause any AAEC in India is void. Therefore, section 3 only covers agreements which have an effect in India. In this backdrop, section 3(5)(ii) provides for an export exemption. Section 3(5)(ii) of the Competition Act provides a safe harbour for the enterprise to enter into an anti-competitive agreement relating to the export of goods from India. Therefore, the Competition Act exempts export cartels, i.e., a cartel between enterprises located in India which intend to cartelize in markets outside India, as opposed to import cartels, i.e., a cartel between enterprises located outside India with the aim of cartelizing in a relevant market within India. Similarly, any vertical agreement which has the sole purpose of exporting goods outside India will not be covered under section 3 of the Competition Act. In Shri Nirmal Kumar Manshani v. M/s Ruchi Soya and Others,215 Shri Nirmal Kumar Manshani v. M/s Ruchi Soya and Others, Case No. 76 of 2012, para. 99. the parties to the horizontal anti-competitive agreement pleaded that the agreement pertained to export sales, and therefore, export cartels would be exempted under section 3(5)(ii) because such an agreement had no effects on the markets in India. But, the CCI rejected the plea because neither the Parties were exporters, nor was the agreement exclusively related to exports. Merely because the end product was being exported by the millers and the processors would not immunize the contravention of section 3 of the Competition Act. The exemption under section 3(5)(ii) of the Competition Act can only be granted to enterprises which are actual exporters of goods from India. In Re: Cartelisation by Shipping Lines in the matter of provision of Maritime Motor Vehicle Transport Services to the Original Equipment Manufacturers v. Nippon Yusen Kabushiki Kaisha and Others,216 In Re: Cartelisation by Shipping Lines in the matter of provision of Maritime Motor Vehicle Transport Services to the Original Equipment Manufacturers v. Nippon Yusen Kabushiki Kaisha and Others, Suo Motu Case No. 10 of 2014, para. 37.2. the CCI rejected the plea for the exemption under section 3(5)(ii) because the opposite parties were not the exporters as per the provision but were merely providing maritime transport services to the OEMs who were actually exporters. It was further noted that the exemption under section 3(5)(ii) would not be allowed merely because the end product was being exported out of India. To claim such an exemption, the enterprises must have entered into an anti-competitive agreement exclusively for the supply distribution or control of any exported goods or services. There are not many orders on the exemption under section 3(5)(ii) of the Competition Act. Thus, the jurisprudence on exemption from the anti-competitive agreements pertaining to the export of products has not been developed. In the coming years, we can expect the CCI to apply and analyse this exemption in a detailed manner.

§2.20 CONCLUSION Over the past year, the CCI has dealt with various cases involving anti-competitive agreements. Through active enforcement of the Competition Act, the market is protected from the ill effects of anti-competitive agreements. This chapter discussed past case laws and how anti-competitive agreements are treated under competition law. Looking forward, we can expect stronger enforcement and a stricter stance towards cartels and vertical restraints. Along with these two traditional anti-competitive agreements, CCI will also deal with ‘hub-and-spoke cartels’. Also, on the side of enforcement, it would be interesting to see how CCI is going to use ‘leniency plus’ to detect more cartels. Thus, competition law is expecting a huge development in its jurisprudence.

title: Chapter 3: Abuse of Dominance and Competition Law
container_title: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition)

sections.title: LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK
sections.text: §3.01 LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK Section 4 of the Competition Act provides that no ‘enterprise’ or ‘group’ will abuse its dominant position. The said section deals with unilateral conduct. Dominant position has been defined under the Competition Act as ‘position of strength, enjoyed by an enterprise, in the relevant market, in India, which enables it to (i) operate independently of competitive forces prevailing in the relevant market; or (ii) affect its competitors or consumers or the relevant market, in its favour’.1 Competition Act, section 4 (2) a – Explanation. In India, the determination of ‘dominance’ is based on a qualitative assessment of the prevalent market dynamics and the relative position of strength enjoyed by the market participants. Section 4 of the Competition Act stipulates specific practices, i.e., imposition of unfair or discriminatory conditions on price in purchase or sale (including predatory pricing), limiting or restricting the production of goods, denial of market access, and leveraging market position in one relevant market to enter into another relevant market, by dominant enterprises shall amount to abuse of dominance. [A] Textual Interpretation of Section 4 of the Competition Act Decisional practice suggests that the key analysis that is done by the CCI, the erstwhile COMPAT and the NCLAT, to date, in analysing dominance cases is whether the enterprise under investigation has a ‘position of strength’.
