Such ‘position of strength’ so enjoyed by the enterprise should enable it to affect the competitors or consumers and the market dynamics in its favour. There have been various indices (provided under section 19(4)) which have been given under the Competition Act for determining whether the concerned enterprise, under inquiry, is dominant. In light of the factors mentioned, the CCI looks at the market dynamics and behaviour of the enterprise under inquiry to analyse whether the concerned enterprise is dominant or not. Akin to the position in foreign antitrust jurisdictions, it must be noted that merely having a position of dominance is not bad under the provisions of the Competition Act, but it must be proved that the dominant enterprise has also ‘abused’ its dominance. This is a marked departure from the earlier legislative framework under the previous regime under the Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969, wherein emphasis was placed on the size of the concerned player, rather than the actual abusive practice by, or conduct of such a player. [B] Dominance Per Se Is Not Illegal In a marked departure from the earlier regime, as discussed at the beginning of this chapter, the Competition Act does not prohibit dominance, only its abuse. Thus, dominance per se is not illegal, rather, it is the abuse of dominance that is prohibited. Several complaints have been filed before the CCI seeking intervention in cases of mergers which are likely to create dominant enterprises with the capacity to abuse this dominance.2 Consumer Unity & Trust Society v. PVR Limited and INOX Leisure Limited, Case No. 29 of 2022; Vikas Verma v. Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone Ltd. & Others, Case No. 02 of 2021; Satyen Narendra Bajaj v. PayU Payments Private Limited, Case No. 23 of 2019. However, CCI has consistently held that ‘the mere existence of dominance bereft of any abusive conduct under the provisions of Section 4 of the Act, cannot be held to be the basis to order an investigation’.3 Vikas Verma v. Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone Ltd. & Others, Case No. 02 of 2021. However, ‘post-facto, if any matter of abusive conduct under Section 4 of the Act is brought to the notice of the Commission, the same may be looked into at that stage in terms of the provisions of the Act’. Similarly, in Satyen Narendra Bajaj v. PayU Payments Private Limited,4 Satyen Narendra Bajaj v. PayU Payments Private Limited, Case No. 23 of 2019. it was alleged that pursuant to the combination between PayU and Wibmo, the Combined Entity would attempt to monopolize the e-payment gateway market in India, which was likely to have exploitative as well as exclusionary effects in the market of e-payment processing gateways. However, the CCI held that the same was insufficient to give rise to proceedings for abuse of dominance. It observed that:…while the Act prohibits an abuse of dominant position by an enterprise, mere existence of dominant position, without any prima facie evidence of its abuse, is not recognised as an anti-competitive conduct in the scheme of the Act. In order to establish a prima facie case for intervention, an abuse of a dominant position cannot be based on a mere existence or potential to achieve dominance in the market. Therefore, a mere apprehension of abuse of dominance is not enough to initiate an investigation under the Competition Act because dominance per se is not anti-competitive and only abusive conduct is if the same falls within the provisions of section 4 of the Competition Act. [C] Procedure Adopted in an Abuse of Dominance Investigation Based on the definition of dominance and the scheme of the Competition Act, it is to be noted that in every dominance investigation, there are three key elements which are analysed by the CCI:(A) definition of relevant market; (B) to analyse whether the entity under investigation is ‘dominant’ in the relevant market; (C) if the entity is found to be dominant, whether the dominant entity has engaged in any actions which can be considered an abuse of the dominant position under section 4 of the Competition Act. Every investigation of abuse of dominance proceeds as per this structure, and the analysis will be incomplete without a detailed analysis of each of these elements. We have dealt with, in detail, the concepts of ‘enterprise’ and ‘relevant market’ in an earlier chapter and the same is not repeated here for the sake of brevity. As such, practically, it must be noted that most of the decisions depend on the definition of relevant market, and it is for this reason a substantial amount of analysis is done in every dominance investigation on the definition of relevant market to be adopted. Unless an enterprise enjoys a dominant position in the relevant market, its conduct cannot be subjected to an abuse inquiry. Hence, it is a pre-condition for applying section 4 of the Competition Act, which decrees a set of standards of conduct which are applicable to dominant firms and not to non-dominant firms. This is because what may be commercially justified for the latter may become exploitative and exclusionary when practised by the former. A particular conduct when exhibited by a non-dominant player may not be violative of section 4, but the same conduct by a dominant player could be a contravention.5 Umar Javeed and Ors. v. Google LLC and Anr., Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 572. The entire Competition Act is predicated on the fact that there are certain activities which done by a dominant player will cause market distortion due to its sheer size, and thus, such activities cannot be allowed. This is why dominant players cannot defend their conduct on the basis of the fact that other industry players also follow the same conduct. On this point, it is relevant to note the analysis of the CCI in Belaire Owner’s Association v. DLF Limited 6 Belaire Owner’s Association v. DLF Limited, Case No. 19 of 2010. (‘DLF case’).7 Ibid., para. 12.20. While refuting the competition law concerns emanating from the agreements between builders and buyers, DLF argued that such one-sided agreements are standard industry practices and all the players in the market follow such practices. The CCI, however, did not give importance to the defence because it noted that DLF is a market leader and a trendsetter, and it is not bound by practices of other players, and in any case, the practice itself is anti-competitive and abusive (as has been discussed later in the chapter). As such, after observing the market practice of DLF, the other players have aped the practice of executing one-sided agreements because they do not have the competitive might of the dominant enterprise. In fact, if DLF were to modify its agreement to make it more buyer-friendly, it would be able to put sufficient pressure on its competitors to follow suit. Accordingly, the CCI concluded that DLF has acted in a manner which has affected competitor and consumer behaviour, to its advantage. Hence, the above sequential exercise must be necessarily followed while determining an allegation of contravention of section 4 of the Competition Act.8 Coal India Limited, West Bengal v. Competition Commission of India [2017], CompLR 0447 (CompAT), para. 20.2-20.4.

sections.title: POSITION OF STRENGTH
sections.text: §3.02 POSITION OF STRENGTH The decisional practice of the CCI, erstwhile COMPAT and NCLAT would show that they undertake a holistic analysis to understand whether the entity under investigation enjoys a position of strength to influence the competitive forces prevalent in the market to its advantage.9 RKG Hospitalities Pvt. Ltd. v. Oravel Stays Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 03 of 2019, para. 43. In the case of MCX Stock Exchange Ltd. v. NSE India Ltd 10 MCX Stock Exchange Ltd. v. NSE India Ltd, Case No. 13 of 2009, para. 10.36. (‘NSE case’), a ‘position of strength’ was analysed by CCI on the basis of the comparative advantages enjoyed by NSE over its competitors in terms of financial resources, technical capabilities, brand value, historical legacy, etc., and whether an as efficient competitor would be able to engage in the same conduct as NSE.11 Ibid., para. 10.38-10.49. The reason is that such an enterprise can force its competitors to act in a certain manner which would make the market and consumers respond or react such that it is beneficial to the dominant enterprise but detrimental to the competitors. In addition, the analysis is whether such advantages allow the enterprise to undertake certain activities (like low pricing) which the competitors would find difficult to follow on a sustained basis. By virtue of the said observation and approach taken by the CCI in the NSE case, it seems to have adopted the efficient competitor test for dominance investigations. This means that whether a certain conduct is exclusionary or abusive depends on whether a competitor, which is as efficient as the dominant enterprise, would be able to sacrifice profits in the same manner in the long run as the dominant enterprise. Herein, it was found that NSE’s competitors, i.e., MCX, would not be able to sustain the below-cost pricing adopted by NSE, and hence, NSE was held to be in a dominant position. This approach of the CCI for determining a ‘position of strength’ was reaffirmed in XYZ and Ors v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors 12 XYZ and Ors v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., Case Nos 07 of 2020, 12 of 2021 and 35 of 2021. (‘Play Store case’). In the said case, it was alleged that Google was abusing its control over the Play Store and Android Operating System (OS) in the markets for licensable mobile OS for smart mobile devices and app stores for Android OS in violation of the provisions of section 4 of the Competition Act. This was facilitated through, inter alia, mandating app developers to use Google’s proprietary Google Play Billing System (GPBS) for in-app purchases and paid apps on the Play Store and advantaging Google Pay, an United Payment Interface (UPI) over competing UPI apps. On the aspect of position of strength, the CCI observed that the moment there is any imposition of any unfair or discriminatory condition by a dominant player, the statutory prohibitions shall trigger for all instances of abuse enshrined under section 4(2) of the Competition Act. In this case, Google enjoyed unparalleled competitive advantages which even its nearest rivals were unable to match.13 Ibid., para. 81. It is to be noted that the aspect of position of strength is not to be viewed in a snapshot manner at a point in time, but the assessment of position of strength must be made over a period of time. The CCI has noted that an enterprise takes years to acquire a position of strength, and therefore, its conduct must be examined over a period of time while undertaking dominance investigations. The CCI has opined in the DLF case that an examination of the dominant enterprise needs to be done over a period of time to examine whether a case of abuse under section 4(2) of the Competition Act is made out. The relevant portion of the order is as follows:It should be noted that dominance is neither acquired nor asserted in a transient moment in time. It takes years for an enterprise to acquire a position of strength and its conduct has to be examined over a period of time in most cases to ascertain whether it constituted abuse. Use of snapshot data of market shares or market presence based on current active stock figures would give a false picture that, if applied in the context of the Act, may even cause avoidable harm to the market [emphasis supplied]. Therefore, our assessment of dominant position is by looking at the profile, presence and achievements of DLF Ltd. over the years as well as its conduct, particularly in relation to consumers, over a period of time. At the same time, it must be borne in mind that markets by their very nature are dynamic and keep changing with time. Hence, while analysing dominance, an important factor that needs to be taken into consideration is the time-period during which the contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act is alleged. For instance, after the DLF case, when similar allegations were once again raised for identical conduct by DLF two years later in Amit Mittal v. DLF Limited,14 Amit Mittal v. DLF Limited, Case No. 73 of 2014, para. 33. it was found that the market scenario had changed, and DLF was no longer dominant and consequently, no case of abuse can be made out. This also highlights that precedents may not always be valuable in competition law cases as all analysis is based on the effect of market forces. Similarly, in Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd. (Ola case),15 Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Case Nos 6 & 74 of 2015. an objection was taken that the period of investigation of Ola’s conduct was stretched to September 2015 instead of January 2015, when the first order for investigation was passed by the CCI. The CCI clarified that ‘though the allegations highlighted in the information serve as a starting point for investigation, the same does not determine its scope in entirety. Moreover, the purpose of investigation in the said markets is not limited to visualizing the static nature of competition, but to have an overall examination of the dynamic aspects’. Further, the proceedings before CCI are in rem, not in personam. Hence, this brings us to the point that it is not necessary for the period of assessment of dominance to be strictly restricted to the information filed, especially if it’s a continuing conduct. [A] Parameters of Dominance under the Competition Act To determine position of strength, the definition of dominance under the Competition Act provides for two broad parameters which the CCI needs to look into to identify whether the enterprise in question enjoys a position of strength. The enterprise must:(1) operate independently of competitive forces prevailing in the relevant market; (2) affect its competitors or consumers or the relevant market in its favour. [1] Operate Independently of Competitive Forces Prevailing in the Relevant Market A market operates where there is an interplay of market forces of demand and supply. Further, a market participant is constrained in its behaviour because of various competitive forces prevalent in the market, like other efficient competitors, strong buyers, market structure, market entry, or any other relevant factors in the concerned market. A market participant may also be constrained by other factors like strict government regulations. Therefore, for the purpose of ascertaining dominance, it is important to examine the ability of an enterprise to operate independently of such competitive forces prevalent in the market, especially competitive forces generated by its rivals. However, this does not mean that the analysis is only limited to direct competitors since the word used is ‘competitive forces’. Further, an enterprise cannot behave in a manner which is totally lopsided in its favour without keeping into account the concerns of the other market participants, unless the entity in question has an appreciable position of strength. As elaborated earlier, the CCI, erstwhile COMPAT and NCLAT, in their decisional practice, have always given emphasis on whether the enterprise has a position of strength, which can be derived from its market share, financial muscle, regulatory barriers, structural barriers emanating from the operations of dominant enterprise and other operational barriers which have become entry barriers for new players in the market. In this regard, it is apposite to note the approach of the CCI in the DLF case. The CCI reviewed the agreements entered into between DLF and the buyers in great detail. We have already discussed how the CCI rejected DLF’s defence that such one-sided agreements are standard industry practices on account of DLF’s position as a dominant enterprise. Further, the CCI also noted that, under normal competitive market conditions, having such one-sided agreements in place should result in loss of customers since the consumers will shift to some other real estate builder, but such events were not happening. This clearly amplifies the point that DLF is operating independently of the competitive forces. The relevant observations of the CCI in this regard are as follows: … The moot point in this case and indeed the competition concern is that a dominant builder/developer is in a position to impose such blatantly unfair conditions in its ‘agreement’ with its customers and bind them in such one-sided contractual obligation. In a competitive scenario, where the enterprise indulges in such anti-consumer conduct, there is sufficient competition in the market to provide easy alternatives for the consumer. The competitive forces would ensure that the builder/developer would soon face loss of customers, which would force it to become more consumer-friendly. [emphasis supplied] However, only when a dominant enterprise indulges in such conduct is there little hope for the consumers because not only that enterprise indulges in such behaviour with impunity but smaller competitors would want to enjoy as much advantage by following the leader. Since a weaker competitor is not in a position to take on the competitive might of the dominant enterprise, it would rather emulate the dominant enterprise and take similar advantage of the consumers. If an enterprise has the ability to influence its competitors or the consumers in a relevant market, as demonstrated above, there could be little doubt in its ability to influence the market itself in its favour. An announcement of several large projects by DLF Ltd. at one go could make its competitors react by holding some of their own projects to avoid market saturation. Similarly, prospective consumers may defer their demand in expectation of availability of projects to be offered by the market leader. Thus, DLF Ltd. would be able to influence both the supply and demand of projects in the relevant market. These possibilities indicate that DLF Ltd. has a position of strength as envisaged in Explanation (a)(ii) to section 4 of the Act… [2] Affect Its Competitors or Consumers or the Relevant Market in Its Favour This is the second limb in the definition of dominance. The language used in the provision is ‘… operate independently of competitive forces prevailing in the relevant market or affect its competitors or consumers or the relevant market, in its favour’. Hence, dominance can be established by proving either limb of this definition. As seen in M/s Kansan News Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s Fastway Transmission Pvt. Ltd. & Ors., 16 M/s Kansan News Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s Fastway Transmission Pvt. Ltd. & Ors., Case No. 36/2011, para. 6.4.10. the CCI observed that the large subscriber base of the multi-system operators (MSOs), which carried the channels offered by broadcasters, meant that every broadcaster was dependent on their network. In such a situation, the MSOs were found to be in a position to affect the market in their favour. It is critical to find out whether the entity can operate independently of other competitive forces emanating from competitors and consumers. This limb of the definition deals with the link between the position of strength held by the enterprise concerned and the competitive process, i.e., the way in which the concerned enterprise and other relevant stakeholders interact in the market. Dominance is the ability to prevent effective competition from being maintained on the market and to act to an appreciable extent independently of other players. A non-dominant firm cannot operate independently of competitive forces, and hence, regulatory intervention in the commercial decisions of such enterprises is neither warranted nor desired. The credible threat of availability of alternative suppliers and power of the consumer to exercise choice operate as forces to deter exploitation. However, in the case of a dominant enterprise, consumers cannot check exploitation as their choices are constrained, and hence, statutory prohibition and, consequently, timely intervention are required to prevent abuse of dominance. Thus, abuse cannot be looked into in isolation of the market position of the enterprise because an unfair condition constitutes an ‘abuse’ or offence only for a dominant enterprise.17 Coal India Limited, West Bengal v. Competition Commission of India [2017], CompLR 0447 (CompAT), para. 20.3. Generally, in a competitive market, any behaviour of a market participant will have a corresponding response from other players in the relevant market. For example, if a market participant increased the price of its good or imposed unreasonable conditions to the detriment of the counterparty, the consumer would choose the product of the competitor of such market participant. Further, an increase in price will also result in lowering of sales if there is a substitute good available in the relevant market. However, when such behaviour is being imposed by the dominant enterprise, the consumers have no option but to abide by the conditions imposed by such a dominant undertaking. They are faced with a Hobson’s choice/‘take it or leave it’ proposition by such an enterprise. Similarly, in the event the dominant undertaking starts undercutting the price of its product, the other players in the market have no option but to follow suit just to remain competitive in the market. An enterprise which is capable of substantially increasing prices above the competitive level for a significant period of time is generally in a position of strength and possesses the requisite ability to act to an appreciable extent independently of competitors, customers and consumers. Further, unlike enterprises in a market characterized by effective competitive constraints, a dominant enterprise is able to price above the competitive level and earn supra-normal profits in the process. In addition, dominant enterprise can also maintain supra-normal profits by reducing output and, hence, escalating demand for the product. The key point is whether the enterprise can conduct its business operations irrespective of the behaviour of the competitors, its customers and consumers. If the answer is yes, then the enterprise/group will be considered to be in a position of strength. As was the situation in the NSE case, the competitors of NSE had no option but to follow the practice of transaction waivers given by NSE just to remain competitive in the market. In the process, apart from NSE, all the other competitors incurred heavy losses, some in fact, exited the market. Therefore, the key analysis to be done is whether the enterprise under investigation can affect the behaviour of consumers or competitors to its advantage. On this aspect, the CCI in the DLF case, observed that an enterprise may have the capability to not only operate independently of competitive forces but also influence its competitors or consumers or the relevant market itself in its favour. This is a higher degree of strength which allows the enterprise to freely adopt price or non-price strategies to overcome downward pressures on its profit from its competitors, capture or bind consumers or create a market environment that would deter newer competition, both in terms of competing enterprises or rival products.18 DLF case, para. 12.47. This aspect of acting independently was also discussed in the case of Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Limited & Ors.19 Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Limited & Ors., Case No. 03 of 2011, para. 20.5.59. (‘Automobile Spare Parts case’). The CCI, in this case, observed that the underlying principle in the definition of a dominant position is linked to the concept of market power which allows an enterprise to act independently of competitive constraints prevalent in the market. Such independence affords such an enterprise the capacity to affect the conditions in the relevant market, in its favour, and to the economic detriment of its competitors and consumers. The notion of independence, which is the special feature of dominance, is related to the level of competitive constraint facing the enterprise/group under investigation. For dominance to exist, the enterprise or the group concerned must not be subject to effective competitive constraints. In other words, it thus must have substantial market power. Market power is generally noted in matured antitrust jurisdictions as the power to influence market prices, output, innovation, the variety or quality of goods and services, or other parameters of competition on the market for a significant period of time. In the Automobile Spare Parts case, the CCI relied on the jurisprudence of the EU to analyse the aspect of independence from the competitive forces. It found that as per the EU Guidance on the Commission’s Enforcement Priorities in Applying Article 82 EC Treaty to Abusive Exclusionary Conduct by Dominant Undertakings, ‘the capacity of an enterprise to operate independently of competitive forces’ is related to the degree of competitive constraints that are exerted on the concerned enterprise. Dominance implies that such competitive constraints are not sufficiently effective, and as a result, the enterprise is able to enjoy substantial market power over time. Hence, the notion of independence means that an enterprise’s actions are largely insensitive to the actions and reactions of competitors, customers and, ultimately, the consumers in the market. It is pertinent to note that the term independence does not mean that there is an absence of any other competitor in the relevant market. It implies that the enterprise in question does not face substantial competitive constraints from the other competitors in the market, and thereby, the enterprise has the market power to change the market dynamics in its favour. The CCI as well as the erstwhile COMPAT have also made their observations on this point in the NSE case. In this case, the NSE started giving repeated transaction fee waivers for transactions done in the currency derivative segment. It must be noted that a stock exchange cannot determine the price at which a particular scrip will be sold in the market. A stock exchange just provides a platform on which scrip can be traded, and it gets a transaction fee for each transaction done on its exchange. Based on the same, it is apposite to note that for a stock exchange, transaction fee is the single most important source of revenue. However, since NSE was providing transaction fee waivers for transactions done in the Currency Derivative Segment, MCX-SX and other players which offered services only in the Currency Derivatives had no other option but to follow suit and provide waivers in the Currency Derivative Segment. In the process, the other players, including MCX-SX incurred huge financial losses and depletion in their financial resources. However, NSE continued to make supra-normal profits because it had the licence to operate in other segments (equity, futures/options and wholesale debt market) from the securities regulator which MCX-SX did not have. Thus, in the process, MCX had to adopt the policy to sustain itself in the market for the CD segment. In this factual background, the erstwhile COMPAT noted the following with respect to the behavioural conduct of NSE while establishing dominance:The most relevant question was as to whether the NSE could continue with its no transaction fee policy, as compared to its competitor MCX-SX. It is absolutely clear that while NSE could continue because of its relative strength in other segments, MCX-SX could not have continued with that policy and per force it had to adopt the same policy of no transaction fees, as otherwise it could not have even entered the market, forget about its sustenance in that market. [B] Special Responsibility of a Dominant Player Dominant enterprises are under a special responsibility to prevent their conduct from impairing genuine undistorted competition. In the DLF case, the erstwhile COMPAT observed that being the market leader, DLF had a special duty to be within the four corners of the law. On this basis, it refused to reduce the penalty imposed on DLF since its fight with the allottees was not a fight among equals. It was observed that ‘we cannot be unmindful of the fact that the individual allottees even if they come from the highest strata of the society, either collectively or individually, were pitted against a mighty company, which brand itself to be the biggest real-estate developer. We also cannot ignore the fact that the DLF was a market leader and it was stressed before us by the counsel appearing for the allottees that the other real estate company have also adopted almost the same language in the agreements, which is used by the Appellant with their allottees.’ The special responsibility becomes even more important when a dominant player is in the position of a gatekeeper and guarding the entry of a market. For instance, in Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC (‘Matrimony case’),20 Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC, Case Nos 07 and 30 of 2021. the CCI observed that since ‘Google is the gateway to the internet for a vast majority of internet users,’ it is obligated to discharge its special responsibility. ‘As Google has the ability and the incentive to abuse its dominant position, its “special responsibility” is critical in ensuring not only the fairness of the online web search and search advertising markets, but also the fairness of all online markets given that these are primarily accessed through search engines.’ In the Umar Javeed v. Google LLC (‘Google Android case’),21 Umar Javeed and Ors. v. Google LLC and Anr., Case No. 39 of 2018, paras 495, 557. the CCI opined that markets should be allowed to compete on merits and the onus is on the dominant players (in the present case, Google) to ensure that its conduct does not impinge this competition on merits. Hence, Google was clothed with a special responsibility to not indulge in the conducts enumerated under section 4(2) of the Competition Act and to be mindful of the exclusionary implications of the restrictions imposed by it on OEMs. Google’s special responsibility as a dominant player obligated it to use more competition-friendly alternatives so as to not take unfair advantage of its position and ensure fairness and reasonableness in its conduct. Further, even in the Play Store case, in the market for UPI apps, the CCI found that Google was applying different technological standards on its own Google Pay UPI app and competing UPI apps. The CCI held that Google, being a dominant player in the app store for Android OS, has a special responsibility to ensure that access to its platform is available on non-discriminatory terms for all trading partners. Simply because the Google Pay UPI app is in-house, it does not grant Google any right to self-preference and offer dissimilar treatment to other competing UPI apps. This discrimination was not explained or justified by Google on any objective and rational basis, such as any technical constraints limiting seamless integration of third-party apps (TPAP). Hence, it had no reason to escape its special responsibility. [C] Collective Dominance Now that we have analysed the two limbs of the definition of a dominant position, it is pertinent to take note that the CCI has observed in the case of Unitech 22 Jagmohan Chhabra v. Unitech Ltd, Case No. 21 of 2011, para. 11. that there can only be one enterprise that is dominant in a relevant market. In the said case, the CCI held that because DLF was already dominant in the relevant market, no other entity could be held to be in a dominant position. Further, the language of section 4 of the Competition Act is very different from Article 102 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU; in the light of which the Courts in Europe have held that there is a collective dominant position that can be exercised by undertakings in Europe). In fact, the CCI has also consistently observed that section 4 also does not permit a collective dominant position,23 Indian Sugar Mills Association v. Indian Jute Mills Association, Case No. 38 of 2011; Brickwork Ratings India Pvt. Ltd v. CRISIL Ltd., Case No. 47 of 2019, para. 33; also take note that in the Competition (Amendment) Bill, 2012 wanted to amend section 4 to include abuse either jointly or singly but this bill was not passed. and there is no concept of ‘collective dominance’ or ‘joint dominance’ in the Competition Act.24 Builders Association of India v. Cement Manufacturers’ Association and Ors., Case No. 29 of 2010, para. 177; Dwarikesh Sugar Industries Limited v. Wave Distilleries & Breweries Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 47 of 2014, para. 47; Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and its affiliated entities and Anr., Case No. 40 of 2019, para. 15; Mr. Arjun v. Vaicom 18 and Ors., Case No. 57 of 2017, para. 10. Therefore, as the law stands today, the CCI will hold that there can neither be more than one enterprise that is dominant in a relevant market nor can there be a collective dominant position. This position was clarified in detail in the Ola case, wherein it was argued that Uber and Ola could both be held dominant simultaneously in the relevant market. The CCI refuted this argument and held that there cannot be more than one dominant enterprise in the relevant market at a particular point in time. This is on the basis of the following factors:25 Ola case, paras 106-116. (i) Provisions of section 4(2) of the Competition Act clearly stipulate that dominant position can be held by only one enterprise or one group. ‘There shall be an abuse of dominant position, if an enterprise or a group –.’ The term ‘a’/‘an’ used evidently states the singular form, which shows that the intention of the legislature was never to hold more than one enterprise to be in a dominant position unless they are part of the group within the meaning of section 5 of the Competition Act. (ii) The explanation (a) to section 4 uses the phrase ‘operate independently’ which clearly shows that the concept of ‘dominance’ is meant to be ascribed to only one entity. The whole essence of section 4 lies in proscribing unilateral conduct exercised by a single entity or group independent of its competitors or consumers. In the presence of more than one dominant entity, none of those entities would be able to act independently of one another. (iii) If there was any scope for more than one entity being envisaged by the Competition Act, factors like ‘size and resources of competitors’, ‘economic power of the enterprise including commercial advantages over competitors’, etc., would not have found place under section 19(4) of the Competition Act. (iv) Section 28 of the Competition Act, which specifically deals with the division of enterprises enjoying dominant position, the usage of the words unambiguously indicates that the Competition Act does not provide for more than one enterprise to be dominant in the relevant market. (v) Clause 4 of the Competition (Amendment) Bill, 2012 (Bill No. 136 of 2012) which lapsed due to the dissolution of Lok Sabha, stated as follows:In section 4 of the principal Act, in sub-section (1), after the words ‘or group’, the words ‘jointly or singly’ shall be inserted. (emphasis supplied) Hence, the aforesaid proposed amendment further reinforces the proposition that there is no scope in the present scheme of the Competition Act, either expressly or by implication, to contemplate the presence of two independent entities as dominant at the same time in the relevant market, else this amendment would not have been required. Further, the fact that collective dominance was not included in subsequent legislative discussions on the Competition Act, i.e., the Draft Competition (Amendment) Bill, 2020 and Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, clearly highlights that legislative intention runs contrary to the inclusion of ‘collective dominance’ in the legal regime. Having said that, in a real-world marketplace, there are several examples where one will notice that in an oligopolistic marketplace, there are two big competitors, and the other players are small players in the market. Some common examples are Pepsi & Coca-Cola, Boeing & Airbus, Visa & Master Card, Nestle & Hindustan Unilever, P&G & Hindustan Unilever, etc. The Competition Act does not deal in a vacuum but in the manner in which the enterprises operate in the market. Thus, the practical realities and impact faced in the market should also be considered under the Competition Act.26 It would also be relevant to note that under section 19(4)(j) the market structure is also a factor that has to be considered while determining dominant position. Therefore, in these markets, it is possible that each of the entities faces competition from one another, and to say that neither would be dominant in a relevant market would not take into account the market dynamics since all the other stakeholders in the market are completely dependent on the said entities. It is very likely that the two enterprises are above the rest of the competitors, and the latter is unable to compete with the two competitors, and their market conduct is dependent on the behaviour of the bigger players. The question of whether two enterprises can be dominant in the same market is pending before the Supreme Court in the Ola case. In any event, the conduct of two enterprises which have market power in the same market is amenable to investigation under section 3(4) of the Competition Act, which deals with vertical arrangements between upstream and downstream players. For instance, investigations are currently pending in the case of All India Vendors Association v. Flipkart 27 All India Vendors Association v. Flipkart, Case No. 20 of 2018. (where Amazon and Flipkart operate in the same market) and National Restaurant Association of India v. Zomato Limited 28 National Restaurant Association of India v. Zomato Limited, Case No. 16 of 2021. (where Swiggy and Zomato operate in the same market). Reference may be made to the discussion on section 3(4) in earlier chapters.

sections.title: EXAMINING POSITION OF STRENGTH BY CONDUCT OF THE ENTERPRISE
sections.text: §3.03 EXAMINING POSITION OF STRENGTH BY CONDUCT OF THE ENTERPRISE The CCI, erstwhile COMPAT and NCLAT have examined the conduct of the enterprise under investigation to analyse whether the enterprise enjoys a position of strength. In the process, the authorities assess whether the enterprise can operate independently of the market forces. The CCI has further observed that the market leader enjoys a unique position in the market; it has the ability to influence the market, and it often lays down the rules of the game. Generally, such market power naturally tends to be exercised by such enterprises to the detriment of the interest of its competitors and consumers. It is pointed out that one of the aspects which are considered in an ex post analysis is whether the conduct itself shows that the enterprise in question is dominant, i.e., could the enterprise have engaged in such conduct had it not been dominant. This logic is a circular fallacy, however, it is a sensible approach in practice because in an ex post conduct analysis, the conduct has already happened. This is unlike merger analysis, where conduct is always analysed ex ante. [A] NSE Case Both the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT have examined the aspect of position of strength in detail in the NSE case, which emanated from the information filed by MCX-SX relating to an allegation of predatory pricing by NSE by using its overwhelmingly dominant position in the market for stock exchange services in India. NSE was providing complete transaction waiver (zero pricing) in the currency derivative segment. It was also alleged that NSE engaged in exclusionary behaviour by providing a substantial reduction in admission and trade-related fee structure in the currency derivative segment which was aimed at: (i) eliminating competition from the currency derivative segment, (ii) discouraging potential entrants from entering the relevant market for stock exchange services, and (iii) achieving the foreclosure of all competition in the market for stock exchange services. NSE was given the regulatory licence by the Indian securities regulator to operate in all segments of the stock exchange, i.e., equity segment, futures and options segment, wholesale debt segment and currency derivative segment. However, MCX-SX had licence to operate only in the currency derivative segment. It was also alleged that NSE’s strategy of providing transaction fee waivers, admission fee and data fee waivers and the low level of deposit requirements were only in its currency derivative segment and were completely at variance with its conduct in other segments. Such conduct of zero pricing followed by the NSE was aimed at eliminating competition from the currency derivative segment. The other competitors in the currency derivative segment, including MCX-SX, had no option but to follow the pricing policy of the NSE and provide transaction waivers and other benefits like low deposit requirements to merely sustain in the market, let alone remain competitive. In providing such repeated waivers, MCX-SX and the other competitors incurred huge financial losses to the extent of leaving the market. We will deal with the findings on dominance and abuse of dominance by the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT later in the chapter. For examining position of strength, both the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT raised the following questions to deliberate on the position of strength enjoyed by NSE.29 NSE case, para. 10.38-10.49. [1] Can NSE Sustain Zero Pricing Policy in the Relevant Market Long Enough to Outlive Effective Competition? The CCI looked at the financial statements of NSE and observed, on the basis of the reserves, surplus and profits after tax on the balance sheet that NSE has the capacity to defer profits from the currency derivate segment since they were earning supra normal profits from the other segments. Further, based on the financial statements of NSE and MCX-SX, the CCI also observed that the long-term risk of possible market failure in the currency derivative segment of NSE was lesser compared to MCX-SX. [2] Does the Policy of NSE to Provide Complete Transaction Waivers Make Any Commercial Sense? Based on the financial strength of NSE and its market conduct in the entire stock exchange market, it was observed by the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT that the policy of NSE to provide such waivers, only in the currency derivative segment, did not make any commercial sense. The only logic for deliberately incurring losses in the currency derivative segment and providing such continuous and repeated waivers for more than two years of operation was to eliminate competition in the currency derivative segment. The CCI noted that the NSE was fully aware of its financial powers and noted that in spite of providing transaction waivers only in the currency derivative segment, it was recording a profit in excess of INR 300 crores. However, MCX-SX which was operating only in the currency derivative segment was incurring huge losses and financial depletion. The CCI observed that it is:unthinkable that a professionally managed modern enterprise can afford such financial complacency in the face of competition unless it is part of a bigger strategy of waiting for the competition to die out. This complacence can only point to awareness of its own strength and the realization that sooner or later, it would be possible to start generating profits from the business, once the competition is sufficiently reduced. [3] Would the NSE Be in a Position to Provide Complete Transaction Waivers Endlessly Had It Not Have Such Financial Muscle? The CCI examined the market conduct and analysed the financial muscle that NSE possessed. It opined that NSE had undeniable advantages over its competitors, and it was in a position to continue with its conduct because of such strengths vis-à-vis competitors. It was this financial strength which allowed the NSE to continue with its conduct and affect the competitors in its favour. Based on the above parameters, the CCI noted that NSE was in a position of strength. Thus, one can see that CCI looks at the conduct of the enterprise under investigation to determine whether they are in a position of strength. It is worthwhile to note that while the Competition Act does provide for indices of dominance, the CCI makes a holistic assessment to find out whether the entity in question is operating independently of the competitive forces. As such, the CCI, in the DLF case, had also made an observation that it can look at the factors outside the relevant market to analyse whether the entity in question is operating independently and, thus, enjoys a position of strength. Thus, there may be situations in which the relevant market is defined as Market X, but the CCI can look into their past behaviour in Market Y and also the financial muscle of the enterprise or its group holistically to conclude that the concerned enterprise enjoys a dominant position.

sections.title: INDICES OF DOMINANCE: ‘ALL OR ANY FACTOR’
sections.text: §3.04 INDICES OF DOMINANCE: ‘ALL OR ANY FACTOR’ As mentioned above, dominance is a position of strength enjoyed by an enterprise/group which enables it to prevent effective competition from being maintained in the relevant market by behaving to an appreciable extent independently of its competitors and consumers. Section 19(4) of the Competition Act provides for certain indices which the CCI can take into account while determining whether the entity, under investigation, enjoys a dominant position or not. The CCI, in its advocacy booklet on abuse of dominance, has noted that ‘It is the ability of the enterprise to behave/act independently of the market forces that determines its dominant position. In a perfectly competitive market no enterprise has control over the market, especially in the determination of price of the product. However, perfect market conditions are more of an economic “ideal” than reality. Keeping this in view, the Act specifies a number of factors that should be taken into account while determining whether an enterprise is dominant or not’. It is to be noted that in conducting a dominance analysis, it is relevant to consider the market position of the alleged dominant enterprise, market position of the competitors, barriers to entry and expansion, regulatory norms and buyer power. The existence of dominant position may be derived from several factors which, taken separately, may not necessarily be determinative. While the Competition Act provides for the indices for determining dominant position under section 19(4), these are not exhaustive, and the CCI makes a holistic assessment to understand whether the entity under investigation enjoys a position of strength unique to its own facts and market under consideration.30 Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Case Nos 6 & 74 of 2015, para. 71. As mentioned above, it can look at market situations in different relevant markets too, to determine whether the entity under investigation enjoys dominant position.31 DLF case, para. 12.61. The erstwhile COMPAT has also observed that there is no tick-the-box approach while determining whether an entity enjoys a dominant position. Further, it must also be noted that in rapidly changing markets such as digital markets and platform markets, market assessment cannot have a static approach, and hence, competitive assessments are based on market realities as they exist at the time of assessment.32 Lifestyle Equities C.V. and Anr. v. Amazon Seller Services Private Limited and Anr., Case No. 09 of 2020, para. 27. The Supreme Court has also observed that section 19(4) empowers the CCI to have regard to ‘all’ or ‘any’ of the factors to arrive at the finding of whether or not an enterprise enjoys a dominant position. Hence, even a single factor being ‘any’ factor may form the foundation to find whether an enterprise enjoys dominance.33 Coal India Limited and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Anr. [2023], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 2845 of 2017, para. 86. No priority is given to any factor enlisted under section 19(4) of the Competition Act while determining dominance. Now, we look at each factor mentioned in the said section for determining dominance. [A] Market Share The analysis of the market position of the allegedly dominant enterprise vis-à-vis its competitors provides insight into the degree of actual competition prevalent in the relevant market. As a rule of thumb, as a first step, the CCI analyses the market share of the enterprise under investigation to determine whether it has a dominant position or not. In this regard, the CCI has relied on the jurisprudence of the EU in the Automobile Spare Parts case and has opined that market share provides a useful first indication of the market structure and of the relevant importance of the other competitors active in the market.34 Reliance was placed on Hilti v. Commission [1991] ECR II-1439, paras 90, 91 and 92; Case T-340/03 France Télécom v. Commission [2007] ECR II-107, para. 100. In the event the enterprise under investigation has a high market share compared to other competitors present in the relevant market, it will be noted by the CCI as an indication of dominance, provided that such market share has been held for some length of time.35 Informant (Confidential) v. Grasim Industries Limited, Case Nos 51, 54 & 56 of 2017; JSW Paints Private Limited v. Asian Paints Limited, Case Nos 36 of 2019 & 17 of 2021, para. 48. Hence, for a firm to be dominant, it should be able to sustain its market share for a reasonable period of time. While there is no arithmetic guidance provided as to the minimum level of market share which an enterprise must command to determine whether an entity is in a dominant position or not, it has been generally seen that a market share of more than 50% gives a guidance that the entity is dominant.36 Automotive Tyres Manufacturers Association v. General Insurance Corporation of India, Case No. 21 of 2020, para. 15; Indian National Shipowners’ Association v. Oil and Natural Gas Corporation Limited, Case No. 01 of 2018, para. 122. It is also pointed out that uniform market share thresholds and a standard time period to assess the durability of market share cannot be applied in the same manner to all sectors due to variance across industries.37 Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Case Nos 6 & 74 of 2015, para. 84. The CCI and the DG have generally relied on industry reports, consumer surveys, internal records of companies and other reports to determine the market share of the players in the relevant market. For calculating market share, the CCI looks into both volume and value sales.38 DLF Limited v. Competition Commission of India, [2014] Comp AT 12. In the Google Android case, Google was found to command a stable, consistent and significant market share of more than 90% based on the investigation of the DG in each of the following markets:(i) market for licensable OS for smart mobile devices in India;39 Google Android case, paras 95-104.3. (ii) market for app stores for Android smart mobile OS;40 Ibid., paras 156-162. (iii) market for general web search in India;41 Ibid., paras 222-229. (iv) market for non-OS specific web browsers in India;42 Ibid., paras 284-289. (v) market for online video hosting platforms in India.43 Ibid., paras 308-316. In the Play Store case also, market share was the first factor for determining dominance, and the CCI reaffirmed Google’s overwhelmingly high market share of more than 95% in the market for licensable OS for smart mobile devices in India and market for app stores for android OS (as discussed above). [1] Market Share Is Not the Sole Criterion for Determining Dominance The primary reason for calculating market share is to determine the real market position of the entity under investigation. While the CCI has always analysed the market share in a dominance investigation, it has observed in numerous decisions (like the NSE case and the DLF case) that it looks into other factors under section 19(4) of the Competition Act and does not limit itself only to market shares to determine whether the entity enjoys a position of strength. Even in the case of Ramakant Kini v. Dr L H Hiranandani Hospital, Powai, 44 Ramakant Kini v. Dr L H Hiranandani Hospital, Powai, Case No. 39 of 2012. the CCI, while assessing the dominance of the Hiranandani hospital in the relevant market for provision of maternity services by super speciality and high-end hospitals within a distance of 12 km from the Hiranandani Hospital, held that the market share of an entity is ‘only one of the factors that decides whether an enterprise is dominant or not, but that factor alone cannot be decisive proof of dominance’. This position of the CCI has been reiterated in a slew of other cases as well.45 Biocon Limited and Anr. v. F. Hoffmann-La Roche AG and Ors. Case No. 68 of 2016, para. 54; Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Case Nos 6 & 74 of 2015, para. 104; Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and another v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. and others with Rubtub Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. and others, Case 14 of 2019 & 01 of 2020, para. 229; Indian National Shipowners’ Association v. Oil and Natural Gas Corporation Limited, Case No. 01 of 2018, para. 123. While the CCI has noted that market share provides a first indicator of the market success of the concerned enterprise in comparison to its competitors, it is not the sole criterion for determining dominance. In the Automobile Spare Parts case,46 Automobile Spare Parts case, para. 20.5.65-20.5.68. the CCI has noted that market shares are the initial indications which are to be put in perspective in light of the other factors mentioned under section 19(4) to make an overall assessment of the market power of the firm under investigation. Even in the DLF case, 47 DLF case, para. 12.54. the CCI has noted that the provisions of section 19(4) make it evident that market share need not necessarily be the single predominant factor, and often, a host of other factors have to be considered. It is pointed out that if sufficient and undisputed data is not available to determine market share in a credible manner, it becomes even more important to draw on other corroborating data and analyse the other factors in greater depth to off-set the difficulties in working out specified market shares on account of data constraints, and/or to complement the market share when margins between competitors are not wide enough in determining the strength of the enterprise in terms of affecting market forces as set out in the Explanation (a) to section 4. Further, it is to be noted that high market share (of say more than 50%) assumes greater dominance in a market which has a low level of concentration than in a relatively concentrated but equally divided market. In this regard, it is apt to point out the observation of the Report of the High Level Committee on Competition Law, 2000, which noted that:But then, this ambiguity has a justification having regard to the fact that even a firm with a low market share of just 20% with the remaining 80% diffusedly held by a large number of competitors may be in a position to abuse its dominance, while a firm with say 60% market share with the remaining 40% held by a competitor may not be in a position to abuse its dominance because of the key rivalry in the market. [emphasis supplied] This was seen in the Ola case,48 Ola case, para. 84. wherein it was held that a market share of more than 50% could not lead to a presumption of dominance, especially when the scheme of the Competition Act does not specify any numerical threshold. Competition and competition law is not about counting the number of firms in a particular relevant market to determine whether or not that market is competitive. Every market is unique, with a unique number of players that are determined organically by competitive forces. In appeal, the NCLAT noted that there was a significant rising trend in the assessment period, whereafter Ola’s market share plateaued or witnessed a gradual decline. Hence, there can be markets which may not be competitive even with a large number of players and equally possibly there can be markets which can work perfectly well with fewer players, constraining the conduct of each other (in this case, Uber constrained the conduct of Ola as shall be discussed under abuse). What is significant is that the existing firms are effective enough to constrain the behaviour of one another so as to dissuade independent abusive conduct by any of them.49 Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. Uber India Systems Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 96 of 2015. [B] Strength of the Enterprise Vis-à-Vis the Competitors To determine dominance, it is essential for the CCI to analyse whether the enterprise can operate independently and affect the competitors in its favour. For this, the CCI analyses the competitive strengths of the enterprise under investigation vis-à-vis the other competitors. The CCI, in the DLF case, has clearly mentioned that it is critical to evaluate the relative position of strength in terms of the size and resources of the enterprise vis-à-vis the competitors in a dominance investigation. CCI also takes into account financial parameters like profit after tax, net income, sales, capital employed, etc. The CCI made a very interesting observation in the DLF case50 DLF case, para. 12.60. that the relative strength of the dominant enterprise vis-à-vis other competitors need not be confined only to the relevant market as that would defeat the very purpose of the parameters under sections 19(4)(a), (b) and (c) of the Competition Act, limiting the analysis to a specific product or market alone. Rather, the overall size and resources of the enterprise and competitors in question can also be considered to arrive at a comparative position of strength. In the DLF case, while the market was defined to be limited to Gurgaon, the CCI actually looked into the financial reserves of the entire DLF group as a whole. In appeal, the erstwhile COMPAT also held that the size and resources of DLF, its capital employed, and its total business were nowhere comparable to its competitors. It also observed that while comparing the size and strength of the companies, inter se, all the factors have to be taken into consideration as to whether the size and resources of a particular company make it dominant or not.51 DLF Limited v. Competition Commission of India, [2014] Comp AT 12, para. 93. The CCI also discussed in detail the issue of competitive strengths of the enterprise vis-à-vis its competitors in the case of HT Media Ltd v. Super Cassettes Ltd 52 HT Media Ltd v. Super Cassettes Ltd, Case No. 40 of 2011. (‘Super Cassettes case’). The Super Cassettes case was initiated due to information filed by HT Media Ltd which ran an FM radio channel, alleging that Super Cassettes had abused their dominant position by: (a) charging excessive amount as licence fees/royalty in licensing its copyright of ‘Bollywood’ movie songs; (b) imposing a minimum commitment charge irrespective of actual needle hour; and (c) making conclusion of licensing arrangement subject to acceptance of excessive licence fees and minimum commitment charge. While determining the issue of whether Super Cassettes were dominant in the relevant market, the CCI took note of the industry in question and made the following comparisons between Super Cassettes and other competitors to determine the competitive strength of Super Cassettes:53 Ibid., paras 179-180. (1) income of Super Cassettes vis-à-vis its competitors; (2) purchase of rights by Super Cassettes (forty-eight Bollywood films in 2010 and 2011) compared to its closest competitors (like Sony) which could not even purchase more than ten to eleven films per year; (3) purchase of rights by Super Cassettes of all movies which had bankable stars, i.e., top Bollywood stars. In this regard, the CCI observed that Super Cassettes has purchased a number of films with ‘bankable’ stars, and while the purchase of music rights of films which have ‘bankable’ stars does not guarantee that the music is a hit, the presence of ‘bankable’ star is likely to generate much more interest in such movies than a normal film without superstars, and therefore, the likelihood of its success is more than a film which has lesser known actors. Therefore, the CCI observed that the superior financial strength in the market coupled with superior resources, as in this case, is an important indicator of dominance of an enterprise. Keeping these factors in mind, the CCI noted that Super Cassettes had a superior market position vis-à-vis its competitors. However, the strength of an enterprise cannot always lead to a presumption of dominance and, instead, may be attributed to competitive efficiency and innovation. For instance, in the Ola case, the informant suggested relying on the ability of Ola to engage in predatory pricing as a factor of its dominance. In this case, Ola and Uber were aggressively competing by engaging in below-cost pricing (discussed in more detail in the abuse part). The CCI held that most firms, dominant or not, can engage in practices, such as exclusive dealing, below-cost pricing, loyalty discounts, etc., especially new entrants, so as to gain a toehold in the market. Hence, holding them dominant on this basis can have a chilling effect on competition as an entrant armed with a new idea, a superior product or a technological solution that challenges the status quo in a market and shifts a large consumer base in its favour would have to be erroneously held dominant. This brings us to the point that market power is a matter of degree. In most markets, every enterprise will have some degree of market power, by virtue of which they can affect consumers or competitors in its favour to some extent. The key consideration is to determine dominance. The NCLAT54 Meru Travels Solutions Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2017], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 19 of 2017, paras 21-24. elaborated on this and noted that the end-to-end provision of service from booking to alighting from the taxi with tracking system of the taxi – all provide a high degree of reliability and assurance of availability of taxis anywhere, including security to riders and enough demand to drivers. This is a new way of doing business and Ola could translate this concept into reality by employing technology in a more effective and enabling fashion to provide services which previous radio taxi operators were not able to. Before the advent of technology-leveraged, network-based aggregator models, taxi services such as Meru, Fast Track, Easy Cab, Taxi For Sure, etc., also leveraged technology to provide ease of booking and taxi rides, yet Ola’s model differed from them. Also, Ola offered customer discounts and drivers’ incentives in conjunction with these facilities and conveniences to attract riders and create and reinforce a brand image over a period of time. This helped Ola to grab a big chunk of the market. Further, Uber’s presence in the market around the same time provided competition to Ola, and it had to calibrate and change its market strategy, including pricing, to respond to Uber’s competition. Thus, in effect, the NCLAT held that innovation should not be stifled. It is an acknowledgement of the fact that robust market players can also emerge as a result of pro-competitive and legitimate business practices by offering superior products and adopting innovative and disruptive technologies through investment in research, development and infrastructure. Hence, competitive efficiency and innovation do not always lead to a presumption of dominance but can be an indicator of healthy competition in the market. Conversely, in the Play Store case55 Play Store case, paras 103-1110. and Google Android case,56 Google Android case, paras 75-84. Google argued that it faced competitive constraints from Apple’s iOS, which itself had access to significant resources equivalent to Google’s scale. However, the CCI rejected this on the grounds that app stores are OS specific, and an OEM cannot change the app store once the device has been manufactured with a specific OS. Hence, the CCI held that Apple (iOS) is not substitutable with Google (Android). Similarly, app developers making apps for iOS cannot automatically port them to Android as they are based on different source codes, and users cannot download an app from the Google Play Store for their iOS devices. Android and iOS cater to a distinct set of customers and are complementary rather than substitutable distribution channels. Hence, Apple’s App Store is not an option at all and thus cannot be considered a substitute. This brings us to the point that in considering the strength of competitors, it is first necessary that they should belong in the same relevant market and offer substitutable goods or services, as opposed to being a complementary distribution channel. Non-substitutable products or services cannot pose effective constraints. [C] Economic Power of the Enterprise Including Commercial Advantages over Competitors In the DLF case,57 DLF case, para. 12.69-12.76. the CCI created a lucid picture of the comparative strengths of DLF as opposed to its nearest competitors. It noted that DLF had started its business way back in 1946, and apart from the residential sector, it had leading positions in commercial, retail and office space sectors as well, which was clear from its Red Hearing Prospectus. In fact, DLF started bookings in its projects despite not having the necessary clearances. This itself indicated the position of strength enjoyed by DLF in the eyes of consumers since they bought the properties even without any clearances. Hence, DLF was held to be dominant on the basis of its profile, presence and achievements over the years, as well as its conduct, particularly in relation to consumers, over a period of time. This detailed treatment was also upheld by the erstwhile COMPAT in appeal. [1] Network Effects Network effects refer to a phenomenon where the value of a platform increases as more people use it. For instance, a social media platform will become more valuable to users with an increase in the number of users they can connect with. Generally, the strength of the network varies with the number of users; i.e., more users make the network more valuable and this keeps reinforcing the strength of the network once it reaches a critical mass of users. In two-sided markets, network effects may enable a large platform/network to become dominant and insulate itself from potential competition, as entrants may find it difficult to challenge the large incumbent. The strength of network effects thus becomes a key factor in the determination of dominance in such markets.58 Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Case Nos 6 & 74 of 2015, paras 90-92; Vijay Gopal v. Big Tree Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 46 of 2021, para. 31. Network effect in turn ensures that customers do not switch to other platforms easily unless there is a new competitor entering the market with an altogether disruptive technology.59 Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc. and Facebook Inc., Case No. 15 of 2020, para. 84. In CCI’s decisional practice, it has observed that digital markets such as online search,60 Umar Javeed and Ors. v. Google LLC and Anr., Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 252. digital advertising,61 Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc and Ors., Case No. 41 of 2021, paras 19, 291. e-commerce,62 All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Private Limited and Anr., Case No. 20 of 2018, para. 28. etc., are characterised by network effects which operate as entry barriers as new players are unable to match the scale of operations and users enjoyed by the incumbent platform, which reinforces its dominant position. This has been discussed in more detail in Chapter 6 on digital markets. In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users (‘WhatsApp case’),63 In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users, Suo Moto Case No. 01 of 2021. the CCI, in their order directing investigation, found WhatsApp to be dominant in the relevant market for over the top (OTT) messaging apps through smartphones in India, inter alia, on the basis that WhatsApp’s pronounced network effects make it difficult for the users to switch apps and force users to stay locked into its network:In India, the network effects have indubitably set in for WhatsApp, which undergird its position of strength and limit its substitutability with other functionally similar apps/platforms. This, in turn, causes a strong lock-in effect for users, switching to another platform for whom gets difficult and meaningless until all or most of their social contacts also switch to the same other platform. Users wishing to switch would have to convince their contacts to switch and these contacts would have to persuade their other contacts to switch. Thus, while it may be technically feasible to switch, the pronounced network effects of WhatsApp significantly circumscribe the usefulness of the same. In the Play Store case,64 Play Store case, para. 102. the CCI observed that OS exhibits indirect network effects; i.e., the benefit to users of an OS increases with the volume and quality of native apps they can access on that OS, and similarly, the benefit to app developers increases with the number of users they can access on an OS. This is likely to act as a barrier to new entry and expansion as it creates a ‘chicken and egg’ problem, i.e., an OS needs a critical mass of users to attract app developers but also needs a critical mass of app developers to attract users. In the Google Android case, CCI explained this proposition of network effects with the following figure:65 Google Android case, paras 111,112. Thus, more app developers creating apps for an OS increases the value of the OS in the eyes of the users, leading to an increase in the number of users, which means more devices based on the said OS are sold. This is a positive feedback loop which feeds into itself thereby increasing the value of the Android ecosystem at large. In Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and another v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. and others (‘MMT case’),66 Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and another v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. and others with Rubtub Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. and others, Case 14 of 2019 & 01 of 2020, para. 226. the CCI held that positive indirect network effects operate in the market in that both hotels and consumers positively value the presence of more users on the other side of the platform, as it creates higher visibility and potential sales (for hotels) and wider variety and options (for consumers). However, it is not always the case that network effects are seen as an impregnable barrier to entry to expansion. For example, in the Ola case,67 Ola case, paras 90-93. it was found that both Ola and Uber were aggressively competing with each other to attract participants, i.e., drivers and riders, on both sides of their platforms. However, despite Ola having the largest network, the network effect was not strong enough to deter entry and rapid expansion of Uber. Thus, the consideration of whether network effects act as a barrier to entry and expansion in the market requires a case-by-case analysis. While network effects undoubtedly play a significant role in digital markets (as shall be discussed in detail in Chapter 6), it is essential to assess the extent of such barriers to fully understand their implications on the market. This requires an analysis of the unique characteristics of each market and the strategies employed by the market participants. [D] Consumer Dependence The dependence of consumers on an enterprise also strengthens its position. One of the important aspects to note herein is the identification of the consumers in a particular case. In Biocon Limited v. F. Hoffman-La Roche AG 68 Biocon Limited v. F. Hoffman-La Roche AG, Case No. 68 of 2016, para. 56. (‘Roche case’), the CCI ordered an investigation into Roche for alleged anti-competitive conduct with respect to its biological cancer drug, Trastuzumab. The issues of CCI’s jurisdiction to decide competition issues arising out of patents have been discussed in Chapter 5 on IPR. In assessing Roche’s dominance, the CCI observed that the introduction of Trastuzumab gave its innovator Roche, a significant edge over its competitors. Further, existing patients who were already undergoing treatment based on the reference biological drug did not switch to its substitutes, as the doctors might not have prescribed an alternative medicine during ongoing treatment, especially considering the nature of the disease. Hence, consumer dependence on Trastuzumab increased Roche’s position of strength. In the MMT case,69 MMT case, para. 231. the CCI, while delineating the relevant product market, highlighted the non-substitutability of MMT with other forms of booking options which creates dependence of hotels on online travel aggregators (OTAs) like MMT. Given that MakeMyTrip, Goibibo (‘MMT-Go’) was the biggest OTA, having the highest market share during the period under investigation, the dependence of the hotel side on MMT-Go for their survival and growth, was evident. In the Google Android case,70 Google Android case, paras 25-49. theoretically, Google claimed to make the source code of Android available for free via the Android Open-Source Project and under an open-source licence known as ‘Apache licence’. However, in reality, this was not a substitute for Google Android because unlike the open-source licence, each version of Android introduced new APIs, bug fixes and security fixes that brought new features to the OEMs. This was perpetuated through Google’s application programming interface (APIs) which were contingent on OEMs signing Google’s agreements. Further, even access to critical and ‘must have’ apps like the Google Play Store (which was the largest app store across all mobile OSs in India) was dependent on signing these agreements. Without access to Google Play Services, a smart device would not be commercially viable or at least the OEM offering these devices would be competitively disadvantaged. Further, OEMs were also dependent on Google because it owns the IPRs related to Android OS, and even the ‘Android’ name and the Android logo are property of Google and not part of the assets available through the Android Open-Source Project. This conduct along with the abuse emanating out of the same has been discussed in Chapter 6 on digital markets. The CCI on the basis of the investigation report of the DG noted that though Android OS theoretically is an open-source project, it was actually controlled by Google. It found that in order to have access to the proprietary apps of Google, including Play Store, Play Services, Google Search, etc., as well as the trademarks related to Android, OEMs have to sign multiple agreements with Google such as Mobile Application Distribution Agreement (MADA), Anti-fragmentation Agreement (AFA), Android Compatibility Commitment Agreement (ACC) and Revenue Sharing Agreement (RSA). The cumulative effect of these agreements has been described in the following flowchart: Hence, due to the compatibility obligations of OEMs following these agreements, they were restrained from manufacturing and selling devices incorporating the forked version71 an operating system that is a modified, competing version of Android OS based on the Android source code. of Android OS. Therefore, any OEM who wants to offer these ‘must have’ services to its customers must sign and accept the restrictions laid down in MADA, AFA/ACC and, thereafter, RSAs to maximize the revenue. Hence, competing general search service providers were not in a position to nullify the competitive edge that Google secured for itself through pre-installation as well as premium placement under MADA. This meant that Google had manufactured unyielding consumer dependence in its favour. The anti-competitive effects of these agreements, as has been elaborated by the CCI, have been discussed under abuse. Herein, it is pointed out that in appeal, Google argued before NCLAT that all three agreements (MADA, AFA/ACC, RSA) should be separately looked into. However, the NCLAT rejected this on the premise that when all three agreements are signed by an OEM, its consequence has to be conjointly looked into. This implies that when the subject of all three agreements relate to Android open source and are interrelated, they have to be read conjointly to examine their cumulative effect to examine the dependence of consumers and the extent of network effects as a barrier to entry. [E] Entry Barriers The CCI in its decisional practice of abuse of dominance, has always looked into entry barriers while determining whether an entity is dominant or not. It has been noted that low barriers to entry or expansion by actual or potential competitors can deter a company from raising prices if expansion or entry would be likely, timely and sufficient. Having said that, expansion or entry must be immediate and of sufficient scope and magnitude to constitute an efficient, competitive constraint on the enterprise concerned. Barriers to entry can include not only legal barriers but also advantages peculiar to the dominant company. They include all those behaviour patterns which a new entrant will have to follow in order to be sustainable in the market. Such patterns may be a result of the dominant incumbent. The CCI also considers the behaviour of the dominant undertaking, which may in turn become a market barrier for other enterprises in the market. For example, in the NSE case, NSE started providing transaction waivers only in the currency derivative market. In such a situation where the rules of the game are set by the dominant incumbent, a new player has no option but to match the behaviour of the dominant enterprise, in this case, which was providing transaction waivers to start with to enter the said market, leave alone being competitive. Entry analysis provides information on the significance of potential competitors for competition in the market concerned and thus about market power and its durability – to the extent such power is found. The relevant question to be asked here is whether entry or expansion will pose a credible competitive constraint on the incumbent. The CCI analyses whether the potential entrant, if any, will dilute the strength of the dominant incumbent. If the potential entry does not dilute the strength, then entry barriers are too high in the market, and that entry shall not be swift to constrain the behaviour of the dominant enterprise.
