To determine whether a firm’s market power is durable, the CCI takes into account whether the potential entrant is sufficient to preclude the exercise of market power.72 Amit Mittal v. DLF Limited, Case No. 73 of 2014, para. 31. Expansion or entry must also be sufficient to defeat the exercise of market power, i.e., it must be large enough, and of a character, to constrain the behaviour of a firm alleged to be dominant or in possession of substantial market power to a sufficient extent. Further, the entry must be swift and within a short time frame to pose an effective competitive constraint on the dominant incumbent. [1] Legal Barriers to Entry Barriers to entry include legal barriers to entry (like applicable governmental rules and regulations on the number of players in the market or any entry fees). For example, laws/governmental regulations which limit the number of market participants (like the Adani case discussed below), such as by granting special or exclusive rights in the shape of concessions, licences or IPR. Legislative measures that grant a single enterprise, including public bodies, the exclusive right to perform a certain activity exclude rivals and may lead to such an enterprise having a statutory monopoly in a relevant market.73 Central Organisation for Railway Electrification v. M/s PPS International, Ref. Case No. 05 of 2018, para. 14; Confederation of Indian Alcoholic Beverage Companies and Anr. v. Kerala State Beverages (Manufacturing and Marketing) Corporation Ltd., Case No. 10 of 2021, para. 58; Confederation of Real Estate Developers Association of India v. Greater Noida Industrial Development Authority and Confederation of Real Estate Developers Association of India v. New Okhla Industrial Development Authority and Supertech Limited v. Greater Noida Industrial Development Authority, Case Nos 34, 37 & 38 of 2020, para. 49; Department of Sports v. Athletics Federation of India, Ref. Case No. 01 of 2015, paras 42-44; Dhanraj Pillay and Others v. M/s Hockey India, Case No. 73 of 2011, para. 10.10.2; Hemant Sharma and Ors. v. All India Chess Federation, Case No. 79 of 2011, paras 41-44; HPCL-Mittal Pipelines Limited v. Gujarat Energy Transmission Corporation Limited and Ors., Case No. 39 of 2017, para. 61; Mr. Shravan Yadav and Ors. v. Volleyball Federation of India and Anr., Case No. 1 of 2019, para. 30; Starlight Bruchem Ltd. v. Flora and Fauna Housing & Land, Case No. 53 of 2017, para. 97. Further, regulatory approvals that may be required to be obtained for the development, manufacturing/import and marketing of a product also constitute a legal barrier to entry, such as in the pharmaceutical sector where significant regulatory approvals are required for releasing a drug in the market.74 Biocon Limited and Anr. v. F. Hoffmann-La Roche AG and Ors., Case No. 68 of 2016, para. 57. [2] Strategic Barriers to Entry As mentioned above, barriers to entry include not only legal barriers but also strategic barriers which the potential entrant has to overcome to provide an effective competitive constraint on the behaviour of the dominant incumbent. Such strategic barriers will include barriers like high sunk costs, time, expertise, resources, advertising and promotional expenses, distribution and supply network, vertical integration, innovation, research and development, and inflow of huge capital which confers a competitive advantage on the dominant incumbent, which makes it difficult for the other enterprises to enter the market and compete effectively with the alleged dominant enterprise.75 Biocon Limited and Anr. v. F. Hoffmann-La Roche AG and Ors., Case No. 68 of 2016, para. 57; Department of Agriculture, Cooperation & Farmers v. M/s Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited and M/s Nuziveedu Seeds Limited (NSL) v. Mahyco Monsanto Biotech (India) Limited (MMBL) & Ors, Reference Case No. 2 of 2015 & Case No. 107 of 2015, para. 38; East India Petroleum Pvt. Ltd. v. South Asia LPG Company Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 76 of 2011, para. 46. The CCI analysed the issue of such barriers in the Automobile Spare Parts case and the Super Cassettes case. The Automobile Spare Parts case76 Automobile Spare Parts case, para. 20.5.66, 20.5.67. revolved around the issue of non-availability of spare parts in the aftermarket. The CCI analysed the market and noted that the technical compatibility of a consumable secondary product (aftermarket, i.e., spare parts) is intrinsically linked to the durable primary product (the car). The owner of the primary product is locked in and has to necessarily use the spare parts/diagnostic tools compatible with the car. Such technical compatibility requirement is itself an entry barrier for other competitors to enter the relevant market for aftermarket since they would need to procure the relevant parts which are technically compatible with the primary product to service the consumer. Further, the CCI also noted that by way of agreements entered into with suppliers, the OEMs (i.e., manufacturers of the primary products) have put in place restrictions to ensure that independent repairers do not have access to such spare parts. The independent repairers require spare parts and diagnostic tools to effectively compete with the authorized dealers in the aftermarket of repairs and maintenance for each brand of cars manufactured by the OEMs. By virtue of a network of agreements, it was observed in the market that suppliers of spare parts were prohibited by OEMs from supplying the spare parts in the open market which can then be readily procured by independent repairers or players who want to enter into the market of repair and maintenance. However, in the given market situation, independent repairers and new entrants will have to rely on authorized dealers for spare parts and diagnostic tools. In sum and substance, the CCI noted that by virtue of such agreements, the entire market across the value chain was locked, which in turn, led to consumer loss in terms of increased prices. Even in the Super Cassettes case, the CCI noted there are significant strategic barriers to entry in the market.77 Super Cassettes case, paras 183, 184. It was noted that to be successful in the business of licensing music, music rights have to be purchased (the purchase price can go up to INR 10 crores), and massive investments, in the form of sunk costs and acquisition costs, are required for promotion of the music and, to establish an efficient distribution network. Further, a new player, to be an efficient competitor to the dominant incumbent, has to build a repertoire of music which takes a considerable amount of time and requires significant investments. The CCI further analysed that, for a new entrant to act as an effective competitive constraint on the behaviour of the dominant enterprise, such entry must be done within a quick time span. Based on the above, the CCI, in the Super Cassettes case, noted that there are high barriers to entry. In the Matrimony case,78 Matrimony case, para. 122. the CCI observed that Google has an insurmountable scale advantage and given that only market participants in the online general web search market can compete in the search advertising market, the barriers in the one market also effectively restrict entry into the second market. In the Play Store case,79 Play Store case, paras 92-102. the CCI found that there were significant barriers to entry in the form of financial and human capital and investment in research and development, along with overcoming the problem of ensuring wider acceptability of the new OS among the OEMs (since commercial success of an OS depends on the willingness of OEMs to sell devices based on that OS), app developers and specially users. Google’s restrictions on the use of Android due to its agreements with OEMs and interoperability also foreclose the entry of new entrants as they give financial incentives for pre-installation of certain Google apps and, thus, work as significant barriers to entry in the relevant market. The fact that no new OS developer has been able to enter the market of licensable OS in the last five years and existing players like Windows Phone OS have left the market was also taken as evidence of the existence of entry barriers. These same barriers exist in the context of app stores as well, which require significant investment for development, marketing and updates. In addition, prominent placement given to Google’s app results in a status-quo bias of consumers. Further, many of the pre-installed apps could also not be deleted due to the operation of the agreements entered into between Google and OEMs. This, in the view of the CCI, also operated as an entry barrier. In the Google Android case,80 Google Android case, paras 534-536. the lack of availability of compatible application programming interfaces (‘APIs’ are a software layer working in the background of Android that is used to update Google apps and other apps from the Google Play Store) operated as an entry barrier. This is because without this access, OEMs could not update Google services, fix bugs, etc., and if they operated without access to Google’s proprietary apps, they would be at a competitive disadvantage as the most popular Google services like Google Maps, Google Search, etc., rely on these interfaces to function. The ‘forked’ OS based on the open-source licence-based OSs do not stand any chance before Google and, thus, are not in a position to constrain Google in any manner as they lack the whole gamut of functionalities offered by Google. [F] Countervailing Buyer Power One of the factors mentioned under section 19(4) of the Competition Act is the dependence of consumers on the enterprise. Buyer power is an essential factor to consider in case of dominance investigations. A firm with a high market share may not be able to raise price or wield market power if the counterparty is a large and effective buyer. Buyer power has been identified as the ability of the buyer to influence the terms and conditions on which it purchases goods. The buyer can switch to competing suppliers, promote new entry or vertically integrate into the upstream market, thus deterring a firm with a high market share from specifying exploitative conditions. The key factors to be taken into account while determining buyer power are the size and resources of the buyer, switching costs and the presence of an effective substitute.81 Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc. and Facebook Inc., Case No. 15 of 2020, para. 87. As elaborated earlier (while deliberating on entry barriers), the CCI had noted, in the Automobile Spare Parts case, that there is limited interchangeability of spare parts between the automobiles manufactured by various OEMs because of differences in technical features between cars manufactured by each OEM. In light of the fact that there is no technical compatibility between spare parts manufactured by each OEM, each consumer of an OEM is completely dependent upon the concerned OEM. Further, the OEMs, through a network of contracts with their suppliers, have ensured that they are the sole supplier of the spare parts and diagnostic tools used to repair their brand of automobiles in the aftermarket. Therefore, the independent repairers, who are consumers of the OEMs in the aftermarket for spare parts and diagnostic tools, are also solely dependent upon such enterprises. Similarly, in the DLF case,82 DLF case, para. 12.75. the CCI noted that the consumers have very little or no countervailing buying power. The CCI further noted that there are very few developers/builders of luxury, high-end apartments and thousands of prospective buyers. Such market conditions would ensure that consumers will be the price takers and would have no option but to accept any conditions that are imposed upon them by the developer. In such a market, any seller in a position of strength would have no need to sacrifice its producer’s surplus to meet competition. In fact, the market followers would find it advantageous to adopt similar price and non-price strategies as the market leader since in such a market, the consumer does not possess much market power. Also, in the Super Cassettes case, the CCI noted that there is no effective buyer in the said market, keeping in mind the following factors:(1) Based on the available statistics of Top 100 and Top 20 songs/music broadcast on radio, Super Cassettes owned a majority of the music labels, and it has a 58% share of the top 100 songs played on private FM channels. Apart from market share, the CCI noted the vast repertoire of Super Cassettes, which comprised Bollywood music, is extremely popular with listeners and consequently popular with advertisers. Due to such popular content, Super Cassettes commanded a position of strength. Given such market dynamics, the CCI noted that the customers of Super Cassettes are heavily dependent on the music content of Super Cassettes. (2) Further, as discussed above, the CCI noted that Super Cassettes imposed a minimum commitment charge ranging from 30%–50% of play out which radio stations are required to pay irrespective of whether they play that amount of music on the FM stations or not. Apart from Super Cassettes, no other music provider imposed a charge like the minimum commitment charge. Further, the evidence on record suggested that the radio stations had shown a strong resistance initially to the imposition of minimum commitment charge by Super Cassettes. In spite of such resistance, Super Cassettes continued to impose the same on the radio operators. As such, one of the radio stations, Radio City, terminated its contract with Super Cassettes due to the imposition of such unfair conditions as the minimum commitment charge. After lapse of sometime post the termination, it entered into a fresh contract with Super Cassettes having the same earlier clause of imposition of minimum commitment charge, which incidentally was the reason why the earlier contract was terminated. The CCI noted the statements made by Radio City to the DG, during the investigation process, and observed that the business of Radio City was hampered post the termination of their agreement with Super Cassettes. Radio City was not able to broadcast a huge repertoire of music owned by Super Cassettes. Further, during that relevant period when Radio City was not broadcasting the music of Super Cassettes, the other radio stations were performing considerably better than Radio City in terms of revenues as Radio City did not have the licence to play the music of Super Cassettes. Radio City’s response is also evidence of the fact that the radio station could not effectively compete without playing the music of Super Cassettes. Dependence of consumers and disparity in size and resources are crucial factors in determining the presence or absence of countervailing buyer power.83 Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC, Case Nos 07 and 30 of 2021, para. 124. The case of Kapoor Glass Private Limited v. Schott Glass India Private Limited (‘Schott Glass case’)84 Kapoor Glass Private Limited v. Schott Glass India Private Limited, Case No. 22 of 2010, para. 9.2.22-9.2.26. concerned glass tubes manufactured by Schott Glass which were then sold to the manufacturers of glass ampoules, vials, cartridges and syringes, etc., which were in turn sold to pharmaceutical companies for storing drugs. Thus, the market of borosilicate glass tubes was the upstream market, whereas the market of ampoules, vials, cartridges, syringes, etc., was the downstream market. To vertically integrate its operations with the downstream manufacturing business, Schott Glass entered into a joint venture agreement with a downstream manufacturer Kaisha Manufacturers Pvt Ltd to form Schott Kaisha Private Ltd (Schott Kaisha). The CCI found that all other convertors except Scott Kaisha lacked the requisite size or financial strength to exercise countervailing buying power and were dependent on Schott for their supplies. Hence, the CCI held that Schott was dominant in the upstream market and Schott Kaisha was dominant in the downstream market. It is pointed out that ultimately, the erstwhile COMPAT85 Schott Glass India Pvt. Ltd v. Competition Commission of India, [2014] Comp AT 1, para. 42. held in appeal that the discriminatory treatment met out to other convertors on account of the fact that they did not have the capacity to require the same quantity was justified, and hence, no case of abuse made out on this count. This has been discussed in detail in the abuse part. The CCI in the Play Store case,86 Play Store case, paras 103-113. on the basis of the investigation report of the DG, found that OEMs lack sufficient countervailing buyer power vis-à-vis Google. The large established user base of Android devices, contributing to strong network effects, makes the ecosystem attractive to OEMs and App developers. Given the fact that Android OS enjoys significant consumer demand in India, OEMs are not able to develop/identify alternatives to compete with Android OS. Rather, they have to comply with Google’s agreements. There has been a series of information filed against Coal India,87 For instance, M/s Maharashtra State Power Generation Company Ltd. v. Coal India and Ors., Case Nos 03 of 2012, 11 of 2012 and 59 of 2012; Madhya Pradesh Power Generating Company Limited. v. Coal India Ltd. and Ors., Case Nos 05 of 2013, 07 of 2013 and 44 of 2013; M/s West Bengal Power Development Corporation Ltd. v. M/s Coal India limited & Ors., Case No. 37 of 2013; M/s GHCL Limited v. M/s Coal India Limited & Ors., Case No. 08 of 2014. and CCI has opined on each of the cases. Collectively, the cases are referred here as the Coal India case. In the Coal India case, when Coal India challenged its dominance before the COMPAT, it argued that there existed countervailing buyer power. However, the erstwhile COMPAT negated this argument by elaborating on the lack of substitutes or alternative suppliers. It held that there was no effective countervailing buyer power since there were virtually no alternative suppliers in view of the market structure and regulatory barriers which prevented the buyers from sponsoring new entry or entering the supply market themselves. [G] Statutory Monopoly The Competition Act provides that the CCI can analyse the behaviour of enterprises that have acquired their dominant position because of a statute or a governmental policy/regulation. These are enterprises where dominance is acquired as a result of the policy of the government.88 Beach Mineral Producers Association v. IREL (India) Ltd., Case No. 26 of 2022, paras 46, 47. The inclusion of statutory monopolies within the ambit of competition law can be traced to the High-Level Committee Report which had recommended that except for government entities engaged in sovereign functions, there is no valid justification to exclude any other entity, including government enterprises, from the ambit of the law. Competition law should extend to all consumers, regardless of the purpose for which they purchase goods or services. Hence, State monopolies, government procurement and foreign companies – are all subject to competition law.89 High Level Committee Report, para. 4.8.8. In fact, the CCI has analysed the behaviour of Coal India in a vast number of cases. Coal India is a public sector undertaking which has a monopoly due to a policy of the government. In the Coal India case, it was noted by the CCI that the dominant position of Coal India was acquired as a result of the policy of the Government of India by creating a public sector undertaking in the name of Coal India Limited and vesting the ownership of the private mines in it. Coal India’s dominance was affirmed by the erstwhile COMPAT in an appeal which noted that ‘dominance, even if created statutorily is within the purview of the Act and subject to prohibitory regime under Section 4 …’.90 Coal India Limited, West Bengal v. Competition Commission of India, [2017] CompLR 0447 (CompAT). Further, the Supreme Court in subsequent appeal also observed that ‘a monopoly, undoubtedly, stood created by the Nationalisation Act’ and a monopoly/dominant position, whether acquired as a result of statute/being a government company/public sector undertaking, is a relevant factor91 Coal India Limited and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Anr. [2023], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 2845 of 2017. and that such a statutory monopoly is amenable to the jurisdiction of the CCI. Further, the CCI also noted the conduct of Coal India and held that they are a dominant entity, not only because of their status of a statutory monopoly but also because of their conduct towards their customers. While analysing the market dynamics in the case, the CCI noted that the National Coal Distribution Policy had laid down that the power sector is to be supplied 100% of its normative requirement. In spite of this mandate, Coal India had been able to decide the quantity of coal to be supplied based on its commercial feasibility and entering into one-sided Fuel Supply Agreements and MOUs with its buyers, where Coal India was eligible for incentives even if their supplies were below 100% requirement (as mandated under the National Coal Distribution Policy). Such factors showed that Coal India was dominant. We will deal with the abuse, as discussed by the CCI, erstwhile COMPAT and Supreme Court in the Coal India case, in the later parts of the chapter. Similarly, the issue of a monopoly created because of a government regulation was discussed in the case of Faridabad Industries Association v. Adani Gas Limited 92 Faridabad Industries Association v. Adani Gas Limited, Case No. 71 of 2012. (‘Adani Gas case’). The Adani Gas case was initiated due to an information filed by Faridabad Industries Association. It was alleged that Adani Gas Limited, being the dominant player in the market of supply and distribution of natural gas in the region of Faridabad, had put unconscionable terms and conditions in the Gas Sales Agreement. Such terms made the Gas Sales Agreement totally unilateral, lopsided and heavily tilted in favour of Adani Gas Limited. It was further alleged that Adani Gas Limited, in the garb of executing the said Gas Sales Agreement, had imposed its diktat upon the buyers of natural gas, who are members of Faridabad Industries Association. Further, it was also pointed out that the Gas Sales Agreement had been drafted unilaterally by Adani Gas Limited without leaving any scope for the members of the association, who are buyers of gas and are solely dependent on supplies from Adani Gas Limited. Based on the industry in question, the CCI noted that Adani Gas Limited has a 100% market share since it is the only entity which is authorized under the relevant law and government regulations (the regulations framed under the Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board Act, 2006 gave twenty-five years exclusivity) to set up, expand and operate a city gas distribution system in the city of Faridabad. Subsequently, the NCLAT also supported the CCI’s finding on the dominant position of Adani Gas Limited.93 M/s Adani Gas Limited v. Competition Commission of India and Anr [2020], TA (AT) (Competition) No. 33 of 2017. It reiterated that all the buyers of natural gas were solely dependent upon supplies of natural gas by Adani Gas Limited, which was the only supplier in the market. The NCLAT specifically observed that the fact that the pipeline infrastructure setup by Adani Gas Limited can subsequently be used now by any other competitor to distribute natural gas does not create any dent in the aforesaid finding. Hence, even if there are subsequent pro-competitive changes in the nature of a monopolized service, that would not have a retrospective effect in assessing dominance and abuse. We will deal with the abuse part, as discussed by the CCI and NCLAT in the Adani Gas case, in the later portions of the chapter. In Air Works India (Engineering) Private Limited v. GMR Hyderabad International Airport Limited (GMR) 94 Air Works India (Engineering) Private Limited v. GMR Hyderabad International Airport Limited (GMR),Case No. 30/2019. (‘GMR case’) also, the concept of statutory monopoly or state-conferred dominant position was discussed. In this case, GMR which was the manager and operator of the Rajiv Gandhi International Airport Hyderabad (RGIA), entered into a license agreement with the informant, which was a provider of third-party services to airlines and general aviation, namely the Line Maintenance Services and the Base Maintenance Services. Licensees such as the informant were dependent on GMR to grant the necessary license to operate from the Airport for the provision of its services to various airlines, and it was an understanding among all licensees who are operating at RGIA for the technical support services that as long as they are carrying on their operations, the license would continue to be granted. However, GMR owing to its sole control over RGIA, abused its dominance in order to protect and promote its own group entity, which was a competitor of the informant in the downstream market for provision of Line Maintenance Services at the RGIA, by terminating the license of the informant. The aspect of abuse has been discussed in the subsequent parts of this chapter. On the question of dominance, the CCI observed that in the case of airports, there has been an emerging trend where the ownership and management of the airport is undertaken in a public-private partnership. In the case of RGIA, a concession agreement was signed between GMR and the Government of India for thirty years which effectively made GMR the exclusive operator and manager of RGIA. The conferment of this exclusive privilege on GMR undoubtedly turned it into a monopoly:95 Ibid., paras 39,40. In International Spirits and Wines Association of India v. Uttarakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board, 96 International Spirits and Wines Association of India v. Uttarakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board, Case No. 02 of 2016. alcoholic beverage manufacturers were entirely dependent on the opposite parties for access to retailers and in turn, the end-consumers in the State of Uttarakhand. This was because a dominant position was bestowed upon them owing to the Excise Policy and the Liquor Wholesale Order in place, making the opposite parties the exclusive procurement agency and sole distributors of Indian-made foreign liquor, and no alternate access route to the market was available. The CCI held that the ‘State of Uttarakhand, like other states in the country, has created monopolies by canalising liquor procurement.’ Hence, such regulatory exclusion of competition virtually allowed the opposite parties to enjoy a monopoly in their respective functions. [H] Using the Regulatory Power to Enter Commercial Activities The CCI has grappled with the issue of a regulator entering commercial space. Such overlap has been found by the CCI largely in the sports sector, which operates in a pyramid structure, i.e., a single national sport association per sport and Member State, which operates under the umbrella of a single continental/national Federation and a single worldwide Federation, which is at the top of the pyramid. This means that only one apex body is responsible for the management of the sport at the national level, thereby conferring it with a monopoly status. Competition cases relating to sports associations/federations usually arise due to the conflict between their regulatory functions and their economic activities.97 Department of Sports v. Athletics Federation of India, Ref. Case No. 01 of 2015; Hemant Sharma and Ors. v. All India Chess Federation, Case No. 79 of 2011, paras 41-44. In such cases, by using their dominance by virtue of being a regulator, they enter into commercial activities like granting franchisee rights, media rights, etc. For instance, the CCI held that the Board for Control of Cricket in India had abused its dominant position.98 Surinder Singh Barmi v. Board for Control of Cricket in India, Case No. 61 of 2010. When the matter went up in appeal, as discussed in Chapter 1, the erstwhile COMPAT remanded the matter back on the issue of due process and the legal value of the evidence relied on by the CCI while deciding the case.99 Board of Cricket Control v. Competition Commission of India, [2015] CompLR 0548. The aspects of due process and natural justice were highlighted in Chapter 1. Accordingly, the CCI ordered the DG to conduct a further investigation into the matter in accordance with the directions contained in the above order of COMPAT. The CCI considered the Supplementary Investigation Report and found that the regulator was intruding into commercial activities. It observed that Board of Cricket Control in India (BCCI) historical evolution enabled it to attain a monopoly status in the organization of cricket events in India such that it had now become the de facto regulator of the sport. This was on account of the pyramidical structure of sports governance and endorsement from International Cricket Council (ICC), the national body for cricket in India which vested power into its members like BCCI to decide on any matter relating to the sport by declaring it as the ‘custodian’ of cricket in the concerned territory. Section 32 of the ICC Manual made BCCI the exclusive authority to sanction/approve cricket events in India, and any tournament/match not approved by it was regarded as ‘disapproved cricket’ in India. A similar situation transpired with regards to Hockey India (HI), a national association for hockey in India which was vested with certain rights by the Federation of Indian Hockey (FIH), prime among them being the right to sanction/approve hockey events in India. The CCI found that the most significant source of dominance of HI is its regulatory powers, which empower it along with FIH to create entry barriers for other rival (i.e., private) leagues by requiring them to obtain sanctions for their tournament and requiring players to obtain NOCs from HI to participate in rival tournaments. The aspects of granting sanctions for a league and giving NOCs for participation are regulatory in nature but are in a clear position to impact the market for organizing events and are a vital source of dominance. The CCI also held that HI’s dominance stems from its role as an organizer of international hockey events, which allows it to control a pool of players by entering into agreements with them for playing in such events. Since players are a vital input to the organization of any sport, the ability to control top players leads to dominance of HI. In doing so, the CCI considered the cases settled in other jurisdictions in this aspect and noted the observations in the ELPA case, where the Court on the issue of dominance of sports association insisted that ‘… a system of undistorted competition, such as that provided for by the treaty, can be guaranteed only if equality of opportunity is secured between the various economic operators. To entrust a legal person such as ELPA, the National Association for Motorcycling in Greece, which itself organizes and commercially exploits motorcycling events, the task of giving the competent administration its consent to applications for authorization to organize such events, is tantamount de facto to conferring upon it the power to designate the persons authorized to organize those events and to set the conditions in which those events are organized, thereby placing that entity at an obvious advantage over its competitors. Such a right may therefore lead the undertaking which possesses it to deny other operators access to the relevant market.’ Another example of regulatory dominance can be seen in the case involving the Confederation of Professional Baseball Softball Clubs,100 Case No. 03 of 2021. which is the apex institution for organising baseball events and selecting players for national and international tournaments. It operates in a similar pyramidal structure with like powers and relevant international affiliations as the HI and the Board of Cricket Control. As a result, the CCI held its regulatory powers, including sanctioning/disapproving proposals for the organization of baseball events and subjecting players to disciplinary action, were found to make it the predominant controller of the services provided by baseball players who have less bargaining power. The CCI also found that ‘there are high regulatory barriers in the sport of baseball since Association of Basketball Federation of India is the only body recognized by Ministry of Youth Affairs & Sports, Government of India, International Baseball Federation and Indian Olympic Association’ thereby solidifying its dominant position. [I] Other Factors In the Coal India case, Coal India argued that it was subject to social obligations and social costs in terms of section 19(4)(k), and its work constituted contribution to economic development as under section 19(4)(l) of the Competition Act. However, the erstwhile COMPAT noted that Coal India failed to demonstrate ‘any social obligations or social costs or developmental objectives permeating its functioning in determining the conditions of sale so as to impact its market power’. As mentioned earlier, the Competition Act provides the CCI with the flexibility to look into factors that are not covered explicitly in section 19(4) but are relevant for determining dominance in the relevant market. The objective is to identify the ability of the enterprise concerned to operate independently of the competitive forces prevailing in the relevant market or to affect its competitors or consumers or the relevant market in its favour. The importance attached to various factors would differ depending on the facts of each case, the specificity of each piece of information and the sector of economic activity involved.101 Air Cargo Agents Association of India v. International Air Transport Association, Case No. 79 of 2012. For instance, the investigation of the DG highlighted a few important findings regarding Google’s control over Android OS in the Play Store case102 Play Store case, paras 78-81. which was held to be an important factor in concluding the dominance of Google:(i) Even though the source code for the Android mobile OS is released by Google for free under an open-source license, the Android-based OEMs are highly dependent on Google. Google as the sponsor of the Android platform enforces platform rules through a combination of compatibility provisions, contracts, and trademark licenses. This includes seeking Google’s final written approval for using the licence, even though technically it is available for free if they want to use a suite of proprietary apps known as Google Mobile Services (GMS) which includes a variety of Google apps, such as Google Maps, YouTube, Google Play, and Google Search. (ii) Google owns the IPRs of Android OS. Further, the use of ‘Android’ which is a trademark of Google, is restricted to Android-compatible devices only and is subject to the OEMs’ compliance with separate contractual and technical obligations (collectively referred to as ‘Android Compatibility Program’). This compliance has to be undertaken every time Google releases a new version of Android OS. In addition, Google argued before the CCI that OEMs can customize and modify Android OS to build differentiated versions, thereby offering competition to Android OS. However, the CCI found that this is irrelevant to determining Google’s dominance since even these customizations have to comply with the Android Compatibility Program in order to pre-install GMS. Consequently, such customised OEMs do not pose any competitive constraints on Google. On this basis, the CCI held that although Android is ostensibly open-source, Google holds a significant, unparalleled and incontrovertible influence in its development. Some other factors which are increasingly considered in the analysis of dominance in digital markets have been discussed in detail in Chapter 6 on digital markets. The above factors clearly demonstrate the factors which the CCI takes into account while determining dominance. While the factors laid down under section 19(4) of the Competition Act are indicative, the CCI looks into all factors, including the behaviour of the dominant undertaking to determine whether the entity derives a position of strength.

sections.title: ABUSE OF DOMINANCE
sections.text: §3.05 ABUSE OF DOMINANCE It must be noted that the Competition Act provides that only abuse of dominance is in violation of the provisions of the Competition Act. An enterprise merely holding a dominant position is not a violation of the Competition Act. The CCI has held that the types of abuses identified under section 4(2) of the Competition Act are exhaustive and not illustrative. However, considering the language, structure and the overall purpose of the Competition Act, it may be argued in an appropriate case, the kinds of abusive conduct mentioned in section 4(2) are only specific instances of abuses and not exhaustive of all the abusive conducts covered under section 4. [A] Statutory Scheme The substantive contravention of abuse of a dominant position is covered under section 4(1) which reads – ‘No enterprise or group shall abuse its dominant position’. It would be interesting to note that the statutory scheme under sections 3(1), 4(1) and 6(1) are the same:– Section 3 (1) – ‘No enterprise … shall enter into any agreement … which causes or is likely to cause an appreciable adverse effect on competition in India’. – Section 4 (1) – ‘No enterprise or group shall abuse its dominant position ’. – Section 6 (1) – ‘No person or enterprise shall enter into a combination which causes or is likely to cause an appreciable adverse effect on competition in India.’ The language of the three provisions is uniform and similar, and a perusal of these provisions would demonstrate that the substantive obligations are covered under sections 3(1), 4(1) and 6(1). The provision stipulates that the enterprises should not enter into any agreement causing or likely to cause an AAEC in India or abuse its dominant position or enter into a combination causing or likely to cause and AAEC in India. This is also further demonstrated by section 19(1) of the Competition Act which states, ‘The Commission may inquire into any alleged contravention of the provisions contained in sub-section (1) of Section 3 or sub-section (1) of Section 4 …’. Therefore, the Competition Act supports the view that the contravention that the CCI will inquire into is a contravention under section 4(1). Therefore, the scheme of the statute clearly provides that the substantive obligation is enshrined in section 4(1). The CCI has also correctly held in the Hiranandani case (discussed in Chapter 2) that section 3(1) is the genus, and agreements covered under sections 3(3) and 3(4) are but particular species of agreements that will otherwise be covered under section 3(1) and that agreements not falling within section 3(3) and 3(4) will still fall under section 3(1).103 Hiranandani case, para. 15. This logic should equally apply in the case of section 4. Section 4 provides the substantive obligation that an enterprise cannot abuse its dominant position. Section 4(2) identifies certain conduct as abusive in nature for the purposes of section 4(1). It reads – ‘There shall be an abuse of dominant position under sub-section (1), if an enterprise or group’ undertakes any of the actions mentioned therein. Section 4(2) independently does not exhaustively identify all the abusive conduct under section 4(1); it only states that the specific conducts identified in section 4(2) are considered to be an abuse of dominant position. Therefore, conduct that is abusive in nature will still be covered under section 4(1) as the emphasis is on ‘abuse’. The natural meaning of the term ‘abuse’ would be applicable. If it were to be held that section 4(2) is exhaustive, then it would result in holding section 4(1) nugatory and otiose. It has been held by the Supreme Court in a large number of cases that interpretation will be adopted which would give meaning to a provision rather than to hold it nugatory. Therefore, the statutory scheme of section 4 and the legislative scheme of the Competition Act would demonstrate that the abusive conduct mentioned in section 4(2) is not exhaustive but is merely illustrative of certain kinds of abusive conduct.

sections.title: TYPES OF ABUSE
sections.text: §3.06 TYPES OF ABUSE We will deal with the abuses mentioned under each of the sub-sections of section 4 and the manner in which the CCI, the erstwhile COMPAT and the NCLAT have analysed the various headings of abuse mentioned in this section. It is important to note that the section is applicable to both enterprise and group. [A] Unfair Terms and Conditions Section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act states that there shall be an abuse of dominant position if an enterprise or a group imposes unfair or discriminatory condition in purchase or sale of goods or service. It must be noted that the approach of the CCI is to see whether the dominant undertaking, being in a position of strength, is using its muscle power to force the counterparties into agreements which are one-sided and commercially beneficial only for the dominant undertaking, without any business justification. Unless there is an imposition of unfair or discriminatory condition or price, as the case may be, there will be no breach of section 4(2)(a)(i) or 4(2)(a)(ii) of the Competition Act. There can be no dispute that the moment there is imposition of such a condition, the mischief of section 4 will be attracted.104 DLF Limited v. Competition Commission of India, [2014] Comp AT 12, para. 56. The CCI has taken an approach to see whether the condition, so imposed by the dominant undertaking because of its power, is unfair qua the counterparty. What is unfair and fair would depend on the facts and circumstances of each case. As such, the essence of any contract is consensus ad idem. Conversely, there can be situations wherein a counterparty executes a contract with a dominant undertaking, they have no option but to follow and sign the contract which is heavily in favour of the dominant undertaking. Such a contract would be considered by the CCI as unfair since there is no consensus ad idem. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations made in the dissent order passed by the CCI in the case of Sunil Bansal v. M/s Jaiprakash Associates Ltd. & Others: 105 Case No. 72/2011. It is to be noted that the dictionary meaning of the word “impose” is force on someone, or take advantage of someone by demanding their attention or commitment. The meaning of unfair is “not based on, or behaving according to the principles of equality and justice”. Thus, imposing unfair conditions would mean that one of the party who enjoys a higher bargaining power is in a position to dictate terms to the weak party who is not in a position to negotiate [emphasis supplied]. Based on the above, the CCI analyses the conduct of the dominant undertaking to determine whether they have imposed any unfair terms and conditions. The factors that are generally kept in mind are whether the rights and obligations of the parties to the agreements are one-sided or mutual. For example, if an agreement is entered into by parties for sale and purchase of any good, any delay on the part of the supplier to supply goods and the purchaser to provide timely instalments must give rise to a penalty including interest. However, if the penalty is only levied on one party (as occurred in the DLF case, Coal India case and the Adani Gas case) and not on the dominant enterprise (like no penalty on Coal India for non-supply of graded coal on time), CCI would view such clauses to be unfair and hence a case of abuse of dominant position would be made out.106 Naveen Kataria v. Jaiprakash Associates Limited, Case No. 99 of 2014. Unfair terms are often in the nature of mandatory acceptance of the dominant firm’s policies, even if they are harmful. CCI’s decisional practice indicates that contractual terms that are one-sided with no option besides acceptance are unfair.107 Madhya Pradesh Power Generating Company Limited v. South Eastern Coalfields Ltd. and Anr., Case Nos 05, 07, 37 & 44 of 2013; Neha Gupta v. Tata Motors Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 21 of 2019 and Case No. 16 of 2020, paras 121, 122. For instance, in the WhatsApp case, WhatsApp updated its Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for users to the effect that consent to sharing and integration of user data with other Facebook Companies for a range of purposes, including marketing and advertising, was made a precondition for availing WhatsApp service. There was also opacity, vagueness and open-endedness about the nature, kind and extent of data sharing that the user was being compelled to consent to. There was also no option to opt out of the said Policy update, and the users now have to mandatorily agree if they want to continue using WhatsApp Messenger. On this basis, the CCI ordered an investigation into WhatsApp’s conduct for imposing unfair terms by propagating a take-it-or-leave-it’ policy. Subsequently, WhatsApp challenged the jurisdiction of CCI to pass such an order before the Delhi High Court owing to potentially conflicting opinions when the Supreme Court was already looking into whether the Policy was violative of the right to privacy under Article 21 of the Constitution. However, the Delhi High Court clarified that the investigation by the CCI is confined to whether the Policy furthers the dominant position occupied by WhatsApp and institutes anti-competitive practices. The Supreme Court is not analysing the Policy through the prism of competition law. The spheres of operation of both are vastly different. Hence, it allowed the investigation to proceed.108 WhatsApp LLC v. Competition Commission of India, Delhi High Court LPA 163/2021&LPA 164/2021; WhatsApp LLC v. Competition Commission of India, Delhi High Court W.P.(C) 4378/2021 & CM 13336/2021. The Delhi High also affirmed the prima facie findings of CCI that WhatsApp’s dominance due to lack of/restricted interoperability between platforms, its ability to engage in data concentration and the opacity, vagueness, open-endedness and incomplete disclosures in the Policy have the potential to undermine the competitive processes and create further barriers to market entry. A key concept of unfair conduct is exclusionary conduct, was seen in the Google Android case. The complex, self-reinforcing and pervasive nature of Google’s agreements have been described earlier. The collective unfair impositions under these agreements, as elaborated by the CCI, have been described in the following diagram: By virtue of the agreements discussed above, Google ensured that users continued to use its search services on mobile devices which facilitated uninterrupted growth of advertisement revenue for Google. Further, it also helped Google to further invest and improve its services to the exclusion of others. Thus, the underlying objective of Google in imposing various restrictions via MADA, AFA/ACC and RSAs was to protect and strengthen its dominant position in general search services and, thus, its revenues via search advertisements. Per the CCI, Google’s agreements, i.e., MADA, AFA, ACC and RSA, collectively manifested the following exclusionary conducts:(i) MADA requires non-exclusive preinstallation of Google Search, Search Widget, and Google Chrome (with Google Search as default) on all Android devices. Further, Google also secures exclusivity for its search services on all the OEMs’ devices through RSAs. However, the competing search service providers, due to Google’s preinstallation through MADA, cannot secure exclusivity even on devices not covered by RSAs. At most, these rivals can achieve pre-installation along with Google, that too at an inferior position since Google demands default home screen placement which itself is premium real estate with limited availability (since it would get users hooked, as explained below). (ii) Similarly, in the case of search services, Android devices have Google Search, Search Widget, and Google Chrome (with Google Search as default) preinstalled on the default home screen. These three Search Access Points generate a significant volume of search queries (which, again, Google monetizes in its other verticals). Given the mandatory presence of Google search services on the home screen, the OEMs may not like to pre-install another search widget/search app on the home screen itself to avoid any bad user experience, limited system memory/space, etc. (iii) Through Google’s prominent presence on the default home screen of the device, the user is more likely to use Google on the home screen as compared to rival services on the second or third screen. This ‘status quo bias’ cannot be overcome by rivals. Given the preference for Google’s services by users (which are also created out of perpetuating these obligations), the OEM cannot afford to offer devices without Google’s apps (which are only available through MADA, etc.). These default positions for search queries are also true for mobile web browsers and desktop/PCs (Google Chrome with Google Search set as default). (iv) Google offers a bouquet of services and with their increased usage, it is in an unassailable position to exploit economies of scale and scope with enormous data generated by all these apps together. The competitors being unable to offer these services and being placed at an inferior position in the Android ecosystem are at a competitive disadvantage. Google, with additional data that feeds into its search-advertising business model, increase its competitive advantage over other rivals. Hence, these agreements prohibit alternative vendors from outcompeting Google’s apps on merits and foreclose competition even in the adjacent markets by creating impenetrable walls. Google’s rivals can neither compete by creating forked OS (due to AFA/ACC) nor by offering their services to OEMs because even if they do manage to enter into pre-installation arrangements with OEMs, a significant volume of search queries will be diverted to Google due to the operation of MADA and RSA. There is also no option but to sign these agreements and accept all the contained restrictions due to the ‘must have’ nature of Google’s services which makes them commercially necessary. Thus, the mandatory pre-installation of the entire GMS suite (with no option to un-install the same) and their prominent placement amounts to imposition of an unfair condition on the device manufacturers and thereby in contravention of the provisions of section 4(2)(a)(i). Subsequently, the NCLAT in appeal also upheld that pre-installation of the entire GMS Suite amounts to imposing unfair conditions on OEMs which is an abuse of dominant position by Google. It added that this is because there is an element of compulsion and OEMs have no negotiation power, and they have to accept the terms and conditions offered by Google which compel them to enter into such one-sided agreements for commercial viability. Herein, it is appropriate to mention Google’s contention before the NCLAT that no OEM complained against the unfairness of MADA, which was the counterparty in the agreement. Google cited CCI’s own previous decisions to argue that unfairness must be judged from the testimonies of the counterparty, in this case, the OEMs and not any other third party. It was strenuously argued by Google that OEMs were happy with the Google bouquet of must-have apps which they were offering as a bundle. However, the NCLAT held that competition law being a public law, is concerned with the effect on the entire market. Hence, the regulator (i.e., CCI) which is obliged to discharge its statutory function cannot confine its analysis and decision only on the basis of evidence of countervailing parties and competitors.109 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, paras 81, 82. Ultimately, NCLAT ruled that the action of Google of tying their apps together is in violation of the Competition Act. As a result, CCI ordered several remedies, inter alia, including that Google shall not impose anti-fragmentation obligations on OEMs, licensing of Google Play Services to OEMs shall not be linked with the requirement of pre-installing any other application of Google, etc. It also specifically directed Google not to enforce the anti-competitive clauses identified in the respective agreements (MADA, AFA/ACC and RSAs). On the point of exploitative conduct constituting an unfair condition under section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act, it is relevant to mention that examination of exploitative conduct which involves imposition of unfair conditions by a dominant enterprise in a business to business transaction is essentially to undertake a fairness or reasonability test, which requires examining both how the condition affects the trading partners of the dominant enterprise as well as whether there is any legitimate and objective necessity for the enterprise to impose such condition.110 Indian National Shipowners’ Association v. Oil and Natural Gas Corporation Limited, Case No. 01 of 2018. Appreciation of the context and rationale becomes even more important in the cases of buyer power; otherwise, large players may be penalized in the mere attempt of negotiating competitive terms. [1] Discriminatory Conditions Apart from unfair conditions, discriminatory conditions are also held to be an abuse of dominant position.111 Clear Media (India) Private Limited v. Prasar Bharti, Case No. 19 of 2017, para. 16. Similar to the aspect of discriminatory pricing, if the dominant undertaking makes any conditions which are discriminatory in nature among similar set of consumers without any rational business justification, such discriminatory clauses would be covered under the heading of abuse. This also includes preferential treatment granted to certain users over others.112 Confederation of Indian Alcoholic Beverage Companies and Anr. v. Kerala State Beverages (Manufacturing and Marketing) Corporation Ltd., Case No. 10 of 2021, para. 65. For instance, in the Schott Glass case, the CCI observed that the increased quantity supplied to Schott Kaisha could result in lower overall cost for Schott which can be passed on to the consumers in the form of a more favourable discount. Schott was also giving favourable conditions to Schott Kaisha, such as in its target discounts (based on quantity purchased) and functional discounts (which imposed additional conditions on the convertors to promote Schott’s products). On this basis, the CCI found Schott to be in violation of sections 4(2)(a)(i) and (ii) of the Competition Act on account of discriminatory treatment between Schott Kaisha and other convertors purchasing from Schott.113 Schott Glass case, para. 9.83-9.87. However, the CCI minority opinion held that for a discount policy to be held as ‘discriminatory’, two conditions should be met: (i) dissimilar treatment to equivalent transactions; and (ii) harm to competition or is likely harm to competition such that the buyers suffer a competitive disadvantage in the downstream market. In an appeal before the COMPAT,114 Schott Glass India Pvt. Ltd v. Competition Commission of India, [2014] Comp AT 1, paras 43-45.1. Schott argued that CCI misunderstood the concept of ‘discriminatory pricing’ and ignored the basic principle that the prices could be called ‘discriminatory’ only if they were for equivalent transactions, meaning thereby that if a different price was charged for the same quantity of the same product, only then would the pricing would have been discriminatory. Here, the facts were that the quantity purchased by Schott Kaisha far exceeded the purchases of any other converter, and no other convertor had the capacity to require the same quantity. The prices with the convertors were also negotiated on a one-to-one basis based on the quantity purchased. Therefore, the cost differential in inputs caused by the discount scheme of Schott did not affect the end price of the final products. Discount scheme of Schott did not harm the competitive ability of the customers and competition in the downstream relevant markets. The erstwhile COMPAT relied on the observations of the minority order which alluded to the EU and American jurisprudence, which provided that discrimination in prices between different purchasers of the same products is condemned, where the effect of such discrimination may be to substantially lessen competition. Hence, in an application of the two conditions mentioned above, it was deduced that the discount in this case was given on a slab basis, and the same rate of discount was applicable to transactions falling in the same quantity slab. Hence, (i) transactions of different volumes of tubes could not be inferred as equivalent transactions warranting equivalent treatment from the seller, and (ii) Schott’s conduct did not affect downstream competition since prices of final products charged by Schott Kaisha to the pharmaceutical companies were same or in some instances higher as compared to the prices charged by the other players. In fact, if any other converter matched the quantity purchased by Schott Kaisha, they were entitled to the same kind of discount. Further, this functional discount policy had been applied uniformly to all the converters at the same flat rate since its inception in a non-discriminatory manner. Therefore, such a policy could not be deemed to be discriminatory in terms of price differentiation. The erstwhile COMPAT affirmed this position and also added that there would have to be some reasonable discretion in the manufacturer for conducting business. Hence, the findings of the CCI were overturned. This position has also been followed in other cases.115 TAM Media Research Private Limited v. Prasar Bharati (Broadcasting Corporation of India), Case No. 70 of 2012, para. 9.4., 9.5; Rajendra Khare v. Information Systems Audit and Control Association, Case No. 42 of 2021, para. 32. Hence, this brings us to the concept of equality among equals: a dominant enterprise is obligated to treat only similar entities equally since businesses operating under similar circumstances have the freedom to develop and implement their own marketing and discounting strategies based on their unique objectives, market conditions, and customer preferences. Consequently, the aspect of discriminatory pricing cannot impede the ability of a dominant enterprise to have an objective-based discounting and marketing policy, as the aim of competition law is to maintain a balance between promoting fair competition and allowing businesses, including dominant ones, to operate effectively and innovatively. This is an extension of the principle of rational business justification, which means that if dominant enterprises have a rational justification to justify their business practice, then the same cannot be termed as anti-competitive. Further, in the Play Store case, the CCI concluded that Google’s various conducts and policies in relation to the Play Store were in violation of section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act. These are as follows:(i) Making access to the Play Store for app developers, dependent on mandatory usage of GPBS for paid apps and in-app purchases, constitutes an imposition of unfair condition on app developers. (ii) Google has gradually increased the scope of the mandatory requirement to use its own billing system by explicit additions in the policy. This has to be agreed to by all app developers who want to list their app on the Play Store without any scope for changes/negotiations. Since Android is the most widely used smart device ecosystem in the country, Google acts as the gatekeeper between the app store and the smart device user. As a result of its financial might and its control over the entire ecosystem, Google undoubtedly has a much stronger bargaining position vis-à-vis app developers, who are forced to accept Google’s unilateral terms that deter legitimate competition and increase their costs of operation. (iii) Google also restricts the ability of app developers to inform consumers within an app of the ability to purchase in-app content elsewhere, such as on a website (‘Anti-steering Provisions’). Thus, app developers do not have the freedom to choose their communication channel to interact with their users. This is an unfair imposition as it impedes the ability of app developers to increase their usage/membership, and they are prohibited from using third-party payment processors that may offer greater benefits and innovative features to the app users. (iv) Google is following discriminatory practices by not using GPBS for its own applications, i.e., YouTube. This also amounts to imposition of discriminatory conditions as well as pricing, as YouTube is not paying the service fee as being imposed on other apps covered in the GPBS requirements. (v) Google was also found to have access to a significant volume of user data which it did not share with the app developers, affecting their ability to improve their services and compete effectively. However, this confiscated data was then exploited by Google which is particularly exacerbating since Google itself admitted that it considers app developers to be its competitors. Data as a factor of abuse of dominance has been discussed in more detail in the Chapter on Digital Markets. (vi) Google takes significantly more time for settlement of payment to app developers, i.e., fifteen to forty-five days from the transaction, as opposed to the settlement period followed by other payment processors which take only one to three days to remit the payment to app developers. Google receives the payments within a shorter and stricter deadline from the payment aggregators. However, it fails to observe such a strict timeline when it comes to making payments to app developers. This unjust enrichment on the part of Google is unfair to the app developers, especially small app developers who require regular and prompt cash flows. (vii) Service fee is imposed by Google on apps offering digital content, but the distinction between physical and digital content is ambiguous, without adequate transparency or justification for the differential treatment accorded to apps by Google, leaving it susceptible to discrimination. It is pointed out that Google argued that there is no violation of section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act because Google’s Payments Policy is fair, and the use of standard terms reduces the potential for discrimination. Further, that Google’s policies – including the requirement that apps use GPBS for IAPs of digital goods – apply to all apps on Google Play, including Google’s own app. However, the CCI found that the service fee imposed on apps that compete with Google’s proprietary apps disadvantages Google’s rivals and distorts the competition dynamic in the apps market. For instance, the fee charged to ShareChat, a video distribution app, was not made applicable to YouTube, which was Google’s own app. Google, thus, sets out rules for the Play Store that may implicitly exclude or discriminate against downstream competition. In the build-up to the network effects, Google required app developers to develop apps that would attract consumers to the app stores. The CCI observed that once Google also started developing these competing apps and entered into these ‘complementor’ markets, its incentives changed. This meant that Google lost its incentives to compete on and ensure fair terms on its platform upon entering into the downstream market of app distribution: now, instead of just being the owner of the platform, i.e., the Play Store, Google started competing with its customers on the platform, i.e., the app developers. The CCI also noted that Google admits that some of the app developers are its competitors. Non-disclosure of essential and competitively relevant information is also an unfair imposition.116 Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC, Case Nos 07 and 30 of 2021. For instance, in Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., 117 Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc and Ors., Case No. 41 of 2021. the CCI ordered an investigation into Google’s conduct, resulting in denial of fair share in the digital advertising revenue to news publishers and disclosure of inadequate information to reach a fair settlement. There ostensibly existed opacity on critical aspects like data and audience management practices, generation and sharing of revenue with publishers, etc. Information asymmetry, i.e., lack of transparency with respect to these aspects was prima facie prejudicial to the interest of publishers, by in turn affecting the quality of their services and innovation, thereby causing consumer detriment. Hence, such policies of Google constituted imposition of unfair conditions and price in violation of section 4(2)(a) and thus merited an investigation. The investigation of the same is pending. [2] Analysis of Specific Unfair Terms and Conditions In its decisional practice, the CCI has examined abusive conduct having regard to the terms of the contract, out of which it emanates from the standpoint of the provisions of the Competition Act. The DLF case was the first major case on the issue of a dominant enterprise imposing unfair terms and conditions on the counterparty. DLF, a real estate player, was imposing one-sided terms on the buyers. The CCI went into great depth and analysed the clause of the Apartment Buyers Agreement. It is to be noted that when the matter was heard at COMPAT, it did not analyse the issue of the said agreements because they were entered into prior to 20 May 2009 (the date when section 4 of the Competition Act was notified). However, the erstwhile COMPAT did hold DLF guilty of abuse of dominant position through imposition of one-sided or unfair conditions through the Apartment Buyers Agreement. It observed that prior to the enactment of the Competition Act, the element of dominance was absent from the statute book, and hence, it cannot be argued that one-sided agreements which were ‘imposed’ on the allottees (rather, they were voluntarily executed) is contrary to the law since sections 3 and 4 were not available when the agreements were executed. Consequently, it rejected CCI’s approach of examining all the clauses of the Apartment Buyers Agreement. Conversely, when the matter was initially heard at CCI, it took great pains to identify the specific clauses of the Agreement and the conduct of DLF towards its buyers, based on which it ultimately held that DLF was guilty of abuse of dominant position. While the erstwhile COMPAT did not analyse the specific clauses of the buyer agreement like the CCI did (although it specifically noted that ‘it was only because of the question of retrospectivity that we did not go into the merits of the analysis of various clauses of the ABA’), it is noteworthy to see the approach of the CCI. The relevant clauses under the Apartment Buyers Agreement which were held to be abuse of dominant position by the CCI were broadly as under:118 DLF case, para. 12.90-12.103. (1) Unilateral changes in agreement and supersession of terms by DLF without any discussion or even informing the allottees, such as changing the layout plan, inter se areas earmarked for different uses like residential, commercial, etc., increasing/decreasing the super area, zoning plans, usage patterns, carpet area and the like. The allottees had no option but to accept the sweeping changes, even if made without assigning reasons, and were not allowed to raise any objections. This was after the allottees were compelled to pay almost 95% of the consideration amount within twenty-seven months of booking. Hence, the buyer got locked in with DLF, having paid a substantial amount with no free exit option to go to other real estate service providers. (2) Additional charges for preferential location determined at the discretion of DLF were to be paid upfront, but if the allottee did not get the location, he would only get the refund at the time of the last instalment, that too without any interest. DLF also retained total discretion regarding the arrangement for power supply and rates levied for the same, even if it exceeded the rate charged by other power suppliers. DLF’s exit clause gave them full discretion, including abandoning the project, without any penalty. However, the allottees had no exit option except when DLF fails to deliver possession within the agreed time. Even in that event, they get their money refunded without interest only after the sale of said apartment by DLF to someone else. (3) DLF continued to enjoy full rights on the community buildings/sites/recreational and sporting activities, including maintenance, but allottees did not. In fact, third-party rights were also created without the allottee’s consent. For instance, even if the buyer has paid the full amount, the builder can raise a subordinate mortgage on the property for financing its own purposes, and the consumers would be subjected to this mortgage. Further, high punitive penalty was imposed for default by allottees along with excessive interest (@15% per annum and for all periods of delay exceeding the first ninety days after the due date an additional penal interest @3% per annum) and insignificant penalty for DLF’s default (in the event of delay, the builder would pay compensation at INR 5 per square foot per month for delays beyond three years). Based on these practices, the allottees become captured consumers who were subject to abuse by DLF through imposition of unfair conditions contained in the agreement. DLF’s conduct also revealed that such abuse was not a one-time practice but continued (and often aggravated) throughout the period of construction. Thus, the CCI held that by conduct and by the provisions of the agreement, DLF was guilty of abuse of dominant position. These are some of the clauses that show how heavily loaded the buyers’ agreement is in favour of DLF Ltd. and against the buyer. Under a normal market scenario, a seller would be wary of including such one-sided and biased clauses in its agreements with consumers. The CCI held that the impunity with which these clauses have been imposed, the brutal disregard for consumer rights that has been displayed in its action of cancelling allotments and forfeiting deposits and the deliberate strategy of obfuscating the terms and keeping buyers in the dark about the eventual shape, size, location, etc., of the apartment cannot be termed as fair. The course the progress of the project has taken again indicates that DLF Ltd. beguiled and entrapped buyers through false solicitations and promises contrary to consumer interest. The person who chooses a particular apartment does so after considering factors such as floor area ratio, availability of bigger common areas, common facilities, etc. If the number of apartments in a project is substantially increased, as in this case, there is considerable reduction in consumer welfare. For example, if a hundred residents are supposed to share one swimming pool, their satisfaction from that common pool would be far less if suddenly the builder tells them they have to share the pool with 200 residents. In economics, ‘value’ is based on the perceived utility of any product or service. Consumers pay according to this perception. If something is done that drastically or substantially reduces that perceived value, it can only be termed as an unfair conduct by the seller. Under Competition Act, such unfair conduct by a dominant enterprise is contravention of the provisions of the Act. In the DLF case, the erstwhile COMPAT also observed that CCI confining itself to only the one-sided nature of the agreement was not the be-all and end-all of the matter, and it had to be seen whether DLF’s actions contravened section 4 of the Competition Act. This primarily included non-disclosure of the additional construction and then proceeding with the construction. It was the element of secrecy and uncertainty with which DLF conducted its business which made it a tainted condition. In this regard, inter alia, the erstwhile COMPAT held:119 DLF Limited v. Competition Commission of India, [2014] Comp AT 12, paras 74, 99-121. (i) Increasing the number of floors without increasing the number of lifts and thus, a lift which was to be shared with a hundred persons would now have to be shared with five hundred persons, which would add to day-to-day woes and all this without giving the faintest idea to the allottees as to how many floors were to be actually constructed and applied for. This mysterious vagueness amounts to imposition of an unfair condition since it left the allottees in the continued position of a trapped customer as they have no idea how far the DLF could go higher and how many floors it could add to each building. (ii) DLF argued that the original allottees were given an assurance that instead of their previously selected apartment, they could select an apartment out of the added floor. However, this meant the allottees had to forego their claims on the improvements made in the originally selected apartment, the special features, etc. Further, DLF was very vague in its offer, as it could accept moving some allottees while rejecting others. This offer itself amounts to an imposition of discriminatory condition since where a provision could enable an authority to discriminate, such provision itself is bad. (iii) DLF tried to argue that the modifications were accepted by the authorities and, hence, were free of fault. However, erstwhile COMPAT categorically held that it was not concerned with the ‘the legality or validity of the construction’, but ‘the impact that it made on the allottees, who did not have even a ghost of idea, as to how many persons they would have to share lifts with or their common area, or for that matter their swimming pool and gymnasium’. This highlighted the sinister unfairness on the part of DLF, which still had a duty to disclose the action. (iv) The allottees were allured by DLF’s promise of luxury facilities, without which the apartments could not be termed ‘luxury apartments’. Hence, DLF was duty-bound to let the allottees know about the proposed increase and obtain their consent. (v) If the allottee did not concur with the increase of INR 5 to INR 10 in case of holding charges, he would not be entitled to compensation on account of delay in construction. This is nothing but a ruse to avoid the payment of compensation on account of delayed construction. Such a unilateral increase in the holding charges and the threatened consequence amounted to abuse of dominance by DLF. (vi) The CCI also failed to consider that what was being offered to the allottees was a piece of illegal and unauthorized construction, which amounts to imposing unfair conditions against the wishes of the consumer. ‘We cannot imagine as to what would have happened had the approvals not come in the year 2009 by which time, the 29 floors of Belaire residential housing scheme was already over’. Based on the DLF case, it was noted that the approach of the CCI is to see whether the consumer has any bargaining choice vis-à-vis DLF or if they are subject to continuous abusive tactics of the DLF. Similarly, the CCI dealt with the similar issue of unfair terms and conditions imposed by Coal India on thermal and non-thermal power producers. The Coal India case120 Coal India case, Case Nos 03, 11 & 59 of 2012, paras 155-259. revolved around imposition of unfair and discriminatory terms in the Fuel Supply Agreement entered into between Coal India and the buyers. Further, there were certain existing Fuel Supply Agreements which were already in place with some power plants and some new Fuel Supply Agreements were signed with new power plants/buyers. Before delving deep into the issue of unfair and discriminatory terms and conditions contained in the Fuel Supply Agreement and identifying the unfair clauses as such, the CCI analysed the repercussions of having unfair terms under the Fuel Supply Agreement. It was noted by the CCI that a Fuel Supply Agreement is a long-term agreement between a buyer and Coal India which has to be differentiated from other ordinary long-term agreements due to the cascading and systemic effects which may flow from its terms, conditions and their execution. The consumer in a Fuel Supply Agreement is a power generating company, however, the effect has consequences further down the value chain, given that the cost of electricity constitutes between 60% and 70% of the tariff paid by the end consumer. Further, CCI noted that while the obligations and responsibility of supply of coal by Coal India stop at the level of the Fuel Supply Agreements, the obligations and responsibilities of the power generating companies extend beyond the power sector in as much as any inefficiency and anti-competitive conduct in one segment of the value chain would have the effect of creating and causing a systemic risk in the entire economy. Based on the same, CCI noted that it is imperative that a Fuel Supply Agreement address the issue of mutual obligations and responsibilities holistically rather than merely from a perspective of ordinary contractual obligations. Accordingly, the CCI was of the view that assessment of the obligations and responsibilities enshrined within the Fuel Supply Agreement calls for an integrated and holistic approach that considers not only the anti-competitive effects on power-generating companies but also on the entire power sector as a whole. Based on the above rationale of having an efficient Fuel Supply Agreement in the interest of the power sector and the end consumer, the CCI noted121 Ibid., para. 158. that the Fuel Supply Agreement must guarantee commercial and operational viability for both sellers and buyers of coal by consisting of three essential characteristics – quality, quantity and deliver schedules/timelines. The sellers must supply good quality coal at pre-agreed prices in a timely manner, and the buyers must pay promptly and intimate their requirements in advance, with penalties for non-fulfilment of these obligations. Any price/supply shocks should be mutually resolved between the parties. Since the outcome of Fuel Supply Agreements (FSA) is directly related to the Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) signed between power generators and distributors, any mismatch between FSA and PPA results in a cascading effect affecting the tariffs to consumers. Considering the above, the CCI looked into, in great detail, each of the clauses of the Fuel Supply Agreements and found the following terms and conditions unfair and discriminatory: (a) The unilateral drafting of the Fuel Supply Agreement:122 Ibid., paras 151-160. The terms and conditions of a commercial contract should be negotiated by the buyer and seller. However, the Fuel Supply Agreement was unilaterally drafted by Coal India without even consulting the buyers of the coal. A process of discussion on the drafting of the fuel supply agreements did happen, but these were commercial discussions limited to the Ministry of Power, which was not the key stakeholder as far as the Fuel Supply Agreement was concerned.
