The investigation and evidence on record revealed that the inputs of the power utilities, who were the buyers, were not considered. Based on this unilateral approach of Coal India, the CCI highlighted that under certain market conditions, some contracts become unconscionable especially when the markets are not functioning in a competitive manner. In such a scenario, the party with superior bargaining power is able to dictate terms that are overwhelmingly one-sided. Then, the other party is confronted with the ‘take it or leave it’ proposition [emphasis supplied]. (b) Issue of supply of coal through MOUs:123 Ibid., paras 242-249. Coal India had been insisting on the buyers to execute additional MOUs in addition to the Fuel Supply Agreements, which became integral to the contract. These MOUs, inter alia, provided that the quantum of coal supply will be decided by Coal India but shall not exceed 50% of the annual contracted quantity. Also, for the calculation of compensation for short-supply or short-lifting, the annual contracted quantity under the Fuel Supply Agreement shall be reduced by 50%. Hence, they diluted the supply obligations of Coal India. The CCI noted that the MOUs were framed in such a manner which not only reduced the obligation of Coal India to supply the necessary quantity but also reduced the compensation payable in case of a short supply. Further, the purchasers had no option but to accept such one-sided terms and conditions as there was no scope for negotiation, giving an upper hand to Coal India. Hence, the CCI observed that the MOU constituted imposition of unfair terms. (c) Procedure for sample collection for assessment of quality of coal:124 Ibid., paras 172-190. An examination of the Fuel Supply Agreements executed between Coal India and the new power producers, existing power producers and public sector undertakings revealed that there were different methods adopted for sample collection. For existing public sector undertaking power producers, there was a provision for automatic mechanical sampling for coal, whereas, for existing private producers and new private power producers, it was manual till 2012 when the words ‘or any suitable mechanical arrangement’ were inserted in the agreements. These changes were themselves found to be abstract and ambiguous, besides having the potential to cause a conflict of interest. Based on the same, the CCI was of the opinion that that the provisions for sampling of coal are ex facie discriminatory between public sector undertakings and private producers. The CCI noted that in an appropriate case, the distinction based on two consumers may have some rational justification, but presently, it was not readily inferred as to how a distinction in the matter between the various buyers (be it existing power producers, new power producers or public sector undertakings) relating to sample collection may have any justification, leave alone a justification which may satisfy the requirements of law. Thus, the order of the CCI showed that an artificial distinction among consumers would not be accepted as a defence for discriminatory pricing. To differentiate among various kinds of consumers, the undertaking must have a rational business justification. What is rational would depend on market situations, the obligation of the dominant undertaking and the facts and circumstances of each case. (d) Sampling of coal at loading and unloading points:125 Ibid., paras 191-194. The sampling was done at both ends, i.e., loading and unloading points, by an independent party. However, Coal India made unilateral changes in the sampling procedure. A comparison between the sampling procedure adopted by the other players in the market revealed that the latter provided for sampling at the loading end only. However, there was a provision for analysis by both parties at their respective labs, and for this purpose, three sets of samples (one each for seller, buyer and referee) were taken. Thus, the buyers have been given opportunity to test the sample to their satisfaction. Hence, compared with another player in the relevant market, the procedure of sample collection and analysis adopted by Coal India was heavily tilted in their favour. Thus, these terms and conditions regarding the commitment of Coal India to ensure the quality of coal were held to be unfair and prejudicial towards consumers. (e) Supply of ungraded coal:126 Ibid., paras 195-199. When the price of coal is based on the grade/quality, the buyer has the right to get the grade for which he is paying the price. However, the Fuel Supply Agreement did not impose a strict liability upon Coal India and its subsidiaries to supply only the agreed grades but only mentioned making adequate arrangements to assess the quality and for providing monitoring mechanism to prevent loading of ungraded coal. It merely provided that Coal India need only try that the ungraded coal is not loaded, however, if the ungraded coal was loaded and transported, there was no provision for compensation. Further, even in case of supply of ungraded coal which was in breach of the agreed quality under the Fuel Supply Agreement, the buyer was saddled with all the expenses on transportation, royalty, taxes, etc., without any justification. However, other players in the market reimbursed the freight costs borne by the buyer in case of supply of ungraded coal. The CCI did note that the ungraded coal may have some value and Coal India may be able to sell such ungraded coal in the open market to the willing buyer. However, forcing the buyer who has specifically contracted for supply of graded coal to pay for such goods transported by default was not fair in any circumstances. The CCI held that Coal India cannot absolve itself of the responsibility of supplying only graded coal. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations of the CCI:the consumers cannot be forced to pay the cost of ungraded coal. Such condition not only gives an upper hand to the seller but also reduces its commitment to supply right quality of coal. The conduct of the seller is guided by the FSA and if there is no obligation on the seller to assure the supply of only graded coal, the coal companies are not bound to do the same. [emphasis supplied] It is not out of place to mention that such provisions also result in misappropriation or mishandling of the limited resources of railways in supply of rake to the coal industry as well as of the buyer. The ultimate sufferer is the end user of power on whom the increased cost is passed on. Thus, the terms and conditions in FSA regarding supply of quality coal should be guided by the strict adherence to the desired quality and the measures relating to grading, sampling and testing of the coal needs to be incorporated in the agreement to the satisfaction of both the parties. (f) Compensation for supply of oversized, wet and sticky coal:127 Ibid., paras 200-208. In the process of supply of coal, there are chances of dispatch of oversized, wet and sticky coal along with stones. For any Fuel Supply Agreement to have a semblance of fairness, it must necessarily provide for payment of compensation in such an event and operate in a non-discriminatory manner among all classes of buyers. However, the compensation under the Fuel Supply Agreement for new power producers was capped at 0.75% of the total quantity of coal, whereas there was no cap on compensation for existing power producers. Hence, the CCI held that the same is ex facie unfair since the cap was not based on actual quantity or any other reasonable basis besides discrimination between the two classes of buyers. (g) Unilateral termination of agreement: The CCI noted that Coal India enjoys a situation of statutory monopoly, and the buyers are heavily dependent upon Coal India. In light of the same, insertion of a clause reserving the right to unilaterally terminate the agreement without having scope for review by any independent agency is not fair under the extant regulatory framework operating in the coal sector.128 Ibid., para. 228. The erstwhile COMPAT reaffirmed the findings of the CCI that Coal India abused its dominant position in contravention of section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act. Certain relevant observations of the erstwhile COMPAT and their analysis in addition to the findings of the CCI have been stated below:129 Ibid., para. 22. (i) Clause 9 stipulated that the bid of successful buyers would be forfeited if they failed to lift the coal within the specified period. However, there was no corresponding clause to provide compensation to successful bidders if Coal India failed to supply the coal. In fact, Coal India itself admitted that during the period of assessment, it defaulted in 701 instances in providing coal to the successful bidders, and no compensation was paid for such defaults. (ii) Coal India was also unable to rebut the assertion of the buyers that its failure to supply coal to the successful bidders affected them financially (since they lost interest on the money deposited) while Coal India enjoyed this credit free of cost. Such clauses are one-sided and disturb the normal contractual equilibrium, and the uneven obligations were a result of the market power exercised by Coal India. The fact that buyers agreed to such unequal terms despite adverse financial effects made it apparent that there was no competitive pressure on Coal India. (iii) Coal India’s argument that such forfeiture clauses without compensation were standard industry practice was rejected because of its failure to prove that the entities issuing such notices were dominant in their relevant markets. This once again brings us to the point that unfair conditions require intervention by the CCI only if the enterprise is dominant. The Coal India case is currently pending an appeal before the Supreme Court to be decided on merits. However, in its interim observations, the Supreme Court has held that even if Coal India were to fall within the description of the state, it will not absolve Coal India of its liability under the Competition Act. The Supreme Court has noted that Coal India ‘act with caprice, or unfairly or treat otherwise similarly situated persons or things with discrimination… We may only add that in judicial review the appellants would be held to the standard of fairness as also the duty not to discriminate.’ 130 Coal India Limited and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Anr. [2023], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 2845 of 2017, pp. 99, 100. As also discussed earlier, to treat the classes of buyers differently, Coal India ought to have had a rational business justification which could rationalise dissimilar treatment. However, per CCI, it did not have any reasonable basis to treat similarly placed buyers in a discriminatory manner. Similar to the Coal India case where Coal India had an absolute monopoly and thereby had the power to impose such unfair and discriminatory conditions, the CCI and the DG undertook a detailed exercise of analysing the various provisions of the Gas Sales Agreement and also the industry in question in the Adani Gas case. The industry dynamics involved in the present case was that Adani Gas Limited was not the producer of natural gas, but it was a supplier of natural gas. The natural gas was sourced by Adani Gas Limited from GAIL (India) Limited by entering into multiple upstream contracts with GAIL (India) Limited. This fact was taken into account by CCI while reviewing the clauses of the Gas Sales Agreement to see whether Adani Gas Limited was acting unilaterally and unfairly.131 Adani Gas case, paras 78-104. The NCLAT supported the finding that Adani Gas Limited abused its dominant position qua the Industrial Customers by imposing unfair, lopsided, unilateral and harsh conditions that were detrimental to the interests of the consumers under the Gas Sales Agreement. It also remarked that even Adani Gas Limited must have been conscious of such conditions being unfair to consumers and abusive of its dominant position which is clearly inferable from its conduct in substituting the original Gas Sales Agreement with a revised one modifying the contravening terms and conditions.132 M/s Adani Gas Limited v. Competition Commission of India and Anr, TA (AT) (Competition) No. 33 of 2017, para. 29. The aspect of unfair conditions was also discussed in the Super Cassettes case. It was alleged that Super Cassettes was imposing minimum commitment charges per month irrespective of actual needle hour (each aggregate of sixty minutes of actual broadcast of sound recordings by FM radio station excluding commercials, advertisements, voice-over, anchor time, etc.) of broadcast of the music content of Super Cassettes by the radio station. The CCI, relying on the investigation report of the DG, found that no other competitor of Super Cassettes was levying a minimum commitment charge. Further, a minimum commitment charge was required to be paid by the radio operators irrespective of the actual number of needle hours of the music of Super Cassettes that is broadcast on the radio channel. On a review of the agreements for granting licenses to the radio operators containing the provision for minimum commitment charge, it was observed that the minimum committed needle hours for the playoff of the songs of Super Cassettes imposed by it are as high as 50%. This was done to ensure that the business share of Super Cassettes in the relevant market and that if half of the total songs played by the FM stations have been fixed by Super Cassettes, the other music companies will be left with only 50% of the total market share of the relevant market. Further, the investigation had revealed that the condition of minimum commitment charge is distorting the competition in the relevant market. On the one hand, it increases the cost of music for FM radio stations as they are forced to pay extra money even if they are not playing songs of Super Cassettes, and on the other hand, it also forces FM stations to play at least the minimum guaranteed needle hour even though there is no demand of such songs from the listeners. The CCI agreed with the report of the DG and noted that minimum commitment charge is exploitative and exclusionary in nature. It is exploitative as it forces the customers to pay for music that they may not play. Exclusionary conduct is characterized by improper strengthening of market power by the dominant enterprise. The CCI thereby observed that the imposition of minimum commitment charge by Super Cassettes has an anti-competitive effect on the market as it forecloses other competitors from a substantial share of the market. Since the private radio station is contractually bound to pay Super Cassettes a minimum guarantee, they are likely to broadcast the amount of music that they have already paid for. Therefore, a certain amount of music that plays out on private FM radio stations is already fixed for Super Cassettes. This results in the competitors of Super Cassettes not being able to compete for and being foreclosed from broadcasting their music on this prefixed play out of 50% reserved for Super Cassettes. In the MMT case, an information against MMT-Go and Oravel Stays Private Limited (OYO) alleging an abuse of dominant position by indulging in predatory pricing, exorbitant commissions, etc. MMT-Go held a dominant position in the relevant market for online intermediation services for booking hotels in India. MMT imposed parity obligations through Across Platform Parity Agreements which are stipulations where the sellers guarantee online selling platform terms that are at least as favourable as those granted to any other platform, thus ensuring the former a competitive advantage over its competitors. MMT-Go imposed rate parity obligations (i.e., that a hotel will not be able to supply its room night to any other OTA at a price lesser than the price offered to MMT-Go) in conjunction with room parity obligations (i.e., at any given point of time, the hotels rooms offered for sale at MMT-Go would not be lesser than any other OTA), deep discounting strategies and exclusivity conditions. The effect of these parity obligations has been explained through the following example: Consider that Hotel A is listed on all OTAs and decides to supply a room night to MMT-Go at INR 1,000 per night. Now, Hotel A will not be able to supply the room night at a price lower than INR 1,000 per night to any other OTA. Considering that MMT-Go charges a commission of 20% from Hotel A on every room night sold through MMT-Go’s portal, Hotel A will get INR 800 per room night sold while MMT-Go will keep INR 200 per night. The same logic applies to room parity obligations as well. Based on an in-depth assessment, the CCI found that the collective operation of deep discounts (discussed under predatory pricing), exclusivity condition and parity conditions by MMT-Go created a self-reinforcing ecosystem that reinforces MMT-Go’s dominant position in the relevant market. As a result, the parity obligations increase the distribution cost for hotels and prohibit them from delisting from MMT-Go without losing considerable sales and visibility due to the following reasons: First, it helps MMT-Go to retain and further increase its network of users/travellers, who would increasingly use the platform for availing the best deals. Second, it impedes the competitive process between OTAs by limiting the competitive levers/instruments at the disposal of other portals that, for instance, cannot get better prices from hotels by offering lower commission rates. The hotels were so dependent on MMT-Go that they had no option but to accept the onerous terms. Third, the consequent adverse effect on sale of rooms through other platforms/channels and their user bases further accentuates the dependence of hotels on MMT-Go as well as the bargaining power imbalance that already exists between MMT-Go and its hotel partners. Fourth, the increased sales through MMT-Go may lead to unilaterally determined higher commissions charged by it, giving it the ability to also pass on discounts which are admittedly funded through these commissions, which may adversely impact the prices at which the hotel rooms are being offered to end consumers. The conduct of MMT-Go was, thus, found to be in contravention of section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act.133 MMT case, paras 248-266. Based on the above, we see that the approach of the CCI, while deciding whether a clause is unfair or not, is to see whether the counterparty has any choice but to accept the onerous and one-sided terms imposed by the dominant undertaking as well as its collective impact on the market. In doing so, the analysis of the clause may not be limited to the counterparty who chooses whether to complain or not but the entire market, as the aim of competition law is to protect competition in the market. This aspect was clarified by the NCLAT in the Google Android case134 Google Android case, paras 81, 82. discussed earlier, wherein Google had argued that since there was no complaint made by any OEM regarding abuse of dominance by Google, the CCI could not have analysed the impositions to which they were subjected. However, the NCLAT confirmed that CCI is statutorily obliged to promote and sustain competition, protect the interests of consumers and ensure freedom of trade for all market participants. Hence, its analysis cannot be confined only to the evidence led by countervailing parties and competitors but also extends to any conduct or arrangement concerning the interests of consumers. [B] Unfair and Discriminatory Pricing Section 4(2)(a)(ii) of the Competition Act provides that there shall be an abuse of dominant position if an enterprise or a group imposes unfair or discriminatory pricing in the purchase or sale (including predatory pricing) of goods or service. It is to be noted that there are two sets of pricing abuses: unfair and discriminatory. Unfair pricing is one where the pricing conduct of the dominant player is unfair qua the other stakeholders in the market, be it the consumers or other competitors. What is unfair or fair would depend on the facts and circumstances of each case. The CCI found on the basis of the investigation of the DG in the Play Store case, that Google has not subjected its own app to the conditions and service fee as being forced upon the other apps in violation of section 4(2)(a)(ii) of the Competition Act. These discriminatory practices are exploitative, resulting in a competitive disadvantage and harming competitors of Google in the downstream market, i.e., app developers, by increasing their cost. The app developers have two choices: either absorb this increased cost or pass on the increased cost to the end users of their apps.135 Play Store case, para. 273. The decisional practice indicates looking at the harm the pricing behaviour of the dominant enterprise causes to the ultimate consumer. Even while undertaking a dominance investigation in the case of intermediate goods which are not consumed by the final consumer, the CCI undertakes a value chain analysis and sees whether the unfair price, so charged, will have any impact on the final consumer. This kind of value chain analysis was also done by the CCI in the Coal India case.136 Coal India case, paras 158, 271. Having said that, it must be noted that the CCI looks into the aspect of unfair pricing only to see whether there is an abuse of dominant position and not to see whether any individual players are getting excluded from the market on the grounds of their own inefficiency. The wording of the section is unfair pricing, and it would cover all instances of pricing abuses like fidelity discounts, margin squeezes, etc. The CCI, in the NSE case has alluded to the efficient competitor test.137 NSE case, para. 28.2. The efficient competitor is the test where the CCI will analyse, for price-based alleged abuses, whether a competitor, which is as efficient as the dominant company, can compete against the pricing behaviour of the dominant company. The question is whether the dominant company itself would be able to survive the exclusionary conduct in the event that it would be the target. The CCI noted in the Super Cassettes case that price abuses may be ‘exclusionary’, i.e., pricing strategies adopted by dominant firms to foreclose competitors.138 Super Cassettes case, paras 196, 197. Such strategies include a wide variety of measures, such as predatory pricing, price squeezes, loyalty rebates, etc. Pricing abuses may also be ‘exploitative’, i.e., which cover instances where a dominant firm is accused of exploiting its customers by setting excessive prices. Evidently, a dominant firm, under the Competition Act, abuses its dominance if it charges ‘unfair prices’ to its customers, which includes both unfairly high or excessive price and unfairly low or predatory price. Further, the clause also provides that discriminatory pricing is also an abuse. As the name suggests, discriminatory prices are instances wherein the dominant enterprise imposes different pricing parameters for a similarly situated class of consumers without any intelligible differentia or rational business justification. Furthermore, section 4 provides that no enterprise or group will engage in any unfair pricing. Therefore, there may be situations of margin squeeze where two enterprises belonging to the same group engage in unfair tactics to remove their competitors in the downstream market, which have no upstream presence. The key part to be analysed is whether the pricing strategy adopted by the dominant enterprise, or its group, is unfair qua the competitors and consumers. The decisional practice indicates undertaking an analysis of whether the dominant enterprise, due to its position of strength, is engaged in practice where the counterparties/competitors have no option but to follow the diktat of the dominant enterprise. In the process, the market operates in the favour of the dominant enterprise. We will now discuss the practices which have been identified by the CCI, COMPAT/NCLAT as well as the Supreme Court as instances of unfair pricing. [1] Excessive Pricing The CCI, in the Automobile Spare Parts case and the Super Cassettes case, discussed the issue of excessive pricing in great detail. It relied on the jurisprudence of excessive pricing as laid down by the EC while analysing the test of excessive pricing under Article 102 (earlier 82) of the EC treaty. The CCI, in the Super Cassettes case and the Automobile Spare Parts case, relied on the United Brand case decided by the ECJ139 United Brands Co v. Commission of the European Union [1978] ECR 207. which held that a price charged is excessive if it has no reasonable relation to the economic value of the product supplied. It was noted by the CCI that the ECJ, in United Brands, proposed a cost-based test to assess the relationship between the economic value of the product/service and the price charged for it by a dominant undertaking. Based on international jurisprudence, the CCI observed that the key issue to be determined in an investigation of excessive pricing is whether the difference between the costs actually incurred and the price actually charged is excessive and, if the answer to this question is in the affirmative, whether a price has been imposed which is either unfair in itself or when compared to competing products. The CCI, relying on the United Brands case, noted that the test for a claim under excessive pricing is two-fold: the first stage aims to identify the profit margin of the dominant enterprise, and the second stage is to identify whether the said profit margin is ‘excessive’. If it is, the second stage is to consider whether the excessive price is unfairly high and consequently abusive. The first stage of the test for exploitative pricing involves calculating the difference between the production cost and the price of the product/service in order to identify the profit earned by the dominant enterprise. Although the legal test adopted by the ECJ in the United Brands case was adopted by the CCI, it noted in the Automobile Spare Parts case the practical difficulties while analysing the cost benchmarks and allocating costs. CCI noted that analysis of costs is difficult considering an enterprise may have diverse production techniques and market operations which incur various categories of costs, and working out production costs may raise great difficulties, especially determining what costs should be taken into account cost price ratio to show that price charged exceeds the costs incurred. Further, practical difficulty in ascertaining costs would include an apportionment of the indirect costs and general expenditure which may vary significantly according to the size of the enterprise under investigation, its object, the group structure, including its relationship with its subsidiaries, its territorial area of operations and whether it is a single product or a multi-product firm. A multi-product enterprise will have common and joint costs, and how to allocate such costs to each area of operations is an area of concern. The problem gets further compounded if multiple businesses are housed under a single enterprise instead of multiple enterprises in the same group. As elaborated earlier, the Automobile Spare Parts case dealt with the aspect of the non-availability of spare parts in the open market by OEMs and substantial price markup on spare parts by OEMs. The CCI undertook a detailed cost-price comparison in this case. The methodology adopted by the CCI, to determine whether there exists an abuse of excessive pricing in this case, was as follows:(a) Cost incurred by the OEMs to manufacture/source the spare parts: 140 Automobile Spare Parts case, para. 20.5.87. Based on the investigation by the DG, the CCI noted that the OEMs source their components/spare parts to be used for the assembly line requirements as well as aftermarket requirements primarily from the local OESs. Several components assembled in the car are also imported from overseas suppliers. Therefore, the cost of production of a spare part for an OEM is the price at which the spare part is sourced from the OESs or overseas suppliers. Since the OEMs source a majority of their spare parts, both for assembly line and aftermarket requirements from OESs or other overseas suppliers, a starting point for the cost price analysis used by the CCI was the price at which the spare parts are sourced by OEMs from the OESs’ and other suppliers. (b) Price charged by OEMs to analyse markup: 141 Ibid., para. 20.5.88-20.5.90. In order to analyse the mark up from the point of the OEM to the final customer, the CCI undertook an analysis of the price difference between the price at which the spare parts are sourced by the OEMs to the price charged by OEMs to the final customer. It was noticed by the CCI, based on the investigation report of the DG, that the OEMs were charging an average mark up of 100% and in some cases, the markup was as high as 5,000%. The entire objective of the price-cost analysis is to establish the profit margin of the enterprise under investigation. As such, an enterprise merely having a profit margin does not itself indicate that the price is excessive. Every enterprise will charge a markup to ensure that their business is profitable. At this point in time, such profit margin becomes a case of excessive pricing, and thereby, unfairness will be dependent on the facts and circumstances of each case. It is very difficult to put down an arithmetic test as to what percentage of the markup will be considered excessive. Section 4(2)(a)(ii) does not prohibit profit margins but prohibits unfair pricing. For instance, initial high prices can be attributable to being the reward for innovation as innovators may have invested huge sums in research and development on product development.142 Biocon Limited and Anr. v. F. Hoffmann-La Roche AG and Ors, Case No. 68 of 2016, para. 80. Similarly, the fact that a particular company is making more profits than the others can never be a competition consideration unless the company taking advantage of its dominant position starts abusing its dominance.143 Schott Glass India Pvt. Ltd v. Competition Commission of India, [2014] Comp AT 1, para. 57. Having said that, where it has been established that a price substantially exceeds the cost of production, it is necessary to assess whether the difference is so great as to be ‘excessive’, triggering a review of the fairness of the ultimate price so charged. In the Automobile Spare Parts case, the CCI noted that the ‘concept of unfairness of a price is related to the notion that such price is unrelated to the “economic value” of the product and that such price are being charged by the enterprise because of its capacity to use its market power or position of strength in that relevant market to affect its competitors or consumers in its favour [emphasis supplied]’. The CCI noted that markups of an average of 100% and in some cases, markups as high as 5,000% are disproportionate to the economic value of the products supplied by OEMs. Further, the CCI noted that a case of unfair pricing has been made out, considering the following characteristics of the industry:(a) Procurement of spare parts is necessary, irrespective of the price: The CCI noted that the actual cost of procuring the spare parts is much lower than the price at which they are being sold to the ultimate consumer. In spite of this, the value of such spare parts to the consumer is great because without such spare parts, the owner will not be able to effectively repair his automobile. The locked-in consumer is forced to purchase spare parts in order to effectively render the much more expensive primary market equipment, i.e., the automobile itself, in a workable condition. In a way, it can be said that when the demand for the goods is inelastic and, therefore, the manufacture of those goods charges a high price and earns supra normal profits in the process, such manufacturer can be said to be engaged in unfair pricing.144 Automobile Spare Parts case, para. 20.5.94. (b) Lack of effective competition in the aftermarket: The CCI noted that all the OEMs have entered into agreements with their overseas suppliers, and most of the local OESs contractually prohibit the suppliers from selling spare parts directly in the open market. Therefore, the OEMs are the only source of genuine spare parts available in the aftermarket. The OEMs require their authorized dealers to source the spare parts only from the authorized dealers of the OEMs and further restrict the over-the-counter sale of spare parts and diagnostic tools to independent repairers. Such restrictions had a twin-fold effect: (i) they created entry barriers for the OES who could produce matching quality spare parts by eliminating direct access by OES to supplying genuine spare parts of an OEM in the aftermarket. In the process, OEMs were foreclosing competition in the supply of genuine spare parts and diagnostic tools for the various models of automobiles manufactured by the OEMs; and (ii) the independent repairers were foreclosed from having genuine spare parts from the market to effectively repair the automobile. By having such a restriction, the independent repairers failed to pose an effective competitive restraint on the OEMs and their dealers in the market for repair and maintenance.145 Ibid., para. 20.5.96. (c) Reaping trading benefits that would not have been available in a competitive market: Based on these industry dynamics, the CCI opined that in order to analyse the unfairness of the prices charged by the OEMs, what has to be ascertained is whether the dominant firms have leveraged the opportunities arising out of their dominant position to reap trading benefits, i.e., economic advantages, that would otherwise have been beyond their reach in a normal and sufficiently competitive market. Further, as mentioned above, the CCI noted the fact that the OEMs are the only source of supplying spare parts for its brand of automobiles in the aftermarkets. This factor was key in analysing the enterprise’s degree and intent of exploitative pricing. In this regard, the CCI relied on the observations of the ECJ in the case of General Motors Continental NV v. Commission,146 General Motors Continental NV v. Commission [1975] ECR 1376. where it was observed that the holder of the exclusive position may abuse the market by fixing a price – for a service which it is alone in a position to provide. Even in this industry dynamic, OEMs were the sole provider of the service of repair and maintenance due to such restrictive clauses in the agreement between OEMs and the suppliers. Based on these facts, the CCI noted that the OEMs are the only source of genuine spare parts compatible with its brand of automobiles in the aftermarket which allows such OEMs to use the opportunities arising out of its dominant position to reap trading benefits which it would not have reaped if there had effective competition in the relevant market.147 Automobile Spare Parts case, para. 20.5.97. (d) No consumer benefits: As elaborated earlier, the consumers of the original equipment manufacturers (OEMS) are completely dependent on the OEMs for their spare parts requirements. Apart from the lack of alternate suppliers of spare parts, the CCI noted that there are efficiencies enjoyed by the OEMs, in the form of cost savings, in having its distribution channel not passed on to the customer in the form of low prices of spare parts. On the contrary, the spare parts are being sold at a very high markup.148 Ibid. In appeal, the COMPAT149 Toyota Kirloskar Motor Private Limited and Ors. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors., Competition Appeal Nos 60, 61 and 62 of 2014, paras 90-92. observed that the DG’s investigation adopted a relatively simplistic approach to assessing the price of the spare parts charged by the OEMs. Unfairness need not necessarily come from the price being excessive. It could also come from the special circumstances of a given situation when a monopoly has been proved and consumers have nowhere else to go. In such cases, significantly higher prices can also operate as unfair prices. In this case, there was exculpatory evidence to suggest that the prices that were charged were abnormally high. In fact, a sizable 55% of the total profits from the business were generated from the sale of spare parts alone, while the OEMs incurred losses on the sale of cars. This was possible because the margins from spare parts business exceeded the margin from car business substantially since the OEMs were able to mark up the price of spare parts without any competitive constraints. Another key point analysed by the erstwhile COMPAT was the OEMs’ attempt to justify the restrictions on account of protecting their IPR and preventing spurious/counterfeit spare parts in the market. However, the erstwhile COMPAT rejected this argument, stating that the primary reason for the large availability of counterfeit spare parts was the significantly high price of the original spare parts. If the OEMs were to make genuine spare parts available in the market at reasonable prices without any artificial constraints, then consumers will automatically be incentivized to purchase the original spare parts, thereby increasing competition in the market.150 Ibid., paras 113, 119, 156, 157. The aspect of excessive pricing was also discussed in the Super Cassettes case. In that case, CCI did not find a case of excessive pricing made out because of the practical difficulty in apportioning the cost. The CCI noticed that the price charged by Super Cassettes was higher than that charged by its competitors. Having said that, CCI said that a claim of excessive pricing cannot be made solely because the price charged by Super Cassettes is more than its competitors. This observation of the CCI is very pertinent because it shows that even after a thorough investigation by the DG and a review process by the CCI, they could not apportion the actual cost required for an excessive pricing investigation. In the Play Store case, it was alleged that Google engaged in excessive pricing by charging 15%-30% commission in the name of service fee on each transaction of in-app purchases and paid apps; this was also corroborated by the DG in the investigation report. Google, inter alia, claimed that the CCI could not act as a price-setting regulator and decide what Google Play’s service fee model ought to be. The CCI observed that Google’s monetization model is based on cross-subsidization, where 3% of the apps offering paid apps or IAPs are made to bear the entire cost of the Play Store, even though all the apps are using similar services of the Play Store. Further, Google has other revenue streams from the ‘free apps’ listed on Play Store, in the form of advertisement-related revenue earned by Google from the apps hosted on Play Store. These revenue streams are also contributing towards recoupment of the costs associated with Play Store and Android ecosystem, in addition to the service fee. However, the CCI declined to give any finding on excessive pricing due to lack of sufficient information on Google’s monetization model. Yet, it did lay down that ‘In general, any conditions (including price related conditions) imposed on business users, by the gatekeepers or platform operators, should be fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory.’ [a] The CCI Is Not a Price Regulator In its decisional practice, the CCI has observed that it is not a price regulator, and hence, it cannot set or control the prices in the market. Its purpose is to ensure a competitive market structure where prices are determined by market forces and fair competition among businesses. Price regulation can lead to incorrect prices and distort market outcomes. For instance, in Vijay Gopal v. Big Tree Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. (BookMyShow) and Others, 151 Vijay Gopal v. Big Tree Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. (BookMyShow) and Others, Case No. 46 of 2021, para. 35. it was alleged that BookMyShow was charging high convenience fee from consumers for using its ticketing portal through exclusivity agreements with cinema owners. The CCI opined that it cannot act as a price regulator to determine the correct fee. Nevertheless, it also observed that exclusivity arrangements by BookMyShow may result in the softening of competition and, therefore, bolster the market power of BookMyShow without any incentive for it to lower such fees in future. Hence, it is the impact on the competitive process and its ability to decide a fair price that has to be assessed. In Manjit Singh Sachdeva v. Director General 152 Manjit Singh Sachdeva v. Director General, Case No. 68 of 2012, para. 3. also, the CCI declined to address the issue of excessive airline ticket prices by laying down a MRP on tickets. The CCI observed that it can ‘neither go into the issue of MRP i.e., what should be the MRP for any product or service and fix the MRP, nor the Commission can give direction to the Government of India that it should fix MRP of a service being provided by private entrepreneur.’ It held that air fares should be determined by the market forces of demand and supply and fixing the price would be contrary to the spirit of competition law. The Supreme Court in Rajasthan Cylinders v. Union of India 153 Rajasthan Cylinders v. Union of India, [2019] (4) SCJ 247, para. 97. has also clarified that the CCI should not serve as a price regulator. ‘Competition authorities should not be price regulators; they should be the guardians of the competitive process.’ It noted that as a matter of policy, direct regulation of price should be a remedy of last resort. Price controls are by definition designed to suspend the operation of the market mechanism and introduce artificial intervention in the working of the free markets. Therefore, price determination by market forces of demand and supply encourages players to innovate and compete according to the conditions of competition in a free market. It was perhaps the idea behind the CCI not interfering in the pricing % in the Play Store case wherein the CCI had to enter into a price adjudication issue; rather, the CCI held that options must be made available to app developers to use the third-party payment processors of their choice and consequently, the CCI ensured that the market remains competitive. [2] Predatory Pricing Given the definition of predatory pricing as enshrined under explanation (b) to section 4 of the Competition Act, the major elements involved in the determination of predatory behaviour are:(a) pricing below cost for the relevant product in the relevant market by the dominant enterprise; (b) such low pricing is done with the intention of reducing competition or eliminating competitors. This is traditionally known as the predatory intent test. For determination of ‘Cost’, the CCI passed the CCI (Determination of Cost of Production) Regulations, 2009, (hereinafter referred to as ‘Cost Regulations’). Further, the Cost Regulations provide for a proxy cost benchmark which will be used as a benchmark for predatory price investigations and shall generally be the average variable cost. Regulation 3 of the Cost Regulations provides that. [3] Determination of Cost (1) ‘Cost’ in the Explanation to section 4 of the Act shall, generally, be taken as an average variable cost, as a proxy for marginal cost: Provided that in specific cases, for reasons to be recorded in writing, the Commission may, depending on the nature of the industry, market and technology used, consider any other relevant cost concept such as avoidable cost, long run average incremental cost, market value. Based on Regulation 3(1) of Cost Regulations, ‘Cost’ in the Explanation to section 4 of the Act shall, generally, be taken as an average variable cost as a proxy for marginal cost. Having said that, the Cost Regulations give the Competition Commission of India the flexibility to adopt any other cost methods, such as average avoidable cost, long-run average incremental cost or market value, depending on the concerned industry. Regulation 2(c) of the Cost Regulations provides a definition of the various cost benchmarks which may be followed in a predatory pricing investigation:(a) ‘total cost’ means the actual cost of production, including items, such as cost of material consumed, direct wages and salaries, direct expenses, work overheads, quality control cost, research and development cost, packaging cost, finance and administrative overheads attributable to the product during the referred period; (b) ‘total variable cost’ means the total cost minus the fixed cost and share of fixed overheads, if any, during the referred period; (c) ‘total avoidable cost’ means the cost that could have been avoided if the enterprise had not produced the quantity of extra output during the referred period; (d) ‘average avoidable cost’ is the total avoidable cost divided by the total output considered for estimating ‘total avoidable cost’; (e) ‘long run average incremental cost’ is the increment to long-run average cost on account of an additional unit of product, where long-run cost includes both capital and operating costs; (f) ‘market value’ means the consideration which the customer pays or agrees to pay for a product which is sold or provided or can be sold or provided, as the case may be. (2) In arriving at the cost figures, the Commission/Director General may take the help of suitable experts. However, the Cost Regulations provide that CCI, depending on the industry in question, may adopt an alternate definition of costing to appropriately define the cost benchmark while determining a case of predatory pricing. There has only been one decided case by the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT on predatory pricing, which was the NSE case. Therein, they did not delve into actual cost benchmark because NSE was providing complete transaction waivers for transactions in the currency derivative segment. Thus, it was a case of zero pricing being charged by the NSE. Therefore, the approach adopted by both the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT was that zero pricing is a case of unfair pricing without identifying any cost benchmark. Such an approach was taken because zero pricing would be lesser than any cost benchmark, be it average variable cost, average avoidable cost or long-run incremental cost. The relevant facts on the pricing abuse were that NSE, being the super dominant enterprise, was providing complete transaction waivers, i.e., zero pricing on trading done in the currency derivative segment. It is to be noted that NSE has been given the licence by the Indian stock market regulator to operate on all segments of the stock exchange, i.e., the equity segment, futures and options segment, wholesale debt market segment and the currency derivative segment. It is to be noted that the key revenue stream of the stock exchange is the transaction fee. Having said that, since NSE was giving complete waivers on any transaction done in the currency derivative segment, MCX-SX and other competitors, who had the licence to operate in the currency derivative segment only, had no option but to follow suit and provide complete waivers to remain competitive in the market. Further, such waivers were also coupled with other exclusionary tactics like low deposit fees, etc. In the process, MCX-SX faced huge losses to the point of great financial depletion which would have caused MCX-SX to exit the market. During the deliberations of the same before the CCI and COMPAT, the NSE took the argument that it does not incur any variable cost in operating in the currency derivative segment and hence zero pricing is not below cost. The erstwhile COMPAT did not agree with the fact that NSE did incur any variable cost, looking into the investigation report prepared by the DG. Based on the same, the erstwhile COMPAT noted that the action of providing complete transaction waivers by the NSE was below cost. Both the CCI and the erstwhile COMPAT observed that it is not required (in light of the facts of the NSE case) to delve into the appropriate cost benchmark since zero pricing will be below any cost benchmark adopted. It is apposite to note that the approach of both the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT was to show how the behaviour of NSE was a case of broader abuse of unfair pricing without going into too many technical details of cost benchmark to be adopted, as required for a predatory pricing claim. The CCI opined on when a price charged by a dominant enterprise can be regarded as unfair. By way of their approach, the CCI seems to have relied on the efficient competitor test. The CCI noted that the behaviour by NSE of providing complete transaction waivers was a case of unfair pricing, an abuse under section 4(2)(a)(ii) of the Competition Act because the act was unfair qua its competitors. The CCI noted that the term ‘unfair’ mentioned in section 4(2) of the Competition Act is to be examined either in the context of unfairness in relation to a customer or in relation to a competitor. The test that was adopted was whether the dominant company itself would be able to survive the exclusionary conduct in the event that it would be the target. Relying on the report prepared by the DG, the CCI noted that NSE has deep pockets which allowed it to carry on giving transaction fee waivers over a prolonged period of time in the currency derivative segment. In fact, the CCI noted that, even after providing transaction waivers, NSE was earning supra-normal profits because of its operations in other segments, whereas MCX-SX are incurring huge losses by giving such waivers. Based on the same, it was observed that zero pricing by NSE was a clear case of unfair pricing. The relevant observations of the CCI are as follows:As discussed above, NSE has a position of strength which has enabled it to resort to zero pricing since August, 2008. MCX-SX does not have such strength or deep pockets. There is practically no justifiable reason for NSE to continue offering its services free of charge for such a long duration when it is paying for manpower and other resources for running the business. It is also a fact that no enterprise would have the intention to engage in a profit-less venture for eternity. MCS-SX, which operates only in the CD segment, has no other source of income. This is a major constraint. In these circumstances, the zero price policy of NSE cannot be termed as anything but unfair. If this Commission were to treat it as fair, it would go against the grain of the Competition Act and betray the economic philosophy behind it. If even zero pricing by dominant player cannot be interpreted as unfair, while its competitor is slowly bleeding to death, then this Commission would ever be able to prevent any form of unfair pricing including predatory pricing in future. Had NSE and MCX-SX been on equal footing in terms of resources directly available, spectrum and scale of operation, nationwide presence, length of existence etc. perhaps perception of unfairness would not have been so blatant and impossible to ignore, but in this case, the sense of the two being equal or even almost equal does not exist. Therefore, this Commission concludes that the zero price policy of NSE in the relevant market is unfair. In this case, the conduct of zero pricing by the NSE is beyond the parameters of promotional or penetrative pricing. It can, in fact, be termed as annihilating or destructive pricing. When the matter went up in appeal, the erstwhile COMPAT upheld the reasoning of the CCI and also ruled that NSE engaged in unfair pricing, considering the position of strength that NSE derived in the relevant market. NSE offered no justification to continue with the predatory pricing except the inference that it intended to eliminate MCX-SX from the market. NSE attempted to draw a distinction between zero-pricing, as a facet of unfairly low price versus predatory pricing. However, the erstwhile COMPAT observed that for unfairly low price also, there has to be some price somewhere, which is certainly more than zero. In other words, even if NSE was offering its services free of charge, that does not mean that these services did not have any cost. Hence, NSE’s conduct was a clear-cut example of predatory pricing, and its policy was well-intentioned to oust the competitors from the market.154 NSE case, paras 92-96. In the Ola case, investigation was initiated because of information filed by Fast Track Call Cab Private Limited which alleged that Ola cabs, being the dominant player in the city of Bangalore, was engaging in predatory pricing. Materials were placed on record to show how Ola Cabs was giving unrealistic invectives to drivers and very low fares to consumers. However, Ola was not found to be dominant on account of competitive constraints posed by Uber, which was engaging in the same strategy of below-cost pricing. Yet, CCI analysed the conduct of unfair pricing prevalent in the relevant market and expressed its hesitation to interfere in the market that was at a nascent stage and yet to evolve. Some relevant observations are as follows:155 Ola case, paras 101, 122-123. (i) Ola did not initiate the strategy of aggressive pricing, and its negative indexed margin since June 2014 seems more to be a reactive strategy to Uber’s aggressive pricing. Further, Uber’s loss in the relevant market has remained substantially higher than Ola’s loss except for a brief period from December 2014 to January 2015. Moreover, Ola’s response to the aggressive pricing strategy of Uber in the relevant market since June 2014 is indicative of the competitive constraint posed to it by Uber. (ii) ‘The Commission does not fully disagree with the Informants that the low prices of OP are not because of cost efficiency, but because of the funding it has received from the private equity funds.’ However, there was no evidence that access to such funding was inequitable, not competitive and had aberrations. Moreover, it was their penetrative pricing strategy that facilitated them to garner high market shares in a short span of time as well as develop the networks to a size that could provide sufficient positive externalities to the participants of the network. (iii) The long-term impact of this pricing strategy is difficult to determine at this stage as the market is yet to mature. ‘Without going into the legitimacy of OP’s pricing strategy, suffice to say that besides statutory compulsion of non-intervention in the present case, as OP is not dominant in the relevant market, the Commission is hesitant to interfere in a market, which is yet to fully evolve’. It is an interesting proposition whether CCI would have intervened to stop the predatory pricing if the market was not at a nascent stage. In almost an extension to the CCI’s reasoning, the NCLAT observed that although it was clear that the major discounts and incentives offered by Ola coincided with its rapid spurts in market share, it essentially engaged in a practice of increasing supply to meet the demand to create a win-win situation for riders and drivers. Ola first expanded its market share by generating demand through customer discounts, and once there was a good increase in demand, it started offering drivers incentives. Thus, Ola’s pricing strategy was in response to market conditions, helped by a heavy infusion of funding. Further, Uber was a significant competitor in the relevant market, and Ola was found to be responding to the pricing actions of Uber while trying to establish itself as a relatively new entrant in the market. On this basis, the NCLAT concluded that OLA’s conduct to establish/consolidate its brand in the market and increase its market share simultaneously was not below-cost pricing for any sustained period of time which could be labelled as predatory pricing and abuse of its dominant position.156 Meru Travels Solutions Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Anr. [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 19 of 2017. para. 39. Rather, it was to establish itself as an effective and reliable brand in the market and also open up a latent market to its advantage through awareness generation about its brand and network/platform through promotional initiatives like discounts and incentives, attracting new customers and gaining riders’ confidence. Thus, the CCI and NCLAT have in effect, observed that it may be an effective and legitimate business strategy to engage in predation to create a market. Similarly, an investigation was ordered against Uber for prima facie engaging in predatory pricing because Uber was losing INR 204 per trip in respect of every trip made by the cars of the fleet owners due to the fidelity discounts offered to the drivers to keep them attached to Uber’s network, which does not make any economic sense other than pointing to Uber’s intent to eliminate competition in the market during the period of assessment.157 Meru Travels Solutions Private Limited v. Competition Commission of India, (COMPAT) Competition Appeal No. 31 of 2016. Though the CCI had found that Uber was not dominant, the erstwhile COMPAT directed the DG to cause an investigation on the basis that there were good reasons for a prima facie case. Uber challenged the order of the erstwhile COMPAT before the Supreme Court, which dismissed the challenge. Although eventually, Uber was not found to be guilty of predatory pricing because it was not found to be dominant in the market owing to the highly fluctuating market shares of Uber and Ola,158 Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. Uber India Systems Pvt. Ltd. and Others, Case No. 96 of 2015. the observations of the Supreme Court on predatory pricing are relevant.159 Uber India Systems Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2019], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 641 of 2017. It noted that if, in fact, a loss is made for trips made, section 4(2)(a)(ii) ‘would prima facie be attracted inasmuch as this would certainly affect the appellant’s competitors in the appellant’s favour or the relevant market in its favour. Insofar as “abuse” of dominant position is concerned, under section 4(2)(a), so long as this dominant position, whether directly or indirectly, imposes an unfair price in purchase or sale including predatory price of services, abuse of dominant position also gets attracted. Explanation (b) which defines “predatory price” means sale of services at a price which is below cost.’ It must be noted herein that the NCLAT has interpreted the observation of the Supreme Court in the Uber case as a qualifier for a view for conducting an investigation; however, as a final conclusion, NCLAT has ruled that engaging in pricing below cost may have some rational business justification. It is to be noted that there are two limbs to the definition of predatory pricing under the Competition Act: (a) pricing below cost, as determined under the Cost Regulations; and (b) the pricing below cost was undertaken with a view to reduce competition or eliminate the competitors. There are two distinct expressions which are used in the second limb of the definition, i.e., reduce competition or eliminate the competitors. While the former refers to reduction in the degree of competitive pressure exerted by competitors, the latter refers to exit of competitors. Further, the use of the terms ‘with a view’ used in the definition of predatory pricing contemplates an objective standard that any rational business undertaking would view, given the circumstances. The Indian Parliament, while selecting the words of law, is well aware of its consequences and the term ‘with a view’ does not contemplate the degree of proof of intent followed in criminal law. Therefore, the CCI has to undertake an intention analysis to see whether the action of the dominant enterprise to price their goods below cost was with a view to eliminating competition. The key questions that need to be asked while deriving intention would be:(a) whether the pricing behaviour only makes commercial sense or is it a part of a predatory strategy or; (b) are there also other reasonable explanations? Is there an actual or likely exclusionary effect, the scale, duration and continuity of such low pricing? Thus, providing free or below-cost services cannot by itself raise competition concerns unless the same is offered by a dominant enterprise and shown to be tainted with an anti-competitive objective of excluding competition/competitors.160 Bharti Airtel Limited v. Reliance Industries Limited and Anr., Case No. 03 of 2017, para. 22. The aspect of intention was also deliberated in detail in the NSE case, both by the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT, that the strategy of NSE in adopting transaction fee waivers only in the currency derivative segment was specifically targeting reducing the competitive pressures exerted by MCX-SX and other competitors. Based on the same, NSE was found to be engaging in unfair pricing. The relevant observations made by both authorities while analysing the conduct of NSE are as follows:(a) It was argued by NSE that their action of providing transaction fee waivers was to promote the new currency derivative segment and penetrate the new market. However, this contention was rejected. It was noted by the CCI that zeal or foresight for the development of a new product market can definitely lead to initiatives such as promotional or penetrative pricing. However, such practice of promotional pricing can be understandable for a period of a few months or even a year and not for a continued prolonged period of more than three years. The CCI noted that the continuance of such pricing practice well into the third year of existence of a market can only be seen as an instance of astute strategy for market capture or extreme commercial self-interest. Further, the erstwhile COMPAT also relied on the investigation report of the DG, which noted that for the other segments, NSE did not follow such a policy of promotional pricing when those segments were first introduced. Therefore, it was observed that NSE does not have a policy of providing transaction fees to develop the market, but they selectively gave repeated waivers in the currency derivative segment for a prolonged period of time with the intention of eliminating competition from the market. (b) The erstwhile COMPAT also noticed that the NSE was giving opaque and repeated waivers of transaction fees. As stated earlier, transaction fees are the major source of revenue stream for any stock exchange. NSE was giving repeated waivers, and MCX-SX had no option but to follow suit and in the process incur huge losses. The erstwhile COMPAT observed that as on 20 May 2009 (date when section 4 of the Competition Act came into effect), there were only two major players: NSE and MCX-SX. NSE knew that MCX-SX only operated in the currency derivatives segment, making them direct competitors, even if we only consider the relevant market to be currency derivatives. NSE offered zero transaction fees, and it was clear that if they continued with this, MCX-SX would have no choice but to follow suit, which is exactly what happened. MCX-SX had to maintain zero transaction fees to stay competitive. Consequently, in the first year, MCX-SX suffered significant losses of INR 29.81 crores, while NSE made a profit of INR 515.54 crores. It was well-known worldwide that MCX-SX only had a license to operate in the CD segment, so if they stuck with zero transaction fees, which was a direct result of NSE’s policy, they would face severe financial difficulties. Even though they managed to survive, this was not a justification for NSE’s conduct. NSE ignored section 4 of the Competition Act and continued with its policy of zero transaction fees even after the provision came into effect. Analysing this situation, it was evident that MCX-SX couldn’t effectively compete with NSE due to NSE’s pricing strategy. Hence, there was substantial evidence that proved that NSE’s prices had the potential to push out MCX-SX, their only competitor at the time, as well as any other competitors without NSE’s resources. This shows that NSE had the intent of predation, and hence, it was found to be in violation of section 4(2)(a)(ii) of the Competition Act. In the MMT case, it was alleged that MMT-Go offered deep discounts on hotel rooms and provided rooms at prices which are impossible for the hotels to themselves offer, thereby eliminating competition as well as tarnishing the reputation of the hotels. During the investigation, the DG found that the average room revenue was less than the average variable cost during the period under investigation, and MMT-Go was found to have incurred a net variable loss on per room night basis. On this basis, the DG concluded that MMT-Go engaged in deep discounting and predatory pricing. However, CCI concluded that platforms often incur lack of profitability to build network effects through continuous investment to attract both sides of the platform to interact and, thus, create value/demand for other users to join the platform. Other OTAs were also incurring losses or facing heavy competition as they invested in the growing market. But, such lack of profitability does not necessarily imply predatory pricing. Thus, the CCI held that a finding of predation based on simple cost categorization is not substantiated by the data and evidence presented in the Investigation Report.161 MMT case, paras 273, 274. Therefore, an analysis of CCI’s decisional practice reveals that CCI has always exercised restraint in concluding whether a certain price is unfair or not to avoid being perceived as a price regulator. Its main goal is to eliminate any bottlenecks in the market, free up the market and in the process, facilitate healthy competition which can allow the market to naturally influence prices. [4] Differential Pricing As mentioned above, section 4(2)(a)(ii) provides for two prohibited pricing practices: unfair and discriminatory pricing. Unfair pricing would cover all kinds of pricing abuses like predatory pricing, excessive pricing, etc. Differential, as the name suggests, would mean differentiating among similar kinds of customers on pricing without any intelligible differentia. In Asianet Digital Network (P) Ltd. v. Star India Private Limited and Ors, 162 Asianet Digital Network (P) Ltd. v. Star India Private Limited and Ors., Case No. 09 of 2022. the CCI considered the price discrimination practised by Star India Private Limited (SIPL). The informant was a MSO who received broadcasting signals from SIPL for monetary consideration for supplying the channels of SIPL to customers. The relevant regulatory framework provided that the subscription fee for the same would be determined strictly according to the Interconnection Regulations 2017, no extra benefits will be offered to any MSO and the maximum permissible discount is capped at 35% (a minimum of 20% distribution fee and other marketing discounts of maximum 15%). However, the informant started losing subscribers to Kerala Communicators Cable Limited (KCCL) as the latter was able to offer low prices to subscribers. This was because KCCL was getting the channels at about 30% of the MRP with about 70% discount (special discounts of up to 50% added with a distribution fee of 20%). The resultant impact was that the informant was constrained to price its channels higher than KCCL and ultimately paid the price by losing consumers consistently, whereas KCCL gained new consumers. Thus, SIPL was found to have been providing a bouquet of channels to the competitor of the informant at lesser prices, resulting in a denial of market access and also amounting to unfair/discriminatory pricing. Hence, it can be said that by offering different subscription and distribution fees to similarly placed cable operators, SIPL discriminated between the two. The result of that discrimination could be quantified in the increased prices which the informant had to pass on to the consumer, thereby losing its subscriber base. The adverse impact of differential pricing can, therefore, be seen on the entire market: consumers (who have to bear high prices or poor services at the same price and end up dropping such service providers), the competitor (who is resultantly forced to leave the market) and the market (which loses on healthy competition and choice of service providers). [a] Meeting Competition Defence It must be noted that the statutory scheme of the Competition Act provides for meeting competition defence on the part of the dominant enterprise. The meeting competition law defence can be availed by the dominant enterprise in both unfair pricing investigation and unfair terms investigation, discussed in detail earlier. Explanation to section 4(2)(a) reads as:For the purposes of this clause, the unfair or discriminatory condition in purchase or sale of goods or service referred to in sub-clause (i) and unfair or discriminatory price in purchase or sale of goods (including predatory price) or service referred to in sub-clause (ii) shall not include such condition or price163 The words ‘discriminatory condition or price’ were substituted by the words ‘condition or price’ via the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023. which may be adopted to meet the competition. Previously, section 4(2)(a) allowed dominant enterprises to engage in ‘discriminatory’ pricing if such discriminatory pricing has been adopted to meet the competition in the market. This limited the scope of the defence and suggested that dominant enterprises are free to use only discriminatory condition or price to meet competition. It did not allow the dominant enterprise to adopt unfair pricing as a part of the meeting competition defence. So, in determining the pricing of its product, a dominant enterprise could not engage in pricing below cost just because a competitor was pricing its product below cost. This was modified by way of the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, by extending it to unfair price as well, besides discriminatory prices. Meeting competition has not yet been argued in any of the cases before the CCI to date. As such, the Act provides for an inherent right which is given to dominant undertakings to take any action to protect their own commercial interest. However, the defence of meeting competition ought to be given a very strict interpretation and action by the dominant enterprise must be strictly proportionate to the legitimate commercial objective being pursued. This is especially so considering the special responsibility of a dominant player to prevent their conduct from impairing genuine undistorted competition. It must be noted that even the dominant enterprise must have the right to defend its own commercial and economic interest in the face of action taken by certain competitors. In other words, to minimize the short-run losses resulting directly from competitors’ actions can be a legitimate aim. This automatically implies that an objective justification is not possible if the dominant company is not able to show that its conduct is only a response to differential pricing by others or if the CCI demonstrates that the objective aim of the conduct is to foreclose competitors directly. In order to fulfil the proportionality test, the dominant company must in the first-place show that the chosen conduct is a suitable way to achieve the legitimate aim of meeting competition. Suffice it to say, as noted by the CCI in the Ola case,164 Ola case, para. 98. wherein a case of unfair pricing was not found due to severe competition among the players in the market, regulatory intervention is not warranted to either protect the existing players or to increase the number of players in the market when the conditions of competition are fair. Therefore, competition is not an end in itself; it is a means towards a greater end which presumes that competition in or for the market, inter alia, leads to desirable outcomes for the consumers, ensuring a wide variety of quality products/services at the best possible prices. [C] Limiting or Restricting Production and Technical Development The Competition Act provides that there shall be an abuse of dominant position if the dominant player limits or restricts the production of goods or provision of services. Although this provision was not specifically discussed in the Automobile Spare Parts case, it could have been argued that OEMs, by entering into restrictive agreements with the suppliers and prohibiting the supply of spare parts to the independent repairers, are limiting the provision of repair services. This clause is worded in a very broad manner, and it would include all practices, whether by way of entering into restrictive covenants with channel partners or other commercial partners, which limit the production of goods or provision of services. The aspect of limiting technical development was also discussed in the Google Android case. In the Google Android case, Google imposed anti-fragmentation obligations, i.e., the OEMs were prohibited from taking any actions and/or allowing/encouraging any third party to take any action that may cause or result in the fragmentation of Android. Thus, for example, OEMs using alternative OS could lose their access to Google’s proprietary apps because this could be viewed by Google as a fragmentation of Android.165 Google Android case, paras 34, 120. This resulted in reducing the incentives of market participants to develop Android forks, providing smart mobile devices with distinctive features and additional functionalities, i.e., reduced consumer choice. Moreover, Android fork developers would have an incentive to set up credible and efficient systems to ensure the correct functioning of apps on devices running their Android fork, leading to a wider pool of apps. However, the obligations imposed by Google which extended to the entire portfolio of the devices, restricted technical and scientific development of smart device OSs by thwarting potential development attempts of OEMs that could have borne fruits and added to the variety of OSs in the market. Hence, the restrictions imposed by Google deprive the market of the fruits of fair competition in contravention of section 4(2)(b)(ii) of the Competition Act.166 Ibid., paras 504-583. Google was also found to be in violation of the provisions of section 4(2)(b)(ii) of the Competition Act in the Play Store case because the CCI held that mandatory imposition of GPBS. Through this policy, Google forced all app developers on its platform to exclusively process all app and in-app purchases on the Play Store through its proprietary billing system GPBS. For this, Google also charged a commission of 15%-30% on each transaction. As a result, competing payment processors were no longer allowed to offer their services and app developers were denied the benefits of free competition, such as competition on reducing the commission fee, better services, etc. The CCI found that GPBS disturbs innovation incentives and the ability of both the payment processors and app developers to undertake technical development and innovate, thus limiting technical development in the market for in-app payment processing services.167 Play Store case, para. 313. [D] Denial of Market Access Denial of market access under section 4(2)(c) requires that there should be a conduct and, second, that the conduct should have resulted in a denial of market access, i.e., anti-competitive effect/distortion in the market in which denial has taken place.168 XYZ v. REC Power Distribution Company Ltd, Case No. 33 of 2014. In its decisional practice, the CCI has also clarified that though free entry is one of the necessary conditions for competition to flourish, in view of the specificities of certain sectors, entry may be subject to regulatory conditions. However, such denial must be evaluated on a case-by-case basis by considering the legitimate regulatory goals, such as quality, safety, orderly growth of the sector, etc., to ensure that there is no anti-competitive conduct without a plausible regulatory rationale.169 Surinder Singh Barmi v. Board for Control of Cricket in India, Case No. 61 of 2010. In East India Petroleum Pvt. Ltd. v. South Asia LPG Company Pvt. Ltd., 170 East India Petroleum Pvt. Ltd. v. South Asia LPG Company Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 76 of 2011. issues were raised regarding access to LPG terminal infrastructure at Vishakhapatnam Port, which was owned by South Asia LPG Company Pvt. Ltd. (SALPG). SALPG required users to necessarily use the cavern and pay higher charges. Basis the investigation of the DG, the CCI found that the restrictions levied by SALPG constituted an unfair imposition in the provision of terminalling services and were likely to discourage imports and restrict the services otherwise offered by the informant, i.e., East India Petroleum Pvt. Ltd. (EIPL), which was also a provider of terminalling services. Because of these restrictions, SALPG did not allow EIPL to connect its apparatus to SALPG’s infrastructure or allow the use of its infrastructure on a standalone basis. This prevented EIPL from receiving its cargo, thereby causing foreclosure. SALPG argued that the restrictions on access to terminalling infrastructure were justified as they ensured the safety and technical feasibility of the entire apparatus and created business efficiencies. However, these efficiency justifications advanced by SALPG were held to be unfounded since they ignored the inefficiencies/losses resulting from the prohibition of bypass of cavern and the resultant foreclosure of competition. Since the bypass restrictions were found to have restricted the business volumes of the EIPL without any reasonable grounds, it was also held to be a denial of market access in contravention of section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act. Subsequently, the NCLAT also upheld the reasoning of the CCI and noted that SALPG’s plea that the services offered by other players are less efficient was an afterthought, and the restrictions were imposed by it primarily to protect its commercial interest at the cost of competition.171 Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India & Ors., Competition Appeal (AT) No. 69 of 2018. [1] Freedom to Choose Trading Partners In the GMR case, while analysing the abuse of dominance by GMR in giving preference to its group entity GAT in the downstream market over the informant, the CCI made a general observation regarding the ability of every entity, including a dominant player, to choose trading partners of their choice. Accordingly, the CCI observed that:As a general principle, every entity, dominant or otherwise, has freedom to choose its trading partners. It is a basic and fundamental right to decide with whom an enterprise wishes to deal and as such refusal to deal is not per se sufficient to conclude contravention of the provisions of the Act. However, there is no dearth of literature and decided case laws to provide guidance on when a refusal to deal by a dominant enterprise/undertaking constitutes an abuse. ‘Para 76 of the Guidance on the Commission’s enforcement priorities in applying Article 82 of the EC Treaty (now Article 102 TFEU) to abusive exclusionary conduct by dominant undertakings [2009] OJ C 045/7 provides the following:‘Typically, competition problems arise when the dominant undertaking competes on the “downstream” market with the buyer whom it refuses to supply.
