The term “downstream market” is used to refer to the market for which the refused input is needed in order to manufacture a product or provide a service.’ However, every refusal to deal by a dominant enterprise may not amount to a contravention of the Competition Act. For instance, a manufacturer can protect the sanctity of its distribution channel and goodwill in relation to the goods/services offered under its brand names, i.e., it has no legal obligation to warrant genuineness of products/services outside its distribution channel, and any insistence that the same be bought from its authorized distributors in itself cannot prima facie be considered abusive/exclusionary. Hence, there is no inherent right for third parties to become an authorized distributor in such a case.172 Hexa Communications Private Limited v. Atos India Private Limited, Case No. 07 of 2022, para. 52. Dominant firms also have the autonomy to choose their trading partners as long as the exercise of such autonomy does not affect the fair functioning of the markets as based on their market power, their conduct on refusal to deal may lead to foreclosure of market for other players.173 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Britannia Industries Ltd., Case No. 18 of 2021; Mr. Dushyant v. National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories, Case No. 48 of 2021, para. 18. A refusal, either total or partial, could also have underlying valid justifications with commercial consideration. Hence, the CCI cannot substitute its regulatory wisdom for that of the commercial wisdom of the businesses unless the conduct is ex facie unfair and/or discriminatory and designed with a view to exclude/eliminate competition on merits.174 Hiveloop Technology Pvt. Ltd. v. Parle Products Private Limited, Case No. 28 of 2021. The key point is assessing whether the refusal to deal has an anti-competitive effect on the entire market. As with other abusive conduct, competition law is concerned with the impact on the entire market as opposed to a few players exiting the market, especially because of their own inefficiencies. At the same time, this freedom to choose trading partners is not unrestrained. This brings us to the concept of essential facilities doctrine. The language used in the provision is ‘denial of market access’ and not entry, which means that the concern is not mere entry into the market but also accessibility of the market. This distinction is crucial because it shifts the focus from the mere act of entering a market to the broader issue of whether market participants can readily and fairly access that market. In practical terms, this means that it’s not just about being able to set up shop in a market but also about being able to operate, compete, and access the necessary facilities or resources to do so. Hence, the doctrine may not be applicable with the same force in the Indian scenario. [2] Essential Facilities Doctrine By way of section 4(2)(c), the legislature wanted to introduce the aspect of essential facilities into the Indian context. The CCI came out with an advisory booklet on the issue of denial of market access and essential facilities doctrine. The CCI, in the booklet, observed that the presence of entry barriers preventing new businesses from entering a specific market poses a significant challenge to promoting healthy competition. In situations where a dominant enterprise controls an infrastructure or facility necessary for accessing the market, and this infrastructure is not easily replicable at a reasonable cost in the short term and can’t be substituted with other products or services, that enterprise should generally share this facility with its competitors at a fair cost unless there are valid justifications to do otherwise. This principle is known as the essential facilities doctrine. In the GMR case, the CCI found that the informant was essentially a ‘buyer’ (a customer) of GMR for availing access to the location/space at RGIA which seems to be a necessary ‘input’ for providing Line Maintenance Services. Although the CCI took note of the freedom of all enterprises including dominant players to choose their own trading partners, it highlighted that a dominant player cannot refuse access to an input if it is indispensable or non-substitutable and cannot be obtained through an alternative source. This brings us to three guiding conditions for deciding if a refusal to deal by a dominant entity constitutes a contravention of the Competition Act: (i) first, the refused input is indispensable for an entity in order to compete on the downstream market; (ii) second, refusal shall most likely eliminate competition on the downstream market; and (iii) third, refusal shall most likely damage consumers. This is a simplified consideration of essential facilities doctrine. In the GMR case, the CCI held on the basis of existing jurisprudence of essential facilities doctrine that an asset is considered to be essential facility if it fulfils certain main economic conditions. These conditions and GMR’s corresponding fulfilment of the same have been mentioned below:(a) The dominant entity controls access to an essential facility – RGIA was an essential facility since it is not possible for the Line Maintenance Service provider to station itself outside the airport premises as the service is required between the landing and take-off of the aircraft. (b) The facility cannot reasonably be duplicated by the competitor – The level of competition in the downstream market is low since apart from the informant, other dedicated service provider of Line Maintenance Services is GMR’s own group company, GAT. The informant and GAT were the most significant players in the downstream market, posing competitive constraints on each other. The remaining players are either small or provide such services only for captive consumption so as not to provide any effective competitive constraint in the downstream market. (c) The dominant entity denies access to the competitor – Given that physical presence at the airport premises is indispensable to provide Line Maintenance Services, non-renewal of the informant’s license by GMR prima facie seems to amount to the Informant’s exclusion from the downstream market, which may skew the market in favour of GAT. The informant also alleged that GMR had made attempts to poach its technically skilled employees and disabled its electricity supply, apart from finally locking the said premises. (d) There should be no alternative means of entering the relevant market at a reasonable cost without having access to the essential facility – For providing Line Maintenance Services, the service provider needs to be located at the airport since the services are rendered during the intermittent interval between the arrival of an aircraft and its next take-off, requiring the service provider to be physically present at the airport to attend to such aircraft on an immediate basis. (e) There must be spare capacity on the facility in question – The provision of Line Maintenance Services was always undertaken through licenses with third parties, and airlines could choose their service provider. However, GMR terminated the license to refuse access to the facility, even though spare capacity existed. Consequently, the CCI found a prima facie case of denial of market access since all the conditions for refusal to deal, i.e., indispensable nature of input, elimination of competitors and damage to consumers, were made out. In the Google Android case, it was found that Google restricted access to its application programming interfaces, i.e., its proprietary software which is essential for the functionality of apps in the Google Play Store as well as other core Android services. Accordingly, the CCI directed Google to provide access to the same to its competitors, app developers and OEMs without going through necessary technical and commercial engagement with Google. However, the NCLAT subsequently set aside this direction on the grounds that such proprietary software must fetch value for Google and, therefore, remain an incentive for a technological company to further carry out such development and monetize it through its commercial use. This has been discussed in more detail in Chapter 5. In Informant (Confidential) v. Grasim Industries Limited, 175 Informant (Confidential) v. Grasim Industries Limited, Case Nos 51, 54 & 56 of 2017, paras 32, 33. the CCI, while dealing with access to VSF yarn which was an indispensable input in the VSF spinning industry, noted that dominant enterprises have a special responsibility with respect to the supply in the market. This is because if dominant firms deny access to an indispensable input, players may be driven out of the market/marginalized and hence, such conduct would amount to exclusionary abuse and refusal to deal. [3] ‘In Any Manner’: Expression of Wide Import The CCI, by way of its booklets and decisional practice, wanted to interpret this provision in an extremely broad manner to cover all practices wherein the dominant enterprise refuses to share the essential facility, not only with its competitors but also indulges in practice which results in denial in market access for any the downstream markets. However, the erstwhile COMPAT in the case of M/s Fast way transmission Pvt. Ltd. v. Kansan News Pvt. Ltd., 176 M/s Fast way transmission Pvt. Ltd. v. Kansan News Pvt. Ltd., (COMPAT) Appeal No. 116 of 2012. attempted to reduce the ambit of the interpretation adopted by the CCI for this section by holding that section 4(2)(c) would only apply in case of denial of access to a competitor. The logic given was that if two players are not competing with each other in equivalent products, transactions or services, then they could not engage in denial of market to the other. However, the Supreme Court reversed the findings of the erstwhile COMPAT on this interpretation of denial of market access.177 Competition Commission of India v. M/s Fast Way Transmission Pvt Ltd and Ors. [2018], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 7215 of 2014, paras 13-16. The words ‘in any manner’ in section 4(a)(c) are of wide import, so the logic of the erstwhile COMPAT that broadcasters and MSOs cannot be in competition with each other is incorrect. This means that it is not necessary for the players in the market to be dealing in equivalent or identical products, transactions or services to cause denial of market access to the other. Once an enterprise has been held to be dominant, it is irrelevant who it is in competition with. The Supreme Court found that dominant position under section 4 was clearly made out on account of 85% market share, and this dominance was abused by causing denial of market access to a broadcaster. At the same time, it set aside the penalty levied by the CCI on the ground that the reason for termination given by the respondents was otherwise justifiable. The observations of the Supreme Court on the denial of market access are as follows:The words ‘in any manner’ are words of wide import and must be given their natural meaning. This being the case, it is difficult to appreciate the reasoning of the Appellate Tribunal that, as the broadcaster and MSOs are not in competition with one another, the provisions of Sections 3 and 4 do not get attracted. As has been held by us, the ‘dominant position’ held by the respondent MSOs is clearly established for the purpose of Section 4 in the present case, and the Commission finding in that behalf is also not set aside by the Appellate Tribunal. If this be so, then once a dominant position is made out on facts, whether a broadcaster is in competition with MSOs is a factor that is irrelevant for the purpose of application of Section 4(2)(c) which, as has been found by us, becomes applicable for the simple reason that the broadcaster is denied market access due to an unlawful termination of the agreement between the said broadcaster and the respondents 1-4. Hence, any denial of market access, partial or complete, will amount to contravention under section 4(2)(c), and it is not necessary that such contravention has to be by a competitor qua another. In International Spirits and Wines Association of India (ISWAI) v. Uttarakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board 178 International Spirits and Wines Association of India (ISWAI) v. Uttarakhand Agricultural Produce Marketing Board, Case No. 02 of 2016, para. 129. also, the CCI held that it is not necessary for the alleged conduct to have caused an ‘absolute denial of market access’ to invoke the prohibition of section 4(2)(c). The phrase ‘in any manner’ depicts the presence of wide scope, and any denial of market access, partial or complete, will amount to contravention under section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act. Similarly, in the Roche case,179 Roche case, para. 77. the CCI held that a partial denial of market access would also hurt the ability of a competitor to effectively compete on merits in the relevant market. It must be noted that the words in any manner in this clause were added by way of an amendment in 2007. The intent of this amendment was to include all strategies which may be carried out by the dominant enterprise to deter entry or defer potential competition. The said clause seeks to cover all instances wherein dominant undertaking by its actions is creating insurmountable barriers to entry for new entrants to come into the market. This interpretation has been reiterated in subsequent decisions of the CCI, such as the Play Store case wherein it was observed that denial of market access ‘in any manner’ would fall under the ambit of section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act, regardless of whether it is a denial of access to competitors or denial of access to players in vertically affected markets. In the Play Store case, the various policies of Google were found to be in violation of section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act. Through mandatory and exclusive imposition of GPBS, app developers were forced to only use GPBS for payment processing of paid apps and in-app purchases on the Play Store. As a result, app developers were prevented from conducting business with payment processors of their choice and correspondingly, payment processors were prohibited from offering their services to app developers listed on the Play Store. The analysis of the CCI has been described below:(i) Mandatory imposition of GPBS by Google results in denial of market access for payment aggregators as well as app developers. By virtue of imposition of GPBS, the app developers would be mandated to use Google’s services for payment processing. Further, the anti-steering provisions of Google forbid app developers from re-directing users to websites for payment outside the app. In the absence of these stipulations, the app developers could have negotiated better terms and conditions with other payment service providers in a competitive market. Hence, app developers are deprived of commercial freedom. (ii) This also forecloses the opportunity for other payment processing service providers to serve the app developers. This would also discourage app developers from developing their own in-app payment processor. With the mandatory imposition of GPBS, another payment processor will lose out on the market collectively valued over USD 3 billion, impacting their ability to carry out any innovations and product improvements, which can be catastrophic in such network-effect-driven markets. Further, payment processors were placed vertically with Google in relation to providing Play Store services to app developers and app users. Google’s practices of prohibiting app developers from using any other payment processors caused denial of market access to the payment processors in the vertically affected market. Google had argued that there was no violation of section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act because Google was not itself active in the alleged payment processing market and, as such, could not have abused that position to deny payment processors access to that market. In India, the CCI noted that Google subcontracts with third parties to conduct the payment processing for payments made through Google Play. Google also argued that the DG had not defined the relevant market in which the denial was alleged in order to assess whether there had been any anti-competitive effect. In this regard, the CCI noted that there is no legal requirement to precisely define a separate relevant market where the impact of the abusive conduct takes place. Accordingly, the CCI found that Google abused its dominant position in the app store market to cause denial to payment processors in general. This is sufficient to give a finding under section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act.180 Play Store case, para. 315. In the Automobile Spare Parts case, the CCI noticed that there had been restrictions imposed by the OEMs on the spare parts suppliers not to sell the spare parts in the open market. In the process, the independent repairers were not able to procure the spare parts over the counter and had no other option but to procure the spare parts from the authorized dealers. Through a network of agreements between OEMs and suppliers, it was ensured that the OEMs were the only source of supply of spare parts in the aftermarket. Based on the facts, the CCI concluded that the OEMs are engaging in practices which amount to denial of market access, and hence an abuse under section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act. The CCI noted that a dominant enterprise is engaging in an abuse when it itself competes in both the upstream and downstream markets and is in effective control of a product/service in the upstream market which is required by the enterprises in the downstream market to effectively carry out their economic activity, as this amounts to engaging in practices which may exclude a competitor from such downstream market. Such exclusion may be occasioned by refusing to supply such products/services to enterprises in the downstream market. The CCI relied on the investigation report of the DG, wherein the OEMs had submitted that the spare parts, diagnostic tools and workshop manuals are their proprietary materials and, therefore, accessible only to the authorized dealer network of each OEM. Further, the CCI observed that the make of each brand of car is unique, and the independent service providers require the spare parts and diagnostic tools compatible with the various models of automobiles manufactured by the various OEMs to carry out their economic activity of providing repair and maintenance services in the Indian automobile aftermarket. However, it was noted that each OEM, which is already a dominant player in the aftermarket for the supply of spare parts and diagnostic tools through their own or related network of authorized distributors, also operates in the aftermarket for after-sale repair and maintenance services of their own brand of cars as the sole supplier of their spare parts and diagnostic tools. Each OEM has two types of customers: (a) car owners who purchase the automobiles manufactured by the OEMs in the primary market, and (b) independent service providers in the aftermarket. An owner of a car cannot fit the spare parts into the machine by himself and requires the services of a specialized technician. Therefore, the owners of automobiles not only operate in the aftermarket as purchasers of spare parts but also require the service of firms engaged in maintenance and repair work. The independent repairers, who are not part of the official dealer network of the OEMs, do purchase the spare parts of the automobiles manufactured by the OEMs from the OEMs. Therefore, the independent service providers are customers of the OEMs in the aftermarket and further compete with the OEMs in the repairs and maintenance service aftermarket. Also, it was noticed by the CCI that even the authorized dealers of the OEMs are forbidden to supply spare parts to independent repairers over the counter. Based on these collective facts, the CCI observed that OEMs have effectively denied access to spare parts to these independent repairers by entering into agreements with the suppliers forbidding the suppliers to sell the spare parts in the open market. It concluded that such practice of preventing the independent repairers from competing with the authorized dealers of the OEMs amounted to denial of market access under section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act. The CCI also relied on the jurisprudence of the EC181 United Brands Co v. Commission of the European Union, Case 27/76 [1978] ECR 207, para. 189. that such behaviour cannot be countenanced if its ‘actual purpose is to strengthen this dominant position and abuse it’. Hence, denial of market access aimed at excluding a competitor from the market by means other than legitimate competition allows the OEMs to further strengthen their dominant position and abuse it. The CCI also touched upon briefly on the issue of denial of market access in the NSE case. It was alleged that NSE had taken a stake of about 26% in Omnesys, which is a technology vendor providing software for financial and securities market. The product of Omnesys, known as ‘NOW’ was made, which was a substitute for software called ‘ODIN’. ODIN was developed by FTIL, the promoter of MCX-SX and the market leader in the brokerage solutions sector. It was alleged that after taking an equity stake in Omnesys, DotEx intentionally wrote individually to the members/brokers of NSE, offering them NOW free of cost for the next three years. NSE also simultaneously refused to share its currency derivative segment Application Programme Interface Code (APIC) with FTIL, thus disabling the ODIN users (who constituted about 85% of the stock brokers/traders) from connecting to the currency derivative segment of the NSE from trading platform through their preferred mode, ODIN. The product thus thrust upon everyone (consumers) desirous of accessing the currency derivative segment of NSE was the product ‘NOW’ developed by Omnesys in place of ODIN. Further, it was alleged that NSE had put ‘ODIN’ on the watch list across all its segments, thereby deterring its members and their constituents from using ‘ODIN’ as their front-end solution. Based on the investigation, the CCI noted that ODIN and NOW are competing software and such software is an essential facility without which trading cannot be done. Relying on jurisprudence from matured anti-trust jurisdictions, it was noted by the CCI that the: (i) action of NSE of placing FTIL, the developer of ODIN (and one of the promoters of MCX-SX) on its watch list; and (ii) denying APIC for interface between its own software NOW and ODIN for the currency derivative segment, prevents the clients of the NSE, most of who use ODIN for all other segments, from choosing ODIN for the CD segment trade on NSE. The CCI noted that the said action is anti-competitive in the aftermarket of trading software for the currency derivative segment of the NSE. The CCI noted that if the APIC had been provided to ODIN, there would have been healthy competition between the two software platforms, ODIN and NOW, for clients. This competitive environment would have driven advancements in the technical development of all such trading software, benefiting the market. Based on this discussion, the CCI concluded that NSE/DotEX’s actions in denying APIC to ODIN and placing FTIL on a watch list amounted to exclusionary practice as they hindered competition both in the aftermarket for trading software on NSE and in the relevant market. [a] Denial of Market Access Includes Non-price Parameters Also The aspect of denial of market access in any manner is very wide, and it includes all strategies adopted by the dominant enterprise to deter or defer competition. This also includes non-price strategies adopted by dominant firms to unlawfully raise their rivals’ costs or exclude them from the market. An investigation was ordered, where it is alleged that the dominant enterprise has engaged in denial of market access by engaging in bad faith litigation and abusing the judicial process. The prima facie case made in this regard is against JCB India in the case of M/s Bull Machines Pvt Ltd. v. JCB India and Ors. 182 M/s Bull Machines Pvt Ltd. v. JCB India and Ors, Case No. 105 of 2013. (JCB case). The JCB case was initiated based on information filed by Bull Machines. Bull Machines was engaged in the manufacturing of low-cost backhoe loaders, called ‘Bull Smart’, a light construction equipment. It was alleged that during the formal launch of ‘Bull Smart’ at the ‘Excon 2011 Exhibition’, Bull Machines was served with an ex parte interim injunction order granted by the High Court of Delhi by JCB wherein it was alleged that Bull Machines had infringed the design registrations/copyright of JCB in developing the backhoe loader ‘Bull Smart’. The said order of the Delhi High Court restrained Bull Machines and its dealers ‘from making, selling, offering for sale, dispatch, advertising, directly or indirectly dealing in/launching backhoe loaders in any manner’. It is in the case of the Bull Machines that JCB obtained the ex parte ad interim injunction order based on misrepresentation of images/design registration number/documents and bogus numbers, suppression of its pre-existing UK patent, misrepresentation by comparing the wrong angle of the images in the application and reliance upon fraudulent design registrations which were pre-existing in the public domain. It is further alleged that JCB, armed with the ex parte order of the Hon’ble Delhi High Court, forced Bull Machines to remove the backhoe loader ‘Bull Smart’ from ‘Excon 2011 Exhibition’ in front of a huge crowd of dealers, existing and potential customers, end customers, financing company officials, bankers, suppliers, foreign delegates and peers in the industry and media. Post the order by the Delhi High Court, Bull Machines filed an application for vacation of the ex parte interim stay order and submitted all the evidence required to show that there is no similarity in the design of the backhoe loader ‘Bull Smart’ developed by it and the designs registered by JCB. Bull Machines also submitted evidence to the High Court showing that JCB misrepresented the images and the design registration numbers/documents and misled the Delhi High Court to secure an ad interim ex parte injunction order in its favour. It was averred by Bull Machines that after nearly ten months from the filing of the said suit, JCB voluntarily withdrew its application for ex parte interim injunction without submitting any inspection report or reply. In view of the above, the Delhi High Court dismissed the petition and the ex parte interim order procured by JCB by misrepresenting facts before the Delhi High Court was vacated. However, the case of Bull Machines was that huge irreparable damages had already been caused to Bull Machines, and in the process, JCB’s objective of deferring the entry of Bull Machines was achieved. The entire case of abuse made by Bull Machines is based upon the alleged bad faith litigation by JCB before the Hon’ble High Court of Delhi, which itself was based on designs and copyrights obtained fraudulently by JCB. It alleged that the bad faith litigation initiated by JCB against it alleging infringement of its design rights was totally false and that the said legal proceedings before the Delhi High Court were only initiated to harass it and prevent the launch of ‘Bull Smart’, which in effect would have competed with backhoe loaders of JCB in the relevant market. Based on the evidence on record, the CCI passed a prima facie view and asked the DG to investigate whether JCB has denied market access and foreclosed the entry of ‘Bull Smart’ by engaging itself in bad faith litigation. This was premised on its preliminary finding that predation through abuse of judicial processes presents an increasing threat to competition, owing to its relatively low anti-trust visibility. In contrast, in the Roche case, a case for denial or market access on account of vexatious litigation was not made out. The CCI noted that abusive strategies include rendering rivals’ products incompatible without adding any technical improvement to the replaced product; indulging in vexatious litigation purely aimed at harassing rivals; influencing government or regulatory procedures; and impeding the entry of generics/biosimilars by denigrating or disparaging rivals’ products. In this case, it was alleged that Roche indulged in vexatious civil litigation as a tactic to exhaust smaller rivals’ resources and delay or prevent their entry into the relevant market. The CCI observed that to cause denial of market access by engaging in legal action, such legal action should be aimed at harassing the competitor respondent without any objective basis and be conceived with an anti-competitive intent/plan to eliminate competition. However, in the Roche case, it could not be proved that the litigations filed by Roche were baseless or tainted with ulterior motives. Yet, a prima facie case for denial of market access was made out on the ground that Roche tried to influence regulatory authorities in its favour by misrepresenting the status of approvals granted to the informant’s drug.183 Roche case, para. 74. Further, when Roche was not successful in evading entry, it also approached doctors, hospitals, tender authorities, etc., to influence their perception of the efficacy and safety of the informants’ products. The latter behaviour crossed the line of puffery aimed at promoting one’s own product and actually amounted to maligning the image of the informant, thereby causing competitive advantages to them and consequent harm to the informant and the market as a whole. The abovementioned cases are a testament to the fact that the import of the expression ‘denial of market access in any manner’ is very wide and encompasses both price and non-price conduct. [E] Imposing Supplementary Obligations Section 4(2)(d) of the Competition Act provides that there shall be an abuse of dominant position if the dominant enterprise or group makes the conclusion of contracts subject to acceptance of supplementary obligations by other parties which, by their nature or according to commercial usage, have no connection with the subject of such contracts. This includes instances of tying and bundling. Tying instances would include cases of technical tying, contractual tying, tying of products with services, etc. It is to be noted that the practice of adopting tying practices, in itself, will not constitute a breach of the provisions of the Competition Act. As such, tying is a legitimate business practice, and an enterprise can make considerable savings in production, distribution and transaction costs. Based on international jurisprudence, the following needs to be addressed for an abuse of dominance of tying to be made out:(i) the enterprise has a dominant position regarding the tying product; (ii) the tied product is a distinct product from the tying product; (iii) element of coercion; (iv) anti-competitive foreclosure. In Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc., 184 Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc. and Facebook Inc., Case No. 15 of 2020. the CCI clarified that while ‘tying’ refers to a practice where the seller of a product or service (‘tying product’) requires the buyers to also purchase another separate product or service (‘tied product’); ‘bundling’ typically means that the two products are sold by the seller in a fixed proportion as a bundled package at a particular price. Based on economic literature, the CCI laid down four conditions, as described above, to assess whether WhatsApp was tying its messaging services with its UPI enabled Digital Payments App: (i) the tying and tied products are two separate products (WhatsApp Messenger and WhatsApp Pay are two distinct products with different functionalities operating in different markets); (ii) the entity concerned is dominant in the market for the tying product (WhatsApp is prima facie dominant in the relevant market, i.e., ‘market for OTT messaging apps through smartphones in India’); (iii) the customers do not have a choice to only obtain the tying product without the tied product (Installation of the WhatsApp messenger does not appear to explicitly mandate/coerce the user to use WhatsApp Pay exclusively); and (iv) the tying is capable of restricting/foreclosing competition in the market (UPI digital payments market is very competitive and consists of various established players so WhatsApp Pay will not automatically gain market share due to pre-installation). Thus, since the elements of coercion and foreclosure were absent, the allegation of tying against WhatsApp was not made out. Even if one of the above elements is missing, then a case for tying would not be made out. For instance, in Baglekar Akash Kumar v. Google LLC, 185 Baglekar Akash Kumar v. Google LLC, Case No. 39 of 2020. it was alleged that Google had tied its video-conferencing app Google Meet with its email service, i.e., Gmail. However, the CCI found that Google Meet is also available as an independent app outside the Gmail ecosystem, and users have the choice to use either of the apps with all their functionalities without necessarily having to use the other. Hence, no case for tying is made out since there is no coercion. Tying cases can be covered under both section 4(2)(d) and section 4(2)(e), which deals with leveraging. It can also include practices where an IPR holder licences its IPR only on some additional condition. This has been covered in the chapter on IPR and competition law. In the Google Android case,186 Google Android case, paras 345-373. as discussed earlier, Google had a dominant position in the market of licensable OS, i.e., Android, as well as the supply of each of the services offered under GMS, i.e., Search, YouTube, Chrome and Play Store. Further, we have also discussed how the OEMs had weak countervailing power with respect to Google since each of Google’s services was a ‘must have’, and the OEMs had no option except to agree to Google’s terms in order to access these services. Since Android-based applications are written to work with Google Play Services, a smart device without them would not be commercially viable or at least the OEM offering these devices would be competitively disadvantaged because the customers also would not find such a smart device attractive. Google did not negotiate on key terms of the MADA, which were found to result in anti-competitive conduct: pre-installation of the entire suit of GMS as well as prominent placement thereof. By foreclosing the market for rivals, MADA also reduced the potential choice for users. Based on the investigation, the CCI also concluded that Google Play and other apps of Google covered under GMS have different functionalities and thus, there is no technical necessity to offer these services together. Hence, the CCI found the obligations under MADA, RSA, AFA and ACC to be in the nature of supplementary obligations imposed by Google on OEMs since if they wish to pre-install even a single app of Google, they had to agree to the pre-installation requirements of the entire bouquet of Google’s apps. Additionally, the said Google apps were to be prominently placed on the mobile; thus, it was a case of both pre-installation and primary placement. Thus, Google’s conduct was held to be in contravention of section 4(2)(d) of the Competition Act. Subsequently, the NCLAT187 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, paras 94-98. upheld the findings of the CCI in light of Google’s anti-competitive requirements under MADA regarding prominent placement, pre-installation and other conditions applied to OEMs. It noted that: Clause 4.4 of MADA enjoins upon the OEM to – (i) distribute all Core Applications, (ii) distribute on the Default Home Screen a Google – provided widget, Google Play Store icon; and an icon that provides direct access to Core Applications which is labelled as ‘Google’, and among other conditions also stipulates that any Google Application that is not a Core Application is placed no more than one level below the Default Home Screen. There is also a condition that the OEM shall implement the “Home button animation” as per Google’s guidelines if Google Assistant is enable on the Android device and also implement Google Hotword, if it is supported by the device. On this basis, the NCLAT concluded that the conditions which are applied on OEMs through MADA which essentially provides Google Applications, are in the form of ‘supplementary obligations’ attracting section 4(2)(d). [F] Leveraging of Dominant Position Section 4(2)(e) of the Competition Act provides that there shall be an abuse of dominant position when the dominant undertaking uses its dominant position in one relevant market to enter into or protect another relevant market. The Competition Act has expressly included the aspect of leveraging as an abuse of dominant position. A contravention of section 4(2)(e) requires two markets to be identified, the first relevant market in which the erring entity is alleged to be dominant and the second market in which the said entity is entering or protecting its position, directly or indirectly, by virtue of dominance in the first relevant market. The key point of consideration is that there must be an exploitation of market power by the dominant player in the market in which the enterprise is dominant to enter into or protect its position in the market in which such player is not dominant. There are situations, especially in a multi-product company or group, where there are a lot of joint costs and cross-subsidization. When cross-subsidization becomes an issue of leveraging depends on the facts and circumstances of each case:(a) The CCI has examined the provision relating to leveraging in the NSE case and the Automobile Spare Parts case. The CCI has noted that the dominant enterprise in the first market need not possess a dominant market position in the second market where it makes attempts to leverage its market power. The aspect of leveraging deals with a situation where an enterprise in dominant position in any delineable relevant market uses its strength therein to enter or protect any other delineable relevant market. The Competition Act does not provide that there has to be a high degree of associational link between the two markets (the market in which the enterprise is dominant and the second market which it intends to enter or protect) being considered for the abuse of leveraging. The CCI, in the NSE case observed that:The Indian Competition Act recognizes leveraging as an act by an enterprise or group that “uses by its dominant position in one relevant market to enter into, or protect, other relevant market.” Nowhere does the Act indicate that there has to be a high degree of associational link between the two markets being considered for this sub section. This is so because competition concerns are much higher in India than in more mature jurisdictions because of the historical lack of competition laws. In India, if an enterprise dominant in the market of audio-visual (AV) equipment enters into the market of say, computers, it is possible for it to use its strength in terms of finances, technological expertise, sales network etc. in the AV market to muscle its way into and protect its position in the computer market, even though the two markets are not at all connected. That is why the Act does not indicate any requirement of associational link. (b) As observed in the NSE case, the section uses the term ‘relevant market’ and other relevant market. Hence, it is incorrect to argue that the whole of section 4 pivots around determination of only one ‘relevant market’. There can be a second relevant market which may also be treated as the ‘other market’ for the purpose of section 4(2)(e). The Competition Act recognizes the fact that an enterprise may be multi-product and may be operating in two (or more) markets. In case of multi-product enterprises, such enterprises can use their position of strength derived in one market to leverage their position and gain an unfair advantage over their competitors in the other market. While its conduct in the second market has to be separately examined for abuse if and after it acquires a dominant position in the second market, the fact that it has used the strengths from the first market to wrongfully enter into or to protect the second market is itself an abuse under the Competition Act. It is to be further noted that in the event such an enterprise, after entering the other relevant market through such leveraging and thereafter acquires dominant position in the other market, commits further acts of abuse in that relevant market, then there would be a separate violation of section 4(2)(a)–(d) of the Competition Act. The ‘relevant market’ of the explanation (a) applies equally in intent for sections 4(1) and (2), but the relevant market in respect of clauses (a) to (d) of section 4(2) can be different than the relevant market for the purpose of clause (e). The CCI observed in the NSE case as follows:To further clarify, if an enterprise merely uses its dominant position in any ‘relevant market’ to enter or protect some other “relevant market” wrongfully, it can only be held guilty of contravening section 4(2)(e). But if the enterprise, after entering the other relevant market through such leveraging and acquiring dominant position there, commits further acts of abuse (such as unfair pricing) in that relevant market, then there would be a separate violation of section 4(2)(a). It is worthwhile to observe here that the language of section 4(2)(e) does not exclude the possibility that the enterprise is dominant in both, the “relevant market” as well as the ‘other relevant market’. An enterprise can be dominant in one market and can enter another market, acquire position of strength there and then commit acts to protect its position. Based on the above approach, the CCI noted that NSE, a super dominant entity in the non-currency derivative market, leveraged its dominant position by using its strength in the non-currency derivative segment to enter into and protect its position in the currency derivative segment. The CCI observed that they could issue periodical waivers in the transaction fees in the currency derivative segment and yet earn profits in the process, while the others earned huge losses because of their strength in the non-currency derivative segment. It is noteworthy to point out that when the matter went up in appeal before the erstwhile COMPAT, it disagreed with the definition of the relevant market adopted by the CCI. The CCI had identified two markets: a market for currency derivative and a market for non-currency derivative segment. After defining the market, the CCI observed that the NSE is using its dominance in the non-currency derivate segment market to protect its position in the currency derivate segment market. However, the erstwhile COMPAT held that there is only one definition of relevant market, that is, the market for stock exchange services market where NSE is committing the abuse of unfair pricing (discussed above).188 National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. v. CCI, [2014] Comp AT 129, paras 116-121. Since there was only one definition of relevant market adopted by erstwhile COMPAT, it held that NSE could not have been guilty of breaching section 4(2)(e) of the Competition Act. Having said that, NSE was found to be guilty of section 4(2)(a) of the Competition Act for unfair pricing. Increasingly, there is a focus on cross-leveraging by digital platforms which are able to exploit user data and create functional entry barriers in the market by entrenching themselves in neighbouring or even in unrelated markets.189 WhatsApp case. The aspect of data as a tool for abuse has been discussed in Chapter 6. In the Google Android case,190 Google Android case, paras 374-465. it was found that Google engaged in tying (through pre-installation) and leveraging by using its dominant position in one market to enter and protect the other market. Google engaged in tying conduct in several ways – it tied access to Play Store with its services of Search, Chrome as well as YouTube through imposition of MADA which required pre-installation of the entire bouquet of Google’s apps even if the OEMs wanted to access only one app. Google’s different services constituted different markets in each case and Google was found to be dominant in both. The NCLAT191 Paragraphs 136-143. affirmed the findings of the CCI that tying between Play Store and Search, Chrome and YouTube was used to achieve and perpetuate dominance by Google in the markets of the latter services, thereby causing anti-competitive effects such as foreclosure and denial of consumer choice. Similarly, in the Play Store case, the CCI held Google to be guilty of leveraging its dominance in markets other than the relevant markets delineated by it. It was found that the practices followed by Google resulted in leveraging its dominance in the: (i) market for licensable mobile OS and app stores for Android OS to protect its position in the downstream markets; and (ii) market for app stores for Android OS to protect its position in the market for apps facilitating payments through UPI in India, in violation of the provisions of section 4(2)(e) of the Competition Act.192 Play Store case, paras 317, 358. In Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc and Ors., Google was found to prima facie occupy a significant position in the relevant markets: Online General Web Search Services, Online Search Advertising Services, and the market for Online Digital Advertising Services. It was observed that Google was using its dominant position in the relevant markets to enter/protect its position in the market for news aggregation services in violation of section 4(2)(e) of the Competition Act. The issue of leveraging was also discussed in detail in the Automobile Spare Parts case. As has been discussed at length above, it was noted by the CCI that the OEMs have a dominant position in the market for spare parts and diagnostic tools because of the limited inter-brand interchangeability of spare parts. Further, the OEMs, pursuant to a network of restrictive contracts and commercial practices, became the sole supplier of genuine spare parts and diagnostic tools in the aftermarket. Additionally, the OEMs are also active in the market for repair and maintenance through their network of authorized dealers. The CCI noted that there are interlinkages between the repair/service markets and the spare parts/diagnostic tools market, and the OEMs have a commercial incentive to leverage their dominance from the relevant market of spare parts/diagnostic tools to that of repairs and maintenance services. The CCI noted that OEMs (acting through their authorized dealer network) deny 94.99% of the total service providers active in the Indian automobile aftermarket (consisting of multi-brand retailers, semi-organized service stations and unorganized garage workshops), effective access to the Indian aftermarket on competitive terms. It was noted that the OEMs typically provide after-sale services and repair of their brand of automobiles through a network of authorized dealers. These authorized distributors are also the source of supply of genuine spare parts for various models of automobiles manufactured by an OEM. As discussed above, the OEMs restrict the ability of independent repairers to obtain spare parts from authorized dealers. Further, the OEMs restrict the suppliers from supplying spare parts in the open market. In addition, the OEMs had also imposed a warranty condition on the consumers that their warranty would be denied if the cars were serviced by an independent repairer or other multi-brand service repairers. The CCI also noted that in the limited instances where spare parts are available to the actual owners, the owners have to buy such spare parts at the MRP and then avail the services of the independent repairers at additional costs, whereas the authorized dealers are able to provide such services at cheaper rates since the applicable spare parts are available to the authorized dealers at a discount over the MRP. Therefore, in practice, in most cases, the owners of various brands of automobiles are completely dependent on the authorized dealer network of the OEMs and are not in a position to exercise the option of availing services of independent repairers. Therefore, the users of cars wanting to purchase the spare parts have to necessarily avail the services of the authorized dealers of the OEM. Based on these facts, the CCI held that the OEMs are leveraging their dominant position in the market for the supply of spare parts to protect their market of maintenance of cars.

sections.title: OBJECTIVE JUSTIFICATION
sections.text: §3.07 OBJECTIVE JUSTIFICATION Dominant enterprises often seek to justify their conduct that may appear anti-competitive on the surface based on business efficiencies. The legitimacy of such objective justifications is based on a determination of the facts and circumstances. At the outset, it is clarified that such a justification must be able to stand on its own legs without itself being illegal. For instance, in the DLF case, DLF argued that the (abusive) terms of its agreements with the buyers were a standard ‘industry practice’. However, the CCI observed that ‘even without taking into account the fact that being an industry practice cannot be a defence for anti-competitive practices/conduct, DLF for one certainly cannot claim the defence of having adopted an industry practice for practices which are found to be anti-competitive and/or abusive.’ As discussed earlier, the CCI, in their decisional practice, has adopted the effects test. The effect test implies that unilateral conduct may escape the prohibition of section 4 of the Competition Act in case the dominant enterprise can provide an objective justification for its behaviour or it can demonstrate that its conduct produces efficiencies which outweigh the negative effect on competition. The burden of proof for such an objective justification or efficiency defence will be on the dominant enterprise. The possible types of objective justification would be in cases where it can be shown that dominant enterprise can show that the otherwise abusive conduct is actually necessary conduct on the basis of objective business rationale. The NSE also raised multiple business rationales for engaging in fee waivers in the currency derivative segment to promote the currency derivative. NSE raised the point of promotional/penetrative pricing.193 NSE case, para. 10.57. It is to be noted that although these defences were not held to be valid by the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT, yet they heard the matter on objective justification at great length. Their approach implies that the CCI will take into account objective justification as a defence. In this background, it becomes all the more necessary for companies, that have a high market share, to adopt proper documentation of their business processes and decisions which can be used in defence in an abuse of dominance investigation. The CCI has also held that protection of commercial interest by a dominant enterprise, at the cost of competition, is contrary to its responsibility cast under the Competition Act. Effective competition does not necessarily mean prevalence of the most efficient to the exclusion of relatively less efficient choices to consumers. In any case, there is no justification for imposing restrictions on competition by a dominant enterprise to protect its commercial interest.194 East India Petroleum Pvt. Ltd. v. South Asia LPG Company Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 76 of 2011. In the Google Android case,195 Google Android case, para. 489. Google had argued that users and OEMs prefer Google’s services due to their superior nature. In fact, this may be a common argument taken by any dominant enterprise that the reason for their success is the strength of their product. However, it is pointed out that if that is the case, then the market has to be the judge, and this ‘preference’ cannot be forced on users. On similar lines, the CCI observed that:If Google is of the view that the presence of its applications provides a great out-of-the-box experience, then the user or the OEM would itself decide to use its application. There does not appear to be any necessity to impose restrictions through MADA, RSAs and AFA/ACC, to achieve the same. The market forces would itself take care of that. In the MMT case,196 MMT case, paras 264-266. MMT-Go claimed that its parity obligations were an industry practice, particularly to address the problem of free-riding – i.e., to prevent hotels from benefitting from the investment of the OTA in advertisement and promotion of the hotel for free. However, the CCI rejected this since the effect and implications of a conduct indulged in by a dominant platform on its users and competition in the relevant market is fundamentally different from when a smaller insignificant player does it. Comparing the combined effect that deep discounting and parity together have, when imposed by a dominant platform that has already reaped the benefits of network effects in its favour, with that of the parity conditions of other platforms is not a like-to-like comparison. Further, the aspect of the special responsibility of a dominant player like MMT-Go was also brought into the picture due to the potential adverse effects that it may have on competition as well as hotels. [A] Effects Doctrine The agreements or combinations having or likely to have an AAEC in India are declared void under sections 3(2) and 6(1) of the Competition Act, respectively. However, section 4 does not have any such similar provision. Sections 3 and 6 are concerned with the ‘effects’ of an agreement or a combination. In contrast, under section 4, because of the dominant position enjoyed by an enterprise, the legislature in its wisdom has decided that an ‘enterprise’ shall not ‘abuse’ such a position. The emphasis under section 4(1) is on ‘abuse’ and the word ‘abuse’ has not been defined under the Competition Act; hence, the presumption was that the legislature has wisely left it open to be decided on a case-by-case basis because the ‘nature and type of abuse’ may differ with time and depending upon the market in question. However, this position has now been clarified by the NCLAT which has held that the effects doctrine is applicable to section 4 as well, as shall be discussed below. While the wording of the Competition Act may show that the principle of strict liability is laid down and no effects have to be seen (an ‘unlawful per se’ approach), the CCI has incorporated the effects doctrine in its analysis of dominance investigations, in line with global practice. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations of the CCI in the case of Dhanraj Pillai v. Hockey India, wherein the CCI looked into the effects of the restrictive conditions imposed and noted that the conditions did not amount to abuse of dominance as they were intrinsic and proportionate to the objectives of the organization:197 Dhanraj Pillai v. Hockey India, Case No. 73 of 2011. Commission opines that proportionality of the regulations can only be decided by considering the manner in which regulations are applied [emphasis supplied]. It is the manner of applying regulations that raise competition concerns as it may be used as a tool for foreclosing new entrants. Similarly, in the Schott Glass case, the erstwhile COMPAT found that unlawful price discrimination required a showing of both ‘(i) dissimilar treatment to equivalent transactions; and (ii) harm to competition or likely harm to competition in the sense that the buyers suffer a competitive disadvantage against each other leading to competitive injury in the downstream market.’ The erstwhile COMPAT found the CCI had wrongly ignored the second limb and that the evidence showed there was no effect on the downstream market and the ultimate consumer did not suffer as a result of the alleged conduct. In this case, not only was an effects-based analysis undertaken, but also the objective justifications raised by the parties to justify their conduct were considered. The NCLAT made elaborate observations on applicability of the effects doctrine to section 4 in the Google Android case and laid down the test as follows: For proving abuse of dominance under section 4, effect analysis is required to be done, and the test to be employed in the effect analysis is whether the abusive conduct is anti-competitive or not. 198 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023. For this, the NCLAT undertook a detailed analysis, which has been summarised below:(i) The legislative intent is clear in respect of the expression ‘AAEC’ being only present in sections 3 and 6, and not section 4. But what needs to be examined is whether, even if the AAEC test is not attracted in section 4, any analysis of the conduct of an enterprise or group of anti-competitive has to be looked into or not? The answer is yes. (ii) The 2009 Guidance to Article 102 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the EU noted that for exclusionary abuses in Article 102, the European Commission (EC) will intervene if there is any likelihood of anticompetitive foreclosure. Even in its recent case laws, the EC has adopted an effects-based approach while analysing exclusionary abuse. However, such an effects-based approach is not mandated for exploitative abuses under Article 102. (iii) In Intel v. Commission, 199 Intel Corp. v. European Commission, Case C-413/14 P. the General Court continued to adopt a strict per se approach to exclusivity rebates, which it said were illegal unless the dominant firm could show an objective justification for granting them. However, there was an increasing intellectual consensus against the application of per se rules to unilateral behaviour, and the judgment of the General Court in Intel attracted particular hostility because of its formalistic approach. This has been confirmed in recent decisions such as in Paroxetine 200 Generics (UK) Ltd and Others v. Competition and Markets Authority, Case C-307/18, para. 154. [‘…having regard to all relevant facts, conduct may be characterised as abusive only if it is capable of restricting competition and, in particular, producing exclusionary effects.’] and TeliaSonera 201 Konkurrensverket v. TeliaSonera Sverige AB, Case C-52/09, para. 68. [in order to establish whether (a margin squeeze) is abusive, that practice must have an anti-competitive effect on the market.] Further, in its decisional practice, it has sought to produce evidence of anti-competitive effects. Importantly, the NCLAT relied on the findings of the Report of the High Level Committee on Competition Law, which preceded the Competition Act, and noted that the dominance investigations should be undertaken based on the effects doctrine. The key issue that needs to be analysed is whether the conduct of the dominant enterprise is harming the competitive process prevalent in the relevant market. The relevant observations of the report are as follows:Discriminatory behaviour and any other exercise of market power leading to the prevention, restriction or distortion of competition would obviously be included. We probably need to clarify that there is a fine distinction between defending one’s market position or market share which is perfectly legal and legitimate and may involve certain level of aggressive competitive behaviour and exclusionary and anti-competitive behaviour. Key questions for adjudication on abuse of dominance could include:– How will the practice harm competition? – Will it deter or prevent entry? – Will it reduce incentives of the firm and its rivals to compete aggressively? – Will it provide the dominant firm with an additional capacity to raise prices? – Will it prevent investments in research and innovation? – Do consumers benefit from lower prices and/or greater product and service availability? In order to understand the aim of the provision, the discussion on abuse in the Raghavan Committee Report was perused. The Committee took note that this report indicates that an effects based approach was contemplated to establish abuse of dominance under Section 4. … Based on the above, the Committee discussed that the CCI has interpreted Section 4(2) keeping in mind that one of the key aims of the Act is to prevent practices which adversely affect competition in India. It has therefore, wherever appropriate, analysed the effects of alleged abusive conduct by dominant entities before passing orders regarding such conduct. The CCI has relied on the effects built into some of the clauses of Section 4(2) to support its approach, e.g. ‘denial of market access in any manner’ in Section 4(2)(c). After conducting an analysis of the CCI’s orders, the Committee came to the conclusion that the current text of Section 4(2) has not proven to be a hindrance to the CCI’s ability to assess effects in abuse of dominance disputes. It was agreed that since it may not be necessary to undertake an effects analysis in all kinds of abuse, e.g. exploitative abuse, it may not be appropriate to mandate an effects analysis in Section 4(2). Therefore, it was concluded that no legislative amendment is required in this regard. [emphasis supplied] This approach taken by the Report of the High Level Committee on Competition Law suggests that conduct in question must in the first place have the capability, by its nature, to distort competition or foreclose competitors from the market. To establish such capability, it is in general sufficient to investigate the form and nature of the conduct in question. Second, it implies that, in the specific market context, a likely market-distorting foreclosure effect must be established. The competition law concern is to prevent exclusionary conduct of the dominant firm202 Bharti Airtel Limited v. Reliance Industries Limited and Anr, Case No. 03 of 2017. which is likely to limit the remaining competitive constraints on the dominant company, including the entry of newcomers, so as to avoid consumers being harmed. This means that it is competition, and not competitors as such, that is to be protected. By foreclosure, it means that actual or potential competitors are completely or partially denied profitable access to a market. Foreclosure may discourage the entry or expansion of rivals or encourage their exit. Foreclosure thus can be found even if the foreclosed rivals are not forced to exit the market: it is sufficient that the rivals are disadvantaged and consequently led to compete less aggressively. [B] IPR as a Defence Protection of IPRs has been separately included as a defence to anti-competitive conduct under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. However, this defence is only limited to anti-competitive agreements. There have been several discussions to extend the benefit of this defence to abuse of dominance as well. In fact, the Parliamentary Standing Committee Report, 2022 also recommended the inclusion of a defence allowing reasonable conditions and restrictions for protecting IPR may be provided in cases of abuse of dominance on the lines with the international jurisdictions, that is, the EU, the US and the UK which provide for such exemption. However, the Parliament rejected the same, and so far, the provision has not found its way into the Competition Act due to concerns in the era of the new age economy that mention of IPR defence explicitly may allow a dominant player to abuse its position of dominance. However, it is apposite to note that the NCLAT, in its decisional practice, seems to have upheld the use of IPR as a defence by dominant enterprises in the Google Android case.203 Google Android case, paras 188-190. The CCI had directed Google not to deny access to its APIs to OEMS, app developers and existing or potential app developers. Google argued that it cannot be asked to share APIs as it constitutes the IPR of Google. The NCLAT upheld this argument and set aside the direction of CCI to allow open access to Google’s APIs by observing that Google’s APIs constitute its proprietary software, into which Google has made investments for scientific and technical development. Thus, the same should fetch value to Google and allowing open access to such proprietary software will decrease the incentive for technological companies to continue innovation.

sections.title: CONCLUSION
sections.text: §3.08 CONCLUSION The decisional practice and statutory regime of abuse of dominance indicates a holistic consideration of all facts and circumstances to hold an entity guilty of abuse. It is not a static consideration but gives scope for commercial justifications and business efficiencies. At the same time, dominant players have to be cautious about employing business practices in line with their special responsibility to prevent genuine and undistorted growth of competition in the market. The idea is to strike a balance – to ensure a competitive and level playing field for all market participants and prevent monopolistic practices that may harm consumers and hinder economic growth and, at the same time, prevent excessive curbs on dominant enterprises and avoid hindering innovation in the market.

plain_text:
§3.01 LEGISLATIVE FRAMEWORK Section 4 of the Competition Act provides that no ‘enterprise’ or ‘group’ will abuse its dominant position. The said section deals with unilateral conduct. Dominant position has been defined under the Competition Act as ‘position of strength, enjoyed by an enterprise, in the relevant market, in India, which enables it to (i) operate independently of competitive forces prevailing in the relevant market; or (ii) affect its competitors or consumers or the relevant market, in its favour’.1 Competition Act, section 4 (2) a – Explanation. In India, the determination of ‘dominance’ is based on a qualitative assessment of the prevalent market dynamics and the relative position of strength enjoyed by the market participants. Section 4 of the Competition Act stipulates specific practices, i.e., imposition of unfair or discriminatory conditions on price in purchase or sale (including predatory pricing), limiting or restricting the production of goods, denial of market access, and leveraging market position in one relevant market to enter into another relevant market, by dominant enterprises shall amount to abuse of dominance. [A] Textual Interpretation of Section 4 of the Competition Act Decisional practice suggests that the key analysis that is done by the CCI, the erstwhile COMPAT and the NCLAT, to date, in analysing dominance cases is whether the enterprise under investigation has a ‘position of strength’. Such ‘position of strength’ so enjoyed by the enterprise should enable it to affect the competitors or consumers and the market dynamics in its favour. There have been various indices (provided under section 19(4)) which have been given under the Competition Act for determining whether the concerned enterprise, under inquiry, is dominant. In light of the factors mentioned, the CCI looks at the market dynamics and behaviour of the enterprise under inquiry to analyse whether the concerned enterprise is dominant or not. Akin to the position in foreign antitrust jurisdictions, it must be noted that merely having a position of dominance is not bad under the provisions of the Competition Act, but it must be proved that the dominant enterprise has also ‘abused’ its dominance. This is a marked departure from the earlier legislative framework under the previous regime under the Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices Act, 1969, wherein emphasis was placed on the size of the concerned player, rather than the actual abusive practice by, or conduct of such a player. [B] Dominance Per Se Is Not Illegal In a marked departure from the earlier regime, as discussed at the beginning of this chapter, the Competition Act does not prohibit dominance, only its abuse. Thus, dominance per se is not illegal, rather, it is the abuse of dominance that is prohibited. Several complaints have been filed before the CCI seeking intervention in cases of mergers which are likely to create dominant enterprises with the capacity to abuse this dominance.2 Consumer Unity & Trust Society v. PVR Limited and INOX Leisure Limited, Case No. 29 of 2022; Vikas Verma v. Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone Ltd. & Others, Case No. 02 of 2021; Satyen Narendra Bajaj v. PayU Payments Private Limited, Case No. 23 of 2019. However, CCI has consistently held that ‘the mere existence of dominance bereft of any abusive conduct under the provisions of Section 4 of the Act, cannot be held to be the basis to order an investigation’.3 Vikas Verma v. Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone Ltd. & Others, Case No. 02 of 2021. However, ‘post-facto, if any matter of abusive conduct under Section 4 of the Act is brought to the notice of the Commission, the same may be looked into at that stage in terms of the provisions of the Act’. Similarly, in Satyen Narendra Bajaj v. PayU Payments Private Limited,4 Satyen Narendra Bajaj v. PayU Payments Private Limited, Case No. 23 of 2019. it was alleged that pursuant to the combination between PayU and Wibmo, the Combined Entity would attempt to monopolize the e-payment gateway market in India, which was likely to have exploitative as well as exclusionary effects in the market of e-payment processing gateways. However, the CCI held that the same was insufficient to give rise to proceedings for abuse of dominance. It observed that:…while the Act prohibits an abuse of dominant position by an enterprise, mere existence of dominant position, without any prima facie evidence of its abuse, is not recognised as an anti-competitive conduct in the scheme of the Act. In order to establish a prima facie case for intervention, an abuse of a dominant position cannot be based on a mere existence or potential to achieve dominance in the market. Therefore, a mere apprehension of abuse of dominance is not enough to initiate an investigation under the Competition Act because dominance per se is not anti-competitive and only abusive conduct is if the same falls within the provisions of section 4 of the Competition Act. [C] Procedure Adopted in an Abuse of Dominance Investigation Based on the definition of dominance and the scheme of the Competition Act, it is to be noted that in every dominance investigation, there are three key elements which are analysed by the CCI:(A) definition of relevant market; (B) to analyse whether the entity under investigation is ‘dominant’ in the relevant market; (C) if the entity is found to be dominant, whether the dominant entity has engaged in any actions which can be considered an abuse of the dominant position under section 4 of the Competition Act. Every investigation of abuse of dominance proceeds as per this structure, and the analysis will be incomplete without a detailed analysis of each of these elements. We have dealt with, in detail, the concepts of ‘enterprise’ and ‘relevant market’ in an earlier chapter and the same is not repeated here for the sake of brevity. As such, practically, it must be noted that most of the decisions depend on the definition of relevant market, and it is for this reason a substantial amount of analysis is done in every dominance investigation on the definition of relevant market to be adopted. Unless an enterprise enjoys a dominant position in the relevant market, its conduct cannot be subjected to an abuse inquiry. Hence, it is a pre-condition for applying section 4 of the Competition Act, which decrees a set of standards of conduct which are applicable to dominant firms and not to non-dominant firms. This is because what may be commercially justified for the latter may become exploitative and exclusionary when practised by the former. A particular conduct when exhibited by a non-dominant player may not be violative of section 4, but the same conduct by a dominant player could be a contravention.5 Umar Javeed and Ors. v. Google LLC and Anr., Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 572. The entire Competition Act is predicated on the fact that there are certain activities which done by a dominant player will cause market distortion due to its sheer size, and thus, such activities cannot be allowed. This is why dominant players cannot defend their conduct on the basis of the fact that other industry players also follow the same conduct. On this point, it is relevant to note the analysis of the CCI in Belaire Owner’s Association v. DLF Limited 6 Belaire Owner’s Association v. DLF Limited, Case No. 19 of 2010. (‘DLF case’).7 Ibid., para. 12.20. While refuting the competition law concerns emanating from the agreements between builders and buyers, DLF argued that such one-sided agreements are standard industry practices and all the players in the market follow such practices. The CCI, however, did not give importance to the defence because it noted that DLF is a market leader and a trendsetter, and it is not bound by practices of other players, and in any case, the practice itself is anti-competitive and abusive (as has been discussed later in the chapter). As such, after observing the market practice of DLF, the other players have aped the practice of executing one-sided agreements because they do not have the competitive might of the dominant enterprise. In fact, if DLF were to modify its agreement to make it more buyer-friendly, it would be able to put sufficient pressure on its competitors to follow suit. Accordingly, the CCI concluded that DLF has acted in a manner which has affected competitor and consumer behaviour, to its advantage. Hence, the above sequential exercise must be necessarily followed while determining an allegation of contravention of section 4 of the Competition Act.8 Coal India Limited, West Bengal v. Competition Commission of India [2017], CompLR 0447 (CompAT), para. 20.2-20.4.

§3.02 POSITION OF STRENGTH The decisional practice of the CCI, erstwhile COMPAT and NCLAT would show that they undertake a holistic analysis to understand whether the entity under investigation enjoys a position of strength to influence the competitive forces prevalent in the market to its advantage.9 RKG Hospitalities Pvt. Ltd. v. Oravel Stays Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 03 of 2019, para. 43. In the case of MCX Stock Exchange Ltd. v. NSE India Ltd 10 MCX Stock Exchange Ltd. v. NSE India Ltd, Case No. 13 of 2009, para. 10.36. (‘NSE case’), a ‘position of strength’ was analysed by CCI on the basis of the comparative advantages enjoyed by NSE over its competitors in terms of financial resources, technical capabilities, brand value, historical legacy, etc., and whether an as efficient competitor would be able to engage in the same conduct as NSE.11 Ibid., para. 10.38-10.49. The reason is that such an enterprise can force its competitors to act in a certain manner which would make the market and consumers respond or react such that it is beneficial to the dominant enterprise but detrimental to the competitors. In addition, the analysis is whether such advantages allow the enterprise to undertake certain activities (like low pricing) which the competitors would find difficult to follow on a sustained basis. By virtue of the said observation and approach taken by the CCI in the NSE case, it seems to have adopted the efficient competitor test for dominance investigations. This means that whether a certain conduct is exclusionary or abusive depends on whether a competitor, which is as efficient as the dominant enterprise, would be able to sacrifice profits in the same manner in the long run as the dominant enterprise. Herein, it was found that NSE’s competitors, i.e., MCX, would not be able to sustain the below-cost pricing adopted by NSE, and hence, NSE was held to be in a dominant position. This approach of the CCI for determining a ‘position of strength’ was reaffirmed in XYZ and Ors v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors 12 XYZ and Ors v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., Case Nos 07 of 2020, 12 of 2021 and 35 of 2021. (‘Play Store case’). In the said case, it was alleged that Google was abusing its control over the Play Store and Android Operating System (OS) in the markets for licensable mobile OS for smart mobile devices and app stores for Android OS in violation of the provisions of section 4 of the Competition Act. This was facilitated through, inter alia, mandating app developers to use Google’s proprietary Google Play Billing System (GPBS) for in-app purchases and paid apps on the Play Store and advantaging Google Pay, an United Payment Interface (UPI) over competing UPI apps. On the aspect of position of strength, the CCI observed that the moment there is any imposition of any unfair or discriminatory condition by a dominant player, the statutory prohibitions shall trigger for all instances of abuse enshrined under section 4(2) of the Competition Act. In this case, Google enjoyed unparalleled competitive advantages which even its nearest rivals were unable to match.13 Ibid., para. 81. It is to be noted that the aspect of position of strength is not to be viewed in a snapshot manner at a point in time, but the assessment of position of strength must be made over a period of time. The CCI has noted that an enterprise takes years to acquire a position of strength, and therefore, its conduct must be examined over a period of time while undertaking dominance investigations. The CCI has opined in the DLF case that an examination of the dominant enterprise needs to be done over a period of time to examine whether a case of abuse under section 4(2) of the Competition Act is made out. The relevant portion of the order is as follows:It should be noted that dominance is neither acquired nor asserted in a transient moment in time. It takes years for an enterprise to acquire a position of strength and its conduct has to be examined over a period of time in most cases to ascertain whether it constituted abuse. Use of snapshot data of market shares or market presence based on current active stock figures would give a false picture that, if applied in the context of the Act, may even cause avoidable harm to the market [emphasis supplied]. Therefore, our assessment of dominant position is by looking at the profile, presence and achievements of DLF Ltd. over the years as well as its conduct, particularly in relation to consumers, over a period of time. At the same time, it must be borne in mind that markets by their very nature are dynamic and keep changing with time. Hence, while analysing dominance, an important factor that needs to be taken into consideration is the time-period during which the contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act is alleged. For instance, after the DLF case, when similar allegations were once again raised for identical conduct by DLF two years later in Amit Mittal v. DLF Limited,14 Amit Mittal v. DLF Limited, Case No. 73 of 2014, para. 33. it was found that the market scenario had changed, and DLF was no longer dominant and consequently, no case of abuse can be made out.
