Similarly, in the Roche case,179 Roche case, para. 77. the CCI held that a partial denial of market access would also hurt the ability of a competitor to effectively compete on merits in the relevant market. It must be noted that the words in any manner in this clause were added by way of an amendment in 2007. The intent of this amendment was to include all strategies which may be carried out by the dominant enterprise to deter entry or defer potential competition. The said clause seeks to cover all instances wherein dominant undertaking by its actions is creating insurmountable barriers to entry for new entrants to come into the market. This interpretation has been reiterated in subsequent decisions of the CCI, such as the Play Store case wherein it was observed that denial of market access ‘in any manner’ would fall under the ambit of section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act, regardless of whether it is a denial of access to competitors or denial of access to players in vertically affected markets. In the Play Store case, the various policies of Google were found to be in violation of section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act. Through mandatory and exclusive imposition of GPBS, app developers were forced to only use GPBS for payment processing of paid apps and in-app purchases on the Play Store. As a result, app developers were prevented from conducting business with payment processors of their choice and correspondingly, payment processors were prohibited from offering their services to app developers listed on the Play Store. The analysis of the CCI has been described below:(i) Mandatory imposition of GPBS by Google results in denial of market access for payment aggregators as well as app developers. By virtue of imposition of GPBS, the app developers would be mandated to use Google’s services for payment processing. Further, the anti-steering provisions of Google forbid app developers from re-directing users to websites for payment outside the app. In the absence of these stipulations, the app developers could have negotiated better terms and conditions with other payment service providers in a competitive market. Hence, app developers are deprived of commercial freedom. (ii) This also forecloses the opportunity for other payment processing service providers to serve the app developers. This would also discourage app developers from developing their own in-app payment processor. With the mandatory imposition of GPBS, another payment processor will lose out on the market collectively valued over USD 3 billion, impacting their ability to carry out any innovations and product improvements, which can be catastrophic in such network-effect-driven markets. Further, payment processors were placed vertically with Google in relation to providing Play Store services to app developers and app users. Google’s practices of prohibiting app developers from using any other payment processors caused denial of market access to the payment processors in the vertically affected market. Google had argued that there was no violation of section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act because Google was not itself active in the alleged payment processing market and, as such, could not have abused that position to deny payment processors access to that market. In India, the CCI noted that Google subcontracts with third parties to conduct the payment processing for payments made through Google Play. Google also argued that the DG had not defined the relevant market in which the denial was alleged in order to assess whether there had been any anti-competitive effect. In this regard, the CCI noted that there is no legal requirement to precisely define a separate relevant market where the impact of the abusive conduct takes place. Accordingly, the CCI found that Google abused its dominant position in the app store market to cause denial to payment processors in general. This is sufficient to give a finding under section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act.180 Play Store case, para. 315. In the Automobile Spare Parts case, the CCI noticed that there had been restrictions imposed by the OEMs on the spare parts suppliers not to sell the spare parts in the open market. In the process, the independent repairers were not able to procure the spare parts over the counter and had no other option but to procure the spare parts from the authorized dealers. Through a network of agreements between OEMs and suppliers, it was ensured that the OEMs were the only source of supply of spare parts in the aftermarket. Based on the facts, the CCI concluded that the OEMs are engaging in practices which amount to denial of market access, and hence an abuse under section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act. The CCI noted that a dominant enterprise is engaging in an abuse when it itself competes in both the upstream and downstream markets and is in effective control of a product/service in the upstream market which is required by the enterprises in the downstream market to effectively carry out their economic activity, as this amounts to engaging in practices which may exclude a competitor from such downstream market. Such exclusion may be occasioned by refusing to supply such products/services to enterprises in the downstream market. The CCI relied on the investigation report of the DG, wherein the OEMs had submitted that the spare parts, diagnostic tools and workshop manuals are their proprietary materials and, therefore, accessible only to the authorized dealer network of each OEM. Further, the CCI observed that the make of each brand of car is unique, and the independent service providers require the spare parts and diagnostic tools compatible with the various models of automobiles manufactured by the various OEMs to carry out their economic activity of providing repair and maintenance services in the Indian automobile aftermarket. However, it was noted that each OEM, which is already a dominant player in the aftermarket for the supply of spare parts and diagnostic tools through their own or related network of authorized distributors, also operates in the aftermarket for after-sale repair and maintenance services of their own brand of cars as the sole supplier of their spare parts and diagnostic tools. Each OEM has two types of customers: (a) car owners who purchase the automobiles manufactured by the OEMs in the primary market, and (b) independent service providers in the aftermarket. An owner of a car cannot fit the spare parts into the machine by himself and requires the services of a specialized technician. Therefore, the owners of automobiles not only operate in the aftermarket as purchasers of spare parts but also require the service of firms engaged in maintenance and repair work. The independent repairers, who are not part of the official dealer network of the OEMs, do purchase the spare parts of the automobiles manufactured by the OEMs from the OEMs. Therefore, the independent service providers are customers of the OEMs in the aftermarket and further compete with the OEMs in the repairs and maintenance service aftermarket. Also, it was noticed by the CCI that even the authorized dealers of the OEMs are forbidden to supply spare parts to independent repairers over the counter. Based on these collective facts, the CCI observed that OEMs have effectively denied access to spare parts to these independent repairers by entering into agreements with the suppliers forbidding the suppliers to sell the spare parts in the open market. It concluded that such practice of preventing the independent repairers from competing with the authorized dealers of the OEMs amounted to denial of market access under section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act. The CCI also relied on the jurisprudence of the EC181 United Brands Co v. Commission of the European Union, Case 27/76 [1978] ECR 207, para. 189. that such behaviour cannot be countenanced if its ‘actual purpose is to strengthen this dominant position and abuse it’. Hence, denial of market access aimed at excluding a competitor from the market by means other than legitimate competition allows the OEMs to further strengthen their dominant position and abuse it. The CCI also touched upon briefly on the issue of denial of market access in the NSE case. It was alleged that NSE had taken a stake of about 26% in Omnesys, which is a technology vendor providing software for financial and securities market. The product of Omnesys, known as ‘NOW’ was made, which was a substitute for software called ‘ODIN’. ODIN was developed by FTIL, the promoter of MCX-SX and the market leader in the brokerage solutions sector. It was alleged that after taking an equity stake in Omnesys, DotEx intentionally wrote individually to the members/brokers of NSE, offering them NOW free of cost for the next three years. NSE also simultaneously refused to share its currency derivative segment Application Programme Interface Code (APIC) with FTIL, thus disabling the ODIN users (who constituted about 85% of the stock brokers/traders) from connecting to the currency derivative segment of the NSE from trading platform through their preferred mode, ODIN. The product thus thrust upon everyone (consumers) desirous of accessing the currency derivative segment of NSE was the product ‘NOW’ developed by Omnesys in place of ODIN. Further, it was alleged that NSE had put ‘ODIN’ on the watch list across all its segments, thereby deterring its members and their constituents from using ‘ODIN’ as their front-end solution. Based on the investigation, the CCI noted that ODIN and NOW are competing software and such software is an essential facility without which trading cannot be done. Relying on jurisprudence from matured anti-trust jurisdictions, it was noted by the CCI that the: (i) action of NSE of placing FTIL, the developer of ODIN (and one of the promoters of MCX-SX) on its watch list; and (ii) denying APIC for interface between its own software NOW and ODIN for the currency derivative segment, prevents the clients of the NSE, most of who use ODIN for all other segments, from choosing ODIN for the CD segment trade on NSE. The CCI noted that the said action is anti-competitive in the aftermarket of trading software for the currency derivative segment of the NSE. The CCI noted that if the APIC had been provided to ODIN, there would have been healthy competition between the two software platforms, ODIN and NOW, for clients. This competitive environment would have driven advancements in the technical development of all such trading software, benefiting the market. Based on this discussion, the CCI concluded that NSE/DotEX’s actions in denying APIC to ODIN and placing FTIL on a watch list amounted to exclusionary practice as they hindered competition both in the aftermarket for trading software on NSE and in the relevant market. [a] Denial of Market Access Includes Non-price Parameters Also The aspect of denial of market access in any manner is very wide, and it includes all strategies adopted by the dominant enterprise to deter or defer competition. This also includes non-price strategies adopted by dominant firms to unlawfully raise their rivals’ costs or exclude them from the market. An investigation was ordered, where it is alleged that the dominant enterprise has engaged in denial of market access by engaging in bad faith litigation and abusing the judicial process. The prima facie case made in this regard is against JCB India in the case of M/s Bull Machines Pvt Ltd. v. JCB India and Ors. 182 M/s Bull Machines Pvt Ltd. v. JCB India and Ors, Case No. 105 of 2013. (JCB case). The JCB case was initiated based on information filed by Bull Machines. Bull Machines was engaged in the manufacturing of low-cost backhoe loaders, called ‘Bull Smart’, a light construction equipment. It was alleged that during the formal launch of ‘Bull Smart’ at the ‘Excon 2011 Exhibition’, Bull Machines was served with an ex parte interim injunction order granted by the High Court of Delhi by JCB wherein it was alleged that Bull Machines had infringed the design registrations/copyright of JCB in developing the backhoe loader ‘Bull Smart’. The said order of the Delhi High Court restrained Bull Machines and its dealers ‘from making, selling, offering for sale, dispatch, advertising, directly or indirectly dealing in/launching backhoe loaders in any manner’. It is in the case of the Bull Machines that JCB obtained the ex parte ad interim injunction order based on misrepresentation of images/design registration number/documents and bogus numbers, suppression of its pre-existing UK patent, misrepresentation by comparing the wrong angle of the images in the application and reliance upon fraudulent design registrations which were pre-existing in the public domain. It is further alleged that JCB, armed with the ex parte order of the Hon’ble Delhi High Court, forced Bull Machines to remove the backhoe loader ‘Bull Smart’ from ‘Excon 2011 Exhibition’ in front of a huge crowd of dealers, existing and potential customers, end customers, financing company officials, bankers, suppliers, foreign delegates and peers in the industry and media. Post the order by the Delhi High Court, Bull Machines filed an application for vacation of the ex parte interim stay order and submitted all the evidence required to show that there is no similarity in the design of the backhoe loader ‘Bull Smart’ developed by it and the designs registered by JCB. Bull Machines also submitted evidence to the High Court showing that JCB misrepresented the images and the design registration numbers/documents and misled the Delhi High Court to secure an ad interim ex parte injunction order in its favour. It was averred by Bull Machines that after nearly ten months from the filing of the said suit, JCB voluntarily withdrew its application for ex parte interim injunction without submitting any inspection report or reply. In view of the above, the Delhi High Court dismissed the petition and the ex parte interim order procured by JCB by misrepresenting facts before the Delhi High Court was vacated. However, the case of Bull Machines was that huge irreparable damages had already been caused to Bull Machines, and in the process, JCB’s objective of deferring the entry of Bull Machines was achieved. The entire case of abuse made by Bull Machines is based upon the alleged bad faith litigation by JCB before the Hon’ble High Court of Delhi, which itself was based on designs and copyrights obtained fraudulently by JCB. It alleged that the bad faith litigation initiated by JCB against it alleging infringement of its design rights was totally false and that the said legal proceedings before the Delhi High Court were only initiated to harass it and prevent the launch of ‘Bull Smart’, which in effect would have competed with backhoe loaders of JCB in the relevant market. Based on the evidence on record, the CCI passed a prima facie view and asked the DG to investigate whether JCB has denied market access and foreclosed the entry of ‘Bull Smart’ by engaging itself in bad faith litigation. This was premised on its preliminary finding that predation through abuse of judicial processes presents an increasing threat to competition, owing to its relatively low anti-trust visibility. In contrast, in the Roche case, a case for denial or market access on account of vexatious litigation was not made out. The CCI noted that abusive strategies include rendering rivals’ products incompatible without adding any technical improvement to the replaced product; indulging in vexatious litigation purely aimed at harassing rivals; influencing government or regulatory procedures; and impeding the entry of generics/biosimilars by denigrating or disparaging rivals’ products. In this case, it was alleged that Roche indulged in vexatious civil litigation as a tactic to exhaust smaller rivals’ resources and delay or prevent their entry into the relevant market. The CCI observed that to cause denial of market access by engaging in legal action, such legal action should be aimed at harassing the competitor respondent without any objective basis and be conceived with an anti-competitive intent/plan to eliminate competition. However, in the Roche case, it could not be proved that the litigations filed by Roche were baseless or tainted with ulterior motives. Yet, a prima facie case for denial of market access was made out on the ground that Roche tried to influence regulatory authorities in its favour by misrepresenting the status of approvals granted to the informant’s drug.183 Roche case, para. 74. Further, when Roche was not successful in evading entry, it also approached doctors, hospitals, tender authorities, etc., to influence their perception of the efficacy and safety of the informants’ products. The latter behaviour crossed the line of puffery aimed at promoting one’s own product and actually amounted to maligning the image of the informant, thereby causing competitive advantages to them and consequent harm to the informant and the market as a whole. The abovementioned cases are a testament to the fact that the import of the expression ‘denial of market access in any manner’ is very wide and encompasses both price and non-price conduct. [E] Imposing Supplementary Obligations Section 4(2)(d) of the Competition Act provides that there shall be an abuse of dominant position if the dominant enterprise or group makes the conclusion of contracts subject to acceptance of supplementary obligations by other parties which, by their nature or according to commercial usage, have no connection with the subject of such contracts. This includes instances of tying and bundling. Tying instances would include cases of technical tying, contractual tying, tying of products with services, etc. It is to be noted that the practice of adopting tying practices, in itself, will not constitute a breach of the provisions of the Competition Act. As such, tying is a legitimate business practice, and an enterprise can make considerable savings in production, distribution and transaction costs. Based on international jurisprudence, the following needs to be addressed for an abuse of dominance of tying to be made out:(i) the enterprise has a dominant position regarding the tying product; (ii) the tied product is a distinct product from the tying product; (iii) element of coercion; (iv) anti-competitive foreclosure. In Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc., 184 Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc. and Facebook Inc., Case No. 15 of 2020. the CCI clarified that while ‘tying’ refers to a practice where the seller of a product or service (‘tying product’) requires the buyers to also purchase another separate product or service (‘tied product’); ‘bundling’ typically means that the two products are sold by the seller in a fixed proportion as a bundled package at a particular price. Based on economic literature, the CCI laid down four conditions, as described above, to assess whether WhatsApp was tying its messaging services with its UPI enabled Digital Payments App: (i) the tying and tied products are two separate products (WhatsApp Messenger and WhatsApp Pay are two distinct products with different functionalities operating in different markets); (ii) the entity concerned is dominant in the market for the tying product (WhatsApp is prima facie dominant in the relevant market, i.e., ‘market for OTT messaging apps through smartphones in India’); (iii) the customers do not have a choice to only obtain the tying product without the tied product (Installation of the WhatsApp messenger does not appear to explicitly mandate/coerce the user to use WhatsApp Pay exclusively); and (iv) the tying is capable of restricting/foreclosing competition in the market (UPI digital payments market is very competitive and consists of various established players so WhatsApp Pay will not automatically gain market share due to pre-installation). Thus, since the elements of coercion and foreclosure were absent, the allegation of tying against WhatsApp was not made out. Even if one of the above elements is missing, then a case for tying would not be made out. For instance, in Baglekar Akash Kumar v. Google LLC, 185 Baglekar Akash Kumar v. Google LLC, Case No. 39 of 2020. it was alleged that Google had tied its video-conferencing app Google Meet with its email service, i.e., Gmail. However, the CCI found that Google Meet is also available as an independent app outside the Gmail ecosystem, and users have the choice to use either of the apps with all their functionalities without necessarily having to use the other. Hence, no case for tying is made out since there is no coercion. Tying cases can be covered under both section 4(2)(d) and section 4(2)(e), which deals with leveraging. It can also include practices where an IPR holder licences its IPR only on some additional condition. This has been covered in the chapter on IPR and competition law. In the Google Android case,186 Google Android case, paras 345-373. as discussed earlier, Google had a dominant position in the market of licensable OS, i.e., Android, as well as the supply of each of the services offered under GMS, i.e., Search, YouTube, Chrome and Play Store. Further, we have also discussed how the OEMs had weak countervailing power with respect to Google since each of Google’s services was a ‘must have’, and the OEMs had no option except to agree to Google’s terms in order to access these services. Since Android-based applications are written to work with Google Play Services, a smart device without them would not be commercially viable or at least the OEM offering these devices would be competitively disadvantaged because the customers also would not find such a smart device attractive. Google did not negotiate on key terms of the MADA, which were found to result in anti-competitive conduct: pre-installation of the entire suit of GMS as well as prominent placement thereof. By foreclosing the market for rivals, MADA also reduced the potential choice for users. Based on the investigation, the CCI also concluded that Google Play and other apps of Google covered under GMS have different functionalities and thus, there is no technical necessity to offer these services together. Hence, the CCI found the obligations under MADA, RSA, AFA and ACC to be in the nature of supplementary obligations imposed by Google on OEMs since if they wish to pre-install even a single app of Google, they had to agree to the pre-installation requirements of the entire bouquet of Google’s apps. Additionally, the said Google apps were to be prominently placed on the mobile; thus, it was a case of both pre-installation and primary placement. Thus, Google’s conduct was held to be in contravention of section 4(2)(d) of the Competition Act. Subsequently, the NCLAT187 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, paras 94-98. upheld the findings of the CCI in light of Google’s anti-competitive requirements under MADA regarding prominent placement, pre-installation and other conditions applied to OEMs. It noted that: Clause 4.4 of MADA enjoins upon the OEM to – (i) distribute all Core Applications, (ii) distribute on the Default Home Screen a Google – provided widget, Google Play Store icon; and an icon that provides direct access to Core Applications which is labelled as ‘Google’, and among other conditions also stipulates that any Google Application that is not a Core Application is placed no more than one level below the Default Home Screen. There is also a condition that the OEM shall implement the “Home button animation” as per Google’s guidelines if Google Assistant is enable on the Android device and also implement Google Hotword, if it is supported by the device. On this basis, the NCLAT concluded that the conditions which are applied on OEMs through MADA which essentially provides Google Applications, are in the form of ‘supplementary obligations’ attracting section 4(2)(d). [F] Leveraging of Dominant Position Section 4(2)(e) of the Competition Act provides that there shall be an abuse of dominant position when the dominant undertaking uses its dominant position in one relevant market to enter into or protect another relevant market. The Competition Act has expressly included the aspect of leveraging as an abuse of dominant position. A contravention of section 4(2)(e) requires two markets to be identified, the first relevant market in which the erring entity is alleged to be dominant and the second market in which the said entity is entering or protecting its position, directly or indirectly, by virtue of dominance in the first relevant market. The key point of consideration is that there must be an exploitation of market power by the dominant player in the market in which the enterprise is dominant to enter into or protect its position in the market in which such player is not dominant. There are situations, especially in a multi-product company or group, where there are a lot of joint costs and cross-subsidization. When cross-subsidization becomes an issue of leveraging depends on the facts and circumstances of each case:(a) The CCI has examined the provision relating to leveraging in the NSE case and the Automobile Spare Parts case. The CCI has noted that the dominant enterprise in the first market need not possess a dominant market position in the second market where it makes attempts to leverage its market power. The aspect of leveraging deals with a situation where an enterprise in dominant position in any delineable relevant market uses its strength therein to enter or protect any other delineable relevant market. The Competition Act does not provide that there has to be a high degree of associational link between the two markets (the market in which the enterprise is dominant and the second market which it intends to enter or protect) being considered for the abuse of leveraging. The CCI, in the NSE case observed that:The Indian Competition Act recognizes leveraging as an act by an enterprise or group that “uses by its dominant position in one relevant market to enter into, or protect, other relevant market.” Nowhere does the Act indicate that there has to be a high degree of associational link between the two markets being considered for this sub section. This is so because competition concerns are much higher in India than in more mature jurisdictions because of the historical lack of competition laws. In India, if an enterprise dominant in the market of audio-visual (AV) equipment enters into the market of say, computers, it is possible for it to use its strength in terms of finances, technological expertise, sales network etc. in the AV market to muscle its way into and protect its position in the computer market, even though the two markets are not at all connected. That is why the Act does not indicate any requirement of associational link. (b) As observed in the NSE case, the section uses the term ‘relevant market’ and other relevant market. Hence, it is incorrect to argue that the whole of section 4 pivots around determination of only one ‘relevant market’. There can be a second relevant market which may also be treated as the ‘other market’ for the purpose of section 4(2)(e). The Competition Act recognizes the fact that an enterprise may be multi-product and may be operating in two (or more) markets. In case of multi-product enterprises, such enterprises can use their position of strength derived in one market to leverage their position and gain an unfair advantage over their competitors in the other market. While its conduct in the second market has to be separately examined for abuse if and after it acquires a dominant position in the second market, the fact that it has used the strengths from the first market to wrongfully enter into or to protect the second market is itself an abuse under the Competition Act. It is to be further noted that in the event such an enterprise, after entering the other relevant market through such leveraging and thereafter acquires dominant position in the other market, commits further acts of abuse in that relevant market, then there would be a separate violation of section 4(2)(a)–(d) of the Competition Act. The ‘relevant market’ of the explanation (a) applies equally in intent for sections 4(1) and (2), but the relevant market in respect of clauses (a) to (d) of section 4(2) can be different than the relevant market for the purpose of clause (e). The CCI observed in the NSE case as follows:To further clarify, if an enterprise merely uses its dominant position in any ‘relevant market’ to enter or protect some other “relevant market” wrongfully, it can only be held guilty of contravening section 4(2)(e). But if the enterprise, after entering the other relevant market through such leveraging and acquiring dominant position there, commits further acts of abuse (such as unfair pricing) in that relevant market, then there would be a separate violation of section 4(2)(a). It is worthwhile to observe here that the language of section 4(2)(e) does not exclude the possibility that the enterprise is dominant in both, the “relevant market” as well as the ‘other relevant market’. An enterprise can be dominant in one market and can enter another market, acquire position of strength there and then commit acts to protect its position. Based on the above approach, the CCI noted that NSE, a super dominant entity in the non-currency derivative market, leveraged its dominant position by using its strength in the non-currency derivative segment to enter into and protect its position in the currency derivative segment. The CCI observed that they could issue periodical waivers in the transaction fees in the currency derivative segment and yet earn profits in the process, while the others earned huge losses because of their strength in the non-currency derivative segment. It is noteworthy to point out that when the matter went up in appeal before the erstwhile COMPAT, it disagreed with the definition of the relevant market adopted by the CCI. The CCI had identified two markets: a market for currency derivative and a market for non-currency derivative segment. After defining the market, the CCI observed that the NSE is using its dominance in the non-currency derivate segment market to protect its position in the currency derivate segment market. However, the erstwhile COMPAT held that there is only one definition of relevant market, that is, the market for stock exchange services market where NSE is committing the abuse of unfair pricing (discussed above).188 National Stock Exchange of India Ltd. v. CCI, [2014] Comp AT 129, paras 116-121. Since there was only one definition of relevant market adopted by erstwhile COMPAT, it held that NSE could not have been guilty of breaching section 4(2)(e) of the Competition Act. Having said that, NSE was found to be guilty of section 4(2)(a) of the Competition Act for unfair pricing. Increasingly, there is a focus on cross-leveraging by digital platforms which are able to exploit user data and create functional entry barriers in the market by entrenching themselves in neighbouring or even in unrelated markets.189 WhatsApp case. The aspect of data as a tool for abuse has been discussed in Chapter 6. In the Google Android case,190 Google Android case, paras 374-465. it was found that Google engaged in tying (through pre-installation) and leveraging by using its dominant position in one market to enter and protect the other market. Google engaged in tying conduct in several ways – it tied access to Play Store with its services of Search, Chrome as well as YouTube through imposition of MADA which required pre-installation of the entire bouquet of Google’s apps even if the OEMs wanted to access only one app. Google’s different services constituted different markets in each case and Google was found to be dominant in both. The NCLAT191 Paragraphs 136-143. affirmed the findings of the CCI that tying between Play Store and Search, Chrome and YouTube was used to achieve and perpetuate dominance by Google in the markets of the latter services, thereby causing anti-competitive effects such as foreclosure and denial of consumer choice. Similarly, in the Play Store case, the CCI held Google to be guilty of leveraging its dominance in markets other than the relevant markets delineated by it. It was found that the practices followed by Google resulted in leveraging its dominance in the: (i) market for licensable mobile OS and app stores for Android OS to protect its position in the downstream markets; and (ii) market for app stores for Android OS to protect its position in the market for apps facilitating payments through UPI in India, in violation of the provisions of section 4(2)(e) of the Competition Act.192 Play Store case, paras 317, 358. In Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc and Ors., Google was found to prima facie occupy a significant position in the relevant markets: Online General Web Search Services, Online Search Advertising Services, and the market for Online Digital Advertising Services. It was observed that Google was using its dominant position in the relevant markets to enter/protect its position in the market for news aggregation services in violation of section 4(2)(e) of the Competition Act. The issue of leveraging was also discussed in detail in the Automobile Spare Parts case. As has been discussed at length above, it was noted by the CCI that the OEMs have a dominant position in the market for spare parts and diagnostic tools because of the limited inter-brand interchangeability of spare parts. Further, the OEMs, pursuant to a network of restrictive contracts and commercial practices, became the sole supplier of genuine spare parts and diagnostic tools in the aftermarket. Additionally, the OEMs are also active in the market for repair and maintenance through their network of authorized dealers. The CCI noted that there are interlinkages between the repair/service markets and the spare parts/diagnostic tools market, and the OEMs have a commercial incentive to leverage their dominance from the relevant market of spare parts/diagnostic tools to that of repairs and maintenance services. The CCI noted that OEMs (acting through their authorized dealer network) deny 94.99% of the total service providers active in the Indian automobile aftermarket (consisting of multi-brand retailers, semi-organized service stations and unorganized garage workshops), effective access to the Indian aftermarket on competitive terms. It was noted that the OEMs typically provide after-sale services and repair of their brand of automobiles through a network of authorized dealers. These authorized distributors are also the source of supply of genuine spare parts for various models of automobiles manufactured by an OEM. As discussed above, the OEMs restrict the ability of independent repairers to obtain spare parts from authorized dealers. Further, the OEMs restrict the suppliers from supplying spare parts in the open market. In addition, the OEMs had also imposed a warranty condition on the consumers that their warranty would be denied if the cars were serviced by an independent repairer or other multi-brand service repairers. The CCI also noted that in the limited instances where spare parts are available to the actual owners, the owners have to buy such spare parts at the MRP and then avail the services of the independent repairers at additional costs, whereas the authorized dealers are able to provide such services at cheaper rates since the applicable spare parts are available to the authorized dealers at a discount over the MRP. Therefore, in practice, in most cases, the owners of various brands of automobiles are completely dependent on the authorized dealer network of the OEMs and are not in a position to exercise the option of availing services of independent repairers. Therefore, the users of cars wanting to purchase the spare parts have to necessarily avail the services of the authorized dealers of the OEM. Based on these facts, the CCI held that the OEMs are leveraging their dominant position in the market for the supply of spare parts to protect their market of maintenance of cars.

§3.07 OBJECTIVE JUSTIFICATION Dominant enterprises often seek to justify their conduct that may appear anti-competitive on the surface based on business efficiencies. The legitimacy of such objective justifications is based on a determination of the facts and circumstances. At the outset, it is clarified that such a justification must be able to stand on its own legs without itself being illegal. For instance, in the DLF case, DLF argued that the (abusive) terms of its agreements with the buyers were a standard ‘industry practice’. However, the CCI observed that ‘even without taking into account the fact that being an industry practice cannot be a defence for anti-competitive practices/conduct, DLF for one certainly cannot claim the defence of having adopted an industry practice for practices which are found to be anti-competitive and/or abusive.’ As discussed earlier, the CCI, in their decisional practice, has adopted the effects test. The effect test implies that unilateral conduct may escape the prohibition of section 4 of the Competition Act in case the dominant enterprise can provide an objective justification for its behaviour or it can demonstrate that its conduct produces efficiencies which outweigh the negative effect on competition. The burden of proof for such an objective justification or efficiency defence will be on the dominant enterprise. The possible types of objective justification would be in cases where it can be shown that dominant enterprise can show that the otherwise abusive conduct is actually necessary conduct on the basis of objective business rationale. The NSE also raised multiple business rationales for engaging in fee waivers in the currency derivative segment to promote the currency derivative. NSE raised the point of promotional/penetrative pricing.193 NSE case, para. 10.57. It is to be noted that although these defences were not held to be valid by the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT, yet they heard the matter on objective justification at great length. Their approach implies that the CCI will take into account objective justification as a defence. In this background, it becomes all the more necessary for companies, that have a high market share, to adopt proper documentation of their business processes and decisions which can be used in defence in an abuse of dominance investigation. The CCI has also held that protection of commercial interest by a dominant enterprise, at the cost of competition, is contrary to its responsibility cast under the Competition Act. Effective competition does not necessarily mean prevalence of the most efficient to the exclusion of relatively less efficient choices to consumers. In any case, there is no justification for imposing restrictions on competition by a dominant enterprise to protect its commercial interest.194 East India Petroleum Pvt. Ltd. v. South Asia LPG Company Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 76 of 2011. In the Google Android case,195 Google Android case, para. 489. Google had argued that users and OEMs prefer Google’s services due to their superior nature. In fact, this may be a common argument taken by any dominant enterprise that the reason for their success is the strength of their product. However, it is pointed out that if that is the case, then the market has to be the judge, and this ‘preference’ cannot be forced on users. On similar lines, the CCI observed that:If Google is of the view that the presence of its applications provides a great out-of-the-box experience, then the user or the OEM would itself decide to use its application. There does not appear to be any necessity to impose restrictions through MADA, RSAs and AFA/ACC, to achieve the same. The market forces would itself take care of that. In the MMT case,196 MMT case, paras 264-266. MMT-Go claimed that its parity obligations were an industry practice, particularly to address the problem of free-riding – i.e., to prevent hotels from benefitting from the investment of the OTA in advertisement and promotion of the hotel for free. However, the CCI rejected this since the effect and implications of a conduct indulged in by a dominant platform on its users and competition in the relevant market is fundamentally different from when a smaller insignificant player does it. Comparing the combined effect that deep discounting and parity together have, when imposed by a dominant platform that has already reaped the benefits of network effects in its favour, with that of the parity conditions of other platforms is not a like-to-like comparison. Further, the aspect of the special responsibility of a dominant player like MMT-Go was also brought into the picture due to the potential adverse effects that it may have on competition as well as hotels. [A] Effects Doctrine The agreements or combinations having or likely to have an AAEC in India are declared void under sections 3(2) and 6(1) of the Competition Act, respectively. However, section 4 does not have any such similar provision. Sections 3 and 6 are concerned with the ‘effects’ of an agreement or a combination. In contrast, under section 4, because of the dominant position enjoyed by an enterprise, the legislature in its wisdom has decided that an ‘enterprise’ shall not ‘abuse’ such a position. The emphasis under section 4(1) is on ‘abuse’ and the word ‘abuse’ has not been defined under the Competition Act; hence, the presumption was that the legislature has wisely left it open to be decided on a case-by-case basis because the ‘nature and type of abuse’ may differ with time and depending upon the market in question. However, this position has now been clarified by the NCLAT which has held that the effects doctrine is applicable to section 4 as well, as shall be discussed below. While the wording of the Competition Act may show that the principle of strict liability is laid down and no effects have to be seen (an ‘unlawful per se’ approach), the CCI has incorporated the effects doctrine in its analysis of dominance investigations, in line with global practice. In this regard, it is apposite to note the observations of the CCI in the case of Dhanraj Pillai v. Hockey India, wherein the CCI looked into the effects of the restrictive conditions imposed and noted that the conditions did not amount to abuse of dominance as they were intrinsic and proportionate to the objectives of the organization:197 Dhanraj Pillai v. Hockey India, Case No. 73 of 2011. Commission opines that proportionality of the regulations can only be decided by considering the manner in which regulations are applied [emphasis supplied]. It is the manner of applying regulations that raise competition concerns as it may be used as a tool for foreclosing new entrants. Similarly, in the Schott Glass case, the erstwhile COMPAT found that unlawful price discrimination required a showing of both ‘(i) dissimilar treatment to equivalent transactions; and (ii) harm to competition or likely harm to competition in the sense that the buyers suffer a competitive disadvantage against each other leading to competitive injury in the downstream market.’ The erstwhile COMPAT found the CCI had wrongly ignored the second limb and that the evidence showed there was no effect on the downstream market and the ultimate consumer did not suffer as a result of the alleged conduct. In this case, not only was an effects-based analysis undertaken, but also the objective justifications raised by the parties to justify their conduct were considered. The NCLAT made elaborate observations on applicability of the effects doctrine to section 4 in the Google Android case and laid down the test as follows: For proving abuse of dominance under section 4, effect analysis is required to be done, and the test to be employed in the effect analysis is whether the abusive conduct is anti-competitive or not. 198 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023. For this, the NCLAT undertook a detailed analysis, which has been summarised below:(i) The legislative intent is clear in respect of the expression ‘AAEC’ being only present in sections 3 and 6, and not section 4. But what needs to be examined is whether, even if the AAEC test is not attracted in section 4, any analysis of the conduct of an enterprise or group of anti-competitive has to be looked into or not? The answer is yes. (ii) The 2009 Guidance to Article 102 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the EU noted that for exclusionary abuses in Article 102, the European Commission (EC) will intervene if there is any likelihood of anticompetitive foreclosure. Even in its recent case laws, the EC has adopted an effects-based approach while analysing exclusionary abuse. However, such an effects-based approach is not mandated for exploitative abuses under Article 102. (iii) In Intel v. Commission, 199 Intel Corp. v. European Commission, Case C-413/14 P. the General Court continued to adopt a strict per se approach to exclusivity rebates, which it said were illegal unless the dominant firm could show an objective justification for granting them. However, there was an increasing intellectual consensus against the application of per se rules to unilateral behaviour, and the judgment of the General Court in Intel attracted particular hostility because of its formalistic approach. This has been confirmed in recent decisions such as in Paroxetine 200 Generics (UK) Ltd and Others v. Competition and Markets Authority, Case C-307/18, para. 154. [‘…having regard to all relevant facts, conduct may be characterised as abusive only if it is capable of restricting competition and, in particular, producing exclusionary effects.’] and TeliaSonera 201 Konkurrensverket v. TeliaSonera Sverige AB, Case C-52/09, para. 68. [in order to establish whether (a margin squeeze) is abusive, that practice must have an anti-competitive effect on the market.] Further, in its decisional practice, it has sought to produce evidence of anti-competitive effects. Importantly, the NCLAT relied on the findings of the Report of the High Level Committee on Competition Law, which preceded the Competition Act, and noted that the dominance investigations should be undertaken based on the effects doctrine. The key issue that needs to be analysed is whether the conduct of the dominant enterprise is harming the competitive process prevalent in the relevant market. The relevant observations of the report are as follows:Discriminatory behaviour and any other exercise of market power leading to the prevention, restriction or distortion of competition would obviously be included. We probably need to clarify that there is a fine distinction between defending one’s market position or market share which is perfectly legal and legitimate and may involve certain level of aggressive competitive behaviour and exclusionary and anti-competitive behaviour. Key questions for adjudication on abuse of dominance could include:– How will the practice harm competition? – Will it deter or prevent entry? – Will it reduce incentives of the firm and its rivals to compete aggressively? – Will it provide the dominant firm with an additional capacity to raise prices? – Will it prevent investments in research and innovation? – Do consumers benefit from lower prices and/or greater product and service availability? In order to understand the aim of the provision, the discussion on abuse in the Raghavan Committee Report was perused. The Committee took note that this report indicates that an effects based approach was contemplated to establish abuse of dominance under Section 4. … Based on the above, the Committee discussed that the CCI has interpreted Section 4(2) keeping in mind that one of the key aims of the Act is to prevent practices which adversely affect competition in India. It has therefore, wherever appropriate, analysed the effects of alleged abusive conduct by dominant entities before passing orders regarding such conduct. The CCI has relied on the effects built into some of the clauses of Section 4(2) to support its approach, e.g. ‘denial of market access in any manner’ in Section 4(2)(c). After conducting an analysis of the CCI’s orders, the Committee came to the conclusion that the current text of Section 4(2) has not proven to be a hindrance to the CCI’s ability to assess effects in abuse of dominance disputes. It was agreed that since it may not be necessary to undertake an effects analysis in all kinds of abuse, e.g. exploitative abuse, it may not be appropriate to mandate an effects analysis in Section 4(2). Therefore, it was concluded that no legislative amendment is required in this regard. [emphasis supplied] This approach taken by the Report of the High Level Committee on Competition Law suggests that conduct in question must in the first place have the capability, by its nature, to distort competition or foreclose competitors from the market. To establish such capability, it is in general sufficient to investigate the form and nature of the conduct in question. Second, it implies that, in the specific market context, a likely market-distorting foreclosure effect must be established. The competition law concern is to prevent exclusionary conduct of the dominant firm202 Bharti Airtel Limited v. Reliance Industries Limited and Anr, Case No. 03 of 2017. which is likely to limit the remaining competitive constraints on the dominant company, including the entry of newcomers, so as to avoid consumers being harmed. This means that it is competition, and not competitors as such, that is to be protected. By foreclosure, it means that actual or potential competitors are completely or partially denied profitable access to a market. Foreclosure may discourage the entry or expansion of rivals or encourage their exit. Foreclosure thus can be found even if the foreclosed rivals are not forced to exit the market: it is sufficient that the rivals are disadvantaged and consequently led to compete less aggressively. [B] IPR as a Defence Protection of IPRs has been separately included as a defence to anti-competitive conduct under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. However, this defence is only limited to anti-competitive agreements. There have been several discussions to extend the benefit of this defence to abuse of dominance as well. In fact, the Parliamentary Standing Committee Report, 2022 also recommended the inclusion of a defence allowing reasonable conditions and restrictions for protecting IPR may be provided in cases of abuse of dominance on the lines with the international jurisdictions, that is, the EU, the US and the UK which provide for such exemption. However, the Parliament rejected the same, and so far, the provision has not found its way into the Competition Act due to concerns in the era of the new age economy that mention of IPR defence explicitly may allow a dominant player to abuse its position of dominance. However, it is apposite to note that the NCLAT, in its decisional practice, seems to have upheld the use of IPR as a defence by dominant enterprises in the Google Android case.203 Google Android case, paras 188-190. The CCI had directed Google not to deny access to its APIs to OEMS, app developers and existing or potential app developers. Google argued that it cannot be asked to share APIs as it constitutes the IPR of Google. The NCLAT upheld this argument and set aside the direction of CCI to allow open access to Google’s APIs by observing that Google’s APIs constitute its proprietary software, into which Google has made investments for scientific and technical development. Thus, the same should fetch value to Google and allowing open access to such proprietary software will decrease the incentive for technological companies to continue innovation.

§3.08 CONCLUSION The decisional practice and statutory regime of abuse of dominance indicates a holistic consideration of all facts and circumstances to hold an entity guilty of abuse. It is not a static consideration but gives scope for commercial justifications and business efficiencies. At the same time, dominant players have to be cautious about employing business practices in line with their special responsibility to prevent genuine and undistorted growth of competition in the market. The idea is to strike a balance – to ensure a competitive and level playing field for all market participants and prevent monopolistic practices that may harm consumers and hinder economic growth and, at the same time, prevent excessive curbs on dominant enterprises and avoid hindering innovation in the market.

title: Chapter 4: Mergers and Competition Law
container_title: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition)

sections.title: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: §4.01 INTRODUCTION It is imperative to have an effective merger control regime to ensure that corporate reorganizations do not distort the competitive structure of the market. Considering combinations create permanent and lasting changes to the market, it is necessary to ensure timely intervention in case the combination causes or is likely to cause any AAEC. At the same time, interventions should not be so strict so as to deter combinations and ease of doing business in the economy. The Indian merger control regime, which comprises the Competition Act and the attendant CCI (Procedure in regard to the transaction of business relating to Combinations) Regulations, 2011 (‘Combination Regulations’), has attempted to strike a balance between these two goals. In this chapter, CCI’s approach to regulation of combinations, viz. the procedure followed, factors considered, obligations of the parties, remedies issued and penalties imposed have been discussed. To date, in its decisional practice, the CCI has not refused approval for any combination. They have imposed both behavioural and structural commitments. In this background, the chapter also highlights the emerging trends in the Indian merger control regime.

sections.title: THRESHOLD LIMITS
sections.text: §4.02 THRESHOLD LIMITS Section 5 of the Competition Act characterizes combinations under four broad categories:(a) acquisition of shares, voting rights, control or assets of an enterprise; (b) acquisition of control of an enterprise by an enterprise engaged in a similar line of business; and (c) mergers or amalgamation; (d) deal value threshold.1 Yet to be notified in the official Gazette. The Indian merger control regime provides for mandatory notification in case the prescribed thresholds under the Competition Act of assets or turnover are met. At the outset, it is also clarified that for the purpose of determining notifiability under the Competition Act, the value of assets and turnover of the acquirer along with the target must be considered. This is clear from the language of section 5(a) which lists the thresholds applicable to the control, shares, assets or voting rights of the parties to the combination ‘jointly’.2 Section 43A Order against Diasys Diagnostics Systems GmbH, 31 Aug. 2016, para. 14. A tabular snapshot of the present threshold limit for assets and turnover is as below. [A] Parties to the Combination Situation Entity Assets Turnover In India Acquirer and Target >INR 2,000 crores3 1 crore = 10 million. Or >INR 6,000 crores OR Worldwide Acquirer and Target >USD 1 billion Or >USD 3 billion Of which in India INR 1,000 crores INR 3,000 crores [B] Group Thresholds Scenario Entity Assets Turnover In India Group and Target >INR 8,000 crores Or >INR 24,000 crores OR Worldwide Group and Target >USD 4 billion Or >USD 12 billion Of which in India INR 1,000 crores INR 3,000 crores Section 20(3) of the Competition Act provides that the Central Government, in consultation with the CCI, can enhance or reduce, or keep at the same level,4 Competition (Amendment) Act 2023, section 20. the threshold limits of the value of assets and turnover mentioned under section 5 of the Competition Act. The enhancement will be based on the wholesale price index on fluctuation in the exchange rate of the rupee or foreign currencies or other such factors deemed relevant by the Central Government. [C] Deal Value Thresholds The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023 amended section 5 to introduce a new deal value threshold to cover those combinations where the value of the transaction in connection with the acquisition of any control, shares, voting rights or assets of an enterprise, merger or amalgamation exceeds INR 2,000 crores and, the target has substantial business operations in India. Noticeably, the de minimis or target exemption (as discussed in the subsequent parts of this chapter) will not be available to a transaction if the deal value thresholds are met, and thus, the transaction will necessarily be notifiable. The objective of the amendment is to cover ‘killer acquisitions’, a term used to refer to combinations in technology and digital markets where big entities acquire smaller rivals, i.e., kill their competitors by acquiring them. Since such firms generate returns over time, often operating on zero or even negative revenue in their initial years, these transactions tend to escape antitrust scrutiny. Some commentators across the globe, including in India, felt that the absence of such deal value thresholds was one of the major reasons why such acquisitions could not be checked in an ex ante framework. This was a significant loophole as several transactions involving industry leaders, such as Facebook/WhatsApp, Flipkart/Myntra, etc., escaped competition assessment by the CCI since the asset and turnover thresholds were not triggered. Further, the Competition Commission of India (Combinations) Regulations, 2023 (‘Draft Regulations’) clarify that ‘value of the transaction’ refers to ‘every valuable consideration, whether direct or indirect, immediate or deferred, cash or otherwise’ and shall include non-compete fees as well as, consideration attributable to interconnected transactions, incidental commercial arrangements entered into within two years of the closing of the notifiable transaction (such as technology assistance agreements, licensing of IPR and supply of materials), options and securities on an as-converted basis and contingencies. In case the transaction value is not specified in the transaction documents, then the value approved by the board of directors or other approving authority of the notifying party shall be considered. If the precise transaction value cannot be determined with certainty, then there is a presumption that the deal value threshold has been met. A target enterprise will be deemed to have substantial business operations in India if at least 10% or more of its global:(i) users, subscribers, customers, or visitors in the twelve months preceding the deal execution date were in India or; (ii) gross merchandise value, for the twelve months preceding the deal execution date was from India or; (iii) turnover for the preceding financial year was in India. All the thresholds prescribed under section 5 are sector-agnostic, which means that they are applicable to all industries regardless of the nature of their operation. Further, the criteria for determining substantial business operations look beyond mere asset and turnover by accounting for factors like users, subscribers, etc. Hence, the deal value threshold is proposed to be a catch-all provision to prevent firms, especially those operating in digital markets, from escaping CCI’s scrutiny.

sections.title: TARGET LEVEL/DE MINIMIS EXEMPTION
sections.text: §4.03 TARGET LEVEL/DE MINIMIS EXEMPTION The de minimis or target exemption was introduced by way of a notification issued by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs, Government of India (MCA Circular) in March 2011 in exercise of the powers under section 54 of the Competition Act.5 MCA Notification S.0. 482(E), published in Gazette dated 4 Mar. 2011. It exempts any transaction from notification requirement where the:(i) value of assets being acquired, taken control of, merged or amalgamated is less than INR 350 crores in India or; (ii) turnover is less than INR 1,000 crores in India. These thresholds were increased from INR 250 crores to INR 350 crores and INR 750 crores to INR 1,000 crores, respectively, in March 2016.6 MCA Notification S.0. 674(E), published in Gazette dated 4 Mar. 2016. Subsequently, the MCA issued another notification in March 2017 to expand the scope of the target exemption, which was earlier available to only certain types of combinations resulting from acquisitions, to combinations resulting from mergers, amalgamations and acquisition of control transactions.7 MCA Notification S.0. 988(E), published in Gazette dated 27 Mar. 2017. Originally, the de minimis exemption was valid for a period of five years till March 2022, but it was subsequently extended till March 2027.8 MCA Notification S.0. 1192(E), published in Gazette dated 16 Feb. 2022. Through the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, the de minimis exemption which was provided through the MCA Circular has been formally included in the Competition Act itself. As mentioned earlier, the target exemption is not available to combinations notifiable on the basis of the deal value threshold. Thus, transactions which do meet the target exemption are not notifiable because ordinarily, such transactions do not cause or are not likely to cause AAEC. However, not making it available to a transaction which triggers the deal value threshold implies that legislative intent is to lend greater scrutiny to combinations involving digital and technology companies since their distortionary impact on the market is not immediately seen. [A] Asset and Turnover Details As discussed above and based on the decisional practice of CCI, a transaction involving the acquisition of a target enterprise which meets the prescribed thresholds will be exempt from merger control filing requirements. It is to be noted that for the de minimis exemption to be applicable, either the asset or turnover requirement has to be met. For the sake of clarity, if the target enterprise has assets of more than INR 350 crores but a turnover of less than INR 1,000 crores, the target exemption will be applicable, and no merger notification will be required to be made. [1] Scope ofDe Minimis Exemption CCI’s earlier approach was that even if only certain assets of an enterprise are being acquired, the entire assets and turnover of the enterprise, on a consolidated basis are to be taken into account for determining notifiability.9 G&K Baby Care Pvt. Ltd. and Danone Asia Pacific Holidays 11/Adyay, Combination Registration No. C-2011/08/03, 24 Aug. 2011; AICA Kogyo Company Limited and Aica Laminates Indian Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2011/09/04, 30 Sep. 2011; NHK Automotive Components India Pvt. Ltd. and NHK Spring Co. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2011/10/05, 4 Nov. 2011. Therefore, such assets, though seemingly not relevant to the transaction in terms of impacting any competitive structure, would still have to be notified. However, this position has undergone a change, as discussed below. In the acquisition of ‘Savlon’ and ‘Shower to Shower’ trademarks along with other related assets from Johnson & Johnson by ITC Limited, i.e., the acquirer, notice was not filed with the CCI.10 Section 43 Order against ITC Limited, 11 Dec. 2017, paras 10, 11. It was argued that acquisition of mere trademarks of an enterprise does not amount to acquisition of ‘assets’. However, the CCI observed that trademarks constitute an asset since they provide economic value to their owners. Further, explanation (e) to section 5 of the Competition Act also includes trademarks and similar other commercial rights provided under various laws within the ambit of ‘value of assets’. Thus, acquisition of trademarks is an acquisition of assets in terms of section 5(a) of the Competition Act and is a combination if the jurisdictional thresholds are met. Further, the CCI also observed that acquisition of immovable properties or manufacturing facilities is not a pre-requisite for attracting section 5 of the Act, as assets include not only tangible assets but also intangible assets, in the form of IPRs, viz. trademarks. The second limb of CCI’s reasoning was rather contentious. In assessing the applicability of the de minimis exemption threshold, the CCI held that where the sale involved a part, a division or business of the selling entity, the assets/turnover figures of the entire seller entity, rather than those of the part, etc., being transferred, should be taken into account. Basis this approach, the exemption was not available and ITC should have notified the acquisition. Accordingly, a penalty was imposed on ITC. Upon appeal, the NCLAT set aside the penalty imposed by CCI by holding that only the assets/turnover attributable to the portion, division or business being transferred should be counted for the target.11 ITC Limited v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 11 of 2018. Another aspect to note is that at the time of notification, the value of the asset or turnover of the target in India had to be below INR 250 crores and INR 750 crores, respectively. During the pendency of the appeal, the thresholds were increased to INR 350 crores and INR 1,000 crores, respectively. As a result, the NCLAT also held that the notification was clarificatory in nature and applied with retrospective effect. As a result, the relevant turnover of the target fell below the threshold, and the de minimis exemption was available for the transaction. Therefore, the NCLAT propounded the legal position by clarifying that the assets and turnover of only the relevant portion of the target’s business have to be calculated to determine the applicability of the de minimis exemption. If the entire assets and turnover were considered, then the target exemption would be rendered meaningless because several transactions would have to be notified, even if the actual value of the transaction (i.e., in terms of the relevant business undergoing combination) is much lower than the prescribed threshold. It appears obvious that an extension of the principle that the assets/turnover of only the relevant portion of the target’s business has to be considered for availing the de minimis exemption would mean that the assets/turnover of the parent company (if a subsidiary is being acquired) would be excluded. However, the CCI rejected this argument in the acquisition of Novartis Animal Health India (target) by Eli Lilly.12 Section 43A Order against Eli Lilly and Company, 14 Jul. 2016, paras 13.1-13.7. Eli Lilly did not file a notice of the acquisition by relying on the de minimis exemption because the target’s turnover and assets fell well below the prescribed levels. However, the CCI observed that since the target was an unincorporated entity, it was not a ‘person or enterprise’ for the purpose of de minimis exemption, so the thresholds should apply to the target’s parent company, i.e., Novartis India. The logic was that a business division of an enterprise does not have a distinct legal personality from that of the enterprise which houses the business division/segment. Since the value of assets and turnover of Novartis India exceeded the thresholds, the de minimis exemption was not applicable to it. The CCI reasoned that the de minimis exemption applies only to the ‘enterprise’, whose assets, control, shares, voting rights or assets are being acquired not of the value of assets and turnover of the segment/particular business being acquired (i.e., the target in the present case). This position was subsequently followed by the CCI in other cases as well.13 Section 43A Order against Reydel Automotive Holdings B.V., 2 Feb. 2017, para. 11.3; Section 43A Order against SRF Ltd, 16 Aug. 2016, para. 8. The NCLAT in appeal, reversed the finding of the CCI.14 M/s. Eli Lilly and Company v. Competition Commission of India [2020], (NCLAT) TA (AT) (Competition) No. 03 of 2017, paras 25-31. It was clarified that the de minimis exemption is applicable to a ‘person’ or ‘enterprise’, and the term ‘enterprise’ also included unincorporated businesses, as per the definition under section 2(h) of the Competition Act. More importantly, the NCLAT held that for the purpose of calculation of assets and turnover, what is being acquired is relevant, as the assets/turnover of what is left over with the sellers after the acquisition will have no role to play in the context of the business conducted by the purchaser post-acquisition. In the present case, Eli Lily acquired only the business of ‘animal health,’ i.e., Novartis Animal Health India and not the entire business of Novartis India Limited, which included both human health and animal health. Hence, since the turnover and assets attributable to the target were below the threshold, the target exemption was available, and the acquisition was not liable to be notified. Herein, it is relevant to note that after the CCI passed its order penalizing Eli Lily in 2016, before the matter was finally decided by the NCLAT, the MCA issued a fresh notification under 2017 which clarified that the value of assets or turnover shall be limited to only such portion/division/business of the enterprise as is being acquired/taken control of/merged/amalgamated with another enterprise.15 MCA Notification S.0. 988(E), published in Gazette dated 27 Mar. 2017. Therefore, it is now clear that to avail the de minimis exemption, the assets and turnover of only the ‘true target’ (i.e., business division/subsidiary being acquired) have to be considered. This will prevent unnecessary burden on the CCI since more entities will be able to avail the target exemption as the value of assets and turnover of a subsidiary is usually likely to be lesser than that of the parent company. Further, the issue of whether intra-group revenue is to be included in assessing the thresholds of the de minimis exemption was raised in Avago Technologies Limited/Broadcom Corporation,16 Section 43A Order against Avago Technologies Limited, 7 Jun. 2017, paras 8.4-8.6. where the acquirer Avago did not notify the transaction on the assumption that target exemption was available because after excluding the revenue generated from the intra-group sales of the target, the target thresholds were not met. However, the CCI clarified that the statutory definition of turnover under the Competition Act does not provide that goods or services provided to group-entities are to be excluded. The value of turnover is taken as provided in the books of accounts of the enterprise concerned. In this case, the books of accounts of the targets’ group companies also did not distinguish between the revenue generated through intra-group sales or through sales to third parties. Thus, it is clear that the entire value of the turnover of the target and its group companies has to be considered as it is in assessing the applicability of the de minimis exemption without drawing a distinction between internal and external sales of the target. [2] Proof of AAEC Is Necessary to Investigate a Combination Which Has Availed the Target Exemption In its decisional practice, the CCI has also held that if a transaction was exempt from the notification requirement on account of the de minimis exemption, such transaction cannot be investigated without actual proof of anticompetitive effects on the market.17 Vikas Verma v. Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone Ltd. & Others, Case No. 02 of 2021; Satyen Narendra Bajaj v. PayU Payments Private Limited & Another, Case No. 23 of 2019. The merger of PVR and INOX, which were among the largest players in the film exhibition industry in India, was not notified because it qualified for the de minimis exemption since the turnover of INOX was less than INR 1,000 crores. An information was filed on the apprehension that the combined entity would be dominant in the relevant market and the proposed agreements will result in short and long-term consumer harm.18 Consumer Unity & Trust Society v. PVR Limited and INOX Leisure Limited, Case No. 29 of 2022. It was contended that the combined entity would become the largest player in the relevant market in terms of market share, thereby leading to consolidation of market power and AAEC as it was likely to reduce consumer choice, adversely impact consumers in terms of high prices and deterioration in food and service quality, prevent other cinemas from accessing movies from distributors and advertising content, high bargaining power of the combined entity, etc. However, the CCI observed19 Consumer Unity & Trust Society v. PVR Limited and INOX Leisure Limited, Case No. 29 of 2022, paras 15-17. that first, no entity, much less a dominant entity, was in existence for assessment of conduct in the present case. Second, even if the proposed transaction was concluded, dominance per se is not anti-competitive, and only conduct is if the same falls within the provisions of section 4 of the Competition Act. The CCI held that ‘apprehension of likelihood of AAEC by an entity which is yet to take form cannot be a subject matter of inquiry/investigation under Section 3 or 4’. Further, section 3 of the Competition Act examines the likelihood of AAEC arising out of conduct qua an agreement and not a likelihood of conduct itself. Such assessment is ex ante. Hence, CCI found no prima facie violation and observed that if any violation was made post facto, it could then be examined in terms of the provisions of the Competition Act. In appeal, the NCLAT affirmed CCI’s view by holding that at the outset, the very filing of information alleging a violation of section 3 of the Competition Act without any agreement existing in terms of section 3(1) was not in accordance with the law.20 Consumer Unity & Trust Society v. CCI and Others [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 61 of 2022, paras 16-18. In any case, the words ‘likely to cause an AAEC in India’ under section 3(1) were of no consequence because in the present case, the parties did not enter into any anti-competitive agreement but only merged with each other. Further, the NCLAT also affirmed CCI’s findings with reference to section 4 and added that liberty existed to examine any abusive conduct post facto, but without any prima facie abusive conduct, no action could be taken. Thus, if an enterprise qualifies for the de minimis exemption, it cannot be forced to notify a transaction merely on the likelihood of AAEC. Since the assessment of combinations is an ex ante analysis that takes place before the combination is actually given effect, antitrust scrutiny is warranted only if the jurisdictional thresholds are triggered and not otherwise. The mere likelihood of dominance or causing AAEC in the future is, therefore, insufficient for initiating an investigation into a proposed combination. [B] Calculation on a Consolidated Basis The threshold limit of assets and turnover for the target enterprise mentioned in the MCA Circular is calculated on a consolidated basis. The calculation of the threshold limits will be aggregated to include the assets and turnover of the target company along with all of the downstream companies which such target company has control over. For example, an acquirer is acquiring shares or control of Company A. Company A has two subsidiaries operating in India, Company B and Company C. Since Company A is the target company, the calculation of assets and turnover of Company A has to be analysed to see if the de minimis exemption can be availed for such transaction. For the said calculation to analyse whether the de minimis exemption would be applicable, the calculation will be aggregated to include the assets and turnover of Company B and Company C, along with Company A. A diagrammatic illustration of the same is as follows: Notes to the graphic illustration: Note 1: Investor acquires shares of the Company A. Note 2: Company A has a 51% shareholding in Company B. Note 3: Company A has 51% shareholding in Company C. Thus, Company B and Company C are subsidiaries of Company A. To ascertain whether the target exemption will be available, the assets and turnover of Company B and Company C will be clubbed together with Company A. In Allcargo/GATI,21 Section 43A Order against Allcargo Logistics Limited, 2002, 2 May 2022, paras 4, 5. Allcargo acquired 46.86% of the equity share capital of GATI without filing a notice with the CCI. Upon examination, the CCI found that Allcargo had considered the assets and turnover of only the target on a standalone basis and not the target ‘group’, i.e., the turnover of the target consolidated with its subsidiaries. Once the latter were considered, the thresholds of the target exemption were met, and the exemption was no longer available. Thus, the position is well-settled that the acquirer must take into account the consolidated financials of the target, i.e., an aggregate of the target’s group entities and not merely the financials of the target on a standalone basis. In the Investcorp case,22 Paragraph 16. the CCI held that pooled investment schemes generally envisage demutualization of investment management and ownership, wherein the subscribers give authority to the investment managers to conduct the operations of the fund. Accordingly, in case of the acquisition of any investment management businesses, the value of assets and turnover of the controlled portfolio entities would also be relevant for the purpose of computing threshold for the purpose of section 5 of the Competition Act. In the present case, including the assets and turnover of the portfolio entities of the target in the calculation process exceeded the de minimis threshold, so the target exemption was not available. Thus, this case is another example of the principle that calculation has to be done on a consolidated basis because the assets and turnover of the target have to be considered on a comprehensive basis. [C] Regulation 5(9) Post the MCA Circular, the CCI amended the Combination Regulations by adding Regulation 5(9),23 Vide Competition Commission of India (Procedure in regard to the Transaction of Business relating to Combinations) Amendment Regulations, 2012. which, in effect, diluted the de minimis exemption. Regulation 5(9) reads as:Where, in a series of steps or individual transactions that are related to each other, assets are being transferred to an enterprise for the purpose of such enterprise entering into an agreement relating to an acquisition or merger or amalgamation with another person or enterprise, for the purpose of section 5 of the Act, the value of assets and turnover of the enterprise whose assets are being transferred shall also be attributed to the value of assets and turnover of the enterprise to which the assets are being transferred. By way of this amendment, the CCI clarified that in cases where assets/business units or division of an enterprise is transferred to an enterprise and, subsequently, the transferee enterprise enters into an acquisition/combination with a third enterprise, the entire value of assets and turnover of the transferor enterprise (as opposed to only the assets and turnover of the divisions being transferred to the transferee enterprise), will be clubbed with the assets and turnover of the transferee enterprise for the purposes of calculating the threshold limits provided under section 5 of the Competition Act.
