The parties had entered into a composite two-step scheme of demerger of SHRIL wherein its shares would be transferred to TCISIL, followed by the amalgamation of its remaining business into TCIL and issuing of certain equity shares of TCIL to SHRIL’s shareholders. The penalty was imposed on account of failure to furnish information and consummation of certain market purchases wherein transfer of equity shares of SHRIL by TCISIL was made. The transaction has been described in the following diagram: Notes to the diagram: i. The obligation to make an open offer under Takeover Code was triggered pursuant to steps (i) and (ii) above. This can be considered step (iii). ii. The above transactions were in lieu of the demerger/amalgamation. iii. TCISIL notified the equity shares purchased via the SPA and preferential allotment through the subscription agreement under steps (i) and (ii) but did not notify the market purchase prior to its consummation. On the one hand, the parties maintained the plea that market purchases had nothing to do with the demerger/amalgamation scheme, and more so because it was an exempted transaction because of the MCA Circular; the CCI, on the other hand, concluded that market purchases were intrinsically connected with the two-stage scheme and was thus required to be notified under section 6(2) of the Competition Act. It must be borne in mind that the two-stage scheme and market purchases were authorized in the same meeting, followed by a joint press release. It also remained an agreed fact that the market purchases were entitled to an exemption under the MCA Circular, as agreed by the CCI as well. However, the CCI declined to give the benefit of this exemption to the parties mainly on the grounds that the market purchases were intrinsically connected with other transactions. The erstwhile COMPAT criticized this approach of the CCI and stated that the latter failed to take cognizance of the fact that the implementation of the two-stage scheme for demerger/amalgamation was not dependent on the market purchase of equity shares of SHRIL. Therefore, the mere fact that various transactions were executed in close proximity to the market purchases of the equity shares of SHRIL by TCISIL would not be sufficient to deny the benefit of the MCA exemption notification to the parties. Further, the erstwhile COMPAT went on to interpret the scope of the exemption in the context of Regulation 9(4) of the Combination Regulations and concluded that the notification is not riddled with any condition. Therefore, if the transaction involving market purchases of equity shares of SHRIL was covered under the exemption notification, the same was not required to be notified under section 6(2) of the Competition Act. It also noted the good faith of the parties who disclosed each and every transaction, including the market purchase of equity shares at the time of notification, even though separate or composite notice was not filed in relation to the market purchases. Though not discussed in the decision of the erstwhile COMPAT, it must be borne in mind that post the order of the CCI in the Thomas Cook case, Regulation 9(5) was inserted in the Combinations Regulation (discussed below). By virtue of Regulation 9(5), substance will take precedence over form and transaction structures, or transactions comprising a combination which has the effect of avoiding notice in respect of whole or part of the combination would be disregarded and still considered notifiable. As a result, a takeaway for companies from the Thomas Cook decisions and Regulation 9(5) of the Combination Regulations would be to avail maximum benefits of various exemptions under the framework of the statute and notifications even in case of inter-connected transactions where a composite notice is being filed. However, if the sole purpose of such structuring, especially by way of performing an inter-connected transaction, is to evade notice, the same may be penalized for non-notification. Subsequently, the Supreme Court also reiterated CCI’s findings.75 Competition Commission of India v. Thomas Cook (India) Ltd. & Anr. [2018], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 13578 of 2015, paras 27-31. It recognized that although the market purchases were not mentioned in the other transaction documents, they were consummated only after almost finalizing the composite combination. This clearly suggested that market purchases would not have taken place in the absence of the demerger scheme and the other acquisitions. When a series of transactions is envisaged to accomplish a combination, all the transactions have to be taken into consideration by the CCI, not an isolated transaction. While it is open for the parties to structure their transactions in a particular way, the substance of the transactions would be more relevant to assess the effect on competition irrespective of whether such transactions are pursued through one or more steps/transactions. Structuring of transactions cannot be permitted in such a manner so as to avoid compliance with the mandatory provisions of the Competition Act. Further, Regulation 9(4) envisages the possibility that a business transaction may be achieved by a combination of inter-connected or inter-dependent steps/transactions. Thus, since market purchases were not independent and could not be used in isolation for the purpose of any exemption, a single composite notice had to be filed for the combination. As a result, the Supreme Court held that the market purchases did not qualify as a combination in view of the target exemption notification. Therefore, the implication of the collective decisions above (‘Thomas Cook case’) is that parties may avail of the exemption notification even in case of an inter-connected transaction where a composite notice is filed. Furthermore, the test of inter-connected transaction is the dependence of one transaction on the other. What is key is the substance of the transaction, which should not be structured to achieve the effect of avoiding notice for the whole or part of the combination. Thus, the applicability of the exemption is determined by the entire composite combination together. The above-discussed understanding of Regulation 9(5) has also been affirmed by CCI in its decisional practice. A landmark case where CCI heavily penalized the failure to disclose inter-connected transactions was Amazon’s failure to notify the details of a shareholders’ agreement requiring Amazon’s prior written consent for Future Coupons Private Limited (FCPL) to decide any matter relating to FRL, as part of Amazon’s acquisition of FCPL. On a holistic appreciation of the facts, it was found that the commercial agreement and acquisition of shares in FCPL to secure shareholding and rights over FRL had been inter-connected to each other and were conceived, contemplated, negotiated and consummated as part of one composite combination (i.e., to ultimately acquire certain rights over FRL).76 Sections 43A, 44 and 45 Order against Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC, 14 Dec. 2021, paras 52-67. Therefore, Amazon failed to give a single notice covering all the inter-connected steps of the combination, as required in Regulation 9(4), read with Regulations 9(5) of the Combination Regulations and section 6(2) of the Competition Act. This has been discussed in more detail under the heading on failure to furnish material information. Another limb to determining the interconnected nature of transactions comes by way of the European Commission’s Consolidated Jurisdictional Notice, which provides that ‘If two or more transactions (each of them bringing about an acquisition of control) take place within a two-year period between the same persons or undertakings, they shall be qualified as a single concentration, irrespective of whether or not those transactions relate to parts of the same business or concern the same sector … It is sufficient if the transactions, although not carried out between the same companies, are carried out between companies belonging to the same respective groups’. This approach, that multiple acquisitions between the same enterprises within a two-year period would be considered inter-related transactions, has also been affirmed by the CCI.77 Section 43A Order against Piramal Enterprises Limited, 2 May 2016, para. 7. In an interesting first, the CCI penalized CPPIB together under both section 43A and section 44 of the Competition Act because while it notified the first acquisition in ReNew (Transaction I), it did not notify ReNew’s acquisition of Ostro which was another energy company (Transaction II).78 Section 43A Order against Canada Pension Plan Investment Board and ReNew Power Limited, 21 Nov. 2019, paras 12.6, 18 and 27. This order is consistent with its decisional practice of mandating a single, composite notification in case of inter-connected transactions.79 Section 43A Order against Telenor ASA, Telenor (India) Communications Private Limited and Telenor South Asia Investments Pte Limited, 3 Jul. 2018; Section 43A Order against Bharti Airtel Limited and Lion Meadow Investment Limited, 23 Aug. 2023. The primary question to determine the inter-connectedness of Transactions I and II was whether the Primary Acquisition in Transaction I was pursued to support Ostro Acquisition. The CCI relied on the observations of the Supreme Court in the Thomas Cook case that the guiding factor for determining the scope of notification to CCI would be the substance of a combination and technical interpretations to isolate two different steps of transactions of a composite combination would be against the spirit and provisions of the Competition Act. Consequently, no part of a composite combination can be seen in isolation for the purpose of any exemption, as also clarified by the Supreme Court in the Thomas Cook case. In concluding that the transactions were inter-connected, the CCI went beyond the transaction documents and scrutinized the press releases issued by the parties as well as internal documents such as e-mail correspondence between the parties. Thus, this order highlights the need for parties to maintain consistency in their internal and external communication with respect to past, current and future transactions that might be inter-connected with the proposed transaction to ensure that there is complete disclosure, inclusive of any potential intent in the future, at the time of notifying the proposed transaction. In such cases, a single, composite notice of all the interconnected transactions must be filed. [D] Substance over Form Regulation 9(5) of the Combination Regulations clarifies that the requirement for the filing of a merger notification will be determined by the substance/intent of the transaction. It provides:The requirement of filing notice under regulation 5 of these regulations shall be determined with respect to the substance of the transaction and any structure of the transaction(s), comprising a combination, that has the effect of avoiding notice in respect of the whole or a part of the combination shall be disregarded. The form of the transaction is rendered moot with respect to filing the merger notification. Regulation 9(5) seeks to ensure that the parties to a combination do not avoid the mandatory CCI filing by adopting innovative structuring to their transactions. It is akin to piercing the veil and analysing the actual substance of the transaction to see whether the transaction in itself would require the approval of the CCI. Read together with the aspect of filing a single notice for an inter-related transaction, it becomes clear that as long as the proposed combination exceeds the applicable thresholds and does not qualify for any of the exemptions mentioned under the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations, the parties will need to take prior approval from the CCI, before consummating the transaction. So, there may be situations where there are a number of intermediate transactions which are consummated by the parties which may not be notifiable, but if the entire set of transactions is viewed as one composite transaction, such transaction will be notifiable. In such cases, the CCI will disregard multiple transactions and mandate the parties to file the merger control notification. While such an interpretation is adopted by the CCI to regard substance over form, the trigger date becomes an issue. To exercise abundant caution, it is better to file for notification before the CCI prior to consummating the first series of transactions. [E] Form of Filing Regulation 5(2) provides that generally the parties to the combination will file Form I (a short form) as specified in the Combination Regulations. However, Regulation 5(3) of the Combination Regulations provides that parties may, at the discretion, file Form II (a detailed lengthy form) preferably in cases where: (i) the parties to the combination are engaged in production, supply, distribution, storage, sale or trade of similar or identical or substitutable goods or provision of similar or identical or substitutable services and the combined market share of the parties to the combination after such combination is more than 15% in the relevant market or (ii) the parties to the combination are engaged at different stages or levels of the production chain in different markets, in respect of production, supply, distribution, storage, sale or trade in goods or provision of services, and their individual or combined market share is more than 25% in the relevant market. Further, in cases where the parties to the combination have filed notice in Form I and the CCI requires information in Form II to form its prima facie opinion whether the combination is likely to cause or has caused AAEC within the relevant market in India, it shall direct the parties to the combination to file notice in Form II (the lengthy form). [F] Green Channel Vide notification dated 13 August 2019, the CCI amended the Combination Regulations and introduced an automatic system of approval for combinations through a ‘Green Channel’ route. Under this process, subject to certain safeguards, combinations would be deemed to be approved where the criteria prescribed under Schedule III of the Combination Regulations are satisfied, i.e., where parties have no horizontal overlaps, no vertical relationships and no complementary businesses. The objective of the green channel is to facilitate faster clearance of the combinations that do not have such overlaps. The green channel is essentially a trust based system created by the CCI that in case the parties affirm that there is no overlap between the parties, then the form can be filed under the green channel route. For availing the green channel benefit, the pertinent question that should be asked is whether the parties to the transaction, their respective group entities and/or any entity in which they, directly or indirectly, hold shares and/or control, exhibit horizontal overlap, vertical overlaps or complementarity, considering all plausible alternative market definitions. If the answer to the question is yes, then the transaction cannot avail the benefit of the green channel approval facility.80 Section 43A Order against Platinum Jasmine A 2018, 18 Aug. 2023, paras 27-29. Under the green channel approval facility, a proposed combination is deemed to have been approved by the CCI under section 31(1) of the Competition Act upon filing of a notice under Regulation 5A(1) of the Combination Regulations and acknowledgement thereof. The green channel approval facility does not envisage a detailed assessment of the likely effect of a combination on competition in India that is otherwise undertaken for all other combinations. If any overlap exists between the parties to the combination, their respective group entities and/or any entity in which they, directly or indirectly, hold shares and/or control, then the case requires a detailed assessment of the likely effect of the combination on competition in India and accordingly is not eligible for the benefit of the green channel approval facility. Further, the first proviso to Regulations 5A(2) of the Combination Regulations provides that where the CCI finds that the combination does not fall under Schedule III and/or the declaration filed pursuant to sub-regulation (1) is incorrect, the notice given and the approval granted under this regulation shall be void ab initio and the CCI shall deal with the combination in accordance with the provisions contained in the Competition Act. This means that if an incorrect green channel notice has been filed, then a penalty can be imposed by CCI if the transaction has already been consummated.81 Section 43A Order against Platinum Jasmine A 2018, 18 Aug. 2023, para. 31. The green channel facility rests on a trust-based mechanism based on self-assessment and correct declaration. Thus, it is incumbent upon the person seeking to avail this route to it is incumbent upon the person choosing to avail the green channel approval facility to observe uberrima fides (utmost good faith) in availing the same and should subject only the eligible combinations for this channel. Needless to say, any deviation from the prescribed eligibility criteria or misuse of this privilege constitutes a breach of trust, warranting repercussions. Accordingly, the CCI has started penalizing enterprises for abusing the green channel route.82 Section 43A Order against Platinum Jasmine A 2018, 18 Aug. 2023, para. 38. The Competition (Amendment) Act has formalized the green channel route by introducing it within the Competition Act. This is a positive step as it provides formal recognition of the green channel route, which has become a popular mechanism to notify non-problematic transactions, i.e., transactions that are unlikely to cause AAEC due to the absence of horizontal overlaps, vertical or complementary relationships – in a hassle-free and less cumbersome manner than through normal filing procedure.

sections.title: PENALTY FOR NON-FILING
sections.text: §4.11 PENALTY FOR NON-FILING The Competition Act read with the attendant Combination Regulations provides for a mandatory notification of the transaction to the CCI. The Competition Act provides for a suspensory regime; i.e., the parties cannot consummate a combination (for which approval of the CCI is required) without obtaining the approval of the CCI. The penalty exposure for non-notification of a combination or consummation of the combination without obtaining the approval of the CCI is huge and exposes the parties to a penalty which may be up to 1% of the total turnover or assets or the value of the transaction, whichever is higher, of such a combination. Further, an approval of a combination under section 31 neither obliterates nor condones the contravention, for which a penalty is to be imposed under section 43A of the Competition Act. The proceedings under both provisions operate in two different fields.83 SCM Soilfert and Deepak Fertilizers and Petrochemicals v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 59 of 2015; Section 43A Order against Intellect Design Arena Limited, 7 May 2018, para. 10.2. This is why, to date, although several orders have been passed under section 43A of the Competition Act,84 Section 43A Order against Clariant Chemicals India Limited, 16 Aug. 2016, para. 7. not a single combination has been denied approval on filing the correct notice. This highlights that CCI’s objective is not to affect the commercial freedom of parties to enter into combinations in furtherance of their business interest but only to ensure that the exercise of such freedom does not distort competition since if combinations are not appropriately notified, they are likely to escape CCI’s scrutiny and if consummated, may cause irreversible harm to the market. For instance, in its acquisition of stake in CSC e-Governance Services India Limited, Axis Bank did not file a notice to the CCI on the premise that it had made a bona fide error in assessing the applicability of the de minimis exemption due to incorrect calculation of turnover.85 Section 43A Order against Axis Bank Limited, 9 Aug. 2023. However, the CCI held that it is immaterial whether failure to give notice under section 6(2) of the Competition Act was ‘bona fide or mala fide; or it was inadvertent or intentional’. In this case, both the assets and turnover of CSC e-Governance were higher than the threshold prescribed in the de minimis exemption. Thus, even if an error was made in assessing the applicability of the de minimis exemption, that did not negate the fact that the transaction was not eligible for the exemption in the first place. The CCI held that the argument of error was not a relevant argument for determining whether section 43A of the Competition Act gets attracted or not.86 Ibid., paras 20, 21. Hence, the position emerges that parties cannot claim to be absolved of their duty to notify a transaction under the Competition Act merely on the grounds of an error. Even after a penalty has been levied for non-filing of notice under section 43A, there is an obligation to notify the concerned combination section 6(2) of the Competition Act. [A] Gun Jumping ‘Jumping the gun’ (or ‘gun-jumping’) refers to the practice of consummating a transaction before receiving the statutory clearance from the relevant competition regulator or illegal coordination between the merging parties prior to closing. CCI in its decisional practice has imposed a penalty upon the parties for jumping the gun under section 43A of the Competition Act since India follows a mandatory and suspensory merger control regime. The basic objective of standstill obligations contained in section 6(2A) of the Competition Act is to ensure that the parties to a combination transaction compete as they were competing before the initiation of the combination process till the time the transaction is reviewed for any AAEC and is approved by the CCI. Hence, the standstill obligations essentially require that the parties maintain the independence and autonomy of the target in the interim stage between notifying and approval of the combination. Accordingly, the basis of examination of a gun-jumping contravention is whether the parties have ceased to compete as they were competing earlier or whether they have ceased to act independently.87 Section 43A Order against Bharti Airtel Limited, 27 Aug. 2018, para. 8; UltraTech case, para. 13.5. While a notional date may be indeed required to ensure certainty in respect of the valuation of a business and further to ensure that the value of the business is preserved, the acquirer of a business may also be permitted to impose customary standstill and interim arrangements on the target. The CCI has clarified that it is incumbent on the acquirer to ensure that the form and scope of the aforesaid customary arrangements imposed by it on the target is inherent and proportionate to the objective of ensuring certainty in business valuation and preservation of the same (inherence proportionality test).88 Section 43A Order against Bharti Airtel Limited, 27 Aug. 2018, paras 10, 36. As laid down in section 43 A, proceedings against Bharti Airtel (‘Airtel decision’) (discussed in more detail in the next section), the inherence proportionality test seeks to balance the concerns of any potential competition distortions. It is only fair that the onus to prove and ensure that the action/agreement under review is inherent and proportionate to the legitimate business objectives, such as ensuring the preservation of economic valuation, is on the parties to a combination. Based on the above decisional practice of the CCI, it is imperative that enterprises do not fall foul of the merger control norms and indulge in gun-jumping. The CCI acknowledged that certain exchanges of information between parties are inherent to the very process of mergers and acquisitions; however, this legitimate objective of the parties can raise gun-jumping concerns. Earlier, procedural gun-jumping was a ground for penalizing parties, i.e., where notice was not filed before the CCI within thirty days of execution of a binding document pursuant to section 6(2) of the Competition Act. However, procedural gun-jumping is no longer a ground for violation by way of exemption granted by the Central Government to parties from filing a prior notification to the CCI within a mandatory period of thirty days.89 MCA Notification S.0. 2039(E), published in Gazette dated 29 Jun. 2017. Thus, now the principal ground for penalizing parties for gun-jumping is a case of substantive gun-jumping wherein it entails an improper pre-closing integration of the parties to a transaction, for example, sharing of commercially sensitive information, allocating customers, and undertaking joint marketing efforts during the waiting period. The first seminal case of substantive gun-jumping was found by the CCI in the Jet/Etihad case. 90 Jet/Etihad case. Etihad, the acquirer, on 1 May 2013, had notified CCI of its proposed acquisition of a 24% equity stake in Jet Airways. The transaction was approved by CCI on 12 November 2013. However, by a separate order under section 43A of the Competition Act, the CCI observed that: (i) certain provisions of the CCA entered into between Etihad and Jet had already been implemented prior to obtaining the approval of the CCI; and (ii) sale of certain landing/take-off slots of Jet at the London Heathrow Airport (LHR Transaction), had not been notified to the CCI for approval and the transaction of sale and lease back of airport slots had been completed. Based on the same, CCI levied a penalty of INR 10 million on Etihad for gun-jumping. In another case, the CCI received a merger notification from Zuari Fertilisers and Chemicals Limited (ZFCL) and Zuari Agro Chemicals Limited (ZACL) (collectively referred to as the Acquirers) pursuant to a shareholders agreement dated 12 May 2014, entered into between the Acquirers, and the UB group comprising UBHL, Kingfisher Finvest India Limited and McDowell Holdings Limited for an acquisition of up to 3,08,13,939 equity shares of MCFL (representing additional 26% stake in MCFL) (‘Zuari case’). The CCI held that during the continuation of the tranche agreements, transactions amounting to approximately 16.43% in the equity share capital of MCFL were consummated prior to giving the merger approval notice to the CCI. The CCI noted that the parties had not provided adequate evidence to show that said acquisition qualifies for an exemption under Entry 1, Schedule 1 of the Combinations Regulations (discussed in detail below). Furthermore, the CCI relied on a television interview of the head of the Acquirer’s company in which the then-Chairman of MCFL and promoter of UB group, i.e., Mr Mallya, wanted to operate MCFL as a joint venture between Zuari and UB group that affirmed the intent of the parties to consummate the transaction to some extent. Based on the above facts, the CCI imposed a penalty of INR 3 crores. In the Adani Green Energy Limited/S.B. Energy Holding Limited combination, the transaction was notified to the CCI after the execution of SPAs by and between the acquirer, i.e., Adani Green Energy and Softbank.91 Section 43A Order against Adani Green Energy Limited, 9 Mar. 2022. The CCI found that the SPA, particularly the clause which, inter alia, allowed the parties to discuss the ongoing business and operations of the target and its subsidiaries, allowed the acquirer to provide inputs on the business of the target, which the target had to take into account in furtherance of its best interests. When tested on the pedestal of the inherence-proportionality framework as laid down in the Airtel decision, the CCI found that even if the intent of the acquirer was to monitor and preserve the economic value of the target (inherence), it was not proportional to this objective since it explicitly included discussions on business and operations in its scope in addition to specific provisions relating to monitoring and preserving the economic value of the target. Further, although in this case the SPA provided for a ‘clean team’ protocol, it was only a lip service.92 Ibid., paras 45-48. While on the one hand, the clause stated that information will be disclosed only to duly constituted clean teams which shall be ring-fenced from management, on the other hand, the inputs were to be taken into account in the best interests of the target, which necessarily implied that information was to be shared with the management to act on the given inputs. Hence, the CCI held that the execution of the SPA amounted to gun-jumping before approval of the CCI prior to the expiry of the timelines contained in section 6(2A) of the Competition Act.93 Ibid., paras 27-44. Another noteworthy aspect is that although the inputs were non-binding in this case, the CCI adopted a hard stance in that the issue of concern in such cases is access to information and the decisions being based on a wider pool of information, including the inputs of a competitor who is in the process of acquiring the business. Under such circumstances, the eventual result may be a coordinated outcome regardless of whether the inputs are binding or otherwise.94 Ibid., para. 55. This marked the first case where the agreement between the parties in broad terms facilitated the exchange of information and discussion on the ongoing business operations of the target, agreeing that the target will take such inputs into account after the execution of definitive agreements before the transaction was approved by the CCI. Further, if clean team protocols are being adopted to mitigate the risk of disclosure of sensitive information, it must be commensurate with the scope and likely effect of the clause. The clause itself is not sufficient to absolve the parties of their standstill obligations. Basis the above, from a plain reading of the Competition Act, it would appear that it does not contemplate imposition of penalties for gun-jumping (and only covers a ‘failure to file’), however, the CCI in its decisional practice has levied penalties on parties under section 43A for gun-jumping, as constituting a breach of the standstill obligation under section 6(2) of the Competition Act. In this regard, it is also relevant to mention that for the imposition of penalty under section 43A, the action may not be mala fide in case there is a breach of the statutory provisions of the civil law, penalty is attracted simply on its violation. The Supreme Court has noted that there is also no requirement of mens rea or intentional breach as an essential element for levying a penalty under section 43A.95 Competition Commission of India v. Thomas Cook (India) Limited [2018], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 13578 of 2015, para. 32; Section 43A Order against Veolia Environnement S.A., 17 May 2022, para. 33; SCM Solifert Limited & Anr v. Competition Commission of India [2018], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 10678 of 2016, para. 23. The Supreme Court noted that:“There was no requirement of mens rea under section 43A or an intentional breach as an essential element for levy of penalty. The Act does not use the expression “the failure has to be willful or mala fide” for the purpose of imposition of penalty. The breach of the provisions of the Act is punishable and considering the nature of the breach, it is discretionary to impose the extent of penalty. Mens rea is important to adjudge criminal or quasicriminal liability, not in case of violation of the civil statutory provision”. In Hindustan Steel Ltd. v. State of Orissa AIR 1970 SC 253, with respect to the failure to comply with the civil obligation this Court has laid down thus: In our opinion, mens rea is not an essential ingredient for contravention of the provision of a civil Act. In our view, the penalty is attracted as soon as a contravention of the statutory obligations as contemplated by the Act is established and, therefore, the intention of the parties committing such violation becomes immaterial. In other words, the breach of a civil obligation which attracts penalty under the provisions of an Act would immediately attract the levy of penalty irrespective of the fact whether the contravention was made by the defaulter with any guilty intention or not. This apart that unless the language of the statute indicates the need to establish the element of mens rea. It is generally sufficient to prove that a default in complying with the statute has occurred. The penalty has to follow and only the quantum of penalty is discretionary. In our considered opinion, the penalty is attracted as soon as the contravention of the statutory obligation as contemplated by the Act and the Regulation is established and hence intention of the parties committing such violation becomes wholly irrelevant. We also further hold that unless the language of the statute indicates the need to establish the presence of men’s rea, it is wholly unnecessary to ascertain whether such a violation was intentional or not. On a careful perusal of section 15(D) (b) and section 15E of the Act, there is nothing which requires that mens rea must be proved before a penalty can be imposed under these provisions. Hence once the contravention is established then the penalty is to follow:The imposition of penalty under section 43A is on account of breach of a civil obligation, and the proceedings are neither criminal nor quasi-criminal. Thus, a penalty has to follow. This implies that even if parties jump the gun unintentionally, that would not be grounds to absolve them of their liability. Therefore, any kind of arrangement between the parties to the transaction that allows collusion, irrespective of the manner of collusion and if and whether such collusion actually materializes, can be said to constitute gun-jumping. The CCI in its decisional practice has identified some arrangements specifically as amounting to gun-jumping. These have been discussed below. [1] Part/pre-payment of Consideration In its decisional practice, the CCI has held that depositing of advance consideration prior to the CCI’s approval amounts to gun-jumping.96 Section 43A Order against LT Foods Limited and LT Foods Middle East DMCC, 11 May 2018, para. 8. The seminal case in this regard is that of Hindustan Colas Private Limited (acquirer), which had made payment in the form of a refundable deposit pursuant to the SPA.97 Section 43A Order against Hindustan Colas Private Limited, 14 Sep. 2016, para. 6. This was held to amount to gun-jumping because the CCI reasoned that while the distinction between refundable and non-refundable may have some merit in assessing the likelihood of reversion to the status quo ante in form, it may not be relevant from the perspective of potential competition distortions. The pre-payment of price (whether refundable/nonrefundable) may have a number of competition-distorting effects viz., (i) it may lead to a strategic advantage for the acquirer; (ii) it may reduce the incentive and will of ‘target’ to compete; and (iii) it may become a reason/basis to access the confidential information of the ‘target’. On an overall basis, it may be said that pre-payment of consideration may have the impact of creating a tacit collusion which may cause an adverse effect on competition even before consummation of the combination. Thus, the CCI held that what is important is pre-payment of consideration, and solely the fact of the same being refundable or otherwise is not relevant. The Competition Act mandates ex ante examination of combinations, and therefore the issues such as whether the parties actually benefitted or not from the impugned conduct or whether there were any commercial exigencies behind a particular conduct are not relevant for a determination under section 6(2) and 6(2A) of the Competition Act. The aforesaid observations on the need and rationale behind the standstill obligations and the concerns in terms of potential competition distortions are not merely abstract generalizations. By implication, the same brings out the test conditions for determining when a combination can be deemed to have come into effect. As has been brought out, the impugned conduct/arrangement, etc., needs to be examined in terms of: (i) reduction in competition intensity test; (ii) infringement with ordinary course of activities test; and (iii) likelihood of causing potential competition distortions, for deciding on the aspect of when a combination can be said to have ‘come into effect’. This view of the CCI that if the merger is not consummated and the advance paid is indeed refunded, the fact of such payment may have already led to the two parties not acting independently, and subsequent refund cannot restore the loss of competition in the interim period, was also upheld in the Airtel decision. 98 Paragraphs 18-23. In the UltraTech case, the acquirer UltraTech had advanced a corporate guarantee worth INR 500 crores to the target JAL as part of the acquisition.99 Paragraphs 13-17. It was argued that since the corporate guarantee was repayable, it did not amount to consummation of the combination so as to constitute gun-jumping. In its assessment, the CCI noted that the sole purpose of extending the corporate guarantee along with disbursement of a short-term loan was for the combination. Hence, the extension of corporate guarantee was not in the ordinary course of business or an independent transaction, but it was in fact pursuant to the combination. Thus, the extension of corporate guarantee by UltraTech in favour of JAL amounted to pre-payment of consideration and consummating a part of the combination before the approval of the same by the CCI. The CCI, in line with the position in the above-discussed cases, held that even if the loan amount is repayable, the same does not mitigate violation of the standstill obligations. The key issue is that such an arrangement may result in the parties to the combination not acting independently as they are required to do till the combination is approved by a competition authority. In the event a transaction is not consummated and such arrangement is restored, the competition would have still been harmed in the interim time period. It may be noted that pre-payment of consideration is considered significant as such an arrangement is potentially likely to facilitate tacit collusion which is considered to be the worst form of collusion and therefore cannot be allowed. In the combination involving Adani Transmission Limited,100 Section 43A Order against Adani Transmission Limited, 30 Jul. 2018, para. 9. the acquirer advanced loans to the seller of the target asset. The circumstances were that a creditor had proposed to acquire assets of the debtor/seller, and the consideration payable for the acquisition was adjustable with the loan advanced. The CCI held that this was also in violation of the standstill obligations under section 6(2A) of the Competition Act because the advancement of the loan was in the nature of advance consideration. Another aspect to note in pre-payment/advance payment of consideration is that the substantive issue involved is that of the conduct of the parties to a combination and not only that of timing of conduct. If only the timing of conduct was material, then it would imply that parties, during the stage of negotiations, may enter into cooperation on any commercial/financial/marketing aspects leading to integration of their operations and yet claim that the conduct cannot amount to gun-jumping, as it occurred prior to the execution of definitive agreements or filing of notice. Hence, what is critical in such cases is determination of the fact whether the alleged conduct is pursuant to the combination and has the effect of consummating a part of a combination and not the timing of the same.101 UltraTech case, para. 7; Section 43A Order against Chhatwal Group Trust, Shrem Infraventure Private Limited and Shrem Roadways Private Limited, 8 Aug. 2018, para. 12. Therefore, the consistent position now is that irrespective of whether any payment is returnable/non-returnable or refundable/non-refundable, any such payment made in advance or as a form of pre-payment towards the combination amounts to gun-jumping because it inherently violates the principle of maintaining independence between the target and the acquirer till the combination has been approved. Hence, CCI’s observations regarding ex ante notification requirement and standstill obligations apply both to consummation of a proposed combination or any part thereof. [2] Escrow Arrangements Escrow arrangements are an ordinary feature of public market transactions. In simple terms, they refer to those arrangements where the acquirer is not entitled to exercise control over such shares pending completion of the open offer. In its decisional practice, the CCI has observed that escrow arrangements amount to gun-jumping for the purposes of combination assessment. The leading case in this regard is the SCM Soilfert case,102 Paragraphs 9, 10. where the acquirers entered into an escrow agreement, whereby the shares acquired were credited into a specifically designated escrow account, which was to be maintained till approval of the acquisition by the CCI. The escrow agreement, inter alia, provided that SCM shall not exercise its legal and beneficial rights accruing upon the acquisition of shares by way of the Second Acquisition (acquisition of shares of 0.8% equity shares of MCFL) till such time the shares are held in the escrow account. The CCI held that since the Second Acquisition was a part of the proposed combination, the decision to keep the acquired shares in an escrow account maintained with the escrow agent and to not exercise any beneficial interest, including voting rights, was that of the acquirers and not due to any statutory requirement in this regard. Further, the Competition Act and Combination Regulations do not exempt a situation wherein a buyer acquires shares but decides not to exercise legal/beneficial rights in them from the purview of the provisions of the Competition Act in general and section 43A of the Act, in particular. Therefore, the keeping of shares in an escrow account amounted to gun-jumping, even if the acquirers were not entitled to exercise any legal or beneficial rights over them till approvals from regulatory bodies were obtained. In appeal, the erstwhile COMPAT upheld CCI’s findings that the creation of an escrow account accompanied by a covenant on the acquirer’s own volition to abstain from exercising the voting rights does not eliminate the statutory requirement of prior notice since notification under section 6(2) of the Competition Act has to be ex ante.103 SCM Soilfert Limited and Deepak Fertilizers and Petrochemical Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 59 of 2015. Subsequently, the Supreme Court also affirmed the findings of the CCI and erstwhile COMPAT that it was irrelevant whether the acquirers could exercise or not exercise beneficial rights over the shares till the proposed combination was approved by the CCI.104 SCM Soilfert and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2018], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 10678 of 2016, paras 17-21. It held that the legislative scheme under section 6(2A) of the Competition Act which prescribes a standstill obligation together with the notice requirement ‘prior to’ entering into a combination under section 6(2) lays down that notification has to be made before entering into the combination. Ex post facto notice has not been contemplated under the legal regime. The intent of the Competition Act is that the CCI has to permit the combination to be formed after it has had an opportunity to assess the ability of the proposed combination to cause AAEC in the market. If notification is made ex post facto, it would defeat the legislative intention of ex ante notification. This position that the acquisition of shares via escrow mechanism cannot be permitted without prior approval of the CCI in an ex ante merger review regime has been consistently upheld by the CCI in other cases as well.105 Section 43A Order against Hindustan Colas Private Limited, 14 Sep. 2016, para. 6.10; Section 43A Order against SABIC International Holdings B.V., 19 Jul. 2022, paras 25-36. Thus, the conclusion is that the argument that notification was not required by keeping the shares in escrow account is incorrect because it amounts to consummation of the transaction and if such transactions are notified ex post facto (i.e., after the escrow arrangement has already been made – like in SCM Soilfert case), then the legislative intention to assess the combination prior to giving it effect will be defeated. [3] Interim Conditions Another form of gun-jumping is through imposition of interim conditions while the combination approval is still pending with the CCI. For instance, in LT Food’s proposed acquisition of the business of processing raw material for rice, marketing, distribution, export and sale of various types of rice, including trademarks and associated goodwill from Hindustan Unilever Limited (HUL),106 Section 43A Order against LT Foods Limited and LT Foods Middle East DMCC, 11 May 2018. the Framework Agreement executed between the parties required certain measures to be undertaken by HUL as a condition precedent to the combination or an obligation post execution of the Framework Agreement. These conditions included requirement to handover certain inventories to the acquirers, acquirer’s introduction and interaction with the suppliers of the seller, restriction on promotional spending, etc. The CCI held that imposing interim conditions prior to CCI’s approval of the combination amounted to a violation of the standstill obligations under sections 6(2) and 6(2A) of the Competition Act.107 Ibid., para. 8.8. Thus, any kind of interim conditions in relation to the combination cannot be enforced prior to CCI’s approval, irrespective of whether such conditions are preparatory in nature or if they actually amount to giving effect to the combination. [B] Importance of Mitigating Factors Section 43A of the Act empowers the CCI to impose a penalty on such person or enterprise which may extend to 1% of the total turnover or the assets, whichever is higher, of such a combination. While arriving at an appropriate amount of penalty in a case, the CCI may consider the factors such as the conduct of the parties during the proceedings, circumstances of the case, etc.108 Section 43A Order against Cummins Inc., 11 Aug. 2023. For instance, in the section 43A proceedings against Dewan Housing Finance Corporation Limited, the acquirer had submitted copies of legal advice/opinion that no filing was required to be made to the CCI for a merger between a parent company and its subsidiary as they would together constitute one single entity. The CCI held that delay in filing notice on account of incorrect legal advice may be regarded as a mitigating factor in levying penalty.109 Section 43A Order against Dewan Housing Finance Corporation Limited, 3 Jan. 2013, paras 4-9. However, the various mitigating factors submitted by the parties have to be assessed against the backdrop of the seriousness of the violation itself. Thus, the failure to file cannot be treated as a routine compliance default, as it could potentially have the grave consequence of defeating the very purpose of providing for regulation of combinations. It is, therefore, imperative for the companies to understand and appreciate the full extent of their responsibility for complying with the requirement of timely filing of the notifications regarding proposed combinations.110 Section 43A Order against Zulia Investments Pte Limited and Kinder Investments Pte Limited, 1 Aug. 2013, para. 16.

sections.title: EXCLUSIONS AND EXEMPTION
sections.text: §4.12 EXCLUSIONS AND EXEMPTION The Competition Act and the Combination Regulations provide for certain exclusions and exemptions from filing merger control notification. Additionally, the Central Government may also exempt certain enterprises from the notification requirement (such as the MCA Circular discussed above) by issuing an official notification. These exemptions and exclusions are discussed herein below. [A] Acquisition by Public Financial Institution, Foreign Portfolio Investor, Bank or Category I Alternative Investment Fund Section 6(9)111 Substituted by section 6(9) by the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023. of the Competition Act provides that the obligation to notify a combination to the CCI for approval is not applicable to a share subscription or financing facility or any acquisition by a public financial institution, foreign portfolio investor, bank or Category I alternative investment fund, pursuant to any covenant of a loan agreement or investment agreement. The CCI, in its analysis of erstwhile section 6(4) of the Competition Act, which was applicable to public financial institution, foreign institutional investor, bank or venture capital fund, has held that public financial institution, foreign institutional investor, bank or venture capital fund acquiring shares directly on the floor of a stock exchange (by way of execution of contract note) which is not pursuant to a loan agreement or investment agreement are not entitled to exemption under section 6(4) of the Competition Act. Thus, such transactions are required to file a notice and seek approval from the CCI for approval. The CCI held that a contract note cannot be considered an investment agreement for the purposes of section 6(4) of the Competition Act because the contract note issued by the stockbroker is in the nature of a receipt and the contract note is issued subsequent to the execution of the trade on the stock exchange. In light of the stance adopted by the CCI that a contract note will not amount to an investment agreement, there have been instances in the past where a foreign institutional investor has approached the CCI for pre-clearance of its trades (by asking for approval for an acquisition in a target entity in tranches within a definite time-period) and not file a post facto intimation under the provisions of section 6(4) of the Competition Act.112 KKR Mauritius Direct Investments I. Ltd., Mauritius and Magma fincorp Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2011/11/10, 13 Dec. 2011. [B] Exempt Transactions Regulation 4 of the Combination Regulations, read with Schedule 1 of the Combination Regulations, provides for certain exempted transactions, i.e., transactions which will not require a merger control filing before the CCI. The wording of the exemption has to be given due importance by the practitioners. There can be situations where a pure technical filing has to be made, which would be expeditiously cleared by the CCI. The exemptions and the manner in which the CCI have analysed the exemptions are discussed herein below: [1] Schedule I Item 1 An acquisition of shares or voting rights, referred to in sub-clause (i) or sub-clause (ii) of clause (a) of section 5 of the Act, solely as an investment or in the ordinary course of business in so far as the total shares or voting rights held by the acquirer directly or indirectly, does not entitle the acquirer to hold twenty five per cent (25%) or more of the total shares or voting rights of the company, of which shares or voting rights are being acquired, directly or indirectly or in accordance with the execution of any document including a shareholders agreement or articles of association, not leading to acquisition of control of the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired. The explanation to Item I states that the acquisition of less than 10% of the total shares or voting rights of an enterprise shall be treated solely as an investment and provides the conditions for the same. This has been discussed subsequently in this section. Earlier, the threshold provided in Schedule I Item I was 15% which was increased by way of an amendment to 25%. The amendment aligned the Combination Regulations with the Takeover Code by increasing the limit of exempted shareholding from 15% to 25% so long as such shareholding was acquired as an investment or in the ordinary course of business and did not result in an acquisition of control of the target enterprise. A case-by-case analysis will have to be undertaken to assess whether the acquirer is acquiring control over the target enterprise, in which case, a merger notification will have to be made even if the acquisition is less than 25%. A diagrammatic illustration of the exemption is as follows: Notes to the diagrammatic representation: Note 1: Acquirer holds 100% shareholding in each of Company A, Company B and Company C. Note 2: Company A, Company B and Company C plan to invest in shares representing 10% shares of the Target. It must be noted that the exemption is worded to include cases where acquirer acquires the shares directly or indirectly. In this case, the acquirer plans to invest 30% shares of Target indirectly through its subsidiaries, Company A, Company B and Company C. Therefore, this series of transactions will not be able to avail of the exemption. Further, as mentioned above, even if the investment is less than 25% in the target company, it has to be seen whether the acquirer, directly or indirectly, will exercise control over the operations of the target enterprise. As delineated above, the manner in which control has been interpreted by the CCI is different from other Indian regulators. Therefore, while structuring any private equity transactions, a private equity investor will have certain affirmative vote items. Such vote items can be construed to mean control, for the purposes of the Competition Act, in which case, a notification will be required to be made. There have been many cases where private equity funds have made merger filings because of the affirmative vote items which are proposed to be held by them post the consummation of the transaction. Further, the exemption is only available to acquisitions solely as an investment or in the ordinary course of business. Based on the same, strategic investment will not be covered by this exemption because strategic investments are not in the ‘ordinary course of business’ or ‘solely as an investment’. In the early years of the merger control regime, the CCI had made an observation in the combination decision of SAAB and Pipavav113 SAAB AB (Publ.) and Pipavav Defence and Offshore Engineering Company Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/11/95, 1 Jan. 2013. that strategic investments are not entitled to an exemption. In this case, the proposed acquisition was in the nature of a strategic technology partnership between SAAB and Pipavav and also provided certain affirmative rights, including the right to nominate one director on the Board of Pipavav to enable it to preserve the value of its investment in the company and prevent misuse of IPRs with respect to the projects. The CCI held that such rights precluded the acquisition from being an acquisition in the ordinary course of business or solely for the purpose of investment. The objective of this provision is to distinguish between instances of ordinary shareholding and strategic shareholding, falling short of the position of acquisition of control. In a subsequent merger approval order, the CCI has also observed that an acquisition of shares or voting rights, even if it is of less than 25%, may raise competition concerns if the acquirer and the target are either engaged in the business of substitutable products/services or are engaged in activities at different stages or levels of the production chain. Based on the observations of the CCI, the following key points emerge: In the above diagram, the Acquirer is engaged in the manufacture of electronic goods. Target 1 is also involved in the manufacture of electronic goods, and Target 2 is involved in the distribution of electronic goods. In such a case, an investment by the acquirer in either Target 1 of Target 2, even if non-controlling and less than 25%, would necessitate an approval of the CCI. Such acquisitions will not be considered by the CCI as an acquisition made solely as an investment or in the ordinary course of business and thus would require a merger filing.114 New Moon B.V., Combination Registration No. C-2014/08/202, 10 Nov. 2014. Item 1 Provision comprises three conditions, i.e., acquisition of less than 25% of shares or voting rights, acquisition done ‘solely as an investment’ or in the ‘ordinary course of business’ and acquisition not leading to control. Even if one of the three conditions prescribed under this item is not satisfied, the benefit of Item 1 Provision is not available. These conditions have been discussed in more detail below. [a] ‘Solely as an Investment’ Item 1 Schedule I was amended to add a proviso to explain what is considered an acquisition ‘solely as an investment’. Explanation to Item 1 of Schedule I of the Combination Regulations treats the acquisition to be ‘solely as an investment’ if the acquirer:(i) acquires less than 10% shareholding; (ii) has the ability to exercise only such rights that are exercisable by the ordinary shareholders of the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired to the extent of their respective shareholding and; (iii) is not a member of the board of directors of the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired and; (iv) does not have a right or intention to nominate a director on the board of directors of the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired and; (v) does not intend to participate in the affairs or management of the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired. All of these conditions must be simultaneously fulfilled to obtain the benefit of the Explanation to Item 1, Schedule 1. It is necessary to note that private equity investors investing in companies in India will have their nominee on the board and will participate in the affairs of the investee company, even if their shareholding is less than 10%. Therefore, such investments will not be regarded as acquisitions made solely as an investment and, thus, will require notification. CCI has clarified that the phrase ‘solely as an investment’ indicates ‘passive investment’ as against a ‘strategic investment’.115 Section 43A Order against Zuari Fertilisers and Chemicals Limited and Zuari Agro Chemicals Limited, 10 Feb. 2015, para. 8. Thus, to qualify for Item 1 exemption, ‘an acquisition must not have been made with an intention of participating in the formulation, determination or direction of the basic business decisions of the target or likely to cause or result in the same’ by means of voting rights, agreements, representation on the board of the target or its affiliate companies, affirmative/veto rights in the target, etc.116 SCM Soilfert case, para. 8.3. In the combination involving PI Opportunities Fund, the CCI examined the different conditions that must be met in order for a combination to be considered ‘solely as an investment’:117 Section 43A Order against PI Opportunities Fund – I and Pioneer Investment Fund, 30 Sep. 2022, paras 32-39. (i) The first condition under proviso (B) to Item 1 prescribes that there should be no representation of the acquirer on the board of directors of the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired. In this case, there was no existing Board seat held by the acquirers in the target, and accordingly, this condition was met. (ii) The second condition of the same proviso requires that there should not be a right or intention to nominate a director on the Board of directors of the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired, and there should not be any intention on the part of the acquirer to participate in the affairs or management of the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired. In this case, the CCI noted that the acquirers and sellers executed a SPA, pursuant to which they acquired a shareholding in target. At the time of execution of the SPA, the acquirers were aware that the seller had a Board seat on the target. This Board seat was offered to the acquirers by the target, and subsequently, the acquirers appointed one Board member. Based on the foregoing, the intention of the acquirers to participate in the affairs and management of the target could not be ruled out. Herein, in the context of the appointment of the board member, the CCI drew a distinction between ‘right’ and ‘intention’ in consideration of the intent and purpose of the Item I Provision. The CCI observed:The reference to ‘right’, i.e., an existing right captured in the transaction documents, and ‘intention’, i.e., a futuristic right or position, is meant to ensure that the regulatory framework is consistent with the core principle of ‘substance over form’. Accordingly, whether the proposal to appoint a director on the board of target was moved by the target or whether the parties first acquired only shares and subsequently negotiated/agreed for board representation or participation in management in related or unrelated developments to the transaction, or where such an arrangement forms intrinsic part of the transaction ab initio, the substance in each case is the parties gaining the ability to participate in management or affairs and, in that context, the mechanism leading up to it is just a question of form. If the Acquirers’ contentions regarding these differences are accepted, this would imply that parties to a combination can structure their transactions separating the acquisition of shares and acquisition of rights/board seat, etc., and claim the benefit of Item I Provision. Clearly, this would defeat the intent and purpose of the Act, which is to ensure that the competitive dynamics are not influenced, as the same can be influenced not only by active decisions but also by way of broad understanding or awareness of competitively sensitive information. The end result in each case is sufficient to lead to coordinated outcomes, frustrating the process of competition. Hence, the combination did not satisfy the conditions of the Explanation to Item 1 Provision, and hence, it was not ‘solely as an investment’. Although CCI did not examine proviso (A) to Item 1, which requires the investor to have the same rights in the target as an ordinary shareholder, it is clear upon analysis that no ordinary shareholder has the right to appoint members on the Board of the target. This analysis was confirmed in the section 43A proceedings against Trian Partners AM Holdco, Ltd. and Trian Fund Management, L.P (‘Trian case’), where CCI proceeded to add a third limb to the determination undertaken in the combination involving PI Opportunities Fund, as discussed above.118 Section 43A Order against Trian Partners AM Holdco, Ltd. and Trian Fund Management, L.P., 30 Sep. 2022, paras 39-42. Herein, the CCI, after finding that the conditions of proviso (B) to explanation of Item 1 Provision were not met, proceeded to analyse proviso (A). It was noted that if the acquirer wants to avail the exemption, it should only have the ability to exercise rights that are exercisable by the ordinary shareholder of the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired to the extent of their respective shareholding. The Item 1 Provision covers the transactions not amounting to the acquisition of control and, accordingly, does not differentiate between various rights, viz., investor protection rights, informational rights, etc. In this case, Trian acquired two Board seats in a matter of less than two months from the date of the closing of the transaction. Such rights are not available to ordinary shareholders. Thus, it was held that the acquirers clearly had the intention to participate in the affairs and management of the target, so Item 1 exemption was not applicable to the case. Hence, based on the above discussion, it can be concluded that the intention to participate in the affairs and management of the target has to be determined after examination of both the conditions, i.e., proviso (A) and (B) to explanation of Item 1 Provision. In the Axis Bank/CSC e-Governance acquisition,119 Section 43A Order against Axis Bank Limited, 9 Aug. 2023, paras 22-24. Axis Bank did not notify its acquisition of a 9.91% stake in CSC e-Governance Services India Limited (CSC) on the premise that the benefit of the Item 1, Schedule I of the Combination Regulations was available to the transaction. It was found that Axis Bank proposed the nomination of a director on the board of directors, had both representation and intention to have representation on the board of directors of CSC and to participate in its management or affairs. Consequently, the transaction could not be regarded as ‘solely as an investment’ and the exemption was not allowed. Thus, the CCI has focused on the acquirer’s ability to control the management or affairs of the target enterprise, such as through the right or intention to appoint members to the board of directors or otherwise. In its decisional practice, CCI has held that such transactions cannot be termed as being solely an investment.120 Kedaara Capital Fund II, Combination Registration No. C-2019/06/666, 20 Jun. 2019; Section 44 Order in the UltraTech case, para. 12.16; Section 43A Order against SABIC International Holdings B.V., 15 Jul. 2022, paras 27, 28. Based on the above discussion, it can be concluded that the intention of the Item 1 provision is to exempt cases of ordinary shareholding and not strategic shareholding. Both the conditions under the proviso to Explanation to Item 1 provision have to be simultaneously fulfilled in order to claim exemption because if even a single condition is violated, the transaction could no longer be said to be solely for the purpose of investment. This implies that wherever there is an attempt in substance to exercise control through an investment, no matter how that control is exercised (the different types of control have been discussed earlier in this chapter), such transaction will no longer remain ‘solely as an investment’ but would trigger notifiability of the transaction. [b] ‘In the Ordinary Course of Business’ Over time, CCI has started treating ‘solely as an investment’ and ‘ordinary course of business’ as two separate and distinct limbs of Item 1, Schedule 1 exemption, instead of painting them with the same brush in the same condition. In the combination involving PI Opportunities Fund, the core issue was whether the combination, i.e., acquisition of 6.03% shareholding in FRL (target) through on-market purchases as a block deal and subsequent appointment of directors on the Board of the target was covered under Item 1 exemption. The findings of the CCI have been analysed and explained below:121 Section 43A Order against PI Opportunities Fund – I and Pioneer Investment Fund, 30 Sep. 2022, para. 40. (i) The first test for examining a transaction for being in the ordinary course of business is whether the same is a revenue transaction or a capital transaction. In its decisional practice,122 Bharti Airtel Limited and Bharti Hexacom Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2017/05/509, 11 May 2018. the CCI has held that the term ‘ordinary course of business’ is meant to refer to transactions which are frequent, routine and usual. Thus, it corresponds to revenue transactions, which are those transactions which are short term and constitute income and expenditure and are accordingly reflected in the profit and loss account or income statement of the enterprise. Capital transactions, however, are those which affect non-current items, such as fixed assets, long-term debt, etc., and affect the position statement of an enterprise. Hence, transactions in the ordinary course of sale and purchase of securities are done solely with the intent to benefit from the short-term price movement of securities which re-emphasizes the underlying aspects of frequency, duration of holding, intent, etc. (ii) What constitutes a revenue or capital transaction varies from business to business, i.e., a capital transaction for one business may be a revenue transaction for the other. In this case, the acquirers contended that their investments in the target were inextricably connected with the objective of constituting and operating the investment fund and such investments are considered to be routine and in the ordinary course of business of the investment funds. For example, for a seller of plant and machinery, sale of plant and machinery may be an activity in the ordinary course of business as the same constitutes its regular trade, while for any player who was using a machine to produce goods for sale, sale of such machine would be a capital transaction. In fact, the same item may also have a different classification. For example, furniture held for sale by a furniture seller would be a current asset, and the sale proceeds of the same would be revenue receipts, but for the same business, the stock of furniture held for use in office furnishings, etc., would be a non-current asset and the expenditure incurred on purchasing the same would be capital expenditure and receipts from the sale of such furniture would be a capital receipt.123 Reliance Jio Infocomm Limited and Reliance Communications Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/516, 11 May 2018, paras 25-28; Bharti Airtel Limited and Videocon, Combination Registration No. C-2017/05/509, 11 May 2018, paras 28-30. However, in this case, the acquirers contended that their investments in the target were inextricably connected with the objective of constituting and operating the investment fund and that such investments are considered to be routine and in the ordinary course of business of the investment funds. (iii) While revenue transactions mean and imply items of income and expenditure, an ‘investment’ by its very definition constitutes a capital transaction being an item of ‘asset’ for the investing firm. Thus, the key consideration for the ‘ordinary course of business’ test is that the activity in question should not even be an ‘investment’. (iv) The moment it is concluded that the activity in question is an ‘investment’, the question for the determination of notifiability is whether the same is ‘solely as an investment’, i.e., the nature of the investment is such which is not likely to impact the competition dynamics or otherwise. Going by the same, once the activity is held to be an ‘investment’, even if it does satisfy ‘solely as an investment’ test, it cannot be considered to be in the ‘ordinary course of business’ by implication. This position was also subsequently upheld in the Trian case,124 Trian case, paras 41-47. where the CCI reiterated the above findings and observed that going by the above reasoning, the key consideration for the ‘ordinary course of business’ test is that the activity in question should not even be an ‘investment’. The key takeaway from the above case is that an investment, being a capital transaction, can never be in the ordinary course of business. This reasoning significantly dilutes the ‘ordinary course of business’ test because it is contradictory to the first limb of Item 1 Provision which exempts transactions solely made as an investment. As a result, it is almost impossible to rely on the ordinary course of business limb because ‘investments’ of all kinds are excluded from its ambit, even though the very purpose of Item 1 Provision was to exempt ordinary investments that are not strategic in nature. Therefore, this interpretation of ‘ordinary course of business’ is likely to render this limb of the exemption as a dead letter because it cannot be practically utilized by parties entering into an ‘investment’. This position has been reiterated in the decisional practice of CCI. For instance, access to knowledge and expertise in the financial inclusion sector by Axis Bank in its acquisition of stake in CSC was found to fall outside the scope of the ordinary course of business because it was deemed to have a strategic intention.125 Section 43A Order against Axis Bank Limited, 9 Aug. 2023, paras 24-26. Thus, only transactions which are undertaken solely to benefit from the short-term price movement of securities can be considered to be done in the ordinary course of business. Therefore, long-term investments made with the strategic intent to participate in the decision-making process or gain access to sensitive information cannot obtain the benefit of Item 1, Schedule 1 exemption. [2] Schedule I Item 1A An acquisition of additional shares or voting rights of an enterprise by the acquirer or its group where the acquirer or its group, prior to acquisition, already holds twenty five per cent (25%) or more shares or voting rights of the enterprise, but does not hold fifty per cent (50%) or more of the shares or voting rights of the enterprise, either prior to or after such acquisition: Provided that such acquisition does not result in acquisition of sole or joint control of such enterprise by the acquirer or its group. The CCI amended this exemption in January 2016. Prior to the exemption, the exemption allowed for a creeping acquisition of 5% every financial year by the acquirer or its group in an enterprise wherein the acquirer or its group already holds 25% or more share or voting rights in the target enterprise but less than 50%. This exemption was linked to the concept of ‘creeping acquisition’ provisions of the Takeover Code. However, there were many technical filings made where investors increased their shareholding from 26% to 49% post the relaxation of the foreign direct investment regime in India. Such transactions required the approval of the CCI even though there was no change in control. Post this amendment, an investor who holds between 25% and 50% of the equity share capital or voting rights of the target enterprise would be allowed to increase the shareholding below 50% without the approval of the CCI, provided such acquisition does not result in acquisition of sole or joint control. [3] Schedule I Item 2 An acquisition of shares or voting rights, referred to in sub-clause (i) or sub-clause (ii) of clause (a) of section 5 of the Act, where the acquirer, prior to acquisition, has fifty percent (50%) or more shares or voting rights in the enterprise whose shares or voting rights are being acquired, except in the cases where the transaction results in transfer from joint control to sole control. The exemption is applicable to further acquisitions in the target enterprise where the acquirer already has more than 50% in the said target enterprise. It is to be noted that on a technical reading of the exemption, the acquirer has to hold more than 50% in the target enterprise for the exemption to be applicable and not acquirer with its group. Further, the exemption will not be applicable when such further acquisition results in a transfer from joint control to sole control. We have discussed the concept of joint and sole control in the earlier parts of the chapter. From a plain reading of the Item 2 Provision, it is clear that the benefit of this provision is not available in instances where the transaction results in a transfer from joint control to sole control. A diagrammatic representation of the exemption is as follows: Shareholder A holds 60% of equity shareholding in the Target company and Shareholder B holds 40% equity shareholding in the Target company. By way of SPA, Shareholder B will transfer its 40% shareholding in Target company to Shareholder A. Such SPA will not avail of the exemption because the transaction will result in a transfer from joint control of Shareholder A and B in the Target company to sole control of Shareholder A. The CCI dealt with this exemption in the MSM case (discussed above while discussing control), where the largest shareholder (holding approximately 67% of the shareholding) purchased the shares from the other existing shareholders. The existing shareholders held approximately 33% of the target enterprise which was purchased by the largest shareholders. The CCI held that the transaction resulted from joint control to sole control, and hence, the exemption was not available. In the proceedings against Bharti Airtel Limited (Bharti) and Warburg Pincus affiliate Lion Meadow Investment Limited (Lion Meadow), Bharti’s acquisition of Lion Meadow’s 20% shareholding in Bharti Telemedia Limited (BTL) and Lion Meadows’ subsequent acquisition of a 0.664% shareholding in Bharti was not notified to the CCI because the parties asserted that Item 2, Schedule 1 exemption was applicable.126 Section 43A Order against Bharti Airtel Limited and Lion Meadow Investment Limited, 23 Aug. 2023, paras 41-48. They argued that Lion Meadow did not have control of BTL before the transaction, and instead, BTL continued to retain sole control as exercised prior to the transaction. The CCI held that when determining the applicability of benefit of Item 2 Provision, ‘joint control’ and ‘sole control’ should be read to include the ability to exercise material influence over the management or affairs or strategic commercial decisions of the target. Further, veto on the matter of economic or commercial significance to the target is always considered conferring control in terms of and for the purposes of section 5 of the Act. In this case, LMIL held a bundle of rights in the target along with its shareholding, including affirmative veto rights in certain matters, information rights, right to appoint a non-executive director on the board of the target, etc. Thus, it had the ability to exert material influence over management or affairs or strategic commercial decisions of the target. These rights were transferred to BTL after the transaction. Hence, the benefit of Item 2 exemption was not available to the transaction since there was a transfer from joint to sole control from BTL to LMIL. Therefore, the key question for determining the applicability of Item 2, Schedule 1 exemption, is whether there was a transfer from joint control to sole control. Here, control is to be assessed as per the decisional practice of CCI under section 5, which has been discussed earlier in this chapter. [4] Schedule I Item 3 An acquisition of assets, referred to in sub- clause (i) or sub-clause (ii) of clause (a) of section 5 of the Act, not directly related to the business activity of the party acquiring the asset or made solely as an investment or in the ordinary course of business, not leading to control of the enterprise whose assets are being acquired except where the assets being acquired represent substantial business operations in a particular location or for a particular product or service of the enterprise, of which assets are being acquired, irrespective of whether such assets are organized as a separate legal entity or not. The exemption is only available in cases of acquisition of assets solely as an investment or in the ordinary course of business. Based on the interpretation adopted by the CCI for exemption outlined in point (1) above, the exemption will not be applicable for strategic investments and where such acquisition leads to acquisition of control. The scope of this exemption was discussed by the CCI in the Jet/Etihad case.127 Jet/Etihad case, para. 6. Etihad and Jet Airways had entered into an agreement regarding the sale of three landing/take-off slots of Jet Airways at London Heathrow Airport to Etihad and the lease of the same slots back to Jet Airways (LHR Transaction).
