Further, the government’s involvement in the market of alcoholic beverages also becomes evident in the distribution channel, wherein the government is involved at both wholesale and retail levels, as well as auctions, lottery systems for distribution to wholesalers, etc. Another aspect is the transportation of alcoholic beverages between the states within India, which is also subject to the imposition of various charges, including export fee taxes, import fees and transportation charges. Thus, the sale or distribution of alcoholic beverages in both the wholesale and retail sectors in India was, in some form or manner, regulated by the State Governments. [F] Role of Efficiencies as a Defence The CCI gives a lot of emphasis on efficiencies in the market, which can result from the combination. As such, the CCI has analysed economic efficiencies resulting from a combination which may be consumer and/or administrative efficiencies.179 Jet/Etihad case. For instance, in the Jet/Etihad case, one of the reasons adopted by the CCI while approving the deal was the potential efficiencies that would have resulted from the combination. The CCI undertook a holistic analysis of the sector and identified the cost and administrative efficiencies while approving the combination. The relevant portions of the order of the CCI are summarized as follows:(i) Airline alliances create substantial opportunities for generating economic benefits, many of which are dependent at least in part on the closer integration achievable. These benefits can be viewed as demand-side –relating to the creation of new or improved services through expanded networks or seamless service, or supply-side – essentially the ability to produce the same services at lower cost, taking advantage of traffic densities, improved utilization of capacity and lower transaction costs. (ii) In the aviation industry, airlines would benefit by integrating complementary networks, which in turn would result in lower fares for passengers. Further, the possibility of coordinating pricing, fares and inventory/yield management will eliminate inherent inefficiencies in pricing and enable the members to offer more attractive fares to customers. (iii) One of the most fundamental potential benefits from closer cooperation and integration arises from economies of traffic density. This type of economy of scale is a key feature of airline network models. Airline alliances extend the Hub and Spoke (H&S) network with a large presence at both ends of the market. Feeder routes and services delivering connecting traffic can increase the traffic density on a city-pair, allowing airlines to operate larger, more efficient aircraft and to spread end-point fixed costs over a larger number of passengers. (iv) The proposed transaction would generate significant synergies for both airlines in terms of network efficiencies and cost savings. Additionally, the parties to the transaction plan to introduce substantial capacity into the Indian market. Both of these factors could and generally do create downward pressure on fares. (v) Airline alliance has an increased incentive to harmonize and improve customer service standards and quality experience throughout the processes of ticketing, seat selection, airport lounges, gate location for connecting services, onboard amenities and service quality, baggage policies and problem resolution, frequent flyer plans and refunds and exchanges. As these aspects are integrated and jointly managed, the customer receives a correspondingly simplified and consistent service. This aspect of cooperation is likely to provide consumer benefit without anti-competitive results due to the intense, global competition between alliances for customer loyalty. (vi) Another potential benefit was the proposed equity infusion and its implication for the Indian aviation sector. Herein, Jet was under a huge financial debt and reported substantial negative equity. Thus, Etihad’s equity infusion would be beneficial to Jet as it would strengthen its operational viability and allow it to continue to compete effectively in the relevant markets in India and internationally.

sections.title: COMMITMENTS
sections.text: §4.17 COMMITMENTS Merger analysis is essentially a forward-looking exercise whereby the competition regulators can either unconditionally clear the transaction, prohibit the transaction, or approve the transaction with commitments. It requires considerable analytical skill on the part of the competition authorities to impose commitments. Remedies are employed by competition agencies to resolve and prevent any consequential harm to the competitive process that may result from combinations. As such, the remedies play an essential role in the merger review process, and their careful crafting is of utmost importance to competition agencies conducting the review. While imposing remedies/commitments, competition regulators must keep in mind the following:(i) Remedies should only be applied if there is a threat to competition. (ii) Remedies should be the least restrictive means to effectively eliminate the competition concerns posed by the combination. Merger remedies are generally classified as either structural, if they require the divestiture of an asset, or behavioural if they impose an obligation on the merged entity to engage in or refrain from a certain conduct. Structural remedies may include both the sale of a physical part of a business or the transfer or licensing of IPRs. They can be imposed either as a condition precedent to a combination, or their completion may be required within a certain period of time after the combination has been approved. Behavioural remedies, however, are always forward-looking in that they consist of limits on future business behaviour or an obligation to perform a specific prescribed conduct for a given, sometimes considerable, period of time following the consummation of the merger. The CCI has the power under the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations to issue commitments/modifications to the parties to the combination to ensure that combinations, once consummated, will not cause any AAEC in the relevant market in India. As discussed earlier, parties can also voluntarily offer commitments during the combination assessment, and if the competition concerns are not allayed, CCI may propose appropriate modifications to the combination. The parties can accept the modifications proposed by the CCI or respond with amendments, which will again be considered by the CCI. Once the parties to the combination accept the modifications suggested by the CCI, they have to carry out such modifications within the time period specified, failing which such combination will be deemed to have an AAEC in the relevant market in India and hence will be rejected by the CCI. CCI’s decisional practice indicates considerable experience with complex transactions which demonstrate effective engagement with the parties to the combination to address competition concerns. In this light, the structural and behavioural remedies proposed and approved by CCI have been discussed below. [A] Behavioural Commitments As mentioned above, the CCI can ask for commitments/modifications even in Phase I review. Regulation 19(2) of the Combination Regulations provides that the CCI may ask the parties to the combination to accept modifications for forming its prima facie order. The decisional practice shows that the CCI has sought behavioural commitments in the form of commitments in its merger approval orders,180 GSPC Distribution Networks Limited, Gujarat Gas Company Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/11/88, 8 Jan. 2013. where it asked the parties to review its existing contracts to ensure that they are in compliance with the provisions of the Competition Act and they will continue to abide by the provisions of the sectoral regulator. Further, the CCI has also sought commitments, during a Phase I review, from the parties to reduce their non-compete period mentioned in the transaction documents.181 Orchid Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals Limited, Hospira Healthcare India Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/09/79, 21 Dec. 2012. The behavioural commitments issued by CCI can be broadly clubbed under two heads, i.e., non-compete restrictions and other safeguards. The key trends that have emerged in the adoption of both these categories of behavioural remedies have been discussed below in detail. Non-compete restrictions: (i) As a part of a composite transaction, there may be numerous contractual arrangements (like provisions relating to non-compete, licensing, et al.) which restrict the freedom of the parties to the combination to compete in the relevant market, post the consummation of the combination. Such restrictions are required to ensure that the acquirer receives the full value of the acquired business, and the seller is restricted from entering/venturing into any competing business with the target entity after the consummation of the combination. (ii) The scope of non-compete clauses must be limited, both in time and geographically, and cannot extend to restrictions which are not ‘directly related’ to the implementation of the combination. They must be extremely precise in their product scope and ought to cover only those products in which the target entity and/or its promoters are presently active and not future products. Further, they should be only limited to the geographic operations of the target entity. For example, in the acquisition by PVR Limited of DLF Utilities Limited’s film exhibition business, the parties proposed to enter into a non-compete and non-solicitation agreement requiring the target to not engage in the business of operating and managing entertainment theatres/multiplexes for a period of 5 years from the consummation of the proposed combination.182 PVR Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/07/288, 4 May 2016, paras 103-105. It was submitted that the non-compete obligations were necessary to preserve the value of the assets being acquired and to safeguard the target business. However, the CCI found the geographic scope and duration of the non-compete obligations to be unrelated, unnecessary and, therefore, unreasonable to the proposed combination. Consequently, the acquirers committed to reducing the duration from five years to three years and geographic scope from India to Delhi-NCR and Chandigarh. (iii) The time period of non-compete restrictions cannot be of an indefinite duration. As a general rule, CCI will not allow a non-compete period that extends for a period of three years where goodwill and know-how are transferred and two years when only goodwill is transferred. Such non-compete restrictions can be for a longer duration if the transaction so permits.183 Orchid Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals Limited, Hospira Healthcare India Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/09/79, 21 Dec. 2012 – CCI allowed non-compete period up to four years. (iv) Until 2017, the CCI analysed competition compliance non-compete clauses within its preliminary review and required parties to undertake appropriate modifications. However, the decisional practice of CCI with respect to non-compete clauses has evolved through the removal of the mandatory query in Form I which sought detailed information on any non-compete obligations. The CCI has reasoned that a one-size-fits-all approach cannot be adopted to non-compete clauses, and now, if the non-compete is not directly related and necessary to the transaction, the CCI will approve the combination without approving the non-compete restriction separately. (v) However, parties have to remain cautious to ensure that their non-competes are reasonable and competition-compliant. The CCI has emphasized that any competition concerns that may arise from non-compete restrictions can be assessed under section 3 (prohibiting anti-competitive agreements) or section 4 (prohibiting abuses of dominant position) of the Competition Act. This means that while the CCI has reduced the burden on the parties to detail their non-compete restrictions while seeking prior approval of the transaction, the parties are not being exempted from indulging in anti-competitive activities. The amendment simply removes the onus on the parties to provide details of the non-compete in the notification form. All in all, this is a business-friendly step as it will give the parties flexibility in determining non-compete restrictions while also reducing the information burden on them. Other Safeguards: (i) CCI’s decisional practice indicates other forms of behavioural commitments apart from non-compete clauses. Prominent among these is information control, which is essentially to prevent transmission of sensitive information between the parties, who are usually competitors at some level in the relevant market. (ii) This can take several forms, such as an information firewall between Airtel and Jio (as discussed earlier)184 Google International LLC and Bharti Airtel Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/03/91, 30 Jun. 2022, para. 24. or a ‘rule of information control’ at the time of completion of combination (basically a prohibition on exchange of sensitive information between the combined entity and the independent businesses of the parties),185 Northern TK Venture Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/09/601, 29 Oct. 2018, para. 18. white labelling, provision of non-exclusive technology licences, price caps186 Schneider Electric India Pvt. Ltd. and MacRitchie Investments Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/07/586, 18 Apr. 2019. and capacity commitments.187 M/s Air India Limited, M/s Tata SIA Airlines Ltd., M/s Tata Sons Private Limited and M/s Singapore Airlines Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2023/04/1022, 1 Sep. 2023, para. 115. (iii) Other variations of information control include prohibition on the directors/officers and the employees of the combined entity from receiving any sensitive information regarding the Non-Integrated Businesses from the directors/officers or employees of the parties, provision of such information to third parties or internally among themselves. A violation of any of the above rules will constitute grounds for disciplinary action and punishment. Such disciplinary action could include, for instance, removal from service, denial of promotion, and demotion to a lower rank.188 Nippon Yusen Kabushiki Kaisha Ltd., Mitsui O.S.K. Lines Ltd. and Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha, Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2016/11/459, 29 Jun. 2017, para. 25. (iv) Other safeguards include the adoption of preventative measures in the commissioning or operation of the combined entity. For instance, the proposed combination for creating a joint venture between Mumbai International Airport Private Limited, IOCL, BPCL, and Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited in Mumbai Aviation Fuel Farm Facility Limited (MAFFFL) raised certain concerns about the suppliers of ATF. Consequently, the parties undertook voluntary commitments that MAFFFL shall publish the applicable tariff, maximum storage capacity used by suppliers, change in such capacity, rates approved by the regulatory authority, etc., on its website. Further, the parties also volunteered to, inter alia, incorporate a clause in the standard supplier agreement to the effect that each supplier shall comply with competition law principles in relation to the usage of the integrated fuel farm facility.189 Mumbai International Airport Private Limited, Indian Oil Corporation Limited, Bharat Petroleum Corporation Limited, Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited, and Mumbai Aviation Fuel Farm Facility Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/04/164, 29 Sep. 2014, paras 19-24. Interestingly, CCI’s decisional practice indicates that it is not averse to the idea of imposing behavioural remedies on a standalone basis, even in cases of combinations involving direct competitors. Cases in point are the merger of Air India Limited with Tata SIA Airlines Limited190 M/s Air India Limited, M/s Tata SIA Airlines Ltd., M/s Tata Sons Private Limited and M/s Singapore Airlines Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2023/04/1022, 1 Sep. 2023. and the acquisition of the entire electrical and automation business of Larsen & Toubro Limited by Schneider Electric India Private Limited.191 Schneider Electric India Pvt. Ltd. and MacRitchie Investments Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/07/586, 18 Apr. 2019. The intention is to preserve competition by striking a balance between allowing a merger for potential efficiency gains while safeguarding consumers against the adverse effects of reduced competition. However, behavioural commitments are not always sufficient to assuage the likely AAEC in the market. Often, structural commitments in the form of divestments are the only viable option to prevent anti-competitive effects on the market. Based on the seriousness of the competition concerns, the CCI may also order hybrid remedies incorporating both structural and behavioural commitments. [B] Structural Commitments CCI has amassed a wealth of experience in dealing with cases requiring structural commitments, and in its decisional practice, it has proposed and accepted parties’ voluntary proposals for divestments. In AGI Greenpac Limited/Hindusthan National Glass & Industries Limited,192 AGI Greenpac Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/11/983, 15 Mar. 2023, paras 94-101. both the parties were involved in the manufacture and supply of container glass, and the CCI found that the proposed combination was likely to significantly increase the level of concentration in the relevant market for container glass in India. Consequently, the parties proposed a voluntary modification in the form of divestiture of one of the plants of the target, i.e., the Rishikesh plant. The CCI laid down a two-step test to be followed in examining divestiture as a proposed modification. First, the adequacy of the divestiture to address the adverse impacts of structural changes in the market resulting from the combination has to be seen. Second, the suitability of the specific assets required to be divested has to be considered. In the present case, the Rishikesh plant was involved in all container glass segments that had self-contained assets such as glass melting furnaces, and divestiture appeared to incentivize new entry or augment the capacity of an existing competitor. A new entrant or competitor would gain an overall presence of 5% in the container glass market while gaining a presence in all segments of container glass. Consequently, CCI approved the combination subject to compliance with the modification. CCI’s acceptance indicates the presence of the following trends or key elements in the mode, manner and conditions of implementation of divestitures:(i) Identifying the divestment business: the necessary components of the business, including, in some cases, key personnel.193 Bayer/Monsanto, para. 177; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 147-150, 154-157, 161-164; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015; China National decision, para. 18. This involves identifying what shall be divested or not divested and often includes tangible as well as intangible assets such as IPR, necessary licences, permits and authorizations issued by any governmental or statutory authority, contracts, leases, understandings, customer records, credit records and other records related to the business and personnel.194 Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190, 30 Mar. 2015; Bayer/Monsanto, para. 178; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015; China National decision, para. 23. (ii) Transitional Support: Transitional support as may be required by the approved purchaser, which is necessary to enable it to conduct the divestment business in at least the same manner as prior to the purchase, is also offered. This includes training and information delivered by appropriate specialists, supply arrangements, information technology and back end support, etc.195 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 180, 187; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015, para. 2; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 16; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 151, 154, 158, 165, 166; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, paras 34.2, 35.2; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 25; Outotec OYJ, paras 41-44. (iii) First Divestiture Period: The divestiture principally takes place in the first divestiture period, which is usually six months from the date of CCI’s approval of the combination.196 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 217-222; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.1; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 27; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 168-172. (iv) Preservation of economic viability, marketability and competitiveness: These measures are to be taken to ensure that the business to be divested is run as a viable business, and that the assets are not degraded, and the economic viability, marketability and competitiveness is maintained.197 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 223-229; China National decision, paras 25-29; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.3; Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190, 30 Mar. 2015; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 28; Outotec OYJ, paras 45-47; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 173-179. (v) Hold separate obligations: If the business to be divested is part of a broader business, the former is to be kept separate with the appointment of a hold separate manager. In the case of divestment of shares, the divesting shareholders are not to exercise voting rights or be involved in the business from the date of the CCI’s decision. Further, the Key Personnel, management and staff of the divestment businesses should have no involvement in any business retained by the Parties and should not report to any individual outside the divestment businesses.198 Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190, 30 Mar. 2015; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 230-233; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.4; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 29; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 180-184; Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 2017, para. 53; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 10. (vi) Ring-fencing and firewall provisions: The confidential information is not to be shared between the parties and/or their affiliates with respect to the divested business.199 Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.5; Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 2017, paras 56, 57; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) paras 14, 15; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 234, 235; China National decision, paras 30-32, 46-49; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 30; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 185, 186. (vii) Non-solicitation: The Parties shall not, and procure that their Affiliates do not employ, or make offers of employment to, any member of Key Personnel transferred with the divestment businesses for the period prescribed in CCI’s order.200 Bayer/Monsanto, para. 236; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.6; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 31. (viii) Due diligence: In order to enable potential purchasers to carry out reasonable due diligence of the divestment businesses, the parties shall, subject to customary confidentiality assurances and dependent on the stage of the divestiture process, provide to potential purchaser(s) sufficient information as regards the divestment businesses; and/or relating to the Key Personnel and Personnel.201 Bayer/Monsanto, para. 237; China National decision, paras 33, 34; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.7; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, paras 32, 33; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, para. 188; Outotec OYJ, para. 70; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 30; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015. (ix) Reporting: The parties must keep the monitoring agency informed of the process and potential purchasers.202 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 238, 239; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.8; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 189-191; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) paras 17-20. (x) No acquisition of influence: the parties are not to acquire direct or indirect influence over the divestment business for a specified period after closing.203 Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 2016, para. 58; China National decision, para. 35; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.9; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 35; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 153, 160, 169; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 4. (xi) Purchaser requirements: Various purchaser requirements are set out to ensure that the purchaser will be an independent and viable player in the market, having no connection with the parties, such as that it should not be their subsidiary, affiliate or associate company, shall have the financial resources, proven expertise and incentive to maintain and develop the divestment business as a viable and active competitive force in competition with the Parties and other competitors.204 Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015; Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 182, 189; China National decision, para. 36; Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 207, para. 59; DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.10; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 152, 159; Outotec OYJ, para. 67. (xii) Approval of sale and purchase agreement and of the purchaser: The final binding sale and purchase agreement relating to the divestiture shall be conditional on the approval of the CCI.205 Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015, para. 3; Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190; Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 207, para. 62; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 36; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 192, 193; Outotec OYJ, paras 68, 69; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 8. Herein, it is pointed out that in all cases other than Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, the CCI has permitted the closing of the main transaction pending the closing of the divestment. In the former, the approval of sale and purchase agreement of the purchaser (Emcure) was done during the approval of the transaction itself. (xiii) Monitoring agency: The CCI generally appoints an independent monitoring agency to supervise the carrying out of the modification.206 Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015, para. 4; Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 242-245; China National decision, paras 52-55; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.12; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 37; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 194-196; Outotec OYJ, paras 48-50. (xiv) Divestiture Agency: If the Closing of the divestment businesses has not taken place as specified in the modifications to the proposed combination, then the CCI shall appoint a Divestiture Agency to divest the divestment businesses.207 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 246-251; China National decision, paras 38-41; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 38; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 197-202; Outotec OYJ, paras 51-59; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) paras 21-26. (xv) Second divestiture period: If the parties fail to divest in the first divestiture period, the CCI may direct the parties to divest alternative divestment products in a second divestiture period.208 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 252-255; China National decision, paras 42-44; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.13; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 39; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 203-206; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 6. (xvi) Duties and obligations of parties: The parties are generally required to cooperate with the Monitoring Agency and, if applicable, the Divestment Agency, provide regular reports on the progress of the divestiture, etc. The CCI may also request information from the parties that is reasonably necessary for the effective implementation of the order at any time. The Monitoring Agency and Divestiture Agency shall have full and complete access to any of the Divestment Businesses’ books, records, documents, management or other personnel, facilities, sites and technical information necessary for fulfilling its duties under the Order.209 Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 207, paras 63-68.; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 256-261; China National decision, paras 53-55; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.15; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 40; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 207-210; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 31; Outotec OYJ, paras 60-66. Therefore, CCI’s remedy packages in divestitures seek to strike a balanced approach between the need to maintain competition in the market and the independence of the parties to the proposed combination.

sections.title: PENALTY FOR MAKING FALSE STATEMENT OR OMISSION TO FURNISH MATERIAL INFORMATION
sections.text: §4.18 PENALTY FOR MAKING FALSE STATEMENT OR OMISSION TO FURNISH MATERIAL INFORMATION In its decisional practice, the CCI has penalized parties for not furnishing material information or making false statements at the time of filing the combination notice under sections 44 and 45 of the Competition Act. Although CCI has so far not reversed its approval on this issue, it has penalized the parties for such conduct. For instance, in the UltraTech case,210 Section 44 Order against UltraTech Cement Limited, 12 Mar. 2018, paras 4-12.1. while filing the notice, UltraTech had reported that it is a subsidiary of Grasim Industries Limited which in turn was a flagship company of Aditya Birla Group. However, it did not provide any details in respect of the shareholding/control of the Kumar Mangalam Family Group. The role of the family in determining what constitutes a group for the purpose of competition assessment of combinations has already been discussed in the earlier parts of this chapter. Consequently, UltraTech was penalized under section 44 of the Competition Act. Noticeably, proceedings were initiated against Amazon for contravening, inter alia, section 44 of the Competition Act for misrepresentation, false statement or suppression/concealment of material facts in relation to the scope and purpose of its combination involving FCPL and its failure to identify and notify a relevant commercial agreement, which was an inter-connected part of the combination (as discussed earlier in the section on inter-connected transactions). After granting approval to the transaction, the CCI received intimation that Amazon had suppressed material information about the combination. Upon examination, the CCI found that the purpose of the combination was for investment in FRL and establishing a strategic alignment/partnership between Amazon and Future groups in the Indian retail sector, contrary to what it had submitted in the notice, wherein it had mentioned that the objective of the combination was the gift card business of FCPL and Amazon’s interest to expand its portfolio in the payments landscape in India.211 Sections 43A, 44 and 45 Order against Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC, 14 Dec. 2021, paras 37-44. Further, Amazon had specifically claimed at the time of approval of the combination that it did not have any direct or indirect shareholding in FRL, and certain rights have been granted to it only with respect to FCPL’s investment in FRL, which was merely shown as a factor of financial strength. However, the internal correspondence of Amazon revealed a different picture: that it was all along solely interested in FRL. The CCI held that the object and purpose of strategic rights in a combination is crucial to establishing a proper understanding of the combination and its objective and, accordingly, deciding the appropriate line of inquiry to assess the effects of the combination on competition. In this case, the purpose of securing strategic interest over FRL and commercial partnership with FRL was entirely different from FRL being merely taken as an element of financial strength and protection to the investment in FCPL. Consequently, it was found that Amazon’s objective in pursuing the combination was not the potential of the gift and loyalty card business of FCPL, but FCPL was envisaged only as a vehicle to get to FRL. Hence, the present case was held to be a clear, conscious and wilful case of omission to state the actual purpose of the combination in violation of the disclosure requirements mentioned in the Combination Regulations. Therefore, not only did Amazon fail to notify all the inter-connected steps of the transaction (as also discussed earlier), but it also failed to give true and complete disclosure with respect to the substance of its combination in violation of sections 44 and 45 of the Competition Act. Subsequently, after a detailed analysis of the factual background and applicable law, the NCLAT stated its ‘complete agreement’ with the CCI’s views that Amazon had intentionally not made known the ‘real ambit and purpose’ of the combination.212 Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2022. Hence, if a party conceals/suppresses and/or misrepresents the scope and purpose of the combination to obtain CCI’s approval, the same would effectively amount to approval/consent having been obtained by way of fraud. Such a breach of trust of the CCI, established under the Competition Act for the benevolent purpose of promoting and sustaining competition in markets in India, manifests a deliberate disregard for the trust-based regulatory mechanism. The competition assessment undertaken and the consequent decision of the CCI is primarily based on the information provided in the notice. Once the CCI has passed an order, it has no control over the subsequent consummation or non-consummation of a transaction and may only be left with the option of revoking the decision in the event it comes to know that the information was either incomplete or incorrect. Thus, parties to a combination are required to provide complete and correct information on all the aspects asked for in the designated form for filing of notice.

sections.title: MISMATCH OF TIMELINE AND REMEDIES: THE CLASH BETWEEN TWIN REGULATORS
sections.text: §4.19 MISMATCH OF TIMELINE AND REMEDIES: THE CLASH BETWEEN TWIN REGULATORS Investments made in a listed company have to adhere to the timelines and procedural compliances mentioned under the Takeover Code and Combination Regulations. For illustration, the merging companies may face a potential mismatch between the timelines for an open offer prescribed under the Takeover Code and the review of a merger notification by the CCI since both regulatory procedures need to be undertaken simultaneously. As discussed in the earlier portions of the chapter, the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations provide for a merger review by the CCI in two phases: (i) CCI to form a prima facie view in thirty days (excluding the clock stops) from the receipt of merger notification & (ii) in case the CCI on a prima facie view is of the opinion that there may be certain anti-competitive effects that can arise because of the combination, it directs an in-depth investigation of the combination, commonly known as Phase II review. Phase II review can be for a time frame of up to 180 days (excluding the clock stops). As mentioned above, the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations provide for a suspensory regime, and the combination cannot be effectuated until the CCI approves the merger notification. However, under the Takeover Code, the acquirer is required to pay the shareholders who have tendered shares within fifteen days from the closure of the open offer process. Post the fifteen days, the acquirer is imposed with the penalty of paying interest until such payment is made. Since the Takeover Code procedure is not suspended while review of the CCI is ongoing, parties cannot claim exemption from paying interest under the Takeover Code on account of a parallel pending regulatory approval. Similar to the timeline mismatch, the CCI and SEBI may collide on account of a mismatch of remedies. As discussed above, the CCI may impose certain commitments (behavioural or structural) to eliminate the anti-competitive effects of the merger. However, under the Takeover Code (which precedes the scrutiny of the CCI), an acquirer must declare its intention to alienate any material assets of the company in the open offer documents. Therefore, in the event that CCI later imposes structural commitments on the parties which includes divestments, an acquirer may have to obtain a special resolution of its shareholders in order to comply with the requirements imposed by the CCI.

sections.title: CONCLUSION
sections.text: §4.20 CONCLUSION Since merger assessment is an ex ante analysis, it hinges on fair and accurate disclosures pursuant to the trust-based mechanism envisaged in the Competition Act, Combination Regulations and CCI’s decisional practice. Thus, adequate and sufficient self-assessment and cooperation by the parties at every step of the process, right from filing of notice to compliance with modifications to the proposed combination, if any, is necessary for CCI to continue with its streak of not refusing approval to any combination. As long as parties comply, CCI is likely to continue with its pro-approval and business-friendly stance.

plain_text:
§4.01 INTRODUCTION It is imperative to have an effective merger control regime to ensure that corporate reorganizations do not distort the competitive structure of the market. Considering combinations create permanent and lasting changes to the market, it is necessary to ensure timely intervention in case the combination causes or is likely to cause any AAEC. At the same time, interventions should not be so strict so as to deter combinations and ease of doing business in the economy. The Indian merger control regime, which comprises the Competition Act and the attendant CCI (Procedure in regard to the transaction of business relating to Combinations) Regulations, 2011 (‘Combination Regulations’), has attempted to strike a balance between these two goals. In this chapter, CCI’s approach to regulation of combinations, viz. the procedure followed, factors considered, obligations of the parties, remedies issued and penalties imposed have been discussed. To date, in its decisional practice, the CCI has not refused approval for any combination. They have imposed both behavioural and structural commitments. In this background, the chapter also highlights the emerging trends in the Indian merger control regime.

§4.02 THRESHOLD LIMITS Section 5 of the Competition Act characterizes combinations under four broad categories:(a) acquisition of shares, voting rights, control or assets of an enterprise; (b) acquisition of control of an enterprise by an enterprise engaged in a similar line of business; and (c) mergers or amalgamation; (d) deal value threshold.1 Yet to be notified in the official Gazette. The Indian merger control regime provides for mandatory notification in case the prescribed thresholds under the Competition Act of assets or turnover are met. At the outset, it is also clarified that for the purpose of determining notifiability under the Competition Act, the value of assets and turnover of the acquirer along with the target must be considered. This is clear from the language of section 5(a) which lists the thresholds applicable to the control, shares, assets or voting rights of the parties to the combination ‘jointly’.2 Section 43A Order against Diasys Diagnostics Systems GmbH, 31 Aug. 2016, para. 14. A tabular snapshot of the present threshold limit for assets and turnover is as below. [A] Parties to the Combination Situation Entity Assets Turnover In India Acquirer and Target >INR 2,000 crores3 1 crore = 10 million. Or >INR 6,000 crores OR Worldwide Acquirer and Target >USD 1 billion Or >USD 3 billion Of which in India INR 1,000 crores INR 3,000 crores [B] Group Thresholds Scenario Entity Assets Turnover In India Group and Target >INR 8,000 crores Or >INR 24,000 crores OR Worldwide Group and Target >USD 4 billion Or >USD 12 billion Of which in India INR 1,000 crores INR 3,000 crores Section 20(3) of the Competition Act provides that the Central Government, in consultation with the CCI, can enhance or reduce, or keep at the same level,4 Competition (Amendment) Act 2023, section 20. the threshold limits of the value of assets and turnover mentioned under section 5 of the Competition Act. The enhancement will be based on the wholesale price index on fluctuation in the exchange rate of the rupee or foreign currencies or other such factors deemed relevant by the Central Government. [C] Deal Value Thresholds The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023 amended section 5 to introduce a new deal value threshold to cover those combinations where the value of the transaction in connection with the acquisition of any control, shares, voting rights or assets of an enterprise, merger or amalgamation exceeds INR 2,000 crores and, the target has substantial business operations in India. Noticeably, the de minimis or target exemption (as discussed in the subsequent parts of this chapter) will not be available to a transaction if the deal value thresholds are met, and thus, the transaction will necessarily be notifiable. The objective of the amendment is to cover ‘killer acquisitions’, a term used to refer to combinations in technology and digital markets where big entities acquire smaller rivals, i.e., kill their competitors by acquiring them. Since such firms generate returns over time, often operating on zero or even negative revenue in their initial years, these transactions tend to escape antitrust scrutiny. Some commentators across the globe, including in India, felt that the absence of such deal value thresholds was one of the major reasons why such acquisitions could not be checked in an ex ante framework. This was a significant loophole as several transactions involving industry leaders, such as Facebook/WhatsApp, Flipkart/Myntra, etc., escaped competition assessment by the CCI since the asset and turnover thresholds were not triggered. Further, the Competition Commission of India (Combinations) Regulations, 2023 (‘Draft Regulations’) clarify that ‘value of the transaction’ refers to ‘every valuable consideration, whether direct or indirect, immediate or deferred, cash or otherwise’ and shall include non-compete fees as well as, consideration attributable to interconnected transactions, incidental commercial arrangements entered into within two years of the closing of the notifiable transaction (such as technology assistance agreements, licensing of IPR and supply of materials), options and securities on an as-converted basis and contingencies. In case the transaction value is not specified in the transaction documents, then the value approved by the board of directors or other approving authority of the notifying party shall be considered. If the precise transaction value cannot be determined with certainty, then there is a presumption that the deal value threshold has been met. A target enterprise will be deemed to have substantial business operations in India if at least 10% or more of its global:(i) users, subscribers, customers, or visitors in the twelve months preceding the deal execution date were in India or; (ii) gross merchandise value, for the twelve months preceding the deal execution date was from India or; (iii) turnover for the preceding financial year was in India. All the thresholds prescribed under section 5 are sector-agnostic, which means that they are applicable to all industries regardless of the nature of their operation. Further, the criteria for determining substantial business operations look beyond mere asset and turnover by accounting for factors like users, subscribers, etc. Hence, the deal value threshold is proposed to be a catch-all provision to prevent firms, especially those operating in digital markets, from escaping CCI’s scrutiny.

§4.03 TARGET LEVEL/DE MINIMIS EXEMPTION The de minimis or target exemption was introduced by way of a notification issued by the Ministry of Corporate Affairs, Government of India (MCA Circular) in March 2011 in exercise of the powers under section 54 of the Competition Act.5 MCA Notification S.0. 482(E), published in Gazette dated 4 Mar. 2011. It exempts any transaction from notification requirement where the:(i) value of assets being acquired, taken control of, merged or amalgamated is less than INR 350 crores in India or; (ii) turnover is less than INR 1,000 crores in India. These thresholds were increased from INR 250 crores to INR 350 crores and INR 750 crores to INR 1,000 crores, respectively, in March 2016.6 MCA Notification S.0. 674(E), published in Gazette dated 4 Mar. 2016. Subsequently, the MCA issued another notification in March 2017 to expand the scope of the target exemption, which was earlier available to only certain types of combinations resulting from acquisitions, to combinations resulting from mergers, amalgamations and acquisition of control transactions.7 MCA Notification S.0. 988(E), published in Gazette dated 27 Mar. 2017. Originally, the de minimis exemption was valid for a period of five years till March 2022, but it was subsequently extended till March 2027.8 MCA Notification S.0. 1192(E), published in Gazette dated 16 Feb. 2022. Through the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, the de minimis exemption which was provided through the MCA Circular has been formally included in the Competition Act itself. As mentioned earlier, the target exemption is not available to combinations notifiable on the basis of the deal value threshold. Thus, transactions which do meet the target exemption are not notifiable because ordinarily, such transactions do not cause or are not likely to cause AAEC. However, not making it available to a transaction which triggers the deal value threshold implies that legislative intent is to lend greater scrutiny to combinations involving digital and technology companies since their distortionary impact on the market is not immediately seen. [A] Asset and Turnover Details As discussed above and based on the decisional practice of CCI, a transaction involving the acquisition of a target enterprise which meets the prescribed thresholds will be exempt from merger control filing requirements. It is to be noted that for the de minimis exemption to be applicable, either the asset or turnover requirement has to be met. For the sake of clarity, if the target enterprise has assets of more than INR 350 crores but a turnover of less than INR 1,000 crores, the target exemption will be applicable, and no merger notification will be required to be made. [1] Scope ofDe Minimis Exemption CCI’s earlier approach was that even if only certain assets of an enterprise are being acquired, the entire assets and turnover of the enterprise, on a consolidated basis are to be taken into account for determining notifiability.9 G&K Baby Care Pvt. Ltd. and Danone Asia Pacific Holidays 11/Adyay, Combination Registration No. C-2011/08/03, 24 Aug. 2011; AICA Kogyo Company Limited and Aica Laminates Indian Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2011/09/04, 30 Sep. 2011; NHK Automotive Components India Pvt. Ltd. and NHK Spring Co. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2011/10/05, 4 Nov. 2011. Therefore, such assets, though seemingly not relevant to the transaction in terms of impacting any competitive structure, would still have to be notified. However, this position has undergone a change, as discussed below. In the acquisition of ‘Savlon’ and ‘Shower to Shower’ trademarks along with other related assets from Johnson & Johnson by ITC Limited, i.e., the acquirer, notice was not filed with the CCI.10 Section 43 Order against ITC Limited, 11 Dec. 2017, paras 10, 11. It was argued that acquisition of mere trademarks of an enterprise does not amount to acquisition of ‘assets’. However, the CCI observed that trademarks constitute an asset since they provide economic value to their owners. Further, explanation (e) to section 5 of the Competition Act also includes trademarks and similar other commercial rights provided under various laws within the ambit of ‘value of assets’. Thus, acquisition of trademarks is an acquisition of assets in terms of section 5(a) of the Competition Act and is a combination if the jurisdictional thresholds are met. Further, the CCI also observed that acquisition of immovable properties or manufacturing facilities is not a pre-requisite for attracting section 5 of the Act, as assets include not only tangible assets but also intangible assets, in the form of IPRs, viz. trademarks. The second limb of CCI’s reasoning was rather contentious. In assessing the applicability of the de minimis exemption threshold, the CCI held that where the sale involved a part, a division or business of the selling entity, the assets/turnover figures of the entire seller entity, rather than those of the part, etc., being transferred, should be taken into account. Basis this approach, the exemption was not available and ITC should have notified the acquisition. Accordingly, a penalty was imposed on ITC. Upon appeal, the NCLAT set aside the penalty imposed by CCI by holding that only the assets/turnover attributable to the portion, division or business being transferred should be counted for the target.11 ITC Limited v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 11 of 2018. Another aspect to note is that at the time of notification, the value of the asset or turnover of the target in India had to be below INR 250 crores and INR 750 crores, respectively. During the pendency of the appeal, the thresholds were increased to INR 350 crores and INR 1,000 crores, respectively. As a result, the NCLAT also held that the notification was clarificatory in nature and applied with retrospective effect. As a result, the relevant turnover of the target fell below the threshold, and the de minimis exemption was available for the transaction. Therefore, the NCLAT propounded the legal position by clarifying that the assets and turnover of only the relevant portion of the target’s business have to be calculated to determine the applicability of the de minimis exemption. If the entire assets and turnover were considered, then the target exemption would be rendered meaningless because several transactions would have to be notified, even if the actual value of the transaction (i.e., in terms of the relevant business undergoing combination) is much lower than the prescribed threshold. It appears obvious that an extension of the principle that the assets/turnover of only the relevant portion of the target’s business has to be considered for availing the de minimis exemption would mean that the assets/turnover of the parent company (if a subsidiary is being acquired) would be excluded. However, the CCI rejected this argument in the acquisition of Novartis Animal Health India (target) by Eli Lilly.12 Section 43A Order against Eli Lilly and Company, 14 Jul. 2016, paras 13.1-13.7. Eli Lilly did not file a notice of the acquisition by relying on the de minimis exemption because the target’s turnover and assets fell well below the prescribed levels. However, the CCI observed that since the target was an unincorporated entity, it was not a ‘person or enterprise’ for the purpose of de minimis exemption, so the thresholds should apply to the target’s parent company, i.e., Novartis India. The logic was that a business division of an enterprise does not have a distinct legal personality from that of the enterprise which houses the business division/segment. Since the value of assets and turnover of Novartis India exceeded the thresholds, the de minimis exemption was not applicable to it. The CCI reasoned that the de minimis exemption applies only to the ‘enterprise’, whose assets, control, shares, voting rights or assets are being acquired not of the value of assets and turnover of the segment/particular business being acquired (i.e., the target in the present case). This position was subsequently followed by the CCI in other cases as well.13 Section 43A Order against Reydel Automotive Holdings B.V., 2 Feb. 2017, para. 11.3; Section 43A Order against SRF Ltd, 16 Aug. 2016, para. 8. The NCLAT in appeal, reversed the finding of the CCI.14 M/s. Eli Lilly and Company v. Competition Commission of India [2020], (NCLAT) TA (AT) (Competition) No. 03 of 2017, paras 25-31. It was clarified that the de minimis exemption is applicable to a ‘person’ or ‘enterprise’, and the term ‘enterprise’ also included unincorporated businesses, as per the definition under section 2(h) of the Competition Act. More importantly, the NCLAT held that for the purpose of calculation of assets and turnover, what is being acquired is relevant, as the assets/turnover of what is left over with the sellers after the acquisition will have no role to play in the context of the business conducted by the purchaser post-acquisition. In the present case, Eli Lily acquired only the business of ‘animal health,’ i.e., Novartis Animal Health India and not the entire business of Novartis India Limited, which included both human health and animal health. Hence, since the turnover and assets attributable to the target were below the threshold, the target exemption was available, and the acquisition was not liable to be notified. Herein, it is relevant to note that after the CCI passed its order penalizing Eli Lily in 2016, before the matter was finally decided by the NCLAT, the MCA issued a fresh notification under 2017 which clarified that the value of assets or turnover shall be limited to only such portion/division/business of the enterprise as is being acquired/taken control of/merged/amalgamated with another enterprise.15 MCA Notification S.0. 988(E), published in Gazette dated 27 Mar. 2017. Therefore, it is now clear that to avail the de minimis exemption, the assets and turnover of only the ‘true target’ (i.e., business division/subsidiary being acquired) have to be considered. This will prevent unnecessary burden on the CCI since more entities will be able to avail the target exemption as the value of assets and turnover of a subsidiary is usually likely to be lesser than that of the parent company. Further, the issue of whether intra-group revenue is to be included in assessing the thresholds of the de minimis exemption was raised in Avago Technologies Limited/Broadcom Corporation,16 Section 43A Order against Avago Technologies Limited, 7 Jun. 2017, paras 8.4-8.6. where the acquirer Avago did not notify the transaction on the assumption that target exemption was available because after excluding the revenue generated from the intra-group sales of the target, the target thresholds were not met. However, the CCI clarified that the statutory definition of turnover under the Competition Act does not provide that goods or services provided to group-entities are to be excluded. The value of turnover is taken as provided in the books of accounts of the enterprise concerned. In this case, the books of accounts of the targets’ group companies also did not distinguish between the revenue generated through intra-group sales or through sales to third parties. Thus, it is clear that the entire value of the turnover of the target and its group companies has to be considered as it is in assessing the applicability of the de minimis exemption without drawing a distinction between internal and external sales of the target. [2] Proof of AAEC Is Necessary to Investigate a Combination Which Has Availed the Target Exemption In its decisional practice, the CCI has also held that if a transaction was exempt from the notification requirement on account of the de minimis exemption, such transaction cannot be investigated without actual proof of anticompetitive effects on the market.17 Vikas Verma v. Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone Ltd. & Others, Case No. 02 of 2021; Satyen Narendra Bajaj v. PayU Payments Private Limited & Another, Case No. 23 of 2019. The merger of PVR and INOX, which were among the largest players in the film exhibition industry in India, was not notified because it qualified for the de minimis exemption since the turnover of INOX was less than INR 1,000 crores. An information was filed on the apprehension that the combined entity would be dominant in the relevant market and the proposed agreements will result in short and long-term consumer harm.18 Consumer Unity & Trust Society v. PVR Limited and INOX Leisure Limited, Case No. 29 of 2022. It was contended that the combined entity would become the largest player in the relevant market in terms of market share, thereby leading to consolidation of market power and AAEC as it was likely to reduce consumer choice, adversely impact consumers in terms of high prices and deterioration in food and service quality, prevent other cinemas from accessing movies from distributors and advertising content, high bargaining power of the combined entity, etc. However, the CCI observed19 Consumer Unity & Trust Society v. PVR Limited and INOX Leisure Limited, Case No. 29 of 2022, paras 15-17. that first, no entity, much less a dominant entity, was in existence for assessment of conduct in the present case. Second, even if the proposed transaction was concluded, dominance per se is not anti-competitive, and only conduct is if the same falls within the provisions of section 4 of the Competition Act. The CCI held that ‘apprehension of likelihood of AAEC by an entity which is yet to take form cannot be a subject matter of inquiry/investigation under Section 3 or 4’. Further, section 3 of the Competition Act examines the likelihood of AAEC arising out of conduct qua an agreement and not a likelihood of conduct itself. Such assessment is ex ante. Hence, CCI found no prima facie violation and observed that if any violation was made post facto, it could then be examined in terms of the provisions of the Competition Act. In appeal, the NCLAT affirmed CCI’s view by holding that at the outset, the very filing of information alleging a violation of section 3 of the Competition Act without any agreement existing in terms of section 3(1) was not in accordance with the law.20 Consumer Unity & Trust Society v. CCI and Others [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 61 of 2022, paras 16-18. In any case, the words ‘likely to cause an AAEC in India’ under section 3(1) were of no consequence because in the present case, the parties did not enter into any anti-competitive agreement but only merged with each other. Further, the NCLAT also affirmed CCI’s findings with reference to section 4 and added that liberty existed to examine any abusive conduct post facto, but without any prima facie abusive conduct, no action could be taken. Thus, if an enterprise qualifies for the de minimis exemption, it cannot be forced to notify a transaction merely on the likelihood of AAEC. Since the assessment of combinations is an ex ante analysis that takes place before the combination is actually given effect, antitrust scrutiny is warranted only if the jurisdictional thresholds are triggered and not otherwise. The mere likelihood of dominance or causing AAEC in the future is, therefore, insufficient for initiating an investigation into a proposed combination. [B] Calculation on a Consolidated Basis The threshold limit of assets and turnover for the target enterprise mentioned in the MCA Circular is calculated on a consolidated basis. The calculation of the threshold limits will be aggregated to include the assets and turnover of the target company along with all of the downstream companies which such target company has control over. For example, an acquirer is acquiring shares or control of Company A. Company A has two subsidiaries operating in India, Company B and Company C. Since Company A is the target company, the calculation of assets and turnover of Company A has to be analysed to see if the de minimis exemption can be availed for such transaction. For the said calculation to analyse whether the de minimis exemption would be applicable, the calculation will be aggregated to include the assets and turnover of Company B and Company C, along with Company A. A diagrammatic illustration of the same is as follows: Notes to the graphic illustration: Note 1: Investor acquires shares of the Company A. Note 2: Company A has a 51% shareholding in Company B. Note 3: Company A has 51% shareholding in Company C. Thus, Company B and Company C are subsidiaries of Company A. To ascertain whether the target exemption will be available, the assets and turnover of Company B and Company C will be clubbed together with Company A. In Allcargo/GATI,21 Section 43A Order against Allcargo Logistics Limited, 2002, 2 May 2022, paras 4, 5. Allcargo acquired 46.86% of the equity share capital of GATI without filing a notice with the CCI. Upon examination, the CCI found that Allcargo had considered the assets and turnover of only the target on a standalone basis and not the target ‘group’, i.e., the turnover of the target consolidated with its subsidiaries. Once the latter were considered, the thresholds of the target exemption were met, and the exemption was no longer available. Thus, the position is well-settled that the acquirer must take into account the consolidated financials of the target, i.e., an aggregate of the target’s group entities and not merely the financials of the target on a standalone basis. In the Investcorp case,22 Paragraph 16. the CCI held that pooled investment schemes generally envisage demutualization of investment management and ownership, wherein the subscribers give authority to the investment managers to conduct the operations of the fund. Accordingly, in case of the acquisition of any investment management businesses, the value of assets and turnover of the controlled portfolio entities would also be relevant for the purpose of computing threshold for the purpose of section 5 of the Competition Act. In the present case, including the assets and turnover of the portfolio entities of the target in the calculation process exceeded the de minimis threshold, so the target exemption was not available. Thus, this case is another example of the principle that calculation has to be done on a consolidated basis because the assets and turnover of the target have to be considered on a comprehensive basis. [C] Regulation 5(9) Post the MCA Circular, the CCI amended the Combination Regulations by adding Regulation 5(9),23 Vide Competition Commission of India (Procedure in regard to the Transaction of Business relating to Combinations) Amendment Regulations, 2012. which, in effect, diluted the de minimis exemption. Regulation 5(9) reads as:Where, in a series of steps or individual transactions that are related to each other, assets are being transferred to an enterprise for the purpose of such enterprise entering into an agreement relating to an acquisition or merger or amalgamation with another person or enterprise, for the purpose of section 5 of the Act, the value of assets and turnover of the enterprise whose assets are being transferred shall also be attributed to the value of assets and turnover of the enterprise to which the assets are being transferred. By way of this amendment, the CCI clarified that in cases where assets/business units or division of an enterprise is transferred to an enterprise and, subsequently, the transferee enterprise enters into an acquisition/combination with a third enterprise, the entire value of assets and turnover of the transferor enterprise (as opposed to only the assets and turnover of the divisions being transferred to the transferee enterprise), will be clubbed with the assets and turnover of the transferee enterprise for the purposes of calculating the threshold limits provided under section 5 of the Competition Act. The two transactions must be an interrelated transaction for it to be applicable. This is called the principle of aggregation or the principle of attributability because the value of assets and turnover of the parent entity is ‘aggregated’ with the joint venture and becomes attributable to the joint venture.24 Etihad Airways PJSC, Jet Privilege Private Limited and Jet Airways (India) Limited., Combination Registration No. C-2013/12/144, 5 Feb. 2014, para. 4. A diagrammatic illustration of the principle of aggregation is as follows: Note to the illustration: Note 1: Transferor company transfers assets to the Transferee company. Note 2: Post the transfer, the Investor invests in the Transferee company. The principle of aggregation laid down by the insertion of Regulation 5(9) has, in effect, diluted the de minimis exemption laid down by the MCA Circular. It is to be noted that because of the principle of aggregation and the outlook of the CCI to look at substance over form, structuring deals to avoid merger notification has been done away with. Thus, CCI is extending its powers to monitor more transactions than what was initially envisaged in 2011, when the Combination Regulations came into force. However, as mentioned earlier, the MCA Circular of March 2017 has clarified that the value of assets and turnover of only the ‘true target’ has to be considered, i.e., only the portion/division/business that is being acquired.25 MCA Notification S.0. 988(E), published in Gazette dated 27 Mar. 2017. This has significantly diluted the ambit of Regulation 5(9) because now only the value of the relevant assets being transferred by the parent(s) and the turnover generated by such relevant assets will be considered. In the past, the CCI has used Regulation 5(9) of the Combination Regulations to expand their ambit to newly created joint ventures by attributing the assets/turnover of the transferring parent to such joint ventures.26 Andhra Pradesh Gas Distribution Corporation Limited, GDF Suez Energy International Global Developments B.V., Shell Gas B.V. and GAIL (India) Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/10/333, 4 Feb. 2016, paras 2-4; Fujitsu Limited, Panasonic Corporation and Development Bank of Japan Inc., Combination Case No. C-2014/09/206, 27 Oct. 2014. Thus, post the MCA Circular of March 2017, where there is no transfer of assets and merely a transfer of capital by the parent entities, target exemption will generally be available as such joint ventures are unlikely to meet the prescribed thresholds. Even where assets are transferred to create a joint venture, only the value of such assets as are attributable to the joint venture, and not that of the transferor, will be considered for determining the applicability of the target exemption.

§4.04 SHARES Section 5 is applicable to acquisition of shares, control, voting rights or assets. Shares have been defined under section 2(v) of the Competition Act to mean the shares in the share capital of a company carrying voting rights and includes any security which entitles the holder to receive shares with voting rights, as well as stocks except where a distinction between stock and share is expressed or implied. Based on the definition, non-voting instruments like preference shares and non-convertible debentures will not be considered ‘shares’ for the purposes of the Competition Act. However, convertible instruments will be considered shares because they entitle the holder to receive shares with voting rights at a future date. It is very common in the structuring of a transaction to issue convertibles (i.e., instruments which will be converted into equity shares at a later point in time at a given conversion price) to the acquirer. Therefore, any convertible instruments which are normally issued to the acquirer (like warrants, convertible debentures, convertible preference shares) will be counted as ‘shares’ for the purposes of the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations. It is apposite in this regard to note the merger approval decision of the CCI in the notice for acquisition of Independent Media Trust,27 Independent Media Trust, RB Media Soft Pvt Ltd., RRB Media Soft Pvt Ltd., RB Media Holdings Pvt Ltd., Adventure Marketing Pvt Ltd., Watermark Infratech Pvt Ltd., and Colorful Media Pvt Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2012/03/47, 24 May 2012, para. 14. where Independent Media Trust was acquiring zero coupon optionally convertible debentures (i.e., debentures which can be converted into equity shares carrying voting rights, at the open of the debenture holder) of the target company. The CCI noted that the optionally convertible debentures were shares for the purposes of section 2(v)(i) of the Competition Act since the conversion option entitled the holder to receive equity shares of the target companies. Similarly, in the acquisition of 26% equity share capital of Mangalore Fertilizers and Chemicals Limited by SCM Soilfert Limited (‘SCM Soilfert case’),28 Section 43A Order against SCM Soilfert Limited and Deepak Fertilizers and Petrochemicals Corporation Limited, 10 Feb. 2015, para. 10.2. in the transaction constituting acquisition of 0.8% equity share capital of Mangalore Chemicals and Fertilizers Limited (MCFL) through open market transactions, it was not disputed that the shares carried voting rights with them. Hence, despite the subsequent decision of the acquirers to not exercise the voting rights associated with such shares, the acquired shares nevertheless were ‘shares’ within the meaning of the Competition Act. Thus, in determining what constitutes shares, whether the option to convert the shares into equity shares or the right to vote is actually exercised does not matter, and the key aspect is the conferment of the holder with the right to vote.

§4.05 GROUP As mentioned in the threshold table, the threshold limits of assets and turnover are applicable on both enterprise level as well as group level. Section 5 explanation (b) of the Competition Act defines group as follows:(b) ‘group’ means two or more enterprises which, directly or indirectly, are in position to – (i) exercise twenty-six per cent or more of the voting rights in the other enterprise; or (ii) appoint more than fifty per cent of the members of the board of directors in the other enterprise; or (iii) control the management or affairs of the other enterprise. Thereafter, the MCA, by a notification, exempted the ‘group’ exercising less than 50% of voting rights in another enterprise from section 5 of the Competition Act for a period of five years.29 The said notification came into effect on 4 Mar. 2011. Thus, from the definition, in the event: (i) an enterprise holds more than 50% of voting rights in another enterprise, directly or indirectly, or (ii) the enterprise has the right to appoint more than 50% of the directors in another enterprise, or (iii) an enterprise controls the management or affairs of another enterprise, then both the enterprises would be considered to be part of the same group. A diagrammatic representation of group is as follows: Note to the diagrammatic representation: Note 1: Company A holds 51% equity shareholding in Company B. Note 2: Company A can appoint three out of five directors on the board of Company C. Note 3: Company A exercises control over Company D. Based on the above diagrammatic representation, Company A, Company B, Company C and Company D all form part of the group of Company A. Further, it is also to be noted that for the purposes of the Competition Act and the attendant Combination Regulations, one company can be a part of two different groups. For the sake of clarity, let us analyse the following diagrammatic representation. Note to the diagrammatic representation: Note 1: Company A holds 51% shareholding in Company C. Note 2: Company B exercises control over the operations of Company C by having affirmative vote items (as discussed below under the heading of control). In such a situation, Company C forms part of the group of both Company A and Company B. It is relevant to mention that ‘family’ constitutes ‘group’ for the purposes of the Competition Act. In the combination related to the transfer of business, assets and operations of Jaiprakash Associates Limited to UltraTech Cement Limited30 Section 44 Order against UltraTech Cement Limited, 12 Mar. 2018, paras 4, 5, 12.2-12.5. (‘UltraTech case’), while filing the notice, UltraTech had reported that it was a subsidiary of Grasim Industries Limited which in turn was a flagship company of Aditya Birla Group. However, UltraTech did not provide any details in respect of shareholding/control of Kumar Mangalam Birla and his family and the companies owned/controlled by them in Century and Kesoram (which were also involved in the cement business). UltraTech argued that the definition of ‘group’ did not include the members of a ‘family’ within its ambit and that ‘family’ has not been defined in the Competition Act. In this regard, the CCI held that what is of the essence here is collective common control of certain individuals and enterprises over an enterprise; the individuals may or may not comprise a family. The assessment of common control has been an integral part of the decisional practice of the CCI.31 Nirma Limited, Banihal Holdings Private Limited, Kargil Holdings Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/11/221, 28 Jan. 2015; Zuari Fertilisers and Chemicals Limited and Zuari Agro Chemicals Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/06/181, 4 Sep. 2014.
