Consequently, the shareholding of the family member was a relevant disclosure for the purposes of the combination that ought to have been made in as much as it constituted part of the same group of companies as UltraTech. Thus, it can be said that determination of what constitutes ‘group’ essentially hinges on what constitutes control. If control is being exercised by one enterprise over another, then irrespective of whether such enterprises belong to the same family or even different groups, they fall within the same group for the purposes of competition assessment. The concept of control has been discussed in more detail below.

§4.06 CONTROL Section 5, Explanation (a) of the Competition Act defines control as follows: (a) ‘control’ means the ability to exercise material influence, in any manner whatsoever, over the management or affairs or strategic commercial decisions by – (i) one or more enterprises, either jointly or singly, over another enterprise or group; or (ii) one or more groups, either jointly or singly, over another group or enterprise The definition of control has far-reaching implications under the merger control regime in India since it is not only required to ascertain the threshold on a group level but also to assess whether the exemptions mentioned in Schedule I of the Combination Regulations (discussed subsequently in this chapter) can be availed by the parties to the combination or its group. [A] Meaning of Control Generally, in various investment structures, control is normally exercised by the majority shareholder in the target enterprise. However, there may be situations where the de facto control is exercised by another person along with the majority shareholder (like grant of quorum rights, contractual minority affirmative rights under a shareholders agreement, etc.). The CCI, in its decisional practice, has reviewed the investment structure and the management structure to analyse the issue of control. As mentioned above, there can be situations under the Competition Act where the same target enterprise is controlled by two separate enterprises. In Century Tokyo Leasing Corporation/Tata Capital Financial Services Limited, the CCI held that affirmative rights relating to the following items would be considered ‘control’ for the purposes of the Competition Act:32 Century Tokyo Leasing Corporation and Tata Capital Financial Services, Combination Registration Number C-2012/09/78, 4 Oct. 2012. (i) annual budget; (ii) annual business plan; (iii) exit and entry into lines of business; (iv) appointment of management; (v) determination of their remuneration; or (vi) strategic business decisions (no materiality threshold specified). Further, in one of the merger approval orders passed by the CCI in the Notice given by Caladium Investment Pte Ltd.,33 Caladium Investment Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2015/05/278, 25 Jun. 2015, para. 7. the following affirmative rights were held to be controlled for the purposes of notification:(i) creation of or entering into or termination of joint ventures or partnerships and/or creation or sale/liquidation of its significant strategic investments, direct/indirect joint ventures or its subsidiaries; and (ii) reorganization or change in the nature of its current business or activities or launch of any new line of business(es). It is appropriate to note that the word control has been used in various statutes in India. With respect to any transaction, there can be multiple regulatory overlaps between the securities market regulator in India, the SEBI and the provisions of the Competition Act and the attendant Combination Regulations. In case of an acquisition of control over a listed company by an acquirer (along with persons acting in concert), a requirement of a mandatory open order is required to be made under the provisions of the Securities and Exchange Board of India Act, 1992 and the SEBI (Substantial Acquisition of Shares and Takeovers) Regulations, 2011 (‘Takeover Code’). Similarly, as discussed above, in case of acquisition of control of a target enterprise which meets the thresholds under the Competition Act, it will require a filing to be made before the CCI for approval. Having said that, the definition of control under the Takeover Code and the Competition Act and the regulatory outlook on control by SEBI and CCI are very different. The threshold of control under the Competition Act is much lower than that of other prevalent regimes in India. As mentioned above, under the provisions of the Competition Act, ‘control’ has been defined in a circular manner to include ‘controlling the affairs or management of one or more enterprises or group, either jointly or singly’. However, the definition of control under the Takeover Code provides that:Control includes the right to appoint a majority of directors, or to control the management or policy decisions exercisable by a person or persons acting individually or in concert, directly or indirectly, including by virtue of their shareholding or management rights or shareholders agreements or voting agreements or in any other manner. A case in point is the merger approval decision of the CCI in the acquisition of 24% equity stake and certain other rights in Jet Airways (India) Limited by Etihad Airways PJSC (‘Jet/Etihad case’) and the treatment given to it by under regulators in India, namely the Foreign Investment Promotion Board and the SEBI. The CCI noted, in its approval, that Etihad had acquired control over the assets of Jet Airways.34 Etihad Airways and Jet Airways, Combination Registration No. C-2013/05/122, 12 Nov. 2013, paras 14-16. Based on the same set of agreements, investment structure and board structure, the Foreign Investment Promotion Board held that Etihad had not acquired control over Jet Airways. Post the observation of the CCI on control, SEBI investigated the Jet/Etihad case in order to determine if the requirement to make an open offer had been triggered by the transaction under the provisions of the Takeover Code. In this regard, SEBI passed an order holding that Etihad had not acquired control over Jet Airways and hence there was no requirement to make an open offer by Etihad Airways. While passing the order, SEBI compared the definition of control under the Foreign Direct Investment Policy, the Competition Act and the Takeover Code, finding that the same are pari materia under the Foreign Direct Investment Policy and the Takeover Code. However, it noted that the definition under the Competition Act, which deals with ‘controlling the affairs and management’, is of much wider import than that under the Takeover Code, which includes the right ‘to appoint majority of the directors’ or controlling ‘the management or policy decisions’. Accordingly, the decision of CCI would not be the guiding factor for SEBI in determining if control has been acquired under the Takeover Code. [1] Control Includes the Ability to Exercise Material Influence Previously, the CCI’s interpretation of the definition of control oscillated between the ‘material influence’35 UltraTech Cement Limited and Jaiprakash Associates Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/02/246, 12 Mar. 2018. standard and the ‘decisive influence’36 Aditya Birla Chemicals Limited and Grasim Industries Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/03/256, 31 Aug. 2015. standard in determining control. Under the decisive influence standard, the ability of a firm to exercise decisive influence over the management or affairs of a target enterprise through majority shareholding, veto rights, or contractual covenants was considered in order to determine control. In contrast, the material influence standard is a lower threshold, wherein the ability of a firm to influence affairs and management of the other enterprise through factors such as shareholding, special rights, status and expertise of an enterprise/person, board representation, and structural/financial arrangements is considered to determine control. Thus, in simple terms, under the material influence standard, a firm will be said to be in control if it exercises a significant level of influence over the decisions of the target, even if such influence is not absolute or decisive in nature. In September 2022, CCI revised its ‘Frequently Asked Questions’ on combinations which brought certainty to CCI’s interpretation of control as unequivocally adopting the threshold of material influence. Thus, material influence as a factor of control was evolved through CCI’s decisional practice, as discussed below and subsequently added to the statute through the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023. In the UltraTech case,37 Section 44 Order against UltraTech Cement Limited, 12 Mar. 2018, paras 12.8-12.11. the CCI observed in competition law practice, control is considered a matter of degree. In particular, if the three limbs of the definition of ‘group’ as under explanation (b) to section 5 of the Competition Act are ‘either/or’ tests and fulfilment of even a single limb would confer control: i.e., the first test of the two enterprises belonging to the same group is the ability to exercise 50% or more voting rights; the second test is in terms of ability to control majority of composition of Board of Directors; and the third test, which is most dynamic, is in terms of ability to control or manage the affairs of the other enterprise. For example, if a particular enterprise does not hold 50% shares in the other enterprise, it may still have the ability to control if it has majority on the Board of Directors. Similarly, in the event an enterprise does not have requisite shareholding nor the ability to control the majority composition of the Board of Directors, it is possible to infer control by virtue of ability to control and manage the affairs of the other enterprise. The international jurisprudence considers ‘material influence’ as the lowest form of control with other higher forms such as de facto control (implies a situation where an enterprise holds less than majority of the voting rights but in practice controls over more than half of the votes actually cast at a meeting) and de jure control or controlling interest (a shareholding conferring more than 50% of the voting rights of an enterprise) in that order. As stated earlier, material influence, the lowest level of control, implies the presence of factors which give an enterprise ability to influence affairs and management of the other enterprise, including factors such as shareholding, special rights/veto rights, status and expertise of an enterprise or person, Board representation, structural/financial arrangements, etc. It may be noted that only one enterprise can have a controlling interest in the other enterprise, but more than one enterprise can control the other enterprise (situation of joint control). Likewise, there are other terms which are used to express control, such as negative control (by virtue of ability to block special resolutions) or operational control (by virtue of commercial cooperation agreements (CCAs) with or without involving equity). Thus, while examining the third limb of the definition of group, regard needs to be given to the likelihood of the aforesaid degrees of control and not just the special rights as considered by the Acquirer. The CCI, in their FAQs has defined the standard of control in the following manner:Material influence – the lowest level of control – implies the presence of factors that give an enterprise/person the ability to influence the affairs and management of the other enterprise, including factors such as shareholding, special rights, status and expertise of an enterprise or person, board representation, structural/financial arrangements, etc. De facto control implies a situation where an enterprise holds less than majority of the voting rights but, in practice, controls over half of the votes actually cast at a meeting. Further, the factors relevant for material influence are relevant for ascertaining de facto control as well. It may be noted that the concepts of material influence and de facto control are very significant in competition law as there can be situations where commercial realities can be more telling than formal agreements and structures. Controlling interest, or de jure control, means a shareholding conferring more than fifty per cent (50%) of the voting rights of an enterprise. It may be noted that only one enterprise can have a controlling interest in the other enterprise, but more than one enterprise can control the other enterprise (situation of joint control). Likewise, there are other terms which are used to express control, such as negative control (by virtue of ability to block special resolutions) or operational control (by virtue of commercial cooperation agreements with or without involving equity). Further, it is relevant to note that whether any right provides material influence over the management or affairs or strategic commercial decisions or not depends upon its nexus with the likely economic behaviour of the target. There may be acquisition of control, including material influence, even if it is not the declared intention of the parties. Control is the possibility of exercising material influence rather than its actual exercise.38 Section 43A Order against Bharti Airtel Limited and Lion Meadow Investment Limited, 23 Aug. 2023, paras 37-40. For instance, the ability to participate in the board meetings by a representative of the acquirer not only provides access to commercially sensitive information but also provides ability to influence strategic commercial decisions of the target. The extent of influence exerted in such situations depends upon several factors, such as the extent of industry knowledge, length of experience, recognition in the business world, length of association with the enterprise, dependence of the target for funding, supply of inputs or sale of product and services, etc. Another emerging concern on CCI’s radar is common ownership of the acquirer in competitors in the same industry. For instance, the combination involving 3% investment by ChrysCapital in Intas Pharmaceuticals, was approved by the CCI on the condition that ChrysCapital would remove its nominee director in a competing portfolio investment (other pharmaceutical companies like Intas Pharmaceuticals) and agree not to exercise veto rights over certain strategic corporate actions. The nature of rights enjoyed by ChrysCapital in its portfolio companies did not enable it to unilaterally undertake or stall any corporate action but allowed it to materially influence the strategic affairs of the said entities. The proposed investment allowed ChrysCapital to gain a similar position pursuant to its investment in Intas. Hence, the CCI observed that in such combinations, the Acquirer’s ability to access non-public information as well as materially influence the strategic affairs of the competitors, whether by veto or consultation or advisory right or by way of representation on the board such as director or observer, merit examination of the impact of combination on the competition between them.39 Canary Investment Limited and Link Investment Trust II, Combination Registration No. C-2020/04/741, 30 Apr. 2020, paras 10, 11. This approach of the CCI is interesting because it was merely a minority acquisition. Yet, it appears to be consistent with CCI’s decisional practice,40 Northern TK Venture Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/09/601, 29 Oct. 2018; General Atlantic Singapore ACK Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2023/04/1017, 6 Jun. 2017. wherein it has addressed the issue of common ownership through practical remedies such as preventing the acquirer from appointing common directors in its competing portfolio companies, accessing non-public information related to the competitors and adopting information firewalls. This indicates a trend of greater scrutiny for acquisitions involving common shareholdings based on the ability of the acquirer to materially influence the affairs of the competing entities in its portfolio. Therefore, the standard of control has been significantly diluted over the years, thereby covering more transactions within its ambit and reducing the scope of availing exemptions (as discussed later in this chapter) since material influence is triggered at a much lower threshold. It is now clear that material influence includes within its ambit de facto control as well as de jure control and, based on how the ability to influence the affairs and management of the other enterprise is exercised, it may be classified as negative control, positive control, sole control or joint control. Each of these classifications has been discussed subsequently. [2] Beneficial Ownership Is not Necessary to Establish Control CCI’s decisional practice indicates that beneficial ownership is not necessary to establish control.41 Section 43A Order against Global Infrastructure Partners India Private Limited, 30 Aug. 2022, para. 11. For instance, in acquisition of real estate fund management and private equity fund management businesses of IDFC Alternatives Limited by Investcorp India Asset Managers Private Limited (‘Investcorp case’),42 Section 43A Order against India Asset Managers Private Limited, 17 Dec. 2021, paras 10-12. the CCI observed that in acquisitions of mutual fund companies, the beneficial interest in these assets lies with the unitholders and the investment manager does not enjoy ownership or controlling rights over the assets of any of the funds. However, these investment structures envisage demutualization of investment management and ownership, wherein subscribers give authority to the investment managers to conduct the operations of the fund. Consequently, although the beneficial ownership of these categories of funds lies with unitholders, the control over the operations and management of the fund is entrusted to the investment manager. Funds have varying degrees of interest, such as sole control, joint control, mere financial interest, etc., in their portfolio entities. The investment manager of the fund, being the authority to exercise and protect such interest, would invariably enjoy control over the portfolio entity where the shareholding and/or contractual rights of the fund are such as to enable material influence or a higher degree of control over the given portfolio entity. Needless to say, when the fund management business of such a fund is acquired, the acquirer would also gain control over such portfolio entities of the fund. This again lowers the threshold of control since it becomes immaterial whether beneficial interest or ownership over the portfolio entities is being acquired or not, thereby necessarily triggering notifiability of the transaction. [B] Indices of Control As discussed above, the CCI has clarified that control includes positive and negative control, such as quorum rights and veto rights with respect to decision-making relating to the appointment of management personnel, annual business plan, annual budget plan, commencement or ceasing of a business activity, operating in new location, etc. Acquisition of positive control includes an acquisition of an affirmative vote items such as the veto right of decision-making relating to changes/amendments to the memorandum and/or articles of association, changes in the capital structure, including through new issues of equity or equity-linked securities, buyback, rights issue, bonus issue, stock/share split, sweat equity shares, redemption of securities, capital reductions, etc., significant changes to the incentive structure of the senior management and appointment or removal of any member of the senior management, reorganization or change in the nature of current business or launch of any new business, appointment or removal of any nominee director, changes to dividend policy,43 Caladium Investment Pte. Ltd. Combination Registration No. C-2015/01/243, 5 Mar. 2015. annual business plan, annual budget plan, commencement or ceasing of a business activity, operating in new location44 Century Tokyo Leasing Corporation and Tata Capital Financial Services, Combination Registration No. C-2012/09/78, 4 Oct. 2012. and other strategic business decisions.45 SPE Investments and SPE Holdings, Combination Registration No. C-2012/06/63, 9 Aug. 2012. The CCI has also clarified in its decisional practice that negative control amounts to control for the purposes of the Competition Act. For instance, the CCI approved an increase in the voting rights percentage of Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPPIB) in ReNew Energy Global plc (ReNew) pursuant to a buyback programme announced by ReNew.46 Canada Pension Plan Investment Board, Combination Registration No. C-2022/06/936, 15 Jul. 2022, paras 4, 5. Ordinarily, a buyback of shares is not notifiable unless it leads to acquisition of control, in lieu of the exemption provided by Item 6 of Schedule I read with Regulation 4 of the Combination Regulations. In this case, the voting rights acquired by CPPIB on the completion of buyback were likely to exceed 25%, thereby conferring it with the ability to block any special resolution in ReNew. Hence, the exemption was not available to CPPIB because it would acquire negative control. Subsequently, the principle that acquisition of shares exceeding 25% amounts to negative control due to the ability to block a special resolution has been upheld in other cases as well.47 Section 43A Order against Telenor ASA, Telenor (India) Communications Private Limited and Telenor South Asia Investments Pte Limited, 3 Jul. 2018, para. 17. Thus, ‘control’ has a very wide ambit in competition law since negative control, which does not amount to control for the purposes of SEBI, requires notification to the CCI. In MSM India/SPE Holdings/SPE Mauritius, the CCI has effectively concluded that the right to block special resolutions (by way of a more than 26% equity stake) amounts to ‘negative control’, which is ‘control’ for the purposes of the Competition Act.48 SPE Investments and SPE Holdings, Combination Registration No. C-2012/06/63, 9 Aug. 2012 – This form of ‘negative control’ is considered to be ‘control’ within the meaning of the Competition Act. However, Securities Appellate Tribunal (SAT) in SEBI v. Subhkam Ventures (I) Pvt Ltd. (2011) has opined that such ‘negative control’ would not amount to control under SEBI’s regime. In the said case, the acquirers collectively held 62% of equity shares in MSM India along with the right to nominate three directors on the Board. Grandway and Atlas, collectively, held 32.39% of equity shares and had the right to nominate two directors on the Board. The Shareholders Agreement provided that certain actions of MSM India could be undertaken only with the prior approval of the shareholders, representing 75% of the total shareholder interest. These actions included certain strategic commercial decisions of MSM India beyond mere investor protection rights, such as MSM India engaging in any new business or opening locations in cities other than Mumbai, Delhi, Bangalore and Chennai and the hiring or termination of the chief executive officer, chief financial officer, head of marketing, head of programming and general counsel or other counsel of MSM India or any other employee whose salary was or was proposed to be greater than USD 30,000 per year and the material terms of the employment of such officers and material terms of employee benefit plans applicable to employees of MSM India. Further, MSM India could not undertake certain actions without the consent of both the Acquirers and Sellers, i.e., Grandway and Atlas. In other words, the Acquirers and Sellers had to agree with each other to make MSM India undertake certain actions in the Shareholders Agreement. Importantly, the collective shareholding of the sellers of 32.39% was sufficient to block/veto any action that required special resolution under the provisions of the Companies Act, 1956. Considering the collective shareholding and the rights of Grandway and Atlas pursuant to such shareholding (including those pursuant to the Shareholders Agreement), it was observed that the facts and circumstances of the case envisaged joint control by the Acquirers and Sellers over MSM India. The aspect of joint control has been discussed in more detail in the next section. [C] Joint Versus Sole Control The definition of control, as provided under the Competition Act, provides for both sole and joint control. Sole control exists when only one entity controls the target enterprise, i.e., only it can determine the strategic commercial decisions of the target enterprise. Such control can be exercised in cases where there is a majority shareholding held by one enterprise over the target enterprise or by a voting arrangement among the shareholders to evidence that only the enterprise has control over the target enterprise. Joint control is deemed to exist in a situation when two enterprises exercise decisive influence over the target enterprise. Unlike sole control which confers a single entity to determine the strategic decisions in the target enterprise, joint control is characterized by the possibility of a deadlock situation resulting from the power of two or more enterprises to reject proposed strategic decisions. Joint control does not require two enterprises to have equal rights or equal degree of control in the other enterprise. A constraining presence on the decision-making process or affairs or management of an enterprise by a person suffices control.49 Section 43A Order against Bharti Airtel Limited and Lion Meadow Investment Limited, 23 Aug. 2023, para. 40. As also discussed above in the context of negative control, joint control existed with the sellers in the MSM India case since they had the ability to block special resolutions in MSM India. Therein, the CCI held that:50 SPE Investments and SPE Holdings, Combination Registration No. C-2012/06/63, 9 Aug. 2012. joint control over an enterprise implies control over the strategic commercial operations of the enterprise by two or more persons. In such a case, each of the persons in joint control would have the right to veto/block the strategic commercial decision(s) of the enterprise which could result in a dead lock situation [emphasis supplied]. Joint control over an enterprise may arise as a result of shareholding or through contractual arrangements between the shareholders. However, careful scrutiny is required to differentiate mere investor protection rights from those rights which result in a situation of joint control. The assessment of joint control over an enterprise would depend on the facts and circumstances of each case with due consideration of relevant factors such as statutory and contractual rights of the shareholders. The issue of control was also discussed in the Jet/Etihad case,51 Jet/Etihad case. where the CCI opined the following based on the equity shareholding, presence of director of investors on board and the manner of future functioning of the target company after consummation of the transaction proposed by the parties to the combination:14. In the instant case, both the Parties are engaged in the business of providing international air transportation services. The background of the IA pursuant to which 24 percent equity interest in Jet is proposed to be acquired categorically states that the Parties wish to enhance their airline business through a number of joint initiatives. In such a case, Etihad’s acquisition of twenty-four (24) percent equity stake and the right to nominate two (2) directors, out of the six (6) shareholder directors, including the Vice Chairman, in the Board of Directors of Jet, is considered significant in terms of Etihad’s ability to participate in the managerial affairs of Jet. 15. With a view to achieve the purported objective of enhancing their airline business through joint initiatives, the Parties have also entered into the CCA. Under the CCA, the Parties have inter alia agreed that: (A) they would frame co-operative procedure in relation to (i) joint route and schedule coordination; (ii) joint pricing; (iii) joint marketing, distribution, sales representation and cooperation; (iv) joint/reciprocal airport representation and handling; (v) joint/reciprocal technical handling and belly-hold cargo and dedicated freight capacity on services (into and out of Abu Dhabi and India and beyond); (B) the Parties intend to establish centres of excellence either in India or Abu Dhabi; (C) Etihad would recommend candidates for the senior management of Jet; (D) Jet would use Abu Dhabi as its exclusive hub for scheduled services to and from Africa, North and South America and UAE; and (E) Jet would refrain from entering into any code sharing agreement with any other airline that has the effect of: (i) bypassing Abu Dhabi as the hub for traffic to and from the above said locations, or (ii) is detrimental to the co-operation contemplated by the CCA. 16. It is observed that the Parties have entered into a composite combination comprising inter alia the IA, SHA and the CCA, with the common/ultimate objective of enhancing their airline business through joint initiatives. The effect of these agreements including the governance structure envisaged in the CCA establishes Etihad’s joint control over Jet, more particularly over the assets and operations of Jet. The key aspect to note herein is that the CCI did not only limit itself to look at the board or the shareholder composition to determine control but also analysed all the transaction agreements to understand whether the acquirer will have any strategic influence over the operations of the target company, post the acquisition. It was noted that apart from acquiring shares in Jet, Etihad will have its nominee directors on the board of Jet, and also, both Etihad and Jet will conduct their operations jointly, as elucidated in the CCA entered into between Jet and Etihad. Hence, the key ingredient of control is that the enterprise must show that it has the possibility to exercise influence on the target enterprise with respect to its ‘strategic business decisions’. Therefore, exercise of control depends on a number of elements and all of these elements have to be assessed comprehensively. For instance, control may be exercised in the following ways: (i) through acquisition of majority shareholding or (ii) through contractually holding rights, including those rights mentioned in the vote sharing agreement, commercial cooperation and technology collaboration agreements, affirmative vote items contained in the investment agreements, shareholder’s agreements, etc. Based on the above approach, the key aspect to be noticed is that the CCI analyses the facts and circumstances of each case, chooses substance over form approach and sees whether control has been acquired by an acquirer. However, purely investor protection rights awarded to financial investors may not be regarded as control.52 Aalok Dilip Shanghvi & Others, Combination Registration No. C-2015/03/254, 29 Apr. 2015. A case-by-case analysis has to be seen even for investor protection rights to see whether such a bucket of rights provided to the investor would amount to control or not.

§4.07 CALCULATION OF ASSET VALUE At the outset, it is relevant to mention that correctly calculating the turnover/assets of the parties is essential to determine whether the notification thresholds stipulated under the Competition Act and Combination Regulations are met.53 Deli CMF, Combination Registration No. C-2019/02/640, 22 Mar. 2019. Asset value has been defined under Explanation (e) of section 554 Introduced via Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023. of the Competition Act as:the value of assets shall be determined by taking the book value of the assets as shown, in the audited books of account of the enterprise, in the financial year immediately preceding the financial year in which the date of proposed merger falls and if such financial statement has not yet become due to be filed with the Registrar under the Companies Act, 2013 then as per the statutory auditor’s report made on the basis of the last available audited accounts of the company in the financial year immediately preceding the financial year in which the notice is filed under sub-section (2) or sub-section (4) of section 6, as reduced by any depreciation, and the value of assets shall include the brand value, value of goodwill, or value of copyright, patent, permitted use, collective mark, registered proprietor, registered trade mark, registered user, homonymous geographical indication, geographical indications, design or layout- design or similar other commercial rights, if any, referred to in sub-section (5) of section 3. Based on the definition, to calculate the asset value, one has to look at the book value of the assets as shown in the audited financial statements of the companies in the preceding year. The financial statements of a company will have a column on asset value, which includes both fixed and current assets.55 For illustration, even if a company has hived off substantial assets during the course of the year and then entered into a transaction, the hived off assets will also become a part of the asset value for the purposes of section 5 of the Competition Act. Under the definition of asset value under the Competition Act, only depreciation can be reduced from the asset value as shown in the balance sheet. There is no netting off of current liabilities or encumbrances or provisions or the like permitted under the Competition Act for calculation of the asset value. A sample balance sheet of a company has been taken for illustration and described in the following table: Particulars Value Assets Non-current assets – Fixed assets – Goodwill – Non-current investments – Deferred tax assets – Long-Term loans and advances A B C D E Gross non-current assets (GNCA) Depreciation A+B+C+D+E F Current assets – Current investments – Inventories – Trade receivables – Cash and bank balances – Short-term loans and advances – Other current assets G H I J K L Gross current assets (GCA) G+H+I+J+K+L Asset value for calculation under section 5 of the Competition Act [GNCA + GCA] – F The Competition Act does not provide for adjustments made in the calculation of an asset value, post the date of the annual audited books, to represent permanent changes in the economic reality of the concerned company. For calculating asset value for a group, the consolidated financial statements of the preceding years are taken (as opposed to standalone financial statements). Use of such consolidated financial statements eliminates double counting which may arise when combining asset value from various standalone financial statements of enterprises falling with the same group.

§4.08 CALCULATION OF TURNOVER The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023 brings certainty to the term ‘turnover’ by defining it under explanation (c) of section 5 as meaning the turnover certified by the statutory auditor on the basis of the last available audited accounts of the company in the financial year immediately preceding the financial year in which notice is filed with the CCI. Specifically, such turnover shall exclude (i) intra-group sales, (ii) indirect taxes, (iii) trade discounts and (iv) all amounts generated through assets or business from customers outside India. It is pointed out that some of these exclusions, such as indirect taxes, income from ancillary operations, and intra-group sales, have already been clarified by the CCI through FAQs on its website. However, uncertainty still prevailed. Thus, the amendment consolidates the legal position by providing much-needed certainty to parties by specifying these exclusions in the Competition Act itself. Further, it is also relevant to mention that any income generated from the securities held by a mutual fund company is considered turnover, irrespective of whether holding those securities confers control to the mutual fund company or not.56 Section 43A Order against Massachusetts Mutual Life Insurance Company, 7 Aug. 2023.

§4.09 KEY ISSUES IN ACCOUNTING The parties have to undertake an accounting assessment on their own to analyse whether the transaction crosses the threshold limits provided under the Competition Act. If yes, the parties will have to notify the CCI for approval unless the transaction qualifies for the de minimis exemption (as explained earlier in the chapter) or falls within one of the exemptions/exclusions mentioned under the Competition Act or Schedule I of the Combination Regulations (discussed subsequently). There are certain key decisional accounting practices which have been followed by the CCI, in its merger approval decisions, which have to be borne in mind. The most pertinent is the approach regarding revenues from exports. The CCI has clarified that even if an Indian enterprise derives turnover largely from exports, revenue figures provided in the consolidated financial statements of the Indian enterprise will be treated as ‘Indian turnover,’ making the jurisdictional threshold applicable to it.57 Mylan Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2013/04/116, 20 Jun. 2013. As long as an enterprise is located or registered in India and notes its global turnover in its Indian books of account, such turnover shall have to be taken into account for the purposes of calculating turnover threshold for the purpose of merger notification, even if they are derived from exported goods/services or those generated by other group companies which have a market presence outside India. This approach adopted by the CCI is likely to have an impact on industries, especially in the information technology, business process outsourcing and pharmaceutical sectors. In such industries, the revenues will primarily be export revenue. In this regard, the Indian approach differs substantially from the models prevailing in other jurisdictions. For illustration, in the EU, turnover for merger jurisdiction is computed based on the allocated turnover which in turn depends on the geography where the service is provided and where the consumers are located. The Indian approach of calculating turnover, though simplistic in terms of accounts’ maintenance, inevitably broadens the scope of coverage of the merger jurisdiction and may include notification of several transactions even when they have a negligible impact on competition in India. Further, there was an erstwhile exemption in Schedule I which gave the parties the option not to notify a combination which had an insignificant local nexus in India, which has hitherto been deleted. This implies that the jurisdiction of the CCI has been enlarged to cover purely offshore combinations. Although a pure offshore combination will be cleared by the CCI since such a transaction will not cause AAEC in India, nevertheless, a notification to the CCI for approval has to be made. Failure to file a notification will attract penalties, which are discussed in the subsequent parts of the chapter.

§4.10 OBLIGATION TO NOTIFY A COMBINATION At the outset, it is relevant to mention that the rationale behind requiring notification of instances of acquisition of assets is that mergers and acquisitions may lead to a situation where economic advantages are conferred upon the parties involved, thereby giving them an ability to influence the competition dynamics of a market. Such economic benefits can be received by acquiring shares, voting rights or control of an enterprise or by acquiring assets of an enterprise, and there is no distinction between any of these in as much as they lead to economic advantages. Accordingly, acquisition of assets is required to be notified, just like acquisition of shares, voting rights and control. In doing so, two things are important:58 Reliance Jio Infocomm Limited and Reliance Communications Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/516, 11 May 2018, paras 11-19; Bharti Airtel Limited and Bharti Hexacom Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2017/05/510, 11 May 2018, paras 18-24. (i) the economic significance or competitive significance is the primary consideration behind requiring asset acquisitions to be notified for merger review purposes. For instance, in case of mergers and acquisitions in the telecom industry, spectrum is a resource which is a ‘must have’ as that is essential to provide telecommunication services. (ii) whether the concerned asset constitutes a business or whether market turnover can be attributed to it. However, their absence does not diminish the economic significance of the asset on a standalone basis. The Competition Act and the Combination Regulation provide for a mandatory suspensory regime. Section 6(2) of the Competition Act provides that a merger notification will have to be filed with the CCI for approval of an acquisition, merger or amalgamation which meets the threshold limits mentioned above. Further, section 6(2A) of the Competition Act provides that the combination will not come into effect until the earlier of: (i) 210 days59 210 days has been substituted for 150 days by the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023 but the provision is yet to be notified. have passed from the date the merger control notification was filed with the CCI or (ii) the CCI approves the combination under section 31 of the Competition Act. From the above, it is apparent that a person or enterprise proposing to enter into a combination needs to give notice to the CCI unless the requirement has been dispensed with. After notice to the CCI, parties to the combination are required to observe a standstill obligation for 210 days from the date of notice to the CCI or the date of approval of the combination by the CCI, whichever is earlier. In this backdrop, we will now discuss certain key procedural steps and issues, which need to be kept in mind while filing a notification with the CCI. [A] Pre-filing Consultation In accordance with international best practices, the CCI allows for informal and verbal consultation with its staff/case team prior to the filing of notice for a proposed combination in terms of section 6(2) of the Competition Act. Such pre-filing consultations (PFC) aim to help the parties identify the information required to file a correct and complete Form I/II/III. The Draft Regulations have codified PFC from what used to be an informal mechanism by amending the law. However, the Draft Regulations reiterate the principle from CCI’s decisional practice that the guidance provided through such PFC is not binding on the CCI. This means that if parties fail/make erroneous notification filing even after seeking PFC, then that is not a ground to absolve the parties from their error. For instance, in the merger involving Akira Marketing Private Limited, Mama Catering Private Limited, Claridges Hospitality Private Limited and Azure Hospitality Private Limited,60 Akira Marketing Private Limited, Mama Catering Private Limited, Claridges Hospitality Private Limited and Azure Hospitality Private Limited, (Combination Registration No. C-2017/05/508, 24 Jan. 2018, paras 7-8. the parties claimed that they became aware of CCI’s opinion that the transaction was notifiable only after seeking a PFC and consequently, they filed a notice subsequent to their consultation. However, the CCI observed that the option for seeking PFC was always open to the parties. Thus, it can be concluded that the PFC is only a clarificatory mechanism made available to the parties to understand if their transaction is notifiable, but it does not dispense with the obligation to notify the CCI. Hence, seeking a PFC belatedly, i.e., after consummating a transaction, does not make the parties immune from the notification requirement. [B] Responsibility to File the Merger Notification As mentioned earlier, the Indian merger control regime is mandatory and suspensory in nature. This means that if a transaction meets the thresholds prescribed under section 5 of the Competition Act and is not exempt under the Combination Regulations or through government notifications such as the MCA circular, then it has to be mandatorily notified to the CCI. Further, the consummation of the transaction shall remain suspended, and there is an obligation on the parties to ‘stand still’ till the completion of 210 days from the date of notification or till CCI approves the combination. Regulation 9 of the Combination Regulations provides that the obligation to file the merger notification with the CCI falls on the acquirer in case of acquisition of shares, control and assets.61 To be filed in Form I or Form II, as the case may and in accordance with Regulation 11 of the Combination Regulations. In cases of a merger or an amalgamation, the obligation to notify is on both parties to the combination.62 Ibid. The obligation to file a notice has been illustrated below: In section 43A proceedings against Zulia Investments Pte Limited and Kinder Investments Pte Limited,63 Section 43A Order against Zulia Investments Pte. Ltd. and Kinder Investments Pte. Ltd., 1 Aug. 2013, paras 15, 16. the CCI had observed that ‘it is expected that the parties must demonstrate a high sense of responsibility in filing combination notifications within the prescribed time limit, after effective and bonafide due diligence’. This is especially important because section 20(1) of the Competition Act precludes the CCI from initiating any inquiry after the expiry of one year from the date on which a combination, which has not been notified, takes effect. Therefore, there is a real possibility in such situations that a combination which may actually cause AAEC, can end up escaping the scrutiny of the CCI if the mandatory notification is not filed. Even if notice is filed belatedly before the expiry of this one year, ‘there could be problems in case the combination has been consummated, since restoring the original position may be as difficult as unscrambling an omelette.’ In the acquisition by Twin Star Technologies Limited (Twin Star) of several companies in the Videocon Group, the notification was filed pursuant to a Resolution Plan submitted by Twin Star under the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process of the target companies.64 Twin Star Technologies Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2020/11/786, 8 Dec. 2020. Twin Star was part of a group headed by Volcan Investments Limited (the acquirer group), and the competition analysis was undertaken on this basis. At the time, Twin Star had submitted that the ultimate acquiring entity had not been finalized, so it was unclear who would actually be the eventual acquirer. In this regard, the CCI held that under the Combination Regulations, a proposed intimation of amendment of the notification to specify a different acquirer is not possible after the making of the order. This is because if the different acquiring entity was jointly controlled, this would require a competition analysis taking account of the joint controller not forming part of the acquirer group. In any case, the CCI clarified that notification by one enterprise and actual acquisition by another was not envisaged by the Combination Regulations, so the clearance order was limited to acquisition by Twin Star. Thus, in effect, this means that any new acquirer, even if it is a part of the same transaction which has already been approved, would have to file a fresh notification since, depending on the identity and nature of the acquirer, it may be necessary to undertake a new and different competition analysis. [1] Triggering Events In case of acquisitions, the trigger for notifying the transaction to the CCI is the execution of any binding agreement or other such document for the acquisition of control/shares/voting rights/assets/control under section 5 of the Competition Act. The aspect of ‘binding document’ was first deliberated by the CCI in one of its decisions in 2012.65 Aditya Birla Nuvo Retail and Ors., Combination Registration No. C-2012/07/69, 14 Aug. 2012. The proposed combination related to the acquisition of the Pantaloons Format Business of Pantaloon Retail (India) Limited by Aditya Birla Nuvo Limited (ABNL) through its wholly owned subsidiary Peter England Fashions and Retail Limited by way of a demerger and merger of Future Value Fashion Retail Limited into Peter England Fashions and Retail Limited, pursuant to a scheme of demerger and merger under sections 391-394 of the Companies Act, 1956 (‘Scheme of Arrangement’) (‘ABNL case’). The parties had entered into a binding MOU. The MOU contemplated a series of other transactions to be consummated alongside the Scheme of Arrangement, including making a voluntary open offer in accordance with the Takeover Code. Further, a wholly owned subsidiary of ABNL would invest an amount of INR 800 crores in Pantaloon Retails India Limited by subscribing to optionally fully convertible debentures. The parties filed the merger notification with the CCI based on the binding MOU being signed between the parties. They were of the opinion that a binding MOU would constitute a binding document for the purposes of Regulation 5(8) of the Combination Regulations and, hence, will trigger the notification norms under the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations. At the time of the filing of the notice with the CCI, the terms of the Scheme of Arrangement were still under finalization and were yet to be approved by the Board of Directors of the parties. Further, at the stage of the execution of the binding MOU, certain key commercials, like the share entitlement ratio, were still under negotiation. The CCI rejected the merger approval notice filed by the parties as premature and held that signing the MOU (even though binding) cannot be the trigger for notification. The reason adopted by the CCI was that signing of the MOU was only the first step towards negotiations between the parties in relation to the finalization of the scheme, valuation, exact scope of the assets to be acquired, share entitlement ratio and also the approval of the same by the Board of Directors of the respective parties. It was noted by the CCI that the binding MOU is only an ‘interim’ arrangement and cannot be considered ‘binding agreement’ for the purposes of the trigger as the MOU will be terminated upon execution of the definitive agreement and the Scheme of Arrangement. Further, Regulation 5(8) also provides that: (1) in cases where the acquisition is without the consent of the target enterprise, any document executed by the acquirer conveying a decision to acquire control, shares or voting rights shall be the ‘other document,’ and (2) where such a document has not been executed but the intention to acquire is communicated to a Statutory Authority, the date of such communication shall be deemed to be the date of execution of the other document for acquisition. It must be pointed out that Regulation 5(8) as mentioned above has undergone two sets of amendments: one on 3 July 2015 and a second amendment on 7 January 2016. Earlier, the Regulation 5(8) of the Combination read as:Provided further that where such a document has not been executed but the intention to acquire is communicated to the Central Government or State Government or a Statutory Authority, the date of such communication shall be deemed to be the date of execution of the other document for acquisition. Post the amendment on 3 July 2015, the relevant part of Regulation 5(8) reads as:Provided further that where such a document has not been executed but the intention to acquire is communicated to the Central Government or State Government or a Statutory Authority, the date of such communication shall be deemed to be the date of execution of the other document for acquisition. Prior to the amendment on 3 July 2015 in the Combination Regulations, the CCI also discussed the issue of binding document in 2014.66 Trent Hypermarket Limited/Tesco Overseas Investments Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/03/162, 27 May 2014. The proposed combination is related to the acquisition of 50% of the issued and paid-up equity share capital of Trent Hypermarket Limited (THL) by Tesco Overseas Investments Limited (TOIL). The notice was given by TOIL, pursuant to the execution of the Joint Venture Agreement and Share Purchase Agreement (SPA) between TOIL, THL and Trent Limited (Trent) on 21 March 2014. TOIL had filed an application dated 17 December 2013 (‘Application’) and sought the approval of the DIPP, Ministry of Commerce & Industry and Foreign Investment and Promotion Board, Ministry of Finance (FIPB) for the acquisition of 50% of the issued and paid-up equity share capital of THL. It was argued by TOIL that had they filed the merger notification for approval within thirty days of the Application, at such a preliminary stage, full details of the proposed combination would not have been available, and the notice would have been incomplete and without relevant and detailed information, necessary for a review by the CCI. Reference was made by TOIL to the ABNL case, discussed above. It was noted by TOIL that CCI had held in the ABNL case that merger notification was premature as the discussions with respect to the terms of the combination were still underway, being subject to definitive agreements. Hence, the preliminary document executed by the parties was an interim arrangement and did not trigger the requirement to file a merger control notification. The CCI, however, disagreed with the contention of TOIL. CCI made a difference between ABNL case and this case and noted that the ABNL case involved a scheme of merger/demerger and did not involve intimation of such proposal to the Central Government or State Government or any Statutory Authority. Thus, CCI noted that TOIL was required to give notice to it within thirty days of its application to the DIPP and the FIPB, i.e., by 16 January 2014. However, the notice was given by TOIL on 31 March 2014, with a delay of around seventy-three days. The second set of amendments came on 7 January 2016 which added a proviso to Regulation 5(8) of the Combination Regulations which states that a public announcement has been made in terms of Takeover Code for acquisition of shares, voting rights or control, such public announcement shall be deemed to be the ‘other document’. Hence, that other document would only mean public announcements made to SEBI in terms of the Takeover Code. Thus, together with the amendment on 3 July 2015, the amendment on 7 January 2016 has now further provided that the communication with a Statutory Authority is no longer a trigger to filing a combination notice with the CCI. Pursuant to the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, ‘other document’ is now defined in the explanation of section 6 of the Competition Act and has been removed from Regulation 5 under the Draft Regulations. This is pari materia to the amendment of 7 January 2016, as discussed above, and formalizes the legal position in the Competition Act itself that where acquisition is without the consent of the target, the public announcement made to the SEBI under the Takeover Code shall be the trigger for notification to CCI. The latest position on trigger documents can be seen from the following cases – on the point of what constitutes a binding document for the purpose of notification. The CCI penalized an acquirer, Baxalta Incorporated (Baxalta), under section 43A of the Competition Act for failure to file notice.67 Section 43A Order against Baxalta Incorporated, 8 Mar. 2016, para. 10.3-10.6. In this case, the notice was filed by Baxalta within thirty days of signing a Global Separation and Distribution Agreement (GSDA) for the transfer of Baxter’s Indian bioscience business and related assets (target business) to a newly created wholly owned subsidiary of Baxter, viz. Baxalta BioScience (India) Private Limited (Baxalta India) after taking a PFC from the CCI. Thereafter, the ownership and control of Baxalta India was to be transferred to Baxalta (implementation of the India leg of the combination). Baxalta did not file a notice prior to the PFC because notification because the India leg of the transaction was to be effected through a separate local implementation agreement. In this regard, the CCI held that the GSDA was the binding document executed between Baxter and Baxalta, which satisfied section 6(2) of the Competition Act. Thus, the argument of the acquirer that the trigger event for the merger notification in India would be the local implementation agreement for the India Separation was not valid. The CCI further reasoned that if the parties to the combination notify the combination only upon the execution of a local agreement and not after execution of the global agreement, in spite of meeting jurisdictional thresholds prescribed under section 5 of the Competition Act, then there is the possibility that the combination would be consummated at the global level even before the CCI has assessed the same (as had happened in the instant case). In such a situation, the independent market behaviour of the parties to the combination has already ceased even before the CCI carried out its assessment of the combination, which would defeat the purpose of the suspensory regime of regulation of combinations. Thus, the position is that the obligation to notify a transaction to the CCI arises upon the execution of the global agreement itself and not the local agreement pursuant to the transaction, if the former is executed prior.68 Section 43A Order against Schulke & Mayr GmbH, 13 Jan. 2017, para. 8.3. Hence, the global agreement is the trigger for notification of the combination. Further, in case of interconnected transactions, the notification requirement is triggered upon the execution of the first binding document. This was clarified in section 43A proceedings initiated against Cairnhill CIPEF Limited and Cairnhill CGPE Limited in their acquisition of 11% equity shares in Mankind Pharma Limited (target).69 Section 43A Order against Cairnhill CIPEF Limited and Cairnhill CGPE Limited, 13 Apr. 2017, paras 4-8. This is pursuant to the principle of interconnected transactions (as discussed subsequently in this chapter) as given under Regulation 9(4) of the Combination Regulations which provides that ‘where the ultimate intended effect of a business is achieved by way of a series of steps or smaller individual transactions which are inter-connected, one or more of which may amount to a combination, a single notice, covering all these transactions, shall be filed by the parties to the combination’. In this regard, in its decisional practice, the CCI has also observed that an interconnected part of the combination cannot escape notification on the technical pretext that binding agreement has not been entered into for such inter-connected transaction. If one agrees otherwise, then notification of a connected transaction can be avoided by just postponing the execution of the binding document for the interconnected part.70 Section 43A Order against Canada Pension Plan Investment Board and ReNew Power Limited, 21 Nov. 2019, para. 18. Thus, while it is true that the notification requirement commences upon the execution of the first binding document and not necessarily the first document, in case of interconnected transactions, since the steps to the transaction are interdependent upon each other to achieve a single, final objective – the obligation to notify is triggered upon the execution of the first relevant agreement in such series of interconnected transactions. In this regard, it is apposite to note the findings of the CCI in the proposed acquisition of shares of Telewings Communications Services Private Limited (Telenor India) by Lakshdeep Investments & Finance Private Limited (Lakshdeep). The transaction involved four steps for which a combined notice was filed pursuant to the execution of a Share Subscription and Shareholders’ Agreement (SSHA) between Telenor South Asia Investment Pte Limited (Telenor South Asia), an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of Telenor ASA (Telenor, the ultimate holding company of Telenor Group), Lakshdeep and Telenor India.71 Section 43A Order against Telenor ASA, Telenor (India) Communications Private Limited and Telenor South Asia Investments Pte Limited, 3 Jul. 2018, para. 21. Essentially, (i) Telenor India was to acquire 2G spectrum and requisite licenses for telecommunication operations in India, then (ii) Lakshdeep was to acquire 51% shares in Telenor India followed by (iii) acquisition of Unitech Wireless (Tamil Nadu) Private Limited’s business on a going concern basis by Telenor India and finally, (iv) after the approval of FIPB, Telenor was to increase its shareholding in Telenor India to 74% by purchasing shares from Lakshdeep, leaving the latter with 26% share in Telenor India. Despite different parties acting as acquirers in the entire transaction, only Lakshdeep filed a notice before the CCI as the acquirer in step (ii). The CCI held that in terms of Regulation 9(1) and Regulation 9(3) of the Combination Regulations respectively, in case of an acquisition or acquiring of control of enterprise(s), the acquirer is required to file the notice and in case of a merger or an amalgamation, parties to the combination are required to jointly file the notice, accordingly, for inter-connected transactions, all the acquirers/parties are required to jointly file the notice. Lakshdeep could not have raised the issue on steps (iii) and (iv) which could have been raised only by Telenor India separately. In order to subject the entire transaction for assessment in terms of Regulation 9(4), the notice must have been filed by all the concerned parties together. Thus, the fact that all the four steps were disclosed did not absolve the parties of their respective obligations to file separate notices or to file a composite notice jointly by each of the relevant parties. This is because the competition assessment of the entire transaction, i.e., including all the parties, has to be undertaken together to assess the impact on the market. Therefore, the takeaway is that parties cannot absolve themselves of their responsibility to file a notice merely because another party has filed a notice for the same transaction(s) unless such notice is jointly filed. One of the only few cases where penalty was not imposed under section 43A was when Sundaram Finance Limited (SFL) filed an application to Reserve Bank of India (RBI) seeking its approval to increase its stake in Royal Sundaram (target) from 49.9% to 75.9% on a unilateral basis without the prior knowledge or consent of the selling shareholder (RSA) or the target.72 Section 43A Order against Sundaram Finance Limited, 12 Jul. 2016, paras 4-9. The application neither contained any details about the contemplated acquisition nor any authorization in the form of a board resolution or otherwise. The issue was whether the communication of the intention to acquire to a statutory authority triggered a notice under section 6(2) of the Competition Act. The CCI observed that the application filed to the RBI could not be termed as a binding document triggering notification. Regulation 31(b) of the Combination Regulations stipulates that the trigger for notification is the date of execution of any agreement or other binding document for acquisition. In the present case, the application to RBI did not reflect any intention or understanding whatsoever between the parties towards the acquisition. Rather, it was merely an application by SFL to seek permission to be put on the same level field as other potential buyers of the shares from the RSA so that it was not handicapped from making an offer, should the opportunity present itself since NBFCs like SFL require prior permission of the RBI to have higher equity in an insurance venture. Thus, the application to RBI was neither an agreement for acquisition nor a binding document for acquisition. Consequently, it was held that SFL was not required to give notice within thirty days of its application to the RBI. Therefore, the crux of the responsibility to file a notification is that it is triggered when the first binding document is executed between the parties, except in cases of interconnected transactions wherein the first step of the combination becomes the trigger point for notifying the combination. [C] Single Notice in Case of an Interconnected Transaction As discussed briefly in the previous section, Regulation 9(4) of the Combination Regulations provides that the parties can file a single notice in case of multiple business transactions which are interrelated and interconnected to each other. The parties to the combination have to analyse whether the transactions are interdependent or interconnected to each other by analysing the ‘economic realities’ of the transaction. In the event that the multiple transactions have a condition that none of the transactions would take place without the other, such multiple transactions can be said to be inter-connected or inter-dependent. In this regard, it is apt to note CCI’s Order with respect to the merger approval notice given by Relay B.V., an indirect wholly owned subsidiary of Diageo Plc. (Diageo) (Both Relay B.V. and Diageo are jointly referred to as the ‘Acquirer’), and United Spirits Limited (USL),73 United Spirits Limited by Relay B.V. (Diageo), Combination Registration No. C-2012/12/97, 26 Feb. 2013. for the proposed acquisition of shares and control of USL. The transaction postulated the following situations:(1) Relay B.V. agreed to subscribe to the new equity shares of USL (‘preferential shares’), representing 10% of USL’s post-issue enlarged share capital, by way of preferential allotment, on terms and subject to the conditions as provided in the Preferential Allotment Agreement (PAA). (2) Further, Relay B.V. agreed to purchase equity shares of USL from certain existing shareholders of USL, including United Breweries (Holdings) Limited (UBHL) and Kingfisher Finvest India Limited (KFinvest), by entering into a SPA, amounting to around 17.4% of USL’s enlarged share capital (i.e., sale shares). Further, the merger notice provided that as per the terms of the SPA, in certain circumstances, where: (i) the preferential allotment is not completed, and (ii) Relay B.V. holds less than 25.1% of the equity shares in USL after taking into account the sale shares acquired under the SPA, the shares acquired pursuant to the mandatory open offer under the Takeover Code or the shares acquired in any other manner, then UBHL and KFinvest will sell and Relay B.V. will acquire, such number of additional equity shares in USL which will take Relay B.V.’s shareholding in USL to 25.1%. Such number of shares of USL which UBHL and KFinvest would require to sell to Relay B.V., under the aforesaid conditions, was termed as ‘additional shares’. In this regard, it has also been stated that if the preferential shares representing 10% of USL’s post-issue enlarged capital are subscribed to by Relay B.V., then no additional shares would be required to be sold to Relay B. V. (3) The execution of the SPA and the PAA triggered an obligation on the part of Relay B.V. to make a mandatory tender offer to the public shareholders of USL under the Takeover Code. Accordingly, on 9 November 2012, Relay B.V. announced its intention to launch a mandatory tender offer under the Takeover Code (MTO) to acquire the equity shares from the public shareholders of USL which represent up to a maximum of 26% of USL’s enlarged share capital (i.e., the offer shares). It was stated in the notice that on acquisition of the sale shares, preferential shares and the offer shares (if the MTO is fully subscribed), Relay B.V. would hold 53.4% of the USL’s enlarged equity share capital. (4) Further, the agreements provided that Relay B.V., or an affiliate of Relay B.V., may also, at any time and from time to time, acquire other shares in USL in order to obtain an aggregate shareholding of at least 50.1% of USL’s equity share capital (i.e., the other shares). It was provided under the Shareholders Agreement (SHA) that if either of UBHL or KFinvest, or any of their controlled affiliates, transfer all or part of their shares in USL to any third party, it shall first offer such shares exclusively to Relay B.V. On receipt of such an offer, Relay B.V. would have the right to elect to acquire all or, in some cases, part of such offered shares (i.e., the ROFO shares) by giving a notice to UBHL or KFinvest, or as the case may be, within a specified period, in terms of the SHA. The CCI, based on the market structure, gave a blanket approval for a period of five years to the Acquirer to acquire shares of up to 53.4% USL. From a competition law analysis, the CCI viewed the entire transaction as interrelated and undertook the competition law analysis based on the entire transaction. The scope of Regulation 9(4) of the Combination Regulations, where the parties have the liberty to file a single notice in relation to inter-connected or inter-dependent transactions, was discussed in a decision by the erstwhile COMPAT in the case of Thomas Cook (India) Ltd. & Ors. v. CCI.74 Thomas Cook (India) Limited & Ors v. Competition Commission of India [2015], (COMPAT) Appeal No. 48 of 2014. erstwhile COMPAT overturned the penalty imposed by the CCI on parties (Thomas Cook India (TCIL), its subsidiary (TCISIL) and Sterling Holiday Resort (SHRIL)) operating in the travel and hotel related services in and from India. The parties had entered into a composite two-step scheme of demerger of SHRIL wherein its shares would be transferred to TCISIL, followed by the amalgamation of its remaining business into TCIL and issuing of certain equity shares of TCIL to SHRIL’s shareholders. The penalty was imposed on account of failure to furnish information and consummation of certain market purchases wherein transfer of equity shares of SHRIL by TCISIL was made. The transaction has been described in the following diagram: Notes to the diagram: i. The obligation to make an open offer under Takeover Code was triggered pursuant to steps (i) and (ii) above. This can be considered step (iii). ii. The above transactions were in lieu of the demerger/amalgamation. iii. TCISIL notified the equity shares purchased via the SPA and preferential allotment through the subscription agreement under steps (i) and (ii) but did not notify the market purchase prior to its consummation. On the one hand, the parties maintained the plea that market purchases had nothing to do with the demerger/amalgamation scheme, and more so because it was an exempted transaction because of the MCA Circular; the CCI, on the other hand, concluded that market purchases were intrinsically connected with the two-stage scheme and was thus required to be notified under section 6(2) of the Competition Act. It must be borne in mind that the two-stage scheme and market purchases were authorized in the same meeting, followed by a joint press release. It also remained an agreed fact that the market purchases were entitled to an exemption under the MCA Circular, as agreed by the CCI as well. However, the CCI declined to give the benefit of this exemption to the parties mainly on the grounds that the market purchases were intrinsically connected with other transactions. The erstwhile COMPAT criticized this approach of the CCI and stated that the latter failed to take cognizance of the fact that the implementation of the two-stage scheme for demerger/amalgamation was not dependent on the market purchase of equity shares of SHRIL. Therefore, the mere fact that various transactions were executed in close proximity to the market purchases of the equity shares of SHRIL by TCISIL would not be sufficient to deny the benefit of the MCA exemption notification to the parties. Further, the erstwhile COMPAT went on to interpret the scope of the exemption in the context of Regulation 9(4) of the Combination Regulations and concluded that the notification is not riddled with any condition. Therefore, if the transaction involving market purchases of equity shares of SHRIL was covered under the exemption notification, the same was not required to be notified under section 6(2) of the Competition Act. It also noted the good faith of the parties who disclosed each and every transaction, including the market purchase of equity shares at the time of notification, even though separate or composite notice was not filed in relation to the market purchases. Though not discussed in the decision of the erstwhile COMPAT, it must be borne in mind that post the order of the CCI in the Thomas Cook case, Regulation 9(5) was inserted in the Combinations Regulation (discussed below). By virtue of Regulation 9(5), substance will take precedence over form and transaction structures, or transactions comprising a combination which has the effect of avoiding notice in respect of whole or part of the combination would be disregarded and still considered notifiable. As a result, a takeaway for companies from the Thomas Cook decisions and Regulation 9(5) of the Combination Regulations would be to avail maximum benefits of various exemptions under the framework of the statute and notifications even in case of inter-connected transactions where a composite notice is being filed. However, if the sole purpose of such structuring, especially by way of performing an inter-connected transaction, is to evade notice, the same may be penalized for non-notification. Subsequently, the Supreme Court also reiterated CCI’s findings.75 Competition Commission of India v. Thomas Cook (India) Ltd. & Anr. [2018], (SC) Civil Appeal No. 13578 of 2015, paras 27-31. It recognized that although the market purchases were not mentioned in the other transaction documents, they were consummated only after almost finalizing the composite combination. This clearly suggested that market purchases would not have taken place in the absence of the demerger scheme and the other acquisitions. When a series of transactions is envisaged to accomplish a combination, all the transactions have to be taken into consideration by the CCI, not an isolated transaction. While it is open for the parties to structure their transactions in a particular way, the substance of the transactions would be more relevant to assess the effect on competition irrespective of whether such transactions are pursued through one or more steps/transactions. Structuring of transactions cannot be permitted in such a manner so as to avoid compliance with the mandatory provisions of the Competition Act. Further, Regulation 9(4) envisages the possibility that a business transaction may be achieved by a combination of inter-connected or inter-dependent steps/transactions. Thus, since market purchases were not independent and could not be used in isolation for the purpose of any exemption, a single composite notice had to be filed for the combination. As a result, the Supreme Court held that the market purchases did not qualify as a combination in view of the target exemption notification. Therefore, the implication of the collective decisions above (‘Thomas Cook case’) is that parties may avail of the exemption notification even in case of an inter-connected transaction where a composite notice is filed. Furthermore, the test of inter-connected transaction is the dependence of one transaction on the other. What is key is the substance of the transaction, which should not be structured to achieve the effect of avoiding notice for the whole or part of the combination. Thus, the applicability of the exemption is determined by the entire composite combination together. The above-discussed understanding of Regulation 9(5) has also been affirmed by CCI in its decisional practice. A landmark case where CCI heavily penalized the failure to disclose inter-connected transactions was Amazon’s failure to notify the details of a shareholders’ agreement requiring Amazon’s prior written consent for Future Coupons Private Limited (FCPL) to decide any matter relating to FRL, as part of Amazon’s acquisition of FCPL. On a holistic appreciation of the facts, it was found that the commercial agreement and acquisition of shares in FCPL to secure shareholding and rights over FRL had been inter-connected to each other and were conceived, contemplated, negotiated and consummated as part of one composite combination (i.e., to ultimately acquire certain rights over FRL).76 Sections 43A, 44 and 45 Order against Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC, 14 Dec. 2021, paras 52-67. Therefore, Amazon failed to give a single notice covering all the inter-connected steps of the combination, as required in Regulation 9(4), read with Regulations 9(5) of the Combination Regulations and section 6(2) of the Competition Act. This has been discussed in more detail under the heading on failure to furnish material information. Another limb to determining the interconnected nature of transactions comes by way of the European Commission’s Consolidated Jurisdictional Notice, which provides that ‘If two or more transactions (each of them bringing about an acquisition of control) take place within a two-year period between the same persons or undertakings, they shall be qualified as a single concentration, irrespective of whether or not those transactions relate to parts of the same business or concern the same sector … It is sufficient if the transactions, although not carried out between the same companies, are carried out between companies belonging to the same respective groups’. This approach, that multiple acquisitions between the same enterprises within a two-year period would be considered inter-related transactions, has also been affirmed by the CCI.77 Section 43A Order against Piramal Enterprises Limited, 2 May 2016, para. 7. In an interesting first, the CCI penalized CPPIB together under both section 43A and section 44 of the Competition Act because while it notified the first acquisition in ReNew (Transaction I), it did not notify ReNew’s acquisition of Ostro which was another energy company (Transaction II).78 Section 43A Order against Canada Pension Plan Investment Board and ReNew Power Limited, 21 Nov. 2019, paras 12.6, 18 and 27. This order is consistent with its decisional practice of mandating a single, composite notification in case of inter-connected transactions.79 Section 43A Order against Telenor ASA, Telenor (India) Communications Private Limited and Telenor South Asia Investments Pte Limited, 3 Jul. 2018; Section 43A Order against Bharti Airtel Limited and Lion Meadow Investment Limited, 23 Aug. 2023. The primary question to determine the inter-connectedness of Transactions I and II was whether the Primary Acquisition in Transaction I was pursued to support Ostro Acquisition. The CCI relied on the observations of the Supreme Court in the Thomas Cook case that the guiding factor for determining the scope of notification to CCI would be the substance of a combination and technical interpretations to isolate two different steps of transactions of a composite combination would be against the spirit and provisions of the Competition Act. Consequently, no part of a composite combination can be seen in isolation for the purpose of any exemption, as also clarified by the Supreme Court in the Thomas Cook case.
