While reviewing the same, CCI observed that Telenor had already consummated Steps (iii) and (iv) above and also increased its shareholding from 74% to 100% (‘tranche 2’) without notifying the same. Since Lakshdeep had 26% stake in Telenor India after tranche 1, CCI observed that Lakshdeep still had joint control over Telenor India, and therefore, the transfer of the same had the effect of changing control of Telenor India from joint control of Lakshdeep and Telenor to sole control of Telenor. Consequently, tranche 2 was also held to not be an intra-group transaction and, hence, not covered under Item 8, Schedule I. Relying on Ultratech Cement Limited,131 UltraTech Cement Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015-02-246, 10 Apr. 2015. CCI observed that Lakshdeep would have decreased its shareholding to 49% if Telenor did not get the requisite approvals and; to 26% in case, it did. Consequently, in both cases, the minimum shareholding of Lakshdeep would have been 26%, which, with or without any affirmative voting rights, confers the ability under the Companies Act to block special resolutions on Lakshdeep by virtue of its shareholding itself and exert negative control over the management and affairs of Telenor India. Hence, Telenor would not have had the ability to exercise sole control over Telenor India and Uninor. It can be said that the change of control (different aspects of control have already been discussed earlier in the chapter), as well as the change in independence of the merging entities, has a key role to play in availing the Item 8 exemption.132 Section 43A Order against Sarva Haryana Gramin Bank and Punjab National Bank, 14 Feb. 2017; Taurus Ventures Limited and Capricorn Ventures Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/02/251, 26 Mar. 2015. [10] Schedule I Item 9 A merger or amalgamation of two enterprises where one of the enterprises has more than fifty per cent (50%) shares or voting rights of the other enterprise, and/or merger or amalgamation of enterprises in which more than fifty per cent (50%) shares or voting rights in each of such enterprises are held by enterprise(s) within the same group. Provided that the transaction does not result in transfer from joint control to sole control. The exemption provides for intra-group merger exemption. As such, like the intra-group acquisition exemption, the intra-group merger exemption is not an absolute exemption under the Combination Regulations. The diagrammatic representation of the exemption is as follows: In the above diagrammatic representation, Hold Co. holds 60% in Company A and Company B. Based on the same, Hold Co., Company A and Company B fall within the same group. Shareholder A also holds 40% in Company B; thus, Company B is a part of two groups. Company A and Company B enter into a scheme of arrangement under the Companies Act for merger. This merger, although within the same group, will not be able to avail of the exemption because it is a case of transfer of joint to sole control. [11] Schedule I, Item 10 Acquisition of shares, control, voting rights or assets by a purchaser approved by the Commission pursuant to and in accordance with its order under section 31 of the Act. This exemption covers situations wherein the CCI has imposed a structural commitment on the parties to the combination to divest some assets. Upon some divesture, a third party acquires such assets. Earlier, even the acquisition of assets by a third party, pursuant to an order of divesture by the CCI, would have required a fresh application for approval to be filed before the CCI. Since this exemption was not available, the third-party buyer, as an abundant caution, used to file a merger notification. Now, the CCI has plugged this loophole and added this exemption. [12] Sector-Specific Exemptions: Government Notifications As mentioned earlier, the Central Government can exempt any class of enterprises from application of any provisions of the Competition Act under section 54 under public interest. This power has been exercised to exempt the following enterprises from notification requirements under sections 5 and 6 of the Competition Act: (i) Banking company in respect of which the Central Government has issued a notification under section 45 of the Banking Regulation Act, 1949.133 Vide Notification S.O. 1034(E) published in Gazette dated 11 Mar. 2022. (ii) Regional rural banks in respect of which the Central Government has issued a notification under section 23A(1) of the Regional Rural Banks Act, 1976.134 Vide notification no. S.O. 2561(E) published in Gazette dated 10 Aug. 2017. (iii) All cases of combinations reconstitution, transfer and amalgamation of nationalized banks under the Banking Companies (Acquisition and Transfer of Undertakings) Act, 1970 and the Banking Companies (Acquisition and Transfer of Undertakings) Act, 1980.135 Vide notification no. S.O. 2828(E) published in Gazette dated 30 Aug. 2017. (iv) All cases of combinations involving central public sector enterprises operating in the oil and gas sectors under the Petroleum Act, 1934 and the rules made thereunder or under the Oilfields (Regulation and Development) Act, 1948 and the rules made thereunder, along with their wholly or partly owned subsidiaries operating in the oil and gas sectors.136 Vide notification no. S.O. 3714(E) published in Gazette dated 22 Nov. 2017. In the context of amalgamation of regional rural banks, it is relevant to note that prior to the exemption granted by the Central Government, such amalgamations triggered the requirement to file a notice to the CCI, even if they were undertaken pursuant to the orders issued by the Central Government and not on regional rural banks’ own volition. In fact, in two instances, the CCI has also penalized regional rural banks137 Section 43A Order against Rajasthan Marudhara Gramin Bank and State Bank of Bikaner and Jaipur, 14 Feb. 2017; Section 43A Order against Sarva Haryana Gramin Bank, 14 Feb. 2017. for consummating the amalgamation without seeking its approval. The CCI reasoned therein that the fact of the decision of amalgamation being taken by the Central Government does not obliterate the Parties from their responsibility to notify the amalgamation to the CCI. Thus, the granting of exemption by way of the Central Government notification prevents notices for mere technical approval of the CCI, which is unnecessary when such amalgamations have been sanctioned by the Central Government itself. [C] Open Market Purchases At present, the Competition Act prohibits parties from acquiring any shares or paying any consideration pending approval of the combination by CCI. We have already discussed how CCI penalizes parties for gun-jumping by part/pre-payment of consideration and placing the consideration in an escrow account. The consequence of this is that any acquisition of shares in the open market, i.e., listed on the stock exchange, including potential hostile takeovers in India, is not allowed. As a result, parties were precluded from capitalizing on open market purchases, which happen nearly instantaneously. The Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, changes this position by allowing parties to derogate from the standstill obligations for open market purchases so that they can capitalize on open market opportunities. Further, the Draft Regulations require that such transactions have to be notified to the CCI within thirty days from the completion of such on-market transactions. They also specify certain rights that can be exercised before obtaining CCI approval, including: (a) benefiting from economic benefits such as dividends; (b) disposing of shares; or (c) exercising voting rights in matters relating to liquidation or insolvency proceedings. However, the acquirer must not directly or indirectly influence the target enterprise in any way. Upon implementation, it will become easier for parties to initiate and implement open market purchases.

§4.13 TREATMENT OF OFFSHORE COMBINATIONS There was an erstwhile exemption (which has hitherto been deleted) on offshore combination. Earlier, the un-amended Combinations Regulations envisaged that combinations taking place outside India with insignificant local nexus and effect on markets in India would be exempted from the merger control application regime (offshore exemption). However, the 2014 amendment did away with this category of exemption. Hence, offshore combinations are directly amenable to the jurisdiction of the CCI as long as the threshold limit is met. The CCI has the power to review global transactions by virtue of section 32 of the Competition Act, which states that the CCI ‘shall, notwithstanding that a combination has taken place outside India or any party to combination is outside India have power to inquire into such combination if such combination has, or is likely to have, an appreciable adverse effect on competition in the relevant market in India’. In its decisional practice, the CCI has noted that once the jurisdictional thresholds are breached, the CCI can enquire into combinations taking place outside India, and the residential status of the parties to the combination is immaterial and, further, an AAEC is not a prerequisite for notification of a proposed transaction to the CCI.138 Section 43A Order against SABIC International Holdings B.V, 15 Jul. 2022, paras 22-24. The scope of the Offshore Exemption was first tested in the case of Tata Chemicals Limited/Wyoming I, 139 Tata Chemicals Ltd. and Wyoming 1 (Mauritius) Pvt. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2011/12/12, 28 Nov. 2011. where the CCI was notified of the proposed merger of Wyoming I, which was an offshore wholly owned subsidiary (incorporated in Mauritius) of Tata Chemicals Limited, into its holding company, located in India. The parties claimed, inter alia, that the Offshore Exemption was applicable in this instance, given that the transaction was an ‘entirely outbound stream of acquisition’ by the holding company. However, the CCI did not agree and observed that since the parties exceeded the jurisdictional thresholds in India and one of the parties to the merger was located in India, the Offshore Exemption was unavailable. This interpretation was further widened with the CCI holding in Tetra Laval/Alfa Laval 140 Tatra Laval B.V. and Alfa Laval AB, Combination Registration No. C-2012/02/40, 12 Apr. 2012. that if the target enterprise has any direct or indirect presence in India through its subsidiaries in excess of the jurisdictional thresholds, there is a significant local nexus in India and the transaction is notifiable. It is by virtue of this expanded interpretation that combinations get captured under the radar of CCI by involving Indian subsidiaries, even if they remain in unrelated businesses, not forming the subject matter of the acquisition.141 Nestle, Pfizer, Combination Registration No. C-2012/05/57, 1 Aug. 2012; Glory Investments A Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/08/76, 11 Oct. 2012; Titan International Inc. and Titan Europe PLC, Combination Registration No. C-2013/02/109, 2 Apr. 2013. An illustration of the same is the acquisition by Nestle of Pfizer’s global nutrition business, which did not have any presence or business operations in India but was notified to the CCI on account of the fact that Pfizer has other subsidiaries in India, which are engaged in completely unrelated business activities, i.e., the pharmaceutical and animal healthcare sector. For the sake of illustration, let us take an example of a purely overseas transaction: In the structure above, the Acquirer wishes to acquire 25% in Target, which has a subsidiary in India. Further, the subsidiary of Target has different business operations than that of Target, and the Acquirer has no business operations in India. In such a situation, the transaction is purely offshore with no effect on the market in India. Even in such a case, the transaction will be a notifiable transaction if the subsidiary exceeds the jurisdictional thresholds mentioned under the MCA Circular and the transaction crosses the threshold limits mentioned under section 5 of the Competition Act. The enforcement regime of CCI with respect to offshore combinations has also strengthened by virtue of imposition of penalties in case of belated filings. For instance, in Titan International/Titan Europe, 142 Titan International Inc. and Titan Europe PLC, Combination Registration No. C-2013/02/109, 2 Apr. 2013. Titan International, Inc., based in the USA, was acquiring the entire share capital of Titan Europe PLC, based in the UK. In its process, Titan International indirectly had to acquire Titan Europe’s shareholding of 35.91% equity interest in Wheels India Limited (not a subsidiary of Titan Europe but an associate company of Titan Europe). Though the transaction took place entirely offshore, it only indirectly affected the Indian entity (i.e., on the strength of shares held by the target in an Indian entity), and this shareholding did not even provide the target control over the Indian entity (Wheels India). Yet, CCI considered this shareholding to give the target a local presence in India, and since Wheels India exceeded the prescribed assets and turnover thresholds, the CCI held that a merger notification ought to have been filed to obtain the regulatory clearance from it within the prescribed time-limit under section 6(2) of the Competition Act. Since the application (voluntarily made by parties) was made after the thirty-day time limit, a penalty was imposed by the CCI. Herein, it is relevant to note that as mentioned earlier, the thirty-day time limit to file a notification for CCI’s approval after execution of the binding document has been removed. Now that the Offshore Exemption has been deleted from the Combination Regulations (which in any event was not given accord to by the CCI in its decisional practice), numerous offshore transactions are notified to the CCI which are purely technical filings.

§4.14 PROCEDURE FOR INVESTIGATION OF A COMBINATION In its decisional practice, to date, the CCI has not refused approval to any combination, even in cases of belated filing or gun-jumping, provided the notice was subsequently filed. When a combination is likely to cause AAEC in India, the CCI undertakes a detailed enquiry into the combination, including inviting opinions from relevant third parties and allows parties to submit voluntary modifications or propose modifications of their own. This procedure has been discussed below. [A] Phase I The scheme of the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations provides that the CCI will form a prima facie opinion on whether the proposed combination is likely to cause any AAEC in India. Regulation 19 of the Combination Regulations provides the prima facie opinion will be formed by the CCI within thirty days of the receipt of the merger notification. In the event that the CCI forms a prima facie view that the combination would not cause AAEC on competition in the relevant market in India, CCI approves the combination, under section 31 of the Competition Act, in Phase I itself. The said period of thirty days of forming a prima facie opinion does not include clock stops. It must be noted that even in Phase 1, Regulation 19 empowers the CCI to call for information from any third party to enquire if the combination has caused or is likely to cause any AAEC in India. As such, the CCI has used Regulation 19(3) of the Combination Regulations to call for information from Air India while approving the Jet/Etihad deal. Regulation 19(2) of the Combination Regulations provides that the CCI may ask the parties to the combination to accept modifications for forming its prima facie order. The CCI has previously sought behavioural commitments in the form of commitments in its combination decisions in Phase I itself, where it asked the parties to review its existing contracts to ensure that they are in compliance with the provisions of the Competition Act and the parties continue to abide by the provisions of the sectoral regulator. In this regard, it is apposite to note that in July 2015, the CCI amended the filing requirements prescribed under the Combinations Regulations with the aim to make the filing simpler and readily acceptable to various stakeholders, thereby aligning its practice with other matured anti-trust jurisdictions. The key element introduced is the role of third parties in the merger approval process: third parties can give their comments on the likely competition concerns arising in the relevant market as a result of the combination. This has been discussed in more detail subsequently. A timeline for a Phase I investigation, with the relevant provisions under the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations, is as follows: Days Procedure 0-30 (thirty days) Parties to notify the combination to the CCI within thirty days from the date of trigger of merger/acquisition. X-30 (thirty working days) From the date of receipt of notice, CCI has to form a prima facie opinion on whether the combination causes or is likely to cause AAEC in India. If the combination causes or is likely to cause AAEC in India, then CCI shall issue a show cause notice to the parties directing them to respond within thirty calendar days of the receipt of such notice, furnishing reasons as to why the detailed investigation in respect of such combination should not be conducted under section 29(1) of the Competition Act. X-30 (thirty days) During the continuation of the X-30 days period, the CCI, at any stage, may request the parties to file additional information or accept modification if offered by the parties. The time taken by parties to furnish information is considered ‘the clock stops period’ as this period is excluded from the X-30 days (thirty days) period. Fifteen days In case modification is offered by the parties to the combination, fifteen days are given to the parties to accept them or not. Again, this period is considered ‘the clock stops period’ as this period is excluded from the X-30 days (thirty days) period. If the CCI is not satisfied with the response of the parties, it initiates a detailed Phase II investigation. [B] Phase 2 In the event that the CCI forms a prima facie opinion that the combination will cause an AAEC in the relevant market in India, it can issue a show cause notice to the parties to the combination to respond, within thirty days, as to why a detailed investigation (Phase II investigation) should not be commenced against them with respect to the combination. On response from the parties to the combination, the CCI may call for a report from the DG to investigate the proposed combination. Thereafter, if the CCI is of the opinion that the proposed combination causes or is likely to cause an AAEC in India, it will direct the parties to the combination to publish details of the proposed combination (Schedule II – Form IV of the Combination Regulations). This aims to bring to the knowledge of or information to the public and persons affected or likely to be affected by such combination and may invite such public or person to file objections, if any. Thereafter, written objection to the combination, if invited by the CCI, will have to be provided within fifteen days from the date of publication of details of the combination by the parties to the combination. A timeline for a Phase II investigation, with the relevant provisions under the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations, is as follows: Days Procedure Y Issuance of show cause notice If the combination causes or is likely to cause AAEC in India, then CCI shall issue a show cause notice to the parties directing them to respond within thirty calendar days of the receipt of such notice, furnishing reasons as to why the detailed investigation in respect of such combination should not be conducted under section 29(1) of the Competition Act. No timeline specified From the date of receipt of response from the parties, the CCI may direct the Director General to investigate and formulate a report under section 29(1A) of the Competition Act. Y+ 7 days From the date of receipt of response from the parties or the report prepared by the Director General, whichever is later, CCI directs parties to publish details of the combination. Y+17 Parties to furnish such details and the same is brought to public knowledge. Under section 29(2) of the Competition Act, if the CCI is of the prima facie opinion that the combination has or is likely to cause AAEC, it shall direct the parties, within seven working days from the receipt of their response pursuant to section 29(1) or on the receipt of the report of the Director General (whichever is later), to publish the details of the combination within ten working days of such direction to bring it to the knowledge of public and persons likely affected by such combination. Y + 32 Under section 29(3) of the Competition Act, the CCI invites third parties to file objections to the combination, if any, within fifteen working days from the date when the details of the combination were published. Y+47 From the expiry of fifteen days from the period in section 29(3), the CCI may ask the parties to furnish additional documents regarding the combination under section 29(4) of the Competition Act. Y+ 62 Pursuant to section 29(5) of the Competition Act, the parties are to submit the additional documents as required by the CCI within fifteen days from the expiry of the period mentioned under section 29(4). Y + 107 Pursuant to section 29(6) of the Competition Act, from the date of receipt of all information by the CCI and within a period of forty-five days from the expiry of the period under section 29(5), CCI will assess all documents on record and proceed to deal with the case in accordance with section 31. It is pointed out that under the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, the timeline for the CCI to make a prima facie determination on Phase II (or approve the transaction) is thirty calendar days (as opposed to the current thirty-working-day review period under the present law). Further, if the clearance is not issued or an initial order to conduct Phase II is not issued within the thirty-calendar-day period, the transaction would be deemed approved. [1] Role of Third Parties As mentioned above, third parties may be involved in both Phase I and Phase II of the combination review process. In Phase I, CCI may reach out to third parties for their comments and observations on the combination. Further, in Phase II, the parties mandatorily have to publish the details of the combination to which any member of the public can file their written objections within fifteen days of such publication. As stated earlier, a noteworthy aspect of the July 2015 amendment is the insertion of a transparency element in case of merger filing procedure. The amendment provides that the parties to the combination will submit a 500-page summary of the combination (which will contain the details of the combination, parties and the relevant market) to the CCI, and the said summary will be hosted on the website of the CCI. This disclosure would help any stakeholder in the market to provide their comments on the combination to the CCI. The amendment should have been a step forward for the existing regime in third-party to give their comments on the combination. Prior to this amendment, and as discussed above, Regulation 19(3) of the Combination Regulations provided for discretionary powers to the CCI (as it deemed necessary) to call for information from third parties in its inquiry whether the combination has caused or is likely to cause an AAEC in India. Similarly, CCI could seek the opinion of any other agency or statutory authority in relation to combination pursuant to Regulation 34 of the Combination Regulation. Under its own statutory power to regulate its procedure (section 36 of the Competition Act), the CCI holds the power to invite experts or direct persons with respect to the trade in question and gather information. In fact, these provisions have been applied in merger cases such as in the merger between Denki Kagaku Kogyo Kabushiki Kaisha and Mitsui & Co. Ltd. CCI sought the opinion of the Rubber Board, Ministry of Commerce and Industry as well as certain users of chloroprene rubber in India.143 Denki Kagaku Kogyo Kabushiki Kaisha and Mitsui & Co. Ltd, Combination Registration No. C-2015/01/239, 24 Apr. 2015. This amendment has the following implications. It provides an opportunity for any stakeholder or interested party to provide their comments on a merger application filed with the CCI. However, mere filing of any representation does not give such third party a vested right to participate in the combination proceedings. Regulation 19(3) of the Combination Regulations empowers the CCI to call for information from any other enterprise while inquiring as to whether a combination has caused or is likely to cause AAEC in India. However, although the CCI is empowered to invite information, the scheme does not entitle any other person other than those who have given notice to participate in the proceedings. This position was clarified by the NCLAT recently in U.P. Glass Manufacturers Syndicate v. Competition Commission of India & Ors. 144 Competition Appeal (AT) No. 07 of 2023, paras 73, 74. The U.P. Glass Manufacturers Syndicate (UPGMS), an industry body of glass manufacturers, sought intervention in CCI’s assessment of the proposed acquisition of 100% shares of Hindustan National Glass & Industries Limited by AGI Greenpac Limited, which were two of the biggest market players. The NCLAT held that the right of participation of the general public in the combination assessment process arises when the CCI directs the parties to the combination to publish the details of the combination, for bringing the combination to the knowledge or information of the public and persons affected or likely to be affected. In this case, the stage of direction to publish details of combination had not arisen, since there was no prima facie opinion formed at the second stage review envisaged under section 29(2) of the Competition Act. As a result, UPGMS was rightly not allowed to participate in the combination assessment process by the CCI. Thus, third parties do not have any vested right to participate in a pending combination proceeding until and unless the CCI itself specifically directs the parties to publish the details of the combination and seeks comments from the public. Similarly, earlier, the competitors on their own could only make submissions during Phase II of the investigation or when they were asked by the CCI to provide their comments on a combination under Regulation 19(3) of the Combination Regulations. Now, any stakeholder can provide comments in order to assist the CCI in making its determination of merger, and the same can be exercised at any stage, i.e., a prima facie stage or a later stage of merger investigation. As such, as stated above, mere filing of representation does not give such parties a vested right to participate. In its decisional practice, CCI has frequently sought the comments of third parties in the combination review process.145 Schneider Electric India Pvt. Ltd. and MacRitchie Investments Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/07/586, 18 Apr. 2019, paras 64, 65; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, para. 14; PVR/DT Cinemas, Combination Registration No. C-2015/07/288, 4 May 2016; Dish TV India Limited & Videocon D2h Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2016/12/463, 4 May 2017, para. 15. Some of these cases have been discussed below. The merger between Bayer Aktiengesellschaft (Bayer), Monsanto Company (Monsanto) and KWA Investment Co (Merger Sub), a wholly owned subsidiary of Bayer, raised several competition concerns, inter alia, pertaining to the vegetable seeds business due to the presence of several horizontal overlaps in the parties’ businesses (Bayer/Monsanto).146 Bayer AG and Monsanto Company, Combination Registration No. C-2017/08/523, 14 Jun. 2018, paras 14-18. Further, Bayer’s response to the show cause notice did not allay the said concerns. Consequently, CCI asked Bayer to publish the details of the combination, pursuant to which CCI received written objections from several parties, which were considered in the assessment of the merger. Ultimately, the CCI ordered the parties to undertake divestment of certain businesses (as has been discussed under the heading of commitments). Herein, it is relevant to note that after inviting public comments on the combination, CCI generally gives the parties a chance to respond to the comments. Similarly, in the acquisition of Metso OYJ’s mineral business by Outotec OYJ (Outotec OYJ decision),147 Outotec OYJ and Metso OYJ, Combination Registration No. C-2020/03/735, 18 Jun. 2020, para. 33. the CCI considered the responses of competitors and their submissions regarding Entry Barriers (Technical, Regulatory, etc.), position of parties, countervailing buying power, possession of IPRs & drawing including designs, competitive advantage, etc. This has been discussed in more detail in the next section, under substantive analysis of combinations. Another example is the merger between PayU and BillDesk, both operating in the market of payment processing (PayU/BillDesk).148 PayU Payments Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/04/920, 5 Jun. 2022, para. 42. The parties had submitted that payment aggregation services provided by payment aggregators (PAs), such as the parties, were interchangeable with the business model of the merchant integrating itself on a TPAP, wherein the transactions are initiated on the TPAP app. To clarify this, the CCI sought the views of merchant stakeholders who submitted that they do not consider integrating a PA platform on their website/app and services and integrating their store on a TPAP to be interchangeable due to higher cost implications for the merchant, need for high level of monitoring, success rates and transaction management by merchant, concern of discoverability, need for a merchant to integrate with multiple TPAPs in order to provide users with varied options, lack of control over payment logic, lack of data insights, etc. Based on these responses, the CCI of the Online P2M Payment Services is required to be restricted to only PA services, which enable receipt of payments on the merchant website/app. Hence, it is safe to conclude that the concerns of third parties, which include consumers, competitors and general public, increasingly play an important role in CCI’s assessment of combinations. [C] Voluntary Modifications In its decisional practice, the CCI has granted approval to combinations which are potentially likely to cause AAEC to be subject to compliance with voluntary modifications offered by the parties at the time of combination assessment if such modifications are sufficient to address the potential competition concerns flagged by the CCI.149 Northern TK Venture Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/09/601, 29 Oct. 2018, para. 18; Tata Sons Private Limited, Singapore Airlines Limited, Air India Limited, Talace Private Limited and Tata SIA Airlines Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2023/04/1022, 1 Sep. 2023, paras 115-133; TRIL Urban Transport Private Limited, Valkyrie Investment Pte Limited and Solis Capital (Singapore) Pte. Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2019/07/676, 1 Oct. 2019, para. 32. Herein, it is relevant to point out that there exists a ‘grey zone’ between the formation of a prima facie opinion by CCI that a combination will have an AAEC and the decision to launch an investigation. CCI’s decisional practice indicates that the parties may submit voluntary modifications during this grey zone. For instance, in China National Agrochemical Corporation’s (acquirer) acquisition of 67% equity shares in and indirect sole control of Syngenta AG, the CCI prima facie found that there were horizontal overlaps between the Parties vis-à-vis crop protection products leading to concerns of AAEC (China National decision).150 China National Agrochemical Corporation, Combination Registration No. C-2016/08/424, 16 May 2017, para. 22. Accordingly, the CCI issued a show cause notice to China National as to why an investigation of the proposed combination should not be conducted. The Acquirer filed a response along with a voluntary remedy proposal. The combination was approved subject to compliance with these voluntary modifications, and CCI did not issue commitments of this own. In the merger between Abbot and St. Jude Medical, Inc. (SJM), the CCI found that the combined market share of the parties in the small-hole vascular closure devices segment was in the range of 90%-100%.151 Abbott Laboratories, Combination Registration No. C-2016/08/418, 13 Dec. 2016, paras 9-11. As a result, the proposed combination enhanced the combined entity’s market power in this segment. The merger was accepted upon the parties submitting a voluntary modification in the form of a plan of divesture of all the small-hole vascular closure devices of SJM. The divestiture involved the sale of the entire overlapping business, including products, trademarks, technology, know-how, manufacturing facilities, as well as transitional services for support, to an independent purchaser. Such voluntary modifications have been accepted by the CCI in the approval of other combinations as well.152 Mumbai International Airport Private Limited, Indian Oil Corporation Limited, Bharat Petroleum Corporation Limited, Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited and Mumbai Aviation Fuel Farm Facility Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/04/164, 29 Sep. 2014, para. 24; Nippon Yusen Kabushiki Kaisha Ltd., Mitsui O.S.K. Lines Ltd. and Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha, Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2016/11/459, 29 Jun. 2017, para. 25. Subsequently, this option of offering voluntary modifications was formalized by amending the Combination Regulations to allow parties to offer modifications along with their response to the show cause notice during the Phase I review. However, no format was added for the same. CCI’s decisional practice indicates a trend that it is keen to approve remedies without progressing to Phase II review provided the voluntary remedies offered by the parties actually allay the AAEC concerns identified by the CCI.153 ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, paras 75-77; Outotec OYJ and Metso OYJ, Combination Registration No. C-2020/03/735, 18 Jun. 2020 paras 16-20; Canary Investment Limited and Link Investment Trust II, Combination Registration No. C-2020/04/741, 20 Apr. 2019, para. 15; Ipca Laboratories Limited, CCI, Combination Registration No. C-2023/05/1028, 26 Jul. 2023, para. 18; General Atlantic Singapore ACK Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2023/04/1017, 6 Jun. 2023, para. 10; Culver Max Entertainment Private Limited, Zee Entertainment Enterprises Limited, Bangla Entertainment Private Limited, and Essel Group Participants, Combination Registration No. C-2022/04/923, 4 Oct. 2022, para. 88; Umang Commercial Company Private Limited and Aditya Marketing and Manufacturing Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/07/952, 30 Aug. 2022, para. 48; Dish TV India Limited & Videocon D2h Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2016/12/463, 4 May 2017, para. 18; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, paras 24-40. These remedies may not necessarily be in the nature of divestments. For instance, in the proposed acquisition by automobile manufacturers Hyundai and Kia for minority shareholdings in ride-sharing company ANI Technologies (OLA) and its electric vehicles arm, OLA Electric Mobility, the CCI highlighted its concerns that drivers of Hyundai and Kia cars might be favoured in the OLA marketplace. In a first-of-its-kind remedy, the CCI accepted voluntary modifications offered by the parties that the strategic collaboration would be on a non-exclusive basis and that the algorithm/programme of the OLA marketplace would not give preference to the drivers driving Hyundai/Kia cars or discriminate against drivers based solely on the brand of the passenger vehicles manufactured by any other automobile manufacturer. The CCI noted that the above modification would ensure that the radio taxi marketplace would act in an objective manner and that the strategic collaboration would not result in discrimination against any driver who does not drive a vehicle of Hyundai/Kia make.154 Hyundai Motor Company and Kia Motors Corporation, Combination Registration No. C-2019/09/682, 30 Oct. 2019, paras 23-25. Similarly, CCI conditionally approved Google International LLC’s acquisition of a non-controlling minority stake of 1.28% in Bharti Airtel Limited owing to Google’s stake in Jio Platforms Limited (Jio) which is also active in similar businesses to Airtel. The CCI raised concerns of a potential flow of commercial sensitive information between Airtel and Jio which were allayed by certain voluntary behavioural remedies including an information firewall between Airtel and Jio.155 Google International LLC and Bharti Airtel Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/03/91, 30 Jun. 2022, para. 24. Hence, as long as voluntary modifications proposed by the parties are comprehensive to address the anticompetitive concerns emerging from the proposed combinations, the CCI is likely to accept the modification package as it is. In a build-up of this trend, the Draft Regulations prescribe a format for offering voluntary modifications/remedies to the CCI. The items that must be included are: (a) summary of the modifications offered; (b) details on how the modifications address the identified concerns; (c) various details on the divestment products, if any; and (d) monitoring arrangements. The Draft Regulations also add timelines to the offering of modifications, thereby adding greater certainty to the process. Therefore, on a comprehensive consideration of the above discussion, it can be said that the CCI has adopted a pro-approval stance subject to compliance in the combination assessment by the parties. If parties themselves propose adequate and sufficient commitments, the CCI is not likely to proceed to a detailed investigation of the combination (Phase II). Thus, it can be said that the cooperation by the parties is central to CCI’s approach to approvals.

§4.15 TYPES OF MERGERS Depending on the nature of the business and at which level of the supply chain the parties are operating, a merger can be categorized into the following three types. The issues arising out of each kind of merger have also been discussed. [A] Horizontal Mergers Horizontal mergers occur when companies operating in the same or similar industries and offering similar products or services combine their operations. These mergers typically involve businesses that are direct competitors within the same market segment. It is to be noted that a merger of close competitors can generate horizontal-anticompetitive effects, whether coordinated or non-coordinated if the combined entity significantly constrains the behaviour of other firms active in the market.156 Outotec OYJ and Metso OYJ, Combination Registration No. C-2020/03/735, 18 Jun. 2020, para. 35. [1] Issues with Horizontal Mergers The following issues need to be considered while assessing the permissibility of horizontal mergers. First, as in the case of horizontal agreements, it must first be established as to what is the relevant market. This requires a focus on the demand side to establish whether the products are close enough substitutes or not. On the supply side, it is important to identify the market shares of the firms. Clearly, it is not enough to go on current market shares. It is important to assess how the relevant market is likely to evolve in the near future. This would depend on whether entry is easy and whether there are potential entrants that could easily enter, if profitability in the sector increases, how foreign competition is likely to evolve and the growth (or decline) of other incumbent firms. For instance, in Bayer/Monsanto,157 Bayer/Monsanto, para. 81. the CCI observed that the present market shares of Bayer in the market of cotton seeds may undergo change and do not represent near future market dynamics as the other seed companies may be out of the market in the near future. Further, the parties were among very few players with a pan-India distribution network who had across the value chain presence in the cotton seed market, which puts them in an advantageous position as compared to their competitors in the downstream market. Therefore, the CCI concluded that any potential harm to competition in future could not be completely ruled out. The second important step is to establish whether the higher concentration in the market resulting from the merger will increase the possibility of collusive or unilaterally harmful behaviour. Collusion is more likely in industries producing relatively homogeneous products and characterized by small and frequent transactions, the terms of which cannot be kept secret. The merger is likely to be unilaterally harmful when the two merging firms produce similar products in a concentrated differentiated product market. The third issue is regarding potential contestability. Even if no potential entrants are immediately visible, a large enough price increase (or high enough profitability) could encourage entry. So, it needs to be established, how high the expected price increase is likely to be. Following this, it is important to consider whether entry is really likely, how quick it will be and whether it will be sufficient to make up for the reduced competition resulting from the merger. Fourth, the case can be made that even mergers that lead to an uncompetitive outcome could result in certain ‘efficiencies’ that more than make up for the welfare loss resulting from this. The fifth concern is the emergence of complementary or portfolio effects as a result of the merger. For instance, in Bayer/Monsanto,158 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 154, 155. while Bayer was focused primarily on agrochemicals and vegetable seeds, Monsanto’s strengths lied in non-selective herbicides, traits, and agricultural seeds. Generally, portfolio effects included potential anti-competitive effects that might arise due to a merger uniting complementary products in which one or more parties enjoy significant market power. Thus, CCI concluded that the combined entity would have the ability to bundle their portfolios of crop protection products, seeds and traits which may have a negative impact on competitors and customers. For example, the combined entity may develop new traits for seeds for which only they will be able to develop agrochemical products, and therefore, the rest of the competitors would be excluded from the market. Similarly, in PayU/Bill Desk,159 PayU Payments Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/04/920, 5 Sep. 2022. an analysis of the complementary relationships between the parties revealed that despite the individual strengths and weaknesses of each party, complementary/portfolio effects of the combined entity were significantly reduced since it did not have a position of strength in any individual market. Upon a holistic consideration of all these factors, CCI concluded that the transaction did not raise competition concerns. Thus, complementary effects cannot be assumed in all situations of horizontal mergers, and a case-by-case analysis has to be done. [B] Vertical Mergers Competition Law must not normally have any objections to vertical mergers since they are measures for improving production and distribution efficiencies. The process internalizes the benefits of supply chain management and, as such, cannot be perceived as injuries to competition. There could, however, be some specific objections to vertical integration, as described below. [1] Fear of Foreclosure It is supposed that, through vertical integration, a firm can create captive distribution channels. This will foreclose the rival firms from the market, represented by the captive distribution network. This may be a problem if it threatens competition in general. For instance, in Bayer/Monsanto,160 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 84, 85. allegations of anti-competitive practices by imposing unfair and discriminatory conditions in the sub-licence agreements relating to Bt. Technology were made by the downstream competitors of Bayer and Monsanto, who were involved in the market for commercialization of Bt. cotton seed in India. The CCI noted that any foreclosure strategy by the combined entity would have a significant detrimental effect on competition in the downstream market, as it would require potential competitors to enter both the downstream and the upstream level in order to compete effectively in either market. This would raise entry barriers as it would be significantly time- and cost-intensive. [2] Entry Blocking Monopolies can have the ability to prevent the entry of firms into the market. Sometimes, it is claimed that even competitors can come together to prevent a potential entrant. This is sometimes referred to as collective foreclosure. If through integration, firms are able to internalize different levels of production, artificial barriers to entry could be created. This implies that because of the size of the incumbent, a potential entrant’s capital requirements will be high. [3] Price Squeezes Vertical mergers and integration internalize the process of production and enable a firm to perhaps reduce costs. This will result in a reduction in output prices, which is usually interpreted as a price squeeze. The law should question only those monopolies resulting from vertical mergers (integration) that lead to output restriction rather than preventing vertical integration. [C] Conglomerate Mergers A conglomerate merger is a merger that is neither horizontal nor vertical. For example, a merger between a car manufacturer and a textile firm is a conglomerate merger. The theories for ‘restraining’ vertical and horizontal mergers are well formulated. There, however, is no clear mechanism for similar restraints on conglomerate mergers except those that are based on folklore. There is sufficient evidence to suggest that conglomerate mergers do not pose any threat to competition. In fact, the green channel route for merger approval, as discussed earlier in the chapter, is an illustration of the fact that CCI ordinarily does not consider conglomerate mergers to be problematic from a competition perspective. Yet, conglomerate mergers are objected to on several grounds. Some of the objections to conglomerate mergers are:(a) They create deep pockets which enables that firm to devastate its rivals. (b) Lower costs below the marginal cost of the industry. (c) Raise barriers to entry. (d) Engage in reciprocal dealing to the disadvantage of the rivals. (e) Eliminate potential competition. We examine some of these objections below. [1] The Theory of Deep Pockets It is believed that firms operating in many markets can devastate their rivals through their potentially infinite capital resources. This suggests that conglomerates can engage in predatory pricing. However, the law cannot presume that possession of capital can lead to harmful pricing practices, even though predatory pricing is a discredited theory. An objection based on the fact of possession of capital cannot be construed as a serious objection. [2] Raising Barriers to Entry Conglomerate mergers help in pooling the capital resources. It is believed that conglomerate mergers can lead to the erection of entry barriers. For example, a firm that had a limited promotional budget might now make use of the other firm’s promotional expertise. However, if competition is equated with consumer welfare then, one should really ask why is it not a valuable efficiency to bring capital to a firm that can use it? Why is it not good for the consumers if the single-product firm shares the cost savings in advertising and promotion that normally accrue to a multi-product firm? [3] Loss of Potential Competition Two arguments are proposed to support this position. First, it is believed that because of the merger, there is less ‘space’ for new firms. Second, if, instead of the merger, the larger firm had tried to enter a market on its own, the threat of entry would have forced the existing firms to become more competitive and efficient.

§4.16 SUBSTANTIVE ANALYSIS OF COMBINATIONS Section 6(1) of the Competition Act prevents combinations which cause or are likely to cause AAEC within the relevant market in India. In concluding whether it would cause or is likely to cause AAEC, the CCI takes into account the factors mentioned under section 20(4) of the Competition Act. The aspect of merger control analysis was done in great detail by the High Level Committee on Competition Law which stated that horizontal mergers would have to be scrutinized more than a vertical or a conglomerate merger. The relevant portions of the High Level Committee Report on the approach to be adopted for merger control analysis and the key issues to be considered are as follows:As in the case of agreements, mergers are typically classified into horizontal and vertical mergers. In addition, merger between enterprises operating in different markets are called conglomerate mergers. Mergers are a legitimate means by which firms can grow and are generally as much part of the natural process of industrial evolution and restructuring as new entry, growth and exit. From the point of view of Competition Policy it is horizontal mergers that are generally the focus of attention. As in the case of horizontal agreements, such mergers have a potential for reducing competition. In rare cases, where an enterprise in a dominant position makes a vertical merger with another firm in a (vertically) adjacent market to further entrench its position of dominance, the merger may provide cause for concern. Conglomerate mergers should generally be beyond the purview of any law on mergers. A merger leads to a ‘bad’ outcome only if it creates a dominant enterprise that subsequently abuses its dominance. To some extent the issue is analogous to that of agreements among enterprises and also overlaps with the issue of dominance and its abuse discussed in the previous sections. Viewed in this way, there is probably no need to have a separate law on mergers. The reason that such a provision exists in most laws is to pre-empt the potential abuse of dominance where it is probable, as subsequent unbundling can be both difficult and socially costly. Thus, the general principle, in keeping with the overall goal, is that mergers should be challenged only if they reduce or harm competition and adversely affect welfare. Also, the CCI has taken an approach that they will block mergers only if the merger itself causes an AAEC, not issues which are predating the combination. For example, if an enterprise has a 90% market share and it then acquires a company which has a 1% market share, the said merger would not be blocked since the said entity so combined is in a dominant position since even before the merger, the entity was already in a commanding 90% market share position. Some of the factors routinely analysed in CCI’s decisional practice of substantive assessment of combinations have been discussed below. [A] Combined Market Share Market share is typically the most important factor in assessing whether the combination will cause or is likely to cause AAEC in the relevant market. Market share can be calculated both in terms of value sales and volume sales. The CCI looks at the combined market share of the parties post the combination, and in the event the market share post the combination is insignificant, the CCI has cleared the transaction in Phase I itself.161 Indus Ind, Combination Registration No. C-2015/04/268, 15 Jun. 2015; ATC Telecom Tower Corporation Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/04/269, 26 May 2015. Further, the CCI also takes into account whether the market is fragmented and whether there are sufficient competitors in the relevant market which will continue to pose effective competitive constraints on the parties post the combination.162 Inox, Fame India, Fame Motion, Big Pictures and Headstrong Films, Combination Registration No. C – 2012/10/84, 23 Oct. 2012, CTLC/TCFSL, Combination Registration No. C-2012/09/78, 4 Oct. 2012. It must be noted that the CCI looks at the increment/change in the market share as a result of the combination while analysing a combination. The CCI does not intervene in cases where the increment in market share is negligible/marginal, even if the combined market share of the parties is more than 50%.163 Sutlej Textiles and Industries Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/04/266, 15 Jun. 2015; Pfizer Inc, Combination Registration No. C-2015/03/255, 11 Jun. 2015; Johnson Controls Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2015/02/247, 7 May 2015; Torrent Pharmaceuticals Limited and Elder Pharmaceuticals Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/01/148, 26 Mar. 2014; United Spirits Limited by Relay B.V. (Diageo), Combination Registration No. C-2012/12/97, 26 Feb. 2013. The rationale for the same is that competition law concerns, if any, was pre-existing the combination, and it is not created as a result of the combination. [B] Presence of Overlaps While analysing a combination, the CCI also looks at pre-existing horizontal and vertical overlaps of the parties to the combination. For analysing overlaps, the CCI assesses whether the parties to the combination operate in different geographic areas in the relevant geographic market.164 Mahindra and Mahindra Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2013/01/105, 4 Feb. 2013; GSPC Distribution Networks Limited, Gujarat Gas Company Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/11/88, 8 Jan. 2013. The CCI has also engaged in price point analysis to see the manner in which the parties to the combination have priced their products in the relevant market to see whether parties operate at different price points to analyse overlaps.165 United Spirits Limited by Relay B.V. (Diageo), Combination Registration No. C-2012/12/97, 26 Feb. 2013. It is apposite in this regard to observe the price point analysis of various segments of beverages in the whisky segment, vodka segment, rum, wine and gin segment adopted by the CCI in the merger approval order of acquisition of shares of USL by Relay, a subsidiary of Diageo. The relevant observations of the CCI have been summarized below:(i) Whisky Segment: Whisky accounted for 60% of the total sales volume, constituting the largest segment. It was further segmented into IMFL Whisky, Scotch Whisky and Imported Whisky:– Out of this, IMFL Whisky sales constituted a large chunk of around 98.54% of the total Whisky segment, and all the IMFL Whisky brands were priced below INR 800. None of Diageo’s Whisky brands was significantly present below this price point, thereby indicating no significant market concentration in the IMFL Whisky segment post-combination. – The Scotch Whisky and the Imported Whisky segments, which together constituted less than 2% of the overall Whisky segment, were characterized by the presence of a large number of brands positioned across various premium and luxury price points starting from INR 800 and going up to INR 10,000 and above. It was observed that the consumers of these segments may have a higher degree of brand affinity, basis the relatively high sales volume of brands like Pernod Ricard’s Royal Salute, Chivas Regal 18/12 Year Old, Ballantine’s Finest, 100 Pipers, Beam Global’s Teacher’s 50, Teacher’s Original; WM Grant’s Glenfiddich, Grant’s Family Reserve, Diageo’s Johnnie Walker Blue/Red/Black, USL’s Black Dog, etc. – In the price point segment from INR 800 to INR 1,600 which constituted around 1% of the overall Whisky segment, both USL and Diageo were present with their strong brands like Black Dog 12 Years Old, Black Dog (BII) and Whyte & Mackay in the USL portfolio and brands such as Johnnie Walker Red Label, Black & White and VAT 69 in the Diageo portfolio. As such in this price segment, there was also a significant presence of other brands like Pernod Ricard’s 100 Pipers, 100 Pipers (BII), Ballantine’s Finest and Passport (BII), Beam Global’s Teacher’s and its variants, WM Grant’s Family Reserve, etc. Further, if USL’s Black Dog 12 Years Old had a strong presence at the price point of around INR 1,600, there were equally strong competitive brands of Teacher’s 50 and Teacher’s Original at around the same price point. Similar was the case with Diageo’s Johnnie Walker Red Label and USL’s Black Dog (BII) with other competitors like Beam Global with Teacher’s and Pernod Ricard with Ballantine’s Finest at a price point of INR 1,200. Thus, even if the overall volume in this segment was minuscule, consumers had reasonable choices available at various price points, thereby minimizing any concern about the elimination of competitive constraints. The competitors’ brands in the above segment also had high growth rates, and these brands were, therefore, considered to be effective competitors to the brands of the parties to the combination. – In regard to the narrow price sub-segments in the overall Whisky segment, in which even if the brands of USL and Diageo were considered to be positioned as close competitors, there were multiple other brands effectively competing with USL and Diageo in those segments, and the volume in these price segments was also minuscule in comparison to the overall volume of the Whisky segment. (ii) Vodka Segment: The Vodka segment constituted around 4% of the overall branded spirits segment in India. In 2011, nearly 88% of the Vodka sold in India was priced at INR 500 or below. Diageo, with its brand Smirnoff and its variants, was present in the INR 500+ price segment, and USL, with its flagship brand Romanov and its variants, was present in the price range below INR 500. However, in both these price segments, other competitors like Radico Khaitan were also present with its brand ‘Magic Moments’ as well as Pernod Ricard’s Absolut and its variants, Brown Forman’s Finlandia and its variants, etc., which provided stiff competition to USL and Diageo. Further, the entry of 27 new brands in the market indicated that the vodka segment was rapidly growing and evolving in India. (iii) Rum, Gin and Wine: The Rum segment constituted around 17% of the overall branded spirits segment in India. Diageo had an insignificant share and marginal presence of less than 1% in all three segments, i.e., rum, gin and wine. Therefore, despite the existence of overlaps, the market shares of the merging parties were marginal and there was healthy competition in each segment of liquor. On this basis, the merger was approved. Hence, if the presence of overlaps is negligible and the merger is likely to expand consumer choice (like in this case, the proposed combination was likely to bring new products and more variants of the existing brands at different price points), that does not pose a threat to competition in the market. For global parties to the combination, while there may be global overlaps, the CCI analyses only the horizontal and vertical overlaps in India to analyse the competitive effects to determine whether the combination will cause an AAEC in India.166 Fujitsu Limited, Panasonic Corporation and Development Bank of Japan Inc. Combination Registration No. C-2014/09/206, 27 Oct. 2014. On the aspect of vertical overlap, the CCI will intervene only in situations where there is a likelihood of foreclosure in the upstream or downstream market(s) in which the parties are present in India.167 Hyundai Hysco Co. Ltd. And Hyundai Steel Company, Combination Registration No. C-2015/05/272, 5 Jun. 2015. While analysing a combination involving an acquisition by a fund, the CCI looks at the horizontal or vertical overlap between the target and other portfolio companies operating in India, where the acquirer has made an investment.168 Sion Investment Holdings Pte. Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/03/253, 24 Oct. 2015; TPG Asia VI SF Private Limited and Manipal Health Enterprises Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/12/234, 15 Jan. 2015; KKR Floorline Investments Pte Ltd and Gland Celsus Bio Chemicals Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2013/12/145, 28 Jan. 2014; Vault Bermuda Holding Company Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/03/157, 3 Apr. 2014. The decisional practice of the CCI suggests that it gives more credence to portfolio companies, over which acquirer has direct control to determine overlaps rather than mere passive investments.169 Caladium Investment Pte. Ltd, Combination Registration No. C-2015/01/243, 5 Mar. 2015. In a first, voluntary modifications in the form of behavioural remedies were accepted by the CCI in relation to potential overlaps.170 Ipca Laboratories Limited, CCI, Combination Registration No. C-2023/05/1028, 26 Jul. 2023, paras 15-18. In the acquisition of stake in Ipca Laboratories by Unichem Laboratories Limited, the parties exhibited potential horizontal overlaps in the market for manufacture of formulations and vertical overlaps in the market for the manufacture of APIs (upstream) and manufacture of formulations (downstream). These overlaps were ‘potential’ in nature because while Ipca sold its formulations in India, Unichem’s formulations business was completely export-oriented, with no intention to sell in India. Yet, the CCI undertook a competition assessment of the potential overlaps. Even though the parties had insignificant market share in the concerned markets, they submitted a voluntary behavioural commitment that Unichem would will not re-enter the Indian formulations market for at least thirty-six months from the date of closing of the proposed combination. Although the remedy was voluntary proposed by the parties, this decision has the potential to expose potential overlaps between parties to more detailed scrutiny than was expected earlier. [C] Barriers to Entry A combination that materially increases market concentration would not give rise to sustained anti-competitive effects if new firms can enter the market (or existing firms expand) and deter the combining parties (and others) from exploiting their position in the market, post the combination. Barriers to entry are specific features of the market which give incumbent firms advantages over potential competitors. When entry barriers are low, the combining parties are more likely to be constrained by the entry of new firms, disciplining the existing firms upon entry. Conversely, when entry barriers are high, price increases by the combining firms would not be significantly constrained by entry. The CCI has, to date, approved most cases of combinations (in Phase I itself) because it has found barriers to entry in the relevant market to be very few and/or negligible.171 Sg Investors, PDPPL, EPDL, Combination Registration No. C-2012/08/75, 1 Oct. 2012 – CCI observed that there were no significant entry barriers in the relevant market for development and management of commercial and office space in India and therefore approved the combination. However, in several cases, especially where commitments have been ordered (as discussed in the next section), high barriers to entry have been highlighted by the CCI.172 Schneider Electric India Pvt. Ltd. and MacRitchie Investments Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/07/586, 18 Apr. 2019, paras 42-56; China National decision, paras 12, 17; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 68-70; Outotec OYJ decision, paras 34-36. For instance, in Bayer/Monsanto,173 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 36, 50. the CCI noted that there were substantial entry barriers in the crop protection segment in the form of upfront R&D costs, regulatory know-how, national distribution network, IPRs, patents, etc. Both parties were integrated R&D players with significant capabilities, and therefore, the entry of a new player so as to be an effective long-term competitor of the parties would be difficult. Further, both Bayer and Monsanto were leading innovators in developing nonselective herbicides as well as corresponding herbicide-tolerant traits and trait stacks. The CCI observed that R&D in traits is characterized by high barriers to entry. Limited number of integrated R&D players have the capability and resources to compete in trait innovation, and the process is also time- and cost-intensive. When coupled with regulatory uncertainty in relation to approvals, it deters other players from entering the market. [D] Buyer Power The competitive pressure on a supplier is not only exercised by competitors but can also come from its customers. Even firms with very high market shares may not be in a position, post-combination, to exercise market power if customers possess countervailing buyer power. In this context, countervailing buyer power means the bargaining strength that the buyer has vis-à-vis the seller in commercial negotiations due to its size, the commercial significance of the buyer vis-à-vis the seller and its ability to switch to alternative suppliers.174 Denki Kagaku Kogyo Kabushiki Kaisha and Mitsui & Co. Ltd, Combination Registration No. C-2015/01/239, 24 Apr. 2015. In AGI Greenpac Limited/Hindusthan National Glass & Industries Limited,175 AGI Greenpac Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/11/983, 15 Mar. 2023, paras 63-68. both parties were the only significant organized players in the market for container class in both the alco-beverage and F&B sub-segments. AGI submitted that since the customers in the glass segment were large conglomerates with deep pockets and the suppliers were dependent on these customers, there was no question of the parties being able to act independently of competitive force post the combination. However, the CCI observed that ‘countervailing buyer power is considered more a function of relative differences in concentration on the demand and supply side rather than just the size of customers, unless vertical integration trends are clearly discernible’. In this case, it was not easy for customers to switch suppliers because of the concentrated nature of the glass market, which would increase post the proposed combination. The CCI held that since the parties were the biggest players in the market, countervailing buyer power was limited, and hence, the proposed combination implied the elimination of the most credible option to switch for customers. Thus, it can be said that even if there is a high degree of buyer power present in the market, the impact of such buyer power on the market from the perspective of the proposed combination has to be seen. If the proposed combination decreases the level of choice and increases the concentration, then even a high degree of buyer power cannot mitigate the likely competition concerns. In other cases also, the CCI has assessed whether the proposed combination is likely to affect the bargaining power, if any, enjoyed by the buyers and whether it would make them more dependent on the parties post the combination.176 Culver Max Entertainment Private Limited, Zee Entertainment Enterprises Limited, Bangla Entertainment Private Limited, and Essel Group Participants, Combination Registration No. C-2022/04/923, 4 Oct. 2022, paras 43-48; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, para. 69; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 73-76. If the answer is yes, and the proposed combination reduces the competitive alternatives presently available to the buyers, then it means that it is likely to cause AAEC in the relevant market. [E] Presence of a Sectoral Regulator The CCI takes into account, while analysing the competitive effects of a combination, whether there is a sectoral regulator which prevents anti-competitive behaviour and promotes competition in a particular sector/industry. The CCI has noted the presence and role of regulators like the TRAI177 UTV Global Broadcasting Limited C-2013/01/107; Bharti Airtel Limited and Bharti Hexacom Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2017/05/509, 11 May 2018. and the Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board178 GSPC Distribution Networks Limited, Gujarat Gas Company Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/11/88, 8 Jan. 2013. to prevent anti-competitive practices in a sector while approving transactions. As such, the CCI, in the USL Diageo transaction, looked into the aspects of the manufacturing, distribution and pricing of alcoholic beverages in India which are under the regulatory purview of individual state governments for the production, manufacture, possession, transport, license, bottling, introduction of new brands, pricing, purchase and sale of alcoholic beverages in India. Further, the government’s involvement in the market of alcoholic beverages also becomes evident in the distribution channel, wherein the government is involved at both wholesale and retail levels, as well as auctions, lottery systems for distribution to wholesalers, etc. Another aspect is the transportation of alcoholic beverages between the states within India, which is also subject to the imposition of various charges, including export fee taxes, import fees and transportation charges. Thus, the sale or distribution of alcoholic beverages in both the wholesale and retail sectors in India was, in some form or manner, regulated by the State Governments. [F] Role of Efficiencies as a Defence The CCI gives a lot of emphasis on efficiencies in the market, which can result from the combination. As such, the CCI has analysed economic efficiencies resulting from a combination which may be consumer and/or administrative efficiencies.179 Jet/Etihad case. For instance, in the Jet/Etihad case, one of the reasons adopted by the CCI while approving the deal was the potential efficiencies that would have resulted from the combination. The CCI undertook a holistic analysis of the sector and identified the cost and administrative efficiencies while approving the combination. The relevant portions of the order of the CCI are summarized as follows:(i) Airline alliances create substantial opportunities for generating economic benefits, many of which are dependent at least in part on the closer integration achievable. These benefits can be viewed as demand-side –relating to the creation of new or improved services through expanded networks or seamless service, or supply-side – essentially the ability to produce the same services at lower cost, taking advantage of traffic densities, improved utilization of capacity and lower transaction costs. (ii) In the aviation industry, airlines would benefit by integrating complementary networks, which in turn would result in lower fares for passengers. Further, the possibility of coordinating pricing, fares and inventory/yield management will eliminate inherent inefficiencies in pricing and enable the members to offer more attractive fares to customers. (iii) One of the most fundamental potential benefits from closer cooperation and integration arises from economies of traffic density. This type of economy of scale is a key feature of airline network models. Airline alliances extend the Hub and Spoke (H&S) network with a large presence at both ends of the market. Feeder routes and services delivering connecting traffic can increase the traffic density on a city-pair, allowing airlines to operate larger, more efficient aircraft and to spread end-point fixed costs over a larger number of passengers. (iv) The proposed transaction would generate significant synergies for both airlines in terms of network efficiencies and cost savings. Additionally, the parties to the transaction plan to introduce substantial capacity into the Indian market. Both of these factors could and generally do create downward pressure on fares. (v) Airline alliance has an increased incentive to harmonize and improve customer service standards and quality experience throughout the processes of ticketing, seat selection, airport lounges, gate location for connecting services, onboard amenities and service quality, baggage policies and problem resolution, frequent flyer plans and refunds and exchanges. As these aspects are integrated and jointly managed, the customer receives a correspondingly simplified and consistent service. This aspect of cooperation is likely to provide consumer benefit without anti-competitive results due to the intense, global competition between alliances for customer loyalty. (vi) Another potential benefit was the proposed equity infusion and its implication for the Indian aviation sector. Herein, Jet was under a huge financial debt and reported substantial negative equity. Thus, Etihad’s equity infusion would be beneficial to Jet as it would strengthen its operational viability and allow it to continue to compete effectively in the relevant markets in India and internationally.

§4.17 COMMITMENTS Merger analysis is essentially a forward-looking exercise whereby the competition regulators can either unconditionally clear the transaction, prohibit the transaction, or approve the transaction with commitments. It requires considerable analytical skill on the part of the competition authorities to impose commitments. Remedies are employed by competition agencies to resolve and prevent any consequential harm to the competitive process that may result from combinations. As such, the remedies play an essential role in the merger review process, and their careful crafting is of utmost importance to competition agencies conducting the review. While imposing remedies/commitments, competition regulators must keep in mind the following:(i) Remedies should only be applied if there is a threat to competition. (ii) Remedies should be the least restrictive means to effectively eliminate the competition concerns posed by the combination. Merger remedies are generally classified as either structural, if they require the divestiture of an asset, or behavioural if they impose an obligation on the merged entity to engage in or refrain from a certain conduct. Structural remedies may include both the sale of a physical part of a business or the transfer or licensing of IPRs. They can be imposed either as a condition precedent to a combination, or their completion may be required within a certain period of time after the combination has been approved. Behavioural remedies, however, are always forward-looking in that they consist of limits on future business behaviour or an obligation to perform a specific prescribed conduct for a given, sometimes considerable, period of time following the consummation of the merger. The CCI has the power under the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations to issue commitments/modifications to the parties to the combination to ensure that combinations, once consummated, will not cause any AAEC in the relevant market in India. As discussed earlier, parties can also voluntarily offer commitments during the combination assessment, and if the competition concerns are not allayed, CCI may propose appropriate modifications to the combination. The parties can accept the modifications proposed by the CCI or respond with amendments, which will again be considered by the CCI. Once the parties to the combination accept the modifications suggested by the CCI, they have to carry out such modifications within the time period specified, failing which such combination will be deemed to have an AAEC in the relevant market in India and hence will be rejected by the CCI. CCI’s decisional practice indicates considerable experience with complex transactions which demonstrate effective engagement with the parties to the combination to address competition concerns. In this light, the structural and behavioural remedies proposed and approved by CCI have been discussed below. [A] Behavioural Commitments As mentioned above, the CCI can ask for commitments/modifications even in Phase I review. Regulation 19(2) of the Combination Regulations provides that the CCI may ask the parties to the combination to accept modifications for forming its prima facie order. The decisional practice shows that the CCI has sought behavioural commitments in the form of commitments in its merger approval orders,180 GSPC Distribution Networks Limited, Gujarat Gas Company Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/11/88, 8 Jan. 2013. where it asked the parties to review its existing contracts to ensure that they are in compliance with the provisions of the Competition Act and they will continue to abide by the provisions of the sectoral regulator. Further, the CCI has also sought commitments, during a Phase I review, from the parties to reduce their non-compete period mentioned in the transaction documents.181 Orchid Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals Limited, Hospira Healthcare India Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/09/79, 21 Dec. 2012. The behavioural commitments issued by CCI can be broadly clubbed under two heads, i.e., non-compete restrictions and other safeguards. The key trends that have emerged in the adoption of both these categories of behavioural remedies have been discussed below in detail. Non-compete restrictions: (i) As a part of a composite transaction, there may be numerous contractual arrangements (like provisions relating to non-compete, licensing, et al.) which restrict the freedom of the parties to the combination to compete in the relevant market, post the consummation of the combination. Such restrictions are required to ensure that the acquirer receives the full value of the acquired business, and the seller is restricted from entering/venturing into any competing business with the target entity after the consummation of the combination. (ii) The scope of non-compete clauses must be limited, both in time and geographically, and cannot extend to restrictions which are not ‘directly related’ to the implementation of the combination. They must be extremely precise in their product scope and ought to cover only those products in which the target entity and/or its promoters are presently active and not future products. Further, they should be only limited to the geographic operations of the target entity. For example, in the acquisition by PVR Limited of DLF Utilities Limited’s film exhibition business, the parties proposed to enter into a non-compete and non-solicitation agreement requiring the target to not engage in the business of operating and managing entertainment theatres/multiplexes for a period of 5 years from the consummation of the proposed combination.182 PVR Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/07/288, 4 May 2016, paras 103-105. It was submitted that the non-compete obligations were necessary to preserve the value of the assets being acquired and to safeguard the target business. However, the CCI found the geographic scope and duration of the non-compete obligations to be unrelated, unnecessary and, therefore, unreasonable to the proposed combination.
