Consequently, the acquirers committed to reducing the duration from five years to three years and geographic scope from India to Delhi-NCR and Chandigarh. (iii) The time period of non-compete restrictions cannot be of an indefinite duration. As a general rule, CCI will not allow a non-compete period that extends for a period of three years where goodwill and know-how are transferred and two years when only goodwill is transferred. Such non-compete restrictions can be for a longer duration if the transaction so permits.183 Orchid Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals Limited, Hospira Healthcare India Private Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2012/09/79, 21 Dec. 2012 – CCI allowed non-compete period up to four years. (iv) Until 2017, the CCI analysed competition compliance non-compete clauses within its preliminary review and required parties to undertake appropriate modifications. However, the decisional practice of CCI with respect to non-compete clauses has evolved through the removal of the mandatory query in Form I which sought detailed information on any non-compete obligations. The CCI has reasoned that a one-size-fits-all approach cannot be adopted to non-compete clauses, and now, if the non-compete is not directly related and necessary to the transaction, the CCI will approve the combination without approving the non-compete restriction separately. (v) However, parties have to remain cautious to ensure that their non-competes are reasonable and competition-compliant. The CCI has emphasized that any competition concerns that may arise from non-compete restrictions can be assessed under section 3 (prohibiting anti-competitive agreements) or section 4 (prohibiting abuses of dominant position) of the Competition Act. This means that while the CCI has reduced the burden on the parties to detail their non-compete restrictions while seeking prior approval of the transaction, the parties are not being exempted from indulging in anti-competitive activities. The amendment simply removes the onus on the parties to provide details of the non-compete in the notification form. All in all, this is a business-friendly step as it will give the parties flexibility in determining non-compete restrictions while also reducing the information burden on them. Other Safeguards: (i) CCI’s decisional practice indicates other forms of behavioural commitments apart from non-compete clauses. Prominent among these is information control, which is essentially to prevent transmission of sensitive information between the parties, who are usually competitors at some level in the relevant market. (ii) This can take several forms, such as an information firewall between Airtel and Jio (as discussed earlier)184 Google International LLC and Bharti Airtel Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/03/91, 30 Jun. 2022, para. 24. or a ‘rule of information control’ at the time of completion of combination (basically a prohibition on exchange of sensitive information between the combined entity and the independent businesses of the parties),185 Northern TK Venture Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/09/601, 29 Oct. 2018, para. 18. white labelling, provision of non-exclusive technology licences, price caps186 Schneider Electric India Pvt. Ltd. and MacRitchie Investments Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/07/586, 18 Apr. 2019. and capacity commitments.187 M/s Air India Limited, M/s Tata SIA Airlines Ltd., M/s Tata Sons Private Limited and M/s Singapore Airlines Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2023/04/1022, 1 Sep. 2023, para. 115. (iii) Other variations of information control include prohibition on the directors/officers and the employees of the combined entity from receiving any sensitive information regarding the Non-Integrated Businesses from the directors/officers or employees of the parties, provision of such information to third parties or internally among themselves. A violation of any of the above rules will constitute grounds for disciplinary action and punishment. Such disciplinary action could include, for instance, removal from service, denial of promotion, and demotion to a lower rank.188 Nippon Yusen Kabushiki Kaisha Ltd., Mitsui O.S.K. Lines Ltd. and Kawasaki Kisen Kaisha, Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2016/11/459, 29 Jun. 2017, para. 25. (iv) Other safeguards include the adoption of preventative measures in the commissioning or operation of the combined entity. For instance, the proposed combination for creating a joint venture between Mumbai International Airport Private Limited, IOCL, BPCL, and Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited in Mumbai Aviation Fuel Farm Facility Limited (MAFFFL) raised certain concerns about the suppliers of ATF. Consequently, the parties undertook voluntary commitments that MAFFFL shall publish the applicable tariff, maximum storage capacity used by suppliers, change in such capacity, rates approved by the regulatory authority, etc., on its website. Further, the parties also volunteered to, inter alia, incorporate a clause in the standard supplier agreement to the effect that each supplier shall comply with competition law principles in relation to the usage of the integrated fuel farm facility.189 Mumbai International Airport Private Limited, Indian Oil Corporation Limited, Bharat Petroleum Corporation Limited, Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited, and Mumbai Aviation Fuel Farm Facility Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/04/164, 29 Sep. 2014, paras 19-24. Interestingly, CCI’s decisional practice indicates that it is not averse to the idea of imposing behavioural remedies on a standalone basis, even in cases of combinations involving direct competitors. Cases in point are the merger of Air India Limited with Tata SIA Airlines Limited190 M/s Air India Limited, M/s Tata SIA Airlines Ltd., M/s Tata Sons Private Limited and M/s Singapore Airlines Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2023/04/1022, 1 Sep. 2023. and the acquisition of the entire electrical and automation business of Larsen & Toubro Limited by Schneider Electric India Private Limited.191 Schneider Electric India Pvt. Ltd. and MacRitchie Investments Pte. Ltd., Combination Registration No. C-2018/07/586, 18 Apr. 2019. The intention is to preserve competition by striking a balance between allowing a merger for potential efficiency gains while safeguarding consumers against the adverse effects of reduced competition. However, behavioural commitments are not always sufficient to assuage the likely AAEC in the market. Often, structural commitments in the form of divestments are the only viable option to prevent anti-competitive effects on the market. Based on the seriousness of the competition concerns, the CCI may also order hybrid remedies incorporating both structural and behavioural commitments. [B] Structural Commitments CCI has amassed a wealth of experience in dealing with cases requiring structural commitments, and in its decisional practice, it has proposed and accepted parties’ voluntary proposals for divestments. In AGI Greenpac Limited/Hindusthan National Glass & Industries Limited,192 AGI Greenpac Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/11/983, 15 Mar. 2023, paras 94-101. both the parties were involved in the manufacture and supply of container glass, and the CCI found that the proposed combination was likely to significantly increase the level of concentration in the relevant market for container glass in India. Consequently, the parties proposed a voluntary modification in the form of divestiture of one of the plants of the target, i.e., the Rishikesh plant. The CCI laid down a two-step test to be followed in examining divestiture as a proposed modification. First, the adequacy of the divestiture to address the adverse impacts of structural changes in the market resulting from the combination has to be seen. Second, the suitability of the specific assets required to be divested has to be considered. In the present case, the Rishikesh plant was involved in all container glass segments that had self-contained assets such as glass melting furnaces, and divestiture appeared to incentivize new entry or augment the capacity of an existing competitor. A new entrant or competitor would gain an overall presence of 5% in the container glass market while gaining a presence in all segments of container glass. Consequently, CCI approved the combination subject to compliance with the modification. CCI’s acceptance indicates the presence of the following trends or key elements in the mode, manner and conditions of implementation of divestitures:(i) Identifying the divestment business: the necessary components of the business, including, in some cases, key personnel.193 Bayer/Monsanto, para. 177; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 147-150, 154-157, 161-164; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015; China National decision, para. 18. This involves identifying what shall be divested or not divested and often includes tangible as well as intangible assets such as IPR, necessary licences, permits and authorizations issued by any governmental or statutory authority, contracts, leases, understandings, customer records, credit records and other records related to the business and personnel.194 Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190, 30 Mar. 2015; Bayer/Monsanto, para. 178; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015; China National decision, para. 23. (ii) Transitional Support: Transitional support as may be required by the approved purchaser, which is necessary to enable it to conduct the divestment business in at least the same manner as prior to the purchase, is also offered. This includes training and information delivered by appropriate specialists, supply arrangements, information technology and back end support, etc.195 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 180, 187; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015, para. 2; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 16; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 151, 154, 158, 165, 166; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, paras 34.2, 35.2; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 25; Outotec OYJ, paras 41-44. (iii) First Divestiture Period: The divestiture principally takes place in the first divestiture period, which is usually six months from the date of CCI’s approval of the combination.196 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 217-222; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.1; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 27; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 168-172. (iv) Preservation of economic viability, marketability and competitiveness: These measures are to be taken to ensure that the business to be divested is run as a viable business, and that the assets are not degraded, and the economic viability, marketability and competitiveness is maintained.197 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 223-229; China National decision, paras 25-29; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.3; Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190, 30 Mar. 2015; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 28; Outotec OYJ, paras 45-47; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 173-179. (v) Hold separate obligations: If the business to be divested is part of a broader business, the former is to be kept separate with the appointment of a hold separate manager. In the case of divestment of shares, the divesting shareholders are not to exercise voting rights or be involved in the business from the date of the CCI’s decision. Further, the Key Personnel, management and staff of the divestment businesses should have no involvement in any business retained by the Parties and should not report to any individual outside the divestment businesses.198 Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190, 30 Mar. 2015; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 230-233; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.4; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 29; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 180-184; Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 2017, para. 53; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 10. (vi) Ring-fencing and firewall provisions: The confidential information is not to be shared between the parties and/or their affiliates with respect to the divested business.199 Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.5; Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 2017, paras 56, 57; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) paras 14, 15; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 234, 235; China National decision, paras 30-32, 46-49; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 30; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 185, 186. (vii) Non-solicitation: The Parties shall not, and procure that their Affiliates do not employ, or make offers of employment to, any member of Key Personnel transferred with the divestment businesses for the period prescribed in CCI’s order.200 Bayer/Monsanto, para. 236; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.6; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 31. (viii) Due diligence: In order to enable potential purchasers to carry out reasonable due diligence of the divestment businesses, the parties shall, subject to customary confidentiality assurances and dependent on the stage of the divestiture process, provide to potential purchaser(s) sufficient information as regards the divestment businesses; and/or relating to the Key Personnel and Personnel.201 Bayer/Monsanto, para. 237; China National decision, paras 33, 34; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.7; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, paras 32, 33; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, para. 188; Outotec OYJ, para. 70; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 30; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015. (ix) Reporting: The parties must keep the monitoring agency informed of the process and potential purchasers.202 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 238, 239; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.8; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 189-191; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) paras 17-20. (x) No acquisition of influence: the parties are not to acquire direct or indirect influence over the divestment business for a specified period after closing.203 Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 2016, para. 58; China National decision, para. 35; Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.9; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 35; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 153, 160, 169; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 4. (xi) Purchaser requirements: Various purchaser requirements are set out to ensure that the purchaser will be an independent and viable player in the market, having no connection with the parties, such as that it should not be their subsidiary, affiliate or associate company, shall have the financial resources, proven expertise and incentive to maintain and develop the divestment business as a viable and active competitive force in competition with the Parties and other competitors.204 Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015; Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 182, 189; China National decision, para. 36; Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 207, para. 59; DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.10; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 152, 159; Outotec OYJ, para. 67. (xii) Approval of sale and purchase agreement and of the purchaser: The final binding sale and purchase agreement relating to the divestiture shall be conditional on the approval of the CCI.205 Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015, para. 3; Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190; Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 207, para. 62; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 36; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 192, 193; Outotec OYJ, paras 68, 69; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 8. Herein, it is pointed out that in all cases other than Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, the CCI has permitted the closing of the main transaction pending the closing of the divestment. In the former, the approval of sale and purchase agreement of the purchaser (Emcure) was done during the approval of the transaction itself. (xiii) Monitoring agency: The CCI generally appoints an independent monitoring agency to supervise the carrying out of the modification.206 Sun Pharmaceutical Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2014/05/170, 17 Mar. 2015, para. 4; Holcim, Lafarge, Combination Registration No. C-2014/07/190; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 242-245; China National decision, paras 52-55; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.12; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 37; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 194-196; Outotec OYJ, paras 48-50. (xiv) Divestiture Agency: If the Closing of the divestment businesses has not taken place as specified in the modifications to the proposed combination, then the CCI shall appoint a Divestiture Agency to divest the divestment businesses.207 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 246-251; China National decision, paras 38-41; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 38; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 197-202; Outotec OYJ, paras 51-59; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) paras 21-26. (xv) Second divestiture period: If the parties fail to divest in the first divestiture period, the CCI may direct the parties to divest alternative divestment products in a second divestiture period.208 Bayer/Monsanto, paras 252-255; China National decision, paras 42-44; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.13; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 39; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 203-206; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 6. (xvi) Duties and obligations of parties: The parties are generally required to cooperate with the Monitoring Agency and, if applicable, the Divestment Agency, provide regular reports on the progress of the divestiture, etc. The CCI may also request information from the parties that is reasonably necessary for the effective implementation of the order at any time. The Monitoring Agency and Divestiture Agency shall have full and complete access to any of the Divestment Businesses’ books, records, documents, management or other personnel, facilities, sites and technical information necessary for fulfilling its duties under the Order.209 Agrium Inc. and Potash Corporation of Saskatchewan, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/443, 27 Oct. 207, paras 63-68.; Bayer/Monsanto, paras 256-261; China National decision, paras 53-55; Dow Chemical Company, E. I. du Pont De Nemours and Company, DowDuPont Inc., Diamond Merger Sub Inc. and Orion Merger Sub Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/05/400, 8 Jun. 2017, para. 35.15; FMC Corporation, Dow Chemical Company, E.I. du Pont de Nemours and Company Combination Registration No. C-2017/06/519, 18 Sep. 2017, para. 40; Linde Aktiengesellschaft and Praxair, Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2018/01/545, 6 Sep. 2018, paras 207-210; ZF Friedrichshafen AG, Combination Registration No. C-2019/11/703, 14 Feb. 2020, Annex A(II) para. 31; Outotec OYJ, paras 60-66. Therefore, CCI’s remedy packages in divestitures seek to strike a balanced approach between the need to maintain competition in the market and the independence of the parties to the proposed combination.

§4.18 PENALTY FOR MAKING FALSE STATEMENT OR OMISSION TO FURNISH MATERIAL INFORMATION In its decisional practice, the CCI has penalized parties for not furnishing material information or making false statements at the time of filing the combination notice under sections 44 and 45 of the Competition Act. Although CCI has so far not reversed its approval on this issue, it has penalized the parties for such conduct. For instance, in the UltraTech case,210 Section 44 Order against UltraTech Cement Limited, 12 Mar. 2018, paras 4-12.1. while filing the notice, UltraTech had reported that it is a subsidiary of Grasim Industries Limited which in turn was a flagship company of Aditya Birla Group. However, it did not provide any details in respect of the shareholding/control of the Kumar Mangalam Family Group. The role of the family in determining what constitutes a group for the purpose of competition assessment of combinations has already been discussed in the earlier parts of this chapter. Consequently, UltraTech was penalized under section 44 of the Competition Act. Noticeably, proceedings were initiated against Amazon for contravening, inter alia, section 44 of the Competition Act for misrepresentation, false statement or suppression/concealment of material facts in relation to the scope and purpose of its combination involving FCPL and its failure to identify and notify a relevant commercial agreement, which was an inter-connected part of the combination (as discussed earlier in the section on inter-connected transactions). After granting approval to the transaction, the CCI received intimation that Amazon had suppressed material information about the combination. Upon examination, the CCI found that the purpose of the combination was for investment in FRL and establishing a strategic alignment/partnership between Amazon and Future groups in the Indian retail sector, contrary to what it had submitted in the notice, wherein it had mentioned that the objective of the combination was the gift card business of FCPL and Amazon’s interest to expand its portfolio in the payments landscape in India.211 Sections 43A, 44 and 45 Order against Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC, 14 Dec. 2021, paras 37-44. Further, Amazon had specifically claimed at the time of approval of the combination that it did not have any direct or indirect shareholding in FRL, and certain rights have been granted to it only with respect to FCPL’s investment in FRL, which was merely shown as a factor of financial strength. However, the internal correspondence of Amazon revealed a different picture: that it was all along solely interested in FRL. The CCI held that the object and purpose of strategic rights in a combination is crucial to establishing a proper understanding of the combination and its objective and, accordingly, deciding the appropriate line of inquiry to assess the effects of the combination on competition. In this case, the purpose of securing strategic interest over FRL and commercial partnership with FRL was entirely different from FRL being merely taken as an element of financial strength and protection to the investment in FCPL. Consequently, it was found that Amazon’s objective in pursuing the combination was not the potential of the gift and loyalty card business of FCPL, but FCPL was envisaged only as a vehicle to get to FRL. Hence, the present case was held to be a clear, conscious and wilful case of omission to state the actual purpose of the combination in violation of the disclosure requirements mentioned in the Combination Regulations. Therefore, not only did Amazon fail to notify all the inter-connected steps of the transaction (as also discussed earlier), but it also failed to give true and complete disclosure with respect to the substance of its combination in violation of sections 44 and 45 of the Competition Act. Subsequently, after a detailed analysis of the factual background and applicable law, the NCLAT stated its ‘complete agreement’ with the CCI’s views that Amazon had intentionally not made known the ‘real ambit and purpose’ of the combination.212 Amazon.com NV Investment Holdings LLC v. Competition Commission of India and Others [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2022. Hence, if a party conceals/suppresses and/or misrepresents the scope and purpose of the combination to obtain CCI’s approval, the same would effectively amount to approval/consent having been obtained by way of fraud. Such a breach of trust of the CCI, established under the Competition Act for the benevolent purpose of promoting and sustaining competition in markets in India, manifests a deliberate disregard for the trust-based regulatory mechanism. The competition assessment undertaken and the consequent decision of the CCI is primarily based on the information provided in the notice. Once the CCI has passed an order, it has no control over the subsequent consummation or non-consummation of a transaction and may only be left with the option of revoking the decision in the event it comes to know that the information was either incomplete or incorrect. Thus, parties to a combination are required to provide complete and correct information on all the aspects asked for in the designated form for filing of notice.

§4.19 MISMATCH OF TIMELINE AND REMEDIES: THE CLASH BETWEEN TWIN REGULATORS Investments made in a listed company have to adhere to the timelines and procedural compliances mentioned under the Takeover Code and Combination Regulations. For illustration, the merging companies may face a potential mismatch between the timelines for an open offer prescribed under the Takeover Code and the review of a merger notification by the CCI since both regulatory procedures need to be undertaken simultaneously. As discussed in the earlier portions of the chapter, the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations provide for a merger review by the CCI in two phases: (i) CCI to form a prima facie view in thirty days (excluding the clock stops) from the receipt of merger notification & (ii) in case the CCI on a prima facie view is of the opinion that there may be certain anti-competitive effects that can arise because of the combination, it directs an in-depth investigation of the combination, commonly known as Phase II review. Phase II review can be for a time frame of up to 180 days (excluding the clock stops). As mentioned above, the Competition Act and the Combination Regulations provide for a suspensory regime, and the combination cannot be effectuated until the CCI approves the merger notification. However, under the Takeover Code, the acquirer is required to pay the shareholders who have tendered shares within fifteen days from the closure of the open offer process. Post the fifteen days, the acquirer is imposed with the penalty of paying interest until such payment is made. Since the Takeover Code procedure is not suspended while review of the CCI is ongoing, parties cannot claim exemption from paying interest under the Takeover Code on account of a parallel pending regulatory approval. Similar to the timeline mismatch, the CCI and SEBI may collide on account of a mismatch of remedies. As discussed above, the CCI may impose certain commitments (behavioural or structural) to eliminate the anti-competitive effects of the merger. However, under the Takeover Code (which precedes the scrutiny of the CCI), an acquirer must declare its intention to alienate any material assets of the company in the open offer documents. Therefore, in the event that CCI later imposes structural commitments on the parties which includes divestments, an acquirer may have to obtain a special resolution of its shareholders in order to comply with the requirements imposed by the CCI.

§4.20 CONCLUSION Since merger assessment is an ex ante analysis, it hinges on fair and accurate disclosures pursuant to the trust-based mechanism envisaged in the Competition Act, Combination Regulations and CCI’s decisional practice. Thus, adequate and sufficient self-assessment and cooperation by the parties at every step of the process, right from filing of notice to compliance with modifications to the proposed combination, if any, is necessary for CCI to continue with its streak of not refusing approval to any combination. As long as parties comply, CCI is likely to continue with its pro-approval and business-friendly stance.

title: Chapter 5: Intellectual Property and Competition Law
container_title: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition)

sections.title: FRIENDS IN DISAGREEMENT
sections.text: §5.01 FRIENDS IN DISAGREEMENT The interface between competition law and IPR is an ever- evolving field due to rapid technological advancements in the global economy. While the objective of competition law is to promote fair competition and prevent anti-competitive practices, IPR aims to encourage and safeguard innovation through grant of exclusive rights in the form of patents, copyrights, trademarks, etc. Although the issue of jurisdiction of CCI in matters concerning IPR has been largely resolved in recent years, with competition concerns having been held to fall squarely within the domain of India’s antitrust regulator, new jurisdictional challenges continue to emerge regarding anti-competitive conduct that may arise from the exercise of IPR. One of the primary concerns in this interface is the potential abuse of IPR by competitors to create or maintain monopolistic positions in the market. There is an apparent antagonism between the IPR law regime and the competition law regime, considering that the essential attribute of IPR is the grant of exclusion, where the state grants a monopoly to the IPR holder, whereas competition law aims at attaining maximum production and allocation of resources, thereby achieving allocative efficiency. However, it has been noticed that IPR and competition law regime work in tandem and perform complementary roles. Together, they contribute towards spurring innovation. Therefore, the legal regimes of competition law and IPR are often termed as ‘friends in disagreement’. Given that the regime performs a complementary role, the competition authorities must be cautious that the regulatory interventions by them are timely and take place only in cases of abuse of rights guaranteed to the IPR holder, which potentially distorts the competitive structure of the market. Hence, there is a delicate balance to be struck between incentivizing and promoting innovation on the one hand and ensuring that the exclusive rights granted in the form of IPR do not harm competition or restrict consumer choice in any manner on the other hand.

sections.title: JURISDICTION OF THE CCI OVER IPR ISSUES
sections.text: §5.02 JURISDICTION OF THE CCI OVER IPR ISSUES The issue of jurisdiction of the competition law regulator to hear disputes that sit on the cusp of the interface between competition law and IPR can be traced back to the predecessor regime which existed i.e. Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practice ( MRTP) Commission. [A] MRTP Commission In Dr. Vallal Peruman and Anr. v. Godfrey Phillips (India) Ltd., 1 UTP Enquiry No. 180/92; The MRTP Commission in Manju Bhardwaj v. Zee Telefilms Ltd. and Ors.; UTPE No. 148/1985, reiterated the findings of Dr. Vallal Peruman and Anr. v. Godfrey Phillips (India) Ltd. to hold that if there is misuse of a trademark by manipulation, distortion, contrivances, and embellishments, then the MRTP Commission has jurisdiction to proceed under MRTP Act. a complaint was filed by a consumer that Godfrey Phillips (India) Ltd. was manufacturing, marketing and selling cigarettes under the brand name Marcovitch Red & White, a reputed foreign company, even though the cigarettes were neither produced in collaboration with or with the knowledge of the foreign company which owned the said mark and brand name. It was alleged that this constituted an unfair trade practice because the complainants were misled by the mark and brand name being used by Godfrey Phillips. At the outset, Godfrey Phillips had argued that the authorities under the Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958 (‘TMM Act’) have exclusive jurisdiction in regard to the complaints pertaining to the use of trademark by the holder of the certificate of registration. Resultantly, the MRTP Commission observed that it is true that there is a potential conflict between IPR and antitrust law because the exercise of the monopoly power conferred upon the holder of IPR may produce anticompetitive effects. However, there are powerful public policy justifications for maintaining these rights as well as a basis to suggest that this potential conflict may not be as severe as it appears at first blush. Reliance was placed on the solution worked out by the ECJ, which has adopted a practical and balanced approach by distinguishing between the mere ‘existence’ of IPR rights and how they are actually ‘exercised’. The same principle was extended to resolve the conflict between the TMM Act and the MRTP Act: a certificate of registration entitles an individual/undertaking to use a trademark so long as such certificate of registration is in operation, and the trade mark is used strictly in conformity with the terms and conditions subject to which it was granted. However, if the holder of the certificate misuses the trademark by engaging in manipulation, distortion, contrivances and embellishments, etc., to mislead or confuse the consumers, then action can be taken against them under section 36A(7) for indulging in unfair trade practices under the MRTP Act. Thus, the provisions of the MRTP Act would be attracted only where there is an abuse in the exercise of the rights protected and granted under the TMM Act. [B] Challenges to CCI’s Jurisdiction On similar lines, the jurisdiction of the CCI in adjudicating market distortions from abuse of IPRs has been challenged before various High Courts in India. While a majority of the decisions have noted that the CCI has the appropriate jurisdiction to look at the misuse of IPRs under the lens of competition law, a very recent decision of the Delhi High Court has said that the CCI must not exercise jurisdiction due to a similar provision which exists in Patent laws. This has been discussed subsequently in this chapter. The challenge has been on the grounds that the CCI does not have the requisite jurisdiction to decide on competition issues which have an interface with IPR. [1] Competition Act Versus Copyright Act One of the first challenges to the jurisdiction of the CCI was before the Bombay High Court in the case of Aamir Khan Production Private Limited v. Competition Commission of India. 2 Aamir Khan Production Private Limited v. Competition Commission of India [2010], (Bombay High Court) 2011 (1) BomCR 802. The case involved certain film production companies who organized themselves under the umbrella of a trade association and took a collective decision not to release films to multiplexes from 4 April 2009 with the objective of extracting a higher revenue sharing ratio from the multiplex. On receipt of information from the multiplex association, CCI took cognizance of the matter and referred the matter for detailed investigation to the DG. Post a detailed investigation by the DG, the CCI issued a show cause notice to the film production companies. Thereafter, Aamir Khan Production Private Limited approached the Bombay High Court challenging the jurisdiction of the CCI, raising the following arguments:(i) The principal point of contention was that the exhibition of a feature film, which is a subject matter of copyright exploitation alone, is specifically excluded under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. (ii) The Indian copyright law enjoins upon the copyright holder the exclusive right to do or authorize the doing or to authorize to sell or give on hire or offer for sale or hire any copy of the film. (iii) The producer of the film has the exclusive right to decide as to whom it shall sell or give on hire any copy of the film for communicating the film to the public. (iv) Section 18 of the Copyright Act, 1957 confers upon the owner of the copyright the right to assign to any person a copyright either wholly or partially. Further, section 30 of the Copyright Act recognizes the right of the owner of the copyright to grant any interest in the right by license in writing. Based on the said legal provision, it was argued that when a producer makes a cinematograph film, he has an exclusive right to sell or give on hire any copy of the film, and the show cause notice by the CCI must be quashed on these grounds. However, based on the reading of the provision of the Competition Act, the Bombay High Court rejected the petition made by Aamir Khan Productions Private Limited. The language of the provisions of the Competition Act clearly indicates that the CCI has the jurisdiction to determine whether the preliminary state of facts (on which the further exercise of its jurisdiction depends) exists. Further, the question of CCI’s jurisdiction is itself a mixed question of law and fact which the CCI is competent to decide. The Bombay High Court held that the CCI has the power to determine whether it has the jurisdiction to entertain a matter and analyse whether the conduct of the parties can avail of the exemption made under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. The contour of the IPR exemption made under section 3(5) of the Competition Act is dealt with in detail later in this chapter. The issue of the jurisdiction of CCI into IPR-related matters was further discussed before the CCI in the case of Ms HT Media v. M/s Super Cassettes Industries Limited.3 Ms HT Media v. M/s Super Cassettes Industries Limited, Case No. 40 of 2011. The case concerned Super Cassettes, the largest private publisher of Indian music and allegations that it was abusing its dominant position in contravention of the provisions of section 4 of the Competition Act. The allegations against Super Cassettes involved the following:(i) charging excessive amounts as licence fees/royalty from the radio operators for grant of rights for the broadcast of its music content; and (ii) imposing minimum commitment charges to be paid by the radio operators per month irrespective of actual needle hour (each aggregate of sixty minutes of the actual broadcast of sound recordings by FM radio station excluding commercials, advertisements, voice-over, anchor time, etc.) of broadcast of its music content by the radio operators; (iii) making conclusion of licensing arrangements with radio operators subject to the acceptance of licence fees and minimum commitment charge imposed by Super Cassettes. It was further alleged that such imposition of exorbitant licence fees and minimum commitment charge by Super Cassettes is an unfair condition imposed by it for granting licence to broadcast its music content on radio under the Competition Act. The licence agreement permitted the licensee to broadcast music subject to acceptance of onerous conditions such as minimum commitment charge obligations, which has the effect of restricting around 30%-40% of the radio stations’ broadcast to the music content of Super Cassettes. The said action of Super Cassettes limited the choice of music for the end consumers to only the music content of Super Cassettes. Further, such an action of Super Cassettes resulted in denial of market access for other music companies (publishers, copyright societies, etc.) with less market share and bargaining power. Based on the above facts, the CCI initiated an investigation into the conduct of Super Cassettes. Super Cassettes challenged the jurisdiction of the CCI on the ground that the matter under dispute between Super Cassettes and radio operators vests with the Copyright Board. It was contended by Super Cassettes that the Copyright Board is the only authority to decide whether the terms (not just rates) of a licence between a copyright owner and a radio broadcaster are reasonable and set new terms if existing terms are unreasonable. Further, it was argued by Super Cassettes that in the event the market is heavily regulated by the Copyright Board, and where the market mechanism is substituted by a regulatory body, the role of competition law is greatly diminished and, in this case, completely ousted. The CCI did not find merit in the arguments of Super Cassettes and stated that the regulatory domain of the Copyright Board and the CCI are completely different. It was observed by CCI that it is a market regulator which has the mandate of eliminating practices which have an adverse effect on competition in India. The CCI also observed that the ambit of the powers of the Copyright Board and the CCI is different, and they continued with the investigation of the conduct of Super Cassettes under the provisions of the Competition Act. Super Cassettes thereafter preferred to challenge the jurisdiction of the CCI before the Delhi High Court. The Delhi High Court did not grant a stay on the proceedings, and CCI continued the investigation on Super Cassettes and found them guilty of abuse of dominant position. The pricing abuse of Super Cassettes relating to IPRs will be discussed subsequently in this chapter. On the aspect of jurisdiction, the CCI observed4 Ibid., paras 127, 128. that while the Copyright Board does have the right and obligation to determine licence fee and the reasonableness thereof under the Copyright Act, apart from that, none of the other issues envisaged by section 3 of the Competition Act can be decided by the Copyright Board. Similarly, under section 4 of the Competition Act, the CCI is obligated to adjudicate and give findings on alleged abuse of dominance, inter alia, on account of one-sided, discriminatory or unfair terms of the agreement. However, the Copyright Board does not have such jurisdiction. Further, the rights protected under the Copyright Act are also protected under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. In the present case, the unreasonableness of the licence fee was not the sole reason for referring the matter for investigation. Rather, it was the fact that radio stations had no choice but to accede to the arbitrary and unfair conditions imposed by T-Series by virtue of its dominant position. Since T-Series was the only music company imposing a minimum commitment charge upon the music companies, this prima facie amounted to an abuse of dominance, meriting an investigation by the DG. Thus, the CCI exercised its jurisdiction within the four corners of the Competition Act alone and did not encroach upon the areas where the Copyright Board had sole and exclusive jurisdiction, i.e., the determination of the licence fee and reasonableness of the same. It must be noted that in relation to broadcasting sound recordings, section 31 of the Copyright Act enables a person to seek a compulsory licence. The remedy of compulsory licence applies only when the copyright holder ‘refuses’ to license that work. As such, entering into a licence agreement subject to acceptance of certain anti-competitive conditions by the licensee may be a subject matter of the Competition Act and, at the same time, could potentially be considered a case of constructive ‘refusal’ for the purposes of enabling a section 31 proceeding under the Copyright Act. Further, the CCI can also pass any order as it may deem fit, including compulsory licensing if required, to remedy an anti-competitive situation. This issue will equally spill over even to the patent domain since the Patents Act, 1970, contains provisions relating to compulsory licence. Under the Patents Act, a compulsory licence could be sought by a competitor only after ‘refusal’ by the patentee to provide a voluntary licence and when other circumstances are established. Current jurisprudence indicates that before seeking a compulsory licence to a patent, the parties must engage in licence negotiations in ‘good faith’ and therefore, perhaps, refusal to license the patent but for accepting certain anti-competitive conditions may also justify seeking a compulsory licence. It is quite possible that in certain cases, this remedy under the Patents Act and the remedy under the Competition Act may overlap. Most recently, the Division Bench judgment5 Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (Delhi High Court) W.P.(C) 8379/2015 & LPA 247/2016, 150/2020, 550/2016 & 246/2016. in the Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) v. Competition Commission of India 6 Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (Delhi High Court) W.P.(C) 464/2014 & CM Nos 911/2014 & 915/2014. (‘Ericsson case’) has given primacy to the Patents Act over the Competition Act. This has been discussed in more detail in the next section. [2] Competition Act Versus Patents Act The leading case on the overlaps between the Patents Act and the Competition Act is the Ericsson case. In this case, Ericsson challenged the section 26(1) orders of the CCI to investigate an abuse of dominance by Ericsson with regards to its SEPs, pursuant to the information filed by Micromax and Intex. The facts of these cases have been discussed in detail in the sub-chapter on SEPs. However, the Ericsson case assumes importance here because the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court made notable observations on the jurisdiction of CCI to hear anti-competitive related matters which had an interface with patent-related matters. Ericsson challenged the maintainability of the orders passed by the CCI before the Delhi High Court, arguing that the CCI lacks the jurisdiction to commence any proceeding in relation to a claim of royalty by a proprietor of a patent (i.e., a ‘patentee’) since the same would fall within the scope of the Patents Act. The CCI in its section 26(1) order expressly held that the pendency of a civil suit in the High Court does not take away the jurisdiction of the CCI to proceed under the Competition Act.7 Micromax Informatics Limited v. Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ), Case No. 50 of 2013; Intex Technologies (India) Limited v. Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ), Case No. 76 of 2013. However, Ericsson argued before the Delhi High Court that the Patents Act, being a special Act, provided not only the patentee’s rights but also a framework for the exercise and remedy for the abuse of such rights. Consequently, a recourse to the Competition Act would be barred. The Single Judge of the Delhi High Court disagreed and concluded that the same facts may give rise to an action under the Competition Act as well as the Patents Act and that the remedies and legislative intention behind both statutes are distinct and independent of each other. As a result, an agreement which imposes reasonable conditions for protecting patent rights (which concerns specific individual rights) is allowed however, assessing the ‘reasonability’ of such conditions is the responsibility of the CCI (since it concerns the market at large). The key observations of the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court have been mentioned below:(i) Ericsson had argued that it was not an ‘enterprise’ under section 2(h) of the Competition Act as it was a patentee, and hence, section 4 of the Competition Act would not be applicable to it. In this regard, the CCI observed that the nature of patent rights – the right to exclude without the right to use – does not in any manner exclude patent rights from the scope of ‘goods’ as defined under the Sale of Goods Act, 1930. All kinds of property (other than actionable claims, money and immovable property) would fall within the definition of ‘goods’, and this would also include intangible and incorporeal property such as patents. Hence, Ericsson’s contention that patents are not ‘goods’ was dismissed, and it was held to be an ‘enterprise’ under section 2(h) of the Competition Act. (ii) Ericsson contended that the CCI would not have the jurisdiction to entertain complaints regarding abuse of dominance in view of the specific provisions under the Patents Act enacted to address the issue of abuse of dominance by a patent holder. The intention of the Parliament in enacting the Competition Act was not to curtail or whittle down the full scope of any other law, and, therefore, it is expressly stated that the Competition Act would be in addition to, and not in derogation of any other Act. (iii) Section 60 of the Competition Act expressly provides that the provisions of the Competition Act shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent in any other law. However, the said provision must be read in the context of the Competition Act as a whole and the mischief that is sought to be addressed by the Competition Act. (iv) The same facts can give rise to an action under both statutes. For instance, in certain cases, threats by a proprietor of a SEP, who is found to be in a dominant position, could be held to be an abuse of dominance. Similarly, certain specific remedies have been provided under the Patents Act in respect of certain practices that may constitute abuse of dominance or may be considered anti-competitive such as grant of compulsory licences under section 84(7) of the Patents Act. Thus, whereas patent laws are concerned with grants of rights enabling the patent holder to exclude others from exploiting the invention, and in that sense promoting rights akin to a monopoly, competition law is essentially aimed to promote competition and, thus, fundamentally opposed to monopolization as well as unfair and anticompetitive practices that are associated with monopolies. The remedies provided under section 27 of the Competition Act for abuse of dominant position are materially different from the remedies available under section 84 of the Patents Act. Hence, the remedies under the two enactments are not mutually exclusive; grant of one is not destructive of the other. Thus, it may be open for a prospective licensee to approach the Controller of Patents for grant of compulsory licence in certain cases. The same is not inconsistent with the CCI passing an appropriate order under section 27 of the Competition Act. It is also necessary to bear in mind that whereas section 84 of the Patents Act provides specific remedy to the person seeking relief, the orders passed by CCI are in rem. Hence, the operative width of the two enactments is different. (v) The language of the provisions also indicates the legislative intention that the Competition Act and the Patents Act should work harmoniously. The legislature while enacting a law has complete knowledge of the existing laws and, therefore, where an express provision for the repeal of an earlier statute is not provided unless it is concluded that the provisions of the two legislations cannot co-exist, the question of reading the provisions of a statute to have been impliedly repealed does not arise. (vi) The proviso to section 84(6)(iv) of the Patents Act provides that a prospective licensee who applies for a compulsory licence is expected to have made, prior to his application, efforts to obtain a licence on reasonable terms. However, it further specifies that this consideration would not be relevant where the conduct of a patentee is found to be anti-competitive. This cannot be read to mean that a patentee’s anti-competitive conduct would have to be first established in proceedings under the Competition Act before the Controller could take that into account. Rather, it only lists certain factors required to be considered by the Controller. However, if CCI has finally found a patentee’s conduct to be anti-competitive and its finding has attained finality, the Controller would also proceed on the said basis and – on the principle akin to issue estoppel – the patentee would be estopped from contending to the contrary. (vii) Section 3(5) of the Competition Act expressly provides that section 3 would not restrict the right of any person to impose reasonable conditions for protecting its right, inter alia, under the Patents Act. Hence, whilst an agreement which imposes reasonable conditions for protecting Patent Rights is permissible, an anti-competitive agreement which imposes unreasonable conditions would not be afforded the safe harbour of section 3(5) of the Competition Act and would fall foul of section 3 of the Competition Act. The question as to whether a condition imposed under the agreement is reasonable or not would be a matter which could only be decided by the CCI under the provisions of the Competition Act. Neither the Controller of Patents discharging his functions in terms of the Patents Act nor a Civil Court would have any jurisdiction to adjudicate whether an agreement falls foul of section 3 of the Competition Act. Thus, there is no irreconcilable repugnancy or conflict between the Competition Act and the Patents Act. And, in the absence of any irreconcilable conflict between the two legislations, the jurisdiction of CCI to entertain complaints for abuse of dominance in respect of Patent rights cannot be ousted. Hence, the possible overlaps between the competition and patents domain are not contrary to each other and the regulatory authorities for both have separate and distinct mandates to follow. However, in appeal, the Division Bench of the Delhi High Court in the Ericsson case8 Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (Delhi High Court) W.P.(C) 8379/2015 & LPA 247/2016, 150/2020, 550/2016 & 246/2016. has reversed the findings of the Singh Judge Bench. This has been discussed subsequently. In Monsanto Holdings Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India 9 Monsanto Holdings Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India, [2020] (Delhi High Court) W.P.(C) 1776/2016 and CM Nos 7606/2016, 12396/2016 & 16685/2016. (‘Monsanto case’), Mahyco Monsanto Biotech India Pvt. Ltd. (MMBL) challenged the maintainability of the order passed by CCI to investigate the trait fee charged by MMBL and the other terms and conditions imposed by it through its sub-licence agreement for using the technology for manufacturing Bt. Cotton Seeds. The facts of the case were as follows: MMBL sub-licensed the technology licensed by Monsanto to various seed manufacturers in India, including the informants (i.e., the sub-licensees) through agreements which were extendable from time to time. The consideration for sub-licensing the said technology was divided into two parts. The first was a non-refundable fee, which was required to be paid upfront (which was as high as INR 50 lakhs), and the second part was a recurring fee, which was referred to as ‘trait value’ and was determined on the basis of the Maximum Retail Price (MRP) fixed for Bt. Cotton Seeds. The sub-licensees requested MMBL to charge a reasonable trait value considering the increase in the cost of production and fixation of lower MRP of cotton seeds by the State Governments. However, MMBL refused to do so and instead, terminated the licences and also initiated claims for its payments against the sub-licensees. Consequently, the informants alleged that:(i) Monsanto and MMBL abused their dominant position by charging excessive and unfair prices for Bt. Technology. They set prices which are significantly higher than those that would result if there was effective competition in the relevant market. Further, it was alleged that linking the trait value to the MRP of cotton seeds is unreasonable and not reflective of the economic value of the said technology. (ii) MMBL had imposed unfair conditions in the sub-licence agreements and the same violated section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act. In terms of Clause 2.05(c) of the sub-licence agreements, the informants were required to notify MMBL in case their affiliates entered into negotiations with any competitor of MMBL/intended to deal with any competitor of MMBL within thirty days of commencement of such negotiations and failure to give such prior notice entitled MMBL to terminate the sub-licence agreement. This clause was alleged to be unfair as commercial negotiations are confidential, and any disclosure of such negotiations would have an adverse effect on the informants. Further, there was a reasonable apprehension that dealing with any competitor of MMBL would invite an adverse action against the informants, such as through selective licensing and refusal to sub-license Bt. Technology. (iii) Article 9.4 of the sub-licence agreements also imposed an unfair condition as, in terms of the said clause, the informants were required to destroy all parent lines or cotton germplasm, which had been modified to contain Monsanto Technology after the sub-licence was terminated. It was argued that destroying germplasm (parent line) would effectively amount to destroying their intellectual property, which normally takes about five to ten years to develop. It was alleged that through these restrictive and unfair conditions, MMBL had virtually eliminated all potential competition for its technology. (iv) MMBL and Monsanto Group have violated section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act as they discriminated against the informants. It was alleged that whereas Bt. Technology was provided to other group companies of Monsanto without entering into any sub-licence agreements containing unfair conditions, MMBL insisted on entering into unfair sub-licence agreements with other seed manufacturers. This had an appreciable adverse effect on the competition (AAEC) in the downstream market of cotton hybrid seeds. (v) It was also alleged that the sub-licence arrangements contravened section 3(1) and section 3(4) of the Competition Act by causing refusal to deal. The CCI held that MMBL was dominant in the relevant market of ‘provision of Bt. Cotton Technology in India’ as well as the downstream market of ‘manufacture and sale of Bt. Cotton seeds in India’. It further found the allegations made by the informants to be prima facie merited since the stringent conditions imposed in the sub-licence agreements discouraged the seed companies from dealing with competitors and amounted to restricting the development of alternate technologies. Further, these conditions were harsh and not reasonable for protecting IPR. Accordingly, the CCI passed an order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act directing the DG to investigate the matter. MMBL sought to oust the jurisdiction of CCI by citing repugnancy between the Competition Act and the Patents Act through a Writ Petition in the Delhi High Court. MMBL argued that:(i) In order for the CCI to determine whether its conduct is anti-competitive, it would be necessary to have a finding on whether the royalty fee/trait value charged is unreasonable and excessive and the terms of the sub-licence agreement are unreasonable, and these latter issues had to be determined by the Controller of Patents under the Patents Act. (ii) The Patents Act occupied the entire field in respect of not only the grant of patents but also the exercise of rights granted to a patentee. (iii) The powers of the Controller of Patents are akin to those of the TRAI in the telecom sector, and in Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel Ltd. (‘Bharti Airtel case’),10 Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel and Ors. [2018], (SC) Civil Appeal Nos 11843-11852 of 2018. the Supreme Court held that the regulation of Points of Interconnection (POIs) fell under the jurisdiction of TRAI and not CCI. We have discussed the findings of the Bharti Airtel case in the jurisdiction heading in Chapter 1. However, the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court dismissed these contentions by relying on its observations in the Ericsson case. It was held that the CCI is not prevented from exercising its jurisdiction relating to specific rights, such as compulsory licensing under the Patents Act if the same gives rise to anti-competitive conduct or abuse of dominance. While the patentee is free to approach the Controller of Patents for grant of a compulsory licence, the same does not prohibit the CCI from passing an order under section 27 of the Competition Act because the provisions of the two enactments do not intend to substitute one another. Further, although Chapter XVI and section 140 of the Patents Act contain provisions which have a common subject matter with the Competition Act, the remedies provided in the Competition Act are materially different from the Patents Act. Hence, there are dual remedies for the same conduct, but the nature of relief in both enactments is substantially different. Therefore, there is no irreconcilable conflict between the Patents Act and the Competition Act and the jurisdiction of CCI to entertain complaints regarding abuse of dominance with respect to patent rights cannot be excluded. The key observations of the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court are as follows:(i) In conformity with the TRIPS Agreement, the Patents Act was amended and now contains provisions for grant of compulsory licences in certain cases. Further, section 140 of the Patents Act also prohibits inclusion of certain restrictive conditions in certain contracts – contracts relating to sale or lease of a patented article, licence to manufacture a patented article or to work any patented process. However, the jurisdiction of CCI is not excluded from examining matters relating to certain rights granted under the Patents Act or any alleged anticompetitive conduct of any enterprise, including abuse of its dominant position. (ii) Although section 60 of the Competition Act expressly provides that it would be given an overriding effect, the same would not whittle down the provisions of the Patents Act. Section 62 of the Competition Act which expressly provides that the Competition Act would be in addition to and not in derogation of the provisions of any other law for the time being in force, clearly expresses the legislative intent that the Competition Act is in addition to other laws and not in substitution thereof. (iii) Section 84 of the Patents Act provides for grant of compulsory licences in certain cases where reasonable requirement of the public in respect of patented inventions has not been satisfied. Section 85 of the Patents Act provides for revocation of patents if, after the expiry of two years from the date of grant of compulsory licence, the patented invention has not been worked in the territory of India and the reasonable requirements of the public with respect to the patent have not been satisfied. The instances listed in section 84(7) of the Patents Act can in certain circumstances be considered an abuse of dominance if the patentee is dominant in the relevant market. The orders that can be passed by the CCI under section 27 of the Competition Act in respect of abuse of dominant position are materially different from the remedies that are available under section 84 of the Patents Act. In certain cases, it may be open for a prospective licensee to approach the Controller for grant of a compulsory licence. However, the same would not be inconsistent with the CCI passing an appropriate order under section 27 of the Competition Act. (iv) Under section 21A of the Competition Act, the CCI can make a reference to any regulator where in the course of proceedings, the CCI proposes to take any decision which may be contrary to provisions of any statute, the implementation of which has been entrusted to any statutory authority. Similarly, section 21 of the Competition Act enables any statutory authority, which is charged with administration of any statute, to make a reference to CCI if it proposes to take any decision which may be contrary to the provisions of the Competition Act. These provisions clearly indicate that the intention of the Parliament was to ensure that the provisions of the Competition Act were implemented in addition to the provisions of other statutes. Hence, there was no irreconcilable repugnancy or conflict between the Competition Act and the Patents Act. (v) A comparison with TRAI in the Bharti Airtel case is misplaced since the issue of whether an adequate number of POIs had been provided by service providers for connecting with the service rolled out fell squarely within TRAI’s scope of regulatory powers, which are all pervasive. A ‘Controller’ does not regulate the exercise of patent rights in such a pervasive manner due to the obvious reason that ‘patents’ are not an industry. The nature of the role performed by a Controller thus cannot be equated to that of the CCI since the expertise and the nature of inquiry conducted, and the relief that can be granted by both the bodies is materially different. Hence, the Supreme Court’s decision in the Bharti Airtel case is not an authority for the proposition that wherever there is a statutory regulator, the complaint must be first brought before the Regulator and examination of a complaint by the CCI is contingent on the findings of the Regulator. On the aforesaid basis, the Delhi High Court upheld CCI’s order. However, the position of law has taken a U-turn with the Division Bench judgment in the Ericsson case. It held that the CCI cannot exercise jurisdiction over the actions of an enterprise that are in exercise of its rights as a patentee. As a result, it set aside the findings of the Single Judge Bench that there is no overlap between the two enactments and that the CCI has powers that the Controller does not have. It also set aside the finding to the effect that the Patents Act is only defining the contours of patent rights, whereas the Competition Act deals with the abuse of rights, including patent rights, which can also not be sustained. These findings were premised on the following analysis:(i) The question of whether an agreement under which a patent is licensed will cause an AAEC within India or will amount to an abuse of dominant position is not one that is reserved for the CCI. On the contrary, the factors that the CCI is required to consider under section 19(3) and 19(4) when assessing a potential violation of section 3 or 4 of the Competition Act are not very different from those that the Controller, in exercise of power to grant a compulsory licence, will consider in terms of section 84(6) and 84(7), read with other provisions of the Patents Act. Consequently, the inquiry proposed to be conducted by the CCI in respect of an assertion of patent rights is nearly identical to that which the Controller will conduct under Chapter XVI of the Patents Act. (ii) The legislative intent of the Patents Act is for the field pertaining to patents, unreasonable conditions in agreements of licensing, abuse of status as a patentee, inquiry in respect thereof, and relief that is to be granted are all to be governed by the Patents Act, and Chapter XVI of the Patents Act is a complete code in itself on all issues in this regard. On the other hand, the Competition Act is a general legislation pertaining to anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominant position generally. The inclusion of section 84(6)(iv) in the Patents Act by way of an amendment after the Competition Act was passed with section 3(5)(i)(b) is particularly instructive of the above legislative intent as regards anti-competitive agreements. (iii) For deciding an application for compulsory licensing, the Controller is empowered by the Patents Act to consider the reasonability of conditions imposed in a licence agreement. The CCI is empowered under the Competition Act to examine anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominant position. However, under section 3(5)(i)(b) of the Competition Act, reasonable conditions being imposed in an agreement concerning exercise of rights under the Patents Act are exempted from examination. This is indicative of the legislature’s intendment as to the exclusive domain of the Patents Act regarding reasonable conditions. (iv) It also observed that the Patents Act is the special statute and not the Competition Act. Since Chapter XVI of the Patents Act is a subsequent legislation as compared to the Competition Act, an application of the maxims generalia specialibus non derogant (i.e., the general does not detract from the specific) or lex posterior derogat priori (i.e., the later law prevails over the earlier law), the Patents Act must prevail over the Competition Act on the issue of exercise of rights by a patentee under the Patents Act. (v) An assessment of even the purpose and policy underlying the two enactments and the clear intendment conveyed by the language of the relevant provisions therein reveals that the Patents Act must necessarily prevail over that of the Competition Act. This is because while the Competition Act deals with the subjects of anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominant position ‘generally’, the Patents Act ‘deals with these subjects specifically in the context of patents’. It is pointed out that the Division Bench judgment is likely to be challenged by the CCI. The Supreme Court’s finding on this aspect will be critical since the latest decision of the High Court seeks to dislodge the jurisprudence of CCI examining anti-competitive conduct. Further, it may be argued that the Division Bench judgment of the Delhi High Court is contrary to the observations of the Supreme Court in the Bharti Airtel case, wherein it has been mentioned that the CCI is the primary regulator which has the mandate to look into anti-competitive practices, even where is an industry regulator. The Supreme Court in Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel and Ors.11 Ibid., paras 86-92. held that there were no overlaps in the jurisdiction of TRAI (telecom regulator) and CCI (competition regulator) because the function of the CCI is distinct from the function of TRAI under the TRAI Act, and the Competition Act is also a special statute which deals with anti-competition. Moreover, it is within the exclusive domain of the CCI to find out whether a particular agreement will cause AAEC within the relevant market in India. On this basis, the submission that between the two statutes, the TRAI Act would prevail as it is a complete code in itself which regulates the telecom sector in its entirety, including the aspects of competition, was rejected. The said case dealt with a factual matrix wherein the Supreme Court held that the matter under inquiry involved certain aspects which require telecom expertise; hence, the Supreme Court ordered that TRAI must look at such jurisdictional aspects, and thereafter, CCI can look into the competition law aspect. Hence, the same logic may be argued in the Ericsson matter as well when the CCI decides to appeal the Ericsson order before the Supreme Court. The issue of jurisdiction of CCI to determine the anti-competitive nature of conduct emanating out of IPRs was also raised in the case of Biocon Limited v. F. Hoffman-La Roche AG 12 Biocon Limited v. F. Hoffman-La Roche AG, Case No. 68 of 2016. (‘Roche case’). Roche had developed a monoclonal antibody called ‘Trastuzumab’, which targeted a specific form of breast cancer. This was marketed through a drug formulation named ‘HERCEPTIN’, which was priced at INR 1,20,000 per vial. In 2012, Roche withdrew HERCEPTIN from the market and introduced a low-cost version of Trastuzumab known as ‘BICELTIS’ which was priced at INR 75,000 per vial, due to the threat of development of a biosimilar version of Trastuzumab by its competitors and to avoid the imposition of a compulsory licence. Subsequently, Biocon and Mylan Pharmaceuticals (informants) collaborated to introduce a biosimilar version of Trastuzumab which was to be priced at INR 57,500 per vial, after receiving a licence from the Drugs Control Department, Karnataka. In order to prevent the entry of new players into the market of Trastuzumab, Roche filed a suit before the Delhi High Court. The informants filed the information for abuse of dominant position by Roche before the CCI. Roche challenged the maintainability of the information by arguing that the issues raised in the information are squarely covered by the Civil Suit pending before the Delhi High Court. The informants responded by arguing that the reliefs available from both the forums, i.e., the Delhi High Court and the CCI, are distinct. While the issues before the Delhi High Court pertain to the validity of approvals granted by the Drug Controller General of India (DCGI) to Biocon, the primary issue before the CCI was whether the Roche Group’s conduct in the market is abusive or not. The CCI upheld the submissions of the informants by relying on the observations of the Singh Judge Bench Judgment by the Delhi High Court in the Ericsson case, as discussed previously. The main issues before the Delhi High Court relate to the propriety of the approvals granted by the DCGI to the informants’ products and the alleged passing off by the informants of their products as Roche’s products. The CCI in the Roche case held that even if various issues have been highlighted by the informants in their defence in the Civil Suit, the Delhi High Court will only adjudicate upon the issues which are pending before it, and it cannot take away their right to approach the CCI for a remedy enshrined under section 27 of the Competition Act. Whether Roche Group is dominant and has abused its dominant position or not is a question that is covered under the provisions of the Act and falls under the exclusive jurisdiction of the CCI under section 61 of the Competition Act. Hence, the pending Civil Suit in the Delhi High Court does not impede the CCI’s jurisdiction to look into the present matter. Once the investigation commenced, the DG sought various information from Roche, including copies of board meetings and marketing arrangements entered into between the petitioner and its parent company, along with pricing structure, etc. Consequently, Roche filed a writ petition before the Delhi High Court seeking a stay against the directions of the DG. Although the Delhi High Court granted the stay, it is important to note that the basis of the same was not an issue of jurisdiction of the CCI but rather the nature of the inquiry being conducted by the DG. The Delhi High Court did not make any adverse observations regarding the power of CCI to cause an investigation into the alleged abusive conduct of Roche. It is reiterated that this stay on investigation should not be confused as precluding or whittling down the jurisdiction of the CCI in investigating competition issues arising out of patent rights. The stay has been granted owing to the nature of the investigation being conducted and not the right to conduct the investigation in the first place. Thus, the position as it exists today is that the CCI is not excluded from examining the abuse of dominant position emanating out of the conduct of the patent holder as there is no conflict between the Competition Act and the Patents Act.

sections.title: SCOPE OF THE IPR EXEMPTION
sections.text: §5.03 SCOPE OF THE IPR EXEMPTION As mentioned in the preceding chapter, section 3 of the Competition Act deals with the provision relating to anti-competitive agreements and cartels.
