A refusal to license IP exclusively held by a dominant enterprise may also be considered a constructive refusal to supply under the provisions of the Competition Act, and such a refusal may be construed to be an abuse under section 4 of the Competition Act. Section 27(g) of the Competition Act empowers the CCI to pass ‘any other order’ as it deems fit besides imposing a penalty if it finds that a dominant enterprise has acted in contravention of section 4 of the Competition Act. The term ‘any other order’ is like an omnibus clause which will include an order by the CCI issuing compulsory licence if the CCI feels that such order will remedy the market situation distorted by the dominant enterprise. Competition authorities in matured antitrust jurisdictions have granted compulsory licences under the competition provisions of their respective statutes, notwithstanding similar provisions in IP laws. One must note that mere refusal to license IPR will not be considered abuse. Internationally also, a mere refusal to license IPRs is not in itself objectionable under competition laws. However, in exceptional circumstances, the refusal by a dominant company to grant a licence under its IPRs can be an abuse under Article 102 of the TFEU. In Magill, 39 Joined Cases C-241/91 P and C-242/91, Commission of the European Communities v. Magill TV Guide Ltd, paras 48-58. the ECJ held that the refusal by copyright holders in the United Kingdom and Ireland to grant licences to third parties to provide a new comprehensive television listings magazine was abusive under Article 102 TFEU. In the Microsoft 40 Case T-201/04, Microsoft Corp. v. The Computing Technology Industry Association, Inc. and Ors., II-3634, paras 36-41, 330-332, 373-400, 688-712. decision, Microsoft abused its dominant position by refusing to license the specifications required to ensure interoperability between its operating system and work group server operating systems of competitors. Further, the ECJ confirmed that a refusal by a dominant company to license its IPR is an abuse under Article 102 TFEU where three conditions are satisfied, namely: (i) that the refusal is preventing the emergence of a new product for which there is a potential consumer demand, (ii) that it is unjustified, and (iii) that it is such as to exclude any competition on a secondary market. [A] Decisional Practice of CCI We have dealt with the concepts of refusal to deal, essential facilities and denial of market access in the chapter on abuse of dominance. There have been some discussions on this doctrine in the CCI so far. In a few of its decisions, the CCI appears to have endorsed the applicability of the ‘essential facilities doctrine’ under section 4 of the Competition Act. [1] Refusal to Deal In Air Works India (Engineering) Private Limited v. GMR Hyderabad International Airport Limited and Anr., 41 Air Works India (Engineering) Private Limited v. GMR Hyderabad International Airport Limited and Anr., Case No. 30 of 2019. the informant entered into a licence agreement with GMR (the operator and manager of Rajiv Gandhi International Airport Hyderabad) to provide Line Maintenance Services to various airlines. As per the facts, there was an understanding among all licensees operating at the airport for the technical support services that as long as they are carrying on their operations, the licence would continue to be granted. It was alleged by the informant that GMR, owing to its sole control over the airport premises, denied access to the informant for providing the Line Maintenance Services, with an intent to give advantage to its group company, i.e., GAT, which competes directly with the informant at the airport in the downstream market for providing Line Maintenance Services. It was also alleged that the termination of the licence was motivated by the fact that GAT was under tremendous financial difficulty and required support from its parent company, GMR. While assessing the alleged abuse of dominance by GMR in the upstream relevant market for provision of access to airport facilities/premises, the CCI observed that as a general principle, every entity, dominant or otherwise, has the freedom to choose its trading partners. It is a basic and fundamental right to decide with whom an enterprise wishes to deal, and as such refusal to deal is not per se sufficient to conclude contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act. However, there are three guiding conditions, as per the existing literature, where refusal to deal amounts to abuse:42 Ibid., paras 48-59. (i) the refused input is indispensable for an entity in order to compete in the downstream market; (ii) refusal shall most likely eliminate competition on the downstream market; (iii) refusal shall most likely damage consumers. In the context of IPR, the first criterion, i.e., indispensability of the input, becomes important. Substitutability or indispensability is established only when the input refused cannot be obtained through an alternative source. The doctrine of essential facility originated in the US antitrust law, though it soon gained recognition with various antitrust authorities. As per the existing jurisprudence, an asset is essential facility if it fulfils the following main economic conditions:(i) the dominant entity controls access to an essential facility; (ii) the facility cannot reasonably be duplicated by the competitor; (iii) the dominant entity denies access to the competitor; (iv) there should be no alternative means of entering the relevant market at a reasonable cost without having access to the essential facility; (v) there must be spare capacity on the facility in question. In the case of GMR, all the aforesaid conditions have been met with respect to access to the facilities/premises at the airport for providing Line Maintenance Services. GMR, vide the Concession/Licence Agreement, had the exclusive right and privilege to carry out the development, design, financing, construction, commissioning, maintenance, operation and management of the airport for a period of thirty years, which was further extendable for another thirty years at the option of GMR. Hence, GMR was a government-conferred monopoly which controlled access to the airport premises, i.e., the ‘essential facility’ here. Further, the Line Maintenance Services pertain to such requirements and procedures which can be undertaken only at the airport itself. An entity providing such services, therefore, cannot do so without being present in the premises as the service is required between the landing and take-off of the aircraft. Accordingly, the CCI in its prima facie opinion held that given that physical presence at the airport premises is indispensable to provide Line Maintenance Services, non-renewal of the licence arrangement amounts to the informant’s exclusion from the downstream market, which may skew the market in favour of GMR’s group entity, i.e., GAT. Further, in prima facie contravention of section 4(2)(b), the lack of competitive constraints on GMR in such a case where the very entity which is dominant in the upstream market is providing Line Maintenance Services in the downstream market can potentially lead to over-charging from the airlines and also cause adverse impact on the consumers in the long run. In the Vifor case43 Vifor case, para. 78. also, as discussed earlier in this chapter, a contention was raised regarding Vifor’s freedom to choose its trading partners. In this regard, the CCI held that this legal position is not absolute: not every company has a right to seek access to the patent of Vifor unless it demonstrates that there is indeed a need for such access based on the existing supply conditions of an essential product/facility as against its demand by the consumers, so as to affect the market adversely by non-dealing on the part of the entity with significant market power. Further, any company requesting a grant of access should also demonstrate its ability to the patent holder, to satisfy the requirements specified for receipt of the grant of licence. However, one cannot state with legal certainty that the ‘essential facilities’ doctrine is the only basis on which an entity’s refusal to license its intellectual property would amount to an abuse of dominance, and a comprehensive analysis must be undertaken. The words used in the Competition Act are denial of market access, which may not be as high a threshold as required for essential facilities. CCI has also seen issues where market players refuse to license their technology, thereby causing appreciable adverse impact on competition in India. The appropriate relief under the relevant IPR statutes in India in this case would be to force the holder of the technology to license the same, which would be something akin to a compulsory licence. Such refusals under the Competition Act, when limited to decisions of a single entity, will be examined under section 4, though where the refusals involve multiple entities, i.e., ‘group boycott’, the examination may proceed under section 3 of the Competition Act. [2] Compulsory Licensing The leading decision in this regard is the Google Android case, wherein the NCLAT decided in favour of Google on the issue of access to Google’s APIs, i.e., its proprietary IPR, as discussed in the earlier parts of this chapter. The CCI directed Google not to deny access to its APIs so as to disadvantage OEMs, app developers and its existing or potential competitors. However, the NCLAT set aside CCI’s direction and upheld Google’s argument that as the owner of proprietary APIs, it is entitled to reap the benefits of the investment made by it into the scientific and technical development of the APIs. The relevant observations of the NCLAT are mentioned below:44 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, paras 185-188. ‘The various core device features that Google Play Services provide services relating to security and reliability (through installation of Google Play Protect which provides protection from malware), facility to developers through thousands of continually updated APIs such as “Google Cast” for streaming, Google Maps, provision of accurate location information and sending notifications through a messaging transport layer and enabling core services, such as making emergency call, autofill services, sending and receiving files etc. Thus, it is clear that the APIs which include Google Play Services are APIs that provide essential services to the Apps which are part of Google Play Store. These are privately developed and maintained by Google and are provided by Google to App developers and OEMs.’ ‘It is abundantly clear … that APIs as developed by Google and of which Google Play Services are an integral and important part, are developed and maintained by Google and continually updated through scientific and technical development by the teams of Google and these APIs are necessary for functionality of the Apps in Google Play Store. We are therefore, clear that the APIs and Google Play Services, which are proprietary items of Google cannot be given in through unhindered access to App developers, OEMs and Google’s existing and potential competitors. … proprietary software such as APIs, which are developed through scientific and technical innovation should fetch value to Google and, therefore, remain an incentive for a technological company/Google to further carry out such development and monetize it through its commercial use.’ As a result, the NCLAT set aside the decision issued by CCI on the basis that access to such APIs cannot be provided to Google’s competitors without going through necessary technical and commercial engagements with Google. As mentioned earlier, the said observations are presently under challenge before the Supreme Court. In essence, the NCLAT has ruled against allowing compulsory licensing, and in this sense, it has laid down the proposition that there cannot be an absolute rule of licensing under the framework of the Indian competition regime. By recognizing that the IPR holder has a right to innovate and compulsory licensing may affect the enjoyment of this right, the NCLAT has strayed from the earlier jurisprudence of the COMPAT, which in some cases upheld the CCI orders providing for licensing like in the Automobile Spare parts case. [B] Practical Guidance As mentioned above, some of the IPR statutes in India provide for provisions relating to compulsory licensing. For instance, under section 84 of the Patents Act, 1970, the Controller of Patents has the power to grant a compulsory licence after the expiry of three years from grant of patent in case the patented invention does not meet the ‘reasonable requirements of the public’, or is ‘not available to the public at a reasonably affordable price’ or ‘is not worked in the territory of India’. In 2012, in an order that was upheld on appeal, the Controller of Patents granted a compulsory licence to Bayer’s patent covering a cancer drug to a generic Indian drug manufacturer on all three grounds.45 Natco Pharma v. Bayer Corporation, Controller of Patents Mumbai, March 2012; Bayer Corporation v. Union of India & Ors. [2013] (IPAB, Chennai). With the ratio of the Division Bench Judgment in the Ericsson case, it appears to have become the domain of the Controller of Patents in exercise of its power to grant a compulsory licence under sections 83, 84 and 89 of the Patents Act to look into questions of unreasonable conditions in agreements of licensing of patents, abuse of status as a patentee, inquiry in respect thereof and relief that is to be granted therefor. There can be similar situations under the Competition Act because the CCI has the power to pass ‘any other order’ under section 27 of the Competition Act which may include provisions for compulsory licensing. However, there is no guidance presently as to under what conditions will CCI pass an order for a compulsory licence. The Copyright Act, 1957, contains similar provisions, though of a narrower scope. Compulsory licence may be issued pursuant to section 31(1)(b) of the Copyrights Act, 1957. It may be issued when the Copyright Board is satisfied that the copyright owners have refused to allow communication to the public, and such refusal is not reasonable. This provision extends only to certain types of copyrighted works and not all. In Music Broadcast Pvt Ltd v. Phonographic Performance Limited,46 Music Choice India Pvt Ltd v. Phonographic Performance Limited, (Copyright Board) (Second Order), 2010. the Copyright Board issued a compulsory licence to musical works in favour of the FM radio industry on a revenue-sharing model; i.e., 2% of the net advertisement earnings of each FM radio station would be set aside to pay the music providers. It will be interesting to see whether the CCI would attempt to adopt the factors considered by the relevant IP authorities and fit them within the scope of the Competition Act or whether the CCI would distance itself from such factors and instead assess the issue under other factors and pass appropriate orders if required. The CCI, therefore, attempts to maintain a critical balance between the rights of the IPR holders and imposing a relief such as compulsory licensing in an abuse of dominance investigation. This appears synonymous with the decisional practice under the EU law where the IP holders do not have a general obligation to license their IPR to competitors, and the ECJ in such context has opined that in ‘exceptional circumstances’, such refusal could result in an abuse of dominance.

sections.title: IP REMEDIES IN MERGERS
sections.text: §5.06 IP REMEDIES IN MERGERS The CCI has grappled with the issue of IP remedies in a merger situation. As mentioned in detail in the chapter on merger control, merger analysis is a forward-looking exercise, and the CCI has the powers under the Competition Act to issue commitments/remedies to the parties of combination to ensure that the behavioural conduct of the parties to the combination, post the consummation of the combination, does not create any competition law concerns. Commitments in a merger situation may be structural or behavioural in nature. The issue of whether the licensing of IPR constitutes the ‘acquisition’ or ‘transfer’ of assets and consequently requires notification has been a topic of debate. IPR can be transferred through many methods, such as assignment, exclusive licensing, non-exclusive licensing. Hence, there is a need to identify situations where such licensing might trigger merger notification. The CCI, in the merger control order of AT&T and Time Warner Inc.,47 AT&T and Time Warner Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/11/456, 13 Feb. 2017. clarified that the licensing of an IP will not in itself constitute a transfer or acquisition if the licence is demonstrably non-exclusive. For this, inter alia, the licensor must establish that it itself continues to use the IPR, or indeed licenses out the IPR to others for wider use, and that the licence is not exclusive, irrespective of terminology used in the licence agreements. Thus, IPR licences that do not meet this requirement will be treated as asset acquisitions that require merger notification to the CCI if no statutory exemption is available. The CCI has dealt with the issuance of structural commitments which involved IP remedies. Structural commitments consist of a divestment package, and the competition regulator must ensure that the divestment so designed consists of a viable business, which, in the hands of the purchaser, is capable of competing with the merged entity. In order to determine the scope of the business to be divested, all tangible and intangible elements – including IPR, know-how and goodwill – must be specified. Also, the manner in which intellectual property will be transferred must also form part of the divestment package. Generally, the intellectual property is transferred by way of licences, and such licences should generally be exclusive licences, free from field or geographical restrictions, and grant the licensee the ability to compete effectively with the parties to the merger. [A] Remedies Issued by CCI In the merger control order of Sun Pharmaceuticals Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited,48 Sun Pharmaceuticals Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No: C-2014/05/170, 5 Dec. 2014. CCI discussed the issue of IP remedies/commitments to alleviate the competition law concerns emanating from the merger of Sun and Ranbaxy. Apart from the brands and assets which formed a part of the divestment package, the CCI noted that for intangible assets (including IPR) which contribute to the current operation or are necessary to ensure the economic viability, marketability and competitiveness of products in the divestment package (retained by the Sun/Ranbaxy for use in their other business), the parties will grant a non-exclusive, irrevocable, royalty-free and perpetual licence to the purchaser. Further, CCI also noted that customer and credit records with respect to divestment products must also be given to the purchaser. These IP remedies were designed with the aim of maintaining the existing level of competition in the market by creating a viable, independent and long-term competitor and ensuring that the approved purchaser had the necessary components to compete with the merged entity. In approving the merger, the CCI directed structural, inter alia, ordering the parties to divest seven products under various brand names and assign all trade mark rights related to these Divestment Brands. The parties in return proposed an amendment that in respect of three trademarks, they be allowed to license them exclusively, perpetually, irrevocably and on a royalty-free basis with the right to sub-license to the purchaser of the trademarks instead of assignment. This was allowed by the CCI on the purchaser’s commitment that it does not require assignment of the said trademarks as the licence, as above, would suffice to enable it to independently sell and market the said three Divestment Products. Hence, the CCI’s order reflected a balanced approach to the transfer of IPR from the IPR holders while ensuring that competitors in the market are able to compete effectively. Further, in the merger of Sony/Zee, 49 Culver Max Entertainment Private Limited, Bangla Entertainment Private Limited, Zee Entertainment Enterprises Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/04/923, 4 Oct. 2022. the amalgamation of Culver Max Entertainment Private Limited, Zee Entertainment Enterprises Limited, Bangla Entertainment Private Limited, and Essel Group Participants presented several competition concerns, inter alia, due to existing licence agreements pertaining to content rights, music rights, broadcasting rights, etc. To allay the concerns of the combination, the parties offered the following voluntary structural modifications:(i) Divestment of three TV channels engaged in the Hindi GEC and Hindi Films channels market, i.e., Big Magic, Zee Action and Zee Classic. This would include licensing trademarks, channel names, logos, etc., and transfer of applicable licences, permits, authorizations, and applicable agreements on content licensing and employees. (ii) The purchaser of the divested channels shall, inter alia, be independent/unconnected with the combined entity and have the resources and incentive to sustain the same as a viable and active competitor. Accordingly, SIPL and Viacom18 Media Private Limited were disqualified from bidding for the divested channels. (iii) The parties shall not acquire direct or indirect stake/influence in the divested channels for a period of five years from such sale. Therefore, it can be seen that in a merger situation, CCI places a lot of emphasis on IPR remedies. It will be interesting to note the stand of the CCI in future where they grant remedies in nature of access remedies, rebranding, etc.

sections.title: CONCLUSION
sections.text: §5.07 CONCLUSION The totality of the discussion above reveals that the CCI has adopted a nuanced approach in its decisional practice by considering the competitive implications of the exercise of IPR so as to maintain the balance between promoting competition and protecting IPR. While new issues on the interface of competition and IPR may continue to crop up in the future, an attempt has to be made to preserve this delicate balance. Innovation must not be curbed and hence remedies, if any, must spur and not stifle dynamic innovation.

plain_text:
§5.01 FRIENDS IN DISAGREEMENT The interface between competition law and IPR is an ever- evolving field due to rapid technological advancements in the global economy. While the objective of competition law is to promote fair competition and prevent anti-competitive practices, IPR aims to encourage and safeguard innovation through grant of exclusive rights in the form of patents, copyrights, trademarks, etc. Although the issue of jurisdiction of CCI in matters concerning IPR has been largely resolved in recent years, with competition concerns having been held to fall squarely within the domain of India’s antitrust regulator, new jurisdictional challenges continue to emerge regarding anti-competitive conduct that may arise from the exercise of IPR. One of the primary concerns in this interface is the potential abuse of IPR by competitors to create or maintain monopolistic positions in the market. There is an apparent antagonism between the IPR law regime and the competition law regime, considering that the essential attribute of IPR is the grant of exclusion, where the state grants a monopoly to the IPR holder, whereas competition law aims at attaining maximum production and allocation of resources, thereby achieving allocative efficiency. However, it has been noticed that IPR and competition law regime work in tandem and perform complementary roles. Together, they contribute towards spurring innovation. Therefore, the legal regimes of competition law and IPR are often termed as ‘friends in disagreement’. Given that the regime performs a complementary role, the competition authorities must be cautious that the regulatory interventions by them are timely and take place only in cases of abuse of rights guaranteed to the IPR holder, which potentially distorts the competitive structure of the market. Hence, there is a delicate balance to be struck between incentivizing and promoting innovation on the one hand and ensuring that the exclusive rights granted in the form of IPR do not harm competition or restrict consumer choice in any manner on the other hand.

§5.02 JURISDICTION OF THE CCI OVER IPR ISSUES The issue of jurisdiction of the competition law regulator to hear disputes that sit on the cusp of the interface between competition law and IPR can be traced back to the predecessor regime which existed i.e. Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practice ( MRTP) Commission. [A] MRTP Commission In Dr. Vallal Peruman and Anr. v. Godfrey Phillips (India) Ltd., 1 UTP Enquiry No. 180/92; The MRTP Commission in Manju Bhardwaj v. Zee Telefilms Ltd. and Ors.; UTPE No. 148/1985, reiterated the findings of Dr. Vallal Peruman and Anr. v. Godfrey Phillips (India) Ltd. to hold that if there is misuse of a trademark by manipulation, distortion, contrivances, and embellishments, then the MRTP Commission has jurisdiction to proceed under MRTP Act. a complaint was filed by a consumer that Godfrey Phillips (India) Ltd. was manufacturing, marketing and selling cigarettes under the brand name Marcovitch Red & White, a reputed foreign company, even though the cigarettes were neither produced in collaboration with or with the knowledge of the foreign company which owned the said mark and brand name. It was alleged that this constituted an unfair trade practice because the complainants were misled by the mark and brand name being used by Godfrey Phillips. At the outset, Godfrey Phillips had argued that the authorities under the Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958 (‘TMM Act’) have exclusive jurisdiction in regard to the complaints pertaining to the use of trademark by the holder of the certificate of registration. Resultantly, the MRTP Commission observed that it is true that there is a potential conflict between IPR and antitrust law because the exercise of the monopoly power conferred upon the holder of IPR may produce anticompetitive effects. However, there are powerful public policy justifications for maintaining these rights as well as a basis to suggest that this potential conflict may not be as severe as it appears at first blush. Reliance was placed on the solution worked out by the ECJ, which has adopted a practical and balanced approach by distinguishing between the mere ‘existence’ of IPR rights and how they are actually ‘exercised’. The same principle was extended to resolve the conflict between the TMM Act and the MRTP Act: a certificate of registration entitles an individual/undertaking to use a trademark so long as such certificate of registration is in operation, and the trade mark is used strictly in conformity with the terms and conditions subject to which it was granted. However, if the holder of the certificate misuses the trademark by engaging in manipulation, distortion, contrivances and embellishments, etc., to mislead or confuse the consumers, then action can be taken against them under section 36A(7) for indulging in unfair trade practices under the MRTP Act. Thus, the provisions of the MRTP Act would be attracted only where there is an abuse in the exercise of the rights protected and granted under the TMM Act. [B] Challenges to CCI’s Jurisdiction On similar lines, the jurisdiction of the CCI in adjudicating market distortions from abuse of IPRs has been challenged before various High Courts in India. While a majority of the decisions have noted that the CCI has the appropriate jurisdiction to look at the misuse of IPRs under the lens of competition law, a very recent decision of the Delhi High Court has said that the CCI must not exercise jurisdiction due to a similar provision which exists in Patent laws. This has been discussed subsequently in this chapter. The challenge has been on the grounds that the CCI does not have the requisite jurisdiction to decide on competition issues which have an interface with IPR. [1] Competition Act Versus Copyright Act One of the first challenges to the jurisdiction of the CCI was before the Bombay High Court in the case of Aamir Khan Production Private Limited v. Competition Commission of India. 2 Aamir Khan Production Private Limited v. Competition Commission of India [2010], (Bombay High Court) 2011 (1) BomCR 802. The case involved certain film production companies who organized themselves under the umbrella of a trade association and took a collective decision not to release films to multiplexes from 4 April 2009 with the objective of extracting a higher revenue sharing ratio from the multiplex. On receipt of information from the multiplex association, CCI took cognizance of the matter and referred the matter for detailed investigation to the DG. Post a detailed investigation by the DG, the CCI issued a show cause notice to the film production companies. Thereafter, Aamir Khan Production Private Limited approached the Bombay High Court challenging the jurisdiction of the CCI, raising the following arguments:(i) The principal point of contention was that the exhibition of a feature film, which is a subject matter of copyright exploitation alone, is specifically excluded under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. (ii) The Indian copyright law enjoins upon the copyright holder the exclusive right to do or authorize the doing or to authorize to sell or give on hire or offer for sale or hire any copy of the film. (iii) The producer of the film has the exclusive right to decide as to whom it shall sell or give on hire any copy of the film for communicating the film to the public. (iv) Section 18 of the Copyright Act, 1957 confers upon the owner of the copyright the right to assign to any person a copyright either wholly or partially. Further, section 30 of the Copyright Act recognizes the right of the owner of the copyright to grant any interest in the right by license in writing. Based on the said legal provision, it was argued that when a producer makes a cinematograph film, he has an exclusive right to sell or give on hire any copy of the film, and the show cause notice by the CCI must be quashed on these grounds. However, based on the reading of the provision of the Competition Act, the Bombay High Court rejected the petition made by Aamir Khan Productions Private Limited. The language of the provisions of the Competition Act clearly indicates that the CCI has the jurisdiction to determine whether the preliminary state of facts (on which the further exercise of its jurisdiction depends) exists. Further, the question of CCI’s jurisdiction is itself a mixed question of law and fact which the CCI is competent to decide. The Bombay High Court held that the CCI has the power to determine whether it has the jurisdiction to entertain a matter and analyse whether the conduct of the parties can avail of the exemption made under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. The contour of the IPR exemption made under section 3(5) of the Competition Act is dealt with in detail later in this chapter. The issue of the jurisdiction of CCI into IPR-related matters was further discussed before the CCI in the case of Ms HT Media v. M/s Super Cassettes Industries Limited.3 Ms HT Media v. M/s Super Cassettes Industries Limited, Case No. 40 of 2011. The case concerned Super Cassettes, the largest private publisher of Indian music and allegations that it was abusing its dominant position in contravention of the provisions of section 4 of the Competition Act. The allegations against Super Cassettes involved the following:(i) charging excessive amounts as licence fees/royalty from the radio operators for grant of rights for the broadcast of its music content; and (ii) imposing minimum commitment charges to be paid by the radio operators per month irrespective of actual needle hour (each aggregate of sixty minutes of the actual broadcast of sound recordings by FM radio station excluding commercials, advertisements, voice-over, anchor time, etc.) of broadcast of its music content by the radio operators; (iii) making conclusion of licensing arrangements with radio operators subject to the acceptance of licence fees and minimum commitment charge imposed by Super Cassettes. It was further alleged that such imposition of exorbitant licence fees and minimum commitment charge by Super Cassettes is an unfair condition imposed by it for granting licence to broadcast its music content on radio under the Competition Act. The licence agreement permitted the licensee to broadcast music subject to acceptance of onerous conditions such as minimum commitment charge obligations, which has the effect of restricting around 30%-40% of the radio stations’ broadcast to the music content of Super Cassettes. The said action of Super Cassettes limited the choice of music for the end consumers to only the music content of Super Cassettes. Further, such an action of Super Cassettes resulted in denial of market access for other music companies (publishers, copyright societies, etc.) with less market share and bargaining power. Based on the above facts, the CCI initiated an investigation into the conduct of Super Cassettes. Super Cassettes challenged the jurisdiction of the CCI on the ground that the matter under dispute between Super Cassettes and radio operators vests with the Copyright Board. It was contended by Super Cassettes that the Copyright Board is the only authority to decide whether the terms (not just rates) of a licence between a copyright owner and a radio broadcaster are reasonable and set new terms if existing terms are unreasonable. Further, it was argued by Super Cassettes that in the event the market is heavily regulated by the Copyright Board, and where the market mechanism is substituted by a regulatory body, the role of competition law is greatly diminished and, in this case, completely ousted. The CCI did not find merit in the arguments of Super Cassettes and stated that the regulatory domain of the Copyright Board and the CCI are completely different. It was observed by CCI that it is a market regulator which has the mandate of eliminating practices which have an adverse effect on competition in India. The CCI also observed that the ambit of the powers of the Copyright Board and the CCI is different, and they continued with the investigation of the conduct of Super Cassettes under the provisions of the Competition Act. Super Cassettes thereafter preferred to challenge the jurisdiction of the CCI before the Delhi High Court. The Delhi High Court did not grant a stay on the proceedings, and CCI continued the investigation on Super Cassettes and found them guilty of abuse of dominant position. The pricing abuse of Super Cassettes relating to IPRs will be discussed subsequently in this chapter. On the aspect of jurisdiction, the CCI observed4 Ibid., paras 127, 128. that while the Copyright Board does have the right and obligation to determine licence fee and the reasonableness thereof under the Copyright Act, apart from that, none of the other issues envisaged by section 3 of the Competition Act can be decided by the Copyright Board. Similarly, under section 4 of the Competition Act, the CCI is obligated to adjudicate and give findings on alleged abuse of dominance, inter alia, on account of one-sided, discriminatory or unfair terms of the agreement. However, the Copyright Board does not have such jurisdiction. Further, the rights protected under the Copyright Act are also protected under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. In the present case, the unreasonableness of the licence fee was not the sole reason for referring the matter for investigation. Rather, it was the fact that radio stations had no choice but to accede to the arbitrary and unfair conditions imposed by T-Series by virtue of its dominant position. Since T-Series was the only music company imposing a minimum commitment charge upon the music companies, this prima facie amounted to an abuse of dominance, meriting an investigation by the DG. Thus, the CCI exercised its jurisdiction within the four corners of the Competition Act alone and did not encroach upon the areas where the Copyright Board had sole and exclusive jurisdiction, i.e., the determination of the licence fee and reasonableness of the same. It must be noted that in relation to broadcasting sound recordings, section 31 of the Copyright Act enables a person to seek a compulsory licence. The remedy of compulsory licence applies only when the copyright holder ‘refuses’ to license that work. As such, entering into a licence agreement subject to acceptance of certain anti-competitive conditions by the licensee may be a subject matter of the Competition Act and, at the same time, could potentially be considered a case of constructive ‘refusal’ for the purposes of enabling a section 31 proceeding under the Copyright Act. Further, the CCI can also pass any order as it may deem fit, including compulsory licensing if required, to remedy an anti-competitive situation. This issue will equally spill over even to the patent domain since the Patents Act, 1970, contains provisions relating to compulsory licence. Under the Patents Act, a compulsory licence could be sought by a competitor only after ‘refusal’ by the patentee to provide a voluntary licence and when other circumstances are established. Current jurisprudence indicates that before seeking a compulsory licence to a patent, the parties must engage in licence negotiations in ‘good faith’ and therefore, perhaps, refusal to license the patent but for accepting certain anti-competitive conditions may also justify seeking a compulsory licence. It is quite possible that in certain cases, this remedy under the Patents Act and the remedy under the Competition Act may overlap. Most recently, the Division Bench judgment5 Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (Delhi High Court) W.P.(C) 8379/2015 & LPA 247/2016, 150/2020, 550/2016 & 246/2016. in the Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) v. Competition Commission of India 6 Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) v. Competition Commission of India [2016], (Delhi High Court) W.P.(C) 464/2014 & CM Nos 911/2014 & 915/2014. (‘Ericsson case’) has given primacy to the Patents Act over the Competition Act. This has been discussed in more detail in the next section. [2] Competition Act Versus Patents Act The leading case on the overlaps between the Patents Act and the Competition Act is the Ericsson case. In this case, Ericsson challenged the section 26(1) orders of the CCI to investigate an abuse of dominance by Ericsson with regards to its SEPs, pursuant to the information filed by Micromax and Intex. The facts of these cases have been discussed in detail in the sub-chapter on SEPs. However, the Ericsson case assumes importance here because the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court made notable observations on the jurisdiction of CCI to hear anti-competitive related matters which had an interface with patent-related matters. Ericsson challenged the maintainability of the orders passed by the CCI before the Delhi High Court, arguing that the CCI lacks the jurisdiction to commence any proceeding in relation to a claim of royalty by a proprietor of a patent (i.e., a ‘patentee’) since the same would fall within the scope of the Patents Act. The CCI in its section 26(1) order expressly held that the pendency of a civil suit in the High Court does not take away the jurisdiction of the CCI to proceed under the Competition Act.7 Micromax Informatics Limited v. Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ), Case No. 50 of 2013; Intex Technologies (India) Limited v. Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ), Case No. 76 of 2013. However, Ericsson argued before the Delhi High Court that the Patents Act, being a special Act, provided not only the patentee’s rights but also a framework for the exercise and remedy for the abuse of such rights. Consequently, a recourse to the Competition Act would be barred. The Single Judge of the Delhi High Court disagreed and concluded that the same facts may give rise to an action under the Competition Act as well as the Patents Act and that the remedies and legislative intention behind both statutes are distinct and independent of each other. As a result, an agreement which imposes reasonable conditions for protecting patent rights (which concerns specific individual rights) is allowed however, assessing the ‘reasonability’ of such conditions is the responsibility of the CCI (since it concerns the market at large). The key observations of the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court have been mentioned below:(i) Ericsson had argued that it was not an ‘enterprise’ under section 2(h) of the Competition Act as it was a patentee, and hence, section 4 of the Competition Act would not be applicable to it. In this regard, the CCI observed that the nature of patent rights – the right to exclude without the right to use – does not in any manner exclude patent rights from the scope of ‘goods’ as defined under the Sale of Goods Act, 1930. All kinds of property (other than actionable claims, money and immovable property) would fall within the definition of ‘goods’, and this would also include intangible and incorporeal property such as patents. Hence, Ericsson’s contention that patents are not ‘goods’ was dismissed, and it was held to be an ‘enterprise’ under section 2(h) of the Competition Act. (ii) Ericsson contended that the CCI would not have the jurisdiction to entertain complaints regarding abuse of dominance in view of the specific provisions under the Patents Act enacted to address the issue of abuse of dominance by a patent holder. The intention of the Parliament in enacting the Competition Act was not to curtail or whittle down the full scope of any other law, and, therefore, it is expressly stated that the Competition Act would be in addition to, and not in derogation of any other Act. (iii) Section 60 of the Competition Act expressly provides that the provisions of the Competition Act shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent in any other law. However, the said provision must be read in the context of the Competition Act as a whole and the mischief that is sought to be addressed by the Competition Act. (iv) The same facts can give rise to an action under both statutes. For instance, in certain cases, threats by a proprietor of a SEP, who is found to be in a dominant position, could be held to be an abuse of dominance. Similarly, certain specific remedies have been provided under the Patents Act in respect of certain practices that may constitute abuse of dominance or may be considered anti-competitive such as grant of compulsory licences under section 84(7) of the Patents Act. Thus, whereas patent laws are concerned with grants of rights enabling the patent holder to exclude others from exploiting the invention, and in that sense promoting rights akin to a monopoly, competition law is essentially aimed to promote competition and, thus, fundamentally opposed to monopolization as well as unfair and anticompetitive practices that are associated with monopolies. The remedies provided under section 27 of the Competition Act for abuse of dominant position are materially different from the remedies available under section 84 of the Patents Act. Hence, the remedies under the two enactments are not mutually exclusive; grant of one is not destructive of the other. Thus, it may be open for a prospective licensee to approach the Controller of Patents for grant of compulsory licence in certain cases. The same is not inconsistent with the CCI passing an appropriate order under section 27 of the Competition Act. It is also necessary to bear in mind that whereas section 84 of the Patents Act provides specific remedy to the person seeking relief, the orders passed by CCI are in rem. Hence, the operative width of the two enactments is different. (v) The language of the provisions also indicates the legislative intention that the Competition Act and the Patents Act should work harmoniously. The legislature while enacting a law has complete knowledge of the existing laws and, therefore, where an express provision for the repeal of an earlier statute is not provided unless it is concluded that the provisions of the two legislations cannot co-exist, the question of reading the provisions of a statute to have been impliedly repealed does not arise. (vi) The proviso to section 84(6)(iv) of the Patents Act provides that a prospective licensee who applies for a compulsory licence is expected to have made, prior to his application, efforts to obtain a licence on reasonable terms. However, it further specifies that this consideration would not be relevant where the conduct of a patentee is found to be anti-competitive. This cannot be read to mean that a patentee’s anti-competitive conduct would have to be first established in proceedings under the Competition Act before the Controller could take that into account. Rather, it only lists certain factors required to be considered by the Controller. However, if CCI has finally found a patentee’s conduct to be anti-competitive and its finding has attained finality, the Controller would also proceed on the said basis and – on the principle akin to issue estoppel – the patentee would be estopped from contending to the contrary. (vii) Section 3(5) of the Competition Act expressly provides that section 3 would not restrict the right of any person to impose reasonable conditions for protecting its right, inter alia, under the Patents Act. Hence, whilst an agreement which imposes reasonable conditions for protecting Patent Rights is permissible, an anti-competitive agreement which imposes unreasonable conditions would not be afforded the safe harbour of section 3(5) of the Competition Act and would fall foul of section 3 of the Competition Act. The question as to whether a condition imposed under the agreement is reasonable or not would be a matter which could only be decided by the CCI under the provisions of the Competition Act. Neither the Controller of Patents discharging his functions in terms of the Patents Act nor a Civil Court would have any jurisdiction to adjudicate whether an agreement falls foul of section 3 of the Competition Act. Thus, there is no irreconcilable repugnancy or conflict between the Competition Act and the Patents Act. And, in the absence of any irreconcilable conflict between the two legislations, the jurisdiction of CCI to entertain complaints for abuse of dominance in respect of Patent rights cannot be ousted. Hence, the possible overlaps between the competition and patents domain are not contrary to each other and the regulatory authorities for both have separate and distinct mandates to follow. However, in appeal, the Division Bench of the Delhi High Court in the Ericsson case8 Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ) v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (Delhi High Court) W.P.(C) 8379/2015 & LPA 247/2016, 150/2020, 550/2016 & 246/2016. has reversed the findings of the Singh Judge Bench. This has been discussed subsequently. In Monsanto Holdings Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India 9 Monsanto Holdings Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India, [2020] (Delhi High Court) W.P.(C) 1776/2016 and CM Nos 7606/2016, 12396/2016 & 16685/2016. (‘Monsanto case’), Mahyco Monsanto Biotech India Pvt. Ltd. (MMBL) challenged the maintainability of the order passed by CCI to investigate the trait fee charged by MMBL and the other terms and conditions imposed by it through its sub-licence agreement for using the technology for manufacturing Bt. Cotton Seeds. The facts of the case were as follows: MMBL sub-licensed the technology licensed by Monsanto to various seed manufacturers in India, including the informants (i.e., the sub-licensees) through agreements which were extendable from time to time. The consideration for sub-licensing the said technology was divided into two parts. The first was a non-refundable fee, which was required to be paid upfront (which was as high as INR 50 lakhs), and the second part was a recurring fee, which was referred to as ‘trait value’ and was determined on the basis of the Maximum Retail Price (MRP) fixed for Bt. Cotton Seeds. The sub-licensees requested MMBL to charge a reasonable trait value considering the increase in the cost of production and fixation of lower MRP of cotton seeds by the State Governments. However, MMBL refused to do so and instead, terminated the licences and also initiated claims for its payments against the sub-licensees. Consequently, the informants alleged that:(i) Monsanto and MMBL abused their dominant position by charging excessive and unfair prices for Bt. Technology. They set prices which are significantly higher than those that would result if there was effective competition in the relevant market. Further, it was alleged that linking the trait value to the MRP of cotton seeds is unreasonable and not reflective of the economic value of the said technology. (ii) MMBL had imposed unfair conditions in the sub-licence agreements and the same violated section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act. In terms of Clause 2.05(c) of the sub-licence agreements, the informants were required to notify MMBL in case their affiliates entered into negotiations with any competitor of MMBL/intended to deal with any competitor of MMBL within thirty days of commencement of such negotiations and failure to give such prior notice entitled MMBL to terminate the sub-licence agreement. This clause was alleged to be unfair as commercial negotiations are confidential, and any disclosure of such negotiations would have an adverse effect on the informants. Further, there was a reasonable apprehension that dealing with any competitor of MMBL would invite an adverse action against the informants, such as through selective licensing and refusal to sub-license Bt. Technology. (iii) Article 9.4 of the sub-licence agreements also imposed an unfair condition as, in terms of the said clause, the informants were required to destroy all parent lines or cotton germplasm, which had been modified to contain Monsanto Technology after the sub-licence was terminated. It was argued that destroying germplasm (parent line) would effectively amount to destroying their intellectual property, which normally takes about five to ten years to develop. It was alleged that through these restrictive and unfair conditions, MMBL had virtually eliminated all potential competition for its technology. (iv) MMBL and Monsanto Group have violated section 4(2)(a)(i) of the Competition Act as they discriminated against the informants. It was alleged that whereas Bt. Technology was provided to other group companies of Monsanto without entering into any sub-licence agreements containing unfair conditions, MMBL insisted on entering into unfair sub-licence agreements with other seed manufacturers. This had an appreciable adverse effect on the competition (AAEC) in the downstream market of cotton hybrid seeds. (v) It was also alleged that the sub-licence arrangements contravened section 3(1) and section 3(4) of the Competition Act by causing refusal to deal. The CCI held that MMBL was dominant in the relevant market of ‘provision of Bt. Cotton Technology in India’ as well as the downstream market of ‘manufacture and sale of Bt. Cotton seeds in India’. It further found the allegations made by the informants to be prima facie merited since the stringent conditions imposed in the sub-licence agreements discouraged the seed companies from dealing with competitors and amounted to restricting the development of alternate technologies. Further, these conditions were harsh and not reasonable for protecting IPR. Accordingly, the CCI passed an order under section 26(1) of the Competition Act directing the DG to investigate the matter. MMBL sought to oust the jurisdiction of CCI by citing repugnancy between the Competition Act and the Patents Act through a Writ Petition in the Delhi High Court. MMBL argued that:(i) In order for the CCI to determine whether its conduct is anti-competitive, it would be necessary to have a finding on whether the royalty fee/trait value charged is unreasonable and excessive and the terms of the sub-licence agreement are unreasonable, and these latter issues had to be determined by the Controller of Patents under the Patents Act. (ii) The Patents Act occupied the entire field in respect of not only the grant of patents but also the exercise of rights granted to a patentee. (iii) The powers of the Controller of Patents are akin to those of the TRAI in the telecom sector, and in Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel Ltd. (‘Bharti Airtel case’),10 Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel and Ors. [2018], (SC) Civil Appeal Nos 11843-11852 of 2018. the Supreme Court held that the regulation of Points of Interconnection (POIs) fell under the jurisdiction of TRAI and not CCI. We have discussed the findings of the Bharti Airtel case in the jurisdiction heading in Chapter 1. However, the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court dismissed these contentions by relying on its observations in the Ericsson case. It was held that the CCI is not prevented from exercising its jurisdiction relating to specific rights, such as compulsory licensing under the Patents Act if the same gives rise to anti-competitive conduct or abuse of dominance. While the patentee is free to approach the Controller of Patents for grant of a compulsory licence, the same does not prohibit the CCI from passing an order under section 27 of the Competition Act because the provisions of the two enactments do not intend to substitute one another. Further, although Chapter XVI and section 140 of the Patents Act contain provisions which have a common subject matter with the Competition Act, the remedies provided in the Competition Act are materially different from the Patents Act. Hence, there are dual remedies for the same conduct, but the nature of relief in both enactments is substantially different. Therefore, there is no irreconcilable conflict between the Patents Act and the Competition Act and the jurisdiction of CCI to entertain complaints regarding abuse of dominance with respect to patent rights cannot be excluded. The key observations of the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court are as follows:(i) In conformity with the TRIPS Agreement, the Patents Act was amended and now contains provisions for grant of compulsory licences in certain cases. Further, section 140 of the Patents Act also prohibits inclusion of certain restrictive conditions in certain contracts – contracts relating to sale or lease of a patented article, licence to manufacture a patented article or to work any patented process. However, the jurisdiction of CCI is not excluded from examining matters relating to certain rights granted under the Patents Act or any alleged anticompetitive conduct of any enterprise, including abuse of its dominant position. (ii) Although section 60 of the Competition Act expressly provides that it would be given an overriding effect, the same would not whittle down the provisions of the Patents Act. Section 62 of the Competition Act which expressly provides that the Competition Act would be in addition to and not in derogation of the provisions of any other law for the time being in force, clearly expresses the legislative intent that the Competition Act is in addition to other laws and not in substitution thereof. (iii) Section 84 of the Patents Act provides for grant of compulsory licences in certain cases where reasonable requirement of the public in respect of patented inventions has not been satisfied. Section 85 of the Patents Act provides for revocation of patents if, after the expiry of two years from the date of grant of compulsory licence, the patented invention has not been worked in the territory of India and the reasonable requirements of the public with respect to the patent have not been satisfied. The instances listed in section 84(7) of the Patents Act can in certain circumstances be considered an abuse of dominance if the patentee is dominant in the relevant market. The orders that can be passed by the CCI under section 27 of the Competition Act in respect of abuse of dominant position are materially different from the remedies that are available under section 84 of the Patents Act. In certain cases, it may be open for a prospective licensee to approach the Controller for grant of a compulsory licence. However, the same would not be inconsistent with the CCI passing an appropriate order under section 27 of the Competition Act. (iv) Under section 21A of the Competition Act, the CCI can make a reference to any regulator where in the course of proceedings, the CCI proposes to take any decision which may be contrary to provisions of any statute, the implementation of which has been entrusted to any statutory authority. Similarly, section 21 of the Competition Act enables any statutory authority, which is charged with administration of any statute, to make a reference to CCI if it proposes to take any decision which may be contrary to the provisions of the Competition Act. These provisions clearly indicate that the intention of the Parliament was to ensure that the provisions of the Competition Act were implemented in addition to the provisions of other statutes. Hence, there was no irreconcilable repugnancy or conflict between the Competition Act and the Patents Act. (v) A comparison with TRAI in the Bharti Airtel case is misplaced since the issue of whether an adequate number of POIs had been provided by service providers for connecting with the service rolled out fell squarely within TRAI’s scope of regulatory powers, which are all pervasive. A ‘Controller’ does not regulate the exercise of patent rights in such a pervasive manner due to the obvious reason that ‘patents’ are not an industry. The nature of the role performed by a Controller thus cannot be equated to that of the CCI since the expertise and the nature of inquiry conducted, and the relief that can be granted by both the bodies is materially different. Hence, the Supreme Court’s decision in the Bharti Airtel case is not an authority for the proposition that wherever there is a statutory regulator, the complaint must be first brought before the Regulator and examination of a complaint by the CCI is contingent on the findings of the Regulator. On the aforesaid basis, the Delhi High Court upheld CCI’s order. However, the position of law has taken a U-turn with the Division Bench judgment in the Ericsson case. It held that the CCI cannot exercise jurisdiction over the actions of an enterprise that are in exercise of its rights as a patentee. As a result, it set aside the findings of the Single Judge Bench that there is no overlap between the two enactments and that the CCI has powers that the Controller does not have. It also set aside the finding to the effect that the Patents Act is only defining the contours of patent rights, whereas the Competition Act deals with the abuse of rights, including patent rights, which can also not be sustained. These findings were premised on the following analysis:(i) The question of whether an agreement under which a patent is licensed will cause an AAEC within India or will amount to an abuse of dominant position is not one that is reserved for the CCI. On the contrary, the factors that the CCI is required to consider under section 19(3) and 19(4) when assessing a potential violation of section 3 or 4 of the Competition Act are not very different from those that the Controller, in exercise of power to grant a compulsory licence, will consider in terms of section 84(6) and 84(7), read with other provisions of the Patents Act. Consequently, the inquiry proposed to be conducted by the CCI in respect of an assertion of patent rights is nearly identical to that which the Controller will conduct under Chapter XVI of the Patents Act. (ii) The legislative intent of the Patents Act is for the field pertaining to patents, unreasonable conditions in agreements of licensing, abuse of status as a patentee, inquiry in respect thereof, and relief that is to be granted are all to be governed by the Patents Act, and Chapter XVI of the Patents Act is a complete code in itself on all issues in this regard. On the other hand, the Competition Act is a general legislation pertaining to anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominant position generally. The inclusion of section 84(6)(iv) in the Patents Act by way of an amendment after the Competition Act was passed with section 3(5)(i)(b) is particularly instructive of the above legislative intent as regards anti-competitive agreements. (iii) For deciding an application for compulsory licensing, the Controller is empowered by the Patents Act to consider the reasonability of conditions imposed in a licence agreement. The CCI is empowered under the Competition Act to examine anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominant position. However, under section 3(5)(i)(b) of the Competition Act, reasonable conditions being imposed in an agreement concerning exercise of rights under the Patents Act are exempted from examination. This is indicative of the legislature’s intendment as to the exclusive domain of the Patents Act regarding reasonable conditions. (iv) It also observed that the Patents Act is the special statute and not the Competition Act. Since Chapter XVI of the Patents Act is a subsequent legislation as compared to the Competition Act, an application of the maxims generalia specialibus non derogant (i.e., the general does not detract from the specific) or lex posterior derogat priori (i.e., the later law prevails over the earlier law), the Patents Act must prevail over the Competition Act on the issue of exercise of rights by a patentee under the Patents Act. (v) An assessment of even the purpose and policy underlying the two enactments and the clear intendment conveyed by the language of the relevant provisions therein reveals that the Patents Act must necessarily prevail over that of the Competition Act. This is because while the Competition Act deals with the subjects of anti-competitive agreements and abuse of dominant position ‘generally’, the Patents Act ‘deals with these subjects specifically in the context of patents’. It is pointed out that the Division Bench judgment is likely to be challenged by the CCI. The Supreme Court’s finding on this aspect will be critical since the latest decision of the High Court seeks to dislodge the jurisprudence of CCI examining anti-competitive conduct. Further, it may be argued that the Division Bench judgment of the Delhi High Court is contrary to the observations of the Supreme Court in the Bharti Airtel case, wherein it has been mentioned that the CCI is the primary regulator which has the mandate to look into anti-competitive practices, even where is an industry regulator. The Supreme Court in Competition Commission of India v. Bharti Airtel and Ors.11 Ibid., paras 86-92. held that there were no overlaps in the jurisdiction of TRAI (telecom regulator) and CCI (competition regulator) because the function of the CCI is distinct from the function of TRAI under the TRAI Act, and the Competition Act is also a special statute which deals with anti-competition. Moreover, it is within the exclusive domain of the CCI to find out whether a particular agreement will cause AAEC within the relevant market in India. On this basis, the submission that between the two statutes, the TRAI Act would prevail as it is a complete code in itself which regulates the telecom sector in its entirety, including the aspects of competition, was rejected. The said case dealt with a factual matrix wherein the Supreme Court held that the matter under inquiry involved certain aspects which require telecom expertise; hence, the Supreme Court ordered that TRAI must look at such jurisdictional aspects, and thereafter, CCI can look into the competition law aspect. Hence, the same logic may be argued in the Ericsson matter as well when the CCI decides to appeal the Ericsson order before the Supreme Court. The issue of jurisdiction of CCI to determine the anti-competitive nature of conduct emanating out of IPRs was also raised in the case of Biocon Limited v. F. Hoffman-La Roche AG 12 Biocon Limited v. F. Hoffman-La Roche AG, Case No. 68 of 2016. (‘Roche case’). Roche had developed a monoclonal antibody called ‘Trastuzumab’, which targeted a specific form of breast cancer. This was marketed through a drug formulation named ‘HERCEPTIN’, which was priced at INR 1,20,000 per vial. In 2012, Roche withdrew HERCEPTIN from the market and introduced a low-cost version of Trastuzumab known as ‘BICELTIS’ which was priced at INR 75,000 per vial, due to the threat of development of a biosimilar version of Trastuzumab by its competitors and to avoid the imposition of a compulsory licence. Subsequently, Biocon and Mylan Pharmaceuticals (informants) collaborated to introduce a biosimilar version of Trastuzumab which was to be priced at INR 57,500 per vial, after receiving a licence from the Drugs Control Department, Karnataka. In order to prevent the entry of new players into the market of Trastuzumab, Roche filed a suit before the Delhi High Court. The informants filed the information for abuse of dominant position by Roche before the CCI. Roche challenged the maintainability of the information by arguing that the issues raised in the information are squarely covered by the Civil Suit pending before the Delhi High Court. The informants responded by arguing that the reliefs available from both the forums, i.e., the Delhi High Court and the CCI, are distinct. While the issues before the Delhi High Court pertain to the validity of approvals granted by the Drug Controller General of India (DCGI) to Biocon, the primary issue before the CCI was whether the Roche Group’s conduct in the market is abusive or not. The CCI upheld the submissions of the informants by relying on the observations of the Singh Judge Bench Judgment by the Delhi High Court in the Ericsson case, as discussed previously. The main issues before the Delhi High Court relate to the propriety of the approvals granted by the DCGI to the informants’ products and the alleged passing off by the informants of their products as Roche’s products. The CCI in the Roche case held that even if various issues have been highlighted by the informants in their defence in the Civil Suit, the Delhi High Court will only adjudicate upon the issues which are pending before it, and it cannot take away their right to approach the CCI for a remedy enshrined under section 27 of the Competition Act. Whether Roche Group is dominant and has abused its dominant position or not is a question that is covered under the provisions of the Act and falls under the exclusive jurisdiction of the CCI under section 61 of the Competition Act. Hence, the pending Civil Suit in the Delhi High Court does not impede the CCI’s jurisdiction to look into the present matter. Once the investigation commenced, the DG sought various information from Roche, including copies of board meetings and marketing arrangements entered into between the petitioner and its parent company, along with pricing structure, etc. Consequently, Roche filed a writ petition before the Delhi High Court seeking a stay against the directions of the DG. Although the Delhi High Court granted the stay, it is important to note that the basis of the same was not an issue of jurisdiction of the CCI but rather the nature of the inquiry being conducted by the DG. The Delhi High Court did not make any adverse observations regarding the power of CCI to cause an investigation into the alleged abusive conduct of Roche. It is reiterated that this stay on investigation should not be confused as precluding or whittling down the jurisdiction of the CCI in investigating competition issues arising out of patent rights. The stay has been granted owing to the nature of the investigation being conducted and not the right to conduct the investigation in the first place. Thus, the position as it exists today is that the CCI is not excluded from examining the abuse of dominant position emanating out of the conduct of the patent holder as there is no conflict between the Competition Act and the Patents Act.

§5.03 SCOPE OF THE IPR EXEMPTION As mentioned in the preceding chapter, section 3 of the Competition Act deals with the provision relating to anti-competitive agreements and cartels. There is an exemption provided in section 3(5) which states that the provisions with respect to anti-competitive agreements will not be applicable to agreement entered into by any person for restraining any infringement or imposing reasonable conditions for protecting its rights guaranteed under the IPR statutes in India. It has been deliberated earlier that section 3(5) of the Competition Act does not simply remove the jurisdiction of the CCI over IPR-related cases. This aspect was highlighted in Swapan Dey v. Vifor International (AG) (‘Vifor case’),13 Swapan Dey v. Vifor International (AG), Case No. 05 of 2022. wherein it was alleged that Vifor contravened the provisions of section 3(4) and section 4 of the Competition Act by restricting access to its patents of Soluble Ferric Carboxymaltose (FCM) Iron Injectables. FCM is an injectable used for the treatment of iron deficiency/anaemia. Before making its prima facie opinion, CCI sought the response of Vifor, which challenged the jurisdiction of the CCI and raised several preliminary objections. Instead of filing a response to the CCI, Vifor filed a writ petition before the Delhi High Court, challenging the jurisdiction of the CCI to entertain the present matter. It argued that the CCI neither has the expertise nor competence to decide a matter falling within the domain of Patent Law. The Delhi High Court, on similar lines mentioned in the Monsanto case, as discussed earlier, held that information under the Competition Act would not be sustainable only if the subject matter of such an information relates to the provisions of the Patents Act. Merely because section 3(5) exempts rights under the Patents Act does not mean that the jurisdiction of CCI would be completely ousted from fulfilling its mandate under the Competition Act. Accordingly, the Delhi High Court directed Vifor to raise its contentions before the CCI. At the outset, the CCI held that the fact that Vifor enjoys patent protection for its product granted under the patent regime in India and exploitation of this right, albeit in a limited manner, through arrangements with two Indian companies under a licensing arrangement with it, is more relevant to the ascertainment of jurisdiction than the presence of physical office (which is an irrelevant consideration for ascertaining jurisdiction). Section 3(5)(i) of the Competition Act does not absolutely exclude the jurisdiction of the CCI in matters falling under section 3 or merely because the matter is also a subject matter of the Patent Act or any other intellectual property law statute. On the contrary, the very construct of section 3(5)(i) of the Competition Act shows that it is applicable on matters which involve the existence of a right under any of the IPR statutes mentioned therein. It rather allows the CCI to examine whether the alleged anti-competitive agreements have some justifications in relation to protecting any IPR of any person, restraining any infringement of such rights or imposing reasonable conditions, as may be necessary for protecting any of such rights. Such a determination of reasonability can be done only when the CCI examines the condition within the overall contours of the Competition Act. The only limitation is that in appropriate cases, CCI has to determine either the existence or non-existence of a right, flowing through IPR held by the party or derived by it and the reasonability of the exercise of such right when posed as a defence before it, in an allegation of violation of section 3 of the Competition Act. There is nothing that ex facie warrants the CCI to circumscribe its powers, in aid of exercise towards sustaining a free and fairer market, when distortions can be induced through an anti-competitive conduct arising out of IPR, the use or non-use of which borders on unreasonableness. The exemption provided under section 3(5) of the Competition Act provides for two different situations: (i) the right of the party to restrain a third party from infringing the relevant IPRs; and (ii) right to impose reasonable conditions to protect the rights conferred under the relevant IPR legislation. The ambit of the exemption has been deliberated by the CCI in various cases, as discussed below. [A] Reasonable Restrictions The term ‘reasonable restriction’ mentioned in section 3(5) of the Competition Act was discussed in detail by the CCI in the case of FICCI – Multiplex Association of India v. United Producers/Distributors Forum.14 FICCI – Multiplex Association of India v. United Producers/Distributors Forum, Case No. 01 of 2009. The facts of the case are similar to the one discussed before the Bombay High Court in the case of Aamir Khan Production Private Limited. It was argued by the United Producers Forum that the copyright owner has the right to exploit the copyright in a manner as it deems fit and that no multiplex owner can demand that the film be released in their theatre and dictate the commercial terms on which such film is to be released. Furthermore, it was also argued by the distributors forum that based on section 3(5) of the Competition Act, the producers have the right to impose reasonable restrictions for protecting rights conferred upon them under the Copyright Act. The CCI disregarded the contention of the producers’ forum by emphasizing the imposition of ‘reasonable’ conditions for ‘protecting’ IPR rights and noted the following:(i) Intellectual property laws do not have any absolute overriding effect on competition law. The IPR exemption mentioned under section 3(5) only enables the right holder to impose reasonable conditions as may be necessary for protecting the rights [emphasis supplied] conferred upon the IPR holders by the IPR statutes mentioned under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. (ii) The producers/distributors had failed to produce any evidence to show the act of not releasing their movies to the multiplexes is a reasonable condition to protect their rights conferred to them under the Copyright Act. The key word is protection, as opposed to commercial exploitation. The connotation and implication of both the terms are different: while ‘exploitation’ denotes taking advantage of the copyright and cannot be allowed if it infringes the right to fair access and competition; a ‘restriction’ can be imposed to protect against the infringement of the rights of the copyright holder, i.e., to prevent commercial exploitation of the copyright by anyone else apart from the copyright owner themselves, so long as it is reasonable. Further, based on the investigation conducted by the DG, it was noted that due to the action of the producers/distributors, the price of tickets of multiplex theatres increased which was borne by the ‘common man’. In such a situation, CCI rejected the plea that the collective decision of not releasing the movie to the multiplex was a reasonable condition to protect their rights. [B] Coverage of IPR Exemption The CCI had dealt, in great detail, with the coverage of the IPR exemption mentioned under section 3(5) of the Competition Act in the Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Limited & Ors.15 Shamsher Kataria v. Honda Siel Cars India Limited & Ors., Case No. 03 of 2011. (‘Automobile Spare Parts case’). The facts of the case are discussed in detail in the chapter on abuse of dominance. With respect to the exemption under section 3(5) of the Competition Act, the case pertained to an agreement entered into between OEMs (i.e., car manufacturers) and OESs (i.e., suppliers of spare parts) for procurement of spare parts by OEMs for both assembly line and aftermarket requirements. The spare parts to be supplied by the OES were of the following categories:(i) where the design, drawing, technical specification, technology, know-how, tools (which are large machines to manufacture the parts), quality parameters, etc., are provided by the OEMs. The OES are required to manufacture and supply such spare parts only to the OEMs according to the specified parameters; (ii) where the patents, know-how, and technology belong to the OES, however, the parts are manufactured based on the specifications, drawings, and designs supplied by the OEM. The tooling/tooling cost may also be borne by the OEM in some of these cases; and (iii) where the spare parts are developed and sold by the OES and are made to their own specification or designs and commonly used in the automobile industry. It was observed by the DG that under the agreement entered into between OEM and OES, the OES were prohibited from supplying the spare parts in the aftermarket unless they received the prior consent of the OEM. As such, in practice, it was found that most of the OESs were not selling directly in the aftermarket. Among other defences raised by the OEMs (which have been discussed in the chapter on abuse of dominance), it was argued by several OEMs that the restrictions of sales on OESs of their proprietary parts to third parties without prior consent would fall within the ambit of reasonable condition to prevent infringements of their IPRs. It was further contended that significant investments are made into research and development facilities based on which these products are manufactured. The CCI did not agree with the contention of the OEMs and analysed the scope of the exemption and made the following key remarks:16 Automobile Spare Parts case, paras 9.1.10-9.1.15. (i) Scope of the exemption and when can it be availed: The CCI clarified that section 3(5) of the Competition Act allows an IPR holder to impose ‘reasonable’ conditions to protect his rights which have been conferred or may be conferred upon him under the relevant IPR statues in India. Therefore, the ambit of the exemption is clear that the IPR must have been conferred prior to availing of the exemption under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. Based on the documents submitted by the OEMs, the CCI concluded that the OEMs have not been able to provide documents to substantiate their claim over a particular type of IPR under the applicable Indian IPR laws. Further, some of the OEMS had contended that the overseas parent corporation of the OEMs had validly held IPRs, which were thereafter transferred to the OEMs by means of a validly executed technology transfer agreement. The CCI, however, noted that the IPR claimed by the parent of the OEMs is territorial in nature and is vested upon the IPR holder only in a particular jurisdiction.
