Thus, in the event that the parent corporation of the OEMs held such IPR rights in the territories where the IPR rights were originally granted, the said rights cannot be said to be granted to the OEMs operating in India by entering into a technology transfer agreement, unless the rights have been granted upon the OEMs which is recognized under the relevant IPR statutes (mentioned in section 3(5) of the Competition Act) in India. Based on the observations of the CCI, for exemption of section 3(5) of the Competition Act to be availed of protection ought to have been granted to the IPR holder, or a process ought to have been initiated for grant of such protection under the relevant IPR statutes in India. (ii) Necessary to protect IPRs – The CCI has adopted an extremely strict interpretation and has held that section 3(5) of the Competition Act only allows the IPR holder to impose reasonable conditions necessary for the protection of the IPRs. Although what is necessary and reasonable would necessarily depend on the facts and circumstances of each case, the CCI in this case held that the restriction imposed on the OES by OEMs not to supply the spare parts in the aftermarket is not necessary to protect their rights guaranteed under the IPR statutes since merely selling spare parts which are manufactured end products, does not necessarily compromise the IPR held by car manufacturers in such products. Further, the CCI observed that the car manufacturers could contractually protect their IPR against these dealers and could still permit them to sell their products. Certain key observations made by the CCI in the Automobile Spare Parts case on the IPR exemption under section 3(5) of the Competition Act are as follows:In determining whether the agreements entered between the OEMs and the OESs would fall within the ambit of the provisions of section 3(5)(i) of the Competition Act, it is necessary to consider, inter alia, the following: a) whether the right which is put forward is correctly characterized as protecting an intellectual property; and b) whether the requirements of the law granting the IPRs are in fact being satisfied. The DG during the course of the investigation had provided an opportunity to the OEMs to confirm the status of the IPRs held by them in India along with the necessary details justifying the claim of exemption pursuant to section 3(5)(i) of the Competition Act. The CCI noted that none of the OEMs had submitted the relevant documentary evidence to successfully establish the grant of the applicable IPRs in India, with respect to the various spare parts pursuant to which such OEMs have claimed the exemption under section 3(5)(i) of the Competition Act. The important criteria for determining whether the exemption under section 3(5)(i) is available or not is to assess whether the condition imposed by the IPR holder can be termed as ‘imposition of a reasonable condition, as may be necessary for the protection of any of his rights ‘which have been or may be conferred upon him under’ the specified IPR statutes mentioned therein’. The CCI was of the view that the concept of protection of an IPR is qualified by the word ‘necessary’. So, the relevant question is whether in the absence of the restrictive condition, would the IPR holder be able to protect his IPR. Further, the statute is clear in its requirement that an IPR must have been conferred (or may be conferred) upon the IPR holder prior to the exception under section 3(5)(i) being available. Based on the analysis mentioned above and other factors (elaborated in Chapter III on abuse of dominance), the CCI directed (apart from levying hefty fines) that the OEMs have to put in place an effective system to make the spare parts and diagnostic tools easily available through an efficient network. Further, OEMS must allow the OES to sell the spare parts in the open market without any restrictions. The matter is currently pending before the Supreme Court; however, it is apposite to mention the relevant observations of the erstwhile COMPAT in this regard. In an appeal before the erstwhile COMPAT,17 Toyota Kirloskar Motor Private Limited and Ors. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors., Cases 60, 61 and 62 of 2014. the above findings of the CCI with respect to reasonability and necessity of IPR restrictions were re-affirmed. The erstwhile COMPAT noted that the restrictions imposed on the sale of spare parts were different for different kinds of vendors, and all of them could not be painted with the same brush. Broadly, it identified two such broad categories of vendors based on the OES-OEM relationship and analysed the impact of the restrictions for each of them:(i) All cases where technology, knowhow, equipment, and tools have been made available to vendors by OEMs themselves, but they are not allowed to sell in the aftermarket. Here, the erstwhile COMPAT observed that if this category of vendors ‘are exclusively manufacturing for one OEM and are entirely dependent upon the OEM for knowhow, technology, designs, drawings, specifications, intellectual property even finances in some cases, then they could well be considered as sub-contractors and there is a rationale for them not to sell in the aftermarket’. (ii) In cases where vendors supply products which are already in their stable but are made to the specifications of the OEMs, they are allowed only with the consent of the OEM. However, no such consent has been ever given. Here, the erstwhile COMPAT observed that those vendors who manufacture their own spare parts but only take the designs and dimensions specific to each model of the vehicle from the OEMs cannot be restricted from selling their products in the aftermarket. This is because, unlike category 1 above, these products are not the exclusive products of the OEMs but the product of the vendor, which has been produced by using its own technology and know-how. Hence, they are dubbed ‘matching quality products’ because they are specific to the vehicle models (and not generic parts) and can be sold independently in the market. Thus, the erstwhile COMPAT found no reason for such vendors to also seek the permission of the OEMs to sell what is essentially their own products, not exclusive to the OEM. However, the only condition in this case would be that such products cannot be sold under the brand names of the OEMs since they are not produced for/by the OEMs. An important argument made by the OEMs regarding the necessity and the reasonableness of restrictions was that the Indian aftermarket is full of counterfeit/spurious spare parts, and by imposing these restrictions, they were simply trying to check the potential of mixing the original with the counterfeit. In determining the reasonability of these restrictions, the erstwhile COMPAT found that the appellants were unable to prove that, first, the restrictions were imposed for the protection of their intellectual property and, second, to check the potential mixing of original and counterfeit products in the market. On the first point, it observed that the ‘protection of intellectual property’ by itself cannot be considered a reasonable condition, especially in light of the overriding character of the Competition Act. On the second point, it held that making genuine spare parts available at reasonable prices is the solution, as having highly priced spare parts with constrained availability coupled with a lack of regulatory environment was the cause of counterfeiting in the first place. Thus, it became necessary to primarily address the situations that were creating the artificial restraints on the release of genuine spare parts in the aftermarket because the restraint on the supply of genuine spare parts is what caused the counterfeit industry to fill up the deficit in the market. Further, this caused the unavailability of genuine spare parts to independent repairers, and ultimately, the customers did not receive genuine parts, thereby impacting the safety and maintenance of their vehicles. Addressing this situation along with the purpose of protecting IPR could be achieved through contractual means and common law remedies. Therefore, the restrictions imposed by the OEMs were neither reasonable nor necessary. It is thus important to consider that merely having justifications would not suffice for the purpose of availing the exemption; the Court would only be convinced if such exemption can be given on facts and circumstances of the case, and the ambit of such exemption is very narrow. Hence, the restrictions imposed by OEMs are neither reasonable nor necessary in terms of the IPR exemption. Thus, the restrictions, if any such are imposed, must meet the reasonability and proportionality test. On this basis, the erstwhile COMPAT generally upheld the directions of the CCI to the OEMs to allow the sale of their respective brands’ spare parts in the aftermarket. It also imposed a penalty of 2% on the OEMs’ annual average turnover from spare parts for the three financial years preceding the year of inquiry and issued eight specific directions to each of the appellants towards opening the spare parts and repairs and maintenance aftermarkets such as making information regarding their spare parts, warranty, maintenance cost, etc., available in the public domain. [1] Reasonability Depends on Facts and Circumstances The scope of section 3(5) of the Competition Act was also clarified in the Monsanto case, wherein MMBL relied on section 3(5) to argue that the issue of whether any of the conditions imposed by a patentee is reasonable or not could only be examined in respect of conditions other than those that relate to restraining infringement of any rights granted under the Patents Act. However, the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court dismissed this contention by observing that imposing reasonable conditions to protect IPR is permitted to the extent that such rights fall within the ambit of the specified statutes under section 3(5) of the Competition Act and the conditions are ‘reasonable’ and ‘necessary’. If the conditions are unreasonable or unnecessary, as in the case of MMBL, then the safe harbour of section 3(5) will not be available. The Delhi High Court observed18 Monsanto Holdings Pvt. Ltd. and Ors. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2020], (Delhi High Court) W.P.(C) Nos 1776/2016 & 3556/2017, paras 48, 49. that the plain language of section 3(5) of the Competition Act makes it apparent that section 3 does not prevent/restrain a person from invoking their right to restrain the infringement of their IPR or to impose reasonable conditions for protecting their IPR. Rather, section 3(5) specifically recognizes these rights of the IPR holder granted under specified statutes such that any agreement entered into with the purpose of infringing a person’s IPR falls afoul of section 3 of the Competition Act. However, such rights are not unqualified, and the safe harbour of section 3(5) is only extended to those agreements and only to such extent as is ‘necessary for protecting’ IPR. The statutory interpretation of the words ‘or to impose reasonable conditions’ placed between two commas must be interpreted as being placed in parenthesis. This means that the safe harbour of section 3(5) allows the IPR holder to impose only reasonable conditions and not unreasonable conditions which are not necessary for the protection of their IPR. Further, in the case of K Sera Sera Digital Cinema Ltd. v. Pen India Ltd. and Ors., 19 K Sera Sera Digital Cinema Ltd. v. Pen India Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 34 of 2018. the informant, i.e., K Sera Sera Digital Cinema Ltd., alleged that the producers and presenters of the movie ‘Kahaani 2’ refused to provide it with the content of the movie but provided the same to other digital cinema service providers alone, in violation of provisions of sections 3 and 4 of the Competition Act. In response, the other digital cinema providers, i.e., UFO Moviez India Ltd. and Real Image Media Technologies Pvt. Ltd., argued that quality and security were the factors for deciding the release of a movie and the informant had caused infringement of copyright in the recent past, by enabling online piracy of another movie called ‘Force 2’. Force 2 was screened in the theatres of the informant, but the movie was leaked and pirated from there. In such a situation, it would be counterintuitive for the producers of the movie ‘Kahaani – 2’ to provide content of the said movie to the informant which was released within two weeks after the incident of piracy. The CCI held that:20 Ibid., paras 10-12. (i) An objective business rationale to protect the commercial interest of the producers and presenters of the movie cannot be overlooked in a proceeding under the Competition Act unless the same is shown to have exclusionary effects or is tainted with an anti-competitive objective. Presently, the movie ‘Kahaani – 2’ was released on 2 December 2016 which was fourteen days after the release of the movie ‘Force 2’. In such a situation, it would be counterintuitive for the producers of the movie ‘Kahaani – 2’ to provide content of the said movie to the informant which was released within two weeks after the incident of piracy. (ii) Further, since the producers and presenters of the movie are likely to have spent considerable effort and money to develop their film ‘Kahaani – 2’, they have every right to decide their business strategy to release the same. The producers of the movie ‘Kahaani 2’ have copyright over the content created by them, and to be protected from any sort of leak/piracy, they must be entitled to take suitable measures to protect their properties from being exploited illegally in the market. (iii) Section 3(5)(i)(a) of the Competition Act clearly provides that application of section 3 shall not restrict the right of any person to impose reasonable conditions as may be necessary for protecting any of its rights conferred upon under the Copyright Act. The decision of the producers and presenters to refuse to exhibit their movies through the informant’s digital service, with whom other producers have had issues of piracy earlier, appears to be taken as a precautionary step to prevent any loss due to piracy. (iv) In any case, they were availing the services of the informant prior to the incident of the piracy. Thus, the above decisions of the CCI make it clear that what is reasonable or unreasonable would depend on the facts and circumstances of each case. The CCI must do a balancing analysis to ensure that the exercise of exclusivities guaranteed under the various IPR statutes in India to an IPR holder does not cause a disruptive influence on the market. Also, CCI must be cautious to ensure that the rights guaranteed to the IPR holder under the IPR legislations are not rendered infructuous. In this regard, it must be noted that the balancing test needs to be done wherein equal importance must be given to safeguarding the business interest of the IPR holder as well as the market situation. Based on the decisional practice of the CCI, it is therefore imperative that the IPR holders are aware that when they enter into any agreement with respect to licensing or otherwise, they cannot enter a practice which may amount to a refusal to deal or other kinds of anti-competitive practices. The IPR exemption is only for the limited purpose of entering into an agreement which may be necessary to protect any of the rights guaranteed under the IPR statutes in India, mentioned therein. As such, the CCI is the body to determine what is necessary and what is reasonable based on the facts and circumstances of each case. It seems like the CCI will allow only those agreements which are least restrictive and totally necessary to protect the rights conferred upon them by the relevant IPR statutes. The key point to be mentioned here is that section 3(5) speaks about the protection of the rights and restrains infringement of the rights under the IPR statutes as opposed to exploitation of the rights guaranteed under the IPR statutes. Further, as deliberated in the Automobile Spare Parts case, unless the IPRs are registered under Indian laws, there is no protection available under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. So, there may be circumstances (like what happened in the Automobile Spare Parts case) where a company possesses a valid patent under a foreign jurisdiction, which is licensed to its group company based in India. The group company in India cannot avail of the IPR exemption unless it can be shown by way of clear and unambiguous documentary evidence that the rights of the group company in India are conferred under or will be conferred under the relevant IPR statutes in India. Further, CCI, as well as the erstwhile COMPAT, have taken a very strict stand on what constitutes ‘necessary’ to protect IPR rights. Given that the idea of IPR rights is to incentivize innovation, the finding of both bodies that one can allow the licensee to sell spare parts in the open market without affecting the IP rights of the IP holder seems to be an interesting proposition. The CCI opined that merely allowing the open market sale of spare parts, which are manufactured end products, would not necessarily compromise the IPR held by car manufacturers in the spare parts. The CCI was of the view that it was very much possible for the car manufacturers to contractually protect their IPR and permit the OESs to sell their products directly in the open market. In circumstances where the products in question (whether spare parts or otherwise) are being manufactured through sub-contracting arrangements, where the R&D costs and the IP remain with the principal, the sub-contractors as a matter of fact are extensions of the OEM’s factory. This is complemented by the opinion of the erstwhile COMPAT that allowing the sale of spare parts in the aftermarket will actually prevent the problem of counterfeiting, which was cited as one of the necessary conditions for imposing the restriction in the first place by the OEMs.21 Toyota Kirloskar Motor Private Limited and Ors. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors., Cases 60, 61 and 62 of 2014. [C] Applicability of Section 3(5) to Trade Secrets The protection of certain IPRs has long been considered an exception to anti-competitive agreements under the Competition Act. However, only those IPRs specified in the Competition Act for this purpose would be considered an exception to such agreements – i.e., a list of statutes enumerated under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. The report prepared by the DG in the Automobile Spare Parts case proceeded on the point that since ‘trade secret’ is not expressly mentioned in section 3(5), it will be able to avail the exemption mentioned under section 3(5) of the Competition Act. Given the interpretation adopted by the CCI for interpreting section 3(5) of providing protection to the rights which are specifically conferred upon under the specific statues mentioned therein, it was likely that the CCI will not provide a safe harbour to trade secrets. However, with the introduction of sub-section (g) in section 3(5) of the Competition Act by virtue of the Competition Amendment Act, 2023, in addition to the above-specified legislations, rights under any other law relating to the protection of other IPR would also be considered an exception to anti-competitive agreements. Thus, the scope of this provision has been expanded to all IPRs. Accordingly, protection would now extend to rights accruing under, inter alia, the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers Rights Act, 2001, Biological Diversity Act, 2002, as well as trade secrets and confidential information (for the protection of which no specific legislation exists), which were previously excluded from the ambit of the provision. [D] Applicability of IPR Defence to Section 4 The statute provides for IPR exemption only for section 3 and not practices covered under section 4 (provision relating to abuse of dominance) since section 4 does not have a parallel exemption of IPR (on the lines of section 3(5), Competition Act). The Report of the Competition Law Review Committee had recommended the inclusion of IPR as a defence of abuse of dominant position pari materia to section 3(5) in the Competition Act. Unlike section 3, which has carved out an exception to anti-competitive agreements if the same is in reasonable exercise of IPR, section 4 of the Competition Act does not provide any such provision. Due to the absence of any such explicit exception under the Competition Act, CCI cannot allow dominant entities to rely on reasonable protection of their IPR in cases of abuse of dominant position. In light of the above recommendation, section 4A was suggested to be included vide the Competition Amendment Bill, 2020, providing for the protection of IPR holders against section 4 as well, empowering them to impose reasonable conditions in the protection of their IPR. The same was also reiterated in the recommendations of the Parliamentary Standing Committee on Finance for the Draft Competition (Amendment) Bill, 2022. However, this amendment was not accepted by the legislature and has not made its way into the Competition (Amendment) Act, 2023, on the basis that IPR defence need not be explicitly included in section 4 since, in the era of the new age economy, mention of IPR defence explicitly may allow a dominant player to abuse its position of dominance. Further, the various legal provisions and the decisional practice of CCI with respect to section 4 are covered in the chapter on abuse of dominance. It has also been confirmed by the CCI, in the Automobile Spare Parts case, that the safe harbour of IPR exemption is not applicable for abuse of dominance actions mentioned under section 4(2) of the Competition Act. Therefore, if an enterprise is found to be dominant pursuant to Explanation (a) to section 4(2) and is indulging in practices amounting to denial of market access or refusal to deal, it is no defence to suggest that such exclusionary conduct is within the scope of their IPR. In appeal, the erstwhile COMPAT reiterated the observations of the CCI that abusive practices under section 4 of the Competition Act cannot be protected under the defence of section 3(5)(i). However, the stringent approach to non-applicability of the IPR defence to section 4 has been substantially diluted by the NCLAT to the extent that a dominant enterprise cannot be forced to share its proprietary rights in Umar Javeed and ors. v. Google LLC and Anr 22 Umar Javeed and ors. v. Google LLC and Anr., Case No. 39 of 2018. (‘Google Android case’), which has been discussed in detail in the chapter on abuse of dominance. In this case, the question arose of access to Google’s APIs, i.e., its intellectual property to its rivals, OEMs and app developers. Google owns the IPR to the Android Operating System (OS), and as per its Brand Guidelines, the ‘Android’ name and the Android logo are property of Google LLC and not part of the assets available through the Android Open-Source Project. Further, the use of the ‘Android’ trademark on hardware, packaging or marketing materials of devices is restricted to Android-compatible devices only. Therefore, to use these IPRs, the OEMs must adhere to the obligations imposed by Google. Accordingly, the CCI observed that Google controls the licensing of trademarks related to Android, and OEMs are dependent on Google for using IPR related to Android. Consequently, CCI directed that: ‘Google shall not deny access to its Play Services APIs to disadvantage OEMs, app developers and its existing or potential competitors. This would ensure interoperability of apps between Android OS which complies with compatibility requirements of Google and Android Forks…’ In an appeal before the NCLAT, Google argued that it cannot be asked to share APIs as it constitutes the IPR of Google and that the CCI did not demonstrate that Play Services APIs fulfil the criteria of ‘essential facility’. Interestingly, the NCLAT affirmed the argument of Google by holding that Google’s APIs constitute its proprietary software into which Google has made investments for scientific and technical development. Thus, Google is allowed to enjoy the fruits of its labour. Accordingly, the NCLAT set aside the direction of CCI to allow open access to Google’s APIs. This is an important observation as it accounts for one of the rare cases where a dominant firm has been allowed to restrict access to its IPR. The other aspects of this case have been discussed in more detail under the heading of compulsory licensing. It is relevant to mention that the said observations are presently under challenge before the Supreme Court.

§5.04 ANTI-COMPETITIVE PRACTICES EMANATING OUT OF IPRS With competition law in India having surpassed more than fourteen years of existence, the CCI has discussed the anti-competitive prices emanating out of IPRs on several occasions. Further, the CCI, in its advocacy measures, has highlighted certain practices of abusing IPR rights which may fall afoul of the provisions of the Competition Act. This part of the chapter is divided into two categories based on the decisional practice of the CCI and advocacy initiatives: pricing and non-pricing abuse. As has been discussed above, the scope of section 3(5) of the Competition Act is very limited, and the decisional practice of the CCI suggests that it will rarely be applicable. Therefore, the licensor must be aware of the nuances of competition law prior to entering into agreements with licensors to monetize their operations by way of earning royalty. The CCI must consider the fact that the creation of IPRs enhances economic efficiency and that it entails substantial investment. As a result, it is felt that the innovator must not be unduly restricted in the exploitation of valuable IPRs and that licensing as such is pro-competitive. Competition law seeks to strike a balance between rewarding the holder of IPRs by allowing many forms of legitimate exploitation of those rights while at the same time safeguarding competition. Yet, since the licence agreements may contain provisions which may potentially cause market distortions, the same ought to be investigated under section 3 or section 4 of the Competition Act. [A] Standard Essential Patents Before explaining pricing and non-pricing abuse, it is important to understand the concept of SEPs. In M/s Best IT World (India) Private Limited (iBall) v. M/s Telefonaktiebolaget L M Ericsson (Publ) and Anr 23 M/s Best IT World (India) Private Limited (iBall) v. M/s Telefonaktiebolaget L M Ericsson (Publ) and Anr., Case 04 of 2015, para. 8. (‘iBall case’), iBall alleged that the practices adopted by Ericsson with regards to its SEPs in respect of 2G, 3G and 4G patents used in smartphones, tablets, etc., are discriminatory through imposition of unfair terms. The CCI noted that ‘standardisation is the process of developing and implementing technical standards. Such technological standards are termed as SEPs. Once a patent is declared as SEP, it faces no competition from other patents until that patent becomes obsolete due to new technology/inventions’. There are three pending investigations (which are presently quashed due to a Division Bench order in the Ericsson case) which are being conducted by the DG investigating the conduct of Ericsson on the licensing terms of SEPs, based on information filed by Micromax Informatics Limited,24 Micromax Informatics Limited v. Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ), Case No. 50 of 2013. Intex Technologies (India) Limited25 Intex Technologies (India) Limited v. Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ), Case No. 76 of 2013. and M/s Best IT World (India) Private Limited (iBall).26 iBall case. The cases deal with the competition law concerns emanating from the licensing of SEPs. As evident, a patent by nature is a statutory monopoly, but an SEP is above a normal patent (to an extent) because the licensee or implementer has no choice but to take a licence of the SEP. It is in this situation a competition law regulator must be vigilant that the SEP holder is not abusing its power and dictating terms to the licensees. Therefore, SEPs are monitored and ensured that the licences are given by the SEP holders on terms that are really Fair, Reasonable, and Non-Discriminatory (FRAND). As also recorded in the observations of CCI in the iBall case, FRAND licences are primarily intended to prevent Patent Hold-up and Royalty Stacking. In the absence of FRAND licences, a licensor can exploit their SEPs for higher royalties because the nature of such patents would make them essential to the business of the licensees, and they would have no other option. It must be noted that licence agreements (especially licence agreements pertaining to SEP) have a lot of benefits and cause efficiencies since the licensee already has access to the necessary production assets, and the licence agreement allows the licensee to gain access to a technology that can be combined with these assets, allows the licensee to exploit new or improved technologies and helps them to launch new products which will, in turn, add consumer benefits. While the investigation against Ericsson has been quashed by virtue of the Division Bench judgment discussed earlier, the same is likely to go up in appeal since, as discussed above, there are some areas of challenge. Nevertheless, the observations of the CCI assume importance in the context of how misuse of SEPs can distort competition in the market. The CCI observed that27 Ibid., para. 10. since the usefulness of complex products and services depends on their interoperability, competing manufacturers, customers and suppliers engage in standard-setting practices to enhance the value of these complex products by setting technological standards for the design and use of such products. Consequently, there is a possibility of a ‘hold-up’ by the owner of the patent where such standard technologies have patent protection. Hold-up refers to a situation where the patent owner can demand higher royalties or impose more costly or burdensome licensing terms because of the patent having become a market standard, i.e., been declared as an SEP. This can undermine the integrity of standard-setting activities and the competitive process of choosing among technologies because, ultimately, the high costs of such SEPs are transferred to the end consumers. Similarly, a single product may use different patents of the same or different licensors. When this happens, the manufacturer of the product has to add up or ‘stack-together’ all these different royalties or costs to determine the total royalty burden to be borne by them. This is called ‘royalty-stacking.’ In the Ericsson case, the Single Judge of Delhi High Court observed that there are grounds to hold that seeking injunctive reliefs by an SEP holder in certain circumstances may amount to abuse of its dominant position. The rationale for this is that the risk of suffering injunctions would, in certain circumstances, clearly exert undue pressure on an implementer and, thus, place him in a disadvantageous bargaining position vis-à-vis an SEP holder. A patent holder has a statutory right to file a suit for infringement, but the Competition Act is not concerned with the rights of a person or an enterprise but the exercise of such rights. The position of a proprietor of an SEP cannot be equated with a proprietor of a patent which is not essential to an industry standard. While in the former case, a non-infringing patent is not available to a dealer/manufacturer, in the latter case, the dealer/manufacturer may have other non-infringing options. It is, thus, essential that the bargaining power of a dealer/manufacturer implementing the standard be protected and preserved. As a result, licence agreements may also foreclose competitors by raising their costs or by denying access to essential inputs (also known as the essential facilities doctrine). While the said decision has been overturned by the Division Bench of the High Court, the matter will most likely be challenged in appeal before the Supreme Court. Notably, the Division Bench of the High Court did not consider some of the aspects which were held by the Single Judge; thus, it would be interesting to see how the Supreme Court addresses such legal issues. In any case, there is an argument to make that the jurisdiction of the CCI cannot be ousted completely due to the express language of section 3(5) of the Act. Apart from the above, another form of non-pricing abuse emanating out of IPRs is fraudulent litigation, wherein deceptive practices like sham litigation, strategic timing of litigation, enforcement of invalid patents, etc., are employed by an IPR holder to assert their rights and harm their competitors. This has been discussed subsequently under the heading of non-pricing abuse. [B] Pricing Abuse There are certain investigation orders passed by the CCI relating to abuse of dominance. The prima facie case was for abuse with respect to imposing excessive and discriminatory pricing, among other non-pricing abuses. [1] Excessive Pricing One of the points of investigation in the Ericsson case pertains to excessive pricing. Based on the information received, the CCI determined, prima facie, that the relevant product market would be GSM technologies like 3G, EDGE, etc., for 2G, 3G and 4G devices. The CCI observed that Ericsson holds many GSM and CDMA patents. It was observed that Ericsson has 33,000 patents to its credit, with 400 of these patents granted in India. It is the largest holder of SEPs for mobile communications like 2G, 3G and 4G patents used for smartphones, tablets, etc. Further, since Ericsson holds SEPs and there is no other alternate technology in the market, it enjoys ‘complete dominance’ over its present and prospective licensees in the relevant product market. On this basis, Ericsson was found prima facie to be a dominant enterprise in the relevant market. The validity of the terms under a licence agreement on SEPs and the fixing of royalty rates for such SEPs is under investigation. It must be pointed out here that while SEP has immense benefits, it is vulnerable to abuse of market power that it confers upon the SEP holder. In these two cases, CCI determined that prima facie, the proposed licence terms were both unfair and discriminatory. The information filed by Micromax and Intex stated that Ericsson was demanding unfair, discriminatory and exorbitant royalty for its SEPs. On an examination of the evidence produced by the Informant, the CCI noted that the royalty rates charged by Ericsson had no linkage to the patented product since the technology resided only within the chipset, but Ericsson calculated its royalty on the retail price of the entire phone. Therefore, while the technology remained the same, the royalty may vary for two manufacturers based on the pricing of their phones. Thus, the increase in the royalty for patent holders was without any contribution to the product of the licensee. Charging of two different licence fees per unit phone for use of the same technology prima facie was held to be discriminatory and also reflected excessive pricing vis-à-vis high-cost phones. Further, the matter pertains to section 4 of the Competition Act, which does not have any IPR exemption. The pricing-related abuse under investigation in this case is excessive pricing and differential pricing. Considering that the CCI has become a very assertive regulator, an IPR holder must be cautious in entering into licence agreements and exercising its statutory right to charge royalty. It will be interesting to gather the regulatory outlook on such an issue once the matter is finally decided. The CCI has also analysed pricing abuse with respect to excessive pricing relating to the exploitation of rights conferred upon the IPR statutes in the case of HT Media v. Super Cassettes. 28 HT Media v. Super Cassettes, Case No. 40 of 2011, paras 193-199. The abuse in question is related to whether the licence fee imposed by Super Cassettes on the radio operator amounts to excessive pricing and whether the minimum commitment charge being imposed by Super Cassettes amounts to abuse of dominant position. On the issue of excessive pricing, the CCI elaborated that the case of excessive pricing can be made out once the cost incurred by the dominant player is obtained and a comparison is made to the economic value of the product vis-à-vis the cost. It was contended by Super Cassettes that cost analysis for setting a licence fee is not possible since the cost of a sound recording is reflected in the acquisition price paid as a royalty to the owners, and in the event the sound recording is developed in-house, the cost is recognized as recording expense. Further, with respect to direct costs, Super Cassettes has various avenues for commercially exploiting the same, and it is very difficult to apportion the cost of acquisition of sound recording to different revenue streams. Based on the same, the CCI noted that in the absence of a cost date, it is not possible to ascertain whether the fee charged at INR 661 per needle hour would amount to an abuse of excessive pricing or not. Further, CCI stated that the price charged by a dominant player cannot be said to be excessive on the sole ground that the price charged is higher than that charged by its competitors. On the issue of the economic value of the product, the CCI observed that, in this case, the value of a particular sound recording would depend upon its popularity and not its cost. Based on these factors, the CCI held that no case of excessive pricing is being made out. In the Roche case29 Roche case, para. 80. also, the CCI dismissed the challenge of excessive pricing by holding that, being the innovator, Roche might have invested huge sums in the research and development of Trastuzumab. Thus, initial high prices can be attributable to being the reward for innovation. It must be noted that even internationally, it is challenging for a case of excessive pricing to be made out since it is very difficult for authorities to determine the economic value of the product. The approach of the CCI on excessive pricing also suggests that it is not a straightforward exercise to determine whether a fee so charged is excessive. As also discussed in the chapter on abuse of dominance, the decisional practice of CCI indicates the position that it is not a price regulator and does not intend to be one. Hence, it cannot determine the correct fee to be charged. While the CCI did not rule out the possibility of reviewing whether licensing fees charged amount to an abuse of dominant position as being unfair and excessive, the finding that one must assess factors beyond just the actual cost in this market segment to analyse the economic value of the product is apt. Such a finding will have very interesting implications when applied in the context of other IP rights, in particular, patents and know-how. Further, in Ericsson’s case, the CCI was making a foray into the determination of FRAND royalties to determine whether the practice of Ericsson amounts to excessive pricing. Further, it is crucial to note that compulsory licence proceedings under the Copyright Act involve the relevant authority in determining the ‘reasonable royalties’ to be paid to the copyright holder. In the future, an issue that may arise is whether the ‘reasonable royalties’ determined in a given case under the Copyright Act would become the benchmark to determine unfair pricing issues under the Competition Act to decide the cost of the product. Similar provisions under the other IPR statutes in India, like the Patents Act, 1970, contain provisions relating to compulsory licence, and this proceeding will also involve an assessment of the royalties to a patentee. Further, another area of overlap may occur in the case of IP infringement proceedings. IP infringement proceedings could result in monetary damages being awarded to the IPR owner, and one of the methods of determining the quantum of damages is ‘reasonable royalties’ that the IPR owner may have otherwise obtained. In fact, such a dichotomy has already occurred in India, wherein Ericsson was successful in an infringement litigation that entitled Ericsson to receive royalty payments calculated as a percentage of the price of the product. This decision has also recently been affirmed by the Delhi High Court. Whereas, as discussed above, the CCI has ruled that seeking royalties calculated as a percentage of the price of the product as a whole rather than just the chip within that product embodying Ericsson’s patent was prima facie an abuse of dominance and ordered an investigation. It will, therefore, be interesting to view the outlook of the CCI in such a case. It is further important to monitor the regulatory outlook because the IPR holders have the right to monetize their rights, enter into a licence agreement and obtain a stream of revenue by way of royalties. However, the IPR holders must be careful that they do not enter into arrangements which seem to be foul of section 4 of the Competition Act. [2] Differential Pricing Apart from the issue pertaining to excessive pricing, the CCI is also investigating the issue of differential pricing against Ericsson. Apart from the issue of excessive pricing, it was also alleged that the royalty demanded by Ericsson was excessive when compared to royalties charged by other patentees for patents similar to or comparable to the patents held by Ericsson. The CCI noted that Ericsson had subjected all its present and prospective licensees to Non-Disclosure Agreements which prohibit the licensees from disclosing commercial terms between similarly placed patent seekers. CCI noted that entering into such a Non-Disclosure Agreement which prohibits such disclosure of commercial terms implies that royalty being charged from a licensee (Micromax, Intex and iBall in this case) may be more than the royalty being charged from similarly placed licensees. The CCI, in its prima facie order, has noted that refusal to share commercial terms with the informant fortifies the accusation of alleged discriminatory commercial terms imposed by Ericsson on the informant vis-à-vis other licensees. Accordingly, on the issue of differential pricing, the CCI in its prima facie order observed the royalty rates were excessive, unfair, discriminatory and non-transparent because two different licence fees per unit phone were being charged for the use of the same technology.30 Micromax Informatics Limited v. Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ), Case No. 50 of 2013, para. 17; Intex Technologies (India) Limited v. Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (Publ), Case No. 76 of 2013, para. 17. The allegation of discriminatory and excessive pricing was strengthened by the fact that Ericsson forced an Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) upon the consumers, after which each user of the SEPs was unable to know the terms of royalty offered to other users. Since transparency is the hallmark of fairness, such conduct is contrary to the spirit of applying FRAND terms uniformly and fairly to all similarly placed players. Hence, forcing a party to sign such an NDA and imposition of excessive and unfair royalty rates, both prima facie amount to abuse of dominance contrary to section 4 of the Competition Act. In its prima facie order in the iBall case, 31 iBall case, para. 14. the CCI opined that the practices adopted by Ericsson appeared to be discriminatory by charging two different licence fees per phone for use of the same technology, as well as contrary to FRAND terms, by imposing royalties linked with the cost of manufacturing product. Further, the royalty rate charged by Ericsson had no linkage to the functionality of the patented product; rather, it had a linkage to the final price of the manufactured product in which the patent is being used. The CCI also held that forcing a party to execute NDA contrary to the spirit of applying FRAND terms fairly and uniformly to similarly placed players and imposing excessive and unfair royalty rates, prima facie, amount to abuse of dominance in violation of section 4 of the Act. However, mere price discrimination by itself cannot be claimed to be a violation of FRAND terms or abusive in terms of section 4. In the Vifor case discussed under the scope of section 3(5) of the Competition Act above, the informant, inter alia, claimed that Vifor was engaging in price discrimination between its licensees, i.e., Lupin and Emcure. It was alleged that there was no effective price competition between the brands which were sold by the said licensee companies, which offered a lower price while supplying under the public procurement initiative of the government, as opposed to high-priced retail sale. The differences in prices at which these soluble Iron Injectables were offered in other countries were also highlighted by the Informant. The CCI observed that price differentiation based on reasonable classification can be allowed and did not find the same to be discriminatory in nature:32 Case No. 05 of 2022, para. 77. all price differentiations may not be discriminatory, more so when the same is based on reasonable classification of consumers to which they are offered. Prices offered in government procurement may not be comparable with the products being sold in open market on quantity criteria (bulk vs. individual buying) as well as purpose (public purpose or distribution free of cost vs private consumption). The approach taken by the CCI gives a very valuable insight for IPR holders to treat their licensees on an equal footing and ensure uniformity in application to similarly placed players. The CCI has noted that FRAND licences are primarily intended to prevent situations of patent hold-up. This means that when standard technologies are protected by patent rights, there is a possibility for ‘hold-up’ by the patent owner to demand higher royalties or burdensome licensing terms before the standard is chosen. Ultimately, the high costs of such patents get transferred to the final consumers, thus impeding consumer welfare. By standardization, which is a voluntary process, several companies reach a consensus for setting common technology standards under the support of a standard-setting organization, which in the present case is the European Telecommunications Standards Institute. The CCI analysed the policy of the European Telecommunications Standards Institute with respect to licensing of SEPs in the Ericsson matter and noted, based on the IPR policy of the European Telecommunications Standards Institute, that an SEP owner is required to give an irrevocable written undertaking that it is prepared to grant irrevocable licences on FRAND Terms. The same must be applied fairly and uniformly to similarly placed player, i.e., reasonable classification is not precluded. [3] Minimum Commitment Charge In the Super Cassettes case, CCI also discussed the issue of minimum commitment charge. As discussed above, the radio operators had complained to the CCI that Super Cassettes were imposing a minimum commitment charge to be paid per month by the radio operators to Super Cassettes irrespective of actual needle hour (each aggregate of sixty minutes of the actual broadcast of sound recordings by FM radio station excluding commercials, advertisements, voice-over, anchor time, etc.) of broadcast of the music content of Super Cassettes by the radio operators. On investigation by the DG, it was noticed that Super Cassettes requires minimum commitment charge to be paid by the radio operators irrespective of the actual number of needle hours of the music of Super Cassettes that is broadcast. It was further noticed by the DG that, except for Super Cassettes, no other music company is imposing minimum commitment charge. Considering the strength of Super Cassettes, the radio operators have no choice but to accept the onerous conditions. On perusal of some of the agreements, the DG found that the minimum committed needle hours for the playoff of the songs of the opposite party imposed by it are as high as 50%. The CCI agreed with the opinion of the DG that held that imposition of minimum commitment charge is exploitative and exclusionary in nature. The CCI noted that the imposition of a minimum commitment charge by Super Cassettes has an anti-competitive effect on the market as it forecloses other competitors from a substantial share of the market. Since the private radio station is contractually bound to pay Super Cassettes a minimum guaranteed amount, CCI noted that they are likely to broadcast the amount of music that they have already paid for. Therefore, a certain amount of music that plays out on private FM radio stations is already fixed for Super Cassettes. Based on the above, CCI noted that imposition of minimum commitment charge by Super Cassettes is an abuse of dominance. The ruling of the CCI on the issue of minimum commitment charge in this case is certainly an indication to all IP holders (patentees and copyright holders included) that care must be taken in requiring mandatory minimum royalties. Going by the outlook of the CCI in case of Super Cassettes and prima facie order of Ericsson, it appears that CCI is more in favour of licence fee/royalty that has a nexus to the use or value of the licensed product/work. Once the threshold of dominant position is crossed, minimum commitment charge-type clauses will undergo strict scrutiny. [4] The Advocacy Initiatives of the CCI Apart from the decisional guidance by the CCI on the issue of interface between IPR and competition law, the CCI has issued an advocacy booklet on the interface between IPR and competition law,33 Advocacy booklet on Competition law, available at https://www.cci.gov.in/advocacy/publications/advocacy-booklets. highlighting certain enumerative (not exhaustive) pricing practices which may be viewed as anti-competitive and hence void under section 3 and section 4 of the Competition Act:(i) an agreement which provides that royalty will continue to be paid even after the patent has expired or that royalties shall be payable in respect of unpatented know-how as well as the subject matter of the patent; (ii) fixing of price by the licensor at which the licensee would sell. [C] Non-pricing Abuse From the advocacy initiatives of the CCI, it can be noticed that it gives paramount importance to the rights of the IPR holders while also ensuring that the IPR holders, because of their statutory monopoly do not distort the market in its favour. In the Roche case, as discussed above, the CCI observed that in the pharmaceutical industry, apart from pricing strategies, firms also indulge in non-price strategies to unlawfully raise their rivals’ costs or exclude them from the market. Some of these practices which have gained a reasonable degree of acceptance by other competition authorities as being abusive when adopted by dominant entities are as follows:(i) rendering rivals’ products incompatible without adding any technical improvement to the replaced product; (ii) indulging in vexatious litigation purely aimed at harassing rivals; (iii) influencing government or regulatory procedures; and (iv) impeding the entry of generics/biosimilars by denigrating or disparaging rivals’ products. Hence, non-pricing abuse can take many forms, such as discriminatory terms of access to the licence, engaging in vexatious litigation, enforcement of weak or invalid patents, etc. Some of the non-pricing abuses elaborated by the CCI are discussed below: [1] Onerous Terms in the Licence Agreement Apart from the abuse of excessive and discriminatory pricing, CCI has also shown its inclination to inquire into other terms of the licence agreement, which may be anti-competitive. As deliberated above, the prima facie order of the CCI noted that Ericsson is forcing its licensees to enter into a non-disclosure agreement. The non-disclosure agreement provided jurisdiction of Singapore courts. Based on the same, CCI has held that apart from imposing excessive and unfair royalties, imposing of jurisdiction clause which debars the licensees from getting disputes adjudicated in the country where both parties were in business and vesting jurisdiction in a foreign land prima facie was also an abuse of dominance. This finding of CCI assumes importance because CCI is looking at the overall conduct of the IPR holder, which can be evidenced in the manner that they are dealing with the licensees and prospective licensees to analyse whether there is a prima facie case of abuse of dominance. It will be interesting to look at the CCI judgment on this issue in the times to come. In the Monsanto case, the Single Judge of the Delhi High Court expressly observed that section 3(5) of the Competition Act does not mean that a patentee would be free to include onerous conditions under the guise of protecting its rights. In M/s ATOS Worldline India Private Limited v. M/s Verifone India Sales Private Limited, 34 M/s ATOS Worldline India Private Limited v. M/s Verifone India Sales Private Limited, Case No. 56 of 2012. ATOS (a provider of Value-Added Services (VAS) as a third-party processor) alleged that Verifone (a manufacturer of point of sale (POS) terminals) abused its dominance while granting licences for its Software Development Kits (SDK) through unfair and discriminatory conditions in the SDK agreement which enabled the operation of POS terminals. Inter alia, the SDK agreement had a ‘Purpose Clause’ which restricted licensees from using any third party to develop or assist in developing any software that it develops or attempts to develop using the licensed software without first obtaining prior permission from Verifone and disclosing certain confidential information, including the names of its customers. Verifone claimed that the restrictive clauses of the SDK agreement are meant for the purpose of protecting its IPRs. During the investigation, the DG found that this IPR justification was untenable since none of the other POS Terminals vendors have incorporated such restrictive clauses in their SDK agreement. The DG also found that the clauses of the SDK agreement are limiting and restricting the technical or scientific development relating to the prejudice of consumers and the intent of the restrictive clauses in regard to licensing, selling or otherwise transferring any software that the licensee develops was to not allow them its further use. On this basis, the CCI concluded that the intent of Verifone was to exploit the VAS players by either restricting them or sharing the revenue with them because the VAS market is highly profitable and has recurring benefits. Being in a dominant position in the relevant market for POS Terminals in India, Verifone was strengthening its position in the downstream market by imposing restrictive clauses in the SDK agreement and by refusing the VAS providers to allow access to development tools like SDK on reasonable terms and conditions. Hence, Verifone’s arguments that the restrictions were to ensure the safety of its IPR were rejected, and the terms imposed by Verifone in its licence agreement were found to be unfair and discriminatory. The decision of the CCI was also subsequently upheld by the NCLAT, which reaffirmed the CCI’s observations by holding that the clauses of Verifone’s SDK agreement were not as per the prevailing industry practice.35 Verifone India Sales Private Limited v. Competition Commission of India and Anr. [2020], (NCLAT) TA(AT) (Competition) Nos 1 and 2 of 2017 (Old Appeals Nos 82 and 83 of 2015). Being in a position of strength, the requirement of prior disclosure to Verifone about the VAS developed by ATOS amounted to imposition of unfair condition and also limited the provision of VAS service in the market. Further, in M/s. K.C. Marketing v. OPPO Mobiles MU Private Limited, 36 M/s. K.C. Marketing v. OPPO Mobiles MU Private Limited, Case No. 34 of 2018. it was alleged by the informant that certain clauses in the Sub-Super Distributorship Agreement through which it was appointed as the exclusive Sub-Super Mobile Distributor of OPPO mobiles in South and Central Maharashtra were anti-competitive in nature, specifically Clauses 8 and 9 which penalized and restricted online sales and cross-regional sales. It was alleged that such terms amount to using unfair practices. OPPO argued that as far as the restriction on online sales is concerned, the same does not cause AAEC in India and is imposed upon all Sub-Super Distributors pan India without any discrimination because OPPO makes online sales of its products on its own. This OPPO is entitled to do, as being the intellectual property holder of OPPO products, it is free to exploit its intellectual property in any way it pleases. Even though the CCI observed that OPPO smartphones are freely available in the market at competitive prices and are also easily available for purchase online on all major e-commerce websites at discounted rate and by such restriction, no inter-brand or intra-brand competition has been restricted, it is important to note that the CCI expressly rejected the intellectual property argument put forth by OPPO by stating that the same is not a valid justification for imposition of such restriction. [2] Denial of Market Access by Fraudulent Litigation Another manifestation of abuse of dominant position in the context of IPR is through frivolous/fraudulent/vexatious litigation. By misusing the adjudicatory machinery, such as obtaining ex-parte injunctions against smaller players, dominant entities foreclose the market by engaging in sham litigations. This is because such malafide proceedings drain the resources of the competitors of the dominant enterprise, prevent them from using their product/service in the market and thereby, lead to the very antithesis of the objective of IPR protection, i.e., to confer an exclusive right to the owner of the IPR to exploit the same for their benefit. The discussion of denial of market access by fraudulent litigation with regards to IPR emerged in the case of M/s Bull Machines Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s JCB India Ltd. 37 M/s Bull Machines Pvt. Ltd. v. M/s JCB India Ltd, Case No. 105 of 2013. Bull Machines (informant) and JCB were engaged in the development and manufacturing of construction and earthmoving equipment. JCB served Bull Machines with an ex parte interim injunction order at the formal launch of the latter’s low-cost backhoe loaders called ‘Bull Smart’. As a result, Bull Machines was restrained from making, selling, offering for sale, dispatching, advertising, directly or indirectly dealing in/launching backhoe loaders in any manner. Hence, the dealerships of Bull Machines were closed even before the formal sale of Bull Smart began; the informant received negative publicity in the market for its new product and its promotional material, documents, components, etc., were seized. Subsequently, Bull Machines filed a suit for vacation of the ex parte interim stay order before the Delhi High Court and submitted evidence to show that the design registrations of JCB were fraudulently obtained. During the course of the proceedings, the Delhi High Court passed a consent order which, inter alia: (i) suspended the interim injunction and (ii) allowed JCB to inspect Bull Smart at a mutually agreed time, date and place within ten days of the passing of the order. However, JCB voluntarily withdrew its application for ex parte interim injunction nearly ten months after filing the original suit without submitting any inspection report or reply. On this basis, Bull Machines sought intervention from CCI. The entire case of abuse made by Bull Machines was predicated upon the alleged bad faith litigation filed by JCB before the Delhi High Court, alleging that infringement of its design rights was totally false and that the said legal proceedings were only initiated to harass it and prevent the launch of ‘Bull Smart’, which in effect would have competed with backhoe loaders of JCB in the relevant market. Furthermore, Bull Machines argued that the injunction was obtained on the basis it had allegedly infringed the registered designs and copyrights of JCB while manufacturing ‘Bull Smart’, which designs/copyrights themselves were obtained fraudulently. The CCI directed the DG to conduct an investigation into the matter by making the following observations:(i) On the basis of market share, size and resources, dependence of consumers on it, etc., prima facie, it appears that JCB is a dominant entity in the relevant market for the manufacture and sale of backhoe loaders in India. (ii) The predation through abuse of judicial processes presents an increasing threat to competition, particularly due to its relatively low anti-trust visibility. (iii) By abusing their dominant position in the relevant market, JCB sought to stifle competition by denying market access and foreclosing entry of ‘Bull Smart’ in contravention of the provisions of section 4 of the Competition Act. In the Roche case, which was discussed under the jurisdiction above, with regard to vexatious litigation, the CCI held that unsuccessful litigation is not an indicator that the same was initiated with a mala-fide intention. Even if the litigation initiated by a dominant enterprise fails, it does not change the possible abusive nature of such tactics which drain the resources of smaller competitors. The CCI also laid down a two-step test to identify if a litigation is vexatious – the legal action (i) must be objectively baseless and (ii) have an anti-competitive intention.38 Roche case, para. 62. If these parameters are satisfied, then there is a prima facie case of vexatious litigation. Under the first limb, it has to be proved that the impugned legal action is objectively baseless and is being used as a tool to harass the defendant. Under the second limb, it has to be proved that the legal action was conceived with an anti-competitive intent/plan to eliminate the competition. Anticompetitive legislation of this kind by a dominant enterprise as a tactic to exhaust a smaller rival’s resources/hinder their entry in the market amounts to abuse of dominance. On application to the facts of the case, CCI did not find the litigations filed by Roche to be baseless or tainted with ulterior motives. Since recourse to legal proceedings is the right of every party, a determination of vexatious litigation should be done in exceptional cases, that too cautiously and sparingly. It is also apposite to mention that while the allegations of vexation litigation were dismissed by the CCI, the CCI expressed that it was cognizant that the informants’ drugs had received approvals from DCGI and had entered the market, despite the attempts made by Roche to discourage and adversely affect the availability of biosimilars in the market. There were a few important observations by the CCI in this regard:(i) The denial of market access within the meaning of section 4(2)(c) of the Competition Act need not be complete and absolute in nature. Even a partial denial of market access that takes away the freedom of a substitute to compete effectively and on merits in the relevant market may amount to a contravention of section 4(2)(c). (ii) It is well-acclaimed and acknowledged that the introduction of generics intervenes with the monopoly position of an innovator/originator drug and infuses competition in the market, which not only brings affordability because of reduced prices but also ensures accessibility. However, since competition intervenes with the monopoly position of the innovator drug, such innovators often resort to strategies to delay or oust the entry of generics/biosimilars. While efforts aimed at meeting competition on merits, e.g., reducing prices and improving quality by introducing improved drugs that leave the generic/bio-similar entrants behind, are legitimate under the Competition Act, resorting to anti-competitive strategies to distort genuine competition goes against the very aim that competition law seeks to achieve. (iii) Competitors, in normal business parlance, indulge in tactics to belittle competitors’ products. However, there is a difference between puffery aimed at promoting one’s own product and adopting practices which disparage or malign the image of competitors, thereby causing competitive disadvantages. This is even more harmful in the pharmaceutical sector, where such disparagement is made to the doctors who are treating the patients of cancer. The line of difference between these two business strategies is very thin, however, when crossed by a dominant enterprise to its own illegal advantage, it warrants intervention by the competition authority. Such disparagement may also have ripple effects within the medical community. In this scenario, those biosimilar manufacturers who do not have strong marketing channels among doctors may be forced out of the market because of abusive denigration by a dominant player. (iv) A dominant enterprise is endowed with a special responsibility not to allow its conduct to impair undistorted competition in the relevant market. The Competition Act places a special responsibility on such enterprise not to conduct its business in a manner which is prohibited under section 4(2) of the Competition Act. Therefore, vexatious or fraudulent litigation under the pretext of IPR protection may not be condoned, and the same can result in a finding of abuse of dominance based on the facts and circumstances of the case. The non-pricing abuse tactics adopted by dominant players are viewed with greater scrutiny owing to the possibility of market distortion and denial of market access to competitors. [3] Advocacy Initiatives of CCI As mentioned above, the CCI in their advocacy booklet has highlighted the following practices (which are enumerative and not exhaustive) of practices of IPR holders as anti-competitive:(i) The practice of patent pooling is where the firms in a manufacturing industry decide to pool their patents and agree not to grant licences to third parties, at the same time fixing quotas and prices. Such a practice will have an anti-competitive effect since the firms will earn supra-normal profits and keep new entrants out of the market. If all the technology is locked in a few hands by a pooling agreement, it will be difficult for outsiders to compete. (ii) Tie-in arrangement where a licensee may be required to acquire goods (unpatented materials, e.g., raw materials) solely from the patentee, thus foreclosing the opportunities of other producers. There could be an arrangement forbidding a licensee to compete or handle goods which compete with those of the patentee. (iii) A clause in the licence agreement which restricts competition in R&D or prohibits a licensee from using rival technology. (iv) Clause in the licence agreement subjecting the licensee to a condition not to challenge the validity of the IPR in question. (v) The licensee may be restricted territorially or according to categories of customers. (vi) A licensee may be coerced by the licensor to take several licences in the intellectual property, even though the licensee may not need all of them. (vii) A condition imposing quality control on the licensed patented product beyond those necessary for guaranteeing the effectiveness of the licensed patent may be an anti-competitive practice. (viii) Restricting the right of the licensee to sell the product of the licensed know-how to persons other than those designated by the licensor may be in violation of competition. (ix) Indemnification of the licensor to meet expenses and action in infringement proceedings is likely to be regarded as anti-competitive. (x) Limiting the maximum amount of use the licensee may make of the patented invention may affect competition. (xi) A condition imposed on the licensee to employ or use staff designated by the licensor is likely to be regarded as anti-competitive. From the above-mentioned enumerations provided by the CCI, one may decipher that while the CCI gives due importance to an IPR holder, the CCI would view all practices as anti-competitive, wherein an IPR holder would misuse the rights conferred upon it by the IPR statutes in India. Given the fact that CCI has interpreted section 3(5) to be applicable in limited circumstances and no parallel protection to IPR in an abuse of dominance investigation, it will be interesting to see the jurisprudence in this arena going forward.

§5.05 REFUSAL TO DEAL AND COMPULSORY LICENSING Section 4 of the Competition Act deals with abuse of dominant position by an enterprise or a group, and one of the abuses of one’s dominant position is to indulge in any practice or practices that result in denial of market access in any manner. We have dealt with aspects of abuse of dominance in the previous chapter. A refusal to license IP exclusively held by a dominant enterprise may also be considered a constructive refusal to supply under the provisions of the Competition Act, and such a refusal may be construed to be an abuse under section 4 of the Competition Act. Section 27(g) of the Competition Act empowers the CCI to pass ‘any other order’ as it deems fit besides imposing a penalty if it finds that a dominant enterprise has acted in contravention of section 4 of the Competition Act. The term ‘any other order’ is like an omnibus clause which will include an order by the CCI issuing compulsory licence if the CCI feels that such order will remedy the market situation distorted by the dominant enterprise. Competition authorities in matured antitrust jurisdictions have granted compulsory licences under the competition provisions of their respective statutes, notwithstanding similar provisions in IP laws. One must note that mere refusal to license IPR will not be considered abuse. Internationally also, a mere refusal to license IPRs is not in itself objectionable under competition laws. However, in exceptional circumstances, the refusal by a dominant company to grant a licence under its IPRs can be an abuse under Article 102 of the TFEU. In Magill, 39 Joined Cases C-241/91 P and C-242/91, Commission of the European Communities v. Magill TV Guide Ltd, paras 48-58. the ECJ held that the refusal by copyright holders in the United Kingdom and Ireland to grant licences to third parties to provide a new comprehensive television listings magazine was abusive under Article 102 TFEU. In the Microsoft 40 Case T-201/04, Microsoft Corp. v. The Computing Technology Industry Association, Inc. and Ors., II-3634, paras 36-41, 330-332, 373-400, 688-712. decision, Microsoft abused its dominant position by refusing to license the specifications required to ensure interoperability between its operating system and work group server operating systems of competitors. Further, the ECJ confirmed that a refusal by a dominant company to license its IPR is an abuse under Article 102 TFEU where three conditions are satisfied, namely: (i) that the refusal is preventing the emergence of a new product for which there is a potential consumer demand, (ii) that it is unjustified, and (iii) that it is such as to exclude any competition on a secondary market. [A] Decisional Practice of CCI We have dealt with the concepts of refusal to deal, essential facilities and denial of market access in the chapter on abuse of dominance. There have been some discussions on this doctrine in the CCI so far. In a few of its decisions, the CCI appears to have endorsed the applicability of the ‘essential facilities doctrine’ under section 4 of the Competition Act. [1] Refusal to Deal In Air Works India (Engineering) Private Limited v. GMR Hyderabad International Airport Limited and Anr., 41 Air Works India (Engineering) Private Limited v. GMR Hyderabad International Airport Limited and Anr., Case No. 30 of 2019. the informant entered into a licence agreement with GMR (the operator and manager of Rajiv Gandhi International Airport Hyderabad) to provide Line Maintenance Services to various airlines. As per the facts, there was an understanding among all licensees operating at the airport for the technical support services that as long as they are carrying on their operations, the licence would continue to be granted. It was alleged by the informant that GMR, owing to its sole control over the airport premises, denied access to the informant for providing the Line Maintenance Services, with an intent to give advantage to its group company, i.e., GAT, which competes directly with the informant at the airport in the downstream market for providing Line Maintenance Services. It was also alleged that the termination of the licence was motivated by the fact that GAT was under tremendous financial difficulty and required support from its parent company, GMR. While assessing the alleged abuse of dominance by GMR in the upstream relevant market for provision of access to airport facilities/premises, the CCI observed that as a general principle, every entity, dominant or otherwise, has the freedom to choose its trading partners. It is a basic and fundamental right to decide with whom an enterprise wishes to deal, and as such refusal to deal is not per se sufficient to conclude contravention of the provisions of the Competition Act. However, there are three guiding conditions, as per the existing literature, where refusal to deal amounts to abuse:42 Ibid., paras 48-59. (i) the refused input is indispensable for an entity in order to compete in the downstream market; (ii) refusal shall most likely eliminate competition on the downstream market; (iii) refusal shall most likely damage consumers. In the context of IPR, the first criterion, i.e., indispensability of the input, becomes important. Substitutability or indispensability is established only when the input refused cannot be obtained through an alternative source. The doctrine of essential facility originated in the US antitrust law, though it soon gained recognition with various antitrust authorities. As per the existing jurisprudence, an asset is essential facility if it fulfils the following main economic conditions:(i) the dominant entity controls access to an essential facility; (ii) the facility cannot reasonably be duplicated by the competitor; (iii) the dominant entity denies access to the competitor; (iv) there should be no alternative means of entering the relevant market at a reasonable cost without having access to the essential facility; (v) there must be spare capacity on the facility in question. In the case of GMR, all the aforesaid conditions have been met with respect to access to the facilities/premises at the airport for providing Line Maintenance Services. GMR, vide the Concession/Licence Agreement, had the exclusive right and privilege to carry out the development, design, financing, construction, commissioning, maintenance, operation and management of the airport for a period of thirty years, which was further extendable for another thirty years at the option of GMR. Hence, GMR was a government-conferred monopoly which controlled access to the airport premises, i.e., the ‘essential facility’ here. Further, the Line Maintenance Services pertain to such requirements and procedures which can be undertaken only at the airport itself. An entity providing such services, therefore, cannot do so without being present in the premises as the service is required between the landing and take-off of the aircraft. Accordingly, the CCI in its prima facie opinion held that given that physical presence at the airport premises is indispensable to provide Line Maintenance Services, non-renewal of the licence arrangement amounts to the informant’s exclusion from the downstream market, which may skew the market in favour of GMR’s group entity, i.e., GAT. Further, in prima facie contravention of section 4(2)(b), the lack of competitive constraints on GMR in such a case where the very entity which is dominant in the upstream market is providing Line Maintenance Services in the downstream market can potentially lead to over-charging from the airlines and also cause adverse impact on the consumers in the long run. In the Vifor case43 Vifor case, para. 78. also, as discussed earlier in this chapter, a contention was raised regarding Vifor’s freedom to choose its trading partners. In this regard, the CCI held that this legal position is not absolute: not every company has a right to seek access to the patent of Vifor unless it demonstrates that there is indeed a need for such access based on the existing supply conditions of an essential product/facility as against its demand by the consumers, so as to affect the market adversely by non-dealing on the part of the entity with significant market power. Further, any company requesting a grant of access should also demonstrate its ability to the patent holder, to satisfy the requirements specified for receipt of the grant of licence. However, one cannot state with legal certainty that the ‘essential facilities’ doctrine is the only basis on which an entity’s refusal to license its intellectual property would amount to an abuse of dominance, and a comprehensive analysis must be undertaken. The words used in the Competition Act are denial of market access, which may not be as high a threshold as required for essential facilities. CCI has also seen issues where market players refuse to license their technology, thereby causing appreciable adverse impact on competition in India. The appropriate relief under the relevant IPR statutes in India in this case would be to force the holder of the technology to license the same, which would be something akin to a compulsory licence. Such refusals under the Competition Act, when limited to decisions of a single entity, will be examined under section 4, though where the refusals involve multiple entities, i.e., ‘group boycott’, the examination may proceed under section 3 of the Competition Act. [2] Compulsory Licensing The leading decision in this regard is the Google Android case, wherein the NCLAT decided in favour of Google on the issue of access to Google’s APIs, i.e., its proprietary IPR, as discussed in the earlier parts of this chapter. The CCI directed Google not to deny access to its APIs so as to disadvantage OEMs, app developers and its existing or potential competitors. However, the NCLAT set aside CCI’s direction and upheld Google’s argument that as the owner of proprietary APIs, it is entitled to reap the benefits of the investment made by it into the scientific and technical development of the APIs. The relevant observations of the NCLAT are mentioned below:44 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, paras 185-188. ‘The various core device features that Google Play Services provide services relating to security and reliability (through installation of Google Play Protect which provides protection from malware), facility to developers through thousands of continually updated APIs such as “Google Cast” for streaming, Google Maps, provision of accurate location information and sending notifications through a messaging transport layer and enabling core services, such as making emergency call, autofill services, sending and receiving files etc. Thus, it is clear that the APIs which include Google Play Services are APIs that provide essential services to the Apps which are part of Google Play Store.
