These are privately developed and maintained by Google and are provided by Google to App developers and OEMs.’ ‘It is abundantly clear … that APIs as developed by Google and of which Google Play Services are an integral and important part, are developed and maintained by Google and continually updated through scientific and technical development by the teams of Google and these APIs are necessary for functionality of the Apps in Google Play Store. We are therefore, clear that the APIs and Google Play Services, which are proprietary items of Google cannot be given in through unhindered access to App developers, OEMs and Google’s existing and potential competitors. … proprietary software such as APIs, which are developed through scientific and technical innovation should fetch value to Google and, therefore, remain an incentive for a technological company/Google to further carry out such development and monetize it through its commercial use.’ As a result, the NCLAT set aside the decision issued by CCI on the basis that access to such APIs cannot be provided to Google’s competitors without going through necessary technical and commercial engagements with Google. As mentioned earlier, the said observations are presently under challenge before the Supreme Court. In essence, the NCLAT has ruled against allowing compulsory licensing, and in this sense, it has laid down the proposition that there cannot be an absolute rule of licensing under the framework of the Indian competition regime. By recognizing that the IPR holder has a right to innovate and compulsory licensing may affect the enjoyment of this right, the NCLAT has strayed from the earlier jurisprudence of the COMPAT, which in some cases upheld the CCI orders providing for licensing like in the Automobile Spare parts case. [B] Practical Guidance As mentioned above, some of the IPR statutes in India provide for provisions relating to compulsory licensing. For instance, under section 84 of the Patents Act, 1970, the Controller of Patents has the power to grant a compulsory licence after the expiry of three years from grant of patent in case the patented invention does not meet the ‘reasonable requirements of the public’, or is ‘not available to the public at a reasonably affordable price’ or ‘is not worked in the territory of India’. In 2012, in an order that was upheld on appeal, the Controller of Patents granted a compulsory licence to Bayer’s patent covering a cancer drug to a generic Indian drug manufacturer on all three grounds.45 Natco Pharma v. Bayer Corporation, Controller of Patents Mumbai, March 2012; Bayer Corporation v. Union of India & Ors. [2013] (IPAB, Chennai). With the ratio of the Division Bench Judgment in the Ericsson case, it appears to have become the domain of the Controller of Patents in exercise of its power to grant a compulsory licence under sections 83, 84 and 89 of the Patents Act to look into questions of unreasonable conditions in agreements of licensing of patents, abuse of status as a patentee, inquiry in respect thereof and relief that is to be granted therefor. There can be similar situations under the Competition Act because the CCI has the power to pass ‘any other order’ under section 27 of the Competition Act which may include provisions for compulsory licensing. However, there is no guidance presently as to under what conditions will CCI pass an order for a compulsory licence. The Copyright Act, 1957, contains similar provisions, though of a narrower scope. Compulsory licence may be issued pursuant to section 31(1)(b) of the Copyrights Act, 1957. It may be issued when the Copyright Board is satisfied that the copyright owners have refused to allow communication to the public, and such refusal is not reasonable. This provision extends only to certain types of copyrighted works and not all. In Music Broadcast Pvt Ltd v. Phonographic Performance Limited,46 Music Choice India Pvt Ltd v. Phonographic Performance Limited, (Copyright Board) (Second Order), 2010. the Copyright Board issued a compulsory licence to musical works in favour of the FM radio industry on a revenue-sharing model; i.e., 2% of the net advertisement earnings of each FM radio station would be set aside to pay the music providers. It will be interesting to see whether the CCI would attempt to adopt the factors considered by the relevant IP authorities and fit them within the scope of the Competition Act or whether the CCI would distance itself from such factors and instead assess the issue under other factors and pass appropriate orders if required. The CCI, therefore, attempts to maintain a critical balance between the rights of the IPR holders and imposing a relief such as compulsory licensing in an abuse of dominance investigation. This appears synonymous with the decisional practice under the EU law where the IP holders do not have a general obligation to license their IPR to competitors, and the ECJ in such context has opined that in ‘exceptional circumstances’, such refusal could result in an abuse of dominance.

§5.06 IP REMEDIES IN MERGERS The CCI has grappled with the issue of IP remedies in a merger situation. As mentioned in detail in the chapter on merger control, merger analysis is a forward-looking exercise, and the CCI has the powers under the Competition Act to issue commitments/remedies to the parties of combination to ensure that the behavioural conduct of the parties to the combination, post the consummation of the combination, does not create any competition law concerns. Commitments in a merger situation may be structural or behavioural in nature. The issue of whether the licensing of IPR constitutes the ‘acquisition’ or ‘transfer’ of assets and consequently requires notification has been a topic of debate. IPR can be transferred through many methods, such as assignment, exclusive licensing, non-exclusive licensing. Hence, there is a need to identify situations where such licensing might trigger merger notification. The CCI, in the merger control order of AT&T and Time Warner Inc.,47 AT&T and Time Warner Inc., Combination Registration No. C-2016/11/456, 13 Feb. 2017. clarified that the licensing of an IP will not in itself constitute a transfer or acquisition if the licence is demonstrably non-exclusive. For this, inter alia, the licensor must establish that it itself continues to use the IPR, or indeed licenses out the IPR to others for wider use, and that the licence is not exclusive, irrespective of terminology used in the licence agreements. Thus, IPR licences that do not meet this requirement will be treated as asset acquisitions that require merger notification to the CCI if no statutory exemption is available. The CCI has dealt with the issuance of structural commitments which involved IP remedies. Structural commitments consist of a divestment package, and the competition regulator must ensure that the divestment so designed consists of a viable business, which, in the hands of the purchaser, is capable of competing with the merged entity. In order to determine the scope of the business to be divested, all tangible and intangible elements – including IPR, know-how and goodwill – must be specified. Also, the manner in which intellectual property will be transferred must also form part of the divestment package. Generally, the intellectual property is transferred by way of licences, and such licences should generally be exclusive licences, free from field or geographical restrictions, and grant the licensee the ability to compete effectively with the parties to the merger. [A] Remedies Issued by CCI In the merger control order of Sun Pharmaceuticals Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited,48 Sun Pharmaceuticals Industries Limited and Ranbaxy Laboratories Limited, Combination Registration No: C-2014/05/170, 5 Dec. 2014. CCI discussed the issue of IP remedies/commitments to alleviate the competition law concerns emanating from the merger of Sun and Ranbaxy. Apart from the brands and assets which formed a part of the divestment package, the CCI noted that for intangible assets (including IPR) which contribute to the current operation or are necessary to ensure the economic viability, marketability and competitiveness of products in the divestment package (retained by the Sun/Ranbaxy for use in their other business), the parties will grant a non-exclusive, irrevocable, royalty-free and perpetual licence to the purchaser. Further, CCI also noted that customer and credit records with respect to divestment products must also be given to the purchaser. These IP remedies were designed with the aim of maintaining the existing level of competition in the market by creating a viable, independent and long-term competitor and ensuring that the approved purchaser had the necessary components to compete with the merged entity. In approving the merger, the CCI directed structural, inter alia, ordering the parties to divest seven products under various brand names and assign all trade mark rights related to these Divestment Brands. The parties in return proposed an amendment that in respect of three trademarks, they be allowed to license them exclusively, perpetually, irrevocably and on a royalty-free basis with the right to sub-license to the purchaser of the trademarks instead of assignment. This was allowed by the CCI on the purchaser’s commitment that it does not require assignment of the said trademarks as the licence, as above, would suffice to enable it to independently sell and market the said three Divestment Products. Hence, the CCI’s order reflected a balanced approach to the transfer of IPR from the IPR holders while ensuring that competitors in the market are able to compete effectively. Further, in the merger of Sony/Zee, 49 Culver Max Entertainment Private Limited, Bangla Entertainment Private Limited, Zee Entertainment Enterprises Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2022/04/923, 4 Oct. 2022. the amalgamation of Culver Max Entertainment Private Limited, Zee Entertainment Enterprises Limited, Bangla Entertainment Private Limited, and Essel Group Participants presented several competition concerns, inter alia, due to existing licence agreements pertaining to content rights, music rights, broadcasting rights, etc. To allay the concerns of the combination, the parties offered the following voluntary structural modifications:(i) Divestment of three TV channels engaged in the Hindi GEC and Hindi Films channels market, i.e., Big Magic, Zee Action and Zee Classic. This would include licensing trademarks, channel names, logos, etc., and transfer of applicable licences, permits, authorizations, and applicable agreements on content licensing and employees. (ii) The purchaser of the divested channels shall, inter alia, be independent/unconnected with the combined entity and have the resources and incentive to sustain the same as a viable and active competitor. Accordingly, SIPL and Viacom18 Media Private Limited were disqualified from bidding for the divested channels. (iii) The parties shall not acquire direct or indirect stake/influence in the divested channels for a period of five years from such sale. Therefore, it can be seen that in a merger situation, CCI places a lot of emphasis on IPR remedies. It will be interesting to note the stand of the CCI in future where they grant remedies in nature of access remedies, rebranding, etc.

§5.07 CONCLUSION The totality of the discussion above reveals that the CCI has adopted a nuanced approach in its decisional practice by considering the competitive implications of the exercise of IPR so as to maintain the balance between promoting competition and protecting IPR. While new issues on the interface of competition and IPR may continue to crop up in the future, an attempt has to be made to preserve this delicate balance. Innovation must not be curbed and hence remedies, if any, must spur and not stifle dynamic innovation.

title: Chapter 6: Digital Markets and Competition Law
container_title: Competition Law in India: A Practical Guide (Second Edition)

sections.title: INTRODUCTION
sections.text: §6.01 INTRODUCTION With digitization, markets have moved beyond the traditional physical realm to the world of the internet. Dubbed ‘digital markets’, this contemporary shift has given rise to an efficient system which reduces costs for businesses. Correspondingly, it reduces a consumer’s search costs, i.e., the cost the consumer would bear to look for the good or service that would best satisfy their want (for, e.g., consider the fuel spent on reaching the markets and the time spent on looking for the desired product). The ‘savings’ in digital markets go beyond price parameters and include time and convenience, which are essential costs in a fast-paced world. Thus, it is clear that the digital wave presents an opportunity for the country to leverage its potential and increase efficiencies, promote consumer welfare and create a favourable ecosystem for businesses.1 Report of the Competition Law Review Committee, 2019, para. 1.1. However, the specific characteristics of digital markets, such as economies of scale, network effects and the multi-sided nature of the markets, make them more susceptible to anti-competitive conduct as there is an increased likelihood of entrenched market power for the entities operating in the market as compared to traditional industries.2 Case COMP/C-3/37.792 Microsoft, decided on 24 Mar. 2004, at para. 470. Consequently, a winner-takes-all scenario emerges and the concentration of market power is amplified quickly. Given the rapid growth and evolution of digital markets, timely and swift interventions by competition authorities become all the more necessary because the pace at which these markets evolve necessitates proactive regulatory measures to mitigate potential harm to competition and consumers, i.e., an ex ante regulatory approach. As digital markets move much beyond nascency and have now become a regular, vital and quintessential feature of the economy, the decisional practice of the CCI and regulatory changes therein merit a discourse. Accordingly, this chapter shall provide an overview of the features, challenges and developments in the interface between digital markets and competition law.

sections.title: MARKET DEFINITION
sections.text: §6.02 MARKET DEFINITION The tools and techniques of market definition remain unchanged whether the market is traditional or digital, being born out of the statutory provisions as discussed earlier in this book. However, an analysis of CCI’s approach reveals that market definition in digital markets may be slightly nuanced. For instance, new trends have emerged in how the CCI deals with the substitutability of different channels of distribution, the peculiar characteristics as well as the wider scope of the relevant geographic market in the context of platform markets. This has been elaborated below. [A] Substitutability of Online and Offline Markets Relevant market determination is based on market realities and an assessment of the factual matrix of each case. An analysis of CCI’s decisional practice reveals a shift in determining the substitutability of different channels/modes of distribution. When CCI received its first case against an online platform in the year 2014,3 Mr. Ashish Ahuja v. Snapdeal.com and Anr., Case No. 17 of 2014, para. 16. i.e., against ‘Snapdeal’, an e-commerce platform, it observed that buyers are shifting from offline to online retail market because of heavy discounts, better choices and convenience. However, if the prices in the online market increase significantly, then the consumer is likely to shift towards the offline market and vice versa. On this basis, the CCI opined that online and offline markets were only two different channels of distribution and not two different relevant markets. This view was subsequently followed in other cases also.4 Mr. Deepak Verma v. Clues Network Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 34 of 2016, paras 10, 11; Bharti Retail Limited and Future Retail Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/05/281, 19 Aug. 2015. In the same year, in Mohit Manglani v. Flipkart and Ors.,5 Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Ors., Case No. 80 of 2014, para. 18. the CCI considered another piece of information alleging anti-competitive effects arising out of the exclusive agreements between e-commerce websites and sellers for the sale of selected products. Therein, while finding that online platforms and brick-and-mortar companies are distinct channels in the same relevant market, the CCI also observed that ‘[i]rrespective of whether we consider e-portal market as a separate relevant product market or as a sub-segment of the market for distribution, none of the entities seemed to be individually dominant’. Thus, this marked the initiation of the idea that online channels may be a different relevant market in themselves. The departure in CCI’s stand became more nuanced in 2018 in All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Private Ltd. and Anr. 6 All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Private Ltd. and Anr., Case No. 20 of 2018, para. 22. (‘AIOVA v. Flipkart case’). In this case, the CCI discussed the increasing interdependence and integration of online e-commerce platforms and offline business retailers. In order to assess the alleged anti-competitive practices of Flipkart, the CCI defined the relevant market as ‘Services provided by online marketplace platforms in India’. This was based on the following reasoning:23. No doubt, to the end consumers, the distinction line between online and offline sellers is sometimes blurry, yet it cannot be denied that online marketplaces offer convenience for sellers as well as the buyers. For the sellers, they save costs in terms of setting up of a store, sales staff, electricity and other maintenance charges. The benefits afforded to buyers includes comfort of shopping from their homes thus saving time, commuting charges and at the same time they can compare multiple goods … . Hence, the relevant market was defined narrowly for the first time by drawing a distinction between online and offline channels of distribution owing to their distinct and different features. This in itself shows that market definition, particularly in digital markets, is not a static concept but a dynamic concept which changes with time, and the relevant market assessment must be done at each point in time. The transition from CCI’s earlier position becomes clearer when we analyse the travel services market. In 2017, CCI approved the merger of MakeMyTrip and Ibibo, where both parties offered travel-related services through the internet.7 MIH Internet SEA Pte Limited, Singapore, Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/451, 18 Jan. 2017, paras 8-10. Therein, CCI observed that online and offline channels of providing travel services were substitutable with each other. As a result, the relevant market was defined as the market of ‘sale of travel and travel related services’, based on a substitutability assessment between online travel agents with other distribution channels from the consumers’ point of view. However, in just a couple of years and by 2019, while examining the allegations of anti-competitive conduct and abuse of dominance by the same entities, the CCI noted that market realities had changed which necessitated a different market definition. In Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Anr. v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. 8 Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Anr. v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 14 of 2019, paras 181-191. (‘MMT-Go case’), the relevant market was delineated as the ‘market for online intermediation services for booking of hotels in India’ on the basis of the unique features of online platforms such as network effects in terms of customer reach and pricing strategies (discussed later in the chapter). Not only was the substitutability analysis carried out from the hotels’ point of view in this case but also the CCI noted that timing is a crucial factor and the assessment in rapidly changing digital markets (such as online travel services) cannot have a static approach. Hence, online and offline channels of distribution were considered to be separate and distinct.9 Ibid., paras 190-216. Online and offline modes, being characteristically different from each other, do not cater to the same set of consumers and, thus, are not interchangeable from the perspective of not only hotels but also end consumers. This has been discussed in more detail under the multi-homing and online travel aggregation. What is important to note is that the legal principles of relevant market delineation remain the same irrespective of whether the firm is digital or traditional; the approach to defining the relevant market will change depending on the market concerned. The approach is: first, the starting point would be the service offered by the enterprise under scrutiny (i.e., MMT-Go, offers to hotels); second, the next step would be to identify such alternate services that are substitutable in terms of price, characteristics and intended use from the perspective of the affected user group (i.e., the hotels’ perspective). The trend that emerges is that in digital markets, there does not exist any one-size-fits-all approach in delineating the relevant market. The peculiar characteristics of the platform, the users served by that platform along with the timing and prevailing market circumstances together necessitate a dynamic approach in conducting the substitutability analysis. Yet, it can be said that the clear takeaway, as upheld in several other cases,10 Shri Vishal Gupta v. Google LLC and Ors., Case Nos 06 & 46 of 2014, para. 64; Vijay Gopal v. Big Tree Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 46 of 2021, paras 24-26. is that online and offline channels are not substitutable or even comparable while determining the relevant market because the services offered by online (marketplace) platforms are not substitutable/interchangeable from the consumer’s perspective. The focal point of difference is that the services offered by online marketplace platforms are marked differently from offline mode. These services are discussed in more detail in the subsequent sections. [B] Platform Markets In the Market Study on E-Commerce in India11 Market Study on E-Commerce in India, 2020, para. 34. published by the CCI, it was observed that ‘online marketplaces are multi-sided platforms that connect sellers, buyers and advertisers to facilitate transactions between them. A platform offers a multisided environment that internalizes transaction costs and takes advantage of the network effects across the different user groups’. Intermediation by online platforms allows businesses to have an online presence to access consumers without the need to operate their own websites. The degree of dependence of sellers on online platforms is rising since consumers increasingly rely on these platforms to search for and purchase products on the internet. This tends to create a scenario of a ‘winner-takes-all’ market. This was further clarified by the CCI in AIOVA v. Flipkart case, 12 AIOVA v. Flipkart case, para. 24. wherein the CCI differentiated between online retail stores and online marketplace platforms on the basis that in online retail stores, the seller (who may or may not own a brick-and-mortar store) owns their portal to sell products thorough an online website. However, in online marketplace platforms, the owner of the online portal offers a platform for buyers and sellers to transact. Hence, the sellers would be interested in selling on the online platforms which are able to attract an increasing number of buyers, thus characterizing the online platforms with network effects. The functioning of platforms was also analysed in the MMT-Go case. Therein, the CCI drew a distinction between transaction and non-transaction platforms,13 MMT-Go case, para. 191. as highlighted in the following flowchart: The CCI further went on to observe that:Even within these sub-segments, the platforms may have different features, e.g., the transaction platforms can be purely ‘matching-making platforms’ where users on different sides of the platform are brought together to effectuate one to one transaction, e.g., cab aggregators like Uber/Ola, or they can be like OTAs where multiple users can see the various options available and decide whether to effectuate a transaction or not. Given the diversity of platform markets, and their different network effects and pricing strategies, it is imperative that the specificities of each platform are appropriately factored in while weighing different approaches and alternatives to market definition. One size fit all approach may lead to absurd outcomes in such markets. Further, the timing is also a crucial factor. The assessment in rapidly changing digital markets cannot have a static approach. Further, platform markets have several unique characteristic features such as network effects, economies of scale, etc., as have been discussed in the subsequent sections of this chapter. The operation of these characteristics makes platform markets distinct and non-substitutable with traditional physical markets. Hence, it emerges that platform markets (which may be further categorized based on the nature of the platform) have distinct characteristics from traditional markets. As a result, assessing the competition concerns and regulating such platforms requires a nuanced and flexible approach so as to adapt to the needs of rapidly evolving digital markets. [C] Wider Scope of Relevant Geographic Market The CCI has traditionally taken an approach in GHCL Limited v. Coal India Limited and Anr. (‘Coal India case’)14 GHCL Limited v. Coal India Limited and Anr., Case No. 08 of 2014, paras 28-30. that only India can be the relevant geographic market under the Competition Act, and the same cannot be global. The CCI has taken this interpretation based on the technical reading of the provisions of the Competition Act. However, transactions in the digital economy are a global phenomenon. Unlike traditional markets which are confined to physical boundaries, digital markets are borderless and may even span across international jurisdictions. The CCI in its decisional practice pertaining to online platforms has observed that the conditions of competition are homogeneous pan-India and hence, the relevant geographic market in such cases is taken as the entirety of India.15 All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Private Limited and Anr., Case No. 20 of 2018, para. 24; Baglekar Akash Kumar v. Google LLC and Anr., Case No. 39 of 2020, para. 16. For instance, in Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc. and Facebook Inc. (‘WhatsApp case’),16 Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc. and Facebook Inc., Case No. 15 of 2020, para. 76. the CCI observed that the functionality of OTT messaging apps through smartphones is the same irrespective of the region or country concerned in terms of price, functionality, operating system (OS) or otherwise. However, the competitive conditions, regulatory architecture and players may vary in different countries/regions. Since conditions for competition are homogenous in India, the geographic area of India was taken as the relevant geographic market for the purposes of assessment. Thus, there is already a recognition that the characteristics, features and services offered by the platform under consideration may be consistent across borders. Therefore, there is perhaps a need for relooking at widening the scope of relevant geographic market in appropriate cases when considering digital markets, which tend to be borderless. Therefore, there may be a need to relook at the CCI interpretation in the Coal India case and hold that in appropriate cases, the relevant market may be global.

sections.title: CHARACTERISTIC FEATURES OF DIGITAL MARKETS
sections.text: §6.03 CHARACTERISTIC FEATURES OF DIGITAL MARKETS All digital markets have certain characteristic features on the basis of which they are not substitutable with traditional markets. The collective operation of these characteristics often tends to make online platforms gain and accumulate market power in a short span of time. [A] Multi-sided Markets The ‘multi-sided’ nature of platforms means that they serve multiple distinct groups of users, with users in at least one of those groups valuing the platform more highly as the number or activity of users in the other group increases. For example, an app store serves both consumers and app developers, with each group valuing increased participation by members of the other. When a business is multi-sided, the profit-maximizing levels of price and output on one side of the platform may depend, in part, on competitive conditions on the other side. For example, some platform businesses may charge a zero or negative price to users on one side of the platform (e.g., consumers), relying on revenue from users on another side (e.g., advertisers) in order to maximize overall profitability. This has been discussed in more detail subsequently. In case of two-sided or multi-sided markets, a platform caters to different user groups which may be interlinked and interdependent. In this context, in the MMT-Go case, 17 MMT-Go case, paras 190, 295. the CCI noted that the intermediation service provided by MMT-Go is provided through a two-sided platform which connects two distinct user groups, i.e., consumers and hotels/hoteliers. Both these user groups positively value the presence of more users on the other side of the platform, as it creates higher visibility and potential sales (for hotels) and a wider variety of options (for consumers). Consequently, any effect analysis needs to take into account both these sets of users to gauge the net effects and to ascertain whether, on a rule of reason analysis, such effects are adverse and appreciable. Similarly, in the Play Store case, 18 Play Store case, para. 116. the CCI observed that app stores can be characterized as multi-sided platforms as they serve different interdependent groups, i.e., OEMs, smartphone users and app developers. It is plausible that in trying to gain favour with one side of the market, the platform may tend to cause or threaten harm to competition on the other side of the market. Hence, an analysis of CCI’s approach reveals that the multi-sided nature of platforms requires an assessment of the interdependence of the different sides on each other, as well as whether the appreciable adverse effects of competition (AAEC) are being felt by both sides of the market. The interdependence of the different sides of the market and the importance of the platform to the different user groups is assessed on the basis of network effects which enable the platform to create an ecosystem and then ‘tip’ the market in its favour. These factors are discussed below. [1] Network Effects Network effects are characterized as the increased usefulness of a service to the users as the number of users using the service increases. This creates a positive feedback loop for services that are offered, wherein an increase in the number of users/input to a service improves the value of the service to other users and, therefore, further attracts new users. This can be understood from the following flowchart which indicates that if we start from any of the points in the cycle, a positive feedback loop comes into operation: As the value derived by each producer increases, the number of producers finding the economic activity profitable also increases. Consequently, as more producers join the economic activity, the value to the consumer increases (for instance, in the form of increasing choice in the market). This then further attracts more consumers, completing the loop. This is a self-reinforcing loop that can be seen in operation in case of platforms such as e-commerce platforms, wherein the number of consumers increases as the number of sellers providing different and more variety of goods increase, which consequently choose the platform to sell products because of access to more consumers. Similarly, operation of network effects in cases of platforms are observed in case of platform service providers for social networking services, wherein a person joins the network only if a sufficient number of people are already part of the user base. This quality of the platform, as discussed in the next section, has the quality of creating barriers to entry for new entrants. The operation of network effects has been discussed by the CCI in several cases in the digital market. For instance, in Umar Javeed v. Google LLC and Ors. (‘Google Android case’),19 Umar Javeed v. Google LLC and Ors., Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 189. it was observed that the Google Play Store, i.e., an app store is a two-sided market which has been able to attract a large number of Android users on one side (due to the presence of a large number of apps) and a large number of app developers on the other side (due to potential to reach a large audience). On one hand, the OS which can run maximum number of apps would be most popular among the users and thus would attract the greatest number of users. On the other hand, app developers would prefer to develop apps for the OS which has the maximum number of users because access to the large user base would result in substantial economies of scale, given the high fixed costs and very low marginal costs associated with the development of such apps. Thus, on this basis, Google was found to enjoy a strong ‘network effect’ of a large user base and app developers in the market for app stores for Android OS. Similar to the Google Android case, cross-side network effects were also found to exist in the prima facie case in Together We Fight Society v. Apple Inc. and Anr. 20 Together We Fight Society v. Apple Inc. and Anr., Case No. 24 of 2021, para. 17. (‘Apple case’), between app developers and app users on Apple’s App Store. In the Google Android case, cross-platform network effects were also found to emanate from the linkages between the general web search market and the adjacent web browser market, such that Google’s dominance in the general web search market had a significant impact on the web browser market due to close links between the web browser market and general web search market.21 Google Android case, para. 290. Similarly, in XYZ v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors. 22 XYZ and Ors. v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., Case Nos 07 of 2020, 14 of 2021 and 35 of 2021, paras 82, 102. (‘Play Store case’), the CCI found that OSs exhibit indirect network effects, i.e., the benefit to users of an OS increases with the volume and quality of native apps they can access on that OS, and similarly, the benefit to app developers increases with the number of users they can access on an OS. It further observed that this is ‘likely to act as a barrier to new entry and expansion as it creates a ‘chicken and egg’ problem i.e., an operating system needs a critical mass of users to attract app developers, but also need a critical mass of app developers to attract users.’ Thus, network effects result in entry barriers because most consumers prefer an OS for which a large number of apps have already been written, and most developers prefer to create apps for an OS that already has a substantial user base. This ‘chicken-and-egg’ situation arising out of the barriers to entry means that apps will continue to be written for existing OS, which in turn ensures that consumers will continue to prefer it over new entrant OSs.23 Ibid., para. 86. Hence, it can be said that the positive feedback loop continues and makes it much more difficult for new entrants to achieve a commercially viable scale. The positive feedback loop is a consequence of the interlinkage and interdependence of the different sides of the market on each other in a multi-sided market. As highlighted in the MMT-Go case, 24 MMT-Go case, para. 190. through its intermediation service, the platform connects two distinct user groups, i.e., consumers and hotels, both of which positively value the increasing presence of each other on the platform. For the assessment of dominance, what was important was the role of the network effects in understanding the degree of dependence of the aggrieved hotels on MMT-Go’s platform.25 Ibid., para. 226. This was also illustrated in the cases against WhatsApp,26 WhatsApp case, paras 86, 87; In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc, Suo Moto Case No. 01 of 2021, para. 26. which is the most widely used app for instant messaging in India. A communication network/platform gets more valuable as more users join it, thereby benefiting from network effects. This, in turn, causes a strong lock-in effect for users, as switching to another platform gets difficult and meaningless until all or most of their social contacts also switch to the same other platform. Consequently, consumers were found to be dependent on WhatsApp, and network effects ensured that customers did not switch to other platforms easily. Barriers to entry may arise indirectly as a result of network effects since they lead to increased switching costs. As a result, new players may be disincentivized from entering the market. In this context, in the AIOVA v. Flipkart case, 27 AIOVA v. Flipkart case, para. 28. it was recognized that while it may be possible for new entrants to create online marketplace platforms, it is difficult for them to breach the advantage gained by incumbent players due to network effects. However, the operation of network effects is not ipso facto a factor which contributes to dominance. This means that that there can be certain countervailing market forces that reduce the ability of even a very large platform to insulate itself from competition. This was seen in the case of Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd. (‘Ola case’),28 Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Case Nos 6 & 74 of 2015, paras 122-132. where the CCI was examining the alleged abusive conduct of Ola in the market of radio taxi services. Basis the facts of the case, CCI was of the opinion that despite Ola having the largest network, the network effects were not strong enough to deter entry and rapid expansion of another big and fierce competitor ‘Uber’ in the relevant market of ‘radio taxi services in Bengaluru’. Further, since radio taxi apps could be easily downloaded on smartphones for free and could co-exist on the same handset, multi-homing was found to be possible for both drivers and riders. A successful network/platform requires that both sides of the market be thick, i.e., there are a large number of participants on both sides of the network/platform so that each participant has a substantial number of potential matches on the other side of the market. More riders means more demand scattered across a geographic region owing to a higher density of riders, leading to more ride requests on a particular platform as compared to its competitor, which in turn fuels the demand for more drivers to serve such riders. In this case, both Uber and Ola were found to be aggressively competing with each other to attract participants, i.e., drivers and riders, and hence, the network effects of one platform were not strong enough to deter the expansion of the other platform. Thus, CCI’s decisional practice29 MMT-Go case, para. 232; Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. Uber India Systems Pvt. Ltd and Ors., Case No. 96 of 2015. indicates that network effects play a pivotal role in dominance assessment, and the important aspect is the strength of the network, as that is what enables a large platform/network to stay dominant and insulate itself from potential competition by making it difficult for new entrants to challenge the established network. A comparison of the Google Android case and the Play Store case with the Ola case, for instance, reveals that network effects by themselves are not a factor for dominance because in all cases it cannot be said that they create insurmountable entry barriers. The takeaway is that what has to be seen is not only the size of the network but also its strength, such that it should have an actual effect on the state of competition in the market. At the same time, the role of interdependencies and interlinkages arising out of the network effects should not be overestimated but should be limited to understanding the impact of such interactions on the substitutability from primarily the aggrieved user side. This has to be assessed based on the facts and circumstances of the case. In the Ola case, the effect of Ola’s network on riders and drivers was mitigated due to corresponding network effects by Uber. However, in the cases pertaining to Google, the CCI noted that there were no effective alternatives to Google for the OEMs and app developers which could overcome the strength of Google’s network and consequently, Google has held to be in a super dominant position. [2] Tipping Effect The operation of economies of scale and network externalities not only favour a concentrated market but also erect entry barriers and present the distinct possibility of all consumers in the market deciding to opt for the product of a particular firm or for a particular technology, therefore leading to a ‘tipping’ of the market in favour of that firm and conferring it with a monopolist position in the market. This results in winner-take-all market outcomes. Thus, digital markets often ‘tip’ quickly and one or two winners or leading players emerge in a short span of time. Attaining a critical mass of users can lead to firms assuming position of entrenched market power. In such markets, it becomes extremely difficult for new entrants (even if such firms do not face any dearth of capital) to dislodge the incumbent from its position and for new players to enter the market. This can be illustrated from the findings of the CCI in the Play Store case. Once there was a critical mass of app developers and users on the Google Play Store, other app stores found it difficult to dislodge Google from its position because the market had tipped in favour of Google. This could be why Google’s Play Store became a ‘must have’ for OEMs, app developers and app users. In the MMT-Go case, 30 MMT-Go case, section 33 Order, para. 114. the CCI granted interim relief to the delisted hotels (informants) by ordering MMT-Go to relist them on its platform. These hotels were delisted pursuant to MMT-Go’s exclusive arrangement with OYO, which was a competitor of the informants. An analysis of CCI’s order reveals that winner-takes-all markets require immediate remedy (here, relisting of the hotels) because otherwise, the market would tip in favour of the incumbent player (MMT-Go and OYO). The following observations of CCI assume importance:Thus, as the facts appear before the Commission, the impugned exclusion under the Agreement has the dangerous probability to irreversibly alter the competitive landscape, especially in the franchisee budget hotel downstream market and may tip the market in favour of OYO causing irreparable harm to competition. A continued operationalisation of this exclusive agreement will therefore, compel all independent hotels seeking franchise services, for improving their brand recognition and for the purpose of signalling to the consumer the unobservable feature of quality to forcibly tie up with OYO. Any other franchisee arrangement with competitors like FabHotels and Treebo would delist them from the dominant intermediary i.e. MMT-Go reducing their visibility and footfalls. It becomes a Hobson’s choice in as much as the ‘market for franchising services for budget hotels in India’ is concerned. Such a likely outcome cannot be ignored and hence requires an immediate redressal. In winner takes all platform markets, if the impugned conduct is not based on the merits, eliminating such anti-competitive behaviour at the earliest assumes utmost importance. Network effects coupled with even small actions by the platforms may exclude and marginalize rivals, and further strengthen these effects that may be difficult to dilute at a later stage. Any remedy at that stage will be too little and too late as the suppliers’ side of the market, i.e. the franchisee service providers, can be potentially eliminated due to the alleged anticompetitive conduct. This case was very interesting since as a matter of practice, the CCI rarely grants interim relief. Therefore, what emerges from this discussion is the tendency of digital markets to tip in favour of a particular player, and thus, there is a discussion on whether India should adopt ex ante regulation. It is because multi-sided markets tend to create winner-takes-all outcome that the tipping effect is not only a factor in the assessment of dominance or anti-competitive effects of an agreement but also a factor in determining how abusive that dominance can be or how such an agreement can cause AAEC. This is why anticompetitive actions need to be nipped in the bud. At the same time, it is to be seen in actuality whether the market actually has a tendency to tip in favour of a particular player, viz. Google’s lack of competition tipped the market in its favour, and Ola and Uber’s competition prevented the tipping from taking place. [3] Creation of an Ecosystem The consequence of network effects and market tipping in favour of the incumbent platform is the creation of an ecosystem. Thus, in order to build up their network effects, platforms aim to create an ecosystem which the users find difficult to leave or attractive to stay within. For instance, in the Google Android case, through a combination of its commercial arrangements, such as anti-fragmentation obligations, lack of access to Google’s proprietary APIs due to which fork developers could not attract app developers, non-availability of ‘must-have’ apps like Play Store, Google Search, etc.31 Google Android case, para. 566. Google created an ecosystem consisting of the following markets: market for licensable OS for smart mobile devices in India, market for app stores for android smart mobile OS, market for general web search in India, market for non-OS specific web browsers in India and market for online video hosting platforms in India. These ecosystems do not operate in silos but are highly interdependent on each other, where a particular conduct of the platform operator influences various other segments of the ecosystem. It has been the continuous defence of Google that their services are offered for ‘free’ which is to the benefit of consumers. However, the said defence has not been appreciated by CCI and NCLAT when it notes that Google is building an ecosystem to derive advertisement revenue. In appeal, the NCLAT32 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, para. 211. observed that the substratum of Android OS on which the mobile ecosystem is based is, therefore, the foundation of the Google ecosystem, comprising of Google search-driven apps and other apps that derive data from users and funnel the traffic and data from them into Google Search results which becomes one of the important bases of its advertisement revenue. To illustrate in the context of one market, once the OEM has installed a particular app store on its devices, the same becomes a gateway between app developers and the users. An app developer to reach the user base on a particular OS/ecosystem (i.e., Android or iOS), must rely on the app store on that particular OS only. Thus, an app developer cannot reach an iOS user through Google’s Play Store and an Android user through Apple’s App Store, so they cannot confine their offerings exclusively to one of the ecosystems in order to expand their reach to the maximum set of consumers.33 Google Android case, para. 199.3. The CCI has also discussed the concept of an ecosystem in the Apple case, 34 Apple case, para. 14. wherein it noted that the smart device ecosystem of Apple (based on iOS) and Google (based on Android OS) have emerged as the two major mobile ecosystems, former being non-licensable and closed source whereas latter being licensable and open source. Being closed source means that Apple’s ecosystem is tightly knit and vertically and exclusively integrated throughout the value chain, wherein it offers apps, an app store as well as smart devices. This unique strategy of platforms to lock in consumers within the ecosystem of their service offerings by leveraging network effects has to be analysed in the context of securing market control. Therefore, the peculiar tendency of platforms to create an ecosystem to further entrench their position in the market also becomes a crucial factor in determining the anti-competitiveness and abusiveness of their conduct, if any. [B] Economies of Scale In the words of the Competition Law Review Committee, ‘Economies of Scale’ or ‘returns to scale’ means that ‘the cost of production is lesser in proportion to the number of customers served. As the customer base of a digital platform increases, the average cost incurred by such companies in providing services will reduce significantly.’35 Report of the Competition Law Review Committee, para. 1.3. While this characteristic may also be present in traditional markets, the tendency of digital markets is such that the returns are maximized (due to network effects, as discussed earlier), thereby creating competitive advantages for incumbent players. This is attributable to the fact that in digital markets, the cost of producing the information/service/value is much less than what is proportional to the number of customers served. The Parliamentary Standing Committee on Finance in its Report on Anti-Competitive Practices by Big Tech Companies (‘Big Tech Report’)36 Standing Committee on Finance, Anti-Competitive Practices by Big Tech Companies (53rd Report, 2022). has also observed that digital markets are essentially different from traditional physical markets because while the former experiences increasing returns to scale (as the size of the firm increases, so do the returns, with rapidly decreasing marginal costs), the latter experiences diminishing returns to scale (as the size of a business entity increases, its returns increase only up to the maximum efficiency point, or the equilibrium point, and then continue to decrease). This means that growing bigger is a competitive disadvantage in physical markets, but in digital markets, it is a significant advantage. To illustrate, in the Play Store case, 37 Play Store case, para. 407. the CCI observed that the different products and services offered by Google to multiple sides of the market are not only ‘intricately intertwined and interwoven with each other, but the products/services offered by the platform operator (Google in this case) derive strength from each other due to economies of scope and scale.’ As a result, it becomes extremely difficult for new entrants to replicate such an ecosystem because ‘Competition in such a scenario is among ecosystems and not just the verticals or independent services. In such a case, the entire platform has to be taken as one unit to account for the cross-market externalities between platform sides, and revenue generated therefrom has to be seen as a whole.’ An analysis of CCI’s approach reveals that economies of scale are interpreted as barriers to entry in the market because the bigger the platform, the more difficult it is for a new or existing player to compete with the strength of that platform. In fact, economies of scale are a rather difficult barrier to overcome because even where entry is not completely deterred, entrants may take time to achieve efficient levels of production, raise capital and build the necessary framework but even despite this entry and sinking of capital, the incumbent could nevertheless retain market power for a substantial period of time. Hence, the existence of such expansion barriers is a key factor in competition assessment and CCI has taken it into account in assessment of large platforms in determining entry and expansion barriers. [C] Switching Costs As the name suggests, switching costs refer to the ‘costs’ incurred by users when they ‘switch’ from one product/service provider to another. These can be of various types, such as financial, psychological (time, efforts) and technological (learning new technologies, etc.). In the Play Store case, 38 Ibid., para. 91. the CCI identified in detail the switching costs of shifting from one OS to another for app developers as well as app users: (i) App developers – Apps developed for one OS cannot be run on another OS due to various platform-differentiating factors, such as programming languages and APIs which are designed and developed specifically for each OS. Porting applications across OS is a tiresome task and requires significant time, resources and effort.39 Ibid., paras 124, 126. (ii) App users – Since apps and in-app content are OS-specific, switching may result in denial of access to these apps. Further, factors such as consumer inertia, satisfaction with the characteristics of Android devices and brand loyalty also act as switching costs. Switching costs can be tangible, for instance, the cost of switching from low-priced Android OS devices to high-priced Apple devices, as well as intangible, such as learning new interfaces to navigate a different mobile OS, transferring existing data, downloading and purchasing existing apps on the new device, etc.40 Ibid., paras 69, 108. In contrast, in the Ola case, riders could easily switch between Ola and Uber through the touch of a button on their smartphones. This means that there were nil switching costs. In Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC and Ors. (‘Google Search Bias’),41 Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC and Ors., Case Nos 07 and 30 of 2012, paras 118, 199, 367. while examining Google’s dominance in the online general web search and search advertising markets, the CCI observed that ‘considering the while high technology services demand continuous innovation, given barriers to entry and Google’s scale advantage, it is unlikely that a large number of users would switch to a competing search engine in the short or medium term.’ Network effects can raise switching costs for users and barriers to entry for potential competitors, and hence, users switch less frequently to other suppliers, which has a market power-enhancing effect. However, in this case, the CCI ultimately found that users are free to switch search engines in their browsers and there is no restriction to this effect. Thus, it can perhaps be said that where there are costs to be incurred, whether financial (e.g., purchasing a new app) or intangible (e.g., loss of data), switching becomes difficult, and we can see ‘platform stickiness,’ i.e., the tendency of users to stick to a platform. The insistence on default settings is also a key point in analysing stickiness. The trend that emerges from CCI’s decisional practice is that switching costs have been assessed in every case pertaining to platform markets, and the substitutability analysis effectively hinges on the same: where switching costs exist, substitutability is low, and where they are absent, substitutability is high. As a result, the presence or absence of switching costs becomes a relevant tool to prove the level of competition and presence of effective alternatives in the market. For example, in the Ola case, the CCI found that there is no switching cost for riders and drivers to switch between Ola and Uber; hence, there was no platform stickiness; conversely, in the Google Android case, due to the default setting, it was found that there is stickiness due to both pre-installation and premium placement requirements imposed by Google on OEMs. [D] Multi-homing Multi-homing refers to the ability of users to simultaneously use and interact with multiple competing platforms or service providers. Rather than ‘single-homing’, i.e., being locked into one platform/service provider, users are free to utilize and choose from multiple competitors. It is important to note that even the prevalence of multi-homing in a digital market is not a necessary indication of competition in the market. This is because digital platforms often operate in niche markets and target distinct user groups or serve separate purposes which may not be substitutable for other platforms that appear to offer similar services. For instance, in the Play Store case, 42 Play Store case, para. 110. the CCI observed that multi-homing by app developers across Apple’s iOS and the App Store on the one hand and Google’s Android OS and the Play Store on the other hand, does not tantamount to substitutability as the two OSs serve a different and distinct set of customers. App developers need to maximize their reach and, thus, would not like to confine their offerings exclusively to one of the ecosystems as it would imply losing a sizable portion of the potential consumers’ revenue who are available on the other platform. Further, the ability to switch between platforms or substitute one platform with another is not the same as multi-homing. This was clarified by the CCI in the Google Android case,43 Google Android case, para. 133. wherein it was observed that (m)ulti-homing by app developers should not be confused with demand-side substitution. The app developers have their apps on both licensable and non-licensable mobile OS, but the fact that app developers have apps both on Play Store and Apple app store does not tantamount to substitutability since the app developers need to have their app on both app stores to reach the maximum consumer base. Thus, these are complementary markets – this does not tantamount to substitutability. This is because they cannot substitute one OS for the other since Android and iOS users are two different and distinct sets of customers. App developers offer their apps in both ecosystems so as to not lose out on a sizable portion of the potential consumers’ revenue who are available on the other platform. In the MMT-Go case44 MMT-Go case, para. 198. also, it was argued that consumers multi-home on different channels of hotel booking. On this point, the CCI observed that even if there are consumers who use both these modes (online as well as offline), they did not appear to be using these channels of booking interchangeably for the same booking, given the characteristically different offering that online and offline modes offer. Hence, ‘multi-homing even by the same class of customers may not necessarily imply substitution in the sense used for the purpose of defining markets. Even if, most customers use multiple methods to book hotels his in itself implies that these methods/channels are complementary and not substitutable even from a customer’s perspective.’ Therefore, the position is that evidence of multi-homing by itself does not mean that CCI will exonerate a firm from dominance. Digital markets and the functioning of platforms in these markets are very complex, such that what may seem to be a perceived alternative may not necessarily amount to demand-side substitution. Further, the key part to analyse here is that the relevant market is defined not from the product perspective (i.e., hotel rooms) but from the service perspective (online versus offline booking service). From the cases discussed above, it can be concluded that even though consumers were multi-homing between online and offline channels of hotel booking in the MMT-Go case and app developers had made their apps available across licensable and non-licensable OS in the Google cases, it was not held to be in the same relevant market. Hence, what has to be seen is whether functional substitutes are available and if they are actually substitutable from the perspective of the consumers (both end consumers and B2B). [E] Prevalence of Zero-Price Strategy In digital markets, the producer only has to bear the cost of offering and developing an initial service. Once the initial service is produced, the cost of offering an additional consumer the option of using that service is very small or zero. For instance, a software developer needs to first expend capital in creating a new software. However, once the software has been developed, the marginal cost incurred by the developer in serving that software to an additional consumer would be very low in relation to the cost incurred while creating the first copy of the software. This feature of digital markets coupled with the propensity for strong network externalities has led to the emergence of a marketing strategy of providing products for free, or the ‘zero-price’ strategy. It is sustainable for firms to adopt this strategy because the digital economy presents many services that may be offered free of charge. Hence, market demand tends to rise which in turn increases the perceived value of the product. Thus, digital firms are able to adopt unique strategies which would not have been viable in a traditional market, such as: introducing free products to leverage network externalities of a market (for instance, as a new entrant in the telecommunications industry); taking advantage of cross-network effects and by pricing services at a zero-price and choosing to earn from other side of the market like advertising revenues instead (e.g., newspapers, free social media, etc.); ‘Freemium’ models, where firms may offer a basic version of the product for free, and charge for premium versions/added features (e.g., music subscriptions). In the Google Android case, 45 Google Android case, paras 208-210. Google had argued that it provided search services to users for free. In this regard, the DG in its investigation report noted that offering a service free of charge is an advantageous commercial strategy for two-sided platforms that connect distinct but interdependent demands: the two sides being general search services and online search advertising. Therefore, advertisers indirectly fund the general search services offered to users. The CCI concluded that the collection and monetization of user data for funding business operations (as discussed in detail subsequently in this chapter) implies that the provision of general search services is an economic activity, even if it is offered for free. Keeping Android OS open and ‘free’ of monetary consideration is thus in as much Google’s interest as it claims it to be for the OEMs and users.46 Ibid., para. 470. In appeal, the NCLAT47 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, para. 210. observed that the multi-sided digital platforms, such as the one operated by Google, consist of software that are interconnected and interact at the machine level and the user level through flow of traffic and data, and therefore, the business model, incentives and the revenue streams are a net result of such interplay of software and programs. Thus, while Google purports to offer its mobile OS ‘free’, there are a number of apps and services on the same device which give rise to huge traffic and data which are effectively monetized through advertisements which bring revenue to Google. The multi-sided nature of these digital platforms is clear from the fact that on one side, there are the OEMs and app developers, while on the second side are the users that generate data and traffic, and on the third side are the advertisers who use these digital platforms for advertising their goods and services. Thus, the substratum of Android OS on which the mobile ecosystem is based, is the foundation of the Google ecosystem, comprising of Google search-driven apps and other apps that derive data from users and funnel the traffic and data from them into Google Search results which becomes one of the important bases of its advertisement revenue. The payment received from advertisers is, therefore, dependent on the data and traffic coming from the various apps, including the search apps feeding primarily in and also other strengthening and refining Google search service. Therefore, an analysis of the decisions of the CCI and the NCLAT reveals that in competition assessment in digital markets, strictly looking at price from the perspective of one side of the market is insufficient because it is possible that one side does not pay in revenue terms at all (e.g., users pay through their data, but search services are effectively zero priced). Rather, a holistic analysis has to be undertaken to assess how the pricing strategy of the firm is functioning across multiple sides of the same market and even in interdependent markets. [F] Access to Data One of the preliminary legal points which may be discussed is whether having access to a large amount of data can be a determining factor in analysing whether an enterprise enjoys a dominant position or not. The Competition Law Review Committee also noted in its Report that section 19(4)(b) of the Competition Act expressly refers to ‘resources of the enterprise’ as a factor for determining dominant position.48 Report of the Competition Law Review Committee, para. 2.15. Hence, it is broad enough to include control over data as a factor for determining the dominant position of an enterprise. Big tech companies, such as social networking, e-commerce, online search platforms, etc., do not follow the conventional model of charging their customers to make a profit, and most of their services are free. One of the ways by which consumers pay for the services is through their data. Data is what these companies require to provide better services or more personalized services, which ultimately hooks the user to such services. This is why it is often said that ‘data is the new oil’ because access to data in large volumes in digital markets is crucial to obtaining and sustaining market power. Once an enterprise has enough number of users and their data, as well as the capacity to convert such data into useful information, it is very difficult for a new enterprise to enter the market. The data advantage of market leaders over a period of time overshadows the offerings of newer platforms in terms of quality and data-fed personalization, while a small entrant struggles to attain a critical mass of users and user data. The more users or businesses get onboarded to a tech giant’s business, the more data the platform is likely to accumulate. With their data pool constantly increasing, the tech giants continue to entrench their position in the digital market. Hence, access to data also acts as an entry barrier. In Vijay Gopal v. Big Tree Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., 49 Vijay Gopal v. Big Tree Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 46 of 2021, para. 34. while examining the data collection capacity of BookMyShow, an online ticket booking platform, the CCI opined that exclusivity relating to data ownership can increase the bargaining power of the platform over time. This is because access to data strengthens network effects, thereby limiting inter-platform competition. Consequently, the aspect of exclusive ownership of and access to data by the intermediary was directed to be investigated. [1] Using Data as a Competitive Advantage In several recent cases, the CCI has discussed the competitive advantage gained by a platform which has access to user data. For instance, in the Google Android case, 50 Google Android case, para. 263. the CCI observed that vertically integrated business operations of Google allowed it to pool the data gathered from each service, and process the same to refine the service offered. This resulted in a superior monetization ability through online ads, thereby conferring a significant and overwhelming competitive edge to Google over its competitors. In its appeal,51 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, paras 210-216. the NCLAT noted that the business model, incentives and revenue streams of digital platforms such as Google in the present case are interconnected through data. Even services which are seemingly offered for free are essentially aimed at giving rise to huge traffic and data which is subsequently monetized through advertisement revenue. In fact, the different services offered by Google are a channel to derive user data and funnel the same into Google Search to refine its services and improve its monetization. On this basis, that the revenue of Google is fuelled by its data advantage and its bundled and interwoven services, the NCLAT refused to reduce the penalty imposed by the CCI upon Google. Similarly, in the Play Store case, 52 Play Store case, paras 278-283, 307, 308. the CCI found on the basis of the DG investigation that Google had access to a significant volume and category of granular data of the app users including complete personal as well as financial transaction information. However, this data was not shared with the app developers, thereby impacting their ability to improve their services and compete effectively in their respective domains. This confiscation resulted in Google having the ability to exploit a vast amount of extremely valuable user data, such as by offering targeted offers and promotions. By having control on the data, Google was also in a position to put its competitors in a disadvantageous position in the downstream markets. What was noteworthy was that CCI took into account that Google’s proprietary apps were competing with the other apps in the ecosystem, and such use of data worked to Google’s advantage. The same situation was discussed in the Apple case, 53 Apple case, para. 29. wherein CCI in its prima facie opinion observed that Apple had access to data collected from the users of its downstream competitors, i.e., app developers which would enable it to improve its own services. However, such competitors may not have access to this data for improvisation/innovation of their own app. This would result in a competitive advantage for Apple over its competitors. In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc.,54 In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc., Suo Moto Case No. 01 of 2021, para. 30. another aspect of competition vis-à-vis data was considered. WhatsApp was alleged to be sharing the personal data of users with its group companies, i.e., other Facebook companies. The CCI observed that one of the stated purposes of data sharing was to target ad offerings on other Facebook products by building user profiles through cross-linking of data collected across services. Such data sharing by WhatsApp with Facebook amounted to degradation of non-price parameters of competition, such as quality, which resulted in objective detriment to consumers without any acceptable justification. This was found to prima facie amount to imposition of unfair terms and conditions upon the users. Therefore, the CCI has considered data to be a tool to boost innovation of the controller of the data to the detriment of other players in the market (who may not have access to such data). As more data accumulates, the competitiveness of the firm also increases, and rivals start to get foreclosed on the market. Further, the above cases also highlight that data not only confers a competitive advantage in the existing business but also often grants a competitive advantage to the firm in related markets as well. [2] Data as a Tool to Create Revenue and New Business Verticals The primary source of revenue for digital platforms collecting data is through monetization of this data in the form of advertisements. Further, with large data to process, an enterprise has the opportunity to anticipate consumer needs and demand in more than one sector. Armed with this data, the enterprise can create its own products/services which the data shows consumers may prefer. This can be seen in emerging digital markets, as discussed in the later parts of this chapter. The Big Tech Report also analysed how access to data gives an advantage to digital platforms by enabling them to ‘collect and store large amounts of data for comprehensively tracking and profiling end customers and business users. Privileged data from one market may be, thus, used by a leading platform in another market to gain competitive advantage. This further leads to the position of the leading platform getting entrenched.’55 Big Tech Report, para. 1.18. In the Google Android case, 56 Google Android case, paras 472, 473. it was found that mandatory pre-installation of Google search (widget on the default home screen) on all Android devices under MADA ensures user traffic and valuable user data for Google that it can collect, combine and leverage for its search advertising business. Hence, through MADA requirements, Google was in a position to leverage its dominant position in the Android ecosystem to maintain and strengthen its dominance in the search advertising sector. The data generated from each app of Google, such as Google Search, Maps, YouTube, etc., can complement the other and, thus, gives ‘Google a big data advantage over its rivals. Importantly, it also raises the willingness to pay by advertisers. Thus, in presence of cross market data advantage, pushing Android with other apps is a business strategy for Google.’ In the Play Store case57 Play Store case, para. 282. also, it was found that Google uses the data of the app developers to support its vast advertisement business by, among other things, selling access to the data back to the developers. This observation was strengthened by virtue of the advertising revenue earned by Google through advertisements in the Google Play Store. Therefore, an analysis of the CCI and NCLAT’s findings on data reveals that data is a factor of market control, and access to large troves of data allows a firm to further entrench its market power. Access to data has wide-ranging implications on competition right from securing a competitive advantage, using it to create entry barriers and foreclosing competition by improving one’s own innovation to the exclusion of others and also expanding into new markets. Since firms cannot be stopped altogether from exploiting the data that they have gathered, at best, they need to be cautious about not abusing such access as a tool to prevent their rivals from competing on merits. [G] Across Platform Parity Agreements Across Platform Parity Agreements (APPAs), also called Retail Most Favoured Nation (MFN) clauses, are stipulations where the sellers guarantee an online platform, terms (whether price and/or non-price) that are at least as favourable as those granted to any other platform, thus ensuring the former an advantage over its competitors. By securing such favourable terms, the platforms attempt to guarantee the best available price and/or terms for a given product to its final consumers. The Competition Law Review Committee also noted in its Report that:58 Report of the Competition Law Review Committee, para. 2.9. MFN clauses can potentially give rise to both pro-competitive benefits and anti-competitive effects on the market. The extent of the effect depends on various factors such as the market structure, the form of the MFN clause used, and the characteristics of the sellers and buyers in the market. In light of this Committee agreed that such agreements should be analysed under the ‘rule of reason’ or ‘effects’ test under Section 3(4) of the Competition Act. Having said that, the Committee noted that in cases where such clauses seek to facilitate a cartel, fix price or supply terms, it will be hit by the provisions of Section 3(3) of the Competition Act. In the MMT-Go case, 59 MMT-Go case, para. 247. the CCI discussed the two categories of APPAs, i.e., wide and narrow parity clauses, as described in the following flowchart: Thus, to illustrate, on the one hand, a wide parity clause prevents a hotel from offering better prices and terms on all other sales/distribution channels (including the hotel’s own website and other OTAs). On the other hand, a narrow parity clause prevents a hotel from offering better prices and terms only on its own website, but it can offer better prices on other channels. In this case, on the basis of the investigation conducted by the DG, the CCI found that MMT-Go imposed two kinds of wide parity clauses on the hotel partners:60 Ibid., paras 246-260. (i) Price/Rate parity obligations – whereby the hotel partners were restrained from offering better prices to other OTAs or on their own websites. (ii) Room parity – whereby hotels could not refuse to provide rooms on MMT-Go’s platform at any given point of time if the rooms were being provided on any other OTA. MMT-Go’s wide price parity obligations were held anti-competitive because of their ability to restrict price competition and, thus, making it difficult for an entrant or an existing player to establish a market presence, thereby creating entry/expansion barriers for new/other platforms due to the hotels’ price parity clause with MMT-Go. Parallelly, the room parity condition restricted the hotels from providing differential availability of rooms to other OTAs/alternate portals, including such OTAs that charge lower commission rates or have unmet demand at a given point in time. Resultantly, the hotels were made to relinquish their price-setting freedom on the MMT-Go platform, which decided the final price payable by consumers after a discount on its platform, and also on all other platforms/channels through the price parity condition. The impact of these parity obligations was that they reduced the incentives for other OTAs to compete with MMT-Go. For instance, even if they offered lower commission than what was charged by MMT-Go, the parity obligations imposed by MMT-Go prevented the hotels from offering better rates to the other OTAs. As a result, the price competition softened and there could be no competition on the prices offered to the end consumer. Given the lack of incentive to compete on commission, the only way to compete would be on the basis of discounts, and consequently, the OTA which would have the maximum capacity to fund discounts would end up intermediating the maximum bookings. Further, the dominant OTA, i.e., MMT-Go, would end up intermediating the maximum number of bookings, and consequently, the hotels would have to pay the maximum commission to MMT-Go. Per the CCI, this had a cascading effect and created an ecosystem which reinforced the strength of MMT-Go and foreclosed competition for other OTAs. In the MMT-Go case, 61 Ibid., para. 264. the CCI also observed that parity terms imposed by MMT-Go cannot be justified on the grounds that they are purportedly an ‘industry practice’. Rather, ‘the effect and implications of a conduct indulged in by a dominant platform on its users and competition in the relevant market is fundamentally different from when a smaller insignificant player does it.’ On this basis, the CCI observed that since presence on MMT-Go was indispensable for hotels to access online customers on account of its dominance, hotels, especially budget hotels with no brand recognition had two choices: either accept the terms that MMT-Go offers or forego significant online opportunity of sale and discoverability.
