Thus, a comparison of the combined effect of deep discounting and parity obligations imposed by a dominant platform (that has already reaped the benefits of network effects in its favour) with the parity conditions of other platforms was not held to be a like-to-like comparison. Hence, this is an extension of the principle of special responsibility of dominant players. In National Restaurant Association of India v. Zomato Limited and Bundl Technologies Private Limited (‘Zomato Swiggy case’)62 National Restaurant Association of India v. Zomato Limited and Bundl Technologies Private Limited, Case No. 16 of 2021, paras 77, 85, 86. also, allegations were raised regarding price parity terms in the agreements/contracts between Zomato/Swiggy and restaurant partners, which prevented the latter from developing their own direct ordering channels or listing on a competing platform by offering more competitive rates. In its prima facie opinion, the CCI observed that such ‘widely defined price parity arrangements/restrictions may result in removal of the incentive for platforms to compete on the commission they charge to restaurants, may inflate the commissions and final prices paid by consumers and may also prevent entry of new low-cost platforms’. Thus, agreements were likely to cause AAEC in the market and were directed to be investigated. [1] Free Riding CCI’s Market Study on E-Commerce63 Market Study on E-Commerce in India, 2020, paras 93-95. noted that price parity clauses can give rise to the following competition concerns:(i) Existing platforms, in the presence of parity clauses, may not have sufficient incentive to compete on commission rates. (ii) A new platform could charge lower commissions to service providers that offered a discounted rate in order to gain a toehold in the market. However, parity clauses imposed by incumbent platforms may serve as entry barriers for such low-cost platforms. (iii) Parity clauses can also help cement coordination or tacit understanding that may exist between platforms by disincentivizing deviation from a consensus rate of commission. Thus, they can potentially lead to higher commission rates and discourage entry. However, parity clauses can be justified on the grounds that they protect investment incentives by preventing free riding. Platforms make considerable investments to improve and better their functionalities. Free riding means that a service provider may take advantage of these features of a superior quality platform to draw customer attention to its product and then sell the product through its website or another platform at a lower price. As a result, competition would be reduced to price alone, and quality would no longer be a consideration since service providers would want to offer their services where they are charged the lowest price. Thus, without price parity restrictions, competition on non-price parameters may be harmed. However, it is a fact-specific question whether a price parity clause may be justified as procompetitive in any market and under what circumstances. The analysis of price parity clauses will, inter alia, consider how plausible the free-riding concern is and to what extent it would affect the investment incentive for platforms versus the direct loss to price competition caused by a price parity restriction. Hence, mere assertion would not suffice, and it is for the incumbent platform to demonstrate how price parity solves the free-riding problem. For instance, in the MMT-Go case, 64 MMT-Go case, para. 265. MMT-Go had argued that parity conditions are necessary to prevent free riding. However, the CCI held that the free-riding concerns were mitigated due to the discounts offered by MMT-Go to build its network as well as the increased competition between OTAs, as these factors together meant that users do not have any incentive to free ride. Further, any investments made by MMT-Go on its platform were not hotel-specific, so even if hotels did engage in free riding, that did not take away MMT-Go’s incentives to invest in its platform. Yet, narrow parity clauses were found to be justifiable. The relevant extracts are as follows:MMT-Go claims the parity condition to be driven by the need to address free riding concerns. The Commission is of the view that the potential free riding problem does not have any perceptible relevance in the presence of the discounts that MMT-Go offers over and above the rate at which a hotel lists its rooms. When looked at in light of the deep discounts that MMT-Go has been offering over the years (as the data on record bring out clearly), it does not appear to be a realistic possibility that a user would discover a hotel on MMT-Go and then book it through another channel/OTA at a higher price point. It is MMT-Go’s own submission that discounts were provided to build network. Thus, through its discounting strategy MMT-Go ensured both user footfalls and conversions on its platform. Further, when hotels have more OTA choices, the increased competition between the OTAs may lower fees/commission paid to hotels, so consumers may not have as much incentive to free ride. Moreover, what emerged from the cases dealt by other authorities, it has been observed that there may not be any concern about the effect of free riding on incentives to invest (in case of OTAs) because the investments of the platform are not “specific” to a particular hotel – thus, if there is free riding on the part of a particular hotel, this does not undermine the incentive of the platform to invest overall. Be that as it may, the Commission finds some merit in the finding of the DG that the narrow price parity clauses may be justifiable on the grounds of free riding problem. Hotels and OTA are in a contractual relationship and therefore, free riding of hotels on the investment made by OTAs in advertisement and promotion can be detrimental to the business of OTAs. However, wide price parity obligation is not justifiable as it reduces the competition between the OTAs and may have an adverse impact on prices charged to end-consumers. Therefore, the discussion on parity clauses can be concluded by stating that the free-riding concerns of a platform in terms of its incentives to invest have to be balanced with the freedom of business users to list on multiple distribution channels without being restricted in terms of price since ultimately competition largely boils down to competing on price. It is exactly this balance (which narrow parity clauses seem to meet) that CCI analyses in checking whether parity obligations are anticompetitive or not.

sections.title: EMERGING DIGITAL MARKETS
sections.text: §6.04 EMERGING DIGITAL MARKETS [A] Technology Technology is crucial to the development of any digital platform. In this section, platforms whose business revolves around leveraging technology and its derivatives, such as OS, software, etc., have been described. In the Play Store case and Google Android case, the CCI on the basis of the investigation of the DG identified several factors which contributed to the dominance of Google. These factors, as outlined below, can be considered common features of technology companies:(i) Barriers to entry in the form of significant capital and investment for research and development of the platform. These investments are required not only at the stage of initial development, launch and marketing but also subsequently in maintenance, updating, and development of new versions and new features/releases.65 Play Store case, paras 92-95; Google Android case, paras 115-116.6. (ii) APIs and limited or restricted access to licences and other IPRs of the dominant platform also hinder the entry of new players in the market.66 Play Store case, paras 98-100. Without access to the same, bug fixes, automatic updates, new releases, enhancements included in the new versions of Android, etc., will not be possible. While it is technically feasible to ship a phone without Google Play Services, OEMs who do so will be at a significant disadvantage to OEMs that ship with Google Play Services.67 Ibid., para. 184; Google Android case, para. 171. (iii) These restrictions are facilitated through Google’s commercial arrangements with OEMs, i.e., MADA/RSA/AFA, etc., through which OEMs were/are restrained from manufacturing and selling devices incorporating the forked version of Android OS (as discussed in detail in Chapter 3). (iv) Status quo bias in favour of Google because access to the Google Play Store was important for both smart mobile device users as well as app developers. Since Google’s Play Store offers the highest number of apps, users seem to have a preference for it and consider it as a ‘must have’ app.68 Play Store case, paras 186, 193. A combined operation of the above factors makes Google indispensable. As network effects increase, the strength of Google’s network increases further. These factors are likely to be present in any dominant technology platform. [1] Concept of Gatekeepers The term ‘gatekeeper’ is used to describe the role and function of dominant platforms in digital markets. In a literal sense, a gatekeeper is a person or entity that controls access to a particular place or service, determining the rules of the ecosystem, i.e., who can enter and under what conditions. In the digital context, the term is applied because these dominant platforms act as intermediaries that control access to specific markets, services, or opportunities. In the Google Android case, 69 Google Android case, paras 89-94. the CCI observed that Google governs the running of Android as it determines the roadmap and decides on features and new releases. It also controls the compatibility of derivatives of the Android OS by placing various restrictions on the OEMs. For instance, since Google owns the IPR to the Android, the use of the ‘Android’ trademark on hardware, packaging or marketing materials of devices is restricted to Android-compatible devices only. Therefore, in order to use these IPRs, the OEMs must adhere to the obligations imposed by Google. Thus, even though the Android mobile OS is released by Google for free under an open-source licence, OEMs are highly dependent on Google as they have to ensure that all the hardware and software features of their devices are compatible with the latest versions of Android released by Google from time to time; otherwise, such devices will become incompatible, and all apps on the same will be dysfunctional. This is one instance of gatekeeping as Google seeks to control access to Android. Similarly, in the context of online searches, the CCI found that Google has used Android as a vehicle, especially to cement the dominance of its search engine. ‘Google’s strategy rests on the reach, scale and market power of Android, which allows Google to have control over a vast majority of smart mobile devices that serve as key gateways to the internet. Keeping Android OS open and ‘free’ of monetary consideration is thus in as much Google’s interest as it claims it to be for the OEMs and users.’ For app developers, gatekeepers have also been found to restrict installation of TPAP. A leading example of this is through anti-steering provisions. Primarily linked with app stores, ‘anti-steering’ provisions refer to prohibitions laid down by app stores which prevent app developers and app users from moving out of the platform of the app store and using alternatives. This issue came to the fore in the Play Store case, 70 Play Store case, para. 255. where the CCI held that Google’s gatekeeper position in the Android mobile ecosystem makes it ‘well placed to leverage its power to protect and further enhance its dominance in online search by making it difficult for rival search service providers to enter and compete effectively in the mobile search space. The well-regarded benefits of the open-source system of Android cannot legitimize an exclusionary conduct that causes harm to competition in any specific area/markets.’ Accordingly, in the Play Store case, 71 Ibid., para. 268. the CCI observed that ‘gatekeepers like Google should not use their unassailable position to require the app developers, which are dependent on them, to use any of their additional or other service together with its core services viz. app store services’. This is because such conduct by the gatekeeper can be detrimental to app developers ‘by reducing their ability to choose their business partners in respect of these additional or other services and also affects their ability to offer innovative solutions’ as they have to accept the gatekeeper’s services. In the context of the service fee charged by Google to paid apps and apps offering paid in-app content, the CCI laid down the following proposition:72 Ibid., para. 326. in general any conditions (including price related conditions) imposed on business users, by the gatekeepers or platform operators, should be fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory. The pricing and other general conditions should not grant any benefit on the gatekeeper which is disproportionate to the service provided by the gatekeeper to business users. The NCLAT, in appeal73 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, para. 211. to the Google Android case, also observed that Google Search increasingly gains value through network effects as greater traffic on its search engine means a greater volume of data is being generated. This data is then sold to advertisers, thereby helping Google Search gather increased advertising revenue and cement Google’s position in the mobile search market. The NCLAT categorically observed that Google’s business model makes it clear that:there is no single app or service that can be singled out to say that the revenue of Google is derived only out of its user functionality because the user traffic and data comes from not only Google Search and You Tube but also other apps like Google Maps, Google Cloud, Play Store and Gmail etc. In fact, the entire Android System in the mobile device, through tracking device usage, becomes an important source of data for the generic search result. Thus, the data and traffic from multiple apps and service and the entire Android eco-system is funneled into Google Search which is monetized through advertisements. This illustrates how once gatekeepers have created an ecosystem, they can continue to feed and sustain the ecosystem through a complex interdependence between their various service offerings. An analysis of the above cases reveals that CCI has taken a hard stance on technology platforms owing to their gatekeeper position because the abusive and anticompetitive implications of their conduct have worsened since they control all the key access points to the market for the users. Armed with the powerful tools of digital markets, i.e., network effects, data, etc., they are able to chokehold competition. This is why a need is being felt to perhaps introduce sweeping changes in the statutory and regulatory scheme, as has been discussed in the last part of this chapter. [B] Social Media and Communication The leading cases pertaining to social media and communication platforms are the prima facie cases against WhatsApp. In the WhatsApp case, 74 WhatsApp case, para. 84. the CCI observed that WhatsApp Messenger was the most widely used app for social messaging, followed by Facebook Messenger in the relevant market for OTT messaging apps through smartphones in India. Since WhatsApp Messenger and Facebook Messenger were owned by the same group, they were not constrained by each other, rather, they possessed combined strength as a group. The key factor was the presence of direct network effects whereby if more people started using WhatsApp, then it would become more valuable to the existing users since they would have more contacts to communicate with. This leads to a win-win situation for both the users as well as the platform. However, competition concerns arise out of the flip side of the same situation: a stronger network of WhatsApp will deter new and existing messaging apps since no users will be willing to switch to a different messaging app unless all of their contacts also switch platforms. This allows the incumbent platform to exploit its dominance, as was seen in In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc.75 In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc., Suo Moto Case No. 01 of 2021, paras 22-32. In this case, WhatsApp was found to be prima facie abusing its dominance by sharing data with its group companies, i.e., other companies of Facebook without obtaining user consent. Such data-sharing was allegedly for marketing and advertising and was made a mandatory precondition for availing WhatsApp’s services. In a previous instance, similar allegations were raised against WhatsApp, but the differentiating factor was that users had the option to opt out’ of sharing user account information with ‘Facebook’ within thirty days of agreeing to the updated terms of service and privacy policy.76 Vinod Kumar Gupta v. WhatsApp Inc, Case No. 99 of 2016. However, no such option was granted under the present policy update. In the present case, WhatsApp attempted to abuse its dominant position to exploit user data and compel users to share such data once it had built a strong network by not giving a similar opt-out/carve-out to the policy update. Reduction in consumer data protection and loss of control over their personalized data by the users can also be taken as reduction in quality under antitrust law since it is a non-price parameter of competition. Access to such data can also lead to exclusionary effects since WhatsApp/Facebook would be able to further entrench/reinforce their position and leverage themselves in neighbouring or even in unrelated markets such as the display advertising market. Thus, these circumstances are an example of how dominant platforms can abuse their dominant position and reduce competition in the market after building a strong network on which users are locked in. CCI’s inquiries into WhatsApp also mark a first-of-its-kind investigation into a non-price factor for abuse (i.e., user privacy and data) by an allegedly dominant platform. Therefore, it can also be deduced that CCI’s approach is pragmatic and takes into account the immediate (such as unfair imposition in the nature of a take-it-or-leave-it option to users) as well as long term likely effects (such as foreclosure of competition and creation of new business verticals by exploiting user data) of policy decisions. [C] Online Search The CCI has examined competition issues in the online search market in two decided cases – Google Search Bias and Google Android case. Presently, the search market is also being examined in the context of an investigation ordered into the digital advertising market, as described in the next section. In Google Search Bias, the CCI found Google to be dominant in the relevant markets of ‘Online General Web Search Services in India’ and ‘Online Search Advertising Services in India’. On the basis of an investigation by the DG, Google was held to be fixing search results on certain general online searches which meant that users were not seeing organic results but rather the results that were already ‘fixed’ by Google. Another important finding was that Google collected data from the search side of the platform through its search engines and then monetized it on the search advertising side of the market. This again highlights how platforms use data as a means to leverage and boost their competitiveness on multiple sides of the market. It is made possible by virtue of the critical mass of users that are locked into the platform. In the Google Android case, 77 Google Android case, paras 235-237. the CCI noted several characteristics of search platforms, which were also discussed earlier in this chapter, namely:(i) Presence of status quo bias, i.e., users generally do not download competing search apps where they are presented with an existing pre-installed search app on their mobile devices. This is enhanced by the importance of pre-installation as a distribution channel (enforced through Google’s commercial arrangements), wherein a majority of the users tend to stick to the usage of the pre-loaded apps and do not download other apps which may be perceived to provide the same kind of services. (ii) Presence of barriers to entry and expansion in the form of significant investment required in terms of time and resources, such as acquiring and maintaining sufficient hardware to process the data required, hiring skilled developers to build the necessary algorithms and the software services, building a comprehensive search index, and most importantly, attracting and processing user queries. A key feature that is relevant for assessment in online search is the volume of search queries since the greater the number of search queries, the faster the ability of the platform to observe user behaviour and refine and update search results. Therefore, any competing search service needs to attract and process user queries to refine its algorithm for populating the most relevant search results. Further, there exists a positive feedback loop between the two sides of the search platform, i.e., general search services and online search advertising also operate as entry barriers. When a search engine does not have a sufficient scale on the search user side, the advertisers would not be willing to use the search advertising services of such a search engine. Therefore, the key factor that is the focal point of analysis in the online search market is access to user search queries or traffic, i.e., data. The analysis of CCI takes into account the fact that data, which is essentially a non-price parameter of competition, transcends into a crucial price factor because of its capacity to be monetized through valuable targeting and profiling services. This is primarily done through advertisements, as has been discussed in the next section. [D] Digital Advertising Platforms which attract consumers for free or negligible fee, target consumers by capitalizing on their data. This business model is called ‘digital advertising’ wherein, unlike traditional advertising, the firms are able to target consumers with personalized advertisements based on their data. Such targeted advertising is made possible because of profiling of consumers based on search habits and insights gained from their data, such as their browsing behaviour and interests, geographical location, and social, demographic and economic characteristics such as age, gender, income, etc. This data is available because consumers increasingly rely on ‘free’ services, such as location tracking, general search services, etc., in their daily lives which are offered by digital platforms, thereby generating vast swathes of data which can be used for creating user profiles. In Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., 78 Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., Case No. 41 of 2021. Google was found to be prima facie abusing its dominance in the market for online general web search services in India and market for online search advertising services in India by, inter alia, causing denial of fair advertising revenues to news publishers and non-sharing of data pertaining to the amount of revenue earned by Google by placing advertisements on the publisher’s websites and fairness thereof. In this context, the following observations of the CCI assume importance:(i) In consonance with its findings in the Google Search Bias case, 79 Google Search Bias case. the CCI observed that the market for online general web search services and the market for online search advertising services are distinct markets. This is because, first, online general web search services cannot be substituted with direct search options by typing the URL of websites in internet browsers, and accordingly, search engines become the first port of call for a user looking for information online. Second, online and offline advertising services are not comparable, and the characteristics, intended use and price of search and non-search advertising are different from one another.80 Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., para. 16. (ii) The relevant market appeared to be characterized by entry barriers resulting from the significant investment required to develop sophisticated algorithms as well as to reach a sufficient scale of search queries. In addition, these markets also exhibited network effects wherein the online general web search and online search advertising are interdependent, and the increase in the number of users on the search engine increases the value of search advertising offered by Google for the advertisers.81 Ibid., paras 17-19. (iii) Discoverability of publishers is dependent on search results from Google. This dependence also implies that they primarily rely on the traffic generated by Google to sell advertising space on their respective websites.82 Ibid., para. 20. On this basis, the CCI observed that the alleged power imbalance between Google and digital news publishers flowed from the alleged position enjoyed by Google as a necessary trading partner for the latter. Google also offered its services as a vertically integrated entity which is present at each level of the digital advertising value chain, i.e., from the demand side to the supply side. Thus, it operated as a gateway for publishers and advertisers to interact with each other. As a result, on the face of it, publishers had no choice but to accept the terms and conditions imposed by Google. Consequently, the CCI directed the DG to investigate the lack of transparency, information asymmetry and other unfair impositions in the ad tech services provided by Google.83 Ibid., paras 22, 23, 32-41. The outcome of the investigation is likely to be an important factor in assessing competition issues in digital advertising. At the same time, it is relevant to mention that in Google LLC v. DRS Logistics P. Ltd. and Ors., 84 Google LLC v. DRS Logistics P. Ltd. and Ors. [2023], (Delhi High Court) FAO (OS) (COMM) 2/2022. the Delhi High Court noted the key role played by Google in the digital advertising value chain and held that Google is not a mere passive ‘intermediary’ in the advertising process but is an active participant at each step of the process.85 Ibid., paras 182-186. On this basis, it denied the safe harbour exemption which is a protection made available to intermediaries to prevent them from being held liable for third-party information on their platform. The principle may extend to competition law to conclude that platforms cannot shirk their liability by virtue of being mere intermediaries in any process. Therefore, together with the aspect of data discussed earlier, the takeaway is that the ability to monetize and generate revenue through advertisements (which is made possible by having access to large volumes of data) has become a focal point of analysis in almost all investigations pertaining to platform markets. Inevitably, each policy decision is related to some facet of data or advertising. [E] E-Commerce In its Market Study on E-Commerce in India,86 Market Study on E-Commerce in India, 2020, paras 30, 31. the CCI explained that in the goods category of e-commerce platforms, there exist two types of business models: (i) Marketplace Model – platform operators facilitate transactions between buyers and sellers and can fulfil the orders by handling logistics, deliveries and returns. (ii) Inventory-based Model – allows online retailers to own inventory and directly sell to consumers through their own platform. Further, some e-commerce platforms, besides providing marketplace/intermediation services to connect sellers and consumers, also provide related services such as warehousing facilities, transactional support services, promotion and advertising, centralized payment processing, shipment and delivery of goods, refund and replacement, etc. The nature of the fee charged varies across platforms and can be a fixed upfront fee or a commission per transaction. The other sources of revenue for such platforms are advertisements and customer subscription programs. The CCI discussed the role and emerging characteristics of e-commerce marketplaces in the market in the AIOVA v. Flipkart 87 AIOVA v. Flipkart, para. 22. by observing that e-commerce marketplaces function as a connecting link between buyers and sellers by providing them with a platform to interact and transact their business. Further, it noted that e-commerce companies now offer an integrated shopping experience: while such companies have started opening physical offline stores to offer online buyers the touch-and-feel experience, simultaneously, various offline retailers have started their online ventures or partnered with leading e-commerce companies to attract customers on the electronic marketplace. Thus, it can be said that online and offline businesses are increasingly becoming interdependent. Further, in Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and Amazon Seller Services Private Limited, 88 Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and Amazon Seller Services Private Limited, Case No. 40 of 2019, paras 16-26. the CCI held that Flipkart and Amazon are e-commerce entities, following a marketplace-based model of e-commerce as they provide online intermediation services to sellers on one side and consumers on the other. On the one hand, online intermediation services are key enablers of entrepreneurship by offering sellers access to new markets and increasing consumers’ choice of goods; on the other hand, they also result in growing dependence of businesses on these platforms. These platforms/marketplaces and the sellers selling on these platforms also operate at different stages of the vertical/supply chain. Consequently, the CCI found these platforms to be prima facie engaging in four types of anti-competitive agreements:(i) Preferred sellers on the marketplaces – Amazon and Flipkart have their own set of preferred sellers, and these preferred sellers have nexus with the e-commerce platforms either by way of direct or indirect ownership (through common investors, directors, shareholders, etc.) or some understanding. Hence, it was alleged that these preferred sellers are extension of the marketplaces, operating through different ‘proxy’ entities blessed with the support of these marketplaces. (ii) Exclusive launch of mobile phones – Only a few online sellers were observed to be selling exclusively launched smartphones either through Amazon or Flipkart. Thus, prima facie, an exclusive partnership was found between smartphone manufacturers and e-commerce platforms for exclusive launch of smartphone brands. (iii) Deep discounting – The CCI perused the prices for different smartphone brands sold through Flipkart and Amazon, i.e., original price and discounted price and observed that certain smartphone brands/models were available at significantly discounted price and were largely sold by the preferred sellers identified above. (iv) Preferential listing/promotion of private labels – The product listings of the preferred sellers dominate the first few pages of search results on the platforms, whereas the products with the same ratings sold by non-preferred sellers are listed on later pages. Hence, competition in the e-commerce marketplace may be influenced in favour of exclusive brands and sellers through higher discounts and preferential listing. Thus, exclusive launch coupled with preferential treatment to a few sellers and the discounting practices create an ecosystem that may lead to AAEC. Accordingly, the CCI directed the DG to investigate under section 3(1), read with section 3(4) of the Competition Act, whether the alleged exclusive arrangements with preferred sellers, deep-discounting and preferential listing were being used as an exclusionary tactic to foreclose competition and are resulting in AAEC. The order for investigation has also been upheld by the Supreme Court.89 Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2021], (SC) SLP No. 11558/2021. In order to understand why this order has far-reaching implications on e-commerce, it is relevant to highlight the key takeaways of the Market Study on E-Commerce in India in terms of the conduct of the e-commerce marketplace. Therein, the CCI found that the issues of lack of platform neutrality, unfair platform-to-business contract terms, exclusive contracts between online marketplace platforms and sellers/service providers, platform price parity restrictions and deep discounts may hinder the realization of the full pro-competitive potential of e-commerce. As a result, the following key takeaways were identified to mitigate any issues in e-commerce:(i) Increasing transparency to create incentives for competition and to reduce information asymmetry. This includes clearly setting out the platforms’ terms and conditions (including discount policy) without disclosure of any information that can enable or facilitate manipulation/exploitation of search results on the platform. Further, the basis of funding the discounts and the implications of participation/non-participation in the discount schemes must also be disclosed. (ii) Setting out a clear and transparent policy on data that is collected on the platform, the use of such data by the platform and also the potential and actual sharing of such data with third parties or related entities. [F] Aggregation [1] Online Cab Aggregation In Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. (Meru) v. Uber India Systems Pvt. Ltd. Ors.,90 Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. (Meru) v. Uber India Systems Pvt. Ltd. Ors., Case No. 96 of 2015, paras 141-151. the CCI explained the model of cab aggregation as being digital platforms that connect riders to independent drivers through an app which takes a request for a ride from the prospective rider, searches for available (nearby) taxis on its platform, makes a match and puts the driver and the rider in contact with each other. An algorithm then sets the price, and the revenue is shared between the driver and cab aggregator. Leveraging the technology, the cab-aggregator model reduces transaction costs associated with matching dispersed drivers and riders which also means lower search cost for these two sides of participants. Unlike the traditional asset-owned model, where cabs are owned by radio taxi operators, cab aggregators like Uber and Ola rely on their network of driver partners with their own cars to provide ride services. In order to build a large network size (since a large network implies more riders and thereby more drivers), the platforms design the pricing structure so as to make ‘joining’ the network and ‘staying committed’ to it attractive to both sides. This was done through discounts and incentives offered to riders and drivers, respectively. However, as the network grows and reaches a critical mass, the need to offer discounts/incentives gets obviated. The CCI held that in a competitive market where Uber and Ola place competitive constraints on each other, such penetrative pricing strategies (where they price below cost) for the creation of a network seem to be a compelling business strategy to induce loyalty by offering incentives to driver in the early stages of network creation to attract cab-owning drivers. Thus, no reason was found for antitrust intervention. Further, unlike an e-marketplace platform or an online delivery app, in the cab-aggregator model, there is no opportunity for the rider or driver to choose each other, as may be possible in other digital platform transaction because once a rider books his/her ride at any given time, the same is accepted by an anonymous driver available in the area to whom Uber/Ola allocates such ride. Hence, the drivers do not have any individual identities or brand value vis-à-vis the consumers, e.g., a consumer buying through Zomato App, an online delivery app, may have a preference for a particular restaurant, or a consumer booking a hotel through MakeMyTrip may wish to know the options available in terms of their offerings and characteristics, etc. In the case of a cab aggregator, the consumers have no material information about the drivers available in their area of demand. As such, the rides offered by individual drivers through Uber or Ola App constitute a homogenous composite product where riders are indifferent between different drivers registered with a particular cab aggregator. Consequently, despite actually being digital platforms, the cab aggregators are perceived to be the service providers of the ‘ride’ as a product. On this basis, the CCI held that cab aggregators fall in the same market as the radio taxi service providers operating under the asset-owned model. In Meru Travels Solutions Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. CCI and Anr.,91 Meru Travels Solutions Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. CCI and Anr. [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 19 of 2017. an appeal was filed against the CCI’s order in the Ola case which dismissed the claim of Ola’s abuse of dominance on account of its below-cost pricing strategies. The NCLAT held that Ola is a technology company in that it leverages smartphone/internet-based technology to provide the riders not just ease of booking and payment for the ride but also facilitates booking a taxi at a very short notice and almost anywhere in its area of operation. This ‘end to end’ provision of service from booking to alighting from the taxi with a GPS-based tracking system of the taxi – all provided a high degree of reliability and assurance of availability of taxis anywhere, including security to riders and enough demand to drivers. This is a new way of doing business, and Ola could translate this concept into reality. Hence, along with this technology, Ola provided ease of taxi bookings, rider security, payments and drivers’ welfare which made many riders comfortable with the network of Ola. Consequently, the customer incentives worked in conjunction with these facilities and conveniences to attract riders to Ola and a certain brand image was created and reinforced over a period of time. In order to compete with Uber which was present in the market at the same time, Ola had to calibrate and change its market strategy, including pricing, to respond to competition of Uber. On this basis, the CCI held that the pricing strategies of Ola were part of a broader strategy to open up a latent market and establish itself firmly in the market. Hence, it did not constitute abuse of dominance or predatory pricing. The cab-aggregator market is an example of non-intervention by the CCI because there was effective competition in the market between Uber and Ola. This goes on to show that CCI only intervenes when there is harm or likelihood of harm by virtue of the policies adopted by the incumbent platforms. As long as competition exists and is on merits, platform markets do not warrant an intervention merely because factors like network effects may be in existence. [2] Online Food Services Aggregation In the Zomato Swiggy case, Zomato and Swiggy were observed to be online food delivery platforms operating as online intermediaries for food ordering and delivery. The CCI directed an investigation under section 3(4) of the Competition Act into the following conduct:(i) A conflict of interest arose because of the presence of commercial interest in the downstream market. These platforms operated cloud kitchens and listed their own private brand which were competing with the downstream restaurant partners on the same platform. The platforms accorded them with preferential treatment in search ranking. Hence, this violated the principle of platform neutrality as the intermediaries themselves had a vested interest in the competition taking place on their platforms.92 MMT-Go case, paras 72-74. (ii) The price parity clauses (as discussed under APPAs) discourage the entry of other platforms because there can be no competition on price if prices are the same across all platforms. Similar to the discussions on e-commerce above, the outcome of this investigation will have a bearing on how CCI looks at platform neutrality, ranking and exploitation of data. If the trend of the MMT-Go case is followed, then the parity clauses imposed by Zomato and Swiggy may warrant intervention only if they are wide, but narrow parity clauses may be allowed to deal with the free-riding concerns of the platform. Having said that, the ambit of such clauses will be seen considering the industry and that there cannot be a one-size-fits-all approach. [3] Online Travel Services Aggregation The leading case discussing competition issues in the context of OTAs is the MMT-Go case. This case has already been discussed in Chapter 3 on Dominance. However, to recapitulate, the CCI relied on the following factors to conclude that MMT is dominant in the market for online intermediation services for booking hotels in India:(i) Barriers to entry and expansion inform the state of competitiveness in a market. In this case, the last entry in the market was several years ago, and thereafter, despite the market size growing manifold, there has not been a new entry.93 Ibid., para. 231. (ii) Due to the wide choice available on MMT-Go’s portal, consumers also prefer MMT-Go to other portals, which in turn incentivizes hotels/franchisee service providers to list on it. Further, MMT-Go offers several benefits and features which coupled with its market position have resulted in network effects. In turn, such network effects also create insurmountable entry barriers for existing as well as potential market participants.94 Ibid., para. 232. (iii) Commercial arrangements in the nature of APPAs, i.e., price and room parity obligations (discussed above under APPAs) further fortified the position of MMT-Go in the market as it caused a large number of consumers to also book hotels on the said website which then leads to a great influx of hotel owners who are willing to list their hotels on MMT-Go.95 Ibid., para. 233. Presence on MMT-Go was essential as it was a dominant platform and an important gateway for hotels to reach end consumers. Consequently, hotels did not have any real choice to leave the platform.96 Ibid., para. 249. (iv) Deep discounting practices, as discussed earlier, meant that competition between OTAs was reduced to their capacity to fund discounts. Since most of the bookings were made through the dominant platform, which was also the one charging the highest commission, the dominant platform was also the one which would end up being most capable of funding those discounts through the commissions earned. The CCI held that this will further make MMT-Go more attractive to end-consumers as compared to its rival OTAs, leading to more bookings through it and more commissions to fund more discount, in a vicious loop, thereby causing foreclosure of competition for other OTAs.97 Ibid., para. 255. While it may be argued that discounts are beneficial for the end-consumers, the net impact may be adverse when seen in light of higher commissions funding the higher discounts, leading to overall higher prices on which the discounts are applied.98 Ibid., para. 263. Based on the foregoing, the CCI held that the deep discounts, exclusivity condition and parity conditions, in conjunction, create an ecosystem that reinforces MMT-Go’s dominant position in the relevant market. This was a seminal decision as it set out the contours for dealing with parity clauses. Further, it also highlights how CCI collectively analyses the entire ecosystem when assessing the conduct of multi-sided intermediation platforms; the CCI does not look at conduct in a silo manner but looks at the collective impact on the market. Most importantly, this decision marks that merely because a certain policy is an industry practice (for instance, the parity and discounting obligations), it does not exonerate the platform yielding market power from the illegality of such a practice. Market assessment is dynamic, and firms need to be mindful of their market power and special responsibility by virtue of that market power, as that has a bearing on all the players at different stages in the market.

sections.title: TRENDS FROM DECISIONAL PRACTICE
sections.text: §6.05 TRENDS FROM DECISIONAL PRACTICE In this section, the key trends that have emerged from CCI’s decisional practice with regard to big tech and digital markets, as also discussed in the relevant previous chapters of this book, have been briefly summarized. [A] Exploitative and Exclusionary Abuse In the Google Android case, 99 Google Android case, paras 373-415. the exclusionary and exploitative consequences of Google’s conduct on the market were explained in detail, as discussed in Chapter 3. As an example, it is helpful to recapitulate one of the practices, i.e., the harm caused by Google’s commercial arrangements with OEMs, i.e., MADA, RSA, AFA/ACC. The CCI on the basis of the DG investigation concluded that these covenants are in the nature of imposition of unfair conditions on OEMs who have no choice but to accept the same. They resulted in anticompetitive harm, viz. pre-installation of the entire suit of GMS as well as prominent placement thereof as:(i) Due to these arrangements, competitors can only secure a relatively inferior positioning on the second or third screen of the device. Thus, they cannot achieve a more prominent display on the devices and remain inferior to Google’s placement, and Google has retained its competitive edge. (ii) The preinstallation of GMS suite as the default app on any device created a status quo bias in the mind of the users and helped Google attain a ‘must have status’. Consequently, rivals lose significant traffic and have no incentive to enter into contractual arrangements with OEMs as they would only have a secondary treatment on the devices. (iii) By foreclosing the market for rivals, these covenants also reduced the potential choice for users. (iv) The preinstallation requirement also constituted the imposition of a supplementary obligation on the OEMs. Hence, the cumulative impact of the restrictions posed by these covenants prohibited alternative vendors from outcompeting Google’s apps on merit. They also allowed Google to protect and strengthen its market position in the relevant markets, keeping in mind that its strategy rests on the reach, scale and market power of Android, which allows Google to have control over a vast majority of smart mobile devices that serve as key gateways to the internet. Recognizing Google’s gatekeeper position, the CCI held that the well-regarded benefits of the open-source system of Android cannot legitimize an exclusionary conduct that causes harm to competition in any specific area/market.100 Ibid., para. 470. In the Play Store case, 101 Play Store case, para. 273. Google was found to have engaged in exploitative and exclusionary conduct. A clear example of one such conduct was the impact of the mandatory commission charged by Google to app developers by making it necessary for them to use Google’s billing system for offering paid apps and paid in-app content, as illustrated in the following flowchart: It was found that Google’s own apps, such as YouTube, did not have to pay such a commission, and, in any case, if the commission was paid, it would be internalized. Hence, this illustrates how the platform can exploit users on one end of the market, i.e., app developers and also cause their exclusion in order to access the other side of the market, i.e., app users. Similar conduct has also been found in the prima facie Apple case, 102 Apple case, para. 28. wherein the CCI noted that Apple’s proprietary apps are competing with TPAP on the iOS platform. Resultantly, any commission or fee charged by Apple from these apps (for having access to the platform) would increase the cost of Apple’s competitors and thus might affect their competitiveness vis-à-vis Apple’s own apps (the fee in respect of which, in any case, would be internalized). This will lead to a competitive disadvantage in the downstream markets and limit the ability of the app developers to offer payment processing solutions of their choice to the users. Therefore, it is clear that CCI assesses the implications of the platform’s conduct in the upstream as well as downstream markets and does not look at it in isolation. The abusive conduct of platforms can turn exploitative and exclusionary when seen in light of the cumulative practices propagated by the platform in its ecosystem. [B] Special Responsibility In the context of digital platforms, especially those which act as gatekeepers and host other businesses on their platforms (e.g., online marketplaces), the special responsibility to prevent their conduct from impairing genuine undistorted competition becomes even more important. CCI has reiterated this principle in the context of the various decided cases against Google.103 Google Search Bias case; Play Store case. Particularly, in the Google Android case, 104 Google Android case, paras 557-561. the CCI observed that ‘Google being a dominant entity has the special responsibility not to impinge the process of competition and ensure fairness and reasonableness in its conduct…Dominant enterprises must not take unfair advantage of their position as such abusive conduct not only impacts the market as a whole but also may affect the entry and sustenance of other market participants into complementary markets associated with the platform.’ Hence, Google ought to be mindful of the exclusionary implications of the restrictions imposed on OEMs and should have used more competition-friendly alternatives. Hence, platforms, being gatekeepers of their respective markets, have a special responsibility to maintain a level playing field and ensure that their conduct does not have adverse consequences for other players in the market. [C] Efficiency Gains and Competition Harms in Mergers On the one hand, the characteristic features of digital markets have the potential to increase competition relative to traditional markets by allowing consumers to use multiple services in a single platform simultaneously, the ability to use digital tools to compare prices and features, etc. On the other hand, they also create risks of undesirable concentration by leveraging the advantages of economies of scale and scope, data advantages for incumbents, network effects, etc. These advantages and disadvantages get heightened in case of mergers since the strength of already powerful entities in markets with little competition virtually doubles, thereby making the merged entity into a behemoth with incomparable strength that cannot be challenged by existing smaller players. In the case of the Google/Jio combination,105 Google International LLC, Combination Registration No. C-2020/09/775, 11 Nov. 2020. there was an overlap between the activities of the parties in the spheres of mobile app distribution, supply of apps & mobile/web services, supply of advertisement services, and supply of mobile OS. Further, the OTT applications and content of Google and the telecommunication services offered by a subsidiary of Jio were found to be complementary/vertical to each other. Consequently, the CCI assessed the ability and incentive of both platforms to discriminate between competitors in their respective businesses and whether their access to each other’s resources created any undue advantages.106 Ibid., para. 10. In particular, Reliance (the parent company of Jio) was found to deploy KaiOS on its mobile devices, offering competition to Google’s Android OS, and the proposed business collaboration was likely to reduce the incentive for Reliance to continue with KaiOS-based devices. However, since Reliance confirmed that it would continue to sell KaiOS phones and shall not degrade the quality of these phones to favour the new smartphone to be made in collaboration with Google, the CCI concluded that the proposed combination did not raise any competition concerns in relation to supply of Android or customized OS.107 Ibid., para. 14. Another concern was data-backed market power, arising from possible data sharing between Google and Jio.108 Ibid., paras 30, 31. The CCI observed that the assessment in such instances needs to focus on the incentives of parties to pool or share their databank and monetize such data in the best possible means. In this case, since the proposed combination was an acquisition of 7.73% stake in Jio by Google, it was observed that it may not result in unrestricted access to each other’s resources, including user data. Nevertheless, the parties may have incentives to engage in mutually beneficial data sharing. The CCI consequently allowed the combination by observing that any anticompetitive conduct resulting from any data sharing or otherwise, in the future could be taken up under sections 3 and/or 4 of the Competition Act. Similarly, in the Facebook/Reliance 109 Jaadhu Holdings, LLC, Combination Registration No. C-2020/06/747. combination, CCI approved the acquisition of 9.99% stake by a wholly owned subsidiary of Facebook in Reliance Jio Infocomm Limited, which is the largest telecommunications company in India. The investment was aimed at collaborations between the two undertakings in the online advertising and e-commerce sectors: for instance, it envisaged a commercial arrangement to connect the users of WhatsApp with Reliance’s e-commerce marketplace, JioMart. Despite a recognition of how data-backed market power can allow firms to pool their data, leading to anti-competitive outcomes and have bearings on platform neutrality, CCI approved the combination while recognizing its pro-competitive effects and the growing synergies between the telecommunication industry and the digital technology sectors. While the parties submitted that they did not intend to share data, this did not have a bearing on CCI’s decision, even though there may have been certain complementary overlaps in the data possessed by Facebook and Reliance. In line with its decisional practice in combinations, CCI left the scope open for ex post review in the future in case of any anticompetitive effects. Therefore, the above cases highlight how CCI takes a pro-market balanced approach to regulation so as to focus on market synergies and pro-competitive efficiencies instead of distant possible anticompetitive harm. To date, CCI has not withdrawn any approval but has only subjected combinations that have anti-competitive potential alone to detailed review and subsequent modifications, as required in the factual circumstances. [D] Protection of Incentives to Innovate While it is important to keep a check on the anti-competitive and abusive potential of digital platforms, at the same time, excessive antitrust intervention can reduce the incentives of such platforms to innovate and compete in the market. This can be illustrated qua the Google Android case, 110 Google Android case, paras 530-557, 617.3. wherein the CCI directed that Google shall not deny access to its Play Services APIs to disadvantage OEMs, app developers and its existing or potential competitors. This would ensure the interoperability of apps between Android OS which complies with compatibility requirements of Google and Android Forks. This was done in light of the finding that Google used its control and proprietary rights over APIs to obstruct the development of alternative and competing Android fork versions, thereby hindering technical or scientific development, innovation, research and development. Further, by excluding these APIs from the Android OS and not making Google Play services available on forked versions of Android, Google prevented apps developed for Android from being ported onto the forked versions of Android without additional cost and investments. In appeal, the NCLAT111 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, paras 183-186. overturned CCI’s direction on the ground that the APIs which provide essential services to the apps which are part of the Play Store, are privately developed, maintained and continually updated by Google and are provided by Google to app developers and OEMs. Consequently, unhindered access to such proprietary items of Google cannot be given to app developers, OEMs and Google’s existing and potential competitors. Further, such proprietary software which is ‘developed through scientific and technical innovation should fetch value to Google and, therefore, remain an incentive for a technological company/Google to further carry out such development and monetize it through its commercial use’. Thus, unhindered access to the same cannot be allowed without going through necessary technical and commercial engagement with Google. The matter is pending appeal before the Supreme Court. This was also recognized by the CCI in the AIOVA v. Flipkart, 112 AIOVA v. Flipkart, para. 34. wherein it observed that since the technology-driven and marketplace-based e-commerce model is still evolving and relatively nascent, regard must be had to the growth potential as well as the efficiencies and consumer benefits that such markets can provide. Hence, any intervention in such markets needs to be carefully crafted lest it stifles innovation. CCI has refrained from acting as a price regulator. In the Play Store case,113 Play Store case, para. 325. CCI did not hold that the 15%-30% commission charged by Google through imposition of a mandatory GPBS on each transaction pertaining to a paid app/digital in-app purchase was excessive. Similarly, in the MMT-Go case, CCI did not give a finding on deep discounting by MMT-Go.114 MMT-Go case, para. 274. Thus, it is clear that CCI has refrained itself from acting as a price regulator or regulating the commercial wisdom of enterprises. Rather, the CCI has focused on freeing the market at every level of the value chain by removing bottlenecks and promoting dynamic efficiency and innovation in the market (such as by issuing remedial directions). Therefore, the stand taken by the CCI is pro-competition without interfering with the business decisions of the platforms.

sections.title: REGULATORY INTERVENTION
sections.text: §6.06 REGULATORY INTERVENTION There has been a significant growth of Indian markets and a paradigm shift in the way businesses operate. There have been multiple reasons which have been advanced which bring to the fore the different regulatory challenges facing the digital economy, which perhaps necessitate a new regulatory approach. Some of those factors have been discussed hereinabove. The rise of the digital economy has accelerated manifold due to the pandemic-induced lockdown in several countries. The regulators need to make sure that the steps towards recovery do not irreversibly alter markets and that competition principles can be respected along the way, especially by ‘gatekeeper’ firms that provide online intermediation services. An increasing part of economic activity is going to be facilitated by digital players and channelled through the internet, and a small number of firms have occupied key intermediation positions. This tectonic shift in markets is thus posing challenges for lawmakers and enforcers on how to address the consequent bargaining power imbalance and information asymmetry between platforms and their business users. This imbalance and the resulting concerns were elaborately discussed in the e-commerce market study of the CCI (discussed supra). Following the study, multiple issues have been raised before the CCI, showing that these digital platforms have, perhaps, in effect, become private regulators dictating terms and conditions to participants in their ecosystems. Even if the business users are uncomfortable with those terms, there are often few viable alternatives to some of the services offered. Strong network effects increase the value of a digital service for consumers and business users, and switching to newcomers is unattractive. Thus, it becomes very important that the rules of engagement between business users and platforms are such that this unequal power relative to other businesses in a vertical relationship is not exploited by platforms, and competition on merits is preserved. Increasingly, competition regulatory architecture must be mindful of the dynamics of these relationships rather than narrowly focusing on static price competition. The explosion of online commerce has changed the dynamics of consumer preferences and transactions. As a market regulator, it is thus imperative that the competition regulator ensures that all stakeholders get an opportunity to compete on merits and get a fair chance to be part of digital commerce. The search and comparison functionalities of the intermediary platforms, their reach and scale, and the network effects that work in their favour lead to huge consumer footfalls on these platforms, thereby making presence on these platforms critical for the visibility and competitive ability of sellers/service providers. These trends must not be ignored by lawmakers because the counterfactual – allowing privately owned, democratic legitimacy lacking digital platforms to set the rules of Indian e-commerce without identifying and mitigating the long-term risks and the economic harm they can cause- is a scenario which we cannot afford. Keeping the above in mind, the government of India constituted the Competition Law Review Committee in 2018, to examine and suggest the modifications in the Competition Act, which submitted its report to the government in 2019. After a review of these recommendations by the Parliamentary Standing Committee, several amendments were mooted, inter alia, relating to digital platforms. These have been discussed below. [A] Competition Law (Amendment) Act, 2022 So far, the following provisions have not yet been notified by the government. Only those amendments which are relevant to digital markets and platforms have been discussed below. The remaining amendments have been highlighted in the relevant chapters. [1] Deal Value Threshold The existing merger control framework in India is based on an asset and turnover threshold. As a result, a lot of combinations pertaining to digital markets were escaping antitrust scrutiny. This is because the business model in digital markets is such that they may fail to generate significant revenue in the initial years since the focus is typically on user growth in order to exploit the network effects of such markets. Further, such industries are asset light. Consequently, high-value transactions which did not meet the thresholds specified under section 5 of the Competition Act, were escaping CCI’s scrutiny. The most notable example is that of a slew of ‘killer acquisitions,’ i.e., acquisitions by dominant enterprises of nascent competitors, particularly in digital markets, such as the WhatsApp/Facebook merger, Zomato’s acquisition of Uber Eats, Flipkart’s acquisition of Jabong.com through its subsidiary Myntra, and Ola Cab’s acquisition of TaxiForSure. These deals were significant because the entities involved were tech aggregators with significant market power, but they escaped the scrutiny of the CCI despite likely competition concerns since they did not meet the existing asset/turnover threshold. In this background, a new deal value threshold has been introduced under section 5 of the Competition Act. This requires that in addition to the criteria of assets and turnover to determine the notifiability of a transaction, if the value of any transaction, in connection with the acquisition of any control, shares, voting rights, or assets of an enterprise, merger or amalgamation exceeds INR 2,000 crore, then it has to be notified to the CCI. The Proviso states that the which is being acquired or taken control of or merged or amalgamated must have substantial business operations in India. Through the CCI Draft (Combination) Regulations, 2023, CCI has defined ‘value of transaction’ in an expansive manner to include ‘every valuable consideration, whether direct or indirect, immediate or deferred, cash or otherwise’. Further, the target will be deemed to have ‘substantial business operations in India’ if at any point in time in the preceding twelve months of the transaction date, its: (i) users, subscribers, customers, or visitors were from India or; (ii) gross merchandise value was from India or turnover for the preceding financial year derived from all products or services was from India. Therefore, the swift framing of Draft Regulations immediately after introducing the amendments demonstrates India’s resolve and commitment to bring the merger regime at par with international best practices as well as more suited to the needs of India’s rapidly growing digital economy.

sections.title: DIGITAL COMPETITION ACT
sections.text: §6.07 DIGITAL COMPETITION ACT Anti-competitive behaviour in digital markets has been dealt with through ex-post provisions in competition law around the world, as it has been in India. However, the rise of digital markets has led to new challenges and complexities in the field of competition law. Traditional competition laws were primarily designed for physical markets, and they may not be fully equipped to address the unique features of digital markets. As a result, there is an emerging global consensus to identify and regulate what the Big Tech Report has termed as Systematically Important Digital Intermediaries (SIDIs), which refer to a small number of leading players in digital markets to ensure free and fair markets. [A] Digital Markets Act: The European Approach The EU’s Digital Markets Act (DMA) is a legislation from the European Commission intended to ensure a higher level of competition in European digital markets by defining gatekeeper platforms and laying down clearly their rights and obligations. It will become fully applicable by 2024. Under DMA, a platform can be designated as a gatekeeper if it:115 Digital Markets Act 2022, Article 2 read with Article 3. (i) Provides a core platform service that serves as an important gateway for business users to reach end users. ‘Core platform service’ includes any of the following: online intermediation services, online search engines, online social networking services, video-sharing platform services, number-independent interpersonal communications services, operating systems, web browsers, virtual assistants, cloud computing services and online advertising services (including any advertising networks, advertising exchanges and any other advertising intermediation services). (ii) Has a significant impact on the internal EU market, i.e., has an annual turnover of at least EUR 7.5 billion within the EU in each of the past three financial years or an average market valuation of at least EUR 75 billion in the past financial year, and it provides the same core platform service in at least three Member States. (iii) Enjoys an (established or expected) entrenched and durable position, i.e., the platform has on average a minimum of 45 million monthly end users established or located in the EU and at least 10,000 yearly business users established in the EU in each of the previous three financial years. Important prohibitions placed by the DMA on gatekeepers include restrictions on processing personal data of users for online advertising services without taking prior consent,116 DMA, Article 5(2)(a). preventing wide and narrow parity clauses,117 DMA, Article 5(3). preventing gatekeepers from bundling one core platform service with another core service,118 DMA, Article 5(8). restricting end users from switching between different apps and services,119 DMA, Article 6(6). etc. Some key obligations imposed by DMA on gatekeepers include allowing communication and access to content between platform users,120 DMA, Article 5(4)(5). ensuring price transparency in advertising in advertising intermediation services,121 DMA, Article 5(9), 5(10). allowing end users to change default settings on their devices and uninstall any apps on an OS besides essential apps,122 DMA, Article 6(3). etc. [B] Does India Need a Digital Competition Act? The Big Tech Report proposed a set of ex ante obligations against SIDIs to regulate the digital market in India. It has been argued that India’s digital ecosystem is likely to become the world’s largest, and to this end, it must also be open and innovative, as in a globalized world, interconnected digital markets will rapidly demonstrate monopolistic outcomes that prevent fair competition. This will restrict consumer choice, inhibit business users, and prevent the rise of dynamic new companies. Hence, a need was felt to introduce a new regulatory framework for SIDIs. This ignited a debate on whether India truly needs such ex ante regulation through separate guidelines or whether it is an attempt at mirroring the standards adopted by Europe. Long story short, the national and international developing jurisprudence on competition law in digital markets deals with the same gatekeepers and has the same objectives, i.e., to make the digital markets more competitive, protect consumer choice and innovation as well as limit the harm caused by the self-reinforcing position of the incumbent platforms. Although the effects-based approach, as reaffirmed by the NCLAT in the Google Android case and discussed in earlier parts of this book, allows sufficient scope for adjudicating the issues arising out of anti-competitive practices adopted by digital platforms, the traditional tools of assessment may not be able to analyse the peculiar characteristics of digital markets. This is because while the Competition Act was drafted in a comprehensive, inclusive and forward-thinking manner, it does change the fact that digital markets and platforms were simply not on the horizon at the time it was framed. This is why ex ante approach may be required instead of ex post analysis: since traditional investigations take time and digital platforms evolve rapidly, decisions may often lose their relevance since it fails to properly curb the anti-competitive effects in time. This, coupled with the fact that there are delays in market corrections due to the appeal process, paves the way for the entire discussion of having an ex ante approach to market corrections by digital gatekeepers. Thus, time is of the essence. At the same time, cautiousness is warranted in adopting an ex ante approach so as not to inadvertently stifle innovation in the market. Some of the key recommendations of the Big Tech Report for ex-ante regulation of SIDIs and digital gatekeepers are as follows:(i) SIDIs should not condition access to the platform or preferred status or placement on the platform on the purchase or use of other products or services offered by the platform that are not part of or intrinsic to the platform. (ii) SIDIs must not favour its own offers over the offers of its competitors when mediating access to supply and sales markets, in particular, when presenting its own offers in a more favourable manner; and when exclusively pre-installing its own offers on devices or integrating them in any other way in offers provided by the platform. (iii) SIDIs should not force business users or end users to subscribe to or register with any further services as a condition for being able to use, access, sign up for or register with any of that platform’s core platform service. (iv) SIDIs should not process personal data of users for providing online advertising services using services of third parties that make use of core services of the platform; combine personal data from the relevant core service of the platform with personal data from any further core services or from any other services provided by the platform or with personal data from third-party services; cross-use personal data from the relevant core service in other services provided separately by the platform, including other core services of the platform, and vice-versa; and sign in end users to other services of the platform in order to combine personal data, unless the end-user has been presented with the specific choice and has given consent. (v) SIDIs should pre-inform the CCI of any intended concentration, where the merging entities or the target of concentration provide services in the digital sector or enable the collection of data, irrespective of whether it is notifiable to the CCI or not. (vi) SIDIs should not prevent business users from offering the same products or services to end users through third-party online intermediation services or through their own direct online sales channel at prices or conditions that are different from those offered through the online intermediation services of the platform. (vii) SIDIs must provide any third-party undertaking providing online search engines, at their request, with access to fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms to rank, query, click and view data in relation to free and paid search generated by end users on its online search engines.

sections.title: CONCLUSION
sections.text: §6.08 CONCLUSION It is clear that the CCI has been quick to adapt to the emerging issues of the digital markets by undertaking timely investigations, approving combinations and swiftly bringing in regulatory changes. It is imperative for competition regulation to keep pace with the digital economy which brings both opportunities for growth and challenges to tackle. As platforms tend to become the necessary gateways of distribution due to a combination of the features outlined above, a question arises regarding what extent such a platform can be allowed to protect its ecosystem and, if so, whether there can be any limits to the same. In the end, it is fair to conclude by saying that in any market, genuine and undistorted market forces should ultimately inform the functioning of the state of competition.

plain_text:
§6.01 INTRODUCTION With digitization, markets have moved beyond the traditional physical realm to the world of the internet. Dubbed ‘digital markets’, this contemporary shift has given rise to an efficient system which reduces costs for businesses. Correspondingly, it reduces a consumer’s search costs, i.e., the cost the consumer would bear to look for the good or service that would best satisfy their want (for, e.g., consider the fuel spent on reaching the markets and the time spent on looking for the desired product). The ‘savings’ in digital markets go beyond price parameters and include time and convenience, which are essential costs in a fast-paced world. Thus, it is clear that the digital wave presents an opportunity for the country to leverage its potential and increase efficiencies, promote consumer welfare and create a favourable ecosystem for businesses.1 Report of the Competition Law Review Committee, 2019, para. 1.1. However, the specific characteristics of digital markets, such as economies of scale, network effects and the multi-sided nature of the markets, make them more susceptible to anti-competitive conduct as there is an increased likelihood of entrenched market power for the entities operating in the market as compared to traditional industries.2 Case COMP/C-3/37.792 Microsoft, decided on 24 Mar. 2004, at para. 470. Consequently, a winner-takes-all scenario emerges and the concentration of market power is amplified quickly. Given the rapid growth and evolution of digital markets, timely and swift interventions by competition authorities become all the more necessary because the pace at which these markets evolve necessitates proactive regulatory measures to mitigate potential harm to competition and consumers, i.e., an ex ante regulatory approach. As digital markets move much beyond nascency and have now become a regular, vital and quintessential feature of the economy, the decisional practice of the CCI and regulatory changes therein merit a discourse. Accordingly, this chapter shall provide an overview of the features, challenges and developments in the interface between digital markets and competition law.

§6.02 MARKET DEFINITION The tools and techniques of market definition remain unchanged whether the market is traditional or digital, being born out of the statutory provisions as discussed earlier in this book. However, an analysis of CCI’s approach reveals that market definition in digital markets may be slightly nuanced. For instance, new trends have emerged in how the CCI deals with the substitutability of different channels of distribution, the peculiar characteristics as well as the wider scope of the relevant geographic market in the context of platform markets. This has been elaborated below. [A] Substitutability of Online and Offline Markets Relevant market determination is based on market realities and an assessment of the factual matrix of each case. An analysis of CCI’s decisional practice reveals a shift in determining the substitutability of different channels/modes of distribution. When CCI received its first case against an online platform in the year 2014,3 Mr. Ashish Ahuja v. Snapdeal.com and Anr., Case No. 17 of 2014, para. 16. i.e., against ‘Snapdeal’, an e-commerce platform, it observed that buyers are shifting from offline to online retail market because of heavy discounts, better choices and convenience. However, if the prices in the online market increase significantly, then the consumer is likely to shift towards the offline market and vice versa. On this basis, the CCI opined that online and offline markets were only two different channels of distribution and not two different relevant markets. This view was subsequently followed in other cases also.4 Mr. Deepak Verma v. Clues Network Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 34 of 2016, paras 10, 11; Bharti Retail Limited and Future Retail Limited, Combination Registration No. C-2015/05/281, 19 Aug. 2015. In the same year, in Mohit Manglani v. Flipkart and Ors.,5 Mr. Mohit Manglani v. M/s Flipkart India Private Limited and Ors., Case No. 80 of 2014, para. 18. the CCI considered another piece of information alleging anti-competitive effects arising out of the exclusive agreements between e-commerce websites and sellers for the sale of selected products. Therein, while finding that online platforms and brick-and-mortar companies are distinct channels in the same relevant market, the CCI also observed that ‘[i]rrespective of whether we consider e-portal market as a separate relevant product market or as a sub-segment of the market for distribution, none of the entities seemed to be individually dominant’. Thus, this marked the initiation of the idea that online channels may be a different relevant market in themselves. The departure in CCI’s stand became more nuanced in 2018 in All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Private Ltd. and Anr. 6 All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Private Ltd. and Anr., Case No. 20 of 2018, para. 22. (‘AIOVA v. Flipkart case’). In this case, the CCI discussed the increasing interdependence and integration of online e-commerce platforms and offline business retailers. In order to assess the alleged anti-competitive practices of Flipkart, the CCI defined the relevant market as ‘Services provided by online marketplace platforms in India’.
