This was based on the following reasoning:23. No doubt, to the end consumers, the distinction line between online and offline sellers is sometimes blurry, yet it cannot be denied that online marketplaces offer convenience for sellers as well as the buyers. For the sellers, they save costs in terms of setting up of a store, sales staff, electricity and other maintenance charges. The benefits afforded to buyers includes comfort of shopping from their homes thus saving time, commuting charges and at the same time they can compare multiple goods … . Hence, the relevant market was defined narrowly for the first time by drawing a distinction between online and offline channels of distribution owing to their distinct and different features. This in itself shows that market definition, particularly in digital markets, is not a static concept but a dynamic concept which changes with time, and the relevant market assessment must be done at each point in time. The transition from CCI’s earlier position becomes clearer when we analyse the travel services market. In 2017, CCI approved the merger of MakeMyTrip and Ibibo, where both parties offered travel-related services through the internet.7 MIH Internet SEA Pte Limited, Singapore, Combination Registration No. C-2016/10/451, 18 Jan. 2017, paras 8-10. Therein, CCI observed that online and offline channels of providing travel services were substitutable with each other. As a result, the relevant market was defined as the market of ‘sale of travel and travel related services’, based on a substitutability assessment between online travel agents with other distribution channels from the consumers’ point of view. However, in just a couple of years and by 2019, while examining the allegations of anti-competitive conduct and abuse of dominance by the same entities, the CCI noted that market realities had changed which necessitated a different market definition. In Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Anr. v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd. 8 Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India and Anr. v. MakeMyTrip India Pvt. Ltd., Case No. 14 of 2019, paras 181-191. (‘MMT-Go case’), the relevant market was delineated as the ‘market for online intermediation services for booking of hotels in India’ on the basis of the unique features of online platforms such as network effects in terms of customer reach and pricing strategies (discussed later in the chapter). Not only was the substitutability analysis carried out from the hotels’ point of view in this case but also the CCI noted that timing is a crucial factor and the assessment in rapidly changing digital markets (such as online travel services) cannot have a static approach. Hence, online and offline channels of distribution were considered to be separate and distinct.9 Ibid., paras 190-216. Online and offline modes, being characteristically different from each other, do not cater to the same set of consumers and, thus, are not interchangeable from the perspective of not only hotels but also end consumers. This has been discussed in more detail under the multi-homing and online travel aggregation. What is important to note is that the legal principles of relevant market delineation remain the same irrespective of whether the firm is digital or traditional; the approach to defining the relevant market will change depending on the market concerned. The approach is: first, the starting point would be the service offered by the enterprise under scrutiny (i.e., MMT-Go, offers to hotels); second, the next step would be to identify such alternate services that are substitutable in terms of price, characteristics and intended use from the perspective of the affected user group (i.e., the hotels’ perspective). The trend that emerges is that in digital markets, there does not exist any one-size-fits-all approach in delineating the relevant market. The peculiar characteristics of the platform, the users served by that platform along with the timing and prevailing market circumstances together necessitate a dynamic approach in conducting the substitutability analysis. Yet, it can be said that the clear takeaway, as upheld in several other cases,10 Shri Vishal Gupta v. Google LLC and Ors., Case Nos 06 & 46 of 2014, para. 64; Vijay Gopal v. Big Tree Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 46 of 2021, paras 24-26. is that online and offline channels are not substitutable or even comparable while determining the relevant market because the services offered by online (marketplace) platforms are not substitutable/interchangeable from the consumer’s perspective. The focal point of difference is that the services offered by online marketplace platforms are marked differently from offline mode. These services are discussed in more detail in the subsequent sections. [B] Platform Markets In the Market Study on E-Commerce in India11 Market Study on E-Commerce in India, 2020, para. 34. published by the CCI, it was observed that ‘online marketplaces are multi-sided platforms that connect sellers, buyers and advertisers to facilitate transactions between them. A platform offers a multisided environment that internalizes transaction costs and takes advantage of the network effects across the different user groups’. Intermediation by online platforms allows businesses to have an online presence to access consumers without the need to operate their own websites. The degree of dependence of sellers on online platforms is rising since consumers increasingly rely on these platforms to search for and purchase products on the internet. This tends to create a scenario of a ‘winner-takes-all’ market. This was further clarified by the CCI in AIOVA v. Flipkart case, 12 AIOVA v. Flipkart case, para. 24. wherein the CCI differentiated between online retail stores and online marketplace platforms on the basis that in online retail stores, the seller (who may or may not own a brick-and-mortar store) owns their portal to sell products thorough an online website. However, in online marketplace platforms, the owner of the online portal offers a platform for buyers and sellers to transact. Hence, the sellers would be interested in selling on the online platforms which are able to attract an increasing number of buyers, thus characterizing the online platforms with network effects. The functioning of platforms was also analysed in the MMT-Go case. Therein, the CCI drew a distinction between transaction and non-transaction platforms,13 MMT-Go case, para. 191. as highlighted in the following flowchart: The CCI further went on to observe that:Even within these sub-segments, the platforms may have different features, e.g., the transaction platforms can be purely ‘matching-making platforms’ where users on different sides of the platform are brought together to effectuate one to one transaction, e.g., cab aggregators like Uber/Ola, or they can be like OTAs where multiple users can see the various options available and decide whether to effectuate a transaction or not. Given the diversity of platform markets, and their different network effects and pricing strategies, it is imperative that the specificities of each platform are appropriately factored in while weighing different approaches and alternatives to market definition. One size fit all approach may lead to absurd outcomes in such markets. Further, the timing is also a crucial factor. The assessment in rapidly changing digital markets cannot have a static approach. Further, platform markets have several unique characteristic features such as network effects, economies of scale, etc., as have been discussed in the subsequent sections of this chapter. The operation of these characteristics makes platform markets distinct and non-substitutable with traditional physical markets. Hence, it emerges that platform markets (which may be further categorized based on the nature of the platform) have distinct characteristics from traditional markets. As a result, assessing the competition concerns and regulating such platforms requires a nuanced and flexible approach so as to adapt to the needs of rapidly evolving digital markets. [C] Wider Scope of Relevant Geographic Market The CCI has traditionally taken an approach in GHCL Limited v. Coal India Limited and Anr. (‘Coal India case’)14 GHCL Limited v. Coal India Limited and Anr., Case No. 08 of 2014, paras 28-30. that only India can be the relevant geographic market under the Competition Act, and the same cannot be global. The CCI has taken this interpretation based on the technical reading of the provisions of the Competition Act. However, transactions in the digital economy are a global phenomenon. Unlike traditional markets which are confined to physical boundaries, digital markets are borderless and may even span across international jurisdictions. The CCI in its decisional practice pertaining to online platforms has observed that the conditions of competition are homogeneous pan-India and hence, the relevant geographic market in such cases is taken as the entirety of India.15 All India Online Vendors Association v. Flipkart India Private Limited and Anr., Case No. 20 of 2018, para. 24; Baglekar Akash Kumar v. Google LLC and Anr., Case No. 39 of 2020, para. 16. For instance, in Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc. and Facebook Inc. (‘WhatsApp case’),16 Harshita Chawla v. WhatsApp Inc. and Facebook Inc., Case No. 15 of 2020, para. 76. the CCI observed that the functionality of OTT messaging apps through smartphones is the same irrespective of the region or country concerned in terms of price, functionality, operating system (OS) or otherwise. However, the competitive conditions, regulatory architecture and players may vary in different countries/regions. Since conditions for competition are homogenous in India, the geographic area of India was taken as the relevant geographic market for the purposes of assessment. Thus, there is already a recognition that the characteristics, features and services offered by the platform under consideration may be consistent across borders. Therefore, there is perhaps a need for relooking at widening the scope of relevant geographic market in appropriate cases when considering digital markets, which tend to be borderless. Therefore, there may be a need to relook at the CCI interpretation in the Coal India case and hold that in appropriate cases, the relevant market may be global.

§6.03 CHARACTERISTIC FEATURES OF DIGITAL MARKETS All digital markets have certain characteristic features on the basis of which they are not substitutable with traditional markets. The collective operation of these characteristics often tends to make online platforms gain and accumulate market power in a short span of time. [A] Multi-sided Markets The ‘multi-sided’ nature of platforms means that they serve multiple distinct groups of users, with users in at least one of those groups valuing the platform more highly as the number or activity of users in the other group increases. For example, an app store serves both consumers and app developers, with each group valuing increased participation by members of the other. When a business is multi-sided, the profit-maximizing levels of price and output on one side of the platform may depend, in part, on competitive conditions on the other side. For example, some platform businesses may charge a zero or negative price to users on one side of the platform (e.g., consumers), relying on revenue from users on another side (e.g., advertisers) in order to maximize overall profitability. This has been discussed in more detail subsequently. In case of two-sided or multi-sided markets, a platform caters to different user groups which may be interlinked and interdependent. In this context, in the MMT-Go case, 17 MMT-Go case, paras 190, 295. the CCI noted that the intermediation service provided by MMT-Go is provided through a two-sided platform which connects two distinct user groups, i.e., consumers and hotels/hoteliers. Both these user groups positively value the presence of more users on the other side of the platform, as it creates higher visibility and potential sales (for hotels) and a wider variety of options (for consumers). Consequently, any effect analysis needs to take into account both these sets of users to gauge the net effects and to ascertain whether, on a rule of reason analysis, such effects are adverse and appreciable. Similarly, in the Play Store case, 18 Play Store case, para. 116. the CCI observed that app stores can be characterized as multi-sided platforms as they serve different interdependent groups, i.e., OEMs, smartphone users and app developers. It is plausible that in trying to gain favour with one side of the market, the platform may tend to cause or threaten harm to competition on the other side of the market. Hence, an analysis of CCI’s approach reveals that the multi-sided nature of platforms requires an assessment of the interdependence of the different sides on each other, as well as whether the appreciable adverse effects of competition (AAEC) are being felt by both sides of the market. The interdependence of the different sides of the market and the importance of the platform to the different user groups is assessed on the basis of network effects which enable the platform to create an ecosystem and then ‘tip’ the market in its favour. These factors are discussed below. [1] Network Effects Network effects are characterized as the increased usefulness of a service to the users as the number of users using the service increases. This creates a positive feedback loop for services that are offered, wherein an increase in the number of users/input to a service improves the value of the service to other users and, therefore, further attracts new users. This can be understood from the following flowchart which indicates that if we start from any of the points in the cycle, a positive feedback loop comes into operation: As the value derived by each producer increases, the number of producers finding the economic activity profitable also increases. Consequently, as more producers join the economic activity, the value to the consumer increases (for instance, in the form of increasing choice in the market). This then further attracts more consumers, completing the loop. This is a self-reinforcing loop that can be seen in operation in case of platforms such as e-commerce platforms, wherein the number of consumers increases as the number of sellers providing different and more variety of goods increase, which consequently choose the platform to sell products because of access to more consumers. Similarly, operation of network effects in cases of platforms are observed in case of platform service providers for social networking services, wherein a person joins the network only if a sufficient number of people are already part of the user base. This quality of the platform, as discussed in the next section, has the quality of creating barriers to entry for new entrants. The operation of network effects has been discussed by the CCI in several cases in the digital market. For instance, in Umar Javeed v. Google LLC and Ors. (‘Google Android case’),19 Umar Javeed v. Google LLC and Ors., Case No. 39 of 2018, para. 189. it was observed that the Google Play Store, i.e., an app store is a two-sided market which has been able to attract a large number of Android users on one side (due to the presence of a large number of apps) and a large number of app developers on the other side (due to potential to reach a large audience). On one hand, the OS which can run maximum number of apps would be most popular among the users and thus would attract the greatest number of users. On the other hand, app developers would prefer to develop apps for the OS which has the maximum number of users because access to the large user base would result in substantial economies of scale, given the high fixed costs and very low marginal costs associated with the development of such apps. Thus, on this basis, Google was found to enjoy a strong ‘network effect’ of a large user base and app developers in the market for app stores for Android OS. Similar to the Google Android case, cross-side network effects were also found to exist in the prima facie case in Together We Fight Society v. Apple Inc. and Anr. 20 Together We Fight Society v. Apple Inc. and Anr., Case No. 24 of 2021, para. 17. (‘Apple case’), between app developers and app users on Apple’s App Store. In the Google Android case, cross-platform network effects were also found to emanate from the linkages between the general web search market and the adjacent web browser market, such that Google’s dominance in the general web search market had a significant impact on the web browser market due to close links between the web browser market and general web search market.21 Google Android case, para. 290. Similarly, in XYZ v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors. 22 XYZ and Ors. v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., Case Nos 07 of 2020, 14 of 2021 and 35 of 2021, paras 82, 102. (‘Play Store case’), the CCI found that OSs exhibit indirect network effects, i.e., the benefit to users of an OS increases with the volume and quality of native apps they can access on that OS, and similarly, the benefit to app developers increases with the number of users they can access on an OS. It further observed that this is ‘likely to act as a barrier to new entry and expansion as it creates a ‘chicken and egg’ problem i.e., an operating system needs a critical mass of users to attract app developers, but also need a critical mass of app developers to attract users.’ Thus, network effects result in entry barriers because most consumers prefer an OS for which a large number of apps have already been written, and most developers prefer to create apps for an OS that already has a substantial user base. This ‘chicken-and-egg’ situation arising out of the barriers to entry means that apps will continue to be written for existing OS, which in turn ensures that consumers will continue to prefer it over new entrant OSs.23 Ibid., para. 86. Hence, it can be said that the positive feedback loop continues and makes it much more difficult for new entrants to achieve a commercially viable scale. The positive feedback loop is a consequence of the interlinkage and interdependence of the different sides of the market on each other in a multi-sided market. As highlighted in the MMT-Go case, 24 MMT-Go case, para. 190. through its intermediation service, the platform connects two distinct user groups, i.e., consumers and hotels, both of which positively value the increasing presence of each other on the platform. For the assessment of dominance, what was important was the role of the network effects in understanding the degree of dependence of the aggrieved hotels on MMT-Go’s platform.25 Ibid., para. 226. This was also illustrated in the cases against WhatsApp,26 WhatsApp case, paras 86, 87; In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc, Suo Moto Case No. 01 of 2021, para. 26. which is the most widely used app for instant messaging in India. A communication network/platform gets more valuable as more users join it, thereby benefiting from network effects. This, in turn, causes a strong lock-in effect for users, as switching to another platform gets difficult and meaningless until all or most of their social contacts also switch to the same other platform. Consequently, consumers were found to be dependent on WhatsApp, and network effects ensured that customers did not switch to other platforms easily. Barriers to entry may arise indirectly as a result of network effects since they lead to increased switching costs. As a result, new players may be disincentivized from entering the market. In this context, in the AIOVA v. Flipkart case, 27 AIOVA v. Flipkart case, para. 28. it was recognized that while it may be possible for new entrants to create online marketplace platforms, it is difficult for them to breach the advantage gained by incumbent players due to network effects. However, the operation of network effects is not ipso facto a factor which contributes to dominance. This means that that there can be certain countervailing market forces that reduce the ability of even a very large platform to insulate itself from competition. This was seen in the case of Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd. (‘Ola case’),28 Fast Track Call Cab Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. ANI Technologies Pvt. Ltd., Case Nos 6 & 74 of 2015, paras 122-132. where the CCI was examining the alleged abusive conduct of Ola in the market of radio taxi services. Basis the facts of the case, CCI was of the opinion that despite Ola having the largest network, the network effects were not strong enough to deter entry and rapid expansion of another big and fierce competitor ‘Uber’ in the relevant market of ‘radio taxi services in Bengaluru’. Further, since radio taxi apps could be easily downloaded on smartphones for free and could co-exist on the same handset, multi-homing was found to be possible for both drivers and riders. A successful network/platform requires that both sides of the market be thick, i.e., there are a large number of participants on both sides of the network/platform so that each participant has a substantial number of potential matches on the other side of the market. More riders means more demand scattered across a geographic region owing to a higher density of riders, leading to more ride requests on a particular platform as compared to its competitor, which in turn fuels the demand for more drivers to serve such riders. In this case, both Uber and Ola were found to be aggressively competing with each other to attract participants, i.e., drivers and riders, and hence, the network effects of one platform were not strong enough to deter the expansion of the other platform. Thus, CCI’s decisional practice29 MMT-Go case, para. 232; Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. v. Uber India Systems Pvt. Ltd and Ors., Case No. 96 of 2015. indicates that network effects play a pivotal role in dominance assessment, and the important aspect is the strength of the network, as that is what enables a large platform/network to stay dominant and insulate itself from potential competition by making it difficult for new entrants to challenge the established network. A comparison of the Google Android case and the Play Store case with the Ola case, for instance, reveals that network effects by themselves are not a factor for dominance because in all cases it cannot be said that they create insurmountable entry barriers. The takeaway is that what has to be seen is not only the size of the network but also its strength, such that it should have an actual effect on the state of competition in the market. At the same time, the role of interdependencies and interlinkages arising out of the network effects should not be overestimated but should be limited to understanding the impact of such interactions on the substitutability from primarily the aggrieved user side. This has to be assessed based on the facts and circumstances of the case. In the Ola case, the effect of Ola’s network on riders and drivers was mitigated due to corresponding network effects by Uber. However, in the cases pertaining to Google, the CCI noted that there were no effective alternatives to Google for the OEMs and app developers which could overcome the strength of Google’s network and consequently, Google has held to be in a super dominant position. [2] Tipping Effect The operation of economies of scale and network externalities not only favour a concentrated market but also erect entry barriers and present the distinct possibility of all consumers in the market deciding to opt for the product of a particular firm or for a particular technology, therefore leading to a ‘tipping’ of the market in favour of that firm and conferring it with a monopolist position in the market. This results in winner-take-all market outcomes. Thus, digital markets often ‘tip’ quickly and one or two winners or leading players emerge in a short span of time. Attaining a critical mass of users can lead to firms assuming position of entrenched market power. In such markets, it becomes extremely difficult for new entrants (even if such firms do not face any dearth of capital) to dislodge the incumbent from its position and for new players to enter the market. This can be illustrated from the findings of the CCI in the Play Store case. Once there was a critical mass of app developers and users on the Google Play Store, other app stores found it difficult to dislodge Google from its position because the market had tipped in favour of Google. This could be why Google’s Play Store became a ‘must have’ for OEMs, app developers and app users. In the MMT-Go case, 30 MMT-Go case, section 33 Order, para. 114. the CCI granted interim relief to the delisted hotels (informants) by ordering MMT-Go to relist them on its platform. These hotels were delisted pursuant to MMT-Go’s exclusive arrangement with OYO, which was a competitor of the informants. An analysis of CCI’s order reveals that winner-takes-all markets require immediate remedy (here, relisting of the hotels) because otherwise, the market would tip in favour of the incumbent player (MMT-Go and OYO). The following observations of CCI assume importance:Thus, as the facts appear before the Commission, the impugned exclusion under the Agreement has the dangerous probability to irreversibly alter the competitive landscape, especially in the franchisee budget hotel downstream market and may tip the market in favour of OYO causing irreparable harm to competition. A continued operationalisation of this exclusive agreement will therefore, compel all independent hotels seeking franchise services, for improving their brand recognition and for the purpose of signalling to the consumer the unobservable feature of quality to forcibly tie up with OYO. Any other franchisee arrangement with competitors like FabHotels and Treebo would delist them from the dominant intermediary i.e. MMT-Go reducing their visibility and footfalls. It becomes a Hobson’s choice in as much as the ‘market for franchising services for budget hotels in India’ is concerned. Such a likely outcome cannot be ignored and hence requires an immediate redressal. In winner takes all platform markets, if the impugned conduct is not based on the merits, eliminating such anti-competitive behaviour at the earliest assumes utmost importance. Network effects coupled with even small actions by the platforms may exclude and marginalize rivals, and further strengthen these effects that may be difficult to dilute at a later stage. Any remedy at that stage will be too little and too late as the suppliers’ side of the market, i.e. the franchisee service providers, can be potentially eliminated due to the alleged anticompetitive conduct. This case was very interesting since as a matter of practice, the CCI rarely grants interim relief. Therefore, what emerges from this discussion is the tendency of digital markets to tip in favour of a particular player, and thus, there is a discussion on whether India should adopt ex ante regulation. It is because multi-sided markets tend to create winner-takes-all outcome that the tipping effect is not only a factor in the assessment of dominance or anti-competitive effects of an agreement but also a factor in determining how abusive that dominance can be or how such an agreement can cause AAEC. This is why anticompetitive actions need to be nipped in the bud. At the same time, it is to be seen in actuality whether the market actually has a tendency to tip in favour of a particular player, viz. Google’s lack of competition tipped the market in its favour, and Ola and Uber’s competition prevented the tipping from taking place. [3] Creation of an Ecosystem The consequence of network effects and market tipping in favour of the incumbent platform is the creation of an ecosystem. Thus, in order to build up their network effects, platforms aim to create an ecosystem which the users find difficult to leave or attractive to stay within. For instance, in the Google Android case, through a combination of its commercial arrangements, such as anti-fragmentation obligations, lack of access to Google’s proprietary APIs due to which fork developers could not attract app developers, non-availability of ‘must-have’ apps like Play Store, Google Search, etc.31 Google Android case, para. 566. Google created an ecosystem consisting of the following markets: market for licensable OS for smart mobile devices in India, market for app stores for android smart mobile OS, market for general web search in India, market for non-OS specific web browsers in India and market for online video hosting platforms in India. These ecosystems do not operate in silos but are highly interdependent on each other, where a particular conduct of the platform operator influences various other segments of the ecosystem. It has been the continuous defence of Google that their services are offered for ‘free’ which is to the benefit of consumers. However, the said defence has not been appreciated by CCI and NCLAT when it notes that Google is building an ecosystem to derive advertisement revenue. In appeal, the NCLAT32 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, para. 211. observed that the substratum of Android OS on which the mobile ecosystem is based is, therefore, the foundation of the Google ecosystem, comprising of Google search-driven apps and other apps that derive data from users and funnel the traffic and data from them into Google Search results which becomes one of the important bases of its advertisement revenue. To illustrate in the context of one market, once the OEM has installed a particular app store on its devices, the same becomes a gateway between app developers and the users. An app developer to reach the user base on a particular OS/ecosystem (i.e., Android or iOS), must rely on the app store on that particular OS only. Thus, an app developer cannot reach an iOS user through Google’s Play Store and an Android user through Apple’s App Store, so they cannot confine their offerings exclusively to one of the ecosystems in order to expand their reach to the maximum set of consumers.33 Google Android case, para. 199.3. The CCI has also discussed the concept of an ecosystem in the Apple case, 34 Apple case, para. 14. wherein it noted that the smart device ecosystem of Apple (based on iOS) and Google (based on Android OS) have emerged as the two major mobile ecosystems, former being non-licensable and closed source whereas latter being licensable and open source. Being closed source means that Apple’s ecosystem is tightly knit and vertically and exclusively integrated throughout the value chain, wherein it offers apps, an app store as well as smart devices. This unique strategy of platforms to lock in consumers within the ecosystem of their service offerings by leveraging network effects has to be analysed in the context of securing market control. Therefore, the peculiar tendency of platforms to create an ecosystem to further entrench their position in the market also becomes a crucial factor in determining the anti-competitiveness and abusiveness of their conduct, if any. [B] Economies of Scale In the words of the Competition Law Review Committee, ‘Economies of Scale’ or ‘returns to scale’ means that ‘the cost of production is lesser in proportion to the number of customers served. As the customer base of a digital platform increases, the average cost incurred by such companies in providing services will reduce significantly.’35 Report of the Competition Law Review Committee, para. 1.3. While this characteristic may also be present in traditional markets, the tendency of digital markets is such that the returns are maximized (due to network effects, as discussed earlier), thereby creating competitive advantages for incumbent players. This is attributable to the fact that in digital markets, the cost of producing the information/service/value is much less than what is proportional to the number of customers served. The Parliamentary Standing Committee on Finance in its Report on Anti-Competitive Practices by Big Tech Companies (‘Big Tech Report’)36 Standing Committee on Finance, Anti-Competitive Practices by Big Tech Companies (53rd Report, 2022). has also observed that digital markets are essentially different from traditional physical markets because while the former experiences increasing returns to scale (as the size of the firm increases, so do the returns, with rapidly decreasing marginal costs), the latter experiences diminishing returns to scale (as the size of a business entity increases, its returns increase only up to the maximum efficiency point, or the equilibrium point, and then continue to decrease). This means that growing bigger is a competitive disadvantage in physical markets, but in digital markets, it is a significant advantage. To illustrate, in the Play Store case, 37 Play Store case, para. 407. the CCI observed that the different products and services offered by Google to multiple sides of the market are not only ‘intricately intertwined and interwoven with each other, but the products/services offered by the platform operator (Google in this case) derive strength from each other due to economies of scope and scale.’ As a result, it becomes extremely difficult for new entrants to replicate such an ecosystem because ‘Competition in such a scenario is among ecosystems and not just the verticals or independent services. In such a case, the entire platform has to be taken as one unit to account for the cross-market externalities between platform sides, and revenue generated therefrom has to be seen as a whole.’ An analysis of CCI’s approach reveals that economies of scale are interpreted as barriers to entry in the market because the bigger the platform, the more difficult it is for a new or existing player to compete with the strength of that platform. In fact, economies of scale are a rather difficult barrier to overcome because even where entry is not completely deterred, entrants may take time to achieve efficient levels of production, raise capital and build the necessary framework but even despite this entry and sinking of capital, the incumbent could nevertheless retain market power for a substantial period of time. Hence, the existence of such expansion barriers is a key factor in competition assessment and CCI has taken it into account in assessment of large platforms in determining entry and expansion barriers. [C] Switching Costs As the name suggests, switching costs refer to the ‘costs’ incurred by users when they ‘switch’ from one product/service provider to another. These can be of various types, such as financial, psychological (time, efforts) and technological (learning new technologies, etc.). In the Play Store case, 38 Ibid., para. 91. the CCI identified in detail the switching costs of shifting from one OS to another for app developers as well as app users: (i) App developers – Apps developed for one OS cannot be run on another OS due to various platform-differentiating factors, such as programming languages and APIs which are designed and developed specifically for each OS. Porting applications across OS is a tiresome task and requires significant time, resources and effort.39 Ibid., paras 124, 126. (ii) App users – Since apps and in-app content are OS-specific, switching may result in denial of access to these apps. Further, factors such as consumer inertia, satisfaction with the characteristics of Android devices and brand loyalty also act as switching costs. Switching costs can be tangible, for instance, the cost of switching from low-priced Android OS devices to high-priced Apple devices, as well as intangible, such as learning new interfaces to navigate a different mobile OS, transferring existing data, downloading and purchasing existing apps on the new device, etc.40 Ibid., paras 69, 108. In contrast, in the Ola case, riders could easily switch between Ola and Uber through the touch of a button on their smartphones. This means that there were nil switching costs. In Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC and Ors. (‘Google Search Bias’),41 Matrimony.com Limited v. Google LLC and Ors., Case Nos 07 and 30 of 2012, paras 118, 199, 367. while examining Google’s dominance in the online general web search and search advertising markets, the CCI observed that ‘considering the while high technology services demand continuous innovation, given barriers to entry and Google’s scale advantage, it is unlikely that a large number of users would switch to a competing search engine in the short or medium term.’ Network effects can raise switching costs for users and barriers to entry for potential competitors, and hence, users switch less frequently to other suppliers, which has a market power-enhancing effect. However, in this case, the CCI ultimately found that users are free to switch search engines in their browsers and there is no restriction to this effect. Thus, it can perhaps be said that where there are costs to be incurred, whether financial (e.g., purchasing a new app) or intangible (e.g., loss of data), switching becomes difficult, and we can see ‘platform stickiness,’ i.e., the tendency of users to stick to a platform. The insistence on default settings is also a key point in analysing stickiness. The trend that emerges from CCI’s decisional practice is that switching costs have been assessed in every case pertaining to platform markets, and the substitutability analysis effectively hinges on the same: where switching costs exist, substitutability is low, and where they are absent, substitutability is high. As a result, the presence or absence of switching costs becomes a relevant tool to prove the level of competition and presence of effective alternatives in the market. For example, in the Ola case, the CCI found that there is no switching cost for riders and drivers to switch between Ola and Uber; hence, there was no platform stickiness; conversely, in the Google Android case, due to the default setting, it was found that there is stickiness due to both pre-installation and premium placement requirements imposed by Google on OEMs. [D] Multi-homing Multi-homing refers to the ability of users to simultaneously use and interact with multiple competing platforms or service providers. Rather than ‘single-homing’, i.e., being locked into one platform/service provider, users are free to utilize and choose from multiple competitors. It is important to note that even the prevalence of multi-homing in a digital market is not a necessary indication of competition in the market. This is because digital platforms often operate in niche markets and target distinct user groups or serve separate purposes which may not be substitutable for other platforms that appear to offer similar services. For instance, in the Play Store case, 42 Play Store case, para. 110. the CCI observed that multi-homing by app developers across Apple’s iOS and the App Store on the one hand and Google’s Android OS and the Play Store on the other hand, does not tantamount to substitutability as the two OSs serve a different and distinct set of customers. App developers need to maximize their reach and, thus, would not like to confine their offerings exclusively to one of the ecosystems as it would imply losing a sizable portion of the potential consumers’ revenue who are available on the other platform. Further, the ability to switch between platforms or substitute one platform with another is not the same as multi-homing. This was clarified by the CCI in the Google Android case,43 Google Android case, para. 133. wherein it was observed that (m)ulti-homing by app developers should not be confused with demand-side substitution. The app developers have their apps on both licensable and non-licensable mobile OS, but the fact that app developers have apps both on Play Store and Apple app store does not tantamount to substitutability since the app developers need to have their app on both app stores to reach the maximum consumer base. Thus, these are complementary markets – this does not tantamount to substitutability. This is because they cannot substitute one OS for the other since Android and iOS users are two different and distinct sets of customers. App developers offer their apps in both ecosystems so as to not lose out on a sizable portion of the potential consumers’ revenue who are available on the other platform. In the MMT-Go case44 MMT-Go case, para. 198. also, it was argued that consumers multi-home on different channels of hotel booking. On this point, the CCI observed that even if there are consumers who use both these modes (online as well as offline), they did not appear to be using these channels of booking interchangeably for the same booking, given the characteristically different offering that online and offline modes offer. Hence, ‘multi-homing even by the same class of customers may not necessarily imply substitution in the sense used for the purpose of defining markets. Even if, most customers use multiple methods to book hotels his in itself implies that these methods/channels are complementary and not substitutable even from a customer’s perspective.’ Therefore, the position is that evidence of multi-homing by itself does not mean that CCI will exonerate a firm from dominance. Digital markets and the functioning of platforms in these markets are very complex, such that what may seem to be a perceived alternative may not necessarily amount to demand-side substitution. Further, the key part to analyse here is that the relevant market is defined not from the product perspective (i.e., hotel rooms) but from the service perspective (online versus offline booking service). From the cases discussed above, it can be concluded that even though consumers were multi-homing between online and offline channels of hotel booking in the MMT-Go case and app developers had made their apps available across licensable and non-licensable OS in the Google cases, it was not held to be in the same relevant market. Hence, what has to be seen is whether functional substitutes are available and if they are actually substitutable from the perspective of the consumers (both end consumers and B2B). [E] Prevalence of Zero-Price Strategy In digital markets, the producer only has to bear the cost of offering and developing an initial service. Once the initial service is produced, the cost of offering an additional consumer the option of using that service is very small or zero. For instance, a software developer needs to first expend capital in creating a new software. However, once the software has been developed, the marginal cost incurred by the developer in serving that software to an additional consumer would be very low in relation to the cost incurred while creating the first copy of the software. This feature of digital markets coupled with the propensity for strong network externalities has led to the emergence of a marketing strategy of providing products for free, or the ‘zero-price’ strategy. It is sustainable for firms to adopt this strategy because the digital economy presents many services that may be offered free of charge. Hence, market demand tends to rise which in turn increases the perceived value of the product. Thus, digital firms are able to adopt unique strategies which would not have been viable in a traditional market, such as: introducing free products to leverage network externalities of a market (for instance, as a new entrant in the telecommunications industry); taking advantage of cross-network effects and by pricing services at a zero-price and choosing to earn from other side of the market like advertising revenues instead (e.g., newspapers, free social media, etc.); ‘Freemium’ models, where firms may offer a basic version of the product for free, and charge for premium versions/added features (e.g., music subscriptions). In the Google Android case, 45 Google Android case, paras 208-210. Google had argued that it provided search services to users for free. In this regard, the DG in its investigation report noted that offering a service free of charge is an advantageous commercial strategy for two-sided platforms that connect distinct but interdependent demands: the two sides being general search services and online search advertising. Therefore, advertisers indirectly fund the general search services offered to users. The CCI concluded that the collection and monetization of user data for funding business operations (as discussed in detail subsequently in this chapter) implies that the provision of general search services is an economic activity, even if it is offered for free. Keeping Android OS open and ‘free’ of monetary consideration is thus in as much Google’s interest as it claims it to be for the OEMs and users.46 Ibid., para. 470. In appeal, the NCLAT47 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, para. 210. observed that the multi-sided digital platforms, such as the one operated by Google, consist of software that are interconnected and interact at the machine level and the user level through flow of traffic and data, and therefore, the business model, incentives and the revenue streams are a net result of such interplay of software and programs. Thus, while Google purports to offer its mobile OS ‘free’, there are a number of apps and services on the same device which give rise to huge traffic and data which are effectively monetized through advertisements which bring revenue to Google. The multi-sided nature of these digital platforms is clear from the fact that on one side, there are the OEMs and app developers, while on the second side are the users that generate data and traffic, and on the third side are the advertisers who use these digital platforms for advertising their goods and services. Thus, the substratum of Android OS on which the mobile ecosystem is based, is the foundation of the Google ecosystem, comprising of Google search-driven apps and other apps that derive data from users and funnel the traffic and data from them into Google Search results which becomes one of the important bases of its advertisement revenue. The payment received from advertisers is, therefore, dependent on the data and traffic coming from the various apps, including the search apps feeding primarily in and also other strengthening and refining Google search service. Therefore, an analysis of the decisions of the CCI and the NCLAT reveals that in competition assessment in digital markets, strictly looking at price from the perspective of one side of the market is insufficient because it is possible that one side does not pay in revenue terms at all (e.g., users pay through their data, but search services are effectively zero priced). Rather, a holistic analysis has to be undertaken to assess how the pricing strategy of the firm is functioning across multiple sides of the same market and even in interdependent markets. [F] Access to Data One of the preliminary legal points which may be discussed is whether having access to a large amount of data can be a determining factor in analysing whether an enterprise enjoys a dominant position or not. The Competition Law Review Committee also noted in its Report that section 19(4)(b) of the Competition Act expressly refers to ‘resources of the enterprise’ as a factor for determining dominant position.48 Report of the Competition Law Review Committee, para. 2.15. Hence, it is broad enough to include control over data as a factor for determining the dominant position of an enterprise. Big tech companies, such as social networking, e-commerce, online search platforms, etc., do not follow the conventional model of charging their customers to make a profit, and most of their services are free. One of the ways by which consumers pay for the services is through their data. Data is what these companies require to provide better services or more personalized services, which ultimately hooks the user to such services. This is why it is often said that ‘data is the new oil’ because access to data in large volumes in digital markets is crucial to obtaining and sustaining market power. Once an enterprise has enough number of users and their data, as well as the capacity to convert such data into useful information, it is very difficult for a new enterprise to enter the market. The data advantage of market leaders over a period of time overshadows the offerings of newer platforms in terms of quality and data-fed personalization, while a small entrant struggles to attain a critical mass of users and user data. The more users or businesses get onboarded to a tech giant’s business, the more data the platform is likely to accumulate. With their data pool constantly increasing, the tech giants continue to entrench their position in the digital market. Hence, access to data also acts as an entry barrier. In Vijay Gopal v. Big Tree Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., 49 Vijay Gopal v. Big Tree Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. and Ors., Case No. 46 of 2021, para. 34. while examining the data collection capacity of BookMyShow, an online ticket booking platform, the CCI opined that exclusivity relating to data ownership can increase the bargaining power of the platform over time. This is because access to data strengthens network effects, thereby limiting inter-platform competition. Consequently, the aspect of exclusive ownership of and access to data by the intermediary was directed to be investigated. [1] Using Data as a Competitive Advantage In several recent cases, the CCI has discussed the competitive advantage gained by a platform which has access to user data. For instance, in the Google Android case, 50 Google Android case, para. 263. the CCI observed that vertically integrated business operations of Google allowed it to pool the data gathered from each service, and process the same to refine the service offered. This resulted in a superior monetization ability through online ads, thereby conferring a significant and overwhelming competitive edge to Google over its competitors. In its appeal,51 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, paras 210-216. the NCLAT noted that the business model, incentives and revenue streams of digital platforms such as Google in the present case are interconnected through data. Even services which are seemingly offered for free are essentially aimed at giving rise to huge traffic and data which is subsequently monetized through advertisement revenue. In fact, the different services offered by Google are a channel to derive user data and funnel the same into Google Search to refine its services and improve its monetization. On this basis, that the revenue of Google is fuelled by its data advantage and its bundled and interwoven services, the NCLAT refused to reduce the penalty imposed by the CCI upon Google. Similarly, in the Play Store case, 52 Play Store case, paras 278-283, 307, 308. the CCI found on the basis of the DG investigation that Google had access to a significant volume and category of granular data of the app users including complete personal as well as financial transaction information. However, this data was not shared with the app developers, thereby impacting their ability to improve their services and compete effectively in their respective domains. This confiscation resulted in Google having the ability to exploit a vast amount of extremely valuable user data, such as by offering targeted offers and promotions. By having control on the data, Google was also in a position to put its competitors in a disadvantageous position in the downstream markets. What was noteworthy was that CCI took into account that Google’s proprietary apps were competing with the other apps in the ecosystem, and such use of data worked to Google’s advantage. The same situation was discussed in the Apple case, 53 Apple case, para. 29. wherein CCI in its prima facie opinion observed that Apple had access to data collected from the users of its downstream competitors, i.e., app developers which would enable it to improve its own services. However, such competitors may not have access to this data for improvisation/innovation of their own app. This would result in a competitive advantage for Apple over its competitors. In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc.,54 In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc., Suo Moto Case No. 01 of 2021, para. 30. another aspect of competition vis-à-vis data was considered. WhatsApp was alleged to be sharing the personal data of users with its group companies, i.e., other Facebook companies. The CCI observed that one of the stated purposes of data sharing was to target ad offerings on other Facebook products by building user profiles through cross-linking of data collected across services. Such data sharing by WhatsApp with Facebook amounted to degradation of non-price parameters of competition, such as quality, which resulted in objective detriment to consumers without any acceptable justification. This was found to prima facie amount to imposition of unfair terms and conditions upon the users. Therefore, the CCI has considered data to be a tool to boost innovation of the controller of the data to the detriment of other players in the market (who may not have access to such data). As more data accumulates, the competitiveness of the firm also increases, and rivals start to get foreclosed on the market. Further, the above cases also highlight that data not only confers a competitive advantage in the existing business but also often grants a competitive advantage to the firm in related markets as well. [2] Data as a Tool to Create Revenue and New Business Verticals The primary source of revenue for digital platforms collecting data is through monetization of this data in the form of advertisements. Further, with large data to process, an enterprise has the opportunity to anticipate consumer needs and demand in more than one sector. Armed with this data, the enterprise can create its own products/services which the data shows consumers may prefer. This can be seen in emerging digital markets, as discussed in the later parts of this chapter. The Big Tech Report also analysed how access to data gives an advantage to digital platforms by enabling them to ‘collect and store large amounts of data for comprehensively tracking and profiling end customers and business users. Privileged data from one market may be, thus, used by a leading platform in another market to gain competitive advantage. This further leads to the position of the leading platform getting entrenched.’55 Big Tech Report, para. 1.18. In the Google Android case, 56 Google Android case, paras 472, 473. it was found that mandatory pre-installation of Google search (widget on the default home screen) on all Android devices under MADA ensures user traffic and valuable user data for Google that it can collect, combine and leverage for its search advertising business. Hence, through MADA requirements, Google was in a position to leverage its dominant position in the Android ecosystem to maintain and strengthen its dominance in the search advertising sector. The data generated from each app of Google, such as Google Search, Maps, YouTube, etc., can complement the other and, thus, gives ‘Google a big data advantage over its rivals. Importantly, it also raises the willingness to pay by advertisers. Thus, in presence of cross market data advantage, pushing Android with other apps is a business strategy for Google.’ In the Play Store case57 Play Store case, para. 282. also, it was found that Google uses the data of the app developers to support its vast advertisement business by, among other things, selling access to the data back to the developers. This observation was strengthened by virtue of the advertising revenue earned by Google through advertisements in the Google Play Store. Therefore, an analysis of the CCI and NCLAT’s findings on data reveals that data is a factor of market control, and access to large troves of data allows a firm to further entrench its market power. Access to data has wide-ranging implications on competition right from securing a competitive advantage, using it to create entry barriers and foreclosing competition by improving one’s own innovation to the exclusion of others and also expanding into new markets. Since firms cannot be stopped altogether from exploiting the data that they have gathered, at best, they need to be cautious about not abusing such access as a tool to prevent their rivals from competing on merits. [G] Across Platform Parity Agreements Across Platform Parity Agreements (APPAs), also called Retail Most Favoured Nation (MFN) clauses, are stipulations where the sellers guarantee an online platform, terms (whether price and/or non-price) that are at least as favourable as those granted to any other platform, thus ensuring the former an advantage over its competitors. By securing such favourable terms, the platforms attempt to guarantee the best available price and/or terms for a given product to its final consumers. The Competition Law Review Committee also noted in its Report that:58 Report of the Competition Law Review Committee, para. 2.9. MFN clauses can potentially give rise to both pro-competitive benefits and anti-competitive effects on the market. The extent of the effect depends on various factors such as the market structure, the form of the MFN clause used, and the characteristics of the sellers and buyers in the market. In light of this Committee agreed that such agreements should be analysed under the ‘rule of reason’ or ‘effects’ test under Section 3(4) of the Competition Act. Having said that, the Committee noted that in cases where such clauses seek to facilitate a cartel, fix price or supply terms, it will be hit by the provisions of Section 3(3) of the Competition Act. In the MMT-Go case, 59 MMT-Go case, para. 247. the CCI discussed the two categories of APPAs, i.e., wide and narrow parity clauses, as described in the following flowchart: Thus, to illustrate, on the one hand, a wide parity clause prevents a hotel from offering better prices and terms on all other sales/distribution channels (including the hotel’s own website and other OTAs). On the other hand, a narrow parity clause prevents a hotel from offering better prices and terms only on its own website, but it can offer better prices on other channels. In this case, on the basis of the investigation conducted by the DG, the CCI found that MMT-Go imposed two kinds of wide parity clauses on the hotel partners:60 Ibid., paras 246-260. (i) Price/Rate parity obligations – whereby the hotel partners were restrained from offering better prices to other OTAs or on their own websites. (ii) Room parity – whereby hotels could not refuse to provide rooms on MMT-Go’s platform at any given point of time if the rooms were being provided on any other OTA. MMT-Go’s wide price parity obligations were held anti-competitive because of their ability to restrict price competition and, thus, making it difficult for an entrant or an existing player to establish a market presence, thereby creating entry/expansion barriers for new/other platforms due to the hotels’ price parity clause with MMT-Go. Parallelly, the room parity condition restricted the hotels from providing differential availability of rooms to other OTAs/alternate portals, including such OTAs that charge lower commission rates or have unmet demand at a given point in time. Resultantly, the hotels were made to relinquish their price-setting freedom on the MMT-Go platform, which decided the final price payable by consumers after a discount on its platform, and also on all other platforms/channels through the price parity condition. The impact of these parity obligations was that they reduced the incentives for other OTAs to compete with MMT-Go. For instance, even if they offered lower commission than what was charged by MMT-Go, the parity obligations imposed by MMT-Go prevented the hotels from offering better rates to the other OTAs. As a result, the price competition softened and there could be no competition on the prices offered to the end consumer. Given the lack of incentive to compete on commission, the only way to compete would be on the basis of discounts, and consequently, the OTA which would have the maximum capacity to fund discounts would end up intermediating the maximum bookings. Further, the dominant OTA, i.e., MMT-Go, would end up intermediating the maximum number of bookings, and consequently, the hotels would have to pay the maximum commission to MMT-Go. Per the CCI, this had a cascading effect and created an ecosystem which reinforced the strength of MMT-Go and foreclosed competition for other OTAs. In the MMT-Go case, 61 Ibid., para. 264. the CCI also observed that parity terms imposed by MMT-Go cannot be justified on the grounds that they are purportedly an ‘industry practice’. Rather, ‘the effect and implications of a conduct indulged in by a dominant platform on its users and competition in the relevant market is fundamentally different from when a smaller insignificant player does it.’ On this basis, the CCI observed that since presence on MMT-Go was indispensable for hotels to access online customers on account of its dominance, hotels, especially budget hotels with no brand recognition had two choices: either accept the terms that MMT-Go offers or forego significant online opportunity of sale and discoverability. Thus, a comparison of the combined effect of deep discounting and parity obligations imposed by a dominant platform (that has already reaped the benefits of network effects in its favour) with the parity conditions of other platforms was not held to be a like-to-like comparison. Hence, this is an extension of the principle of special responsibility of dominant players. In National Restaurant Association of India v. Zomato Limited and Bundl Technologies Private Limited (‘Zomato Swiggy case’)62 National Restaurant Association of India v. Zomato Limited and Bundl Technologies Private Limited, Case No. 16 of 2021, paras 77, 85, 86. also, allegations were raised regarding price parity terms in the agreements/contracts between Zomato/Swiggy and restaurant partners, which prevented the latter from developing their own direct ordering channels or listing on a competing platform by offering more competitive rates. In its prima facie opinion, the CCI observed that such ‘widely defined price parity arrangements/restrictions may result in removal of the incentive for platforms to compete on the commission they charge to restaurants, may inflate the commissions and final prices paid by consumers and may also prevent entry of new low-cost platforms’. Thus, agreements were likely to cause AAEC in the market and were directed to be investigated. [1] Free Riding CCI’s Market Study on E-Commerce63 Market Study on E-Commerce in India, 2020, paras 93-95. noted that price parity clauses can give rise to the following competition concerns:(i) Existing platforms, in the presence of parity clauses, may not have sufficient incentive to compete on commission rates. (ii) A new platform could charge lower commissions to service providers that offered a discounted rate in order to gain a toehold in the market. However, parity clauses imposed by incumbent platforms may serve as entry barriers for such low-cost platforms. (iii) Parity clauses can also help cement coordination or tacit understanding that may exist between platforms by disincentivizing deviation from a consensus rate of commission. Thus, they can potentially lead to higher commission rates and discourage entry. However, parity clauses can be justified on the grounds that they protect investment incentives by preventing free riding. Platforms make considerable investments to improve and better their functionalities. Free riding means that a service provider may take advantage of these features of a superior quality platform to draw customer attention to its product and then sell the product through its website or another platform at a lower price. As a result, competition would be reduced to price alone, and quality would no longer be a consideration since service providers would want to offer their services where they are charged the lowest price. Thus, without price parity restrictions, competition on non-price parameters may be harmed. However, it is a fact-specific question whether a price parity clause may be justified as procompetitive in any market and under what circumstances. The analysis of price parity clauses will, inter alia, consider how plausible the free-riding concern is and to what extent it would affect the investment incentive for platforms versus the direct loss to price competition caused by a price parity restriction. Hence, mere assertion would not suffice, and it is for the incumbent platform to demonstrate how price parity solves the free-riding problem. For instance, in the MMT-Go case, 64 MMT-Go case, para. 265. MMT-Go had argued that parity conditions are necessary to prevent free riding. However, the CCI held that the free-riding concerns were mitigated due to the discounts offered by MMT-Go to build its network as well as the increased competition between OTAs, as these factors together meant that users do not have any incentive to free ride. Further, any investments made by MMT-Go on its platform were not hotel-specific, so even if hotels did engage in free riding, that did not take away MMT-Go’s incentives to invest in its platform. Yet, narrow parity clauses were found to be justifiable. The relevant extracts are as follows:MMT-Go claims the parity condition to be driven by the need to address free riding concerns. The Commission is of the view that the potential free riding problem does not have any perceptible relevance in the presence of the discounts that MMT-Go offers over and above the rate at which a hotel lists its rooms. When looked at in light of the deep discounts that MMT-Go has been offering over the years (as the data on record bring out clearly), it does not appear to be a realistic possibility that a user would discover a hotel on MMT-Go and then book it through another channel/OTA at a higher price point. It is MMT-Go’s own submission that discounts were provided to build network. Thus, through its discounting strategy MMT-Go ensured both user footfalls and conversions on its platform. Further, when hotels have more OTA choices, the increased competition between the OTAs may lower fees/commission paid to hotels, so consumers may not have as much incentive to free ride. Moreover, what emerged from the cases dealt by other authorities, it has been observed that there may not be any concern about the effect of free riding on incentives to invest (in case of OTAs) because the investments of the platform are not “specific” to a particular hotel – thus, if there is free riding on the part of a particular hotel, this does not undermine the incentive of the platform to invest overall. Be that as it may, the Commission finds some merit in the finding of the DG that the narrow price parity clauses may be justifiable on the grounds of free riding problem. Hotels and OTA are in a contractual relationship and therefore, free riding of hotels on the investment made by OTAs in advertisement and promotion can be detrimental to the business of OTAs. However, wide price parity obligation is not justifiable as it reduces the competition between the OTAs and may have an adverse impact on prices charged to end-consumers. Therefore, the discussion on parity clauses can be concluded by stating that the free-riding concerns of a platform in terms of its incentives to invest have to be balanced with the freedom of business users to list on multiple distribution channels without being restricted in terms of price since ultimately competition largely boils down to competing on price. It is exactly this balance (which narrow parity clauses seem to meet) that CCI analyses in checking whether parity obligations are anticompetitive or not.

§6.04 EMERGING DIGITAL MARKETS [A] Technology Technology is crucial to the development of any digital platform. In this section, platforms whose business revolves around leveraging technology and its derivatives, such as OS, software, etc., have been described. In the Play Store case and Google Android case, the CCI on the basis of the investigation of the DG identified several factors which contributed to the dominance of Google. These factors, as outlined below, can be considered common features of technology companies:(i) Barriers to entry in the form of significant capital and investment for research and development of the platform. These investments are required not only at the stage of initial development, launch and marketing but also subsequently in maintenance, updating, and development of new versions and new features/releases.65 Play Store case, paras 92-95; Google Android case, paras 115-116.6. (ii) APIs and limited or restricted access to licences and other IPRs of the dominant platform also hinder the entry of new players in the market.66 Play Store case, paras 98-100. Without access to the same, bug fixes, automatic updates, new releases, enhancements included in the new versions of Android, etc., will not be possible. While it is technically feasible to ship a phone without Google Play Services, OEMs who do so will be at a significant disadvantage to OEMs that ship with Google Play Services.67 Ibid., para. 184; Google Android case, para. 171. (iii) These restrictions are facilitated through Google’s commercial arrangements with OEMs, i.e., MADA/RSA/AFA, etc., through which OEMs were/are restrained from manufacturing and selling devices incorporating the forked version of Android OS (as discussed in detail in Chapter 3). (iv) Status quo bias in favour of Google because access to the Google Play Store was important for both smart mobile device users as well as app developers. Since Google’s Play Store offers the highest number of apps, users seem to have a preference for it and consider it as a ‘must have’ app.68 Play Store case, paras 186, 193. A combined operation of the above factors makes Google indispensable. As network effects increase, the strength of Google’s network increases further. These factors are likely to be present in any dominant technology platform. [1] Concept of Gatekeepers The term ‘gatekeeper’ is used to describe the role and function of dominant platforms in digital markets. In a literal sense, a gatekeeper is a person or entity that controls access to a particular place or service, determining the rules of the ecosystem, i.e., who can enter and under what conditions. In the digital context, the term is applied because these dominant platforms act as intermediaries that control access to specific markets, services, or opportunities. In the Google Android case, 69 Google Android case, paras 89-94. the CCI observed that Google governs the running of Android as it determines the roadmap and decides on features and new releases. It also controls the compatibility of derivatives of the Android OS by placing various restrictions on the OEMs. For instance, since Google owns the IPR to the Android, the use of the ‘Android’ trademark on hardware, packaging or marketing materials of devices is restricted to Android-compatible devices only. Therefore, in order to use these IPRs, the OEMs must adhere to the obligations imposed by Google. Thus, even though the Android mobile OS is released by Google for free under an open-source licence, OEMs are highly dependent on Google as they have to ensure that all the hardware and software features of their devices are compatible with the latest versions of Android released by Google from time to time; otherwise, such devices will become incompatible, and all apps on the same will be dysfunctional. This is one instance of gatekeeping as Google seeks to control access to Android. Similarly, in the context of online searches, the CCI found that Google has used Android as a vehicle, especially to cement the dominance of its search engine. ‘Google’s strategy rests on the reach, scale and market power of Android, which allows Google to have control over a vast majority of smart mobile devices that serve as key gateways to the internet. Keeping Android OS open and ‘free’ of monetary consideration is thus in as much Google’s interest as it claims it to be for the OEMs and users.’ For app developers, gatekeepers have also been found to restrict installation of TPAP. A leading example of this is through anti-steering provisions. Primarily linked with app stores, ‘anti-steering’ provisions refer to prohibitions laid down by app stores which prevent app developers and app users from moving out of the platform of the app store and using alternatives. This issue came to the fore in the Play Store case, 70 Play Store case, para. 255. where the CCI held that Google’s gatekeeper position in the Android mobile ecosystem makes it ‘well placed to leverage its power to protect and further enhance its dominance in online search by making it difficult for rival search service providers to enter and compete effectively in the mobile search space. The well-regarded benefits of the open-source system of Android cannot legitimize an exclusionary conduct that causes harm to competition in any specific area/markets.’ Accordingly, in the Play Store case, 71 Ibid., para. 268. the CCI observed that ‘gatekeepers like Google should not use their unassailable position to require the app developers, which are dependent on them, to use any of their additional or other service together with its core services viz. app store services’. This is because such conduct by the gatekeeper can be detrimental to app developers ‘by reducing their ability to choose their business partners in respect of these additional or other services and also affects their ability to offer innovative solutions’ as they have to accept the gatekeeper’s services. In the context of the service fee charged by Google to paid apps and apps offering paid in-app content, the CCI laid down the following proposition:72 Ibid., para. 326. in general any conditions (including price related conditions) imposed on business users, by the gatekeepers or platform operators, should be fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory. The pricing and other general conditions should not grant any benefit on the gatekeeper which is disproportionate to the service provided by the gatekeeper to business users. The NCLAT, in appeal73 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, para. 211. to the Google Android case, also observed that Google Search increasingly gains value through network effects as greater traffic on its search engine means a greater volume of data is being generated. This data is then sold to advertisers, thereby helping Google Search gather increased advertising revenue and cement Google’s position in the mobile search market. The NCLAT categorically observed that Google’s business model makes it clear that:there is no single app or service that can be singled out to say that the revenue of Google is derived only out of its user functionality because the user traffic and data comes from not only Google Search and You Tube but also other apps like Google Maps, Google Cloud, Play Store and Gmail etc. In fact, the entire Android System in the mobile device, through tracking device usage, becomes an important source of data for the generic search result. Thus, the data and traffic from multiple apps and service and the entire Android eco-system is funneled into Google Search which is monetized through advertisements. This illustrates how once gatekeepers have created an ecosystem, they can continue to feed and sustain the ecosystem through a complex interdependence between their various service offerings. An analysis of the above cases reveals that CCI has taken a hard stance on technology platforms owing to their gatekeeper position because the abusive and anticompetitive implications of their conduct have worsened since they control all the key access points to the market for the users. Armed with the powerful tools of digital markets, i.e., network effects, data, etc., they are able to chokehold competition. This is why a need is being felt to perhaps introduce sweeping changes in the statutory and regulatory scheme, as has been discussed in the last part of this chapter. [B] Social Media and Communication The leading cases pertaining to social media and communication platforms are the prima facie cases against WhatsApp. In the WhatsApp case, 74 WhatsApp case, para. 84. the CCI observed that WhatsApp Messenger was the most widely used app for social messaging, followed by Facebook Messenger in the relevant market for OTT messaging apps through smartphones in India. Since WhatsApp Messenger and Facebook Messenger were owned by the same group, they were not constrained by each other, rather, they possessed combined strength as a group. The key factor was the presence of direct network effects whereby if more people started using WhatsApp, then it would become more valuable to the existing users since they would have more contacts to communicate with. This leads to a win-win situation for both the users as well as the platform. However, competition concerns arise out of the flip side of the same situation: a stronger network of WhatsApp will deter new and existing messaging apps since no users will be willing to switch to a different messaging app unless all of their contacts also switch platforms. This allows the incumbent platform to exploit its dominance, as was seen in In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc.75 In Re: Updated Terms of Service and Privacy Policy for WhatsApp Users v. WhatsApp LLC and Facebook, Inc., Suo Moto Case No. 01 of 2021, paras 22-32. In this case, WhatsApp was found to be prima facie abusing its dominance by sharing data with its group companies, i.e., other companies of Facebook without obtaining user consent. Such data-sharing was allegedly for marketing and advertising and was made a mandatory precondition for availing WhatsApp’s services. In a previous instance, similar allegations were raised against WhatsApp, but the differentiating factor was that users had the option to opt out’ of sharing user account information with ‘Facebook’ within thirty days of agreeing to the updated terms of service and privacy policy.76 Vinod Kumar Gupta v. WhatsApp Inc, Case No. 99 of 2016. However, no such option was granted under the present policy update. In the present case, WhatsApp attempted to abuse its dominant position to exploit user data and compel users to share such data once it had built a strong network by not giving a similar opt-out/carve-out to the policy update.
