Reduction in consumer data protection and loss of control over their personalized data by the users can also be taken as reduction in quality under antitrust law since it is a non-price parameter of competition. Access to such data can also lead to exclusionary effects since WhatsApp/Facebook would be able to further entrench/reinforce their position and leverage themselves in neighbouring or even in unrelated markets such as the display advertising market. Thus, these circumstances are an example of how dominant platforms can abuse their dominant position and reduce competition in the market after building a strong network on which users are locked in. CCI’s inquiries into WhatsApp also mark a first-of-its-kind investigation into a non-price factor for abuse (i.e., user privacy and data) by an allegedly dominant platform. Therefore, it can also be deduced that CCI’s approach is pragmatic and takes into account the immediate (such as unfair imposition in the nature of a take-it-or-leave-it option to users) as well as long term likely effects (such as foreclosure of competition and creation of new business verticals by exploiting user data) of policy decisions. [C] Online Search The CCI has examined competition issues in the online search market in two decided cases – Google Search Bias and Google Android case. Presently, the search market is also being examined in the context of an investigation ordered into the digital advertising market, as described in the next section. In Google Search Bias, the CCI found Google to be dominant in the relevant markets of ‘Online General Web Search Services in India’ and ‘Online Search Advertising Services in India’. On the basis of an investigation by the DG, Google was held to be fixing search results on certain general online searches which meant that users were not seeing organic results but rather the results that were already ‘fixed’ by Google. Another important finding was that Google collected data from the search side of the platform through its search engines and then monetized it on the search advertising side of the market. This again highlights how platforms use data as a means to leverage and boost their competitiveness on multiple sides of the market. It is made possible by virtue of the critical mass of users that are locked into the platform. In the Google Android case, 77 Google Android case, paras 235-237. the CCI noted several characteristics of search platforms, which were also discussed earlier in this chapter, namely:(i) Presence of status quo bias, i.e., users generally do not download competing search apps where they are presented with an existing pre-installed search app on their mobile devices. This is enhanced by the importance of pre-installation as a distribution channel (enforced through Google’s commercial arrangements), wherein a majority of the users tend to stick to the usage of the pre-loaded apps and do not download other apps which may be perceived to provide the same kind of services. (ii) Presence of barriers to entry and expansion in the form of significant investment required in terms of time and resources, such as acquiring and maintaining sufficient hardware to process the data required, hiring skilled developers to build the necessary algorithms and the software services, building a comprehensive search index, and most importantly, attracting and processing user queries. A key feature that is relevant for assessment in online search is the volume of search queries since the greater the number of search queries, the faster the ability of the platform to observe user behaviour and refine and update search results. Therefore, any competing search service needs to attract and process user queries to refine its algorithm for populating the most relevant search results. Further, there exists a positive feedback loop between the two sides of the search platform, i.e., general search services and online search advertising also operate as entry barriers. When a search engine does not have a sufficient scale on the search user side, the advertisers would not be willing to use the search advertising services of such a search engine. Therefore, the key factor that is the focal point of analysis in the online search market is access to user search queries or traffic, i.e., data. The analysis of CCI takes into account the fact that data, which is essentially a non-price parameter of competition, transcends into a crucial price factor because of its capacity to be monetized through valuable targeting and profiling services. This is primarily done through advertisements, as has been discussed in the next section. [D] Digital Advertising Platforms which attract consumers for free or negligible fee, target consumers by capitalizing on their data. This business model is called ‘digital advertising’ wherein, unlike traditional advertising, the firms are able to target consumers with personalized advertisements based on their data. Such targeted advertising is made possible because of profiling of consumers based on search habits and insights gained from their data, such as their browsing behaviour and interests, geographical location, and social, demographic and economic characteristics such as age, gender, income, etc. This data is available because consumers increasingly rely on ‘free’ services, such as location tracking, general search services, etc., in their daily lives which are offered by digital platforms, thereby generating vast swathes of data which can be used for creating user profiles. In Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., 78 Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., Case No. 41 of 2021. Google was found to be prima facie abusing its dominance in the market for online general web search services in India and market for online search advertising services in India by, inter alia, causing denial of fair advertising revenues to news publishers and non-sharing of data pertaining to the amount of revenue earned by Google by placing advertisements on the publisher’s websites and fairness thereof. In this context, the following observations of the CCI assume importance:(i) In consonance with its findings in the Google Search Bias case, 79 Google Search Bias case. the CCI observed that the market for online general web search services and the market for online search advertising services are distinct markets. This is because, first, online general web search services cannot be substituted with direct search options by typing the URL of websites in internet browsers, and accordingly, search engines become the first port of call for a user looking for information online. Second, online and offline advertising services are not comparable, and the characteristics, intended use and price of search and non-search advertising are different from one another.80 Digital News Publishers Association v. Alphabet Inc. and Ors., para. 16. (ii) The relevant market appeared to be characterized by entry barriers resulting from the significant investment required to develop sophisticated algorithms as well as to reach a sufficient scale of search queries. In addition, these markets also exhibited network effects wherein the online general web search and online search advertising are interdependent, and the increase in the number of users on the search engine increases the value of search advertising offered by Google for the advertisers.81 Ibid., paras 17-19. (iii) Discoverability of publishers is dependent on search results from Google. This dependence also implies that they primarily rely on the traffic generated by Google to sell advertising space on their respective websites.82 Ibid., para. 20. On this basis, the CCI observed that the alleged power imbalance between Google and digital news publishers flowed from the alleged position enjoyed by Google as a necessary trading partner for the latter. Google also offered its services as a vertically integrated entity which is present at each level of the digital advertising value chain, i.e., from the demand side to the supply side. Thus, it operated as a gateway for publishers and advertisers to interact with each other. As a result, on the face of it, publishers had no choice but to accept the terms and conditions imposed by Google. Consequently, the CCI directed the DG to investigate the lack of transparency, information asymmetry and other unfair impositions in the ad tech services provided by Google.83 Ibid., paras 22, 23, 32-41. The outcome of the investigation is likely to be an important factor in assessing competition issues in digital advertising. At the same time, it is relevant to mention that in Google LLC v. DRS Logistics P. Ltd. and Ors., 84 Google LLC v. DRS Logistics P. Ltd. and Ors. [2023], (Delhi High Court) FAO (OS) (COMM) 2/2022. the Delhi High Court noted the key role played by Google in the digital advertising value chain and held that Google is not a mere passive ‘intermediary’ in the advertising process but is an active participant at each step of the process.85 Ibid., paras 182-186. On this basis, it denied the safe harbour exemption which is a protection made available to intermediaries to prevent them from being held liable for third-party information on their platform. The principle may extend to competition law to conclude that platforms cannot shirk their liability by virtue of being mere intermediaries in any process. Therefore, together with the aspect of data discussed earlier, the takeaway is that the ability to monetize and generate revenue through advertisements (which is made possible by having access to large volumes of data) has become a focal point of analysis in almost all investigations pertaining to platform markets. Inevitably, each policy decision is related to some facet of data or advertising. [E] E-Commerce In its Market Study on E-Commerce in India,86 Market Study on E-Commerce in India, 2020, paras 30, 31. the CCI explained that in the goods category of e-commerce platforms, there exist two types of business models: (i) Marketplace Model – platform operators facilitate transactions between buyers and sellers and can fulfil the orders by handling logistics, deliveries and returns. (ii) Inventory-based Model – allows online retailers to own inventory and directly sell to consumers through their own platform. Further, some e-commerce platforms, besides providing marketplace/intermediation services to connect sellers and consumers, also provide related services such as warehousing facilities, transactional support services, promotion and advertising, centralized payment processing, shipment and delivery of goods, refund and replacement, etc. The nature of the fee charged varies across platforms and can be a fixed upfront fee or a commission per transaction. The other sources of revenue for such platforms are advertisements and customer subscription programs. The CCI discussed the role and emerging characteristics of e-commerce marketplaces in the market in the AIOVA v. Flipkart 87 AIOVA v. Flipkart, para. 22. by observing that e-commerce marketplaces function as a connecting link between buyers and sellers by providing them with a platform to interact and transact their business. Further, it noted that e-commerce companies now offer an integrated shopping experience: while such companies have started opening physical offline stores to offer online buyers the touch-and-feel experience, simultaneously, various offline retailers have started their online ventures or partnered with leading e-commerce companies to attract customers on the electronic marketplace. Thus, it can be said that online and offline businesses are increasingly becoming interdependent. Further, in Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and Amazon Seller Services Private Limited, 88 Delhi Vyapar Mahasangh v. Flipkart Internet Private Limited and Amazon Seller Services Private Limited, Case No. 40 of 2019, paras 16-26. the CCI held that Flipkart and Amazon are e-commerce entities, following a marketplace-based model of e-commerce as they provide online intermediation services to sellers on one side and consumers on the other. On the one hand, online intermediation services are key enablers of entrepreneurship by offering sellers access to new markets and increasing consumers’ choice of goods; on the other hand, they also result in growing dependence of businesses on these platforms. These platforms/marketplaces and the sellers selling on these platforms also operate at different stages of the vertical/supply chain. Consequently, the CCI found these platforms to be prima facie engaging in four types of anti-competitive agreements:(i) Preferred sellers on the marketplaces – Amazon and Flipkart have their own set of preferred sellers, and these preferred sellers have nexus with the e-commerce platforms either by way of direct or indirect ownership (through common investors, directors, shareholders, etc.) or some understanding. Hence, it was alleged that these preferred sellers are extension of the marketplaces, operating through different ‘proxy’ entities blessed with the support of these marketplaces. (ii) Exclusive launch of mobile phones – Only a few online sellers were observed to be selling exclusively launched smartphones either through Amazon or Flipkart. Thus, prima facie, an exclusive partnership was found between smartphone manufacturers and e-commerce platforms for exclusive launch of smartphone brands. (iii) Deep discounting – The CCI perused the prices for different smartphone brands sold through Flipkart and Amazon, i.e., original price and discounted price and observed that certain smartphone brands/models were available at significantly discounted price and were largely sold by the preferred sellers identified above. (iv) Preferential listing/promotion of private labels – The product listings of the preferred sellers dominate the first few pages of search results on the platforms, whereas the products with the same ratings sold by non-preferred sellers are listed on later pages. Hence, competition in the e-commerce marketplace may be influenced in favour of exclusive brands and sellers through higher discounts and preferential listing. Thus, exclusive launch coupled with preferential treatment to a few sellers and the discounting practices create an ecosystem that may lead to AAEC. Accordingly, the CCI directed the DG to investigate under section 3(1), read with section 3(4) of the Competition Act, whether the alleged exclusive arrangements with preferred sellers, deep-discounting and preferential listing were being used as an exclusionary tactic to foreclose competition and are resulting in AAEC. The order for investigation has also been upheld by the Supreme Court.89 Flipkart Internet Pvt. Ltd. v. Competition Commission of India and Ors. [2021], (SC) SLP No. 11558/2021. In order to understand why this order has far-reaching implications on e-commerce, it is relevant to highlight the key takeaways of the Market Study on E-Commerce in India in terms of the conduct of the e-commerce marketplace. Therein, the CCI found that the issues of lack of platform neutrality, unfair platform-to-business contract terms, exclusive contracts between online marketplace platforms and sellers/service providers, platform price parity restrictions and deep discounts may hinder the realization of the full pro-competitive potential of e-commerce. As a result, the following key takeaways were identified to mitigate any issues in e-commerce:(i) Increasing transparency to create incentives for competition and to reduce information asymmetry. This includes clearly setting out the platforms’ terms and conditions (including discount policy) without disclosure of any information that can enable or facilitate manipulation/exploitation of search results on the platform. Further, the basis of funding the discounts and the implications of participation/non-participation in the discount schemes must also be disclosed. (ii) Setting out a clear and transparent policy on data that is collected on the platform, the use of such data by the platform and also the potential and actual sharing of such data with third parties or related entities. [F] Aggregation [1] Online Cab Aggregation In Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. (Meru) v. Uber India Systems Pvt. Ltd. Ors.,90 Meru Travel Solutions Pvt. Ltd. (Meru) v. Uber India Systems Pvt. Ltd. Ors., Case No. 96 of 2015, paras 141-151. the CCI explained the model of cab aggregation as being digital platforms that connect riders to independent drivers through an app which takes a request for a ride from the prospective rider, searches for available (nearby) taxis on its platform, makes a match and puts the driver and the rider in contact with each other. An algorithm then sets the price, and the revenue is shared between the driver and cab aggregator. Leveraging the technology, the cab-aggregator model reduces transaction costs associated with matching dispersed drivers and riders which also means lower search cost for these two sides of participants. Unlike the traditional asset-owned model, where cabs are owned by radio taxi operators, cab aggregators like Uber and Ola rely on their network of driver partners with their own cars to provide ride services. In order to build a large network size (since a large network implies more riders and thereby more drivers), the platforms design the pricing structure so as to make ‘joining’ the network and ‘staying committed’ to it attractive to both sides. This was done through discounts and incentives offered to riders and drivers, respectively. However, as the network grows and reaches a critical mass, the need to offer discounts/incentives gets obviated. The CCI held that in a competitive market where Uber and Ola place competitive constraints on each other, such penetrative pricing strategies (where they price below cost) for the creation of a network seem to be a compelling business strategy to induce loyalty by offering incentives to driver in the early stages of network creation to attract cab-owning drivers. Thus, no reason was found for antitrust intervention. Further, unlike an e-marketplace platform or an online delivery app, in the cab-aggregator model, there is no opportunity for the rider or driver to choose each other, as may be possible in other digital platform transaction because once a rider books his/her ride at any given time, the same is accepted by an anonymous driver available in the area to whom Uber/Ola allocates such ride. Hence, the drivers do not have any individual identities or brand value vis-à-vis the consumers, e.g., a consumer buying through Zomato App, an online delivery app, may have a preference for a particular restaurant, or a consumer booking a hotel through MakeMyTrip may wish to know the options available in terms of their offerings and characteristics, etc. In the case of a cab aggregator, the consumers have no material information about the drivers available in their area of demand. As such, the rides offered by individual drivers through Uber or Ola App constitute a homogenous composite product where riders are indifferent between different drivers registered with a particular cab aggregator. Consequently, despite actually being digital platforms, the cab aggregators are perceived to be the service providers of the ‘ride’ as a product. On this basis, the CCI held that cab aggregators fall in the same market as the radio taxi service providers operating under the asset-owned model. In Meru Travels Solutions Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. CCI and Anr.,91 Meru Travels Solutions Pvt. Ltd. and Anr. v. CCI and Anr. [2022], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 19 of 2017. an appeal was filed against the CCI’s order in the Ola case which dismissed the claim of Ola’s abuse of dominance on account of its below-cost pricing strategies. The NCLAT held that Ola is a technology company in that it leverages smartphone/internet-based technology to provide the riders not just ease of booking and payment for the ride but also facilitates booking a taxi at a very short notice and almost anywhere in its area of operation. This ‘end to end’ provision of service from booking to alighting from the taxi with a GPS-based tracking system of the taxi – all provided a high degree of reliability and assurance of availability of taxis anywhere, including security to riders and enough demand to drivers. This is a new way of doing business, and Ola could translate this concept into reality. Hence, along with this technology, Ola provided ease of taxi bookings, rider security, payments and drivers’ welfare which made many riders comfortable with the network of Ola. Consequently, the customer incentives worked in conjunction with these facilities and conveniences to attract riders to Ola and a certain brand image was created and reinforced over a period of time. In order to compete with Uber which was present in the market at the same time, Ola had to calibrate and change its market strategy, including pricing, to respond to competition of Uber. On this basis, the CCI held that the pricing strategies of Ola were part of a broader strategy to open up a latent market and establish itself firmly in the market. Hence, it did not constitute abuse of dominance or predatory pricing. The cab-aggregator market is an example of non-intervention by the CCI because there was effective competition in the market between Uber and Ola. This goes on to show that CCI only intervenes when there is harm or likelihood of harm by virtue of the policies adopted by the incumbent platforms. As long as competition exists and is on merits, platform markets do not warrant an intervention merely because factors like network effects may be in existence. [2] Online Food Services Aggregation In the Zomato Swiggy case, Zomato and Swiggy were observed to be online food delivery platforms operating as online intermediaries for food ordering and delivery. The CCI directed an investigation under section 3(4) of the Competition Act into the following conduct:(i) A conflict of interest arose because of the presence of commercial interest in the downstream market. These platforms operated cloud kitchens and listed their own private brand which were competing with the downstream restaurant partners on the same platform. The platforms accorded them with preferential treatment in search ranking. Hence, this violated the principle of platform neutrality as the intermediaries themselves had a vested interest in the competition taking place on their platforms.92 MMT-Go case, paras 72-74. (ii) The price parity clauses (as discussed under APPAs) discourage the entry of other platforms because there can be no competition on price if prices are the same across all platforms. Similar to the discussions on e-commerce above, the outcome of this investigation will have a bearing on how CCI looks at platform neutrality, ranking and exploitation of data. If the trend of the MMT-Go case is followed, then the parity clauses imposed by Zomato and Swiggy may warrant intervention only if they are wide, but narrow parity clauses may be allowed to deal with the free-riding concerns of the platform. Having said that, the ambit of such clauses will be seen considering the industry and that there cannot be a one-size-fits-all approach. [3] Online Travel Services Aggregation The leading case discussing competition issues in the context of OTAs is the MMT-Go case. This case has already been discussed in Chapter 3 on Dominance. However, to recapitulate, the CCI relied on the following factors to conclude that MMT is dominant in the market for online intermediation services for booking hotels in India:(i) Barriers to entry and expansion inform the state of competitiveness in a market. In this case, the last entry in the market was several years ago, and thereafter, despite the market size growing manifold, there has not been a new entry.93 Ibid., para. 231. (ii) Due to the wide choice available on MMT-Go’s portal, consumers also prefer MMT-Go to other portals, which in turn incentivizes hotels/franchisee service providers to list on it. Further, MMT-Go offers several benefits and features which coupled with its market position have resulted in network effects. In turn, such network effects also create insurmountable entry barriers for existing as well as potential market participants.94 Ibid., para. 232. (iii) Commercial arrangements in the nature of APPAs, i.e., price and room parity obligations (discussed above under APPAs) further fortified the position of MMT-Go in the market as it caused a large number of consumers to also book hotels on the said website which then leads to a great influx of hotel owners who are willing to list their hotels on MMT-Go.95 Ibid., para. 233. Presence on MMT-Go was essential as it was a dominant platform and an important gateway for hotels to reach end consumers. Consequently, hotels did not have any real choice to leave the platform.96 Ibid., para. 249. (iv) Deep discounting practices, as discussed earlier, meant that competition between OTAs was reduced to their capacity to fund discounts. Since most of the bookings were made through the dominant platform, which was also the one charging the highest commission, the dominant platform was also the one which would end up being most capable of funding those discounts through the commissions earned. The CCI held that this will further make MMT-Go more attractive to end-consumers as compared to its rival OTAs, leading to more bookings through it and more commissions to fund more discount, in a vicious loop, thereby causing foreclosure of competition for other OTAs.97 Ibid., para. 255. While it may be argued that discounts are beneficial for the end-consumers, the net impact may be adverse when seen in light of higher commissions funding the higher discounts, leading to overall higher prices on which the discounts are applied.98 Ibid., para. 263. Based on the foregoing, the CCI held that the deep discounts, exclusivity condition and parity conditions, in conjunction, create an ecosystem that reinforces MMT-Go’s dominant position in the relevant market. This was a seminal decision as it set out the contours for dealing with parity clauses. Further, it also highlights how CCI collectively analyses the entire ecosystem when assessing the conduct of multi-sided intermediation platforms; the CCI does not look at conduct in a silo manner but looks at the collective impact on the market. Most importantly, this decision marks that merely because a certain policy is an industry practice (for instance, the parity and discounting obligations), it does not exonerate the platform yielding market power from the illegality of such a practice. Market assessment is dynamic, and firms need to be mindful of their market power and special responsibility by virtue of that market power, as that has a bearing on all the players at different stages in the market.

§6.05 TRENDS FROM DECISIONAL PRACTICE In this section, the key trends that have emerged from CCI’s decisional practice with regard to big tech and digital markets, as also discussed in the relevant previous chapters of this book, have been briefly summarized. [A] Exploitative and Exclusionary Abuse In the Google Android case, 99 Google Android case, paras 373-415. the exclusionary and exploitative consequences of Google’s conduct on the market were explained in detail, as discussed in Chapter 3. As an example, it is helpful to recapitulate one of the practices, i.e., the harm caused by Google’s commercial arrangements with OEMs, i.e., MADA, RSA, AFA/ACC. The CCI on the basis of the DG investigation concluded that these covenants are in the nature of imposition of unfair conditions on OEMs who have no choice but to accept the same. They resulted in anticompetitive harm, viz. pre-installation of the entire suit of GMS as well as prominent placement thereof as:(i) Due to these arrangements, competitors can only secure a relatively inferior positioning on the second or third screen of the device. Thus, they cannot achieve a more prominent display on the devices and remain inferior to Google’s placement, and Google has retained its competitive edge. (ii) The preinstallation of GMS suite as the default app on any device created a status quo bias in the mind of the users and helped Google attain a ‘must have status’. Consequently, rivals lose significant traffic and have no incentive to enter into contractual arrangements with OEMs as they would only have a secondary treatment on the devices. (iii) By foreclosing the market for rivals, these covenants also reduced the potential choice for users. (iv) The preinstallation requirement also constituted the imposition of a supplementary obligation on the OEMs. Hence, the cumulative impact of the restrictions posed by these covenants prohibited alternative vendors from outcompeting Google’s apps on merit. They also allowed Google to protect and strengthen its market position in the relevant markets, keeping in mind that its strategy rests on the reach, scale and market power of Android, which allows Google to have control over a vast majority of smart mobile devices that serve as key gateways to the internet. Recognizing Google’s gatekeeper position, the CCI held that the well-regarded benefits of the open-source system of Android cannot legitimize an exclusionary conduct that causes harm to competition in any specific area/market.100 Ibid., para. 470. In the Play Store case, 101 Play Store case, para. 273. Google was found to have engaged in exploitative and exclusionary conduct. A clear example of one such conduct was the impact of the mandatory commission charged by Google to app developers by making it necessary for them to use Google’s billing system for offering paid apps and paid in-app content, as illustrated in the following flowchart: It was found that Google’s own apps, such as YouTube, did not have to pay such a commission, and, in any case, if the commission was paid, it would be internalized. Hence, this illustrates how the platform can exploit users on one end of the market, i.e., app developers and also cause their exclusion in order to access the other side of the market, i.e., app users. Similar conduct has also been found in the prima facie Apple case, 102 Apple case, para. 28. wherein the CCI noted that Apple’s proprietary apps are competing with TPAP on the iOS platform. Resultantly, any commission or fee charged by Apple from these apps (for having access to the platform) would increase the cost of Apple’s competitors and thus might affect their competitiveness vis-à-vis Apple’s own apps (the fee in respect of which, in any case, would be internalized). This will lead to a competitive disadvantage in the downstream markets and limit the ability of the app developers to offer payment processing solutions of their choice to the users. Therefore, it is clear that CCI assesses the implications of the platform’s conduct in the upstream as well as downstream markets and does not look at it in isolation. The abusive conduct of platforms can turn exploitative and exclusionary when seen in light of the cumulative practices propagated by the platform in its ecosystem. [B] Special Responsibility In the context of digital platforms, especially those which act as gatekeepers and host other businesses on their platforms (e.g., online marketplaces), the special responsibility to prevent their conduct from impairing genuine undistorted competition becomes even more important. CCI has reiterated this principle in the context of the various decided cases against Google.103 Google Search Bias case; Play Store case. Particularly, in the Google Android case, 104 Google Android case, paras 557-561. the CCI observed that ‘Google being a dominant entity has the special responsibility not to impinge the process of competition and ensure fairness and reasonableness in its conduct…Dominant enterprises must not take unfair advantage of their position as such abusive conduct not only impacts the market as a whole but also may affect the entry and sustenance of other market participants into complementary markets associated with the platform.’ Hence, Google ought to be mindful of the exclusionary implications of the restrictions imposed on OEMs and should have used more competition-friendly alternatives. Hence, platforms, being gatekeepers of their respective markets, have a special responsibility to maintain a level playing field and ensure that their conduct does not have adverse consequences for other players in the market. [C] Efficiency Gains and Competition Harms in Mergers On the one hand, the characteristic features of digital markets have the potential to increase competition relative to traditional markets by allowing consumers to use multiple services in a single platform simultaneously, the ability to use digital tools to compare prices and features, etc. On the other hand, they also create risks of undesirable concentration by leveraging the advantages of economies of scale and scope, data advantages for incumbents, network effects, etc. These advantages and disadvantages get heightened in case of mergers since the strength of already powerful entities in markets with little competition virtually doubles, thereby making the merged entity into a behemoth with incomparable strength that cannot be challenged by existing smaller players. In the case of the Google/Jio combination,105 Google International LLC, Combination Registration No. C-2020/09/775, 11 Nov. 2020. there was an overlap between the activities of the parties in the spheres of mobile app distribution, supply of apps & mobile/web services, supply of advertisement services, and supply of mobile OS. Further, the OTT applications and content of Google and the telecommunication services offered by a subsidiary of Jio were found to be complementary/vertical to each other. Consequently, the CCI assessed the ability and incentive of both platforms to discriminate between competitors in their respective businesses and whether their access to each other’s resources created any undue advantages.106 Ibid., para. 10. In particular, Reliance (the parent company of Jio) was found to deploy KaiOS on its mobile devices, offering competition to Google’s Android OS, and the proposed business collaboration was likely to reduce the incentive for Reliance to continue with KaiOS-based devices. However, since Reliance confirmed that it would continue to sell KaiOS phones and shall not degrade the quality of these phones to favour the new smartphone to be made in collaboration with Google, the CCI concluded that the proposed combination did not raise any competition concerns in relation to supply of Android or customized OS.107 Ibid., para. 14. Another concern was data-backed market power, arising from possible data sharing between Google and Jio.108 Ibid., paras 30, 31. The CCI observed that the assessment in such instances needs to focus on the incentives of parties to pool or share their databank and monetize such data in the best possible means. In this case, since the proposed combination was an acquisition of 7.73% stake in Jio by Google, it was observed that it may not result in unrestricted access to each other’s resources, including user data. Nevertheless, the parties may have incentives to engage in mutually beneficial data sharing. The CCI consequently allowed the combination by observing that any anticompetitive conduct resulting from any data sharing or otherwise, in the future could be taken up under sections 3 and/or 4 of the Competition Act. Similarly, in the Facebook/Reliance 109 Jaadhu Holdings, LLC, Combination Registration No. C-2020/06/747. combination, CCI approved the acquisition of 9.99% stake by a wholly owned subsidiary of Facebook in Reliance Jio Infocomm Limited, which is the largest telecommunications company in India. The investment was aimed at collaborations between the two undertakings in the online advertising and e-commerce sectors: for instance, it envisaged a commercial arrangement to connect the users of WhatsApp with Reliance’s e-commerce marketplace, JioMart. Despite a recognition of how data-backed market power can allow firms to pool their data, leading to anti-competitive outcomes and have bearings on platform neutrality, CCI approved the combination while recognizing its pro-competitive effects and the growing synergies between the telecommunication industry and the digital technology sectors. While the parties submitted that they did not intend to share data, this did not have a bearing on CCI’s decision, even though there may have been certain complementary overlaps in the data possessed by Facebook and Reliance. In line with its decisional practice in combinations, CCI left the scope open for ex post review in the future in case of any anticompetitive effects. Therefore, the above cases highlight how CCI takes a pro-market balanced approach to regulation so as to focus on market synergies and pro-competitive efficiencies instead of distant possible anticompetitive harm. To date, CCI has not withdrawn any approval but has only subjected combinations that have anti-competitive potential alone to detailed review and subsequent modifications, as required in the factual circumstances. [D] Protection of Incentives to Innovate While it is important to keep a check on the anti-competitive and abusive potential of digital platforms, at the same time, excessive antitrust intervention can reduce the incentives of such platforms to innovate and compete in the market. This can be illustrated qua the Google Android case, 110 Google Android case, paras 530-557, 617.3. wherein the CCI directed that Google shall not deny access to its Play Services APIs to disadvantage OEMs, app developers and its existing or potential competitors. This would ensure the interoperability of apps between Android OS which complies with compatibility requirements of Google and Android Forks. This was done in light of the finding that Google used its control and proprietary rights over APIs to obstruct the development of alternative and competing Android fork versions, thereby hindering technical or scientific development, innovation, research and development. Further, by excluding these APIs from the Android OS and not making Google Play services available on forked versions of Android, Google prevented apps developed for Android from being ported onto the forked versions of Android without additional cost and investments. In appeal, the NCLAT111 Google LLC and Anr. v. Competition Commission of India [2023], (NCLAT) Competition Appeal (AT) No. 01 of 2023, paras 183-186. overturned CCI’s direction on the ground that the APIs which provide essential services to the apps which are part of the Play Store, are privately developed, maintained and continually updated by Google and are provided by Google to app developers and OEMs. Consequently, unhindered access to such proprietary items of Google cannot be given to app developers, OEMs and Google’s existing and potential competitors. Further, such proprietary software which is ‘developed through scientific and technical innovation should fetch value to Google and, therefore, remain an incentive for a technological company/Google to further carry out such development and monetize it through its commercial use’. Thus, unhindered access to the same cannot be allowed without going through necessary technical and commercial engagement with Google. The matter is pending appeal before the Supreme Court. This was also recognized by the CCI in the AIOVA v. Flipkart, 112 AIOVA v. Flipkart, para. 34. wherein it observed that since the technology-driven and marketplace-based e-commerce model is still evolving and relatively nascent, regard must be had to the growth potential as well as the efficiencies and consumer benefits that such markets can provide. Hence, any intervention in such markets needs to be carefully crafted lest it stifles innovation. CCI has refrained from acting as a price regulator. In the Play Store case,113 Play Store case, para. 325. CCI did not hold that the 15%-30% commission charged by Google through imposition of a mandatory GPBS on each transaction pertaining to a paid app/digital in-app purchase was excessive. Similarly, in the MMT-Go case, CCI did not give a finding on deep discounting by MMT-Go.114 MMT-Go case, para. 274. Thus, it is clear that CCI has refrained itself from acting as a price regulator or regulating the commercial wisdom of enterprises. Rather, the CCI has focused on freeing the market at every level of the value chain by removing bottlenecks and promoting dynamic efficiency and innovation in the market (such as by issuing remedial directions). Therefore, the stand taken by the CCI is pro-competition without interfering with the business decisions of the platforms.

§6.06 REGULATORY INTERVENTION There has been a significant growth of Indian markets and a paradigm shift in the way businesses operate. There have been multiple reasons which have been advanced which bring to the fore the different regulatory challenges facing the digital economy, which perhaps necessitate a new regulatory approach. Some of those factors have been discussed hereinabove. The rise of the digital economy has accelerated manifold due to the pandemic-induced lockdown in several countries. The regulators need to make sure that the steps towards recovery do not irreversibly alter markets and that competition principles can be respected along the way, especially by ‘gatekeeper’ firms that provide online intermediation services. An increasing part of economic activity is going to be facilitated by digital players and channelled through the internet, and a small number of firms have occupied key intermediation positions. This tectonic shift in markets is thus posing challenges for lawmakers and enforcers on how to address the consequent bargaining power imbalance and information asymmetry between platforms and their business users. This imbalance and the resulting concerns were elaborately discussed in the e-commerce market study of the CCI (discussed supra). Following the study, multiple issues have been raised before the CCI, showing that these digital platforms have, perhaps, in effect, become private regulators dictating terms and conditions to participants in their ecosystems. Even if the business users are uncomfortable with those terms, there are often few viable alternatives to some of the services offered. Strong network effects increase the value of a digital service for consumers and business users, and switching to newcomers is unattractive. Thus, it becomes very important that the rules of engagement between business users and platforms are such that this unequal power relative to other businesses in a vertical relationship is not exploited by platforms, and competition on merits is preserved. Increasingly, competition regulatory architecture must be mindful of the dynamics of these relationships rather than narrowly focusing on static price competition. The explosion of online commerce has changed the dynamics of consumer preferences and transactions. As a market regulator, it is thus imperative that the competition regulator ensures that all stakeholders get an opportunity to compete on merits and get a fair chance to be part of digital commerce. The search and comparison functionalities of the intermediary platforms, their reach and scale, and the network effects that work in their favour lead to huge consumer footfalls on these platforms, thereby making presence on these platforms critical for the visibility and competitive ability of sellers/service providers. These trends must not be ignored by lawmakers because the counterfactual – allowing privately owned, democratic legitimacy lacking digital platforms to set the rules of Indian e-commerce without identifying and mitigating the long-term risks and the economic harm they can cause- is a scenario which we cannot afford. Keeping the above in mind, the government of India constituted the Competition Law Review Committee in 2018, to examine and suggest the modifications in the Competition Act, which submitted its report to the government in 2019. After a review of these recommendations by the Parliamentary Standing Committee, several amendments were mooted, inter alia, relating to digital platforms. These have been discussed below. [A] Competition Law (Amendment) Act, 2022 So far, the following provisions have not yet been notified by the government. Only those amendments which are relevant to digital markets and platforms have been discussed below. The remaining amendments have been highlighted in the relevant chapters. [1] Deal Value Threshold The existing merger control framework in India is based on an asset and turnover threshold. As a result, a lot of combinations pertaining to digital markets were escaping antitrust scrutiny. This is because the business model in digital markets is such that they may fail to generate significant revenue in the initial years since the focus is typically on user growth in order to exploit the network effects of such markets. Further, such industries are asset light. Consequently, high-value transactions which did not meet the thresholds specified under section 5 of the Competition Act, were escaping CCI’s scrutiny. The most notable example is that of a slew of ‘killer acquisitions,’ i.e., acquisitions by dominant enterprises of nascent competitors, particularly in digital markets, such as the WhatsApp/Facebook merger, Zomato’s acquisition of Uber Eats, Flipkart’s acquisition of Jabong.com through its subsidiary Myntra, and Ola Cab’s acquisition of TaxiForSure. These deals were significant because the entities involved were tech aggregators with significant market power, but they escaped the scrutiny of the CCI despite likely competition concerns since they did not meet the existing asset/turnover threshold. In this background, a new deal value threshold has been introduced under section 5 of the Competition Act. This requires that in addition to the criteria of assets and turnover to determine the notifiability of a transaction, if the value of any transaction, in connection with the acquisition of any control, shares, voting rights, or assets of an enterprise, merger or amalgamation exceeds INR 2,000 crore, then it has to be notified to the CCI. The Proviso states that the which is being acquired or taken control of or merged or amalgamated must have substantial business operations in India. Through the CCI Draft (Combination) Regulations, 2023, CCI has defined ‘value of transaction’ in an expansive manner to include ‘every valuable consideration, whether direct or indirect, immediate or deferred, cash or otherwise’. Further, the target will be deemed to have ‘substantial business operations in India’ if at any point in time in the preceding twelve months of the transaction date, its: (i) users, subscribers, customers, or visitors were from India or; (ii) gross merchandise value was from India or turnover for the preceding financial year derived from all products or services was from India. Therefore, the swift framing of Draft Regulations immediately after introducing the amendments demonstrates India’s resolve and commitment to bring the merger regime at par with international best practices as well as more suited to the needs of India’s rapidly growing digital economy.

§6.07 DIGITAL COMPETITION ACT Anti-competitive behaviour in digital markets has been dealt with through ex-post provisions in competition law around the world, as it has been in India. However, the rise of digital markets has led to new challenges and complexities in the field of competition law. Traditional competition laws were primarily designed for physical markets, and they may not be fully equipped to address the unique features of digital markets. As a result, there is an emerging global consensus to identify and regulate what the Big Tech Report has termed as Systematically Important Digital Intermediaries (SIDIs), which refer to a small number of leading players in digital markets to ensure free and fair markets. [A] Digital Markets Act: The European Approach The EU’s Digital Markets Act (DMA) is a legislation from the European Commission intended to ensure a higher level of competition in European digital markets by defining gatekeeper platforms and laying down clearly their rights and obligations. It will become fully applicable by 2024. Under DMA, a platform can be designated as a gatekeeper if it:115 Digital Markets Act 2022, Article 2 read with Article 3. (i) Provides a core platform service that serves as an important gateway for business users to reach end users. ‘Core platform service’ includes any of the following: online intermediation services, online search engines, online social networking services, video-sharing platform services, number-independent interpersonal communications services, operating systems, web browsers, virtual assistants, cloud computing services and online advertising services (including any advertising networks, advertising exchanges and any other advertising intermediation services). (ii) Has a significant impact on the internal EU market, i.e., has an annual turnover of at least EUR 7.5 billion within the EU in each of the past three financial years or an average market valuation of at least EUR 75 billion in the past financial year, and it provides the same core platform service in at least three Member States. (iii) Enjoys an (established or expected) entrenched and durable position, i.e., the platform has on average a minimum of 45 million monthly end users established or located in the EU and at least 10,000 yearly business users established in the EU in each of the previous three financial years. Important prohibitions placed by the DMA on gatekeepers include restrictions on processing personal data of users for online advertising services without taking prior consent,116 DMA, Article 5(2)(a). preventing wide and narrow parity clauses,117 DMA, Article 5(3). preventing gatekeepers from bundling one core platform service with another core service,118 DMA, Article 5(8). restricting end users from switching between different apps and services,119 DMA, Article 6(6). etc. Some key obligations imposed by DMA on gatekeepers include allowing communication and access to content between platform users,120 DMA, Article 5(4)(5). ensuring price transparency in advertising in advertising intermediation services,121 DMA, Article 5(9), 5(10). allowing end users to change default settings on their devices and uninstall any apps on an OS besides essential apps,122 DMA, Article 6(3). etc. [B] Does India Need a Digital Competition Act? The Big Tech Report proposed a set of ex ante obligations against SIDIs to regulate the digital market in India. It has been argued that India’s digital ecosystem is likely to become the world’s largest, and to this end, it must also be open and innovative, as in a globalized world, interconnected digital markets will rapidly demonstrate monopolistic outcomes that prevent fair competition. This will restrict consumer choice, inhibit business users, and prevent the rise of dynamic new companies. Hence, a need was felt to introduce a new regulatory framework for SIDIs. This ignited a debate on whether India truly needs such ex ante regulation through separate guidelines or whether it is an attempt at mirroring the standards adopted by Europe. Long story short, the national and international developing jurisprudence on competition law in digital markets deals with the same gatekeepers and has the same objectives, i.e., to make the digital markets more competitive, protect consumer choice and innovation as well as limit the harm caused by the self-reinforcing position of the incumbent platforms. Although the effects-based approach, as reaffirmed by the NCLAT in the Google Android case and discussed in earlier parts of this book, allows sufficient scope for adjudicating the issues arising out of anti-competitive practices adopted by digital platforms, the traditional tools of assessment may not be able to analyse the peculiar characteristics of digital markets. This is because while the Competition Act was drafted in a comprehensive, inclusive and forward-thinking manner, it does change the fact that digital markets and platforms were simply not on the horizon at the time it was framed. This is why ex ante approach may be required instead of ex post analysis: since traditional investigations take time and digital platforms evolve rapidly, decisions may often lose their relevance since it fails to properly curb the anti-competitive effects in time. This, coupled with the fact that there are delays in market corrections due to the appeal process, paves the way for the entire discussion of having an ex ante approach to market corrections by digital gatekeepers. Thus, time is of the essence. At the same time, cautiousness is warranted in adopting an ex ante approach so as not to inadvertently stifle innovation in the market. Some of the key recommendations of the Big Tech Report for ex-ante regulation of SIDIs and digital gatekeepers are as follows:(i) SIDIs should not condition access to the platform or preferred status or placement on the platform on the purchase or use of other products or services offered by the platform that are not part of or intrinsic to the platform. (ii) SIDIs must not favour its own offers over the offers of its competitors when mediating access to supply and sales markets, in particular, when presenting its own offers in a more favourable manner; and when exclusively pre-installing its own offers on devices or integrating them in any other way in offers provided by the platform. (iii) SIDIs should not force business users or end users to subscribe to or register with any further services as a condition for being able to use, access, sign up for or register with any of that platform’s core platform service. (iv) SIDIs should not process personal data of users for providing online advertising services using services of third parties that make use of core services of the platform; combine personal data from the relevant core service of the platform with personal data from any further core services or from any other services provided by the platform or with personal data from third-party services; cross-use personal data from the relevant core service in other services provided separately by the platform, including other core services of the platform, and vice-versa; and sign in end users to other services of the platform in order to combine personal data, unless the end-user has been presented with the specific choice and has given consent. (v) SIDIs should pre-inform the CCI of any intended concentration, where the merging entities or the target of concentration provide services in the digital sector or enable the collection of data, irrespective of whether it is notifiable to the CCI or not. (vi) SIDIs should not prevent business users from offering the same products or services to end users through third-party online intermediation services or through their own direct online sales channel at prices or conditions that are different from those offered through the online intermediation services of the platform. (vii) SIDIs must provide any third-party undertaking providing online search engines, at their request, with access to fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms to rank, query, click and view data in relation to free and paid search generated by end users on its online search engines.

§6.08 CONCLUSION It is clear that the CCI has been quick to adapt to the emerging issues of the digital markets by undertaking timely investigations, approving combinations and swiftly bringing in regulatory changes. It is imperative for competition regulation to keep pace with the digital economy which brings both opportunities for growth and challenges to tackle. As platforms tend to become the necessary gateways of distribution due to a combination of the features outlined above, a question arises regarding what extent such a platform can be allowed to protect its ecosystem and, if so, whether there can be any limits to the same. In the end, it is fair to conclude by saying that in any market, genuine and undistorted market forces should ultimately inform the functioning of the state of competition.

title: Communication from the Commission – Commission Notice on the definition of the relevant market for the purposes of Union competition law
container_title: Selected Materials from the European Union

sections.text: EU source text (PDF)

sections.title: 1. INTRODUCTION
sections.text: 1. INTRODUCTION 1.1 Purpose of this Notice 1. The Commission uses the concept of market definition in its enforcement of Union competition law, namely: (a) the enforcement of antitrust rules under Articles 101 and 102 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (‘TFEU’) pursuant to Council Regulation (EC) No 1/20031 Council Regulation (EC) No 1/2003 of 16 December 2002 on the implementation of the rules on competition laid down in Articles 81 and 82 of the Treaty (OJ L 1, 4.1.2003, p. 1).; (b) merger control pursuant to Council Regulation (EC) No 139/20042 Council Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 of 20 January 2004 on the control of concentrations between undertakings (OJ L 24, 29.1.2004, p. 1). (‘the Merger Regulation’). (c) the enforcement of equivalent provisions set out in the Agreement on the European Economic Area3 Throughout this Notice, references to Articles 101 and 102 TFEU and to the Merger Regulation, in particular Article 2 thereof, are to be understood as referring to the respective equivalent provisions in the EEA Agreement, see Articles 53 and 54 of and Annex XIV to the EEA Agreement. (‘the EEA Agreement’). 2. The Commission generally defines the relevant market in those cases4 In general, market definition plays less of a role in the Commission’s enforcement practice under Article 106 and 107 TFEU. In those cases, the assessment focusses on the industry/sector concerned rather than identification of competitive constraints faced by the undertakings. Nevertheless, when considerations of market power and accordingly of the relevant market are raised in particular cases pursued under Article 106 or Article 107 TFEU, the approach outlined in this Notice may be relevant for the assessment. In view of the more limited application of market definition in these two types of assessments, this Notice will not discuss their specific aspects further. where it is important to appraise the relative competitive strength of undertakings5 In Union competition law, an undertaking is any entity engaged in an economic activity, namely an activity consisting in offering goods or services on a given market, regardless of its legal status and the way in which it is financed. See judgment of 23 April 1991, Höfner and Elser v Macrotron, C-41/90, EU:C:1991:161, paragraph 21, and judgment of 12 December 2006, SELEX Sistemi Integrati v Commission, T-155/04, EU:T:2006:387, paragraph 50.. 3. The purpose of this Notice is to provide guidance on how the Commission applies the concept of relevant market in its enforcement of Union competition law. Competition policy preserves well-functioning markets and addresses market failures, thereby contributing to the twin green and digital transitions and the resilience of the single market6 See “A competition policy fit for new challenges”, Communication from the Commission to the European Parliament, the Council, the European Economic and Social Committee and the Committee of the Regions, COM (2021) 713, 18 November 2021, page 6.. It aims to ensure that markets remain competitive, open and dynamic. Accordingly, competition policy can contribute to preventing excessive dependency and increasing the resilience of the Union economy by enabling strong and diversified supply chains7 Ibid, page 16., and can complement the Union’s regulatory framework on environmental sustainability by taking into account sustainability factors to the extent relevant to the competition assessment, including as part of market definition. Against that background, the updated guidance provided in this Notice takes into account the significant developments of the past twenty years. These include digitalisation and new ways of offering goods and services, as well as the increasingly interconnected and globalised nature of commercial exchanges. 4. By publishing the methodology that it follows and by indicating the main criteria and evidence on which it relies when defining relevant markets, the Commission aims to increase the transparency of its policy and decision-making when applying Union competition law. The Commission also aims to reduce the burden on the resources of its own services and those of external stakeholders, making competition assessments more efficient. 5. Increasing the transparency and consequently the predictability of the Commission’s assessments under Union competition law will also increase legal certainty for undertakings and their advisers. They will be able to better assess the scope of the relevant markets and the likelihood that the Commission may identify competition concerns in a particular case. They will be able to take these factors into account in their internal decision-making when contemplating, for instance, acquiring other undertakings, creating joint ventures, concluding certain agreements, or engaging in certain behaviour unilaterally. The guidance provided in this Notice will also assist undertakings in anticipating the type of information the Commission considers relevant for the purposes of market definition. 1.2 Role of market definition 6. Market definition is a tool that the Commission uses to identify and define the boundaries of competition between undertakings. The main purpose of market definition is to identify in a systematic way the effective and immediate competitive constraints faced by the undertakings involved8 For the purposes of this Notice, an ‘undertaking involved’ is an undertaking whose competitive strength is being assessed. These are generally the parties to the agreement in investigations under Article 101 TFEU; the undertaking(s) being investigated in Article 102 TFEU cases; and the parties to the concentration in investigations under the Merger Regulation. It can also be the complainant in a competition case, an undertaking in an upstream or downstream market, or any other undertaking relevant to the competition case, depending on the facts of the case at hand. when they offer particular products9 For the purpose of this Notice, the term ‘product’ is used as shorthand to also comprise services or technologies, where appropriate. The term ‘production’ correspondingly also covers the provision or the supply of such services or the licensing or development of technologies. in a particular area. Market definition leads to the identification of the relevant competitors of the undertaking(s) involved when they offer those products, as well as the relevant customers. Only products that exert effective and immediate competitive constraints within the relevant timeframe form part of the same relevant market as those of the undertaking(s) involved, while other less effective, or merely potential, constraints are considered as part of the competitive assessment. 7. The same considerations apply when defining relevant markets for the purchase of particular products in a particular area (‘purchasing markets’)10 See, for example, cases M.10201 Ahold Delhaize/Deen Assets, paragraphs 20-25; M.9847 Aldi/FPLPH Assets, paragraphs 19-22 and M.9409 Aurubis/Metallo, section 7.1. The position taken by the Commission on market definition in any of the cases quoted in this Notice does not prejudge the outcome of its market definition assessments in future cases, as explained in paragraph 14.. In that case, the main purpose of market definition is to identify in a systematic way the effective and immediate competitive constraints faced by the undertaking(s) involved when they purchase those products11 Whether the Commission analyses the relative competitive strength of undertaking(s) that purchase particular products in a particular area depends on the case and the possible theories of harm. Further detail on such theories of harm can be found in the Commission’s guidelines on substantive assessments in competition cases – see, for example, the Commission’s guidelines on the applicability of Article 101 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union to horizontal co-operation agreements (OJ C 259, 21.7.2023, p. 1; ‘Guidelines for the assessment of horizontal cooperation agreements’), paragraphs 273-309 and the Commission’s guidelines on the assessment of horizontal mergers under the Council Regulation on the control of concentrations between undertakings (OJ C 31, 5.2.2004, p. 5; ‘Horizontal Merger Guidelines’), paragraphs 61-63. See, for example, case M.9409 Aurubis/Metallo, paragraphs 376 and 397-398.. Accordingly, market definition leads to the identification of the relevant competitors of the undertaking(s) involved when they purchase the products, as well as the relevant suppliers. The remainder of this Notice will not discuss purchasing markets further, but the guidance set out in this Notice also applies to purchasing markets, taking into account their specificities12 For example, in purchasing markets the substitution assessment focuses on alternatives available to suppliers, rather than on alternatives available to customers. and the facts of the case at hand. 8. The Commission generally uses market definition where there is a need to assess the relative competitive strength of undertakings as part of the competitive assessment13 For the purposes of this Notice, the term ‘competitive assessment’ means those parts of the Commission’s substantive assessment of an issue of Union competition law that do not relate to market definition. That includes in particular the assessment of the existence of a dominant position, where applicable, and the assessment of the likely effects on competition of the conduct of the undertaking(s) involved or of a concentration. and, most notably, to assess whether an undertaking holds market power14 According to the Guidelines for the assessment of horizontal cooperation agreements, footnote 40, “[m]arket power is the ability to profitably maintain prices above competitive levels for a period of time or to profitably maintain output in terms of product quantities, product quality and variety or innovation below competitive levels for a period of time.”. Market definition is thus an intermediate tool to structure and facilitate the competitive assessment in appropriate cases and is not a mandatory step in all assessments under Union competition law. When subsequently conducting the competitive assessment and analysing market power, the Commission carries out an overall assessment of all relevant constraints on the undertaking(s) involved in the relevant product and geographic markets, which may include an assessment of barriers to entry or expansion, the impact of scale economies (which may include those that may be drawn from out-of-market activities) or network effects, access to specific assets and inputs, as well as product differentiation15 For further explanations in the Commission’s guidelines on substantive assessments in competition cases, see for instance the Horizontal Merger Guidelines, sections III and IV. See also the Guidelines for the assessment of horizontal cooperation agreements, e.g. paragraph 236.. That assessment may also include sufficiently foreseeable changes in such constraints when the case calls for a forward-looking assessment. As an intermediate step in the overall assessment process, it is necessary for market definition to be up-to-date at the time of the relevant conduct or concentration and based on facts, as further explained in paragraph 14. 9. The Commission’s use of market definition is closely related to the objectives pursued by the various Union competition law instruments: (a) in assessments under Article 102 TFEU, the Commission generally defines the relevant market when assessing the existence of a dominant position16 Judgment of 21 February 1973, Europemballage and Continental Can v Commission, C-6/72, EU:C:1973:22, paragraph 32.; (b) in assessments under the Merger Regulation, the Commission regularly defines the relevant market when assessing the effects on competition of a concentration17 Judgment of 21 February 1973, Europemballage and Continental Can v Commission, C-6/72, EU:C:1973:22, paragraph 32; judgment of 31 March 1998, France and Others v Commission (‘Kali & Salz’), joined cases C-68/94 and C-30/95, EU:C:1998:148, paragraph 143; judgment of 6 June 2002, Airtours v Commission, T-342/99, EU:T:2002:146, paragraph 19; judgment of 7 May 2009, NVV and Others v Commission, T-151/05, EU:T:2009:144, paragraph 51. Where relevant, assessing the effects on competition of a concentration can include an assessment of potential efficiencies, such as those derived from cost savings due to increased scale, and in particular whether the efficiencies would benefit consumers in those relevant markets where it is otherwise likely that competition concerns would occur, Horizontal Merger Guidelines, paragraph 79; referenced in paragraphs 53 and 115 of the Non-Horizontal Merger Guidelines.; (c) in assessments under Article 101 TFEU, the Commission uses market definition in particular to determine whether an appreciable restriction of competition exists or to establish whether the condition in Article 101(3), point (b), TFEU for an exemption from the application of Article 101(1) TFEU is met18 The Union Courts have held in this context that there is an obligation for the Commission to define the market in a decision applying Article 101 TFEU where it is impossible, without such a definition, to determine whether the behaviour at issue has as its object or effect the prevention, restriction or distortion of competition within the internal market, judgment of 6 July 2000, Volkswagen v Commission, T-62/98, EU:T:2000:180, paragraph 230; judgment of 8 July 2004, Mannesmannröhren-Werke v Commission, T-44/00, EU:T:2004:218, paragraph 132. See also paragraph 10 for further examples where market definition under Article 101 TFEU would be relevant.. In practice, the Commission tends to use market definition when assessing agreements19 Unless otherwise indicated, references in this Notice to agreements in the context of Article 101 TFEU include concerted practices and decisions by associations of undertakings. that have as their effect the prevention, restriction or distortion of competition. By contrast, the Commission usually does not define the relevant market when assessing agreements that have as their object the prevention, restriction or distortion of competition, such as cartel agreements, and is under no obligation to do so20 Judgment of 8 July 2004, Mannesmannröhren-Werke v Commission, T-44/00, EU:T:2004:218, paragraph 132; judgment of 28 June 2016, Telefónica v Commission, T-216/13, EU:T:2016:369, paragraph 214.. 10. Market definition makes it possible to calculate market shares, which the Commission may use, among other elements, to assess an undertaking’s competitive strength for the purposes of the competitive assessment. When analysing market power, the Commission may also look at trends which are likely to continue or foreseeable developments in relation to such market shares, as well as at constraints that may not be evident from market shares alone, as further explained in paragraphs 106 and 113. The Commission may also use market shares as a first screening tool to assess whether competition concerns may arise. Market share thresholds are one of the parameters used to determine the scope of the block exemption regulations applying Article 101(3) TFEU to certain categories of agreements21 The market share thresholds limit the applicability of the safe harbour provided by the block exemption to agreements between undertakings holding a share in the relevant market(s) that does not exceed the thresholds set out in the regulations. See, for example, Article 3 of Commission Regulation (EU) 2022/720 of 10 May 2022 on the application of Article 101(3) of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union to categories of vertical agreements and concerted practices, C/2022/3015 (OJ L 134, 11.5.2022, p. 4), Article 6 of Commission Regulation (EU) 2023/1066 of 1 June 2023 on the application of Article 101(3) of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union to certain categories of research and development agreements (OJ L 143, 2.6.2023, p. 9), Article 3 of Commission Regulation (EU) 2023/1067 of 1 June 2023 on the application of Article 101(3) of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union to certain categories of specialisation agreements (OJ L 143, 2.6.2023, p. 20).; to assess whether there may be an effect on trade under Articles 101 and 102 TFEU22 Commission Notice: Guidelines on the effect on trade concept contained in Articles 81 and 82 of the Treaty (‘Guidelines on the effect on trade’) (OJ C 101, 27.4.2004, p. 81), section 2.4. The Commission may use market shares to assess whether there may be an effect on trade, but that assessment does not necessarily require that relevant markets be defined and market shares calculated (see paragraph 48 of the Guidelines on the effect on trade), and the Commission is under no obligation to do so., and to identify concentrations deemed from the outset not to raise competition concerns under the Merger Regulation with respect to any markets or with respect to certain markets23 See in this regard the definition of ‘affected markets’ and of markets ‘in which the notified concentration may have a significant impact’ in paragraph 25(g) and Section 6.3 of Annex I to Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2023/914 of 20 April 2023 implementing the Merger Regulation (‘the Merger Implementing Regulation’) (OJ L 119, 5.5.2023, p. 22), and the Commission Notice on a simplified treatment for certain concentrations under the Merger Regulation (OJ C 160, 5.5.2023, p. 1), paragraphs 5 and 8.. 1.3 General principles of market definition 11. The Commission applies a number of principles when it defines relevant markets for the purpose of enforcing Union competition law. Those principles are outlined in paragraphs 12 to 21. 12. First, in line with the case law of the Court of Justice and the General Court of the European Union (‘the Union Courts’) and the Commission’s case practice, the relevant market within which the Commission appraises competition dynamics typically comprises a product and a geographic dimension24 Judgment of 14 February 1978, United Brands v Commission, C-27/76, EU:C:1978:22, paragraphs 10 and 11.. (a) The relevant product market comprises all those products that customers regard as interchangeable or substitutable to the product(s) of the undertaking(s) involved, based on the products’ characteristics, their prices and their intended use, taking into consideration the conditions of competition and the structure of supply and demand on the market25 Judgment of 30 January 2020, Generics (UK) and Others, C-307/18, EU:C:2020:52, paragraph 129; and judgment of 13 February 1979, Hoffmann-La Roche v Commission, C-85/76, EU:C:1979:36, paragraph 51. See also paragraph 25(b) of Annex I to the Merger Implementing Regulation.. (b) The relevant geographic market comprises the geographic area in which the undertakings involved supply or demand relevant products, in which the conditions of competition are sufficiently homogeneous for the effects of the conduct or concentration under investigation to be able to be assessed, and which can be distinguished from other geographic areas, in particular because conditions of competition are appreciably different in those areas26 Judgment of 30 September 2003, Cableuropa and Others v Commission, T‑346/02 and T‑347/02, EU:T:2003:256, paragraph 115; judgment of 7 May 2009, NVV and Others v Commission, T‑151/05, EU:T:2009:144, paragraph 52; and judgment of 14 February 1978, United Brands v Commission, C-27/76, EU:C:1978:22, paragraph 11. See also Article 9(7) of the Merger Regulation and paragraph 25(c) of Annex I to the Merger Implementing Regulation.. 13. In certain cases, temporal considerations may also be relevant when defining the relevant market, for example where factors such as seasonality or peak/off-peak time considerations substantially affect customer preferences or the structure of supply27 Temporal considerations were relevant for market definition, for example, in case M.5467 RWE/Essent, paragraph 32, where the Commission distinguished between peak and off-peak hours for the generation and wholesale supply of electricity, and in case M.8869 Ryanair/Laudamotion, paragraphs 96 and 97, where it distinguished between the summer and winter season in air passenger transport.. 14. Second, market definition is based on the facts of the case28 EFTA Court judgment of 5 May 2022, Telenor and Telenor Norge v EFTA Surveillance Authority, E-12/20, paragraph 173: “market definition must be made on a case-by-case basis”.. Relevant markets within the meaning of Union competition law differ from sector to sector, at different levels of the supply chain and sometimes across geographic areas. Where past Commission decisions concerning a specific market exist, the Commission may start its analysis from such prior decisions and verify whether the definition of the relevant market used in those past decisions may be applied to the case at hand. However, the Commission is not bound to apply the definition of a relevant market from its past decisions in future cases29 The General Court clarified in this respect in its judgment of 7 May 2009, NVV and Others v Commission, T‑151/05, EU:T:2009:144, paragraph 136: “In particular, the applicants cannot have entertained such a legitimate expectation on the ground that the Commission had defined markets in a particular way in a previous decision, since the Commission – and, a fortiori, the Court – is not bound by the findings made in such a decision”; see also judgment of 14 December 2005, General Electric v Commission, T-210/01, EU:T:2005:456, paragraph 120; judgment of 18 May 2022, Wieland-Werke v Commission, T-251/19, EU:T:2022:296, paragraph 79; judgment of 13 May 2015, Niki Luftfahrt v Commission, T-162/10, EU:T:2015:283, paragraph 144; judgment of 25 March 2015, Slovenská pošta v Commission, T-556/08, EU:T:2015:189, paragraph 197; judgment of 23 May 2019, KPN v Commission, T-370/17, EU:T:2019:354, paragraph 79. See also EFTA Court Judgment of 5 May 2022, Telenor and Telenor Norge v EFTA Surveillance Authority, E-12/20, paragraph 97. and will always be attentive to possible changes driven by broader trends such as digitalisation, shifts in value chains or in sourcing by customers, or developments in the degree of globalisation of commercial exchanges. 15. Third, when defining the relevant market, the Commission takes into account the various parameters of competition that customers consider relevant in the area and period assessed.
